text: “President, I protest to you with outrage—this is a counterfeit, a deceit. You are aware the accused is my daughter’s husband. My daughter, and all she holds precious, mean more to me than my own existence. Who is this lying plotter, and where is he, who claims I accuse my child’s husband!”
source_text: “President, I indignantly protest to you that this is a forgery and a fraud. You know the accused to be the husband of my daughter. My daughter, and those dear to her, are far dearer to me than my life. Who and where is the false conspirator who says that I denounce the husband of my child!”

text: Slightly aware of the smoky lamps; of the men, pipes clenched in their teeth, idly handling worn cards and faded dominoes; of a single workman, stripped to the waist, arms bare and skin blackened with soot, reading a newspaper aloud while the rest listened; of the weapons carried or set down close at hand; of the few customers slumped forward in sleep, their high-shouldered, shaggy black coats making them look, in that posture, like dozing bears or hounds—the two foreign customers stepped up to the counter and indicated what they required.
source_text: Slightly observant of the smoky lights; of the people, pipe in mouth, playing with limp cards and yellow dominoes; of the one bare-breasted, bare-armed, soot-begrimed workman reading a journal aloud, and of the others listening to him; of the weapons worn, or laid aside to be resumed; of the two or three customers fallen forward asleep, who in the popular high-shouldered shaggy black spencer looked, in that attitude, like slumbering bears or dogs; the two outlandish customers approached the counter, and showed what they wanted.

text: As the light waned toward evening and the atmosphere, briefly lucid enough to reveal the distant shoreline of France, thickened once more with haze and damp, a corresponding gloom settled upon Mr. Lorry’s spirit. When dusk had fallen and he lingered by the hearth in the coffee-room, waiting for his supper just as he had waited for his morning meal, his imagination worked feverishly, delving, delving, delving into the glowing crimson embers.
source_text: As the day declined into the afternoon, and the air, which had been at intervals clear enough to allow the French coast to be seen, became again charged with mist and vapour, Mr. Lorry’s thoughts seemed to cloud too. When it was dark, and he sat before the coffee-room fire, awaiting his dinner as he had awaited his breakfast, his mind was busily digging, digging, digging, in the live red coals.

text: “Well, my dear,” said Miss Pross, with a firm nod, “the plain truth is, I am a loyal subject of His Most Gracious Majesty King George the Third.” At the name, she dipped in a curtsey. “And as such, my motto is: Blast their schemes, Thwart their deceitful games, In him our trust remains, God save the King!”
source_text: “Well, my sweet,” said Miss Pross, nodding her head emphatically, “the short and the long of it is, that I am a subject of His Most Gracious Majesty King George the Third;” Miss Pross curtseyed at the name; “and as such, my maxim is, Confound their politics, Frustrate their knavish tricks, On him our hopes we fix, God save the King!”

text: “May kind and forgiving Heaven prevent it!” exclaimed Miss Pross. “I would sooner never lay eyes on you again, my dear Solomon, though I have always loved you faithfully and always will. Speak just one tender word to me, and assure me there is no resentment or distance between us, and I will keep you no longer.”
source_text: “The gracious and merciful Heavens forbid!” cried Miss Pross. “Far rather would I never see you again, dear Solomon, though I have ever loved you truly, and ever shall. Say but one affectionate word to me, and tell me there is nothing angry or estranged between us, and I will detain you no longer.”

text: He assisted Mr. Lorry in bundling himself into several layers of coats and cloaks, then accompanied him out of the snug warmth of the ancient Bank and into the foggy chill of Fleet-street. “Give my affection to Lucie, and to dear little Lucie,” Mr. Lorry said as they parted, “and guard them carefully until I return.” As the carriage trundled off, Charles Darnay gave a doubtful smile and shook his head.
source_text: He helped Mr. Lorry to wrap himself in a number of coats and cloaks, and went out with him from the warm atmosphere of the old Bank, into the misty air of Fleet-street. “My love to Lucie, and to little Lucie,” said Mr. Lorry at parting, “and take precious care of them till I come back.” Charles Darnay shook his head and doubtfully smiled, as the carriage rolled away.

text: Mr. Lorry was well aware of Miss Pross’s fiercely protective nature, but he had also come to understand that beneath her odd exterior lay one of those selfless souls—found only among women—who, out of sheer devotion and admiration, willingly enslave themselves to youth they no longer possess, to beauty they never had, to talents they were never blessed with, to radiant hopes that never brightened their own shadowed lives. He had seen enough of the world to recognize that nothing surpasses the heart’s loyal service; given so freely and untouched by any hint of greed, he held it in such profound esteem that, in the private moral reckonings he made—as we all do, in our own ways—he placed Miss Pross far closer to the angels of the lower spheres than many ladies infinitely more polished by both birth and artifice, who kept accounts at Tellson’s.
source_text: Mr. Lorry knew Miss Pross to be very jealous, but he also knew her by this time to be, beneath the service of her eccentricity, one of those unselfish creatures--found only among women--who will, for pure love and admiration, bind themselves willing slaves, to youth when they have lost it, to beauty that they never had, to accomplishments that they were never fortunate enough to gain, to bright hopes that never shone upon their own sombre lives. He knew enough of the world to know that there is nothing in it better than the faithful service of the heart; so rendered and so free from any mercenary taint, he had such an exalted respect for it, that in the retributive arrangements made by his own mind--we all make such arrangements, more or less--he stationed Miss Pross much nearer to the lower Angels than many ladies immeasurably better got up both by Nature and Art, who had balances at Tellson’s.

text: To Soho, accordingly, Mr. Ashcombe carved a path from the Inns of Court, in those first fresh days of the Long Vacation when its newness still shimmered over the city. Anyone observing him thrusting into Soho while he was still on the eastern verge of Chancery Lane, barrelling along the footway in his overblown manner and shouldering aside all feebler souls, would have marked at once how secure and solid a man he was.
source_text: Towards Soho, therefore, Mr. Stryver shouldered his way from the Temple, while the bloom of the Long Vacation’s infancy was still upon it. Anybody who had seen him projecting himself into Soho while he was yet on Saint Dunstan’s side of Temple Bar, bursting in his full-blown way along the pavement, to the jostlement of all weaker people, might have seen how safe and strong he was.

text: It did not take him long to realize that addressing the man was worse than futile, for any insistence only deepened his unease. He gave up that effort by the close of the first afternoon, choosing instead to remain constantly in his line of sight—a wordless objection to the delusion that had seized him, or was seizing him. So he stayed in his chair beside the casement, occupied with books and letters, and conveyed by every gentle and unforced means he could devise that this was a place of liberty.
source_text: He was not long in discovering that it was worse than useless to speak to him, since, on being pressed, he became worried. He abandoned that attempt on the first day, and resolved merely to keep himself always before him, as a silent protest against the delusion into which he had fallen, or was falling. He remained, therefore, in his seat near the window, reading and writing, and expressing in as many pleasant and natural ways as he could think of, that it was a free place.

text: For several months, Miss Pross and Mr. Cruncher had served as the household’s provisioners—she bearing the purse, he the basket. Each afternoon, around the hour when the streetlamps were kindled, they set out together on this errand, selecting and bringing back whatever was required. Though Miss Pross, thanks to her many years among a French family, might have absorbed their tongue as readily as her own had she wished, she had no such wish; consequently she understood no more of that “gibberish” (as she liked to term it) than Mr. Cruncher did. Her method of shopping was therefore to thrust a bare noun at a shopkeeper, without any polite preamble, and if it failed to name the desired object, to scan the premises, seize the item, and grip it fast until the transaction was complete. She invariably struck her bargain by displaying, as the fair price, one finger fewer than the merchant held aloft, no matter what his tally.
source_text: For some months past, Miss Pross and Mr. Cruncher had discharged the office of purveyors; the former carrying the money; the latter, the basket. Every afternoon at about the time when the public lamps were lighted, they fared forth on this duty, and made and brought home such purchases as were needful. Although Miss Pross, through her long association with a French family, might have known as much of their language as of her own, if she had had a mind, she had no mind in that direction; consequently she knew no more of that “nonsense” (as she was pleased to call it) than Mr. Cruncher did. So her manner of marketing was to plump a noun-substantive at the head of a shopkeeper without any introduction in the nature of an article, and, if it happened not to be the name of the thing she wanted, to look round for that thing, lay hold of it, and hold on by it until the bargain was concluded. She always made a bargain for it, by holding up, as a statement of its just price, one finger less than the merchant held up, whatever his number might be.

text: “You foresee my words, though you cannot grasp the depth of my conviction, the intensity of my feeling, without understanding the hidden chambers of my heart, and the burdens of hope, fear, and longing it has carried for so long. Dear Doctor Manette, I love your daughter with a tender, selfless, unwavering devotion. If love has ever truly existed, then I love her. You yourself have known love; let that past love plead on my behalf!”
source_text: “You anticipate what I would say, though you cannot know how earnestly I say it, how earnestly I feel it, without knowing my secret heart, and the hopes and fears and anxieties with which it has long been laden. Dear Doctor Manette, I love your daughter fondly, dearly, disinterestedly, devotedly. If ever there were love in the world, I love her. You have loved yourself; let your old love speak for me!”

text: So, Saint Antoine was deep in his wine-soaked mood until noon. At the peak of midday, two dust-covered men walked through his streets and beneath his swaying lamps: one was Monsieur Defarge, the other a road-mender in a blue cap. Parched and dusty, the pair stepped into the wine-shop. Their coming had sparked something like a blaze in Saint Antoine’s heart, spreading swiftly as they moved, and it quivered and danced in the faces at nearly every door and window. Still, nobody trailed behind them, and no voice broke the silence when they entered the wine-shop, though every gaze in the room fixed upon them.
source_text: Thus, Saint Antoine in this vinous feature of his, until midday. It was high noontide, when two dusty men passed through his streets and under his swinging lamps: of whom, one was Monsieur Defarge: the other a mender of roads in a blue cap. All adust and athirst, the two entered the wine-shop. Their arrival had lighted a kind of fire in the breast of Saint Antoine, fast spreading as they came along, which stirred and flickered in flames of faces at most doors and windows. Yet, no one had followed them, and no man spoke when they entered the wine-shop, though the eyes of every man there were turned upon them.

text: “Perhaps, though I truly cannot say, even though they were murmuring right beside me: they had positioned themselves at the head of the cabin stairs to catch the glow of the lantern suspended there; it cast a feeble light, and their voices were so hushed that I caught nothing of their words, only noticing that they were examining documents.”
source_text: “Possibly, but indeed I don’t know, although they stood whispering very near to me: because they stood at the top of the cabin steps to have the light of the lamp that was hanging there; it was a dull lamp, and they spoke very low, and I did not hear what they said, and saw only that they looked at papers.”

text: Just as Darnay caught the sudden flash in the crowd’s eyes—a surge that in another moment would have engulfed him—the postmaster wheeled his horse into the courtyard, the guard pressing tight against its flanks, and slammed shut the rickety double gates, bolting them fast. The farrier banged his hammer against the wood, and the mob let out a groan; but nothing further came of it.
source_text: At the instant when Darnay saw a rush in the eyes of the crowd, which another instant would have brought upon him, the postmaster turned his horse into the yard, the escort rode in close upon his horse’s flanks, and the postmaster shut and barred the crazy double gates. The farrier struck a blow upon them with his hammer, and the crowd groaned; but, no more was done.

text: “When I talk about winning, I mean winning over the young lady; and when I talk about factors and arguments that might make winning likely, I mean factors and arguments that will carry weight with her. The young lady, my dear fellow,” Mr. Lorry said, gently patting Stryver’s arm, “the young lady. She comes before everything.”
source_text: “When I speak of success, I speak of success with the young lady; and when I speak of causes and reasons to make success probable, I speak of causes and reasons that will tell as such with the young lady. The young lady, my good sir,” said Mr. Lorry, mildly tapping the Stryver arm, “the young lady. The young lady goes before all.”

text: He understood that the appointed time was three o’clock, and he expected to be called a little before then, since the carts rumbled ponderously and crawled along the streets. So he decided to fix his thoughts on two o’clock as the decisive moment, and to gather his strength during that stretch so that afterward he could fortify those around him.
source_text: He had been apprised that the final hour was Three, and he knew he would be summoned some time earlier, inasmuch as the tumbrils jolted heavily and slowly through the streets. Therefore, he resolved to keep Two before his mind, as the hour, and so to strengthen himself in the interval that he might be able, after that time, to strengthen others.

text: Mr. Stryver, a darling of the Old Bailey and the Sessions alike, had started carefully to kick away the lower rungs of the ladder he was climbing. Now the Sessions and Old Bailey were compelled to beckon their cherished advocate into their eager embrace with special summons; and thrusting itself toward the face of the Lord Chief Justice in the Court of King’s Bench, Mr. Stryver’s ruddy visage could be spotted daily, erupting from the wig-beds like an enormous sunflower straining toward the sun amid a dense garden of garish blooms.
source_text: A favourite at the Old Bailey, and eke at the Sessions, Mr. Stryver had begun cautiously to hew away the lower staves of the ladder on which he mounted. Sessions and Old Bailey had now to summon their favourite, specially, to their longing arms; and shouldering itself towards the visage of the Lord Chief Justice in the Court of King’s Bench, the florid countenance of Mr. Stryver might be daily seen, bursting out of the bed of wigs, like a great sunflower pushing its way at the sun from among a rank garden-full of flaring companions.

text: “I suggest we first see your sister safely to the end of her street. Take my arm, Miss Pross. At this hour, the city isn’t safe for you to be out alone; and since your escort is acquainted with Mr. Barsad, I’ll ask him to join us at Mr. Lorry’s. Shall we go? Off we are, then!”
source_text: “I propose that we first conduct your sister safely to the corner of her own street. Let me take your arm, Miss Pross. This is not a good city, at this time, for you to be out in, unprotected; and as your escort knows Mr. Barsad, I will invite him to Mr. Lorry’s with us. Are we ready? Come then!”

text: Carton’s hand did not return to his chest. The prisoner leaped to his feet, his eyes flashing with accusation, but Carton pressed his hand tightly and steadily against his nostrils, while his left arm encircled the man’s waist. For a brief moment, he feebly resisted the one who had arrived to sacrifice himself in his stead; yet, in under a minute, he lay sprawled unconscious on the floor.
source_text: Carton’s hand moved back to his breast no more. The prisoner sprang up with a reproachful look, but Carton’s hand was close and firm at his nostrils, and Carton’s left arm caught him round the waist. For a few seconds he faintly struggled with the man who had come to lay down his life for him; but, within a minute or so, he was stretched insensible on the ground.

text: On the day of the wedding, Doctor Manette had pressed upon Charles Darnay a single, emphatic plea: that the truth of that name should remain a sacred trust between them alone, unless he himself chose to release Darnay from the vow. No one else was aware it belonged to him; even his wife had no inkling of the matter, and Mr. Lorry could have none.
source_text: On the marriage morning, Doctor Manette had made it his one urgent and express request to Charles Darnay, that the secret of this name should be--unless he, the Doctor, dissolved the obligation--kept inviolate between them. Nobody else knew it to be his name; his own wife had no suspicion of the fact; Mr. Lorry could have none.

text: Upon the English throne sat a king with a heavy jaw and a queen of unremarkable features; upon the French throne sat a king with a heavy jaw and a queen of pleasing features. In both realms, it was more transparent than glass to the lords who guarded the state's store of bread and fish that the order of things was fixed for all time.
source_text: There were a king with a large jaw and a queen with a plain face, on the throne of England; there were a king with a large jaw and a queen with a fair face, on the throne of France. In both countries it was clearer than crystal to the lords of the State preserves of loaves and fishes, that things in general were settled for ever.

text: He spoke with the hesitant caution of someone who understood how easily the mind’s fragile balance could be overturned, yet also with the steady conviction of a man who had earned his certainty through long suffering and private trials. It was not his friend’s place to weaken that conviction. He declared himself more comforted and heartened than he truly felt, and moved on to his second and final matter. This, he sensed, would be the hardest of all; but recalling that old Sunday morning talk with Miss Pross, and recalling all he had witnessed over the past nine days, he knew he must confront it.
source_text: He spoke with the diffidence of a man who knew how slight a thing would overset the delicate organisation of the mind, and yet with the confidence of a man who had slowly won his assurance out of personal endurance and distress. It was not for his friend to abate that confidence. He professed himself more relieved and encouraged than he really was, and approached his second and last point. He felt it to be the most difficult of all; but, remembering his old Sunday morning conversation with Miss Pross, and remembering what he had seen in the last nine days, he knew that he must face it.

text: On no other evening did the sun set with such splendid brilliance over that tranquil Soho nook as on the unforgettable night when the Doctor and his daughter rested together beneath the plane tree. Nor did the moon ever ascend over vast London with a gentler glow than on that evening, when it discovered them still lingering under the tree and cast its light through the foliage onto their faces.
source_text: Never did the sun go down with a brighter glory on the quiet corner in Soho, than one memorable evening when the Doctor and his daughter sat under the plane-tree together. Never did the moon rise with a milder radiance over great London, than on that night when it found them still seated under the tree, and shone upon their faces through its leaves.

text: When the working day began, Mr. Lorry’s commercial instincts immediately fixed on a troubling point: he had no authority to endanger Tellson’s Bank by giving refuge under its roof to the wife of an exiled prisoner. For Lucie and her child, he would have risked his own property, security, even his life without a second thought; but the weighty responsibility he carried belonged to others, and in matters of business duty he was rigorously exact.
source_text: One of the first considerations which arose in the business mind of Mr. Lorry when business hours came round, was this:--that he had no right to imperil Tellson’s by sheltering the wife of an emigrant prisoner under the Bank roof. His own possessions, safety, life, he would have hazarded for Lucie and her child, without a moment’s demur; but the great trust he held was not his own, and as to that business charge he was a strict man of business.

text: In a gesture born of urgency, compelled by her condition and the conductor’s summoning, he pulled the trembling arm from his shoulder around his neck, raised her slightly, and hastened her inside. He settled her just past the threshold, steadying her as she clung to him.
source_text: Rendered in a manner desperate, by her state and by the beckoning of their conductor, he drew over his neck the arm that shook upon his shoulder, lifted her a little, and hurried her into the room. He sat her down just within the door, and held her, clinging to him.

text: I step aside, gentlemen, beside my pile of stones, to watch the soldiers and their captive go by (for this is a lonely road, where any sight is worth observing), and at first, as they draw near, I see only that they are six soldiers escorting a tall man in restraints, and they appear nearly black to my eyes—except along the edge where the sun is setting, where they glow with a crimson rim, gentlemen. I also notice their stretched shadows falling across the sunken ridge on the far side of the road, and climbing the slope beyond it, resembling the shadows of giants. I notice too that they are coated in dust, and the dust travels with them as they advance, tramp, tramp! But when they come quite close to me, I know the tall man, and he knows me. Ah, how gladly he would fling himself down the hillside once more, just as on that evening when he and I first met, near this very place!
source_text: “I stand aside, messieurs, by my heap of stones, to see the soldiers and their prisoner pass (for it is a solitary road, that, where any spectacle is well worth looking at), and at first, as they approach, I see no more than that they are six soldiers with a tall man bound, and that they are almost black to my sight--except on the side of the sun going to bed, where they have a red edge, messieurs. Also, I see that their long shadows are on the hollow ridge on the opposite side of the road, and are on the hill above it, and are like the shadows of giants. Also, I see that they are covered with dust, and that the dust moves with them as they come, tramp, tramp! But when they advance quite near to me, I recognise the tall man, and he recognises me. Ah, but he would be well content to precipitate himself over the hill-side once again, as on the evening when he and I first encountered, close to the same spot!”

text: When her husband entered, she cast him a glance so steadying, so heartening, so brimming with adoring love and compassionate gentleness, yet so brave on his behalf, that it flushed his cheeks with color, lit his eyes, and stirred his spirit. Had anyone observed the effect of that look on Sydney Carton, they would have seen it was precisely the same.
source_text: When her husband was brought in, she turned a look upon him, so sustaining, so encouraging, so full of admiring love and pitying tenderness, yet so courageous for his sake, that it called the healthy blood into his face, brightened his glance, and animated his heart. If there had been any eyes to notice the influence of her look, on Sydney Carton, it would have been seen to be the same influence exactly.

text: Moved and struck with dread as they both stood before this scene of desolation, they could not afford to surrender to such feelings. His solitary child, stripped of her last comfort and stay, called out to each of them too powerfully. Once more, as though by silent pact, their eyes met with a single purpose written on their faces. It was Carton who broke the stillness first:
source_text: Affected, and impressed with terror as they both were, by this spectacle of ruin, it was not a time to yield to such emotions. His lonely daughter, bereft of her final hope and reliance, appealed to them both too strongly. Again, as if by agreement, they looked at one another with one meaning in their faces. Carton was the first to speak:

text: A sudden realization struck Miss Pross: every door stood wide open, hinting at escape. At once, she moved to close them. Four doors lined the room, and she shut each one. Then she positioned herself squarely before the entrance to Lucie’s chamber.
source_text: It flashed upon Miss Pross’s mind that the doors were all standing open, and would suggest the flight. Her first act was to shut them. There were four in the room, and she shut them all. She then placed herself before the door of the chamber which Lucie had occupied.

text: “Now! There’s a word—chivalry. What I intend is simply this,” declared Ashcombe, swelling with self-regard as he mixed the rum, “that I am a fellow who sets greater store by pleasing, who labours more earnestly to please, who understands far better the art of pleasing, in the company of ladies, than ever you shall.”
source_text: “Well! I’ll say gallantry. My meaning is that I am a man,” said Stryver, inflating himself at his friend as he made the punch, “who cares more to be agreeable, who takes more pains to be agreeable, who knows better how to be agreeable, in a woman’s society, than you do.”

text: “--any whims, any justifications, any misgivings, anything at all, recent or ancient, concerning the man she truly cherished--provided the immediate blame did not rest upon him--they would all be erased for her benefit. She is my whole world; greater to me than agony, greater to me than injustice, greater to me--Ah! This is foolish chatter.”
source_text: “--any fancies, any reasons, any apprehensions, anything whatsoever, new or old, against the man she really loved--the direct responsibility thereof not lying on his head--they should all be obliterated for her sake. She is everything to me; more to me than suffering, more to me than wrong, more to me--Well! This is idle talk.”

text: From the moment we dismounted at the outer gate—which stood locked and was unbarred by one of the brothers only long enough to let us through before he secured it again—I could hear wailing from an upper room. I was led directly there, the sounds swelling with each step we climbed, until I entered and found a patient tossing on a bed, deep in the grip of a brain fever.
source_text: “From the time of our alighting at the outer gate (which we found locked, and which one of the brothers had opened to admit us, and had relocked), I had heard cries proceeding from an upper chamber. I was conducted to this chamber straight, the cries growing louder as we ascended the stairs, and I found a patient in a high fever of the brain, lying on a bed.

text: Yet as the splendid sun ascended, its long, brilliant rays seemed to carry those words—the weight of the night—directly and tenderly into his heart. Gazing along those beams with eyes reverently shielded, he saw a bridge of light arching through the air between himself and the sun, while the river glittered beneath it.
source_text: But, the glorious sun, rising, seemed to strike those words, that burden of the night, straight and warm to his heart in its long bright rays. And looking along them, with reverently shaded eyes, a bridge of light appeared to span the air between him and the sun, while the river sparkled under it.

text: Sydney Carton never once stood out in the Manette household, if he ever stood out anywhere. Over the course of an entire year, he had visited repeatedly, yet he remained the same sullen, brooding fixture. On the rare occasions he felt like speaking, his words carried weight; but the inner light that might have broken through was almost always smothered by the fatal shadow of his indifference to everything.
source_text: If Sydney Carton ever shone anywhere, he certainly never shone in the house of Doctor Manette. He had been there often, during a whole year, and had always been the same moody and morose lounger there. When he cared to talk, he talked well; but, the cloud of caring for nothing, which overshadowed him with such a fatal darkness, was very rarely pierced by the light within him.

text: Jerry Cruncher, his eyes bloodshot and his face severe as though he'd spent the night at a gathering that had soured into something far from festive, merely picked at his breakfast instead of eating it, muttering over the food like some caged beast in a zoo. As nine o'clock drew near, he composed his disheveled demeanor, and, cloaking his true nature beneath the most decent and industrious front he could muster, stepped out to take up the day's work.
source_text: Exceedingly red-eyed and grim, as if he had been up all night at a party which had taken anything but a convivial turn, Jerry Cruncher worried his breakfast rather than ate it, growling over it like any four-footed inmate of a menagerie. Towards nine o’clock he smoothed his ruffled aspect, and, presenting as respectable and business-like an exterior as he could overlay his natural self with, issued forth to the occupation of the day.

text: The Marquis, riding in his travel coach—which could have been less cumbersome—was dragged up a sharp incline by four post-horses and two postilions. The flush on his face did nothing to diminish his noble stature; it came from no inner source, but from an outside force he could not command—the sinking sun.
source_text: Monsieur the Marquis in his travelling carriage (which might have been lighter), conducted by four post-horses and two postilions, fagged up a steep hill. A blush on the countenance of Monsieur the Marquis was no impeachment of his high breeding; it was not from within; it was occasioned by an external circumstance beyond his control--the setting sun.

text: Ah! Cry out, Vengeance, cry with all your might, and still she may barely catch the sound. Shout louder, Vengeance, throw in a muttered curse or two, and even then she will scarcely be summoned. Dispatch other women to search high and low for her, as she lingers somewhere out of reach; and yet, though these emissaries have committed terrible acts, one must wonder if they truly possess the will to venture far enough to discover her!
source_text: Ay! Louder, Vengeance, much louder, and still she will scarcely hear thee. Louder yet, Vengeance, with a little oath or so added, and yet it will hardly bring her. Send other women up and down to seek her, lingering somewhere; and yet, although the messengers have done dread deeds, it is questionable whether of their own wills they will go far enough to find her!

text: “Yet isn’t it passing odd—at this hour, above all, isn’t it passing odd,” said Defarge, almost coaxing his wife to concede the point, “that after all our pity for Monsieur her father, and for her too, the name of her husband should lie condemned by your own script just now, alongside that fiendish cur’s who only this moment departed?”
source_text: “But it is very strange--now, at least, is it not very strange”--said Defarge, rather pleading with his wife to induce her to admit it, “that, after all our sympathy for Monsieur her father, and herself, her husband’s name should be proscribed under your hand at this moment, by the side of that infernal dog’s who has just left us?”

text: The upstanding servant, Roger Cly, delivered his testimony with a torrent of oaths. Four years earlier, he had entered the prisoner’s employment with honest and simple intentions. On the Calais ferry, he had offered himself as a useful hand, and the prisoner had hired him. It had never crossed his mind to ask for the position out of pity—not for a moment. Before long, he began to grow suspicious and to watch the prisoner closely. While sorting the prisoner’s garments during their travels, he had repeatedly come across lists just like these tucked into the pockets. He had retrieved these particular lists from a drawer in the prisoner’s desk. He had not planted them there beforehand. He had observed the prisoner displaying these very same lists to French gentlemen in Calais, and similar ones to French gentlemen in both Calais and Boulogne. He was devoted to his country, could stomach it no longer, and had turned informant. He had never stood accused of stealing a silver teapot; there had been some slander about a mustard pot, but it proved to be merely silver-plated. He had been acquainted with the previous witness for seven or eight years; that was nothing more than happenstance. He wouldn’t describe it as a particularly odd happenstance; most happenstances were odd. Nor would he call it a strange happenstance that genuine patriotism was his sole motivation as well. He was a true Englishman, and trusted many others shared his sentiments.
source_text: The virtuous servant, Roger Cly, swore his way through the case at a great rate. He had taken service with the prisoner, in good faith and simplicity, four years ago. He had asked the prisoner, aboard the Calais packet, if he wanted a handy fellow, and the prisoner had engaged him. He had not asked the prisoner to take the handy fellow as an act of charity--never thought of such a thing. He began to have suspicions of the prisoner, and to keep an eye upon him, soon afterwards. In arranging his clothes, while travelling, he had seen similar lists to these in the prisoner’s pockets, over and over again. He had taken these lists from the drawer of the prisoner’s desk. He had not put them there first. He had seen the prisoner show these identical lists to French gentlemen at Calais, and similar lists to French gentlemen, both at Calais and Boulogne. He loved his country, and couldn’t bear it, and had given information. He had never been suspected of stealing a silver tea-pot; he had been maligned respecting a mustard-pot, but it turned out to be only a plated one. He had known the last witness seven or eight years; that was merely a coincidence. He didn’t call it a particularly curious coincidence; most coincidences were curious. Neither did he call it a curious coincidence that true patriotism was _his_ only motive too. He was a true Briton, and hoped there were many like him.

text: “So long as you remain unsure if they are inside that room, you cannot decide how to act,” Miss Pross murmured to herself; “and I will make certain you stay unsure, if it is within my power; and whether you discover the truth or not, you shall not quit this place while I have the strength to detain you.”
source_text: “As long as you don’t know whether they are in that room or not, you are uncertain what to do,” said Miss Pross to herself; “and you shall not know that, if I can prevent your knowing it; and know that, or not know that, you shall not leave here while I can hold you.”

text: “It had to be this way,” the prisoner said. “Everything has unfolded exactly as it was meant to. My doomed attempt to fulfill my poor mother’s trust is what first drew my cursed presence to you. No good could ever spring from such wickedness; a joyful conclusion was impossible from so wretched a start. Take solace, and pardon me. May Heaven keep you!”
source_text: “It could not be otherwise,” said the prisoner. “All things have worked together as they have fallen out. It was the always-vain endeavour to discharge my poor mother’s trust that first brought my fatal presence near you. Good could never come of such evil, a happier end was not in nature to so unhappy a beginning. Be comforted, and forgive me. Heaven bless you!”

text: Charles Darnay sat by himself in his prison cell, holding onto no comforting illusions from the moment the Tribunal had delivered him there. Each thread of the story he had listened to wove unmistakably toward his doom. He understood with complete clarity that no individual intervention could rescue him now—he had been condemned by the countless masses, and solitary voices meant nothing against their roar.
source_text: Charles Darnay, alone in a cell, had sustained himself with no flattering delusion since he came to it from the Tribunal. In every line of the narrative he had heard, he had heard his condemnation. He had fully comprehended that no personal influence could possibly save him, that he was virtually sentenced by the millions, and that units could avail him nothing.

text: Yet whenever he gazed out into the drizzle and fog, at the trembling glow of the carriage lanterns and the hedgerows that shuddered backward in fits, the darkness beyond the window would merge with the darkness inside his mind. The actual counting-house near Ludgate, the actual dealings of the previous hours, the actual iron vaults, the actual courier dispatched in pursuit, and the actual reply that followed—all would gather around him. From their center, that spectral visage would surface, and he would address it once more.
source_text: Yet even when his eyes were opened on the mist and rain, on the moving patch of light from the lamps, and the hedge at the roadside retreating by jerks, the night shadows outside the coach would fall into the train of the night shadows within. The real Banking-house by Temple Bar, the real business of the past day, the real strong rooms, the real express sent after him, and the real message returned, would all be there. Out of the midst of them, the ghostly face would rise, and he would accost it again.

text: “Well, would you just look at yourselves!” the figure bellowed at the inn staff. “Why are you all gawking at me instead of fetching what’s needed? I’m hardly a spectacle, am I? Go on, fetch things this instant! I’m warning you—if you don’t bring smelling salts, cold water, and vinegar at once, you’ll answer for it.”
source_text: “Why, look at you all!” bawled this figure, addressing the inn servants. “Why don’t you go and fetch things, instead of standing there staring at me? I am not so much to look at, am I? Why don’t you go and fetch things? I’ll let you know, if you don’t bring smelling-salts, cold water, and vinegar, quick, I will.”

text: Only the delivery of the tormented Gabelle’s letter from his cell in the Abbaye could have driven him onward to this point. The trouble he had met at the guard-house in this little town had been so severe that he sensed his whole undertaking was reaching a breaking point. So he was as unsurprised as anyone could be when he found himself roused in the dead of night at the modest inn where he had been sent to wait for morning.
source_text: Nothing but the production of the afflicted Gabelle’s letter from his prison of the Abbaye would have got him on so far. His difficulty at the guard-house in this small place had been such, that he felt his journey to have come to a crisis. And he was, therefore, as little surprised as a man could be, to find himself awakened at the small inn to which he had been remitted until morning, in the middle of the night.

text: The jackal, with evident reluctance, undid his garment, stepped into a nearby chamber, and returned bearing a large pitcher of cold water, a basin, and a couple of towels. He soaked the towels in the water, wrung them out just enough, then draped them over his head in a grotesque fashion that was dreadful to see. Settling himself at the table, he announced, “Now I am ready!”
source_text: Sullenly enough, the jackal loosened his dress, went into an adjoining room, and came back with a large jug of cold water, a basin, and a towel or two. Steeping the towels in the water, and partially wringing them out, he folded them on his head in a manner hideous to behold, sat down at the table, and said, “Now I am ready!”

text: Out of a fitful sleep in his small chamber, the boy Tom was roused at the grey edge of dawn by sounds from the main room below. His father had returned in a foul temper—this much Tom gathered at once, seeing the man grip Mistress Meech by her hair and thump her skull against the bedframe.
source_text: From his oppressed slumber, Young Jerry in his closet was awakened after daybreak and before sunrise, by the presence of his father in the family room. Something had gone wrong with him; at least, so Young Jerry inferred, from the circumstance of his holding Mrs. Cruncher by the ears, and knocking the back of her head against the head-board of the bed.

text: By now the sun had fully risen, and the village stirred to life. Casement windows swung open, crooked doors were unlatched, and people stepped out shivering—still chilled by the crisp, unfamiliar air. Then began the day’s barely relieved labor among the villagers. Some headed to the fountain; others to the fields; men and women in one place set to digging and hoeing; elsewhere, they tended the scrawny livestock and led bony cows out to whatever grazing could be found along the roadside. In the church and at the Cross, a figure or two knelt; beside those praying at the Cross, a led cow nosed through the weeds at its base, searching for a meager breakfast.
source_text: Now, the sun was full up, and movement began in the village. Casement windows opened, crazy doors were unbarred, and people came forth shivering--chilled, as yet, by the new sweet air. Then began the rarely lightened toil of the day among the village population. Some, to the fountain; some, to the fields; men and women here, to dig and delve; men and women there, to see to the poor live stock, and lead the bony cows out, to such pasture as could be found by the roadside. In the church and at the Cross, a kneeling figure or two; attendant on the latter prayers, the led cow, trying for a breakfast among the weeds at its foot.

text: Yet, still lay a fractured land, stark and exposed—a hamlet nestled at the hill's foot, a wide expanse ascending past it, a steeple, a mill, a hunting wood, and a cliff crowned by a stronghold turned to a gaol. Over all these deepening shapes as dusk descended, the Marquis gazed, bearing the look of a man drawing close to his own threshold.
source_text: But, there remained a broken country, bold and open, a little village at the bottom of the hill, a broad sweep and rise beyond it, a church-tower, a windmill, a forest for the chase, and a crag with a fortress on it used as a prison. Round upon all these darkening objects as the night drew on, the Marquis looked, with the air of one who was coming near home.

text: A table was set for two in the third chamber—a circular space nestled within one of the château’s four turrets, each capped like a snuffer. The room was compact and high-ceilinged, its window thrown wide, yet the wooden slatted shutters were drawn, letting the black night slip through only as thin horizontal strips of darkness, set between the wider bands of pale stone.
source_text: A supper-table was laid for two, in the third of the rooms; a round room, in one of the chateau’s four extinguisher-topped towers. A small lofty room, with its window wide open, and the wooden jalousie-blinds closed, so that the dark night only showed in slight horizontal lines of black, alternating with their broad lines of stone colour.

text: “‘This is my sister, Physician. These Lords have long exercised their foul privileges upon the modesty and purity of our womenfolk, year upon year, yet we have raised virtuous daughters in our midst. I know this to be true, for my father himself declared it. She was a virtuous daughter. She was promised to a worthy young man, too—a tenant under that same lord. All of us were tenants under him—that man who stands before you now. The other is his brother, the vilest scion of a vile lineage.’”
source_text: “‘She is my sister, Doctor. They have had their shameful rights, these Nobles, in the modesty and virtue of our sisters, many years, but we have had good girls among us. I know it, and have heard my father say so. She was a good girl. She was betrothed to a good young man, too: a tenant of his. We were all tenants of his--that man’s who stands there. The other is his brother, the worst of a bad race.’

text: Within the dark keep of the Conciergerie, the day’s condemned held vigil for their end. Their count matched the full cycle of the seasons’ weeks. Fifty-two souls were to be swept that afternoon upon the city’s living current into the shoreless, eternal ocean. Ere their chambers were emptied of them, fresh lodgers had been named; before their lifeblood joined the gore shed the day before, the blood destined to blend with theirs on the morrow was already chosen.
source_text: In the black prison of the Conciergerie, the doomed of the day awaited their fate. They were in number as the weeks of the year. Fifty-two were to roll that afternoon on the life-tide of the city to the boundless everlasting sea. Before their cells were quit of them, new occupants were appointed; before their blood ran into the blood spilled yesterday, the blood that was to mingle with theirs to-morrow was already set apart.

text: For a provincial servant of his low standing, far grimmer lodgings than Defarge’s wine-shop could have been found in Paris. Apart from an eerie dread of madame that never left him, his days had grown strangely fresh and pleasant. Yet madame sat behind her counter all day long, so pointedly oblivious of him, so fixed in her refusal to acknowledge that his presence might touch anything beneath the surface, that his wooden shoes trembled whenever his gaze fell on her. He argued with himself that it was impossible to guess what that woman might choose to feign next; and he felt certain that if the whim ever took her brightly ornamented head to pretend she had seen him commit a murder and then flay the corpse, she would carry it through relentlessly until the whole drama was played out.
source_text: Worse quarters than Defarge’s wine-shop, could easily have been found in Paris for a provincial slave of that degree. Saving for a mysterious dread of madame by which he was constantly haunted, his life was very new and agreeable. But, madame sat all day at her counter, so expressly unconscious of him, and so particularly determined not to perceive that his being there had any connection with anything below the surface, that he shook in his wooden shoes whenever his eye lighted on her. For, he contended with himself that it was impossible to foresee what that lady might pretend next; and he felt assured that if she should take it into her brightly ornamented head to pretend that she had seen him do a murder and afterwards flay the victim, she would infallibly go through with it until the play was played out.

text: Jerry the messenger, having quietly drawn his own conclusions while distractedly gnawing the rust from his fingertips, craned his neck to catch their identities. The throng around him had squeezed the question forward to the closest guard, and from him it had been relayed back, more sluggishly, through the press of bodies; finally, it reached Jerry.
source_text: Jerry, the messenger, who had made his own observations, in his own manner, and who had been sucking the rust off his fingers in his absorption, stretched his neck to hear who they were. The crowd about him had pressed and passed the inquiry on to the nearest attendant, and from him it had been more slowly pressed and passed back; at last it got to Jerry:

text: Bearing a hope that dimmed with each passing hour, and carrying a spirit that sank ever lower, Dr. Manette endured this fretful season. The concealment held firm, and Lucie remained unknowing and serene; yet he could not help but notice that the cobbler, whose fingers had once faltered, was becoming alarmingly adept, and that his attention had never been so fixed upon his labor, and that his motions had never been so swift and practiced, as in the twilight of the ninth night.
source_text: With a hope ever darkening, and with a heart always growing heavier and heavier, Mr. Lorry passed through this anxious time. The secret was well kept, and Lucie was unconscious and happy; but he could not fail to observe that the shoemaker, whose hand had been a little out at first, was growing dreadfully skilful, and that he had never been so intent on his work, and that his hands had never been so nimble and expert, as in the dusk of the ninth evening.

text: “So! Yet on other moonlit nights, when the hush and the sorrow reached me in another manner—stirring in me a mournful tranquility, the kind that has pain at its root—I would picture her entering my cell and guiding me out into the liberty beyond the prison walls. Her shape has often appeared to me in the moonlight, just as I see you now; only I never held her close; she would stand between the narrow barred window and the door. But you realize, that was not the child I mean?”
source_text: “So! But on other moonlight nights, when the sadness and the silence have touched me in a different way--have affected me with something as like a sorrowful sense of peace, as any emotion that had pain for its foundations could--I have imagined her as coming to me in my cell, and leading me out into the freedom beyond the fortress. I have seen her image in the moonlight often, as I now see you; except that I never held her in my arms; it stood between the little grated window and the door. But, you understand that that was not the child I am speaking of?”

text: At first, even though she was a blissful young bride, there were moments when her sewing would slip unnoticed from her fingers and a mist would cloud her gaze. For something was stirring within the reverberations—something faint, distant, and barely perceptible—that moved her heart beyond bearing. Fleeting hopes and misgivings—hopes of a love she had never known, misgivings that she might not stay on this earth to savor that fresh joy—tugged at her in equal measure. Then, among those echoes, she would catch the tread of feet approaching her own untimely grave; and visions of the husband who would be left so forlorn, who would grieve for her so deeply, would rise to her eyes and break against them like surf.
source_text: At first, there were times, though she was a perfectly happy young wife, when her work would slowly fall from her hands, and her eyes would be dimmed. For, there was something coming in the echoes, something light, afar off, and scarcely audible yet, that stirred her heart too much. Fluttering hopes and doubts--hopes, of a love as yet unknown to her: doubts, of her remaining upon earth, to enjoy that new delight--divided her breast. Among the echoes then, there would arise the sound of footsteps at her own early grave; and thoughts of the husband who would be left so desolate, and who would mourn for her so much, swelled to her eyes, and broke like waves.

text: “Avert your eyes!” shouted Mr. Lorry, seized by utter despair. “No, Elara, my sweet, nor you!” He wrapped his arm around her, steadying her trembling form. “You must not be so frightened, my darling. I vow to you upon my honor that I know of no injury befalling Julian; I had not even the faintest inkling he was in this accursed spot. In which gaol do they hold him?”
source_text: “Don’t look!” cried Mr. Lorry, absolutely desperate. “No, Lucie, my dear, nor you!” He got his arm round her, and held her. “Don’t be so terrified, my love. I solemnly swear to you that I know of no harm having happened to Charles; that I had no suspicion even of his being in this fatal place. What prison is he in?”

text: Yes. Like the sailor in the ancient tale, the currents and tides had carried him into the pull of the Lodestone Rock, and it was reeling him in, and he had no choice. Every thought that surfaced in his mind pushed him onward, swifter and swifter, more and more inexorably, toward that dreadful gravity. His buried disquiet had been this: that wicked schemes were being executed in his own stricken country by wicked agents, and that he—who could not help but know he was better than them—was absent, making no effort to halt the slaughter or to champion the cause of compassion and decency. With this disquiet half smothered, half accusing him, he had been led to the sharp contrast between himself and the steadfast old man in whom duty burned so fiercely; on the heels of that contrast (so damaging to himself) had come the mockery of Monseigneur, which had cut him deeply, and the taunts of Stryver, which above all were vulgar and chafing, for reasons long past. After those had come Gabelle’s letter: the cry of a blameless captive, facing death, to his sense of fairness, his integrity, and his reputation.
source_text: Yes. Like the mariner in the old story, the winds and streams had driven him within the influence of the Loadstone Rock, and it was drawing him to itself, and he must go. Everything that arose before his mind drifted him on, faster and faster, more and more steadily, to the terrible attraction. His latent uneasiness had been, that bad aims were being worked out in his own unhappy land by bad instruments, and that he who could not fail to know that he was better than they, was not there, trying to do something to stay bloodshed, and assert the claims of mercy and humanity. With this uneasiness half stifled, and half reproaching him, he had been brought to the pointed comparison of himself with the brave old gentleman in whom duty was so strong; upon that comparison (injurious to himself) had instantly followed the sneers of Monseigneur, which had stung him bitterly, and those of Stryver, which above all were coarse and galling, for old reasons. Upon those, had followed Gabelle’s letter: the appeal of an innocent prisoner, in danger of death, to his justice, honour, and good name.

text: Monseigneur held one genuinely grand conviction regarding public affairs at large: that they should be left to run their natural course. Concerning specific matters of state, he cherished another equally grand conviction—that they must all serve his own interests, feeding both his authority and his coffers. As for his pleasures, whether broad or particular, Monseigneur embraced yet another lofty belief: that the world had been fashioned for their gratification. The wording of his decree (altered from the original by a single pronoun, a trifling change) declared: “The earth and all its abundance belong to me, proclaims Monseigneur.”
source_text: Monseigneur had one truly noble idea of general public business, which was, to let everything go on in its own way; of particular public business, Monseigneur had the other truly noble idea that it must all go his way--tend to his own power and pocket. Of his pleasures, general and particular, Monseigneur had the other truly noble idea, that the world was made for them. The text of his order (altered from the original by only a pronoun, which is not much) ran: “The earth and the fulness thereof are mine, saith Monseigneur.”

text: “Long before our time at Ashford, during those Ashford days, and in all the years after Ashford,” Carton went on, “you have steadily claimed your place, while I have drifted into mine. Back when we shared a shabby attic in the Latin Quarter, gathering scraps of Parisian speech, Parisian statutes, and other Parisian trifles that profited us little, you were already a man of substance, and I was already a ghost of a man.”
source_text: “Before Shrewsbury, and at Shrewsbury, and ever since Shrewsbury,” pursued Carton, “you have fallen into your rank, and I have fallen into mine. Even when we were fellow-students in the Student-Quarter of Paris, picking up French, and French law, and other French crumbs that we didn’t get much good of, you were always somewhere, and I was always nowhere.”

text: It was a stifling day, and after their meal Lucie suggested they bring the wine outside beneath the plane tree and sit there in the open air. Since everything centered on her and moved around her, they all went out under the tree, and she brought the wine down especially for Mr. Lorry. She had long since appointed herself his personal cup-bearer, and as they sat beneath the plane tree conversing, she made sure his glass stayed full. The hidden backs and corners of houses peered at them while they spoke, and the plane tree murmured its own secrets overhead.
source_text: It was an oppressive day, and, after dinner, Lucie proposed that the wine should be carried out under the plane-tree, and they should sit there in the air. As everything turned upon her, and revolved about her, they went out under the plane-tree, and she carried the wine down for the special benefit of Mr. Lorry. She had installed herself, some time before, as Mr. Lorry’s cup-bearer; and while they sat under the plane-tree, talking, she kept his glass replenished. Mysterious backs and ends of houses peeped at them as they talked, and the plane-tree whispered to them in its own way above their heads.

text: “Mark me well,” continued Mr. Aldridge. “In my capacity as a solicitor, I possess no warrant to utter a syllable regarding this affair, for, in that professional role, I am wholly ignorant of its particulars. Yet, as a man of years, who once cradled Miss Darracott in his arms, who stands as the cherished confidant of Miss Darracott and her father alike, and who harbours a deep fondness for each of them, I have unburdened myself. The trust was not solicited by me, bear in mind. Now, do you imagine I may be mistaken?”
source_text: “Now understand me,” pursued Mr. Lorry. “As a man of business, I am not justified in saying anything about this matter, for, as a man of business, I know nothing of it. But, as an old fellow, who has carried Miss Manette in his arms, who is the trusted friend of Miss Manette and of her father too, and who has a great affection for them both, I have spoken. The confidence is not of my seeking, recollect. Now, you think I may not be right?”

text: The fountain stood slightly apart, where the street widened into a small square, perhaps ten or twelve yards across. When the tall man abruptly rose from the earth and rushed toward the carriage, Monsieur the Marquis swiftly laid his hand upon the hilt of his sword for a brief moment.
source_text: The fountain was a little removed; for the street opened, where it was, into a space some ten or twelve yards square. As the tall man suddenly got up from the ground, and came running at the carriage, Monsieur the Marquis clapped his hand for an instant on his sword-hilt.

text: The Dover mail coach was in its customary state of mutual wariness: the guard eyed the passengers with suspicion, the passengers distrusted each other and the guard, everyone viewed their fellows with misgiving, and the coachman had faith in nothing save the horses—and regarding those beasts, he could have sworn with an easy conscience on both Testaments that they were utterly unfit for the road ahead.
source_text: The Dover mail was in its usual genial position that the guard suspected the passengers, the passengers suspected one another and the guard, they all suspected everybody else, and the coachman was sure of nothing but the horses; as to which cattle he could with a clear conscience have taken his oath on the two Testaments that they were not fit for the journey.

text: He rolled down the window and gazed at the sun climbing over the horizon. A ridge of tilled earth stretched before him, a plough still resting where it had been abandoned the evening before when the horses were freed from their harness. Farther off lay a silent coppice, its trees still clinging to leaves of fiery crimson and glowing gold. The ground beneath was cold and damp, yet the sky above shone clear, and the sun lifted itself serene, brilliant, and lovely.
source_text: He lowered the window, and looked out at the rising sun. There was a ridge of ploughed land, with a plough upon it where it had been left last night when the horses were unyoked; beyond, a quiet coppice-wood, in which many leaves of burning red and golden yellow still remained upon the trees. Though the earth was cold and wet, the sky was clear, and the sun rose bright, placid, and beautiful.

text: He leaned down to the golden hair, pressed his mouth to her flushed lips, and gathered her into his embrace. Had a solitary wanderer, drifting through the shadowed streets, caught the sound of her guileless confession and glimpsed her husband kissing away the tears of compassion from the tender blue eyes that gazed at him with such devotion, he might have called out to the darkness—and the cry would not have left his lips for the first time.
source_text: He bent over the golden head, and put the rosy lips to his, and folded her in his arms. If one forlorn wanderer then pacing the dark streets, could have heard her innocent disclosure, and could have seen the drops of pity kissed away by her husband from the soft blue eyes so loving of that husband, he might have cried to the night--and the words would not have parted from his lips for the first time--

text: Mr. Jarvis Lorry and Miss Manette stepped out of the wine-shop and joined Monsieur Defarge at the entrance where he had just gathered his own group. The doorway led from a foul little dark courtyard and served as the common entry to a massive block of buildings, packed with countless tenants. In the dim, tile-floored vestibule that gave onto the dim, tile-floored stairway, Monsieur Defarge dropped to one knee before his former master’s daughter and pressed her hand to his lips. The gesture was tender, yet the execution was anything but; in a matter of moments, a striking change had swept over him. All trace of geniality and openness had vanished from his face, leaving behind a guarded, wrathful, menacing figure.
source_text: Mr. Jarvis Lorry and Miss Manette, emerging from the wine-shop thus, joined Monsieur Defarge in the doorway to which he had directed his own company just before. It opened from a stinking little black courtyard, and was the general public entrance to a great pile of houses, inhabited by a great number of people. In the gloomy tile-paved entry to the gloomy tile-paved staircase, Monsieur Defarge bent down on one knee to the child of his old master, and put her hand to his lips. It was a gentle action, but not at all gently done; a very remarkable transformation had come over him in a few seconds. He had no good-humour in his face, nor any openness of aspect left, but had become a secret, angry, dangerous man.

text: The manor stood abandoned to the mercy of the flames. Amid the howling fury of the firestorm, a scorching gale, blasting as though from the depths of hell, appeared to sweep the structure into oblivion. As the inferno surged and waned, the carved visages seemed to writhe in agony. Whenever huge blocks of masonry and beams crashed down, the countenance with the twin notches upon its nose vanished into darkness; then it would wrest itself from the billowing smoke once more, like the face of the pitiless Lord, aflame at the pyre and grappling with the blaze.
source_text: The chateau was left to itself to flame and burn. In the roaring and raging of the conflagration, a red-hot wind, driving straight from the infernal regions, seemed to be blowing the edifice away. With the rising and falling of the blaze, the stone faces showed as if they were in torment. When great masses of stone and timber fell, the face with the two dints in the nose became obscured: anon struggled out of the smoke again, as if it were the face of the cruel Marquis, burning at the stake and contending with the fire.

text: The prisoner, fully aware that in the minds of everyone present he was already being strung up, decapitated, and dismembered, neither recoiled from his predicament nor struck any dramatic pose. He remained still and watchful, following the early formalities with a solemn focus, his hands resting so steadily on the wooden rail before him that not a single herb scattered across it had been disturbed. The entire chamber was strewn with herbs and misted with vinegar, a safeguard against the stench and contagion of the jail.
source_text: The accused, who was (and who knew he was) being mentally hanged, beheaded, and quartered, by everybody there, neither flinched from the situation, nor assumed any theatrical air in it. He was quiet and attentive; watched the opening proceedings with a grave interest; and stood with his hands resting on the slab of wood before him, so composedly, that they had not displaced a leaf of the herbs with which it was strewn. The court was all bestrewn with herbs and sprinkled with vinegar, as a precaution against gaol air and gaol fever.

text: The judges on the Bench wore feathered hats, but the rough red cap with its tricolour cockade was the more common headgear. Gazing at the jury and the restless crowd, he might have imagined the natural order overturned, as though criminals were sitting in judgment on honest men. The basest, most pitiless, and worst elements of a city—never lacking in such—steered the proceedings: loudly weighing in, cheering, condemning, leaping ahead, and forcing the outcome without restraint. Most of the men were armed in some fashion; among the women, some carried knives, others daggers, some ate and drank while watching, and many knitted. One of these knitters had a spare piece of work tucked under her arm as her needles moved. She sat in a front row, beside a man he had not seen since reaching the Barrier, but whom he instantly recognized as Defarge. He noticed she whispered in his ear once or twice and appeared to be his wife; yet what struck him most about the pair was that, though they stood as near to him as possible, they never once glanced his way. They seemed to be waiting, with a stubborn fixedness, for something, and they looked only at the jury. Below the President sat Doctor Manette, in his customary quiet attire. As far as the prisoner could tell, he and Mr. Lorry were the only men there, apart from the Tribunal, who still wore their ordinary clothes and had not adopted the rough garb of the Carmagnole.
source_text: His judges sat upon the Bench in feathered hats; but the rough red cap and tricoloured cockade was the head-dress otherwise prevailing. Looking at the Jury and the turbulent audience, he might have thought that the usual order of things was reversed, and that the felons were trying the honest men. The lowest, cruelest, and worst populace of a city, never without its quantity of low, cruel, and bad, were the directing spirits of the scene: noisily commenting, applauding, disapproving, anticipating, and precipitating the result, without a check. Of the men, the greater part were armed in various ways; of the women, some wore knives, some daggers, some ate and drank as they looked on, many knitted. Among these last, was one, with a spare piece of knitting under her arm as she worked. She was in a front row, by the side of a man whom he had never seen since his arrival at the Barrier, but whom he directly remembered as Defarge. He noticed that she once or twice whispered in his ear, and that she seemed to be his wife; but, what he most noticed in the two figures was, that although they were posted as close to himself as they could be, they never looked towards him. They seemed to be waiting for something with a dogged determination, and they looked at the Jury, but at nothing else. Under the President sat Doctor Manette, in his usual quiet dress. As well as the prisoner could see, he and Mr. Lorry were the only men there, unconnected with the Tribunal, who wore their usual clothes, and had not assumed the coarse garb of the Carmagnole.

text: Ever spinning the bright filament that knit their spirits as one, threading the quiet power of her gentle presence into the fabric of each existence yet never letting it overshadow any, Clara listened through the long corridors of time and caught only kind and calming murmurs. Her husband’s tread fell steady and assured amid their days; her father’s, resolute and unwavering. And there—dear Miss Pross, rigged in twine, rousing the quiet like a restive mare, flicked into obedience, stamping and chafing the ground beneath the sycamore in the yard!
source_text: Ever busily winding the golden thread that bound them all together, weaving the service of her happy influence through the tissue of all their lives, and making it predominate nowhere, Lucie heard in the echoes of years none but friendly and soothing sounds. Her husband’s step was strong and prosperous among them; her father’s firm and equal. Lo, Miss Pross, in harness of string, awakening the echoes, as an unruly charger, whip-corrected, snorting and pawing the earth under the plane-tree in the garden!

text: The atmosphere was so dense and oppressive, the crowds so savagely vindictive and erratic, the blameless so frequently executed on mere suspicion and dark spite, and it was so impossible to dismiss the thought that many as guiltless as her husband and as cherished by others as he was by her daily met the doom he had narrowly escaped, that her spirit could not shake off its burden as she knew it should. The gloom of the winter dusk was creeping in, and even at that hour the grim tumbrels were rumbling through the thoroughfares. Her thoughts tracked them, seeking his face among the doomed; then she pressed nearer to his living form and shivered more violently.
source_text: All the air round was so thick and dark, the people were so passionately revengeful and fitful, the innocent were so constantly put to death on vague suspicion and black malice, it was so impossible to forget that many as blameless as her husband and as dear to others as he was to her, every day shared the fate from which he had been clutched, that her heart could not be as lightened of its load as she felt it ought to be. The shadows of the wintry afternoon were beginning to fall, and even now the dreadful carts were rolling through the streets. Her mind pursued them, looking for him among the Condemned; and then she clung closer to his real presence and trembled more.

text: The trio pressed forward, and Young Jerry followed, until they halted beneath a steep bank that loomed over the road. Atop the bank sat a squat brick wall topped with an iron rail. Shrouded in the gloom of the bank and wall, they veered off the road and into a dead-end lane, its one side formed by that same wall—now towering eight or ten feet high. Huddled in a corner, squinting up the lane, the next thing Young Jerry spotted was the silhouette of his revered father, starkly outlined against a bleary, cloud-streaked moon, briskly clambering over an iron gate. He cleared it in a moment, then the second fisherman scrambled over, followed by the third. They each landed softly on the ground inside the gate and lay still for a beat—perhaps straining to hear. Then, they crept forward on all fours.
source_text: The three went on, and Young Jerry went on, until the three stopped under a bank overhanging the road. Upon the top of the bank was a low brick wall, surmounted by an iron railing. In the shadow of bank and wall the three turned out of the road, and up a blind lane, of which the wall--there, risen to some eight or ten feet high--formed one side. Crouching down in a corner, peeping up the lane, the next object that Young Jerry saw, was the form of his honoured parent, pretty well defined against a watery and clouded moon, nimbly scaling an iron gate. He was soon over, and then the second fisherman got over, and then the third. They all dropped softly on the ground within the gate, and lay there a little--listening perhaps. Then, they moved away on their hands and knees.

text: “Jerry, if you’d like to grab a bite, go ahead. Just stay close by. You’ll definitely hear when the jury returns. Don’t fall a second behind them—I need you to carry the verdict straight to the bank. You’re the fastest courier I’ve got, and you’ll reach Temple Bar well ahead of me.”
source_text: “Jerry, if you wish to take something to eat, you can. But, keep in the way. You will be sure to hear when the jury come in. Don’t be a moment behind them, for I want you to take the verdict back to the bank. You are the quickest messenger I know, and will get to Temple Bar long before I can.”

text: The hour had not yet arrived when the starved effigies of that countryside, hollow-eyed and motionless, would study the lamplighter so obsessively in their barren waiting that they would dream of refining his craft—of hoisting men by those same cords and wheels to blaze against the blackness of their plight. But the hour was still distant, and every gust that swept across the land stirred the scarecrows’ tatters uselessly, for the birds, elegant in plume and melody, heeded nothing.
source_text: For, the time was to come, when the gaunt scarecrows of that region should have watched the lamplighter, in their idleness and hunger, so long, as to conceive the idea of improving on his method, and hauling up men by those ropes and pulleys, to flare upon the darkness of their condition. But, the time was not come yet; and every wind that blew over France shook the rags of the scarecrows in vain, for the birds, fine of song and feather, took no warning.

text: Twice more through the black night the bell at the main gate clanged, and again the invasion surged in, while the grindstone spun and hissed. “What is that?” Lucie cried out in terror. “Quiet, now. The soldiers are sharpening their blades there,” Mr. Lorry replied. “This place has become national property, my dear—it serves as a sort of armoury.”
source_text: Twice more in the darkness the bell at the great gate sounded, and the irruption was repeated, and the grindstone whirled and spluttered. “What is it?” cried Lucie, affrighted. “Hush! The soldiers’ swords are sharpened there,” said Mr. Lorry. “The place is national property now, and used as a kind of armoury, my love.”

text: “If my reply seemed careless,” said Ashford, “I ask your pardon for it. I meant only to brush away a trifle that, to my astonishment, appears to weigh upon you so heavily. I swear to you, by the honour of a gentleman, that I have long since cast it from my thoughts. Dear God, what was there to cast away! Had I nothing of greater consequence to recall, in the noble aid you gave me on that occasion?”
source_text: “If it was a light answer,” returned Darnay, “I beg your forgiveness for it. I had no other object than to turn a slight thing, which, to my surprise, seems to trouble you too much, aside. I declare to you, on the faith of a gentleman, that I have long dismissed it from my mind. Good Heaven, what was there to dismiss! Have I had nothing more important to remember, in the great service you rendered me that day?”

text: The hand he held was weaker than he had imagined. Mr. Barsad perceived losing cards in it that Sydney Carton remained entirely unaware of. Dismissed from his respectable post in England for excessive and fruitless perjury—not because his services were unneeded there; our English habit of boasting about our disdain for secrecy and espionage is a rather recent development—he had made his way across the Channel and taken up work in France: initially, as a seducer and listener among his own compatriots; later, as a seducer and listener among the locals. He understood that under the fallen regime he had spied upon Saint Antoine and Defarge’s wineshop; had obtained from the vigilant authorities certain key details about Doctor Manette’s captivity, liberation, and past, meant to pave his way into casual talk with the Defarges; and had tested these on Madame Defarge, only to fail with them spectacularly. He recalled with dread and shivering that the fearsome woman had knitted while he spoke to her, and had fixed him with a menacing gaze as her needles clicked. He had since watched her, in the Saint Antoine district, repeatedly pull out her stitched lists and name individuals whose lives the guillotine then promptly consumed. He understood, as everyone in his line of work did, that he was perpetually at risk; that escape was out of the question; that he was bound tightly beneath the blade’s shadow; and that despite his most extreme flip-flopping and betrayals in service of the ruling terror, a single utterance could bring it crashing down on him. Once accused, and on such serious charges as had just flashed through his thoughts, he anticipated that the merciless woman—whose pitiless nature he had witnessed ample evidence of—would wield that deadly record against him and extinguish his final hope of survival. Besides, all covert operatives are easily frightened men, and here were certainly enough cards of a single dark suit to make the holder turn rather pale as he flipped them through.
source_text: It was a poorer hand than he suspected. Mr. Barsad saw losing cards in it that Sydney Carton knew nothing of. Thrown out of his honourable employment in England, through too much unsuccessful hard swearing there--not because he was not wanted there; our English reasons for vaunting our superiority to secrecy and spies are of very modern date--he knew that he had crossed the Channel, and accepted service in France: first, as a tempter and an eavesdropper among his own countrymen there: gradually, as a tempter and an eavesdropper among the natives. He knew that under the overthrown government he had been a spy upon Saint Antoine and Defarge’s wine-shop; had received from the watchful police such heads of information concerning Doctor Manette’s imprisonment, release, and history, as should serve him for an introduction to familiar conversation with the Defarges; and tried them on Madame Defarge, and had broken down with them signally. He always remembered with fear and trembling, that that terrible woman had knitted when he talked with her, and had looked ominously at him as her fingers moved. He had since seen her, in the Section of Saint Antoine, over and over again produce her knitted registers, and denounce people whose lives the guillotine then surely swallowed up.
