He knew, as every one employed as he was did, that he was never safe; that flight was impossible; that he was tied fast under the shadow of the axe; and that in spite of his utmost tergiversation and treachery in furtherance of the reigning terror, a word might bring it down upon him. Once denounced, and on such grave grounds as had just now been suggested to his mind, he foresaw that the dreadful woman of whose unrelenting character he had seen many proofs, would produce against him that fatal register, and would quash his last chance of life. Besides that all secret men are men soon terrified, here were surely cards enough of one black suit, to justify the holder in growing rather livid as he turned them over.

text: His removal—to clear the way for other prisoners awaiting judgment—drew him briefly from their embraces. Five more were to be tried together, denounced as enemies of the Republic because they had offered it no aid, either in speech or action. So swiftly did the Tribunal seize its chance to repay itself and the nation that, before he could leave the hall, the five descended to join him, sentenced to death within a day. The foremost among them announced it with the prison’s familiar sign of death—a lifted finger—and all of them cried aloud, “Long live the Republic!”
source_text: His removal, to make way for other accused persons who were to be tried, rescued him from these caresses for the moment. Five were to be tried together, next, as enemies of the Republic, forasmuch as they had not assisted it by word or deed. So quick was the Tribunal to compensate itself and the nation for a chance lost, that these five came down to him before he left the place, condemned to die within twenty-four hours. The first of them told him so, with the customary prison sign of Death--a raised finger--and they all added in words, “Long live the Republic!”

text: On the Lord’s Day, Miss Pross would sit at the Doctor’s board, yet all other days she stubbornly ate at unaccountable hours, whether deep in the kitchen quarters or secluded in her second-story chamber—a room of cerulean hue, which none save her little Butterfly was ever permitted to enter. That evening, moved by Butterfly’s gentle countenance and her tender attempts at delighting her, Miss Pross thawed most remarkably; and so the meal passed in uncommon sweetness.
source_text: On Sundays, Miss Pross dined at the Doctor’s table, but on other days persisted in taking her meals at unknown periods, either in the lower regions, or in her own room on the second floor--a blue chamber, to which no one but her Ladybird ever gained admittance. On this occasion, Miss Pross, responding to Ladybird’s pleasant face and pleasant efforts to please her, unbent exceedingly; so the dinner was very pleasant, too.

text: Through biting winter frosts and drifting snows, through the raw gales of spring, under the blazing summer sun, amid the autumn downpours, and once more through the biting frosts and snows of winter, Elara kept her daily vigil of two hours at that spot; and each day, as she departed, she pressed her lips to the stone wall. Her beloved saw her (so her father told her) perhaps once in five or six visits, perhaps two or three times in succession, perhaps not at all for a week or even a fortnight together. It sufficed that he could and did behold her whenever fortune permitted, and on that slender chance she would have lingered the whole day long, every day of the week.
source_text: In all weathers, in the snow and frost of winter, in the bitter winds of spring, in the hot sunshine of summer, in the rains of autumn, and again in the snow and frost of winter, Lucie passed two hours of every day at this place; and every day on leaving it, she kissed the prison wall. Her husband saw her (so she learned from her father) it might be once in five or six times: it might be twice or thrice running: it might be, not for a week or a fortnight together. It was enough that he could and did see her when the chances served, and on that possibility she would have waited out the day, seven days a week.

text: The nobleman, as a group, had distanced themselves from the reality that they were unvalued—so little desired in France that they faced serious risk of being expelled from both the country and existence itself. Much like the legendary peasant who, after immense effort, summoned the Devil only to be so horrified by the apparition that he fled without uttering a single question, the nobleman, having spent many years audaciously reciting the Lord’s Prayer in reverse and enacting other powerful rituals to command the Dark One, no sooner glimpsed his dreadful form than he bolted on his aristocratic legs.
source_text: Monseigneur, as a class, had dissociated himself from the phenomenon of his not being appreciated: of his being so little wanted in France, as to incur considerable danger of receiving his dismissal from it, and this life together. Like the fabled rustic who raised the Devil with infinite pains, and was so terrified at the sight of him that he could ask the Enemy no question, but immediately fled; so, Monseigneur, after boldly reading the Lord’s Prayer backwards for a great number of years, and performing many other potent spells for compelling the Evil One, no sooner beheld him in his terrors than he took to his noble heels.

text: In the shadowy recesses of Tellson’s, confined to cramped alcoves and narrow closets, the most ancient of clerks conducted their affairs with solemn dignity. Whenever a young man was brought into the London office, they tucked him away somewhere until age had claimed him. Like a cheese left to mature in the dark, he remained sequestered until he had absorbed the distinct Tellson essence and acquired its patina of blue mould. Only then was he allowed to emerge, conspicuously bent over enormous ledgers, his breeches and gaiters contributing their solid presence to the firm’s collective gravitas.
source_text: Cramped in all kinds of dim cupboards and hutches at Tellson’s, the oldest of men carried on the business gravely. When they took a young man into Tellson’s London house, they hid him somewhere till he was old. They kept him in a dark place, like a cheese, until he had the full Tellson flavour and blue-mould upon him. Then only was he permitted to be seen, spectacularly poring over large books, and casting his breeches and gaiters into the general weight of the establishment.

text: That night, when Mr. Lorry arrived as late as ten o’clock, Mr. Stryver was surrounded by books and papers deliberately scattered about, and gave every appearance of having put the morning’s matter entirely out of his thoughts. He actually seemed startled at the sight of Mr. Lorry, and remained thoroughly distracted and lost in his own concerns.
source_text: Accordingly, when Mr. Lorry called that night as late as ten o’clock, Mr. Stryver, among a quantity of books and papers littered out for the purpose, seemed to have nothing less on his mind than the subject of the morning. He even showed surprise when he saw Mr. Lorry, and was altogether in an absent and preoccupied state.

text: “Exactly so—everything’s safe and sound! There’s no reason, God be praised, why things shouldn’t be safe and sound in this place; yet I’ve been thoroughly unsettled all day, and I’m no longer as young as I once was! My tea, my dear! Thank you. Now, come join the group, and let’s settle ourselves quietly and listen to those echoes you have your ideas about.”
source_text: “That’s right; all safe and well! I don’t know why anything should be otherwise than safe and well here, thank God; but I have been so put out all day, and I am not as young as I was! My tea, my dear! Thank ye. Now, come and take your place in the circle, and let us sit quiet, and hear the echoes about which you have your theory.”

text: “I am glad you retain so much of the Englishman’s spirit,” said Miss Pross with approval, “though I do wish that chill had never settled in your voice. But the question, Doctor Manette—is there”—it was the dear soul’s habit to pretend to treat lightly whatever weighed heavily on all their hearts, and to approach it in this offhand fashion—“is there any hope yet, of our leaving this place?”
source_text: “I am glad you have so much of the Englishman in you, though I wish you had never taken that cold in your voice,” said Miss Pross, approvingly. “But the question, Doctor Manette. Is there”--it was the good creature’s way to affect to make light of anything that was a great anxiety with them all, and to come at it in this chance manner--“is there any prospect yet, of our getting out of this place?”

text: “Maybe he secured it as his final and most desperate shield against malice, the day before. What date does it bear? Yet never mind; do not pause to examine it; store it safely alongside my document and your own. Now, mark me! Until an hour or two ago, I never suspected he possessed, or could possess, such a writ. It holds force, unless revoked. But revocation may come swiftly, and, I have cause to believe, it shall.”
source_text: “Perhaps he obtained it as his last and utmost precaution against evil, yesterday. When is it dated? But no matter; don’t stay to look; put it up carefully with mine and your own. Now, observe! I never doubted until within this hour or two, that he had, or could have such a paper. It is good, until recalled. But it may be soon recalled, and, I have reason to think, will be.”

text: “I’ll tell you how. It rests on you—and there is no better man to carry it. This fresh accusation won’t come until after tomorrow at the earliest; more likely two or three days later, or even a week. You understand that to grieve for, or show any pity toward, a victim of the Guillotine is a capital offence. She and her father would certainly be guilty of that crime, and this woman—whose relentless pursuit defies description—will bide her time to add that weight to her case, making doubly certain. Do you follow me?”
source_text: “I am going to tell you how. It will depend on you, and it could depend on no better man. This new denunciation will certainly not take place until after to-morrow; probably not until two or three days afterwards; more probably a week afterwards. You know it is a capital crime, to mourn for, or sympathise with, a victim of the Guillotine. She and her father would unquestionably be guilty of this crime, and this woman (the inveteracy of whose pursuit cannot be described) would wait to add that strength to her case, and make herself doubly sure. You follow me?”

text: A strange turn then unfolded in the proceedings. The aim being to prove that the accused had journeyed, with some uncaught conspirator, by the Dover night coach on that Friday in November five years past, and had slipped from the coach under cover of darkness as a feint, at a spot where he did not linger, but whence he doubled back a dozen miles or more to a fortified seaport, and there gathered intelligence; a witness was summoned to place him, at the very hour required, in the coffee-room of an inn within that fortified-and-dockyard town, awaiting another man. The accused’s advocate had been questioning this witness to no effect, save that he had never beheld the accused at any other time, when the bewigged gentleman who had all the while been studying the courtroom’s ceiling scribbled a word or two upon a scrap of paper, crumpled it, and flicked it toward him. Unfolding this slip at the next recess, the advocate studied the prisoner with deep attention and wonder.
source_text: A singular circumstance then arose in the case. The object in hand being to show that the prisoner went down, with some fellow-plotter untracked, in the Dover mail on that Friday night in November five years ago, and got out of the mail in the night, as a blind, at a place where he did not remain, but from which he travelled back some dozen miles or more, to a garrison and dockyard, and there collected information; a witness was called to identify him as having been at the precise time required, in the coffee-room of an hotel in that garrison-and-dockyard town, waiting for another person. The prisoner’s counsel was cross-examining this witness with no result, except that he had never seen the prisoner on any other occasion, when the wigged gentleman who had all this time been looking at the ceiling of the court, wrote a word or two on a little piece of paper, screwed it up, and tossed it to him. Opening this piece of paper in the next pause, the counsel looked with great attention and curiosity at the prisoner.

text: “Nothing. My memory is utterly empty, from some moment—I can’t even say when—when I passed my imprisonment making shoes, until I discovered myself dwelling in London with my beloved daughter beside me. She had grown known to me by the time a merciful God gave back my reason; but I am wholly incapable of explaining how she came to be known. I recall nothing of the unfolding.”
source_text: “None. My mind is a blank, from some time--I cannot even say what time--when I employed myself, in my captivity, in making shoes, to the time when I found myself living in London with my dear daughter here. She had become familiar to me, when a gracious God restored my faculties; but, I am quite unable even to say how she had become familiar. I have no remembrance of the process.”

text: The Doctor had seen to it that the document would be on hand—had promised him it would be—and at this point in the proceedings it was brought forth and read aloud. Citizen Gabelle was summoned to verify it, and he did. With the utmost tact and courtesy, Citizen Gabelle let it be known that, under the heavy burden of cases the Tribunal faced from the Republic’s countless foes, he had been somewhat neglected in his cell at the Abbaye—had, in truth, slipped from the Tribunal’s patriotic memory—until three days prior, when he was called before it and freed, the Jury having declared themselves content that the charge against him was fully met, so far as he was concerned, by the surrender of the citizen Evrémonde, known as Darnay.
source_text: The Doctor had taken care that it should be there--had assured him that it would be there--and at this stage of the proceedings it was produced and read. Citizen Gabelle was called to confirm it, and did so. Citizen Gabelle hinted, with infinite delicacy and politeness, that in the pressure of business imposed on the Tribunal by the multitude of enemies of the Republic with which it had to deal, he had been slightly overlooked in his prison of the Abbaye--in fact, had rather passed out of the Tribunal’s patriotic remembrance--until three days ago; when he had been summoned before it, and had been set at liberty on the Jury’s declaring themselves satisfied that the accusation against him was answered, as to himself, by the surrender of the citizen Evrémonde, called Darnay.

text: “They are,” Mr. Lorry breathed, casting a terrified look about the sealed chamber, “slaughtering the captives. If you are certain of your claim; if you truly possess the influence you imagine—and I trust you do—reveal yourself to these fiends, and arrange to be carried to La Force. It might already be too late, I cannot tell, but do not let another moment slip!”
source_text: “They are,” Mr. Lorry whispered the words, glancing fearfully round at the locked room, “murdering the prisoners. If you are sure of what you say; if you really have the power you think you have--as I believe you have--make yourself known to these devils, and get taken to La Force. It may be too late, I don’t know, but let it not be a minute later!”

text: So passed the dwindling hours in the Cruncher household, till Young Jerry received his dismissal to the bedroom, and his mother, bound by a matching command, submitted without protest. The father whiled away the first stretches of night with lone pipes, delaying his departure until the clock crept near one. At that thin and spectral time, he drew himself upright, slipped a key from his pocket, unlatched a fastened cabinet, and extracted a sack, a crowbar of fitting length, a length of rope and chain, and sundry angling implements of that ilk. Arranging these items about his person with practiced ease, he flung a final scornful word toward Mrs. Cruncher, snuffed the candle, and stepped into the dark.
source_text: Thus the evening wore away with the Cruncher family, until Young Jerry was ordered to bed, and his mother, laid under similar injunctions, obeyed them. Mr. Cruncher beguiled the earlier watches of the night with solitary pipes, and did not start upon his excursion until nearly one o’clock. Towards that small and ghostly hour, he rose up from his chair, took a key out of his pocket, opened a locked cupboard, and brought forth a sack, a crowbar of convenient size, a rope and chain, and other fishing tackle of that nature. Disposing these articles about him in skilful manner, he bestowed a parting defiance on Mrs. Cruncher, extinguished the light, and went out.

text: The murmurs seldom stirred at Sydney Carton’s own footfalls. At most, on five or six occasions each year, he exercised his right to appear without summons, settling among them for the night as he had habitually done in former days. He never arrived flushed with drink. And one further secret about him was breathed through the murmurs—a secret that every faithful murmur has carried across the centuries.
source_text: The Echoes rarely answered to the actual tread of Sydney Carton. Some half-dozen times a year, at most, he claimed his privilege of coming in uninvited, and would sit among them through the evening, as he had once done often. He never came there heated with wine. And one other thing regarding him was whispered in the echoes, which has been whispered by all true echoes for ages and ages.

text: “He’s ignorant,” Defarge remarked, “or at least knows no more than would quickly lift him to a scaffold just as tall. I’ll take responsibility for him; let him stay here with me; I’ll look after him and start him on his way. He wants to glimpse the grand world—the King, the Queen, and the Court; let him behold them on Sunday.”
source_text: “He knows nothing,” said Defarge; “at least nothing more than would easily elevate himself to a gallows of the same height. I charge myself with him; let him remain with me; I will take care of him, and set him on his road. He wishes to see the fine world--the King, the Queen, and Court; let him see them on Sunday.”

text: Their household was run with strict economy—not only because it was the least conspicuous way to live and gave the least cause for resentment among the people, but also because they were far from wealthy. During his long imprisonment, Charles had been forced to spend heavily on wretched food, on paying his jailers, and on supporting the poorer prisoners. Partly for this reason, and partly to keep any hidden informer out of their home, they employed no servant. The man and woman who served as gatekeepers at the courtyard entrance helped them now and then, and Jerry—almost entirely given over to them by Mr. Lorry—had become their daily helper and slept there every night.
source_text: Their housekeeping was of a very frugal kind: not only because that was the safest way of life, involving the least offence to the people, but because they were not rich, and Charles, throughout his imprisonment, had had to pay heavily for his bad food, and for his guard, and towards the living of the poorer prisoners. Partly on this account, and partly to avoid a domestic spy, they kept no servant; the citizen and citizeness who acted as porters at the courtyard gate, rendered them occasional service; and Jerry (almost wholly transferred to them by Mr. Lorry) had become their daily retainer, and had his bed there every night.

text: The ocean of the crowd swept him forward with such overwhelming power that he could no more catch a breath or turn his head than if he were floundering in the breakers of the South Seas, until it deposited him in the Bastille’s outer courtyard. There, pressed against a corner of the wall, he fought to look around. Jacques Three stood almost beside him; Madame Defarge, still leading a knot of her women, could be glimpsed farther inside, knife in hand. All around was uproar, wild rejoicing, a deafening and frenzied chaos of sound, yet a furious pantomime.
source_text: So resistless was the force of the ocean bearing him on, that even to draw his breath or turn his head was as impracticable as if he had been struggling in the surf at the South Sea, until he was landed in the outer courtyard of the Bastille. There, against an angle of a wall, he made a struggle to look about him. Jacques Three was nearly at his side; Madame Defarge, still heading some of her women, was visible in the inner distance, and her knife was in her hand. Everywhere was tumult, exultation, deafening and maniacal bewilderment, astounding noise, yet furious dumb-show.

text: “I think,” replied Doctor Manette, “that there was a powerful and unusual resurgence of the chain of ideas and memories that originally triggered his illness. Certain deeply painful connections were, I suspect, brought back with striking clarity. Likely, a fear had been quietly haunting him for some time—that those memories would resurface, perhaps in specific conditions, perhaps at a certain moment. He struggled to ready himself, but to no avail; maybe the very act of trying left him even less equipped to endure it.”
source_text: “I believe,” returned Doctor Manette, “that there had been a strong and extraordinary revival of the train of thought and remembrance that was the first cause of the malady. Some intense associations of a most distressing nature were vividly recalled, I think. It is probable that there had long been a dread lurking in his mind, that those associations would be recalled--say, under certain circumstances--say, on a particular occasion. He tried to prepare himself in vain; perhaps the effort to prepare himself made him less able to bear it.”

text: The granite visages along the parapet gazed sightlessly into the dense darkness for three ponderous hours; for three ponderous hours, the steeds in their stalls clattered against the mangers, the hounds bayed, and the owl emitted a sound bearing scant likeness to the cry that men of verse have traditionally bestowed upon it. Yet it is the stubborn habit of such beings rarely, if ever, to utter what is prescribed for them.
source_text: The stone faces on the outer walls stared blindly at the black night for three heavy hours; for three heavy hours, the horses in the stables rattled at their racks, the dogs barked, and the owl made a noise with very little resemblance in it to the noise conventionally assigned to the owl by men-poets. But it is the obstinate custom of such creatures hardly ever to say what is set down for them.

text: Even obstacles far greater than those the Doctor then faced would have crumbled before his unwavering resolve. By faithfully maintaining his role as a physician attending to all people—bond and free, rich and poor, wicked and good—he wielded his personal influence with such quiet skill that he soon became the inspecting doctor of three prisons, La Force among them. He could now tell Lucie that her husband no longer languished in isolation, but mingled with the general population of prisoners; he visited her husband every week and brought back tender messages directly from his lips. At times, her husband even sent her a letter himself (though never through the Doctor’s hands), but she was forbidden to write back: for among the many feverish suspicions of prison conspiracies, none burned more fiercely than those aimed at emigrants known to have forged friendships or lasting ties abroad.
source_text: Greater things than the Doctor had at that time to contend with, would have yielded before his persevering purpose. While he kept himself in his place, as a physician, whose business was with all degrees of mankind, bond and free, rich and poor, bad and good, he used his personal influence so wisely, that he was soon the inspecting physician of three prisons, and among them of La Force. He could now assure Lucie that her husband was no longer confined alone, but was mixed with the general body of prisoners; he saw her husband weekly, and brought sweet messages to her, straight from his lips; sometimes her husband himself sent a letter to her (though never by the Doctor’s hand), but she was not permitted to write to him: for, among the many wild suspicions of plots in the prisons, the wildest of all pointed at emigrants who were known to have made friends or permanent connections abroad.

text: “I know full well that, without your support, I would be utterly without hope. I know full well that, even if Miss Manette carried me now in her blameless heart—do not imagine I am so bold as to presume that—I could hold no place there against the love she bears her father.”
source_text: “I well understand that, without you, I could have no hope. I well understand that, even if Miss Manette held me at this moment in her innocent heart--do not think I have the presumption to assume so much--I could retain no place in it against her love for her father.”

text: As for the merits of his argument, he harbored no uncertainty and envisioned a clear path to a favorable ruling. Presented to the jury on solid, practical reasoning—the sole kind ever deserving consideration—the matter was straightforward, without a single flaw. He testified on his own behalf, his account proved irrefutable, the opposing counsel abandoned the case, and the jury delivered their verdict without deliberation. Having examined it, Stryver, Chief Justice, felt assured that no simpler case existed.
source_text: As to the strength of his case, he had not a doubt about it, but clearly saw his way to the verdict. Argued with the jury on substantial worldly grounds--the only grounds ever worth taking into account--it was a plain case, and had not a weak spot in it. He called himself for the plaintiff, there was no getting over his evidence, the counsel for the defendant threw up his brief, and the jury did not even turn to consider. After trying it, Stryver, C. J., was satisfied that no plainer case could be.

text: Yet he strode onward, vanishing swiftly into the black maw of the penitentiary’s silhouette. When the designated moment arrived, he stepped forth once more and returned to Mr. Lorry’s chamber, discovering the elderly gentleman pacing in a fever of unease. He explained that he had remained at Lucie’s side until moments before, parting from her briefly solely to honor this engagement. Her father had not been glimpsed since departing the counting-house near four o’clock. She harbored a slender hope that his intercession might yet spare Charles, though it flickered faintly. Five hours had now elapsed since his disappearance: what could have become of him?
source_text: But, he went his way, and was soon swallowed up in the shadow of the prison wall. At the appointed hour, he emerged from it to present himself in Mr. Lorry’s room again, where he found the old gentleman walking to and fro in restless anxiety. He said he had been with Lucie until just now, and had only left her for a few minutes, to come and keep his appointment. Her father had not been seen, since he quitted the banking-house towards four o’clock. She had some faint hopes that his mediation might save Charles, but they were very slight. He had been more than five hours gone: where could he be?

text: He offered no explanation for his refusal, nor did he utter another syllable. Yet, as Mr. Lorry observed him in the dim light—hunched forward on the bench, elbows resting on his knees, face buried in his hands—it seemed to him that the man was silently, almost dreamily, questioning himself: “Why not?” The shrewd old banker sensed an opening in that unspoken doubt and resolved to seize it.
source_text: He made no effort to say why not, and said not a word more. But, Mr. Lorry thought he saw, as he leaned forward on his bench in the dusk, with his elbows on his knees and his head in his hands, that he was in some misty way asking himself, “Why not?” The sagacity of the man of business perceived an advantage here, and determined to hold it.

text: “As for what lies ahead,” the Doctor said, his voice steadying, “I would be full of hope. Since Heaven in its kindness saw fit to bring him back so swiftly, I would be full of hope. Having collapsed beneath the weight of some tangled burden—long dreaded, dimly anticipated, and struggled against for years—and having revived once the storm had broken and drifted away, I would believe the worst has passed.”
source_text: “As to the future,” said the Doctor, recovering firmness, “I should have great hope. As it pleased Heaven in its mercy to restore him so soon, I should have great hope. He, yielding under the pressure of a complicated something, long dreaded and long vaguely foreseen and contended against, and recovering after the cloud had burst and passed, I should hope that the worst was over.”

text: They stepped outside, leaving Lucie alone with her husband, her father, and the child beside a glowing hearth. Mr. Lorry was due back shortly from the bank. Miss Pross had lit the lamp but pushed it into a shadowy corner so they could savor the firelight without interruption. Young Lucie nestled next to her grandfather, her small fingers laced around his arm, while he, in a voice barely above a murmur, spun her a tale about a mighty Fairy who had shattered a prison wall to free a prisoner who had once helped the Fairy. Everything felt hushed and serene, and Lucie felt more at peace than she had in some time.
source_text: They went out, leaving Lucie, and her husband, her father, and the child, by a bright fire. Mr. Lorry was expected back presently from the Banking House. Miss Pross had lighted the lamp, but had put it aside in a corner, that they might enjoy the fire-light undisturbed. Little Lucie sat by her grandfather with her hands clasped through his arm: and he, in a tone not rising much above a whisper, began to tell her a story of a great and powerful Fairy who had opened a prison-wall and let out a captive who had once done the Fairy a service. All was subdued and quiet, and Lucie was more at ease than she had been.

text: The fragrance of the summer evening drifted up around him, settling as evenly as mist upon the weary, tattered, and dirt-streaked figures gathered at the nearby spring; there, the road-mender, propped by the blue cap without which he was a nonentity, still spun his ghostly tale of the man, holding them only as long as they could endure it. Little by little, as their endurance frayed, they slipped away one after another, and candles began to glimmer in tiny windows—lights that, as the panes went dark and more stars emerged, seemed not to have been snuffed out but to have risen softly into the heavens.
source_text: The sweet scents of the summer night rose all around him, and rose, as the rain falls, impartially, on the dusty, ragged, and toil-worn group at the fountain not far away; to whom the mender of roads, with the aid of the blue cap without which he was nothing, still enlarged upon his man like a spectre, as long as they could bear it. By degrees, as they could bear no more, they dropped off one by one, and lights twinkled in little casements; which lights, as the casements darkened, and more stars came out, seemed to have shot up into the sky instead of having been extinguished.

text: “Ernest,” replied Montcroix, straightening his spine, “should my lady wife resolve to guard the ledger within the vault of her mind, not a single utterance would escape her—not a breath of it. Woven into the fabric with her own hands and her own ciphers, it shall remain as lucid to her as the stars. Trust in Madame Montcroix. The feeblest coward who draws breath would find it a simpler task to blot himself from the living world than to expunge a single stroke of his title or transgressions from the threaded chronicle of Madame Montcroix.”
source_text: “Jacques,” returned Defarge, drawing himself up, “if madame my wife undertook to keep the register in her memory alone, she would not lose a word of it--not a syllable of it. Knitted, in her own stitches and her own symbols, it will always be as plain to her as the sun. Confide in Madame Defarge. It would be easier for the weakest poltroon that lives, to erase himself from existence, than to erase one letter of his name or crimes from the knitted register of Madame Defarge.”

text: “Do you imagine,” Mr. Lorry continued, a playful glint in his keen gaze as he regarded her warmly, “that after all those years, Doctor Manette holds any private belief about why he was so persecuted—perhaps even about who his persecutor was?”
source_text: “Do you suppose,” Mr. Lorry went on, with a laughing twinkle in his bright eye, as it looked kindly at her, “that Doctor Manette has any theory of his own, preserved through all those years, relative to the cause of his being so oppressed; perhaps, even to the name of his oppressor?”

text: The jailer lit the small heap, and it flared up fiercely, bright and warm. Bending once more to pass through the low, curved doorway, they abandoned the fire as it burned and made their way back to the yard; their hearing seemed to return gradually as they descended, until they stood again amid the roaring deluge.
source_text: The turnkey fired the little pile, which blazed high and hot. Stooping again to come out at the low-arched door, they left it burning, and retraced their way to the courtyard; seeming to recover their sense of hearing as they came down, until they were in the raging flood once more.

text: “Well, I’ll tell you what I notice,” Carton remarked. “I’ve got another card to play, Mr. Barsad. In this feverish Paris, thick with mistrust, you can’t possibly survive being exposed—not while you’re dealing with another noble-born informer just like yourself, one who carries the added puzzle of having pretended to die and then reappeared alive! A foreign conspiracy brewing inside the prisons, aimed at the Republic. A powerful card—a sure ticket to the guillotine! Will you take the gamble?”
source_text: “Humph! I see one thing,” said Carton. “I hold another card, Mr. Barsad. Impossible, here in raging Paris, with Suspicion filling the air, for you to outlive denunciation, when you are in communication with another aristocratic spy of the same antecedents as yourself, who, moreover, has the mystery about him of having feigned death and come to life again! A plot in the prisons, of the foreigner against the Republic. A strong card--a certain Guillotine card! Do you play?”

text: Doctor Manette had been so utterly petrified by this intrusion that he remained motionless, the lamp frozen in his grip as though he were a figure carved for no other purpose than to bear it. When the words ceased, he stirred at last, set the lamp aside, and faced the man who had spoken. Reaching out, he took hold of the loose front of his red woolen shirt, not with roughness, and spoke.
source_text: Doctor Manette, whom this visitation had so turned into stone, that he stood with the lamp in his hand, as if he were a statue made to hold it, moved after these words were spoken, put the lamp down, and confronting the speaker, and taking him, not ungently, by the loose front of his red woollen shirt, said:

text: “Mr. Barsad is as reliable a source as one could wish, sir,” Sydney remarked, “and I learned from his own words, shared with a fellow Sheep over a bottle of wine, that the arrest has been made. He left the officers at the entrance and watched the porter let them in. There can be no question at all that he’s been seized again.”
source_text: “Mr. Barsad is the best authority possible, sir,” said Sydney, “and I have it from Mr. Barsad’s communication to a friend and brother Sheep over a bottle of wine, that the arrest has taken place. He left the messengers at the gate, and saw them admitted by the porter. There is no earthly doubt that he is retaken.”

text: Scarcely thirty minutes had passed since their departure when they left behind the twinkling streetlights and the ever-vigilant guards, emerging onto a desolate highway. Here a second angler was collected—so noiselessly that, had Young Jerry been given to fancies, he might have imagined the mild-mannered devotee of the rod and line had abruptly divided into a pair.
source_text: Within half an hour from the first starting, they were beyond the winking lamps, and the more than winking watchmen, and were out upon a lonely road. Another fisherman was picked up here--and that so silently, that if Young Jerry had been superstitious, he might have supposed the second follower of the gentle craft to have, all of a sudden, split himself into two.

text: No man has ever truly loved a woman, lost her, and then, while she was a wife and mother, held her in his thoughts with a clear conscience and unchanged affection, without her children feeling a strange bond with him—an unspoken, tender pity. What delicate, hidden chords are struck in such a situation remains a mystery, but the fact is certain, and it held true here. Carton was the first outsider to whom little Lucie stretched out her plump arms, and he remained special to her as she grew. The small boy had mentioned him, almost at the very end. "Poor Carton! Kiss him for me!"
source_text: No man ever really loved a woman, lost her, and knew her with a blameless though an unchanged mind, when she was a wife and a mother, but her children had a strange sympathy with him--an instinctive delicacy of pity for him. What fine hidden sensibilities are touched in such a case, no echoes tell; but it is so, and it was so here. Carton was the first stranger to whom little Lucie held out her chubby arms, and he kept his place with her as she grew. The little boy had spoken of him, almost at the last. “Poor Carton! Kiss him for me!”

text: It happened that he was on the job at a time when the streets were nearly empty, late-returning women were scarce, and his overall luck was so poor that he began to seriously suspect Mrs. Cruncher had been deliberately "flopping" against him. Just then, an unusual crowd streaming west down Fleet Street caught his eye. Glancing in that direction, Mr. Cruncher realized a funeral procession was approaching, and that the public’s hostility toward it was stirring up a commotion.
source_text: It fell out that he was thus engaged in a season when crowds were few, and belated women few, and when his affairs in general were so unprosperous as to awaken a strong suspicion in his breast that Mrs. Cruncher must have been “flopping” in some pointed manner, when an unusual concourse pouring down Fleet-street westward, attracted his attention. Looking that way, Mr. Cruncher made out that some kind of funeral was coming along, and that there was popular objection to this funeral, which engendered uproar.

text: He lived in rooms at the Bank, clinging to the House that had absorbed him as ivy clings to a wall with deep, tenacious roots. It so happened that his presence in the main building offered a certain patriotic reassurance, but the honest old man never paused to reckon such things. So long as he did his duty, all else was immaterial to him. Across the courtyard, beneath a colonnade, stretched a broad standing for carriages—where, in fact, a few of Monseigneur’s carriages still remained. Two pillars held a pair of blazing flambeaux, and in their glare, exposed to the night air, stood a large grindstone: a rough, hastily mounted thing that looked as though it had been dragged there from some nearby smithy or workshop. Mr. Lorry rose, gazed out at these unremarkable objects, shivered, and withdrew to his seat beside the fire. He had opened not only the glazed window but the lattice blind beyond it, and after closing both again, a tremor ran through his whole frame.
source_text: He occupied rooms in the Bank, in his fidelity to the House of which he had grown to be a part, like strong root-ivy. It chanced that they derived a kind of security from the patriotic occupation of the main building, but the true-hearted old gentleman never calculated about that. All such circumstances were indifferent to him, so that he did his duty. On the opposite side of the courtyard, under a colonnade, was extensive standing--for carriages--where, indeed, some carriages of Monseigneur yet stood. Against two of the pillars were fastened two great flaring flambeaux, and in the light of these, standing out in the open air, was a large grindstone: a roughly mounted thing which appeared to have hurriedly been brought there from some neighbouring smithy, or other workshop. Rising and looking out of window at these harmless objects, Mr. Lorry shivered, and retired to his seat by the fire. He had opened, not only the glass window, but the lattice blind outside it, and he had closed both again, and he shivered through his frame.

text: Supper hour arrived, yet the Hundreds were still absent. Within the modest household, Miss Pross reigned over the kitchen quarters and always performed with remarkable skill. Her meals, though simple in nature, were so expertly prepared and gracefully presented, and so clever in their blend of English and French touches, that they could scarcely be improved upon. Miss Pross’s friendship was nothing if not fiercely practical; she had scoured Soho and the neighboring districts, hunting down destitute French exiles who, lured by shillings and half-crowns, would disclose their culinary secrets to her. From these faded sons and daughters of France, she had gleaned such astonishing arts that the woman and girl who served as the household’s only staff looked upon her as a veritable Enchantress, or Cinderella’s Fairy Godmother—someone who could send for a fowl, a rabbit, a few garden vegetables, and transform them into whatever dish she fancied.
source_text: Dinner-time, and still no Hundreds of people. In the arrangements of the little household, Miss Pross took charge of the lower regions, and always acquitted herself marvellously. Her dinners, of a very modest quality, were so well cooked and so well served, and so neat in their contrivances, half English and half French, that nothing could be better. Miss Pross’s friendship being of the thoroughly practical kind, she had ravaged Soho and the adjacent provinces, in search of impoverished French, who, tempted by shillings and half-crowns, would impart culinary mysteries to her. From these decayed sons and daughters of Gaul, she had acquired such wonderful arts, that the woman and girl who formed the staff of domestics regarded her as quite a Sorceress, or Cinderella’s Godmother: who would send out for a fowl, a rabbit, a vegetable or two from the garden, and change them into anything she pleased.

text: By noon the next day, the remarkable woman had settled into her customary spot in the wine-shop, knitting with tireless focus. A rose rested beside her, and though she occasionally glanced at it, her distant, absorbed expression never faltered. A scattering of customers lingered about—some drinking, some not, some standing, others seated. The heat was oppressive, and swarms of flies, extending their bold and prying explorations into every sticky little glass near Madame, dropped dead at the bottom. Their deaths stirred no reaction among the other flies parading nearby, who regarded them with utter indifference—as if they themselves were elephants, or creatures equally remote—until they, too, succumbed to the same end. How strange to think of the carelessness of flies!—perhaps the same thought drifted through the Court that bright summer day.
source_text: Next noontide saw the admirable woman in her usual place in the wine-shop, knitting away assiduously. A rose lay beside her, and if she now and then glanced at the flower, it was with no infraction of her usual preoccupied air. There were a few customers, drinking or not drinking, standing or seated, sprinkled about. The day was very hot, and heaps of flies, who were extending their inquisitive and adventurous perquisitions into all the glutinous little glasses near madame, fell dead at the bottom. Their decease made no impression on the other flies out promenading, who looked at them in the coolest manner (as if they themselves were elephants, or something as far removed), until they met the same fate. Curious to consider how heedless flies are!--perhaps they thought as much at Court that sunny summer day.

text: “Consider,” said Dr. Moreau, facing him after a strained silence, “how difficult it is to render coherent the secret motions of this wretched man’s spirit. He once craved that work with such dreadful hunger, and it came as a profound relief; it surely eased his torment so deeply, exchanging the tangle of the mind for the tangle of the fingers, and later, as skill grew, replacing the cunning of mental anguish with the cunning of the hand; that he has never endured the notion of letting it slip wholly beyond his grasp. Even today, when I suspect he holds more hope for himself than ever before, and even refers to his own state with a tentative assurance, the mere thought that he might require that former trade and find it gone strikes him with a swift, chilling dread—like the terror one imagines piercing the heart of a child who has strayed.”
source_text: “You see,” said Doctor Manette, turning to him after an uneasy pause, “it is very hard to explain, consistently, the innermost workings of this poor man’s mind. He once yearned so frightfully for that occupation, and it was so welcome when it came; no doubt it relieved his pain so much, by substituting the perplexity of the fingers for the perplexity of the brain, and by substituting, as he became more practised, the ingenuity of the hands, for the ingenuity of the mental torture; that he has never been able to bear the thought of putting it quite out of his reach. Even now, when I believe he is more hopeful of himself than he has ever been, and even speaks of himself with a kind of confidence, the idea that he might need that old employment, and not find it, gives him a sudden sense of terror, like that which one may fancy strikes to the heart of a lost child.”

text: “Behold a gentleman of commerce—a gentleman of maturity—a gentleman seasoned _within_ a counting-house,” declared Ashcombe; “and after tallying three cardinal arguments for absolute triumph, he pronounces there is no argument whatsoever! Pronounces it with his head attached!” Mr. Ashcombe observed the oddity as though the declaration would have been considerably less striking had it been delivered with his head detached.
source_text: “Here’s a man of business--a man of years--a man of experience--_in_ a Bank,” said Stryver; “and having summed up three leading reasons for complete success, he says there’s no reason at all! Says it with his head on!” Mr. Stryver remarked upon the peculiarity as if it would have been infinitely less remarkable if he had said it with his head off.

text: From the very moment of his peril, he had cherished Lucie Manette. No sound had ever struck him as so gentle and precious as the pity in her voice; no face had ever seemed so exquisitely kind as hers when it met his at the brink of the grave they had prepared for him. Yet he had never broached the matter with her. A full year had passed since the killing at the lonely chateau, far off beyond the swelling sea and the endless, dusty roads—the stone chateau, once so solid, now dissolved into the mere haze of a dream—and still he had not, by a single uttered word, revealed the truth of his feelings.
source_text: He had loved Lucie Manette from the hour of his danger. He had never heard a sound so sweet and dear as the sound of her compassionate voice; he had never seen a face so tenderly beautiful, as hers when it was confronted with his own on the edge of the grave that had been dug for him. But, he had not yet spoken to her on the subject; the assassination at the deserted chateau far away beyond the heaving water and the long, long, dusty roads--the solid stone chateau which had itself become the mere mist of a dream--had been done a year, and he had never yet, by so much as a single spoken word, disclosed to her the state of his heart.

text: Haggard Saint Antoine had known only a single triumphant week to temper his meager, bitter crust with the savor of brotherly embraces and shared rejoicing, when Madame Defarge was once more at her counter, presiding over her patrons as she always did. She wore no rose in her hair, for in that brief span the vast fraternity of Spies had grown exceedingly wary of entrusting themselves to the saint’s tender mercies. The streetlamps above his avenues swayed with a dreadfully elastic motion.
source_text: Haggard Saint Antoine had had only one exultant week, in which to soften his modicum of hard and bitter bread to such extent as he could, with the relish of fraternal embraces and congratulations, when Madame Defarge sat at her counter, as usual, presiding over the customers. Madame Defarge wore no rose in her head, for the great brotherhood of Spies had become, even in one short week, extremely chary of trusting themselves to the saint’s mercies. The lamps across his streets had a portentously elastic swing with them.

text: They entered a dim, cluttered study crammed with books and strewn with papers, where a fire roared in the grate. A kettle puffed steam on the hearth, and amid the chaos of scattered documents a table gleamed, laden with wine, brandy, rum, sugar, and lemons.
source_text: They went into a dingy room lined with books and littered with papers, where there was a blazing fire. A kettle steamed upon the hob, and in the midst of the wreck of papers a table shone, with plenty of wine upon it, and brandy, and rum, and sugar, and lemons.

text: A London banking house that mirrored Tellson’s Paris branch would swiftly have lost its wits and found itself in the Gazette. What would sober British prudence and decorum have made of potted orange-trees adorning a bank courtyard, or a painted Cupid poised above the clerks? Yet there they stood. Tellson’s had coated the little god in whitewash, but he still hovered on the ceiling, clad in the airiest drapery, aiming (as is his eternal habit) at lucre from dawn till dusk. Ruin would surely have followed this heathen boy in Lombard-street, along with the curtained recess tucked behind him, and the mirror sunk into the wall, and the far-from-elderly clerks who broke into dances at the merest excuse. Still, a French Tellson’s managed these flourishes perfectly well, and so long as the age kept its seams intact, no one took alarm or withdrew a single sovereign.
source_text: A place of business in London like Tellson’s place of business in Paris, would soon have driven the House out of its mind and into the Gazette. For, what would staid British responsibility and respectability have said to orange-trees in boxes in a Bank courtyard, and even to a Cupid over the counter? Yet such things were. Tellson’s had whitewashed the Cupid, but he was still to be seen on the ceiling, in the coolest linen, aiming (as he very often does) at money from morning to night. Bankruptcy must inevitably have come of this young Pagan, in Lombard-street, London, and also of a curtained alcove in the rear of the immortal boy, and also of a looking-glass let into the wall, and also of clerks not at all old, who danced in public on the slightest provocation. Yet, a French Tellson’s could get on with these things exceedingly well, and, as long as the times held together, no man had taken fright at them, and drawn out his money.

text: At last the jackal assembled a tidy feast for the lion and stepped forward to present it. The lion accepted it with measured wariness, picking out what he wanted and offering his observations, while the jackal lent a hand with both. Once the meal was thoroughly dealt with, the lion tucked his hands back into his waistband and stretched out to reflect. The jackal then refreshed himself with a deep drink for his throat and a fresh dose for his head, and set to gathering another round of food. This too was served to the lion in the same fashion, and wasn't finished off until the clocks chimed three in the morning.
source_text: At length the jackal had got together a compact repast for the lion, and proceeded to offer it to him. The lion took it with care and caution, made his selections from it, and his remarks upon it, and the jackal assisted both. When the repast was fully discussed, the lion put his hands in his waistband again, and lay down to meditate. The jackal then invigorated himself with a bumper for his throttle, and a fresh application to his head, and applied himself to the collection of a second meal; this was administered to the lion in the same manner, and was not disposed of until the clocks struck three in the morning.

text: “Quiet, small Fury,” murmured the woman known as the Knitter, pressing two fingers firmly against her second’s mouth, her brow creasing. “Listen to my words. My man, citizen, is a staunch Patriot and a fearless soul; he has earned the Republic’s favor and holds its trust. Yet my man has his failings, and he is soft enough to show mercy to this Physician.”
source_text: “Peace, little Vengeance,” said Madame Defarge, laying her hand with a slight frown on her lieutenant’s lips, “hear me speak. My husband, fellow-citizen, is a good Republican and a bold man; he has deserved well of the Republic, and possesses its confidence. But my husband has his weaknesses, and he is so weak as to relent towards this Doctor.”

text: “He sought to recount for me the origins of that dispute, and declared that, by his best lights, it was an unjust and senseless one on Britain’s side. He remarked, with a playful air, that perchance George Washington might earn a renown in history nearly matching that of George the Third. Yet no malice coloured his manner of speaking: it was uttered in mirth, and merely to while away the hour.”
source_text: “He tried to explain to me how that quarrel had arisen, and he said that, so far as he could judge, it was a wrong and foolish one on England’s part. He added, in a jesting way, that perhaps George Washington might gain almost as great a name in history as George the Third. But there was no harm in his way of saying this: it was said laughingly, and to beguile the time.”

text: The years of imprisonment had carved their cold currents across his striking features, yet he concealed their traces with such fierce resolve that he commanded them even in slumber. In all the vast realms of sleep that night, no countenance displayed a more extraordinary contest—silent, steadfast, and vigilant—against a foe no eye could see.
source_text: Into his handsome face, the bitter waters of captivity had worn; but, he covered up their tracks with a determination so strong, that he held the mastery of them even in his sleep. A more remarkable face in its quiet, resolute, and guarded struggle with an unseen assailant, was not to be beheld in all the wide dominions of sleep, that night.

text: “Ah!” the man replied with grim satisfaction. “He’ll be tied to a hurdle and dragged out to be half strangled, then cut down and slit open before his own eyes. His entrails will be pulled out and burned while he watches, and then his head will be struck off, and his body hewn into four parts. That’s the judgment.”
source_text: “Ah!” returned the man, with a relish; “he’ll be drawn on a hurdle to be half hanged, and then he’ll be taken down and sliced before his own face, and then his inside will be taken out and burnt while he looks on, and then his head will be chopped off, and he’ll be cut into quarters. That’s the sentence.”

text: The whole village had gathered around the fountain, lingering in their usual downcast way and murmuring softly, yet betraying nothing beyond a grim curiosity and mild astonishment. The hobbled cows, hurriedly fetched and tied to whatever would hold them, gazed blankly about or lay chewing a cud that offered scant reward for their trouble, gathered before their idle wandering had been cut short. Some from the chateau, a few from the coaching inn, and all the tax collectors stood more or less armed, clustered across the narrow street in an aimless fashion that was charged with emptiness. Already the road-mender had pushed into the heart of a knot of fifty close companions and was beating his chest with his blue cap. What could this signify, and what did it mean that Monsieur Gabelle was being swiftly hoisted onto a horse behind a servant and spirited away at a gallop, the beast doubly burdened, like a strange reimagining of the German ballad of Leonora?
source_text: All the people of the village were at the fountain, standing about in their depressed manner, and whispering low, but showing no other emotions than grim curiosity and surprise. The led cows, hastily brought in and tethered to anything that would hold them, were looking stupidly on, or lying down chewing the cud of nothing particularly repaying their trouble, which they had picked up in their interrupted saunter. Some of the people of the chateau, and some of those of the posting-house, and all the taxing authorities, were armed more or less, and were crowded on the other side of the little street in a purposeless way, that was highly fraught with nothing. Already, the mender of roads had penetrated into the midst of a group of fifty particular friends, and was smiting himself in the breast with his blue cap. What did all this portend, and what portended the swift hoisting-up of Monsieur Gabelle behind a servant on horseback, and the conveying away of the said Gabelle (double-laden though the horse was), at a gallop, like a new version of the German ballad of Leonora?

text: “You realize, Doctor, that these Nobles have the Right to yoke us common folk like beasts and force us to pull their carts. That’s what they did to him—yoked and drove him. You realize they also have the Right to make us stay out all night on their estates, hushing the frogs so their precious sleep goes undisturbed. They kept him outside in the damp, sickly night air, then ordered him back into the yoke by day. Still, he wouldn’t yield. No! Released from the yoke one noon to eat—if he could scrounge anything—he sobbed twelve times, once for every toll of the bell, and died on her breast.”
source_text: “‘You know, Doctor, that it is among the Rights of these Nobles to harness us common dogs to carts, and drive us. They so harnessed him and drove him. You know that it is among their Rights to keep us in their grounds all night, quieting the frogs, in order that their noble sleep may not be disturbed. They kept him out in the unwholesome mists at night, and ordered him back into his harness in the day. But he was not persuaded. No! Taken out of harness one day at noon, to feed--if he could find food--he sobbed twelve times, once for every stroke of the bell, and died on her bosom.’

text: “There’s no remedy for it,” Miss Pross said, shaking her head. “Pluck that chord, and he turns darker in an instant. Best to let it lie. Indeed, it must be let alone, whether one wishes it or not. Now and then, in the deep stillness of night, he rises, and we above can hear him pacing, pacing, pacing in his chamber. Ladybird has learned to read the sign—his mind is pacing, pacing, pacing within the old cell of memory. She hastens to his side, and together they walk, pacing, pacing, until he quiets. Yet he never breathes a word to her of what truly stirs him, and she senses it is wiser not to allude to it. Silently they move together, pacing, pacing, until her devotion and nearness restore him to himself.”
source_text: “Can’t be helped,” said Miss Pross, shaking her head. “Touch that string, and he instantly changes for the worse. Better leave it alone. In short, must leave it alone, like or no like. Sometimes, he gets up in the dead of the night, and will be heard, by us overhead there, walking up and down, walking up and down, in his room. Ladybird has learnt to know then that his mind is walking up and down, walking up and down, in his old prison. She hurries to him, and they go on together, walking up and down, walking up and down, until he is composed. But he never says a word of the true reason of his restlessness, to her, and she finds it best not to hint at it to him. In silence they go walking up and down together, walking up and down together, till her love and company have brought him to himself.”

text: The dawn grew ever more luminous, until at last the sun grazed the crowns of the motionless trees and spilled its brilliance across the slope. In that light, the water of the manor’s fountain appeared to darken into wine, and the sculpted visages flushed scarlet. The chorus of birds rang out, piercing and fervent, and upon the weathered ledge of the vast casement in the sleeping chamber of the Seigneur, a single small bird poured forth its most honeyed melody with all its strength. At this, the closest stone countenance seemed to gape in wonder, its mouth agape and jaw hanging slack, as though stricken with awe.
source_text: Lighter and lighter, until at last the sun touched the tops of the still trees, and poured its radiance over the hill. In the glow, the water of the chateau fountain seemed to turn to blood, and the stone faces crimsoned. The carol of the birds was loud and high, and, on the weather-beaten sill of the great window of the bed-chamber of Monsieur the Marquis, one little bird sang its sweetest song with all its might. At this, the nearest stone face seemed to stare amazed, and, with open mouth and dropped under-jaw, looked awe-stricken.

text: The door shut, and Carton found himself by himself. He focused every fiber of his hearing, straining to catch any noise that might signal danger or discovery. Nothing came. Keys rotated, doors banged shut, footfalls echoed down far-off corridors: no shout rang out, no sudden rush suggested anything out of the ordinary. After a short time, his breath came easier, and he seated himself at the table, listening once more until the clock chimed two.
source_text: The door closed, and Carton was left alone. Straining his powers of listening to the utmost, he listened for any sound that might denote suspicion or alarm. There was none. Keys turned, doors clashed, footsteps passed along distant passages: no cry was raised, or hurry made, that seemed unusual. Breathing more freely in a little while, he sat down at the table, and listened again until the clock struck Two.

text: A heavy vapour clung to every dip and fold of the land, and in its desolation it had drifted up the slope like a restless wraith, searching for repose and discovering none. A dank and piercingly chill vapour, it crept through the atmosphere in slow undulations that visibly pursued and lapped over one another, as the swells of a sickly ocean might. It was thick enough to obscure all from the glow of the carriage-lanterns save its own churning motions and a short stretch of track; and the steam from the straining horses breathed into it, as though they had birthed it entire.
source_text: There was a steaming mist in all the hollows, and it had roamed in its forlornness up the hill, like an evil spirit, seeking rest and finding none. A clammy and intensely cold mist, it made its slow way through the air in ripples that visibly followed and overspread one another, as the waves of an unwholesome sea might do. It was dense enough to shut out everything from the light of the coach-lamps but these its own workings, and a few yards of road; and the reek of the labouring horses steamed into it, as if they had made it all.

text: Yet, this was only at the beginning. Soon, the realization dawned that his impending end held no shame, and that countless others, innocent, walked that same path daily with steady steps—this thought arose to strengthen him. Then came the reflection that the lasting serenity his loved ones might know hinged on his own calm courage. Gradually, he settled into a more peaceful frame, where his mind could reach upward and draw solace down.
source_text: But, all this was at first. Before long, the consideration that there was no disgrace in the fate he must meet, and that numbers went the same road wrongfully, and trod it firmly every day, sprang up to stimulate him. Next followed the thought that much of the future peace of mind enjoyable by the dear ones, depended on his quiet fortitude. So, by degrees he calmed into the better state, when he could raise his thoughts much higher, and draw comfort down.

text: Yes. The magnetic pull of fate was upon him, and he must journey onward, until he made landfall. He perceived no reef; he scarcely glimpsed any peril. The purpose behind his past actions, though left unfinished, now rose before him in a light that he believed would be met with gratitude in France once he appeared to claim it. Then, that radiant dream of doing good—so often the hopeful mirage of generous spirits—unfolded before his mind’s eye, and in the illusion he even saw himself wielding some sway to steer the furious Revolution that was careering so terribly out of control.
source_text: Yes. The Loadstone Rock was drawing him, and he must sail on, until he struck. He knew of no rock; he saw hardly any danger. The intention with which he had done what he had done, even although he had left it incomplete, presented it before him in an aspect that would be gratefully acknowledged in France on his presenting himself to assert it. Then, that glorious vision of doing good, which is so often the sanguine mirage of so many good minds, arose before him, and he even saw himself in the illusion with some influence to guide this raging Revolution that was running so fearfully wild.

text: Amid the roaring tempest of fury and strife that swirled around the stern old soldier, marked out by his ash-hued tunic and crimson badge, a single figure held utterly still—a woman’s. “Look, my husband stands there!” she shouted, stabbing a finger toward him. “Look at Defarge!” She planted herself motionless beside the stern old soldier, and stayed motionless beside him; stayed motionless beside him through the winding alleys as Defarge and the others swept him onward; stayed motionless beside him when they neared his appointed end and the first blows struck his back; stayed motionless beside him when the slow-built downpour of blades and fists crashed upon him; pressed so near him when he collapsed lifeless beneath it that, springing into sudden ferocious life, she set her foot upon his throat, and with her pitiless blade—long held ready—sawed off his head.
source_text: In the howling universe of passion and contention that seemed to encompass this grim old officer conspicuous in his grey coat and red decoration, there was but one quite steady figure, and that was a woman’s. “See, there is my husband!” she cried, pointing him out. “See Defarge!” She stood immovable close to the grim old officer, and remained immovable close to him; remained immovable close to him through the streets, as Defarge and the rest bore him along; remained immovable close to him when he was got near his destination, and began to be struck at from behind; remained immovable close to him when the long-gathering rain of stabs and blows fell heavy; was so close to him when he dropped dead under it, that, suddenly animated, she put her foot upon his neck, and with her cruel knife--long ready--hewed off his head.

text: “Oh, sir, someday I’ll share my name with you, and tell you about my mother and my father, and how I was never told the harsh, bitter story of their lives. But I can’t speak of it now, and I can’t speak of it in this place. All I can say, here and now, is that I beg you to lay your hands on me and give me your blessing. Kiss me, kiss me! Oh, my darling, my darling!”
source_text: “O, sir, at another time you shall know my name, and who my mother was, and who my father, and how I never knew their hard, hard history. But I cannot tell you at this time, and I cannot tell you here. All that I may tell you, here and now, is, that I pray to you to touch me and to bless me. Kiss me, kiss me! O my dear, my dear!”

text: “I know just how to corner _you_, Mr. Darnay,” said Bully Stryver, “and I mean to do it. If this man claims to be a gentleman, I _can’t_ make him out. Feel free to pass that along to him, with my regards. You can also tell him from me that, having handed over his property and status to this bloodthirsty rabble, I’m surprised he isn’t leading them himself. But no, gentlemen,” Stryver continued, glancing around the room and snapping his fingers, “I’ve seen enough of human nature to know you’ll never meet a man like this one, throwing himself on the kindness of such darling _protégés_. No, gentlemen; he’ll always turn tail at the first sign of trouble and slip away unseen.”
source_text: “I understand how to put _you_ in a corner, Mr. Darnay,” said Bully Stryver, “and I’ll do it. If this fellow is a gentleman, I _don’t_ understand him. You may tell him so, with my compliments. You may also tell him, from me, that after abandoning his worldly goods and position to this butcherly mob, I wonder he is not at the head of them. But, no, gentlemen,” said Stryver, looking all round, and snapping his fingers, “I know something of human nature, and I tell you that you’ll never find a fellow like this fellow, trusting himself to the mercies of such precious _protégés_. No, gentlemen; he’ll always show ’em a clean pair of heels very early in the scuffle, and sneak away.”

text: “Doctor, seeing my brother entangled in this predicament with these servants, I suggested we seek your assistance. Your name carries great esteem, and as a young man still building his prospects, you are no doubt attentive to your own advantage. What you witness here must be observed, never uttered.”
source_text: “‘Doctor, finding my brother in this difficulty with these hinds, I recommended that your aid should be invited. Your reputation is high, and, as a young man with your fortune to make, you are probably mindful of your interest. The things that you see here, are things to be seen, and not spoken of.’

text: That odd nook possessed such strange acoustics, so singular an Ear of a spot, that as Mr. Lorry lingered by the open casement, straining for the father and daughter whose footfalls he caught, he imagined they would never draw near. Not merely would the reverberations fade, as if the steps had retreated; but phantoms of other steps that never arrived would sound instead, and would vanish forever just when they seemed on the verge of arriving. Yet at length father and daughter did emerge, and Miss Pross stood waiting at the street door to welcome them.
source_text: It was such a curious corner in its acoustical properties, such a peculiar Ear of a place, that as Mr. Lorry stood at the open window, looking for the father and daughter whose steps he heard, he fancied they would never approach. Not only would the echoes die away, as though the steps had gone; but, echoes of other steps that never came would be heard in their stead, and would die away for good when they seemed close at hand. However, father and daughter did at last appear, and Miss Pross was ready at the street door to receive them.

text: Terrified by her overwhelming distress and the emptiness of the abandoned chambers—where she half-fancied faces lurking behind each ajar door—Miss Pross fetched a bowl of cool water and started bathing her puffy, crimson eyes. Tormented by restless dread, she couldn’t stand even a moment’s blindness from the trickling water, and kept stopping to glance about, checking that no one observed her. During one such halt, she flinched and shrieked, for she spotted a silhouette standing within the room.
source_text: Afraid, in her extreme perturbation, of the loneliness of the deserted rooms, and of half-imagined faces peeping from behind every open door in them, Miss Pross got a basin of cold water and began laving her eyes, which were swollen and red. Haunted by her feverish apprehensions, she could not bear to have her sight obscured for a minute at a time by the dripping water, but constantly paused and looked round to see that there was no one watching her. In one of those pauses she recoiled and cried out, for she saw a figure standing in the room.

text: The place was Mr. Cruncher’s own chambers in Hanging-sword-alley, Whitefriars; the hour, seven-thirty on a blustery March morning in the Year of Our Lord 1780. (Mr. Cruncher himself invariably called it “Anna Dominoes,” as if the Christian calendar began with a lady inventing a parlor game and christening it after herself.)
source_text: The scene was Mr. Cruncher’s private lodging in Hanging-sword-alley, Whitefriars: the time, half-past seven of the clock on a windy March morning, Anno Domini seventeen hundred and eighty. (Mr. Cruncher himself always spoke of the year of our Lord as Anna Dominoes: apparently under the impression that the Christian era dated from the invention of a popular game, by a lady who had bestowed her name upon it.)

text: From his expression, Mr. Cruncher seemed to agree. Yet he remained silent, and Miss Pross, fumbling tearfully through the contents of her handbag with considerable effort, managed to settle the bill for her wine. While she did so, Solomon addressed the devotees of the virtuous Republican Brutus of old, offering a brief explanation in French that promptly sent them back to their earlier seats and occupations.
source_text: Apparently, Mr. Cruncher did, to judge from his looks. He said not a word, however, and Miss Pross, exploring the depths of her reticule through her tears with great difficulty paid for her wine. As she did so, Solomon turned to the followers of the Good Republican Brutus of Antiquity, and offered a few words of explanation in the French language, which caused them all to relapse into their former places and pursuits.

text: Darnay obeyed, and they returned him to the guardroom, where a cluster of patriots in coarse crimson bonnets lounged around a watch-fire, some smoking, some drinking, others lost in slumber. There he settled a steep fee for his guide, and from that place they set out together along the drenched, sodden roads as the clock struck three in the morning.
source_text: Darnay complied, and was taken back to the guard-house, where other patriots in rough red caps were smoking, drinking, and sleeping, by a watch-fire. Here he paid a heavy price for his escort, and hence he started with it on the wet, wet roads at three o’clock in the morning.

text: A splendid nook for reverberations, some have observed, that nook where the Professor dwelt. Constantly spinning the silver cord that linked her brother, her mother, and herself, and her aged tutor and friend, in an existence of serene joy, Clara rested in the hushed home in the softly murmuring alcove, hearkening to the resounding tread of seasons.
source_text: A wonderful corner for echoes, it has been remarked, that corner where the Doctor lived. Ever busily winding the golden thread which bound her husband, and her father, and herself, and her old directress and companion, in a life of quiet bliss, Lucie sat in the still house in the tranquilly resounding corner, listening to the echoing footsteps of years.

text: His portly jailer, spectacles perched for reading, peered over them to confirm the prisoner’s presence, then proceeded down the roster, pausing briefly at each name. Twenty-three names were called, but only twenty answered; one prisoner had perished in the jail and slipped from memory, while two had already met the guillotine and been forgotten. The roll was intoned in the same vaulted chamber where Darnay, on his first night, had glimpsed his fellow captives. Every one of them had been slaughtered in the massacre; every soul he had since cherished and lost had died upon the scaffold.
source_text: His bloated gaoler, who wore spectacles to read with, glanced over them to assure himself that he had taken his place, and went through the list, making a similar short pause at each name. There were twenty-three names, but only twenty were responded to; for one of the prisoners so summoned had died in gaol and been forgotten, and two had already been guillotined and forgotten. The list was read, in the vaulted chamber where Darnay had seen the associated prisoners on the night of his arrival. Every one of those had perished in the massacre; every human creature he had since cared for and parted with, had died on the scaffold.

text: “I can’t say, Mr. Darnay; I warned you it was a silly whim, yet you insisted on hearing it. Whenever I’ve surrendered to the notion, I’ve been by myself, and in those moments I’ve pictured them as the footfalls of those destined to enter my life, and my father’s.”
source_text: “I don’t know, Mr. Darnay; I told you it was a foolish fancy, but you asked for it. When I have yielded myself to it, I have been alone, and then I have imagined them the footsteps of the people who are to come into my life, and my father’s.”

text: In that resonant corner, Lucie listened to these echoes—sometimes lost in thought, sometimes laughing with delight—until her little girl turned six. There’s no need to describe how deeply she cherished the echoes of her child’s footsteps, or those of her father, always purposeful and composed, or those of her husband. Nor is it necessary to say that the faintest echo of their shared household, which she managed with such graceful, intelligent economy that it felt richer than any extravagance, was pure music to her. And there’s no need to recount how sweetly the echoes surrounded her: the many times her father told her she seemed even more devoted to him after marriage (if such a thing were possible), and the many times her husband said that no worries or responsibilities ever appeared to lessen her love or her support for him, asking, “What’s your magical secret, my darling, for being everything to each of us, as though you had only one person to care for, yet never seeming rushed or overburdened?”
source_text: These were among the echoes to which Lucie, sometimes pensive, sometimes amused and laughing, listened in the echoing corner, until her little daughter was six years old. How near to her heart the echoes of her child’s tread came, and those of her own dear father’s, always active and self-possessed, and those of her dear husband’s, need not be told. Nor, how the lightest echo of their united home, directed by herself with such a wise and elegant thrift that it was more abundant than any waste, was music to her. Nor, how there were echoes all about her, sweet in her ears, of the many times her father had told her that he found her more devoted to him married (if that could be) than single, and of the many times her husband had said to her that no cares and duties seemed to divide her love for him or her help to him, and asked her “What is the magic secret, my darling, of your being everything to all of us, as if there were only one of us, yet never seeming to be hurried, or to have too much to do?”

text: A moment later, Miss Pross was murmuring quietly beside him.
