Any lingering uncertainty he might have harbored would certainly have been dispelled by her words, but his mind was already lucid and free of doubt. He suggested they allow the hours to pass until the usual breakfast time, then greet the Doctor as though the morning were entirely ordinary. Should the Doctor show his typical frame of mind, Mr. Lorry would delicately attempt to draw out the counsel he had so desperately been hoping to secure.
source_text: Within a few minutes, Miss Pross stood whispering at his side. If he had had any particle of doubt left, her talk would of necessity have resolved it; but he was by that time clear-headed, and had none. He advised that they should let the time go by until the regular breakfast-hour, and should then meet the Doctor as if nothing unusual had occurred. If he appeared to be in his customary state of mind, Mr. Lorry would then cautiously proceed to seek direction and guidance from the opinion he had been, in his anxiety, so anxious to obtain.

text: As they pulled him from her, his wife let go and remained gazing after him, her hands pressed together as if in prayer, her face alight with a radiance that held even a consoling smile. When he passed through the prisoners’ door, she turned, rested her head tenderly against her father’s chest, tried to say something to him, and collapsed at his feet.
source_text: As he was drawn away, his wife released him, and stood looking after him with her hands touching one another in the attitude of prayer, and with a radiant look upon her face, in which there was even a comforting smile. As he went out at the prisoners’ door, she turned, laid her head lovingly on her father’s breast, tried to speak to him, and fell at his feet.

text: He composed himself with such swiftness that Mr. Lorry began to question his practiced gaze. The golden giant’s limb in the foyer stood no steadier than he did, as he paused beneath it to observe that he remained unfortified against trifling shocks (and might never be otherwise), and that the downpour had caught him off guard.
source_text: He recovered himself so quickly, however, that Mr. Lorry had doubts of his business eye. The arm of the golden giant in the hall was not more steady than he was, when he stopped under it to remark to them that he was not yet proof against slight surprises (if he ever would be), and that the rain had startled him.

text: “Therefore,” murmured Mr. Lorry, now nudged back into the circle by the very barrister who had earlier edged him aside—“therefore I shall petition Doctor Manette to dissolve this gathering and bid us each return to our dwellings. Miss Lucie appears unwell, Mr. Darnay has endured a harrowing day, and we are all spent.”
source_text: “And as such,” quoth Mr. Lorry, whom the counsel learned in the law had now shouldered back into the group, just as he had previously shouldered him out of it--“as such I will appeal to Doctor Manette, to break up this conference and order us all to our homes. Miss Lucie looks ill, Mr. Darnay has had a terrible day, we are worn out.”

text: As the horseman clattered down the slope once more and through the village lane, the settlement was springing to light. The road-mender and his two hundred and fifty close companions, fired by a single shared impulse to illuminate, had scattered into their dwellings and begun setting candles in every dingy little windowpane. The universal shortage of all things obliged them to demand candles from Monsieur Gabelle in a tone verging on command; and when that official wavered and hesitated, the road-mender, once so meek before authority, observed that carriages made excellent kindling for bonfires, and that post-horses would roast nicely.
source_text: As the rider rattled down the hill again and through the street, the village was illuminating. The mender of roads, and the two hundred and fifty particular friends, inspired as one man and woman by the idea of lighting up, had darted into their houses, and were putting candles in every dull little pane of glass. The general scarcity of everything, occasioned candles to be borrowed in a rather peremptory manner of Monsieur Gabelle; and in a moment of reluctance and hesitation on that functionary’s part, the mender of roads, once so submissive to authority, had remarked that carriages were good to make bonfires with, and that post-horses would roast.

text: Two matters instantly struck Mr. Lorry as supremely urgent: first, that this situation must be concealed from Lucie; second, that it must be hidden from everyone who knew him. Working with Miss Pross, he at once acted on the latter concern by announcing that the Doctor was unwell and needed several days of absolute quiet. To support the gentle falsehood they would present to his daughter, Miss Pross was to compose a letter explaining that he had been summoned away on professional business, and to mention a brief, imaginary note of two or three scribbled lines in his own handwriting, supposedly sent to her by the same mail.
source_text: Two things at once impressed themselves on Mr. Lorry, as important above all others; the first, that this must be kept secret from Lucie; the second, that it must be kept secret from all who knew him. In conjunction with Miss Pross, he took immediate steps towards the latter precaution, by giving out that the Doctor was not well, and required a few days of complete rest. In aid of the kind deception to be practised on his daughter, Miss Pross was to write, describing his having been called away professionally, and referring to an imaginary letter of two or three hurried lines in his own hand, represented to have been addressed to her by the same post.

text: He was a curious soul, and now and then, when she had drifted far from any thought of him—her eyes fixed on the prison’s roof and bars, her spirit reaching out toward her husband—she would startle back to herself and catch him watching her, one knee braced on his bench, his saw halted mid-stroke. “But it’s none of my affair!” he would usually say in those moments, and then turn briskly back to his sawing.
source_text: He was an inquisitive fellow, and sometimes when she had quite forgotten him in gazing at the prison roof and grates, and in lifting her heart up to her husband, she would come to herself to find him looking at her, with his knee on his bench and his saw stopped in its work. “But it’s not my business!” he would generally say at those times, and would briskly fall to his sawing again.

text: “Heaven knows we don’t,” Miss Pross replied, “but don’t worry about me. Meet me at the cathedral at three o’clock, or as close to it as possible, and I’m certain that will be safer than leaving from here. I’m sure of it. There now! God bless you, Mr. Cruncher! Think—not of me, but of the lives that may rest on both of us!”
source_text: “Heaven knows we don’t,” returned Miss Pross, “but have no fear for me. Take me in at the cathedral, at Three o’Clock, or as near it as you can, and I am sure it will be better than our going from here. I feel certain of it. There! Bless you, Mr. Cruncher! Think-not of me, but of the lives that may depend on both of us!”

text: Announced by a herald riding ahead, and by the snap of his outriders’ lashes coiling serpent-like above their heads in the twilight, as though the Furies themselves escorted him, the Lord Marquis halted his coach at the inn gate. It stood near the village well, and the labourers paused in their work to gaze at him. He gazed back, and perceived in them, unawares, the patient erosion of care-carved features and frames, destined to render the gauntness of the French peasant an English legend that would outlast the fact for nearly a century.
source_text: Heralded by a courier in advance, and by the cracking of his postilions’ whips, which twined snake-like about their heads in the evening air, as if he came attended by the Furies, Monsieur the Marquis drew up in his travelling carriage at the posting-house gate. It was hard by the fountain, and the peasants suspended their operations to look at him. He looked at them, and saw in them, without knowing it, the slow sure filing down of misery-worn face and figure, that was to make the meagreness of Frenchmen an English superstition which should survive the truth through the best part of a hundred years.

text: The gentleman found himself so thoroughly unsettled by an inquiry that defied any simple reply, that he could merely observe from afar with a diminished and meek compassion, as the resolute lady, having dismissed the tavern staff under some veiled threat of “informing them” of an unspoken matter should they linger and gawk, retrieved her ward through a measured sequence of gentle steps, and persuaded her to rest her sinking head against her shoulder.
source_text: Mr. Lorry was so exceedingly disconcerted by a question so hard to answer, that he could only look on, at a distance, with much feebler sympathy and humility, while the strong woman, having banished the inn servants under the mysterious penalty of “letting them know” something not mentioned if they stayed there, staring, recovered her charge by a regular series of gradations, and coaxed her to lay her drooping head upon her shoulder.

text: After speaking, she put away her knitting and soon removed the rose from the handkerchief wrapped around her hair. Whether Saint Antoine instinctively knew the offensive ornament had vanished, or had been waiting for it to disappear, the man soon found the nerve to drift inside, and the wine-shop returned to its usual look.
source_text: She rolled up her knitting when she had said those words, and presently took the rose out of the handkerchief that was wound about her head. Either Saint Antoine had an instinctive sense that the objectionable decoration was gone, or Saint Antoine was on the watch for its disappearance; howbeit, the Saint took courage to lounge in, very shortly afterwards, and the wine-shop recovered its habitual aspect.

text: Cottages in pairs and clusters drift past us, lonely homesteads, crumbling outbuildings, fulling mills, tanyards, and suchlike, bare fields, rows of skeletal trees. The rigid, broken road lies beneath us, the thick, sucking mire stretches away on both sides. Now and then, we lurch into the bordering sludge to escape the cobbles that batter and jolt us; now and then, we founder in furrows and quagmires there. The torment of our urgency grows so fierce that in our frantic terror and haste we are ready to leap out and flee—conceal ourselves—do anything rather than halt.
source_text: Houses in twos and threes pass by us, solitary farms, ruinous buildings, dye-works, tanneries, and the like, open country, avenues of leafless trees. The hard uneven pavement is under us, the soft deep mud is on either side. Sometimes, we strike into the skirting mud, to avoid the stones that clatter us and shake us; sometimes, we stick in ruts and sloughs there. The agony of our impatience is then so great, that in our wild alarm and hurry we are for getting out and running--hiding--doing anything but stopping.

text: For a year and three months, Lucie lived with the constant dread that each passing hour might be her husband’s last before the Guillotine claimed him the following day. Day after day, the carts rumbled heavily through the stone-paved streets, loaded with the Condemned. Beautiful girls; radiant women with brown hair, black hair, or silvered locks; young men; the strong and the aged; nobles and peasants alike—all poured out like crimson wine for La Guillotine, fetched daily from the foul darkness of the hideous prisons and paraded through the streets to quench her insatiable thirst. Liberty, equality, fraternity, or death—the last, by far the simplest gift to grant, O Guillotine!
source_text: One year and three months. During all that time Lucie was never sure, from hour to hour, but that the Guillotine would strike off her husband’s head next day. Every day, through the stony streets, the tumbrils now jolted heavily, filled with Condemned. Lovely girls; bright women, brown-haired, black-haired, and grey; youths; stalwart men and old; gentle born and peasant born; all red wine for La Guillotine, all daily brought into light from the dark cellars of the loathsome prisons, and carried to her through the streets to slake her devouring thirst. Liberty, equality, fraternity, or death;--the last, much the easiest to bestow, O Guillotine!

text: Yet, he had wronged no soul, he had confined no one; indeed, so distant was he from cruelly demanding what was owed that he had freely surrendered his claims, cast himself upon an indifferent world, carved out his own quiet corner there, and labored for his daily sustenance. Monsieur Delacour had managed the burdened and tangled property under written orders, to shield the tenants, to offer them whatever meager relief there was to offer—such firewood as the stern creditors permitted in the cold months, and such harvest as could be wrested from the same clutch in the warm ones—and surely he had entered this as both defense and evidence, for his own preservation, so that it must now come to light.
source_text: But, he had oppressed no man, he had imprisoned no man; he was so far from having harshly exacted payment of his dues, that he had relinquished them of his own will, thrown himself on a world with no favour in it, won his own private place there, and earned his own bread. Monsieur Gabelle had held the impoverished and involved estate on written instructions, to spare the people, to give them what little there was to give--such fuel as the heavy creditors would let them have in the winter, and such produce as could be saved from the same grip in the summer--and no doubt he had put the fact in plea and proof, for his own safety, so that it could not but appear now.

text: “Should you imagine I’d be astonished,” her brother Solomon remarked, “you’d be mistaken; I was aware of your presence; I keep track of nearly everyone who comes here. If you truly wish to spare my livelihood—though I suspect you half desire its ruin—then take your leave without delay, and permit me to take mine. I have matters to attend to. I hold a public post.”
source_text: “If you expect me to be surprised,” said her brother Solomon, “I am not surprised; I knew you were here; I know of most people who are here. If you really don’t want to endanger my existence--which I half believe you do--go your ways as soon as possible, and let me go mine. I am busy. I am an official.”

text: Though he spoke with a solemn, earnest smile and even pressed the old man’s hand to his lips, he did not leave him then. He helped rouse the swaying figure before the dying fire, enough to drape a cloak and hat over it and coax it outside to seek the bench and tools it still moaned and pleaded for. He walked beside it, sheltering it to the courtyard of the house where the anguished heart—so joyful in that unforgettable time when he had bared his own lonely heart to it—kept vigil through the dreadful night. He stepped into the courtyard and stood there a few moments alone, gazing up at the light in her window. Before departing, he breathed a blessing toward it, and a farewell.
source_text: Though he said it with a grave smile of earnestness, and though he even put the old man’s hand to his lips, he did not part from him then. He helped him so far to arouse the rocking figure before the dying embers, as to get a cloak and hat put upon it, and to tempt it forth to find where the bench and work were hidden that it still moaningly besought to have. He walked on the other side of it and protected it to the courtyard of the house where the afflicted heart--so happy in the memorable time when he had revealed his own desolate heart to it--outwatched the awful night. He entered the courtyard and remained there for a few moments alone, looking up at the light in the window of her room. Before he went away, he breathed a blessing towards it, and a Farewell.

text: “It guided me to this place, it guided me to my tomb. Once I stepped free of the doorway, a dark cloth was clamped fast across my lips from the rear, and my wrists were seized. The pair of siblings emerged from a shadowed recess across the lane, and with one silent motion they marked me. The Count drew from his coat the note I had penned, held it before my eyes, set it aflame in the glow of a raised lamp, and crushed the cinders beneath his heel. No syllable passed. I was led here, I was led to my breathing sepulchre.”
source_text: “It brought me here, it brought me to my grave. When I was clear of the house, a black muffler was drawn tightly over my mouth from behind, and my arms were pinioned. The two brothers crossed the road from a dark corner, and identified me with a single gesture. The Marquis took from his pocket the letter I had written, showed it me, burnt it in the light of a lantern that was held, and extinguished the ashes with his foot. Not a word was spoken. I was brought here, I was brought to my living grave.

text: With heads bowed and tails quivering, they labored through the heavy mire, lurching and tripping now and then, as though about to come apart at every major joint. Each time the driver halted them with a cautious “Wo-ho! so-ho-then!” the near leader tossed his head and all its trappings in a violent jangle—like a horse emphatically insisting the coach would never make the hill. At every clatter, the passenger flinched, as a skittish traveler might, and felt his nerves unsettle.
source_text: With drooping heads and tremulous tails, they mashed their way through the thick mud, floundering and stumbling between whiles, as if they were falling to pieces at the larger joints. As often as the driver rested them and brought them to a stand, with a wary “Wo-ho! so-ho-then!” the near leader violently shook his head and everything upon it--like an unusually emphatic horse, denying that the coach could be got up the hill. Whenever the leader made this rattle, the passenger started, as a nervous passenger might, and was disturbed in mind.

text: “And that’s the honest truth. The fact is, my dear Charles,” Mr. Lorry shifted his gaze toward the distant building and dropped his voice, “you can’t imagine how hard it is to carry on our affairs, or what danger our records and papers over there are facing. Heaven only knows the ruinous fallout that would strike so many people if certain documents were seized or destroyed—and that could happen any moment, as you well know, for who’s to say Paris won’t be in flames today or looted tomorrow! Now, a careful sorting of those papers, with the briefest possible delay, and hiding them, or somehow getting them out of danger, is a task that practically no one but me can manage without wasting precious time. And should I hold back, when Tellson’s knows this and insists on it—Tellson’s, whose livelihood I’ve shared for sixty years—just because my joints are a little creaky? Good Lord, sir, next to half a dozen old fossils here, I’m a mere youngster!”
source_text: “And I am, in plain reality. The truth is, my dear Charles,” Mr. Lorry glanced at the distant House, and lowered his voice, “you can have no conception of the difficulty with which our business is transacted, and of the peril in which our books and papers over yonder are involved. The Lord above knows what the compromising consequences would be to numbers of people, if some of our documents were seized or destroyed; and they might be, at any time, you know, for who can say that Paris is not set afire to-day, or sacked to-morrow! Now, a judicious selection from these with the least possible delay, and the burying of them, or otherwise getting of them out of harm’s way, is within the power (without loss of precious time) of scarcely any one but myself, if any one. And shall I hang back, when Tellson’s knows this and says this--Tellson’s, whose bread I have eaten these sixty years--because I am a little stiff about the joints? Why, I am a boy, sir, to half a dozen old codgers here!”

text: In a profound hush and motionlessness—the accused gazing tenderly at his spouse, she glancing away from him only to watch her father with anxious care, Doctor Manette holding his stare upon the speaker, Madame Defarge never shifting her gaze from the accused, Defarge never moving his from his eagerly attentive wife, and every other pair of eyes fixed on the Doctor, who perceived none of them—the document was read aloud, as written below.
source_text: In a dead silence and stillness--the prisoner under trial looking lovingly at his wife, his wife only looking from him to look with solicitude at her father, Doctor Manette keeping his eyes fixed on the reader, Madame Defarge never taking hers from the prisoner, Defarge never taking his from his feasting wife, and all the other eyes there intent upon the Doctor, who saw none of them--the paper was read, as follows.

text: He pressed his knuckles against his eyelids and shook off his drowsiness, yet once fully alert, he wondered if he had truly woken. Approaching the doorway of the Doctor’s chamber and peering inside, he saw that the cobbler’s worktable and implements had been stowed away once more, and the Doctor himself was seated by the window with a book. He wore his customary morning attire, and his countenance—clearly visible to Mr. Lorry—remained quite pallid but bore an expression of quiet, focused study.
source_text: He rubbed his eyes and roused himself; but he doubted, when he had done so, whether he was not still asleep. For, going to the door of the Doctor’s room and looking in, he perceived that the shoemaker’s bench and tools were put aside again, and that the Doctor himself sat reading at the window. He was in his usual morning dress, and his face (which Mr. Lorry could distinctly see), though still very pale, was calmly studious and attentive.

text: Madame Defarge regarded her with contempt, yet shared something of Miss Pross’s instinct that the two of them stood cornered. Before her she perceived a compact, rigid, sinewy woman, just as Mr. Lorry had once glimpsed in that same form a woman of firm grip in earlier days. She understood perfectly that Miss Pross was the household’s loyal protector; Miss Pross understood perfectly that Madame Defarge was the household’s vicious foe.
source_text: Madame Defarge looked at her scornfully, but still with something of Miss Pross’s own perception that they two were at bay. She saw a tight, hard, wiry woman before her, as Mr. Lorry had seen in the same figure a woman with a strong hand, in the years gone by. She knew full well that Miss Pross was the family’s devoted friend; Miss Pross knew full well that Madame Defarge was the family’s malevolent enemy.

text: Mr. Lorry, half-regretting his mild qualifier, answered, “No, no, no. Certainly not. But to resume our business—does it not strike you as singular that Doctor Manette, whom we all know to be wholly blameless of any offense, should never once allude to that matter? I do not mean with me, though he had professional dealings with me long ago and we are now close friends; I mean with the dear daughter to whom he is so tenderly bound, and who is so tenderly bound to him? Trust me, Miss Pross, I raise this subject with you not from idle curiosity, but from earnest concern.”
source_text: Rather wishing his modest adjective away, Mr. Lorry replied, “No, no, no. Surely not. To return to business:--Is it not remarkable that Doctor Manette, unquestionably innocent of any crime as we are all well assured he is, should never touch upon that question? I will not say with me, though he had business relations with me many years ago, and we are now intimate; I will say with the fair daughter to whom he is so devotedly attached, and who is so devotedly attached to him? Believe me, Miss Pross, I don’t approach the topic with you, out of curiosity, but out of zealous interest.”

text: What funds would be withdrawn from Tellson’s from that point forward, and what would remain there, abandoned and unremembered; what silverware and jewelry would grow dull in Tellson’s vaults while their owners decayed in cells, and when they met violent ends; how many unsettled balances at Tellson’s, never to be reconciled in this life, would have to be carried into the afterlife—no one could have said that night, any more than Mr. Jarvis Lorry could, though he pondered these matters with a heavy heart. He sat beside a freshly kindled wood fire (the barren, wasted year had turned cold early), and on his frank and brave face lay a shadow deeper than the hanging lamp could cast, or any object in the room could distort and reflect—a shadow of dread.
source_text: What money would be drawn out of Tellson’s henceforth, and what would lie there, lost and forgotten; what plate and jewels would tarnish in Tellson’s hiding-places, while the depositors rusted in prisons, and when they should have violently perished; how many accounts with Tellson’s never to be balanced in this world, must be carried over into the next; no man could have said, that night, any more than Mr. Jarvis Lorry could, though he thought heavily of these questions. He sat by a newly-lighted wood fire (the blighted and unfruitful year was prematurely cold), and on his honest and courageous face there was a deeper shade than the pendent lamp could throw, or any object in the room distortedly reflect--a shade of horror.

text: The gleaming Bull’s Eye of the Court had vanished; otherwise it would have drawn a storm of the nation’s gunfire. It had never served well for sight—long clouded by the mote of Lucifer’s pride, Sardanapalus’s excess, and a mole’s blindness—but now it had fallen away and was no more. The Court, from its innermost sanctum to its farthest decayed rim of scheming, venality, and deceit, had collapsed as one. Royalty itself had disappeared; besieged within its Palace and “suspended,” according to the latest reports that had arrived.
source_text: The shining Bull’s Eye of the Court was gone, or it would have been the mark for a hurricane of national bullets. It had never been a good eye to see with--had long had the mote in it of Lucifer’s pride, Sardanapalus’s luxury, and a mole’s blindness--but it had dropped out and was gone. The Court, from that exclusive inner circle to its outermost rotten ring of intrigue, corruption, and dissimulation, was all gone together. Royalty was gone; had been besieged in its Palace and “suspended,” when the last tidings came over.

text: Grumbling, and moreover sprinkling out taunts like “Oh, indeed! So you’re pious as well. You’d never set yourself against the welfare of your husband and child, would you? Certainly not you!” while flinging off further ironic sparks from the spinning wheel of his wrath, Mr. Cruncher retreated into the cleaning of his boots and the broader readying of his trade. Meanwhile, his boy, whose crown was tufted with softer spikes and whose youthful eyes sat near each other, just as his father’s did, maintained the ordered vigil over his mother. He harassed the wretched woman at intervals, bursting from his sleeping nook, where he dressed, with a stifled shriek of “You mean to flop, mother. —Hark, father!” and, having sounded this false alarm, scuttled back inside with a disrespectful smirk.
source_text: Growling, in addition, such phrases as “Ah! yes! You’re religious, too. You wouldn’t put yourself in opposition to the interests of your husband and child, would you? Not you!” and throwing off other sarcastic sparks from the whirling grindstone of his indignation, Mr. Cruncher betook himself to his boot-cleaning and his general preparation for business. In the meantime, his son, whose head was garnished with tenderer spikes, and whose young eyes stood close by one another, as his father’s did, kept the required watch upon his mother. He greatly disturbed that poor woman at intervals, by darting out of his sleeping closet, where he made his toilet, with a suppressed cry of “You are going to flop, mother. --Halloa, father!” and, after raising this fictitious alarm, darting in again with an undutiful grin.

text: The attic, originally intended as a storage space for firewood and similar supplies, lay in deep shadow. Its dormer window was actually a roof door, fitted with a small hoist for lifting goods from the street below. It had no glass and split shut down the middle, like any typical French door. To keep out the cold, one panel was sealed tight, while the other stood open just a crack. So little light filtered through this arrangement that, upon first entering, you could scarcely make anything out. Only years of familiarity could have gradually trained someone to perform delicate tasks in such gloom. And yet, delicate work was indeed underway up there: a white-haired man sat on a low stool, his back to the door and his face turned toward the window where the wine-shop keeper stood observing him. Hunched forward, he was absorbed in making shoes.
source_text: The garret, built to be a depository for firewood and the like, was dim and dark: for, the window of dormer shape, was in truth a door in the roof, with a little crane over it for the hoisting up of stores from the street: unglazed, and closing up the middle in two pieces, like any other door of French construction. To exclude the cold, one half of this door was fast closed, and the other was opened but a very little way. Such a scanty portion of light was admitted through these means, that it was difficult, on first coming in, to see anything; and long habit alone could have slowly formed in any one, the ability to do any work requiring nicety in such obscurity. Yet, work of that kind was being done in the garret; for, with his back towards the door, and his face towards the window where the keeper of the wine-shop stood looking at him, a white-haired man sat on a low bench, stooping forward and very busy, making shoes.

text: Utterly motionless and mute, she remained beneath his touch, not even slumped against the chairback, wholly unresponsive; her gaze wide and locked on him, that final look etched as if graven or seared upon her brow. Her grip on his forearm was so fierce that he dared not pull away, afraid of harming her; so he shouted for help, standing rooted where he was.
source_text: Perfectly still and silent, and not even fallen back in her chair, she sat under his hand, utterly insensible; with her eyes open and fixed upon him, and with that last expression looking as if it were carved or branded into her forehead. So close was her hold upon his arm, that he feared to detach himself lest he should hurt her; therefore he called out loudly for assistance without moving.

text: It was not a wild gesture, the way he spoke those words into the racing sky, nor did it betray carelessness any more than rebellion. It was the steady bearing of a weary man, who had roamed and fought and lost his way, but who had finally found his path and glimpsed its close.
source_text: It was not a reckless manner, the manner in which he said these words aloud under the fast-sailing clouds, nor was it more expressive of negligence than defiance. It was the settled manner of a tired man, who had wandered and struggled and got lost, but who at length struck into his road and saw its end.

text: It froze him where he stood. The jailer beside him, and the others drifting through their duties—men whose looks would have passed without remark in the common run of their work—now seemed grotesquely rough set against the grieving mothers and fresh-faced daughters gathered there, against the spectres of the flirt, the youthful beauty, the gently reared matron. The shadowy scene, already a reversal of all that was natural and probable, sharpened into something absolute. Surely, every one a phantom. Surely, the long hallucinatory journey some fevered advance of illness that had delivered him into these sombre depths.
source_text: It struck him motionless. The gaoler standing at his side, and the other gaolers moving about, who would have been well enough as to appearance in the ordinary exercise of their functions, looked so extravagantly coarse contrasted with sorrowing mothers and blooming daughters who were there--with the apparitions of the coquette, the young beauty, and the mature woman delicately bred--that the inversion of all experience and likelihood which the scene of shadows presented, was heightened to its utmost. Surely, ghosts all. Surely, the long unreal ride some progress of disease that had brought him to these gloomy shades!

text: From the shadowy corridors of the courtroom, the final dregs of the human mass that had simmered there throughout the day were slowly draining away, as Doctor Manette, his daughter Lucie Manette, Mr. Lorry, the defense attorney, and his counsel Mr. Stryver, clustered around Mr. Charles Darnay—newly acquitted—offering him their congratulations on his deliverance from death.
source_text: From the dimly-lighted passages of the court, the last sediment of the human stew that had been boiling there all day, was straining off, when Doctor Manette, Lucie Manette, his daughter, Mr. Lorry, the solicitor for the defence, and its counsel, Mr. Stryver, stood gathered round Mr. Charles Darnay--just released--congratulating him on his escape from death.

text: That day, Defarge had introduced himself as the proprietor of a wine-shop in the Saint Antoine district. Anyone familiar with the city could locate his establishment without needing directions. Once Carton had fixed its position in his mind, he left the narrow lanes behind, stopped at an eating-house for a meal, and sank into a deep sleep right after dining. For the first time in ages, he drank no hard liquor. Since the previous evening, he had consumed only a modest glass of pale, weak wine, and that same evening he had poured the brandy slowly onto Mr. Lorry's hearth, as though he were finished with it for good.
source_text: Defarge had described himself, that day, as the keeper of a wine-shop in the Saint Antoine suburb. It was not difficult for one who knew the city well, to find his house without asking any question. Having ascertained its situation, Carton came out of those closer streets again, and dined at a place of refreshment and fell sound asleep after dinner. For the first time in many years, he had no strong drink. Since last night he had taken nothing but a little light thin wine, and last night he had dropped the brandy slowly down on Mr. Lorry’s hearth like a man who had done with it.

text: The fever of work consumed itself—and him along with it—until the Bank finally shut its doors. Once more he found himself alone in the same room as the night before, turning over his next move, when a footfall sounded on the stairs. Moments later, a man stood before him, studying him with a sharp, discerning gaze, and spoke his name.
source_text: It wore itself out, and wore him out with it, until the Bank closed. He was again alone in his room of the previous night, considering what to do next, when he heard a foot upon the stair. In a few moments, a man stood in his presence, who, with a keenly observant look at him, addressed him by his name.

text: Marcel Three, gripped by his habitual hunger and visibly chafing at the conversation’s drift away from any hint of slaughter, clutched Defarge’s sleeve just as Defarge grasped the jailer’s. Their three skulls had drawn near during that fleeting exchange, and even so they strained to catch each other’s words: so deafening was the clamor of the human tide as it burst into the Bastille and flooded the yards, corridors, and stairwells. Beyond the walls, too, it pounded the stone with a low, gravelly thunder, from which now and then stray shrieks of riot erupted and vaulted skyward like flung spume.
source_text: Jacques Three, with his usual craving on him, and evidently disappointed by the dialogue taking a turn that did not seem to promise bloodshed, held by Defarge’s arm as he held by the turnkey’s. Their three heads had been close together during this brief discourse, and it had been as much as they could do to hear one another, even then: so tremendous was the noise of the living ocean, in its irruption into the Fortress, and its inundation of the courts and passages and staircases. All around outside, too, it beat the walls with a deep, hoarse roar, from which, occasionally, some partial shouts of tumult broke and leaped into the air like spray.

text: France, not quite as blessed in spiritual matters as her naval and military counterpart, coasted downhill with remarkable ease, churning out paper currency and squandering it. Under the direction of her Christian shepherds, she also amused herself with such compassionate deeds as condemning a young man to have his hands severed, his tongue ripped out with pincers, and his body consumed by flames, all because he failed to kneel in the rain to pay respect to a grimy parade of monks that crossed his line of sight, some fifty or sixty yards away. It is quite possible that, deep in the forests of France and Norway, trees stood already marked by the Woodman, Fate, to be felled and cut into planks for constructing a certain portable device with a sack and a blade, notorious in history, at the very moment that victim was executed. It is quite possible that in the crude sheds of farmers working the heavy soil near Paris, there rested that same day rough wagons, splattered with country filth, sniffed at by swine, and used as roosts by chickens, which the Farmer, Death, had already designated to serve as his revolutionary tumbrils. But that Woodman and that Farmer, though they labor endlessly, labor in silence, and nobody caught the sound of their soft-footed work—especially since entertaining any notion that they were stirring was considered both godless and treasonous.
source_text: France, less favoured on the whole as to matters spiritual than her sister of the shield and trident, rolled with exceeding smoothness down hill, making paper money and spending it. Under the guidance of her Christian pastors, she entertained herself, besides, with such humane achievements as sentencing a youth to have his hands cut off, his tongue torn out with pincers, and his body burned alive, because he had not kneeled down in the rain to do honour to a dirty procession of monks which passed within his view, at a distance of some fifty or sixty yards. It is likely enough that, rooted in the woods of France and Norway, there were growing trees, when that sufferer was put to death, already marked by the Woodman, Fate, to come down and be sawn into boards, to make a certain movable framework with a sack and a knife in it, terrible in history. It is likely enough that in the rough outhouses of some tillers of the heavy lands adjacent to Paris, there were sheltered from the weather that very day, rude carts, bespattered with rustic mire, snuffed about by pigs, and roosted in by poultry, which the Farmer, Death, had already set apart to be his tumbrils of the Revolution. But that Woodman and that Farmer, though they work unceasingly, work silently, and no one heard them as they went about with muffled tread: the rather, forasmuch as to entertain any suspicion that they were awake, was to be atheistical and traitorous.

text: Time dissolved around him as he paced the room, the chimes tolling each hour he would never reclaim. Nine slipped into silence, ten faded into silence, eleven dissolved into silence, twelve approaching its own vanishing. Having wrestled through the tangled thought that had lately ensnared him, he had emerged victorious. He moved back and forth, murmuring their names like a quiet litany. The fiercest of the battle was done. He could walk the length of the room, his mind clear of wayward visions, offering prayers for his own soul and for theirs.
source_text: The hours went on as he walked to and fro, and the clocks struck the numbers he would never hear again. Nine gone for ever, ten gone for ever, eleven gone for ever, twelve coming on to pass away. After a hard contest with that eccentric action of thought which had last perplexed him, he had got the better of it. He walked up and down, softly repeating their names to himself. The worst of the strife was over. He could walk up and down, free from distracting fancies, praying for himself and for them.

Darnay traced the script of his document with a deliberate thumb, his expression intent and scholarly. The others rested their elbows on the ledge, huddled near one another, murmuring in hushed tones. When a brief stillness fell, their collective gaze drifted toward him, yet they did not interrupt his apparent focus on the revolutionary journal. Then their talk quietly resumed.
source_text: Carton followed the lines and words of his paper, with a slow forefinger, and with a studious and absorbed face. They were all leaning their arms on the counter close together, speaking low. After a silence of a few moments, during which they all looked towards him without disturbing his outward attention from the Jacobin editor, they resumed their conversation.

text: “I trust, sir,” implored the flustered Mr. Cruncher, “that a gentleman of your standing, for whom I’ve had the privilege of doing odd jobs till my hair’s turned silver, would hesitate before doing me harm—even supposing it were true, which I’m not admitting, but just supposing. And you’d have to consider that if it were, it still wouldn’t be all one-sided. There’d be another angle. Right now there might be medical doctors pocketing their guineas where an honest working man can’t scrape together his farthings—farthings! no, not even his half-farthings—half-farthings! no, not even his quarters—vanishing like mist into Tellson’s vaults, while they slyly squint their medical eyes at that working man, slipping in and out of their private carriages—ah! just as much like mist, if not more so. Well, that would be taking liberties with Tellson’s too. For you can’t sauce the goose and leave the gander untouched. And here’s Mrs. Cruncher, or at least she was in the old England days, and would be again tomorrow, given half a chance, flopping down against the trade to the point of ruin—downright ruin! Whereas those medical doctors’ wives don’t flop—you won’t catch them at it! Or, if they do flop, their flopping only brings in more patients, and how can you fairly have one without the other? Then, what with undertakers, and what with parish clerks, and what with sextons, and what with private watchmen (all greedy and all in on it), a man wouldn’t gain much by it, even if it were so. And whatever little a man did gain would never bring him any blessing, Mr. Lorry. He’d never get any good from it; he’d be forever longing to quit the trade, if he could only spy a way out, once he’d gotten in—even if it were so.”
source_text: “I hope, sir,” pleaded the abashed Mr. Cruncher, “that a gentleman like yourself wot I’ve had the honour of odd jobbing till I’m grey at it, would think twice about harming of me, even if it wos so--I don’t say it is, but even if it wos. And which it is to be took into account that if it wos, it wouldn’t, even then, be all o’ one side. There’d be two sides to it. There might be medical doctors at the present hour, a picking up their guineas where a honest tradesman don’t pick up his fardens--fardens! no, nor yet his half fardens--half fardens! no, nor yet his quarter--a banking away like smoke at Tellson’s, and a cocking their medical eyes at that tradesman on the sly, a going in and going out to their own carriages--ah! equally like smoke, if not more so. Well, that ’ud be imposing, too, on Tellson’s. For you cannot sarse the goose and not the gander. And here’s Mrs. Cruncher, or leastways wos in the Old England times, and would be to-morrow, if cause given, a floppin’ again the business to that degree as is ruinating--stark ruinating! Whereas them medical doctors’ wives don’t flop--catch ’em at it! Or, if they flop, their floppings goes in favour of more patients, and how can you rightly have one without t’other? Then, wot with undertakers, and wot with parish clerks, and wot with sextons, and wot with private watchmen (all awaricious and all in it), a man wouldn’t get much by it, even if it wos so. And wot little a man did get, would never prosper with him, Mr. Lorry. He’d never have no good of it; he’d want all along to be out of the line, if he, could see his way out, being once in--even if it wos so.”

text: She seemed aged, yet her years were few. Her bearing spoke of fervent sorrow; in fits she wrung her corded, gnarled fingers with fierce vigor, then rested a hand upon the coach panel—softly, strokingly, as though it were a living bosom that might sense the imploring caress.
source_text: She looked an old woman, but was young. Her manner was one of passionate grief; by turns she clasped her veinous and knotted hands together with wild energy, and laid one of them on the carriage-door--tenderly, caressingly, as if it had been a human breast, and could be expected to feel the appealing touch.

text: “Listen once more, Jacques!” urged the man whose hand twitched and whose expression betrayed an insatiable hunger. “That prisoner’s name was Damiens, and the whole thing took place in broad daylight, right out in the open streets of Paris; and what stood out most among the enormous crowd that witnessed it was the flock of highborn, stylish women, utterly absorbed right up to the final moment—right to the very end, Jacques, dragged out until dusk, when he’d already lost both legs and an arm, and was still drawing breath! And it was carried out—tell me, how old are you?”
source_text: “Listen once again then, Jacques!” said the man with the restless hand and the craving air. “The name of that prisoner was Damiens, and it was all done in open day, in the open streets of this city of Paris; and nothing was more noticed in the vast concourse that saw it done, than the crowd of ladies of quality and fashion, who were full of eager attention to the last--to the last, Jacques, prolonged until nightfall, when he had lost two legs and an arm, and still breathed! And it was done--why, how old are you?”

text: Madame Defarge gave a small wave of her hand, signaling that she had taken note and could be counted on to show up punctually, then trudged off through the mire and disappeared around the bend of the prison wall. The Vengeance and the Juryman, watching her retreat, greatly admired her striking form and her remarkable strength of character.
source_text: Madame Defarge slightly waved her hand, to imply that she heard, and might be relied upon to arrive in good time, and so went through the mud, and round the corner of the prison wall. The Vengeance and the Juryman, looking after her as she walked away, were highly appreciative of her fine figure, and her superb moral endowments.

text: By now, Mr. Cruncher had cleaned a fair meal’s worth of rust from his fingers while tracking the testimony. He now had to listen as Mr. Stryver tailored the defendant’s case to the jury like a snug suit, demonstrating how the so-called patriot Barsad was a paid informer and traitor, a shameless dealer in blood, and one of the earth’s worst villains since the damned Judas—whom he did, in truth, somewhat resemble. How the upright servant Cly was his crony and partner, and deserved to be; how those forgers and perjurers had fixed their vigilant gaze on the accused as their prey, because certain family matters in France, since he was of French descent, obliged him to make those Channel crossings—though what those matters were, out of regard for those closest to his heart, he could not reveal, even to save his life. How the testimony twisted and extracted from the young woman, whose distress in delivering it they had seen, amounted to nothing, dealing only with the trivial, harmless flirtations and courtesies that might naturally occur between any young man and young woman cast together—except for that mention of George Washington, which was so wildly overblown and absurd it could only be taken as a monstrous jest. How it would be a failing in the government to falter in this bid to court popularity by exploiting the basest national hatreds and anxieties, and so the Attorney-General had squeezed it for all it was worth; how, regardless, it was built on nothing but that sordid and disgraceful type of testimony that too often mars such trials, and of which the State Trials of this nation were replete. But at this, my Lord cut in (with a face as stern as if it had been false), declaring he could not occupy that Bench and permit such insinuations.
source_text: Mr. Cruncher had by this time taken quite a lunch of rust off his fingers in his following of the evidence. He had now to attend while Mr. Stryver fitted the prisoner’s case on the jury, like a compact suit of clothes; showing them how the patriot, Barsad, was a hired spy and traitor, an unblushing trafficker in blood, and one of the greatest scoundrels upon earth since accursed Judas--which he certainly did look rather like. How the virtuous servant, Cly, was his friend and partner, and was worthy to be; how the watchful eyes of those forgers and false swearers had rested on the prisoner as a victim, because some family affairs in France, he being of French extraction, did require his making those passages across the Channel--though what those affairs were, a consideration for others who were near and dear to him, forbade him, even for his life, to disclose. How the evidence that had been warped and wrested from the young lady, whose anguish in giving it they had witnessed, came to nothing, involving the mere little innocent gallantries and politenesses likely to pass between any young gentleman and young lady so thrown together;--with the exception of that reference to George Washington, which was altogether too extravagant and impossible to be regarded in any other light than as a monstrous joke. How it would be a weakness in the government to break down in this attempt to practise for popularity on the lowest national antipathies and fears, and therefore Mr. Attorney-General had made the most of it; how, nevertheless, it rested upon nothing, save that vile and infamous character of evidence too often disfiguring such cases, and of which the State Trials of this country were full. But, there my Lord interposed (with as grave a face as if it had not been true), saying that he could not sit upon that Bench and suffer those allusions.

text: He drifted off, sometimes for minutes on end, his hands methodically shifting positions all the while. At last his gaze slowly returned to the face it had strayed from; the moment it settled there, he flinched, and picked up again, like someone jolted from sleep, going back to a topic from the night before.
source_text: He lapsed away, even for minutes, ringing those measured changes on his hands the whole time. His eyes came slowly back, at last, to the face from which they had wandered; when they rested on it, he started, and resumed, in the manner of a sleeper that moment awake, reverting to a subject of last night.

text: The magistrates were required to appear in an open-air civic procession, so the court suspended its session. The hurried murmur and shuffle of the chamber draining through its several doors had barely subsided, when Lucie rose and reached toward her husband, her countenance holding nothing but tenderness and solace.
source_text: The Judges having to take part in a public demonstration out of doors, the Tribunal adjourned. The quick noise and movement of the court’s emptying itself by many passages had not ceased, when Lucie stood stretching out her arms towards her husband, with nothing in her face but love and consolation.

text: Gently, she placed her hand upon his cherished chest, whispering a hope that she could always remain as faithful to him as her devotion longed to be, and as his griefs merited. After that, she pulled back her hand, pressed one more kiss to his lips, and departed. Thus, dawn arrived, and the shifting leaf-shadows from the plane tree drifted across his face, as tenderly as her lips had stirred while she prayed for him.
source_text: She timidly laid her hand on his dear breast, and put up a prayer that she might ever be as true to him as her love aspired to be, and as his sorrows deserved. Then, she withdrew her hand, and kissed his lips once more, and went away. So, the sunrise came, and the shadows of the leaves of the plane-tree moved upon his face, as softly as her lips had moved in praying for him.

text: A soft stirring spread through the dwelling from the handful of souls still lingering there, and soon a steed was cinched and ridden off. Through the blackness came the splash of hooves and the jab of spurs, until the reins were hauled taut by the hamlet’s well, and the lathered mount halted at Citizen Gabelle’s threshold. “Aid me, Gabelle! Aid, all of you!” The alarm bell clamored with fretful urgency, yet no other succor (if succor it could be called) appeared. The road-mender, flanked by two hundred and fifty chosen comrades, stood with arms crossed beside the well, watching the column of flame that towered in the heavens. “No less than forty feet high,” they muttered, dourly; and did not stir.
source_text: A faint murmur arose about the house from the few people who were left there, and there was a saddling of a horse and riding away. There was spurring and splashing through the darkness, and bridle was drawn in the space by the village fountain, and the horse in a foam stood at Monsieur Gabelle’s door. “Help, Gabelle! Help, every one!” The tocsin rang impatiently, but other help (if that were any) there was none. The mender of roads, and two hundred and fifty particular friends, stood with folded arms at the fountain, looking at the pillar of fire in the sky. “It must be forty feet high,” said they, grimly; and never moved.

text: I have a cousin—my sole remaining family, an orphan just as I am—whom I cherish with all my heart. She is five years my junior and dwells in a farmhouse down in the southern countryside. Destitution drove us apart, and she remains ignorant of what has become of me, for I lack the skill to write; and even if I possessed it, how could I ever explain my circumstances to her? Perhaps things are best left as they stand.
source_text: “I have a cousin, an only relative and an orphan, like myself, whom I love very dearly. She is five years younger than I, and she lives in a farmer’s house in the south country. Poverty parted us, and she knows nothing of my fate--for I cannot write--and if I could, how should I tell her! It is better as it is.”

text: That blade harvested so many heads that both it and the fouled earth beneath lay steeped in putrid crimson. It was dismantled like a puzzle-box crafted for a fledgling demon, then reassembled whenever the moment demanded. It silenced the silver-tongued, toppled the mighty, erased the lovely and the virtuous. Twenty-two prominent public figures—twenty-one still breathing, one already gone—it had decapitated in a single morning, in as many minutes. The name of that Old Testament champion had been passed down to the chief officer who wielded it; yet, so equipped, he surpassed his namesake in strength and in blindness, and each day he ripped apart the gates of God’s own sanctuary.
source_text: It sheared off heads so many, that it, and the ground it most polluted, were a rotten red. It was taken to pieces, like a toy-puzzle for a young Devil, and was put together again when the occasion wanted it. It hushed the eloquent, struck down the powerful, abolished the beautiful and good. Twenty-two friends of high public mark, twenty-one living and one dead, it had lopped the heads off, in one morning, in as many minutes. The name of the strong man of Old Scripture had descended to the chief functionary who worked it; but, so armed, he was stronger than his namesake, and blinder, and tore away the gates of God’s own Temple every day.

text: He pulled himself together in a moment. Large raindrops were truly falling now, and he held out the back of his hand, glistening with wet. Yet he spoke not one syllable about the revelation that had just been shared. As they stepped indoors, Mr. Lorry’s practiced gaze either caught, or imagined it caught, crossing his face when it shifted toward Charles Darnay—that same peculiar expression that had fixed on him in the corridors of the Old Bailey.
source_text: He recovered himself almost instantly. Rain was really falling in large drops, and he showed the back of his hand with rain-drops on it. But, he said not a single word in reference to the discovery that had been told of, and, as they went into the house, the business eye of Mr. Lorry either detected, or fancied it detected, on his face, as it turned towards Charles Darnay, the same singular look that had been upon it when it turned towards him in the passages of the Court House.

text: While carrying out renovations, the laborers stumbled upon an ancient cell that had long been sealed off and erased from memory. Its interior walls were entirely covered with carvings left by inmates—dates, names, laments, and entreaties. On a corner stone set into an alcove, one prisoner, apparently facing execution, had scratched three letters as his final act. They were etched with a crude tool, hastily, by a trembling hand. Initially they were deciphered as D. I. C., but closer inspection revealed the last letter to be G. No records or tales existed of any captive bearing those initials, and many futile conjectures were offered about what the name might have been. Eventually, someone proposed that the letters were not initials at all, but the full word, DIG. The floor directly beneath the carving was meticulously searched, and in the soil under a stone, or perhaps a tile or broken bit of paving, lay the charred remnants of a document, mixed with the ashes of a small leather pouch or case. Whatever the nameless prisoner inscribed will forever remain unknown, yet he had set down something, and concealed it to keep it from his jailer.
source_text: “In making some alterations, the workmen came upon an old dungeon, which had been, for many years, built up and forgotten. Every stone of its inner wall was covered by inscriptions which had been carved by prisoners--dates, names, complaints, and prayers. Upon a corner stone in an angle of the wall, one prisoner, who seemed to have gone to execution, had cut as his last work, three letters. They were done with some very poor instrument, and hurriedly, with an unsteady hand. At first, they were read as D. I. C.; but, on being more carefully examined, the last letter was found to be G. There was no record or legend of any prisoner with those initials, and many fruitless guesses were made what the name could have been. At length, it was suggested that the letters were not initials, but the complete word, DIG. The floor was examined very carefully under the inscription, and, in the earth beneath a stone, or tile, or some fragment of paving, were found the ashes of a paper, mingled with the ashes of a small leathern case or bag. What the unknown prisoner had written will never be read, but he had written something, and hidden it away to keep it from the gaoler.”

text: The attorney was shrewd enough to perceive that the financier would never have ventured so deep into his own convictions without the bedrock of absolute certitude. Though he had not braced himself for the enormous draught he was forced to imbibe, he drained it nonetheless. “Thereupon,” declared Mr. Harrow, levelling his courtroom finger at the whole of the Inns of Court once the thing was swallowed, “my sole escape from this tangle is to cast every one of you into error.”
source_text: The barrister was keen enough to divine that the banker would not have gone so far in his expression of opinion on any less solid ground than moral certainty. Unprepared as he was for the large pill he had to swallow, he got it down. “And now,” said Mr. Stryver, shaking his forensic forefinger at the Temple in general, when it was down, “my way out of this, is, to put you all in the wrong.”

text: “‘She caught my voice and hurried inside. I warned her to keep her distance until he had breathed his last. He entered and flung a few coins at my feet; then lashed out with a riding crop. Yet I, a mere cur, struck back in such a way that he was forced to unsheathe his blade. Shatter into a thousand fragments if it pleases him, the steel he fouled with my low-born blood; he drew to preserve his own skin--lunged at me with every ounce of his practiced art to save his life.’
source_text: “‘She heard me, and ran in. I told her not to come near us till he was dead. He came in and first tossed me some pieces of money; then struck at me with a whip. But I, though a common dog, so struck at him as to make him draw. Let him break into as many pieces as he will, the sword that he stained with my common blood; he drew to defend himself--thrust at me with all his skill for his life.’

text: He became the very image of the downpour’s clamour and surge, and it brought him to a halt, for no sound could rise above it. With that deluge came an unforgettable tempest of thunderbolts and blinding flashes, and there was no pause in the cracking, the blazing, and the torrent until the moon climbed into the sky at midnight.
source_text: It was the rush and roar of rain that he typified, and it stopped him, for no voice could be heard in it. A memorable storm of thunder and lightning broke with that sweep of water, and there was not a moment’s interval in crash, and fire, and rain, until after the moon rose at midnight.

text: For three oppressive hours, the chateau’s stone faces—both lion and human—stared sightlessly into the night. A dead blackness draped the countryside, and that dead blackness added its own silence to the quiet dust muffling every road. The graveyard had reached a state where its sparse mounds of meager grass blurred into one another; the figure on the Cross might as well have descended, for all that anyone could discern of it. In the village, tax collectors and the taxed alike lay deep in slumber. Dreaming, perhaps, of lavish feasts, as the starving often do, and of comfort and repose, as the whipped slave and the yoked ox might, its gaunt inhabitants slept soundly, and were nourished and set free.
source_text: For three heavy hours, the stone faces of the chateau, lion and human, stared blindly at the night. Dead darkness lay on all the landscape, dead darkness added its own hush to the hushing dust on all the roads. The burial-place had got to the pass that its little heaps of poor grass were undistinguishable from one another; the figure on the Cross might have come down, for anything that could be seen of it. In the village, taxers and taxed were fast asleep. Dreaming, perhaps, of banquets, as the starved usually do, and of ease and rest, as the driven slave and the yoked ox may, its lean inhabitants slept soundly, and were fed and freed.

text: “Will it feel,” the patient gaze, brimming with such forbearance, grows wet, and the mouth loosens faintly, quivering: “like an age to me, as I bide for her in that kinder realm where I hope both you and I shall find gentle refuge?”
source_text: “Do you think:” the uncomplaining eyes in which there is so much endurance, fill with tears, and the lips part a little more and tremble: “that it will seem long to me, while I wait for her in the better land where I trust both you and I will be mercifully sheltered?”

text: Mr. Lorry lingered until the clock struck ten, yet Doctor Manette still hadn’t come back, and he couldn’t bear to leave Lucie alone any longer. So they agreed he would return to her side and then come back to the bank at midnight. In the meantime, Carton would stay by the fire on his own, waiting for the Doctor.
source_text: Mr. Lorry waited until ten; but, Doctor Manette not returning, and he being unwilling to leave Lucie any longer, it was arranged that he should go back to her, and come to the banking-house again at midnight. In the meanwhile, Carton would wait alone by the fire for the Doctor.

text: During that era of widespread dread and suspicion, every ordinary, innocent routine was transformed. In the Doctor’s modest home, just as in countless others, the necessities of daily life were bought each night, in tiny amounts from several different little stores. The common aim was to escape attention and to offer as few reasons as possible for gossip or resentment.
source_text: In the universal fear and distrust that darkened the time, all the usual harmless ways of life were changed. In the Doctor’s little household, as in very many others, the articles of daily consumption that were wanted were purchased every evening, in small quantities and at various small shops. To avoid attracting notice, and to give as little occasion as possible for talk and envy, was the general desire.

text: The Doctor was at his finest, appearing almost youthful. In these moments, the likeness between him and Lucie grew remarkably vivid; as they sat close together, her head nestled against his shoulder and his arm draped along the back of her chair, it was a quiet pleasure to follow the lines of that resemblance.
source_text: The Doctor was in his best condition, and looked specially young. The resemblance between him and Lucie was very strong at such times, and as they sat side by side, she leaning on his shoulder, and he resting his arm on the back of her chair, it was very agreeable to trace the likeness.

text: Though a steady stream of patrons filled the room, the owner of the wine-shop remained absent. No one noticed his absence; those who stepped through the door did not seek him, inquire after him, or find it strange that only Madame Defarge sat in her place, overseeing the pouring of wine. Before her rested a bowl of worn, dented coins, their original markings nearly erased—much like the battered scraps of humanity from whose threadbare pockets they had been drawn.
source_text: Notwithstanding an unusual flow of company, the master of the wine-shop was not visible. He was not missed; for, nobody who crossed the threshold looked for him, nobody asked for him, nobody wondered to see only Madame Defarge in her seat, presiding over the distribution of wine, with a bowl of battered small coins before her, as much defaced and beaten out of their original impress as the small coinage of humanity from whose ragged pockets they had come.

text: Emerging from the open fields, we re-entered a landscape of crumbling structures, isolated farmsteads, dyeing sheds, tanning pits, and similar scenes, with small clusters of dwellings and rows of bare-branched trees. Had these fellows tricked us, leading us back along a different route? Could this be the very same spot we passed before? Thank God, it’s not. A hamlet. Glance behind us, glance behind—check if anyone is chasing! Quiet now! the coaching inn.
source_text: Out of the open country, in again among ruinous buildings, solitary farms, dye-works, tanneries, and the like, cottages in twos and threes, avenues of leafless trees. Have these men deceived us, and taken us back by another road? Is not this the same place twice over? Thank Heaven, no. A village. Look back, look back, and see if we are pursued! Hush! the posting-house.

text: “I have no doubt of it. Edward Ashford, secrets spring from intimate affection just as surely as from vast estrangement; in the first instance, they are fine-spun and elusive, and hard to pierce. My child Eleanor is, in this single matter, just such an enigma to me; I cannot divine the temper of her heart.”
source_text: “I am sure of it. Charles Darnay, mysteries arise out of close love, as well as out of wide division; in the former case, they are subtle and delicate, and difficult to penetrate. My daughter Lucie is, in this one respect, such a mystery to me; I can make no guess at the state of her heart.”

text: “Indeed, yes—of Beauvais. The gentleman, like Monsieur Manette, your father, hailed from Beauvais. And like your father, he was a man of considerable standing in Paris. I had the privilege of his acquaintance there. Our dealings were professional, yet intimate. At that time I served in our French branch, and had done so for—oh! twenty years.”
source_text: “Why, yes, of Beauvais. Like Monsieur Manette, your father, the gentleman was of Beauvais. Like Monsieur Manette, your father, the gentleman was of repute in Paris. I had the honour of knowing him there. Our relations were business relations, but confidential. I was at that time in our French House, and had been--oh! twenty years.”

text: A hidden power now pulled him swiftly toward its core, every current and gust aligning with fierce purpose in that single direction. He entrusted his two letters to a reliable porter, with strict orders they be handed over no earlier than half an hour before midnight; then he mounted his horse at Dover and set out. “For the love of Heaven, for justice, for mercy, for the honour of your noble name!”—that desperate prisoner’s plea was what he used to steady his faltering spirit as he abandoned everything precious in this world and drifted toward the Loadstone Rock.
source_text: The unseen force was drawing him fast to itself, now, and all the tides and winds were setting straight and strong towards it. He left his two letters with a trusty porter, to be delivered half an hour before midnight, and no sooner; took horse for Dover; and began his journey. “For the love of Heaven, of justice, of generosity, of the honour of your noble name!” was the poor prisoner’s cry with which he strengthened his sinking heart, as he left all that was dear on earth behind him, and floated away for the Loadstone Rock.

text: They’re in terrible peril. They face denouncement by Madame Defarge. I heard it straight from her mouth. Tonight I caught fragments of that woman’s talk that painted their danger for me in vivid hues. I acted at once, and afterward I met with the spy. He backs me up. He knows a wood-sawyer who lives beside the prison wall is in the Defarges’ pocket, and that Madame Defarge has coached him to claim he saw Her”—he never spoke Lucie’s name—“signaling to prisoners. You can easily guess the charge will be the usual one, a prison conspiracy, and that it will cost her life—maybe her child’s—maybe her father’s—since both were spotted with her near that spot. Don’t stare in such horror. You’ll rescue them all.
source_text: “They are in great danger. They are in danger of denunciation by Madame Defarge. I know it from her own lips. I have overheard words of that woman’s, to-night, which have presented their danger to me in strong colours. I have lost no time, and since then, I have seen the spy. He confirms me. He knows that a wood-sawyer, living by the prison wall, is under the control of the Defarges, and has been rehearsed by Madame Defarge as to his having seen Her”--he never mentioned Lucie’s name--“making signs and signals to prisoners. It is easy to foresee that the pretence will be the common one, a prison plot, and that it will involve her life--and perhaps her child’s--and perhaps her father’s--for both have been seen with her at that place. Don’t look so horrified. You will save them all.”

text: “Pay attention, Jacques,” the first man called by that name cut in sharply. “Understand that a plea was delivered to the King and Queen. Everyone present, apart from you, witnessed the King accept it, inside his coach on the street, seated next to the Queen. It is Defarge, the man you observe here, who risked his life, springing out ahead of the horses, clutching the petition.”
source_text: “Listen then, Jacques,” Number One of that name sternly interposed. “Know that a petition was presented to the King and Queen. All here, yourself excepted, saw the King take it, in his carriage in the street, sitting beside the Queen. It is Defarge whom you see here, who, at the hazard of his life, darted out before the horses, with the petition in his hand.”

text: Twenty years ago, perhaps; but at this stage of my existence, no. Because as I edge ever nearer to the finish, I find myself circling back, closer and closer to the start. It feels like one of those gentle softenings and readinesses that pave the path ahead. My heart stirs now with memories long dormant, of my lovely young mother (and I so aged!), and with echoes from a time when what we term the World had not yet hardened into truth for me, and my failings were not yet fixed within me.
source_text: “Twenty years back, yes; at this time of my life, no. For, as I draw closer and closer to the end, I travel in the circle, nearer and nearer to the beginning. It seems to be one of the kind smoothings and preparings of the way. My heart is touched now, by many remembrances that had long fallen asleep, of my pretty young mother (and I so old!), and by many associations of the days when what we call the World was not so real with me, and my faults were not confirmed in me.”

text: In the Saint Germain Quarter of Paris, Tellson’s Bank occupied a wing of a grand residence, reached through a courtyard and sealed off from the street by a towering wall and a sturdy gate. The mansion had been the home of a powerful nobleman, who had fled the upheavals disguised in his own cook’s clothing and managed to slip across the frontier. A hunted creature bolting from pursuers, he remained in that altered state nothing less than the same Monseigneur whose morning chocolate had once required the labor of three robust men beyond the very cook whose garments he wore.
source_text: Tellson’s Bank, established in the Saint Germain Quarter of Paris, was in a wing of a large house, approached by a courtyard and shut off from the street by a high wall and a strong gate. The house belonged to a great nobleman who had lived in it until he made a flight from the troubles, in his own cook’s dress, and got across the borders. A mere beast of the chase flying from hunters, he was still in his metempsychosis no other than the same Monseigneur, the preparation of whose chocolate for whose lips had once occupied three strong men besides the cook in question.

text: That night, when they called me away, she rested her head against my shoulder—she was afraid for me to go, though I felt no fear myself—and later, when they took me to the North Tower, these were discovered on my sleeve. “Will you leave them with me? They can’t help me escape in body, but perhaps in spirit.” That is what I told her. I recall those words with perfect clarity.
source_text: “She had laid her head upon my shoulder, that night when I was summoned out--she had a fear of my going, though I had none--and when I was brought to the North Tower they found these upon my sleeve. ‘You will leave me them? They can never help me to escape in the body, though they may in the spirit.’ Those were the words I said. I remember them very well.”

text: The boy did as he was told, and the throng drew near, shrieking and jeering around a soiled hearse and a soiled carriage of grief, within which sat a solitary figure clad in the grimy vestments deemed necessary for the role’s solemnity. Yet the role seemed to bring him no satisfaction, for a swelling mob pressed close about the carriage, mocking him, twisting their faces into sneers, and endlessly howling and shouting: “Yah! Spies! Tst! Yaha! Spies!”—along with abundant taunts too many and too harsh to set down.
source_text: His son obeyed, and the crowd approached; they were bawling and hissing round a dingy hearse and dingy mourning coach, in which mourning coach there was only one mourner, dressed in the dingy trappings that were considered essential to the dignity of the position. The position appeared by no means to please him, however, with an increasing rabble surrounding the coach, deriding him, making grimaces at him, and incessantly groaning and calling out: “Yah! Spies! Tst! Yaha! Spies!” with many compliments too numerous and forcible to repeat.

text: “Heaven prevent it,” Mr. Grimshaw continued, with deeper gravity, a more measured pace, and a stronger impulse to preach and prolong, “that any word I ever uttered or deed I ever did should be laid against my heartfelt prayers for those poor souls this day! Heaven prevent it that we shouldn’t every one fall down (if it were anyhow fitting) to draw ’em clear of this dreadful peril! Prevent it, ma’am! That’s what I say—pre-_vent_ it!” This was Mr. Grimshaw’s finish, after a long but fruitless search for a finer one.
source_text: “Forbid it,” proceeded Mr. Cruncher, with additional solemnity, additional slowness, and additional tendency to hold forth and hold out, “as anything wot I have ever said or done should be wisited on my earnest wishes for them poor creeturs now! Forbid it as we shouldn’t all flop (if it was anyways conwenient) to get ’em out o’ this here dismal risk! Forbid it, miss! Wot I say, for-_bid_ it!” This was Mr. Cruncher’s conclusion after a protracted but vain endeavour to find a better one.

text: An hour and a half dragged its weary way through the crowded lower corridors, thick with thieves and rogues, despite the brief relief of mutton pies and ale. The hoarse messenger, settled uncomfortably on a bench after his meager meal, had slipped into a doze when a sudden swell of voices and a swift current of people surging up the courthouse stairs swept him along in their wake.
source_text: An hour and a half limped heavily away in the thief-and-rascal crowded passages below, even though assisted off with mutton pies and ale. The hoarse messenger, uncomfortably seated on a form after taking that refection, had dropped into a doze, when a loud murmur and a rapid tide of people setting up the stairs that led to the court, carried him along with them.

text: It meant nothing to her that a blameless soul was to perish for the transgressions of his ancestors; her gaze fell not upon him, but upon them. It meant nothing to her that his wife would be left a widow, his child an orphan; such a penalty was too meagre, for they were her born adversaries and her quarry, and as such possessed no claim to existence. To beseech her was rendered futile by her utter lack of compassion, even toward herself. Had she been struck down in the roadway, in any of the countless skirmishes she had fought, she would have felt no self-pity; nor, had she been sentenced to the blade tomorrow, would she have approached it with any gentler emotion than a savage wish to swap fates with the man who condemned her.
source_text: It was nothing to her, that an innocent man was to die for the sins of his forefathers; she saw, not him, but them. It was nothing to her, that his wife was to be made a widow and his daughter an orphan; that was insufficient punishment, because they were her natural enemies and her prey, and as such had no right to live. To appeal to her, was made hopeless by her having no sense of pity, even for herself. If she had been laid low in the streets, in any of the many encounters in which she had been engaged, she would not have pitied herself; nor, if she had been ordered to the axe to-morrow, would she have gone to it with any softer feeling than a fierce desire to change places with the man who sent her there.

text: The following morning, Mr. Lorry greeted him warmly by name and brought up subjects they had often discussed in recent days.
