The man offered no answer, yet it was clear he absorbed the words and mulled them over, albeit in a muddled way. Heartened by this, Mr. Lorry arranged for Miss Pross to sit with her needlework in the room at intervals throughout the day; during these moments, they chatted softly about Lucie and about her father who sat right there, exactly as they normally would, as though nothing were wrong. They did this without any showy fuss, keeping it brief and infrequent enough not to strain him; and it eased Mr. Lorry’s kind spirit to notice that the man glanced up more regularly and seemed unsettled by some dim awareness of the contradictions around him.
source_text: On this second day, Mr. Lorry saluted him cheerfully by his name, and spoke to him on topics that had been of late familiar to them. He returned no reply, but it was evident that he heard what was said, and that he thought about it, however confusedly. This encouraged Mr. Lorry to have Miss Pross in with her work, several times during the day; at those times, they quietly spoke of Lucie, and of her father then present, precisely in the usual manner, and as if there were nothing amiss. This was done without any demonstrative accompaniment, not long enough, or often enough to harass him; and it lightened Mr. Lorry’s friendly heart to believe that he looked up oftener, and that he appeared to be stirred by some perception of inconsistencies surrounding him.

text: “Do not be distressed, my good Miss Pross. I came upon Mr. Lorry quite unexpectedly last evening; it was settled between us that I should keep out of sight elsewhere until matters were mended, or unless my presence could serve some purpose. I have come here now to ask for a short conversation with your brother. I could wish, for your sake, that your brother were more honorably occupied than Mr. Barsad. I could wish he were not a Prison Spy.”
source_text: “Don’t be alarmed, my dear Miss Pross. I arrived at Mr. Lorry’s, to his surprise, yesterday evening; we agreed that I would not present myself elsewhere until all was well, or unless I could be useful; I present myself here, to beg a little talk with your brother. I wish you had a better employed brother than Mr. Barsad. I wish for your sake Mr. Barsad was not a Sheep of the Prisons.”

text: As the clock’s hands neared the hour when the Bank would shut, a tide of speakers drifted by Mr. Lorry’s desk. He extended the letter with a questioning air; and Monseigneur, embodied in one scheming, resentful refugee after another, glanced at it—this one and that one, and yet another—each finding something slighting to murmur, in French or in English, about the Marquis who remained elusive.
source_text: The hands of the clock verging upon the hour of closing the Bank, there was a general set of the current of talkers past Mr. Lorry’s desk. He held the letter out inquiringly; and Monseigneur looked at it, in the person of this plotting and indignant refugee; and Monseigneur looked at it in the person of that plotting and indignant refugee; and This, That, and The Other, all had something disparaging to say, in French or in English, concerning the Marquis who was not to be found.

text: He was a man of around sixty, elegantly attired, with an imperious bearing and a countenance like an exquisite mask. His face possessed a translucent pallor; every feature was sharply cut, fixed in a single unvarying expression. The nose, though handsomely sculpted, was faintly pinched at the crest of each nostril. In those two slight indentations lay the only alteration the face ever betrayed. They would sometimes shift in hue, and now and then dilate and contract with what seemed a subtle throb; at such moments, they lent the whole visage an air of treachery and cruelty. Studied closely, the capacity for that expression could be traced in the line of the mouth and the set of the eyes, which were far too level and thin; yet, in its total impression, it remained a handsome face, and a striking one.
source_text: He was a man of about sixty, handsomely dressed, haughty in manner, and with a face like a fine mask. A face of a transparent paleness; every feature in it clearly defined; one set expression on it. The nose, beautifully formed otherwise, was very slightly pinched at the top of each nostril. In those two compressions, or dints, the only little change that the face ever showed, resided. They persisted in changing colour sometimes, and they would be occasionally dilated and contracted by something like a faint pulsation; then, they gave a look of treachery, and cruelty, to the whole countenance. Examined with attention, its capacity of helping such a look was to be found in the line of the mouth, and the lines of the orbits of the eyes, being much too horizontal and thin; still, in the effect of the face made, it was a handsome face, and a remarkable one.

text: At Dover, when the morning post arrived without mishap, the chief steward of the King’s Arms Inn unfastened the carriage door in his usual manner. He performed the act with a certain ceremonious sweep, for a winter mail-coach run from London was a feat worthy of saluting any daring passenger.
source_text: When the mail got successfully to Dover, in the course of the forenoon, the head drawer at the Royal George Hotel opened the coach-door as his custom was. He did it with some flourish of ceremony, for a mail journey from London in winter was an achievement to congratulate an adventurous traveller upon.

text: Within a hundred-mile radius, illuminated by the glow of distant blazes, other officials met grimmer fates that night and on many nights thereafter—dawn discovered them dangling above streets once tranquil, in the very towns where they’d grown up. Likewise, there were other peasants and townsfolk less lucky than the road-mender and his companions, against whom the authorities and soldiers prevailed, stringing them up in retaliation. Yet the relentless figures pressed steadily onward in every direction, regardless; and no matter who swung, the fires kept burning. No official, no matter how clever his reckoning, could ever compute the height of the scaffold that might dissolve into a flood and extinguish the flames.
source_text: Within a hundred miles, and in the light of other fires, there were other functionaries less fortunate, that night and other nights, whom the rising sun found hanging across once-peaceful streets, where they had been born and bred; also, there were other villagers and townspeople less fortunate than the mender of roads and his fellows, upon whom the functionaries and soldiery turned with success, and whom they strung up in their turn. But, the fierce figures were steadily wending East, West, North, and South, be that as it would; and whosoever hung, fire burned. The altitude of the gallows that would turn to water and quench it, no functionary, by any stretch of mathematics, was able to calculate successfully.

text: “If, when I say to you, beloved heart, that your suffering is ended, and that I have journeyed here to deliver you from it, and that we shall travel to England to find stillness and repose, I stir in you the thought of your worthy life cast into ruin, and of our own France so cruel to you, shed tears for it, shed tears for it! And if, when I speak to you of my name, and of my father who yet breathes, and of my mother who lies in the grave, you discover that I must bow before my revered father, and beg his forgiveness for never having laboured by day and kept vigil by night for his sake, because devotion to my poor mother concealed his anguish from me, shed tears for it, shed tears for it! Weep for her, then, and for me! Kind sirs, praise Heaven! I feel his hallowed tears upon my cheek, and his weeping shakes my very soul. O, behold! Praise Heaven for us, praise Heaven!”
source_text: “If, when I tell you, dearest dear, that your agony is over, and that I have come here to take you from it, and that we go to England to be at peace and at rest, I cause you to think of your useful life laid waste, and of our native France so wicked to you, weep for it, weep for it! And if, when I shall tell you of my name, and of my father who is living, and of my mother who is dead, you learn that I have to kneel to my honoured father, and implore his pardon for having never for his sake striven all day and lain awake and wept all night, because the love of my poor mother hid his torture from me, weep for it, weep for it! Weep for her, then, and for me! Good gentlemen, thank God! I feel his sacred tears upon my face, and his sobs strike against my heart. O, see! Thank God for us, thank God!”

text: The sun hung so low that it seemed to vanish on the instant. Once the heavy drag had been set against the wheel and the carriage began its descent, sliding downhill in a haze of dust and the sharp scent of cinders, the crimson light fled swiftly. Sun and Marquis sank as one, and by the time the drag was released, no trace of that glow remained.
source_text: In effect, the sun was so low that it dipped at the moment. When the heavy drag had been adjusted to the wheel, and the carriage slid down hill, with a cinderous smell, in a cloud of dust, the red glow departed quickly; the sun and the Marquis going down together, there was no glow left when the drag was taken off.

text: Beneath the swaying streetlamps—glowing ever more brilliant in the finer quarters, and ever more feeble in the poorer—past bright shopwindows, lively throngs, lit-up cafés, and theatre entrances, they rolled on toward one of the city gates. At the guardhouse, soldiers stood holding lanterns. “Your documents, travellers!” “Here, if you please, Monsieur the Officer,” said Defarge, climbing down and drawing him soberly aside. “These belong to the gentleman inside, the one with the white hair. He was entrusted to me, along with the gentleman himself, at the—” He lowered his voice; the military lanterns bobbed and flickered, and one was passed into the carriage by a uniformed arm. The eyes above that arm fixed on the white-haired gentleman with a look that was no ordinary glance, by day or night. “All is in order. Proceed!” came from the uniform. “Farewell!” from Defarge. And so, beneath a brief avenue of ever-weakening, swaying lamps, they emerged into the vast avenue of stars.
source_text: Under the over-swinging lamps--swinging ever brighter in the better streets, and ever dimmer in the worse--and by lighted shops, gay crowds, illuminated coffee-houses, and theatre-doors, to one of the city gates. Soldiers with lanterns, at the guard-house there. “Your papers, travellers!” “See here then, Monsieur the Officer,” said Defarge, getting down, and taking him gravely apart, “these are the papers of monsieur inside, with the white head. They were consigned to me, with him, at the--” He dropped his voice, there was a flutter among the military lanterns, and one of them being handed into the coach by an arm in uniform, the eyes connected with the arm looked, not an every day or an every night look, at monsieur with the white head. “It is well. Forward!” from the uniform. “Adieu!” from Defarge. And so, under a short grove of feebler and feebler over-swinging lamps, out under the great grove of stars.

text: The man tipped his cap with a smirk, hoping for a coin to reward his trouble; yet the elderly stranger, regarding him with a look of distaste, cast a fretful glance about, as though he himself meant to bolt: a plan he might well have tried, and so provoked a second pursuit, had not a constable (usually the final figure to appear at such scenes) just then pushed through the throng and caught the boy by the neck.
source_text: The fellow touched his hat with a grin, expecting something for his pains; but, the old gentleman, eyeing him with an expression of dislike, look anxiously round, as if he contemplated running away himself: which it is very possible he might have attempted to do, and thus have afforded another chase, had not a police officer (who is generally the last person to arrive in such cases) at that moment made his way through the crowd, and seized Oliver by the collar.

text: The Sowerberrys had stepped out for the evening, and since Noah Claypole never cared to exert himself beyond the minimal effort required for eating and drinking, the shop remained open well past its normal closing time. Mr. Bumble rapped his cane against the counter more than once, but when no one responded and he noticed a glow filtering through the glass pane of the small back parlour, he ventured to look inside and observe the scene—and what he saw astonished him greatly.
source_text: Now, Mr. and Mrs. Sowerberry having gone out to tea and supper: and Noah Claypole not being at any time disposed to take upon himself a greater amount of physical exertion than is necessary to a convenient performance of the two functions of eating and drinking, the shop was not closed, although it was past the usual hour of shutting-up. Mr. Bumble tapped with his cane on the counter several times; but, attracting no attention, and beholding a light shining through the glass-window of the little parlour at the back of the shop, he made bold to peep in and see what was going forward; and when he saw what was going forward, he was not a little surprised.

text: One twilight, perhaps seven days after the incident of the portrait, while he sat conversing with Mrs. Bedwin, a summons arrived from Mr. Brownlow, requesting that if Oliver Twist were sufficiently recovered, he might attend him in his library for a brief conversation.
source_text: One evening, about a week after the affair of the picture, as he was sitting talking to Mrs. Bedwin, there came a message down from Mr. Brownlow, that if Oliver Twist felt pretty well, he should like to see him in his study, and talk to him a little while.

text: “Well, the officers nabbed him, and that’s the end of it,” the Dodger muttered darkly. “Now, take your hands off me, will you!” And with a single twist, he slipped free of the oversized coat, leaving it dangling in the Jew’s grip, then seized the toasting fork and jabbed at the merry old gentleman’s waistcoat—a blow that, had it landed, would have released a good deal more merriment than could have been readily put back.
source_text: “Why, the traps have got him, and that’s all about it,” said the Dodger, sullenly. “Come, let go o’ me, will you!” And, swinging himself, at one jerk, clean out of the big coat, which he left in the Jew’s hands, the Dodger snatched up the toasting fork, and made a pass at the merry old gentleman’s waistcoat; which, if it had taken effect, would have let a little more merriment out than could have been easily replaced.

text: The words were spoken in jest, yet had the speaker caught the sinister smirk twisting the Jew’s bloodless lips as he turned toward the cupboard, he might have judged the warning not entirely unwarranted—or at least suspected that the wish to outdo the distiller’s craft was not so distant from the old man’s mirthful heart.
source_text: This was said in jest; but if the speaker could have seen the evil leer with which the Jew bit his pale lip as he turned round to the cupboard, he might have thought the caution not wholly unnecessary, or the wish (at all events) to improve upon the distiller’s ingenuity not very far from the old gentleman’s merry heart.

text: The murderer had been gripped all day by a strange, stubborn irresolution that clung to him against his will. Realizing no one pursued him and that the passersby likely dismissed him as some sullen drunk, he retraced his steps through the town. Steering clear of the bright lamps of a stage-coach waiting in the street, he moved past it—then halted. He recognized the London mail, standing at the little post-office. Some presentiment of what would follow stirred in him, yet he crossed over and listened.
source_text: With the same perversity of feeling and irresolution that had fastened upon him, despite himself, all day, the murderer, finding that he was not followed, and that they most probably considered him some drunken sullen fellow, turned back up the town, and getting out of the glare of the lamps of a stage-coach that was standing in the street, was walking past, when he recognised the mail from London, and saw that it was standing at the little post-office. He almost knew what was to come; but he crossed over, and listened.

text: In the midst of various clever conjectures, a fresh debate arose over whether Mr. Giles had actually struck anyone at all. When they inspected the second pistol—the mate to the one he had discharged—it was found to contain nothing more harmful than gunpowder and brown paper. This revelation impressed nearly everyone present, save the doctor, who had extracted the bullet some ten minutes earlier. Yet no one seized upon the notion more eagerly than Mr. Giles himself, who had spent hours tormented by the dread of having dealt a fatal wound; he now clung to this comforting thought with fervent relief. In the end, the officers, troubling themselves little over Oliver, left the Chertsey constable at the house and retired for the night into the town, vowing to return the following morning.
source_text: Among other ingenious surmises, the question was then raised, whether Mr. Giles had really hit anybody; and upon examination of the fellow pistol to that which he had fired, it turned out to have no more destructive loading than gunpowder and brown paper: a discovery which made a considerable impression on everybody but the doctor, who had drawn the ball about ten minutes before. Upon no one, however, did it make a greater impression than on Mr. Giles himself; who, after labouring, for some hours, under the fear of having mortally wounded a fellow-creature, eagerly caught at this new idea, and favoured it to the utmost. Finally, the officers, without troubling themselves very much about Oliver, left the Chertsey constable in the house, and took up their rest for that night in the town; promising to return the next morning.

text: “Do you consider this a respectful or fitting manner, Mrs. Mann,” asked Mr. Bumble, clutching his walking stick, “to leave parish officers standing at your garden gate, when they arrive on parish business concerning the parish orphans? Are you aware, Mrs. Mann, that you are, so to speak, a parish delegate and a salaried official?”
source_text: “Do you think this respectful or proper conduct, Mrs. Mann,” inquired Mr. Bumble, grasping his cane, “to keep the parish officers a waiting at your garden-gate, when they come here upon porochial business with the porochial orphans? Are you aweer, Mrs. Mann, that you are, as I may say, a porochial delegate, and a stipendiary?”

text: “Now,” Mr. Brownlow began, his voice gentler yet graver than Oliver had ever heard it, “I need you to listen very carefully, my boy, to what I am about to tell you. I will speak to you openly, because I am certain you can comprehend me as well as many who are much older.”
source_text: “Now,” said Mr. Brownlow, speaking if possible in a kinder, but at the same time in a much more serious manner, than Oliver had ever known him assume yet, “I want you to pay great attention, my boy, to what I am going to say. I shall talk to you without any reserve; because I am sure you are well able to understand me, as many older persons would be.”

text: Still, the little cottage maintained its tranquil rhythm, and a steady, quiet contentment reigned among those who lived there. Oliver had long since become robust and well; yet whether in sickness or in health, his deep affection for many remained unchanged. He was still the same tender, devoted, loving soul he had been when pain and hardship had drained his vigor, and when he had relied on those who cared for him for every small kindness and solace.
source_text: Still, the same quiet life went on at the little cottage, and the same cheerful serenity prevailed among its inmates. Oliver had long since grown stout and healthy; but health or sickness made no difference in his warm feelings of a great many people. He was still the same gentle, attached, affectionate creature that he had been when pain and suffering had wasted his strength, and when he was dependent for every slight attention, and comfort on those who tended him.

text: “Oh, my hair, my hair!” shouted Master Charles Bates, the source of the laughter: “there he stands! oh, shout it, there he is! Oh, Fagin, just see him! Fagin, you must see him! I can’t stand it; it’s too merry a prank, I can’t stand it. Somebody catch me, while I laugh myself empty.”
source_text: “Oh, my wig, my wig!” cried Master Charles Bates, from whose lungs the laughter had proceeded: “here he is! oh, cry, here he is! Oh, Fagin, look at him! Fagin, do look at him! I can’t bear it; it is such a jolly game, I can’t bear it. Hold me, somebody, while I laugh it out.”

text: “There, there, don’t trouble yourself with that, my love,” the old woman said. “It has nothing to do with your soup, and it’s high time you ate it. The doctor says Mr. Brownlow may look in on you this morning, and we must put on our finest faces, for the brighter we appear, the happier he’ll be.” With these words, she busied herself heating a small saucepan filled to the brim with broth—so rich, Oliver reckoned, that if watered down to the workhouse standard, it would have made a generous meal for no fewer than three hundred and fifty paupers.
source_text: “Well, never you mind that, my dear,” said the old lady; “that’s got nothing to do with your broth; and it’s full time you had it; for the doctor says Mr. Brownlow may come in to see you this morning; and we must get up our best looks, because the better we look, the more he’ll be pleased.” And with this, the old lady applied herself to warming up, in a little saucepan, a basin full of broth: strong enough, Oliver thought, to furnish an ample dinner, when reduced to the regulation strength, for three hundred and fifty paupers, at the lowest computation.

text: Master Bates roared with such violent laughter at this grandiose threat that Fagin had to wait a good while before he could cut in and assure Mr. Bolter he ran no real risk by showing up at the police station. Since no report of the minor escapade he’d taken part in, nor any likeness of his features, had yet reached the capital, it was highly unlikely anyone suspected he’d sought refuge there. And if he put on a convincing disguise, the place would be as secure a haunt as anywhere in London—precisely because it was the last spot anyone would imagine he’d willingly choose to visit.
source_text: Master Bates laughed so vehemently at this magnificent threat, that it was some time before Fagin could interpose, and represent to Mr. Bolter that he incurred no possible danger in visiting the police-office; that, inasmuch as no account of the little affair in which he had engaged, nor any description of his person, had yet been forwarded to the metropolis, it was very probable that he was not even suspected of having resorted to it for shelter; and that, if he were properly disguised, it would be as safe a spot for him to visit as any in London, inasmuch as it would be, of all places, the very last, to which he could be supposed likely to resort of his own free will.

text: Mr. Fagin’s expression held such grave conviction that Charley Bates, ever inclined to err on the side of caution and not at all convinced he wouldn’t be the next to feel a grip at his throat, sank to the floor and let out a long, steady, unbroken howl—a sound halfway between a wounded bear and a foghorn.
source_text: Mr. Fagin looked so very much in earnest, that Charley Bates, who deemed it prudent in all cases to be on the safe side, and who conceived it by no means improbable that it might be his turn to be throttled second, dropped upon his knees, and raised a loud, well-sustained, and continuous roar—something between a mad bull and a speaking trumpet.

text: “Then,” Sikes said, pushing the Jew’s hand away with a flick of contempt, “have it done whenever you please. Toby and I scaled the garden-wall the night before last, testing the door panels and shutters. The place is bolted tight after dark, like a prison, but there’s one spot we can prise open, quiet and sure.”
source_text: “Then,” said Sikes, thrusting aside the Jew’s hand, with some disdain, “let it come off as soon as you like. Toby and me were over the garden-wall the night afore last, sounding the panels of the door and shutters. The crib’s barred up at night like a jail; but there’s one part we can crack, safe and softly.”

text: One evening, the Artful Dodger and Charley Bates had business elsewhere, so the former, on a sudden whim, decided to fuss over his appearance—a vanity, it must be said, that rarely troubled him. With this purpose in mind, he loftily ordered Oliver to help him dress at once.
source_text: One afternoon, the Dodger and Master Bates being engaged out that evening, the first-named young gentleman took it into his head to evince some anxiety regarding the decoration of his person (to do him justice, this was by no means an habitual weakness with him); and, with this end and aim, he condescendingly commanded Oliver to assist him in his toilet, straightway.

text: “So do I,” shouted Charley Bates. “Ha! ha! ha! so do I. I can picture the whole scene, upon my word I can, Fagin. What sport! What splendid sport! All the grand gentlemen straining to keep grave faces, and Jack Dawkins chatting to them as easy and familiar as if he were the judge’s own son delivering an after-dinner speech—ha! ha! ha!”
source_text: “So do I,” cried Charley Bates. “Ha! ha! ha! so do I. I see it all afore me, upon my soul I do, Fagin. What a game! What a regular game! All the big-wigs trying to look solemn, and Jack Dawkins addressing of ’em as intimate and comfortable as if he was the judge’s own son making a speech arter dinner—ha! ha! ha!”

text: He drifted down the street, musing on the contentment that should have been his, and on what he would sacrifice for a single glimpse of wretched little Tom—who, hungry and bruised, might at that very instant be sobbing in despair—when a piercing shriek from a young woman jolted him alert. “Oh, my beloved brother!” Before he could even raise his eyes to discover the cause, he found himself halted, a pair of slender arms flung fast about his neck.
source_text: He was walking along, thinking how happy and contented he ought to feel; and how much he would give for only one look at poor little Dick, who, starved and beaten, might be weeping bitterly at that very moment; when he was startled by a young woman screaming out very loud. “Oh, my dear brother!” And he had hardly looked up, to see what the matter was, when he was stopped by having a pair of arms thrown tight round his neck.

text: Noah Claypole made his way along these roads, pulling Charlotte behind him. At times he stepped into the gutter to take in the full outward look of some modest tavern; then he’d move on, deciding a certain air about the place made it too conspicuous for his ends. Eventually he halted before one that seemed shabbier and filthier than any he’d passed so far. After crossing the street to inspect it from the far curb, he condescendingly declared they would lodge there for the night.
source_text: Through these streets, Noah Claypole walked, dragging Charlotte after him; now stepping into the kennel to embrace at a glance the whole external character of some small public-house; now jogging on again, as some fancied appearance induced him to believe it too public for his purpose. At length, he stopped in front of one, more humble in appearance and more dirty than any he had yet seen; and, having crossed over and surveyed it from the opposite pavement, graciously announced his intention of putting up there, for the night.

text: “Indeed, I find it quite handsome,” the warden remarked, casting a satisfied look at the weighty silver clasps adorning his tunic. “The stamp mirrors the parish emblem—the Merciful Stranger tending the wounded traveler. The council bestowed it upon me at Christmastide, Mr. Hargrave. I recall fastening it, for the first occasion, when I went to the inquiry on that impoverished merchant, who perished on a stoop in the dead of night.”
source_text: “Yes, I think it rather pretty,” said the beadle, glancing proudly downwards at the large brass buttons which embellished his coat. “The die is the same as the porochial seal—the Good Samaritan healing the sick and bruised man. The board presented it to me on Newyear’s morning, Mr. Sowerberry. I put it on, I remember, for the first time, to attend the inquest on that reduced tradesman, who died in a doorway at midnight.”

text: “Not a soul stirring,” replied Mr. Crackit, drawing up his collar. “It’s been dead as ditchwater. You should stump up something generous, Fagin, to pay me back for minding the place all this while. Blast it, I’m as bored as a judge at sessions; I’d have dropped off as quick as a condemned man if I hadn’t been kind enough to keep this lad entertained. Dreadfully tedious, strike me if it isn’t!”
source_text: “Not a living leg,” answered Mr. Crackit, pulling up his collar; “it’s been as dull as swipes. You ought to stand something handsome, Fagin, to recompense me for keeping house so long. Damme, I’m as flat as a juryman; and should have gone to sleep, as fast as Newgate, if I hadn’t had the good natur’ to amuse this youngster. Horrid dull, I’m blessed if I an’t!”

text: Oliver’s gaze darted between the pair, utterly bewildered, yet he had no chance to ask questions. The two lads were already slipping quietly across the street, creeping up just behind the elderly man who had caught his notice moments before. Oliver trailed them a short distance, then halted, uncertain whether to move forward or draw back, and simply stared in mute astonishment.
source_text: Oliver looked from one to the other, with the greatest surprise; but he was not permitted to make any inquiries; for the two boys walked stealthily across the road, and slunk close behind the old gentleman towards whom his attention had been directed. Oliver walked a few paces after them; and, not knowing whether to advance or retire, stood looking on in silent amazement.

text: Urged forward by this kindness, Edwin gave a soft knock upon the library door. At Mr. Ashworth’s summons to enter, he stepped into a modest rear chamber, densely lined with volumes, its casement overlooking a cluster of tranquil garden plots. Drawn close to the window stood a writing desk, where Mr. Ashworth sat absorbed in a book. Upon noticing Edwin, he slid the volume aside and bade him approach the desk and take a seat. Edwin obeyed, wondering all the while where enough readers could be discovered to peruse so vast a collection of books—books that seemed designed to render the world more knowing. A wonder, indeed, that still perplexes souls far older than Edwin Ashworth, each and every day of their existence.
source_text: Thus encouraged, Oliver tapped at the study door. On Mr. Brownlow calling to him to come in, he found himself in a little back room, quite full of books, with a window, looking into some pleasant little gardens. There was a table drawn up before the window, at which Mr. Brownlow was seated reading. When he saw Oliver, he pushed the book away from him, and told him to come near the table, and sit down. Oliver complied; marvelling where the people could be found to read such a great number of books as seemed to be written to make the world wiser. Which is still a marvel to more experienced people than Oliver Twist, every day of their lives.

text: “You should be dead—stone dead from the terror of it,” declared the stout gentleman. “Why on earth didn’t you call for help? Good heavens, my servant would have been there in an instant; and so would I; and my assistant would have been thrilled to assist; or anyone at all, I daresay, given the situation. Oh, dear me! So unforeseen! And in the dead of night, no less!”
source_text: “You ought to be dead; positively dead with the fright,” said the fat gentleman. “Why didn’t you send? Bless me, my man should have come in a minute; and so would I; and my assistant would have been delighted; or anybody, I’m sure, under such circumstances. Dear, dear! So unexpected! In the silence of the night, too!”

text: They did not stop at any cottage in Shepperton, though the exhausted child had hoped they would; instead they trudged onward, through mire and shadow, along sunken byways and across bleak, wind-scoured heaths, till at last the glimmer of a town rose before them, not far off. Peering hard into the distance, Oliver made out the dark gleam of water beneath their path and understood that they were nearing the base of a bridge.
source_text: They turned into no house at Shepperton, as the weary boy had expected; but still kept walking on, in mud and darkness, through gloomy lanes and over cold open wastes, until they came within sight of the lights of a town at no great distance. On looking intently forward, Oliver saw that the water was just below them, and that they were coming to the foot of a bridge.

text: They kept moving at that steady pace until they had rounded Hyde Park Corner and were heading toward Kensington. Then Sikes slowed his steps, letting an empty cart that trailed a short way behind catch up with them. Spotting the word “Hounslow” painted on its side, he mustered what politeness he could and asked the driver if he would carry them as far as Isleworth.
source_text: They held their course at this rate, until they had passed Hyde Park corner, and were on their way to Kensington: when Sikes relaxed his pace, until an empty cart which was at some little distance behind, came up. Seeing “Hounslow” written on it, he asked the driver with as much civility as he could assume, if he would give them a lift as far as Isleworth.

text: The fellow had crumpled inward, utterly subdued by the mob's savage fury and the hopelessness of flight; yet perceiving this abrupt shift with a swiftness equal to its arrival, he vaulted upright, resolved to stake everything on a final bid for survival by plunging into the trench, and, despite the danger of suffocation, striving to slither off through the murk and turmoil.
source_text: The man had shrunk down, thoroughly quelled by the ferocity of the crowd, and the impossibility of escape; but seeing this sudden change with no less rapidity than it had occurred, he sprang upon his feet, determined to make one last effort for his life by dropping into the ditch, and, at the risk of being stifled, endeavouring to creep away in the darkness and confusion.

text: “Oh, you tiny monster!” Charlotte shrieked, grabbing Oliver with all the strength she could muster—roughly matching that of a fit, well-conditioned man. “Oh, you thankless, bloodthirsty, dreadful little devil!” With every word she spat out, she struck him as hard as she could, letting out a piercing cry each time, as if to warn the world.
source_text: “Oh, you little wretch!” screamed Charlotte: seizing Oliver with her utmost force, which was about equal to that of a moderately strong man in particularly good training. “Oh, you little un-grate-ful, mur-de-rous, hor-rid villain!” And between every syllable, Charlotte gave Oliver a blow with all her might: accompanying it with a scream, for the benefit of society.

text: Mrs. Craik guided the parish officer into a snug sitting room paved with red tiles; she drew out a chair for his comfort, then, with bustling attention, laid his tricorn and walking stick upon the nearby sideboard. Mr. Grimes dabbed from his brow the moisture that the journey had summoned, cast a satisfied look at the tricorn, and allowed himself a grin. Indeed, he grinned. Parish officers are merely human: and Mr. Grimes grinned.
source_text: Mrs. Mann ushered the beadle into a small parlour with a brick floor; placed a seat for him; and officiously deposited his cocked hat and cane on the table before him. Mr. Bumble wiped from his forehead the perspiration which his walk had engendered, glanced complacently at the cocked hat, and smiled. Yes, he smiled. Beadles are but men: and Mr. Bumble smiled.

text: “Hush, my child,” the elderly woman murmured gently. “You must stay perfectly still, or your sickness will return; and you have been dreadfully unwell—as ill as anyone could be, nearly. Lie back down now, there’s a good soul.” As she spoke, she tenderly laid Oliver’s head on the pillow, then brushed the hair from his brow and gazed into his face with such warmth and affection that he instinctively placed his small, frail hand in hers and drew it around his neck.
source_text: “Hush, my dear,” said the old lady softly. “You must be very quiet, or you will be ill again; and you have been very bad,—as bad as bad could be, pretty nigh. Lie down again; there’s a dear!” With those words, the old lady very gently placed Oliver’s head upon the pillow; and, smoothing back his hair from his forehead, looked so kindly and loving in his face, that he could not help placing his little withered hand in hers, and drawing it round his neck.

text: “You three, bear witness!” shouted the boy, brandishing his fist, his agitation mounting with every word. “Bear witness—I’m not scared of him—if they come looking for him, I’ll hand him over. I will. I’m telling you straight away. He can murder me for it if he wants, or if he has the nerve, but as long as I’m standing here, I’ll turn him in. I’d turn him in even if they were going to boil him alive. Murder! Help! If any of you three has an ounce of courage, you’ll back me. Murder! Help! Strike him down!”
source_text: “Witness you three,” cried the boy shaking his clenched fist, and becoming more and more excited as he spoke. “Witness you three—I’m not afraid of him—if they come here after him, I’ll give him up; I will. I tell you out at once. He may kill me for it if he likes, or if he dares, but if I am here I’ll give him up. I’d give him up if he was to be boiled alive. Murder! Help! If there’s the pluck of a man among you three, you’ll help me. Murder! Help! Down with him!”

text: “Because it’s not on the record, is it?” said Charley, his irritation with his old friend sharpening into outright rebellion under the tide of his own grief. “Because it won’t appear in the indictment; because no one will ever know half of what he truly was. How will he be remembered in the Newgate Calendar? Perhaps he won’t even be mentioned. Oh, my eye, my eye, what a crushing blow it is!”
source_text: “’Cause it isn’t on the rec-ord, is it?” said Charley, chafed into perfect defiance of his venerable friend by the current of his regrets; “’cause it can’t come out in the ’dictment; ’cause nobody will never know half of what he was. How will he stand in the Newgate Calendar? P’raps not be there at all. Oh, my eye, my eye, wot a blow it is!”

text: He lingered at the foot of a sharp incline until a mail-coach approached, and then entreated the travelers perched on its roof; yet scarcely any regarded him at all, and those who did bade him bide until they reached the crest, and then show them what distance he could dash for a farthing. Wretched Oliver strove to match the coach’s pace a short way, but could not sustain it, owing to his weariness and blistered feet. When the roof-riders observed this, they slipped their farthings back into their purses, pronouncing him a worthless young scamp who merited no charity; and the coach clattered onward, abandoning nothing but a swirl of dust behind.
source_text: He waited at the bottom of a steep hill till a stage-coach came up, and then begged of the outside passengers; but there were very few who took any notice of him: and even those told him to wait till they got to the top of the hill, and then let them see how far he could run for a halfpenny. Poor Oliver tried to keep up with the coach a little way, but was unable to do it, by reason of his fatigue and sore feet. When the outsides saw this, they put their halfpence back into their pockets again, declaring that he was an idle young dog, and didn’t deserve anything; and the coach rattled away and left only a cloud of dust behind.

text: “What’s your game here?” Sikes demanded, reinforcing the question with a vulgar oath aimed at the loveliest part of the human face—a curse that, if the heavens ever took notice of even one in fifty thousand of its earthly utterances, would make blindness as widespread as the common cold. “What exactly are you playing at? Damn my flesh! Do you have any clue who you are, and just what sort of creature you’ve become?”
source_text: “What do you mean by this?” said Sikes; backing the inquiry with a very common imprecation concerning the most beautiful of human features: which, if it were heard above, only once out of every fifty thousand times that it is uttered below, would render blindness as common a disorder as measles: “what do you mean by it? Burn my body! Do you know who you are, and what you are?”

text: Climbing onto a stool, he carefully pressed his eye against the windowpane—a hidden vantage point from which he observed Mr. Claypole helping himself to cold beef from the platter and porter from the jug, then doling out meager portions of both to Charlotte, who sat quietly nearby, partaking only as he saw fit.
source_text: Mounting a stool, he cautiously applied his eye to the pane of glass, from which secret post he could see Mr. Claypole taking cold beef from the dish, and porter from the pot, and administering homeopathic doses of both to Charlotte, who sat patiently by, eating and drinking at his pleasure.

text: Gasping for air, the aged man faltered over a syllable; and in that heartbeat stemmed the flood of his fury, transforming his entire bearing. Seconds earlier, his balled fists had seized the void; his stare had widened; and his visage had paled with rage; but now, he crumpled into a seat, and, huddling inward, quaked with the dread of having himself unveiled some secret transgression. After a brief stillness, he dared to glance toward his companion. He seemed somewhat comforted, on finding her still in the same vacant posture from which he had originally stirred her.
source_text: Panting for breath, the old man stammered for a word; and in that instant checked the torrent of his wrath, and changed his whole demeanour. A moment before, his clenched hands had grasped the air; his eyes had dilated; and his face grown livid with passion; but now, he shrunk into a chair, and, cowering together, trembled with the apprehension of having himself disclosed some hidden villainy. After a short silence, he ventured to look round at his companion. He appeared somewhat reassured, on beholding her in the same listless attitude from which he had first roused her.

text: “And what use would that be?” asked the practical Mr. Sikes. “Unless you could toss down a file and twenty yards of strong, sturdy rope, you might as well be fifty miles away, or not moving at all, for all the help it would give me. Move along, and stop standing there lecturing.”
source_text: “And what good would that do?” inquired the unsentimental Mr. Sikes. “Unless you could pitch over a file and twenty yards of good stout rope, you might as well be walking fifty mile off, or not walking at all, for all the good it would do me. Come on, and don’t stand preaching there.”

text: “What is it?” Fagin pressed, seething with fury. “The boy is worth a fortune to me—am I to let a prize that luck itself delivered slip through my fingers, all because of the reckless whims of a pack of drunks whose lives I could snuff out with a snap of my fingers? And me shackled, no less, to a fiend from birth who lacks nothing but the desire, yet holds every means, to—”
source_text: “What is it?” pursued Fagin, mad with rage. “When the boy’s worth hundreds of pounds to me, am I to lose what chance threw me in the way of getting safely, through the whims of a drunken gang that I could whistle away the lives of! And me bound, too, to a born devil that only wants the will, and has the power to, to—”

text: The boy, called now by another name, stood stricken before the assembly of stern-faced men, his small frame quivering with dread. A sharp prod from the officer’s staff against his back undid him further, and tears welled up unbidden. Under this double weight of fear, he could barely whisper his faltering reply, each word catching in his throat. At this, a man clad in a pale waistcoat pronounced him a simpleton—a remark delivered with such brisk authority that it was plainly meant to hearten the lad and set him wholly at peace.
source_text: Oliver was frightened at the sight of so many gentlemen, which made him tremble: and the beadle gave him another tap behind, which made him cry. These two causes made him answer in a very low and hesitating voice; whereupon a gentleman in a white waistcoat said he was a fool. Which was a capital way of raising his spirits, and putting him quite at his ease.

text: “The spread’s decent, I’ll grant you,” said Mr. Sikes, his temper easing a fraction as his eyes swept the table. “But what excuse can you offer for deserting me here, low in spirits, sick, short of cash, the whole lot—and paying me no more mind all this everlasting while than you would that dog there. Send him off, Charley!”
source_text: “The things is well enough in their way,” observed Mr. Sikes: a little soothed as he glanced over the table; “but what have you got to say for yourself, why you should leave me here, down in the mouth, health, blunt, and everything else; and take no more notice of me, all this mortal time, than if I was that ’ere dog.—Drive him down, Charley!”

text: “Wouldn’t that be a fine sight—stopping at the first tavern just outside town, so Sowerberry, if he comes chasing after us, can stick his old nose in and have us hauled back in a cart wearing handcuffs,” said Mr. Claypole mockingly. “No! I’ll go lose myself in the tightest alleys I can hunt down, and won’t halt till we reach the most tucked-away hovel I can lay eyes on. Lord, you can thank your lucky stars I’ve got a brain; for if we hadn’t deliberately taken the wrong road at the start and cut back across the fields, you’d have been locked up tight a week ago, my lady. And it would have served you right for being such an idiot.”
source_text: “A pretty thing it would be, wouldn’t it to go and stop at the very first public-house outside the town, so that Sowerberry, if he come up after us, might poke in his old nose, and have us taken back in a cart with handcuffs on,” said Mr. Claypole in a jeering tone. “No! I shall go and lose myself among the narrowest streets I can find, and not stop till we come to the very out-of-the-wayest house I can set eyes on. Cod, yer may thanks yer stars I’ve got a head; for if we hadn’t gone, at first, the wrong road a purpose, and come back across country, yer’d have been locked up hard and fast a week ago, my lady. And serve yer right for being a fool.”

text: “You should have heard the crowd moan,” said Chitling; “the constables battled like demons, or they’d have ripped him away. He fell once, but they formed a circle around him and forced a path through. You should have seen how he stared around, all caked in mud and bleeding, and held onto them as though they were his closest companions. I can still see them now, barely able to stand upright from the crush of the mob, hauling him along in their midst; I can see the people leaping up, one after another, baring their teeth and lunging at him; I can see the blood matting his hair and beard, and hear the shrieks with which the women pushed themselves into the heart of the throng at the street corner, swearing they’d rip his heart out!”
source_text: “You should have heard the people groan,” said Chitling; “the officers fought like devils, or they’d have torn him away. He was down once, but they made a ring round him, and fought their way along. You should have seen how he looked about him, all muddy and bleeding, and clung to them as if they were his dearest friends. I can see ’em now, not able to stand upright with the pressing of the mob, and draggin him along amongst ’em; I can see the people jumping up, one behind another, and snarling with their teeth and making at him; I can see the blood upon his hair and beard, and hear the cries with which the women worked themselves into the centre of the crowd at the street corner, and swore they’d tear his heart out!”

text: The old gentleman’s rigid principles, which Oliver had now witnessed firsthand, only deepened the boy’s eagerness to be kept in constant use. When the Dodger or Charley Bates slunk back at nightfall with nothing to show for the day, their master would launch into a fervent sermon on the wretchedness of sloth and shiftlessness, and would drive the lesson home by dispatching them to their beds without a morsel of supper. Once, indeed, he even thrust them both headlong down a staircase—an application of his moral teachings that went somewhat beyond his customary severity.
source_text: Oliver was rendered the more anxious to be actively employed, by what he had seen of the stern morality of the old gentleman’s character. Whenever the Dodger or Charley Bates came home at night, empty-handed, he would expatiate with great vehemence on the misery of idle and lazy habits; and would enforce upon them the necessity of an active life, by sending them supperless to bed. On one occasion, indeed, he even went so far as to knock them both down a flight of stairs; but this was carrying out his virtuous precepts to an unusual extent.

text: Her years had been wasted in the city’s alleys, amid the foulest taverns and rookeries of the capital, yet a remnant of her first womanly spirit still clung to her; and at the sound of a soft tread nearing the door across from the one she had come through, as she pictured the vast gulf that would soon fill that cramped chamber, a weight of profound disgrace settled upon her, and she drew back as if she could hardly endure the nearness of the one she had come to meet.
source_text: The girl’s life had been squandered in the streets, and among the most noisome of the stews and dens of London, but there was something of the woman’s original nature left in her still; and when she heard a light step approaching the door opposite to that by which she had entered, and thought of the wide contrast which the small room would in another moment contain, she felt burdened with the sense of her own deep shame, and shrunk as though she could scarcely bear the presence of her with whom she had sought this interview.

text: “Right then! I’ll step up,” returned the Trickster, smoothing his cap with a sweep of his knuckles. “Aye! (to the Magistrates) spare your anxious stares; I’ll grant you not a scrap of pity, not a farthing’s worth. _You_ shall answer for this, my grand gentlemen. I’d not trade places with you for a purse! I’d not walk free, even if you dropped to your bended knees and begged it of me. Come, haul me to the gaol! Bear me hence!”
source_text: “Oh ah! I’ll come on,” replied the Dodger, brushing his hat with the palm of his hand. “Ah! (to the Bench) it’s no use your looking frightened; I won’t show you no mercy, not a ha’porth of it. _You’ll_ pay for this, my fine fellers. I wouldn’t be you for something! I wouldn’t go free, now, if you was to fall down on your knees and ask me. Here, carry me off to prison! Take me away!”

text: The young woman paced back and forth in short, uneasy strides—all the while under the keen scrutiny of the one who watched from the shadows—as the great bell of St. Paul’s rang out, mourning the passing of yet another day. Midnight had descended upon the teeming metropolis. The royal court, the underground tavern, the prison, the asylum: the rooms where lives began and ended, where vigor met decay, the stiffened mask of the dead and the untroubled slumber of the infant: midnight covered them all.
source_text: The girl had taken a few restless turns to and fro—closely watched meanwhile by her hidden observer—when the heavy bell of St. Paul’s tolled for the death of another day. Midnight had come upon the crowded city. The palace, the night-cellar, the jail, the madhouse: the chambers of birth and death, of health and sickness, the rigid face of the corpse and the calm sleep of the child: midnight was upon them all.

text: Time crept by so slowly in that desolate spot, and the spy burned so fiercely to uncover the purpose behind a meeting so unlike anything he had been primed to anticipate, that more than once he abandoned hope, convincing himself that they had either halted far upstream or chosen some wholly different place for their secret exchange. He was just about to slip from his concealment and climb back to the road above when the tread of feet reached him, followed at once by voices murmuring almost against his ear.
source_text: So tardily stole the time in this lonely place, and so eager was the spy to penetrate the motives of an interview so different from what he had been led to expect, that he more than once gave the matter up for lost, and persuaded himself, either that they had stopped far above, or had resorted to some entirely different spot to hold their mysterious conversation. He was on the point of emerging from his hiding-place, and regaining the road above, when he heard the sound of footsteps, and directly afterwards of voices almost close at his ear.

text: “Should this place bring him torment,” she reflected, “what torment it shall bring me! Yet maybe he will stay away; he might send a letter, or arrive and carefully avoid my presence—as he did upon his departure. I scarcely believed he would; yet it proved kinder for the pair of us.” And here Iris let the quill fall, and averted her face, as if even the sheet destined to carry her words must not witness her tears.
source_text: “If it be painful to him,” she thought, “to come back here, how painful it will be to me! But perhaps he will not come; he may write, or he may come himself, and studiously abstain from meeting me—he did when he went away. I hardly thought he would; but it was better for us both.” And here Rose dropped the pen, and turned away, as though the very paper which was to be her messenger should not see her weep.

text: “My dear son,” Mrs. Maylie said, resting her hand on his shoulder, “I believe youth is full of generous impulses that fade, and some, once indulged, grow all the more fleeting. Above all,” she continued, her gaze steady on his face, “I believe that when an ardent, ambitious man marries a woman whose name carries a stain—one born of no fault of hers, yet one that cold and sordid people will hold against her, and against his children too, and which, precisely as his worldly success rises, will be thrown in his teeth and made a sneer against him—he may, however generous and good his nature, one day repent the bond he formed in early life. And she may suffer the pain of knowing that he does.”
source_text: “I think, my dear son,” returned Mrs. Maylie, laying her hand upon his shoulder, “that youth has many generous impulses which do not last; and that among them are some, which, being gratified, become only the more fleeting. Above all, I think” said the lady, fixing her eyes on her son’s face, “that if an enthusiastic, ardent, and ambitious man marry a wife on whose name there is a stain, which, though it originate in no fault of hers, may be visited by cold and sordid people upon her, and upon his children also: and, in exact proportion to his success in the world, be cast in his teeth, and made the subject of sneers against him: he may, no matter how generous and good his nature, one day repent of the connection he formed in early life. And she may have the pain of knowing that he does so.”

text: Whether Noah Claypole—a young man whose greed was remarkably wide-ranging—would have agreed even to such tempting conditions had he been entirely free to choose is highly uncertain. But remembering that, if he declined, his new companion could at once hand him over to the authorities (and stranger things had already happened), he slowly softened and said he believed the arrangement would do.
source_text: Whether Noah Claypole, whose rapacity was none of the least comprehensive, would have acceded even to these glowing terms, had he been a perfectly free agent, is very doubtful; but as he recollected that, in the event of his refusal, it was in the power of his new acquaintance to give him up to justice immediately (and more unlikely things had come to pass), he gradually relented, and said he thought that would suit him.

text: Now that darkness had fallen, the shutter was fastened shut, and a candle was lit and set on the table. The dreadful occurrences of the past two days had left a profound mark on each of them, deepened by the peril and precariousness of their own situation. They pulled their chairs nearer to one another, flinching at every noise. Their words were few and uttered in hushed tones, and they sat as quiet and horror-bound as though the body of the slain woman rested in the adjoining chamber.
source_text: It being now dark, the shutter was closed, and a candle lighted and placed upon the table. The terrible events of the last two days had made a deep impression on all three, increased by the danger and uncertainty of their own position. They drew their chairs closer together, starting at every sound. They spoke little, and that in whispers, and were as silent and awe-stricken as if the remains of the murdered woman lay in the next room.

text: “He’s as obliging as any true believer, damn my eyes if he’s not!” said Sikes, eyeing the creature with a dark, savage satisfaction. “Now you understand what’s coming, sir, so shout all you want; the beast will put an end to that trick soon enough. Move along, boy!”
source_text: “He’s as willing as a Christian, strike me blind if he isn’t!” said Sikes, regarding the animal with a kind of grim and ferocious approval. “Now, you know what you’ve got to expect, master, so call away as quick as you like; the dog will soon stop that game. Get on, young ’un!”

text: “I’ll bring him at once,” murmured Mrs. Grimes, stepping out to do just that. By now, the outermost crust of grime that had caked Oliver’s cheeks and fingers had been scoured away—as much, at least, as a single scrubbing could strip—and his kindly guardian guided him into the chamber.
source_text: “I’ll fetch him directly,” said Mrs. Mann, leaving the room for that purpose. Oliver, having had by this time as much of the outer coat of dirt which encrusted his face and hands, removed, as could be scrubbed off in one washing, was led into the room by his benevolent protectress.

text: She moved about the table with quiet industry, tending to its small, homely duties. When she happened to glance up and found the older woman watching her, she swept back the plain braid that rested on her brow with a light, teasing gesture, and let her shining gaze fill with such fondness and unstudied grace that even gentle angels, looking on, might have been moved to smile.
source_text: She was busily engaged in the little offices of the table. Chancing to raise her eyes as the elder lady was regarding her, she playfully put back her hair, which was simply braided on her forehead; and threw into her beaming look, such an expression of affection and artless loveliness, that blessed spirits might have smiled to look upon her.

text: “—This Monk,” the girl went on, “had chanced upon him one afternoon with a pair of our lads, the very day he first slipped from us, and knew him at once for the boy he had been seeking—though the reason escaped me entirely. A compact was made with Fagin: if Oliver were recovered, a fixed price would be paid; and a larger sum promised if he could be turned into a pickpocket, which this Monk desired for some private design of his own.”
source_text: “—That Monks,” pursued the girl, “had seen him accidently with two of our boys on the day we first lost him, and had known him directly to be the same child that he was watching for, though I couldn’t make out why. A bargain was struck with Fagin, that if Oliver was got back he should have a certain sum; and he was to have more for making him a thief, which this Monks wanted for some purpose of his own.”

text: On the stage, in all well-crafted murderous melodramas, it is customary to alternate tragic and comic scenes as regularly as the red and white streaks in a side of bacon. The hero collapses onto his straw pallet, burdened by chains and calamity; in the following scene, his loyal but oblivious squire treats the audience to a comic song. With pounding hearts, we watch the heroine caught in the grip of a haughty, pitiless baron: her virtue and her life both imperiled, she draws a dagger to save one at the expense of the other. Then, just as our anticipation reaches its peak, a whistle sounds, and we are instantly whisked away to the castle’s great hall, where a grey-haired seneschal sings a jolly chorus with an even jollier band of vassals, who roam freely through every kind of place—from church crypts to palaces—and wander together, carolling without end.
source_text: It is the custom on the stage, in all good murderous melodramas, to present the tragic and the comic scenes, in as regular alternation, as the layers of red and white in a side of streaky bacon. The hero sinks upon his straw bed, weighed down by fetters and misfortunes; in the next scene, his faithful but unconscious squire regales the audience with a comic song. We behold, with throbbing bosoms, the heroine in the grasp of a proud and ruthless baron: her virtue and her life alike in danger, drawing forth her dagger to preserve the one at the cost of the other; and just as our expectations are wrought up to the highest pitch, a whistle is heard, and we are straightway transported to the great hall of the castle; where a grey-headed seneschal sings a funny chorus with a funnier body of vassals, who are free of all sorts of places, from church vaults to palaces, and roam about in company, carolling perpetually.

text: “My poor lad, this is more than enough heartache for a single day,” the doctor said. “More than sufficient for the two of us. If we stop at the bookstall keeper’s now, we’ll surely discover he’s died, or torched his own shop, or fled. No—straight back home!” And yielding to the doctor’s impulse, home they returned.
source_text: “My poor boy, this is disappointment enough for one day,” said the doctor. “Quite enough for both of us. If we go to the book-stall keeper’s, we shall certainly find that he is dead, or has set his house on fire, or run away. No; home again straight!” And in obedience to the doctor’s impulse, home they went.

text: The old man pressed her with further inquiries, each designed to probe whether his careless disclosures had taken root; yet she parried them with such swift composure, and remained so wholly indifferent beneath his probing gaze, that his first suspicion—that she was rather more than slightly drunk—settled into certainty. Nancy, in truth, shared a frailty common among the master’s female wards, one that in their younger days was quietly fostered rather than suppressed. Her rumpled dress, and a thick reek of gin that saturated the close room, lent weighty corroboration to the old man’s judgment; and when, after that brief storm of temper already recounted, she sank first into a sullen stupor, and then into a muddled tide of emotion—weeping one moment, and the next crying out scraps like “Keep your spirits up!” and hazarding various reckonings on what the chances might be so long as a lady or gentleman stayed cheerful—Mr. Fagin, long seasoned in such scenes, observed with deep contentment that she was thoroughly gone.
source_text: Fagin put several other questions: all with the same drift of ascertaining whether the girl had profited by his unguarded hints; but, she answered them so readily, and was withal so utterly unmoved by his searching looks, that his original impression of her being more than a trifle in liquor, was confirmed. Nancy, indeed, was not exempt from a failing which was very common among the Jew’s female pupils; and in which, in their tenderer years, they were rather encouraged than checked. Her disordered appearance, and a wholesale perfume of Geneva which pervaded the apartment, afforded strong confirmatory evidence of the justice of the Jew’s supposition; and when, after indulging in the temporary display of violence above described, she subsided, first into dullness, and afterwards into a compound of feelings: under the influence of which she shed tears one minute, and in the next gave utterance to various exclamations of “Never say die!” and divers calculations as to what might be the amount of the odds so long as a lady or gentleman was happy, Mr. Fagin, who had had considerable experience of such matters in his time, saw, with great satisfaction, that she was very far gone indeed.

text: Mr. Sikes hauled Oliver along, shoving a path through the densest part of the throng, and paid scant regard to the many spectacles and noises that held the boy in such wonder. He gave a brief nod now and then to acquaintances who passed by and, declining several offers to stop for a morning drink, pushed on without pause until they broke free of the chaos and emerged from Hosier Lane into Holborn.
source_text: Mr. Sikes, dragging Oliver after him, elbowed his way through the thickest of the crowd, and bestowed very little attention on the numerous sights and sounds, which so astonished the boy. He nodded, twice or thrice, to a passing friend; and, resisting as many invitations to take a morning dram, pressed steadily onward, until they were clear of the turmoil, and had made their way through Hosier Lane into Holborn.

text: It was exactly so. Yet, to be fair to Mr. Claypole, he was never one to place blind, reckless trust in another. He had confided in Charlotte only so far as to ensure that, should they be overtaken, the money would be discovered on her person. This would allow him to protest his ignorance of any robbery and greatly improve his odds of slipping away. Naturally, he offered no account of his reasoning at that moment, and the pair continued on their way in perfect amity.
source_text: This was indeed the case; but as it was not Mr. Claypole’s habit to repose a blind and foolish confidence in anybody, it should be observed, in justice to that gentleman, that he had trusted Charlotte to this extent, in order that, if they were pursued, the money might be found on her: which would leave him an opportunity of asserting his innocence of any theft, and would greatly facilitate his chances of escape. Of course, he entered at this juncture, into no explanation of his motives, and they walked on very lovingly together.

text: Through a barrage of disruptions and fresh affronts, Mr. Brownlow managed to lay out his account, noting how, startled by the suddenness of it all, he had pursued the lad simply because he had spotted him fleeing. He voiced his wish that, should the magistrate judge the boy not to be the actual culprit but still linked to the thieves, he might treat him with all the clemency the law would permit.
source_text: With many interruptions, and repeated insults, Mr. Brownlow contrived to state his case; observing that, in the surprise of the moment, he had run after the boy because he had seen him running away; and expressing his hope that, if the magistrate should believe him, although not actually the thief, to be connected with the thieves, he would deal as leniently with him as justice would allow.

text: Where Snow Hill and Holborn Hill converge, there branches off—on the right as one departs the City—a cramped and cheerless passage that descends toward Saffron Hill. Within its grubby shopfronts, vast bundles of second-hand silk kerchiefs, in every dimension and design, are laid out for purchase; for here dwell the dealers who acquire them from pickpockets. Scores of these kerchiefs sway from hooks beyond the casements or flutter against the doorframes, while the inner shelves brim with them. Cramped though Field Lane may be, it boasts its own barber, its coffee-house, its alehouse, and its fried-fish cellar. It is a self-contained mart of commerce: the capital of petty theft: frequented at first light and at the fall of dusk by mute traffickers, who barter in shadowed back-rooms and depart as mysteriously as they arrive. Here, the costermonger, the shoe-patcher, and the rag-broker spread their wares like signposts for the small-time thief; here, stockpiles of rusted iron and bones, and mounds of mouldering woollen scraps and linen, moulder and decay in soot-blackened vaults.
source_text: Near to the spot on which Snow Hill and Holborn Hill meet, opens, upon the right hand as you come out of the City, a narrow and dismal alley, leading to Saffron Hill. In its filthy shops are exposed for sale huge bunches of second-hand silk handkerchiefs, of all sizes and patterns; for here reside the traders who purchase them from pick-pockets. Hundreds of these handkerchiefs hang dangling from pegs outside the windows or flaunting from the door-posts; and the shelves, within, are piled with them. Confined as the limits of Field Lane are, it has its barber, its coffee-shop, its beer-shop, and its fried-fish warehouse. It is a commercial colony of itself: the emporium of petty larceny: visited at early morning, and setting-in of dusk, by silent merchants, who traffic in dark back-parlours, and who go as strangely as they come. Here, the clothesman, the shoe-vamper, and the rag-merchant, display their goods, as sign-boards to the petty thief; here, stores of old iron and bones, and heaps of mildewy fragments of woollen-stuff and linen, rust and rot in the grimy cellars.

text: She seized the hand that Oliver had reached toward her without thought, extinguished the lamp, and led him up the staircase into the night. A figure hidden in the blackness unlatched the entrance swiftly, and the moment they stepped through, it shut fast again. A hired cab stood ready; with the same fierce urgency she had shown in speaking to Oliver, she hauled him inside beside her and yanked the blinds shut. The coachman needed no word, but whipped his horse to a gallop at once.
source_text: She caught the hand which Oliver instinctively placed in hers, and, blowing out the light, drew him after her up the stairs. The door was opened, quickly, by some one shrouded in the darkness, and was as quickly closed, when they had passed out. A hackney-cabriolet was in waiting; with the same vehemence which she had exhibited in addressing Oliver, the girl pulled him in with her, and drew the curtains close. The driver wanted no directions, but lashed his horse into full speed, without the delay of an instant.

text: “They’ve caught the gentleman who owns the snuffbox; two or three more are on their way to identify him; and the Artful’s got his ticket for transportation,” answered Master Bates. “I’ll need a complete set of mourning clothes, Fagin, and a black hatband, to visit him in before he starts on his journey. To imagine Jack Dawkins—clever Jack—the Dodger—the Artful Dodger—being sent overseas for a worthless little snuffbox! I never believed he’d stoop to anything less than a gold watch, chain, and seals at the very least. Oh, why didn’t he rob some wealthy old gentleman of all his valuables, and depart like a gentleman, instead of like a common thief, with no honor or glory!”
source_text: “They’ve found the gentleman as owns the box; two or three more’s a coming to ’dentify him; and the Artful’s booked for a passage out,” replied Master Bates. “I must have a full suit of mourning, Fagin, and a hatband, to wisit him in, afore he sets out upon his travels. To think of Jack Dawkins—lummy Jack—the Dodger—the Artful Dodger—going abroad for a common twopenny-halfpenny sneeze-box! I never thought he’d a done it under a gold watch, chain, and seals, at the lowest. Oh, why didn’t he rob some rich old gentleman of all his walables, and go out as a gentleman, and not like a common prig, without no honour nor glory!”

text: “Because they serve me no purpose, my darling,” the old man answered, somewhat flustered, “not worth the trouble. Their very faces betray them the moment they’re caught, and I lose every one. With this lad, handled right, my dears, I might manage what twenty others could never yield. What’s more,” he went on, steadying himself, “he’s got us in his grip now, if only he could slip free again; so he must share our fate. How he came to it matters nothing; it’s enough for my hold on him that he took part in a theft—that’s all I require. And see how much cleaner this is than having to dispose of the poor little fellow—which would be perilous, and cost us into the bargain.”
source_text: “Because they’re of no use to me, my dear,” replied the Jew, with some confusion, “not worth the taking. Their looks convict ’em when they get into trouble, and I lose ’em all. With this boy, properly managed, my dears, I could do what I couldn’t with twenty of them. Besides,” said the Jew, recovering his self-possession, “he has us now if he could only give us leg-bail again; and he must be in the same boat with us. Never mind how he came there; it’s quite enough for my power over him that he was in a robbery; that’s all I want. Now, how much better this is, than being obliged to put the poor leetle boy out of the way—which would be dangerous, and we should lose by it besides.”

text: At the center of that huddle of dwellings, right where the river lapped against the sagging upper floors, rose a sprawling edifice once given over to some forgotten trade. In its prime, it must have offered work to the folk crammed into the neighboring shacks. But decay had claimed it years ago. Vermin, rot, and the seep of moisture had gnawed through the timbers that held it aloft; a great slice of the structure had already slumped into the current, while what remained—swaying and stooping above the black water—seemed merely to bide its time before plunging after its vanished twin and sharing the same grave.
source_text: In the heart of this cluster of huts; and skirting the river, which its upper stories overhung; stood a large building, formerly used as a manufactory of some kind. It had, in its day, probably furnished employment to the inhabitants of the surrounding tenements. But it had long since gone to ruin. The rat, the worm, and the action of the damp, had weakened and rotted the piles on which it stood; and a considerable portion of the building had already sunk down into the water; while the remainder, tottering and bending over the dark stream, seemed to wait a favourable opportunity of following its old companion, and involving itself in the same fate.

text: When Noah realized that the news he had delivered had utterly stunned Mr. Bumble, he heightened the impact by lamenting his terrible injuries with tenfold the volume; and upon spotting a gentleman in a white waistcoat crossing the yard, he made his cries more theatrical than ever, correctly judging it essential to capture the attention and stir the outrage of that very gentleman.
source_text: When Noah saw that the intelligence he communicated perfectly paralysed Mr. Bumble, he imparted additional effect thereunto, by bewailing his dreadful wounds ten times louder than before; and when he observed a gentleman in a white waistcoat crossing the yard, he was more tragic in his lamentations than ever: rightly conceiving it highly expedient to attract the notice, and rouse the indignation, of the gentleman aforesaid.

text: Giles stumbled along as best he could, with Oliver trailing behind; and within a minute or two, Mr. Losberne, who had been strolling abroad and had only just come back, pitched over the hedge in their wake, scrambled to his feet with surprising nimbleness, and set off along the same track at a pace not to be scoffed at, bellowing all the while, at the top of his lungs, to discover what on earth had happened.
source_text: Giles followed as well as he could; and Oliver followed too; and in the course of a minute or two, Mr. Losberne, who had been out walking, and just then returned, tumbled over the hedge after them, and picking himself up with more agility than he could have been supposed to possess, struck into the same course at no contemptible speed, shouting all the while, most prodigiously, to know what was the matter.

text: These steps belong to the bridge and are arranged in three sections. Just beneath the end of the second flight, as you descend, the stone wall on the left finishes in a decorative pilaster facing the Thames. Here the lower steps broaden, so that anyone rounding that corner of the wall is automatically hidden from others on the stairs who might be above him, even by a single step. When the countryman reached this spot, he glanced quickly around; seeing no better place to hide and, since the tide was out, plenty of space, he edged aside, pressed his back against the pilaster, and waited. He felt fairly sure they would not descend any further, and that even if he couldn't catch their words, he could safely trail them again afterward.
source_text: These stairs are a part of the bridge; they consist of three flights. Just below the end of the second, going down, the stone wall on the left terminates in an ornamental pilaster facing towards the Thames. At this point the lower steps widen: so that a person turning that angle of the wall, is necessarily unseen by any others on the stairs who chance to be above him, if only a step. The countryman looked hastily round, when he reached this point; and as there seemed no better place of concealment, and, the tide being out, there was plenty of room, he slipped aside, with his back to the pilaster, and there waited: pretty certain that they would come no lower, and that even if he could not hear what was said, he could follow them again, with safety.

text: “On my word,” returned Sir Alden. “Those men are secured by my bond. Should you protest the loss of your freedom—you possessed both the means and the moment to reclaim it during our passage, yet you chose to hold your peace—I urge you once more, cast yourself upon the law for shelter. I too shall invoke the law; but once you have stepped beyond retreat, do not come begging at my door for mercy, when authority shall rest in other hands; and do not charge that I hurled you into the abyss you leapt into, of your own accord.”
source_text: “By mine,” replied Mr. Brownlow. “Those persons are indemnified by me. If you complain of being deprived of your liberty—you had power and opportunity to retrieve it as you came along, but you deemed it advisable to remain quiet—I say again, throw yourself for protection on the law. I will appeal to the law too; but when you have gone too far to recede, do not sue to me for leniency, when the power will have passed into other hands; and do not say I plunged you down the gulf into which you rushed, yourself.”

text: No one could have imagined that such a mode of cultivation would yield a thriving or abundant harvest. On the morning of his ninth birthday, Oliver Twist was a pallid, slender boy, short for his age and alarmingly narrow around the middle. Still, either through birth or some hidden bequest, a tenacious spirit had taken root inside him.
