It had been granted ample space to grow, owing to the meager rations of the workhouse, and it may well have been this very fact that allowed him to reach a ninth birthday at all. Whatever the truth of it, that day was indeed his ninth birthday, and he was spending it in the coal-cellar alongside a chosen company of two other boys, who, after sharing a vigorous beating with him, had been shut away for the outrageous crime of daring to feel hunger. Just then, Mrs. Mann, the household’s kindly matron, was suddenly alarmed by the sight of Mr. Bumble the beadle, struggling to unfasten the latch of the garden gate.
source_text: It cannot be expected that this system of farming would produce any very extraordinary or luxuriant crop. Oliver Twist’s ninth birthday found him a pale thin child, somewhat diminutive in stature, and decidedly small in circumference. But nature or inheritance had implanted a good sturdy spirit in Oliver’s breast. It had had plenty of room to expand, thanks to the spare diet of the establishment; and perhaps to this circumstance may be attributed his having any ninth birth-day at all. Be this as it may, however, it _was_ his ninth birthday; and he was keeping it in the coal-cellar with a select party of two other young gentleman, who, after participating with him in a sound thrashing, had been locked up for atrociously presuming to be hungry, when Mrs. Mann, the good lady of the house, was unexpectedly startled by the apparition of Mr. Bumble, the beadle, striving to undo the wicket of the garden-gate.

text: Little by little, he sank into that profound and quiet slumber that only the release from fresh agony can bestow—a stillness so serene, a repose so complete, that to stir from it felt like a wound. If this were dying, who would wish to be called back into the strife and clamor of existence—to the frets of the present hour, the dread of what is yet to come, and, heavier than all else, the exhausted memory of what has been?
source_text: Gradually, he fell into that deep tranquil sleep which ease from recent suffering alone imparts; that calm and peaceful rest which it is pain to wake from. Who, if this were death, would be roused again to all the struggles and turmoils of life; to all its cares for the present; its anxieties for the future; more than all, its weary recollections of the past!

text: “That’s because they wetted the straw before setting it alight in the flue, so the lads would scramble back down,” said Marden. “All smoke and no flame, you see; and smoke’s no earthly good for fetching a boy out, it only lulls him off, which is just what pleases him. Boys are dreadful stubborn, gentlemen, and dreadful idle, and nothing serves like a fierce, roaring fire to bring them down at a dash. It’s merciful besides, gentlemen, for if they’ve got wedged in the flue, scorching their soles makes them fight to free themselves.”
source_text: “That’s acause they damped the straw afore they lit it in the chimbley to make ’em come down again,” said Gamfield; “that’s all smoke, and no blaze; vereas smoke ain’t o’ no use at all in making a boy come down, for it only sinds him to sleep, and that’s wot he likes. Boys is wery obstinit, and wery lazy, Gen’l’men, and there’s nothink like a good hot blaze to make ’em come down vith a run. It’s humane too, gen’l’men, acause, even if they’ve stuck in the chimbley, roasting their feet makes ’em struggle to hextricate theirselves.”

text: “That’s the nature of the trade,” returned Silas; “and the household shares the same bent. You’ve struck the very core of the matter, and no bolt-hole in the city could hold you tighter. Not a corner in all this warren breathes more secure than the Lame Fox—when I choose to weave it so. And I’ve grown partial to you and the girl; so the word’s been given, and you may set your hearts at rest.”
source_text: “In that way of business,” rejoined Fagin; “and so are the people of the house. You’ve hit the right nail upon the head, and are as safe here as you could be. There is not a safer place in all this town than is the Cripples; that is, when I like to make it so. And I have taken a fancy to you and the young woman; so I’ve said the word, and you may make your minds easy.”

text: “Jem Spyers,” the officer went on, “stayed quiet for quite a stretch, taking it all in without looking like he was, which proved he knew his trade. Then one morning he strolled into the bar, pulled out his snuffbox, and said, ‘Chickweed, I’ve discovered who pulled off this robbery.’ ‘You have?’ cried Chickweed. ‘Oh, my dear Spyers, just grant me revenge, and I can die happy! Oh, my dear Spyers, where’s the scoundrel?’ ‘Now, now,’ said Spyers, holding out a pinch of snuff, ‘drop the act! You robbed yourself.’ And so he had—and pocketed a tidy sum from it, too. No one would ever have tumbled to it if he hadn’t been so desperately keen to keep up the show!” Mr. Blathers set down his wine-glass and rattled the handcuffs together.
source_text: “Jem Spyers,” resumed the officer, “for a long time said nothing at all, and listened to everything without seeming to, which showed he understood his business. But, one morning, he walked into the bar, and taking out his snuffbox, says ‘Chickweed, I’ve found out who done this here robbery.’ ‘Have you?’ said Chickweed. ‘Oh, my dear Spyers, only let me have wengeance, and I shall die contented! Oh, my dear Spyers, where is the villain!’ ‘Come!’ said Spyers, offering him a pinch of snuff, ‘none of that gammon! You did it yourself.’ So he had; and a good bit of money he had made by it, too; and nobody would never have found it out, if he hadn’t been so precious anxious to keep up appearances!” said Mr. Blathers, putting down his wine-glass, and clinking the handcuffs together.

text: Beneath the tavern’s main counter lay a snug rear chamber, sunk a few treads lower, so that any servant of the establishment, sliding aside a tiny drape that masked a lone square of glass set into the adjoining wall some five feet above the floor, might peer down upon the occupants of that hidden room with scant risk of detection—the pane being nestled in a shadowed recess, where the watcher had to wedge himself between the glass and a stout vertical post—and, by pressing an ear to the thin dividing wall, catch the drift of their talk with fair precision. The innkeeper had kept his gaze fixed to that spy-hole not five minutes, and Barney had scarce slipped back from delivering the message just recounted, when Fagin, pursuing his nocturnal rounds, stepped into the taproom to ask after certain of his youthful charges.
source_text: Now, this back-room was immediately behind the bar, and some steps lower, so that any person connected with the house, undrawing a small curtain which concealed a single pane of glass fixed in the wall of the last-named apartment, about five feet from its flooring, could not only look down upon any guests in the back-room without any great hazard of being observed (the glass being in a dark angle of the wall, between which and a large upright beam the observer had to thrust himself), but could, by applying his ear to the partition, ascertain with tolerable distinctness, their subject of conversation. The landlord of the house had not withdrawn his eye from this place of espial for five minutes, and Barney had only just returned from making the communication above related, when Fagin, in the course of his evening’s business, came into the bar to inquire after some of his young pupils.

text: Oliver obeyed the old lady’s instructions, and though she fretted aloud that there was no time to crimp the narrow frill edging his collar, he appeared so fine and delicate even without that embellishment that she surveyed him from head to toe with immense satisfaction, declaring she truly doubted whether, given far more time, anyone could have improved him in the slightest.
source_text: Oliver did as the old lady bade him; and, although she lamented grievously, meanwhile, that there was not even time to crimp the little frill that bordered his shirt-collar; he looked so delicate and handsome, despite that important personal advantage, that she went so far as to say: looking at him with great complacency from head to foot, that she really didn’t think it would have been possible, on the longest notice, to have made much difference in him for the better.

text: Within a fine apartment, whose appointments suggested the comfort of an earlier age rather than the polish of the present, two ladies were seated at a generously laid breakfast-table. Mr. Ashworth, attired with fastidious exactness in a complete suit of black, stood ready to serve them. He had positioned himself roughly midway between the sideboard and the table, and, with his frame stretched to its utmost stature, his chin tilted slightly upward and canted a hair to the left, his right foot set forward, and his right hand tucked into his waistcoat while his left dangled at his side clutching a salver, he presented the very image of a man basking in the warm conviction of his own worth and consequence.
source_text: In a handsome room: though its furniture had rather the air of old-fashioned comfort, than of modern elegance: there sat two ladies at a well-spread breakfast-table. Mr. Giles, dressed with scrupulous care in a full suit of black, was in attendance upon them. He had taken his station some half-way between the side-board and the breakfast-table; and, with his body drawn up to its full height, his head thrown back, and inclined the merest trifle on one side, his left leg advanced, and his right hand thrust into his waist-coat, while his left hung down by his side, grasping a waiter, looked like one who laboured under a very agreeable sense of his own merits and importance.

text: When the coffee had finished brewing, the Jew slid the pot over to the stovetop’s edge. He lingered there a moment, poised uncertainly, as though unsure what to do with himself. Then he swung around, fixed his gaze on Oliver, and called out to him. The boy gave no reply, seeming, by every indication, to be fast asleep.
source_text: When the coffee was done, the Jew drew the saucepan to the hob. Standing, then in an irresolute attitude for a few minutes, as if he did not well know how to employ himself, he turned round and looked at Oliver, and called him by his name. He did not answer, and was to all appearances asleep.

text: A tall, dark man sat there, draped in a heavy cloak. He carried the unmistakable look of an outsider, his features drawn with weariness and his clothes stained with the dust of a long journey. As Bumble stepped in, the stranger glanced at him sidelong, barely offering a nod in return for his greeting.
source_text: The man who was seated there, was tall and dark, and wore a large cloak. He had the air of a stranger; and seemed, by a certain haggardness in his look, as well as by the dusty soils on his dress, to have travelled some distance. He eyed Bumble askance, as he entered, but scarcely deigned to nod his head in acknowledgment of his salutation.

text: As they drew near the town and finally threaded through its cramped lanes, keeping the boy in check became a considerable challenge. Sowerberry's undertaking establishment stood just as it always had, though now it struck him as shrunken and less grand than his memory had painted it—every familiar shop and dwelling lined the way, nearly each one tied to some small memory of his—Gamfield's wagon, the exact one he once drove, sat outside the old tavern door—the workhouse loomed, that grim jail of his childhood, its cheerless windows scowling down upon the road—the same gaunt gatekeeper lingered at the entrance, and at the sight Oliver instinctively recoiled, then mocked his own foolishness, then wept, then laughed once more—clusters of faces he knew intimately appeared at doorways and casements—almost everything remained as though he had departed only the day before, and all his recent existence had been nothing but a blissful illusion.
source_text: As they approached the town, and at length drove through its narrow streets, it became matter of no small difficulty to restrain the boy within reasonable bounds. There was Sowerberry’s the undertaker’s just as it used to be, only smaller and less imposing in appearance than he remembered it—there were all the well-known shops and houses, with almost every one of which he had some slight incident connected—there was Gamfield’s cart, the very cart he used to have, standing at the old public-house door—there was the workhouse, the dreary prison of his youthful days, with its dismal windows frowning on the street—there was the same lean porter standing at the gate, at sight of whom Oliver involuntarily shrunk back, and then laughed at himself for being so foolish, then cried, then laughed again—there were scores of faces at the doors and windows that he knew quite well—there was nearly everything as if he had left it but yesterday, and all his recent life had been but a happy dream.

text: “You shall be gentle and tender toward him, for you are so with all souls,” Oliver murmured. “It will wring your heart, I am certain, to listen to the tale he bears; yet do not grieve, do not grieve, for it will soon pass, and your laughter will return—I am sure of that as well—when you reflect how utterly he is altered; you showed me the same grace once. He whispered ‘Heaven keep you’ as I fled,” the child exclaimed, his voice breaking with a surge of tender feeling; “and now I shall answer ‘Heaven keep you,’ and let him see how dearly I cherish him for it!”
source_text: “You will be kind and good to him, for you are to every one,” said Oliver. “It will make you cry, I know, to hear what he can tell; but never mind, never mind, it will be all over, and you will smile again—I know that too—to think how changed he is; you did the same with me. He said ‘God bless you’ to me when I ran away,” cried the boy with a burst of affectionate emotion; “and I will say ‘God bless you’ now, and show him how I love him for it!”

text: After Rose and Oliver were left to share their thoughts freely, Mr. Brownlow went ahead into another room. There, Rose gave him a complete account of her meeting with Nancy, which left him deeply surprised and puzzled. She also told him why she had not at first trusted her friend Mr. Losberne with the matter. The old man judged that she had behaved wisely and promptly agreed to have a serious talk with the good doctor himself. To give him an early chance to carry out this plan, they decided he would visit the hotel at eight that evening, and in the meanwhile Mrs. Maylie would be carefully told everything that had happened. Once these details were settled, Rose and Oliver went home.
source_text: Leaving her and Oliver to compare notes at leisure, Mr. Brownlow led the way into another room; and there, heard from Rose a full narration of her interview with Nancy, which occasioned him no little surprise and perplexity. Rose also explained her reasons for not confiding in her friend Mr. Losberne in the first instance. The old gentleman considered that she had acted prudently, and readily undertook to hold solemn conference with the worthy doctor himself. To afford him an early opportunity for the execution of this design, it was arranged that he should call at the hotel at eight o’clock that evening, and that in the meantime Mrs. Maylie should be cautiously informed of all that had occurred. These preliminaries adjusted, Rose and Oliver returned home.

text: “He was stepping out of a carriage,” Oliver said, tears of joy streaming down his face, “and entering a house. I didn’t speak to him—I couldn’t, because he never noticed me, and I was shaking so badly I couldn’t approach him. But Giles asked, on my behalf, whether he lived there, and they said yes. Look,” Oliver continued, unfolding a scrap of paper, “here it is—this is where he lives. I’m going there at once! Oh, goodness, goodness! What will I do when I finally see him and hear his voice again!”
source_text: “Getting out of a coach,” replied Oliver, shedding tears of delight, “and going into a house. I didn’t speak to him—I couldn’t speak to him, for he didn’t see me, and I trembled so, that I was not able to go up to him. But Giles asked, for me, whether he lived there, and they said he did. Look here,” said Oliver, opening a scrap of paper, “here it is; here’s where he lives—I’m going there directly! Oh, dear me, dear me! What shall I do when I come to see him and hear him speak again!”

text: Dr. Ashworth and the boy kept to the far corner of the drawing room while the rapid exchange unfolded. Presently, the physician extended his hand to Julian Marchwood, and they greeted one another with warm, earnest claspings. In answer to the young gentleman’s many inquiries, the doctor then delivered a careful summary of the invalid’s condition—every word as hopeful and reassuring as the boy’s own account had led him to anticipate. And all the while, Mr. Pemble, who made a great show of arranging the baggage, drank in the report with ravenous attention.
source_text: Mr. Losberne and Oliver had remained at another end of the apartment while this hurried conversation was proceeding. The former now held out his hand to Harry Maylie; and hearty salutations were exchanged between them. The doctor then communicated, in reply to multifarious questions from his young friend, a precise account of his patient’s situation; which was quite as consolatory and full of promise, as Oliver’s statement had encouraged him to hope; and to the whole of which, Mr. Giles, who affected to be busy about the luggage, listened with greedy ears.

text: “I could,” said Rose. “Wait,” she continued, pulling her hand free, “why drag out this painful conversation? It hurts me deeply, yet it will bring lasting joy all the same—for it will be a joy to know I once held the place in your esteem that I do now, and every success you achieve in life will fill me with fresh strength and resolve. Goodbye, Harry! As we have met today, we will not meet again; but in bonds different from those this talk has created, we may remain long and happily connected; and may every blessing that the prayers of a sincere, devoted heart can summon from the source of all truth and honesty bring you comfort and success!”
source_text: “I could,” said Rose. “Stay!” she added, disengaging her hand, “why should we prolong this painful interview? Most painful to me, and yet productive of lasting happiness, notwithstanding; for it _will_ be happiness to know that I once held the high place in your regard which I now occupy, and every triumph you achieve in life will animate me with new fortitude and firmness. Farewell, Harry! As we have met today, we meet no more; but in other relations than those in which this conversation have placed us, we may be long and happily entwined; and may every blessing that the prayers of a true and earnest heart can call down from the source of all truth and sincerity, cheer and prosper you!”

text: They veered left just beyond the tavern, then took a road to the right and trudged onward for a great while, skirting spacious gardens and fine residences on either side, pausing only for a quick beer until they came to a town. There, on a house wall, Oliver noticed the name “Hampton” painted in fairly large script. They loitered in the meadows for several hours. Eventually they returned to the town, ducked into a weathered inn with a faded sign, and asked for a meal by the kitchen hearth.
source_text: They turned round to the left, a short way past the public-house; and then, taking a right-hand road, walked on for a long time: passing many large gardens and gentlemen’s houses on both sides of the way, and stopping for nothing but a little beer, until they reached a town. Here against the wall of a house, Oliver saw written up in pretty large letters, “Hampton.” They lingered about, in the fields, for some hours. At length they came back into the town; and, turning into an old public-house with a defaced sign-board, ordered some dinner by the kitchen fire.

text: Weakened by recent sickness, dazed by the sudden blows and the shock of the assault, terrified by the dog’s savage snarling and the man’s brute force, and crushed by the onlookers’ certainty that he was every bit the callous little villain they named him—what could a single helpless child do? Night had fallen; the district was squalid; no aid stood near; struggle was futile. In a heartbeat he was hauled into a maze of black, cramped passageways and driven through them at a speed that made the few cries he managed to let out come forth garbled. It scarcely mattered whether they were clear or not, for there was no one to heed them even had they been perfectly plain.
source_text: Weak with recent illness; stupified by the blows and the suddenness of the attack; terrified by the fierce growling of the dog, and the brutality of the man; overpowered by the conviction of the bystanders that he really was the hardened little wretch he was described to be; what could one poor child do! Darkness had set in; it was a low neighborhood; no help was near; resistance was useless. In another moment he was dragged into a labyrinth of dark narrow courts, and was forced along them at a pace which rendered the few cries he dared to give utterance to, unintelligible. It was of little moment, indeed, whether they were intelligible or no; for there was nobody to care for them, had they been ever so plain.

text: Taking this cue, the lads, with a brief dip of the head to Nancy, gathered their caps and quitted the chamber; the Dodger and his sprightly companion trading, as they departed, a string of jests at young Chitling’s cost. In fairness, there was little in his bearing that stood out as especially remarkable or odd, for a good many dashing young blades about London pay a far steeper toll than Chitling merely to be glimpsed in respectable company, and a good many polished gentlemen—the very flower of that respectable company—build their fame on foundations not unlike those of the flashy Toby Crackit.
source_text: In obedience to this hint, the boys, nodding to Nancy, took up their hats, and left the room; the Dodger and his vivacious friend indulging, as they went, in many witticisms at the expense of Mr. Chitling; in whose conduct, it is but justice to say, there was nothing very conspicuous or peculiar: inasmuch as there are a great number of spirited young bloods upon town, who pay a much higher price than Mr. Chitling for being seen in good society: and a great number of fine gentlemen (composing the good society aforesaid) who established their reputation upon very much the same footing as flash Toby Crackit.

text: “No!” the girl answered. “I didn’t do this for payment. Let me hold on to that thought. Still—give me something you’ve worn: I’d love to have something—no, no, not a ring—your gloves or a handkerchief—anything I can keep, something that was yours, dear lady. There. Bless you! God bless you. Good-night, good-night!”
source_text: “No!” replied the girl. “I have not done this for money. Let me have that to think of. And yet—give me something that you have worn: I should like to have something—no, no, not a ring—your gloves or handkerchief—anything that I can keep, as having belonged to you, sweet lady. There. Bless you! God bless you. Good-night, good-night!”

text: Celia, having gathered her composure in the interval, immediately recounted, in a handful of plain phrases, everything that had happened to Edmund from the moment he departed the residence of Mr. Ashford; she kept back what Nancy had disclosed for the old man’s ear alone, and ended by declaring that his sole grief, through all those many weeks, had been his failure to encounter his onetime patron and companion.
source_text: Rose, who had had time to collect her thoughts, at once related, in a few natural words, all that had befallen Oliver since he left Mr. Brownlow’s house; reserving Nancy’s information for that gentleman’s private ear, and concluding with the assurance that his only sorrow, for some months past, had been not being able to meet with his former benefactor and friend.

text: Upon thorough and deliberate inquiry, it became clear that the remnants of the estate still held by Monks—which had never thrived under his management or his mother’s—would, if split evenly between him and Oliver, amount to little more than three thousand pounds apiece. Under the terms of their father’s will, Oliver was the rightful heir to the entire fortune; yet Mr. Brownlow, reluctant to deny the older son a chance to redeem his past misdeeds and lead an upright life, suggested this equal division, a plan the young ward accepted with gladness.
source_text: It appeared, on full and careful investigation, that if the wreck of property remaining in the custody of Monks (which had never prospered either in his hands or in those of his mother) were equally divided between himself and Oliver, it would yield, to each, little more than three thousand pounds. By the provisions of his father’s will, Oliver would have been entitled to the whole; but Mr. Brownlow, unwilling to deprive the elder son of the opportunity of retrieving his former vices and pursuing an honest career, proposed this mode of distribution, to which his young charge joyfully acceded.

text: The magistrate drew on the sable cap, and the condemned man remained fixed in that same posture and expression. From the spectators’ benches a woman let slip a cry, wrenched out by the awful gravity of the moment; he glanced upward sharply, as though vexed by the disruption, and inclined his frame still more intently forward. The allocution was measured and grave; the verdict harrowing to receive. Yet he endured it like a carven effigy, without a tremor. His gaunt features stayed thrust ahead, his lower lip sagging loose, and his gaze vacant and forward, when the turnkey laid a hand on his sleeve and motioned him to withdraw. He cast a dull, wandering look about him for a heartbeat, and submitted.
source_text: The judge assumed the black cap, and the prisoner still stood with the same air and gesture. A woman in the gallery, uttered some exclamation, called forth by this dread solemnity; he looked hastily up as if angry at the interruption, and bent forward yet more attentively. The address was solemn and impressive; the sentence fearful to hear. But he stood, like a marble figure, without the motion of a nerve. His haggard face was still thrust forward, his under-jaw hanging down, and his eyes staring out before him, when the jailer put his hand upon his arm, and beckoned him away. He gazed stupidly about him for an instant, and obeyed.

text: At daybreak, word spread that three strangers—two grown men and a lad—had been locked in the Kingston jail, seized the previous evening on questionable grounds. Accordingly, Messrs. Crane and Tully set off for Kingston. Yet the questionable grounds, upon closer scrutiny, dwindled to a single detail: they had been found asleep beneath a hayrick. Now, though this is indeed a serious offense, it warrants only confinement, and in the charitable view of English law, with its ample affection for every subject of the Crown, mere slumber under straw, absent any further proof, cannot be taken as certain evidence that the slumberer or slumberers have perpetrated a violent burglary and thus made themselves answerable to the gallows. Crane and Tully, therefore, returned no wiser than when they departed.
source_text: With the next morning, there came a rumour, that two men and a boy were in the cage at Kingston, who had been apprehended over night under suspicious circumstances; and to Kingston Messrs. Blathers and Duff journeyed accordingly. The suspicious circumstances, however, resolving themselves, on investigation, into the one fact, that they had been discovered sleeping under a haystack; which, although a great crime, is only punishable by imprisonment, and is, in the merciful eye of the English law, and its comprehensive love of all the King’s subjects, held to be no satisfactory proof, in the absence of all other evidence, that the sleeper, or sleepers, have committed burglary accompanied with violence, and have therefore rendered themselves liable to the punishment of death; Messrs. Blathers and Duff came back again, as wise as they went.

text: A few women sat in the dock, nodding toward their admirers in the gallery while the clerk recited statements to a pair of policemen and a plain-clothed man leaning across the table. The jailer lounged against the rail, idly tapping his nose with a heavy key, occasionally checking the spectators’ murmurs by calling for order or shooting a stern glance upward and ordering some woman, “Take that baby out,” when a feeble wail, half-muffled in a shawl, interrupted the court’s solemnity. The air was close and foul; the walls stained with grime; the ceiling blackened. An old smoke-darkened bust presided over the mantel, and a dusty clock hung above the dock—the only thing present that seemed to function as it should; for depravity, poverty, or long familiarity with both had coated every living figure with a taint nearly as unpleasant as the thick greasy film that scowled from every lifeless surface.
source_text: There were only a couple of women in the dock, who were nodding to their admiring friends, while the clerk read some depositions to a couple of policemen and a man in plain clothes who leant over the table. A jailer stood reclining against the dock-rail, tapping his nose listlessly with a large key, except when he repressed an undue tendency to conversation among the idlers, by proclaiming silence; or looked sternly up to bid some woman “Take that baby out,” when the gravity of justice was disturbed by feeble cries, half-smothered in the mother’s shawl, from some meagre infant. The room smelt close and unwholesome; the walls were dirt-discoloured; and the ceiling blackened. There was an old smoky bust over the mantel-shelf, and a dusty clock above the dock—the only thing present, that seemed to go on as it ought; for depravity, or poverty, or an habitual acquaintance with both, had left a taint on all the animate matter, hardly less unpleasant than the thick greasy scum on every inanimate object that frowned upon it.

text: It was only on the night of that final, dreadful day that the full, crushing weight of his hopeless, desperate condition descended upon his ruined spirit—not because he had ever clung to any clear or certain expectation of mercy, but because he had never truly faced more than the faint, distant chance of dying so soon. He had said little to the two men who took turns watching over him, and they, for their part, made no attempt to stir him from his silence. He had remained there, eyes open but lost in feverish reverie. Now, he sprang up constantly, gasping for breath, his skin ablaze, pacing wildly back and forth in such violent fits of terror and rage that even they—hardened to such scenes—shrank from him in dread. In the end, he became so horrifying, racked by the torments of his own guilty conscience, that one man could not endure sitting alone, watching him; and so the two kept vigil together.
source_text: It was not until the night of this last awful day, that a withering sense of his helpless, desperate state came in its full intensity upon his blighted soul; not that he had ever held any defined or positive hope of mercy, but that he had never been able to consider more than the dim probability of dying so soon. He had spoken little to either of the two men, who relieved each other in their attendance upon him; and they, for their parts, made no effort to rouse his attention. He had sat there, awake, but dreaming. Now, he started up, every minute, and with gasping mouth and burning skin, hurried to and fro, in such a paroxysm of fear and wrath that even they—used to such sights—recoiled from him with horror. He grew so terrible, at last, in all the tortures of his evil conscience, that one man could not bear to sit there, eyeing him alone; and so the two kept watch together.

text: That remark was meant only as a compliment to the creature’s talents, yet it carried an unintended truth—had young Master Bates been aware—for many a lady and gentleman who profess to be thoroughgoing Christians share, with Mr. Sikes’s hound, certain striking and peculiar likenesses.
source_text: This was merely intended as a tribute to the animal’s abilities, but it was an appropriate remark in another sense, if Master Bates had only known it; for there are a good many ladies and gentlemen, claiming to be out-and-out Christians, between whom, and Mr. Sikes’ dog, there exist strong and singular points of resemblance.

text: The elderly man entered with his customary briskness, yet the moment he had pushed his spectacles up onto his brow and slipped his hands behind the flaps of his robe to survey Oliver properly, his features began to twist through a succession of strange expressions. The boy, still pallid and frail from his illness, struggled to rise in deference to his host, only to collapse back into the seat; and truth be told, Mr. Brownlow’s heart, which could have furnished compassion for half a dozen ordinary benevolent gentlemen, now compelled a rush of moisture to his eyes through some inward mechanism too subtle for our philosophy to describe.
source_text: Now, the old gentleman came in as brisk as need be; but, he had no sooner raised his spectacles on his forehead, and thrust his hands behind the skirts of his dressing-gown to take a good long look at Oliver, than his countenance underwent a very great variety of odd contortions. Oliver looked very worn and shadowy from sickness, and made an ineffectual attempt to stand up, out of respect to his benefactor, which terminated in his sinking back into the chair again; and the fact is, if the truth must be told, that Mr. Brownlow’s heart, being large enough for any six ordinary old gentlemen of humane disposition, forced a supply of tears into his eyes, by some hydraulic process which we are not sufficiently philosophical to be in a condition to explain.

text: He halted and scanned the surroundings for the inn. A pale bank stood nearby, a brick-red brewery, and a town-hall painted yellow; and in one corner rose a sizable house, its wooden trim all done in green, with the sign of “The George” hanging before it. The moment his gaze found it, he hurried toward it.
source_text: Here he paused, and looked about for the inn. There were a white bank, and a red brewery, and a yellow town-hall; and in one corner there was a large house, with all the wood about it painted green: before which was the sign of “The George.” To this he hastened, as soon as it caught his eye.

text: Upstairs, the closing hours of the night slipped by in mirth; for the physician was brimming with gaiety, and however weary or pensive Harry Maylie might have been at the outset, he could not withstand the good gentleman’s exuberance, which spilled forth in a stream of anecdotes, medical reminiscences, and a host of trifling witticisms that Oliver found the most comical utterances he had ever encountered, and which made him laugh in full measure—much to the doctor’s open delight, for he roared at his own humor and, by sheer contagion, drew from Harry a laughter nearly as robust. Thus they formed as merry a company as the hour permitted; and it was deep into the night before they withdrew, with spirits light and grateful, to seek the repose their recent trials and uncertainties had made so needful.
source_text: Above stairs, the remainder of the evening passed cheerfully away; for the doctor was in high spirits; and however fatigued or thoughtful Harry Maylie might have been at first, he was not proof against the worthy gentleman’s good humour, which displayed itself in a great variety of sallies and professional recollections, and an abundance of small jokes, which struck Oliver as being the drollest things he had ever heard, and caused him to laugh proportionately; to the evident satisfaction of the doctor, who laughed immoderately at himself, and made Harry laugh almost as heartily, by the very force of sympathy. So, they were as pleasant a party as, under the circumstances, they could well have been; and it was late before they retired, with light and thankful hearts, to take that rest of which, after the doubt and suspense they had recently undergone, they stood much in need.

text: From dusk till almost the stroke of midnight, small knots of two or three kept arriving at the lodge-gate, their faces taut with worry, to ask if any reprieve had come. Told that none had, they carried the grim news back to gatherings in the street, where people silently marked the door he would emerge from, traced the spot where the scaffold would rise, and then drifted away with reluctant feet, only to turn and summon the scene again in their minds. Little by little the crowd thinned, until for a single hour in the deep of night the street lay abandoned to darkness and stillness.
source_text: From early in the evening until nearly midnight, little groups of two and three presented themselves at the lodge-gate, and inquired, with anxious faces, whether any reprieve had been received. These being answered in the negative, communicated the welcome intelligence to clusters in the street, who pointed out to one another the door from which he must come out, and showed where the scaffold would be built, and, walking with unwilling steps away, turned back to conjure up the scene. By degrees they fell off, one by one; and, for an hour, in the dead of night, the street was left to solitude and darkness.

text: He roamed across vast stretches of land, yet always found himself returning to that familiar spot. Morning turned to noon, the day began to fade, and still he drifted back and forth, circling and retracing his steps, unable to leave that one place behind. Finally he broke free and set his path toward Hatfield.
source_text: He wandered over miles and miles of ground, and still came back to the old place. Morning and noon had passed, and the day was on the wane, and still he rambled to and fro, and up and down, and round and round, and still lingered about the same spot. At last he got away, and shaped his course for Hatfield.

text: “Naturally not,” Sikes answered. “I figured that the moment I spotted him walking through Clerkenwell, those books tucked under his arm. It’s perfectly fine. They’re tender-minded hymn-chanters, else they’d never have sheltered him in the first place; and they won’t go prying into his affairs, afraid they’d have to press charges and get him transported. He’s quite secure.”
source_text: “Of course it couldn’t,” replied Sikes; “I know’d that, directly I see him coming through Clerkenwell, with the books under his arm. It’s all right enough. They’re soft-hearted psalm-singers, or they wouldn’t have taken him in at all; and they’ll ask no questions after him, fear they should be obliged to prosecute, and so get him lagged. He’s safe enough.”

text: “Ha!” the elderly woman exclaimed, “artists forever flatter their sitters into greater beauty than they possess, else they’d find no trade, my dear. The fellow who contrived that contraption for capturing true resemblance might have foreseen its failure—it is far too candid. Far too candid,” she repeated, chuckling with rich amusement at her own sharpness.
source_text: “Ah!” said the old lady, “painters always make ladies out prettier than they are, or they wouldn’t get any custom, child. The man that invented the machine for taking likenesses might have known _that_ would never succeed; it’s a deal too honest. A deal,” said the old lady, laughing very heartily at her own acuteness.

text: “Now, mark me, young sir,” murmured Silas, “when a fellow lives as guardedly as I do, tucked away in a cosy dwelling where no soul comes peeping or sniffing round, it comes as a fair jolt to receive the favour of a call from a young gentleman—however decent and agreeable a partner he might prove at a hand of cards, at one’s ease—placed in the predicament you occupy.”
source_text: “Why, look’e, young gentleman,” said Toby, “when a man keeps himself so very ex-clusive as I have done, and by that means has a snug house over his head with nobody a prying and smelling about it, it’s rather a startling thing to have the honour of a wisit from a young gentleman (however respectable and pleasant a person he may be to play cards with at conweniency) circumstanced as you are.”

text: Reaching out her quivering hands as she murmured, the ancient woman brandished them in triumph near her cheek, then rummaged in her cloak and produced a battered pewter snuffbox, tarnished by age, from which she tapped a small measure into her friend’s open hand and a little more into her own. As they busied themselves so, the older nurse, who had been restlessly observing till the fading crone should stir from her lethargy, drew near the hearth and demanded curtly how much longer she must linger?
source_text: Stretching forth her trembling fingers as she spoke, the old creature shook them exultingly before her face, and fumbling in her pocket, brought out an old time-discoloured tin snuff-box, from which she shook a few grains into the outstretched palm of her companion, and a few more into her own. While they were thus employed, the matron, who had been impatiently watching until the dying woman should awaken from her stupor, joined them by the fire, and sharply asked how long she was to wait?

text: “You must choose at once,” Mr. Brownlow said, his voice steady and unyielding. “Should you want me to bring these accusations out into the open and deliver you to a fate whose full horror I can glimpse with dread but have no power to limit, then—as I’ve told you already—you know the path. Otherwise, if you throw yourself on my leniency and the compassion of those you have so gravely wronged, sit down in that chair without uttering a sound. It has stood empty for you these two full days.”
source_text: “You will decide quickly,” said Mr. Brownlow, with perfect firmness and composure. “If you wish me to prefer my charges publicly, and consign you to a punishment the extent of which, although I can, with a shudder, foresee, I cannot control, once more, I say, for you know the way. If not, and you appeal to my forbearance, and the mercy of those you have deeply injured, seat yourself, without a word, in that chair. It has waited for you two whole days.”

text: The miser appeared deeply irked that the boy showed no keener interest in the matter; yet in truth, though the child burned with unease, the sly intensity of Silas’s gaze and his own whirling thoughts left him too bewildered to press further at that moment. No other chance arose: for the old man stayed sullen and mute until evening, when he made ready to venture out.
source_text: The Jew seemed much vexed by Oliver’s not expressing any greater curiosity on the subject; but the truth is, that, although Oliver felt very anxious, he was too much confused by the earnest cunning of Fagin’s looks, and his own speculations, to make any further inquiries just then. He had no other opportunity: for the Jew remained very surly and silent till night: when he prepared to go abroad.

text: The night was pitch-black. The weather had been dismal all day, and by that late hour, in that spot, barely a soul was about. Those few who did appear went by in a rush, likely without glimpsing—and surely without heeding—either the woman or the man who shadowed her. They weren't dressed to catch the desperate stares of any of London's homeless who might wander across the bridge that night, hunting for some freezing archway or roofless corner to rest in; the pair simply lingered there in silence, neither addressing nor being addressed by a single passerby.
source_text: It was a very dark night. The day had been unfavourable, and at that hour and place there were few people stirring. Such as there were, hurried quickly past: very possibly without seeing, but certainly without noticing, either the woman, or the man who kept her in view. Their appearance was not calculated to attract the importunate regards of such of London’s destitute population, as chanced to take their way over the bridge that night in search of some cold arch or doorless hovel wherein to lay their heads; they stood there in silence: neither speaking nor spoken to, by any one who passed.

text: “He!” the other man appeared to reply. “Could I ever fail to know him, do you imagine? If a throng of spectres assumed his very likeness and he stood among them, something within me would still single him out. If you laid him fifty feet beneath the earth and led me across his grave, I believe I would sense it, even without a marker, that he was buried there.”
source_text: “He!” the other man seemed to answer; “could I mistake him, think you? If a crowd of ghosts were to put themselves into his exact shape, and he stood amongst them, there is something that would tell me how to point him out. If you buried him fifty feet deep, and took me across his grave, I fancy I should know, if there wasn’t a mark above it, that he lay buried there?”

text: After a few moments of quiet reflection, the elderly man made his way, still wearing that pensive expression, into a rear anteroom that led off the courtyard. There, he withdrew into a shadowed corner and summoned to his inward vision an immense amphitheater of countenances, long veiled by a somber curtain. “No,” he murmured, with a shake of his head; “it can only be fancy.”
source_text: After musing for some minutes, the old gentleman walked, with the same meditative face, into a back anteroom opening from the yard; and there, retiring into a corner, called up before his mind’s eye a vast amphitheatre of faces over which a dusky curtain had hung for many years. “No,” said the old gentleman, shaking his head; “it must be imagination.”

text: “Look, we simply don’t have the resources for that right now, my dear,” the Jew answered with a smile. “Besides, it’s not the kind of work we do—though, someday, I might be grateful to have it handled. I know how these women operate, Monks, believe me. The moment the boy starts to grow callous, she’ll value him no more than a chunk of timber. You want him turned into a thief. If he’s still breathing, I can start shaping him into one from this very moment. And if—if—” he continued, leaning in closer to his companion, “it’s not probable, keep in mind—but if the very worst should happen, and he’s dead—”
source_text: “Why, we can’t afford to do that just now, my dear,” replied the Jew, smiling; “and, besides, that sort of thing is not in our way; or, one of these days, I might be glad to have it done. I know what these girls are, Monks, well. As soon as the boy begins to harden, she’ll care no more for him, than for a block of wood. You want him made a thief. If he is alive, I can make him one from this time; and, if—if—” said the Jew, drawing nearer to the other,—“it’s not likely, mind,—but if the worst comes to the worst, and he is dead—”

text: The physician stayed away far longer than he or the women had expected. A broad, shallow case was brought in from the carriage; the bedroom bell sounded again and again; and the household staff hurried endlessly up and down the stairs—signs from which it was rightly inferred that some significant matter was unfolding overhead. Finally he reappeared, and when asked nervously about his patient, he assumed a deeply enigmatic expression and shut the door with great care.
source_text: The doctor was absent, much longer than either he or the ladies had anticipated. A large flat box was fetched out of the gig; and a bedroom bell was rung very often; and the servants ran up and down stairs perpetually; from which tokens it was justly concluded that something important was going on above. At length he returned; and in reply to an anxious inquiry after his patient; looked very mysterious, and closed the door, carefully.

text: They moved at such a sluggish, shifty dawdle that Oliver soon suspected his companions meant to cheat the old gentleman by avoiding work altogether. The Dodger had an unpleasant habit of snatching caps from little boys’ heads and flinging them down basement steps, while Charley Bates displayed a very lax attitude toward property rights, stealing apples and onions from stalls along the gutter and stuffing them into pockets so astonishingly roomy that they seemed to pull his entire outfit out of shape in every direction. These actions appeared so disreputable that Oliver was about to announce he would find his own way back as best he could, when his attention was abruptly diverted by a deeply puzzling shift in the Dodger’s manner.
source_text: The pace at which they went, was such a very lazy, ill-looking saunter, that Oliver soon began to think his companions were going to deceive the old gentleman, by not going to work at all. The Dodger had a vicious propensity, too, of pulling the caps from the heads of small boys and tossing them down areas; while Charley Bates exhibited some very loose notions concerning the rights of property, by pilfering divers apples and onions from the stalls at the kennel sides, and thrusting them into pockets which were so surprisingly capacious, that they seemed to undermine his whole suit of clothes in every direction. These things looked so bad, that Oliver was on the point of declaring his intention of seeking his way back, in the best way he could; when his thoughts were suddenly directed into another channel, by a very mysterious change of behaviour on the part of the Dodger.

text: Upon arriving at the house, he found Mrs. Maylie in the small sitting room. The moment Oliver saw her, his spirits dropped; she had always remained at her niece’s side until now, and he quaked inwardly, dreading what shift in circumstances might have compelled her to step away. He was told that the girl had slipped into a profound slumber, from which she would emerge either restored to health and vitality, or to say her last goodbyes and pass on.
source_text: When he reached home Mrs. Maylie was sitting in the little parlour. Oliver’s heart sank at sight of her; for she had never left the bedside of her niece; and he trembled to think what change could have driven her away. He learnt that she had fallen into a deep sleep, from which she would waken, either to recovery and life, or to bid them farewell, and die.

text: At this admission, Lady Clara let out a ringing peal of laughter; then, downing a tumbler of sherry, she tossed her head with a rebellious flourish and broke into various cries of “Let the sport run on!” “Faint heart never won!” and similar phrases. These appeared to steady both men; for the Count dipped his chin with a pleased expression and settled back into his chair, just as Sir Hugo did the same.
source_text: At this confession, Miss Nancy burst into a loud laugh; and, swallowing a glass of brandy, shook her head with an air of defiance, and burst into sundry exclamations of “Keep the game a-going!” “Never say die!” and the like. These seemed to have the effect of re-assuring both gentlemen; for the Jew nodded his head with a satisfied air, and resumed his seat: as did Mr. Sikes likewise.

text: It is quite conceivable that had Mr. Grimshaw acquired such knowledge sooner in their conversation, he would have cast an altogether different hue upon his modest account. But the moment for that had passed; thus he gave a solemn shake of his head, slipped the five sovereigns into his pocket, and took his leave.
source_text: It is not improbable that if Mr. Bumble had been possessed of this information at an earlier period of the interview, he might have imparted a very different colouring to his little history. It was too late to do it now, however; so he shook his head gravely, and, pocketing the five guineas, withdrew.

text: It seemed the dog had been taken in, at least a little, by Mr. Fagin’s outer coat. As the Jew unfastened it and tossed it across the back of a chair, the creature withdrew to the corner from which he had first emerged, his tail stirring as he went—as much to say he was as content as his nature would allow.
source_text: Apparently, the dog had been somewhat deceived by Mr. Fagin’s outer garment; for as the Jew unbuttoned it, and threw it over the back of a chair, he retired to the corner from which he had risen: wagging his tail as he went, to show that he was as well satisfied as it was in his nature to be.

text: Summer swept in on the heels of a fleeting spring. Where the hamlet had once been lovely, it now basked in the full splendour and abundance of its prime. The towering trees, which had stood gaunt and naked in the leaner months, had swollen with vigorous life; their green boughs reached out over the parched soil, turning bare and open patches into secluded retreats, offering a dense and cooling shade from which to gaze upon the sweeping vista, drenched in sunlight, that rolled away beyond. The land had wrapped itself in a cloak of vivid emerald and breathed its sweetest fragrances into the air. It was the year’s zenith of strength and vitality; every living thing rejoiced and thrived.
source_text: Spring flew swiftly by, and summer came. If the village had been beautiful at first it was now in the full glow and luxuriance of its richness. The great trees, which had looked shrunken and bare in the earlier months, had now burst into strong life and health; and stretching forth their green arms over the thirsty ground, converted open and naked spots into choice nooks, where was a deep and pleasant shade from which to look upon the wide prospect, steeped in sunshine, which lay stretched beyond. The earth had donned her mantle of brightest green; and shed her richest perfumes abroad. It was the prime and vigour of the year; all things were glad and flourishing.

text: Oliver drifted back from the stupor that had held him so long, only to feel a slow, sickening dread coil around his heart—a dread that whispered he would surely die if he stayed motionless. He forced himself upright and tried to walk. Dizziness swam through his head, and he reeled and swayed like a drunkard. Yet he remained on his feet, and with his head lolling heavily against his chest, he staggered forward, blind to where his steps might lead.
source_text: After a short return of the stupor in which he had been so long plunged, Oliver: urged by a creeping sickness at his heart, which seemed to warn him that if he lay there, he must surely die: got upon his feet, and essayed to walk. His head was dizzy, and he staggered to and fro like a drunken man. But he kept up, nevertheless, and, with his head drooping languidly on his breast, went stumbling onward, he knew not whither.

text: For a long while they murmured together in hushed tones. A bystander could scarcely catch more than a scattered syllable or two from the exchange, yet it would have been plain enough that the older man was striving to parry some accusation leveled by his companion, whose temper seemed to be mounting by the minute. They had carried on in this fashion, perhaps, for the better part of twenty minutes, when the fellow whom the other had repeatedly addressed as Blackwood during their murmured talk lifted his voice just enough to break the hush.
source_text: They conversed for some time in whispers. Though nothing of the conversation was distinguishable beyond a few disjointed words here and there, a listener might easily have perceived that Fagin appeared to be defending himself against some remarks of the stranger; and that the latter was in a state of considerable irritation. They might have been talking, thus, for a quarter of an hour or more, when Monks—by which name the Jew had designated the strange man several times in the course of their colloquy—said, raising his voice a little,

text: The sound of Charley’s laughter, along with Miss Betsy’s voice—she had come at just the right moment to splash water on her friend and tend to the other womanly tasks needed to help her revive—would have roused plenty of people in far more pleasant conditions than Oliver’s. Yet he was ill and exhausted, and he quickly dropped into a deep sleep.
source_text: The noise of Charley’s laughter, and the voice of Miss Betsy, who opportunely arrived to throw water over her friend, and perform other feminine offices for the promotion of her recovery, might have kept many people awake under more happy circumstances than those in which Oliver was placed. But he was sick and weary; and he soon fell sound asleep.

text: As he spoke, he slid a pair of sovereigns across the table toward his companion with deliberate care, as if anxious that the clink of coin might carry beyond the room. Once Mr. Bumble had meticulously inspected the gold, assuring himself of its authenticity, and tucked it away with evident pleasure into his waistcoat pocket, he continued.
source_text: As he spoke, he pushed a couple of sovereigns across the table to his companion, carefully, as though unwilling that the chinking of money should be heard without. When Mr. Bumble had scrupulously examined the coins, to see that they were genuine, and had put them up, with much satisfaction, in his waistcoat-pocket, he went on:

text: “The mother,” the woman uttered, straining harder than she had before, “the mother, as death’s first agonies seized her, breathed into my ear that if her infant drew breath and flourished, a time might arrive when the child would not be so ashamed to learn the name of its wretched young mother. ‘And oh, merciful Heaven!’ she cried, clasping her frail hands, ‘whether it be son or daughter, send it allies in this harsh world, and show compassion on a forsaken, solitary child, left to its fate!’”
source_text: “The mother,” said the woman, making a more violent effort than before; “the mother, when the pains of death first came upon her, whispered in my ear that if her baby was born alive, and thrived, the day might come when it would not feel so much disgraced to hear its poor young mother named. ‘And oh, kind Heaven!’ she said, folding her thin hands together, ‘whether it be boy or girl, raise up some friends for it in this troubled world, and take pity upon a lonely desolate child, abandoned to its mercy!’”

text: A heavy stillness settled over them. The Jew sank into profound reflection, his face creased into a look of pure, demonic malice. Sikes stole covert glances at him now and then. Nancy, seemingly afraid of provoking the burglar, kept her gaze locked on the flames, as though she had heard nothing of what had just transpired.
source_text: A long silence ensued; during which the Jew was plunged in deep thought, with his face wrinkled into an expression of villainy perfectly demoniacal. Sikes eyed him furtively from time to time. Nancy, apparently fearful of irritating the housebreaker, sat with her eyes fixed upon the fire, as if she had been deaf to all that passed.

text: Oliver could picture it perfectly in his mind, as clearly as if he had never moved an inch; yet he decided it was kinder not to trouble the gentle old woman. So when she glanced at him, he offered a soft smile, and Mrs. Bedwin, reassured that he was more at ease, set about crumbling toasted bread into the broth with all the busy solemnity the task demanded. Oliver finished it with remarkable speed. He had barely taken the final spoonful when a light knock sounded at the door. "Come in," said the old lady, and Mr. Brownlow entered.
source_text: Oliver _did_ see it in his mind’s eye as distinctly as if he had not altered his position; but he thought it better not to worry the kind old lady; so he smiled gently when she looked at him; and Mrs. Bedwin, satisfied that he felt more comfortable, salted and broke bits of toasted bread into the broth, with all the bustle befitting so solemn a preparation. Oliver got through it with extraordinary expedition. He had scarcely swallowed the last spoonful, when there came a soft rap at the door. “Come in,” said the old lady; and in walked Mr. Brownlow.

text: Whether the gentleman in the white waistcoat was correct is something I mean to reveal later in this account, and so—should this story hold even a scrap of interest—I would likely spoil it if I dared suggest, at this early point, whether Oliver Twist’s life came to such a brutal end or not.
source_text: As I purpose to show in the sequel whether the white waistcoated gentleman was right or not, I should perhaps mar the interest of this narrative (supposing it to possess any at all), if I ventured to hint just yet, whether the life of Oliver Twist had this violent termination or no.

text: All I could have wished to accomplish, and far beyond it. Marrying the wretched child’s native wit with what I already knew, and with the fruits of our worthy colleague’s local investigations, I sealed every avenue of retreat and stripped bare the entire foul plot, which under those gleams shone forth as clear as morning. Pen a note and set the rendezvous for the evening after next, at seven o’clock. We shall arrive some hours beforehand, but we will need refreshment—most of all the young lady, who may require a fortitude deeper than either you or I can fully gauge at present. Yet my spirit seethes to bring justice upon the murderer of this poor soul. What road did they follow?
source_text: “All that I could hope to do, and even more. Coupling the poor girl’s intelligence with my previous knowledge, and the result of our good friend’s inquiries on the spot, I left him no loophole of escape, and laid bare the whole villainy which by these lights became plain as day. Write and appoint the evening after tomorrow, at seven, for the meeting. We shall be down there, a few hours before, but shall require rest: especially the young lady, who _may_ have greater need of firmness than either you or I can quite foresee just now. But my blood boils to avenge this poor murdered creature. Which way have they taken?”

text: Mr. Brownlow took Oliver in as his own child. He relocated with the boy and their elderly housekeeper to a spot just a mile from the parsonage, where his cherished friends lived, fulfilling the last deep and heartfelt desire Oliver held. In this way, they formed a small community whose way of life came as close to true contentment as this ever-shifting world allows.
source_text: Mr. Brownlow adopted Oliver as his son. Removing with him and the old housekeeper to within a mile of the parsonage-house, where his dear friends resided, he gratified the only remaining wish of Oliver’s warm and earnest heart, and thus linked together a little society, whose condition approached as nearly to one of perfect happiness as can ever be known in this changing world.

text: “Switch it up!” shouted the Jew, pushed past all patience by his partner’s sudden stubbornness and the night’s frustrations. “I _will_ switch it up! Mark my words, you slut. Mark my words—I can choke Sikes with six words as surely as if I had his bull neck clenched in my hands right now. If he returns and leaves the boy behind; if he gets away clean, and dead or alive, fails to bring him back to me; kill him yourself if you want him to dodge the hangman. And do it the second he steps through that door, or take heed—it’ll be too late!”
source_text: “Change it!” exclaimed the Jew, exasperated beyond all bounds by his companion’s unexpected obstinacy, and the vexation of the night, “I _will_ change it! Listen to me, you drab. Listen to me, who with six words, can strangle Sikes as surely as if I had his bull’s throat between my fingers now. If he comes back, and leaves the boy behind him; if he gets off free, and dead or alive, fails to restore him to me; murder him yourself if you would have him escape Jack Ketch. And do it the moment he sets foot in this room, or mind me, it will be too late!”

text: “I assure you, should it come to that, and the truth be wrung from him, the matter will end there. There are details in Oliver’s brief past that would be distressing to expose to the public gaze, and once the truth is brought to light, they shall escape all consequences.”
source_text: “I promise you that in that case, if the truth is forced from him, there the matter will rest; there must be circumstances in Oliver’s little history which it would be painful to drag before the public eye, and if the truth is once elicited, they shall go scot free.”

text: “I, Mrs. Mann. We give our foundlings their names by the alphabet. The last was an S—Swubble, I called him. This one’s a T—Twist, that’s what I named him. The next to arrive will be Unwin, and after that Vilkins. I’ve got names all picked out right to the end of the alphabet, and then I’ll start over again once we reach Z.”
source_text: “I, Mrs. Mann. We name our fondlings in alphabetical order. The last was a S,—Swubble, I named him. This was a T,—Twist, I named _him_. The next one comes will be Unwin, and the next Vilkins. I have got names ready made to the end of the alphabet, and all the way through it again, when we come to Z.”

text: Mr. Losberne was staying with them and would remain for two more days. Rose, however, understood the good man’s rash temper all too well and could vividly imagine the fury he would unleash upon the person responsible for Oliver’s capture during that first surge of outrage. She dared not confide in him while no one with more experience could support her arguments on the girl’s behalf. These considerations demanded the utmost prudence and careful restraint in revealing the matter to Mrs. Maylie, whose immediate instinct would certainly be to consult the estimable doctor about it. Turning to any lawyer was equally unthinkable, even had she known how to proceed, for the very same reason. At one moment she considered asking Harry for help, but that thought stirred the memory of their last farewell, and it felt beneath her to summon him back when—tears welled in her eyes as she followed this line of thought—he might by now have learned to forget her and found greater happiness elsewhere.
source_text: Mr. Losberne was with them, and would be for the next two days; but Rose was too well acquainted with the excellent gentleman’s impetuosity, and foresaw too clearly the wrath with which, in the first explosion of his indignation, he would regard the instrument of Oliver’s recapture, to trust him with the secret, when her representations in the girl’s behalf could be seconded by no experienced person. These were all reasons for the greatest caution and most circumspect behaviour in communicating it to Mrs. Maylie, whose first impulse would infallibly be to hold a conference with the worthy doctor on the subject. As to resorting to any legal adviser, even if she had known how to do so, it was scarcely to be thought of, for the same reason. Once the thought occurred to her of seeking assistance from Harry; but this awakened the recollection of their last parting, and it seemed unworthy of her to call him back, when—the tears rose to her eyes as she pursued this train of reflection—he might have by this time learnt to forget her, and to be happier away.

text: For an instant, he remained frozen, the dread coursing through his veins so fiercely that his whole body seemed ablaze. Then, muddled and panicked, he bolted, and without any clear thought, fled as swiftly as his legs could carry him.
source_text: He stood, for a moment, with the blood so tingling through all his veins from terror, that he felt as if he were in a burning fire; then, confused and frightened, he took to his heels; and, not knowing what he did, made off as fast as he could lay his feet to the ground.

text: “All right, all right, Bill; just be patient, be patient,” the Jew answered in a calming tone. “There you have it! Safe and sound!” While saying this, he pulled a worn cotton handkerchief from inside his coat, undid a bulky knot at one end, and brought out a little packet wrapped in brown paper. Sikes grabbed it from him, tore it open quickly, and began tallying the gold sovereigns inside.
source_text: “Yes, yes, Bill; give me time, give me time,” replied the Jew, soothingly. “Here it is! All safe!” As he spoke, he drew forth an old cotton handkerchief from his breast; and untying a large knot in one corner, produced a small brown-paper packet. Sikes, snatching it from him, hastily opened it; and proceeded to count the sovereigns it contained.

text: “And I worry, you understand,” the Jew went on, as though the break in his speech had escaped him, while he studied the other man’s face with care—“I worry that if our enterprise were finished, it would finish a great many others too, and that the fallout, my dear, would land far harder on you than it ever would on me.”
source_text: “And I’m afraid, you see,” added the Jew, speaking as if he had not noticed the interruption; and regarding the other closely as he did so,—“I’m afraid that, if the game was up with us, it might be up with a good many more, and that it would come out rather worse for you than it would for me, my dear.”

text: “Precisely what I anticipated, Mrs. Harlow,” the physician remarked. “A craving for drink is entirely to be expected. You might offer him a small cup of broth, madam, along with a plain crust without any spread. See that he is not overheated, madam, yet take equal care he does not catch a chill; would you be so kind?”
source_text: “Just as I expected, Mrs. Bedwin,” said the doctor. “It’s very natural that he should be thirsty. You may give him a little tea, ma’am, and some dry toast without any butter. Don’t keep him too warm, ma’am; but be careful that you don’t let him be too cold; will you have the goodness?”

text: That very night, the man in the ivory vest declared with unwavering certainty, not merely that the boy would face the noose, but that he would be disemboweled and dismembered as a final measure. Mr. Bumble wagged his head with somber portent, murmuring that he hoped the lad might yet find redemption; to which Mr. Gamfield answered that he hoped the lad might find his way to him instead—a desire which, though he concurred with the beadle on nearly every count, appeared to run in a wholly contrary direction.
source_text: That same evening, the gentleman in the white waistcoat most positively and decidedly affirmed, not only that Oliver would be hung, but that he would be drawn and quartered into the bargain. Mr. Bumble shook his head with gloomy mystery, and said he wished he might come to good; whereunto Mr. Gamfield replied, that he wished he might come to him; which, although he agreed with the beadle in most matters, would seem to be a wish of a totally opposite description.

text: Reeling like a man blasted by a thunderbolt, his footing failed and he pitched across the stone railing. The cord had found his throat. It drew taut beneath his mass, snug as a fiddle’s string and quick as the note it looses. Down he plunged through seven fathoms of air. A violent snap came, a ghastly shudder of the body; and there he dangled, the naked blade still locked in his hardening fist.
source_text: Staggering as if struck by lightning, he lost his balance and tumbled over the parapet. The noose was on his neck. It ran up with his weight, tight as a bow-string, and swift as the arrow it speeds. He fell for five-and-thirty feet. There was a sudden jerk, a terrific convulsion of the limbs; and there he hung, with the open knife clenched in his stiffening hand.

text: “That’s what he said,” Nancy replied, her gaze flicking nervously about the room—a habit she’d barely broken since she started talking, for Sikes’s specter never left her. “And worse. When he mentioned you and the other woman, claiming it felt like Heaven itself, or the devil, had schemed against him by putting Oliver in your path, he let out a laugh and said even that brought a kind of satisfaction, because how many thousands and hundreds of thousands of pounds wouldn’t you hand over, if you could, just to learn who your two-legged lapdog really was.”
source_text: “Those were his words,” said Nancy, glancing uneasily round, as she had scarcely ceased to do, since she began to speak, for a vision of Sikes haunted her perpetually. “And more. When he spoke of you and the other lady, and said it seemed contrived by Heaven, or the devil, against him, that Oliver should come into your hands, he laughed, and said there was some comfort in that too, for how many thousands and hundreds of thousands of pounds would you not give, if you had them, to know who your two-legged spaniel was.”

text: The child lay stretched in deep slumber on a rough pallet set upon the ground; so drained of color by worry, grief, and the stifling air of his confinement that he resembled death itself—not death arrayed in burial linen and a casket, but in the shape it takes the moment life slips away; when a tender young soul has only just soared toward Heaven, and the coarse earthly atmosphere has yet to touch the altered clay it once made sacred.
source_text: The boy was lying, fast asleep, on a rude bed upon the floor; so pale with anxiety, and sadness, and the closeness of his prison, that he looked like death; not death as it shows in shroud and coffin, but in the guise it wears when life has just departed; when a young and gentle spirit has, but an instant, fled to Heaven, and the gross air of the world has not had time to breathe upon the changing dust it hallowed.

text: The two aged gossips, joining their voices, began to lament mournfully that the poor soul was too far spent to recognize her dearest companions, and were making many vows never to desert her, when the matron thrust them from the chamber, shut the door, and came back to the bed. Once shut out, the old women shifted their tune and shrieked through the keyhole that old Sally was intoxicated—which, in truth, seemed probable, since besides a modest measure of laudanum ordered by the surgeon, she was suffering the influence of a parting dram of gin-and-water, secretly given, out of the kindness of their hearts, by the virtuous old pair themselves.
source_text: The two old crones, chiming in together, began pouring out many piteous lamentations that the poor dear was too far gone to know her best friends; and were uttering sundry protestations that they would never leave her, when the superior pushed them from the room, closed the door, and returned to the bedside. On being excluded, the old ladies changed their tone, and cried through the keyhole that old Sally was drunk; which, indeed, was not unlikely; since, in addition to a moderate dose of opium prescribed by the apothecary, she was labouring under the effects of a final taste of gin-and-water which had been privily administered, in the openness of their hearts, by the worthy old ladies themselves.

text: So too, as his gaze drifted to the magistrate, his thoughts wandered to the cut of the man’s coat, its probable price, and the manner in which he had slipped it on. On the bench there sat as well a stout, elderly gentleman who had departed half an hour earlier and only now returned. Idly, he speculated whether the fellow had gone to dine, what dishes he had chosen, and at which establishment; and he followed this meandering reverie until a fresh detail arrested his attention and stirred a different fancy.
source_text: In the same way, when he turned his eyes towards the judge, his mind began to busy itself with the fashion of his dress, and what it cost, and how he put it on. There was an old fat gentleman on the bench, too, who had gone out, some half an hour before, and now come back. He wondered within himself whether this man had been to get his dinner, what he had had, and where he had had it; and pursued this train of careless thought until some new object caught his eye and roused another.

text: One afternoon, at the customary dinner hour, Oliver and Noah went down to the kitchen to feast on a meager cut of mutton—a pound and a half taken from the scrawniest part of the neck. When Charlotte was summoned away for a moment, a short pause followed, and Noah Claypole, both ravenous and spiteful, decided he could put that interval to no better use than tormenting and goading young Oliver Twist.
source_text: One day, Oliver and Noah had descended into the kitchen at the usual dinner-hour, to banquet upon a small joint of mutton—a pound and a half of the worst end of the neck; when Charlotte being called out of the way, there ensued a brief interval of time, which Noah Claypole, being hungry and vicious, considered he could not possibly devote to a worthier purpose than aggravating and tantalising young Oliver Twist.

text: I believe that if you grant me complete and unrestricted authority to put pressure on Giles, and on that young lad Brittles, I can handle the matter. Giles is a loyal man and a long-standing servant, I'm aware; yet you can compensate him in countless ways, and even reward him for being such an excellent marksman. You have no issue with that?
source_text: “I think if you give me a full and unlimited commission to bully Giles, and that little boy, Brittles, I can manage it. Giles is a faithful fellow and an old servant, I know; but you can make it up to him in a thousand ways, and reward him for being such a good shot besides. You don’t object to that?”

text: The lad who posed this question to the youthful traveler was roughly his equal in years, yet among the strangest figures Oliver had ever encountered. His nose was stubby, his brow shallow, his face utterly plain; and he was as grimy a child as one could hope to meet. Still, he carried himself with all the swagger and poise of a grown man. He stood undersized for his age, with slightly bandy legs and small, piercing, disagreeable eyes. His cap perched so precariously atop his crown that it seemed perpetually on the verge of tumbling—and would have done so repeatedly, had the boy not possessed the trick of giving his head an abrupt jerk now and then, which sent it back to its former seat. He sported a man's overcoat, the hem dangling near his ankles. The cuffs were folded halfway up his forearms to free his hands from the sleeves, evidently so he could bury them deep in the pockets of his corduroy breeches; for there they remained. He was, on the whole, as rakish and strutting a young blade as ever measured four feet six—or perhaps a shade less—in his stout boots.
source_text: The boy who addressed this inquiry to the young wayfarer, was about his own age: but one of the queerest looking boys that Oliver had even seen. He was a snub-nosed, flat-browed, common-faced boy enough; and as dirty a juvenile as one would wish to see; but he had about him all the airs and manners of a man. He was short of his age: with rather bow-legs, and little, sharp, ugly eyes. His hat was stuck on the top of his head so lightly, that it threatened to fall off every moment—and would have done so, very often, if the wearer had not had a knack of every now and then giving his head a sudden twitch, which brought it back to its old place again. He wore a man’s coat, which reached nearly to his heels. He had turned the cuffs back, half-way up his arm, to get his hands out of the sleeves: apparently with the ultimate view of thrusting them into the pockets of his corduroy trousers; for there he kept them. He was, altogether, as roystering and swaggering a young gentleman as ever stood four feet six, or something less, in the bluchers.
