text: Without a single hesitation or moment of thought; never once glancing sideways, nor lifting his gaze to the heavens, nor dropping it to the earth, but staring dead ahead with ferocious determination: his teeth clenched so hard the taut jaw looked ready to burst through his flesh; the thief kept his frantic pace, not uttering a sound, not easing a sinew, till he stood before his own threshold. He unlocked it, quietly, with a key; crept swiftly up the steps; and slipping into his chamber, bolted the door twice, heaved a weighty table flush against it, and pulled back the bed’s curtain.
source_text: Without one pause, or moment’s consideration; without once turning his head to the right or left, or raising his eyes to the sky, or lowering them to the ground, but looking straight before him with savage resolution: his teeth so tightly compressed that the strained jaw seemed starting through his skin; the robber held on his headlong course, nor muttered a word, nor relaxed a muscle, until he reached his own door. He opened it, softly, with a key; strode lightly up the stairs; and entering his own room, double-locked the door, and lifting a heavy table against it, drew back the curtain of the bed.

text: “Come now,” Mr. Bumble said, his tone a shade less self-important, since seeing the impact of his words rather pleased him. “Come, Oliver! Dry your tears on your jacket sleeves, and stop weeping into your porridge; that’s a very silly thing to do, Oliver.” And indeed it was, for the gruel was watery enough as it stood.
source_text: “Come,” said Mr. Bumble, somewhat less pompously, for it was gratifying to his feelings to observe the effect his eloquence had produced; “Come, Oliver! Wipe your eyes with the cuffs of your jacket, and don’t cry into your gruel; that’s a very foolish action, Oliver.” It certainly was, for there was quite enough water in it already.

text: Mr. Bumble found himself utterly startled and thoroughly outmatched. He possessed a marked inclination for tyranny, taking no small delight in the practice of casual cruelty, and was therefore—needless to state—a craven at heart. This is far from a slight upon his nature, for many esteemed and admired public figures suffer from comparable frailties. The observation is offered, in truth, more to his credit than not, aiming to furnish the reader with a proper appreciation of his fitness for authority.
source_text: Mr. Bumble was fairly taken by surprise, and fairly beaten. He had a decided propensity for bullying: derived no inconsiderable pleasure from the exercise of petty cruelty; and, consequently, was (it is needless to say) a coward. This is by no means a disparagement to his character; for many official personages, who are held in high respect and admiration, are the victims of similar infirmities. The remark is made, indeed, rather in his favour than otherwise, and with a view of impressing the reader with a just sense of his qualifications for office.

text: Around noon the following day, once the Dodger and Master Bates had departed to attend to their usual trades, Mr. Fagin seized the chance to deliver Oliver a lengthy sermon on the grievous vice of ingratitude. He made it abundantly plain that the boy had been guilty of no small measure of it—first by deliberately withdrawing from the company of his solicitous friends, and worse, by attempting to flee them after they had gone to such pains and expense to reclaim him. Fagin placed particular emphasis on the fact that he had taken Oliver in and cared for him when, without that timely intervention, the child might well have starved; and he recounted the pitiful, moving tale of another young lad whom, out of pure benevolence, he had aided in nearly identical straits, but who—proving unworthy of his trust and showing a regrettable inclination to parley with the police—had unhappily ended up hanged at the Old Bailey one morning. Fagin made no effort to disguise his own part in that catastrophe, yet lamented, with tears standing in his eyes, that the perverse and treacherous conduct of the youth in question had forced the necessity of his becoming the subject of certain Crown evidence—which, if not strictly accurate, was absolutely essential to the security of himself (Mr. Fagin) and a small circle of select companions. He closed by painting a rather disagreeable portrait of the unpleasantness of hanging, and with great cordiality and courteousness of manner expressed his fervent hope that he might never be obliged to subject Oliver Twist to that disagreeable procedure.
source_text: About noon next day, when the Dodger and Master Bates had gone out to pursue their customary avocations, Mr. Fagin took the opportunity of reading Oliver a long lecture on the crying sin of ingratitude; of which he clearly demonstrated he had been guilty, to no ordinary extent, in wilfully absenting himself from the society of his anxious friends; and, still more, in endeavouring to escape from them after so much trouble and expense had been incurred in his recovery. Mr. Fagin laid great stress on the fact of his having taken Oliver in, and cherished him, when, without his timely aid, he might have perished with hunger; and he related the dismal and affecting history of a young lad whom, in his philanthropy, he had succoured under parallel circumstances, but who, proving unworthy of his confidence and evincing a desire to communicate with the police, had unfortunately come to be hanged at the Old Bailey one morning. Mr. Fagin did not seek to conceal his share in the catastrophe, but lamented with tears in his eyes that the wrong-headed and treacherous behaviour of the young person in question, had rendered it necessary that he should become the victim of certain evidence for the crown: which, if it were not precisely true, was indispensably necessary for the safety of him (Mr. Fagin) and a few select friends. Mr. Fagin concluded by drawing a rather disagreeable picture of the discomforts of hanging; and, with great friendliness and politeness of manner, expressed his anxious hopes that he might never be obliged to submit Oliver Twist to that unpleasant operation.

text: “We shall remain here in the city,” Mrs. Maylie declared, “so long as the faintest possibility endures of pursuing this investigation with any hope of a favorable outcome. I shall stint neither effort nor cost in service of the cause that so profoundly engages us all, and I am willing to linger, even should it be for a full year, provided you can promise me that some glimmer of expectation still persists.”
source_text: “We stay in town, of course,” said Mrs. Maylie, “while there remains the slightest prospect of prosecuting this inquiry with a chance of success. I will spare neither trouble nor expense in behalf of the object in which we are all so deeply interested, and I am content to remain here, if it be for twelve months, so long as you assure me that any hope remains.”

text: It was true; for once the candles arrived, they noticed how quickly her complexion had turned a chalky pallor in the brief time since they'd come back. Her features remained lovely, but something had shifted; a strained, worn expression now haunted that tender face, one it had never shown before. A moment later, a deep blush swept over it, and a fierce, unsettled light filled her gentle blue eyes. Then that too vanished, like a shadow from a drifting cloud, and she was deathly pale again.
source_text: She was, indeed; for, when candles were brought, they saw that in the very short time which had elapsed since their return home, the hue of her countenance had changed to a marble whiteness. Its expression had lost nothing of its beauty; but it was changed; and there was an anxious haggard look about the gentle face, which it had never worn before. Another minute, and it was suffused with a crimson flush: and a heavy wildness came over the soft blue eye. Again this disappeared, like the shadow thrown by a passing cloud; and she was once more deadly pale.

text: “I trust it will be so,” the boy answered. “Not till I have passed away, but then it shall. The physician must speak truly, Edmund, for in my sleep I see such visions of Paradise, and winged spirits, and gentle countenances that never visit my waking hours. Embrace me,” he said, scaling the low wooden barrier and casting his slender arms about Edmund’s neck. “Farewell, beloved! May the Lord keep you!”
source_text: “I hope so,” replied the child. “After I am dead, but not before. I know the doctor must be right, Oliver, because I dream so much of Heaven, and Angels, and kind faces that I never see when I am awake. Kiss me,” said the child, climbing up the low gate, and flinging his little arms round Oliver’s neck. “Good-b’ye, dear! God bless you!”

text: The kitchen was an ancient, low-ceilinged chamber, its center spanned by a massive timber beam, and beside the hearth stood high-backed benches. On these sat a handful of coarse men in smock-frocks, drinking and smoking. They paid no heed to Oliver, and scarcely more to Sikes; and since Sikes paid them equally little mind, he and his young companion settled into a corner apart, largely undisturbed by the company.
source_text: The kitchen was an old, low-roofed room; with a great beam across the middle of the ceiling, and benches, with high backs to them, by the fire; on which were seated several rough men in smock-frocks, drinking and smoking. They took no notice of Oliver; and very little of Sikes; and, as Sikes took very little notice of them, he and his young comrade sat in a corner by themselves, without being much troubled by their company.

text: “That’s right, I am,” said the fellow, who appeared slightly affected—for good or ill, depending on your view—by drink; “and I’m not hanging about, either. My horse has no weight to haul on the return trip, unlike the load he carried up this morning; he’ll make short work of it. Here’s to his health. By God! he’s a fine beast!”
source_text: “Yes, I am,” replied the man, who seemed a little the worse—or better, as the case might be—for drinking; “and not slow about it neither. My horse hasn’t got a load behind him going back, as he had coming up in the mornin’; and he won’t be long a-doing of it. Here’s luck to him. Ecod! he’s a good ’un!”

text: “The water,” shouted the killer, stumbling back inside and slamming the door on those staring faces, “the water was high when I climbed here. Get me a cord, a lengthy cord. Everyone’s out front. I can slip into the Folly Ditch and vanish that direction. Hand me a cord, or I’ll commit three more killings and then end my own life.”
source_text: “The tide,” cried the murderer, as he staggered back into the room, and shut the faces out, “the tide was in as I came up. Give me a rope, a long rope. They’re all in front. I may drop into the Folly Ditch, and clear off that way. Give me a rope, or I shall do three more murders and kill myself.”

text: Having unburdened his thoughts through this revelation, and having achieved his dual purpose—both telling the girl what he had overheard that night and confirming firsthand that Sikes was still absent—Mr. Fagin once more set his course toward home, leaving the young woman slumbering with her head resting on the table.
source_text: Having eased his mind by this discovery; and having accomplished his twofold object of imparting to the girl what he had, that night, heard, and of ascertaining, with his own eyes, that Sikes had not returned, Mr. Fagin again turned his face homeward: leaving his young friend asleep, with her head upon the table.

text: “Quiet!” thundered Master Grimshaw, his voice like a lash. “Remove yourself to the cellar, woman. Silas, bar the door of the workshop; breathe no syllable until your employer returns, if you value your hide; and the moment he sets foot inside, inform him that Master Grimshaw commands the delivery of a pauper’s coffin directly after sunrise. Are you attending, boy? Embracing!” cried Master Grimshaw, flinging his arms aloft. “The depravity and corruption among the rabble of this parish beggars belief! Unless the Crown takes their scandalous habits in hand, the realm is doomed, and the very soul of the labouring class extinguished forever!” And with that, the parish officer swept, with a grand and baleful stride, out of the coffin-maker’s shop.
source_text: “Silence!” cried Mr. Bumble, sternly. “Take yourself downstairs, ma’am. Noah, you shut up the shop; say another word till your master comes home, at your peril; and, when he does come home, tell him that Mr. Bumble said he was to send a old woman’s shell after breakfast tomorrow morning. Do you hear sir? Kissing!” cried Mr. Bumble, holding up his hands. “The sin and wickedness of the lower orders in this porochial district is frightful! If Parliament don’t take their abominable courses under consideration, this country’s ruined, and the character of the peasantry gone for ever!” With these words, the beadle strode, with a lofty and gloomy air, from the undertaker’s premises.

text: At dawn on the seventh day since departing his birthplace, the boy dragged his weary steps into the small market town of Marlow. Every shutter was fastened tight; the roadway lay deserted; no living creature had yet stirred to meet the morning’s trade. The sun climbed upward in a blaze of glory, yet its radiance merely revealed to him the full weight of his solitude and wretchedness as he sank onto a cold stone stoop, his feet raw and his clothes thick with grime.
source_text: Early on the seventh morning after he had left his native place, Oliver limped slowly into the little town of Barnet. The window-shutters were closed; the street was empty; not a soul had awakened to the business of the day. The sun was rising in all its splendid beauty; but the light only served to show the boy his own lonesomeness and desolation, as he sat, with bleeding feet and covered with dust, upon a door-step.

text: “You elderly ladies put your faith in nothing but charlatans and made-up tales,” muttered Mr. Grimwig. “I saw it coming from the start. Why didn’t you listen to me from the outset? You’d have done so if he hadn’t been burning with fever, wouldn’t you? He was so captivating, wasn’t he? Captivating! Rubbish!” And with a dramatic gesture, Mr. Grimwig jabbed at the fire.
source_text: “You old women never believe anything but quack-doctors, and lying story-books,” growled Mr. Grimwig. “I knew it all along. Why didn’t you take my advice in the beginning; you would if he hadn’t had a fever, I suppose, eh? He was interesting, wasn’t he? Interesting! Bah!” And Mr. Grimwig poked the fire with a flourish.

text: After some debate, with all three eagerly joining in, they settled that Nancy would go to Fagin’s the following evening once darkness had fallen and bring Oliver out with her. Fagin slyly noted that if the boy showed any reluctance, he’d be more likely to follow the girl who had lately stepped in to help him than anyone else. They also formally agreed that poor Oliver would, for the planned venture, be placed entirely under the authority of Mr. William Sikes, who could handle him however he saw fit and wouldn’t be accountable to the Jew for any misfortune or harm that might need to be inflicted on him. It was understood that, to make this agreement binding, whatever Sikes reported upon returning would have to be backed up and verified in all key details by the word of flash Toby Crackit.
source_text: After some discussion, in which all three took an active part, it was decided that Nancy should repair to the Jew’s next evening when the night had set in, and bring Oliver away with her; Fagin craftily observing, that, if he evinced any disinclination to the task, he would be more willing to accompany the girl who had so recently interfered in his behalf, than anybody else. It was also solemnly arranged that poor Oliver should, for the purposes of the contemplated expedition, be unreservedly consigned to the care and custody of Mr. William Sikes; and further, that the said Sikes should deal with him as he thought fit; and should not be held responsible by the Jew for any mischance or evil that might be necessary to visit him: it being understood that, to render the compact in this respect binding, any representations made by Mr. Sikes on his return should be required to be confirmed and corroborated, in all important particulars, by the testimony of flash Toby Crackit.

text: “Yes, it is!” the girl shot back, her voice not so much speaking as erupting in a single, furious torrent. “It’s how I stay alive; and the freezing, filthy, rain-soaked streets are where I live; and you’re the monster who pushed me into them years ago, and who’ll keep me trapped there, day and night, day and night, until I’m dead!”
source_text: “Aye, it is!” returned the girl; not speaking, but pouring out the words in one continuous and vehement scream. “It is my living; and the cold, wet, dirty streets are my home; and you’re the wretch that drove me to them long ago, and that’ll keep me there, day and night, day and night, till I die!”

text: Oliver was fully aware that he was tucked inside his own snug chamber; his volumes rested on the desk in front of him; the fragrant breeze rustled through the trailing greenery beyond the window. Still, he had drifted off. In an instant, the surroundings shifted; the atmosphere turned stifling and tight; and he felt, with a flash of dread, that he had returned to the Jew’s dwelling. There, in his usual nook, crouched the grotesque old figure, singling him out and murmuring to a companion whose face was turned away, seated close by.
source_text: Oliver knew, perfectly well, that he was in his own little room; that his books were lying on the table before him; that the sweet air was stirring among the creeping plants outside. And yet he was asleep. Suddenly, the scene changed; the air became close and confined; and he thought, with a glow of terror, that he was in the Jew’s house again. There sat the hideous old man, in his accustomed corner, pointing at him, and whispering to another man, with his face averted, who sat beside him.

text: Mr. Fang was a spare, long-torsoed man of middle height, rigid in the neck, with a scant amount of hair that clung only to the back and sides of his skull. His face was severe and deeply flushed. Had he not truly been accustomed to taking a drop more than was strictly beneficial, he might well have sued his own features for defamation and won substantial compensation.
source_text: Mr. Fang was a lean, long-backed, stiff-necked, middle-sized man, with no great quantity of hair, and what he had, growing on the back and sides of his head. His face was stern, and much flushed. If he were really not in the habit of drinking rather more than was exactly good for him, he might have brought action against his countenance for libel, and have recovered heavy damages.

text: The joy was nearly more than he could endure. Edwin stood dazed and bewildered by the sudden revelation; he could not cry, nor utter a word, nor find stillness. Hardly any faculty remained to grasp what had just unfolded, until, after a long wandering through the hushed twilight lanes, a flood of weeping brought release, and he felt himself stir, as if from a deep trance, into the full knowledge of the glad transformation that had come upon him, and of the almost crushing weight of sorrow that had been lifted from his heart.
source_text: It was almost too much happiness to bear. Oliver felt stunned and stupefied by the unexpected intelligence; he could not weep, or speak, or rest. He had scarcely the power of understanding anything that had passed, until, after a long ramble in the quiet evening air, a burst of tears came to his relief, and he seemed to awaken, all at once, to a full sense of the joyful change that had occurred, and the almost insupportable load of anguish which had been taken from his breast.

text: They remained seated in silence for hours, too fearful to speak. The untouched food was cleared away, their distant stares betraying minds elsewhere, as they observed the sun descend gradually and finally spill across the heavens and the land those vivid colors that announce its leave-taking. Their alert ears detected the noise of footsteps drawing near. Both sprang instinctively toward the door just as Mr. Losberne came in.
source_text: They sat, listening, and afraid to speak, for hours. The untasted meal was removed, with looks which showed that their thoughts were elsewhere, they watched the sun as he sank lower and lower, and, at length, cast over sky and earth those brilliant hues which herald his departure. Their quick ears caught the sound of an approaching footstep. They both involuntarily darted to the door, as Mr. Losberne entered.

text: Mr. Sikes’s fury only mounted at this defiance. Dropping to his knees, he launched a vicious attack upon the creature. The dog dodged frantically from side to side, snarling, snapping, and barking all the while; the man lunged and cursed, slashing and swearing with equal violence. The contest teetered on a decisive brink for one of them—when the door abruptly swung open, and the dog shot out, leaving Bill Sikes clutching the poker in one hand and the clasp-knife in the other.
source_text: This resistance only infuriated Mr. Sikes the more; who, dropping on his knees, began to assail the animal most furiously. The dog jumped from right to left, and from left to right; snapping, growling, and barking; the man thrust and swore, and struck and blasphemed; and the struggle was reaching a most critical point for one or other; when, the door suddenly opening, the dog darted out: leaving Bill Sikes with the poker and the clasp-knife in his hands.

text: Master Bates, overcome by an irrepressible surge of laughter, flung himself onto the floor and spent five minutes kicking in a fit of delirious amusement. Springing upright, he seized the cleft stick from the Dodger and circled Oliver, eyeing him from every angle. Meanwhile, the Jew removed his nightcap and offered the bewildered boy a series of deep, exaggerated bows. The Artful, true to his somber nature and rarely one to indulge in mirth when business was at hand, methodically rifled through Oliver’s pockets with unwavering focus.
source_text: With this irrepressible ebullition of mirth, Master Bates laid himself flat on the floor: and kicked convulsively for five minutes, in an ectasy of facetious joy. Then jumping to his feet, he snatched the cleft stick from the Dodger; and, advancing to Oliver, viewed him round and round; while the Jew, taking off his nightcap, made a great number of low bows to the bewildered boy. The Artful, meantime, who was of a rather saturnine disposition, and seldom gave way to merriment when it interfered with business, rifled Oliver’s pockets with steady assiduity.

text: Rose had not exaggerated the depth of the good doctor’s fury. The moment Nancy’s story was laid bare before him, he unleashed a torrent of curses and threats, vowing to make her the first target of the combined talents of Messrs. Blathers and Duff. He even seized his hat, ready to stride out and enlist those gentlemen’s aid. In that first blaze of rage, he would certainly have acted on the impulse without pausing to weigh the consequences, had he not been checked—partly by an equally fierce resistance from Mr. Brownlow, a man of choleric temper himself, and partly by the kind of reasoned pleas and entreaties best suited to divert him from his rash intent.
source_text: Rose had by no means overrated the measure of the good doctor’s wrath. Nancy’s history was no sooner unfolded to him, than he poured forth a shower of mingled threats and execrations; threatened to make her the first victim of the combined ingenuity of Messrs. Blathers and Duff; and actually put on his hat preparatory to sallying forth to obtain the assistance of those worthies. And, doubtless, he would, in this first outbreak, have carried the intention into effect without a moment’s consideration of the consequences, if he had not been restrained, in part, by corresponding violence on the side of Mr. Brownlow, who was himself of an irascible temperament, and partly by such arguments and representations as seemed best calculated to dissuade him from his hotbrained purpose.

text: Dinner concluded—one need not imagine that Oliver felt any hunger for it—Mr. Sikes downed two cups of liquor mixed with water, then flung himself upon the mattress, charging Nancy, with a torrent of curses should she falter, to wake him at five sharp. Oliver lay fully dressed, at the same man’s bidding, on a pallet spread across the boards; and the young woman, stirring the hearth, settled before it, poised to stir them when the hour struck.
source_text: Supper being ended—it may be easily conceived that Oliver had no great appetite for it—Mr. Sikes disposed of a couple of glasses of spirits and water, and threw himself on the bed; ordering Nancy, with many imprecations in case of failure, to call him at five precisely. Oliver stretched himself in his clothes, by command of the same authority, on a mattress upon the floor; and the girl, mending the fire, sat before it, in readiness to rouse them at the appointed time.

text: Beyond the Dockhead, in the Borough of Southwark, there lies a place called Gideon’s Reach, encircled by a turbid channel—seven or nine feet deep, and eighteen or twenty-two wide at the flood—once named Tanner’s Pool, but in the era of this tale styled Rookery Cut. It is a narrow arm of the Thames, and may be replenished at high water by lifting the gates beside the old Tannery that lent the basin its former title. At such moments, an observer, peering from one of the timbered spans crossing it at Ropewalk Lane, will behold the denizens of the bordering tenements letting down from rear casements and doorways, pails, basins, vessels of every household sort, to draw the water upward; and when his gaze shifts from these exertions to the dwellings themselves, a profound wonder will seize him at the prospect. Rickety wooden balconies shared by the backs of several houses, with gaps through which to spy the ooze beneath; panes, shattered and stuffed, with rods jutting forth to air linens that never appear; chambers so cramped, so foul, so stifled, that the atmosphere seems too corrupted even for the squalor they contain; timbered rooms jutting perilously over the mire, as though ready to tumble in—as certain ones have done; walls grimed with soot and footings crumbling; every repugnant feature of want, every nauseating token of decay, refuse, and rot; all these adorn the margins of Rookery Cut.
source_text: In such a neighborhood, beyond Dockhead in the Borough of Southwark, stands Jacob’s Island, surrounded by a muddy ditch, six or eight feet deep and fifteen or twenty wide when the tide is in, once called Mill Pond, but known in the days of this story as Folly Ditch. It is a creek or inlet from the Thames, and can always be filled at high water by opening the sluices at the Lead Mills from which it took its old name. At such times, a stranger, looking from one of the wooden bridges thrown across it at Mill Lane, will see the inhabitants of the houses on either side lowering from their back doors and windows, buckets, pails, domestic utensils of all kinds, in which to haul the water up; and when his eye is turned from these operations to the houses themselves, his utmost astonishment will be excited by the scene before him. Crazy wooden galleries common to the backs of half a dozen houses, with holes from which to look upon the slime beneath; windows, broken and patched, with poles thrust out, on which to dry the linen that is never there; rooms so small, so filthy, so confined, that the air would seem too tainted even for the dirt and squalor which they shelter; wooden chambers thrusting themselves out above the mud, and threatening to fall into it—as some have done; dirt-besmeared walls and decaying foundations; every repulsive lineament of poverty, every loathsome indication of filth, rot, and garbage; all these ornament the banks of Folly Ditch.

text: “Just imagine,” murmured Silas, lifting his palms and drawing them wide; “just reflect. You’ve performed a deed both elegant and dear to my heart; yet a deed that might fasten the silken cord about your neck—the cord that slips on with such ease and comes off with such agony—in simpler words, the noose!”
source_text: “Only think,” said Fagin, shrugging his shoulders, and stretching out his hands; “only consider. You’ve done what’s a very pretty thing, and what I love you for doing; but what at the same time would put the cravat round your throat, that’s so very easily tied and so very difficult to unloose—in plain English, the halter!”

text: Oliver figured the old gentleman had to be a thorough miser, dwelling in such a grimy den amid all those timepieces; yet, supposing his affection for the Dodger and the other lads might drain his purse considerably, he merely gave the Jew a respectful glance and inquired whether he might rise.
source_text: Oliver thought the old gentleman must be a decided miser to live in such a dirty place, with so many watches; but, thinking that perhaps his fondness for the Dodger and the other boys, cost him a good deal of money, he only cast a deferential look at the Jew, and asked if he might get up.

text: Now and then, when an inquest of uncommon curiosity arose over some workhouse child who had been forgotten while shifting an iron cot, or had carelessly been boiled alive on a washday—though this latter mishap was exceedingly uncommon, since anything resembling a proper laundering seldom troubled the establishment—the jurors would whimsically conceive a desire to pose awkward inquiries, or the local folk would mutinously scratch their names upon a protest. Yet these insolences were swiftly quelled by the surgeon’s report and the beadle’s oath; the first of whom had invariably sliced the little corpse open and detected nothing within (a most plausible finding, to be sure), and the second of whom perpetually attested to whatever the parish required; a most self-sacrificing devotion. Moreover, the board made ceremonial excursions to the farm at fixed seasons, and always dispatched the beadle on the eve of their visit to announce their coming. The children were scrubbed and shining when _they_ arrived; and what further could the populace demand!
source_text: Occasionally, when there was some more than usually interesting inquest upon a parish child who had been overlooked in turning up a bedstead, or inadvertently scalded to death when there happened to be a washing—though the latter accident was very scarce, anything approaching to a washing being of rare occurrence in the farm—the jury would take it into their heads to ask troublesome questions, or the parishioners would rebelliously affix their signatures to a remonstrance. But these impertinences were speedily checked by the evidence of the surgeon, and the testimony of the beadle; the former of whom had always opened the body and found nothing inside (which was very probable indeed), and the latter of whom invariably swore whatever the parish wanted; which was very self-devotional. Besides, the board made periodical pilgrimages to the farm, and always sent the beadle the day before, to say they were going. The children were neat and clean to behold, when _they_ went; and what more would the people have!

text: “There now,” the old gentleman said at last, his voice brightening. “I mention this only because your heart is still young, and knowing the great pain and sorrow I have endured, you may be more mindful not to hurt me again. You tell me you are an orphan, without a single friend in the world; all the inquiries I’ve been able to make bear that out. Let me hear your story—where you came from, who raised you, and how you fell into the company where I discovered you. Speak truthfully, and as long as I live, you will not be friendless.”
source_text: “Well, well!” said the old gentleman at length, in a more cheerful tone, “I only say this, because you have a young heart; and knowing that I have suffered great pain and sorrow, you will be more careful, perhaps, not to wound me again. You say you are an orphan, without a friend in the world; all the inquiries I have been able to make, confirm the statement. Let me hear your story; where you come from; who brought you up; and how you got into the company in which I found you. Speak the truth, and you shall not be friendless while I live.”

text: A scattering of modest shops lined the street, but their only visible merchandise seemed to be clusters of children, who, even at that late hour, were wriggling through doorways or shrieking from within. The only establishments that appeared to thrive in the general decay were the public houses, where the roughest Irish folk brawled at the top of their lungs. Alleyways and courtyards branching off the main thoroughfare revealed tight huddles of dwellings, where drunken men and women sprawled openly in squalor; and from several doorways, hulking, unsavory figures stole forth, clearly bent on errands that were far from kindly or innocent.
source_text: There were a good many small shops; but the only stock in trade appeared to be heaps of children, who, even at that time of night, were crawling in and out at the doors, or screaming from the inside. The sole places that seemed to prosper amid the general blight of the place, were the public-houses; and in them, the lowest orders of Irish were wrangling with might and main. Covered ways and yards, which here and there diverged from the main street, disclosed little knots of houses, where drunken men and women were positively wallowing in filth; and from several of the door-ways, great ill-looking fellows were cautiously emerging, bound, to all appearance, on no very well-disposed or harmless errands.

text: So saying, he climbed the ladder first; then, swiftly shutting the hatch’s wooden cover behind them, he let down a lamp suspended by a cord and tackle threaded through a stout roof-beam overhead. Its faint glow fell upon an aged table and three chairs arranged below.
source_text: Thus speaking, he led the way up the ladder; and hastily closing the window-shutter of the room into which it led, lowered a lantern which hung at the end of a rope and pulley passed through one of the heavy beams in the ceiling: and which cast a dim light upon an old table and three chairs that were placed beneath it.

text: Noah attended school on charity, yet he was no foundling of the workhouse. He was no child of chance, for he could trace his lineage directly to his parents, who lived close by; his mother took in washing, and his father was a besotted soldier, discharged with a wooden leg and a daily pension of twopence-halfpenny plus some unutterable fraction. The local errand-boys had long made it their custom to brand Noah in the open streets with shameful names—“leathers,” “charity,” and the like—and Noah had swallowed these taunts without a word. But now that fortune had thrown in his path a nameless orphan, at whom the lowest could point a scornful finger, he repaid the insult upon him with compound interest. This offers a rich morsel for reflection. It shows us how lovely a thing human nature can be shaped to become, and how evenly the same pleasing traits unfold in the noblest lord and the grubbiest charity-boy.
source_text: Noah was a charity-boy, but not a workhouse orphan. No chance-child was he, for he could trace his genealogy all the way back to his parents, who lived hard by; his mother being a washerwoman, and his father a drunken soldier, discharged with a wooden leg, and a diurnal pension of twopence-halfpenny and an unstateable fraction. The shop-boys in the neighbourhood had long been in the habit of branding Noah in the public streets, with the ignominious epithets of “leathers,” “charity,” and the like; and Noah had borne them without reply. But, now that fortune had cast in his way a nameless orphan, at whom even the meanest could point the finger of scorn, he retorted on him with interest. This affords charming food for contemplation. It shows us what a beautiful thing human nature may be made to be; and how impartially the same amiable qualities are developed in the finest lord and the dirtiest charity-boy.

text: The man cast a look at his two companions—something hovering between a smile and a frown—and motioned them onward once more. He hurried across the spacious but low-ceilinged room and made for a steep stair, little more than a ladder, that climbed to the floor of store-rooms above. Just as he set foot on it, a sharp flash of lightning poured through the opening, and a crack of thunder followed, jarring the rickety building to its core.
source_text: Bestowing something half-way between a smile and a frown upon his two companions, and again beckoning them to follow him, the man hastened across the apartment, which was of considerable extent, but low in the roof. He was preparing to ascend a steep staircase, or rather ladder, leading to another floor of warehouses above: when a bright flash of lightning streamed down the aperture, and a peal of thunder followed, which shook the crazy building to its centre.

text: That was all Oliver could learn about the portrait for the moment. Since the elderly woman had treated him with such kindness during his illness, he tried to push the matter from his mind for now; instead, he gave his full attention to the many tales she shared—of her lovely, gracious daughter, married to a lovely, gracious gentleman and settled in the countryside; and of her son, a clerk for a merchant in the West Indies, who was such an upstanding young man and wrote such devoted letters home four times a year that she wept whenever she spoke of them. Once the old lady had dwelt at length on the virtues of her children, and on the merits of her dear, good husband besides—gone now, poor sweet soul, these twenty-six years—it was time for tea. After tea she began to teach Oliver cribbage, which he picked up as swiftly as she could instruct; and they played the game with deep seriousness and enjoyment until the hour arrived for the patient to take his warm wine and water with a slice of dry toast, and then settle comfortably into bed.
source_text: This was all the information Oliver could obtain about the picture at that time. As the old lady had been so kind to him in his illness, he endeavoured to think no more of the subject just then; so he listened attentively to a great many stories she told him, about an amiable and handsome daughter of hers, who was married to an amiable and handsome man, and lived in the country; and about a son, who was clerk to a merchant in the West Indies; and who was, also, such a good young man, and wrote such dutiful letters home four times a year, that it brought the tears into her eyes to talk about them. When the old lady had expatiated, a long time, on the excellences of her children, and the merits of her kind good husband besides, who had been dead and gone, poor dear soul! just six-and-twenty years, it was time to have tea. After tea she began to teach Oliver cribbage: which he learnt as quickly as she could teach: and at which game they played, with great interest and gravity, until it was time for the invalid to have some warm wine and water, with a slice of dry toast, and then to go cosily to bed.

text: “Forget what it is,” Sikes shot back. “I need a boy, and he can’t be a big one. God!” he added, musing, “if only I had that lad of Ned’s, the chimney sweep’s! He deliberately kept him undersized and hired him out for piecework. But then the father gets arrested, and that Juvenile Delinquent Society steps in, snatches the boy from a paying trade, teaches him his letters, and eventually binds him as an apprentice. And that’s how it goes,” Sikes went on, his anger flaring as he recalled his grievances, “that’s how it goes; and if they had enough money (which thank Heaven they don’t), there wouldn’t be six boys left in the whole business within a year or two.”
source_text: “Never mind wot it is!” replied Sikes. “I want a boy, and he musn’t be a big ’un. Lord!” said Mr. Sikes, reflectively, “if I’d only got that young boy of Ned, the chimbley-sweeper’s! He kept him small on purpose, and let him out by the job. But the father gets lagged; and then the Juvenile Delinquent Society comes, and takes the boy away from a trade where he was earning money, teaches him to read and write, and in time makes a ’prentice of him. And so they go on,” said Mr. Sikes, his wrath rising with the recollection of his wrongs, “so they go on; and, if they’d got money enough (which it’s a Providence they haven’t,) we shouldn’t have half a dozen boys left in the whole trade, in a year or two.”

text: As they walked toward the magistrate’s, Mr. Bumble coached Oliver to appear thoroughly delighted and, the moment the gentleman inquired whether he wished to be bound as an apprentice, to declare that he desired it most earnestly. Oliver promised to follow both instructions, all the more readily because Mr. Bumble added a soft warning that failure on either point might bring consequences too dreadful to name. Once they reached the office, he was left alone in a small room, with Mr. Bumble’s strict order to remain there until he returned to collect him.
source_text: On their way to the magistrate, Mr. Bumble instructed Oliver that all he would have to do, would be to look very happy, and say, when the gentleman asked him if he wanted to be apprenticed, that he should like it very much indeed; both of which injunctions Oliver promised to obey: the rather as Mr. Bumble threw in a gentle hint, that if he failed in either particular, there was no telling what would be done to him. When they arrived at the office, he was shut up in a little room by himself, and admonished by Mr. Bumble to stay there, until he came back to fetch him.

text: For many days, Oliver stayed confined to the Jew’s chamber, diligently removing the stitched marks from pocket-handkerchiefs—great bundles of which were delivered to the house—and occasionally joining in the morning game already described, which the two boys and the Jew observed without fail. In time, he began to yearn for open air and seized every chance to implore the old gentleman to let him venture out to work alongside his two companions.
source_text: For many days, Oliver remained in the Jew’s room, picking the marks out of the pocket-handkerchief, (of which a great number were brought home,) and sometimes taking part in the game already described: which the two boys and the Jew played, regularly, every morning. At length, he began to languish for fresh air, and took many occasions of earnestly entreating the old gentleman to allow him to go out to work with his two companions.

text: Forward they pressed, never pausing to catch their breath, till the foremost man, veering sharply toward a corner of the meadow that Oliver had pointed out, commenced to scrutinize with minute care the bordering ditch and hedgerow. This brief halt allowed the others to draw alongside, and gave Oliver the chance to recount to Mr. Losberne the events that had sparked such a fierce and urgent chase.
source_text: On they all went; nor stopped they once to breathe, until the leader, striking off into an angle of the field indicated by Oliver, began to search, narrowly, the ditch and hedge adjoining; which afforded time for the remainder of the party to come up; and for Oliver to communicate to Mr. Losberne the circumstances that had led to so vigorous a pursuit.

text: The man who snarled these words was a solidly built fellow around thirty-five, wearing a black velveteen jacket, filthy dun-colored trousers, lace-up ankle boots, and gray cotton stockings that sheathed a hefty pair of legs with bulging calves—the sort of legs that, in such attire, always seem oddly bare and incomplete without a set of shackles to adorn them. He had a brown hat on his head and a grimy belcher kerchief tied round his neck, whose long frayed ends he used to wipe the beer from his face as he spoke. When he finished, he revealed a broad, heavy face with a three-day stubble and two glowering eyes, one of which showed various mottled signs of having recently taken a punch.
source_text: The man who growled out these words, was a stoutly-built fellow of about five-and-thirty, in a black velveteen coat, very soiled drab breeches, lace-up half boots, and grey cotton stockings which inclosed a bulky pair of legs, with large swelling calves;—the kind of legs, which in such costume, always look in an unfinished and incomplete state without a set of fetters to garnish them. He had a brown hat on his head, and a dirty belcher handkerchief round his neck: with the long frayed ends of which he smeared the beer from his face as he spoke. He disclosed, when he had done so, a broad heavy countenance with a beard of three days’ growth, and two scowling eyes; one of which displayed various parti-coloured symptoms of having been recently damaged by a blow.

text: The wisdom of such a course stood beyond dispute; yet, unhappily, a single formidable obstacle barred its pursuit. It lay in the fact that the Dodger, Charley Bates, Fagin, and Mr. William Sikes all shared, without exception, a fierce and bone-deep aversion to approaching a police station under any circumstance or excuse whatsoever.
source_text: The prudence of this line of action, indeed, was obvious; but, unfortunately, there was one very strong objection to its being adopted. This was, that the Dodger, and Charley Bates, and Fagin, and Mr. William Sikes, happened, one and all, to entertain a violent and deeply-rooted antipathy to going near a police-office on any ground or pretext whatever.

text: “You appear to believe,” the doctor shot back, “that everyone is determined to be callous today, save yourself, Miss Rose. For the benefit of the coming generation of men, I can only hope that some suitable young gentleman finds you in just such a tender and susceptible frame of mind when he seeks your pity; and I wish I were a young man myself, so I could seize this very moment—and such a perfect chance—to do exactly that.”
source_text: “You seem to think,” retorted the doctor, “that everybody is disposed to be hard-hearted today, except yourself, Miss Rose. I only hope, for the sake of the rising male sex generally, that you may be found in as vulnerable and soft-hearted a mood by the first eligible young fellow who appeals to your compassion; and I wish I were a young fellow, that I might avail myself, on the spot, of such a favourable opportunity for doing so, as the present.”

text: “To the city, madam,” continued the unyielding officer, “by carriage. Myself and two destitute souls, Mrs. Grant! A suit is pending, regarding a residency dispute; and the council has chosen me—me, Mrs. Grant—to attend to the affair at the quarter-sessions in Clerkenwell. And I gravely doubt,” added Mr. Thorne, straightening his frame, “that the Clerkenwell magistrates will not discover themselves gravely mistaken ere they conclude their dealings with me.”
source_text: “To London, ma’am,” resumed the inflexible beadle, “by coach. I and two paupers, Mrs. Mann! A legal action is a coming on, about a settlement; and the board has appointed me—me, Mrs. Mann—to dispose to the matter before the quarter-sessions at Clerkinwell. And I very much question,” added Mr. Bumble, drawing himself up, “whether the Clerkinwell Sessions will not find themselves in the wrong box before they have done with me.”

text: Young Thomas felt a chill seep through his veins as he absorbed the fence’s muttered insinuations, grasping only dimly the sinister possibilities they contained. He had long understood that even the law might, through mere proximity, mistake the blameless for the wicked; and now, recalling the tenor of the quarrels between the old man and Mr. Rook—quarrels that seemed to allude to some prior plot of a similarly fatal nature—he found it entirely plausible that schemes for silencing those who knew too much or spoke too freely had been not only conceived but enacted by the fence on multiple occasions. When he ventured a fearful upward glance and encountered the Jew’s penetrating gaze, he sensed at once that his ashen complexion and quivering frame were observed, and savored, by that shrewd and ancient villain.
source_text: Little Oliver’s blood ran cold, as he listened to the Jew’s words, and imperfectly comprehended the dark threats conveyed in them. That it was possible even for justice itself to confound the innocent with the guilty when they were in accidental companionship, he knew already; and that deeply-laid plans for the destruction of inconveniently knowing or over-communicative persons, had been really devised and carried out by the Jew on more occasions than one, he thought by no means unlikely, when he recollected the general nature of the altercations between that gentleman and Mr. Sikes: which seemed to bear reference to some foregone conspiracy of the kind. As he glanced timidly up, and met the Jew’s searching look, he felt that his pale face and trembling limbs were neither unnoticed nor unrelished by that wary old gentleman.

text: Mr. Bumble exhaled slowly, clearly relieved the tale had concluded without any demand to return the twenty-five pounds. Emboldened at last, he mopped the sweat that had been streaming freely down his nose throughout the entire preceding exchange.
source_text: Mr. Bumble drew a long breath, as if he were glad to find that the story was over, and no mention made of taking the five-and-twenty pounds back again; and now he took courage to wipe the perspiration which had been trickling over his nose, unchecked, during the whole of the previous dialogue.

text: It was strange to pick out certain visages that seemed to rise distinctly from the crowd. At the center sat the host himself—the tavern’s proprietor—a thick-set, vulgar, heavy-handed man, who, while the ditties rang on, let his gaze wander freely around, and, though he feigned full surrender to merriment, missed no gesture enacted nor whisper uttered—and those senses were keen. Close beside him clustered the performers: accepting, with the practiced indifference of their trade, the crowd’s flattery, and drinking one by one from a dozen offered tumblers of gin and water, thrust forward by their louder, rowdier partisans—faces so stamped with nearly every shade and species of vice that their very ugliness seized the eye. Guile, savagery, and intoxication in every degree were on display in their most vivid forms; and women: some with the final ghost of youthful bloom fading even as you watched; others with every trace and sign of womanhood so thoroughly hammered out that nothing remained but a sickening vacancy of debauchery and guilt; a few mere girls, others scarcely grown, none beyond the height of their years—these composed the gloomiest, most pitiable fragment of the whole bleak scene.
source_text: It was curious to observe some faces which stood out prominently from among the group. There was the chairman himself, (the landlord of the house,) a coarse, rough, heavy built fellow, who, while the songs were proceeding, rolled his eyes hither and thither, and, seeming to give himself up to joviality, had an eye for everything that was done, and an ear for everything that was said—and sharp ones, too. Near him were the singers: receiving, with professional indifference, the compliments of the company, and applying themselves, in turn, to a dozen proffered glasses of spirits and water, tendered by their more boisterous admirers; whose countenances, expressive of almost every vice in almost every grade, irresistibly attracted the attention, by their very repulsiveness. Cunning, ferocity, and drunkeness in all its stages, were there, in their strongest aspect; and women: some with the last lingering tinge of their early freshness almost fading as you looked: others with every mark and stamp of their sex utterly beaten out, and presenting but one loathsome blank of profligacy and crime; some mere girls, others but young women, and none past the prime of life; formed the darkest and saddest portion of this dreary picture.

text: In a flash, the other two sprang to their feet, and everyone plunged into hurried, purposeful activity. Sikes and his fellow wrapped thick dark mufflers around their necks and chins, then shrugged on their heavy overcoats; Barney flung open a cupboard, pulled out a handful of objects, and swiftly stuffed them into his pockets.
source_text: In an instant, the other two were on their legs, and all were actively engaged in busy preparation. Sikes and his companion enveloped their necks and chins in large dark shawls, and drew on their great-coats; Barney, opening a cupboard, brought forth several articles, which he hastily crammed into the pockets.

text: The wish was fulfilled at once, as a policeman stepped forward who had observed the prisoner try to pick the pocket of a stranger in a crowd, and actually pull out a handkerchief—which, being quite worn, he calmly returned after dabbing his own face with it. On that account, he arrested the Dodger the moment he could get close, and the said Dodger, when searched, was found carrying a silver snuff-box with the owner’s name inscribed on the lid. This gentleman had been located through the Court Guide, and being present in court, swore the snuff-box belonged to him and that he had noticed it gone the day before, just after freeing himself from the crowd mentioned earlier. He had also noticed a young man in the crush who was especially busy worming through the press, and that young man was the prisoner before him.
source_text: This wish was immediately gratified, for a policeman stepped forward who had seen the prisoner attempt the pocket of an unknown gentleman in a crowd, and indeed take a handkerchief therefrom, which, being a very old one, he deliberately put back again, after trying it on his own countenance. For this reason, he took the Dodger into custody as soon as he could get near him, and the said Dodger, being searched, had upon his person a silver snuff-box, with the owner’s name engraved upon the lid. This gentleman had been discovered on reference to the Court Guide, and being then and there present, swore that the snuff-box was his, and that he had missed it on the previous day, the moment he had disengaged himself from the crowd before referred to. He had also remarked a young gentleman in the throng, particularly active in making his way about, and that young gentleman was the prisoner before him.

text: Engrossed in this harmless diversion, Tobias rested his boots upon the linen cloth and tugged at Edmund’s locks, tweaked his earlobes, and declared him a wretched little spy, vowing moreover to attend the hanging the moment that happy occasion arrived, and embarking on a litany of small torments in the manner of the spiteful, sour-tempered workhouse lad he truly was. Yet, having reduced Edmund to tears, Tobias strove to wax still more droll, and in that striving did what plenty of folk still do nowadays when they wish to amuse: he grew somewhat too familiar.
source_text: Intent upon this innocent amusement, Noah put his feet on the table-cloth; and pulled Oliver’s hair; and twitched his ears; and expressed his opinion that he was a “sneak”; and furthermore announced his intention of coming to see him hanged, whenever that desirable event should take place; and entered upon various topics of petty annoyance, like a malicious and ill-conditioned charity-boy as he was. But, making Oliver cry, Noah attempted to be more facetious still; and in his attempt, did what many sometimes do to this day, when they want to be funny. He got rather personal.

text: He had put another four full miles between himself and London before the weight of his journey truly settled upon him. As the thought pressed in, his stride grew shorter, and he began to reckon what means he had to carry him onward. Inside his bundle lay a heel of bread, a rough shirt, and two pairs of stockings. In his pocket rested a single penny—Sowerberry’s token, given after a funeral where he had served with uncommon steadiness. “A clean shirt,” he mused, “is a fine comfort; and two darned pairs of stockings are a fine comfort; and a penny is a fine comfort; yet they offer but meager aid against sixty-five miles of winter road.” But his mind, nimble and eager as it was to name every hardship, could conjure no clear path around them; and so, after much thought that led nowhere, he shifted his small bundle to the other shoulder and walked on.
source_text: He had diminished the distance between himself and London by full four miles more, before he recollected how much he must undergo ere he could hope to reach his place of destination. As this consideration forced itself upon him, he slackened his pace a little, and meditated upon his means of getting there. He had a crust of bread, a coarse shirt, and two pairs of stockings, in his bundle. He had a penny too—a gift of Sowerberry’s after some funeral in which he had acquitted himself more than ordinarily well—in his pocket. “A clean shirt,” thought Oliver, “is a very comfortable thing; and so are two pairs of darned stockings; and so is a penny; but they are small helps to a sixty-five miles’ walk in winter time.” But Oliver’s thoughts, like those of most other people, although they were extremely ready and active to point out his difficulties, were wholly at a loss to suggest any feasible mode of surmounting them; so, after a good deal of thinking to no particular purpose, he changed his little bundle over to the other shoulder, and trudged on.

text: For all that, once the initial flurry of half an hour had passed, the same mute tension and reserve that had attended their downward journey settled over them again. Mr. Brownlow took no meal with the company, but kept to his own chamber. The two other gentlemen passed in and out with troubled looks, and during the brief moments they appeared, they drew aside and murmured together. Once, Mrs. Maylie was summoned from the room, and after nearly an hour’s absence, came back with eyes red and swollen from tears. All this left Rose and Oliver, who were uninitiated in any fresh secret, restless and ill at ease. They sat wondering in wordlessness; or if they ventured a few exchanges, their speech dropped to whispers, as though they dreaded the very sound of their own voices.
source_text: Notwithstanding all this, when the hurry of the first half-hour was over, the same silence and constraint prevailed that had marked their journey down. Mr. Brownlow did not join them at dinner, but remained in a separate room. The two other gentlemen hurried in and out with anxious faces, and, during the short intervals when they were present, conversed apart. Once, Mrs. Maylie was called away, and after being absent for nearly an hour, returned with eyes swollen with weeping. All these things made Rose and Oliver, who were not in any new secrets, nervous and uncomfortable. They sat wondering, in silence; or, if they exchanged a few words, spoke in whispers, as if they were afraid to hear the sound of their own voices.

text: “I returned,” Mr. Ashworth said, after a brief silence, “I returned, once it had ended, to the place of his—I shall speak the word the world would not hesitate to use, since worldly censure or grace mean nothing to him now—of his sinful passion, determined that if my dread proved true, that fallen girl would discover one soul and hearth to shield and pity her. The household had departed that district a fortnight earlier; they had gathered whatever petty obligations remained unpaid, settled them, and stolen away under cover of darkness. Why they fled, or where, no one can say.”
source_text: “I went,” said Mr. Brownlow, after a short pause, “I went, when all was over, to the scene of his—I will use the term the world would freely use, for worldly harshness or favour are now alike to him—of his guilty love, resolved that if my fears were realised that erring child should find one heart and home to shelter and compassionate her. The family had left that part a week before; they had called in such trifling debts as were outstanding, discharged them, and left the place by night. Why, or whither, none can tell.”

text: Following through on this decision, little Oliver, to his utter amazement, was freed from his confinement and told to change into a fresh shirt. He had barely completed this remarkably unfamiliar physical feat when Mr. Bumble personally delivered a bowl of gruel and the special holiday portion of two and a quarter ounces of bread. Confronted by this overwhelming sight, Oliver began to weep most sorrowfully, convinced, not unreasonably, that the board must have resolved to dispatch him for some practical end—otherwise they would never have started to fatten him up in such a manner.
source_text: In pursuance of this determination, little Oliver, to his excessive astonishment, was released from bondage, and ordered to put himself into a clean shirt. He had hardly achieved this very unusual gymnastic performance, when Mr. Bumble brought him, with his own hands, a basin of gruel, and the holiday allowance of two ounces and a quarter of bread. At this tremendous sight, Oliver began to cry very piteously: thinking, not unnaturally, that the board must have determined to kill him for some useful purpose, or they never would have begun to fatten him up in that way.

text: “A fool,” the physician murmured once more, when another long stillness had passed. “Suppose the spot had been correct, and the proper men had stood ready—what could I have accomplished alone? And even with help at my side, I can discern no benefit I might have wrought, save to lay bare my own part, and force a confession of how I have buried this affair. That would have been just recompense, of course. I am forever entangling myself in some predicament, by yielding to whim. It might have taught me a lesson.”
source_text: “An ass,” said the doctor again, after a further silence of some minutes. “Even if it had been the right place, and the right fellows had been there, what could I have done, single-handed? And if I had had assistance, I see no good that I should have done, except leading to my own exposure, and an unavoidable statement of the manner in which I have hushed up this business. That would have served me right, though. I am always involving myself in some scrape or other, by acting on impulse. It might have done me good.”

text: Having settled the matter of these ill-intentioned folk for the evening, Mr. Bumble installed himself in the inn where the coach had halted and partook of a modest repast of steaks, oyster sauce, and porter. After placing a glass of hot gin-and-water on the mantelpiece, he pulled his chair closer to the fire and, interspersing the act with sundry moralisings on the pervasive vice of discontent and grumbling, settled down to peruse the newspaper.
source_text: Having disposed of these evil-minded persons for the night, Mr. Bumble sat himself down in the house at which the coach stopped; and took a temperate dinner of steaks, oyster sauce, and porter. Putting a glass of hot gin-and-water on the chimney-piece, he drew his chair to the fire; and, with sundry moral reflections on the too-prevalent sin of discontent and complaining, composed himself to read the paper.

text: “They lived,” Mr. Brownlow continued, as though the interruption had never reached him, “in a corner of the countryside where your father, in his roaming, had finally settled and made his home. Acquaintance ripened swiftly into closeness, and closeness into friendship. Your father possessed gifts granted to very few. He carried within him his sister’s spirit and her very likeness. As the old officer came to know him better, his affection deepened. I wish with all my heart it had stopped there. His daughter’s heart followed the same path.”
source_text: “They resided,” said Mr. Brownlow, without seeming to hear the interruption, “in a part of the country to which your father in his wandering had repaired, and where he had taken up his abode. Acquaintance, intimacy, friendship, fast followed on each other. Your father was gifted as few men are. He had his sister’s soul and person. As the old officer knew him more and more, he grew to love him. I would that it had ended there. His daughter did the same.”

text: The Hebrew gave a slight turn of his head, and was on the verge of answering, when the newcomer cut him short with a gesture toward the dwelling they had now reached, observing that it would be wiser to speak his piece indoors, since the long halt had frozen his veins and the gale cut straight through his frame.
source_text: The Jew shook his head, and was about to reply, when the stranger, interrupting him, motioned to the house, before which they had by this time arrived: remarking, that he had better say what he had got to say, under cover: for his blood was chilled with standing about so long, and the wind blew through him.

text: “Not at all, not at all, my love!” the old man resumed. “Nor do I resent it in this moment; for had it never come to pass, you might never have laid your gaze upon the lad to mark him, and thus been guided to the revelation that he was the very one you sought. There now! I reclaimed him for your sake through the girl; and then _she_ commences to show him fondness.”
source_text: “No, no, my dear!” renewed the Jew. “And I don’t quarrel with it now; because, if it had never happened, you might never have clapped eyes on the boy to notice him, and so led to the discovery that it was him you were looking for. Well! I got him back for you by means of the girl; and then _she_ begins to favour him.”

text: Young Charley Bates, horrified by what Sikes had done, found himself pondering whether a decent life wasn’t, when all was said and done, the wiser choice. Once he’d settled on the firm belief that it was, he walked away from his old haunts, determined to make a fresh start in some different line of work. For a while he battled hard and endured plenty of hardship, but with a cheerful spirit and a steady resolve, he eventually prevailed; and having once been a farmhand and a carter’s boy, he’s now the jolliest young cattleman in the whole of Northamptonshire.
source_text: Master Charles Bates, appalled by Sikes’s crime, fell into a train of reflection whether an honest life was not, after all, the best. Arriving at the conclusion that it certainly was, he turned his back upon the scenes of the past, resolved to amend it in some new sphere of action. He struggled hard, and suffered much, for some time; but, having a contented disposition, and a good purpose, succeeded in the end; and, from being a farmer’s drudge, and a carrier’s lad, he is now the merriest young grazier in all Northamptonshire.

text: “This young woman,” the gentleman said, “has shared with me—and with a few other friends whose discretion is assured—what you confided to her almost two weeks ago. I admit I was uncertain at first whether I could place full trust in your word, but I am now thoroughly convinced of your reliability.”
source_text: “This young lady,” the gentleman began, “has communicated to me, and to some other friends who can be safely trusted, what you told her nearly a fortnight since. I confess to you that I had doubts, at first, whether you were to be implicitly relied upon, but now I firmly believe you are.”

text: “Quiet!” the man shouted. “That won’t work in this place. One more sound out of you, and I’ll settle you myself with a blow to the skull. It’s silent, just as reliable, and far more refined. Now, Bill, pry that shutter loose. He’s plucky enough by this point, I’d wager. I’ve watched seasoned men twice his years go just as stiff, for a brief spell, on a chilly evening.”
source_text: “Hush!” cried the man; “it won’t answer here. Say another word, and I’ll do your business myself with a crack on the head. That makes no noise, and is quite as certain, and more genteel. Here, Bill, wrench the shutter open. He’s game enough now, I’ll engage. I’ve seen older hands of his age took the same way, for a minute or two, on a cold night.”

text: Left alone in the undertaker’s workshop, Oliver set the lamp on a workbench and peered around with a creeping dread that many far older than he would readily recognize. An unfinished coffin resting on black trestles stood in the center of the room, so grim and corpselike that each time his gaze strayed toward the dismal thing a cold shudder ran through him; he half expected some hideous shape to lift its head slowly and drive him senseless with fear. Along the wall, arranged in rigid order, leaned a long row of elm planks cut to the same outline, which in the dim glow resembled high-shouldered specters with hands thrust into their pockets. Coffin plates, chips of elm, bright-headed nails, and scraps of black cloth lay scattered across the floor, while the wall behind the counter was adorned with a vivid picture of two mutes in very rigid neckcloths, stationed at a grand private entrance, with a hearse drawn by four black horses approaching from the distance. The shop was stuffy and stifling. The air seemed steeped in the odor of coffins. The hollow beneath the counter where his flock mattress had been shoved looked like a grave.
source_text: Oliver, being left to himself in the undertaker’s shop, set the lamp down on a workman’s bench, and gazed timidly about him with a feeling of awe and dread, which many people a good deal older than he will be at no loss to understand. An unfinished coffin on black tressels, which stood in the middle of the shop, looked so gloomy and death-like that a cold tremble came over him, every time his eyes wandered in the direction of the dismal object: from which he almost expected to see some frightful form slowly rear its head, to drive him mad with terror. Against the wall were ranged, in regular array, a long row of elm boards cut in the same shape: looking in the dim light, like high-shouldered ghosts with their hands in their breeches pockets. Coffin-plates, elm-chips, bright-headed nails, and shreds of black cloth, lay scattered on the floor; and the wall behind the counter was ornamented with a lively representation of two mutes in very stiff neckcloths, on duty at a large private door, with a hearse drawn by four black steeds, approaching in the distance. The shop was close and hot. The atmosphere seemed tainted with the smell of coffins. The recess beneath the counter in which his flock mattress was thrust, looked like a grave.

text: Oh, the torment—the sharp, unbearable torment—of waiting helplessly while someone we cherish hovers between life and death! Oh, the frantic ideas that storm the mind, making the heart pound fiercely and the breath come in shallow gasps, driven by the visions they summon before us; the wild urge *to act* to soothe the suffering or reduce the peril we are powerless to ease; the draining of hope and courage brought on by the bitter awareness of our own uselessness—what agony compares to this? What thoughts or efforts, in the surging heat of that moment, can quiet it!
source_text: Oh! the suspense, the fearful, acute suspense, of standing idly by while the life of one we dearly love, is trembling in the balance! Oh! the racking thoughts that crowd upon the mind, and make the heart beat violently, and the breath come thick, by the force of the images they conjure up before it; the desperate anxiety _to be doing something_ to relieve the pain, or lessen the danger, which we have no power to alleviate; the sinking of soul and spirit, which the sad remembrance of our helplessness produces; what tortures can equal these; what reflections or endeavours can, in the full tide and fever of the time, allay them!

text: Let the tears that streamed down and the fragmented whispers shared during that long, tight clasp between the orphans remain hallowed. In that single instant, a father, a sister, a mother were both found and taken away. Sorrow and gladness swirled together in the same draught, yet no sharp tears fell; for even the grief surfaced so mellowed, wrapped in such gentle, cherished memories, that it transformed into a quiet joy, stripped of all suffering.
source_text: Let the tears which fell, and the broken words which were exchanged in the long close embrace between the orphans, be sacred. A father, sister, and mother, were gained, and lost, in that one moment. Joy and grief were mingled in the cup; but there were no bitter tears: for even grief itself arose so softened, and clothed in such sweet and tender recollections, that it became a solemn pleasure, and lost all character of pain.

text: It was nearly midnight. The night was black and bitterly cold, offering him little reason to linger. A biting wind swept through the streets, scouring them as clean of people as of dust and grime; scarcely a soul was out, and those few seemed hurrying briskly home. Yet the wind came from a favorable direction for the Jew, and he pressed straight into it, trembling and shuddering as each new gust shoved him roughly forward.
source_text: It was within an hour of midnight. The weather being dark, and piercing cold, he had no great temptation to loiter. The sharp wind that scoured the streets, seemed to have cleared them of passengers, as of dust and mud, for few people were abroad, and they were to all appearance hastening fast home. It blew from the right quarter for the Jew, however, and straight before it he went: trembling, and shivering, as every fresh gust drove him rudely on his way.

text: As dawn crept forward, the chill deepened, and the fog drifted low across the earth like a heavy pall of smoke. The grass glistened with moisture; the tracks and hollows had turned to mud and standing water; a clammy, sickly wind dragged past with a low, empty groan. Yet Oliver remained where Sikes had abandoned him, still and unfeeling on the cold ground.
source_text: The air grew colder, as day came slowly on; and the mist rolled along the ground like a dense cloud of smoke. The grass was wet; the pathways, and low places, were all mire and water; the damp breath of an unwholesome wind went languidly by, with a hollow moaning. Still, Oliver lay motionless and insensible on the spot where Sikes had left him.

text: Muttering these and similar thoughts to himself, the Jew returned the watch to its secure hiding spot. He then pulled out at least six more from the same case, examining each with the same delight, along with rings, brooches, bracelets, and other pieces of jewelry so rich in material and exquisite in craft that Oliver couldn't even guess what they were called.
source_text: With these, and other muttered reflections of the like nature, the Jew once more deposited the watch in its place of safety. At least half a dozen more were severally drawn forth from the same box, and surveyed with equal pleasure; besides rings, brooches, bracelets, and other articles of jewellery, of such magnificent materials, and costly workmanship, that Oliver had no idea, even of their names.

text: The situation here had not improved in the slightest. Sowerberry was still absent, and Oliver kept up his relentless assault on the cellar door with undiminished energy. The tales of his savagery, as recounted by Mrs. Sowerberry and Charlotte, were so alarming that Mr. Bumble deemed it wise to negotiate before unlocking it. To this end, he delivered a kick to the outer panel as a preliminary gesture, then pressed his mouth to the keyhole and intoned in a deep, commanding voice:
source_text: Here the position of affairs had not at all improved. Sowerberry had not yet returned, and Oliver continued to kick, with undiminished vigour, at the cellar-door. The accounts of his ferocity as related by Mrs. Sowerberry and Charlotte, were of so startling a nature, that Mr. Bumble judged it prudent to parley, before opening the door. With this view he gave a kick at the outside, by way of prelude; and, then, applying his mouth to the keyhole, said, in a deep and impressive tone:

text: “He’s absolutely right,” remarked Blathers, bobbing his head in agreement while idly flipping the handcuffs like they were a set of castanets. “Who’s the lad? What story does he tell? Where’s he from? He didn’t just fall from the sky, did he, sir?”
source_text: “What he says is quite correct,” observed Blathers, nodding his head in a confirmatory way, and playing carelessly with the handcuffs, as if they were a pair of castanets. “Who is the boy? What account does he give of himself? Where did he come from? He didn’t drop out of the clouds, did he, master?”

text: He’d barely finished scrubbing his face and straightening the room, tipping the washbowl through the sash just as the old man had instructed, when the Dodger came back, this time with a lively companion in tow—the same boy Oliver had noticed puffing smoke the evening before, now properly presented as Charley Bates. The four of them settled around the table for breakfast, sharing coffee and the warm rolls and ham the Dodger had carried home balanced in the upturned crown of his hat.
source_text: He had scarcely washed himself, and made everything tidy, by emptying the basin out of the window, agreeably to the Jew’s directions, when the Dodger returned: accompanied by a very sprightly young friend, whom Oliver had seen smoking on the previous night, and who was now formally introduced to him as Charley Bates. The four sat down, to breakfast, on the coffee, and some hot rolls and ham which the Dodger had brought home in the crown of his hat.

text: As the carriage sped forward, Oliver glimpsed a figure in a white nightcap whose face stirred a vague sense of recognition, though the glance was too fleeting for him to place the man. Within moments, the nightcap jutted from the chaise window, and a booming voice roared for the driver to halt—which he did as swiftly as he could rein in the horses. Then the nightcap surfaced again, and the same voice shouted Oliver’s name.
source_text: As it dashed on, Oliver caught a glimpse of a man in a white nightcap, whose face seemed familiar to him, although his view was so brief that he could not identify the person. In another second or two, the nightcap was thrust out of the chaise-window, and a stentorian voice bellowed to the driver to stop: which he did, as soon as he could pull up his horses. Then, the nightcap once again appeared: and the same voice called Oliver by his name.

text: Fagin seemed as though he would have gladly avoided escorting a guest home at such an untimely moment; in fact, he grumbled something about lacking a fire. But when his companion reiterated the demand in an authoritative tone, he unlatched the door and asked him to shut it quietly while he fetched a light.
source_text: Fagin looked as if he could have willingly excused himself from taking home a visitor at that unseasonable hour; and, indeed, muttered something about having no fire; but his companion repeating his request in a peremptory manner, he unlocked the door, and requested him to close it softly, while he got a light.

text: Had it not been for a kindly tollgate keeper and a charitable old woman, Oliver’s miseries might well have ended just as his mother’s had—struck down lifeless upon the public road.
