The tollgate keeper offered him a portion of bread and cheese, and the old woman, whose own shipwrecked grandson roamed barefoot in some far corner of the world, looked with pity on the friendless orphan. She gave him what modest help she could spare—and more besides—along with words so tender and tears of such heartfelt sympathy that they carved themselves deeper into Oliver’s heart than all the hardships he had ever borne.
source_text: In fact, if it had not been for a good-hearted turnpike-man, and a benevolent old lady, Oliver’s troubles would have been shortened by the very same process which had put an end to his mother’s; in other words, he would most assuredly have fallen dead upon the king’s highway. But the turnpike-man gave him a meal of bread and cheese; and the old lady, who had a shipwrecked grandson wandering barefoot in some distant part of the earth, took pity upon the poor orphan, and gave him what little she could afford—and more—with such kind and gentle words, and such tears of sympathy and compassion, that they sank deeper into Oliver’s soul, than all the sufferings he had ever undergone.

text: In prominent households, when no suitable position—whether immediate, future, contingent, or anticipated—can be secured for a boy coming of age, it is quite customary to pack him off to sea. The board, following this prudent and time-honored precedent, deliberated on the wisdom of dispatching Oliver Twist aboard some modest merchant vessel bound for a reliably insalubrious port. The idea presented itself as the finest possible solution for the boy: the odds being that the captain, in a fit of whimsy after dinner one day, would either whip him to death or stove in his skull with an iron rod—both diversions being, as is widely acknowledged, especially cherished and habitual among gentlemen of that profession. The more the board turned the matter over from this angle, the more numerous the benefits of the measure appeared; thus they resolved that the sole effective means of settling Oliver's future was to send him to sea forthwith.
source_text: In great families, when an advantageous place cannot be obtained, either in possession, reversion, remainder, or expectancy, for the young man who is growing up, it is a very general custom to send him to sea. The board, in imitation of so wise and salutary an example, took counsel together on the expediency of shipping off Oliver Twist, in some small trading vessel bound to a good unhealthy port. This suggested itself as the very best thing that could possibly be done with him: the probability being, that the skipper would flog him to death, in a playful mood, some day after dinner, or would knock his brains out with an iron bar; both pastimes being, as is pretty generally known, very favourite and common recreations among gentleman of that class. The more the case presented itself to the board, in this point of view, the more manifold the advantages of the step appeared; so, they came to the conclusion that the only way of providing for Oliver effectually, was to send him to sea without delay.

text: The pigeon then lifted his collar against the chill, adjusted his tricorn, and, after sharing a lingering and tender clasp with his intended bride, stepped once more into the biting night air. He halted briefly in the men’s poorhouse dormitory, solely to harangue its inmates with a few sharp words, convincing himself that he possessed the requisite harshness to serve as the institution’s master. Satisfied on this point, Mr. Pigeon departed the premises with a buoyant spirit, his thoughts alight with gleaming prospects of advancement, which carried him along until he arrived at the coffin-maker’s door.
source_text: The dove then turned up his coat-collar, and put on his cocked hat; and, having exchanged a long and affectionate embrace with his future partner, once again braved the cold wind of the night: merely pausing, for a few minutes, in the male paupers’ ward, to abuse them a little, with the view of satisfying himself that he could fill the office of workhouse-master with needful acerbity. Assured of his qualifications, Mr. Bumble left the building with a light heart, and bright visions of his future promotion: which served to occupy his mind until he reached the shop of the undertaker.

text: “I heard it now, plain as day,” Mr. Giles went on. “‘Someone,’ I said, ‘is breaking in a door or a window; what am I to do? I’ll rouse that poor boy Brittles, and spare him from being murdered in his sleep; otherwise,’ I said, ‘his throat might be slit from right ear to left, and he’d never even know it.’”
source_text: “I heerd it now, quite apparent,” resumed Mr. Giles. “‘Somebody,’ I says, ‘is forcing of a door, or window; what’s to be done? I’ll call up that poor lad, Brittles, and save him from being murdered in his bed; or his throat,’ I says, ‘may be cut from his right ear to his left, without his ever knowing it.’”

text: The old man had reached the street corner before the full weight of Toby Crackit’s news began to settle upon him. He had not slackened his unnatural haste, but still drove forward with that same disordered urgency, when a carriage hurtled past and a clamour of warning from pedestrians who saw his peril forced him back onto the pavement. Steering clear of the principal thoroughfares wherever he could, and slinking only through back lanes and narrow passages, he at last came out upon Snow Hill. There his stride quickened even more; he did not pause until he had turned aside into a court, where, as though sensing he had regained his native element, he dropped into his customary shambling gait and seemed to draw an easier breath.
source_text: The old man had gained the street corner, before he began to recover the effect of Toby Crackit’s intelligence. He had relaxed nothing of his unusual speed; but was still pressing onward, in the same wild and disordered manner, when the sudden dashing past of a carriage: and a boisterous cry from the foot passengers, who saw his danger: drove him back upon the pavement. Avoiding, as much as was possible, all the main streets, and skulking only through the by-ways and alleys, he at length emerged on Snow Hill. Here he walked even faster than before; nor did he linger until he had again turned into a court; when, as if conscious that he was now in his proper element, he fell into his usual shuffling pace, and seemed to breathe more freely.

text: Stirred by a fresh surge of vigor and drive, and spurred on by the clamor inside the house signaling a genuine breach, he braced his foot against the chimney stack, lashed one end of the rope securely around it, and worked the other into a tight slipknot with his hands and teeth in barely a moment. The line would lower him to a point less than his own height from the ground, where he could slice it with the knife already poised in his grip and drop free.
source_text: Roused into new strength and energy, and stimulated by the noise within the house which announced that an entrance had really been effected, he set his foot against the stack of chimneys, fastened one end of the rope tightly and firmly round it, and with the other made a strong running noose by the aid of his hands and teeth almost in a second. He could let himself down by the cord to within a less distance of the ground than his own height, and had his knife ready in his hand to cut it then and drop.

text: “I would like,” he remarked, “to enlist the help of my friend Grimwig. He is an odd fellow, yet a sharp one, and could be of real use to us; I should mention that he was trained for the law, but abandoned the profession in disgust after earning only a single brief and one routine motion in twenty years—though whether that speaks in his favor, you must judge for yourselves.”
source_text: “I should like,” he said, “to call in the aid of my friend Grimwig. He is a strange creature, but a shrewd one, and might prove of material assistance to us; I should say that he was bred a lawyer, and quitted the Bar in disgust because he had only one brief and a motion of course, in twenty years, though whether that is recommendation or not, you must determine for yourselves.”

text: Mr. Bumble stepped out through the workhouse gate at dawn and strode up the High Street with a stately bearing and authoritative tread. He stood at the very peak of his beadle’s glory; his tricorne hat and coat gleamed brilliantly in the early sunlight, and he gripped his walking stick with the firm vigor of robust vitality. Mr. Bumble habitually held his chin aloft, but this morning it rose even higher than normal. A faraway look in his gaze and a loftiness in his demeanor might have signaled to any perceptive passerby that ideas were stirring in the beadle’s head—thoughts far too momentous to be spoken aloud.
source_text: Mr. Bumble emerged at early morning from the workhouse-gate, and walked with portly carriage and commanding steps, up the High Street. He was in the full bloom and pride of beadlehood; his cocked hat and coat were dazzling in the morning sun; he clutched his cane with the vigorous tenacity of health and power. Mr. Bumble always carried his head high; but this morning it was higher than usual. There was an abstraction in his eye, an elevation in his air, which might have warned an observant stranger that thoughts were passing in the beadle’s mind, too great for utterance.

text: The visitor who had rapped at the door was a thickset figure of moderate stature, around fifty years old, his black hair gleaming and trimmed quite short, sporting partial sideburns, a rounded face, and keen eyes. His companion was a gaunt, red-haired man in tall riding boots, possessing a somewhat unpleasant visage and a nose with an unsettling upward tilt.
source_text: The man who had knocked at the door, was a stout personage of middle height, aged about fifty: with shiny black hair, cropped pretty close; half-whiskers, a round face, and sharp eyes. The other was a red-headed, bony man, in top-boots; with a rather ill-favoured countenance, and a turned-up sinister-looking nose.

text: Having delivered this witticism, Mr. Thistlewick lifted his bowler from its hook and set it at a jaunty slant, the gesture of a gentleman convinced he had elegantly affirmed his own ascendancy. He then buried his fists in his coat pockets and ambled toward the exit, his entire bearing radiating an air of smug amusement.
source_text: As he discharged himself of this pleasantry, Mr. Bumble took his hat from a peg, and putting it on, rather rakishly, on one side, as a man might, who felt he had asserted his superiority in a becoming manner, thrust his hands into his pockets, and sauntered towards the door, with much ease and waggishness depicted in his whole appearance.

text: Toby agreed, and they rushed along the empty main street of the small town, abandoned at such a late hour. Here and there, a faint glow flickered from an upstairs window, while the rough barking of dogs now and then shattered the quiet darkness. Yet not a soul stirred outside. Just as the church clock chimed two, they left the town behind.
source_text: Toby acquiesced; and they hurried through the main street of the little town, which at that late hour was wholly deserted. A dim light shone at intervals from some bed-room window; and the hoarse barking of dogs occasionally broke the silence of the night. But there was nobody abroad. They had cleared the town, as the church-bell struck two.

text: Charlotte’s punch carried considerable force; yet, fearing it might not suffice to quell Oliver’s fury, Mrs. Sowerberry rushed into the kitchen and helped pin him down with one hand while raking his face with the other. Seizing this advantageous moment, Noah scrambled up from the floor and pummeled him from behind.
source_text: Charlotte’s fist was by no means a light one; but, lest it should not be effectual in calming Oliver’s wrath, Mrs. Sowerberry plunged into the kitchen, and assisted to hold him with one hand, while she scratched his face with the other. In this favourable position of affairs, Noah rose from the ground, and pommelled him behind.

text: As the blackness gathered, his mind turned to the many souls he had watched perish at the gallows—several sent there by his own hand. They surfaced so rapidly he could scarcely tally them. He had witnessed a few of their final moments, and even mocked them, for they met death with whispered devotions. How the trapdoor clattered as it fell; how swiftly they transformed, from hale and living men into swaying bundles of cloth!
source_text: As it came on very dark, he began to think of all the men he had known who had died upon the scaffold; some of them through his means. They rose up, in such quick succession, that he could hardly count them. He had seen some of them die,—and had joked too, because they died with prayers upon their lips. With what a rattling noise the drop went down; and how suddenly they changed, from strong and vigorous men to dangling heaps of clothes!

text: “I bear no blame if he is!” the other broke in, his face stricken with dread, his trembling fingers gripping the Jew’s arm. “Remember that, Fagin. I had no part in it. I warned you from the start—anything but his death. I’ll spill no blood; it’s always uncovered in the end, and it clings to a man, haunts him besides. If they’ve shot him dead, it was none of my doing—do you hear me? Burn this cursed hole to the ground! What was that?”
source_text: “It’s no fault of mine if he is!” interposed the other man, with a look of terror, and clasping the Jew’s arm with trembling hands. “Mind that. Fagin! I had no hand in it. Anything but his death, I told you from the first. I won’t shed blood; it’s always found out, and haunts a man besides. If they shot him dead, I was not the cause; do you hear me? Fire this infernal den! What’s that?”

text: Oliver studied the old lady with growing concern and noticed that the signs of illness had frightened her—just as they had unsettled him. Yet, seeing her pretend to dismiss them lightly, he tried to match her composure, and between them they managed well enough that when Rose’s aunt convinced her to withdraw for the night, she seemed brighter and even looked a little stronger, promising them she was sure she would wake in the morning perfectly recovered.
source_text: Oliver, who watched the old lady anxiously, observed that she was alarmed by these appearances; and so in truth, was he; but seeing that she affected to make light of them, he endeavoured to do the same, and they so far succeeded, that when Rose was persuaded by her aunt to retire for the night, she was in better spirits; and appeared even in better health: assuring them that she felt certain she should rise in the morning, quite well.

text: “The grand aristocrats,” Harry answered, “by which term I gather you mean my exceedingly dignified uncle, have sent me no word at all since I arrived; and at this season, it’s hardly probable that any matter would arise requiring my prompt presence in their company.”
source_text: “The great nobs,” replied Harry, “under which designation, I presume, you include my most stately uncle, have not communicated with me at all, since I have been here; nor, at this time of the year, is it likely that anything would occur to render necessary my immediate attendance among them.”

text: “Haste!” she urged. “Bid them summon a cab at once, and prepare to accompany me. I shall lead you thither straightaway, without the waste of a single moment. I need merely inform my aunt that we step abroad for an hour, and shall be set the instant you are.”
source_text: “Quick!” she said. “Tell them to fetch a hackney-coach, and be ready to go with me. I will take you there directly, without a minute’s loss of time. I will only tell my aunt that we are going out for an hour, and be ready as soon as you are.”

text: “No,” he declared, slamming his fist upon the table, “I do not. The lad has fresh garments on his back, a collection of fine books beneath his arm, and a five-pound note tucked in his pocket. He’ll fall in with his old companions, the thieves, and mock you. Should that boy ever set foot in this house again, sir, I’ll devour my own head.”
source_text: “No,” he said, smiting the table with his fist, “I do not. The boy has a new suit of clothes on his back, a set of valuable books under his arm, and a five-pound note in his pocket. He’ll join his old friends the thieves, and laugh at you. If ever that boy returns to this house, sir, I’ll eat my head.”

text: Master Harrow strode forward in wide, swinging steps; young Pip clung tightly to the braided hem of his sleeve, scurrying to keep pace and asking after every few hundred yards if they had “almost arrived.” To these questions Master Harrow gave only curt and waspish answers, for the fleeting warmth that a draught of brandy kindles in certain hearts had now faded entirely, and he was once more a parish officer.
source_text: Mr. Bumble walked on with long strides; little Oliver, firmly grasping his gold-laced cuff, trotted beside him, inquiring at the end of every quarter of a mile whether they were “nearly there.” To these interrogations Mr. Bumble returned very brief and snappish replies; for the temporary blandness which gin-and-water awakens in some bosoms had by this time evaporated; and he was once again a beadle.

text: He scrambled down quickly just as the chamber door swung wide and the young woman stepped forth. Fagin tugged him behind a slender, cloth-draped screen, and they stifled their breathing while she walked mere inches from their hiding spot, then slipped out through the same entrance they had used.
source_text: He hastily descended, as the room-door opened, and the girl came out. Fagin drew him behind a small partition which was curtained off, and they held their breaths as she passed within a few feet of their place of concealment, and emerged by the door at which they had entered.

text: During the initial half-year following Oliver Twist’s departure, the scheme ran at full tilt. At the outset it proved somewhat costly, owing to a steep rise in the undertaker’s charges and the need to alter the paupers’ garments, which, after a fortnight of thin gruel, hung slackly on their gaunt and dwindling frames. Yet the workhouse population thinned out along with the paupers themselves, and the board was enraptured.
source_text: For the first six months after Oliver Twist was removed, the system was in full operation. It was rather expensive at first, in consequence of the increase in the undertaker’s bill, and the necessity of taking in the clothes of all the paupers, which fluttered loosely on their wasted, shrunken forms, after a week or two’s gruel. But the number of workhouse inmates got thin as well as the paupers; and the board were in ecstasies.

text: “Then he must pass under a different name among us,” the girl answered, “something I already half believed. A while back, not long after Oliver was brought to your house the night of the theft, I—mistrusting this man—crept close and overheard him speaking with Fagin in the darkness. From what I caught, I learned that Monks—the man I questioned you about, you remember—”
source_text: “Then he goes by some other amongst us,” rejoined the girl, “which I more than thought before. Some time ago, and soon after Oliver was put into your house on the night of the robbery, I—suspecting this man—listened to a conversation held between him and Fagin in the dark. I found out, from what I heard, that Monks—the man I asked you about, you know—”

text: How many times did Marcus spring from his cot that evening, and slip out, with silent tread, to the stairwell, straining to catch any murmur from the invalid’s room! How many times did a shudder ripple through his limbs, and icy beads of dread break out across his temples, when an abrupt clatter of footsteps made him panic that some horror too awful to name had already come to pass! And what was the heat of all the entreaties he had ever whispered, set beside those he now released, in the torment and fervor of his pleading for the life and strength of the tender soul who wavered on the dark pit’s brink!
source_text: How often did Oliver start from his bed that night, and stealing out, with noiseless footstep, to the staircase, listen for the slightest sound from the sick chamber! How often did a tremble shake his frame, and cold drops of terror start upon his brow, when a sudden trampling of feet caused him to fear that something too dreadful to think of, had even then occurred! And what had been the fervency of all the prayers he had ever muttered, compared with those he poured forth, now, in the agony and passion of his supplication for the life and health of the gentle creature, who was tottering on the deep grave’s verge!

text: Doctor Ashworth jammed his fists deep into his coat pockets and paced the length of the study, pausing now and then to rock on his heels with a fearsome scowl. Between muttered cries of “Yes, that’s the thread” and “No, lost again,” and with as many resumptions of his frowning march, he finally stopped stone-still and delivered himself thus:
source_text: Mr. Losberne thrust his hands into his pockets, and took several turns up and down the room; often stopping, and balancing himself on his toes, and frowning frightfully. After various exclamations of “I’ve got it now” and “no, I haven’t,” and as many renewals of the walking and frowning, he at length made a dead halt, and spoke as follows:

text: He was on the verge of flinging himself to the earth, making a last desperate bid for his youthful existence, when he noticed they had halted before a lone dwelling, utterly crumbling and worn. A window flanked either side of the broken-down door, with a single floor above, yet no glimmer shone through. The place was black, stripped bare, and by every indication, empty of life.
source_text: He was about to throw himself on the ground, and make one struggle for his young life, when he saw that they stood before a solitary house: all ruinous and decayed. There was a window on each side of the dilapidated entrance; and one story above; but no light was visible. The house was dark, dismantled: and, to all appearance, uninhabited.

text: Fagin slipped quietly inside just as the pianist, sweeping lightly across the keys in a brief prelude, drew a general clamor for a song. Once the noise settled, a young woman began to charm the room with a four-verse ballad, the accompanist thundering the melody in full after every stanza. When she finished, the chairman offered a toast, and then the two professionals flanking him launched into a duet, which they delivered to enthusiastic acclaim.
source_text: As Fagin stepped softly in, the professional gentleman, running over the keys by way of prelude, occasioned a general cry of order for a song; which having subsided, a young lady proceeded to entertain the company with a ballad in four verses, between each of which the accompanyist played the melody all through, as loud as he could. When this was over, the chairman gave a sentiment, after which, the professional gentleman on the chairman’s right and left volunteered a duet, and sang it, with great applause.

text: “I didn’t,” answered Mr. Brownlow, also getting to his feet, “but over the past two weeks I’ve come to know everything. You have a brother; you’re aware of it, and of him. Your mother destroyed a will, bequeathing the secret and its benefits to you upon her death. It mentioned a child likely born from that wretched affair—a child who was born, and whom you stumbled upon by chance, when his likeness to your father first roused your suspicions. You went to his birthplace. There were documents—long concealed—confirming his birth and lineage. You destroyed those documents, and now, as you told your partner the Jew, ‘the only proofs of the boy’s identity lie at the bottom of the river, and the old hag who got them from the mother is rotting in her coffin.’ Worthless son, coward, liar—you, who conspire with thieves and killers in shadowed rooms at night—you, whose schemes and tricks have dealt a violent death to a man worth millions of your kind—you, who from infancy were venom and sorrow to your own father’s heart, in whom every foul impulse, vice, and debauchery festered until they erupted in a monstrous disease that turned your face into a mirror of your soul—you, Edward Leeford, do you still defy me!”
source_text: “I _did not_,” replied Mr. Brownlow, rising too; “but within the last fortnight I have learnt it all. You have a brother; you know it, and him. There was a will, which your mother destroyed, leaving the secret and the gain to you at her own death. It contained a reference to some child likely to be the result of this sad connection, which child was born, and accidentally encountered by you, when your suspicions were first awakened by his resemblance to your father. You repaired to the place of his birth. There existed proofs—proofs long suppressed—of his birth and parentage. Those proofs were destroyed by you, and now, in your own words to your accomplice the Jew, ‘_the only proofs of the boy’s identity lie at the bottom of the river, and the old hag that received them from the mother is rotting in her coffin_.’ Unworthy son, coward, liar,—you, who hold your councils with thieves and murderers in dark rooms at night,—you, whose plots and wiles have brought a violent death upon the head of one worth millions such as you,—you, who from your cradle were gall and bitterness to your own father’s heart, and in whom all evil passions, vice, and profligacy, festered, till they found a vent in a hideous disease which had made your face an index even to your mind—you, Edward Leeford, do you still brave me!”

text: “Damn you!” the fellow hissed, his fury barely contained behind locked jaws; “had I but possessed the spine to speak it aloud, I could have shed your company ere dawn. A blight upon your brow, and a slow rot upon your soul, you wretch! Why do you linger here?”
source_text: “Rot you!” murmured the man, in a horrible passion; between his clenched teeth; “if I had only had the courage to say the word, I might have been free of you in a night. Curses on your head, and black death on your heart, you imp! What are you doing here?”

text: Mr. Bumble took the undertaker by the arm and guided him inside. Mr. Sowerberry conferred privately with the board for five minutes, and it was settled that Oliver would be sent to him that very evening "on approval"—a term which, for a parish apprentice, signifies that if the master discovers, after a brief trial, that he can extract sufficient labor from a boy without feeding him too generously, he may keep him for a number of years, to treat as he pleases.
source_text: Mr. Bumble grasped the undertaker by the arm, and led him into the building. Mr. Sowerberry was closeted with the board for five minutes; and it was arranged that Oliver should go to him that evening “upon liking”—a phrase which means, in the case of a parish apprentice, that if the master find, upon a short trial, that he can get enough work out of a boy without putting too much food into him, he shall have him for a term of years, to do what he likes with.

text: The elderly man was on the verge of declaring that Oliver must not venture out under any circumstances, when a pointedly hostile cough from Mr. Grimwig hardened his resolve that the boy should indeed go—and that, by carrying out the errand without delay, Oliver would demonstrate just how unfounded those suspicions were, at least in this matter, and at once.
source_text: The old gentleman was just going to say that Oliver should not go out on any account; when a most malicious cough from Mr. Grimwig determined him that he should; and that, by his prompt discharge of the commission, he should prove to him the injustice of his suspicions: on this head at least: at once.

text: The Jew recoiled from the sudden threat with a nimbleness surprising for a man so seemingly frail; snatching up the pot, he made ready to dash it against his attacker's skull. Yet just then, Charley Bates drew his focus with a truly dreadful shriek, and he abruptly shifted aim, hurling it straight at that youthful figure instead.
source_text: The Jew stepped back in this emergency, with more agility than could have been anticipated in a man of his apparent decrepitude; and, seizing up the pot, prepared to hurl it at his assailant’s head. But Charley Bates, at this moment, calling his attention by a perfectly terrific howl, he suddenly altered its destination, and flung it full at that young gentleman.

text: The order was followed; and the furious Mr. Brownlow was escorted outside, clutching the book in one hand and the bamboo cane in the other, seething with wild anger and rebellion. The moment he stepped into the yard, his rage evaporated. Little Oliver Twist was stretched out on his back upon the paving stones, his shirt loosened, his forehead damp with water; his face was ghostly pale, and an icy shudder ran through his entire body.
source_text: The mandate was obeyed; and the indignant Mr. Brownlow was conveyed out, with the book in one hand, and the bamboo cane in the other: in a perfect phrenzy of rage and defiance. He reached the yard; and his passion vanished in a moment. Little Oliver Twist lay on his back on the pavement, with his shirt unbuttoned, and his temples bathed with water; his face a deadly white; and a cold tremble convulsing his whole frame.

text: “We must stir ourselves, Edmund, not sink into idle sorrow,” said Lady Carstairs, pressing a fingertip to her mouth as she fixed her gaze upon him; “this note must reach Dr. Ashworth with the utmost haste. It must be taken to the village: barely three miles distant, if you follow the bridle-path through the copse: and from there sent onward, by a mounted courier, directly to Thornfield. The landlord will pledge to arrange it: and I rely on you to ensure it is carried out, I am certain.”
source_text: “We must be active, Oliver, and not give way to useless grief,” said Mrs. Maylie, laying her finger on her lip, as she looked steadily into his face; “this letter must be sent, with all possible expedition, to Mr. Losberne. It must be carried to the market-town: which is not more than four miles off, by the footpath across the field: and thence dispatched, by an express on horseback, straight to Chertsey. The people at the inn will undertake to do this: and I can trust to you to see it done, I know.”

text: All the while, Dr. Hartwell, though by nature a rather hasty man himself, endured his companion’s eccentricities with remarkable cheerfulness, having long understood them; and as Professor Crane, over tea, condescended to declare the scones wholly excellent, the evening proceeded without a ripple; so that Eleanor, seated among them, began to feel more at home than she ever had before in that stern old scholar’s company.
source_text: All this, Mr. Brownlow, although himself somewhat of an impetuous gentleman: knowing his friend’s peculiarities, bore with great good humour; as Mr. Grimwig, at tea, was graciously pleased to express his entire approval of the muffins, matters went on very smoothly; and Oliver, who made one of the party, began to feel more at his ease than he had yet done in the fierce old gentleman’s presence.

text: These were not the sole sorrows that weighed upon Oliver’s spirit. He found himself solitary in an unfamiliar world; and every soul understands how even the stoutest heart may grow cold and forsaken in such circumstances. The child possessed no companions to cherish, nor any who cherished him. No fresh parting left its sting lingering in his thoughts; no beloved and deeply known countenance was gone, to settle with a heavy ache within his breast.
source_text: Nor were these the only dismal feelings which depressed Oliver. He was alone in a strange place; and we all know how chilled and desolate the best of us will sometimes feel in such a situation. The boy had no friends to care for, or to care for him. The regret of no recent separation was fresh in his mind; the absence of no loved and well-remembered face sank heavily into his heart.

text: “Oh, thank heaven!” the young woman cried out, “I’ve found him! Oh! Oliver! Oliver! You wicked child, putting me through such agony worrying about you! Come home now, darling, come along. Oh, I’ve found him. Thank the blessed, merciful heavens, I’ve found him!” Amid these scattered, half-sobbing words, she broke down into another bout of weeping and grew so violently hysterical that two women who had just arrived asked a butcher’s boy—a lad with a glossy head of hair slicked with grease, who was also standing by—whether he didn’t think it best to run for a doctor. To which the butcher’s boy, who seemed to have a lazy, if not outright idle, manner, answered that he didn’t think so.
source_text: “Oh my gracious!” said the young woman, “I have found him! Oh! Oliver! Oliver! Oh you naughty boy, to make me suffer such distress on your account! Come home, dear, come. Oh, I’ve found him. Thank gracious goodness heavins, I’ve found him!” With these incoherent exclamations, the young woman burst into another fit of crying, and got so dreadfully hysterical, that a couple of women who came up at the moment asked a butcher’s boy with a shiny head of hair anointed with suet, who was also looking on, whether he didn’t think he had better run for the doctor. To which, the butcher’s boy: who appeared of a lounging, not to say indolent disposition: replied, that he thought not.

text: Let no adversary of “the regime” imagine that, throughout his days of isolated confinement, Oliver was deprived of bodily exertion, the comforts of fellowship, or the blessings of spiritual solace. Regarding exertion, the weather being brisk and sharp, he was granted leave to wash each morning beneath the yard pump, upon the flagstones, under the eye of Mr. Bumble, who warded off any chill and sent a smarting glow through his limbs by means of regular strokes of the rod. As to fellowship, every second day he was marched into the dining hall where the lads took their meals, and there companionably whipped as a general caution and spectacle. And far from lacking spiritual solace, he was thrust each evening at prayer hour into that same chamber, and there allowed to hear, and soothe his soul with, a collective entreaty of the boys, containing a particular petition, inserted by the board’s decree, in which they begged to be rendered good, upright, satisfied, and dutiful, and to be shielded from the sins and vices of Oliver Twist—whom the prayer expressly declared to be under the sole patronage and guardianship of the powers of darkness, and an item dispatched straight from the workshop of the Devil himself.
source_text: Let it not be supposed by the enemies of “the system,” that, during the period of his solitary incarceration, Oliver was denied the benefit of exercise, the pleasure of society, or the advantages of religious consolation. As for exercise, it was nice cold weather, and he was allowed to perform his ablutions every morning under the pump, in a stone yard, in the presence of Mr. Bumble, who prevented his catching cold, and caused a tingling sensation to pervade his frame, by repeated applications of the cane. As for society, he was carried every other day into the hall where the boys dined, and there sociably flogged as a public warning and example. And so far from being denied the advantages of religious consolation, he was kicked into the same apartment every evening at prayer-time, and there permitted to listen to, and console his mind with, a general supplication of the boys, containing a special clause, therein inserted by authority of the board, in which they entreated to be made good, virtuous, contented, and obedient, and to be guarded from the sins and vices of Oliver Twist: whom the supplication distinctly set forth to be under the exclusive patronage and protection of the powers of wickedness, and an article direct from the manufactory of the very Devil himself.

text: The storm’s rumbling, now so close it seemed to splinter and crack directly above them, had faded into silence. Corin lifted his head from the board and inclined it, straining to catch the woman’s next words. The three faces drew together until they nearly brushed, the two men hunched across the cramped table in their fervor to hear, the woman bending close to make her murmur clear. The jaundiced glow of the hanging lamp beat straight down upon them, deepening the pallor and dread stamped on their features; ringed about by the thickest shadow, they looked utterly spectral.
source_text: The thunder, which seemed in fact much nearer, and to shiver and break almost over their heads, having subsided, Monks, raising his face from the table, bent forward to listen to what the woman should say. The faces of the three nearly touched, as the two men leant over the small table in their eagerness to hear, and the woman also leant forward to render her whisper audible. The sickly rays of the suspended lantern falling directly upon them, aggravated the paleness and anxiety of their countenances: which, encircled by the deepest gloom and darkness, looked ghastly in the extreme.

text: “Come now, come now, my boy!” the Jew exclaimed, slipping back into his familiar tone with sudden ease. He toyed with the knife for a moment before setting it aside, as though to suggest he’d snatched it up only in jest. “Naturally I’m aware of that, my lad. I was merely giving you a little scare. You’re a plucky fellow. Ha! ha! a truly plucky fellow, Oliver.” He chuckled and rubbed his palms together, though his gaze kept flickering nervously toward the box all the same.
source_text: “Tush, tush, my dear!” said the Jew, abruptly resuming his old manner, and playing with the knife a little, before he laid it down; as if to induce the belief that he had caught it up, in mere sport. “Of course I know that, my dear. I only tried to frighten you. You’re a brave boy. Ha! ha! you’re a brave boy, Oliver.” The Jew rubbed his hands with a chuckle, but glanced uneasily at the box, notwithstanding.

text: Such shifts strike one as ridiculous; yet they are no more strange than they initially appear. The passage in actual existence from laden tables to dying couches, and from funeral attire to festive dress, is every bit as jarring; only, in that sphere, we are engaged participants, rather than idle observers, and that alters everything. The players in the simulated world of the stage are oblivious to sharp turns and sudden surges of emotion or sentiment, which, displayed before the gaze of simple onlookers, are instantly denounced as extravagant and absurd.
source_text: Such changes appear absurd; but they are not so unnatural as they would seem at first sight. The transitions in real life from well-spread boards to death-beds, and from mourning-weeds to holiday garments, are not a whit less startling; only, there, we are busy actors, instead of passive lookers-on, which makes a vast difference. The actors in the mimic life of the theatre, are blind to violent transitions and abrupt impulses of passion or feeling, which, presented before the eyes of mere spectators, are at once condemned as outrageous and preposterous.

text: At the rear of the house, a small latticed window sat roughly five and a half feet from the ground, belonging to a scullery or little brewing-room at the passage’s end. The opening was so modest that the occupants likely deemed it unworthy of sturdier protection, yet it remained ample enough for a boy of Oliver’s slight frame to pass through. With a few deft movements, Mr. Sikes’s skill easily undid the window’s fastening, and soon it, too, gaped wide.
source_text: It was a little lattice window, about five feet and a half above the ground, at the back of the house: which belonged to a scullery, or small brewing-place, at the end of the passage. The aperture was so small, that the inmates had probably not thought it worth while to defend it more securely; but it was large enough to admit a boy of Oliver’s size, nevertheless. A very brief exercise of Mr. Sikes’s art, sufficed to overcome the fastening of the lattice; and it soon stood wide open also.

text: “Some of this,” the girl said, “I’ve pieced together from others at the house I mentioned, for I’ve only laid eyes on him twice, and both times he was wrapped in a heavy cloak. I believe that’s all I can offer to help you recognize him. Wait, though,” she added. “On his throat: high enough that when he turns his head, you can glimpse a part of it beneath his neckerchief—there is—”
source_text: “Part of this,” said the girl, “I have drawn out from other people at the house I tell you of, for I have only seen him twice, and both times he was covered up in a large cloak. I think that’s all I can give you to know him by. Stay though,” she added. “Upon his throat: so high that you can see a part of it below his neckerchief when he turns his face: there is—”

text: “Now, Miss Maylie,” Mr. Brownlow resumed, “let us turn again to the matter that so deeply engages your compassion. Will you share with me what you have learned of that unfortunate boy? I must first assure you that I have left no avenue unexplored in my efforts to trace him. Moreover, during my time abroad, my early conviction—that he had deceived me and, at the urging of his old companions, sought to rob me—has been profoundly unsettled.”
source_text: “Now, Miss Maylie,” said Mr. Brownlow, “to return to the subject in which your humanity is so much interested. Will you let me know what intelligence you have of this poor child: allowing me to promise that I exhausted every means in my power of discovering him, and that since I have been absent from this country, my first impression that he had imposed upon me, and had been persuaded by his former associates to rob me, has been considerably shaken.”

text: For a moment or two, neither of them spoke. Then, as that brief silence passed, Mr. Bumble made his intentions clearer by shifting his left arm from the back of Mrs. Corney’s chair, where it had been resting, to the string of her apron, around which it slowly coiled itself.
source_text: Nothing was said on either side, for a minute or two afterwards. By the expiration of that time, Mr. Bumble had illustrated the position by removing his left arm from the back of Mrs. Corney’s chair, where it had previously rested, to Mrs. Corney’s apron-string, round which it gradually became entwined.

text: The Jew struck Oliver sharply across the shoulders with his club and had already lifted it for another blow when the girl darted forward and tore it from his grasp. She hurled it into the fire with such violence that several glowing coals spun out across the room.
source_text: The Jew inflicted a smart blow on Oliver’s shoulders with the club; and was raising it for a second, when the girl, rushing forward, wrested it from his hand. She flung it into the fire, with a force that brought some of the glowing coals whirling out into the room.

text: The young minister, still draped in his vestments, caught sight of the stranger and moved quietly into the chancel. He murmured something to the guardsman, then motioned toward the sexton, who leaned down to an older woman—likely his spouse—and spoke softly. Together, they too ascended the altar stairs.
source_text: The officiating curate, who had not yet doffed his surplice, perceived the new-comer, and followed him to the communion-space. He whispered to the soldier, and then beckoned to the clerk, who in his turn whispered to an elderly woman, apparently his wife, and they also went up the chancel steps.

text: “No, no, no. Do not call it carelessness! Let me believe it was something deeper—a kind of foreshadowing instinct—the first stirring of a fondness for me. You torment me by insisting it was mere carelessness—I have never once seen it in that way, and I cannot bear it. Oh! If only I possessed the art to capture your heart! But that is beyond me—I can merely wonder whether I hold it already. If I do not, and it is untrue that you have drifted unknowingly toward me as I have toward you, then I have nothing left to utter.”
source_text: “No, no, no. Don’t say thoughtlessness! Make me think it was something more—that it was a sort of prophetic instinct—the beginning of a feeling that you would like me. You torture me to say it was done in thoughtlessness—I never thought of it in that light, and I can’t endure it. Ah! I wish I knew how to win you! but that I can’t do—I can only ask if I have already got you. If I have not, and it is not true that you have come unwittingly to me as I have to you, I can say no more.”

text: Some nights later, beneath a starless sky, Oak slipped with stealthy footfalls from the agent’s threshold in Casterbridge. As he trudged homeward, a heavy step sounded ahead of him, and upon catching up, he discovered it to be Coggan. Side by side they made their way into the hamlet, until they reached a narrow path behind the church that sloped downward to the dwelling of Laban Tall, who had recently been appointed parish clerk and still suffered a mortal dread each Sunday, when his solitary voice rang out among the stern phrases of the Psalms, where no soul dared follow.
source_text: On a dark night, a few days later, Oak came with mysterious steps from the surrogate’s door, in Casterbridge. On the way home he heard a heavy tread in front of him, and, overtaking the man, found him to be Coggan. They walked together into the village until they came to a little lane behind the church, leading down to the cottage of Laban Tall, who had lately been installed as clerk of the parish, and was yet in mortal terror at church on Sundays when he heard his lone voice among certain hard words of the Psalms, whither no man ventured to follow him.

text: She reached out her hand, and Gabriel did the same. As they groped through the dimness to place the coins palm to palm, a fleeting detail spoke volumes. Gabriel’s fingers brushed against the young woman’s wrist. It pulsed with a desperate, tragic rhythm—a beat he knew all too well from the femoral artery of his overdriven lambs. It hinted at a drain on a vitality that, given her slight frame and stature, seemed already far too scant.
source_text: She extended her hand; Gabriel his. In feeling for each other’s palm in the gloom before the money could be passed, a minute incident occurred which told much. Gabriel’s fingers alighted on the young woman’s wrist. It was beating with a throb of tragic intensity. He had frequently felt the same quick, hard beat in the femoral artery of his lambs when overdriven. It suggested a consumption too great of a vitality which, to judge from her figure and stature, was already too little.

text: The upright cow was a Devon, sheathed in a sleek, warm hide of deep Indian red, so flawlessly even from eyes to tail that she might have been steeped in a vat of that hue, her long back ruler-straight. Her companion was dappled grey and white. Beside her, Oak now made out a newborn calf, scarcely a day old, staring witlessly at the two women, plainly still unversed in the strange faculty of sight, and repeatedly turning toward the lantern, which it seemed to take for the moon—ancestral instinct not yet schooled by experience. Lately, amid the sheep and cattle, Lucina had been much at work on Norcombe Hill.
source_text: The cow standing erect was of the Devon breed, and was encased in a tight warm hide of rich Indian red, as absolutely uniform from eyes to tail as if the animal had been dipped in a dye of that colour, her long back being mathematically level. The other was spotted, grey and white. Beside her Oak now noticed a little calf about a day old, looking idiotically at the two women, which showed that it had not long been accustomed to the phenomenon of eyesight, and often turning to the lantern, which it apparently mistook for the moon, inherited instinct having as yet had little time for correction by experience. Between the sheep and the cows Lucina had been busy on Norcombe Hill lately.

text: The musician, however, refused to resume his tune. Bob Coggan was dismissed to his house for his coarse conduct, and peace returned through Jacob Smallbury, who offered up a song as sprawling and unending as the one with which the venerable drunkard Silenus once entertained the lads Chromis and Mnasylus, along with the rest of that era’s merry revelers.
source_text: But the singer could not be set going again. Bob Coggan was sent home for his ill manners, and tranquility was restored by Jacob Smallbury, who volunteered a ballad as inclusive and interminable as that with which the worthy toper old Silenus amused on a similar occasion the swains Chromis and Mnasylus, and other jolly dogs of his day.

text: Even more agitated now, stirred by a backlash of emotion after the first impulses Oak’s conduct had roused in her, she halted in the corridor, staring at the chamber door behind which Fanny rested. She clasped her hands, tilted her face upward, and pressed her burning palms rigidly against her brow, crying out with a quavering, frantic gasp, “God grant that you might speak and reveal your mystery to me, Fanny!... Oh, I pray, pray it is not so—that there are truly two of you!... If I could only steal one fleeting glimpse inside, I would understand everything!”
source_text: More fevered now by a reaction from the first feelings which Oak’s example had raised in her, she paused in the hall, looking at the door of the room wherein Fanny lay. She locked her fingers, threw back her head, and strained her hot hands rigidly across her forehead, saying, with a hysterical sob, “Would to God you would speak and tell me your secret, Fanny!... Oh, I hope, hope it is not true that there are two of you!... If I could only look in upon you for one little minute, I should know all!”

text: “Ah, there it is,” Oak exclaimed, springing to his feet and setting aside, for the moment, his brooding over poor Fanny. “You’re a fine lad to hurry and fetch me, Cain, and one day you’ll have the scent of a grand plum pudding as a reward. But first, Cainy, fetch the tar pot, and we’ll brand this flock and be finished with them.”
source_text: “Oh, that’s it,” said Oak, jumping up, and dimissing for the present his thoughts on poor Fanny. “You are a good boy to run and tell me, Cain, and you shall smell a large plum pudding some day as a treat. But, before we go, Cainy, bring the tarpot, and we’ll mark this lot and have done with ’em.”

text: Five days came and went, dawn and dusk alike. The young woman arrived without fail to milk the thriving cow or tend the ailing one, yet she never once let her gaze drift toward where Oak stood. His lack of tact had wounded her deeply—not because he had glimpsed what he could scarcely avoid, but because he had made it plain that he had seen. Just as transgression requires a rule to exist, impropriety requires a witness; and she seemed to sense that Gabriel’s watching had cast her as improper through no design of her own. The thought gnawed at him with sharp regret; it was also a misstep that stirred a dormant warmth he had already begun to feel in that quarter.
source_text: Five mornings and evenings passed. The young woman came regularly to milk the healthy cow or to attend to the sick one, but never allowed her vision to stray in the direction of Oak’s person. His want of tact had deeply offended her—not by seeing what he could not help, but by letting her know that he had seen it. For, as without law there is no sin, without eyes there is no indecorum; and she appeared to feel that Gabriel’s espial had made her an indecorous woman without her own connivance. It was food for great regret with him; it was also a _contretemps_ which touched into life a latent heat he had experienced in that direction.

text: The morning after the announcement, she discovered Gabriel Oak at the far end of her garden, sharpening his shears for the shearing. Around them, the nearby cottages hummed with the same activity; the hiss of honing rose into the air from every corner of the village, like an armory stirring before battle. In their moments of readiness, peace and war embrace—sickles, scythes, shears, and pruning hooks take their place beside swords, bayonets, and lances, all demanding the same sharp edge.
source_text: The next day to that of the declaration she found Gabriel Oak at the bottom of her garden, grinding his shears for the sheep-shearing. All the surrounding cottages were more or less scenes of the same operation; the scurr of whetting spread into the sky from all parts of the village as from an armoury previous to a campaign. Peace and war kiss each other at their hours of preparation—sickles, scythes, shears, and pruning-hooks, ranking with swords, bayonets, and lances, in their common necessity for point and edge.

text: “Well, no. I once heard a philosopher remark that the ancient builders, those who labored when art was still alive, held no reverence for their predecessors’ work—they tore down and reshaped as they saw fit. Why shouldn’t we do the same? ‘Creation and preservation are ill-matched companions,’ he said, ‘and a million antiquarians could never devise a new style.’ That echoes my own thoughts exactly. I believe in modernizing this place, so we might enjoy some cheer while we still can.”
source_text: “Well, no. A philosopher once said in my hearing that the old builders, who worked when art was a living thing, had no respect for the work of builders who went before them, but pulled down and altered as they thought fit; and why shouldn’t we? ‘Creation and preservation don’t do well together,’ says he, ‘and a million of antiquarians can’t invent a style.’ My mind exactly. I am for making this place more modern, that we may be cheerful whilst we can.”

text: “Leave, you say—what madness will you utter after this? Handling me as though I were some common Jack or Jem, when you well know that but a little while since my standing matched your own! Upon my soul, Bathsheba, it is beyond shameless. You understand, moreover, that I cannot depart without entangling matters in such a snarl as you would not unravel heaven knows when. Unless, of course, you will vow to appoint a sensible fellow as steward, or overseer, or some such. I shall quit this instant if you will swear to that.”
source_text: “Go, indeed—what folly will you say next? Treating me like Dick, Tom and Harry when you know that a short time ago my position was as good as yours! Upon my life, Bathsheba, it is too barefaced. You know, too, that I can’t go without putting things in such a strait as you wouldn’t get out of I can’t tell when. Unless, indeed, you’ll promise to have an understanding man as bailiff, or manager, or something. I’ll go at once if you’ll promise that.”

text: A fierce declaration of intent does not always spring from an equally fierce resolve. Sometimes it is hurled forth like a buttress, shoring up a crumbling belief that, in its prime, had no need to be spoken aloud to confirm its strength. Bathsheba’s “No, I won’t” was, in truth, a whispered “I think I must.”
source_text: The most vigorous expression of a resolution does not always coincide with the greatest vigour of the resolution itself. It is often flung out as a sort of prop to support a decaying conviction which, whilst strong, required no enunciation to prove it so. The “No, I won’t” of Bathsheba meant virtually, “I think I must.”

text: At that moment, neither melody nor movement filled the hall. Down at the far end, set aside for the laborers, a cluster of people murmured together, their expressions shadowed and uneasy. Boldwood had stationed himself near the hearth, and although he was so lost in reveries born of her pledge that he barely registered his surroundings, he now appeared to notice their hushed demeanor and the sidelong glances they cast.
source_text: There was no music or dancing in progress just now. At the lower end, which had been arranged for the work-folk specially, a group conversed in whispers, and with clouded looks. Boldwood was standing by the fireplace, and he, too, though so absorbed in visions arising from her promise that he scarcely saw anything, seemed at that moment to have observed their peculiar manner, and their looks askance.

text: Yes, and she had on a stunning gown of gold silk, edged with black lace, so stiff and full it could have stood by itself with no legs inside it at all. It was a truly charming spectacle; and her hair was brushed to a glorious sheen. And when the sunlight fell across her bright dress and his red coat—my! what a striking pair they made. You could see them the whole length of the street.
source_text: “Ay, and she wore a beautiful gold-colour silk gown, trimmed with black lace, that would have stood alone ’ithout legs inside if required. ’Twas a very winsome sight; and her hair was brushed splendid. And when the sun shone upon the bright gown and his red coat—my! how handsome they looked. You could see ’em all the length of the street.”

text: Yet a restlessness stirred within her, a quality that did her some honor, since it would have troubled few others. Apart from the arguments she used to subdue her own misgivings, she was deeply convinced that, having herself set the affair in motion, she was duty-bound to face the outcome. Still, her resistance lingered. In nearly the same moment she declared it would be unkind not to wed Boldwood, and that she could not bring herself to do it if her life depended on it.
source_text: But a disquiet filled her which was somewhat to her credit, for it would have affected few. Beyond the mentioned reasons with which she combated her objections, she had a strong feeling that, having been the one who began the game, she ought in honesty to accept the consequences. Still the reluctance remained. She said in the same breath that it would be ungenerous not to marry Boldwood, and that she couldn’t do it to save her life.

text: Troy lay asleep on the porch as the rain beyond it swelled. After a while, the gargoyle gave its first spurt. Soon a slender rivulet began to thread the seventy feet of open air from its mouth to the earth, the drops striking the ground with the swift, scattered force of birdshot. The rivulet fattened and gathered strength, its arc reaching farther and farther from the tower’s flank. When the rain settled into a steady, unbroken deluge, the stream plunged down in thick, churning sheets.
source_text: Troy slept on in the porch, and the rain increased outside. Presently the gurgoyle spat. In due time a small stream began to trickle through the seventy feet of aerial space between its mouth and the ground, which the water-drops smote like duckshot in their accelerated velocity. The stream thickened in substance, and increased in power, gradually spouting further and yet further from the side of the tower. When the rain fell in a steady and ceaseless torrent the stream dashed downward in volumes.

text: “Mark me,” came a voice, drifting through the malt-house doorway just as it swung wide, “we’ll have a fair noon, and a squall will bite by moonrise.” The speaker was Elias Crane, who strode toward the hearth, pausing halfway to knock the sleet from his soles. Neither the words nor the entrance struck the maltman as sudden; in that country, folk often shed preambles from speech and action alike, and the maltman, granted the same freedom, took his time before answering. He speared a shard of cheese with his knife, lifting it as a surgeon might lift a suture.
source_text: “I say, says I, we get a fine day, and then down comes a snapper at night,” was a remark now suddenly heard spreading into the malthouse from the door, which had been opened the previous moment. The form of Henery Fray advanced to the fire, stamping the snow from his boots when about half-way there. The speech and entry had not seemed to be at all an abrupt beginning to the maltster, introductory matter being often omitted in this neighbourhood, both from word and deed, and the maltster having the same latitude allowed him, did not hurry to reply. He picked up a fragment of cheese, by pecking upon it with his knife, as a butcher picks up skewers.

text: Caleb lifted his gaze, and uncertain of his next act, wearily took in the landscape. Along the far rim of the Hollow lay a round tarn, and above it dangled the gaunt frame of a brass-pale moon that had but a handful of nights remaining—the dawn star trailing close at her right. The water shone like a blind man’s eye, and as the earth stirred a wind rose, trembling and stretching the moon’s likeness yet leaving it whole, and spinning the star’s shape into a luminous smear across the surface. All this Caleb witnessed and held fast.
source_text: Oak raised his head, and wondering what he could do, listlessly surveyed the scene. By the outer margin of the Pit was an oval pond, and over it hung the attenuated skeleton of a chrome-yellow moon which had only a few days to last—the morning star dogging her on the left hand. The pool glittered like a dead man’s eye, and as the world awoke a breeze blew, shaking and elongating the reflection of the moon without breaking it, and turning the image of the star to a phosphoric streak upon the water. All this Oak saw and remembered.

text: Yes—I confess it—I’m radiant tonight: lighthearted, even more than lighthearted—so much so that a quiet sadness creeps back in, knowing all of this is fleeting. And sometimes, just when I’m brimming with hope and brightness, some trouble is already gathering far off: so that I’ve often learned to welcome my own gloom with a kind of peace, and to mistrust a happy mood. Still, this may be foolish—I sense it is foolish. Perhaps my dawn is breaking at last.
source_text: “Yes—I must own it—I am bright to-night: cheerful and more than cheerful—so much so that I am almost sad again with the sense that all of it is passing away. And sometimes, when I am excessively hopeful and blithe, a trouble is looming in the distance: so that I often get to look upon gloom in me with content, and to fear a happy mood. Still this may be absurd—I feel that it is absurd. Perhaps my day is dawning at last.”

text: The tiny speck of life was laid upon a wisp of hay before the small stove, where a can of milk simmered gently. Oak blew out the lantern and pinched the wick, leaving the cot lit only by a candle dangling from a twisted wire. A rather unforgiving bed, fashioned from a few corn sacks tossed carelessly across the floor, covered half of the little dwelling. Here the young man stretched out, loosened his woollen cravat, and shut his eyes. In less time than it would take someone unused to physical toil to choose which side to lie on, Farmer Oak was sound asleep.
source_text: The little speck of life he placed on a wisp of hay before the small stove, where a can of milk was simmering. Oak extinguished the lantern by blowing into it and then pinching the snuff, the cot being lighted by a candle suspended by a twisted wire. A rather hard couch, formed of a few corn sacks thrown carelessly down, covered half the floor of this little habitation, and here the young man stretched himself along, loosened his woollen cravat, and closed his eyes. In about the time a person unaccustomed to bodily labour would have decided upon which side to lie, Farmer Oak was asleep.

text: The weight of the farmer’s menacing words rested on a secret she alone possessed: her beloved was due to return to Weatherbury within the next day or two. Troy had not, as Boldwood and the rest imagined, gone back to his remote barracks; he had merely travelled to Bath to see some friends, and still had a week or more left of his leave.
source_text: The force of the farmer’s threats lay in their relation to a circumstance known at present only to herself: her lover was coming back to Weatherbury in the course of the very next day or two. Troy had not returned to his distant barracks as Boldwood and others supposed, but had merely gone to visit some acquaintance in Bath, and had yet a week or more remaining to his furlough.

text: The door slammed open, shuddering from frame to base as it struck the wall. Mr. Oak stood in the doorway, his face flushed with heat, twists of hay bound around his ankles to fend off the snow, a leather belt cinched over his smock, the very picture of robust vitality. Four lambs dangled across his shoulders in a tangle of awkward poses, and the dog George, whom Gabriel had managed to bring from Norcombe, paced gravely at his heels.
source_text: The door was flung back till it kicked the wall and trembled from top to bottom with the blow. Mr. Oak appeared in the entry with a steaming face, hay-bands wound about his ankles to keep out the snow, a leather strap round his waist outside the smock-frock, and looking altogether an epitome of the world’s health and vigour. Four lambs hung in various embarrassing attitudes over his shoulders, and the dog George, whom Gabriel had contrived to fetch from Norcombe, stalked solemnly behind.

text: “Ah—well, I’ve no wish to trumpet anything abroad—nothing whatever,” murmured Moulden, falteringly. “Yet we are born to our lots—that much is certain. Still, I’d sooner my small portion stayed concealed; though, perhaps, a lofty nature is a trifle lofty, and at my making all gifts lay within the Creator’s power, and He may have withheld none.... But keep it beneath your hat, Thomas! beneath your hat, I say! A queer instinct, friends, this urge to hide, and no credit in it. And yet there stands the Beatitudes with a roll of the favoured at the start, and certain unassuming souls may find their place there.”
source_text: “Heh-heh! well, I wish to noise nothing abroad—nothing at all,” murmured Poorgrass, diffidently. “But we be born to things—that’s true. Yet I would rather my trifle were hid; though, perhaps, a high nater is a little high, and at my birth all things were possible to my Maker, and he may have begrudged no gifts.... But under your bushel, Joseph! under your bushel with ’ee! A strange desire, neighbours, this desire to hide, and no praise due. Yet there is a Sermon on the Mount with a calendar of the blessed at the head, and certain meek men may be named therein.”

text: “No one can harm a woman who’s already gone,” Coggan pronounced at last, his words falling with mechanical flatness. “Everything that could be rendered her has been rendered—she’s past our reach: and what sense is there in a man working himself into a lather over senseless clay that neither hears nor sees, and hasn’t the least notion what you’re about with her? Had she been living, I’d have been foremost to lend a hand. Were she in need of bread and ale this moment, I’d set down the coin without a second thought. But she’s dead, and no haste of ours will fetch her back. The woman’s gone beyond us—any labour spent on her is squandered: why race to perform what isn’t asked of us? Take a draught, shepherd, and let us be easy, for tomorrow we may lie where she does.”
source_text: “Nobody can hurt a dead woman,” at length said Coggan, with the precision of a machine. “All that could be done for her is done—she’s beyond us: and why should a man put himself in a tearing hurry for lifeless clay that can neither feel nor see, and don’t know what you do with her at all? If she’d been alive, I would have been the first to help her. If she now wanted victuals and drink, I’d pay for it, money down. But she’s dead, and no speed of ours will bring her to life. The woman’s past us—time spent upon her is throwed away: why should we hurry to do what’s not required? Drink, shepherd, and be friends, for to-morrow we may be like her.”

text: “The hut wasn’t really to blame,” she remarked, her voice revealing a rare kind of woman—one who shaped a thought fully before voicing it. “You ought, I believe, to have thought ahead, and not been so unwise as to keep the shutters latched.”
source_text: “It was not exactly the fault of the hut,” she observed in a tone which showed her to be that novelty among women—one who finished a thought before beginning the sentence which was to convey it. “You should, I think, have considered, and not have been so foolish as to leave the slides closed.”

text: He came to the meadow gate. Beyond it, the land sang with the murmur of rippling water, and the sky with larks; the soft bleating of the flock wove through both. Mistress and hired man were busy making a lamb “take”—the trick done whenever a ewe loses her own young and is given one of another ewe’s twins as a substitute. Gabriel had skinned the dead lamb and was binding the skin over the living lamb’s body in the time-honoured way, while Bathsheba held open a small pen made of four hurdles. Into it the mother and the foisted lamb were driven, there to stay until the old sheep grew fond of the young one.
source_text: He approached the gate of the meadow. Beyond it the ground was melodious with ripples, and the sky with larks; the low bleating of the flock mingling with both. Mistress and man were engaged in the operation of making a lamb “take,” which is performed whenever an ewe has lost her own offspring, one of the twins of another ewe being given her as a substitute. Gabriel had skinned the dead lamb, and was tying the skin over the body of the live lamb, in the customary manner, whilst Bathsheba was holding open a little pen of four hurdles, into which the mother and foisted lamb were driven, where they would remain till the old sheep conceived an affection for the young one.

text: Nevertheless, she stayed perfectly still. In truth, Bathsheba existed beyond the reach of action—yet she had not fainted. Her mind was caught in a kind of inner blindness; for that moment, all inward illumination had vanished, though outwardly nothing seemed dimmed at all.
source_text: Nevertheless, she did not move. The truth was that Bathsheba was beyond the pale of activity—and yet not in a swoon. She was in a state of mental _gutta serena_; her mind was for the minute totally deprived of light at the same time no obscuration was apparent from without.

text: The main room, along with the alcove at the far end, had been cleared of all clutter, and this section—roughly two-thirds of the barn—was set aside for the celebration, while the remaining part, heaped to the rafters with oats, was hidden behind a canvas curtain. Bunches and wreaths of fresh leaves adorned the walls, the timbers, and the makeshift light fixtures, and directly across from Oak a small platform had been set up, holding a table and chairs. On it sat three fiddle players, and next to them stood a wild-eyed man with his hair sticking up, sweat running down his face, and a tambourine trembling in his grip.
source_text: The central space, together with the recess at one end, was emptied of all incumbrances, and this area, covering about two-thirds of the whole, was appropriated for the gathering, the remaining end, which was piled to the ceiling with oats, being screened off with sail-cloth. Tufts and garlands of green foliage decorated the walls, beams, and extemporized chandeliers, and immediately opposite to Oak a rostrum had been erected, bearing a table and chairs. Here sat three fiddlers, and beside them stood a frantic man with his hair on end, perspiration streaming down his cheeks, and a tambourine quivering in his hand.

text: “There’s no point in testing my strength against yours. It’s a savage method for resolving a dispute. I’ll be quitting the army soon, driven by that very belief. And now that you understand how matters stand with Bathsheba, it would be a blunder to kill me, wouldn’t it?”
source_text: “It is not worth while to measure my strength with you. Indeed it is a barbarous way of settling a quarrel. I shall shortly leave the army because of the same conviction. Now after that revelation of how the land lies with Bathsheba, ’twould be a mistake to kill me, would it not?”

text: He sprang from his bed, threw on his clothes, raced down the lane through a misty daybreak, and climbed the hill. The early-lambing ewes had been separated from those due to drop later, with two hundred of the later group in Gabriel’s care. Those two hundred appeared to have completely disappeared from the slope. The fifty with their lambs remained penned at the far end just as he had left them, but the remainder, which made up most of the flock, were missing. Gabriel shouted the shepherd’s cry at full volume:
source_text: He jumped out of bed, dressed, tore down the lane through a foggy dawn, and ascended the hill. The forward ewes were kept apart from those among which the fall of lambs would be later, there being two hundred of the latter class in Gabriel’s flock. These two hundred seemed to have absolutely vanished from the hill. There were the fifty with their lambs, enclosed at the other end as he had left them, but the rest, forming the bulk of the flock, were nowhere. Gabriel called at the top of his voice the shepherd’s call:

text: Now, away from the noise and jostle of the market, his rigid, upright frame stood out more sharply. As he paced in thought, each step landed flatly, heel and toe striking the ground together, while his head inclined slightly forward, shadowing the quiet set of his lips and the full, somewhat jutting breadth of his chin. The broad expanse of his forehead remained mostly unlined, save for a few faint, hair-fine creases running across it.
source_text: His square-framed perpendicularity showed more fully now than in the crowd and bustle of the market-house. In this meditative walk his foot met the floor with heel and toe simultaneously, and his fine reddish-fleshed face was bent downwards just enough to render obscure the still mouth and the well-rounded though rather prominent and broad chin. A few clear and thread-like horizontal lines were the only interruption to the otherwise smooth surface of his large forehead.

text: Bathsheba had likely departed Weatherbury well before this hour, yet the hamlet held sufficient allure to draw Oak toward Shottsford market as his subsequent ground of search, for it rested within the Weatherbury district. Besides, the inhabitants of Weatherbury were far from dull in their own right. If rumor held true, they were as robust, jovial, prosperous, and roguish a company as any in the entire shire. Oak determined to lodge in Weatherbury that evening en route to Shottsford, and set forth promptly upon the highway that had been urged as the straightest path to the aforementioned village.
source_text: Bathsheba had probably left Weatherbury long before this time, but the place had enough interest attaching to it to lead Oak to choose Shottsford fair as his next field of inquiry, because it lay in the Weatherbury quarter. Moreover, the Weatherbury folk were by no means uninteresting intrinsically. If report spoke truly they were as hardy, merry, thriving, wicked a set as any in the whole county. Oak resolved to sleep at Weatherbury that night on his way to Shottsford, and struck out at once into the high road which had been recommended as the direct route to the village in question.

text: “Ah!” she answered, a faint quiver threading through the voice of the normally composed young woman. “If you can battle only half as charmingly as you can speak, you might turn a bayonet wound into a delight!” At once, poor Bathsheba realized her blunder in confessing so much; flustered, she rushed to correct herself and only sank deeper. “But don’t imagine that _I_ take any delight in what you say.”
source_text: “Ah!” she replied, and there was even a little tremulousness in the usually cool girl’s voice; “if you can only fight half as winningly as you can talk, you are able to make a pleasure of a bayonet wound!” And then poor Bathsheba instantly perceived her slip in making this admission: in hastily trying to retrieve it, she went from bad to worse. “Don’t, however, suppose that _I_ derive any pleasure from what you tell me.”

text: Oh, you understand, ma’am, his poor mother—not much of a Bible reader—got things muddled at the christening. She thought Abel was the one who killed Cain, so she named him Cain, though she meant Abel all along. The vicar corrected it, but it was too late; the name stuck fast in the village. It’s been a real cross for the lad.
source_text: “Oh you see, mem, his pore mother, not being a Scripture-read woman, made a mistake at his christening, thinking ’twas Abel killed Cain, and called en Cain, meaning Abel all the time. The parson put it right, but ’twas too late, for the name could never be got rid of in the parish. ’Tis very unfortunate for the boy.”

text: “I’ve no lambing shed on this farm, not like the one I kept at Norcombe,” Gabriel remarked. “It’s a sore trial, having to carry the frail newborns indoors. Were it not for your place here, maltster, I’d be at a loss in this biting cold. But how are you faring today, maltster?”
source_text: “We’ve no lambing-hut here, as I used to have at Norcombe,” said Gabriel, “and ’tis such a plague to bring the weakly ones to a house. If ’twasn’t for your place here, malter, I don’t know what I should do i’ this keen weather. And how is it with you to-day, malter?”

text: She knew with a sinking dread that if Troy returned to her now, at this precise and perilous moment, and crossed paths with Boldwood, a violent clash would follow. Her breath quickened with anxious care at the mere thought of harm befalling Troy. The faintest ember would ignite the farmer’s quick-burning fury and envy; all self-command would desert him, as it had only hours ago; Troy’s careless gaiety might sharpen into provocation, veering toward mockery, and Boldwood’s wrath might then veer toward vengeance.
source_text: She felt wretchedly certain that if he revisited her just at this nick of time, and came into contact with Boldwood, a fierce quarrel would be the consequence. She panted with solicitude when she thought of possible injury to Troy. The least spark would kindle the farmer’s swift feelings of rage and jealousy; he would lose his self-mastery as he had this evening; Troy’s blitheness might become aggressive; it might take the direction of derision, and Boldwood’s anger might then take the direction of revenge.

text: After all, how could a lighthearted lover of petticoats remain forever vexed with the fellow? There are moments when women of Bathsheba’s temper will tolerate a fair amount of unorthodox conduct. When they crave admiration, which is frequent; when they long to be commanded, which is occasional; and when they demand propriety, which is rare. At present the first impulse held sway in Bathsheba, tinged faintly with the second. Besides, by fortune or by mischief, the attendant was rendered intriguing beforehand by being a comely outsider who had plainly known finer times.
source_text: After all, how could a cheerful wearer of skirts be permanently offended with the man? There are occasions when girls like Bathsheba will put up with a great deal of unconventional behaviour. When they want to be praised, which is often, when they want to be mastered, which is sometimes; and when they want no nonsense, which is seldom. Just now the first feeling was in the ascendant with Bathsheba, with a dash of the second. Moreover, by chance or by devilry, the ministrant was antecedently made interesting by being a handsome stranger who had evidently seen better days.
