text: The shift in setting and circumstance—from a bedroom’s familiar preening hour to a moment of open-air travel—gave the casual act a freshness it didn’t really have on its own. The scene was subtle. Woman’s age-old frailty had stepped into the daylight, which dressed it in the charm of something new. Though Gabriel Oak wanted to be charitable, a sardonic conclusion was hard to resist as he watched. She had no real need to look in the mirror. She didn’t straighten her hat, or smooth her hair, or coax a dimple into place, or make any gesture showing that such purposes had led her to pick up the glass. She merely examined herself as a lovely specimen of natural womanhood, her mind apparently drifting toward distant but plausible dramas in which men would feature—glimpses of future conquests—her smiles of a sort that hinted hearts were imagined as won and lost. Yet this was only speculation, and the whole string of motions was performed so idly that it would be reckless to claim any intent lay behind them at all.
source_text: The change from the customary spot and necessary occasion of such an act—from the dressing hour in a bedroom to a time of travelling out of doors—lent to the idle deed a novelty it did not intrinsically possess. The picture was a delicate one. Woman’s prescriptive infirmity had stalked into the sunlight, which had clothed it in the freshness of an originality. A cynical inference was irresistible by Gabriel Oak as he regarded the scene, generous though he fain would have been. There was no necessity whatever for her looking in the glass. She did not adjust her hat, or pat her hair, or press a dimple into shape, or do one thing to signify that any such intention had been her motive in taking up the glass. She simply observed herself as a fair product of Nature in the feminine kind, her thoughts seeming to glide into far-off though likely dramas in which men would play a part—vistas of probable triumphs—the smiles being of a phase suggesting that hearts were imagined as lost and won. Still, this was but conjecture, and the whole series of actions was so idly put forth as to make it rash to assert that intention had any part in them at all.

text: Since that time, the name I once meant to keep for the distant views and countryside of a purely imagined realm has steadily gained currency as a handy label; and that dream-realm has gradually hardened into a practical district where folk can settle, lease a cottage, and send letters to the press. Yet I entreat every kind and courteous reader to put this out of mind, and to resist with firm conviction the notion that any denizens of a Victorian Wessex exist beyond the covers of this book and the companion volumes where they first came to light.
source_text: Since then the appellation which I had thought to reserve to the horizons and landscapes of a merely realistic dream-country, has become more and more popular as a practical definition; and the dream-country has, by degrees, solidified into a utilitarian region which people can go to, take a house in, and write to the papers from. But I ask all good and gentle readers to be so kind as to forget this, and to refuse steadfastly to believe that there are any inhabitants of a Victorian Wessex outside the pages of this and the companion volumes in which they were first discovered.

text: At first, Troy chose to feign indifference and let events unfold as they would. That the man had known him seemed exceedingly likely, yet a sliver of uncertainty remained. Then the powerful reluctance he had harbored against letting word of his nearness travel ahead of him to Weatherbury, should he go back—a reluctance born of the conviction that awareness of his current lowly work would further shame him in his wife’s regard—surged back undiminished. Furthermore, if he decided against returning altogether, a report of his survival and his presence close by would prove troublesome; and he was eager to learn the state of his wife’s worldly circumstances before choosing his course.
source_text: At first Troy resolved to take no notice and abide by circumstances. That he had been recognized by this man was highly probable; yet there was room for a doubt. Then the great objection he had felt to allowing news of his proximity to precede him to Weatherbury in the event of his return, based on a feeling that knowledge of his present occupation would discredit him still further in his wife’s eyes, returned in full force. Moreover, should he resolve not to return at all, a tale of his being alive and being in the neighbourhood would be awkward; and he was anxious to acquire a knowledge of his wife’s temporal affairs before deciding which to do.

text: “Here’s a drop of cider to soothe his throat,” said Jan Coggan, hoisting a jug, yanking the stopper free, and pressing its opening to Cainy’s lips; meanwhile, Joseph Poorgrass started to worry uneasily about the dire outcome if Cainy Ball choked to death on his coughing fit, taking the tale of his Bath escapades to the grave.
source_text: “Here’s some cider for him—that’ll cure his throat,” said Jan Coggan, lifting a flagon of cider, pulling out the cork, and applying the hole to Cainy’s mouth; Joseph Poorgrass in the meantime beginning to think apprehensively of the serious consequences that would follow Cainy Ball’s strangulation in his cough, and the history of his Bath adventures dying with him.

text: As though seized by a flash of foresight, Bathsheba choked out, “No, it isn’t true; it can’t be true!” After that, she neither spoke nor perceived anything more. The frozen composure that had lately formed around her shattered, and a flood of emotion broke loose and swept her away. A blackness clouded her vision, and she dropped to the ground.
source_text: As if endowed with the spirit of prophecy, Bathsheba gasped out, “No, it is not true; it cannot be true!” Then she said and heard no more. The ice of self-command which had latterly gathered over her was broken, and the currents burst forth again, and overwhelmed her. A darkness came into her eyes, and she fell.

text: “Draw near, herdsman, and sup. We’ve naught but swig and gulp here—a splash of something, though of little worth,” spoke the brewer, lifting his gaze from the hearth, his eyes stained a raw crimson and filmed from decades of peering into the coals. “Hoist the Mercy-me, Elias. Test if it’s heated, Elias.”
source_text: “Come, shepherd, and drink. ’Tis gape and swaller with us—a drap of sommit, but not of much account,” said the maltster, removing from the fire his eyes, which were vermilion-red and bleared by gazing into it for so many years. “Take up the God-forgive-me, Jacob. See if ’tis warm, Jacob.”

text: Cael had his own grounds for deeming that postponement a thoroughly ill-advised scheme, even though Liddy had dwelt at the steading for many seasons during the lifetime of Seraphina’s uncle. Spectres of sundry unhappy turns that might spring from the delay drifted through his mind. Yet his desire carried no authority, and he stepped within to ask his mistress how she wished the matter settled. He discovered her in a strange temper: the glance she lifted toward him was mistrustful and bewildered, as though weighted by some earlier meditation. Troy had not yet come back. At first Seraphina agreed, with an air of unconcern, to his suggestion that they bear their charge straightway to the chapel; but a moment later, trailing Cael to the wicket, she veered to a pitch of tender anxiety over Liddy’s fate and insisted the maiden be carried indoors. Oak reasoned that it would be simpler to leave her in the cart, exactly as she rested now, encircled by her blossoms and green sprays, merely trundling the conveyance into the carriage-shed until daybreak, but he could not prevail. “It is heartless and uncharitable,” she declared, “to abandon the poor creature in a carriage-shed the whole night.”
source_text: Gabriel had his reasons for thinking the latter a most objectionable plan, notwithstanding that Fanny had been an inmate of the farm-house for several years in the lifetime of Bathsheba’s uncle. Visions of several unhappy contingencies which might arise from this delay flitted before him. But his will was not law, and he went indoors to inquire of his mistress what were her wishes on the subject. He found her in an unusual mood: her eyes as she looked up to him were suspicious and perplexed as with some antecedent thought. Troy had not yet returned. At first Bathsheba assented with a mien of indifference to his proposition that they should go on to the church at once with their burden; but immediately afterwards, following Gabriel to the gate, she swerved to the extreme of solicitousness on Fanny’s account, and desired that the girl might be brought into the house. Oak argued upon the convenience of leaving her in the waggon, just as she lay now, with her flowers and green leaves about her, merely wheeling the vehicle into the coach-house till the morning, but to no purpose. “It is unkind and unchristian,” she said, “to leave the poor thing in a coach-house all night.”

text: It was now the earliest breath of spring—the season for turning the sheep out to grass, when they take their first bite of the meadows before the fields are shut up for hay. The wind, which had blown steadily from the east for weeks, had shifted south, and the heart of spring arrived all at once—almost without preamble. It was that moment in the vernal quarter when one imagines the Dryads stirring from their winter sleep. The realm of plants began to quicken and expand, the sap to climb, until in the utter stillness of solitary gardens and pathless groves, where all things appear helpless and motionless after the chains and bondage of frost, there are rustlings, stretchings, concerted pushes and pulls altogether, beside which the mighty heaves of cranes and pulleys in a clamorous city seem but puny exertions.
source_text: It was now early spring—the time of going to grass with the sheep, when they have the first feed of the meadows, before these are laid up for mowing. The wind, which had been blowing east for several weeks, had veered to the southward, and the middle of spring had come abruptly—almost without a beginning. It was that period in the vernal quarter when we may suppose the Dryads to be waking for the season. The vegetable world begins to move and swell and the saps to rise, till in the completest silence of lone gardens and trackless plantations, where everything seems helpless and still after the bond and slavery of frost, there are bustlings, strainings, united thrusts, and pulls-all-together, in comparison with which the powerful tugs of cranes and pulleys in a noisy city are but pigmy efforts.

text: That evening, as she gazed into the glowing hearth, Bathsheba’s countenance might have stirred unease even in those who cared for her the least. The sorrow of Fanny Robin’s end cast no luster upon Bathsheba’s own, though she played the Esther to this wretched Vashti, and their lots could be read as opposite mirrors. When Liddy entered the chamber again, the lovely eyes that rose to meet her held a dull, depleted cast. By the time she departed, having finished her tale, those same eyes were alive with open anguish. Her plain rural soul, nourished on old-fashioned scruples, was harrowed by what would have barely touched a worldly woman—Fanny and her infant, if infant there had been, lying still in death.
source_text: Bathsheba’s face, as she continued looking into the fire that evening, might have excited solicitousness on her account even among those who loved her least. The sadness of Fanny Robin’s fate did not make Bathsheba’s glorious, although she was the Esther to this poor Vashti, and their fates might be supposed to stand in some respects as contrasts to each other. When Liddy came into the room a second time the beautiful eyes which met hers had worn a listless, weary look. When she went out after telling the story they had expressed wretchedness in full activity. Her simple country nature, fed on old-fashioned principles, was troubled by that which would have troubled a woman of the world very little, both Fanny and her child, if she had one, being dead.

text: The building's broad ends, which in church terms would be the nave and chancel, were sectioned off with wattle hurdles, and all the sheep were massed together inside these two pens. In one corner stood a catching-pen, where three or four sheep were held at the ready so the shearers could grab them without delay. Further back, softened by the amber dimness, the three women—Maryann Money, Temperance Miller, and Soberness Miller—were gathering fleeces and twisting wool ropes with a wimble to bind them. They had passable help from the old maltster, who, once the malting season ran its course from October to April, lent a hand on any of the neighbouring farms.
source_text: The spacious ends of the building, answering ecclesiastically to nave and chancel extremities, were fenced off with hurdles, the sheep being all collected in a crowd within these two enclosures; and in one angle a catching-pen was formed, in which three or four sheep were continuously kept ready for the shearers to seize without loss of time. In the background, mellowed by tawny shade, were the three women, Maryann Money, and Temperance and Soberness Miller, gathering up the fleeces and twisting ropes of wool with a wimble for tying them round. They were indifferently well assisted by the old maltster, who, when the malting season from October to April had passed, made himself useful upon any of the bordering farmsteads.

text: They say that simple relief after agony can feel, for a while, like joy; and the faces of those wretched beasts showed it plainly now. Forty-nine procedures were carried through without mishap. In the frantic haste demanded by the desperate condition of several among the herd, Gabriel’s hand slipped but a single time—missing its target entirely, and dealing a swift, fatal stroke to the tormented ewe. Four had perished; three mended without need of the knife. The full tally of sheep that had wandered and wounded themselves so grievously stood at fifty-seven.
source_text: It has been said that mere ease after torment is delight for a time; and the countenances of these poor creatures expressed it now. Forty-nine operations were successfully performed. Owing to the great hurry necessitated by the far-gone state of some of the flock, Gabriel missed his aim in one case, and in one only—striking wide of the mark, and inflicting a mortal blow at once upon the suffering ewe. Four had died; three recovered without an operation. The total number of sheep which had thus strayed and injured themselves so dangerously was fifty-seven.

text: The villagers on the ground still labored to quell the blaze, though their efforts availed little. All of them were stained orange, with shadows of shifting shapes pressing behind them. Around the edge of the largest stack, just beyond the fire’s direct glare, a pony stood carrying a young woman on its back. Beside her, on foot, was another woman. They kept well back from the flames, careful not to unsettle the horse.
source_text: On the ground the groups of villagers were still occupied in doing all they could to keep down the conflagration, which was not much. They were all tinged orange, and backed up by shadows of varying pattern. Round the corner of the largest stack, out of the direct rays of the fire, stood a pony, bearing a young woman on its back. By her side was another woman, on foot. These two seemed to keep at a distance from the fire, that the horse might not become restive.

text: The sound of the horse drew her outside, and she lifted her gaze. A rider crossed the space between her and the heavens, moving on toward the pasture where the sheep grazed, his face turning away as he went. Gabriel watched her. In that instant, a woman’s expression and her words can speak two entirely different stories. Bathsheba’s face seemed brimming with thankfulness, yet she spoke:—
source_text: She went out when the horse was heard, and looked up. A mounted figure passed between her and the sky, and drew on towards the field of sheep, the rider turning his face in receding. Gabriel looked at her. It was a moment when a woman’s eyes and tongue tell distinctly opposite tales. Bathsheba looked full of gratitude, and she said:—

text: “Please stay, Liddy,” Bathsheba murmured, her voice edged with a faint unease. “I’m absurdly unsettled—I can’t fathom why. I wish I hadn’t felt compelled to attend this dance, but there’s no avoiding it now. I haven’t exchanged a word with Mr. Boldwood since the autumn, when I agreed to meet him at Christmas on a matter of business, but I never imagined anything like this would come of it.”
source_text: “Don’t go away, Liddy,” said Bathsheba, almost timidly. “I am foolishly agitated—I cannot tell why. I wish I had not been obliged to go to this dance; but there’s no escaping now. I have not spoken to Mr. Boldwood since the autumn, when I promised to see him at Christmas on business, but I had no idea there was to be anything of this kind.”

text: While she lingered there, reading and lost in thought, the organ's notes swelled once more from within the church, and she drifted with that same buoyant stride around to the porch, pausing to listen. The entrance stood shut, and the choir was rehearsing an unfamiliar hymn. Feelings she had lately believed long extinguished stirred unexpectedly in Bathsheba. The thin, fragile voices of the children reached her ears, articulating with perfect clarity the lyrics they sang without grasp or awareness—
source_text: Whilst she stood and read and meditated the tones of the organ began again in the church, and she went with the same light step round to the porch and listened. The door was closed, and the choir was learning a new hymn. Bathsheba was stirred by emotions which latterly she had assumed to be altogether dead within her. The little attenuated voices of the children brought to her ear in distinct utterance the words they sang without thought or comprehension—

text: “Move nearer,” breathed Ashford; and on silent feet they crept forward. The sound left no room for doubt now. Troy had pressed his face nearly to the glass, peering within. Yet it was not merely the act of looking that held him; he seemed caught fast by some exchange unfolding inside the malt-house, the speakers unmistakably Oak and the maltster.
source_text: “Let’s come closer,” whispered Samway; and they approached on tiptoe. There was no disbelieving the report any longer. Troy’s face was almost close to the pane, and he was looking in. Not only was he looking in, but he appeared to have been arrested by a conversation which was in progress in the malt-house, the voices of the interlocutors being those of Oak and the maltster.

text: “Of course, I’ll let it go if that’s what you want. But reasons have nothing to do with what’s proper. I’m a man past my prime, ready to shelter you for the rest of our days. On your part, at least, there’s no reckless desire or unseemly rush—on mine, perhaps, there is. Still, I can’t help noticing that if you act out of compassion, and, as you put it, a wish to set things right, by striking a pact with me for some distant future—a covenant that would mend everything and bring me joy, however belated—there’s no blame to lay at your feet as a woman. Wasn’t I the one who stood closest to you? Weren’t you nearly mine once before? Surely you can grant me this much: that you’ll take me back if fate allows? Now, please, say something! Oh, Bathsheba, vow it—just a small vow—that if you ever wed again, you’ll wed me!”
source_text: “Of course, I’ll drop the subject if you wish. But propriety has nothing to do with reasons. I am a middle-aged man, willing to protect you for the remainder of our lives. On your side, at least, there is no passion or blamable haste—on mine, perhaps, there is. But I can’t help seeing that if you choose from a feeling of pity, and, as you say, a wish to make amends, to make a bargain with me for a far-ahead time—an agreement which will set all things right and make me happy, late though it may be—there is no fault to be found with you as a woman. Hadn’t I the first place beside you? Haven’t you been almost mine once already? Surely you can say to me as much as this, you will have me back again should circumstances permit? Now, pray speak! O Bathsheba, promise—it is only a little promise—that if you marry again, you will marry me!”

text: Within the enclosure stood two women and a pair of cows. Beside the animals rested a pail of bran-mash, its warmth rising in faint wisps. The elder of the two had long since crossed the threshold of middle years. Her fellow seemed youthful and lithe in bearing; he could form no settled judgment of her face, for she remained almost directly beneath his gaze, so that he looked down upon her as from a height, much as Milton’s Satan first beheld Paradise. She wore neither bonnet nor hat, but had drawn about herself a heavy cloak, cast loosely over her head like a shroud.
source_text: The place contained two women and two cows. By the side of the latter a steaming bran-mash stood in a bucket. One of the women was past middle age. Her companion was apparently young and graceful; he could form no decided opinion upon her looks, her position being almost beneath his eye, so that he saw her in a bird’s-eye view, as Milton’s Satan first saw Paradise. She wore no bonnet or hat, but had enveloped herself in a large cloak, which was carelessly flung over her head as a covering.

text: Bathsheba, for her part, was under no illusion that Farmer Boldwood had passed by on some errand or merely out of idle wandering. She weighed the likelihoods and understood that his presence there was her own doing. It disturbed her deeply to realize how great a blaze a small spark might ignite. Bathsheba was not one to scheme for a husband, nor did she consciously play with men’s hearts, and any seasoned critic who watched a true coquette at work and then observed her would have been astonished to find Bathsheba so unlike that figure, yet so closely resembling what a flirt is imagined to be.
source_text: As for Bathsheba, she was not deceived into the belief that Farmer Boldwood had walked by on business or in idleness. She collected the probabilities of the case, and concluded that she was herself responsible for Boldwood’s appearance there. It troubled her much to see what a great flame a little wildfire was likely to kindle. Bathsheba was no schemer for marriage, nor was she deliberately a trifler with the affections of men, and a censor’s experience on seeing an actual flirt after observing her would have been a feeling of surprise that Bathsheba could be so different from such a one, and yet so like what a flirt is supposed to be.

text: Bathsheba moved around the buttress and saw the gaping hole and the headstone, its fine-veined marble smeared and blotched exactly as Troy had discovered and abandoned it two hours before. On the far side of the scene stood Gabriel. His gaze was likewise fastened on the grave, and because her approach had been silent, he had not yet noticed her. She did not immediately recognize that the stately headstone and the opened earth belonged to Fanny, and she searched to either side and all about for some simpler mound, heaped with soil and clotted in the ordinary fashion. Then her eyes followed Oak’s, and she read the first words of the inscription:—
source_text: Bathsheba skirted the buttress, and beheld the hole and the tomb, its delicately veined surface splashed and stained just as Troy had seen it and left it two hours earlier. On the other side of the scene stood Gabriel. His eyes, too, were fixed on the tomb, and her arrival having been noiseless, she had not as yet attracted his attention. Bathsheba did not at once perceive that the grand tomb and the disturbed grave were Fanny’s, and she looked on both sides and around for some humbler mound, earthed up and clodded in the usual way. Then her eye followed Oak’s, and she read the words with which the inscription opened:—

text: "And you can craft sundials and carve people’s names onto their wagons near as fine as copperplate engraving, with elegant flourishes and long sweeping tails. It’s a right splendid thing, you being so clever, shepherd. Joseph Poorgrass used to carve on Farmer James Everdene’s wagons before you arrived, and he never could recall which way to twist the J’s and E’s—could you, Joseph?" Joseph shook his head, confirming the utter truth that he could not. "So you’d go and shape them backwards, just like this, wouldn’t you, Joseph?" Matthew traced the marks on the dusty floor with the handle of his whip.
source_text: “And that ye can make sun-dials, and prent folks’ names upon their waggons almost like copper-plate, with beautiful flourishes, and great long tails. A excellent fine thing for ye to be such a clever man, shepherd. Joseph Poorgrass used to prent to Farmer James Everdene’s waggons before you came, and ’a could never mind which way to turn the J’s and E’s—could ye, Joseph?” Joseph shook his head to express how absolute was the fact that he couldn’t. “And so you used to do ’em the wrong way, like this, didn’t ye, Joseph?” Matthew marked on the dusty floor with his whip-handle

text: “I am beside myself over this, utterly furious,” he said. “I’m no stoic to be begging here; yet I do beg you. I wish you could see the devotion I hold for you; but that’s impossible. Out of simple human pity for a solitary man, don’t cast me aside now!”
source_text: “I am beyond myself about this, and am mad,” he said. “I am no stoic at all to be supplicating here; but I do supplicate to you. I wish you knew what is in me of devotion to you; but it is impossible, that. In bare human mercy to a lonely man, don’t throw me off now!”

text: That night, Bathsheba was stirred by an uncommon fervor, her flushed cheeks and vivid lips gleaming against the tangled darkness of her hair. She appeared to be awaiting support, and at her insistence, the chair at the table's foot remained empty until the meal was underway. Then she called upon Gabriel to occupy that seat and assume its attendant responsibilities, which he accepted without hesitation.
source_text: This evening Bathsheba was unusually excited, her red cheeks and lips contrasting lustrously with the mazy skeins of her shadowy hair. She seemed to expect assistance, and the seat at the bottom of the table was at her request left vacant until after they had begun the meal. She then asked Gabriel to take the place and the duties appertaining to that end, which he did with great readiness.

text: Gabriel stood gazing at the thin blue drift of wood-smoke as it coiled from the chimney, lost in a peculiar reverie. As dusk fell, he let his imagination follow that smoke back down the flue to its source—picturing the hearth and Bathsheba seated there, still clad in the outdoor dress she had worn upon the hill. In these early days of his love, those garments had become, through their association with her, inseparable from the person herself; they were part of the tender essence that was Bathsheba Everdene.
source_text: Gabriel had watched the blue wood-smoke curling from the chimney with strange meditation. At evening he had fancifully traced it down the chimney to the spot of its origin—seen the hearth and Bathsheba beside it—beside it in her out-door dress; for the clothes she had worn on the hill were by association equally with her person included in the compass of his affection; they seemed at this early time of his love a necessary ingredient of the sweet mixture called Bathsheba Everdene.

text: Destiny had treated him harshly over the past twenty-four hours. His day had unfolded in a manner sharply at odds with his plans. There is always a resistance to be conquered when embarking on a fresh course of action—a resistance that seems to reside not only within ourselves, but in the confining circumstances around us, which appear to conspire against any novel attempt at improvement.
source_text: Fate had dealt grimly with him through the last four-and-twenty hours. His day had been spent in a way which varied very materially from his intentions regarding it. There is always an inertia to be overcome in striking out a new line of conduct—not more in ourselves, it seems, than in circumscribing events, which appear as if leagued together to allow no novelties in the way of amelioration.

text: “Yes, in those days his mind was fixed on lofty matters,” put in Billy Smallbury. “Once, Parson Thirdly crossed his path and greeted him, ‘Good day, Mr. Everdene; a splendid morning!’ ‘Amen,’ Everdene replied, all distracted, his thoughts purely on faith at the sight of a clergyman. Indeed, he was a deeply devout soul.”
source_text: “Ay, at that time he thought of nothing but high things,” added Billy Smallbury. “One day Parson Thirdly met him and said, ‘Good-Morning, Mister Everdene; ’tis a fine day!’ ‘Amen’ said Everdene, quite absent-like, thinking only of religion when he seed a parson. Yes, he was a very Christian man.”

text: “I’m not ashamed to admit I saw this coming,” Oak said, with his usual openness. “In truth, I’d set my heart on the position. Still, Miss Everdene is perfectly entitled to act as her own bailiff if she wishes—and to leave me as nothing more than an ordinary shepherd.” He drew a long, quiet breath, gazed mournfully into the glowing bed of ashes, and appeared to sink into reflections that held little brightness.
source_text: “I don’t mind owning that I expected it,” said Oak, frankly. “Indeed, I hoped for the place. At the same time, Miss Everdene has a right to be her own baily if she choose—and to keep me down to be a common shepherd only.” Oak drew a slow breath, looked sadly into the bright ashpit, and seemed lost in thoughts not of the most hopeful hue.

text: Because Oak spent six times as many days laboring as he did resting, his working attire became his truest signature—the image his neighbors conjured whenever they thought of him. He wore a low-crowned felt hat, its brim splayed wide from being jammed down firmly against the wind, and a coat reminiscent of Dr. Johnson’s. His legs were sheathed in ordinary leather gaiters, and his feet in boots of such emphatic size that each one formed a roomy chamber, so stoutly built that a man could stand in a river all day and never feel the damp—their maker being a scrupulous craftsman who made up for any shortcomings in his pattern with unsparing dimension and heft.
source_text: Since he lived six times as many working-days as Sundays, Oak’s appearance in his old clothes was most peculiarly his own—the mental picture formed by his neighbours in imagining him being always dressed in that way. He wore a low-crowned felt hat, spread out at the base by tight jamming upon the head for security in high winds, and a coat like Dr. Johnson’s; his lower extremities being encased in ordinary leather leggings and boots emphatically large, affording to each foot a roomy apartment so constructed that any wearer might stand in a river all day long and know nothing of damp—their maker being a conscientious man who endeavoured to compensate for any weakness in his cut by unstinted dimension and solidity.

text: For a brief moment the traveler halted in that rigid quietude which declares itself not a conclusion, but only a pause within an earlier movement. Her posture suggested one who attends, whether to the outer realm of noise, or to the inner murmur of reverie. A sharper scrutiny might have discerned indications that she favored the second course. And, as the sequel revealed, she was curiously applying the inventive gift to the specialty of the ingenious Henri Maillardet, the creator of mechanical replicas for living appendages.
source_text: For a few seconds the wayfarer stood with that tense stillness which signifies itself to be not the end, but merely the suspension, of a previous motion. Her attitude was that of a person who listens, either to the external world of sound, or to the imagined discourse of thought. A close criticism might have detected signs proving that she was intent on the latter alternative. Moreover, as was shown by what followed, she was oddly exercising the faculty of invention upon the speciality of the clever Jacquet Droz, the designer of automatic substitutes for human limbs.

text: Laban moved to the door. As he pulled it open, the low roar of activity spilled forth like a surge washing over a quiet shore—the crowd gathered just within the hall—and sank to a whisper when he shut it once more. Every man stood in taut expectation, gazing at the dark crowns of the trees swaying softly against the heavens, now and then trembling in a faint breeze, as though each found the view absorbing, though none truly did. One among them began to pace back and forth, then returned to his starting point and halted, struck by the feeling that walking had lost all purpose now.
source_text: Laban then went to the door. When he opened it the hum of bustle rolled out as a wave upon a still strand—the assemblage being immediately inside the hall—and was deadened to a murmur as he closed it again. Each man waited intently, and looked around at the dark tree tops gently rocking against the sky and occasionally shivering in a slight wind, as if he took interest in the scene, which neither did. One of them began walking up and down, and then came to where he started from and stopped again, with a sense that walking was a thing not worth doing now.

text: The moon was out tonight, but its light was unlike any he had known. His window caught only a reflected glimmer, and the pale glow took on the inverted quality that snow imparts—rising from below to illuminate his ceiling with an eerie cast, throwing shadows into unfamiliar corners and kindling brightness where darkness once lay.
source_text: The moon shone to-night, and its light was not of a customary kind. His window admitted only a reflection of its rays, and the pale sheen had that reversed direction which snow gives, coming upward and lighting up his ceiling in an unnatural way, casting shadows in strange places, and putting lights where shadows had used to be.

text: “Well—what a business! How absurdly careless of you not to realize I’d taken the trap and horse. I couldn’t rouse Maryann or get indoors, though I rapped on her window-ledge for a full ten minutes. Luckily, I had the key to the coach-house, so I put no one else out. Did it never cross your mind it might be me?”
source_text: “Well—what a thing! How very foolish of you not to know that I had taken the trap and horse. I could neither wake Maryann nor get into the house, though I hammered for ten minutes against her window-sill. Fortunately, I could get the key of the coach-house, so I troubled no one further. Didn’t you think it might be me?”

text: Boldwood pursued him a short distance across the field, paused in uncertainty, then reversed his steps. Summoning a final, irrevocable determination, he followed once more, cutting off all thought of retreat. As he neared the sheltered corner where the sheepfold stood, the farmer withdrew his pocket-book, loosened its clasp, and let it fall open upon his palm. There, exposed, lay a letter—Bathsheba’s.
source_text: Boldwood followed him a little way up the field, hesitated, and turned back. He followed him again with a last resolve, annihilating return. On approaching the nook in which the fold was constructed, the farmer drew out his pocket-book, unfastened it, and allowed it to lie open on his hand. A letter was revealed—Bathsheba’s.

text: “I wouldn’t confess this to another soul—and I’m not fully admitting it to you, either. Yet, there may have been a touch of vanity in my foolish assumption that evening. I realized my words of praise were probably a sentiment you hear so often they bring you no delight, but I truly believed your generous spirit would keep you from condemning a careless tongue so severely—as you have—and from thinking ill of me and hurting me today, while I’m labouring to salvage your hay.”
source_text: “I would not own it to anybody else—nor do I exactly to you. Still, there might have been some self-conceit in my foolish supposition the other night. I knew that what I said in admiration might be an opinion too often forced upon you to give any pleasure, but I certainly did think that the kindness of your nature might prevent you judging an uncontrolled tongue harshly—which you have done—and thinking badly of me and wounding me this morning, when I am working hard to save your hay.”

text: The second matter was the delivery of his garments, which she was now obliged to sort through and confirm as his—though the task had, in truth, been largely accomplished earlier by those who had searched the papers in his pockets. Amid her distress, it struck her with piercing clarity that he had shed his clothing in the absolute certainty of donning it again within moments, so that to imagine anything save death could have hindered him seemed a stubborn folly to indulge.
source_text: The other circumstance was the arrival of his clothes, when it became necessary for her to examine and identify them—though this had virtually been done long before by those who inspected the letters in his pockets. It was so evident to her in the midst of her agitation that Troy had undressed in the full conviction of dressing again almost immediately, that the notion that anything but death could have prevented him was a perverse one to entertain.

text: From the fathomless depths of his coat, Gabriel drew forth a branding stylus, plunged it into the simmering pitch, and upon the rump of each newborn ewe he pressed the cipher that was his secret joy to contemplate—“B. E.” To every soul in the surrounding countryside, those letters proclaimed that from this moment the lambs were the sole property of Mistress Bathsheba Everdene, and of none other.
source_text: Oak took from his illimitable pockets a marking iron, dipped it into the pot, and imprinted on the buttocks of the infant sheep the initials of her he delighted to muse on—“B. E.,” which signified to all the region round that henceforth the lambs belonged to Farmer Bathsheba Everdene, and to no one else.

text: Joseph Poorgrass glanced back at the mournful load, just visible through the blossoming laurustinus, then peered into the deep darkness beneath the towering trees on either side—blurred, shapeless, ghostly in their uniform grey. He felt far from cheerful and longed for even the company of a child or a dog. He pulled the horse to a halt and listened. No footfall or carriage sound reached him anywhere, and the absolute stillness was broken only by a dense droplet plunging from a tree through the evergreens, striking poor Fanny's coffin with a sharp tap. By now the mist had soaked the trees, and this was the first drip from the brimming leaves. The hollow thud of its fall painfully reminded the carter of the grim Leveller. Then close by came another drop, then two or three more. Soon a steady drumming of these weighty drops beat upon the dead leaves, the road, and the travellers. The nearer branches glistened with fog like the grey hair of old men, and the rusty-red beech leaves were hung with similar droplets, like diamonds scattered through auburn hair.
source_text: Joseph Poorgrass looked round upon his sad burden as it loomed faintly through the flowering laurustinus, then at the unfathomable gloom amid the high trees on each hand, indistinct, shadowless, and spectre-like in their monochrome of grey. He felt anything but cheerful, and wished he had the company even of a child or dog. Stopping the horse, he listened. Not a footstep or wheel was audible anywhere around, and the dead silence was broken only by a heavy particle falling from a tree through the evergreens and alighting with a smart rap upon the coffin of poor Fanny. The fog had by this time saturated the trees, and this was the first dropping of water from the overbrimming leaves. The hollow echo of its fall reminded the waggoner painfully of the grim Leveller. Then hard by came down another drop, then two or three. Presently there was a continual tapping of these heavy drops upon the dead leaves, the road, and the travellers. The nearer boughs were beaded with the mist to the greyness of aged men, and the rusty-red leaves of the beeches were hung with similar drops, like diamonds on auburn hair.

text: “Yes, you must be blind, Liddy!” she cried, wild with grief and reckless abandon. “Oh, I love him to the point of madness and anguish and torment! Don’t be afraid of me, though I daresay I could frighten any innocent soul. Come nearer—nearer still.” She wrapped her arms around Liddy’s neck. “I must pour it out to someone; it’s consuming me! Don’t you know me well enough by now to see through that wretched denial? O God, what a falsehood it was! May Heaven and my beloved forgive me. And don’t you understand that a woman truly in love thinks nothing of perjury when it’s weighed against her heart? There, leave the room; I need to be entirely alone.”
source_text: “Yes; you must be a blind thing, Liddy!” she said, in reckless abandonment and grief. “Oh, I love him to very distraction and misery and agony! Don’t be frightened at me, though perhaps I am enough to frighten any innocent woman. Come closer—closer.” She put her arms round Liddy’s neck. “I must let it out to somebody; it is wearing me away! Don’t you yet know enough of me to see through that miserable denial of mine? O God, what a lie it was! Heaven and my Love forgive me. And don’t you know that a woman who loves at all thinks nothing of perjury when it is balanced against her love? There, go out of the room; I want to be quite alone.”

text: The heavens were strikingly clear, and the glitter of the countless stars seemed like the rhythmic throbbing of a single living form, governed by one shared pulse. The North Star sat squarely in the wind’s gaze, and since dusk the Great Bear had wheeled outward around it toward the east, until it now stood at a right angle to the meridian. Here, the subtle differences in the stars’ hues—more often described in books than observed in England—were truly visible. Sirius pierced the eye with a cold, steely brilliance that felt almost regal, Capella glowed a warm yellow, while Aldebaran and Betelgeuse burned with a fierce, fiery red.
source_text: The sky was clear—remarkably clear—and the twinkling of all the stars seemed to be but throbs of one body, timed by a common pulse. The North Star was directly in the wind’s eye, and since evening the Bear had swung round it outwardly to the east, till he was now at a right angle with the meridian. A difference of colour in the stars—oftener read of than seen in England—was really perceptible here. The sovereign brilliancy of Sirius pierced the eye with a steely glitter, the star called Capella was yellow, Aldebaran and Betelgueux shone with a fiery red.

text: “Cain Ball, you are no mere child at the breast any longer,” Joseph intoned, his voice as grave and hollow as the moment required. “You understand what it means to swear an oath. It is a fearsome covenant, mark you—one you speak aloud and bind with your very life’s seal, and the prophet Matthew warns that on whomever it falls, it will crush him utterly to dust. Now, before all the laborers gathered here, can you swear to your testimony as the shepherd demands?”
source_text: “Cain Ball, you be no longer a babe and suckling,” said Joseph in the sepulchral tone the circumstances demanded, “and you know what taking an oath is. ’Tis a horrible testament mind ye, which you say and seal with your blood-stone, and the prophet Matthew tells us that on whomsoever it shall fall it will grind him to powder. Now, before all the work-folk here assembled, can you swear to your words as the shepherd asks ye?”

text: Thus Coggan bore the tidings. “Mark you, rain or shine, gale or sleet, he is to attend,” Jan appended. “The matter is most pressing, truly. The truth of it is, she must set her hand to a legal paper concerning a partnership with another husbandman for a great stretch of years. There, that is the sum of it, and now I have confessed it to you, Mother Tall, in a manner I would not have ventured had I not cherished you so despairingly.”
source_text: So Coggan delivered the message. “Mind, het or wet, blow or snow, he must come,” added Jan. “’Tis very particular, indeed. The fact is, ’tis to witness her sign some law-work about taking shares wi’ another farmer for a long span o’ years. There, that’s what ’tis, and now I’ve told ’ee, Mother Tall, in a way I shouldn’t ha’ done if I hadn’t loved ’ee so hopeless well.”

text: The man she was tethered to gleamed in brass and scarlet. He was a soldier. His abrupt arrival shattered the darkness the way a trumpet blast shatters stillness. The gloom that had always ruled this place was now utterly vanquished, not so much by the lantern’s glow as by what the lantern revealed. The gulf between this vision and her expectation of some menacing shape in drab clothing struck her like a magical metamorphosis.
source_text: The man to whom she was hooked was brilliant in brass and scarlet. He was a soldier. His sudden appearance was to darkness what the sound of a trumpet is to silence. Gloom, the _genius loci_ at all times hitherto, was now totally overthrown, less by the lantern-light than by what the lantern lighted. The contrast of this revelation with her anticipations of some sinister figure in sombre garb was so great that it had upon her the effect of a fairy transformation.

text: Now the gentlest and most yielding aspects of Bathsheba’s heart came to the fore—offered freely for his taking, stripped of the guardedness and pretense that her cooler prudence so often cast over them. Few men could have withstood that lovely face, tilted slightly back and aside in the familiar pose that says more than the words it frames, a look seemingly made for just such moments. Had she not been his wife, Troy would have yielded at once; as things stood, he resolved he would deceive her no more.
source_text: The tenderest and softest phases of Bathsheba’s nature were prominent now—advanced impulsively for his acceptance, without any of the disguises and defences which the wariness of her character when she was cool too frequently threw over them. Few men could have resisted the arch yet dignified entreaty of the beautiful face, thrown a little back and sideways in the well known attitude that expresses more than the words it accompanies, and which seems to have been designed for these special occasions. Had the woman not been his wife, Troy would have succumbed instantly; as it was, he thought he would not deceive her longer.

text: Gabriel could hardly fail to mark, without need of saying so, the farmer’s conduct that evening toward their hostess. Still, there was nothing singular in his gestures save the moment he chose for them. While the others averted their gaze, Boldwood watched her; when they looked her way, he glanced off; if they offered thanks or compliments, he kept quiet; if their attention strayed, he whispered his gratitude. The import resided in the contrast among acts that, taken singly, signified nothing; and the jealous watchfulness that afflicts lovers did not prevent Oak from reading these tokens truly.
source_text: It is scarcely necessary to state that Gabriel could not avoid noting the farmer’s bearing to-night towards their entertainer. Yet there was nothing exceptional in his actions beyond what appertained to his time of performing them. It was when the rest were all looking away that Boldwood observed her; when they regarded her he turned aside; when they thanked or praised he was silent; when they were inattentive he murmured his thanks. The meaning lay in the difference between actions, none of which had any meaning of itself; and the necessity of being jealous, which lovers are troubled with, did not lead Oak to underestimate these signs.

text: “Now that this topic has come up,” she declared with firm conviction, “I welcome the chance to correct a widespread and deeply irritating misunderstanding. I never made any clear commitment to Mr. Boldwood. I have never loved him. I hold him in esteem, and he has pressed me to become his wife. Yet I’ve offered him no definite reply. The moment he comes back, I will give it—and my reply will be that marrying him is out of the question.”
source_text: “Since this subject has been mentioned,” she said very emphatically, “I am glad of the opportunity of clearing up a mistake which is very common and very provoking. I didn’t definitely promise Mr. Boldwood anything. I have never cared for him. I respect him, and he has urged me to marry him. But I have given him no distinct answer. As soon as he returns I shall do so; and the answer will be that I cannot think of marrying him.”

text: Along the toll road connecting Casterbridge and Weatherbury, roughly three miles out from Casterbridge, rises Yalbury Hill—a long, sharp incline typical of the rolling highways in this region of South Wessex. Farmers and other carriage folk customarily climb down at the base and proceed on foot when heading home from market.
source_text: On the turnpike road, between Casterbridge and Weatherbury, and about three miles from the former place, is Yalbury Hill, one of those steep long ascents which pervade the highways of this undulating part of South Wessex. In returning from market it is usual for the farmers and other gig-gentry to alight at the bottom and walk up.

text: Half an hour afterward, Bathsheba stepped across the threshold of her home. In the candlelight her cheeks glowed with that heightened colour and restless fervour which had become almost habitual to her of late. Troy’s parting phrases, spoken at the very door where he had left her, still echoed in her mind. He had taken his leave for a span of two days, explaining that he meant to pass them in Bath among certain acquaintances. He had kissed her a second time as well.
source_text: Half an hour later Bathsheba entered her own house. There burnt upon her face when she met the light of the candles the flush and excitement which were little less than chronic with her now. The farewell words of Troy, who had accompanied her to the very door, still lingered in her ears. He had bidden her adieu for two days, which were, so he stated, to be spent at Bath in visiting some friends. He had also kissed her a second time.

text: “I heard your hound wailing and clawing at the cabin door when I arrived for the evening milking—such a mercy that Daisy’s flow is nearly done for the year, and I shan’t need to come this way beyond a week or two more. The beast caught sight of me, bounded over, and seized the hem of my dress. I crossed the yard at once and circled the hut, my first thought being to check whether the vents were shut. My own uncle keeps a cabin much like this one, and I’ve heard him warn his shepherd never to sleep without a vent cracked open. I drew back the door, and there you lay as still as a corpse. I dashed the milk over you, there being no water to hand, forgetting in my haste that it was warm and would do nothing.”
source_text: “I heard your dog howling and scratching at the door of the hut when I came to the milking (it was so lucky, Daisy’s milking is almost over for the season, and I shall not come here after this week or the next). The dog saw me, and jumped over to me, and laid hold of my skirt. I came across and looked round the hut the very first thing to see if the slides were closed. My uncle has a hut like this one, and I have heard him tell his shepherd not to go to sleep without leaving a slide open. I opened the door, and there you were like dead. I threw the milk over you, as there was no water, forgetting it was warm, and no use.”

text: The gentle autumn sun had now descended lower, and just before the pavilion a small scene unfolded. Bathsheba—conveyed to the fair that morning by her peculiar hand, Poorgrass—had, like the rest, caught wind of the notice that Mr. Francis, the Great Cosmopolitan Equestrian and Roughrider, would assume the role of Turpin, and she was not yet so weathered by years or worry as to lack a flicker of interest in beholding him. This singular attraction towered above all others at the fair, a swarm of lesser booths clustering beneath its shadow like ducklings round a mother. The spectators had streamed inside, and Boldwood, who had lingered all day hoping for a word with her, noticed her standing somewhat apart and drew near.
source_text: And now the mild autumn sun got lower, and in front of the pavilion the following incident had taken place. Bathsheba—who was driven to the fair that day by her odd man Poorgrass—had, like every one else, read or heard the announcement that Mr. Francis, the Great Cosmopolitan Equestrian and Roughrider, would enact the part of Turpin, and she was not yet too old and careworn to be without a little curiosity to see him. This particular show was by far the largest and grandest in the fair, a horde of little shows grouping themselves under its shade like chickens around a hen. The crowd had passed in, and Boldwood, who had been watching all the day for an opportunity of speaking to her, seeing her comparatively isolated, came up to her side.

text: Through the shimmering trails of this military dawn-light, she glimpsed the tint of Troy's blade-hand, diffused in a crimson blur across the arc of its swings, much like a plucked harp string vibrating, and beyond it all Troy himself, mainly turned toward her; occasionally, to demonstrate the backhand strokes, half pivoted away, yet his gaze perpetually sharp, gauging her form and silhouette, his mouth pressed firm with steady exertion. Then his motions decelerated, and she could discern each one distinctly. The sword’s whisper faded, and he came to a full halt.
source_text: Behind the luminous streams of this _aurora militaris_, she could see the hue of Troy’s sword arm, spread in a scarlet haze over the space covered by its motions, like a twanged harpstring, and behind all Troy himself, mostly facing her; sometimes, to show the rear cuts, half turned away, his eye nevertheless always keenly measuring her breadth and outline, and his lips tightly closed in sustained effort. Next, his movements lapsed slower, and she could see them individually. The hissing of the sword had ceased, and he stopped entirely.

text: “Yes,” William went on, “they came strutting along the street, so the story goes, blaring ‘The Girl I Left Behind Me’ in ringing, victorious strains. The thunder of the great drum pounded every onlooker to their very core, and among the tavern folk and the nameless women, there wasn’t a single dry eye left in the whole town!”
source_text: “Yes,” continued William, “they pranced down the street playing ‘The Girl I Left Behind Me,’ so ’tis said, in glorious notes of triumph. Every looker-on’s inside shook with the blows of the great drum to his deepest vitals, and there was not a dry eye throughout the town among the public-house people and the nameless women!”

text: “In his final years, he grew so devout he seemed nearly beatified, didn’t he, Jan?” remarked Joseph Poorgrass. “He underwent confirmation a second time with deepened gravity, and fell into the habit of voicing ‘Amen’ almost as thunderously as the parish clerk, besides copying consoling epitaphs from the gravestones. He would also pass the alms-dish at Let Your Light so Shine, and act as sponsor to poor little chance-begotten infants; on his table he kept a mission box to catch visitors off their guard when they came calling; aye, and he’d cuff the charity-lads about the ears if they snickered during service until they could scarce stand straight, and perform other works of devotion befitting a saintly disposition.”
source_text: “He got so much better, that he was quite godly in his later years, wasn’t he, Jan?” said Joseph Poorgrass. “He got himself confirmed over again in a more serious way, and took to saying ‘Amen’ almost as loud as the clerk, and he liked to copy comforting verses from the tombstones. He used, too, to hold the money-plate at Let Your Light so Shine, and stand godfather to poor little come-by-chance children; and he kept a missionary box upon his table to nab folks unawares when they called; yes, and he would box the charity-boys’ ears, if they laughed in church, till they could hardly stand upright, and do other deeds of piety natural to the saintly inclined.”

text: “He’s no reckless rascal! How dare you insult me outright! I’ve no cause to despise him, or you, or anyone else. But I’m a foolish woman! What difference does his character make to me? You understand it’s of no consequence. He means nothing to me; I’ve no intention of protecting his reputation, not at all. Mark my words, if a single one of you speaks ill of him, you’ll be turned out at once!”
source_text: “He’s _not_ a wild scamp! How dare you to my face! I have no right to hate him, nor you, nor anybody. But I am a silly woman! What is it to me what he is? You know it is nothing. I don’t care for him; I don’t mean to defend his good name, not I. Mind this, if any of you say a word against him you’ll be dismissed instantly!”

text: The cottage lay in unbroken quiet, broken only by a cluster of sparrows gossiping along the eaves; one could easily imagine that scandal and hearsay dominated the chatter of these rooftop circles just as much as those beneath them. The sign proved inauspicious, for at the very moment Oak began his awkward approach by the garden gate, a cat inside contorted itself into grotesque arches and demonic spasms at the sight of his dog George. The dog paid no mind, having long since reached an age where any needless barking was dismissed with weary disdain as a sheer waste of effort—indeed, he never barked at the sheep unless commanded, and then delivered it with a perfectly blank expression, like some grim liturgical curse that, however unpleasant, had to be performed from time to time to keep the flock in wholesome fear.
source_text: Nothing disturbed the stillness of the cottage save the chatter of a knot of sparrows on the eaves; one might fancy scandal and rumour to be no less the staple topic of these little coteries on roofs than of those under them. It seemed that the omen was an unpropitious one, for, as the rather untoward commencement of Oak’s overtures, just as he arrived by the garden gate, he saw a cat inside, going into various arched shapes and fiendish convulsions at the sight of his dog George. The dog took no notice, for he had arrived at an age at which all superfluous barking was cynically avoided as a waste of breath—in fact, he never barked even at the sheep except to order, when it was done with an absolutely neutral countenance, as a sort of Commination-service, which, though offensive, had to be gone through once now and then to frighten the flock for their own good.

text: “There,” Troy said, “now you grasp my predicament. Maybe I’m a scoundrel—a slave to my own whims—drawn into doing what I should avoid. Still, I can’t wed both of them. And I’ve got two grounds for picking Fanny. One, on balance I prefer her, and two, you’ve made it profitable for me.”
source_text: “Now,” said Troy, “you see my dilemma. Perhaps I am a bad man—the victim of my impulses—led away to do what I ought to leave undone. I can’t, however, marry them both. And I have two reasons for choosing Fanny. First, I like her best upon the whole, and second, you make it worth my while.”

text: A memorial was submitted to the Secretary of State, setting forth those particulars which seemed to warrant a plea for a review of the judgment. It was not “thickly subscribed” by the townsfolk of Casterbridge, as petitions commonly are, for Boldwood had never gathered many intimates across the counter. The tradesmen deemed it wholly unsurprising that a fellow who, by buying straight from the grower, had audaciously flouted the foremost axiom of country life—that the Almighty fashioned rural hamlets to furnish custom for the shire-town—should hold muddled notions regarding the Commandments. The movers were a handful of compassionate souls who had, perhaps, too tenderly weighed the facts lately brought to light, and the upshot was that testimony was gathered which, it was trusted, might lift the deed, in a moral regard, out of the class of deliberate killing, and cause it to be seen as a pure issue of insanity.
source_text: A petition was addressed to the Home Secretary, advancing the circumstances which appeared to justify a request for a reconsideration of the sentence. It was not “numerously signed” by the inhabitants of Casterbridge, as is usual in such cases, for Boldwood had never made many friends over the counter. The shops thought it very natural that a man who, by importing direct from the producer, had daringly set aside the first great principle of provincial existence, namely that God made country villages to supply customers to county towns, should have confused ideas about the Decalogue. The prompters were a few merciful men who had perhaps too feelingly considered the facts latterly unearthed, and the result was that evidence was taken which it was hoped might remove the crime in a moral point of view, out of the category of wilful murder, and lead it to be regarded as a sheer outcome of madness.

text: “If you’ll forgive me, I think I ought to leave now.” She was visibly uneasy, recalling the promise she had made and already anticipating what he meant to say. “Still, since the hour isn’t very advanced,” she went on, “I can make my way home on foot, and let my servant and Liddy follow whenever they wish.”
source_text: “If you’ll excuse me, I should like to go now.” Her manner was restive, for she remembered her promise, and imagined what he was about to say. “But as it is not late,” she added, “I can walk home, and leave my man and Liddy to come when they choose.”

text: “Yes, I recognize you now, shepherd,” said Henery Fray, studying Gabriel through bleary eyes as he began his second melody. “Indeed—watching you puff into that flute, I can tell you’re the same fellow I saw performing at Casterbridge, because your lips were all pinched together and your eyes bulged out like a man being choked—exactly as they do at this moment.”
source_text: “Ay, I can mind yer face now, shepherd,” said Henery Fray, criticising Gabriel with misty eyes as he entered upon his second tune. “Yes—now I see ’ee blowing into the flute I know ’ee to be the same man I see play at Casterbridge, for yer mouth were scrimped up and yer eyes a-staring out like a strangled man’s—just as they be now.”

text: It’s wiser to seize whatever opportunity arises and improvise a course of action on the spot than to perfect a scheme and then linger for the right moment to execute it. Gabriel regretted having fixed his identity so firmly as a shepherd, instead of offering himself for any kind of work the fair might demand. Twilight deepened. Near the corn-exchange, a group of jovial men were whistling and singing. Gabriel’s hand, resting idly for some time in the pocket of his smock-frock, brushed against the flute he kept there. Here was a chance to apply the wisdom he had purchased at such cost.
source_text: It is safer to accept any chance that offers itself, and extemporize a procedure to fit it, than to get a good plan matured, and wait for a chance of using it. Gabriel wished he had not nailed up his colours as a shepherd, but had laid himself out for anything in the whole cycle of labour that was required in the fair. It grew dusk. Some merry men were whistling and singing by the corn-exchange. Gabriel’s hand, which had lain for some time idle in his smock-frock pocket, touched his flute which he carried there. Here was an opportunity for putting his dearly bought wisdom into practice.

text: Oak lifted the lantern and stepped into the porch, with Bathsheba close behind, a shawl drawn over her hair. Its glow revealed a cluster of men standing on the gravel before the house, who, the moment they spotted the newly-wedded pair in the doorway, burst into a roaring cheer; at that same instant the cannon in the distance boomed again, and a dreadful racket of music broke out—drum, tambourine, clarinet, serpent, oboe, tenor viol, and double-bass, the last surviving fragments of the original Weatherbury band. Those ancient, woodworm-riddled instruments had once, in their prime, sounded the triumphs of Marlborough beneath the hands of the players’ own ancestors. The musicians stepped forward and advanced to the front.
source_text: Oak took up the light and went into the porch, followed by Bathsheba with a shawl over her head. The rays fell upon a group of male figures gathered upon the gravel in front, who, when they saw the newly-married couple in the porch, set up a loud “Hurrah!” and at the same moment bang again went the cannon in the background, followed by a hideous clang of music from a drum, tambourine, clarionet, serpent, hautboy, tenor-viol, and double-bass—the only remaining relics of the true and original Weatherbury band—venerable worm-eaten instruments, which had celebrated in their own persons the victories of Marlborough, under the fingers of the forefathers of those who played them now. The performers came forward, and marched up to the front.

text: “I hadn’t the nerve to inquire after her, in the end,” stammered Corin. “Everyone was in such a flurry, straining to coax some liveliness into the gathering. The merriment, somehow, smoulders unlit, though all a soul could wish for stands ready, and I could not, for the life of me, step in and cast a chill over it—not if my own breath depended on it, I could not!”
source_text: “I didn’t like to ask for her after all,” Laban faltered out. “They were all in such a stir, trying to put a little spirit into the party. Somehow the fun seems to hang fire, though everything’s there that a heart can desire, and I couldn’t for my soul interfere and throw damp upon it—if ’twas to save my life, I couldn’t!”

text: The van's occupants could no longer detect any hoofbeats from the horseman. Once past Weatherbury, the road surface turned gradually softer and more clay-like, and the recent rain had left it damp and slightly yielding, though not yet muddy. They reached a crossroads. Coggan abruptly reined in Moll and dismounted.
source_text: No sound of the rider in their van could now be discovered. The road-metal grew softer and more clayey as Weatherbury was left behind, and the late rain had wetted its surface to a somewhat plastic, but not muddy state. They came to cross-roads. Coggan suddenly pulled up Moll and slipped off.

text: At first, it baffled her. Had a decent few stood on either side, the situation would have seemed entirely ordinary. Had no one paid her any mind, she would have met it with cool indifference—that had happened before. Had everyone, this man among them, done so, she would have accepted it as perfectly natural—such things were known to occur. But the sheer narrowness of the exception turned it into a riddle.
source_text: It perplexed her first. If there had been a respectable minority on either side, the case would have been most natural. If nobody had regarded her, she would have taken the matter indifferently—such cases had occurred. If everybody, this man included, she would have taken it as a matter of course—people had done so before. But the smallness of the exception made the mystery.

text: “My dear, what a dusty, thirst-ridden world we inhabit!” went on Mrs. Talliver—a ruddy, capable woman whose tones shifted as readily as her moods, who could flip a griddle-cake or swing a broom with the precision of a geometric proof, and who now stood with fingers furred in clumps of dough and forearms powdered white. “Never am I sunk to the elbows, Miss, in a pudding but one of two trials befalls me—either my nose commences that maddening itch and I cannot breathe without clawing at it, or there comes a rapping at the latch. And here is Mr. Sothern, wishful to speak with you, Miss Arden.”
source_text: “Dear, what a thirtover place this world is!” continued Mrs. Coggan (a wholesome-looking lady who had a voice for each class of remark according to the emotion involved; who could toss a pancake or twirl a mop with the accuracy of pure mathematics, and who at this moment showed hands shaggy with fragments of dough and arms encrusted with flour). “I am never up to my elbows, Miss, in making a pudding but one of two things do happen—either my nose must needs begin tickling, and I can’t live without scratching it, or somebody knocks at the door. Here’s Mr. Boldwood wanting to see you, Miss Everdene.”

text: Gabriel noticed her first, and his immediate instinct was to study her face, to see how she took the revelation that he had carved the inscription—a fact that had stirred no small wonder in himself. But such shocks no longer unsettled her. Emotional upheavals had grown almost ordinary in the chronicle of her days. She merely offered him a calm good morning and asked him to fill the hollow with the shovel resting nearby. While he complied, Bathsheba gathered the scattered blossoms and began settling them into the earth with that tender care for root and tendril so marked in a woman’s gardening—a touch that flowers seem to recognize and flourish under. She then bid him have the churchwardens shift the lead spout at the gargoyle’s throat, which yawned directly above them, so that the water might fall to one side and no such mishap recur. Lastly, with the excessive charity of a woman whose narrower passions have brought down sorrow where love might have been, she cleaned the mud from the headstone as though she half cherished its inscription, and turned her steps homeward.
source_text: Oak saw her, and his first act was to gaze inquiringly and learn how she received this knowledge of the authorship of the work, which to himself had caused considerable astonishment. But such discoveries did not much affect her now. Emotional convulsions seemed to have become the commonplaces of her history, and she bade him good morning, and asked him to fill in the hole with the spade which was standing by. Whilst Oak was doing as she desired, Bathsheba collected the flowers, and began planting them with that sympathetic manipulation of roots and leaves which is so conspicuous in a woman’s gardening, and which flowers seem to understand and thrive upon. She requested Oak to get the churchwardens to turn the leadwork at the mouth of the gurgoyle that hung gaping down upon them, that by this means the stream might be directed sideways, and a repetition of the accident prevented. Finally, with the superfluous magnanimity of a woman whose narrower instincts have brought down bitterness upon her instead of love, she wiped the mud spots from the tomb as if she rather liked its words than otherwise, and went again home.[2]

text: On Christmas Eve, the talk of Weatherbury centered on the party Boldwood was to host that night. It was not the scarcity of such gatherings in the parish that made this one remarkable, but the sheer fact that Boldwood was the host. The news had struck the ear as strangely incongruous—as though one were told of croquet being played in a cathedral nave, or of some venerable judge stepping onto the stage. That the affair was meant to be genuinely merry there could be no doubt. A great bough of mistletoe had been fetched from the woods that day and hung in the hall of the bachelor’s house, followed by armloads of holly and ivy. From six in the morning until past noon, the cavernous kitchen fire roared and glittered at full blaze, the kettle, the saucepan, and the three-legged pot glowing amid the flames like Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego; and all the while, roasting and basting went on steadily before the welcoming heat.
source_text: Christmas-eve came, and a party that Boldwood was to give in the evening was the great subject of talk in Weatherbury. It was not that the rarity of Christmas parties in the parish made this one a wonder, but that Boldwood should be the giver. The announcement had had an abnormal and incongruous sound, as if one should hear of croquet-playing in a cathedral aisle, or that some much-respected judge was going upon the stage. That the party was intended to be a truly jovial one there was no room for doubt. A large bough of mistletoe had been brought from the woods that day, and suspended in the hall of the bachelor’s home. Holly and ivy had followed in armfuls. From six that morning till past noon the huge wood fire in the kitchen roared and sparkled at its highest, the kettle, the saucepan, and the three-legged pot appearing in the midst of the flames like Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego; moreover, roasting and basting operations were continually carried on in front of the genial blaze.

text: “It hasn’t travelled far yet, but I sense it will soon—yes, I’m certain of it,” he breathed in a sudden, urgent whisper. “I’ve urged the matter with her, and she seems disposed to show me tenderness, to look on me as a husband somewhere far down the road, and that alone sustains me. What more could I ask? She holds to the idea that a woman ought not to marry within seven years of a husband’s vanishing—her own case, I mean—because his body was never recovered. It may be only this legal scruple that sways her, or perhaps a religious one, but she shrinks from discussing it. Still, she has promised—hinted—that she will seal our engagement tonight.”
source_text: “It has not gone far yet, but I think it will soon—yes, I know it will,” he said, in an impulsive whisper. “I have pressed her upon the subject, and she inclines to be kind to me, and to think of me as a husband at a long future time, and that’s enough for me. How can I expect more? She has a notion that a woman should not marry within seven years of her husband’s disappearance—that her own self shouldn’t, I mean—because his body was not found. It may be merely this legal reason which influences her, or it may be a religious one, but she is reluctant to talk on the point. Yet she has promised—implied—that she will ratify an engagement to-night.”

text: It was a typical, unhurried dawn of the season, and the heavens, a clear amethyst overhead, sagged to slate in the north and thickened to a smoky haze in the east, where, above the frost-bleached pastures of Highbrook Grange and seeming to perch upon the hillcrest, the sun’s bisected disc smoldered without beams, like a ruddy, fireless coal glowing upon a pale altar stone. The entire spectacle mirrored a dusk as infancy mirrors senescence.
source_text: It was one of the usual slow sunrises of this time of the year, and the sky, pure violet in the zenith, was leaden to the northward, and murky to the east, where, over the snowy down or ewe-lease on Weatherbury Upper Farm, and apparently resting upon the ridge, the only half of the sun yet visible burnt rayless, like a red and flameless fire shining over a white hearthstone. The whole effect resembled a sunset as childhood resembles age.

text: Every face made it plain that their thoughts had not strayed to Holland for an instant because of the remark, but were instead lamenting the disparity that had prompted the comparison; and Mark Clark called out, “Hear, hear; exactly what I would have said.” At the same moment, following the shepherd’s threat, the dog George lifted his head, and though his grasp of English was imperfect, started to growl.
source_text: All earnestly expressed by their features that their minds did not wander to Holland for a moment on account of this statement, but were deploring the difference which gave rise to the figure; and Mark Clark cried “Hear, hear; just what I should ha’ said.” The dog George looked up at the same time after the shepherd’s menace, and though he understood English but imperfectly, began to growl.

text: He fixed his stare upon her as she lifted her glance for an instant; Bathsheba dropped her eyes once more, unable to meet the force of that look head-on. Yet from the corner of her vision she had marked that he was lean and youthful, and that three stripes adorned his cuff.
source_text: He looked hard into her eyes when she raised them for a moment; Bathsheba looked down again, for his gaze was too strong to be received point-blank with her own. But she had obliquely noticed that he was young and slim, and that he wore three chevrons upon his sleeve.

text: Later, a glimmer—then twin glimmers—emerged from the distant darkness, swelling as they neared. A coach rumbled down the lane and swept past the entrance. It likely held some tardy supper guests. For an instant, the glow from a single lantern caught the huddled figure, casting her features into stark prominence. The countenance was youthful in its foundation, yet aged in its detail; the broader curves were sinuous and girlish, though the subtler traits had grown angular and spare.
source_text: Afterwards a light—two lights—arose from the remote shade, and grew larger. A carriage rolled along the road, and passed the gate. It probably contained some late diners-out. The beams from one lamp shone for a moment upon the crouching woman, and threw her face into vivid relief. The face was young in the groundwork, old in the finish; the general contours were flexuous and childlike, but the finer lineaments had begun to be sharp and thin.

text: “Of course I shall, Miss Everdene,” Gabriel murmured softly. It struck him as strange that she should ask such a thing just then, for the conflict had ended, and they stood upon a barren ridge, remote from any dwelling, with dusk deepening around them. He halted and let her draw far ahead until her silhouette alone remained against the heavens.
source_text: “Certainly I will, Miss Everdene,” said Gabriel, gently. He wondered that the request should have come at this moment, for the strife was over, and they were on a most desolate hill, far from every human habitation, and the hour was getting late. He stood still and allowed her to get far ahead of him till he could only see her form upon the sky.

text: “I never meant to provoke you—truly, I didn’t!” she replied, summoning all her courage. “But please don’t stay so stern with me. I can bear being told I’ve done wrong, if only you’ll say it softly! Oh, sir, won’t you graciously pardon me and face this with a lighter heart?”
source_text: “I did not take you up—surely I did not!” she answered as heroically as she could. “But don’t be in this mood with me. I can endure being told I am in the wrong, if you will only tell it me gently! O sir, will you not kindly forgive me, and look at it cheerfully?”

text: She flared instantly. "You presume far too much!" she cried, her voice trembling with fury. "The whole world presses on me—the whole world. To assail a woman in this fashion is craven! I have not one soul on earth to stand in my defense; yet no quarter is given. But though ten thousand of you mock and speak ill of me, I _shall not_ be broken!"
source_text: She fired up at once. “You are taking too much upon yourself!” she said, vehemently. “Everybody is upon me—everybody. It is unmanly to attack a woman so! I have nobody in the world to fight my battles for me; but no mercy is shown. Yet if a thousand of you sneer and say things against me, I _will not_ be put down!”
