text: A striking figure rested casually against the window frame, his gaze drifting first toward the sunrise, then back the other way, like someone conducting an unhurried dawn inspection. This was Sergeant Troy. His scarlet tunic hung open, unfastened, and his whole posture spoke of a soldier entirely at leisure.
source_text: A handsome man leaned idly from the lattice. He looked east and then west, in the manner of one who makes a first morning survey. The man was Sergeant Troy. His red jacket was loosely thrown on, but not buttoned, and he had altogether the relaxed bearing of a soldier taking his ease.

text: Bathsheba observed her husband’s absence stretch from hours into days with a faint stir of surprise, and an equally faint stir of relief; yet neither feeling ever rose much above what is commonly called indifference. She was his: the terms of that state were so clearly drawn, and the likely outcomes so narrowly confined, that she could not trouble herself with what-ifs. Having ceased to regard herself as a splendid woman, she adopted the detached perspective of a bystander watching her own probable fate as a singularly wretched creature; for Bathsheba painted herself and her future in shades no reality could outdo in gloom. The robust pride of her youth had withered, and with it had faded all her worries about the years ahead, since worry requires a sense of better and worse paths, and she had concluded that meaningful choices no longer existed for her. Sooner or later—and not very much later—her husband would return. And then their days at the Upper Farm would be numbered. The estate agent had initially viewed Bathsheba’s succession to James Everdene with some misgiving, on account of her sex, her youth, and her beauty; but the unusual terms of her uncle’s will, his repeated praise before his death of her shrewdness in such matters, and her capable management of the many flocks and herds that came abruptly into her hands before the formalities were complete, had earned her trust, and no further objections were made. Lately she had been deeply uncertain about the legal consequences of her marriage for her position; but so far no remark had been made about her change of name, and only one thing was plain—that if she or her husband failed to satisfy the agent at the approaching January rent-day, very little leniency would be granted, and, in truth, very little would be merited. Once turned out of the farm, poverty would surely follow.
source_text: Bathsheba underwent the enlargement of her husband’s absence from hours to days with a slight feeling of surprise, and a slight feeling of relief; yet neither sensation rose at any time far above the level commonly designated as indifference. She belonged to him: the certainties of that position were so well defined, and the reasonable probabilities of its issue so bounded that she could not speculate on contingencies. Taking no further interest in herself as a splendid woman, she acquired the indifferent feelings of an outsider in contemplating her probable fate as a singular wretch; for Bathsheba drew herself and her future in colours that no reality could exceed for darkness. Her original vigorous pride of youth had sickened, and with it had declined all her anxieties about coming years, since anxiety recognizes a better and a worse alternative, and Bathsheba had made up her mind that alternatives on any noteworthy scale had ceased for her. Soon, or later—and that not very late—her husband would be home again. And then the days of their tenancy of the Upper Farm would be numbered. There had originally been shown by the agent to the estate some distrust of Bathsheba’s tenure as James Everdene’s successor, on the score of her sex, and her youth, and her beauty; but the peculiar nature of her uncle’s will, his own frequent testimony before his death to her cleverness in such a pursuit, and her vigorous marshalling of the numerous flocks and herds which came suddenly into her hands before negotiations were concluded, had won confidence in her powers, and no further objections had been raised. She had latterly been in great doubt as to what the legal effects of her marriage would be upon her position; but no notice had been taken as yet of her change of name, and only one point was clear—that in the event of her own or her husband’s inability to meet the agent at the forthcoming January rent-day, very little consideration would be shown, and, for that matter, very little would be deserved. Once out of the farm, the approach of poverty would be sure.

text: Oak let his gaze drift between the two who were wrangling, and sank into a musing stillness. The ring of tuppence was oddly trifling—it seemed barely worth naming. Threepence, by contrast, held the weight of actual coin; it bit into a laborer’s daily pay enough to merit a quarrel over haggling. But tuppence—“Here,” he said, moving forward and placing the copper into the keeper’s palm, “let the girl go through.” He lifted his eyes to her then; she caught his speech, and glanced down.
source_text: Oak looked from one to the other of the disputants, and fell into a reverie. There was something in the tone of twopence remarkably insignificant. Threepence had a definite value as money—it was an appreciable infringement on a day’s wages, and, as such, a higgling matter; but twopence—“Here,” he said, stepping forward and handing twopence to the gatekeeper; “let the young woman pass.” He looked up at her then; she heard his words, and looked down.

text: Their pace could not be hurried. When they came to the lower edge of the town and veered left into the thick darkness beneath an abandoned row of chestnut trees, the streetlights of Casterbridge glimmered ahead like scattered stars cast down to earth, and they circled the borough’s boundary. In this way, they left the town behind and arrived at their destination.
source_text: Their progress was necessarily very slow. They reached the bottom of the town, and the Casterbridge lamps lay before them like fallen Pleiads as they turned to the left into the dense shade of a deserted avenue of chestnuts, and so skirted the borough. Thus the town was passed, and the goal was reached.

text: “I have so few lady callers that I fear my arrangements aren’t quite suitable. Please, take a seat. Here’s one, and another over there. I apologize—all my chairs have wooden seats and are a bit unforgiving, but I—have been meaning to buy some new ones.” Oak set out two or three for her.
source_text: “It is so seldom that I get a lady visitor that I’m afraid I haven’t proper accommodation. Will you sit down, please? Here’s a chair, and there’s one, too. I am sorry that my chairs all have wood seats, and are rather hard, but I—was thinking of getting some new ones.” Oak placed two or three for her.

text: Liddy gazed from the rear casement into the yard below, now steeped in the first muted grays of dusk. A staggered line of figures was making for the kitchen entrance. The entire train of lagging shapes moved with a perfect singleness of purpose, like those strange beings called Chain Salpæ, which, though separate in every other feature, share a single volition across the whole colony. A few wore, as ever, snow-white blouses of linen duck, and others the buff-toned ones of twilled cotton—stitched at the cuffs, chests, shoulders, and arms with honeycomb-patterned needlework. Two or three women in wooden clogs closed the procession.
source_text: Liddy looked from a back window into the courtyard behind, which was now getting low-toned and dim with the earliest films of night. A crooked file of men was approaching the back door. The whole string of trailing individuals advanced in the completest balance of intention, like the remarkable creatures known as Chain Salpæ, which, distinctly organized in other respects, have one will common to a whole family. Some were, as usual, in snow-white smock-frocks of Russia duck, and some in whitey-brown ones of drabbet—marked on the wrists, breasts, backs, and sleeves with honeycomb-work. Two or three women in pattens brought up the rear.

text: Across from the church lay a cobbled square, where a cluster of ancient timber-framed buildings, their upper stories jutting outward, draped the space in a quaint, dappled shadow. As the young man stepped from the doorway and started across the square, he encountered a small woman at its center. The look upon her face, which had been taut with deep unease, fell almost into dread at the sight of him.
source_text: Opposite to the church was a paved square, around which several overhanging wood buildings of old time cast a picturesque shade. The young man on leaving the door went to cross the square, when, in the middle, he met a little woman. The expression of her face, which had been one of intense anxiety, sank at the sight of his nearly to terror.

text: “Prove myself a man of mettle?... Ah, very well! I will accept the title of drunkard meekly—let me be a man bowed on penitent knees—so be it! I am aware that I utter ‘God willing’ before every deed, from my rising to my resting, and I am ready to bear whatever shame such a sacred habit may carry. Hah, indeed!... Yet not a man of spirit? Have I ever permitted pride’s boot to be aimed at my backside without stoutly groaning that I dispute the privilege to do so? I put that question boldly?”
source_text: “Show myself a man of spirit?... Ah, well! let me take the name of drunkard humbly—let me be a man of contrite knees—let it be! I know that I always do say ‘Please God’ afore I do anything, from my getting up to my going down of the same, and I be willing to take as much disgrace as there is in that holy act. Hah, yes!... But not a man of spirit? Have I ever allowed the toe of pride to be lifted against my hinder parts without groaning manfully that I question the right to do so? I inquire that query boldly?”

text: “I’m grateful—truly grateful. Still, my good spirits may hang by a thread. But I believe in that thread. It’s conviction now, not mere wishing. This time, I suspect, I’ve taken the measure of my landlord.—Ash, my fingers are trembling, or some such thing; I can’t fasten this cravat right. Would you mind doing it for me? The truth is, I haven’t been quite myself these past days, as you’re aware.”
source_text: “Thank you—thank you. Yet perhaps my cheerfulness rests on a slender hope. And yet I trust my hope. It is faith, not hope. I think this time I reckon with my host.—Oak, my hands are a little shaky, or something; I can’t tie this neckerchief properly. Perhaps you will tie it for me. The fact is, I have not been well lately, you know.”

text: A troubling reason for her eagerness to be free of him at that moment now came to light. A form seemed to emerge from the ground next to her. The silhouette, without question, belonged to Troy. Oak, unwilling to risk even overhearing, immediately retreated until a full two hundred yards separated the couple from him.
source_text: A distressing explanation of this anxiety to be rid of him at that point now ensued. A figure apparently rose from the earth beside her. The shape beyond all doubt was Troy’s. Oak would not be even a possible listener, and at once turned back till a good two hundred yards were between the lovers and himself.

text: Bathsheba’s gaze happened to rise just then, and she caught the gesture and the lock of hair. A flush of hurt and shock swept over her, and before she could weigh the prudence of speaking, words slipped out. “A woman’s ringlet!” she exclaimed. “Oh, Frank, who does it belong to?”
source_text: Bathsheba’s eyes had been accidentally lifted at that moment, and she saw the action and saw the hair. She flushed in pain and surprise, and some words escaped her before she had thought whether or not it was wise to utter them. “A woman’s curl of hair!” she said. “Oh, Frank, whose is that?”

text: Not a single woman ever had a prayer with that man! He’s been pursued by droves—every lass, high and low, for leagues about, has set her cap at him. Jane Perkins laboured over him two months like a drudge, the Taylor sisters devoted a full year to the project, and Farmer Ives’s girl paid in sleepless weeping and twenty pounds of fresh finery; but good heavens—the coin might as well have been flung straight into the lane.
source_text: “Never was such a hopeless man for a woman! He’s been courted by sixes and sevens—all the girls, gentle and simple, for miles round, have tried him. Jane Perkins worked at him for two months like a slave, and the two Miss Taylors spent a year upon him, and he cost Farmer Ives’s daughter nights of tears and twenty pounds’ worth of new clothes; but Lord—the money might as well have been thrown out of the window.”

text: “All I can tell you, ma’am, is that she came in the morning and was gone by nightfall, without any more to be said. The only words Gabriel Oak and Mr. Boldwood gave me were these. ‘Little Fanny Robin is dead, Joseph,’ Gabriel said, fixing his gaze on me in that calm, old-fashioned manner of his. I felt a real sorrow, and I asked, ‘Ah—and what brought her to it?’ ‘She’s dead in the Casterbridge Union,’ he answered, ‘and the how of it hardly signifies now. She was taken in there early on Sunday morning, and by afternoon she was gone—that much is plain.’ I pressed then about her recent doings, and Mr. Boldwood swung toward me, ceasing to pick at a thistle with the tip of his staff. He told me she had kept herself by needlework in Melchester, as I passed on to you before, and that she set out walking from there at the close of last week, going by this way on Saturday evening in the half-light. They then said I should just breathe a word of her passing to you, and off they went. It may have been the night air that finished her, you know, ma’am; for folk used to say she would sink in a consumption—she had a bad cough in the cold months. Still, it makes little difference to us now, since it’s all done with.”
source_text: “I’m sure that all I know is that she arrived in the morning and died in the evening without further parley. What Oak and Mr. Boldwood told me was only these few words. ‘Little Fanny Robin is dead, Joseph,’ Gabriel said, looking in my face in his steady old way. I was very sorry, and I said, ‘Ah!—and how did she come to die?’ ‘Well, she’s dead in Casterbridge Union,’ he said, ‘and perhaps ’tisn’t much matter about how she came to die. She reached the Union early Sunday morning, and died in the afternoon—that’s clear enough.’ Then I asked what she’d been doing lately, and Mr. Boldwood turned round to me then, and left off spitting a thistle with the end of his stick. He told me about her having lived by seampstering in Melchester, as I mentioned to you, and that she walked therefrom at the end of last week, passing near here Saturday night in the dusk. They then said I had better just name a hint of her death to you, and away they went. Her death might have been brought on by biding in the night wind, you know, ma’am; for people used to say she’d go off in a decline: she used to cough a good deal in winter time. However, ’tisn’t much odds to us about that now, for ’tis all over.”

text: “Oh, do not mock me, lady. That woman, even in death, holds more of my heart than you ever did, or do now, or ever could. Had the devil not lured me with your countenance and those damnable allurements, she would have been my bride. Not one stray fancy possessed me before you crossed my path. God knows I wish it had been so; yet the hour is past mending!” He shifted his gaze to Fanny. “But rest easy, my love,” he murmured; “before Heaven’s eyes you are my true, my own wedded wife!”
source_text: “Ah! don’t taunt me, madam. This woman is more to me, dead as she is, than ever you were, or are, or can be. If Satan had not tempted me with that face of yours, and those cursed coquetries, I should have married her. I never had another thought till you came in my way. Would to God that I had; but it is all too late!” He turned to Fanny then. “But never mind, darling,” he said; “in the sight of Heaven you are my very, very wife!”

text: A gentleman extended me a loan to make a start, yet it shall be discharged before long, and while I remain a plain, ordinary fellow, I have risen somewhat since my youth.” Gabriel spoke the word “somewhat” with an inflection meant to convey to her that it was the self-satisfied version of “a very great deal.” He went on: “Once we are wed, I have no doubt I can labour twice as diligently as I do at present.
source_text: “A man has advanced me money to begin with, but still, it will soon be paid off, and though I am only an every-day sort of man, I have got on a little since I was a boy.” Gabriel uttered “a little” in a tone to show her that it was the complacent form of “a great deal.” He continued: “When we be married, I am quite sure I can work twice as hard as I do now.”

text: The afternoon waned swiftly, and as Poorgrass trudged beside the horse, he glanced seaward to the right. There he saw queer clouds and trailing ribbons of mist unfurling over the long ridges that hemmed the land in that direction. They swelled into thicker volumes, drifting lazily across the hollows between and curling about the dry, paper-like reeds along the moor and riverbanks. Soon their clammy, porous masses sealed off the sky. It was a swift efflorescence of vaporous growths rooted in the nearby sea, and by the time horse, man, and corpse reached Yalbury Great Wood, these mute machinations of an unseen agency had overtaken them, wrapping them entirely—the first arrival of the autumn fogs, and the first fog of the season.
source_text: The afternoon drew on apace, and, looking to the right towards the sea as he walked beside the horse, Poorgrass saw strange clouds and scrolls of mist rolling over the long ridges which girt the landscape in that quarter. They came in yet greater volumes, and indolently crept across the intervening valleys, and around the withered papery flags of the moor and river brinks. Then their dank spongy forms closed in upon the sky. It was a sudden overgrowth of atmospheric fungi which had their roots in the neighbouring sea, and by the time that horse, man, and corpse entered Yalbury Great Wood, these silent workings of an invisible hand had reached them, and they were completely enveloped, this being the first arrival of the autumn fogs, and the first fog of the series.

text: The evening had been chosen by Captain Lorne—now master in his wife’s stead—to host the harvest feast and reel. As Bram drew near the hall, the strains of fiddles and a small drum, together with the steady stamp of many soles, sharpened upon his ear. He stepped up to the great double doors, one left a hand’s breadth open, and peered within.
source_text: This was the night which had been selected by Sergeant Troy—ruling now in the room of his wife—for giving the harvest supper and dance. As Oak approached the building the sound of violins and a tambourine, and the regular jigging of many feet, grew more distinct. He came close to the large doors, one of which stood slightly ajar, and looked in.

text: She faded into the darkness, disappearing from sight, and he caught the soft click of the gate latch as she went through. He continued his work in a daydream now, turning over her tale in his mind, and pondering the contradictions of a woman’s heart that had led her to speak to him this evening with more tenderness than she ever had when she was single and at liberty to speak as tenderly as she wished.
source_text: She diminished in the gloom, and vanished, and he heard the latch of the gate fall as she passed through. He worked in a reverie now, musing upon her story, and upon the contradictoriness of that feminine heart which had caused her to speak more warmly to him to-night than she ever had done whilst unmarried and free to speak as warmly as she chose.

text: “My love, my love, even at this moment I’m torn between throwing caution aside and giving you up entirely, or humbling myself to strive for you once more. Overlook your denial, and let things be as they were before! Tell me, Bathsheba, that you penned that rejection merely in jest—come now, speak the words to me!”
source_text: “Dearest, dearest, I am wavering even now between the two opposites of recklessly renouncing you, and labouring humbly for you again. Forget that you have said No, and let it be as it was! Say, Bathsheba, that you only wrote that refusal to me in fun—come, say it to me!”

text: Catherine gazed after him, as did every other soul present. Swiftly, the rider followed the winding lane through Long Meadow, Brook’s End, High Stitch, The Levels, Gorse Acre, dwindled to a speck, forded the stream, and climbed the far slope by way of Thornhill and Chalk Hollow. The dwelling where Edmund had sought shelter before quitting the district forever stood out as a pale mark upon the distant rise, set against a screen of dark pines. Catherine paced to and fro. The labourers moved among the flock, striving to soothe the suffering of the silent beasts with their hands. It was no use.
source_text: Bathsheba watched. So did all the rest. Tall cantered along the bridle-path through Sixteen Acres, Sheeplands, Middle Field, The Flats, Cappel’s Piece, shrank almost to a point, crossed the bridge, and ascended from the valley through Springmead and Whitepits on the other side. The cottage to which Gabriel had retired before taking his final departure from the locality was visible as a white spot on the opposite hill, backed by blue firs. Bathsheba walked up and down. The men entered the field and endeavoured to ease the anguish of the dumb creatures by rubbing them. Nothing availed.

text: “I never made contact with you,” Troy said in a calm voice. “It was only a trick of the hand. The blade went right past your back. You’re not frightened now, are you? For if you are, I won’t be able to go on. I promise I’ll neither harm you nor so much as brush against you.”
source_text: “I have not touched you,” said Troy, quietly. “It was mere sleight of hand. The sword passed behind you. Now you are not afraid, are you? Because if you are I can’t perform. I give my word that I will not only not hurt you, but not once touch you.”

text: As soon as he arrived in Weatherbury, this man made himself known. A fortnight after the shearing, Bathsheba—feeling an unspoken lightness now that Boldwood was away—went out to her hayfields and peered over the hedge at the workers. They were split fairly evenly between bent, sinewy figures and softer, curving ones: the men and the women, the latter wearing bonnets of pale buff cloth that draped like short veils across their shoulders. Coggan and Mark Clark were scything in a farther field, Clark humming in rhythm with his blade while Jan paid no heed to matching the beat with his own. In the nearer meadow they were already loading the hay, the women raking it into heaps and long rows, the men forking it onto the wagon.
source_text: This person’s public appearance in Weatherbury promptly followed his arrival there. A week or two after the shearing, Bathsheba, feeling a nameless relief of spirits on account of Boldwood’s absence, approached her hayfields and looked over the hedge towards the haymakers. They consisted in about equal proportions of gnarled and flexuous forms, the former being the men, the latter the women, who wore tilt bonnets covered with nankeen, which hung in a curtain upon their shoulders. Coggan and Mark Clark were mowing in a less forward meadow, Clark humming a tune to the strokes of his scythe, to which Jan made no attempt to keep time with his. In the first mead they were already loading hay, the women raking it into cocks and windrows, and the men tossing it upon the waggon.

text: Gabriel Oak’s passive watching ended abruptly when he realized the situation was far graver than his initial impression. A drifting veil of smoke parted, exposing a wheat-stack dangerously close to the rotting one, and beyond it a whole row of others that made up the farm’s chief grain harvest. What he had pictured as a straw-stack standing somewhat apart was in fact linked in a continuous line with the rest of the clustered ricks.
source_text: Oak suddenly ceased from being a mere spectator by discovering the case to be more serious than he had at first imagined. A scroll of smoke blew aside and revealed to him a wheat-rick in startling juxtaposition with the decaying one, and behind this a series of others, composing the main corn produce of the farm; so that instead of the straw-stack standing, as he had imagined comparatively isolated, there was a regular connection between it and the remaining stacks of the group.

text: Our poor Reverend Thirdly hasn't a penny to purchase rings like those," said Matthew Moon, musing. "And he's as fine a soul as ever lived. I doubt poor Thirdly owns even one, not the simplest tin or copper band. What a splendid adornment they'd make for him on a dreary afternoon, standing in the pulpit under the glow of the wax candles! But it can't be done, the poor fellow. Ah, just think how uneven life is.
source_text: “Our poor Parson Thirdly can’t get no money to buy such rings,” said Matthew Moon, thoughtfully. “And as good a man as ever walked. I don’t believe poor Thirdly have a single one, even of humblest tin or copper. Such a great ornament as they’d be to him on a dull afternoon, when he’s up in the pulpit lighted by the wax candles! But ’tis impossible, poor man. Ah, to think how unequal things be.”

text: Elsewhere upon the slope, as the sun climbed toward noon, a wholly separate spectacle commenced to impress itself upon the sight. A round marquee, remarkable for its freshness and uncommon breadth, was being raised upon that spot. With the advancing hours, the sheep passed into other care, easing the herder’s burden; so the men shifted their gaze toward this pavilion and asked a fellow labouring there—whose spirit appeared wholly bent on subduing a vexing tangle without delay—what affair was afoot.
source_text: In another part of the hill an altogether different scene began to force itself upon the eye towards midday. A circular tent, of exceptional newness and size, was in course of erection here. As the day drew on, the flocks began to change hands, lightening the shepherd’s responsibilities; and they turned their attention to this tent and inquired of a man at work there, whose soul seemed concentrated on tying a bothering knot in no time, what was going on.

text: “I shall compensate you handsomely now, settle a provision upon her, and ensure that want never troubles your doorstep again. Let me speak plainly. Bathsheba merely trifles with your affections: you lack the means for her, as I have told you; so abandon this futile pursuit of a grand union you can never attain for a modest and proper one you might secure tomorrow. Pick up your travelling-bag, turn on your heel, depart Weatherbury this very hour, tonight, and fifty pounds shall be yours. Fanny will receive fifty to ready herself for the marriage, once you disclose her whereabouts, and five hundred shall be placed in her hand on the day she weds.”
source_text: “I’ll pay you well now, I’ll settle a sum of money upon her, and I’ll see that you don’t suffer from poverty in the future. I’ll put it clearly. Bathsheba is only playing with you: you are too poor for her as I said; so give up wasting your time about a great match you’ll never make for a moderate and rightful match you may make to-morrow; take up your carpet-bag, turn about, leave Weatherbury now, this night, and you shall take fifty pounds with you. Fanny shall have fifty to enable her to prepare for the wedding, when you have told me where she is living, and she shall have five hundred paid down on her wedding-day.”

text: “I can’t say for sure, but I’d guess it was just a general weakness of the body. She was such a delicate girl that she couldn’t endure any strain, even back when I knew her, and she flickered out like a candle flame, so they say. She fell ill in the morning, and, being so frail and spent, she was gone by evening. By law she belongs to our parish, and Mr. Boldwood plans to send a wagon at three this afternoon to bring her home and lay her to rest.”
source_text: “I don’t know for certain; but I should be inclined to think it was from general neshness of constitution. She was such a limber maid that ’a could stand no hardship, even when I knowed her, and ’a went like a candle-snoff, so ’tis said. She was took bad in the morning, and, being quite feeble and worn out, she died in the evening. She belongs by law to our parish; and Mr. Boldwood is going to send a waggon at three this afternoon to fetch her home here and bury her.”

text: “Yes, indeed. The truth is, these autumn meets ruin everyone. I’ve never witnessed a day like that one! It’s a bleak, exposed spot just outside Budmouth, and a dull gray sea surged toward us like liquid despair. Wind and rain—good heavens! Darkness? Why, it was pitch-black before the final race ended. It was five o’clock, and you couldn’t spot the horses until they were practically upon you, never mind their silks. The turf was heavy as iron, and all a man’s seasoned judgment counted for nothing. Horses, jockeys, spectators—all were tossed about like vessels in a storm. Three tents collapsed, and the poor souls inside scrambled out on hands and knees; in the next field, a dozen hats were aloft at once. Yes, Pimpernel got utterly bogged down about sixty yards out, and when I saw Policy striding forward, it sent my heart slamming against my ribs, I tell you, my dear!”
source_text: “Well, yes. The fact is, these autumn races are the ruin of everybody. Never did I see such a day as ’twas! ’Tis a wild open place, just out of Budmouth, and a drab sea rolled in towards us like liquid misery. Wind and rain—good Lord! Dark? Why, ’twas as black as my hat before the last race was run. ’Twas five o’clock, and you couldn’t see the horses till they were almost in, leave alone colours. The ground was as heavy as lead, and all judgment from a fellow’s experience went for nothing. Horses, riders, people, were all blown about like ships at sea. Three booths were blown over, and the wretched folk inside crawled out upon their hands and knees; and in the next field were as many as a dozen hats at one time. Ay, Pimpernel regularly stuck fast, when about sixty yards off, and when I saw Policy stepping on, it did knock my heart against the lining of my ribs, I assure you, my love!”

text: At last he crested the summit, and a vast, unfamiliar panorama broke upon him with an impact almost like that of the Pacific on Balboa’s eyes. The wide, metallic sea, traced only by faint streaks that seemed etched upon its surface too lightly to disturb its uniform smoothness, spanned his entire forward view and curved away to the right, where, near Budmouth town and harbor, the sun blazed down upon it, stripping away all color and replacing it with a lucent, oily sheen. Nothing stirred in sky, land, or sea, save a fringe of milk-white foam along the nearer angles of the coast, tatters of which lapped the adjacent stones like tongues.
source_text: At last he reached the summit, and a wide and novel prospect burst upon him with an effect almost like that of the Pacific upon Balboa’s gaze. The broad steely sea, marked only by faint lines, which had a semblance of being etched thereon to a degree not deep enough to disturb its general evenness, stretched the whole width of his front and round to the right, where, near the town and port of Budmouth, the sun bristled down upon it, and banished all colour, to substitute in its place a clear oily polish. Nothing moved in sky, land, or sea, except a frill of milkwhite foam along the nearer angles of the shore, shreds of which licked the contiguous stones like tongues.

text: “All right, then; you ought to be grateful I’m staying put. How would this place survive with no one to run it but a woman? But mark my words—I don’t want you thinking you’re in my debt. Not me. What I do, I do. There are moments I’d be as happy as a lark to walk away from it all—don’t go imagining I’m satisfied being a nobody. I’m cut out for greater things. Still, I can’t stand by and watch your affairs fall apart, which they surely will if you carry on with this attitude.... I hate laying myself bare like this, but, on my soul, your maddening manner drives a man to speak thoughts he’d never entertain otherwise! I admit I’m too meddlesome. But you understand perfectly how matters stand, and who it is I care for too deeply, and feel too much a fool over to treat with any courtesy!”
source_text: “Very well, then; you should be thankful to me for biding. How would the farm go on with nobody to mind it but a woman? But mind this, I don’t wish ’ee to feel you owe me anything. Not I. What I do, I do. Sometimes I say I should be as glad as a bird to leave the place—for don’t suppose I’m content to be a nobody. I was made for better things. However, I don’t like to see your concerns going to ruin, as they must if you keep in this mind.... I hate taking my own measure so plain, but, upon my life, your provoking ways make a man say what he wouldn’t dream of at other times! I own to being rather interfering. But you know well enough how it is, and who she is that I like too well, and feel too much like a fool about to be civil to her!”

text: He produced his flute and struck up “Jockey to the Fair” with the easy grace of someone untouched by grief. Oak played with a pastoral sweetness that lifted not only the spirits of the idlers nearby but his own as well. He continued with lively feeling, and within half an hour had collected in pennies what amounted to a modest treasure for a man with nothing.
source_text: He drew out his flute and began to play “Jockey to the Fair” in the style of a man who had never known a moment’s sorrow. Oak could pipe with Arcadian sweetness, and the sound of the well-known notes cheered his own heart as well as those of the loungers. He played on with spirit, and in half an hour had earned in pence what was a small fortune to a destitute man.

text: Samway, Tall, and Smallbury resolved to head over to Warren’s place, slipping out through the gate while the others retreated indoors. The trio soon neared the malt-house, cutting across the bordering orchard instead of following the road. As always, the windowpane glowed with light. Smallbury, walking slightly ahead, halted abruptly and wheeled toward his friends, whispering, “Quiet—look at that.”
source_text: Samway, Tall, and Smallbury agreed to go to Warren’s, and went out at the gate, the remaining ones entering the house. The three soon drew near the malt-house, approaching it from the adjoining orchard, and not by way of the street. The pane of glass was illuminated as usual. Smallbury was a little in advance of the rest when, pausing, he turned suddenly to his companions and said, “Hist! See there.”

text: Driven by a need for penance, she gathered blossoms from a vase near the window and started arranging them around the head of the deceased girl. Bathsheba could conceive of no other gesture of compassion for the dead save offering them flowers. She had no sense of how long she lingered at this task. Time, existence, her surroundings, her own actions—all slipped from her awareness. The sharp clap of the coach-house doors banging shut in the yard jolted her back to herself. A moment later, the front door swung open and then closed, footsteps crossed the hall, and her husband stood in the doorway, gazing in at her.
source_text: In her desire to make atonement she took flowers from a vase by the window, and began laying them around the dead girl’s head. Bathsheba knew no other way of showing kindness to persons departed than by giving them flowers. She knew not how long she remained engaged thus. She forgot time, life, where she was, what she was doing. A slamming together of the coach-house doors in the yard brought her to herself again. An instant after, the front door opened and closed, steps crossed the hall, and her husband appeared at the entrance to the room, looking in upon her.

text: They belonged to a brigantine’s company, put to shore to gather sand. Giving him such scant garments as they could part with among themselves—a meager shield against the sharpening chill of dusk—they resolved to set him on land at daybreak; and then, without lingering, for the hour was drawing on, they turned back toward the anchorage where their ship rode at rest.
source_text: They formed part of a brig’s crew, and had come ashore for sand. Lending him what little clothing they could spare among them as a slight protection against the rapidly cooling air, they agreed to land him in the morning; and without further delay, for it was growing late, they made again towards the roadstead where their vessel lay.

text: “You’ve every right to think so,” Henery said, offended. “A perfectly fair right. But I can see a short way into matters! Having the foresight to fill a bailiff’s post is a scant little thing—though a little more than naught. Still, I survey life with a steady eye. Do you follow me, neighbours? My speech, plain as I can make it, may yet run too deep for certain minds.”
source_text: “Ye have a right to believe it,” said Henery, with dudgeon; “a very true right. But I mid see a little distance into things! To be long-headed enough for a baily’s place is a poor mere trifle—yet a trifle more than nothing. However, I look round upon life quite cool. Do you heed me, neighbours? My words, though made as simple as I can, mid be rather deep for some heads.”

text: The content of the letter had barely engaged his mind beside the sheer event of its delivery. An abrupt thought struck him—perhaps the casing still held some remnant he had overlooked. He sprang from his bed into the uncanny glow, snatched the missive, slid out the frail paper, and rattled the wrapper—probed it. No further trace appeared. Boldwood studied, as he had a hundred times the day before, the commanding crimson wax: “Wed me,” he murmured into the silence.
source_text: The substance of the epistle had occupied him but little in comparison with the fact of its arrival. He suddenly wondered if anything more might be found in the envelope than what he had withdrawn. He jumped out of bed in the weird light, took the letter, pulled out the flimsy sheet, shook the envelope—searched it. Nothing more was there. Boldwood looked, as he had a hundred times the preceding day, at the insistent red seal: “Marry me,” he said aloud.

text: Bathsheba’s mind had fallen into a strange condition, one that revealed how utterly the spirit can be governed by the flesh, the intangible self held hostage to the whims of blood and bone. It would scarcely be an exaggeration to say she sensed herself driven by a power beyond her own volition—not merely toward making a pledge on that oddly distant and ill-defined subject, but into the feeling that she was somehow meant to make it. As the span of weeks separating that evening’s talk from Christmas morning began to shrink in her awareness, her unease and confusion deepened.
source_text: Bathsheba was in a very peculiar state of mind, which showed how entirely the soul is the slave of the body, the ethereal spirit dependent for its quality upon the tangible flesh and blood. It is hardly too much to say that she felt coerced by a force stronger than her own will, not only into the act of promising upon this singularly remote and vague matter, but into the emotion of fancying that she ought to promise. When the weeks intervening between the night of this conversation and Christmas day began perceptibly to diminish, her anxiety and perplexity increased.

text: “I understand you can’t. But he crept in while I was gone and plundered me. Why didn’t he take you earlier, when no one would have mourned?—when no one would have whispered stories. Now the folk mock me—the very hills and heavens appear to jeer at me until I flush with disgrace at my own foolishness. I’ve forfeited my honor, my reputation, my place—lost it, and I’ll never reclaim it. Go and wed your man—go ahead!”
source_text: “I know you cannot. But he stole in in my absence and robbed me. Why didn’t he win you away before, when nobody would have been grieved?—when nobody would have been set tale-bearing. Now the people sneer at me—the very hills and sky seem to laugh at me till I blush shamefully for my folly. I have lost my respect, my good name, my standing—lost it, never to get it again. Go and marry your man—go on!”

text: By now, Cainy was drawing close to the cluster of reapers, and they could see him clutching a thick slab of bread and ham in one hand, biting off chunks as he hurried along, while the other hand was swathed in a bandage. As he neared them, his lips formed a round O, and he broke into a fit of harsh coughing.
source_text: By this time Cainy was nearing the group of harvesters, and was perceived to be carrying a large slice of bread and ham in one hand, from which he took mouthfuls as he ran, the other being wrapped in a bandage. When he came close, his mouth assumed the bell shape, and he began to cough violently.

text: He quickly seized a reason to move up from his place at the back and came riding alongside her. They had covered a couple of miles beneath the moon, chatting idly over the side of her carriage about the market, the land, how useful Oak was to each of them, and other casual matters, when Boldwood spoke out abruptly and plainly—
source_text: He soon found an excuse for advancing from his position in the rear, and rode close by her side. They had gone two or three miles in the moonlight, speaking desultorily across the wheel of her gig concerning the fair, farming, Oak’s usefulness to them both, and other indifferent subjects, when Boldwood said suddenly and simply—

text: The maltster had stretched out fully dressed for a short rest and was now seated at a rickety three-legged table, making his breakfast of bread and bacon. He followed the plateless method: laying a slice of bread directly on the wood, setting the meat flat atop it, spreading a layer of mustard over the meat, and scattering a pinch of salt across everything. Then, using a hefty pocket-knife, he sliced straight down until the blade met the table, speared the severed morsel on the knife-point, lifted it, and dispatched it along its rightful path.
source_text: The maltster, after having lain down in his clothes for a few hours, was now sitting beside a three-legged table, breakfasting off bread and bacon. This was eaten on the plateless system, which is performed by placing a slice of bread upon the table, the meat flat upon the bread, a mustard plaster upon the meat, and a pinch of salt upon the whole, then cutting them vertically downwards with a large pocket-knife till wood is reached, when the severed lump is impaled on the knife, elevated, and sent the proper way of food.

text: So much for the dogs. Beyond Norcombe Hill lay an old chalk quarry, worked for ages to supply the neighboring fields. Two hedgerows ran toward it in a V-shape, though they stopped just short of touching. The slender gap that remained, poised directly above the quarry’s edge, was guarded by a crude wooden rail.
source_text: Thus much for the dogs. On the further side of Norcombe Hill was a chalk-pit, from which chalk had been drawn for generations, and spread over adjacent farms. Two hedges converged upon it in the form of a V, but without quite meeting. The narrow opening left, which was immediately over the brow of the pit, was protected by a rough railing.

text: “I’d never have credited it, not for a moment, if we hadn’t confirmed it with our own eyes, so to speak,” Joseph Poorgrass hiccupped, “that every cup, every last one of the finest knives and forks, and every empty bottle stands exactly where it began, untouched and unthieved.”
source_text: “I’m sure I should never have believed it if we hadn’t proved it, so to allude,” hiccupped Joseph Poorgrass, “that every cup, every one of the best knives and forks, and every empty bottle be in their place as perfect now as at the beginning, and not one stole at all.”

text: The crutches served her faithfully. The soft scuff of her feet and the click of the sticks on the road were the only noises she made now. She had left the last milestone far behind and kept glancing toward the verge with a longing look, as though reckoning the distance to the next. For all their help, the crutches had their bounds. A device merely shifts the toil—it cannot erase it—and the original strain was not gone, only thrust into her shoulders and arms. She was spent, and each forward lurch grew weaker. At length she tilted to one side and dropped.
source_text: The crutches answered well. The pat of her feet, and the tap of her sticks upon the highway, were all the sounds that came from the traveller now. She had passed the last milestone by a good long distance, and began to look wistfully towards the bank as if calculating upon another milestone soon. The crutches, though so very useful, had their limits of power. Mechanism only transfers labour, being powerless to supersede it, and the original amount of exertion was not cleared away; it was thrown into the body and arms. She was exhausted, and each swing forward became fainter. At last she swayed sideways, and fell.

text: At the far side of the lane, two or three hundred cheerful, robust workers had gathered, leaving their fortunes to luck—men of a kind who saw work as little more than a tussle with gravity, and leisure as nothing finer than letting it go. Among them, carters and waggoners stood out by the whipcord knotted around their hats; thatchers sported a twist of woven straw; shepherds gripped their crooks in hand; and in this way, those doing the hiring could read each man’s trade at a single look.
source_text: At one end of the street stood from two to three hundred blithe and hearty labourers waiting upon Chance—all men of the stamp to whom labour suggests nothing worse than a wrestle with gravitation, and pleasure nothing better than a renunciation of the same. Among these, carters and waggoners were distinguished by having a piece of whip-cord twisted round their hats; thatchers wore a fragment of woven straw; shepherds held their sheep-crooks in their hands; and thus the situation required was known to the hirers at a glance.

text: When the morning sun broke over the crest of High Tor and caught the wet meadow on its brow, faint billows of dust could be seen drifting between the parallel hedgerows that scored the broad landscape in every quarter. These slowly drew together toward the foot of the rise, and the herds became distinct one by one, winding up the twisting paths that climbed to the summit. So, in a measured march, they passed into the gateway where the tracks converged, wave upon wave, horned and polled—russet flocks and slate flocks, tawny flocks and dun flocks, even emerald and rose-washed flocks, as the dyer’s whim or the usage of the holding dictated. Men were calling, dogs were yelping, in a high pitch of excitement, but the pressed travellers on so long a road had grown almost callous to such alarms, though they still cried out plaintively at the strangeness of their passage, a gaunt herdsman standing up here and there among them, like a towering fetish amidst a host of kneeling worshippers.
source_text: When the autumn sun slanted over Greenhill this morning and lighted the dewy flat upon its crest, nebulous clouds of dust were to be seen floating between the pairs of hedges which streaked the wide prospect around in all directions. These gradually converged upon the base of the hill, and the flocks became individually visible, climbing the serpentine ways which led to the top. Thus, in a slow procession, they entered the opening to which the roads tended, multitude after multitude, horned and hornless—blue flocks and red flocks, buff flocks and brown flocks, even green and salmon-tinted flocks, according to the fancy of the colourist and custom of the farm. Men were shouting, dogs were barking, with greatest animation, but the thronging travellers in so long a journey had grown nearly indifferent to such terrors, though they still bleated piteously at the unwontedness of their experiences, a tall shepherd rising here and there in the midst of them, like a gigantic idol amid a crowd of prostrate devotees.

text: Gabriel studied the curve of her jaw in the flickering taper-light, tracing the shifting hues that played across it, and the pale, spiraled whorls of her small ear. She drew out her coin-purse and was pressing upon Lestrange her wish to settle her own account for the tea, just as the tent flap parted and Marden stepped inside. Gabriel shivered: his careful masquerade of decency now teetered on the brink. He meant to slip from his hidden vantage, tail Marden into the crowd, and learn whether the former steward had marked him, but the murmur of voices held him fast, and he knew the moment had passed.
source_text: Troy scrutinized her cheek as lit by the candles, and watched each varying shade thereon, and the white shell-like sinuosities of her little ear. She took out her purse and was insisting to Boldwood on paying for her tea for herself, when at this moment Pennyways entered the tent. Troy trembled: here was his scheme for respectability endangered at once. He was about to leave his hole of espial, attempt to follow Pennyways, and find out if the ex-bailiff had recognized him, when he was arrested by the conversation, and found he was too late.

text: “Well—I doubt he’s someone to pin much faith on in a situation like this,” the farmer said quietly, “clever though he is, and capable at anything. There’s a touch of romance about him, too. His mother worked as a French governess, and apparently she and the old Lord Severn had a secret affair. She ended up marrying a struggling doctor, and soon after a child was born; as long as funds kept coming, things went smoothly. The boy’s misfortune was that his closest protectors died, and he then took a post as a junior clerk at a solicitor’s office in Casterbridge. He remained there a good while, and might have risen to some respectable standing if he hadn’t given in to the mad impulse of joining the army. I strongly doubt that little Fanny will ever astonish us in the way she imagines—very strongly doubt it. A foolish girl!—foolish girl!”
source_text: “H’m—I’m afraid not one to build much hope upon in such a case as this,” the farmer murmured, “though he’s a clever fellow, and up to everything. A slight romance attaches to him, too. His mother was a French governess, and it seems that a secret attachment existed between her and the late Lord Severn. She was married to a poor medical man, and soon after an infant was born; and while money was forthcoming all went on well. Unfortunately for her boy, his best friends died; and he got then a situation as second clerk at a lawyer’s in Casterbridge. He stayed there for some time, and might have worked himself into a dignified position of some sort had he not indulged in the wild freak of enlisting. I have much doubt if ever little Fanny will surprise us in the way she mentions—very much doubt. A silly girl!—silly girl!”

text: “She’s blushing at the slight,” Bathsheba whispered, observing the rosy bloom that surfaced and swept across the ewe’s neck and shoulders, exposed now by the snipping blades—a blush so fine in its tenderness that many a socialite queen would covet it, and so swift in its rise that any woman alive might take pride in it.
source_text: “She blushes at the insult,” murmured Bathsheba, watching the pink flush which arose and overspread the neck and shoulders of the ewe where they were left bare by the clicking shears—a flush which was enviable, for its delicacy, by many queens of coteries, and would have been creditable, for its promptness, to any woman in the world.

text: She paused, a bit unsettled by how Oak had ended their casual talk with such an earnest, old-world gravity. “All right,” she replied, offering her hand while pressing her lips into a prim, unreadable line. He clasped it for barely a moment, and, anxious not to seem overly emotional, veered to the other extreme, brushing her fingers with the faint touch of someone lacking nerve.
source_text: She hesitated, somewhat disconcerted at Oak’s old-fashioned earnest conclusion to a dialogue lightly carried on. “Very well,” she said, and gave him her hand, compressing her lips to a demure impassivity. He held it but an instant, and in his fear of being too demonstrative, swerved to the opposite extreme, touching her fingers with the lightness of a small-hearted person.

text: “No, no. I can’t agree with you on that. She was just a girl, after all—how could she have known the true nature of the man? If the story is really so, the penalty is far too harsh, more than she deserves to bear.—Hark, who goes there?” This last was directed at the sound of footsteps drawing near.
source_text: “No, no. I don’t hold with ’ee there. She was no otherwise than a girl mind, and how could she tell what the man was made of? If ’tis really true, ’tis too hard a punishment, and more than she ought to hae.—Hullo, who’s that?” This was to some footsteps that were heard approaching.

text: To observe unseen was her aim, and so she habitually bore a hooded lamp, lifting its beam at intervals to inspect shadowed alcoves and crannies with the detachment of a city constable. That composure sprang less from bravery in the face of foreseeable peril than from an absence of any such dread; the gravest fault she expected to uncover was a poorly stalled horse, a stray hen still at large, or a latch left unfastened.
source_text: As watching is best done invisibly, she usually carried a dark lantern in her hand, and every now and then turned on the light to examine nooks and corners with the coolness of a metropolitan policeman. This coolness may have owed its existence not so much to her fearlessness of expected danger as to her freedom from the suspicion of any; her worst anticipated discovery being that a horse might not be well bedded, the fowls not all in, or a door not closed.

text: “Must I do it? Not at all; I shall present myself as always, for if I put on a bright gown the world would whisper, and I would look to be celebrating while my heart stays heavy every moment. This gathering does not fit my mood in the slightest; yet never you mind, remain and assist in putting the final touches on me.”
source_text: “Is it necessary? No; I will appear as usual, for if I were to wear any light dress people would say things about me, and I should seem to be rejoicing when I am solemn all the time. The party doesn’t suit me a bit; but never mind, stay and help to finish me off.”

text: The man consented, though inwardly he marveled at the visitor’s state of mind, for Troy wore not a single token of grief. Troy then set down the words for the epitaph, paid what was owed, and departed. Later that afternoon he returned and discovered the lettering nearly finished. He lingered in the yard until the headstone was wrapped, watched it being lifted onto the cart, and saw it begin its journey toward Weatherbury, instructing the two bearers to seek out the sexton and ask for the grave of the one named in the inscription.
source_text: The man agreed, and wondered at such a mood in a visitor who wore not a shred of mourning. Troy then wrote the words which were to form the inscription, settled the account and went away. In the afternoon he came back again, and found that the lettering was almost done. He waited in the yard till the tomb was packed, and saw it placed in the cart and starting on its way to Weatherbury, giving directions to the two men who were to accompany it to inquire of the sexton for the grave of the person named in the inscription.

text: The outcome of the appeal was awaited in Weatherbury with anxious attention. The hanging had been set for eight o'clock on a Saturday morning, roughly two weeks after the verdict, and by Friday afternoon no reply had come. That day Gabriel returned from Casterbridge Prison, where he had gone to bid Boldwood farewell, and took a side lane to skirt the town. Once he passed the last cottage, he caught the sound of hammering, and raising his lowered head, he glanced back briefly. Above the rooftops he could make out the top of the prison gate, vivid and gleaming in the late-day sun, and several figures were moving there. They were workmen raising a timber upright inside the railing. He averted his gaze swiftly and hurried on.
source_text: The upshot of the petition was waited for in Weatherbury with solicitous interest. The execution had been fixed for eight o’clock on a Saturday morning about a fortnight after the sentence was passed, and up to Friday afternoon no answer had been received. At that time Gabriel came from Casterbridge Gaol, whither he had been to wish Boldwood good-bye, and turned down a by-street to avoid the town. When past the last house he heard a hammering, and lifting his bowed head he looked back for a moment. Over the chimneys he could see the upper part of the gaol entrance, rich and glowing in the afternoon sun, and some moving figures were there. They were carpenters lifting a post into a vertical position within the parapet. He withdrew his eyes quickly, and hastened on.

text: Two hundred paces along the chalk ridge, Gabriel caught a reedy “yoo-hoo!” flung after him—higher and thinner than the rough call a man sends skimming over a meadow. He turned, and there was a young woman sprinting in his wake, a pale kerchief fluttering above her head.
source_text: When Gabriel had gone about two hundred yards along the down, he heard a “hoi-hoi!” uttered behind him, in a piping note of more treble quality than that in which the exclamation usually embodies itself when shouted across a field. He looked round, and saw a girl racing after him, waving a white handkerchief.

text: Oak barely had a moment to shape these impressions into thought or to notice how uncannily the crimson feather in her hat glowed in that strange light, when the tall tree on the hill ahead seemed to ignite with a white-hot brilliance, and a fresh voice among those terrible sounds joined the final crash of the ones before. It was a numbing blast, raw and merciless, striking their ears with a dead, level force, lacking the resonant drum-echo that rolls through distant thunder. By the sheen reflected from every surface of the earth and from the vast domed hollow overhead, he saw that the tree had been split the entire length of its straight, towering trunk, a great ribbon of bark apparently ripped away. The rest of it stood upright, exposing the stripped wood as a pale slash down its front. The lightning had hit the tree. A sulfurous reek steeped the air; then everything fell silent and black as a cave in Hinnom.
source_text: Oak had hardly time to gather up these impressions into a thought, and to see how strangely the red feather of her hat shone in this light, when the tall tree on the hill before mentioned seemed on fire to a white heat, and a new one among these terrible voices mingled with the last crash of those preceding. It was a stupefying blast, harsh and pitiless, and it fell upon their ears in a dead, flat blow, without that reverberation which lends the tones of a drum to more distant thunder. By the lustre reflected from every part of the earth and from the wide domical scoop above it, he saw that the tree was sliced down the whole length of its tall, straight stem, a huge riband of bark being apparently flung off. The other portion remained erect, and revealed the bared surface as a strip of white down the front. The lightning had struck the tree. A sulphurous smell filled the air; then all was silent, and black as a cave in Hinnom.

text: “That’s precisely what I remarked this morning,” Joseph noted. “‘Justice has arrived to weigh him in the scales,’ I said, in my thoughtful manner, ‘and should he be found lacking, let that be his portion.’ Someone standing by called out, ‘Hear, hear! A fellow who speaks in such a way deserves an audience.’ But I prefer not to linger on it—my few words are just my few words, nothing more; though some men’s talk gets carried far and wide, as if nature had shaped them for it.”
source_text: “The very thing I said this morning,” observed Joseph, “‘Justice is come to weigh him in the balances,’ I said in my reflectious way, ‘and if he’s found wanting, so be it unto him,’ and a bystander said ‘Hear, hear! A man who can talk like that ought to be heard.’ But I don’t like dwelling upon it, for my few words are my few words, and not much; though the speech of some men is rumoured abroad as though by nature formed for such.”

text: The minister slipped into the vestry, and the sexton disappeared. The soldier still hadn't faced around; every woman in the congregation was poised to glimpse his expression, and he seemed aware of it. Finally he did turn, and strode purposefully along the aisle, confronting them all with a tight-set mouth. Two stooped, toothless old pensioners caught each other's eye and snickered, quite harmlessly; yet the noise carried an eerie, uncanny resonance in that setting.
source_text: The clergyman glided into the vestry, and the clerk vanished. The sergeant had not yet turned; every woman in the church was waiting to see his face, and he appeared to know it. At last he did turn, and stalked resolutely down the nave, braving them all, with a compressed lip. Two bowed and toothless old almsmen then looked at each other and chuckled, innocently enough; but the sound had a strange weird effect in that place.

text: The kindly blaze now stirred the half-dead lambs into faint bleating and a sudden twitching of legs upon the straw, as they came dimly to the knowledge of their own existence. Their cries soon swelled into a chorus of wavering baas, at which Oak drew the milk-pail from the hearth, fetched a little teapot from the folds of his smock, brimmed it with warm milk, and showed the orphaned creatures—those who would not be returned to the ewes—how to sup from the spout, a skill they gathered with remarkable quickness.
source_text: The genial warmth of the fire now began to stimulate the nearly lifeless lambs to bleat and move their limbs briskly upon the hay, and to recognize for the first time the fact that they were born. Their noise increased to a chorus of baas, upon which Oak pulled the milk-can from before the fire, and taking a small tea-pot from the pocket of his smock-frock, filled it with milk, and taught those of the helpless creatures which were not to be restored to their dams how to drink from the spout—a trick they acquired with astonishing aptitude.

text: Word had quietly spread through the village that the corpse due to arrive for burial that day was all that remained of poor Fanny Robin, who had trailed the Eleventh Regiment from Casterbridge through Melchester and beyond. Yet, owing to Boldwood’s silence and Oak’s kindness, the man she had pursued had never been singled out as Troy. Gabriel wished that the full story might stay hidden at least until the girl had lain in the ground for a few days, when the softening barriers of soil and passing time, along with the feeling that the affair had slipped partly into forgetfulness, would blunt the pain that exposure and malicious gossip would now cause Bathsheba.
source_text: It had gradually become rumoured in the village that the body to be brought and buried that day was all that was left of the unfortunate Fanny Robin who had followed the Eleventh from Casterbridge through Melchester and onwards. But, thanks to Boldwood’s reticence and Oak’s generosity, the lover she had followed had never been individualized as Troy. Gabriel hoped that the whole truth of the matter might not be published till at any rate the girl had been in her grave for a few days, when the interposing barriers of earth and time, and a sense that the events had been somewhat shut into oblivion, would deaden the sting that revelation and invidious remark would have for Bathsheba just now.

text: A third bolt flared. Above, in the immense hollows of the sky, strange and violent maneuvers were unfolding. The lightning had turned silver now, glittering across the heavens like an armored legion. Rumbles sharpened into rattles. From his high vantage, Gabriel could survey at least six miles of the country before him. Every hedge, bush, and tree stood out with the precision of an engraving. In a paddock in that same direction, a herd of heifers was caught mid-gallop in the wildest, most frantic disorder, hooves and tails flung high, heads driven low. A poplar in the near foreground cut the scene like a stroke of ink on polished tin. Then the vision collapsed, and the darkness that followed was so absolute that Gabriel worked solely by touch.
source_text: Then there came a third flash. Manœuvres of a most extraordinary kind were going on in the vast firmamental hollows overhead. The lightning now was the colour of silver, and gleamed in the heavens like a mailed army. Rumbles became rattles. Gabriel from his elevated position could see over the landscape at least half-a-dozen miles in front. Every hedge, bush, and tree was distinct as in a line engraving. In a paddock in the same direction was a herd of heifers, and the forms of these were visible at this moment in the act of galloping about in the wildest and maddest confusion, flinging their heels and tails high into the air, their heads to earth. A poplar in the immediate foreground was like an ink stroke on burnished tin. Then the picture vanished, leaving the darkness so intense that Gabriel worked entirely by feeling with his hands.

text: Bathsheba moved along the unlit lane, indifferent to where she was heading or how her flight would end. She only became fully aware of her surroundings when she arrived at a gate that opened into a dense copse shadowed by towering oaks and beeches. Peering inside, she realized she had glimpsed this place before in daylight, and that what seemed an impenetrable tangle was merely a patch of ferns rapidly browning with decay. Unable to conceive of any better refuge for her trembling frame, she decided to slip inside and conceal herself; stepping through, she found a spot protected from the clammy mist by a fallen trunk, where she let herself sink onto a matted bed of stalks and leaves. Almost without thinking, she drew heaps of the fronds around her body to fend off the gusts, then shut her eyes.
source_text: Bathsheba went along the dark road, neither knowing nor caring about the direction or issue of her flight. The first time that she definitely noticed her position was when she reached a gate leading into a thicket overhung by some large oak and beech trees. On looking into the place, it occurred to her that she had seen it by daylight on some previous occasion, and that what appeared like an impassable thicket was in reality a brake of fern now withering fast. She could think of nothing better to do with her palpitating self than to go in here and hide; and entering, she lighted on a spot sheltered from the damp fog by a reclining trunk, where she sank down upon a tangled couch of fronds and stems. She mechanically pulled some armfuls round her to keep off the breezes, and closed her eyes.

text: Don’t do that—please don’t. Your precious love, Bathsheba, is so immense compared to your pity that losing both means little more to my grief, and gaining your pity barely eases it. Oh, my darling—how tenderly you spoke to me behind the reed bed at the washing-pool, in the barn during shearing, and that most cherished last time at dusk in your house! What has become of your gentle words—your sincere wish that you might come to love me? Where is your absolute certainty that you would grow to care for me deeply? Truly forgotten?—truly?
source_text: “Do no such thing—do no such thing. Your dear love, Bathsheba, is such a vast thing beside your pity, that the loss of your pity as well as your love is no great addition to my sorrow, nor does the gain of your pity make it sensibly less. O sweet—how dearly you spoke to me behind the spear-bed at the washing-pool, and in the barn at the shearing, and that dearest last time in the evening at your home! Where are your pleasant words all gone—your earnest hope to be able to love me? Where is your firm conviction that you would get to care for me very much? Really forgotten?—really?”

text: Mills now found himself caught in a delicate snare, for if Bathsheba and Troy mended their bond, he would need to reclaim her favour to keep the husband’s backing. “I half fancy she still holds you dear, and is a decent soul beneath it all,” he offered, as a cautious hedge. “Still, a person’s outward show is a poor oracle. Well, you must follow your own mind about returning, Captain, and I, for my part, shall follow your word.”
source_text: Pennyways now felt himself to be in something of a difficulty, for should Bathsheba and Troy become reconciled it would be necessary to regain her good opinion if he would secure the patronage of her husband. “I sometimes think she likes you yet, and is a good woman at bottom,” he said, as a saving sentence. “But there’s no telling to a certainty from a body’s outside. Well, you’ll do as you like about going, of course, sergeant, and as for me, I’ll do as you tell me.”

text: Despite everything, the house lacked a true festive soul. Its owner had never ventured into such an undertaking, and now it unfolded as if forced by a violent twist. The planned merriment kept turning stiff and ceremonial, the entire affair was executed with chilly precision by paid strangers, and a presence seemed to drift through the chambers whispering that these doings were alien to the dwelling and its solitary inhabitant, and therefore ill-omened.
source_text: In spite of all this, the spirit of revelry was wanting in the atmosphere of the house. Such a thing had never been attempted before by its owner, and it was now done as by a wrench. Intended gaieties would insist upon appearing like solemn grandeurs, the organization of the whole effort was carried out coldly, by hirelings, and a shadow seemed to move about the rooms, saying that the proceedings were unnatural to the place and the lone man who lived therein, and hence not good.

text: Farmer Boldwood caught the silent signal that told him they knew he was there, and the realization hit his fresh sensitivity like a blinding glare. He was still on the road, and by continuing forward he hoped neither would guess he had meant to step into the field. He walked on wrapped in a complete and crushing fog of ignorance, awkwardness, and uncertainty. Maybe her bearing held hints that she wanted to see him—maybe not—he could not decipher a woman. The secret code of this amorous philosophy seemed built from the faintest cues delivered in deceptive packages. Every gesture, glance, word, and inflection carried a riddle far removed from its surface meaning, and not one had he ever stopped to study until this moment.
source_text: Farmer Boldwood had read the pantomime denoting that they were aware of his presence, and the perception was as too much light turned upon his new sensibility. He was still in the road, and by moving on he hoped that neither would recognize that he had originally intended to enter the field. He passed by with an utter and overwhelming sensation of ignorance, shyness, and doubt. Perhaps in her manner there were signs that she wished to see him—perhaps not—he could not read a woman. The cabala of this erotic philosophy seemed to consist of the subtlest meanings expressed in misleading ways. Every turn, look, word, and accent contained a mystery quite distinct from its obvious import, and not one had ever been pondered by him until now.

text: Gazing into the room of death she had just left, they discerned by the glow of the candles set upon the chest of drawers a long, upright form resting at the far side of the chamber, sheathed in white. All about lay in perfect order. The doctor stepped inside, and after some moments came back out to the landing, where Oak and the clergyman were still waiting.
source_text: Looking into the chamber of death she had vacated they saw by the light of the candles which were on the drawers a tall straight shape lying at the further end of the bedroom, wrapped in white. Everything around was quite orderly. The doctor went in, and after a few minutes returned to the landing again, where Oak and the parson still waited.

text: Even under the faint glimmer of starlight, a closer look at the ground nearby showed how part of what might loosely be termed a wild hillside had been claimed by Farmer Oak for his chief winter undertaking. Here and there, freestanding wattles padded with straw had been driven into the earth, and among them, beneath them, the pale shapes of his gentle ewes shifted and whispered. The sheep-bell, quiet while he was away, began to sound again, its notes more mellow than distinct, muffled by the thickening fleece around it. It rang on until Oak moved off from the flock once more. He went back to the hut, cradling a newborn lamb whose four legs—each big enough for a full-grown sheep—were joined by a thin membrane that seemed almost negligible, yet for now formed the creature’s entire body.
source_text: A close examination of the ground hereabout, even by the wan starlight only, revealed how a portion of what would have been casually called a wild slope had been appropriated by Farmer Oak for his great purpose this winter. Detached hurdles thatched with straw were stuck into the ground at various scattered points, amid and under which the whitish forms of his meek ewes moved and rustled. The ring of the sheep-bell, which had been silent during his absence, recommenced, in tones that had more mellowness than clearness, owing to an increasing growth of surrounding wool. This continued till Oak withdrew again from the flock. He returned to the hut, bringing in his arms a new-born lamb, consisting of four legs large enough for a full-grown sheep, united by a seemingly inconsiderable membrane about half the substance of the legs collectively, which constituted the animal’s entire body just at present.

text: Bathsheba had reason to suspect a link between her own past and the shadowy tragedy of Fanny’s death—a link that Oak and Boldwood never imagined she might perceive. Her encounter with the solitary woman the previous Saturday night had gone unseen and unmentioned. Oak might have meant well by delaying the full account of Fanny’s fate for as long as he could, but had he realized that Bathsheba’s mind had already been at work on the matter, he would not have prolonged the anxious waiting she now endured—especially since the truth that would end it would turn out to be the very worst she had feared.
source_text: Bathsheba had grounds for conjecturing a connection between her own history and the dimly suspected tragedy of Fanny’s end which Oak and Boldwood never for a moment credited her with possessing. The meeting with the lonely woman on the previous Saturday night had been unwitnessed and unspoken of. Oak may have had the best of intentions in withholding for as many days as possible the details of what had happened to Fanny; but had he known that Bathsheba’s perceptions had already been exercised in the matter, he would have done nothing to lengthen the minutes of suspense she was now undergoing, when the certainty which must terminate it would be the worst fact suspected after all.

text: When he lit a lamp inside, a slender, gleaming trail caught the light on the table’s surface, like a delicate stroke of lacquer pulled faintly across the wood. His gaze traced the winding shine to its far end, where it climbed toward a large brown garden-slug that had wandered in that evening on some private errand. It was Nature’s other means of whispering to him that rough weather lay ahead.
source_text: When he struck a light indoors there appeared upon the table a thin glistening streak, as if a brush of varnish had been lightly dragged across it. Oak’s eyes followed the serpentine sheen to the other side, where it led up to a huge brown garden-slug, which had come indoors to-night for reasons of its own. It was Nature’s second way of hinting to him that he was to prepare for foul weather.

text: “Speak the vow, my darling, and the matter shall be closed; a tender, devoted closeness of six years, and then wedlock—O Bathsheba, speak it!” he implored in a roughened murmur, incapable of keeping to the pretenses of simple friendship a moment more. “Bind yourself to me; I have earned it, truly I have, for I have cherished you beyond any soul on earth! And if I uttered rash phrases or showed unmerited sharpness of bearing toward you, trust me, beloved, I had no wish to wound you; I was in torment, Bathsheba, and I scarcely knew my own words. You would not permit a hound to endure what I have endured, if only you understood! At times I recoil from your ever learning the depths of my feeling, and at times I grieve that you may never grasp its fullness. Show mercy, and yield a fraction to me, when I would surrender my very existence for you!”
source_text: “Say the words, dear one, and the subject shall be dismissed; a blissful loving intimacy of six years, and then marriage—O Bathsheba, say them!” he begged in a husky voice, unable to sustain the forms of mere friendship any longer. “Promise yourself to me; I deserve it, indeed I do, for I have loved you more than anybody in the world! And if I said hasty words and showed uncalled-for heat of manner towards you, believe me, dear, I did not mean to distress you; I was in agony, Bathsheba, and I did not know what I said. You wouldn’t let a dog suffer what I have suffered, could you but know it! Sometimes I shrink from your knowing what I have felt for you, and sometimes I am distressed that all of it you never will know. Be gracious, and give up a little to me, when I would give up my life for you!”

text: It came to light in time that Gabriel, though Bathsheba paid him a steady sum regardless of the harvest’s yield, had struck a compact with Boldwood entitling Oak to a portion of the takings—a modest portion, to be sure, but it was coin of a purer stamp than common wages, and bore within it a germ of increase that wages could never foster. A few now began to murmur that Oak was a “close” fellow, for while his fortunes had plainly risen, he kept to no grander mode of living than before, dwelling in the same humble rooms, peeling his own potatoes, darning his own hose, and sometimes even smoothing his own bedclothes with his own hands. Yet since Oak was not only vexingly careless of the world’s gaze, but a creature who held fast to ancient ways and customs merely because they were ancient, his true intentions remained open to question.
source_text: It was eventually known that Gabriel, though paid a fixed wage by Bathsheba independent of the fluctuations of agricultural profits, had made an engagement with Boldwood by which Oak was to receive a share of the receipts—a small share certainly, yet it was money of a higher quality than mere wages, and capable of expansion in a way that wages were not.
