Some were beginning to consider Oak a “near” man, for though his condition had thus far improved, he lived in no better style than before, occupying the same cottage, paring his own potatoes, mending his stockings, and sometimes even making his bed with his own hands. But as Oak was not only provokingly indifferent to public opinion, but a man who clung persistently to old habits and usages, simply because they were old, there was room for doubt as to his motives.

text: “Aye, had that cursed downpour not come, I’d have pocketed two hundred as smooth as blinking, my darling,” he murmured. “Can’t you grasp how it twisted every likelihood? As some volume I once perused put it, rain-soaked skies compose the running tale, and sunlit hours the scattered scenes, of our land’s chronicle; now, is that not the very truth?”
source_text: “Yes, if it hadn’t been for that wretched rain I should have cleared two hundred as easy as looking, my love,” he was saying. “Don’t you see, it altered all the chances? To speak like a book I once read, wet weather is the narrative, and fine days are the episodes, of our country’s history; now, isn’t that true?”

text: “I can wait up for the master, if you wish, ma’am. I’m not in the least frightened of Fanny, so long as I may stay in my own room with a candle lit. She was such a gentle, timid little creature that I’m certain her spirit couldn’t show itself to anyone, even if it wanted to.”
source_text: “I’ll sit up for master if you like, ma’am. I am not at all afraid of Fanny, if I may sit in my own room and have a candle. She was such a childlike, nesh young thing that her spirit couldn’t appear to anybody if it tried, I’m quite sure.”

text: “There, let’s head home now,” said the older woman, planting her fists on her hips and surveying the entire scene. “I truly hope Daisy pulls through this time. I’ve never been so terrified in all my days, but I don’t care about losing sleep as long as she gets better.”
source_text: “There, now we’ll go home,” said the elder of the two, resting her knuckles upon her hips, and looking at their goings-on as a whole. “I do hope Daisy will fetch round again now. I have never been more frightened in my life, but I don’t mind breaking my rest if she recovers.”

text: “Oh, that’s quite accurate. I was well acquainted with both the husband and wife. The man was called Levi Everdene—I’m certain of it. In my haste, I said ‘man,’ but he actually moved in a more elevated sphere—he was a gentleman-tailor, truly, worth a great many pounds. And he achieved considerable renown by going bankrupt two or three times.”
source_text: “Oh, ’tis true enough. I knowed the man and woman both well. Levi Everdene—that was the man’s name, sure. ‘Man,’ saith I in my hurry, but he were of a higher circle of life than that—’a was a gentleman-tailor really, worth scores of pounds. And he became a very celebrated bankrupt two or three times.”

text: A moment before the clock chimed five, Gabriel Oak and Coggan walked past the village cross and continued side by side toward the fields. They were still just within sight of their mistress’s house when Oak thought he glimpsed a casement swinging open in one of the upper windows. At that instant, the two men stood partly hidden by an elder bush, its branches newly adorned with dark clusters of berries, and they hesitated before stepping out from its shadow.
source_text: Just before the clock struck five Gabriel Oak and Coggan passed the village cross, and went on together to the fields. They were yet barely in view of their mistress’s house, when Oak fancied he saw the opening of a casement in one of the upper windows. The two men were at this moment partially screened by an elder bush, now beginning to be enriched with black bunches of fruit, and they paused before emerging from its shade.

text: Now other footfalls sounded along the lane. Bathsheba’s nerves remained frayed; she ducked back into hiding, and the walker emerged into sight. He was a schoolboy, a satchel for his dinner slung across his shoulder and a book in his hand. He halted at the gate, and without lifting his gaze, went on murmuring words in a voice just loud enough to reach her ears.
source_text: There were now other footsteps to be heard along the road. Bathsheba’s nerves were still unstrung: she crouched down out of sight again, and the pedestrian came into view. He was a schoolboy, with a bag slung over his shoulder containing his dinner, and a book in his hand. He paused by the gate, and, without looking up, continued murmuring words in tones quite loud enough to reach her ears.

text: Bathsheba, not being remotely in love with him, could eventually consider his proposal with composure. It was the sort of offer that many women of her own standing in the district, and quite a few of higher status, would have eagerly seized and proudly announced. From every angle, whether calculated or emotional, it made sense that she, an isolated young woman, should marry—and marry this sincere, prosperous, and esteemed man. He lived nearby; his reputation was solid; his virtues were even excessive. If she had felt any abstract desire for marriage—which she did not—she could not have sensibly turned him down, since she often relied on reason to rescue her from caprices. As a path to matrimony, Boldwood was flawless: she respected and liked him, yet she did not want him. It seems that ordinary men take wives because ownership is impossible without marriage, and ordinary women accept husbands because marriage is impossible without ownership; with wholly different goals, the approach is the same on both sides. But the usual motivation on the woman's part was absent here. Moreover, Bathsheba’s situation as sole mistress of a farm and household was still fresh, and the freshness had not yet started to fade.
source_text: Bathsheba, not being the least in love with him, was eventually able to look calmly at his offer. It was one which many women of her own station in the neighbourhood, and not a few of higher rank, would have been wild to accept and proud to publish. In every point of view, ranging from politic to passionate, it was desirable that she, a lonely girl, should marry, and marry this earnest, well-to-do, and respected man. He was close to her doors: his standing was sufficient: his qualities were even supererogatory. Had she felt, which she did not, any wish whatever for the married state in the abstract, she could not reasonably have rejected him, being a woman who frequently appealed to her understanding for deliverance from her whims. Boldwood as a means to marriage was unexceptionable: she esteemed and liked him, yet she did not want him. It appears that ordinary men take wives because possession is not possible without marriage, and that ordinary women accept husbands because marriage is not possible without possession; with totally differing aims the method is the same on both sides. But the understood incentive on the woman’s part was wanting here. Besides, Bathsheba’s position as absolute mistress of a farm and house was a novel one, and the novelty had not yet begun to wear off.

text: Bathsheba clasped the letter in her right hand. Boldwood extended toward her a plate of sliced bread-and-butter; as she reached for a piece, she shifted the letter to her left hand, where the purse already rested, and then let that hand fall beside her, close to the canvas wall. The moment to seize his chance had arrived, and Troy felt an impulsive certainty that he would play this card. Once more he gazed at the elegant hand, noting the rosy fingertips and the delicate blue veins at the wrist, encircled by a bracelet of coral fragments she always wore: how intimately he knew every detail! Then, with the swift, silent precision in which he excelled, he soundlessly slid his hand beneath the loose bottom of the tent-cloth—far from tightly pegged—lifted it just enough, fixed his sight through the gap, plucked the note from her fingers, let the canvas fall, and fled into the dimness toward the bank and ditch, grinning at her startled cry. Troy slipped down the outer slope of the rampart, hurried along the bottom of the trench for a hundred yards, climbed up again, and sauntered boldly toward the tent’s front entrance. His aim now was to reach Pennyways and forestall any further announcement until a moment of his own choosing.
source_text: Bathsheba held the note in her right hand. Boldwood handed towards her a plate of cut bread-and-butter; when, in order to take a slice, she put the note into her left hand, where she was still holding the purse, and then allowed her hand to drop beside her close to the canvas. The moment had come for saving his game, and Troy impulsively felt that he would play the card. For yet another time he looked at the fair hand, and saw the pink finger-tips, and the blue veins of the wrist, encircled by a bracelet of coral chippings which she wore: how familiar it all was to him! Then, with the lightning action in which he was such an adept, he noiselessly slipped his hand under the bottom of the tent-cloth, which was far from being pinned tightly down, lifted it a little way, keeping his eye to the hole, snatched the note from her fingers, dropped the canvas, and ran away in the gloom towards the bank and ditch, smiling at the scream of astonishment which burst from her. Troy then slid down on the outside of the rampart, hastened round in the bottom of the entrenchment to a distance of a hundred yards, ascended again, and crossed boldly in a slow walk towards the front entrance of the tent. His object was now to get to Pennyways, and prevent a repetition of the announcement until such time as he should choose.

text: “I do not charge you with it—I mourn it. I mistook for sincerity what you now call sport, and now what I beg to be sport you pronounce grim, miserable sincerity. Our temperaments collide at crooked angles. I wish your heart were shaped more like mine, or mine more like yours! Had I only glimpsed the torment that idle piece of mischief would drag me into, how fiercely I would have damned you; but seeing it only in hindsight, I cannot, for I cherish you too deeply. Yet this is feeble, empty rambling to continue so.... Bathsheba, you are the first woman of any complexion or kind whom I have ever gazed upon to adore, and it is having come so close to calling you mine that makes this refusal so cruel to endure. How near you came to pledging yourself! But I do not speak now to stir your pity, or to make you sorrow for my suffering; that is fruitless. I must shoulder it; my anguish would not lighten by wounding you.”
source_text: “I don’t accuse you of it—I deplore it. I took for earnest what you insist was jest, and now this that I pray to be jest you say is awful, wretched earnest. Our moods meet at wrong places. I wish your feeling was more like mine, or my feeling more like yours! Oh, could I but have foreseen the torture that trifling trick was going to lead me into, how I should have cursed you; but only having been able to see it since, I cannot do that, for I love you too well! But it is weak, idle drivelling to go on like this.... Bathsheba, you are the first woman of any shade or nature that I have ever looked at to love, and it is the having been so near claiming you for my own that makes this denial so hard to bear. How nearly you promised me! But I don’t speak now to move your heart, and make you grieve because of my pain; it is no use, that. I must bear it; my pain would get no less by paining you.”

text: He now remembered the spot and its ominous reputation. Over the years, many swimmers had wished for a death ashore, and like Gonzalo, their prayers had gone unheard; Troy began to think he might well join their ranks. No vessel of any sort was visible nearby, but far off Budmouth rested on the water, almost as if it were calmly watching his struggle, and next to the town the harbor revealed itself through a faint tangle of rigging and masts. After nearly draining his strength trying to return to the cove's entrance, swimming feebly a few inches lower than usual, drawing breath solely through his nose, rolling onto his back a dozen times, attempting a butterfly stroke, and so forth, Troy decided as a final measure to tread water at a gentle slant and try to make land wherever possible, simply nudging himself shoreward while drifting with the tide's general flow. This, inevitably a gradual business, proved less taxing than he feared, and although he had no say in where he might come ashore—the landmarks gliding past him in a mournful, unhurried file—he was clearly nearing the tip of a sandbar farther to the right, now sharply outlined against the sunlit edge of the horizon. While the swimmer kept his gaze locked on that spit as his sole hope of rescue this side of oblivion, a moving shape disrupted its farthest line, and at once a ship's boat came into view, crewed by several young sailors, its bow aimed seaward.
source_text: He now recollected the place and its sinister character. Many bathers had there prayed for a dry death from time to time, and, like Gonzalo also, had been unanswered; and Troy began to deem it possible that he might be added to their number. Not a boat of any kind was at present within sight, but far in the distance Budmouth lay upon the sea, as it were quietly regarding his efforts, and beside the town the harbour showed its position by a dim meshwork of ropes and spars. After well-nigh exhausting himself in attempts to get back to the mouth of the cove, in his weakness swimming several inches deeper than was his wont, keeping up his breathing entirely by his nostrils, turning upon his back a dozen times over, swimming _en papillon_, and so on, Troy resolved as a last resource to tread water at a slight incline, and so endeavour to reach the shore at any point, merely giving himself a gentle impetus inwards whilst carried on in the general direction of the tide. This, necessarily a slow process, he found to be not altogether so difficult, and though there was no choice of a landing-place—the objects on shore passing by him in a sad and slow procession—he perceptibly approached the extremity of a spit of land yet further to the right, now well defined against the sunny portion of the horizon. While the swimmer’s eyes were fixed upon the spit as his only means of salvation on this side of the Unknown, a moving object broke the outline of the extremity, and immediately a ship’s boat appeared manned with several sailor lads, her bows towards the sea.

text: Men often dwindle into nothingness and vanish just as much by failing to seize the energy of high spirits when they have them as by lacking that energy when it’s most needed. Gabriel, recently—for the first time since misfortune had laid him low—had been thinking freely and acting decisively to a remarkable degree. These qualities, useless without an opening just as an opening is useless without them, would have carried him surely upward the moment the right circumstances aligned. But this hopeless lingering near Bathsheba Everdene devoured his time to ruinous effect. The spring tides were passing without lifting him out, and soon the neap tide would arrive, which never could.
source_text: Men thin away to insignificance and oblivion quite as often by not making the most of good spirits when they have them as by lacking good spirits when they are indispensable. Gabriel lately, for the first time since his prostration by misfortune, had been independent in thought and vigorous in action to a marked extent—conditions which, powerless without an opportunity as an opportunity without them is barren, would have given him a sure lift upwards when the favourable conjunction should have occurred. But this incurable loitering beside Bathsheba Everdene stole his time ruinously. The spring tides were going by without floating him off, and the neap might soon come which could not.

text: As these words and others like them reached his ears, a sudden, reckless notion seized Gabriel—that they might be talking of Bathsheba. Yet there was nothing to anchor the idea; the wagon, though headed toward Weatherbury, could easily pass through and beyond it, and the woman they spoke of sounded like the owner of some property. They now seemed to be drawing very near to Weatherbury, and unwilling to startle the speakers without cause, Gabriel dropped quietly from the wagon, unnoticed.
source_text: On hearing these and other similar remarks, a wild thought flashed into Gabriel’s mind that they might be speaking of Bathsheba. There were, however, no grounds for retaining such a supposition, for the waggon, though going in the direction of Weatherbury, might be going beyond it, and the woman alluded to seemed to be the mistress of some estate. They were now apparently close upon Weatherbury and not to alarm the speakers unnecessarily, Gabriel slipped out of the waggon unseen.

text: At this point the patch of white halted and shrank further. The shape bent low. A scrap of snow arced over the stream toward the sixth pane. It struck the brickwork some feet wide of its target. The cast was the conception of a male fused with the delivery of a female. No boy who had ever aimed at sparrow, hare, or field mouse in his youth could have flung with such pure foolishness as now revealed itself.
source_text: Here the spot stopped, and dwindled smaller. The figure was stooping. Then a morsel of snow flew across the river towards the fifth window. It smacked against the wall at a point several yards from its mark. The throw was the idea of a man conjoined with the execution of a woman. No man who had ever seen bird, rabbit, or squirrel in his childhood, could possibly have thrown with such utter imbecility as was shown here.

text: To do justice to Bathsheba, a small detail—one that only surfaced much later—should be mentioned here: Troy’s timely appearance by the road that evening was not the result of any clear, prior agreement. He had suggested it; she had refused. She had sent Oak away merely on the possibility that Troy might still come, dreading an encounter between the two men at that moment.
source_text: It is only fair to Bathsheba to explain here a little fact which did not come to light till a long time afterwards: that Troy’s presentation of himself so aptly at the roadside this evening was not by any distinctly preconcerted arrangement. He had hinted—she had forbidden; and it was only on the chance of his still coming that she had dismissed Oak, fearing a meeting between them just then.

text: The night wore a menacing face. A sultry southern wind lazed across the crowns of tall things, while overhead two layers of cloud sailed at cross-purposes, neither keeping time with the breeze beneath. Through those drifting veils, the moon glared a sickly, metallic yellow. The fields lay wan in the tainted glow, all stained to a single hue as though glimpsed through tinted glass. That same evening, the sheep had trudged home in a tight, nose-to-tail file, the rooks had wheeled in baffled patterns, and the horses had stepped with wary, guarded strides.
source_text: The night had a sinister aspect. A heated breeze from the south slowly fanned the summits of lofty objects, and in the sky dashes of buoyant cloud were sailing in a course at right angles to that of another stratum, neither of them in the direction of the breeze below. The moon, as seen through these films, had a lurid metallic look. The fields were sallow with the impure light, and all were tinged in monochrome, as if beheld through stained glass. The same evening the sheep had trailed homeward head to tail, the behaviour of the rooks had been confused, and the horses had moved with timidity and caution.

text: “You’re wrong! It’s you who’d cast away a poor lad’s spirit, Joseph Poorgrass—that’s the sum of it!” declared Abel, his voice breaking into tears. “All I’m saying is that by plain honesty it was Miss Everdene and Sergeant Troy, but in the dreadful God’s-honest truth you’re bent on shaping, it might have been another soul entirely!”
source_text: “No, I don’t! ’Tis you want to squander a pore boy’s soul, Joseph Poorgrass—that’s what ’tis!” said Cain, beginning to cry. “All I mane is that in common truth ’twas Miss Everdene and Sergeant Troy, but in the horrible so-help-me truth that ye want to make of it perhaps ’twas somebody else!”

text: Those who can reproach without speaking may discover it to be a weapon keener than speech. The eye carries inflections the tongue cannot shape, and bloodless lips tell more stories than the ear can ever catch. There is a strange majesty—and a sorrow—in those distant states of feeling that shun the avenue of sound. Boldwood’s gaze admitted no reply.
source_text: Those who have the power of reproaching in silence may find it a means more effective than words. There are accents in the eye which are not on the tongue, and more tales come from pale lips than can enter an ear. It is both the grandeur and the pain of the remoter moods that they avoid the pathway of sound. Boldwood’s look was unanswerable.

text: “Ah, Miss Everdene!” murmured the sergeant, lifting his modest cap with a deferential hand. “I never imagined, that evening past, it was you I addressed. Though, had I paused to consider, the ‘Sovereign of the Corn-market’—truth wears no hour, and I caught that very phrase in Casterbridge only yesterday—the ‘Sovereign of the Corn-market,’ I declare, could belong to none but yourself. I have come across now to implore your pardon a thousandfold for permitting my sentiments to carry me into words too fervent for a stranger’s ear. Indeed, I am hardly a stranger to this soil—I am Sergeant Troy, as I mentioned before, and I lent your uncle a hand in these very acres countless times when I was a boy. To-day I have done the same for you.”
source_text: “Ah, Miss Everdene!” said the sergeant, touching his diminutive cap. “Little did I think it was you I was speaking to the other night. And yet, if I had reflected, the ‘Queen of the Corn-market’ (truth is truth at any hour of the day or night, and I heard you so named in Casterbridge yesterday), the ‘Queen of the Corn-market.’ I say, could be no other woman. I step across now to beg your forgiveness a thousand times for having been led by my feelings to express myself too strongly for a stranger. To be sure I am no stranger to the place—I am Sergeant Troy, as I told you, and I have assisted your uncle in these fields no end of times when I was a lad. I have been doing the same for you to-day.”

text: In later years, Bathsheba could never fully understand the state of mind that propelled her through what she did after whispering that resolution on that unforgettable night. She fetched a screwdriver from the storage closet. After a brief but indistinct interval, she discovered herself in the cramped chamber, trembling with feeling, her vision blurred, a fierce throbbing in her head, positioned next to the open coffin of the young woman whose imagined fate had so completely consumed her thoughts, and murmuring to herself in a rough tone as she peered inside—
source_text: Bathsheba in after times could never gauge the mood which carried her through the actions following this murmured resolution on this memorable evening of her life. She went to the lumber-closet for a screw-driver. At the end of a short though undefined time she found herself in the small room, quivering with emotion, a mist before her eyes, and an excruciating pulsation in her brain, standing beside the uncovered coffin of the girl whose conjectured end had so entirely engrossed her, and saying to herself in a husky voice as she gazed within—

text: All the old men chased after her in a tangled crowd toward the clover meadow, Joseph collapsing midway as if wilting under a sky grown too heavy to bear. Once kindled by the spark her nearness always struck in them, they set to work among the flock with renewed purpose. Most of the stricken creatures lay motionless and could not be roused. These they hoisted up bodily, while the rest were herded into the next pasture. There, after a short while, a few more crumpled to the earth and sprawled, inert and ashen like the others.
source_text: All the ancient men ran in a jumbled throng after her to the clover-field, Joseph sinking down in the midst when about half-way, like an individual withering in a world which was more and more insupportable. Having once received the stimulus that her presence always gave them they went round among the sheep with a will. The majority of the afflicted animals were lying down, and could not be stirred. These were bodily lifted out, and the others driven into the adjoining field. Here, after the lapse of a few minutes, several more fell down, and lay helpless and livid as the rest.

text: Boldwood’s face had settled back into its usual guarded, melancholy cast, yet the mask slipped the moment he spoke Bathsheba’s name, betraying him utterly. As he descended the stairs, his restless agitation betrayed itself still, his fingers drumming a rapid, uneven beat against his thigh.
source_text: A reserved and sombre expression had returned to Boldwood’s face again, but it poorly cloaked his feelings when he pronounced Bathsheba’s name; and his feverish anxiety continued to show its existence by a galloping motion of his fingers upon the side of his thigh as he went down the stairs.

text: “All right. I’ll excuse you from the ones and threes. Now, let’s see the cuts, points, and guards all together.” Troy obediently demonstrated them. “After that comes the pursuit drill, like so.” He performed the motions as he had earlier. “There you have the standard patterns. The infantry employs two fiendish upward slashes, which we’re far too merciful to adopt. Observe—three, four.”
source_text: “Very well. I’ll let you off the ones and threes. Next, cuts, points and guards altogether.” Troy duly exhibited them. “Then there’s pursuing practice, in this way.” He gave the movements as before. “There, those are the stereotyped forms. The infantry have two most diabolical upward cuts, which we are too humane to use. Like this—three, four.”

text: Oak felt a flicker of amusement, tinged perhaps with mild surprise, and after hanging the hat inside his hut, he returned to his flock. An hour slipped by, and the girl came back, now seated properly, a sack of bran balanced before her. As she approached the cattle-shed, a boy carrying a milking-pail met her and steadied the pony’s reins while she dismounted. The boy led the horse away, leaving the pail in the young woman’s care.
source_text: Oak was amused, perhaps a little astonished, and hanging up the hat in his hut, went again among his ewes. An hour passed, the girl returned, properly seated now, with a bag of bran in front of her. On nearing the cattle-shed she was met by a boy bringing a milking-pail, who held the reins of the pony whilst she slid off. The boy led away the horse, leaving the pail with the young woman.

text: The image of the woman composing the letter, accompanying the written words, lacked any distinct identity. She was a hazy figure, and rightly so, given that the real woman was at that instant fast asleep, utterly unaware of any affection or correspondence beneath the heavens. Each time Boldwood drifted off, she assumed a shape and almost stopped being a mere apparition; when he stirred awake, there lay the letter confirming the dream.
source_text: The vision of the woman writing, as a supplement to the words written, had no individuality. She was a misty shape, and well she might be, considering that her original was at that moment sound asleep and oblivious of all love and letter-writing under the sky. Whenever Boldwood dozed she took a form, and comparatively ceased to be a vision: when he awoke there was the letter justifying the dream.

text: From Pennyways's manner and movements, Troy—though he hadn't caught sight of what the former bailiff was scribbling—felt instantly certain the message was about him. He could conceive of no action to stop the revelation. "Damn my fate!" he muttered, then let out curses that stirred the darkness like a blight-laden breeze. All the while Boldwood spoke, lifting the note from her lap—
source_text: From the words and action of Pennyways, Troy, though he had not been able to see what the ex-bailiff wrote, had not a moment’s doubt that the note referred to him. Nothing that he could think of could be done to check the exposure. “Curse my luck!” he whispered, and added imprecations which rustled in the gloom like a pestilent wind. Meanwhile Boldwood said, taking up the note from her lap—

text: “Oh, she paid me little attention, as you can well imagine; but she appeared in fine form, so far as I could tell. She just cast her proud gaze over my wretched, ragged self, then let it drift beyond me to whatever lay yonder, much as though I’d been nothing but a bare winter tree. She’d just dismounted from her horse to watch the season’s final pressing of cider; she’d been out riding, so her complexion was heightened and her breathing a little quick, making her bosom rise and fall—rise and fall—plain to see each time. Aye, and there were the men around her twisting the press and bustling about, calling, ‘Mind the pomace, ma’am: it’ll stain your dress.’ ‘Don’t trouble about me,’ says she. Then Gabe fetched her some of the fresh cider, and she must needs sip it through a straw, and not in a natural fashion at all. ‘Liddy,’ says she, ‘bring a few gallons indoors, and I’ll make some cider-wine.’ Sergeant, I meant no more to her than a bit of rubbish in the woodshed!”
source_text: “Oh, she took no great heed of me, ye may well fancy; but she looked well enough, far’s I know. Just flashed her haughty eyes upon my poor scram body, and then let them go past me to what was yond, much as if I’d been no more than a leafless tree. She had just got off her mare to look at the last wring-down of cider for the year; she had been riding, and so her colours were up and her breath rather quick, so that her bosom plimmed and fell—plimmed and fell—every time plain to my eye. Ay, and there were the fellers round her wringing down the cheese and bustling about and saying, ‘Ware o’ the pommy, ma’am: ’twill spoil yer gown.’ ‘Never mind me,’ says she. Then Gabe brought her some of the new cider, and she must needs go drinking it through a strawmote, and not in a nateral way at all. ‘Liddy,’ says she, ‘bring indoors a few gallons, and I’ll make some cider-wine.’ Sergeant, I was no more to her than a morsel of scroff in the fuel-house!”

text: The spire of Thornfield Chapel rose as a stark, fifteenth-century square, its parapet adorned with a pair of stone gargoyles on every side. From these eight sculpted figures, merely two still fulfilled their original function—channeling rainwater from the inner lead roof. On each face, one spout had been sealed long ago by earlier sextons, deemed unnecessary, while two more lay shattered and clogged—a circumstance of little import to the tower’s health, since the twin mouths that yet gaped and streamed were ample to shoulder the entire burden.
source_text: The tower of Weatherbury Church was a square erection of fourteenth-century date, having two stone gurgoyles on each of the four faces of its parapet. Of these eight carved protuberances only two at this time continued to serve the purpose of their erection—that of spouting the water from the lead roof within. One mouth in each front had been closed by bygone church-wardens as superfluous, and two others were broken away and choked—a matter not of much consequence to the wellbeing of the tower, for the two mouths which still remained open and active were gaping enough to do all the work.

text: At that, the petite magistrate rose from the table and swept out of the hall, her black silk gown catching stray bits of straw and pulling them across the floor with a rasping whisper. Lifting her mood to match the moment out of a feeling of importance, Liddy drifted after Bathsheba with a softer stateliness that bordered on parody, and the door shut behind them.
source_text: Then this small thesmothete stepped from the table, and surged out of the hall, her black silk dress licking up a few straws and dragging them along with a scratching noise upon the floor. Liddy, elevating her feelings to the occasion from a sense of grandeur, floated off behind Bathsheba with a milder dignity not entirely free from travesty, and the door was closed.

text: “You might pass the bundled stalks to me, one after another, ma’am, if you’ve no dread of climbing the rungs in the gloom,” said Aldric. “Each instant matters now, and that would spare us a fair stretch of time. The darkness lifts a little once the lightning has flickered away.”
source_text: “You can bring up some reed-sheaves to me, one by one, ma’am; if you are not afraid to come up the ladder in the dark,” said Gabriel. “Every moment is precious now, and that would save a good deal of time. It is not very dark when the lightning has been gone a bit.”

text: Gabriel felt the sudden vacancy where Farmer Boldwood had sat at the table’s far end. He could not guess how long the man had been absent, but it seemed he had slipped away into the gathering gloom beyond. Even as Oak pondered this, Liddy entered with candles at the rear of the chamber, where the windows gave onto the shearing floor; their quick, dancing light streamed along the board and over the company, scattering the greenish shadows that had pooled behind them. Bathsheba’s figure, still seated as before, now stood out clearly between the watchers and the glow, and by that illumination Oak saw that Boldwood had come indoors and taken a place close beside her.
source_text: Gabriel suddenly missed Farmer Boldwood from his place at the bottom of the table. How long he had been gone Oak did not know; but he had apparently withdrawn into the encircling dusk. Whilst he was thinking of this, Liddy brought candles into the back part of the room overlooking the shearers, and their lively new flames shone down the table and over the men, and dispersed among the green shadows behind. Bathsheba’s form, still in its original position, was now again distinct between their eyes and the light, which revealed that Boldwood had gone inside the room, and was sitting near her.

text: Rowan paused at the edge of the stall, where a Romani woman was toasting flatcakes above a small blaze of twigs and vending them for a copper each, and peered across the crowd gathered inside. He caught no glimpse of Merrick, but soon spotted Seraphine through a gap leading to the cordoned-off section at the far end. Rowan then withdrew, circled the pavilion into the shadows, and strained his ears. He could hear Seraphine’s tones just beyond the canvas; she was speaking with a man. A flush crept over his cheeks: surely she was not so shameless as to dally at a carnival! He wondered whether she now counted his dying as a settled fact. To uncover the truth, Rowan drew a small blade from his coat and quietly incised two tiny crossing slits in the fabric, which, when the corners were folded back, yielded an opening no larger than a coin. He brought his face near this peephole, then jerked it away in a start; for his gaze had rested scarcely a foot from the crown of Seraphine’s head. It was far too close for comfort. He cut a second aperture slightly to one side and farther down, in a dim nook beside her seat, from which he could easily and safely observe her by looking straight ahead.
source_text: Troy stood at the entrance to the booth, where a gipsy-woman was frying pancakes over a little fire of sticks and selling them at a penny a-piece, and looked over the heads of the people within. He could see nothing of Pennyways, but he soon discerned Bathsheba through an opening into the reserved space at the further end. Troy thereupon retreated, went round the tent into the darkness, and listened. He could hear Bathsheba’s voice immediately inside the canvas; she was conversing with a man. A warmth overspread his face: surely she was not so unprincipled as to flirt in a fair! He wondered if, then, she reckoned upon his death as an absolute certainty. To get at the root of the matter, Troy took a penknife from his pocket and softly made two little cuts crosswise in the cloth, which, by folding back the corners left a hole the size of a wafer. Close to this he placed his face, withdrawing it again in a movement of surprise; for his eye had been within twelve inches of the top of Bathsheba’s head. It was too near to be convenient. He made another hole a little to one side and lower down, in a shaded place beside her chair, from which it was easy and safe to survey her by looking horizontally.

text: In the quiet solitude of late evening, seated by a modest fire and far steadier in spirit, Bathsheba lifted Troy’s watch from among the belongings that had been returned to her. She unlatched the case, just as he had done in her presence a week earlier. Inside lay the small twist of pale hair—the spark that had ignited so vast a conflagration.
source_text: When alone late that evening beside a small fire, and much calmed down, Bathsheba took Troy’s watch into her hand, which had been restored to her with the rest of the articles belonging to him. She opened the case as he had opened it before her a week ago. There was the little coil of pale hair which had been as the fuze to this great explosion.

text: A chance turn of events one day drew her into a strangely intimate conversation with Gabriel about her predicament. It brought her a measure of relief—though of a flat and comfortless sort. They were going over the accounts together, and something in the course of their work prompted Oak to remark, with reference to Boldwood, “He’ll never forget you, ma’am, never.”
source_text: One day she was led by an accident into an oddly confidential dialogue with Gabriel about her difficulty. It afforded her a little relief—of a dull and cheerless kind. They were auditing accounts, and something occurred in the course of their labours which led Oak to say, speaking of Boldwood, “He’ll never forget you, ma’am, never.”

text: There was nothing light or whimsical in his makeup, neither in virtue nor in vice. Resolute in the broad strokes of conduct, gentle in the finer points, he moved through life with unbroken gravity. He perceived no ridiculous edges to human foolishness, and so, while he was never quite congenial to the laughers and mockers—those for whom existence is a jest—he was not unbearable to the sincere or to those who had known sorrow. A man who took every scene of life’s drama in earnest, if he failed to charm when the play was comic, at least no flippant handling could be held against him when the final act turned tragic.
source_text: He had no light and careless touches in his constitution, either for good or for evil. Stern in the outlines of action, mild in the details, he was serious throughout all. He saw no absurd sides to the follies of life, and thus, though not quite companionable in the eyes of merry men and scoffers, and those to whom all things show life as a jest, he was not intolerable to the earnest and those acquainted with grief. Being a man who read all the dramas of life seriously, if he failed to please when they were comedies, there was no frivolous treatment to reproach him for when they chanced to end tragically.

text: The farmer’s vague image of her attractiveness, when measured against the actual figure she now cut, underwent not so much a downgrading as a shift in kind. His eye first seized upon her stature as a reference point. She looked tall, yet the bucket was small and the hedge low; allowing for the distortion these objects introduced, she likely stood no higher than the height most women would consider ideal. Her prominent features were sharp and symmetrical. Those who travel the counties with an eye for beauty may have noted that in English women a classically sculpted face rarely pairs with an equally classical body, the finely drawn features tending to overpower the frame; a graceful, eight-heads-high figure usually dissolves into irregular facial lines. Without draping a milkmaid in nymph-like gauze, let us simply say that here judgment paused as if intruding, and regarded her proportions with a prolonged sense of delight. From the shape of her upper body, she must have possessed a lovely neck and shoulders; but no one had glimpsed them since she was a child. Had she been dressed in a low-cut gown, she would have bolted and buried her head in a bush. Yet she was hardly a timid girl; it was only her instinct to place the boundary between the visible and the hidden higher than town dwellers do.
source_text: The adjustment of the farmer’s hazy conceptions of her charms to the portrait of herself she now presented him with was less a diminution than a difference. The starting-point selected by the judgment was her height. She seemed tall, but the pail was a small one, and the hedge diminutive; hence, making allowance for error by comparison with these, she could have been not above the height to be chosen by women as best. All features of consequence were severe and regular. It may have been observed by persons who go about the shires with eyes for beauty, that in Englishwoman a classically-formed face is seldom found to be united with a figure of the same pattern, the highly-finished features being generally too large for the remainder of the frame; that a graceful and proportionate figure of eight heads usually goes off into random facial curves. Without throwing a Nymphean tissue over a milkmaid, let it be said that here criticism checked itself as out of place, and looked at her proportions with a long consciousness of pleasure. From the contours of her figure in its upper part, she must have had a beautiful neck and shoulders; but since her infancy nobody had ever seen them. Had she been put into a low dress she would have run and thrust her head into a bush. Yet she was not a shy girl by any means; it was merely her instinct to draw the line dividing the seen from the unseen higher than they do it in towns.

text: “I thought as much. Now, your good opinion matters to me, just a little, and I need to make something clear—I’ve been aching to do this ever since I came back and you met me with that solemn gaze. Because if I were to die—and I might die soon—it would be unbearable for you to go on thinking wrongly of me. So now, listen.”
source_text: “I thought so. Now, I care a little for your good opinion, and I want to explain something—I have longed to do it ever since I returned, and you looked so gravely at me. For if I were to die—and I may die soon—it would be dreadful that you should always think mistakenly of me. Now, listen.”

text: Not a single drop had fallen yet. He mopped his tired forehead and gazed once more at the dark silhouettes of the exposed hayricks. Was his existence truly so precious to him? What future awaited him that he should be so reluctant to take a chance, when essential and pressing work could not proceed without it? He made up his mind to remain by the rick. Still, he took a safeguard. Beneath the staddle stones lay a long tethering chain, meant to keep stray horses from wandering off. He hauled it up the ladder, threaded his pole through the loop at one end, and let the rest of the chain hang down to the earth. The attached spike he hammered into the ground. Sheltered beneath this makeshift lightning rod, he felt a measure of safety.
source_text: Not a drop of rain had fallen as yet. He wiped his weary brow, and looked again at the black forms of the unprotected stacks. Was his life so valuable to him after all? What were his prospects that he should be so chary of running risk, when important and urgent labour could not be carried on without such risk? He resolved to stick to the stack. However, he took a precaution. Under the staddles was a long tethering chain, used to prevent the escape of errant horses. This he carried up the ladder, and sticking his rod through the clog at one end, allowed the other end of the chain to trail upon the ground. The spike attached to it he drove in. Under the shadow of this extemporized lightning-conductor he felt himself comparatively safe.

text: The timeless melody drew to a close, a deep D resonating from the bass-viol like the boom of distant artillery, and Gabriel hesitated no more. Steering clear of Bathsheba, he edged as close as he could to the stage, where Sergeant Troy had settled in, sipping brandy mixed with water while everyone else stuck to cider and ale. Unable to press near enough to address the sergeant directly, Gabriel dispatched a request, urging him to step down briefly. The sergeant replied that he was unavailable.
source_text: The immortal tune ended, a fine DD rolling forth from the bass-viol with the sonorousness of a cannonade, and Gabriel delayed his entry no longer. He avoided Bathsheba, and got as near as possible to the platform, where Sergeant Troy was now seated, drinking brandy-and-water, though the others drank without exception cider and ale. Gabriel could not easily thrust himself within speaking distance of the sergeant, and he sent a message, asking him to come down for a moment. The sergeant said he could not attend.

text: It would be uncharitable to claim that the freshness of her taking on such a role rivaled the allure of her features and grace. Still, the crowd’s fascination was widespread, and this Saturday’s first appearance in the marketplace—whatever it meant to Bathsheba as a trading farmer—was undeniably a victory for her as a young woman. In fact, the stir was so strong that several times she felt an impulse simply to move like a goddess among these field-hardened men, a small-scale deity’s kin, and to forget about final bids entirely.
source_text: It would be ungallant to suggest that the novelty of her engagement in such an occupation had almost as much to do with the magnetism as had the beauty of her face and movements. However, the interest was general, and this Saturday’s _début_ in the forum, whatever it may have been to Bathsheba as the buying and selling farmer, was unquestionably a triumph to her as the maiden. Indeed, the sensation was so pronounced that her instinct on two or three occasions was merely to walk as a queen among these gods of the fallow, like a little sister of a little Jove, and to neglect closing prices altogether.

text: “In all my years,” the gentleman went on, his voice dropping to a low, musing tone, his gaze resting with quiet appraisal on her inclined profile, “I have encountered countless ladies. Yet not one has possessed a loveliness to rival yours. Accept the words or scorn them—take umbrage or pleasure—it makes no difference to me.”
source_text: “I’ve seen a good many women in my time,” continued the young man in a murmur, and more thoughtfully than hitherto, critically regarding her bent head at the same time; “but I’ve never seen a woman so beautiful as you. Take it or leave it—be offended or like it—I don’t care.”

text: A restless fifteen minutes stretched between the courier’s exit and the renewed thud of hooves beyond the door. She dared not look out now; instead, bent above the ancient escritoire where she had penned her note, she shut her eyes tightly, as though to bar the entry of both dread and expectation.
source_text: It was an anxious quarter of an hour that intervened between the messenger’s departure and the sound of the horse’s tramp again outside. She could not watch this time, but, leaning over the old bureau at which she had written the letter, closed her eyes, as if to keep out both hope and fear.

text: When the woman came to, she discovered she was enveloped in the heart of a night devoid of moon and stars. A thick, seamless blanket of cloud spanned the heavens, erasing every glimmer of the firmament; and far off, a faint aureole suspended above Casterbridge stood out against the black vault, its radiance intensified by the stark opposition of the surrounding gloom. The woman directed her gaze toward that frail, gentle light.
source_text: When the woman awoke it was to find herself in the depths of a moonless and starless night. A heavy unbroken crust of cloud stretched across the sky, shutting out every speck of heaven; and a distant halo which hung over the town of Casterbridge was visible against the black concave, the luminosity appearing the brighter by its great contrast with the circumscribing darkness. Towards this weak, soft glow the woman turned her eyes.

text: This was the case Oak presented on the surface. Yet a person, even in his own eyes, is like a reused manuscript, bearing one visible text and another hidden beneath. Beneath the practical reasoning may have lain this golden truth: “I will aid, to the very limits of my strength, the woman I have so deeply loved.”
source_text: Such was the argument that Oak set outwardly before him. But man, even to himself, is a palimpsest, having an ostensible writing, and another beneath the lines. It is possible that there was this golden legend under the utilitarian one: “I will help to my last effort the woman I have loved so dearly.”

text: Billy Smallbury, who had earlier been riding in the wagon, had by now located a ladder. Mark Clark climbed it and steadied himself next to Oak on the roof’s edge. At this corner the smoke was thick and choking, so Clark—quick and agile—took a bucket passed up to him and doused Oak’s face, splashing water over his head and shoulders. Meanwhile, Gabriel, gripping a long beech branch in one hand and his shepherd’s crook in the other, went on beating the stack, knocking away every burning ember he could reach.
source_text: Billy Smallbury—one of the men who had been on the waggon—by this time had found a ladder, which Mark Clark ascended, holding on beside Oak upon the thatch. The smoke at this corner was stifling, and Clark, a nimble fellow, having been handed a bucket of water, bathed Oak’s face and sprinkled him generally, whilst Gabriel, now with a long beech-bough in one hand, in addition to his crook in the other, kept sweeping the stack and dislodging all fiery particles.

text: “It might be an unusual pact for a man and a woman to strike, but I can’t spot anything truly amiss in it,” Oak said, drawing out the words. “Indeed, the very factor that casts doubt on whether you should wed him at all—your indifference toward him, if I may presume—”
source_text: “Perhaps it would be an uncommon agreement for a man and woman to make: I don’t see anything really wrong about it,” said Oak, slowly. “In fact the very thing that makes it doubtful if you ought to marry en under any condition, that is, your not caring about him—for I may suppose—”

text: Here, Clym, who had drawn from the woman opposite a share of those same impulses, was bound to stir an answering chord in her heart—if not through reason, then by sentiment—however much she masked it for his sake. Her voice wavered a little. “Still, you could have been a man of means had you only held your course. Superintendent of that great diamond house—what more could anyone desire? Such a position of confidence and esteem! I daresay you will follow in your father’s steps; like him, you are growing tired of prospering.”
source_text: Now, Yeobright, having inherited some of these very instincts from the woman before him, could not fail to awaken a reciprocity in her through her feelings, if not by arguments, disguise it as she might for his good. She spoke with less assurance. “And yet you might have been a wealthy man if you had only persevered. Manager to that large diamond establishment—what better can a man wish for? What a post of trust and respect! I suppose you will be like your father; like him, you are getting weary of doing well.”

text: “Not once in three years have I gone,” said Edmund; “for of a Sunday I am so heavy with sleep; and the way there is such a weary distance; and even when you arrive, the hope of being chosen for the high places is so pitiably slender, while so many others are passed by, that I keep to my own hearth and stir not.”
source_text: “I ha’n’t been these three years,” said Humphrey; “for I’m so dead sleepy of a Sunday; and ’tis so terrible far to get there; and when you do get there ’tis such a mortal poor chance that you’ll be chose for up above, when so many bain’t, that I bide at home and don’t go at all.”

text: Mrs. Yeobright found herself suspended in doubt until, one morning, word reached her that her son’s wife had gone to visit her grandfather at Mistover. She resolved to climb the hill, confront Eustacia, and learn from her daughter-in-law’s own mouth whether the family guineas—treasured by Mrs. Yeobright as dearly as ancestral gems are prized by richer matrons—had gone astray.
source_text: Mrs. Yeobright was in this state of uncertainty when she was informed one morning that her son’s wife was visiting her grandfather at Mistover. She determined to walk up the hill, see Eustacia, and ascertain from her daughter-in-law’s lips whether the family guineas, which were to Mrs. Yeobright what family jewels are to wealthier dowagers, had miscarried or not.

text: And so they clashed; the duel ended with the Valiant Soldier felled by Eustacia’s thrust, which was so oddly feeble it seemed uncanny, while Jim, burning with devotion to authentic theatrical craft, toppled like a felled tree onto the stone floor with a force that wrenched his shoulder from its socket. Next, the Turkish Knight delivered a few more lines, spoken rather too weakly, along with boasts that he would take on Saint George and his entire band, whereupon Saint George himself strode in magnificently with that celebrated flourish—
source_text: And fight they did; the issue of the combat being that the Valiant Soldier was slain by a preternaturally inadequate thrust from Eustacia, Jim, in his ardour for genuine histrionic art, coming down like a log upon the stone floor with force enough to dislocate his shoulder. Then, after more words from the Turkish Knight, rather too faintly delivered, and statements that he’d fight Saint George and all his crew, Saint George himself magnificently entered with the well-known flourish—

text: He made his way next to the carriage-house and stalls, wanting to be certain the mare, the trap, and all the tack were ready for a lengthy journey. A full thirty minutes passed in this inspection, and when he came back into the house Wildeve never imagined Thomasin would be anywhere save her bedroom. He had instructed the groom not to remain awake, giving the lad the impression they would set out at three or four in the morning—an odd hour, to be sure, yet far less peculiar than midnight, which was the hour truly fixed, for the Budmouth vessel sailed between one and two.
source_text: He then went to the stable and coach-house to assure himself that the horse, gig, and harness were in a fit condition for a long drive. Nearly half an hour was spent thus, and on returning to the house Wildeve had no thought of Thomasin being anywhere but in bed. He had told the stable lad not to stay up, leading the boy to understand that his departure would be at three or four in the morning; for this, though an exceptional hour, was less strange than midnight, the time actually agreed on, the packet from Budmouth sailing between one and two.

text: “Why, before I took up soldiering with the Bang-up Locals—that’s what they called us—back in ’04,” Grandfer Cantle broke in, his voice bright with pride, “I had no better notion of the wide world than the simplest fellow standing here. And now, dash it all, I won’t go naming what I’m not fit for, hey?”
source_text: “Why, afore I went a soldier in the Bang-up Locals (as we was called), in the year four,” chimed in Grandfer Cantle brightly, “I didn’t know no more what the world was like than the commonest man among ye. And now, jown it all, I won’t say what I bain’t fit for, hey?”

text: His notion of dedicating himself to a teaching career, along with his quiet hopes, had stirred something in Mrs. Yeobright. And how could it not, when he was an extension of her very being—when their conversations felt like a dialogue between the two hands of a single body? He had long given up trying to persuade her through reason, and it struck him almost like a revelation that he could move her instead by a silent pull, a force that lay as far beyond speech as speech lies beyond mere shouts.
source_text: His theory and his wishes about devoting his future to teaching had made an impression on Mrs. Yeobright. Indeed, how could it be otherwise when he was a part of her—when their discourses were as if carried on between the right and the left hands of the same body? He had despaired of reaching her by argument; and it was almost as a discovery to him that he could reach her by a magnetism which was as superior to words as words are to yells.

text: “But you mustn’t surrender yourself to her out of some rigid notion of fairness,” Eustacia shot back. “If your heart isn’t hers, the kindest course, in the end, is to let her remain as she is. That is always the truer path. There—now I’ve spoken in a way unbecoming a woman, I imagine. After you’ve gone, I’m forever vexed with myself for the words I’ve given you.”
source_text: “But you must not sacrifice yourself to her from any sense of justice,” replied Eustacia quickly. “If you do not love her it is the most merciful thing in the long run to leave her as she is. That’s always the best way. There, now I have been unwomanly, I suppose. When you have left me I am always angry with myself for things that I have said to you.”

text: Close at hand, as in scattered spots across the moor, lay patches of upturned sods, their grassy faces pressed to the earth and their ragged roots thrust skyward, waiting for Abel Marchbanks to gather them before the frosts set in. He seized two of these where he crouched, and drew them over his form until one mantled his head and shoulders, the other his spine and thighs. Even in full light the ochre-treader would have vanished utterly; the sods, perched atop him with the heath turned outward, seemed no more than living tufts of the moor. He edged forward again, and the sods upon his back edged with him. Had he advanced unshrouded, the dusk might still have cloaked him; advancing so, he seemed to tunnel through the very ground. In this fashion he drew quite near to where the pair lingered.
source_text: Near him, as in divers places about the heath, were areas strewn with large turves, which lay edgeways and upside down awaiting removal by Timothy Fairway, previous to the winter weather. He took two of these as he lay, and dragged them over him till one covered his head and shoulders, the other his back and legs. The reddleman would now have been quite invisible, even by daylight; the turves, standing upon him with the heather upwards, looked precisely as if they were growing. He crept along again, and the turves upon his back crept with him. Had he approached without any covering the chances are that he would not have been perceived in the dusk; approaching thus, it was as though he burrowed underground. In this manner he came quite close to where the two were standing.

text: “Once you’ve picked the last of the apples,” her aunt called, climbing down the ladder, “come on down, and we’ll set out for holly. The heath’s deserted this afternoon, so you won’t have to worry about prying eyes. We need to gather berries, or Clym will never think we’ve made a proper start.”
source_text: “As soon as you have finished getting the apples,” her aunt said, descending the ladder, “come down, and we’ll go for the holly. There is nobody on the heath this afternoon, and you need not fear being stared at. We must get some berries, or Clym will never believe in our preparations.”

text: “I don’t understand your meaning. Am I to blame for what you’ve done?” Eustacia swayed toward him with a quiver. “What—you dare to weep and stretch out your hand to me? Dear God! Is that possible? No, I won’t have it. I refuse to make the mistake of accepting it.” The hand she had extended fell limp, though the tears kept streaming. “Ah, very well, I’ll take it—if only for the sake of my own foolish kisses, wasted on it before I understood what I was holding. How utterly enchanted I was! What good could there be in a woman everyone spoke ill of?”
source_text: “I don’t know what you mean by that. Am I the cause of your sin?” (Eustacia made a trembling motion towards him.) “What, you can begin to shed tears and offer me your hand? Good God! can you? No, not I. I’ll not commit the fault of taking that.” (The hand she had offered dropped nervelessly, but the tears continued flowing.) “Well, yes, I’ll take it, if only for the sake of my own foolish kisses that were wasted there before I knew what I cherished. How bewitched I was! How could there be any good in a woman that everybody spoke ill of?”

text: At moments during the performance, Eustacia felt a tremor of uncertainty about the safety of her position, yet the experience held a fearful delight. The cascade of attentions directed at her—though in truth aimed at some imagined figure—came from the first man she had ever been disposed to adore, and it tangled her feelings beyond words. She had loved him partly because he stood apart in this setting, partly because she had resolved to love him, but chiefly because, having wearied of Wildeve, she was desperately in need of someone to love. Convinced she must love him almost against her will, she had been swayed in the manner of the second Lord Lyttleton and others, who dreamed they would die on a certain day and, through the force of morbid imagination, actually brought that fate to pass. Once a young woman admits the possibility of falling in love with someone at a given hour and place, the deed is all but accomplished.
source_text: At moments during this performance Eustacia was half in doubt about the security of her position; yet it had a fearful joy. A series of attentions paid to her, and yet not to her but to some imaginary person, by the first man she had ever been inclined to adore, complicated her emotions indescribably. She had loved him partly because he was exceptional in this scene, partly because she had determined to love him, chiefly because she was in desperate need of loving somebody after wearying of Wildeve. Believing that she must love him in spite of herself, she had been influenced after the fashion of the second Lord Lyttleton and other persons, who have dreamed that they were to die on a certain day, and by stress of a morbid imagination have actually brought about that event. Once let a maiden admit the possibility of her being stricken with love for someone at a certain hour and place, and the thing is as good as done.

text: He would occasionally reflect that fortune had dealt him a cruel hand, venturing so far as to declare that birth itself was a stark predicament, and that rather than striving to ascend through life with renown, men ought to plot how to slip from it without disgrace. Yet the conviction that he and his kin had been scornfully and ruthlessly pierced through the spirit with such branding irons did not long endure in him. So it commonly goes, save among the most iron-willed. Mankind, in its magnanimous effort to frame a theory that will not dishonour a Prime Mover, has ever shrunk from imagining a sovereign force of lesser moral stature than its own; and even while sinking down to lament beside the rivers of Babylon, it conjures pardons for the tyranny that draws forth its weeping.
source_text: He did sometimes think he had been ill-used by fortune, so far as to say that to be born is a palpable dilemma, and that instead of men aiming to advance in life with glory they should calculate how to retreat out of it without shame. But that he and his had been sarcastically and pitilessly handled in having such irons thrust into their souls he did not maintain long. It is usually so, except with the sternest of men. Human beings, in their generous endeavour to construct a hypothesis that shall not degrade a First Cause, have always hesitated to conceive a dominant power of lower moral quality than their own; and, even while they sit down and weep by the waters of Babylon, invent excuses for the oppression which prompts their tears.

text: Having spotted Eustacia’s signal from the hill at eight, Wildeve set about at once to aid her escape and, if all went as he wished, to go with her. He was visibly unsettled, and the way he told Thomasin he meant to travel was enough in itself to stir her mistrust. Once she had retired, he gathered the few things he would need and climbed to the money-chest, from which he drew a fairly generous bundle of notes—funds advanced against the property soon to be his—to cover the costs of the move.
source_text: Having seen Eustacia’s signal from the hill at eight o’clock, Wildeve immediately prepared to assist her in her flight, and, as he hoped, accompany her. He was somewhat perturbed, and his manner of informing Thomasin that he was going on a journey was in itself sufficient to rouse her suspicions. When she had gone to bed he collected the few articles he would require, and went upstairs to the money-chest, whence he took a tolerably bountiful sum in notes, which had been advanced to him on the property he was so soon to have in possession, to defray expenses incidental to the removal.

text: Instead of taking that route straightaway, he turned from the rise on his right and lurched along a cart-track gouged with wheel-ruts, arriving at a dwelling that—like every other home scattered across the moor at that hour—was betrayed only by a dim glow seeping from an upper casement. This was the house of Tolly Marden, the broomwright, and he went in.
source_text: However, he did not proceed that way just then; but leaving the hill to the left of him, he stumbled over a rutted road that brought him to a cottage which, like all other habitations on the heath at this hour, was only saved from being visible by a faint shine from its bedroom window. This house was the home of Olly Dowden, the besom-maker, and he entered.

text: What escaped her was a long, drawn-out sigh, as though prompted by some thought that had brought her to this place. The sound came with a sudden, unguarded release, as if her mind had permitted what it could no longer control. One thing was clear from this: she had been living in a state of restraint, not of weariness or stillness.
source_text: What she uttered was a lengthened sighing, apparently at something in her mind which had led to her presence here. There was a spasmodic abandonment about it as if, in allowing herself to utter the sound the woman’s brain had authorized what it could not regulate. One point was evident in this; that she had been existing in a suppressed state, and not in one of languor, or stagnation.

text: She lingered on the sofa a while longer, then roused herself and climbed the staircase. The chamber where she had once rested was preserved almost exactly as she had abandoned it, and the memory it thrust upon her—of her own profoundly altered and immeasurably darkened condition—restored to her features that vague, shapeless anguish they had borne when she first came. She glanced into her grandfather’s bedroom, through which the crisp autumn breeze streamed from the lifted sash. Her gaze was caught by a sight entirely ordinary in itself, yet one that now struck her with fresh meaning.
source_text: She remained on the settee some time longer, when she aroused herself and went upstairs. The room in which she had formerly slept still remained much as she had left it, and the recollection that this forced upon her of her own greatly changed and infinitely worsened situation again set on her face the undetermined and formless misery which it had worn on her first arrival. She peeped into her grandfather’s room, through which the fresh autumn air was blowing from the open window. Her eye was arrested by what was a familiar sight enough, though it broke upon her now with a new significance.

text: He eased the trembling young woman onto a chair, stepped back into the hallway, and swung the door wide. There in the corridor, directly before him, stood Old Man Toller, his voice blending with the singers still gathered outside the cottage. He drifted inside and gave a vague bob of the head toward Harland, his mouth still agape, his face painfully taut as he poured out the final refrain. When it finished, he declared with warmth, “A joyful greeting to the newly wed, and heaven’s favor upon them!”
source_text: He pressed the agitated girl into a seat, returned to the outer room and opened the door. Immediately outside, in the passage, appeared Grandfer Cantle singing in concert with those still standing in front of the house. He came into the room and nodded abstractedly to Wildeve, his lips still parted, and his features excruciatingly strained in the emission of the chorus. This being ended, he said heartily, “Here’s welcome to the new-made couple, and God bless ’em!”

text: “I merely acted as justice demanded, given the situation,” Mrs. Yeobright replied, her voice gentler now. “I would have preferred not to raise this matter just yet, but you leave me no choice. I feel no shame in speaking the plain truth. I was deeply certain he should not make you his wife—so I did everything within my power to turn him aside. Yet it is settled now, and I have no thought of protesting further. I am prepared to welcome you.”
source_text: “I only did what was fair under the circumstances,” said Mrs. Yeobright more softly. “I would rather not have gone into this question at present, but you compel me. I am not ashamed to tell you the honest truth. I was firmly convinced that he ought not to marry you—therefore I tried to dissuade him by all the means in my power. But it is done now, and I have no idea of complaining any more. I am ready to welcome you.”

text: Just as Wildeve emerged onto the open road, Venn arrived at a spot a hundred paces distant; he too caught the sound of approaching wheels and paused for the vehicle to draw near. Recognizing its occupant, his face fell. After a brief hesitation, while the carriage trundled onward, he crossed the lane and struck a path through the gorse and heather, making for a curve where the highroad twisted uphill. Once more he stood ahead of the carriage, which soon approached at a slow walk. Venn moved into view and revealed himself.
source_text: About the same moment that Wildeve stepped into the highway Venn also had reached it at a point a hundred yards further on; and he, hearing the same wheels, likewise waited till the carriage should come up. When he saw who sat therein he seemed to be disappointed. Reflecting a minute or two, during which interval the carriage rolled on, he crossed the road, and took a short cut through the furze and heath to a point where the turnpike road bent round in ascending a hill. He was now again in front of the carriage, which presently came up at a walking pace. Venn stepped forward and showed himself.

text: “He’s exhausted. He left the house at half past four this morning and hasn’t stopped working since. Cutting furze is the only task that spares his failing eyes.” Standing beside the sleeping man, Eustacia felt the painful contrast with Wildeve, who stood before her in a crisp new summer suit and pale hat. She went on: “You can’t imagine how different he looked when we first met, though it was hardly any time ago. His hands used to be as white and soft as my own; now see them—how rough and brown they’ve become. His skin is naturally fair, but that weathered cast he has now, blending into the colour of his leather clothes, comes from the sun’s scorching.”
source_text: “He is very weary. He went out at half-past four this morning, and has been working ever since. He cuts furze because it is the only thing he can do that does not put any strain upon his poor eyes.” The contrast between the sleeper’s appearance and Wildeve’s at this moment was painfully apparent to Eustacia, Wildeve being elegantly dressed in a new summer suit and light hat; and she continued: “Ah! you don’t know how differently he appeared when I first met him, though it is such a little while ago. His hands were as white and soft as mine; and look at them now, how rough and brown they are! His complexion is by nature fair, and that rusty look he has now, all of a colour with his leather clothes, is caused by the burning of the sun.”

text: The room was set up for dancing, the heavy oak table pushed back to serve as a barricade in front of the fireplace. Clustered at either end, behind it, and in the chimney nook were the guests, many flushed and breathless, among whom Eustacia fleetingly noted a few prosperous figures from beyond the heath. Thomasin, as she had anticipated, was nowhere to be seen, and Eustacia remembered the glow of a lamp from an upstairs window when they had been outside—likely the window of Thomasin’s room. A nose, chin, hands, knees, and toes jutted from the seat inside the chimney recess, features she realized belonged to Grandfer Cantle, Mrs. Yeobright’s occasional garden helper and thus one of the invited. Smoke rose from a mound of peat smoldering before him, curled around the notches of the chimney hook, brushed against the salt-box, and vanished among the hanging bacon.
source_text: The room had been arranged with a view to the dancing, the large oak table having been moved back till it stood as a breastwork to the fireplace. At each end, behind, and in the chimney-corner were grouped the guests, many of them being warm-faced and panting, among whom Eustacia cursorily recognized some well-to-do persons from beyond the heath. Thomasin, as she had expected, was not visible, and Eustacia recollected that a light had shone from an upper window when they were outside—the window, probably, of Thomasin’s room. A nose, chin, hands, knees, and toes projected from the seat within the chimney opening, which members she found to unite in the person of Grandfer Cantle, Mrs. Yeobright’s occasional assistant in the garden, and therefore one of the invited. The smoke went up from an Etna of peat in front of him, played round the notches of the chimney-crook, struck against the salt-box, and got lost among the flitches.

text: Mrs. Yeobright’s diplomatic efforts bore unexpected fruit—remarkable in its own way, though not at all what she had foreseen. Wildeve had certainly been affected, but Eustacia felt the impact far more keenly. In her eyes, the man she loved was no longer a thrilling prize contested by other women, one she could keep only through constant rivalry. He had become an excess.
source_text: The fruit of Mrs. Yeobright’s diplomacy was indeed remarkable, though not as yet of the kind she had anticipated. It had appreciably influenced Wildeve, but it was influencing Eustacia far more. Her lover was no longer to her an exciting man whom many women strove for, and herself could only retain by striving with them. He was a superfluity.

text: Her attention was soon seized by another corner of the room. Opposite the chimney stood the high-backed settle, that indispensable companion to a hearth so wide and open that only a stiff wind can draw the smoke upward. It is to the fireplaces of old, cavernous rooms what a sheltering belt of trees is to an exposed estate, or a north wall to a garden. Beyond the settle, candles flicker, strands of hair lift and stir, young women tremble, and old men catch cold. Within it lies Paradise. Not the faintest whisper of a draught troubles the air; the sitters’ backs bask as warmly as their faces, and the snug heat coaxes songs and old stories from them as gently as a frame coaxes melons from the vine.
source_text: Another part of the room soon riveted her gaze. At the other side of the chimney stood the settle, which is the necessary supplement to a fire so open that nothing less than a strong breeze will carry up the smoke. It is, to the hearths of old-fashioned cavernous fireplaces, what the east belt of trees is to the exposed country estate, or the north wall to the garden. Outside the settle candles gutter, locks of hair wave, young women shiver, and old men sneeze. Inside is Paradise. Not a symptom of a draught disturbs the air; the sitters’ backs are as warm as their faces, and songs and old tales are drawn from the occupants by the comfortable heat, like fruit from melon plants in a frame.

text: Eustacia was quiet for a moment, then spoke. “There’s something odd between us now. You’re splitting hairs with extraordinary precision. What you’re saying, Damon, is that you still care for me. That saddens me, because my marriage hasn’t brought me such complete joy that I’m ready to cast you off for admitting it, as I should. But we’ve already spoken too long on this. Do you intend to stay until my husband stirs?”
source_text: Eustacia remained silent for a minute, and she said, “We are in a strange relationship today. You mince matters to an uncommon nicety. You mean, Damon, that you still love me. Well, that gives me sorrow, for I am not made so entirely happy by my marriage that I am willing to spurn you for the information, as I ought to do. But we have said too much about this. Do you mean to wait until my husband is awake?”

text: In my wanderings, I pass close by Budmouth. Budmouth is a marvellous spot—truly marvellous—a vast salt-glittering sea curving inland like a drawn bow—crowds of elegant gentlefolk strolling to and fro—strains of music drifting through the air—naval officers and army officers mingling with the throng—and of every ten souls you encounter, nine are in love.
source_text: “And in my travels I go near Budmouth. Now Budmouth is a wonderful place—wonderful—a great salt sheening sea bending into the land like a bow—thousands of gentlepeople walking up and down—bands of music playing—officers by sea and officers by land walking among the rest—out of every ten folks you meet nine of ’em in love.”

text: We’ve all heard the saying about what comes of a woman making up her mind; and the proverb doesn’t always end with the woman, especially if there’s a particular one involved, and a lovely one at that. Wildeve lingered, then lingered some more, exhaling in confusion, until at last he muttered to himself with a kind of surrender, “Right—by God, I’d better go to her, I expect!”
source_text: We have been told what happens when a woman deliberates; and the epigram is not always terminable with woman, provided that one be in the case, and that a fair one. Wildeve stood, and stood longer, and breathed perplexedly, and then said to himself with resignation, “Yes—by Heaven, I must go to her, I suppose!”

text: “Forgive me for cutting short our conversation,” said Mrs. Yeobright, “but I really must go. I was walking along the Anglebury Road, on my way to my niece’s new house—she’s coming home tonight with her husband—and when I saw the bonfire and caught Olly’s voice among the others, I climbed up to see what was happening. I’d be glad if she would walk with me, since we’re headed the same way.”
source_text: “I am sorry to stop the talk,” said Mrs. Yeobright. “But I must be leaving you now.
