I was passing down the Anglebury Road, towards my niece’s new home, who is returning tonight with her husband; and seeing the bonfire and hearing Olly’s voice among the rest I came up here to learn what was going on. I should like her to walk with me, as her way is mine.”

text: Fidelity for its own sake held little charm for her—less than for most women—but fidelity born from love’s fierce hold meant everything. A sudden blaze of passion, even if it ended in darkness, was preferable to a faint, enduring glimmer that lasted through the years. On this point, she understood by foresight what most women only grasp through living: she had circled love in her mind, surveyed its towers, examined its palaces, and judged it a sorrowful delight. Still, she craved it, as someone lost in a desert would welcome even brackish water.
source_text: Fidelity in love for fidelity’s sake had less attraction for her than for most women; fidelity because of love’s grip had much. A blaze of love, and extinction, was better than a lantern glimmer of the same which should last long years. On this head she knew by prevision what most women learn only by experience—she had mentally walked round love, told the towers thereof, considered its palaces, and concluded that love was but a doleful joy. Yet she desired it, as one in a desert would be thankful for brackish water.

text: The reddleman approached just as they rounded the bend at a leisurely pace. “Forgive me for halting you, Mrs. Wildeve,” he said. “But I’ve something to deliver to you in confidence from Mrs. Yeobright.” He passed her a little bundle—the hundred guineas he had lately gained, carelessly wrapped in a scrap of paper.
source_text: The reddleman came up as they slowly turned the corner. “I beg pardon for stopping you, Mrs. Wildeve,” he said. “But I have something to give you privately from Mrs. Yeobright.” He handed a small parcel; it consisted of the hundred guineas he had just won, roughly twisted up in a piece of paper.

text: “I grant you, it’s not promising,” Fairway conceded. “‘Out of my sight, you loose-limbed, weedy-faced, good-for-nothing dolt’—that’s a harsh fashion of refusing a man. Still, even that could be worn down with time and forbearance, until a silver thread or two shows in the minx’s hair. How old are you, Christian?”
source_text: “Not encouraging, I own,” said Fairway. “‘Get out of my sight, you slack-twisted, slim-looking maphrotight fool,’ is rather a hard way of saying No. But even that might be overcome by time and patience, so as to let a few grey hairs show themselves in the hussy’s head. How old be you, Christian?”

text: Her outlook on life was, in part, the inevitable offspring of her circumstances acting upon her character. To inhabit a heath without deciphering its language was akin to marrying a stranger without ever learning his speech. The heath’s delicate loveliness escaped Eustacia entirely; she perceived only its mists. A setting that might have shaped a contented soul into a poet, a sorrowful one into a mystic, a devout one into a hymnist, or even a frivolous one into a thinker, instead turned a defiant woman sullen.
source_text: Such views of life were to some extent the natural begettings of her situation upon her nature. To dwell on a heath without studying its meanings was like wedding a foreigner without learning his tongue. The subtle beauties of the heath were lost to Eustacia; she only caught its vapours. An environment which would have made a contented woman a poet, a suffering woman a devotee, a pious woman a psalmist, even a giddy woman thoughtful, made a rebellious woman saturnine.

text: “I admitted it. The landscape holds a certain loveliness, I grant you; yet to my soul it is a prison. The gentleman you speak of offers no reprieve from that sensation, though he resides nearby. Had a worthier soul been within reach, I would have felt no regard for him at all.”
source_text: “I did say so. There is a sort of beauty in the scenery, I know; but it is a jail to me. The man you mention does not save me from that feeling, though he lives here. I should have cared nothing for him had there been a better person near.”

text: Amid those mournful November gusts, the sound echoed the frayed remnants of a voice that might linger in the throat of a ninety-year-old. It was a threadbare murmur, brittle and hushed, and it grazed the ear with such clarity that those familiar with it could trace its source in the smallest physical details, as though by fingertips. It sprang from the collective work of countless microscopic agents of the wood, none of which were stalks, foliage, berries, grasses, thorns, lichen, or moss.
source_text: Throughout the blowing of these plaintive November winds that note bore a great resemblance to the ruins of human song which remain to the throat of fourscore and ten. It was a worn whisper, dry and papery, and it brushed so distinctly across the ear that, by the accustomed, the material minutiæ in which it originated could be realized as by touch. It was the united products of infinitesimal vegetable causes, and these were neither stems, leaves, fruit, blades, prickles, lichen, nor moss.

text: The painting unsettled the child. Beside a tiny stove within the wagon crouched a shape crimson from crown to sole—the fellow who had once been Thomasin’s companion. He was mending a stocking, its hue as red as his own form. And as he stitched, he puffed a pipe whose stem and bowl were likewise red.
source_text: The picture alarmed the boy. By a little stove inside the van sat a figure red from head to heels—the man who had been Thomasin’s friend. He was darning a stocking, which was red like the rest of him. Moreover, as he darned he smoked a pipe, the stem and bowl of which were red also.

text: Candle in hand, she stepped into a back room where, amid other household items, stood two broad brown basins holding close to a hundred pounds of liquid honey, the bees’ yield from the summer just past. On a shelf above them rested a firm, yellow hemisphere of beeswax drawn from that same harvest. Susan lifted the block down, sliced off several thin pieces, and piled them into an iron ladle. Carrying it back to the living-room, she set the ladle among the hot embers of the hearth. Once the wax softened to the pliancy of dough, she worked the fragments together. Her expression now sharpened with concentration. She began to shape the wax, and the way her hands moved made it clear she was striving to model a form she already held in mind. The form was a human one.
source_text: She passed with her candle into an inner room, where, among other utensils, were two large brown pans, containing together perhaps a hundredweight of liquid honey, the produce of the bees during the foregoing summer. On a shelf over the pans was a smooth and solid yellow mass of a hemispherical form, consisting of beeswax from the same take of honey. Susan took down the lump, and cutting off several thin slices, heaped them in an iron ladle, with which she returned to the living-room, and placed the vessel in the hot ashes of the fireplace. As soon as the wax had softened to the plasticity of dough she kneaded the pieces together. And now her face became more intent. She began moulding the wax; and it was evident from her manner of manipulation that she was endeavouring to give it some preconceived form. The form was human.

text: The unforeseen absence of the sole local acquaintance Eustacia possessed dealt a sharp blow to her hopes for an afternoon of carefree revelry. To mingle now seemed an awkward undertaking, even though, if she stepped forward, kindly women would press cups of tea upon her and fuss over her as an elegant outsider, far more refined and knowing than themselves. After observing the gathering through the patterns of two dances, she resolved to stroll a little farther, to a cottage where she might find some refreshment, and then make her way homeward in the cool, shadowed hour of evening.
source_text: This unexpected absence of the only local resident whom Eustacia knew considerably damaged her scheme for an afternoon of reckless gaiety. Joining in became a matter of difficulty, notwithstanding that, were she to advance, cheerful dames would come forward with cups of tea and make much of her as a stranger of superior grace and knowledge to themselves. Having watched the company through the figures of two dances, she decided to walk a little further, to a cottage where she might get some refreshment, and then return homeward in the shady time of evening.

text: A heaviness descended on Clym, sudden as a plummet. Earlier that day, when Eustacia had planned her visit to the old captain, Clym had asked her to ride over to Blooms-End and look in on his mother, or to find whatever path she deemed best toward mending their estrangement. She had departed with a light step, and his hopes had risen.
source_text: A weight fell like a stone upon Clym. That same morning, when Eustacia had arranged to go and see her grandfather, Clym had expressed a wish that she would drive down to Blooms-End and inquire for her mother-in-law, or adopt any other means she might think fit to bring about a reconciliation. She had set out gaily; and he had hoped for much.

V. The Crossing of the Moor
VI. An Encounter, and What Befell the Wayfarer
VII. The Sorrowful Reunion of a Pair Long Parted
VIII. Eustacia Learns of Blessings, and Gazes upon Ruin
source_text: BOOK FOURTH—THE CLOSED DOOR I. The Rencounter by the Pool II. He Is Set upon by Adversities but He Sings a Song III. She Goes Out to Battle against Depression IV. Rough Coercion Is Employed V. The Journey across the Heath VI. A Conjuncture, and Its Result upon the Pedestrian VII. The Tragic Meeting of Two Old Friends VIII. Eustacia Hears of Good Fortune, and Beholds Evil

text: “Then why would you ever suggest I take a husband from the town? Whatever you might argue, I’m certain that if I marry anyone, it has to be Diggory. He’s shown me more kindness than anyone, and he’s helped me in countless ways I can’t even begin to name!” Thomasin’s voice now carried a near pout.
source_text: “Then how could you say that I should marry some town man? I am sure, say what you will, that I must marry Diggory, if I marry at all. He has been kinder to me than anybody else, and has helped me in many ways that I don’t know of!” Thomasin almost pouted now.

text: Before turning away, he cast a lingering look at her face, as though uncertain whether leaving her like this was truly kind. He studied her features with a puzzled, distant curiosity, like someone poring over an ancient manuscript whose symbols remain a mystery. He was not so young that he failed to sense some call for compassion, yet not old enough to shed the childhood dread of witnessing pain in adults he had always thought invulnerable. Whether she was more likely to cause trouble or to endure it, whether her sorrow deserved pity or alarm, he simply could not tell. He dropped his gaze and walked on in silence. Before he had covered half a mile, he had forgotten her entirely, save for the fact that she was a woman who had paused to rest.
source_text: Before quite leaving her he threw upon her face a wistful glance, as if he had misgivings on the generosity of forsaking her thus. He gazed into her face in a vague, wondering manner, like that of one examining some strange old manuscript the key to whose characters is undiscoverable. He was not so young as to be absolutely without a sense that sympathy was demanded, he was not old enough to be free from the terror felt in childhood at beholding misery in adult quarters hitherto deemed impregnable; and whether she were in a position to cause trouble or to suffer from it, whether she and her affliction were something to pity or something to fear, it was beyond him to decide. He lowered his eyes and went on without another word. Before he had gone half a mile he had forgotten all about her, except that she was a woman who had sat down to rest.

text: Your face haunts every corner of my dreams, and your voice echoes in every murmur. I wish it didn’t. What I feel overwhelms me. People say a love like this can’t endure. But it must! And yet I recall, once, watching a Hussar officer ride through the streets of Budmouth—a complete stranger who never even glanced my way—and I loved him so fiercely I thought I would perish from it. But I didn’t perish, and in time I stopped caring. How dreadful it would be if a day ever came when I could no longer love you, my Clym!
source_text: “I see your face in every scene of my dreams, and hear your voice in every sound. I wish I did not. It is too much what I feel. They say such love never lasts. But it must! And yet once, I remember, I saw an officer of the Hussars ride down the street at Budmouth, and though he was a total stranger and never spoke to me, I loved him till I thought I should really die of love—but I didn’t die, and at last I left off caring for him. How terrible it would be if a time should come when I could not love you, my Clym!”

text: A call upon the lad came to mind as a way to harvest fresh details, however meager the yield might prove. To search a youngster’s memory after six weeks had slipped by—not for impressions he had witnessed and grasped, but for matters inherently above his comprehension—offered slender hope; still, when every open path lies sealed, one reaches toward the slight and hidden. No other course remained; beyond that, he would let the riddle sink into the gulf of the unknowable.
source_text: A visit to the boy suggested itself as a means of gleaning new particulars; though it might be quite unproductive. To probe a child’s mind after the lapse of six weeks, not for facts which the child had seen and understood, but to get at those which were in their nature beyond him, did not promise much; yet when every obvious channel is blocked we grope towards the small and obscure. There was nothing else left to do; after that he would allow the enigma to drop into the abyss of undiscoverable things.

text: So, each for their own cause, felt the night’s revel not as a glad release but as a gale that swept them off their feet. The music had descended upon their fragile restraint like a sudden onslaught, flinging them back toward familiar transgressions now grown doubly wayward. They whirled through three reels without pause; then, worn by the ceaseless turning, Clymene withdrew from the ring she had already graced too long. Marek guided her to a low turf bank a little apart, where she sank down while he remained standing at her shoulder. Since his first whispered greeting when the figures began, no further word had passed between them.
source_text: Thus, for different reasons, what was to the rest an exhilarating movement was to these two a riding upon the whirlwind. The dance had come like an irresistible attack upon whatever sense of social order there was in their minds, to drive them back into old paths which were now doubly irregular. Through three dances in succession they spun their way; and then, fatigued with the incessant motion, Eustacia turned to quit the circle in which she had already remained too long. Wildeve led her to a grassy mound a few yards distant, where she sat down, her partner standing beside her. From the time that he addressed her at the beginning of the dance till now they had not exchanged a word.

text: Before venturing forth again, the girl lingered motionless upon the earthen rampart, her ears straining against the silence. The spot was every bit as desolate as Rainbarrow, though sunken lower into the land, and it found greater refuge from the lash of the weather thanks to a sparse stand of firs huddled to the north. The bulwark that girded the cottage, sealing it from the unruly wilderness beyond, was raised from dense square-cut turves carved out of the outer trench, then stacked with a subtle backward lean—a modest slant that becomes no trifling fortification where hedgerows refuse to root amid the wind-scoured waste and where stone for walls lies beyond reach. In all other respects the prospect lay bare, sweeping the entire stretch of the vale that ran down to the stream behind Wildeve’s dwelling. High to the right, and looming far nearer than the Quiet Woman Inn, the smudged outline of Rainbarrow broke against the heavens.
source_text: Before going on her walk again the young girl stood still on the bank for a few instants and listened. It was to the full as lonely a place as Rainbarrow, though at rather a lower level; and it was more sheltered from wind and weather on account of the few firs to the north. The bank which enclosed the homestead, and protected it from the lawless state of the world without, was formed of thick square clods, dug from the ditch on the outside, and built up with a slight batter or incline, which forms no slight defense where hedges will not grow because of the wind and the wilderness, and where wall materials are unattainable. Otherwise the situation was quite open, commanding the whole length of the valley which reached to the river behind Wildeve’s house. High above this to the right, and much nearer thitherward than the Quiet Woman Inn, the blurred contour of Rainbarrow obstructed the sky.

text: At eight in the evening, the time Eustacia had set for signaling Wildeve—should she ever choose to do so—she scanned the grounds to ensure no one was near, approached the stack of gorse, and drew out a long-stemmed branch. Carrying it to the corner of the embankment, she glanced back to confirm the shutters were all fastened, then struck a flame and set the gorse alight. Once it was blazing fully, Eustacia grasped it by the stem and swept it through the air above her until the fire consumed itself completely.
source_text: At eight o’clock, the hour at which Eustacia had promised to signal Wildeve if ever she signalled at all, she looked around the premises to learn if the coast was clear, went to the furze-rick, and pulled thence a long-stemmed bough of that fuel. This she carried to the corner of the bank, and, glancing behind to see if the shutters were all closed, she struck a light, and kindled the furze. When it was thoroughly ablaze Eustacia took it by the stem and waved it in the air above her head till it had burned itself out.

text: Venn moved onward through them toward the dwelling of the secluded lady who resided above and held them in contempt. It was the Sabbath; yet at Egdon, where attending worship save for weddings or burials was a rarity, this scarcely mattered. He had resolved on the daring course of seeking a meeting with Miss Vye—to assail her standing as Thomasin’s competitor either through subtlety or outright assault, betraying in the process, perhaps too plainly, that absence of chivalry peculiar to a certain shrewd breed of men, from rustics to rulers. The great Frederick waging war upon the lovely Archduchess, Napoleon denying quarter to the fair Queen of Prussia, were no more insensible to the distinction of sex than the reddleman proved, in his own fashion, while scheming the overthrow of Eustacia.
source_text: Venn passed on through these towards the house of the isolated beauty who lived up among them and despised them. The day was Sunday; but as going to church, except to be married or buried, was exceptional at Egdon, this made little difference. He had determined upon the bold stroke of asking for an interview with Miss Vye—to attack her position as Thomasin’s rival either by art or by storm, showing therein, somewhat too conspicuously, the want of gallantry characteristic of a certain astute sort of men, from clowns to kings. The great Frederick making war on the beautiful Archduchess, Napoleon refusing terms to the beautiful Queen of Prussia, were not more dead to difference of sex than the reddleman was, in his peculiar way, in planning the displacement of Eustacia.

text: Cordelia had passed beyond the dream of some union of unimaginable splendor; still, though her passions burned with undiminished force, she disdained any humbler bond. So we behold her in a curious solitude of spirit. To have surrendered the celestial arrogance that we may shape our fate at will, and yet to have gained no quiet relish for shaping what lies within our grasp, betrays a loftiness of soul that cannot be faulted in principle, for it signals a nature which, though thwarted, refuses all concession. But, if harmonious with high thought, it tends toward peril in the common life. In a sphere where shaping means wedding, and the common life is woven of affections and pledges, the same danger shadows such a state.
source_text: Eustacia had got beyond the vision of some marriage of inexpressible glory; yet, though her emotions were in full vigour, she cared for no meaner union. Thus we see her in a strange state of isolation. To have lost the godlike conceit that we may do what we will, and not to have acquired a homely zest for doing what we can, shows a grandeur of temper which cannot be objected to in the abstract, for it denotes a mind that, though disappointed, forswears compromise. But, if congenial to philosophy, it is apt to be dangerous to the commonwealth. In a world where doing means marrying, and the commonwealth is one of hearts and hands, the same peril attends the condition.

text: “Had I realized you’d be quite unattended,” she said with a solemn air, “and that we’d share such a leisurely, delightful hour—well, joy unlooked-for is half squandered; to foresee it is to redouble its sweetness. All day long I never once imagined I’d possess you entirely this afternoon, and the living instant of any delight vanishes so swiftly.”
source_text: “I wish I had known that you would be here alone,” she said seriously, “and that we were going to have such an idle, pleasant time as this. Pleasure not known beforehand is half wasted; to anticipate it is to double it. I have not thought once today of having you all to myself this afternoon, and the actual moment of a thing is so soon gone.”

text: They came at last to the hollow where the ilex trees clustered, sunken in a funnel-shaped depression so that their crowns rose only a little above the surrounding earth. Into a crotch of one of the shrubs Tamsin climbed, as she had done in brighter days on countless such errands, and with the little billhook they had fetched she set to work severing the branches freighted with crimson fruit.
source_text: They reached the place where the hollies grew, which was in a conical pit, so that the tops of the trees were not much above the general level of the ground. Thomasin stepped up into a fork of one of the bushes, as she had done under happier circumstances on many similar occasions, and with a small chopper that they had brought she began to lop off the heavily berried boughs.

text: Eustacia welcomed the chance to remain a while longer. The stark, icy night outside felt doubly bitter to her now. Yet staying on brought its own complications. Since the main room was cramped, Mrs. Yeobright had set a bench for the mummers halfway inside the pantry doorway, which opened directly off the sitting-room. They arranged themselves there in a line, the door left ajar—so they were still, in effect, part of the same gathering. Mrs. Yeobright then spoke softly to her son, who made his way across the room to the pantry entrance, bumping his head against the hanging mistletoe as he went, and served the mummers with beef and bread, pastries, mead, and elder-wine. He and his mother waited on them so that the young maid could sit among the company as a guest. The mummers lifted off their helmets and fell to eating and drinking.
source_text: Eustacia was happy in the opportunity of staying a little longer. The cold and frosty night without was doubly frigid to her. But the lingering was not without its difficulties. Mrs. Yeobright, for want of room in the larger apartment, placed a bench for the mummers halfway through the pantry door, which opened from the sitting-room. Here they seated themselves in a row, the door being left open—thus they were still virtually in the same apartment. Mrs. Yeobright now murmured a few words to her son, who crossed the room to the pantry door, striking his head against the mistletoe as he passed, and brought the mummers beef and bread, cake pastry, mead, and elder-wine, the waiting being done by him and his mother, that the little maid-servant might sit as guest. The mummers doffed their helmets, and began to eat and drink.

text: The task at hand was to gather and pile the bundles of furze that Humphrey had been cutting for the captain’s needs during the fine weather of the previous days. The stack rose at the end of the cottage, and the two men labouring to build it were Humphrey and Sam, while the old man stood by, watching.
source_text: The performance was that of bringing together and building into a stack the furze faggots which Humphrey had been cutting for the captain’s use during the foregoing fine days. The stack was at the end of the dwelling, and the men engaged in building it were Humphrey and Sam, the old man looking on.

text: As he strayed farther from the enchanted circle of his goddess-like beloved, a sorrow unlike any he had known settled upon his features. The full weight of the predicament his affection had wrought descended upon him once more. Although Eustacia seemed prepared to endure an indefinite and unpromising betrothal until he found his footing in a fresh vocation, he could not shake the sense that she cherished him less as a man pursuing a path at odds with the glittering past she so admired, and more as an emissary from that very realm of brilliance to which she felt herself native. This meant that, while she never demanded his return to Paris, she secretly yearned for it as the outcome of their union; and the thought poisoned many an hour that might otherwise have been sweet. Alongside this grew the ever-deepening rift between him and his mother. Whenever some trifling incident threw into sharper relief the distress he was causing her, it drove him out upon solitary and brooding rambles, or kept him tossing through the small hours in a tempest of self-reproach. If only Mrs. Yeobright could have been made to understand how sound and honorable his ambition truly was, and how little it was tainted by his devotion to Eustacia, how altered her gaze upon him would have been.
source_text: And as he walked further and further from the charmed atmosphere of his Olympian girl his face grew sad with a new sort of sadness. A perception of the dilemma in which his love had placed him came back in full force. In spite of Eustacia’s apparent willingness to wait through the period of an unpromising engagement, till he should be established in his new pursuit, he could not but perceive at moments that she loved him rather as a visitant from a gay world to which she rightly belonged than as a man with a purpose opposed to that recent past of his which so interested her. It meant that, though she made no conditions as to his return to the French capital, this was what she secretly longed for in the event of marriage; and it robbed him of many an otherwise pleasant hour. Along with that came the widening breach between himself and his mother. Whenever any little occurrence had brought into more prominence than usual the disappointment that he was causing her it had sent him on lone and moody walks; or he was kept awake a great part of the night by the turmoil of spirit which such a recognition created. If Mrs. Yeobright could only have been led to see what a sound and worthy purpose this purpose of his was and how little it was being affected by his devotions to Eustacia, how differently would she regard him!

text: “Look at that,” Christian Cantle whispered. “Friends, who’s to say some remnant of the old serpent from God’s garden—the one that gave the apple to the naked young woman—doesn’t live on in adders and snakes even now? See that eye, black and wicked as a currant. Let’s hope he can’t cast a curse on us! There are folk on the heath who’ve already been blighted by an evil glance. I’ll never kill another adder as long as I draw breath.”
source_text: “Look at that,” murmured Christian Cantle. “Neighbours, how do we know but that something of the old serpent in God’s garden, that gied the apple to the young woman with no clothes, lives on in adders and snakes still? Look at his eye—for all the world like a villainous sort of black currant. ’Tis to be hoped he can’t ill-wish us! There’s folks in heath who’ve been overlooked already. I will never kill another adder as long as I live.”

text: This morning in church, we were all on our feet when the parson said, “Let us pray.” Well, thinks I, it’s as good to kneel as to stand—so down I went, and what’s more, the whole congregation seemed just as ready to humour the man as I was. We hadn’t been at it above a minute when a dreadful shriek tore through the church, as though someone had yielded up their very life’s blood. Everybody sprang up, and then we discovered that Susan Nunsuch had jabbed Miss Vye with a long stocking-needle, exactly as she had vowed to do the first moment she could get the young lady to church—which isn’t often. She’d been waiting for this chance for weeks, meaning to draw her blood and break the spell that’s been bewitching Susan’s children for so long. Sue followed her into church, seated herself close beside her, and the instant she found an opening, in went the stocking-needle into my lady’s arm.
source_text: “This morning at church we was all standing up, and the pa’son said, ‘Let us pray.’ ‘Well,’ thinks I, ‘one may as well kneel as stand’; so down I went; and, more than that, all the rest were as willing to oblige the man as I. We hadn’t been hard at it for more than a minute when a most terrible screech sounded through church, as if somebody had just gied up their heart’s blood. All the folk jumped up and then we found that Susan Nunsuch had pricked Miss Vye with a long stocking-needle, as she had threatened to do as soon as ever she could get the young lady to church, where she don’t come very often. She’ve waited for this chance for weeks, so as to draw her blood and put an end to the bewitching of Susan’s children that has been carried on so long. Sue followed her into church, sat next to her, and as soon as she could find a chance in went the stocking-needle into my lady’s arm.”

text: Mrs. Yeobright stayed quiet for a while, trembling, as though words had deserted her. At last she answered, “Best? Is it best for you to wreck your future over a woman so lazy and pleasure-seeking? Can’t you see that your very choice of her shows you have no idea what’s truly good for you? You surrender your entire mind—you devote your whole spirit—to gratifying a woman.”
source_text: Mrs. Yeobright remained for some time silent and shaken, as if she could say no more. Then she replied, “Best? Is it best for you to injure your prospects for such a voluptuous, idle woman as that? Don’t you see that by the very fact of your choosing her you prove that you do not know what is best for you? You give up your whole thought—you set your whole soul—to please a woman.”

text: Clymene’s face mirrored her heart for an instant, and she whispered, raising her fathomless black gaze to meet his with a tremor, “I long to understand the right course. I’ve no desire to treat him coldly; yet I’ve no wish to face him once more; and there are a handful of trifles I must send back to him.”
source_text: Eustacia looked for a moment what she felt, and she murmured, lifting her deep dark eyes anxiously to his, “I wish I knew what to do. I don’t want to be uncivil to him; but I don’t wish to see him again; and I have some few little things to return to him.”

text: Charley devoted himself entirely to his former mistress. The only relief from his own distress came through easing hers. He spent hours thinking about what she might need; he felt a kind of thankfulness just knowing she was there, and even as he cursed the source of her misery, he half-welcomed what it had brought. Maybe she would stay forever, he imagined, and then he could be as content as he once was. What he feared most was that she might decide to go back to Alderworth, and in that fear his eyes, driven by love’s restless curiosity, often searched her face when she wasn’t looking—much as he would study a stockdove’s head to guess if it meant to take flight. Having truly come to her aid, and perhaps saved her from some desperate action, he now silently took on a protector’s duty for her well-being.
source_text: Charley’s attentions to his former mistress were unbounded. The only solace to his own trouble lay in his attempts to relieve hers. Hour after hour he considered her wants; he thought of her presence there with a sort of gratitude, and, while uttering imprecations on the cause of her unhappiness, in some measure blessed the result. Perhaps she would always remain there, he thought, and then he would be as happy as he had been before. His dread was lest she should think fit to return to Alderworth, and in that dread his eyes, with all the inquisitiveness of affection, frequently sought her face when she was not observing him, as he would have watched the head of a stockdove to learn if it contemplated flight. Having once really succoured her, and possibly preserved her from the rashest of acts, he mentally assumed in addition a guardian’s responsibility for her welfare.

text: As dusk gathered on the sixth day, her resolve to leave stirred back to life. Near four in the afternoon, she gathered once more the scant possessions she had snatched in her escape from Alderworth, along with a few of her own things that had remained behind; together they made a parcel slight enough to be borne by hand a mile or two. Outside, the world dimmed; clouds the colour of wet clay sagged low overhead like enormous slung hammocks, and as night deepened a gusting wind sprang up—though no rain had yet fallen.
source_text: Towards evening on the sixth her determination to go away again revived. About four o’clock she packed up anew the few small articles she had brought in her flight from Alderworth, and also some belonging to her which had been left here; the whole formed a bundle not too large to be carried in her hand for a distance of a mile or two. The scene without grew darker; mud-coloured clouds bellied downwards from the sky like vast hammocks slung across it, and with the increase of night a stormy wind arose; but as yet there was no rain.

text: “You can’t measure a man by the common run of people,” Venn replied. “Though I confess I’ve little sense of what sentiments signify these days. I’ve become so tangled in dealings of this kind and that, my tenderer passions have quite evaporated into mist. Indeed, I’ve surrendered myself, flesh and spirit, to the pursuit of wealth. Gold is my sole vision.”
source_text: “You mustn’t judge by folks in general,” said Venn. “Still I don’t know much what feelings are nowadays. I have got so mixed up with business of one sort and t’other that my soft sentiments are gone off in vapour like. Yes, I am given up body and soul to the making of money. Money is all my dream.”

text: Thirty minutes of strolling and conversation carried them to the valley floor where the grassy strip broadened and sloped toward the house's facade. When Eustacia saw the place, the fleeting misgivings she had entertained while walking with the young men vanished, and she felt renewed satisfaction that she had embarked on the venture. She had ventured forth to encounter a man who might possess the strength to free her spirit from a crushing despair. What was Wildeve? Intriguing, yet insufficient. Tonight, perhaps, she would behold a hero who was enough.
source_text: Half-an-hour of walking and talking brought them to the spot in the valley where the grass riband widened and led down to the front of the house. At sight of the place Eustacia who had felt a few passing doubts during her walk with the youths, again was glad that the adventure had been undertaken. She had come out to see a man who might possibly have the power to deliver her soul from a most deadly oppression. What was Wildeve? Interesting, but inadequate. Perhaps she would see a sufficient hero tonight.

text: Clym did not pause the bustle, and returned to his dwelling. At dawn, when Tamsin drew back the linen from her chamber casement, the Maypole already pierced the common’s heart, its crown needling the heavens. It had risen in the darkness—or earliest morning—like a stalk from a fairy tale. She pushed the lattice wide to study the wreaths and nosegays that clothed it. The honeyed breath of the blossoms had already seeped into the unstained air, which bore to her mouth an undiluted draught of the scent cascading from that floral steeple. At the summit, crossed hoops wore a fretwork of tiny blooms; below them hung a creamy band of hawthorn; then a girdle of harebells, then cowslips, then lilac, then ragged-robin, narcissus, and onward, until the lowest tier was met. Tamsin marked each ring, and rejoiced that the Maying would unfold so close at hand.
source_text: Yeobright did not interrupt the preparations, and went home again. The next morning, when Thomasin withdrew the curtains of her bedroom window, there stood the Maypole in the middle of the green, its top cutting into the sky. It had sprung up in the night, or rather early morning, like Jack’s bean-stalk. She opened the casement to get a better view of the garlands and posies that adorned it. The sweet perfume of the flowers had already spread into the surrounding air, which, being free from every taint, conducted to her lips a full measure of the fragrance received from the spire of blossom in its midst. At the top of the pole were crossed hoops decked with small flowers; beneath these came a milk-white zone of Maybloom; then a zone of bluebells, then of cowslips, then of lilacs, then of ragged-robins, daffodils, and so on, till the lowest stage was reached. Thomasin noticed all these, and was delighted that the May revel was to be so near.

text: Abruptly, upon the mound, a voice wove itself into the untamed poetics of the darkness—a tone that blended so seamlessly with the chorus that its inception and close were nearly imperceptible. The crags, the thickets, and the wild thyme had relinquished their quiet; now, so too did the woman; and her speech was merely another cadence in their shared oration. Cast forth upon the gale, it entangled with the rest, and with them it fled.
source_text: Suddenly, on the barrow, there mingled with all this wild rhetoric of night a sound which modulated so naturally into the rest that its beginning and ending were hardly to be distinguished. The bluffs, and the bushes, and the heather-bells had broken silence; at last, so did the woman; and her articulation was but as another phrase of the same discourse as theirs. Thrown out on the winds it became twined in with them, and with them it flew away.

text: An elderly man made his way down the lane, his hair as white as a snow-capped peak, his shoulders stooped, and his whole appearance washed out by time. He had on a shiny hat, a weathered boat-cloak, and shoes adorned with brass buttons stamped with an anchor. Clutched in his hand was a cane topped with silver, which he wielded like a true third limb, steadily tapping the earth with its tip every few inches. You might have guessed that, once upon a time, he had served as some kind of naval officer.
source_text: Along the road walked an old man. He was white-headed as a mountain, bowed in the shoulders, and faded in general aspect. He wore a glazed hat, an ancient boat-cloak, and shoes; his brass buttons bearing an anchor upon their face. In his hand was a silver-headed walking stick, which he used as a veritable third leg, perseveringly dotting the ground with its point at every few inches’ interval. One would have said that he had been, in his day, a naval officer of some sort or other.

text: During the previous month, whenever farmers seeking a fresh supply of reddle asked where Venn might be located, the answer was always the same: “On Egdon Heath.” Day after day, that reply never varied. Yet Egdon was a place of heath-croppers and furze-cutters, not of sheep and shepherds; the downs where most shepherds worked lay north and west of the heath. So his persistence in camping there, like Israel in the wilderness of Zin, seemed inexplicable. The spot was central and had its occasional advantages. But selling reddle was not Diggory’s chief motive for lingering on the heath, especially so late in the season, when most men of his trade had already retreated to winter quarters.
source_text: When the farmers who had wished to buy in a new stock of reddle during the last month had inquired where Venn was to be found, people replied, “On Egdon Heath.” Day after day the answer was the same. Now, since Egdon was populated with heath-croppers and furze-cutters rather than with sheep and shepherds, and the downs where most of the latter were to be found lay some to the north, some to the west of Egdon, his reason for camping about there like Israel in Zin was not apparent. The position was central and occasionally desirable. But the sale of reddle was not Diggory’s primary object in remaining on the heath, particularly at so late a period of the year, when most travellers of his class had gone into winter quarters.

text: “That’s all the water we ever get,” she went on, flinging a pebble into the mere, which rested against the outer edge of the bank like the white of a blind eye missing its center. The pebble struck with a splash, yet no Wildeve emerged on the far side, as he had done once before in that very spot. “My grandfather claims he spent above twenty years at sea drinking water twice as foul,” she added, “and reckons it’s perfectly fit for us here in a pinch.”
source_text: “That’s the only kind of water we have,” she continued, tossing a stone into the pool, which lay on the outside of the bank like the white of an eye without its pupil. The stone fell with a flounce, but no Wildeve appeared on the other side, as on a previous occasion there. “My grandfather says he lived for more than twenty years at sea on water twice as bad as that,” she went on, “and considers it quite good enough for us here on an emergency.”

text: The moment Yeobright stepped out of his mother’s cottage, her fixed expression dissolved into vacant hopelessness. Presently she broke down and wept, the tears easing her a little. All the rest of that day she could only pace the garden path, half-numb with shock. Darkness fell, but brought her scarcely any sleep. The following morning, driven by an urge to blunt her collapse into simple sorrow, she went to her son’s bedroom and carefully tidied it with her own hands, as if for some imagined day when he might come back. She tended her flowers, but her touch was mechanical; they had lost their power to delight her.
source_text: No sooner had Yeobright gone from his mother’s house than her face changed its rigid aspect for one of blank despair. After a while she wept, and her tears brought some relief. During the rest of the day she did nothing but walk up and down the garden path in a state bordering on stupefaction. Night came, and with it but little rest. The next day, with an instinct to do something which should reduce prostration to mournfulness, she went to her son’s room, and with her own hands arranged it in order, for an imaginary time when he should return again. She gave some attention to her flowers, but it was perfunctorily bestowed, for they no longer charmed her.

text: When her temperature settled, she realized that much of the dream had sprung quite naturally from the previous day’s impressions and reveries. Yet this hardly diminished its fascination, which rested in the rich tinder it offered to her freshly sparked ardor. She stood at the shifting threshold between apathy and affection, at that phase known as “taking a liking to someone.” This moment appears once in the life of even the most towering passions, and it is a time when they lie at the mercy of the frailest resolve.
source_text: When she became cooler she perceived that many of the phases of the dream had naturally arisen out of the images and fancies of the day before. But this detracted little from its interest, which lay in the excellent fuel it provided for newly kindled fervour. She was at the modulating point between indifference and love, at the stage called “having a fancy for.” It occurs once in the history of the most gigantic passions, and it is a period when they are in the hands of the weakest will.

text: The place appointed for the parish revel was a grassy clearing such as sometimes, though seldom, broke the monotony of the heathland table. Gorse and bracken ceased sharply at its boundary, leaving the turf seamless. A pale cattle-way traced the edge of the open ground yet never quite escaped the fern’s concealment, and along this track Eustacia moved, meaning to observe the gathering before she mingled with it. The hearty strains of the East Egdon musicians had guided her without mistake, and now she saw the players themselves, perched in a blue cart whose red wheels were scoured to a high gloss and whose frame was arched with poles decked in boughs and blossoms. Before this cart whirled the main dance of fifteen or twenty couples, bordered by lesser sets of humbler folk whose spinning steps did not always keep true time with the melody.
source_text: The site chosen for the village festivity was one of the lawnlike oases which were occasionally, yet not often, met with on the plateaux of the heath district. The brakes of furze and fern terminated abruptly round the margin, and the grass was unbroken. A green cattletrack skirted the spot, without, however, emerging from the screen of fern, and this path Eustacia followed, in order to reconnoitre the group before joining it. The lusty notes of the East Egdon band had directed her unerringly, and she now beheld the musicians themselves, sitting in a blue wagon with red wheels scrubbed as bright as new, and arched with sticks, to which boughs and flowers were tied. In front of this was the grand central dance of fifteen or twenty couples, flanked by minor dances of inferior individuals whose gyrations were not always in strict keeping with the tune.

text: At this season, strolling under the stars felt far more agreeable than in the harsh light of day, and after Clym had been gone for roughly an hour, she abruptly made up her mind to venture toward Blooms-End, hoping she might encounter him on his way back. Just as she arrived at the garden gate, the sound of wheels reached her ears, and turning, she saw her grandfather approaching in his carriage.
source_text: At this time of the year it was pleasanter to walk by night than by day, and when Clym had been absent about an hour she suddenly resolved to go out in the direction of Blooms-End, on the chance of meeting him on his return. When she reached the garden gate she heard wheels approaching, and looking round beheld her grandfather coming up in his car.

text: The sky was draped in a pale shroud, while the earth lay clothed in the deepest vegetation, so that the line where they met at the horizon stood out with stark precision. In this sharp division, the heath looked like a patch of night that had settled there before its appointed hour: darkness had already claimed much of the ground, though day still shone clearly overhead. A furze-cutter glancing upward would have felt inclined to keep working; glancing down, he would have thought better of it, bound his bundle, and made for home. The far edges of land and sky seemed to mark a rift in time as much as in substance. The very complexion of the heath added half an hour to the evening; in the same way it could hold back the dawn, cast a gloom over noon, herald the frown of storms not yet born, and deepen the blackness of a moonless midnight into something that made men tremble.
source_text: The heaven being spread with this pallid screen and the earth with the darkest vegetation, their meeting-line at the horizon was clearly marked. In such contrast the heath wore the appearance of an instalment of night which had taken up its place before its astronomical hour was come: darkness had to a great extent arrived hereon, while day stood distinct in the sky. Looking upwards, a furze-cutter would have been inclined to continue work; looking down, he would have decided to finish his faggot and go home. The distant rims of the world and of the firmament seemed to be a division in time no less than a division in matter. The face of the heath by its mere complexion added half an hour to evening; it could in like manner retard the dawn, sadden noon, anticipate the frowning of storms scarcely generated, and intensify the opacity of a moonless midnight to a cause of shaking and dread.

text: Upon returning the elder’s salutation, he betrayed no wish to prolong the exchange, though they continued to trudge abreast, for the older wayfarer plainly craved companionship. No noise attended them save the low thunder of the wind sweeping the sere, rust-colored grasses, the creak of the wheels, the steady tramp of the men, and the clop of the two rough-coated ponies hauling the cart. These were diminutive, sturdy beasts, a cross between Galloway and Exmoor stock, called “moor-nibblers” in these parts.
source_text: After replying to the old man’s greeting he showed no inclination to continue in talk, although they still walked side by side, for the elder traveller seemed to desire company. There were no sounds but that of the booming wind upon the stretch of tawny herbage around them, the crackling wheels, the tread of the men, and the footsteps of the two shaggy ponies which drew the van. They were small, hardy animals, of a breed between Galloway and Exmoor, and were known as “heath-croppers” here.

text: That brief five minutes of eavesdropping supplied Eustacia with enough daydreams to occupy the entire empty afternoon. Such quiet transformations from inner emptiness can indeed happen without warning. She would never have imagined that morning that her drab interior world could, by evening, become as teeming as a drop of water beneath a lens—and without a single caller appearing. The talk of Sam and Humphrey about the mysterious stranger’s harmony with her worked on her mind like the invading minstrel’s overture in the Castle of Indolence, where countless captive forms suddenly stirred in what had seemed an empty stillness.
source_text: That five minutes of overhearing furnished Eustacia with visions enough to fill the whole blank afternoon. Such sudden alternations from mental vacuity do sometimes occur thus quietly. She could never have believed in the morning that her colourless inner world would before night become as animated as water under a microscope, and that without the arrival of a single visitor. The words of Sam and Humphrey on the harmony between the unknown and herself had on her mind the effect of the invading Bard’s prelude in the Castle of Indolence, at which myriads of imprisoned shapes arose where had previously appeared the stillness of a void.

text: Mistress Craythorne hunted through her belongings until she unearthed a scrap of the slenderest crimson cord, carried it below, and fastened it about the figurine’s throat. Then, retrieving a pot of lampblack and a feather-stuffed counterpane from the tottering escritoire beside the casement, she darkened the feet of the little figure up to the line where slippers would have covered them, and upon the arch of each foot etched crossing strokes to mimic the crisscross thongs of the era’s sandals. At last she looped a strand of jet thread around the crown of the head, a ghost of the caul once used to bind the tresses.
source_text: Mrs. Nunsuch went and searched till she found a fragment of the narrowest red ribbon, which she took downstairs and tied round the neck of the image. Then fetching ink and a quilt from the rickety bureau by the window, she blackened the feet of the image to the extent presumably covered by shoes; and on the instep of each foot marked cross-lines in the shape taken by the sandalstrings of those days. Finally she tied a bit of black thread round the upper part of the head, in faint resemblance to a snood worn for confining the hair.

text: Eustacia sprang lightly into the cart and settled on the stool, hidden from sight on the side facing the track. Before long, she caught the sound of footsteps other than the reddleman’s—a curt, unfriendly “Good day” exchanged between two men as they passed—and then the fading tread of one of them continuing along the way. She craned her neck forward just in time to glimpse a retreating back and shoulders, and a wretched pang of misery seized her, though she could not say why. It was that sickening ache which, if a changed heart retains any generosity, strikes at the sudden sight of someone once loved and now loved no more.
source_text: Eustacia bounded into the vehicle and sat on the stool, ensconced from view on the side towards the trackway. Soon she heard the brushing of other feet than the reddleman’s, a not very friendly “Good day” uttered by two men in passing each other, and then the dwindling of the foot-fall of one of them in a direction onwards. Eustacia stretched her neck forward till she caught a glimpse of a receding back and shoulders; and she felt a wretched twinge of misery, she knew not why. It was the sickening feeling which, if the changed heart has any generosity at all in its composition, accompanies the sudden sight of a once-loved one who is beloved no more.

text: “I’ve strayed from the road leaving Blooms-End, and I’m desperate to reach home. Point the way as fast as you can! It’s foolish of me to be so unfamiliar with Egdon, and I can’t fathom how I wandered off the path. Be quick about it, Diggory, I beg you.”
source_text: “I have lost my way coming from Blooms-End, and I am in a great hurry to get home. Please show me as quickly as you can! It is so silly of me not to know Egdon better, and I cannot think how I came to lose the path. Show me quickly, Diggory, please.”

text: She caught every syllable the wanderers exchanged. No confidences passed between them. They simply shared the lively, familiar talk of kindred spirits long separated in body yet never in heart. But it was not the substance Eustacia attended to; within moments she could not have repeated a single phrase. It was the one voice among them—the voice that had bade her good night—that held her, though it spoke barely a tenth of what was said. At one turn the throat answered Yes, at another No; once it asked after some aged inhabitant of the district. Then it startled her with a comment on the warmth and openness written across the faces of the surrounding hills.
source_text: She could follow every word that the ramblers uttered. They were talking no secrets. They were merely indulging in the ordinary vivacious chat of relatives who have long been parted in person though not in soul. But it was not to the words that Eustacia listened; she could not even have recalled, a few minutes later, what the words were. It was to the alternating voice that gave out about one-tenth of them—the voice that had wished her good night. Sometimes this throat uttered Yes, sometimes it uttered No; sometimes it made inquiries about a time-worn denizen of the place. Once it surprised her notions by remarking upon the friendliness and geniality written in the faces of the hills around.

text: The slanted shaft of daylight that slipped in behind her suited the bride perfectly. It brightened her just as her spirit brightened the moor. In her gestures, in her glance, she called to mind the winged lives that dwelled about her cottage. Every metaphor and likeness drawn from her commenced and concluded with birds. Her motions shifted as diversely as their trajectories. Lost in thought, she became a merlin suspended on unseen wingbeats. Caught in a gale, her slight frame was dashed against trunks and hedges like a crane’s. Startled, she shot away mute as a halcyon. Calm, she glided with the ease of a martin—and that was how she travelled now.
source_text: The oblique band of sunlight which followed her through the door became the young wife well. It illuminated her as her presence illuminated the heath. In her movements, in her gaze, she reminded the beholder of the feathered creatures who lived around her home. All similes and allegories concerning her began and ended with birds. There was as much variety in her motions as in their flight. When she was musing she was a kestrel, which hangs in the air by an invisible motion of its wings. When she was in a high wind her light body was blown against trees and banks like a heron’s. When she was frightened she darted noiselessly like a kingfisher. When she was serene she skimmed like a swallow, and that is how she was moving now.

text: Once all the fires around him had flared to life, Charley lit his own and stacked the wood so it would burn steadily without his attention for a while. Then he returned to the house and hovered near the door and windows, waiting for her to somehow discover what he’d done and step outside to see. But the shutters stayed sealed, the door remained closed, and no one appeared to notice his effort. Unwilling to shout for her, he went back and fed the flames, keeping this up for over half an hour. Only when his pile of fuel had shrunk considerably did he go to the rear door and send word, asking Mrs. Yeobright to open the window-shutters and look at the display outdoors.
source_text: When all the surrounding bonfires had burst into existence Charley kindled his, and arranged its fuel so that it should not require tending for some time. He then went back to the house, and lingered round the door and windows till she should by some means or other learn of his achievement and come out to witness it. But the shutters were closed, the door remained shut, and no heed whatever seemed to be taken of his performance. Not liking to call her he went back and replenished the fire, continuing to do this for more than half an hour. It was not till his stock of fuel had greatly diminished that he went to the back door and sent in to beg that Mrs. Yeobright would open the window-shutters and see the sight outside.

text: It appears that an unbroken succession of disenchanted ages has wholly supplanted the Hellenic vision of existence, or whatever name one might give it. What the ancients merely glimpsed, we now grasp with certainty; what their Sophocles could only dramatize, our schoolroom children instinctively sense. That antique delight in the cosmic order becomes steadily more untenable as we lay bare the flaws in nature’s decrees and perceive the human predicament their workings impose.
source_text: The truth seems to be that a long line of disillusive centuries has permanently displaced the Hellenic idea of life, or whatever it may be called. What the Greeks only suspected we know well; what their Æschylus imagined our nursery children feel. That old-fashioned revelling in the general situation grows less and less possible as we uncover the defects of natural laws, and see the quandary that man is in by their operation.

text: He arrived at his doorstep soaked and spent from the ten-mile tramp. The outset had been anything but auspicious, yet he had fixed his path and resolved not to waver. That night and the next morning passed in tying up final threads for his leaving. Lingering at home even a moment beyond what was needful now that his mind was made up would, he sensed, merely inflict fresh hurt on his mother through some stray phrase, glance, or gesture.
source_text: He reached home damp and weary enough after his ten-mile walk. It had hardly been a propitious beginning, but he had chosen his course, and would show no swerving. The evening and the following morning were spent in concluding arrangements for his departure. To stay at home a minute longer than necessary after having once come to his determination would be, he felt, only to give new pain to his mother by some word, look, or deed.

text: “I’ll split your thick skull for this, you senseless fool!” cried Mrs. Nunsuch, as she stumbled helplessly in a circle with him, her feet pattering like drumsticks among the flying sparks. “My ankles were already burning from tramping through that prickly furze, and now you must torment them further with these blazing cinders!”
source_text: “I’ll crack thy numskull for thee, you mandy chap!” said Mrs. Nunsuch, as she helplessly danced round with him, her feet playing like drumsticks among the sparks. “My ankles were all in a fever before, from walking through that prickly furze, and now you must make ’em worse with these vlankers!”

text: The walker was oblivious to all else at that moment, and a small mishap betrayed her distraction. A thorny vine snagged the hem of her dress, arresting her stride. Rather than freeing herself and pressing forward, she surrendered to the tug, remaining motionless. When at last she set about disentangling the fabric, she did so by revolving slowly, coiling the barbed tendril free. Her mind had drifted into a melancholy daydream.
source_text: The pedestrian noticed nothing just now, and a clue to her abstraction was afforded by a trivial incident. A bramble caught hold of her skirt, and checked her progress. Instead of putting it off and hastening along, she yielded herself up to the pull, and stood passively still. When she began to extricate herself it was by turning round and round, and so unwinding the prickly switch. She was in a desponding reverie.

text: I encountered Mrs. Yeobright, the youthful bride’s aunt, yestereve, and she confided that her nephew Clym would return home at Christmastide. Remarkably sharp, they say—ah, I’d give much to possess all that lies beneath that fellow’s locks. So then, I addressed her in my familiar cheerful fashion, and she retorted, ‘Oh, that a form so august should prattle like a simpleton!’—those were her very words to me. I’ve no regard for her, be damned if I do, and I made it plain. ‘Be damned if I’ve regard for ye,’ I declared. I caught her there—eh?
source_text: I met Mis’ess Yeobright, the young bride’s aunt, last night, and she told me that her son Clym was coming home a’ Christmas. Wonderful clever, ’a believe—ah, I should like to have all that’s under that young man’s hair. Well, then, I spoke to her in my well-known merry way, and she said, ‘O that what’s shaped so venerable should talk like a fool!’—that’s what she said to me. I don’t care for her, be jowned if I do, and so I told her. ‘Be jowned if I care for ’ee,’ I said. I had her there—hey?”

text: “Aren’t you coming inside, Eustacia?” he said. “It’s nearly time for bed. I’ve been back for two hours now, and I’m worn through. Honestly, it’s a bit childish, staying out this late with your bonfires and squandering good fuel. My fine thorn roots—the very best kindling I’d saved especially for Christmas—you’ve gone and burned almost every one of them!”
source_text: “When are you coming indoors, Eustacia?” he asked. “’Tis almost bedtime. I’ve been home these two hours, and am tired out. Surely ’tis somewhat childish of you to stay out playing at bonfires so long, and wasting such fuel. My precious thorn roots, the rarest of all firing, that I laid by on purpose for Christmas—you have burnt ’em nearly all!”

text: Mrs. Yeobright had not once set foot in her son’s dwelling, and its precise whereabouts remained a mystery to her. She ventured up one rising track, then another, only to discover each winding her into confusion. Turning back upon her route, she emerged once more onto a bare stretch of land, where far off she glimpsed a figure labouring. She advanced toward him and asked for direction.
source_text: Mrs. Yeobright had never before been to her son’s house, and its exact position was unknown to her. She tried one ascending path and another, and found that they led her astray. Retracing her steps, she came again to an open level, where she perceived at a distance a man at work. She went towards him and inquired the way.

text: “Truly, none among us could have served as a worthy deputy to him, not even a distant runner-up, had I not once marched with the Dashing Volunteers—so named for our crisp turnout,” declared Elder Trewin. “And even now, we all seem a touch threadbare when set beside him. Yet back in ’04, they claimed there was no grander silhouette in the whole of Lower Cornwall than mine, as I swept past the glaziers’ displays alongside my comrades on the day we charged out of Falmouth, fearing Old Boney had made shore around the headland. There I stood, tall as a sapling ash, with my musket, my blade, my gaiters, my collar near cleaving my chin, and my brasswork glittering like the Pleiades! Aye, friends, I cut a splendid figure in my soldiering prime. You should have glimpsed me in ’04!”
source_text: “Really, there would have been nobody here who could have stood as decent second to him, or even third, if I hadn’t been a soldier in the Bang-up Locals (as we was called for our smartness),” said Grandfer Cantle. “And even as ’tis we all look a little scammish beside him. But in the year four ’twas said there wasn’t a finer figure in the whole South Wessex than I, as I looked when dashing past the shop-winders with the rest of our company on the day we ran out o’ Budmouth because it was thoughted that Boney had landed round the point. There was I, straight as a young poplar, wi’ my firelock, and my bagnet, and my spatterdashes, and my stock sawing my jaws off, and my accoutrements sheening like the seven stars! Yes, neighbours, I was a pretty sight in my soldiering days. You ought to have seen me in four!”

text: “Oh, I can scarcely endure this parting from you!” Eustacia breathed out in a rush of whispered pain. “Your mother will sway you too heavily; I’ll never receive fair judgment—rumours will spread that I am no virtuous woman, and that tale of witchcraft will be heaped on to blacken my name still further!”
source_text: “O! this leaving you is too hard to bear!” exclaimed Eustacia in a sudden whisper of anguish. “Your mother will influence you too much; I shall not be judged fairly, it will get afloat that I am not a good girl, and the witch story will be added to make me blacker!”

text: The next morning he left the house and set out once more across the heath. A full day’s walk lay ahead, his aim being to find a home where he could bring Eustacia once they were married. A month before, he had happened to notice just such a place—small, isolated, its windows shuttered—about two miles past the village of East Egdon, making it six miles away in total; and now he turned his feet toward it.
source_text: Next morning he departed from the house and again proceeded across the heath. A long day’s march was before him, his object being to secure a dwelling to which he might take Eustacia when she became his wife. Such a house, small, secluded, and with its windows boarded up, he had casually observed a month earlier, about two miles beyond the village of East Egdon, and six miles distant altogether; and thither he directed his steps today.

text: His expression was that of an innate buoyancy wrestling with an encroaching melancholy, and never fully prevailing. It hinted at loneliness, yet disclosed something deeper. In keeping with luminous spirits, the god ignobly shackled inside a fleeting mortal frame gleamed through him like a shaft of light.
source_text: As for his look, it was a natural cheerfulness striving against depression from without, and not quite succeeding. The look suggested isolation, but it revealed something more. As is usual with bright natures, the deity that lies ignominiously chained within an ephemeral human carcase shone out of him like a ray.

text: Putting these thoughts aside, he continued on his way home until he stood before his house. Eustacia’s bedroom curtains remained tightly shut, as she never woke early. The only sign of life was a lone thrush on the doorstep, smashing a tiny snail against the stone for its morning meal; the sharp rapping of its beak rang out unnaturally loud in the deep stillness all around. But when Clym reached the door, he discovered it was unlatched—the girl who waited on Eustacia was already up and moving about at the back of the house. Yeobright stepped inside and made directly for his wife’s room.
source_text: But dismissing all this he went onward home, and came to the front of his house. The blinds of Eustacia’s bedroom were still closely drawn, for she was no early riser. All the life visible was in the shape of a solitary thrush cracking a small snail upon the door-stone for his breakfast, and his tapping seemed a loud noise in the general silence which prevailed; but on going to the door Clym found it unfastened, the young girl who attended upon Eustacia being astir in the back part of the premises. Yeobright entered and went straight to his wife’s room.

text: “No. It’s just that I fear dwelling on anything outside this moment. We know what we have right now. We are together, and how long that will last is a mystery—and mystery always floods my thoughts with dreadful outcomes, even when I have every reason to hope for happiness. … Clym, the veiled moonlight falls across your face with an odd, unearthly hue, outlining your features as though they were carved in gold. That must mean you are meant for finer things than this.”
source_text: “No. Only I dread to think of anything beyond the present. What is, we know. We are together now, and it is unknown how long we shall be so; the unknown always fills my mind with terrible possibilities, even when I may reasonably expect it to be cheerful.... Clym, the eclipsed moonlight shines upon your face with a strange foreign colour, and shows its shape as if it were cut out in gold. That means that you should be doing better things than this.”

text: All that could be discerned of her at present was a tall, upright carriage and a certain refinement in her bearing, for her figure lay swathed in a shawl draped in the antiquated diagonal manner, and her head was bound in a broad kerchief—a precaution far from needless in that hour and spot. She had set her back to the wind, which came breathing out of the northwest; yet whether she had turned from that quarter to escape the raw eddies that fretted about her exposed stance, or whether her attention was fixed upon the southeast, was not immediately plain.
source_text: That she was tall and straight in build, that she was lady-like in her movements, was all that could be learnt of her just now, her form being wrapped in a shawl folded in the old cornerwise fashion, and her head in a large kerchief, a protection not superfluous at this hour and place. Her back was towards the wind, which blew from the northwest; but whether she had avoided that aspect because of the chilly gusts which played about her exceptional position, or because her interest lay in the southeast, did not at first appear.

text: Wildeve stepped back into the vacant room, carrying an odd ache in his chest. He leaned his elbow on the mantel and cupped his chin in his palm. When Thomasin came in, he kept what he had learned to himself. The former yearning for Eustacia stirred again within him—largely because he now knew another man meant to claim her.
source_text: Wildeve went indoors to the empty room, a curious heartache within him. He rested his elbow upon the mantelpiece and his face upon his hand. When Thomasin entered the room he did not tell her of what he had heard. The old longing for Eustacia had reappeared in his soul—and it was mainly because he had discovered that it was another man’s intention to possess her.

text: As he approached, he saw it was a spring wagon—common enough in form, yet remarkable in hue, a glaring crimson. The carter strode alongside; and like his cart, he too was wholly red. That single stain drenched his garments, the hat on his head, his boots, his features, and his fists. The colour was not merely daubed upon him for the moment; it had soaked into his very being.
source_text: When he drew nearer he perceived it to be a spring van, ordinary in shape, but singular in colour, this being a lurid red. The driver walked beside it; and, like his van, he was completely red. One dye of that tincture covered his clothes, the cap upon his head, his boots, his face, and his hands. He was not temporarily overlaid with the colour; it permeated him.

text: She pressed the smoldering brand against the earth, breathing softly upon its crimson ember, until the faint glow lit the turf and uncovered a tiny shape—an hourglass, though a watch already circled her wrist. She blew steadily, just long enough to see that every grain of sand had already fallen through.
source_text: She held the brand to the ground, blowing the red coal with her mouth at the same time; till it faintly illuminated the sod, and revealed a small object, which turned out to be an hourglass, though she wore a watch. She blew long enough to show that the sand had all slipped through.

text: Eustacia turned around, went inside, and climbed the stairs to the front bedroom, where a lamp glowed behind a shade. On the bed rested Clym, pallid, worn, fully conscious, shifting restlessly from side to side, his eyes gleaming with a fierce brightness, as though the blaze within them was consuming his very being.
source_text: Eustacia turned, entered the house, and ascended to the front bedroom, where a shaded light was burning. In the bed lay Clym, pale, haggard, wide awake, tossing to one side and to the other, his eyes lit by a hot light, as if the fire in their pupils were burning up their substance.

text: The quiet figure absorbed in his task seemed as insignificant to the world as a beetle. He looked like some creature of the heath, grazing its surface with his daily toil the way a moth nibbles at cloth, wholly consumed by what it yielded, ignorant of all beyond furze, bracken, heather, lichen, and moss.
source_text: The silent being who thus occupied himself seemed to be of no more account in life than an insect. He appeared as a mere parasite of the heath, fretting its surface in his daily labour as a moth frets a garment, entirely engrossed with its products, having no knowledge of anything in the world but fern, furze, heath, lichens, and moss.

text: “Hate you—no,” Thomasin said in a gentle tone. “It’s simply that he cares for her too deeply. Consider it calmly—please do. It’s not such a terrible fault in him. You know, I didn’t think it was the poorest choice he could have made. Miss Vye’s lineage is respectable through her mother, and her father was a fanciful rover—a kind of Grecian Ulysses.”
source_text: “Hate you—no,” said Thomasin soothingly. “It is only that he loves her too well. Look at it quietly—do. It is not so very bad of him. Do you know, I thought it not the worst match he could have made. Miss Vye’s family is a good one on her mother’s side; and her father was a romantic wanderer—a sort of Greek Ulysses.”

text: Eustacia’s features were lost to Charley where she lingered at the threshold, her figure outlined against the heavens, the stable offering only a meager glow; yet the fierce abandon of her bearing seized his gaze. She pivoted and strode off over the yard toward the gate, vanishing behind the earthen rise.
source_text: Eustacia’s face was not visible to Charley as she stood at the doorway, her back being to the sky, and the stable but indifferently lighted; but the wildness of her manner arrested his attention. She turned and walked away across the enclosure to the gate, and was hidden by the bank.

text: It would suit them perfectly at a moment like this! Truly, now I reflect on it, I haven’t shaped my tongue to a proper, full-hearted song since Old Midsummer night, when we sang the ‘Barley Mow’ at the Woman; and it’s a shame to let your finest gift lie idle when so few have the range for such things!
source_text: ’Twould please ’em well at such a time! Really, now I come to think of it, I haven’t turned my tongue in my head to the shape of a real good song since Old Midsummer night, when we had the ‘Barley Mow’ at the Woman; and ’tis a pity to neglect your strong point where there’s few that have the compass for such things!”

text: Through a process of warming and kneading, slicing and twisting, pulling apart and piecing together the emerging form, she shaped it in roughly fifteen minutes into something that reasonably resembled a woman, standing about six inches tall. She placed it on the table to cool and set. Then, taking the candle, she made her way upstairs to where the young boy lay.
source_text: By warming and kneading, cutting and twisting, dismembering and re-joining the incipient image she had in about a quarter of an hour produced a shape which tolerably well resembled a woman, and was about six inches high. She laid it on the table to get cold and hard. Meanwhile she took the candle and went upstairs to where the little boy was lying.

text: The face was finely molded, even strikingly so. Yet the mind within had begun to treat it as a discarded slate on which to scrawl its unfolding eccentricities. The beauty now apparent would soon be mercilessly consumed by its tenant, thought, which might just as easily have settled on a homelier surface where it could do no damage. If Heaven had spared Yeobright the corrosive habit of brooding, people would have said, “A handsome man.” If his intellect had grown within sharper features, they would have said, “A thoughtful man.” But an inner intensity was feeding on an outer grace, and they judged his expression as strange.
source_text: The face was well shaped, even excellently. But the mind within was beginning to use it as a mere waste tablet whereon to trace its idiosyncrasies as they developed themselves. The beauty here visible would in no long time be ruthlessly over-run by its parasite, thought, which might just as well have fed upon a plainer exterior where there was nothing it could harm. Had Heaven preserved Yeobright from a wearing habit of meditation, people would have said, “A handsome man.” Had his brain unfolded under sharper contours they would have said, “A thoughtful man.” But an inner strenuousness was preying upon an outer symmetry, and they rated his look as singular.

text: “This is simply unbearable!” the younger woman rasped, her cheeks flushing and her eyes flashing fire. “How have you the nerve to address me in such a manner? I must tell you again that if I had foreseen my life would unfold as it has from my wedding day until now, I would have refused. I’m not lamenting. I’ve never breathed a word of this to him; but it remains the truth. So I trust that henceforth you will hold your tongue about my supposed enthusiasm. If you harm me now, you harm yourself.”
source_text: “This is too exasperating!” answered the younger woman huskily, her face crimsoning, and her eyes darting light. “How can you dare to speak to me like that? I insist upon repeating to you that had I known that my life would from my marriage up to this time have been as it is, I should have said _No_. I don’t complain.
