I have never uttered a sound of such a thing to him; but it is true. I hope therefore that in the future you will be silent on my eagerness. If you injure me now you injure yourself.”

text: Having made up her mind to flee, Eustacia sometimes found herself half hoping that some obstacle would arise to block her plan. The one thing that could truly alter her situation was Clym’s return. The radiance that had once haloed him as her beloved had faded now; still, some unassuming virtue of his would drift back into her thoughts and spark a fleeting pulse of hope that he might reappear before her. But in a cooler light, it seemed improbable that a rift as deep as theirs would ever mend—she was destined to go on as a wretched figure, alone and out of place. She had once considered the heath itself a hostile spot to inhabit; now she felt the whole world was just as alien.
source_text: Having resolved on flight Eustacia at times seemed anxious that something should happen to thwart her own intention. The only event that could really change her position was the appearance of Clym. The glory which had encircled him as her lover was departed now; yet some good simple quality of his would occasionally return to her memory and stir a momentary throb of hope that he would again present himself before her. But calmly considered it was not likely that such a severance as now existed would ever close up—she would have to live on as a painful object, isolated, and out of place. She had used to think of the heath alone as an uncongenial spot to be in; she felt it now of the whole world.

text: At first light, having settled on his resolve, he got up immediately. He secured the cottage and stepped outside onto the verdant plot that gradually gave way to moorland. Where the white fence bordered the garden, the path split into three forks like the head of an arrow. The rightward way ran toward the Quiet Woman and its vicinity; the center route headed for Mistover Knap; the left-hand branch climbed over the hill to another section of Mistover, where the boy lived. As Yeobright turned onto that last trail, a slow, creeping cold came over him—common enough to many, and likely born of the dawn air untouched by sun. Later, he would recall it as something deeply portentous.
source_text: It was about daybreak when he had reached this decision, and he at once arose. He locked up the house and went out into the green patch which merged in heather further on. In front of the white garden-palings the path branched into three like a broad arrow. The road to the right led to the Quiet Woman and its neighbourhood; the middle track led to Mistover Knap; the left-hand track led over the hill to another part of Mistover, where the child lived. On inclining into the latter path Yeobright felt a creeping chilliness, familiar enough to most people, and probably caused by the unsunned morning air. In after days he thought of it as a thing of singular significance.

text: “Did you truly imagine I was taken in by your marriage?” she pressed, her voice taut with feeling. “Then you misjudged me; and I swear by my life and soul, it cuts me to the core to think you could hold me in such low regard! Damon, you fall short of what I deserve—I see that plainly, and still I love you. No matter, let it pass—I must endure your contempt as best I can.... It is so, isn’t it,” she went on, her anxiety barely hidden when he offered no response, “that you couldn’t bear to relinquish me, and that you will go on loving me above all others?”
source_text: “Did you indeed think I believed you were married?” she again demanded earnestly. “Then you wronged me; and upon my life and heart I can hardly bear to recognize that you have such ill thoughts of me! Damon, you are not worthy of me—I see it, and yet I love you. Never mind, let it go—I must bear your mean opinion as best I may.... It is true, is it not,” she added with ill-concealed anxiety, on his making no demonstration, “that you could not bring yourself to give me up, and are still going to love me best of all?”

text: “Well, whatever I may have believed before, one truth stands clear—I love you beyond all measure or expression. I love you to the point of overwhelming weight—I, who have never known more than a fleeting, agreeable attraction for any woman I have ever encountered. Let me gaze into your moonlit face and trace every contour and delicate line! Only the slightest margins separate this face from others I saw countless times before I met you; yet what a gulf—the difference between all and nothing. One more touch upon those lips! here, and here, and here. Your eyes look drowsy, Eustacia.”
source_text: “Well, whatever I may have thought, one thing is certain—I do love you—past all compass and description. I love you to oppressiveness—I, who have never before felt more than a pleasant passing fancy for any woman I have ever seen. Let me look right into your moonlit face and dwell on every line and curve in it! Only a few hairbreadths make the difference between this face and faces I have seen many times before I knew you; yet what a difference—the difference between everything and nothing at all. One touch on that mouth again! there, and there, and there. Your eyes seem heavy, Eustacia.”

text: A thin rime glazed the earth that evening, and the crescent moon, though scarcely past its first quarter, cast a lively and alluring glow over the outlandish shapes of the guising troupe, whose ribbons and feathers whispered as they moved like wind-stirred leaves. Their course no longer crossed Rainbarrow but descended into a hollow that skirted that old mound a short way to the east. The floor of the dale spread green for perhaps ten yards across, and the glittering crystals of frost on the grass blades seemed to drift along with the shadows of the figures they encircled. The thickets of gorse and heather on either hand remained black as ever; a half-moon could do nothing to brighten such somber forms as theirs.
source_text: There was a slight hoarfrost that night, and the moon, though not more than half full, threw a spirited and enticing brightness upon the fantastic figures of the mumming band, whose plumes and ribbons rustled in their walk like autumn leaves. Their path was not over Rainbarrow now, but down a valley which left that ancient elevation a little to the east. The bottom of the vale was green to a width of ten yards or thereabouts, and the shining facets of frost upon the blades of grass seemed to move on with the shadows of those they surrounded. The masses of furze and heath to the right and left were dark as ever; a mere half-moon was powerless to silver such sable features as theirs.

text: He set out as bidden, and by chance reached the house just as Mrs. Yeobright came to a halt on the hill nearby. After surveying the grounds in the way she had observed, he approached and rapped at the door. A brief silence followed, then the key scraped in the lock, the door swung back, and Eustacia herself stood before him.
source_text: He went accordingly; and it happened that the time of his arrival coincided with that of Mrs. Yeobright’s pause on the hill near the house. When he had looked round the premises in the manner she had noticed he went and knocked at the door. There was a few minutes’ interval, and then the key turned in the lock, the door opened, and Eustacia herself confronted him.

text: Just inside the edge of the moor, Eustacia paused, peering intently toward the dwelling and grounds of Mrs. Yeobright. There was no glimmer of lamplight, no murmur, no stir to be seen or heard. The dusk had grown raw; the place lay shadowed and solitary. She concluded the visitor had not yet arrived, and after waiting a quarter of an hour or so, she turned back for home.
source_text: Eustacia stood just within the heath, straining her eyes in the direction of Mrs. Yeobright’s house and premises. No light, sound, or movement was perceptible there. The evening was chilly; the spot was dark and lonely. She inferred that the guest had not yet come; and after lingering ten or fifteen minutes she turned again towards home.

text: Yes; and he does manage to encounter her whenever she strolls out on pleasant days with the child. But, Mr. Yeobright, I cannot help thinking that your cousin should have married you. It’s a shame to create two lonely hearths when one would suffice. You could win her back from him even now, I believe, if you would only make the effort.
source_text: “Yes; and he do contrive to stumble upon her whenever she walks out on fine days with the chiel. But, Mr. Yeobright, I can’t help feeling that your cousin ought to have married you. ’Tis a pity to make two chimleycorners where there need be only one. You could get her away from him now, ’tis my belief, if you were only to set about it.”

text: “I didn’t mean anything by it,” Wildeve answered. “It was just a passing diversion. Men often indulge in a fleeting attraction for another even while deeply in love, and then that deeper love returns as strong as ever. Your defiant attitude toward me pushed me to act more rashly than I might have; and when you persisted in playing that maddening game, I went further still and married her.” He turned and gazed once more at Clym’s insensible figure, murmuring, “I suspect you don’t truly appreciate what you have, Clym.... He should at least be happier than I am in one respect. He may know what it is to fall in the world and suffer a great personal blow, but he probably doesn’t know what it is to lose the woman he loved.”
source_text: “I meant nothing by it,” replied Wildeve. “It was a mere interlude. Men are given to the trick of having a passing fancy for somebody else in the midst of a permanent love, which reasserts itself afterwards just as before. On account of your rebellious manner to me I was tempted to go further than I should have done; and when you still would keep playing the same tantalizing part I went further still, and married her.” Turning and looking again at the unconscious form of Clym, he murmured, “I am afraid that you don’t value your prize, Clym.... He ought to be happier than I in one thing at least. He may know what it is to come down in the world, and to be afflicted with a great personal calamity; but he probably doesn’t know what it is to lose the woman he loved.”

text: Silence greeted him. The chamber stood vacant, the boy who served as his general attendant having retired for the night. Wildeve re-entered, donned his hat, seized the bottle, and stepped outside, locking the door behind him—the inn had no lodger that evening. Once he gained the open road, the small beacon upon Mistover Knap once more caught his gaze.
source_text: There was no reply. The room was empty, the lad who acted as his factotum having gone to bed. Wildeve came back, put on his hat, took the bottle, and left the house, turning the key in the door, for there was no guest at the inn tonight. As soon as he was on the road the little bonfire on Mistover Knap again met his eye.

text: Curiously, it dawned on him at last that the true difficulty lay not in convincing his dearest ally that a modest station was, for him, the nobler path, but in making peace with his own conscience for having swayed her. By every prudent measure her judgment was so faultless that a quiet ache settled in his chest when he realized he possessed the power to unsettle it.
source_text: Strangely enough he began to feel now that it would not be so hard to persuade her who was his best friend that comparative poverty was essentially the higher course for him, as to reconcile to his feelings the act of persuading her. From every provident point of view his mother was so undoubtedly right, that he was not without a sickness of heart in finding he could shake her.

text: “I did,” she declared with a victorious lilt. “After you slipped away tonight, I reckoned that with the little one slumbering I’d discover where you were heading so secretively, without a word to me. So I darted out and trailed behind you. You paused at the fork in the lane, glanced back at the blaze of the bonfires, and then muttered, ‘To hell with it, I’m going!’ And you hurried off along the rightward path. Then I stopped and kept my eyes on you.”
source_text: “I will,” she answered triumphantly. “When you went out this evening I thought that as baby was asleep I would see where you were going to so mysteriously without telling me. So I ran out and followed behind you. You stopped at the place where the road forks, looked round at the bonfires, and then said, ‘Damn it, I’ll go!’ And you went quickly up the left-hand road. Then I stood and watched you.”

text: On Sunday mornings, Fairway performed these haircuts. The subject would sit on a chopping-block before the house, coatless, while neighbours gathered to gossip and idly watch the snipped locks lift on the breeze and scatter out of sight to the four corners of the sky. Summer or winter, the scene never changed, unless the wind turned unusually blustering; then the stool was simply moved a few feet around the corner. To complain of cold while sitting outdoors bareheaded and without a coat, as Fairway recounted true stories between scissor strokes, would have marked you instantly as less than a man. To flinch, cry out, or move a single muscle of the face at the small stabs beneath the ear from those instruments, or at the comb’s scrapes along the neck, would have been considered a gross breach of manners, given that Fairway did it all for nothing. Any bleeding about the head on a Sunday afternoon was amply explained by the simple statement: “I’ve had my hair cut, you know.”
source_text: These Sunday-morning hair-cuttings were performed by Fairway; the victim sitting on a chopping-block in front of the house, without a coat, and the neighbours gossiping around, idly observing the locks of hair as they rose upon the wind after the snip, and flew away out of sight to the four quarters of the heavens. Summer and winter the scene was the same, unless the wind were more than usually blusterous, when the stool was shifted a few feet round the corner. To complain of cold in sitting out of doors, hatless and coatless, while Fairway told true stories between the cuts of the scissors, would have been to pronounce yourself no man at once. To flinch, exclaim, or move a muscle of the face at the small stabs under the ear received from those instruments, or at scarifications of the neck by the comb, would have been thought a gross breach of good manners, considering that Fairway did it all for nothing. A bleeding about the poll on Sunday afternoons was amply accounted for by the explanation. “I have had my hair cut, you know.”

text: The topic lay dormant until the meal concluded. Then his mother resumed, as though the hours since daybreak had never elapsed. “It troubles me, Clym, to discover you’ve returned harbouring notions of that kind. I never once imagined you’d willingly step backward in life of your own accord. Naturally, I always believed you would press steadily forward, like any man worthy of the title—once he’s been set on a promising course.”
source_text: No more was said on the subject till the end of dinner. His mother then began, as if there had been no interval since the morning. “It disturbs me, Clym, to find that you have come home with such thoughts as those. I hadn’t the least idea that you meant to go backward in the world by your own free choice. Of course, I have always supposed you were going to push straight on, as other men do—all who deserve the name—when they have been put in a good way of doing well.”

text: At moments like these, countless thoughts surge through the mind of a woman in a state of high emotion, each one flickering across her countenance; yet the shifts, though real, are nearly imperceptible. Isadora’s expression traced a measured sequence of such transformations. She brightened; recalling the deceptions of fantasy, she dimmed; then she revived; then she blazed; then she chilled once more. It was a round of visages, born from a round of reveries.
source_text: On such occasions as this a thousand ideas pass through a highly charged woman’s head; and they indicate themselves on her face; but the changes, though actual, are minute. Eustacia’s features went through a rhythmical succession of them. She glowed; remembering the mendacity of the imagination, she flagged; then she freshened; then she fired; then she cooled again. It was a cycle of aspects, produced by a cycle of visions.

text: By this time, the impassioned woman was half enamoured with a phantom. The very extravagance of her feeling, which diminished her as a mind, exalted her as a spirit. Had she possessed a shade more self-command, she would have whittled the emotion to nothing through pure reason, and so extinguished it entirely. Had she had a fraction less pride, she might have approached the Yeobright dwelling at Blooms-End with whatever maidenly self-abasement was required until she caught sight of him. But Eustacia followed neither course. She behaved as the most irreproachable might have behaved under such a spell; she took the air twice or three times a day upon the Egdon heights, and kept her gaze at work.
source_text: The perfervid woman was by this time half in love with a vision. The fantastic nature of her passion, which lowered her as an intellect, raised her as a soul. If she had had a little more self-control she would have attenuated the emotion to nothing by sheer reasoning, and so have killed it off. If she had had a little less pride she might have gone and circumambulated the Yeobrights’ premises at Blooms-End at any maidenly sacrifice until she had seen him. But Eustacia did neither of these things. She acted as the most exemplary might have acted, being so influenced; she took an airing twice or thrice a day upon the Egdon hills, and kept her eyes employed.

text: “I want to speak my piece first,” Venn said with resolve. “Wildeve isn’t the sole suitor who’s proposed to Thomasin; so why shouldn’t someone else have an opportunity? Mrs. Yeobright, I’d be happy to wed your niece, and I would have done so at any point these past two years. There, I’ve said it openly, and I’ve never breathed a word of it to anyone but her before now.”
source_text: “I should like to say a word first,” said Venn firmly. “Mr. Wildeve is not the only man who has asked Thomasin to marry him; and why should not another have a chance? Mrs. Yeobright, I should be glad to marry your niece and would have done it any time these last two years. There, now it is out, and I have never told anybody before but herself.”

text: “You’re obliged to dance at Christmas, for that’s the season’s command; you must dance at weddings, for that’s life’s own occasion. At christenings, people will even sneak in a reel or two, provided it’s no later than the first or second child. And that’s not even counting the songs you’re forced to sing.... For myself, I relish a good solid funeral as much as any festivity. You get just as fine a spread of food and drink as at other gatherings—finer, even. And it doesn’t wear your legs to stumps discussing a poor fellow’s habits the way standing up for hornpipes does.”
source_text: “You be bound to dance at Christmas because ’tis the time o’ year; you must dance at weddings because ’tis the time o’ life. At christenings folk will even smuggle in a reel or two, if ’tis no further on than the first or second chiel. And this is not naming the songs you’ve got to sing.... For my part I like a good hearty funeral as well as anything. You’ve as splendid victuals and drink as at other parties, and even better. And it don’t wear your legs to stumps in talking over a poor fellow’s ways as it do to stand up in hornpipes.”

text: Thus we observe Eustacia—who could, in certain moments, prove far from unendearing—reaching that pitch of awareness where nothing seems to matter, and passing her idle time by elevating Wildeve into an ideal, simply for lack of anyone finer. This alone explained his hold over her: she understood it perfectly. At times her pride revolted against her infatuation, and she even yearned to break loose. Yet only one event could ever displace him, and that was the arrival of a superior man.
source_text: And so we see our Eustacia—for at times she was not altogether unlovable—arriving at that stage of enlightenment which feels that nothing is worth while, and filling up the spare hours of her existence by idealizing Wildeve for want of a better object. This was the sole reason of his ascendency: she knew it herself. At moments her pride rebelled against her passion for him, and she even had longed to be free. But there was only one circumstance which could dislodge him, and that was the advent of a greater man.

text: The cab rolled forward and went by where he stood. It was a rented conveyance, and seated behind the driver were two figures he recognized instantly. There were Eustacia and Yeobright, his arm wrapped about her middle. They swung around the tight bend at the foot of the slope, heading for the makeshift dwelling Clym had leased and set up, some five miles off to the east.
source_text: The vehicle came on and passed before him. It was a hired carriage, and behind the coachman were two persons whom he knew well. There sat Eustacia and Yeobright, the arm of the latter being round her waist. They turned the sharp corner at the bottom towards the temporary home which Clym had hired and furnished, about five miles to the eastward.

text: “Lift your spirits, my love; the clouds will part soon enough. One morning, perhaps, my sight will return as clear as it ever was. And I vow to you now, on my honour, that the moment a worthier occupation presents itself, I shall lay down the furze-hook for good. Surely you don’t truly desire to see me loitering about the house from dawn to dusk?”
source_text: “Come, brighten up, dearest; we shall be all right again. Some day perhaps I shall see as well as ever. And I solemnly promise that I’ll leave off cutting furze as soon as I have the power to do anything better. You cannot seriously wish me to stay idling at home all day?”

text: I’ll leave once I’ve had my say. If anyone claims I came here to accuse you without solid reason, that person is lying. If anyone says I tried to block your wedding through anything but fair methods, that person also speaks falsely. I’ve fallen into dark days; God has wronged me by allowing you to slight me! My son’s happiness, I suspect, won’t be found this side of death, for he’s a foolish man who disregards his father’s counsel. You, Eustacia, are poised on the brink of a cliff without realizing it. Just reveal to my son half the fury you’ve shown me today—and you may well do so before long—and you’ll discover that although he’s as tender as a child with you now, he can turn as hard as steel!
source_text: “I will go when I have spoken a word. If anyone says I have come here to question you without good grounds for it, that person speaks untruly. If anyone says that I attempted to stop your marriage by any but honest means, that person, too, does not speak the truth. I have fallen on an evil time; God has been unjust to me in letting you insult me! Probably my son’s happiness does not lie on this side of the grave, for he is a foolish man who neglects the advice of his parent. You, Eustacia, stand on the edge of a precipice without knowing it. Only show my son one-half the temper you have shown me today—and you may before long—and you will find that though he is as gentle as a child with you now, he can be as hard as steel!”

text:
BOOK THREE—THE ENCHANTMENT  
I. “My Mind Is Its Own Realm”  
II. The Fresh Path Brings Letdown  
III. The Opening Scene of an Age-Old Play  
IV. A Fleeting Hour of Joy and Endless Sorrow  
V. Harsh Words Are Exchanged, and a Breaking Point Arrives  
VI. Yeobright Departs, and the Rift Becomes Final  
VII. The Dawn and Dusk of a Single Day  
VIII. A Strange Power Alters the Flow
source_text: BOOK THIRD—THE FASCINATION I. “My Mind to Me a Kingdom Is” II. The New Course Causes Disappointment III. The First Act in a Timeworn Drama IV. An Hour of Bliss and Many Hours of Sadness V. Sharp Words Are Spoken, and a Crisis Ensues VI. Yeobright Goes, and the Breach Is Complete VII. The Morning and the Evening of a Day VIII. A New Force Disturbs the Current

text: When the reddleman stepped into the garden, the elderly man had his spyglass trained on the distant smudge of blue sea, the tiny anchors on his buttons glinting in the sunlight. He knew Venn as the man who had walked beside him on the road, yet he said nothing of it, only remarking, “Ah, reddleman—so you’ve come? Will you take a glass of grog?”
source_text: When the reddleman entered the garden the old man was looking through his glass at the stain of blue sea in the distant landscape, the little anchors on his buttons twinkling in the sun. He recognized Venn as his companion on the highway, but made no remark on that circumstance, merely saying, “Ah, reddleman—you here? Have a glass of grog?”

text: She hurried to gather the baby, swaddled it snugly, threw on her cloak just as she had before, and smothered the embers with ash to guard against mishap before stepping outside. After a brief pause to tuck the door key back into its familiar nook behind the shutter, she set her face with determination toward the looming wall of starless dark beyond the fence, and walked straight into it. Yet Thomasin’s mind was so wholly absorbed elsewhere that the night and the raw weather held no dread for her—only the tangible sting of discomfort and the strain of the journey.
source_text: She hastily fetched the infant, wrapped it up, cloaked herself as before, and shoveling the ashes over the fire, to prevent accidents, went into the open air. Pausing first to put the door key in its old place behind the shutter, she resolutely turned her face to the confronting pile of firmamental darkness beyond the palings, and stepped into its midst. But Thomasin’s imagination being so actively engaged elsewhere, the night and the weather had for her no terror beyond that of their actual discomfort and difficulty.

text: Eustacia dreaded seeing her husband in that condition, which had become as horrifying to her as the moment of betrayal was to Judas Iscariot. It conjured the image of a haggard woman pleading at a door she refused to unlock, and she recoiled from the vision. Still, it was healthier for Yeobright when he voiced his piercing remorse, for in stillness he suffered far more, sometimes sinking into such prolonged, rigid brooding—devouring himself with relentless thoughts—that she felt compelled to draw him into speech, so his sorrow could partly discharge itself through the act.
source_text: Eustacia was always anxious to avoid the sight of her husband in such a state as this, which had become as dreadful to her as the trial scene was to Judas Iscariot. It brought before her eyes the spectre of a worn-out woman knocking at a door which she would not open; and she shrank from contemplating it. Yet it was better for Yeobright himself when he spoke openly of his sharp regret, for in silence he endured infinitely more, and would sometimes remain so long in a tense, brooding mood, consuming himself by the gnawing of his thought, that it was imperatively necessary to make him talk aloud, that his grief might in some degree expend itself in the effort.

text: “That’s beside the point,” she burst out with sudden fervor. “Let’s set her aside; it’s just you and I we need to consider now.” She studied him for a long moment, then went on in her former quiet intensity: “Do I have to keep feebly revealing matters a woman should keep hidden, and admit there are no words for the despair I’ve felt over that awful conviction I carried until two hours ago—that you had utterly abandoned me?”
source_text: “That’s nothing to do with it,” she cried with quick passionateness. “We will leave her out; there are only you and me now to think of.” After a long look at him she resumed with the old quiescent warmth, “Must I go on weakly confessing to you things a woman ought to conceal; and own that no words can express how gloomy I have been because of that dreadful belief I held till two hours ago—that you had quite deserted me?”

text: Catherine was so taken aback by the disclosure that she entirely neglected to scold the child, who slipped away without a sound. Catherine stirred only enough to shift her gaze toward the grassy clearing where the ribboned pole had once risen. She lingered in thought, then resolved inwardly that she would not venture forth that afternoon, but would instead apply herself diligently to the infant’s half-finished elegant tartan dress, bias-cut in the latest mode. How she contrived to labor so intently, and yet after two hours had advanced no further than before, would have puzzled anyone who did not understand that the fresh disturbance was precisely of a nature to redirect her efforts from a physical to a contemplative course.
source_text: Thomasin was so surprised by the explanation that she quite forgot to lecture the girl, who glided silently away. Thomasin did not move further than to turn her eyes upon the grass-plat where the Maypole had stood. She remained thinking, then said to herself that she would not go out that afternoon, but would work hard at the baby’s unfinished lovely plaid frock, cut on the cross in the newest fashion. How she managed to work hard, and yet do no more than she had done at the end of two hours, would have been a mystery to anyone not aware that the recent incident was of a kind likely to divert her industry from a manual to a mental channel.

text: Initially, Eustacia pressed her mouth tight, as though she would sooner undertake any other deed under the sun than yield to his proposal. Yet the set of her lips eased gradually with reflection, though not to the full tenderness they might have reached, and she replied, “I shall place no obstacle before you; but after all that has occurred, to require me to step forward and extend overtures is to demand far too much.”
source_text: At first Eustacia closed her lips as if she would rather do anything on the whole globe than what he suggested. But the lines of her mouth softened with thought, though not so far as they might have softened, and she said, “I will put nothing in your way; but after what has passed it is asking too much that I go and make advances.”

text: The affection shared by the youth and his mother had become oddly imperceptible. Love, one might note, grows less expressive the further it drifts from worldly concerns. In its purest, most unbreakable state, it sinks to depths where any outward display becomes a kind of ache. So it was between them. Had anyone caught snatches of their talk, they would have murmured, “How distant they seem with one another!”
source_text: The love between the young man and his mother was strangely invisible now. Of love it may be said, the less earthly the less demonstrative. In its absolutely indestructible form it reaches a profundity in which all exhibition of itself is painful. It was so with these. Had conversations between them been overheard, people would have said, “How cold they are to each other!”

text: Wildeve sank into a reverie, his gaze kindling with a private revelation. The sum intended for his wife was something Mrs. Yeobright would not entrust to him. “And yet she can trust that man,” he murmured inwardly. “Why should what is the wife’s not also be the husband’s?”
source_text: Wildeve became lost in thought, and a look of inward illumination came into his eyes. It was money for his wife that Mrs. Yeobright could not trust him with. “Yet she could trust this fellow,” he said to himself. “Why doesn’t that which belongs to the wife belong to the husband too?”

text: “Secured away now. No, she’ll never lay hands on those again. Yet a life can be drained by subtler means than a pistol’s wound. What was the root of that savage dispute you had with her, to push her to such a desperate end? You surely dealt with her cruelly. I never approved of the union, you know—and events have proven me correct.”
source_text: “Safely locked up. O no, she won’t touch them again. But there are more ways of letting out life than through a bullet-hole. What did you quarrel about so bitterly with her to drive her to all this? You must have treated her badly indeed. Well, I was always against the marriage, and I was right.”

text: On one edge of the pond, crude earthen steps allowed anyone who wanted to climb the embankment to do so, and the woman took them. Inside lay a small field left untended, though traces of former cultivation still showed; yet heath and bracken had stealthily encroached, reclaiming their ancient hold. Farther on, barely discernible in the gloom, stood a lopsided cottage, a garden, and some sheds, with a dark stand of firs behind them.
source_text: At one side of the pool rough steps built of clods enabled everyone who wished to do so to mount the bank; which the woman did. Within was a paddock in an uncultivated state, though bearing evidence of having once been tilled; but the heath and fern had insidiously crept in, and were reasserting their old supremacy. Further ahead were dimly visible an irregular dwelling-house, garden, and outbuildings, backed by a clump of firs.

text: Her lips stirred faintly, as though she recognized him, yet no words came. Clym searched his mind for the gentlest way to carry her—she would have to be moved before the dew fell heavy. He was strong, and his mother was slight. He wrapped his arms about her, raised her just a little, and asked, “Does this pain you?”
source_text: She moved her lips, appeared to know him, but could not speak; and then Clym strove to consider how best to move her, as it would be necessary to get her away from the spot before the dews were intense. He was able-bodied, and his mother was thin. He clasped his arms round her, lifted her a little, and said, “Does that hurt you?”

text: The play wound to its close—the Saracen’s head was struck from his shoulders, and Saint George stood triumphant. No one remarked on it, any more than they would have remarked on mushrooms appearing in autumn or snowdrops breaking through in spring. They accepted the performance with the same stolid calm as the players themselves. It was a ritual of good cheer, to be moved through as a matter of course each Christmas; and there was nothing further to add.
source_text: The remainder of the play ended—the Saracen’s head was cut off, and Saint George stood as victor. Nobody commented, any more than they would have commented on the fact of mushrooms coming in autumn or snowdrops in spring. They took the piece as phlegmatically as did the actors themselves. It was a phase of cheerfulness which was, as a matter of course, to be passed through every Christmas; and there was no more to be said.

text: They fell quiet. “Listen,” Yeobright said once more, his voice carrying a trace of dormant emotion. “It strikes me as unkind to Tamsin, letting her marry in this fashion with neither of us present to lift her heart or show the least concern. She’s brought no disgrace on herself, done nothing to warrant such treatment. The wedding being rushed and stripped of ceremony is trial enough, without us staying away on top of it. By my soul, it’s nearly a disgrace. I’ll go.”
source_text: They lapsed into silence. “I’ll tell you what,” said Yeobright again, in a tone which showed some slumbering feeling still. “I don’t think it kind to Tamsin to let her be married like this, and neither of us there to keep up her spirits or care a bit about her. She hasn’t disgraced herself, or done anything to deserve that. It is bad enough that the wedding should be so hurried and unceremonious, without our keeping away from it in addition. Upon my soul, ’tis almost a shame. I’ll go.”

text: “So it ends,” she whispered. “Ah, well—and soon enough, life itself will end. Why must I keep searing my cheeks this way? Weep for a single sorrow, weep for every one; the same strand winds through the entire fabric. Still, we tell ourselves, ‘a season for mirth!’”
source_text: “Then it is over,” she murmured. “Well, well! and life too will be over soon. And why should I go on scalding my face like this? Cry about one thing in life, cry about all; one thread runs through the whole piece. And yet we say, ‘a time to laugh!’”

text: “Shining, you say? What is the moon to someone like me? Let it blaze on—let the world go on, as long as I never face another dawn!... Eustacia, I cannot find a place to rest my eyes—my thoughts pierce me like blades. Oh, if any painter yearns to earn immortality by capturing utter misery, let him come here!”
source_text: “Shining, is it? What’s the moon to a man like me? Let it shine—let anything be, so that I never see another day!... Eustacia, I don’t know where to look—my thoughts go through me like swords. O, if any man wants to make himself immortal by painting a picture of wretchedness, let him come here!”

text: Why did she not come to my door? I would have sheltered her and let her see that, despite everything, my love for her endured. Yet she never came, and I never sought her out, and she perished on the heath like a beast cast aside, with no one to aid her until the hour was past. Had you beheld her, Thomasin, as I did—a wretched dying woman stretched in the darkness on the naked earth, moaning, utterly alone, convinced the whole world had forsaken her—it would have wrung your heart with grief; it would have stirred pity in a brute. And this forsaken creature was my mother! Small wonder she told the child, “You have seen a broken-hearted woman.” To what a pass she must have been driven to utter such words! And whose hand could have brought her there but mine? The thought is too harrowing to bear, and I long for a punishment heavier than the one I carry. How long did I remain in that state they call madness?
source_text: “Why didn’t she come to my house? I would have taken her in and showed her how I loved her in spite of all. But she never came; and I didn’t go to her, and she died on the heath like an animal kicked out, nobody to help her till it was too late. If you could have seen her, Thomasin, as I saw her—a poor dying woman, lying in the dark upon the bare ground, moaning, nobody near, believing she was utterly deserted by all the world, it would have moved you to anguish, it would have moved a brute. And this poor woman my mother! No wonder she said to the child, ‘You have seen a broken-hearted woman.’ What a state she must have been brought to, to say that! and who can have done it but I? It is too dreadful to think of, and I wish I could be punished more heavily than I am. How long was I what they called out of my senses?”

text: Celia chafed at her own scheming. What an utter squandering of herself to stand costumed like this while another basked in admiration! Had she foreseen the full weight of the meeting she would have bent every effort to arrive here unadorned. The command of her features all squandered, the grace of her feelings all masked, the allure of her teasing rendered invisible, only a voice remaining to her; she felt the fate of Echo closing in. “No one here esteems me,” she murmured. She had not reckoned that, stepping among boys as a boy, she would be met as a boy. The cut, though self-inflicted and self-evident, she could not brush aside as unintended, so raw had the moment left her.
source_text: Eustacia was nettled by her own contrivances. What a sheer waste of herself to be dressed thus while another was shining to advantage! Had she known the full effect of the encounter she would have moved heaven and earth to get here in a natural manner. The power of her face all lost, the charm of her emotions all disguised, the fascinations of her coquetry denied existence, nothing but a voice left to her; she had a sense of the doom of Echo. “Nobody here respects me,” she said. She had overlooked the fact that, in coming as a boy among other boys, she would be treated as a boy. The slight, though of her own causing, and self-explanatory, she was unable to dismiss as unwittingly shown, so sensitive had the situation made her.

text: “Nothing but the bones, sir,” murmured Christian, drawing back his fingers at once. “What curious engines of fate these tiny cubes are, Mr. Wildeve! I do believe I could never weary of such a pastime. Might I lift them out and study them a moment, to learn their contrivance? I dared not peer at them closely before the others, lest they reckon me unmannerly.” He scooped them up and inspected them in the cup of his palm beneath the lantern’s glow. “That such trifles can bear such fortune, such enchantment, such bewitchment, and such mastery within them, surpasses all I ever knew or witnessed,” he continued, staring spellbound at the dice, which, as often happened in rural hamlets, were whittled from wood, their pips seared into each facet with a hot wire’s tip.
source_text: “Only the dice, sir,” said Christian, quickly withdrawing his hand. “What magical machines these little things be, Mr. Wildeve! ’Tis a game I should never get tired of. Would you mind my taking ’em out and looking at ’em for a minute, to see how they are made? I didn’t like to look close before the other men, for fear they should think it bad manners in me.” Christian took them out and examined them in the hollow of his hand by the lantern light. “That these little things should carry such luck, and such charm, and such a spell, and such power in ’em, passes all I ever heard or zeed,” he went on, with a fascinated gaze at the dice, which, as is frequently the case in country places, were made of wood, the points being burnt upon each face with the end of a wire.

text: To many, this tract of Egdon seemed a relic stranded from a bygone age, thrust incongruously into the present. It was an anachronism, and scarcely anyone cared to examine it. How could it be otherwise in an era of squared fields, trimmed hedgerows, and meadows irrigated with such rigid geometry that on bright days they gleamed like silver grates? The farmer, riding out, who could smile at cultivated grasses, eye the swelling grain with concern, and sigh mournfully over fly-blighted turnips, offered the far upland heath no more than a scowl. Yet Yeobright, gazing down from the ridges along his route, could not suppress a fierce, primitive pleasure at seeing how, in certain patches where men had tried to reclaim the wild, the ploughed ground, after clinging on for a year or two, had faltered and retreated in defeat, the bracken and gorse stubbornly surging back.
source_text: To many persons this Egdon was a place which had slipped out of its century generations ago, to intrude as an uncouth object into this. It was an obsolete thing, and few cared to study it. How could this be otherwise in the days of square fields, plashed hedges, and meadows watered on a plan so rectangular that on a fine day they looked like silver gridirons? The farmer, in his ride, who could smile at artificial grasses, look with solicitude at the coming corn, and sigh with sadness at the fly-eaten turnips, bestowed upon the distant upland of heath nothing better than a frown. But as for Yeobright, when he looked from the heights on his way he could not help indulging in a barbarous satisfaction at observing that, in some of the attempts at reclamation from the waste, tillage, after holding on for a year or two, had receded again in despair, the ferns and furze-tufts stubbornly reasserting themselves.

text: She searched desperately for some path, however slight, that might ease the present misery, yet discovered nothing. She pictured the Budmouth set, once they heard her fate, whispering among themselves, “See the woman who thought no man measured up!” To Eustacia, the whole circumstance mocked her ambitions so cruelly that if Heaven’s scorn pressed any harder, the grave seemed the sole escape.
source_text: She cast about for any possible course which offered the least improvement on the existing state of things, and could find none. She imagined how all those Budmouth ones who should learn what had become of her would say, “Look at the girl for whom nobody was good enough!” To Eustacia the situation seemed such a mockery of her hopes that death appeared the only door of relief if the satire of Heaven should go much further.

text: “I can’t stop myself,” Clym said, his voice full of distress. “Mother, I despise this tawdry trade. Tell me about men worthy of the name—does any man worth his salt fritter away his days in such unmanly pursuits, when he watches half the world crumbling for lack of someone to roll up his sleeves and show them how to face the suffering they’re born into? I rise every morning and see the whole of creation moaning and straining in agony, as St. Paul puts it, and here I am, dealing in shiny vanities with rich ladies and titled rakes, feeding their shallowest pride—me, with the health and strength to do anything. It’s been gnawing at me all year, and the upshot is I can’t go on with it anymore.”
source_text: “I cannot help it,” said Clym, in a troubled tone. “Mother, I hate the flashy business. Talk about men who deserve the name, can any man deserving the name waste his time in that effeminate way, when he sees half the world going to ruin for want of somebody to buckle to and teach them how to breast the misery they are born to? I get up every morning and see the whole creation groaning and travailing in pain, as St. Paul says, and yet there am I, trafficking in glittering splendours with wealthy women and titled libertines, and pandering to the meanest vanities—I, who have health and strength enough for anything. I have been troubled in my mind about it all the year, and the end is that I cannot do it any more.”

text: As evening fell, she sat beside the fire in the dining-hall, the room they favoured during this season over the parlour because of its great hearth built for turf fires—a fuel the captain liked best in winter. The only objects visible in the chamber were those on the windowsill, their outlines clear against the dimming sky: at the centre stood the old hourglass, flanked by two ancient British urns unearthed from a nearby barrow and now serving as flowerpots for a pair of razor-leaved cactuses. There came a knock at the door. The maid was away, and her grandfather was out as well. After pausing a moment, the visitor stepped inside and rapped on the door of the room.
source_text: It was dusk, and she was sitting by the fire in the dining-room or hall, which they occupied at this time of the year in preference to the parlour, because of its large hearth, constructed for turf-fires, a fuel the captain was partial to in the winter season. The only visible articles in the room were those on the window-sill, which showed their shapes against the low sky, the middle article being the old hourglass, and the other two a pair of ancient British urns which had been dug from a barrow near, and were used as flowerpots for two razor-leaved cactuses. Somebody knocked at the door. The servant was out; so was her grandfather. The person, after waiting a minute, came in and tapped at the door of the room.

text: The bonfire was now burning low, as the fuel hadn’t been the kind that keeps a strong flame going for long. Most of the other fires scattered across the broad landscape were also fading. A careful look at their brightness, hue, and how long they lasted would have told you what was being burned, and from that, something about the natural resources of the area where each fire stood. The clear, regal glow that had marked most of them pointed to heath and gorse country like their own, stretching endlessly in one direction; the quick flares and sudden deaths elsewhere revealed the lightest fuels—straw, beanstalks, and ordinary field refuse. The longest-lasting of all—steady, unwavering lights like planets—indicated wood, such as hazel branches, thorn bundles, and thick logs. Fires made of these last materials were uncommon, and though they seemed small beside the brief, fierce blazes, they were now outlasting them simply by burning on. The big fires had died, but these endured. They held the farthest visible spots—sky-lined peaks rising from dense woodland and plantation country to the north, where the earth was different, and heath was alien and unfamiliar.
source_text: The bonfire was by this time beginning to sink low, for the fuel had not been of that substantial sort which can support a blaze long. Most of the other fires within the wide horizon were also dwindling weak. Attentive observation of their brightness, colour, and length of existence would have revealed the quality of the material burnt, and through that, to some extent the natural produce of the district in which each bonfire was situate. The clear, kingly effulgence that had characterized the majority expressed a heath and furze country like their own, which in one direction extended an unlimited number of miles; the rapid flares and extinctions at other points of the compass showed the lightest of fuel—straw, beanstalks, and the usual waste from arable land. The most enduring of all—steady unaltering eyes like Planets—signified wood, such as hazel-branches, thorn-faggots, and stout billets. Fires of the last-mentioned materials were rare, and though comparatively small in magnitude beside the transient blazes, now began to get the best of them by mere long continuance. The great ones had perished, but these remained. They occupied the remotest visible positions—sky-backed summits rising out of rich coppice and plantation districts to the north, where the soil was different, and heath foreign and strange.

text: On an evening like this, Yeobright came down into the Blooms-End valley from near that same pool, where he had stood with someone else in perfect silence, long enough to catch every faint rustle of nature’s reawakening—yet he had caught none of it. He descended swiftly, his stride buoyant and brisk. Before stepping onto his mother’s property, he paused and drew a breath. The light spilling from the window onto his face showed it was heated and his eyes gleamed. What the glow didn’t reveal was something that rested on his lips like an embossed seal. The lingering mark felt so tangible that he almost couldn’t bring himself to go inside, as though his mother might ask, “What’s that vivid red stain burning on your mouth?”
source_text: On an evening such as this Yeobright descended into the Blooms-End valley from beside that very pool, where he had been standing with another person quite silently and quite long enough to hear all this puny stir of resurrection in nature; yet he had not heard it. His walk was rapid as he came down, and he went with a springy trend. Before entering upon his mother’s premises he stopped and breathed. The light which shone forth on him from the window revealed that his face was flushed and his eye bright. What it did not show was something which lingered upon his lips like a seal set there. The abiding presence of this impress was so real that he hardly dared to enter the house, for it seemed as if his mother might say, “What red spot is that glowing upon your mouth so vividly?”

text: Christian dragged his boots over his feet, and with labored gasps that carried far across the stillness, hauled his frame upright, staggered upright, and stumbled away until the darkness swallowed him. Wildeve began fastening the lantern, intending to go back indoors, for he judged the hour too advanced to set out for Mistover and meet his wife, who was expected to return in the captain’s carriage. As he latched the small horn panel, a shape lifted itself from the shadow of a nearby thicket and stepped into the circle of light. The reddleman was drawing near.
source_text: Christian pulled on his boots, and with heavy breathings, which could be heard to some distance, dragged his limbs together, arose, and tottered away out of sight. Wildeve set about shutting the lantern to return to the house, for he deemed it too late to go to Mistover to meet his wife, who was to be driven home in the captain’s four-wheel. While he was closing the little horn door a figure rose from behind a neighbouring bush and came forward into the lantern light. It was the reddleman approaching.

text: “There weren’t many around here who could match him,” Sam said. “Whenever a club paraded, he’d be out front with the clarinet, playing it so naturally you’d think he’d never held another instrument in his life. Then, the moment they reached the church door, he’d toss the clarinet aside, climb to the gallery, grab the bass viol, and saw away as if that was all he’d ever played. People who truly understood music would say, ‘Surely that can’t be the same man I just heard handling the clarinet so masterfully.’”
source_text: “And there were few in these parts that were upsides with him,” said Sam. “Whenever a club walked he’d play the clarinet in the band that marched before ’em as if he’d never touched anything but a clarinet all his life. And then, when they got to church door he’d throw down the clarinet, mount the gallery, snatch up the bass viol, and rozum away as if he’d never played anything but a bass viol. Folk would say—folk that knowed what a true stave was—‘Surely, surely that’s never the same man that I saw handling the clarinet so masterly by now!’”

text: Rather than inviting him inside, she stepped out onto the porch. They spoke together in hushed, deliberate voices for ten minutes or longer. Afterwards, Mrs. Yeobright returned indoors, and Venn made his way sorrowfully back across the heath. Once he reached his van, he lighted the lantern and, his expression blank, immediately started stripping off his finest garments, so that within minutes he stood once more as the hopeless, dyed-in-the-wool reddleman he had always appeared to be.
source_text: Instead of requesting him to enter she came to the porch. A discourse was carried on between them in low measured tones for the space of ten minutes or more. At the end of the time Mrs. Yeobright went in, and Venn sadly retraced his steps into the heath. When he had again regained his van he lit the lantern, and with an apathetic face at once began to pull off his best clothes, till in the course of a few minutes he reappeared as the confirmed and irretrievable reddleman that he had seemed before.

text: He led the reddleman into the spacious room where the Christmas dance had been held the year before, and they seated themselves side by side on the settle. "Look at the dead fireplace," Clym said. "When that half-charred log and those ashes were still burning, she was living! Nothing here has altered much. I'm incapable of action. My existence drags on at a snail's pace."
source_text: He conducted the reddleman into the large room where the dancing had taken place the previous Christmas, and they sat down in the settle together. “There’s the cold fireplace, you see,” said Clym. “When that half-burnt log and those cinders were alight she was alive! Little has been changed here yet. I can do nothing. My life creeps like a snail.”

text: At times, her expression could sharpen into deep reproach, yet it was aimed less at people than at figments she conjured within—above all, at Fate, which she vaguely suspected was the reason love settled only on fleeting youth, so that any affection she might gain would drain away with the falling grains of an hourglass. She dwelt on this with a swelling awareness of its harshness, an awareness that spurred her toward rash, defiant acts, designed to seize a year’s, a week’s, even a single hour’s fervor from whatever source, while it could still be grasped. Deprived of it, she had sung without joy, owned without pleasure, dazzled without victory. Solitude fed her longing. On Egdon, the chilliest and pettiest embraces came at starvation prices, and where could a mouth equal to hers be discovered?
source_text: She could show a most reproachful look at times, but it was directed less against human beings than against certain creatures of her mind, the chief of these being Destiny, through whose interference she dimly fancied it arose that love alighted only on gliding youth—that any love she might win would sink simultaneously with the sand in the glass. She thought of it with an ever-growing consciousness of cruelty, which tended to breed actions of reckless unconventionality, framed to snatch a year’s, a week’s, even an hour’s passion from anywhere while it could be won. Through want of it she had sung without being merry, possessed without enjoying, outshone without triumphing. Her loneliness deepened her desire. On Egdon, coldest and meanest kisses were at famine prices, and where was a mouth matching hers to be found?

text: “Yes, I feel us growing distant—I see it just as plainly as you do,” she lamented with a sorrowful sigh. “And how wildly we adored each other only two months past! You could never gaze at me enough, nor I at you. Who would have imagined then that by now my eyes would no longer seem so radiant to you, or your lips so sweet to me? Two months—can it truly be? Yes, it is all too real!”
source_text: “Yes, I fear we are cooling—I see it as well as you,” she sighed mournfully. “And how madly we loved two months ago! You were never tired of contemplating me, nor I of contemplating you. Who could have thought then that by this time my eyes would not seem so very bright to yours, nor your lips so very sweet to mine? Two months—is it possible? Yes, ’tis too true!”

text: As Venn spoke, the moon cast its light full upon his features, laying bare every contour to Eustacia’s gaze. His expression was one of mistrust. It was entirely plausible that Venn’s sharp sight had caught what Yeobright’s weak eyes had missed—a figure slipping away from Eustacia’s vicinity.
source_text: The moonlight shone directly upon Venn’s face as he spoke, and revealed all its lines to Eustacia. He was looking suspiciously at her. That Venn’s keen eye had discerned what Yeobright’s feeble vision had not—a man in the act of withdrawing from Eustacia’s side—was within the limits of the probable.

text: Winter returned with its frost-edged breath, its gusting songs, confiding sparrows, and glitter of stars on clear nights. The year before, Thomasin had scarcely noticed the slow creep of the cold; this year she yielded her spirit to every touch of the world outside. The existence of that gentle cousin, her child, and the household help reached Clym only as a sequence of murmurs passing through the wooden wall while he bent over books set in unusually large print; yet in time his hearing grew so finely tuned to these modest stirrings from the adjoining rooms that he could almost see the life they betrayed. A soft, half-second pulse conjured Thomasin swaying the cradle, a trembling drone told him she was crooning the infant toward sleep, a gritty scrape like millstones grinding brought before him the heavy tread of Humphrey, Fairway, or Sam crossing the flagged kitchen floor; a light, youthful step and a sprightly air in a high pitch announced a call from Grandfer Cantle; a sudden halt in the old man’s chatter hinted at a mug of small beer pressed to his mouth, a flurry of doors and hurried thumps meant preparations for market; for Thomasin, despite her widened circle of refinement, lived a comically pinched existence, determined to save every possible shilling for her little girl.
source_text: Winter again came round, with its winds, frosts, tame robins, and sparkling starlight. The year previous Thomasin had hardly been conscious of the season’s advance; this year she laid her heart open to external influences of every kind. The life of this sweet cousin, her baby, and her servants, came to Clym’s senses only in the form of sounds through a wood partition as he sat over books of exceptionally large type; but his ear became at last so accustomed to these slight noises from the other part of the house that he almost could witness the scenes they signified. A faint beat of half-seconds conjured up Thomasin rocking the cradle, a wavering hum meant that she was singing the baby to sleep, a crunching of sand as between millstones raised the picture of Humphrey’s, Fairway’s, or Sam’s heavy feet crossing the stone floor of the kitchen; a light boyish step, and a gay tune in a high key, betokened a visit from Grandfer Cantle; a sudden break-off in the Grandfer’s utterances implied the application to his lips of a mug of small beer, a bustling and slamming of doors meant starting to go to market; for Thomasin, in spite of her added scope of gentility, led a ludicrously narrow life, to the end that she might save every possible pound for her little daughter.

text: From the moment he stepped away from Mrs. Yeobright’s threshold, the ochre trader disappeared from the vicinity of Egdon Heath for a long stretch of months. He melted utterly from sight. The hollow amid the thorns where his cart had rested lay as empty as it had always been by dawn, and barely a trace lingered to mark his presence, save a scattering of chaff and a faint ruddy stain upon the grass, soon rinsed clean by the following downpour.
source_text: From that instant of leaving Mrs. Yeobright’s door, the reddleman was seen no more in or about Egdon Heath for a space of many months. He vanished entirely. The nook among the brambles where his van had been standing was as vacant as ever the next morning, and scarcely a sign remained to show that he had been there, excepting a few straws, and a little redness on the turf, which was washed away by the next storm of rain.

text: Had they merely hinted at some willingness to meet me partway, how smoothly it could all have unfolded! But there’s no hope now. Excluded entirely. She must have turned him against me. Can a form so lovely truly house no heart at all? I believe it can. Even to a neighbour’s cat, I wouldn’t have acted so on a scorching day like this.
source_text: ...“If they had only shown signs of meeting my advances halfway how well it might have been done! But there is no chance. Shut out! She must have set him against me. Can there be beautiful bodies without hearts inside? I think so. I would not have done it against a neighbour’s cat on such a fiery day as this!”

text: The morning after, Yeobright made his way to Humphrey’s cottage and asked to borrow leggings, gloves, a whetstone, and a hook until he could buy his own. Thus equipped, he set out with his new workmate—who was also an old friend—and chose a patch where the furze stood thickest, driving the first stroke of his new trade. His vision, like the wings in Rasselas, though worthless for the great design he had once cherished, proved sufficient for this narrow path, and he sensed that once a little practice had toughened his palms against blisters, the labour would come easily.
source_text: The very next day Yeobright went to Humphrey’s cottage, and borrowed of him leggings, gloves, a whetstone, and a hook, to use till he should be able to purchase some for himself. Then he sallied forth with his new fellow-labourer and old acquaintance, and selecting a spot where the furze grew thickest he struck the first blow in his adopted calling. His sight, like the wings in Rasselas, though useless to him for his grand purpose, sufficed for this strait, and he found that when a little practice should have hardened his palms against blistering he would be able to work with ease.

text: “That’s simple enough to declare,” he replied, “but you fail to grasp the complication. His desire for her far outweighs hers for him, and before I can lend my support to any such prospect, I must have your explicit assurance that you will not meddle to undermine a plan I advance in the conviction that it serves the greatest good. Imagine, once they are betrothed and every detail is harmoniously settled for their union, that you were to interpose yourself and revive your courtship? You might not reclaim her heart, but you could sow considerable misery.”
source_text: “It is easy enough to say that; but you don’t see the difficulty. He wants her much more than she wants him; and before I can encourage anything of the sort I must have a clear understanding from you that you will not interfere to injure an arrangement which I promote in the belief that it is for the best. Suppose, when they are engaged, and everything is smoothly arranged for their marriage, that you should step between them and renew your suit? You might not win her back, but you might cause much unhappiness.”

text: “There is a certain gentleman, if you don’t mind, sir, though I’d ask you not to speak my name to him, for I’ve encountered him in odd circumstances, especially in my sleep. One evening last summer he stared at me with the face of Hunger and War, and it left me so wretched that I left my scant hair uncombed for two whole days. He was positioned, as you might picture it, Mr. Yeobright, right in the centre of the track to Mistover, and your mother approached, her face as white as—”
source_text: “There is one man, please, sir, but I hope you won’t mention my name to him, as I have seen him in strange places, particular in dreams. One night last summer he glared at me like Famine and Sword, and it made me feel so low that I didn’t comb out my few hairs for two days. He was standing, as it might be, Mister Yeobright, in the middle of the path to Mistover, and your mother came up, looking as pale—”

text: Her moods flowed into those locks, and the gentle drawing of a comb through them could always soothe her spirit. While her hair was being smoothed she would lapse at once into quietude and resemble a stone effigy. If, while walking beneath one of the heath’s overhanging thickets, a stray strand happened to snag on a bristling spray of the great _Ulex Europæus_—which serves as a natural brush—she would retrace her steps and brush deliberately against it once more.
source_text: Her nerves extended into those tresses, and her temper could always be softened by stroking them down. When her hair was brushed she would instantly sink into stillness and look like the Sphinx. If, in passing under one of the Egdon banks, any of its thick skeins were caught, as they sometimes were, by a prickly tuft of the large _Ulex Europæus_—which will act as a sort of hairbrush—she would go back a few steps, and pass against it a second time.

text: Charley brushed a hand across his damp lashes, the pane before him swimming faintly, and drew nearer to the window. “It’s Mr. Venn,” he said, “putting the same request to Christian Cantle, and Christian is shifting in his seat like a man half-scared out of his wits, while his father has broken into a song in his place.”
source_text: Charley wiped his own eyes, which were rather blurred with moisture, and stepped closer to the casement. “Mr. Venn is asking Christian Cantle to sing,” he replied, “and Christian is moving about in his chair as if he were much frightened at the question, and his father has struck up a stave instead of him.”

text: “Well, since matters have reached this pass,” Mrs. Yeobright said mournfully, “I will confess to you, reddleman, that I had considered going. I would be far easier in my heart if we made peace. The marriage cannot be undone, my days may be few, and I long to die in charity. He is my only son; and seeing what sons are made of, I am not grieved that I have no other. As for Thomasin, I never looked for much from her, and she has not failed my expectation. But I pardoned her long ago, and I pardon him now. I shall go.”
source_text: “Well, since it has come to this,” said Mrs. Yeobright sadly, “I will own to you, reddleman, that I thought of going. I should be much happier if we were reconciled. The marriage is unalterable, my life may be cut short, and I should wish to die in peace. He is my only son; and since sons are made of such stuff I am not sorry I have no other. As for Thomasin, I never expected much from her; and she has not disappointed me. But I forgave her long ago; and I forgive him now. I’ll go.”

text: Once Mrs. Yeobright had steadied her thoughts, the explanation gathered force, for she could scarcely conceive that Wildeve would brazenly seize funds that were her son’s by right. The half-measure of handing them over to Eustacia was precisely the kind of gesture that would amuse his whims. Yet it stoked the mother’s fury all the same. That Wildeve should have seized control of the guineas at the last, and twisted their destination so that Clym’s portion fell into the keeping of Clym’s wife—because she had once been his lover, and perhaps remained so still—was a torment as keen as any Mrs. Yeobright had ever suffered.
source_text: To Mrs. Yeobright, as soon as she could calmly reflect, there was much likelihood in this, for she could hardly believe that Wildeve would really appropriate money belonging to her son. The intermediate course of giving it to Eustacia was the sort of thing to please Wildeve’s fancy. But it filled the mother with anger none the less. That Wildeve should have got command of the guineas after all, and should rearrange the disposal of them, placing Clym’s share in Clym’s wife’s hands, because she had been his own sweetheart, and might be so still, was as irritating a pain as any that Mrs. Yeobright had ever borne.

text: “I was worried it might interfere with your work—that you’d quit your job or somehow damage your career over it—so I kept quiet. Naturally, if they’d gone ahead and married properly back then, I would have told you straight away.”
source_text: “I thought it might disturb you in your business, and that you might throw up your situation, or injure your prospects in some way because of it, so I said nothing. Of course, if they had married at that time in a proper manner, I should have told you at once.”

text: Thomasin and Wildeve had deliberately set the wedding hour to avoid the discomfort of encountering her cousin Clym, who was due back that very morning. Admitting even a portion of what he had been told would have been painful while the shameful circumstances of the affair remained unresolved. Only once she had made a second, triumphant trip to the altar could she hold her head high and dismiss the first failed venture as mere misfortune.
source_text: The hour chosen for the ceremony by Thomasin and Wildeve had been so timed as to enable her to escape the awkwardness of meeting her cousin Clym, who was returning the same morning. To own to the partial truth of what he had heard would be distressing as long as the humiliating position resulting from the event was unimproved. It was only after a second and successful journey to the altar that she could lift up her head and prove the failure of the first attempt a pure accident.

text: The boy was in fine fettle that day, for the Fifth of November had come again, and he was hatching a fresh scheme to draw his mistress away from the thoughts that consumed her. For two years running, she had seemed to delight in kindling a bonfire on the bank that overlooked the valley; but this year she appeared to have let both the date and the old ritual slip entirely from her mind. He took care not to jog her memory, and pressed on with his hidden preparations for a glad surprise, all the more fervently because he had been away the previous time and unable to lend a hand. In every spare moment he hurried to collect gorse stumps, thorn roots, and other stout fuel from the nearby slopes, stowing them out of plain sight.
source_text: The lad was in good spirits that day, for the Fifth of November had again come round, and he was planning yet another scheme to divert her from her too absorbing thoughts. For two successive years his mistress had seemed to take pleasure in lighting a bonfire on the bank overlooking the valley; but this year she had apparently quite forgotten the day and the customary deed. He was careful not to remind her, and went on with his secret preparations for a cheerful surprise, the more zealously that he had been absent last time and unable to assist. At every vacant minute he hastened to gather furze-stumps, thorn-tree roots, and other solid materials from the adjacent slopes, hiding them from cursory view.

text: The reddleman studied the old man’s countenance. “Well, sir,” he said after a pause, “I had met her before this day, though it might have been wiser if I hadn’t. But she means nothing to me, and I mean nothing to her; and she would not have been riding in my van if there had been a finer carriage to carry her.”
source_text: The reddleman looked in the old man’s face. “Well, sir,” he said at last, “I knew her before today, though perhaps it would have been better if I had not. But she’s nothing to me, and I am nothing to her; and she wouldn’t have been in my van if any better carriage had been there to take her.”

text: “Oh, here they are once more—curse them!” shouted Wildeve, lifting his gaze. The ponies had come back without a sound, standing with their heads high exactly as they had before, their wary eyes locked on the tableau, as though they were puzzled over what business men and lamplight could possibly have in such a place at so strange an hour.
source_text: “O, there they are again—damn them!” cried Wildeve, looking up. The heath-croppers had returned noiselessly, and were looking on with erect heads just as before, their timid eyes fixed upon the scene, as if they were wondering what mankind and candlelight could have to do in these haunts at this untoward hour.

text: The boy was only unsettled by sights and sounds that broke from the ordinary. The heath’s withered murmur never troubled him—it was a voice he knew. The thornbushes that sprouted now and then along his path were less welcome: they sighed in low, mournful whistles, and once darkness fell they had a gruesome way of twisting themselves into the shapes of leaping madmen, splayed giants, and crooked cripples. Lights often appeared on such evenings, but none had ever looked like this one. It was prudence, not terror, that made the boy decide to turn back rather than pass it, intending to ask Miss Eustacia Vye to send her servant home with him.
source_text: Only unusual sights and sounds frightened the boy. The shrivelled voice of the heath did not alarm him, for that was familiar. The thornbushes which arose in his path from time to time were less satisfactory, for they whistled gloomily, and had a ghastly habit after dark of putting on the shapes of jumping madmen, sprawling giants, and hideous cripples. Lights were not uncommon this evening, but the nature of all of them was different from this. Discretion rather than terror prompted the boy to turn back instead of passing the light, with a view of asking Miss Eustacia Vye to let her servant accompany him home.

text: “Mark my words, Thomas,” said Edgar once the elder had departed, “she and Julian Ashcombe would suit each other as neatly as a pair of doves—wouldn’t you agree? If they wouldn’t, I’m a fool struck blind! Both share the same delicate scruples, I’d wager, and are book-learned besides, forever pondering lofty matters—you’d scarcely find a finer match if they were shaped by design. Julian’s lineage stands equal to hers. His father tilled the soil, that much is plain; but his mother had a touch of gentle breeding, as all here know. It would gladden my heart beyond measure to see those two joined in wedlock.”
source_text: “I say, Sam,” observed Humphrey when the old man was gone, “she and Clym Yeobright would make a very pretty pigeon-pair—hey? If they wouldn’t I’ll be dazed! Both of one mind about niceties for certain, and learned in print, and always thinking about high doctrine—there couldn’t be a better couple if they were made o’ purpose. Clym’s family is as good as hers. His father was a farmer, that’s true; but his mother was a sort of lady, as we know. Nothing would please me better than to see them two man and wife.”

text: The time of the events here recounted falls roughly between 1840 and 1850, when the venerable seaside resort referred to as “Budmouth” still glowed with the fading embers of its Georgian elegance and renown—enough to cast a potent spell over the heart of a solitary soul living far from the coast, one rich in fancy and romance.
source_text: The date at which the following events are assumed to have occurred may be set down as between 1840 and 1850, when the old watering place herein called “Budmouth” still retained sufficient afterglow from its Georgian gaiety and prestige to lend it an absorbing attractiveness to the romantic and imaginative soul of a lonely dweller inland.

text: Regarding the matter of being identified, she felt a certain detachment. The players themselves would scarcely know her face. Among the visitors who might gather, however, her safety was less assured. Still, being discovered would not, in the end, prove terribly dire. Only the deed itself could be uncovered, never the real purpose behind it. It would promptly be dismissed as a fleeting caprice of a young woman already deemed eccentric. That she undertook in sober earnest what anyone would assume was done in sport remained, at all events, a guarded confidence.
source_text: On the question of recognition she was somewhat indifferent. By the acting lads themselves she was not likely to be known. With the guests who might be assembled she was hardly so secure. Yet detection, after all, would be no such dreadful thing. The fact only could be detected, her true motive never. It would be instantly set down as the passing freak of a girl whose ways were already considered singular. That she was doing for an earnest reason what would most naturally be done in jest was at any rate a safe secret.

text: “Just so, just so,” murmured Harland. “Now give the mattress a good ruffle. We’ve stuffed it with eighty weight of finest down, and I’d say the casing won’t abide a feather more. A morsel and a sip would suit the hour, I fancy. Colm, fetch the provisions from the nook-press if you can stretch to it, lad, and I’ll pour a drop of something to wash it down.”
source_text: “So ’tis, so ’tis,” said Fairway. “Now gie the bed a shake down. We’ve put in seventy pounds of best feathers, and I think that’s as many as the tick will fairly hold. A bit and a drap wouldn’t be amiss now, I reckon. Christian, maul down the victuals from corner-cupboard if canst reach, man, and I’ll draw a drap o’ sommat to wet it with.”

text: The wild humor of Cedric Harrow spread like fever. The peat-digger caught hold of stout Martha Vye and, with a touch more decorum, wheeled her about in the same fashion. The younger men lost no time in following their betters’ lead, snatching up the girls; Old Man Toller and his cane hopped among the throng like a creature on three legs; and within moments the whole crown of Rainbarrow churned with spinning silhouettes through a mad swirl of embers that sprang waist-high around the revelers. The prevailing sounds were the women’s sharp shrieks, the men’s guffaws, the creak of Susan’s corset-bones and wooden clogs, Martha Vye’s warbling “heu-heu-heu!” and the harp of the wind raking the gorse, which wove a kind of melody for the fiendish reel they danced. Christian kept apart, rocking in disquiet as he muttered, “They should not—how the vlankers do scatter! ’tis courting the Dark One, ’tis.”
