source_text: The vagary of Timothy Fairway was infectious. The turf-cutter seized old Olly Dowden, and, somewhat more gently, poussetted with her likewise. The young men were not slow to imitate the example of their elders, and seized the maids; Grandfer Cantle and his stick jigged in the form of a three-legged object among the rest; and in half a minute all that could be seen on Rainbarrow was a whirling of dark shapes amid a boiling confusion of sparks, which leapt around the dancers as high as their waists. The chief noises were women’s shrill cries, men’s laughter, Susan’s stays and pattens, Olly Dowden’s “heu-heu-heu!” and the strumming of the wind upon the furze-bushes, which formed a kind of tune to the demoniac measure they trod. Christian alone stood aloof, uneasily rocking himself as he murmured, “They ought not to do it—how the vlankers do fly! ’tis tempting the Wicked one, ’tis.”

text: The talk of nuptials faded between them, and presently they came upon a slender forking trail, where the two women took their leave. Before they separated, Olly pressed her friend to remind Mr. Wildeve that he had never delivered the promised flask of wine for her ailing husband, pledged at his own wedding. The broom-maker bent her steps leftward toward her cottage, tucked behind a shoulder of the down, while Mrs. Yeobright kept to the direct path, which soon met the high road beside the Quiet Woman Inn, where she imagined her niece had already arrived with Wildeve after their marriage that morning in Anglebury.
source_text: The wedding subject was no further dwelt upon; and soon a faint diverging path was reached, where they parted company, Olly first begging her companion to remind Mr. Wildeve that he had not sent her sick husband the bottle of wine promised on the occasion of his marriage. The besom-maker turned to the left towards her own house, behind a spur of the hill, and Mrs. Yeobright followed the straight track, which further on joined the highway by the Quiet Woman Inn, whither she supposed her niece to have returned with Wildeve from their wedding at Anglebury that day.

text: Venn gazed at her with a pensive air. "Declaring you mean to sing is far simpler than giving voice to the song; and were it within my power, I would gladly spur you toward the endeavor. Yet since existence holds no weight for me now, bereft of the one element that can never be reclaimed, you must pardon my inability to offer you any spur."
source_text: Wildeve looked thoughtfully at her. “It is easier to say you will sing than to do it; though if I could I would encourage you in your attempt. But as life means nothing to me, without one thing which is now impossible, you will forgive me for not being able to encourage you.”

text: He thought back to her manner with him over the past several weeks, when they had often laboured side by side in the garden, just as they used to do as boy and girl watched over by his mother. What if her care for him were no longer merely that of a kinswoman, as it had once been? To Yeobright any hint of such a shift was a grave affair, and the notion almost unsettled him. Every pulse of romantic feeling that had not been extinguished while Eustacia lived had been buried with her. His love for her had struck too late in his manhood to leave behind enough tinder for another blaze of that kind, as can occur with younger, more boyish affections. Even if he were capable of loving again, that love would be a slow and toilsome growth, stunted and sickly in the end, like a bird hatched in autumn.
source_text: He recalled her conduct towards him throughout the last few weeks, when they had often been working together in the garden, just as they had formerly done when they were boy and girl under his mother’s eye. What if her interest in him were not so entirely that of a relative as it had formerly been? To Yeobright any possibility of this sort was a serious matter; and he almost felt troubled at the thought of it. Every pulse of loverlike feeling which had not been stilled during Eustacia’s lifetime had gone into the grave with her. His passion for her had occurred too far on in his manhood to leave fuel enough on hand for another fire of that sort, as may happen with more boyish loves. Even supposing him capable of loving again, that love would be a plant of slow and laboured growth, and in the end only small and sickly, like an autumn-hatched bird.

text: “Whenever I consider you and your latest whims,” Mrs. Yeobright declared with pointed stress, “I confess I am far less at ease than I was a year ago. I find it scarcely credible that a man who has known the charming women of Paris and other places could be so readily swayed by a girl from the heath. You might just as easily have chosen a different path.”
source_text: “When I think of you and your new crotchets,” said Mrs. Yeobright, with some emphasis, “I naturally don’t feel so comfortable as I did a twelvemonth ago. It is incredible to me that a man accustomed to the attractive women of Paris and elsewhere should be so easily worked upon by a girl in a heath. You could just as well have walked another way.”

text: The resourceful woman trailed the troupe of mummers past the gate set in the pale fence and halted before the open porch. Thick layers of thatch burdened the cottage, sagging between the upper casements; the façade, where the moonlight struck full, had once been white, though a sprawling firethorn now shadowed most of it.
source_text: The enterprising lady followed the mumming company through the gate in the white paling, and stood before the open porch. The house was encrusted with heavy thatchings, which dropped between the upper windows; the front, upon which the moonbeams directly played, had originally been white; but a huge pyracanth now darkened the greater portion.

text: Yeobright set his hand on her arm. "Don't imagine, my innocent girl, that I'm incapable of defying the gods and destiny with a bold Promethean spirit, just as you do. I've breathed more of that fiery mist than you've ever heard whispers of. Yet the longer I live, the more I see that there's nothing truly grand in life's loftiest paths, and so nothing especially lowly in my own path of cutting furze. If I find that the finest gifts we're given aren't worth much, how can I feel it's a heavy blow when they're snatched away? So I sing to fill the hours. Have you truly lost all fondness for me, that you resent my few happy moments?"
source_text: Yeobright placed his hand upon her arm. “Now, don’t you suppose, my inexperienced girl, that I cannot rebel, in high Promethean fashion, against the gods and fate as well as you. I have felt more steam and smoke of that sort than you have ever heard of. But the more I see of life the more do I perceive that there is nothing particularly great in its greatest walks, and therefore nothing particularly small in mine of furze-cutting. If I feel that the greatest blessings vouchsafed to us are not very valuable, how can I feel it to be any great hardship when they are taken away? So I sing to pass the time. Have you indeed lost all tenderness for me, that you begrudge me a few cheerful moments?”

text: Fairway struck a flame to a small lamp, secured it to a second line, and started paying it down into the shaft alongside the first. Clym stepped nearer and peered over the edge. Unfamiliar waxy fronds, indifferent to the turning year, and odd-tempered lichens came into view on the stone walls as the light sank, until at last its glow caught a tangled knot of cord and pail suspended in the chill, black breath of the well.
source_text: Fairway then lit a lantern, tied it to another cord, and began lowering it into the well beside the first: Clym came forward and looked down. Strange humid leaves, which knew nothing of the seasons of the year, and quaint-natured mosses were revealed on the wellside as the lantern descended; till its rays fell upon a confused mass of rope and bucket dangling in the dank, dark air.

text: Then he turned toward home, walking through the drizzle that had utterly changed the landscape. The ferns where he had rested so easily the day before now shed water from every frond, soaking his legs as he passed; and the rabbits that bounded ahead of him had their fur matted into dark tufts by the same wet air.
source_text: Then he turned to pursue his way homeward through the drizzle that had so greatly transformed the scene. The ferns, among which he had lain in comfort yesterday, were dripping moisture from every frond, wetting his legs through as he brushed past; and the fur of the rabbits leaping before him was clotted into dark locks by the same watery surrounding.

text: At that instant, his pulse leaped at the sound of footsteps racing toward him, and two men, alerted by Thomasin, emerged at the top of the bank. They hurried down to Venn and aided him in hauling out the seemingly drowned figures, parting them from one another and stretching them upon the grass. Venn directed the lantern onto their faces. The one who had lain on top was Yeobright; the one who had been fully underwater was Wildeve.
source_text: At this moment his heart bounded to hear footsteps running towards him, and two men, roused by Thomasin, appeared at the brink above. They ran to where Venn was, and helped him in lifting out the apparently drowned persons, separating them, and laying them out upon the grass. Venn turned the light upon their faces. The one who had been uppermost was Yeobright; he who had been completely submerged was Wildeve.

text: “No, the blame is not mine! You could never have kept both; and recall—before I even suspected—you had already veered toward another. That was a careless cruelty on your part. I had no thought of answering with such sport until you first dealt it out yourself.”
source_text: “No, it was not my fault! Two could not belong to you; and remember that, before I was aware, you turned aside to another woman. It was cruel levity in you to do that. I never dreamt of playing such a game on my side till you began it on yours.”

text: On the Sunday following that wedding, a curious scene unfolded atop Rainbarrow. Viewed from afar, there seemed to be a lone, motionless figure standing on the summit of the mound, much as Eustacia had stood on that desolate height some two and a half years earlier. But now the day was fair and mild, stirred only by a light summer breeze, and the hour was early afternoon rather than somber dusk. Those who drew close to the Barrow soon realized that the upright shape cutting against the sky was not truly solitary. Scattered around him on the slopes, a group of heathfolk—men and women alike—lounged or sat at leisure. They listened to the man in the center, who was preaching, while absently plucking heather, stripping fern fronds, or tossing small stones down the incline. This marked the beginning of a series of moral addresses, or Sermons on the Mount, to be given from that same spot every Sunday afternoon as long as the pleasant weather held.
source_text: On the Sunday after this wedding an unusual sight was to be seen on Rainbarrow. From a distance there simply appeared to be a motionless figure standing on the top of the tumulus, just as Eustacia had stood on that lonely summit some two years and a half before. But now it was fine warm weather, with only a summer breeze blowing, and early afternoon instead of dull twilight. Those who ascended to the immediate neighbourhood of the Barrow perceived that the erect form in the centre, piercing the sky, was not really alone. Round him upon the slopes of the Barrow a number of heathmen and women were reclining or sitting at their ease. They listened to the words of the man in their midst, who was preaching, while they abstractedly pulled heather, stripped ferns, or tossed pebbles down the slope. This was the first of a series of moral lectures or Sermons on the Mount, which were to be delivered from the same place every Sunday afternoon as long as the fine weather lasted.

text: Clym Yeobright’s features held a faint foregleam of the face of times to come. If art should ever again reach a classical age, its Phidias might carve such countenances. The habit of seeing life as something merely to be endured, supplanting the fierce appetite for living that marked earlier peoples, must eventually work itself so deeply into the fibre of advanced races that its expression will be recognized as a fresh departure in art. Already there is a sense that a man who passes through the world without ruffling a line of his face or stamping upon it any trace of inward care stands too far outside modern awareness to be a modern figure. Men of physical beauty—the pride of the race in its youth—are now almost an anachronism; and we may well ask whether, at some future day, physically beautiful women may not become an anachronism too.
source_text: In Clym Yeobright’s face could be dimly seen the typical countenance of the future. Should there be a classic period to art hereafter, its Pheidias may produce such faces. The view of life as a thing to be put up with, replacing that zest for existence which was so intense in early civilizations, must ultimately enter so thoroughly into the constitution of the advanced races that its facial expression will become accepted as a new artistic departure. People already feel that a man who lives without disturbing a curve of feature, or setting a mark of mental concern anywhere upon himself, is too far removed from modern perceptiveness to be a modern type. Physically beautiful men—the glory of the race when it was young—are almost an anachronism now; and we may wonder whether, at some time or other, physically beautiful women may not be an anachronism likewise.

text: Stung by the thought of harm coming to Thomasin, the reddleman was at once stirred to cunning. He slipped from the thicket and edged forward on all fours. When he had drawn as near as he dared without risk of being seen, he realized that a crosswind carried the murmured words of the meeting couple away from him.
source_text: The reddleman, stung with suspicion of wrong to Thomasin, was aroused to strategy in a moment. He instantly left the bush and crept forward on his hands and knees. When he had got as close as he might safely venture without discovery he found that, owing to a cross-wind, the conversation of the trysting pair could not be overheard.

text: Nevertheless, it did occur. The shape noticeably abandoned its stillness, drifted a pace or two, and revolved. As though startled, it slipped down the left slope of the mound, like a bead of dew sliding off a petal, and then disappeared. The motion had been enough to reveal more distinctly the contours of the form, and that it belonged to a woman.
source_text: Yet that is what happened. The figure perceptibly gave up its fixity, shifted a step or two, and turned round. As if alarmed, it descended on the right side of the barrow, with the glide of a water-drop down a bud, and then vanished. The movement had been sufficient to show more clearly the characteristics of the figure, and that it was a woman’s.

text: When the apples had all been gathered, Thomasin descended, and the two of them passed through the white gate onto the heath. The bare hills stretched around them, luminous and sharp, and the distant air arranged itself—as it often does on a clear winter day—into separate layers of light, each with its own cast. The nearer slopes lay under a visible drift of saffron radiance that streamed across the land beyond, while beneath it a band of deep blue held, and behind everything the farthest reaches were folded in a cold, grey stillness.
source_text: Thomasin came down when the apples were collected, and together they went through the white palings to the heath beyond. The open hills were airy and clear, and the remote atmosphere appeared, as it often appears on a fine winter day, in distinct planes of illumination independently toned, the rays which lit the nearer tracts of landscape streaming visibly across those further off; a stratum of ensaffroned light was imposed on a stratum of deep blue, and behind these lay still remoter scenes wrapped in frigid grey.

text: “They would indeed, Humphrey. And I’d be right glad to lay eyes on the fellow after all this time. If I had sure word of his arrival, I’d walk out a good three or four miles to greet him and lend a hand with whatever he’s bringing; though I reckon he’s changed some from the lad I remember. They say he can rattle off French as quick as a girl can swallow blackberries, and if that’s true, you can be certain we who’ve never left home will look like mere rubbish to him.”
source_text: “They would, Humphrey. Well, I should like to see the chap terrible much after so many years. If I knew for certain when he was coming I’d stroll out three or four miles to meet him and help carry anything for’n; though I suppose he’s altered from the boy he was. They say he can talk French as fast as a maid can eat blackberries; and if so, depend upon it we who have stayed at home shall seem no more than scroff in his eyes.”

text: “I doubt she holds much of your heart even at this moment,” Clym replied with a flicker of sudden mirth, “else you’d not speak of her with such untroubled ease. Do you mention me to her in that same light tone? Oh, I am certain you must! What first drove you to abandon me? I fear I can never pardon you, save on a single term—that whenever you forsake me, you return once more, penitent for the wrong you dealt me.”
source_text: “I don’t think you care much about her even now,” said Eustacia with sudden joyousness, “for if you did you wouldn’t talk so coolly about her. Do you talk so coolly to her about me? Ah, I expect you do! Why did you originally go away from me? I don’t think I can ever forgive you, except on one condition, that whenever you desert me, you come back again, sorry that you served me so.”

text: “I might nearly tell you it’s just what you deserve—if I weren’t sure you don’t deserve it at all,” Mrs. Yeobright went on, her nature shifting without notice between two moods that sat side by side, a tender one and a fierce one. “Keep in mind, Thomasin, I never went looking for this trouble; from the very start, when you began to lose your head over that man, I cautioned you he’d bring you no joy. I was so certain of it that I did something I’d never have thought myself capable of—I stood up in that church and made myself the subject of gossip for weeks on end. But having once agreed to it, I won’t put up with these whims without solid cause. You must marry him now, after all this.”
source_text: “I could almost say that it serves you right—if I did not feel that you don’t deserve it,” continued Mrs. Yeobright, who, possessing two distinct moods in close contiguity, a gentle mood and an angry, flew from one to the other without the least warning. “Remember, Thomasin, this business was none of my seeking; from the very first, when you began to feel foolish about that man, I warned you he would not make you happy. I felt it so strongly that I did what I would never have believed myself capable of doing—stood up in the church, and made myself the public talk for weeks. But having once consented, I don’t submit to these fancies without good reason. Marry him you must after this.”

text: The day had turned mellow, and Yeobright strolled across the moor for an hour beside his mother. Upon gaining the high crest that parted the vale of Blooms-End from its neighbouring hollow, they paused and surveyed the scene. In one quarter the Quiet Woman Inn could be glimpsed along the low fringe of the heath, while far in the opposite reach loomed Mistover Knap.
source_text: The afternoon was fine, and Yeobright walked on the heath for an hour with his mother. When they reached the lofty ridge which divided the valley of Blooms-End from the adjoining valley they stood still and looked round. The Quiet Woman Inn was visible on the low margin of the heath in one direction, and afar on the other hand rose Mistover Knap.

text: For nearly fifteen minutes, the only sound breaking the stillness was the sufferer’s ragged breath. Then, against the line where the heath met the sky, figures began to stir. Soon Clym appeared, bringing with him Fairway, Humphrey, and Susan Nunsuch; Olly Dowden, who had happened to be at Fairway’s, followed close behind, with Christian and Grandfer Cantle scrambling after in a helter-skelter rush. They had gathered a lantern and matches, water, a pillow, and whatever else their hurried minds could think to bring. Sam had already been sent back for brandy, and a boy had fetched Fairway’s pony, on which he now rode toward the nearest doctor, with orders to stop at Wildeve’s along the way and let Thomasin know her aunt had fallen ill.
source_text: Nearly a quarter of an hour had passed, disturbed only by the broken breathing of the sufferer, when moving figures began to animate the line between heath and sky. In a few moments Clym arrived with Fairway, Humphrey, and Susan Nunsuch; Olly Dowden, who had chanced to be at Fairway’s, Christian and Grandfer Cantle following helter-skelter behind. They had brought a lantern and matches, water, a pillow, and a few other articles which had occurred to their minds in the hurry of the moment. Sam had been despatched back again for brandy, and a boy brought Fairway’s pony, upon which he rode off to the nearest medical man, with directions to call at Wildeve’s on his way, and inform Thomasin that her aunt was unwell.

text: “But do you have to leave for home already?” she said. “I can tell the sand has almost drained through, and the darkness keeps spreading further. Stay a little longer! Wait until the time finishes itself; after that, I won’t urge you anymore. You’ll go back and rest peacefully; I lie here sighing through my sleep! Do you ever see me in your dreams?”
source_text: “But must you go home yet?” she asked. “Yes, the sand has nearly slipped away, I see, and the eclipse is creeping on more and more. Don’t go yet! Stop till the hour has run itself out; then I will not press you any more. You will go home and sleep well; I keep sighing in my sleep! Do you ever dream of me?”

text: During this time he remained perpetually alert for any sign of Clymene. Lest she be uncertain of his whereabouts, he had fastened a placard to the wicket at Thornfield, its pale script announcing his new residence. Whenever a dry petal drifted downward, he started, half-imagining her approaching step. The rustle of a beetle burrowing among the roots of the rose-bed mimicked her fingers brushing the gate's clasp; and at twilight, when low, curious murmurs issued from hollow stems, crevices in the soil, withered foliage, and every fissure where wind, grubs, and creeping things hold sway, he persuaded himself it was Clymene lingering beyond the threshold, whispering overtures of peace.
source_text: During these operations he was constantly on the watch for Eustacia. That there should be no mistake about her knowing where to find him he had ordered a notice board to be affixed to the garden gate at Alderworth, signifying in white letters whither he had removed. When a leaf floated to the earth he turned his head, thinking it might be her foot-fall. A bird searching for worms in the mould of the flower-beds sounded like her hand on the latch of the gate; and at dusk, when soft, strange ventriloquisms came from holes in the ground, hollow stalks, curled dead leaves, and other crannies wherein breezes, worms, and insects can work their will, he fancied that they were Eustacia, standing without and breathing wishes of reconciliation.

text: So far, the reddleman’s rough precautions had worked well enough to curb Wildeve’s urge to wander after dark. That very night, he had cut short any chance encounter between Eustacia and her former lover before it could bloom. Yet what he had not foreseen was that his interference would only shift Wildeve’s course, not halt it. The dice game over the guineas had hardly made him a welcome figure in Clym’s eyes, but a visit to his wife’s kinswoman was perfectly plausible, and he was resolved to see Eustacia. He needed to pick a less awkward hour than ten at night. “If it’s risky to go in the evening,” he told himself, “I’ll go by daylight.”
source_text: Thus far the reddleman had been tolerably successful in his rude contrivances for keeping down Wildeve’s inclination to rove in the evening. He had nipped in the bud the possible meeting between Eustacia and her old lover this very night. But he had not anticipated that the tendency of his action would be to divert Wildeve’s movement rather than to stop it. The gambling with the guineas had not conduced to make him a welcome guest to Clym; but to call upon his wife’s relative was natural, and he was determined to see Eustacia. It was necessary to choose some less untoward hour than ten o’clock at night. “Since it is unsafe to go in the evening,” he said, “I’ll go by day.”

text: On the evening of December twenty-third, the final day of waiting, Eustacia sat by herself at home. She had just spent the last hour grieving over a rumor that had reached her—that Yeobright’s stay with his mother would be brief, ending sometime the following week. “Of course,” she murmured. A man fully absorbed in the bustling life of a lively city couldn’t be expected to remain long on Egdon Heath. The chance that she would meet the source of that stirring voice face to face within the confines of such a short visit was slim, unless she were to hover around his mother’s house like a robin—a course both awkward and improper.
source_text: In the evening of this last day of expectation, which was the twenty-third of December, Eustacia was at home alone. She had passed the recent hour in lamenting over a rumour newly come to her ears—that Yeobright’s visit to his mother was to be of short duration, and would end some time the next week. “Naturally,” she said to herself. A man in the full swing of his activities in a gay city could not afford to linger long on Egdon Heath. That she would behold face to face the owner of the awakening voice within the limits of such a holiday was most unlikely, unless she were to haunt the environs of his mother’s house like a robin, to do which was difficult and unseemly.

text: The July sun blazed over Egdon, kindling the dusky heather into vivid scarlet. This was the single season, and the single weather within that season, when the heath turned truly magnificent. Its blooming marked the second, or noontide, phase in the cycle of shallow transformations that alone could occur here; it came after the green, young-fern phase that signified morning, and before the brown phase, when heathbells and ferns would take on the russet shades of evening—destined, in turn, to yield to the dark tones of the winter phase, which signified night.
source_text: The July sun shone over Egdon and fired its crimson heather to scarlet. It was the one season of the year, and the one weather of the season, in which the heath was gorgeous. This flowering period represented the second or noontide division in the cycle of those superficial changes which alone were possible here; it followed the green or young-fern period, representing the morn, and preceded the brown period, when the heathbells and ferns would wear the russet tinges of evening; to be in turn displaced by the dark hue of the winter period, representing night.

text: “It must be terribly lonely for you out on the heath tonight, ma’am,” said Christian, emerging from the silence he had kept until now. “Take care you don’t lose your way. Egdon Heath is a dangerous place to wander off in, and the wind moans more strangely this evening than I’ve ever known it. Even those who know the heath best have been led astray by pixies here at times.”
source_text: “’Tis very lonesome for ’ee in the heth tonight, mis’ess,” said Christian, coming from the seclusion he had hitherto maintained. “Mind you don’t get lost. Egdon Heth is a bad place to get lost in, and the winds do huffle queerer tonight than ever I heard ’em afore. Them that know Egdon best have been pixy-led here at times.”

text: A rush of warmth surged into Eustacia’s face. She understood at once that Clym, back for only two or three days, could not yet have learned of Thomasin’s distressing entanglement with Wildeve; seeing his cousin living just as before his departure, he suspected nothing amiss. A fierce jealousy of Thomasin seized Eustacia immediately. Even if Thomasin’s heart still inclined elsewhere, how long could that last, shut up here with this worldly, travelled cousin? No one could guess what affection might suddenly kindle between two people thrown so constantly together, with nothing to divert them. Clym’s youthful love for her might have faded, but it could just as easily flare back to life.
source_text: The heat flew to Eustacia’s head and cheeks. She instantly guessed that Clym, having been home only these two or three days, had not as yet been made acquainted with Thomasin’s painful situation with regard to Wildeve; and seeing her living there just as she had been living before he left home, he naturally suspected nothing. Eustacia felt a wild jealousy of Thomasin on the instant. Though Thomasin might possibly have tender sentiments towards another man as yet, how long could they be expected to last when she was shut up here with this interesting and travelled cousin of hers? There was no knowing what affection might not soon break out between the two, so constantly in each other’s society, and not a distracting object near. Clym’s boyish love for her might have languished, but it might easily be revived again.

text: The boy guessed it was a gypsy wagon, and his fear of those roaming folk rose only to a faint tingle—enough to excite, not to hurt. Just a thin stretch of mud wall separated him and his kin from being gypsies themselves. He circled the gravel pit at a careful remove, climbed the incline, and stepped up to its edge so he could peer through the van’s open door and discover the source of the shadow.
source_text: The child assumed that this was the cart of a gipsy, and his dread of those wanderers reached but to that mild pitch which titillates rather than pains. Only a few inches of mud wall kept him and his family from being gipsies themselves. He skirted the gravel pit at a respectful distance, ascended the slope, and came forward upon the brow, in order to look into the open door of the van and see the original of the shadow.

text: “Do you imagine I’ve wanted anything else, even for an instant?” Thomasin replied, her words weighted with a deep sigh. “I see now how foolish I was to care for him, but please don’t wound me by speaking that way, Aunt. Surely you wouldn’t have had me remain under his roof?—and your home is the sole refuge left to me. He claims we can be wed within a day or two.”
source_text: “Do you think I wish to do otherwise for one moment?” said Thomasin, with a heavy sigh. “I know how wrong it was of me to love him, but don’t pain me by talking like that, Aunt! You would not have had me stay there with him, would you?—and your house is the only home I have to return to. He says we can be married in a day or two.”

text: “It is fitting that we have teachers and preachers and men of that kind,” she answered. “But it is also fitting that I should strive to raise you from this existence into something fuller, and that you should not return and make it seem as though I had never tried.”
source_text: “It is right that there should be schoolmasters, and missionaries, and all such men,” she replied. “But it is right, too, that I should try to lift you out of this life into something richer, and that you should not come back again, and be as if I had not tried at all.”

text: By two that afternoon, he had secured a cart and dispatched his belongings. What remained was to acquire some furniture—pieces that could serve in the cottage for now, and later be supplemented with finer items for the house at Budmouth. A market large enough to meet his needs lay at Anglebury, several miles past the place where he intended to settle, and he decided to spend the approaching night there.
source_text: He had hired a conveyance and sent off his goods by two o’clock that day. The next step was to get some furniture, which, after serving for temporary use in the cottage, would be available for the house at Budmouth when increased by goods of a better description. A mart extensive enough for the purpose existed at Anglebury, some miles beyond the spot chosen for his residence, and there he resolved to pass the coming night.

text: Lost in her reverie, she lost all sense of the passing hours. When at last she stirred and noticed the outer world, twilight had fallen. The stack of gorse was complete; the laborers had departed. Eustacia climbed the stairs, thinking she would take her customary evening stroll, and resolved to turn her steps toward Blooms-End, where young Yeobright had been born and where his mother still lived. She had no cause to walk elsewhere, so why not go that way? At nineteen, the landscape of a daydream is reason enough for a pilgrimage. To gaze upon the fence before the Yeobrights’ house felt as solemn as a duty. How odd that such idle wandering should have assumed the weight of an errand.
source_text: Involved in these imaginings she knew nothing of time. When she became conscious of externals it was dusk. The furze-rick was finished; the men had gone home. Eustacia went upstairs, thinking that she would take a walk at this her usual time; and she determined that her walk should be in the direction of Blooms-End, the birthplace of young Yeobright and the present home of his mother. She had no reason for walking elsewhere, and why should she not go that way? The scene of the daydream is sufficient for a pilgrimage at nineteen. To look at the palings before the Yeobrights’ house had the dignity of a necessary performance. Strange that such a piece of idling should have seemed an important errand.

text: Perhaps the dominion of this familiar grace is already waning into its final hour. The fresh Arcadia of tomorrow may be some barren solitude beneath a polar sky; the human spirit might learn to resonate ever more intimately with a starkness that once repelled our youthful kind. Soon, if the moment has not yet come, the stern grandeur of a heath, an ocean, or a peak may stand as the sole landscape wholly attuned to the temper of reflective men. And in time, even the ordinary traveler will seek out the lava fields of the far north as he now seeks the sunlit groves of the Mediterranean; he will rush past the Rhine and the warm spas, indifferent, on his way from the high passes to the cold dunes of the northern shore.
source_text: Indeed, it is a question if the exclusive reign of this orthodox beauty is not approaching its last quarter. The new Vale of Tempe may be a gaunt waste in Thule; human souls may find themselves in closer and closer harmony with external things wearing a sombreness distasteful to our race when it was young. The time seems near, if it has not actually arrived, when the chastened sublimity of a moor, a sea, or a mountain will be all of nature that is absolutely in keeping with the moods of the more thinking among mankind. And ultimately, to the commonest tourist, spots like Iceland may become what the vineyards and myrtle gardens of South Europe are to him now; and Heidelberg and Baden be passed unheeded as he hastens from the Alps to the sand dunes of Scheveningen.

text: “Curious, that. Not five minutes past, I was stretched out here dozing, the door pulled to against the damp, when a rustle of skirts over the gorse just beyond roused me—I’m a light sleeper—and with it came the sound of a woman weeping, or catching her breath. I unlatched the door and thrust out my lantern, and at the very edge of its beam I made out a female figure; she glanced back the instant the glow touched her, then hastened away down the slope. I set the lantern on its hook, and being somewhat stirred, I threw on my coat and followed a little way, but she had vanished utterly. That’s where I was when you arrived; and seeing you, I took you for the same soul.”
source_text: “That’s strange. I was lying down here asleep about five minutes ago, with the door shut to keep out the weather, when the brushing of a woman’s clothes over the heath-bushes just outside woke me up, for I don’t sleep heavy, and at the same time I heard a sobbing or crying from the same woman. I opened my door and held out my lantern, and just as far as the light would reach I saw a woman; she turned her head when the light sheened on her, and then hurried on downhill. I hung up the lantern, and was curious enough to pull on my things and dog her a few steps, but I could see nothing of her any more. That was where I had been when you came up; and when I saw you I thought you were the same one.”

text: “O Clym! Don’t lay the blame on me for what is simply your own stubborn folly. If you were determined to tie yourself to someone beneath you, why come back here to do it? Why not do it in Paris?—it’s far more the custom there. You have returned only to wound me, a solitary woman, and cut short my days. I wish you would give your company where you give your heart!”
source_text: “O Clym! please don’t go setting down as my fault what is your obstinate wrongheadedness. If you wished to connect yourself with an unworthy person why did you come home here to do it? Why didn’t you do it in Paris?—it is more the fashion there. You have come only to distress me, a lonely woman, and shorten my days! I wish that you would bestow your presence where you bestow your love!”

text: Eustacia, watching from the sidelines, could not help but wonder—as perhaps some of the dancers themselves did—how many of those fervent, fleeting embraces might ripen into something lasting. She found herself envying the pirouetting figures, aching for the hope and joy that the spell of the dance appeared to kindle in them. She herself adored dancing with a fierce passion; one of the pleasures she had most anticipated from life in Paris was the chance to abandon herself to this beloved pursuit. Now, that dream was dead inside her, never to be revived.
source_text: How many of those impassioned but temporary embraces were destined to become perpetual was possibly the wonder of some of those who indulged in them, as well as of Eustacia who looked on. She began to envy those pirouetters, to hunger for the hope and happiness which the fascination of the dance seemed to engender within them. Desperately fond of dancing herself, one of Eustacia’s expectations of Paris had been the opportunity it might afford her of indulgence in this favourite pastime. Unhappily, that expectation was now extinct within her for ever.

text: “Yes, Aunt. I’d let you know if he treated me badly.” She paused, her cheeks flushing as she stumbled over her words. “He—I’m not certain I should bring this up with you, but I’m at a bit of a loss. I find myself needing a little money, Aunt—just for small personal purchases—and he never offers me any. I’d rather not approach him about it; still, maybe he simply hasn’t realized. Should I speak to him about this, Aunt?”
source_text: “Yes, Aunt. I would tell you if he were unkind.” She added, blushing, and with hesitation, “He—I don’t know if I ought to complain to you about this, but I am not quite sure what to do. I want some money, you know, Aunt—some to buy little things for myself—and he doesn’t give me any. I don’t like to ask him; and yet, perhaps, he doesn’t give it me because he doesn’t know. Ought I to mention it to him, Aunt?”

text: “If I’d been bitten by a dozen vipers, I doubt I’d have missed even a day’s labor over it,” Grandfer Cantle declared. “That’s the kind of fire I have when my pride’s at stake. Still, I suppose it comes naturally to a man drilled for battle. Lord knows, I’ve weathered plenty—but nothing ever truly rattled me once I signed up with the Local Volunteers in ’04.” He gave a slow shake of his head, smiling as he conjured an image of himself in regimentals. “Back in my youth, I was always the first to leap into the very thickest of the fray!”
source_text: “Now, if I had been stung by ten adders I should hardly have lost a day’s work for’t,” said Grandfer Cantle. “Such is my spirit when I am on my mettle. But perhaps ’tis natural in a man trained for war. Yes, I’ve gone through a good deal; but nothing ever came amiss to me after I joined the Locals in four.” He shook his head and smiled at a mental picture of himself in uniform. “I was always first in the most galliantest scrapes in my younger days!”

text: Thomasin interrupted at this point, her tears still flowing: “He claimed he was just leaving for an unexpected brief trip; but if that were true, why did he need such a large sum of money? Oh, Clym, what do you imagine will come of this? I fear that you, my poor little one, will soon be without a father!”
source_text: Thomasin here broke in, still weeping: “He said he was only going on a sudden short journey; but if so why did he want so much money? O, Clym, what do you think will happen? I am afraid that you, my poor baby, will soon have no father left to you!”

text: You may fight back, and keep fighting! If you showed remorse and laid everything bare, I believe I could grieve for you and feel compassion rather than hatred. But forgiveness is beyond me. I’ll say nothing of your lover—on that point I’ll grant you the benefit of the doubt, since it touches only my own heart. Yet the other matter—if you had half-murdered me, if you had deliberately stolen the light from these weak eyes of mine, I might have pardoned you. But that deed goes past what nature can endure!
source_text: “You can resist, and resist again! Instead of hating you I could, I think, mourn for and pity you, if you were contrite, and would confess all. Forgive you I never can. I don’t speak of your lover—I will give you the benefit of the doubt in that matter, for it only affects me personally. But the other—had you half-killed _me_, had it been that you wilfully took the sight away from these feeble eyes of mine, I could have forgiven you. But _that’s_ too much for nature!”

text: “Oh, you can rise now,” said Fairway. “What a shock it gave me when I spotted him!” he announced to everyone gathered there, the reddleman among them. “Good heavens, I wondered, what blazing scarecrow has arrived to plague us? No offense to your appearance, reddleman—you’re not ill-favored beneath it all, though the outer layer is strange. I only mean to say how odd the sensation was. For a moment I almost believed it was the devil or that crimson phantom the boy spoke about.”
source_text: “Oh, well you can get up,” said Fairway. “What a turn it did give me when I saw him!” he added to the whole group, the reddleman included. “Lord’s sake, I thought, whatever fiery mommet is this come to trouble us? No slight to your looks, reddleman, for ye bain’t bad-looking in the groundwork, though the finish is queer. My meaning is just to say how curious I felt. I half thought it ’twas the devil or the red ghost the boy told of.”

text: She climbed the stairs and retrieved a small box from a locked drawer, tipping out a cache of broad, unblemished guineas that had rested there untouched for years. There were a hundred coins, which she sorted into two equal piles of fifty. After securing each pile in a little canvas pouch, she descended to the garden and hailed Christian Cantle, who was lingering nearby in hopes of a supper he had no real claim to. Mrs. Yeobright handed him the pouches, instructing him to carry them to Mistover and, under no circumstances, to surrender them into anyone’s hands but her son’s and Thomasin’s. Reflecting further, she decided it wise to tell Christian exactly what each pouch held, so he would grasp the full weight of his errand. Christian stowed the money, swore to exercise the utmost caution, and started on his journey.
source_text: She went upstairs and took from a locked drawer a little box, out of which she poured a hoard of broad unworn guineas that had lain there many a year. There were a hundred in all, and she divided them into two heaps, fifty in each. Tying up these in small canvas bags, she went down to the garden and called to Christian Cantle, who was loitering about in hope of a supper which was not really owed him. Mrs. Yeobright gave him the moneybags, charged him to go to Mistover, and on no account to deliver them into any one’s hands save her son’s and Thomasin’s. On further thought she deemed it advisable to tell Christian precisely what the two bags contained, that he might be fully impressed with their importance. Christian pocketed the moneybags, promised the greatest carefulness, and set out on his way.

text: For the rest, her spirits often sank low, and she would take measured, solitary walks to revive them, bearing with her the telescope that had belonged to her grandfather and the hourglass of her grandmother—the latter cherished for the strange satisfaction she found in watching time’s slow, visible drift away. She rarely plotted, yet when she did, her designs revealed the broad, strategic mind of a commander rather than the petty wiles deemed feminine, though she could speak with Delphic ambiguity when directness did not suit her. In heaven, one imagines her seated somewhere between the Héloïses and the Cleopatras.
source_text: For the rest, she suffered much from depression of spirits, and took slow walks to recover them, in which she carried her grandfather’s telescope and her grandmother’s hourglass—the latter because of a peculiar pleasure she derived from watching a material representation of time’s gradual glide away. She seldom schemed, but when she did scheme, her plans showed rather the comprehensive strategy of a general than the small arts called womanish, though she could utter oracles of Delphian ambiguity when she did not choose to be direct. In heaven she will probably sit between the Héloïses and the Cleopatras.

text: Behind them, the converging banks stood stripped of any hedge, except for scattered clumps of furze perched on stalks along the ridge, like severed heads displayed above a city rampart. Whenever the flames surged bright enough, a white mast rigged with spars and nautical gear emerged against the dark clouds. The whole scene resembled a fortification crowned with a blazing beacon.
source_text: The banks meeting behind were bare of a hedge, save such as was formed by disconnected tufts of furze, standing upon stems along the top, like impaled heads above a city wall. A white mast, fitted up with spars and other nautical tackle, could be seen rising against the dark clouds whenever the flames played brightly enough to reach it. Altogether the scene had much the appearance of a fortification upon which had been kindled a beacon fire.

text: “Yes, I know it; I am not raving, Eustacia, nor have I lost my senses at any moment. Do not credit such whispers if they reach you. I am simply crushed by the weight of my own wrongdoing, and that, coupled with my frailty, gives me the look of madness. Yet my mind remains unclouded. Could I recall every detail of my mother’s passing if I were truly deranged? No such mercy. For two months and a half, Eustacia, the final chapter of her existence, my poor mother dwelt forsaken, bewildered and grieving on my account; and I never went to her, though I resided a bare six miles distant. Two months and a half—seventy-five sunrises and sunsets she endured in a solitude no hound should suffer! Strangers with no tie to her would have shown pity, would have sought her out had they glimpsed her sickness and isolation; but I, who ought to have been her whole world, kept away like a base mongrel. If any fairness dwells in Heaven, let it strike me dead this hour. He has all but taken my sight, yet that is insufficient. If He would only sear me with greater torment I would cleave to Him eternally!”
source_text: “Yes, I do; I am not delirious, Thomasin, nor have I been so at all. Don’t you believe that if they say so. I am only in great misery at what I have done, and that, with the weakness, makes me seem mad. But it has not upset my reason. Do you think I should remember all about my mother’s death if I were out of my mind? No such good luck. Two months and a half, Thomasin, the last of her life, did my poor mother live alone, distracted and mourning because of me; yet she was unvisited by me, though I was living only six miles off. Two months and a half—seventy-five days did the sun rise and set upon her in that deserted state which a dog didn’t deserve! Poor people who had nothing in common with her would have cared for her, and visited her had they known her sickness and loneliness; but I, who should have been all to her, stayed away like a cur. If there is any justice in God let Him kill me now. He has nearly blinded me, but that is not enough. If He would only strike me with more pain I would believe in Him forever!”

text: She came back, and Thomasin emerged shortly after. Once she had settled into the carriage beside her husband and the horse began to pivot for departure, Wildeve raised his gaze to the windows of the upper room. Through one of them he could make out a wan, sorrowful countenance observing him as he rode off. It belonged to Eustacia.
source_text: She returned, and Thomasin soon appeared. When she was seated in the gig with her husband, and the horse was turning to go off, Wildeve lifted his eyes to the bedroom windows. Looking from one of them he could discern a pale, tragic face watching him drive away. It was Eustacia’s.

text: Eustacia raised her umbrella and stepped away from the enclosure, climbing the steps over the bank until she was safely out of sight. She circled the pond and took the track toward Rainbarrow, tripping now and then on tangled furze roots, clumps of rush, or soft, seeping masses of fungus that dotted the heath this time of year like the decaying liver and lungs of some vast beast. Cloud and rain had blotted out moon and stars so completely they seemed extinguished. It was the kind of night that turned a wanderer’s mind without effort to tales of nocturnal catastrophe from the world’s annals, to everything fearsome and shadowed in history and myth—the final plague of Egypt, the slaughter of Sennacherib’s army, the anguish in Gethsemane.
source_text: Eustacia opened her umbrella and went out from the enclosure by the steps over the bank, after which she was beyond all danger of being perceived. Skirting the pool, she followed the path towards Rainbarrow, occasionally stumbling over twisted furze roots, tufts of rushes, or oozing lumps of fleshy fungi, which at this season lay scattered about the heath like the rotten liver and lungs of some colossal animal. The moon and stars were closed up by cloud and rain to the degree of extinction. It was a night which led the traveller’s thoughts instinctively to dwell on nocturnal scenes of disaster in the chronicles of the world, on all that is terrible and dark in history and legend—the last plague of Egypt, the destruction of Sennacherib’s host, the agony in Gethsemane.

text: Eustacia considered that she might be unwelcome, or simply unnecessary, during Clym and his mother’s first moments together. In any case, she felt no eagerness to encounter Mrs. Yeobright. She decided to wait until Clym came seeking her, and slipped quietly back into the garden. There she lingered without purpose for a short while, until it became clear that no one was attending to her. She then made her way back through the house to the front door, pausing to listen for any sound from the parlour. Hearing nothing, she opened the door and entered. To her surprise, Clym lay exactly as she and Wildeve had left him, his sleep seemingly undisturbed. The knocking had troubled him enough to provoke dreams and murmurs, but he had not woken. Eustacia hurried to the outer door, and though she was reluctant to open it to a woman who had spoken of her with such bitterness, she unlatched it and peered outside. No one was there. Beside the scraper rested Clym’s hook and the bundle of firewood he had carried home; before her stretched the vacant path, the garden gate slightly ajar, and beyond it the vast valley of purple heather trembling silently under the sun. Mrs. Yeobright had vanished.
source_text: But it was possible that her presence might not be desired by Clym and his mother at this moment of their first meeting, or that it would be superfluous. At all events, she was in no hurry to meet Mrs. Yeobright. She resolved to wait till Clym came to look for her, and glided back into the garden. Here she idly occupied herself for a few minutes, till finding no notice was taken of her she retraced her steps through the house to the front, where she listened for voices in the parlour. But hearing none she opened the door and went in. To her astonishment Clym lay precisely as Wildeve and herself had left him, his sleep apparently unbroken. He had been disturbed and made to dream and murmur by the knocking, but he had not awakened. Eustacia hastened to the door, and in spite of her reluctance to open it to a woman who had spoken of her so bitterly, she unfastened it and looked out. Nobody was to be seen. There, by the scraper, lay Clym’s hook and the handful of faggot-bonds he had brought home; in front of her were the empty path, the garden gate standing slightly ajar; and, beyond, the great valley of purple heath thrilling silently in the sun. Mrs. Yeobright was gone.

text: “Appearances aren’t the whole story,” the reddleman remarked, catching the look. “Plenty of trades earn less than mine does, when it comes down to money; and maybe I’m not so badly placed next to Wildeve. No one’s as poor as those professional men who’ve come to grief; and if my redness puts you off—well, I wasn’t born red, you understand; I only fell into this line on a whim; and I could always set my hand to something else when the moment’s right.”
source_text: “Looks are not everything,” said the reddleman, noticing the glance. “There’s many a calling that don’t bring in so much as mine, if it comes to money; and perhaps I am not so much worse off than Wildeve. There is nobody so poor as these professional fellows who have failed; and if you shouldn’t like my redness—well, I am not red by birth, you know; I only took to this business for a freak; and I might turn my hand to something else in good time.”

text: “Your beauty is a command for Mr. Wildeve. It’s a command for any man who lays eyes on you. They all say, ‘This striking woman approaching—what is she called? How lovely!’ Lovelier than Thomasin Yeobright,” the reddleman insisted, though inwardly he muttered, “Heaven pardon a scoundrel for falsehood!” And she was indeed lovelier, though Venn was far from believing it himself. Eustacia’s loveliness held a certain elusive quality, and Venn’s perception lacked refinement. In her winter attire, as she was now, she resembled the tiger-beetle: in muted settings it appears a subdued, unremarkable hue, but under brilliant light it erupts into stunning radiance.
source_text: “Your comeliness is law with Mr. Wildeve. It is law with all men who see ’ee. They say, ‘This well-favoured lady coming—what’s her name? How handsome!’ Handsomer than Thomasin Yeobright,” the reddleman persisted, saying to himself, “God forgive a rascal for lying!” And she was handsomer, but the reddleman was far from thinking so. There was a certain obscurity in Eustacia’s beauty, and Venn’s eye was not trained. In her winter dress, as now, she was like the tiger-beetle, which, when observed in dull situations, seems to be of the quietest neutral colour, but under a full illumination blazes with dazzling splendour.

text: Out of the shifting pageant, a quieter vision slowly took shape: the heath emerged in shadowy outline behind the brilliant foreground. She was dancing to unearthly music, partnered by the silver-armored man who had moved beside her through all the earlier fantastic transformations, his helmet’s visor shut. The dance’s winding steps were rapturous. A soft whisper reached her ear from beneath the gleaming helmet, and she felt herself a woman in Paradise. Suddenly the two of them spun away from the crowd of dancers, plunged into one of the heath’s dark pools, and surfaced in a shimmering hollow vaulted with rainbows. “It must be here,” said the voice at her side, and when she looked up, blushing, she saw him lifting his helmet to kiss her. In that instant came a sharp crack, and his figure shattered like a pack of cards.
source_text: There was, however, gradually evolved from its transformation scenes a less extravagant episode, in which the heath dimly appeared behind the general brilliancy of the action. She was dancing to wondrous music, and her partner was the man in silver armour who had accompanied her through the previous fantastic changes, the visor of his helmet being closed. The mazes of the dance were ecstatic. Soft whispering came into her ear from under the radiant helmet, and she felt like a woman in Paradise. Suddenly these two wheeled out from the mass of dancers, dived into one of the pools of the heath, and came out somewhere into an iridescent hollow, arched with rainbows. “It must be here,” said the voice by her side, and blushingly looking up she saw him removing his casque to kiss her. At that moment there was a cracking noise, and his figure fell into fragments like a pack of cards.

text: “I’d prefer not to tell you. Perhaps the blame lies with the situation itself, which was uncomfortable to say the least. Oh, Clym—I can’t stop myself from saying it—you’ve put me in a wretched spot. But you have to make it better—yes, promise me you will—because I loathe everything about it now! Yes, carry me off to Paris, and return to your former work, Clym! I don’t care how modestly we start out there, as long as it’s Paris, and not Egdon Heath.”
source_text: “I would rather not say. It may have been the fault of the circumstances, which were awkward at the very least. O Clym—I cannot help expressing it—this is an unpleasant position that you have placed me in. But you must improve it—yes, say you will—for I hate it all now! Yes, take me to Paris, and go on with your old occupation, Clym! I don’t mind how humbly we live there at first, if it can only be Paris, and not Egdon Heath.”

text: “I gave Johnny my word he’d have a bonfire, and he’d be sorry to see it die so soon,” Eustacia said, her tone making it plain she ruled this realm without question. “Grandfather, off to bed with you. I’ll come in shortly. You’re enjoying the fire, aren’t you, Johnny?”
source_text: “I promised Johnny a bonfire, and it pleases him not to let it go out just yet,” said Eustacia, in a way which told at once that she was absolute queen here. “Grandfather, you go in to bed. I shall follow you soon. You like the fire, don’t you, Johnny?”

text: So it was that Redwood, desperate to set things right, had pressed into Marianne’s palm not only the forty sovereigns that were her due, but the forty meant for her brother Edmund as well. His blunder sprang from Varney’s outburst at the start of the wager, when he had hotly insisted the coin was indeed his own. The peddler had failed to grasp that midway through the contest the stakes had shifted to another man’s purse; and that single misjudgment would later breed calamities far outweighing three times the sum in gold.
source_text: Thus Venn, in his anxiety to rectify matters, had placed in Thomasin’s hands not only the fifty guineas which rightly belonged to her, but also the fifty intended for her cousin Clym. His mistake had been based upon Wildeve’s words at the opening of the game, when he indignantly denied that the guinea was not his own. It had not been comprehended by the reddleman that at halfway through the performance the game was continued with the money of another person; and it was an error which afterwards helped to cause more misfortune than treble the loss in money value could have done.

text: After much reflection, I’ve resolved that we must cease all correspondence for good. The longer I dwell on it, the more certain I am that our connection needs to end. If you had remained consistently loyal to me over these two years, you might now have some reason to call me unfeeling; but if you coolly recall what I endured during your absence, and how I quietly tolerated your pursuit of another without a single protest, you will, I believe, admit that I’m entitled to heed my own emotions now that you’ve returned. That those emotions have altered toward you may indeed be a flaw in my character, yet it’s one you can hardly rebuke me for, given that you abandoned me for Thomasin. The small tokens you gave me in our early days are returned with this letter. They should, in truth, have been sent back the moment I learned of your betrothal to her.
source_text: “After some thought I have decided once and for all that we must hold no further communication. The more I consider the matter the more I am convinced that there must be an end to our acquaintance. Had you been uniformly faithful to me throughout these two years you might now have some ground for accusing me of heartlessness; but if you calmly consider what I bore during the period of your desertion, and how I passively put up with your courtship of another without once interfering, you will, I think, own that I have a right to consult my own feelings when you come back to me again. That these are not what they were towards you may, perhaps, be a fault in me, but it is one which you can scarcely reproach me for when you remember how you left me for Thomasin. The little articles you gave me in the early part of our friendship are returned by the bearer of this letter. They should rightly have been sent back when I first heard of your engagement to her.

text: Yeobright hurried up the stairs to complete his attire. During his absence, another knock sounded at the door. Now there was no mistaking it for Eustacia’s—the steps that had come just before were weighty and unhurried. Supposing it might be Fairway bringing a reply in writing, Yeobright went back down and drew the door open.
source_text: Yeobright ran upstairs to finish dressing himself. While he was gone another rapping came to the door. This time there was no delusion that it might be Eustacia’s—the footsteps just preceding it had been heavy and slow. Yeobright thinking it might possibly be Fairway with a note in answer, descended again and opened the door.

text: “Indeed; yet I have wavered endlessly, questioning whether I should, after so fierce an injury. Looking at me now, Thomasin, you cannot conceive the creature I was; the abysses into which I have plunged over these past days. Oh, what a cruel disgrace it was to bar the door against my mother so! Will I ever escape that memory, or even consent to stand before her again?”
source_text: “True; but I have been tossed to and fro in doubt if I ought, after such strong provocation. To see me now, Thomasin, gives you no idea of what I have been; of what depths I have descended to in these few last days. O, it was a bitter shame to shut out my mother like that! Can I ever forget it, or even agree to see her again?”

text: They withdrew to an alcove within the chamber, where a narrower cot held a second form—Wildeve. His countenance showed less stillness than Eustacia’s, yet it was touched by the same glowing youthfulness, and even the coldest onlooker would have sensed, beholding him now, that he had been fashioned for a loftier fate than this. The sole trace of his last battle for breath showed in his fingertips, frayed and offered up during his final, desperate clutch at the face of the weir.
source_text: They went to a recess in the same room, and there, on a smaller bed, lay another figure—Wildeve. Less repose was visible in his face than in Eustacia’s, but the same luminous youthfulness overspread it, and the least sympathetic observer would have felt at sight of him now that he was born for a higher destiny than this. The only sign upon him of his recent struggle for life was in his fingertips, which were worn and sacrificed in his dying endeavours to obtain a hold on the face of the weir-wall.

text: So it came about that the Gallant Trooper of the faithful host could at last scarcely be told from the Paynim Champion by any mark of dress or ornament; and, more grievous still, at a passing glance the Knight of the Cross might well be taken for his mortal foe, the Infidel. The mummers, though they quietly lamented this mingling of identities, dared not risk displeasing those whose aid had so enriched them, and the changes were suffered to remain.
source_text: The result was that in the end the Valiant Soldier, of the Christian army, was distinguished by no peculiarity of accoutrement from the Turkish Knight; and what was worse, on a casual view Saint George himself might be mistaken for his deadly enemy, the Saracen. The guisers themselves, though inwardly regretting this confusion of persons, could not afford to offend those by whose assistance they so largely profited, and the innovations were allowed to stand.

text: She opened her mouth to utter another fierce conviction, yet the sight of his expression stilled the words upon her tongue. Clym paced the chamber once or twice, then abruptly quit the house. When he returned at eleven, he had strayed no farther than the garden’s edge. His mother had retired. A candle burned low on the table, and a cold supper lay arranged. Without pausing to eat, he bolted the doors and ascended to his room.
source_text: His mother parted her lips to begin some other vehement truth, but on looking at him she saw that in his face which led her to leave the words unsaid. Yeobright walked once or twice across the room, and then suddenly went out of the house. It was eleven o’clock when he came in, though he had not been further than the precincts of the garden. His mother was gone to bed. A light was left burning on the table, and supper was spread. Without stopping for any food he secured the doors and went upstairs.

text: “It isn’t Charley,” the Turkish Knight declared, his voice muffled behind the visor. “It’s a kinsman of Miss Vye’s, drawn by curiosity to fill Charley’s role. He had to go search for the heath-croppers that strayed into the meadows, and since he knew he couldn’t return here tonight, I consented to stand in for him. I know the part just as well as he does.”
source_text: “’Tis not Charley,” said the Turkish Knight from within his visor. “’Tis a cousin of Miss Vye’s, come to take Charley’s place from curiosity. He was obliged to go and look for the heath-croppers that have got into the meads, and I agreed to take his place, as he knew he couldn’t come back here again tonight. I know the part as well as he.”

text: The account Diggory delivered of the marriage, accurate in what it covered, lacked one crucial detail, which he had missed because he stood too far toward the rear of the church. As Thomasin shakily set herself to write her name, Wildeve had shot Eustacia a look that declared unmistakably, “I’ve made you pay now.” She had answered softly—and he never guessed how honestly—“You’re wrong; it brings me genuine happiness to see her become your wife this day.”
source_text: The report that Diggory had brought of the wedding, correct as far as it went, was deficient in one significant particular, which had escaped him through his being at some distance back in the church. When Thomasin was tremblingly engaged in signing her name Wildeve had flung towards Eustacia a glance that said plainly, “I have punished you now.” She had replied in a low tone—and he little thought how truly—“You mistake; it gives me sincerest pleasure to see her your wife today.”

text: As dusk fell, he began his trek. The summer heat still pressed down fiercely, but the days had grown noticeably shorter, and before he had walked a mile, the purples, browns, and greens of the heath had dissolved into a single, seamless cloak, flat and unbroken except for scattered flecks of white where tiny mounds of clean quartz sand marked rabbit holes, or where a trail of pale flints threaded like a seam across the slopes. On nearly every stunted, solitary thorn bush scattered over the terrain, a nightjar made itself known, whirring with the steady rattle of a mill for as long as its breath held, then pausing, beating its wings, circling the bush, settling again, and after a quiet spell of listening, resuming its whirr. With each step Clym took, white moths lifted from his path, rising just high enough for the soft western light to catch their powdery wings—light that now skimmed across the hollows and flats of the land without truly illuminating them.
source_text: In the evening he set out on the journey. Although the heat of summer was yet intense the days had considerably shortened, and before he had advanced a mile on his way all the heath purples, browns, and greens had merged in a uniform dress without airiness or graduation, and broken only by touches of white where the little heaps of clean quartz sand showed the entrance to a rabbit burrow, or where the white flints of a footpath lay like a thread over the slopes. In almost every one of the isolated and stunted thorns which grew here and there a nighthawk revealed his presence by whirring like the clack of a mill as long as he could hold his breath, then stopping, flapping his wings, wheeling round the bush, alighting, and after a silent interval of listening beginning to whirr again. At each brushing of Clym’s feet white millermoths flew into the air just high enough to catch upon their dusty wings the mellowed light from the west, which now shone across the depressions and levels of the ground without falling thereon to light them up.

text: Noticing the young woman peering from the window, the elderly woman rapped on the door once more. When no one answered, she picked up the furze-hook, examined it briefly, set it down again, and then turned her attention to the bundle of faggot-bonds. After that, she moved off, crossed over to where I stood, and let out a heavy, labored breath, just so. We fell into step together, the two of us, exchanging a few words, though not many—her breathing was too strained for much talk.
source_text: “And when she saw the young lady look out of the window the old lady knocked again; and when nobody came she took up the furze-hook and looked at it, and put it down again, and then she looked at the faggot-bonds; and then she went away, and walked across to me, and blowed her breath very hard, like this. We walked on together, she and I, and I talked to her and she talked to me a bit, but not much, because she couldn’t blow her breath.”

text: “Before it slips my mind,” he said, “let me tell you how Uncle Capen looked after one of my patients. Long ago, I treated John Ellis—our current postmaster—for a fever. One night, Uncle Capen sat with him; you’ll recall he stayed nimble and brisk right up to ninety. He was supposed to give Ellis a spoonful of ice water each hour. When the first dose was due, he dipped a tablespoon in the dim-lit room and tipped the cold water straight down Ellis’s neck. Ellis flinched as much as a man that ill could, then raised a finger to his lips. ‘Here is my mouth,’ he whispered. ‘Why, mercy,’ said Uncle Capen, ‘is that where it is? I thought it was a crease in your forehead.’”
source_text: “I must tell you, before I forget it,” he said, “how Uncle Capen nursed one of my patients. Years and years ago, I had John Ellis, our postmaster now, down with a fever. One night Uncle Capen watched--you know he was spry and active till he was ninety. Every hour he was to give Ellis a little ice-water; and when the first time came, he took a table-spoonful--there was only a dim light in the room--and poured the ice-water down Ellis's neck. Well, Ellis jumped, as much as so sick a man could, and then lifted his finger to his lips: 'Here 's my mouth,' said he. 'Why, why,' said Uncle Capen, 'is that your mouth? I took that for a wrinkle in your forehead.”

text: “I have to tell you,” he began, a smile struggling against his composure, “about one of Uncle Capen’s dealings. He owned a small parcel of land up near us that the town wanted for a schoolhouse, and he agreed to part with it for exactly what it had cost him. The selectmen, knowing full well that sum was fifty dollars, settled it with him on those terms. But when they gathered to sign the deed, he demanded a hundred. ‘What’s this?’ they asked. ‘You bought it from Captain Sam Bowen for fifty dollars.’ ‘True,’ Uncle Capen said, ‘but look here—this land has run me, on average, five dollars a year for the ten years I’ve held it, what with manure and ploughing and seed, and that makes fifty more.’ ‘But you’ve sold the garden truck off it and pocketed the money,’ they countered. ‘Yes,’ Uncle Capen replied, ‘but that money’s long since spent and eaten up!’”
source_text: “I must tell you,” he said, with an awkwardly repressed smile, “about a trade of Uncle Capen's. He had a little lot up our way that they wanted for a schoolhouse, and he agreed to sell it for what it cost him, and the selectmen, knowing what it cost him,--fifty dollars,--agreed with him that way. But come to sign the deed, he called for a hundred dollars. 'How 's that,' says they; 'you bought it of Captain Sam Bowen for fifty dollars.' 'Yes, but see here,' says Uncle Capen, 'it's cost me on an average five dollars a year, for the ten year I 've had it, for manure and ploughing and seed, and that's fifty dollars more.' But you 've sold the garden stuff off it, and had the money,' says they. 'Yes,' says Uncle Capen, 'but that money 's spent and eat up long ago!'”

text: “You would do me a tremendous kindness, Mrs. Hunter,” the minister said, “—or perhaps Mrs. French—if you could share any details about the life of old Mr. Capen. His relatives appear somewhat touchy on the subject, and they’re counting on a lengthy, traditional funeral oration; yet here I stand, completely stripped of material.”
source_text: “You will really oblige me very much, Mrs. Hunter,” said the minister, “--or Mrs. French,--if you can give me any particulars about old Mr. Capen's life. His family seem to be rather sensitive, and they depend on a long, old-fashioned funeral sermon; and here I am utterly bare of facts.”

text: The chapel brimmed with mourners. Every stall was crammed with mounts, and beasts were hitched along the railings lining the lane on both sides. Rural burials always draw a crowd, and this one possessed a twofold allure. The entire village, having followed old Mr. Capen’s march toward his centenary with keen fascination over many seasons, now anticipated a kind of luminous spectacle, something akin to a glorification.
source_text: The church was crowded. Horses filled the sheds, horses were tied to the fences all up and down the street. Funerals are always popular in the country, and this one had a double element of attractiveness. The whole population of the town, having watched with a lively interest, for years back, Uncle Capen's progress to his hundredth birthday, expected now some electrical effect, analogous to an apotheosis.

text: Before me rests the frame of a gentleman who, having spent his years with no greater honor than simple integrity, nonetheless witnessed some of the most extraordinary upheavals the world has ever known. For a full century, an immense procession of history has streamed past his eyes. I would sooner have lived those hundred years than any other span. It stirs me profoundly to think that he who until recently dwelt within this quiet house of earth links our own age to the era of Washington. Nor can one speak of a soul whose vast longevity gathers us here without summoning the great episodes through which he moved.
source_text: “Before me lies the form of a man who, though he has passed his days with no distinction but that of an honest man, has lived through some of the most remarkable events of all the ages. For a hundred years a mighty pageant has been passing before him. I would rather have lived that hundred years than any other. I am deeply touched to reflect that he who lately inhabited this cold tenement of clay connects our generation with that of Washington. And it is impossible to speak of one whose great age draws together this assembly, without recalling events through which he lived.

text: Consider, for a moment, the marvel of electricity. It is a force we have labeled but never truly understood—one that streaks across the heavens, splinters ancient oaks, sets structures ablaze, and fells a man where he stands in open country. Yet we have taught ourselves to guide it, invisible, from the peaks of our roofs down into the ground; we harness this immense, hidden, mysterious power and press it into service as an everyday courier. And no one today can say how far we will eventually compel its obedience.
source_text: “And think, my hearers, for one moment, of the wonders of electricity. Here is a power which we name but do not know; which flashes through the sky, shatters great trees, burns buildings, strikes men dead in the fields; and we have learned to lead it, all unseen, from our house-tops to the earth; we tame this mighty, secret, unknown power into serving us as a a daily messenger; and no man sets the limits now to the servitude that we shall yet bind it down to.

text: “That’s true, every bit of it,” said Apollos Carver. “When Uncle Capen was a boy, there wasn’t a single railroad spanning the whole width of the United States, and just imagine: nowadays you can ride in a Pullman car all the way across to San Francisco. My daughter lives in Oakland, just a ferry ride away.”
source_text: “That's trew, every word,” said Apollos Carver; “when Uncle Capen was a boy there wasn't not one railroad in the hull breadth of the United States, and just think: why now you can go in a Pullerman car clear'n acrost to San Francisco. My daughter lives in Oakland, just acrost a ferry from there.”

text: He entered the world in this very village. In those days, the settlement stretched to include the neighboring communities both north and south. The countryside was more thinly populated back then, and many homes that once stood have since vanished. As he lay dreaming in his cradle, as his young eyes first opened wide with childhood’s wonder, the great figures whose names we still bless were already scheming to cast off foreign rule. Throughout the carefree seasons of his early youth, the clangor of battle echoed across the narrow stretch of land that then formed the United States. I can scarcely grasp that, as a boy, he heard the exploits of Lexington told as fresh, immediate news from the lips of men still young who had fought there, or sat among a breathless circle by the hearth—or in the old, now-abandoned roadside inn—listening to the tale of Bunker Hill.
source_text: “Our friend was born in this village. This town then included the adjoining towns to the north and south. The region was then more sparsely settled, although many houses standing then have disappeared.
