While he was sleeping peacefully in the cradle, while he was opening on the world childhood's wide, wondering eyes, those great men whose names are our perpetual benediction were planning for freedom from a foreign yoke. While he was passing through the happy years of early-childhood, the fierce clash of arms resounded through the little strip of territory which then made up the United States. I can hardly realize that, as a child, he heard as a fresh, new, real story, of the deeds of Lexington, from the lips of men then young who had been in the fight, or listened as one of an eager group gathered about the fireside, or in the old, now deserted tavern on the turnpike, to the story of Bunker Hill.

text: “You’ve let me peer into an enchanted realm,” Martin said as the ferry churned across the water. “Meeting people like that makes existence feel worthwhile. My thoughts are spinning. I’d never truly grasped idealism before. Yet I can’t embrace it. I know I’ll always be a realist—it’s simply how I’m built, I suppose. Still, I wish I’d answered Kreis and Hamilton, and I believe I’d have had a few things to say to Norton as well. I didn’t see Spencer suffer any real blow. I’m as thrilled as a child stepping into a circus for the first time. Clearly, I need to read more. I’m going to find Saleeby. I remain convinced Spencer is beyond reproach, and next time I intend to join the fray myself.”
source_text: “You have given me a glimpse of fairyland,” Martin said on the ferry-boat. “It makes life worth while to meet people like that. My mind is all worked up. I never appreciated idealism before. Yet I can’t accept it. I know that I shall always be a realist. I am so made, I guess. But I’d like to have made a reply to Kreis and Hamilton, and I think I’d have had a word or two for Norton. I didn’t see that Spencer was damaged any. I’m as excited as a child on its first visit to the circus. I see I must read up some more. I’m going to get hold of Saleeby. I still think Spencer is unassailable, and next time I’m going to take a hand myself.”

text: “I’m pleased to hear you say so,” came the reply. “The thought that my scattered scraps of learning can clear a shorter path to what is true feels deeply comforting. For my own part, I never trouble myself over whether I’m correct. In the end, none of it holds any worth. The final certainties will always lie beyond human grasp.”
source_text: “I am glad to hear it,” was the answer. “That my smattering of knowledge should enable me to short-cut my way to truth is most reassuring. As for myself, I never bother to find out if I am right or not. It is all valueless anyway. Man can never know the ultimate verities.”

text: His mind remained fixed on Joe, and he did not glance toward the door. He caught the faint sound of it shutting. A deep stillness followed. The memory of the knock had already slipped from his thoughts, and he was still gazing vacantly ahead when a woman’s sob reached him. It was unguarded, convulsive, caught short and smothered—he registered that as he turned. In the next breath, he was standing.
source_text: He was still thinking about Joe, and did not turn toward the door. He heard it close softly. There was a long silence. He forgot that there had been a knock at the door, and was still staring blankly before him when he heard a woman’s sob. It was involuntary, spasmodic, checked, and stifled—he noted that as he turned about. The next instant he was on his feet.

text: He moved back to the table, ripped the envelope open, and started reading, letting the stranger collect himself. The stranger noticed and was grateful. He possessed a natural empathy, a perceptive kindness, and even through his outward alarm that sympathetic awareness kept working. He dried his forehead and looked around with a composed expression, though his eyes held the wariness of a wild creature sensing a snare. He was engulfed by unfamiliarity, uneasy about what might come, uncertain how to act, conscious that his movements and bearing were clumsy, terrified that every part of him was just as crippled. He was exquisitely sensitive, painfully self-aware, and the covert, amused look the other man shot him over the letter struck like a blade. He caught the glance but showed nothing, because discipline was one of the lessons he had absorbed. That blade also stabbed his pride. He berated himself for showing up, yet in the same breath decided that, whatever came, he would see it through. His features tightened, and a combative glint entered his gaze. He surveyed the room more casually, with sharp attention, every lovely detail of the tasteful space etching itself into his mind. His eyes were set wide; nothing in their view went unnoticed; and as they drank in the beauty around him, the combative glint faded and a soft warmth replaced it. He was alive to beauty, and here was ample reason to respond.
source_text: He stepped back to the table, tore open the envelope, and began to read, giving the stranger an opportunity to recover himself. And the stranger understood and appreciated. His was the gift of sympathy, understanding; and beneath his alarmed exterior that sympathetic process went on. He mopped his forehead dry and glanced about him with a controlled face, though in the eyes there was an expression such as wild animals betray when they fear the trap. He was surrounded by the unknown, apprehensive of what might happen, ignorant of what he should do, aware that he walked and bore himself awkwardly, fearful that every attribute and power of him was similarly afflicted. He was keenly sensitive, hopelessly self-conscious, and the amused glance that the other stole privily at him over the top of the letter burned into him like a dagger-thrust. He saw the glance, but he gave no sign, for among the things he had learned was discipline. Also, that dagger-thrust went to his pride. He cursed himself for having come, and at the same time resolved that, happen what would, having come, he would carry it through. The lines of his face hardened, and into his eyes came a fighting light. He looked about more unconcernedly, sharply observant, every detail of the pretty interior registering itself on his brain. His eyes were wide apart; nothing in their field of vision escaped; and as they drank in the beauty before them the fighting light died out and a warm glow took its place. He was responsive to beauty, and here was cause to respond.

text: “Fiddlesticks and nonsense! That’s the line they fed us when we were children. Yet there was one truth they kept back—they left us to stumble upon it later on our own.” Olney let the silence gather weight, then said, “And what they withheld was this: every gentleman ought to have been schooled in Latin, but no gentleman ought to know it.”
source_text: “Rot and bosh! That’s what they told us when we were kids. But there is one thing they didn’t tell us then. They let us find it out for ourselves afterwards.” Olney paused for effect, then added, “And what they didn’t tell us was that every gentleman should have studied Latin, but that no gentleman should know Latin.”

text: “I don’t trouble myself with their endless debates,” he remarked as we reached the corner. “Bookish philosophy holds no appeal for me. Still, you’ll discover these men are sharp minds, not middle-class boors. Be careful, though—they’ll talk your ear off about anything imaginable.”
source_text: “Not that I bother my head about their everlasting discussions,” he said at the end of a block. “I’m not interested in book philosophy. But you’ll find these fellows intelligences and not bourgeois swine. But watch out, they’ll talk an arm off of you on any subject under the sun.”

text: So it unfolded. In every place he found himself—at the Journalists’ Hall, among the cedar-paneled rooms of the Elmwood, at rose-scented receptions and literary soirées—the talk invariably circled back to “The Chime of Voices” and “The Sylph and the Sapphire” when those works first saw print. And always, Martin’s furious and silent cry rose within him: Why did you not nourish me then? The labor was already done. “The Chime of Voices” and “The Sylph and the Sapphire” remain untouched in any syllable. They were as finely wrought, as full of merit, in that earlier hour as they are today. Yet you do not sustain me for their excellence, nor for the sake of any other page I have penned. You sustain me because that is the current mode of sustenance, because the entire multitude has grown wild with the notion of sustaining Martin Eden.
source_text: So it went. Wherever he happened to be—at the Press Club, at the Redwood Club, at pink teas and literary gatherings—always were remembered “The Ring of Bells” and “The Peri and the Pearl” when they were first published. And always was Martin’s maddening and unuttered demand: Why didn’t you feed me then? It was work performed. “The Ring of Bells” and “The Peri and the Pearl” are not changed one iota. They were just as artistic, just as worth while, then as now. But you are not feeding me for their sake, nor for the sake of anything else I have written. You’re feeding me because it is the style of feeding just now, because the whole mob is crazy with the idea of feeding Martin Eden.

text: He discovered, too, that each evening he addressed the laborers gathered in City Hall Park, and that amid the radicals and rabble-rousers who stirred the crowd’s passions, he attracted the biggest gatherings and delivered the most incendiary orations. The young reporter sketched a vivid portrait of that cramped little room, with its kerosene heater and single chair, and of the gaunt, skull-faced drifter who shared his quarters—a man who seemed as though he had only just stepped out of two decades of isolation in some prison stronghold.
source_text: He learned, also, that he spoke nightly to the workmen in the City Hall Park, and that among the anarchists and agitators that there inflamed the minds of the people he drew the largest audiences and made the most revolutionary speeches. The cub painted a high-light picture of his poor little room, its oil-stove and the one chair, and of the death’s-head tramp who kept him company and who looked as if he had just emerged from twenty years of solitary confinement in some fortress dungeon.

text: An oil painting seized his attention. A massive surf roared and shattered against a jutting rock; dark storm clouds smothered the sky; and beyond the breaking waves, a pilot schooner, sails hauled tight, leaned so steeply that every line of her deck lay exposed as she drove forward against a turbulent sunset. There was beauty in it, and it pulled him with irresistible force. He forgot his clumsy gait and moved nearer to the painting, very near. The beauty dissolved from the canvas. His face clouded with bewilderment. He peered at what now looked like a careless smear of paint, then stepped back. Instantly all the beauty flared back into the canvas. “A trick picture,” he thought, dismissing it, though amid the countless impressions flooding in on him he still found room for a stab of resentment that so much beauty should be wasted on a trick. He knew nothing of painting. He had been raised on chromolithographs that stayed crisp and distinct, whether viewed up close or from a distance. He had seen oil paintings, it was true, in shop windows, but the glass had always kept his hungry eyes from pressing too near.
source_text: An oil painting caught and held him. A heavy surf thundered and burst over an outjutting rock; lowering storm-clouds covered the sky; and, outside the line of surf, a pilot-schooner, close-hauled, heeled over till every detail of her deck was visible, was surging along against a stormy sunset sky. There was beauty, and it drew him irresistibly. He forgot his awkward walk and came closer to the painting, very close. The beauty faded out of the canvas. His face expressed his bepuzzlement. He stared at what seemed a careless daub of paint, then stepped away. Immediately all the beauty flashed back into the canvas. “A trick picture,” was his thought, as he dismissed it, though in the midst of the multitudinous impressions he was receiving he found time to feel a prod of indignation that so much beauty should be sacrificed to make a trick. He did not know painting. He had been brought up on chromos and lithographs that were always definite and sharp, near or far. He had seen oil paintings, it was true, in the show windows of shops, but the glass of the windows had prevented his eager eyes from approaching too near.

text: Ruth had only a small thing to ask—just that Norman had planned to escort her to a lecture that evening, but he’d come down with a headache, and she felt so let down, and she still held the tickets, and if he happened to be free, might he be kind enough to accompany her instead?
source_text: It was not much that Ruth wanted to say—merely that Norman had been going to take her to a lecture that night, but that he had a headache, and she was so disappointed, and she had the tickets, and that if he had no other engagement, would he be good enough to take her?

text: Yes—and twice in the months that came after, you walked right past me on the pavement without a flicker of recognition, Martin nearly spoke the words into the air. Both times I was hollow with hunger, making my way toward the pawnshop. Still, that labor was done, completed. You didn’t know me then. Why is it you know me now?
source_text: Yes, and twice in the months that followed you passed me on the street and did not know me, Martin almost said aloud. Each time I was hungry and heading for the pawnbroker. Yet it was work performed. You did not know me then. Why do you know me now?

text: “It’s preposterous and unjust,” he had said to Ruth a few weeks earlier, “this ban on talking shop. What on earth draws men and women together, if not to share the finest parts of themselves? And their finest part is whatever absorbs them—the work they live by, the craft they’ve mastered through sleepless nights and even dreamt about. Picture Mr. Butler shackled by polite convention, holding forth on Paul Verlaine or German theatre or D’Annunzio’s novels. We’d perish of tedium. For my part, if I must endure Mr. Butler’s company, I’d rather hear him speak of his law. That’s where his excellence lies, and life is far too brief not to seek the best from every soul I encounter.”
source_text: “It’s absurd and unfair,” he had told Ruth weeks before, “this objection to talking shop. For what reason under the sun do men and women come together if not for the exchange of the best that is in them? And the best that is in them is what they are interested in, the thing by which they make their living, the thing they’ve specialized on and sat up days and nights over, and even dreamed about. Imagine Mr. Butler living up to social etiquette and enunciating his views on Paul Verlaine or the German drama or the novels of D’Annunzio. We’d be bored to death. I, for one, if I must listen to Mr. Butler, prefer to hear him talk about his law. It’s the best that is in him, and life is so short that I want the best of every man and woman I meet.”

text: He marveled at the vast sea of printed words that lay inert. No radiance, no vitality, no hue, pierced through it. There was not a whisper of existence in it, yet it fetched two cents a word, twenty dollars a thousand—the newspaper snippet had declared it. He was confounded by innumerable short tales, crafted with a deft and witty hand he admitted, but drained of vigor or truth. Existence was so peculiar and magnificent, brimming with a multitude of quandaries, of visions, and of valiant struggles, and still these narratives dwelled only on the banalities of living. He sensed the pressure and ache of life, its ardors and drenches and fierce upheavals—surely this was the substance to inscribe! He yearned to exalt the champions of doomed ventures, the wild-eyed lovers, the titans who strove amid pressure and ache, through dread and calamity, making existence snap with the force of their striving. And yet the magazine stories appeared bent on exalting the Mr. Butlers, the grubby wealth-seekers, and the humdrum little amours of humdrum little folk. Was it that the periodical editors were humdrum? he questioned. Or did they shrink from life, these scribes and arbiters and devotees?
source_text: He was amazed at the immense amount of printed stuff that was dead. No light, no life, no color, was shot through it. There was no breath of life in it, and yet it sold, at two cents a word, twenty dollars a thousand—the newspaper clipping had said so. He was puzzled by countless short stories, written lightly and cleverly he confessed, but without vitality or reality. Life was so strange and wonderful, filled with an immensity of problems, of dreams, and of heroic toils, and yet these stories dealt only with the commonplaces of life. He felt the stress and strain of life, its fevers and sweats and wild insurgences—surely this was the stuff to write about! He wanted to glorify the leaders of forlorn hopes, the mad lovers, the giants that fought under stress and strain, amid terror and tragedy, making life crackle with the strength of their endeavor. And yet the magazine short stories seemed intent on glorifying the Mr. Butlers, the sordid dollar-chasers, and the commonplace little love affairs of commonplace little men and women. Was it because the editors of the magazines were commonplace? he demanded. Or were they afraid of life, these writers and editors and readers?

text: The transformation cut deeper than mere surface changes. The cigarettes remained, but the liquor had stopped. Before this, he had considered drinking a masculine duty and had taken satisfaction in his iron constitution, which allowed him to outlast nearly any companion at the bar. When he ran into an old sailing friend—and San Francisco held plenty—he stood rounds as he always had, accepting their hospitality in return, yet now he called for sarsaparilla or soda water and bore their teasing with quiet amusement. As they grew sodden, he observed them, watching the animal surface and seize command, silently grateful that he no longer belonged to their company. They drank to shed their confines, and in their stupor their dull, cramped souls imagined themselves deities, each enthroned in a private paradise of drunken craving. For Martin, the thirst for spirits had simply dissolved. He was intoxicated now by deeper, stranger drafts—by Ruth, who had kindled love in him and offered a vision of a loftier, timeless existence; by books, which had loosed a thousand hungry worms of longing to burrow through his mind; and by the growing sensation of his own purity, which bestowed on him a vitality even more radiant than before and set his entire frame humming with bodily joy.
source_text: But the reform went deeper than mere outward appearance. He still smoked, but he drank no more. Up to that time, drinking had seemed to him the proper thing for men to do, and he had prided himself on his strong head which enabled him to drink most men under the table. Whenever he encountered a chance shipmate, and there were many in San Francisco, he treated them and was treated in turn, as of old, but he ordered for himself root beer or ginger ale and good-naturedly endured their chaffing. And as they waxed maudlin he studied them, watching the beast rise and master them and thanking God that he was no longer as they. They had their limitations to forget, and when they were drunk, their dim, stupid spirits were even as gods, and each ruled in his heaven of intoxicated desire. With Martin the need for strong drink had vanished. He was drunken in new and more profound ways—with Ruth, who had fired him with love and with a glimpse of higher and eternal life; with books, that had set a myriad maggots of desire gnawing in his brain; and with the sense of personal cleanliness he was achieving, that gave him even more superb health than what he had enjoyed and that made his whole body sing with physical well-being.

text: He saw the ghost of his younger self, day after day, darting from school toward the _Enquirer_ alley. He could barely quicken his pace. His body was rigid and hobbled by the endless brawling. His forearms, from wrist to elbow, were mottled with bruises from the innumerable strikes he had deflected, and in places the battered skin was beginning to suppurate. His head, his arms, his shoulders throbbed; the base of his spine ached—his whole frame was one dull, pervasive pain, and his mind felt sodden and stupefied. He did not join in the schoolyard games. He did not study. Merely to sit motionless all day at his desk, as he did, was an agony. It felt like ages since he had entered this cycle of daily combat, and time yawned ahead into a nightmarish, unending succession of daily fights. Why couldn’t Cheese-Face be beaten? he often wondered; that would free him, Martin, from his suffering. The thought of quitting, of letting Cheese-Face thrash him, never once crossed his mind.
source_text: He watched the youthful apparition of himself, day after day, hurrying from school to the _Enquirer_ alley. He could not walk very fast. He was stiff and lame from the incessant fighting. His forearms were black and blue from wrist to elbow, what of the countless blows he had warded off, and here and there the tortured flesh was beginning to fester. His head and arms and shoulders ached, the small of his back ached,—he ached all over, and his brain was heavy and dazed. He did not play at school. Nor did he study. Even to sit still all day at his desk, as he did, was a torment. It seemed centuries since he had begun the round of daily fights, and time stretched away into a nightmare and infinite future of daily fights. Why couldn’t Cheese-Face be licked? he often thought; that would put him, Martin, out of his misery. It never entered his head to cease fighting, to allow Cheese-Face to whip him.

text: At dawn he asked Maria for a dime. He’d rather have gotten it from Brissenden, but that unpredictable man had vanished. Two weeks had slipped by since Martin last encountered him, and he racked his mind in vain for any slight he might have given. The dime took Martin on the ferry to San Francisco, and as he strode up Market Street he considered what would become of him if he couldn’t obtain the money. He’d have no means to get back to Oakland, and he was acquainted with no one in San Francisco who could lend him another dime.
source_text: In the morning he borrowed ten cents from Maria. He would have borrowed it, by preference, from Brissenden, but that erratic individual had disappeared. Two weeks had passed since Martin had seen him, and he vainly cudgelled his brains for some cause of offence. The ten cents carried Martin across the ferry to San Francisco, and as he walked up Market Street he speculated upon his predicament in case he failed to collect the money. There would then be no way for him to return to Oakland, and he knew no one in San Francisco from whom to borrow another ten cents.

text: The broadsheets tallied up Edmund’s earnings from his books. Somehow the lavish sums that prestigious periodicals had dangled before him became public knowledge, and clergymen from the town called on him with cordial overtures, while a tide of calculated charity appeals began to choke his correspondence. Yet none of this compared to the women. His likeness was scattered far and wide, and columnists made much of his sturdy, sun-darkened features, his old wounds, his broad frame, his tranquil, steady gaze, and the faint hollows beneath his cheekbones that suggested a monk’s austerity. At that detail he recalled his reckless younger days and grinned. Frequently, among the ladies he encountered, he would catch first one, then another, studying him, measuring him, singling him out. He chuckled inwardly. He thought of Brissenden’s caution and chuckled once more. These women would never be his undoing, of that he felt sure. He had long since moved beyond such peril.
source_text: The newspapers calculated Martin’s royalties. In some way the magnificent offers certain magazines had made him leaked out, and Oakland ministers called upon him in a friendly way, while professional begging letters began to clutter his mail. But worse than all this were the women. His photographs were published broadcast, and special writers exploited his strong, bronzed face, his scars, his heavy shoulders, his clear, quiet eyes, and the slight hollows in his cheeks like an ascetic’s. At this last he remembered his wild youth and smiled. Often, among the women he met, he would see now one, now another, looking at him, appraising him, selecting him. He laughed to himself. He remembered Brissenden’s warning and laughed again. The women would never destroy him, that much was certain. He had gone past that stage.

text: As drowsiness creeps in, I trade the weighty volume in my hands for something less demanding. When that, too, fails to hold me, I rap my knuckles against my skull to beat back the encroaching fog. I once read a tale—Kipling, I think—about a man so terrified of slumber that he rigged a spur beneath him, so that whenever unconsciousness stole over him, his bare flesh would meet the bite of iron. I’ve done much the same. I glance at the clock and vow that the spur will not be withdrawn until midnight, or one, or two, or three in the morning. And so it jabs me into wakefulness until the hour I’ve set. That spur has shared my bed for months now. I’ve grown so reckless that five and a half hours of rest feels like a sinful indulgence. I allow myself four. I am ravenous for sleep. At times, the deprivation makes my thoughts swim and blur; at times, death itself, with its promise of stillness and oblivion, beckons like a seduction; at times, I am pursued by Longfellow’s verses:
source_text: “When I begin to feel drowsy, I change the heavy book I am reading for a lighter one. And when I doze over that, I beat my head with my knuckles in order to drive sleep away. Somewhere I read of a man who was afraid to sleep. Kipling wrote the story. This man arranged a spur so that when unconsciousness came, his naked body pressed against the iron teeth. Well, I’ve done the same. I look at the time, and I resolve that not until midnight, or not until one o’clock, or two o’clock, or three o’clock, shall the spur be removed. And so it rowels me awake until the appointed time. That spur has been my bed-mate for months. I have grown so desperate that five and a half hours of sleep is an extravagance. I sleep four hours now. I am starved for sleep. There are times when I am light-headed from want of sleep, times when death, with its rest and sleep, is a positive lure to me, times when I am haunted by Longfellow’s lines:

text: He was perpetually burdened by her distance. She existed on a plane so removed from his own that he felt utterly at a loss to bridge the gap. Among women of his own station, he had known easy conquests, yet none of them had stirred his heart; she, however, commanded a love that lifted her beyond mere social distinction. His devotion itself set her apart, rendering her so singular, so unreachable, that he could not fathom how a lover might properly close the space between them. It was true that, as his learning deepened and his speech grew more refined, he was edging closer—speaking her tongue, uncovering shared pleasures and thoughts—but this fell short of his lover’s ache. His adoring fancy had sanctified her, made her too ethereal, too sacred, to share any mortal bond with him. It was his own passion that pushed her away and fashioned her into an impossibility. Love, by its very nature, withheld the prize it most craved.
source_text: But he was oppressed always by her remoteness. She was so far from him, and he did not know how to approach her. He had been a success with girls and women in his own class; but he had never loved any of them, while he did love her, and besides, she was not merely of another class. His very love elevated her above all classes. She was a being apart, so far apart that he did not know how to draw near to her as a lover should draw near. It was true, as he acquired knowledge and language, that he was drawing nearer, talking her speech, discovering ideas and delights in common; but this did not satisfy his lover’s yearning. His lover’s imagination had made her holy, too holy, too spiritualized, to have any kinship with him in the flesh. It was his own love that thrust her from him and made her seem impossible for him. Love itself denied him the one thing that it desired.

text: “There’s nothing else like it in all of writing,” Martin said once he finally found his voice. “It’s astonishing—absolutely astonishing! It’s swept straight into my brain. I’m intoxicated by it. That vast, minute question—I can’t get it out of my head. That searching, timeless, endlessly returning, faint little human cry still echoes in my ears. It’s like a gnat’s funeral dirge set against the trumpeting of elephants and the roar of lions. It’s ravenous with tiny longings. I know I’m babbling now, but the thing has taken hold of me. You are—I can’t even say what you are—you’re simply extraordinary. But how do you manage it? How on earth do you manage it?”
source_text: “There is nothing like it in literature,” Martin said, when at last he was able to speak. “It’s wonderful!—wonderful! It has gone to my head. I am drunken with it. That great, infinitesimal question—I can’t shake it out of my thoughts. That questing, eternal, ever recurring, thin little wailing voice of man is still ringing in my ears. It is like the dead-march of a gnat amid the trumpeting of elephants and the roaring of lions. It is insatiable with microscopic desire. I now I’m making a fool of myself, but the thing has obsessed me. You are—I don’t know what you are—you are wonderful, that’s all. But how do you do it? How do you do it?”

text: Martin did not laugh, nor did he clench his jaw in fury. What washed over him instead was a profound sorrow. Amid the collapse of his entire universe, with love perched at its summit, the downfall of the magazine world and its cherished readership seemed a trivial wreck. Brissenden had been utterly correct about the magazines, and he, Martin, had wasted years of grueling effort just to learn that truth for himself. The magazines were everything Brissenden had claimed—and worse. Well, it was finished, he consoled himself. He had tethered his wagon to a star and ended up mired in a foul swamp. The daydreams of Tahiti—pristine, fragrant Tahiti—visited him more often now. And there were the low Paumotus, and the towering Marquesas; he frequently pictured himself aboard trading schooners or slender little cutters, gliding out at daybreak past the reef at Papeete and commencing the long tack through the pearl-atolls toward Nukahiva and the Bay of Taiohae, where Tamari, he was certain, would slaughter a pig to celebrate his arrival, and where Tamari’s daughters, wreathed in blossoms, would clasp his hands and, with singing and laughter, crown him with flowers. The South Seas were summoning him, and he understood that sooner or later he would heed that summons.
source_text: Martin did not laugh; nor did he grit his teeth in anger. The effect produced upon him was one of great sadness. In the crash of his whole world, with love on the pinnacle, the crash of magazinedom and the dear public was a small crash indeed. Brissenden had been wholly right in his judgment of the magazines, and he, Martin, had spent arduous and futile years in order to find it out for himself. The magazines were all Brissenden had said they were and more. Well, he was done, he solaced himself. He had hitched his wagon to a star and been landed in a pestiferous marsh. The visions of Tahiti—clean, sweet Tahiti—were coming to him more frequently. And there were the low Paumotus, and the high Marquesas; he saw himself often, now, on board trading schooners or frail little cutters, slipping out at dawn through the reef at Papeete and beginning the long beat through the pearl-atolls to Nukahiva and the Bay of Taiohae, where Tamari, he knew, would kill a pig in honor of his coming, and where Tamari’s flower-garlanded daughters would seize his hands and with song and laughter garland him with flowers. The South Seas were calling, and he knew that sooner or later he would answer the call.

text: Her eyes lingered briefly on the powerful neck, thickly sinewed and nearly bovine, darkened by the sun and brimming with robust vitality. Even as he remained there, flushed and meek, she felt herself drawn toward him once more. A reckless notion darted into her mind and startled her. She imagined that if she could just press both hands against that neck, all its vigor and force would pour into her. The thought appalled her. It seemed to uncover a hidden depravity she had never suspected in herself. To her, strength had always seemed coarse and animalistic. Her notion of male beauty had ever favored lean elegance. Still, the thought clung stubbornly. It perplexed her that she should long to set her hands on that sun-darkened neck. In fact, she was anything but sturdy, and what her body and spirit craved was strength. But she was unaware of it. She only knew that no man had ever stirred her as this one did, who continually jolted her with his dreadful grammar.
source_text: Her gaze rested for a moment on the muscular neck, heavy corded, almost bull-like, bronzed by the sun, spilling over with rugged health and strength. And though he sat there, blushing and humble, again she felt drawn to him. She was surprised by a wanton thought that rushed into her mind. It seemed to her that if she could lay her two hands upon that neck that all its strength and vigor would flow out to her. She was shocked by this thought. It seemed to reveal to her an undreamed depravity in her nature. Besides, strength to her was a gross and brutish thing. Her ideal of masculine beauty had always been slender gracefulness. Yet the thought still persisted. It bewildered her that she should desire to place her hands on that sunburned neck. In truth, she was far from robust, and the need of her body and mind was for strength. But she did not know it. She knew only that no man had ever affected her before as this one had, who shocked her from moment to moment with his awful grammar.

text: By now he had unfolded the lone typed page and laid it flat before him. No payment was enclosed. He tilted the envelope toward the lamp, squinting into its hollow, but doubted his own sight and, with a shaky urgency, ripped the paper casing in two. Still no payment. He devoured the words, racing from sentence to sentence, hurtling past the editor’s compliments on his manuscript toward the letter’s core, the explanation for the missing sum. No such explanation awaited him; instead, he encountered something that made him crumple inward. The page slipped from his fingers. His gaze dulled, and he sank back against the mattress, drawing the covers close and tucking them beneath his chin.
source_text: By this time he had drawn the single sheet of type-written letter out and spread it open. There was no check. He peered into the envelope, held it to the light, but could not trust his eyes, and in trembling haste tore the envelope apart. There was no check. He read the letter, skimming it line by line, dashing through the editor’s praise of his story to the meat of the letter, the statement why the check had not been sent. He found no such statement, but he did find that which made him suddenly wilt. The letter slid from his hand. His eyes went lack-lustre, and he lay back on the pillow, pulling the blanket about him and up to his chin.

text: “You know,” he went on, “I pity Mr. Butler. He was too green to understand, but he cheated himself out of living for a thirty-thousand annual income that’s utterly squandered on him. Honestly, thirty thousand in one pile wouldn’t purchase for him today what a dime he was stashing away would have gotten him as a boy—sweets and peanuts, or a spot up in the colored balcony.”
source_text: “Do you know,” he added, “I feel sorry for Mr. Butler. He was too young to know better, but he robbed himself of life for the sake of thirty thousand a year that’s clean wasted upon him. Why, thirty thousand, lump sum, wouldn’t buy for him right now what ten cents he was layin’ up would have bought him, when he was a kid, in the way of candy an’ peanuts or a seat in nigger heaven.”

text: “Hope Ashford’s here.” Brissenden halted and let his fingers go still. “Ashford-Lyle, properly—hyphenated, you understand—descended from old Tidewater lineage. He’s a drifter—the most indolent soul I’ve ever encountered, though he holds a post, or attempts to, at a radical collective shop for six dollars a week. But he’s a thoroughgoing vagrant. Wandered into the city on foot. I’ve watched him linger a whole day on a park seat without a morsel touching his mouth, and come nightfall, when I offered him supper—a café just two streets over—heard him reply, ‘Far too much bother, old fellow. Fetch me a packet of cigarettes instead.’ He was a Spencerian, much like you, until Kreis converted him to materialistic monism. I’ll steer him toward monism if I’m able. Norton’s another monist—only he swears by nothing but spirit. He can supply Kreis and Ashford with all the debate they can handle, as well.”
source_text: “Hope Hamilton’s there.” Brissenden paused and rested his hands. “Strawn-Hamilton’s his name—hyphenated, you know—comes of old Southern stock. He’s a tramp—laziest man I ever knew, though he’s clerking, or trying to, in a socialist coöperative store for six dollars a week. But he’s a confirmed hobo. Tramped into town. I’ve seen him sit all day on a bench and never a bite pass his lips, and in the evening, when I invited him to dinner—restaurant two blocks away—have him say, ‘Too much trouble, old man. Buy me a package of cigarettes instead.’ He was a Spencerian like you till Kreis turned him to materialistic monism. I’ll start him on monism if I can. Norton’s another monist—only he affirms naught but spirit. He can give Kreis and Hamilton all they want, too.”

text: You already smoke far too much, and changing the blend won’t alter a thing. It’s the habit itself that’s so unpleasant, regardless of the label on the tin. You’re a walking chimney, a breathing cinder cone, a strolling smokestack—and an utter disgrace, Martin dear, as you well know.
source_text: “You smoke more than enough as it is, and the brand of tobacco will make no difference. It is the smoking itself that is not nice, no matter what the brand may be. You are a chimney, a living volcano, a perambulating smoke-stack, and you are a perfect disgrace, Martin dear, you know you are.”

text: Her gratitude spilled out in broken phrases, like someone acknowledging an unexpected kindness. Only after she went back to her place in North Oakland, spoke with her own people, and sent the Portuguese shopkeeper to look into the matter, did the truth settle upon her—that the modest home she had inhabited for years, paying rent month after month, now belonged to her.
source_text: She thanked him incoherently, as if for a favor. And it was not until she had returned home to North Oakland and conferred with her own kind, and had the Portuguese grocer investigate, that she really knew that she was the owner of the little house in which she had lived and for which she had paid rent so long.

text: She gave no answer, only remained seated, staring ahead with arid eyes and a stillness so absolute that Martin could feel the knot tightening in her throat, and it pulled at his own. He wished the words back. What he had laid before her seemed shabby now—simple currency—set beside the gift she was bringing. He had offered something external, a thing he could surrender without a flinch, while she was offering her entire self, wrapped in scandal and humiliation, in transgression, and in every dream she had ever harbored of paradise.
source_text: She made no reply, but sat, gazing straight before her, dry-eyed and motionless, but with an ache in the throat which Martin divined so strongly that it made his own throat ache. He regretted that he had spoken. It seemed so tawdry what he had offered her—mere money—compared with what she offered him. He offered her an extraneous thing with which he could part without a pang, while she offered him herself, along with disgrace and shame, and sin, and all her hopes of heaven.

text: After a string of rejections from various magazines, he had finally followed Brissenden’s discarded counsel and sent “The Shame of the Sun” on its own tour of publishing houses. Following several turndowns, Singletree, Darnley & Co. took it on, pledging a fall release. When Martin inquired about an advance against royalties, they replied that such a practice wasn’t their custom, that works of that kind seldom earned back their costs, and that they were skeptical the book would move even a thousand copies. Martin calculated what that modest sale would yield him. Priced at a dollar, with a fifteen-percent royalty, it would net him a hundred and fifty dollars. He resolved that if he could begin again, he would stick strictly to fiction. “Adventure,” a quarter of the length, had earned him twice that sum from *The Millennium*. That newspaper snippet he’d read so long ago had proved accurate after all. The top-tier magazines didn’t pay upon acceptance, and they paid handsomely. Not two cents a word, but four, *The Millennium* had given him. And what’s more, they acquired quality work—weren’t they, after all, buying his? He greeted this final reflection with a grin.
source_text: After it had been refused by a number of magazines, he had taken Brissenden’s rejected advice and started “The Shame of the Sun” on the round of publishers. After several refusals, Singletree, Darnley & Co. accepted it, promising fall publication. When Martin asked for an advance on royalties, they wrote that such was not their custom, that books of that nature rarely paid for themselves, and that they doubted if his book would sell a thousand copies. Martin figured what the book would earn him on such a sale. Retailed at a dollar, on a royalty of fifteen per cent, it would bring him one hundred and fifty dollars. He decided that if he had it to do over again he would confine himself to fiction. “Adventure,” one-fourth as long, had brought him twice as much from _The Millennium_. That newspaper paragraph he had read so long ago had been true, after all. The first-class magazines did not pay on acceptance, and they paid well. Not two cents a word, but four cents a word, had _The Millennium_ paid him. And, furthermore, they bought good stuff, too, for were they not buying his? This last thought he accompanied with a grin.

text: His tone cut like frost, precise and unyielding, each syllable pressed out as though struck by a mechanical stamp. His wife let out a sigh and said nothing. She was a big, heavy woman, perpetually clad in untidy clothes and perpetually worn down by the weight of her body, her labor, and her husband.
source_text: His voice was cold, sharp, and final, his lips stamping the enunciation of each word like the die of a machine. His wife sighed and remained silent. She was a large, stout woman, always dressed slatternly and always tired from the burdens of her flesh, her work, and her husband.

text: As the car passed through the scattering of houses that separated Oakland from Berkeley, he scanned the roadside for a familiar two-story building whose front boasted the sign, HIGGINBOTHAM’S CASH STORE. Martin Eden stepped off at that corner and looked up at the sign for a moment. It spoke to him in a way that went beyond the words themselves. The very letters seemed to radiate a spirit of pettiness, self-importance, and sly deceit. Bernard Higginbotham had married his sister, and Martin knew the man thoroughly. He unlocked the door with his latch-key and made his way up the stairs to the second floor. His brother-in-law lived here; the grocery occupied the ground level. A smell of old vegetables hung in the air. As he felt his way through the dark hallway, he tripped over a toy cart left behind by one of his many nieces and nephews and crashed into a door with a loud bang. “The miser,” he thought; “too cheap to burn two cents’ worth of gas and keep his lodgers from breaking their necks.”
source_text: As the car crossed the zone of scattered dwellings that separated Oakland from Berkeley, he kept a lookout for a familiar, two-story building along the front of which ran the proud sign, HIGGINBOTHAM’S CASH STORE. Martin Eden got off at this corner. He stared up for a moment at the sign. It carried a message to him beyond its mere wording. A personality of smallness and egotism and petty underhandedness seemed to emanate from the letters themselves. Bernard Higginbotham had married his sister, and he knew him well. He let himself in with a latch-key and climbed the stairs to the second floor. Here lived his brother-in-law. The grocery was below. There was a smell of stale vegetables in the air. As he groped his way across the hall he stumbled over a toy-cart, left there by one of his numerous nephews and nieces, and brought up against a door with a resounding bang. “The pincher,” was his thought; “too miserly to burn two cents’ worth of gas and save his boarders’ necks.”

text: Seated diagonally opposite him, to the right of Mr. Morse, was Judge Blount, a judge on the local superior court. Martin had encountered him on several occasions and never warmed to him. The judge and Ruth’s father were deep in talk about union politics, the state of affairs in town, and socialism, and Mr. Morse kept trying to needle Martin about the last subject. Eventually Judge Blount peered across the table, his expression one of kindly, paternal sympathy. Martin kept his amusement to himself.
source_text: Across the table from him, cater-cornered, at Mr. Morse’s right, sat Judge Blount, a local superior court judge. Martin had met him a number of times and had failed to like him. He and Ruth’s father were discussing labor union politics, the local situation, and socialism, and Mr. Morse was endeavoring to twit Martin on the latter topic. At last Judge Blount looked across the table with benignant and fatherly pity. Martin smiled to himself.

text: Martin resumed his article on pearl diving, which might have reached completion sooner had it not been so often interrupted by his ventures into verse. His poems were love poems, born of thoughts of Ruth, yet they remained unfinished. He could not, in a single day, master the art of singing in exalted lines. Rhyme, meter, and form posed challenges enough, but beyond these lay something intangible and elusive—a quality he recognized in all great poetry but could never seize and hold within his own. It was the fugitive soul of poetry itself that he felt and pursued without success. To him, it shimmered like a soft, drifting radiance, a warm and trailing mist forever out of reach, though occasionally he was granted shreds of it, which he wove into phrases that rang in his mind with lingering music or passed before his eyes like the hazy breath of unseen loveliness. It was maddening. He burned to give voice to his feelings, yet could only stammer prosaically, as everyone stammered. He read his fragments aloud. The meter stepped along in perfect measure, and the rhyme beat out an equally flawless cadence, but the glow and high rapture he felt inside were absent. Bewildered, time and again, he fell back in defeat and dejection upon his article. Prose was, without question, a gentler medium.
source_text: Martin went back to his pearl-diving article, which would have been finished sooner if it had not been broken in upon so frequently by his attempts to write poetry. His poems were love poems, inspired by Ruth, but they were never completed. Not in a day could he learn to chant in noble verse. Rhyme and metre and structure were serious enough in themselves, but there was, over and beyond them, an intangible and evasive something that he caught in all great poetry, but which he could not catch and imprison in his own. It was the elusive spirit of poetry itself that he sensed and sought after but could not capture. It seemed a glow to him, a warm and trailing vapor, ever beyond his reaching, though sometimes he was rewarded by catching at shreds of it and weaving them into phrases that echoed in his brain with haunting notes or drifted across his vision in misty wafture of unseen beauty. It was baffling. He ached with desire to express and could but gibber prosaically as everybody gibbered. He read his fragments aloud. The metre marched along on perfect feet, and the rhyme pounded a longer and equally faultless rhythm, but the glow and high exaltation that he felt within were lacking. He could not understand, and time and again, in despair, defeated and depressed, he returned to his article. Prose was certainly an easier medium.

text: He had never before reached such a peak of existence. He lingered on the edges, listening, watching, taking quiet pleasure, and answering in guarded monosyllables—"Yes, miss" and "No, miss" to her, "Yes, ma'am" and "No, ma'am" to her mother. He suppressed the sailor's reflex to say "Yes, sir" and "No, sir" to her brothers. It would strike the wrong note, he thought, a tacit admission of lower standing—and that would never serve if he meant to win her. His pride demanded it too. "By God!" he exclaimed inwardly at one point. "I'm every bit their equal, and if they know plenty I don't, I could teach them a thing or two myself, all the same!" Then, a moment later, when she or her mother called him "Mr. Eden," his bristling pride dissolved, and he glowed, suffused with pleasure. He was a man of cultivation now—that was the truth of it—sitting shoulder to shoulder at dinner with figures he had only met before in books. He himself had stepped into those books, adventuring through the printed pages of bound volumes.
source_text: Never had he been at such an altitude of living, and he kept himself in the background, listening, observing, and pleasuring, replying in reticent monosyllables, saying, “Yes, miss,” and “No, miss,” to her, and “Yes, ma’am,” and “No, ma’am,” to her mother. He curbed the impulse, arising out of his sea-training, to say “Yes, sir,” and “No, sir,” to her brothers. He felt that it would be inappropriate and a confession of inferiority on his part—which would never do if he was to win to her. Also, it was a dictate of his pride. “By God!” he cried to himself, once; “I’m just as good as them, and if they do know lots that I don’t, I could learn ’m a few myself, all the same!” And the next moment, when she or her mother addressed him as “Mr. Eden,” his aggressive pride was forgotten, and he was glowing and warm with delight. He was a civilized man, that was what he was, shoulder to shoulder, at dinner, with people he had read about in books. He was in the books himself, adventuring through the printed pages of bound volumes.

text: As they strode across the thoroughfare, he found himself confronting an unforeseen dilemma. Beneath the harsh glare of the arc lamps, he recognized Lizzie Connolly and her tittering companion. He faltered for only a heartbeat, then raised his hand and removed his hat. He could not betray his own people, and his salute honored more than Lizzie Connolly alone. She acknowledged him with a nod and a brazen stare—not with the tender, dove-like gaze of Ruth, but with eyes that were striking and flinty, and that traveled past him to Ruth, cataloguing her features, her attire, her social rank. And he sensed that Ruth, too, was watching with swift, gentle glances, timid as a sparrow’s, yet sharp enough to catch, in a fleeting sweep, the factory girl in her gaudy dress and beneath the peculiar hat that all factory girls were wearing that season.
source_text: As they crossed Broadway, he came face to face with a new problem. In the blaze of the electric lights, he saw Lizzie Connolly and her giggly friend. Only for an instant he hesitated, then his hand went up and his hat came off. He could not be disloyal to his kind, and it was to more than Lizzie Connolly that his hat was lifted. She nodded and looked at him boldly, not with soft and gentle eyes like Ruth’s, but with eyes that were handsome and hard, and that swept on past him to Ruth and itemized her face and dress and station. And he was aware that Ruth looked, too, with quick eyes that were timid and mild as a dove’s, but which saw, in a look that was a flutter on and past, the working-class girl in her cheap finery and under the strange hat that all working-class girls were wearing just then.

text: He retreated to his cabin and stayed hidden there until the steamer had pulled away from the pier. When he entered the dining saloon for lunch, he was shown to the seat of honor, directly at the captain’s right hand, and it didn’t take him long to realize he was regarded as the most important person aboard. Yet no more discontented celebrity had ever traveled on that vessel. He passed the afternoon slumped in a deck-chair, eyes shut, slipping in and out of a fitful doze, and retired to his berth early that evening.
source_text: He fled to his stateroom, where he lurked until the steamer was clear of the dock. In the dining saloon, at luncheon, he found himself in the place of honor, at the captain’s right; and he was not long in discovering that he was the great man on board. But no more unsatisfactory great man ever sailed on a ship. He spent the afternoon in a deck-chair, with closed eyes, dozing brokenly most of the time, and in the evening went early to bed.

text: Bare as his words were, his gaze brimmed with the lush scene of that sweltering, star-scattered night at Salina Cruz—the pale ribbon of shore, the glow of sugar freighters anchored in the bay, the far-off shouts of tipsy sailors, the shoving dockhands, the fierce heat in the Mexican’s expression, the animal shine of his eyes under the stars, the cold bite of the blade at his throat, the surge of blood, the press of bodies and noise, the two of them, his own form and the Mexican’s, clenched together, churning over and over and gouging the sand, while somewhere distant a guitar rang out softly. That was the image, and he shivered at the recollection, asking himself whether the man who had rendered the pilot schooner on the wall could capture this too. The pale beach, the stars, and the lights of the sugar boats would be striking, he mused, and right in the middle of the sand the dark knot of onlookers circling the brawlers. The knife deserved a place in the composition, he figured, and would stand out, catching a faint glint in the starshine. Yet not a trace of this had slipped into what he said. “He tried to bite off my nose,” he finished.
source_text: Baldly as he had stated it, in his eyes was a rich vision of that hot, starry night at Salina Cruz, the white strip of beach, the lights of the sugar steamers in the harbor, the voices of the drunken sailors in the distance, the jostling stevedores, the flaming passion in the Mexican’s face, the glint of the beast-eyes in the starlight, the sting of the steel in his neck, and the rush of blood, the crowd and the cries, the two bodies, his and the Mexican’s, locked together, rolling over and over and tearing up the sand, and from away off somewhere the mellow tinkling of a guitar. Such was the picture, and he thrilled to the memory of it, wondering if the man could paint it who had painted the pilot-schooner on the wall. The white beach, the stars, and the lights of the sugar steamers would look great, he thought, and midway on the sand the dark group of figures that surrounded the fighters. The knife occupied a place in the picture, he decided, and would show well, with a sort of gleam, in the light of the stars. But of all this no hint had crept into his speech. “He tried to bite off my nose,” he concluded.

text: He was constituted so that he could only work with comprehension. He could not labor sightlessly, in obscurity, unaware of what he was shaping and relying on luck or the fickle brilliance of his own talent to ensure the outcome would be right and exquisite. He had no tolerance for accidental results. He needed to grasp the why and the how. His was a purposeful creative gift, and before he embarked on a story or poem, the entire work was already stirring in his mind, its conclusion visible and the path to that conclusion firmly within his conscious command. Without that, the attempt was bound to fail. Yet he also cherished the unforced flashes of language—words and phrases that slipped into his thoughts with ease and later endured every trial of beauty and force, accruing vast and unutterable resonances. Before such gifts he stood reverent and astonished, recognizing that they lay beyond any mortal’s deliberate craft. And however much he analyzed beauty to uncover the principles that sustain it and render it possible, he remained perpetually conscious of the deepest mystery of beauty, which he could not reach and which no human had ever reached. He understood perfectly, from his reading of Spencer, that ultimate knowledge of anything is forever beyond human grasp, and that the mystery of beauty was no less profound than that of life itself—indeed, more so—that the strands of beauty and life were woven together, and that he himself was merely a fragment of that same incomprehensible fabric, spun from sunlight and cosmic dust and awe.
source_text: He was so made that he could work only with understanding. He could not work blindly, in the dark, ignorant of what he was producing and trusting to chance and the star of his genius that the effect produced should be right and fine. He had no patience with chance effects. He wanted to know why and how. His was deliberate creative genius, and, before he began a story or poem, the thing itself was already alive in his brain, with the end in sight and the means of realizing that end in his conscious possession. Otherwise the effort was doomed to failure. On the other hand, he appreciated the chance effects in words and phrases that came lightly and easily into his brain, and that later stood all tests of beauty and power and developed tremendous and incommunicable connotations. Before such he bowed down and marvelled, knowing that they were beyond the deliberate creation of any man. And no matter how much he dissected beauty in search of the principles that underlie beauty and make beauty possible, he was aware, always, of the innermost mystery of beauty to which he did not penetrate and to which no man had ever penetrated. He knew full well, from his Spencer, that man can never attain ultimate knowledge of anything, and that the mystery of beauty was no less than that of life—nay, more—that the fibres of beauty and life were intertwisted, and that he himself was but a bit of the same nonunderstandable fabric, twisted of sunshine and star-dust and wonder.

text: The cove, as he recalled it, was splendid—its depths ample for the hugest ship on the seas, and so sheltered that the Marquesas Pilot hailed it as the finest careening spot within a thousand-mile radius. He’d acquire a cutter, one of those sleek, copper-sheathed vessels that ran like phantoms, and work the island routes for copra and pearls. The valley and the cove would serve as his base. He’d raise a sprawling native-thatched lodge after the fashion of Tati’s, and stock it and the valley and the cutter with dusky attendants. There he’d host the Taiohae agent, the masters of itinerant traders, and the choicest rogues of the southern seas. He’d keep a generous table and hold court like a nobleman. And he’d put aside the books he’d cracked open and the world that had shown itself a mirage.
source_text: The bay, as he remembered it, was magnificent, with water deep enough to accommodate the largest vessel afloat, and so safe that the South Pacific Directory recommended it as the best careening place for ships for hundreds of miles around. He would buy a schooner—one of those yacht-like, coppered crafts that sailed like witches—and go trading copra and pearling among the islands. He would make the valley and the bay his headquarters. He would build a patriarchal grass house like Tati’s, and have it and the valley and the schooner filled with dark-skinned servitors. He would entertain there the factor of Taiohae, captains of wandering traders, and all the best of the South Pacific riffraff. He would keep open house and entertain like a prince. And he would forget the books he had opened and the world that had proved an illusion.

text: “Overdue” remained abandoned on the table, forgotten. All the other manuscripts he’d sent out were now shoved beneath it. The sole piece he still pushed forward was Brissenden’s “Ephemera.” Once more, his bicycle and dark suit had been pawned, and the typewriter company was again pressing him for payment. Yet these troubles no longer touched him. He was searching for a new direction, and until he found it, his life would have to pause.
source_text: “Overdue” still continued to lie forgotten on the table. Every manuscript that he had had out now lay under the table. Only one manuscript he kept going, and that was Brissenden’s “Ephemera.” His bicycle and black suit were again in pawn, and the type-writer people were once more worrying about the rent. But such things no longer bothered him. He was seeking a new orientation, and until that was found his life must stand still.

text: He rose suddenly and tried to catch his reflection in the grimy mirror above the washstand. He wiped it with a towel and studied his image again, long and intently. It was the first time he had truly looked at himself. His eyes were designed for observation, but until now they had been absorbed by the shifting spectacle of the world; he had been too preoccupied gazing outward to ever examine his own face. He saw the head and features of a young man of twenty, but, unaccustomed to such scrutiny, he had no idea how to judge them. Above a broad, squared forehead, he noticed a thick shock of brown hair—nut-brown, with a gentle wave and subtle curls that any woman would find captivating, her hands itching to smooth it, her fingers longing to weave through it. Yet he dismissed it as worthless in her eyes and lingered instead, deep in thought, on the tall, squared forehead—trying to see through it and discern the nature of what lay within. What kind of mind resided there? was his persistent question. What could it achieve? How far could it carry him? Could it carry him to her?
source_text: He got up abruptly and tried to see himself in the dirty looking-glass over the wash-stand. He passed a towel over it and looked again, long and carefully. It was the first time he had ever really seen himself. His eyes were made for seeing, but up to that moment they had been filled with the ever changing panorama of the world, at which he had been too busy gazing, ever to gaze at himself. He saw the head and face of a young fellow of twenty, but, being unused to such appraisement, he did not know how to value it. Above a square-domed forehead he saw a mop of brown hair, nut-brown, with a wave to it and hints of curls that were a delight to any woman, making hands tingle to stroke it and fingers tingle to pass caresses through it. But he passed it by as without merit, in Her eyes, and dwelt long and thoughtfully on the high, square forehead,—striving to penetrate it and learn the quality of its content. What kind of a brain lay behind there? was his insistent interrogation. What was it capable of? How far would it take him? Would it take him to her?

text: The librarian behind the counter had grown so accustomed to Martin’s daily presence that a genuine warmth had crept into their exchanges; each time Martin stepped through the door, he was met with a gentle smile and a dip of the head. Emboldened by this familiarity, Martin ventured something bold. As he handed over a small stack of volumes and watched the attendant press the date into each slip, he suddenly spoke, the words tumbling out before he could stop them:
source_text: The man at the desk in the library had seen Martin there so often that he had become quite cordial, always greeting him with a smile and a nod when he entered. It was because of this that Martin did a daring thing. Drawing out some books at the desk, and while the man was stamping the cards, Martin blurted out:-

text: On Sunday, he told himself, he would gather his strength and compose a reply to Ruth. Yet on Saturday, once his labor had ended and he had scrubbed himself clean, the longing to escape swallowed him whole. “Might as well stroll over and see how Joe fares,” he murmured inwardly, and knew at once the words were false. But he lacked the will to examine the falsehood. Had he possessed the will, he would have turned from it deliberately, for his sole wish was oblivion. He set off toward the town with measured, indifferent steps, though his stride quickened against his own resolve as he drew closer to the bar.
source_text: He resolved that when Sunday came he would pull himself together and answer Ruth’s letter. But Saturday afternoon, after work was finished and he had taken a bath, the desire to forget overpowered him. “I guess I’ll go down and see how Joe’s getting on,” was the way he put it to himself; and in the same moment he knew that he lied. But he did not have the energy to consider the lie. If he had had the energy, he would have refused to consider the lie, because he wanted to forget. He started for the village slowly and casually, increasing his pace in spite of himself as he neared the saloon.

text: The day arrived when he hauled himself into the alley behind the _Enquirer_ building, and Cheese-Face was nowhere to be seen. He didn’t show up at all. The other boys clapped him on the back, insisting he’d beaten Cheese-Face. But Martin felt no triumph. He hadn’t beaten Cheese-Face, and Cheese-Face hadn’t beaten him. The question between them stood unresolved. Only later did they discover that Cheese-Face’s father had dropped dead without warning that very morning.
source_text: Came the day when he dragged himself into the _Enquirer_ alley, and there was no Cheese-Face. Nor did Cheese-Face come. The boys congratulated him, and told him that he had licked Cheese-Face. But Martin was not satisfied. He had not licked Cheese-Face, nor had Cheese-Face licked him. The problem had not been solved. It was not until afterward that they learned that Cheese-Face’s father had died suddenly that very day.

text: And there the situation remained. The household had settled into an unspoken agreement that Ruth and Martin were betrothed, though no formal declaration was issued. The family assumed such an announcement would never be required. Moreover, a quiet consensus held that the engagement would stretch on for years. They neither pressed Martin to find employment nor to abandon his scribbling. They had no wish to spur him toward self-improvement. And he played right into their coolly laid plans, since taking a regular job was the last thing on his mind.
source_text: So the matter rested. Within the family it was accepted that Ruth and Martin were engaged, but no announcement was made. The family did not think it would ever be necessary. Also, it was tacitly understood that it was to be a long engagement. They did not ask Martin to go to work, nor to cease writing. They did not intend to encourage him to mend himself. And he aided and abetted them in their unfriendly designs, for going to work was farthest from his thoughts.

text: He observed, one Sunday morning, that the Bricklayers’ Picnic was being held that day at Shell Mound Park, and to Shell Mound Park he made his way. In his earlier years he had frequented such working-class outings, and the moment he stepped into the park, the old sensations surged back afresh. These were his people, after all. He had been born to them, had lived among them, and though he had wandered for a while, it felt right to return.
source_text: He noted, one Sunday morning, that the Bricklayers’ Picnic took place that day at Shell Mound Park, and to Shell Mound Park he went. He had been to the working-class picnics too often in his earlier life not to know what they were like, and as he entered the park he experienced a recrudescence of all the old sensations. After all, they were his kind, these working people. He had been born among them, he had lived among them, and though he had strayed for a time, it was well to come back among them.

text: “Let’s just walk away,” Joe said. “Let’s drop everything and take to the road. I’ve never done it before, but it’s got to be ridiculously simple. And absolutely nothing to do. Imagine it—nothing at all to do. I had typhoid once, laid up in the hospital, and it was glorious. I wish I could get sick like that again.”
source_text: “Let’s quit,” Joe suggested. “Let’s chuck it, an’ go hoboin’. I ain’t never tried it, but it must be dead easy. An’ nothin’ to do. Just think of it, nothin’ to do. I was sick once, typhoid, in the hospital, an’ it was beautiful. I wish I’d get sick again.”

text: From the moment the notion took root, it possessed him entirely, and the journey back to San Francisco passed as though in a trance. He was intoxicated by a sense of latent potency, convinced that no endeavor lay beyond his reach. Out on the vast and solitary ocean, his thoughts clarified. For the first time, he beheld Ruth’s world with perfect lucidity. It materialized in his imagination as something solid, something he could lift in both hands, rotate, and scrutinize from every angle. Parts of that realm remained shadowy and ill-defined, yet he perceived it as a unified whole rather than a scatter of fragments, and he discerned the path to its conquest. To become a writer! The notion blazed within him. He would commence the moment he arrived. His first task would be to chronicle the treasure-seekers’ expedition. He would sell the piece to one of the San Francisco dailies. He would breathe no word of it to Ruth, and she would be astonished and delighted upon discovering his name in print. While composing, he could continue his studies. Each day held twenty-four hours. He was unconquerable. He understood the discipline of labor, and the strongholds would crumble before him. He need never return to the sea—as a common sailor; and briefly he glimpsed the image of a steam yacht. Other authors owned steam yachts. Naturally, he reminded himself, success would come gradually at first, and for a while he would be satisfied to earn just enough through his pen to sustain his learning. Then, after some interval—a span utterly indefinite—once he had trained and readied himself, he would produce masterworks and his name would echo on every tongue. But greater than that, infinitely greater and supreme above all, he would have shown himself deserving of Ruth. Renown was pleasant enough, but it was for her sake that his magnificent vision soared. He was no seeker of celebrity, only one of heaven’s impassioned devotees.
source_text: Once the idea had germinated, it mastered him, and the return voyage to San Francisco was like a dream. He was drunken with unguessed power and felt that he could do anything. In the midst of the great and lonely sea he gained perspective. Clearly, and for the first time, he saw Ruth and her world. It was all visualized in his mind as a concrete thing which he could take up in his two hands and turn around and about and examine. There was much that was dim and nebulous in that world, but he saw it as a whole and not in detail, and he saw, also, the way to master it. To write! The thought was fire in him. He would begin as soon as he got back. The first thing he would do would be to describe the voyage of the treasure-hunters. He would sell it to some San Francisco newspaper. He would not tell Ruth anything about it, and she would be surprised and pleased when she saw his name in print. While he wrote, he could go on studying. There were twenty-four hours in each day. He was invincible. He knew how to work, and the citadels would go down before him. He would not have to go to sea again—as a sailor; and for the instant he caught a vision of a steam yacht. There were other writers who possessed steam yachts. Of course, he cautioned himself, it would be slow succeeding at first, and for a time he would be content to earn enough money by his writing to enable him to go on studying. And then, after some time,—a very indeterminate time,—when he had learned and prepared himself, he would write the great things and his name would be on all men’s lips. But greater than that, infinitely greater and greatest of all, he would have proved himself worthy of Ruth. Fame was all very well, but it was for Ruth that his splendid dream arose. He was not a fame-monger, but merely one of God’s mad lovers.

text: At first, he moved with a certain rigidity, weighed down by the consciousness of his own clumsiness—particularly his shoulders, which were once again threatening to send some piece of furniture or decoration crashing. The gathering itself also made him acutely aware of himself. Never before had he encountered such elevated company, let alone so many of them at once. Melville, the bank cashier, held him spellbound, and he made a mental note to study the man as soon as he could. For beneath Martin’s reverence stirred a confident ego, and he felt a growing need to test himself against these people, to discover what they had absorbed from books and living that he himself had missed.
source_text: He bore himself a bit stiffly at first, oppressed by a sense of his own awkwardness, especially of his shoulders, which were up to their old trick of threatening destruction to furniture and ornaments. Also, he was rendered self-conscious by the company. He had never before been in contact with such exalted beings nor with so many of them. Melville, the bank cashier, fascinated him, and he resolved to investigate him at the first opportunity. For underneath Martin’s awe lurked his assertive ego, and he felt the urge to measure himself with these men and women and to find out what they had learned from the books and life which he had not learned.

text: “Olney despises me!” she burst out with fierce emotion. “And I despise Olney. Whenever he’s near, I turn into a cat. I have this urge to be cruel to him, and even when I don’t feel that way, well, he’s cruel to me regardless. But with Martin Eden, I’m joyful. No one has ever loved me before—no man, I mean, not like this. And it’s delicious to be loved—that way. You understand what I’m saying, dear mother. It’s delicious to sense that you are, completely and genuinely, a woman.” She pressed her face into her mother’s lap, weeping. “I know you must think I’m terrible, but I’m being truthful, and I’m telling you exactly what’s in my heart.”
source_text: “Olney hates me!” she exclaimed passionately. “And I hate Olney. I feel always like a cat when he is around. I feel that I must be nasty to him, and even when I don’t happen to feel that way, why, he’s nasty to me, anyway. But I am happy with Martin Eden. No one ever loved me before—no man, I mean, in that way. And it is sweet to be loved—that way. You know what I mean, mother dear. It is sweet to feel that you are really and truly a woman.” She buried her face in her mother’s lap, sobbing. “You think I am dreadful, I know, but I am honest, and I tell you just how I feel.”

text: But Ruth laughed, safe in her own certainty. She was confident, and in just a few days he would sail away. By the time he came back, she would already be gone on her trip out East. Still, there was something magnetic about Martin’s vigor and vitality. He had also heard about her planned journey, and he sensed the pressure to act quickly. Yet he had no idea how to court a girl like Ruth. On top of that, he was burdened by a vast history with women who were nothing like her. They had understood passion and flirtation and the ways of the world, whereas she was utterly untouched by such knowledge. Her staggering innocence silenced him, numbing every heated word on his tongue, and forcing him, against his will, to see his own inadequacy. He faced another obstacle too. He had never actually been in love himself. In that murky former life, he had fancied women, even been captivated by a few, but he had never truly loved any of them. He had whistled with easy, careless authority, and they had come. They were pastimes, fleeting episodes, a minor part of the game men play. But now, for the first time, he was a humble petitioner—gentle, hesitant, full of doubt. He knew neither the language of love nor its rituals, and he stood in awe of his beloved’s transparent innocence.
source_text: But Ruth laughed from security. She was sure of herself, and in a few days he would be off to sea. Then, by the time he returned, she would be away on her visit East. There was a magic, however, in the strength and health of Martin. He, too, had been told of her contemplated Eastern trip, and he felt the need for haste. Yet he did not know how to make love to a girl like Ruth. Then, too, he was handicapped by the possession of a great fund of experience with girls and women who had been absolutely different from her. They had known about love and life and flirtation, while she knew nothing about such things. Her prodigious innocence appalled him, freezing on his lips all ardors of speech, and convincing him, in spite of himself, of his own unworthiness. Also he was handicapped in another way. He had himself never been in love before. He had liked women in that turgid past of his, and been fascinated by some of them, but he had not known what it was to love them. He had whistled in a masterful, careless way, and they had come to him. They had been diversions, incidents, part of the game men play, but a small part at most. And now, and for the first time, he was a suppliant, tender and timid and doubting. He did not know the way of love, nor its speech, while he was frightened at his loved one’s clear innocence.

text: Standing on the _Mariposa_'s deck as departure time arrived, he spotted Lizzie Connolly tucked among the clusters of people on the dock. Bring her along, the impulse whispered. Kindness costs so little. She would be overjoyed. For an instant it nearly seduced him, and then it shifted into dread. The idea sent a jolt of alarm through him. His weary spirit rebelled. With a groan he pulled back from the railing and murmured, "Man, you're far too ill, far too ill."
source_text: From the deck of the _Mariposa_, at the sailing hour, he saw Lizzie Connolly hiding in the skirts of the crowd on the wharf. Take her with you, came the thought. It is easy to be kind. She will be supremely happy. It was almost a temptation one moment, and the succeeding moment it became a terror. He was in a panic at the thought of it. His tired soul cried out in protest. He turned away from the rail with a groan, muttering, “Man, you are too sick, you are too sick.”

text: Martin launched into a story that morning, one he had roughed out weeks earlier and that had been nagging him ever since with its urgent demand to be written.
