It promised to be a brisk sea yarn, a tale of modern adventure and romance, peopled with authentic characters in a recognizable world under plausible circumstances. Yet underneath the narrative’s momentum there was meant to lie something else—something the casual reader would never notice, but that would in no way lessen that reader’s pleasure or engagement. It was this deeper layer, not the plot alone, that drove Martin to compose it. Indeed, it was always some large, timeless theme that sparked his story ideas. Once he had seized on such a theme, he searched for the specific individuals and the precise setting in time and place through which to voice the universal. He had settled on the title “Overdue,” and he figured it would run no longer than sixty thousand words—a mere trifle for him, given his exuberant creative stamina. That first day he approached the work with a conscious thrill at commanding his craft. He no longer fretted that the keen edge of his skill might slip and damage the piece. The many months of rigorous practice and study had paid off. He could now apply himself with a steady hand to the grand design of what he was making; and as he labored on, hour after hour, he sensed, more vividly than ever, the confident, far-reaching grip with which he comprehended existence and its workings. “Overdue” would recount a story true to its specific characters and incidents; but it would also, he felt certain, convey profound, vital truths valid for all ages, all oceans, and all life—credit for which went to Herbert Spencer, he reflected, leaning back briefly from his desk. Yes, thanks to Herbert Spencer and to the master key of existence, evolution, which Spencer had handed him.
source_text: Martin began, that morning, a story which he had sketched out a number of weeks before and which ever since had been worrying him with its insistent clamor to be created. Apparently it was to be a rattling sea story, a tale of twentieth-century adventure and romance, handling real characters, in a real world, under real conditions. But beneath the swing and go of the story was to be something else—something that the superficial reader would never discern and which, on the other hand, would not diminish in any way the interest and enjoyment for such a reader. It was this, and not the mere story, that impelled Martin to write it. For that matter, it was always the great, universal motif that suggested plots to him. After having found such a motif, he cast about for the particular persons and particular location in time and space wherewith and wherein to utter the universal thing. “Overdue” was the title he had decided for it, and its length he believed would not be more than sixty thousand words—a bagatelle for him with his splendid vigor of production. On this first day he took hold of it with conscious delight in the mastery of his tools. He no longer worried for fear that the sharp, cutting edges should slip and mar his work. The long months of intense application and study had brought their reward. He could now devote himself with sure hand to the larger phases of the thing he shaped; and as he worked, hour after hour, he felt, as never before, the sure and cosmic grasp with which he held life and the affairs of life. “Overdue” would tell a story that would be true of its particular characters and its particular events; but it would tell, too, he was confident, great vital things that would be true of all time, and all sea, and all life—thanks to Herbert Spencer, he thought, leaning back for a moment from the table. Ay, thanks to Herbert Spencer and to the master-key of life, evolution, which Spencer had placed in his hands.

text: “You can’t talk to me about Spencer,” he burst out. “You understand no more of Spencer than his own compatriots do. But I don’t blame you, I admit. It’s simply one symptom of the disgraceful ignorance of our age. I stumbled across a specimen of it on my way here tonight. I was perusing an essay on Spencer by Saleeby. You ought to read it. It’s available to everyone. You can purchase it at any bookshop or borrow it from the public library. You’d feel mortified by your feeble insults and your ignorance of that great man, once you see what Saleeby has gathered on the matter. It’s a chronicle of disgrace that would make your disgrace blush.”
source_text: “You can’t discuss Spencer with me,” he cried. “You do not know any more about Spencer than do his own countrymen. But it is no fault of yours, I grant. It is just a phase of the contemptible ignorance of the times. I ran across a sample of it on my way here this evening. I was reading an essay by Saleeby on Spencer. You should read it. It is accessible to all men. You can buy it in any book-store or draw it from the public library. You would feel ashamed of your paucity of abuse and ignorance of that noble man compared with what Saleeby has collected on the subject. It is a record of shame that would shame your shame.

text: Still, he couldn’t shake the sense that Brissenden had been correct all along. The triumph belonged less to his own writing than to “The Shame of the Sun.” His work had merely tagged along. Magazine after magazine had turned it down. It was the publication of “The Shame of the Sun” that ignited a firestorm and set off the avalanche in his favor. Without that piece, there would have been no avalanche; without the inexplicable magic of its release, no avalanche either. Singletree, Darnley & Co. could vouch for that magic. They had printed a cautious first run of fifteen hundred copies, doubtful they’d ever sell them. Seasoned publishers, they were more stunned than anyone by the ensuing success. To them it was a genuine marvel. They never fully recovered from the shock, and every letter they sent him quivered with reverent wonder at that first uncanny event. They offered no explanation. There was none to give. It had simply occurred. Against every known precedent, it had occurred.
source_text: And yet, somehow, he had a feeling that Brissenden had been right, after all. “The Shame of the Sun” had been the cause of his success more than the stuff he had written. That stuff had been merely incidental. It had been rejected right and left by the magazines. The publication of “The Shame of the Sun” had started a controversy and precipitated the landslide in his favor. Had there been no “Shame of the Sun” there would have been no landslide, and had there been no miracle in the go of “The Shame of the Sun” there would have been no landslide. Singletree, Darnley & Co. attested that miracle. They had brought out a first edition of fifteen hundred copies and been dubious of selling it. They were experienced publishers and no one had been more astounded than they at the success which had followed. To them it had been in truth a miracle. They never got over it, and every letter they wrote him reflected their reverent awe of that first mysterious happening. They did not attempt to explain it. There was no explaining it. It had happened. In the face of all experience to the contrary, it had happened.

text: As dusk settled, Martin stepped out of the greengrocer’s and headed back toward his flat, the day’s purchases dangling from his arm. At the intersection, a streetcar had paused, and when he spotted a gaunt, well-known silhouette stepping down, a surge of gladness rushed through him. It was Brissenden—and in that brief instant before the tram jolted onward, Martin caught the bulges in his overcoat pockets: one stuffed with books, the other with a quart of whiskey.
source_text: Twilight was falling as Martin left the fruit store and turned homeward, his marketing on his arm. At the corner an electric car had stopped, and at sight of a lean, familiar figure alighting, his heart leapt with joy. It was Brissenden, and in the fleeting glimpse, ere the car started up, Martin noted the overcoat pockets, one bulging with books, the other bulging with a quart bottle of whiskey.

text: Once, upon the polished floor of the ballroom, he glimpsed Elara Voss swept past in the clasp of a sturdy laborer; afterward, circling the hall, he discovered her seated beside a table of lemonade. Amazement and pleasantries exchanged, he drew her out into the garden, where words need not wrestle the orchestra’s clamor. From the moment he addressed her, she belonged to him. He felt it. She confessed it in the tender haughtiness of her gaze, in every fond motion of her imperiously held frame, and in the way her attention clung to his voice. She was no longer the girl he remembered. She had ripened into womanhood, and Martin observed that her untamed, rebellious loveliness had deepened, surrendering none of its wild edge, while the rebellion and the blaze now seemed tempered by will. “A vision, a flawless vision,” he breathed in hushed admiration. And he understood she was his, that he needed only to whisper “Follow,” and she would journey with him across the earth wherever he chose.
source_text: Once, on the dancing-floor, he saw Lizzie Connolly go by in the arms of a young workingman; and, later, when he made the round of the pavilion, he came upon her sitting by a refreshment table. Surprise and greetings over, he led her away into the grounds, where they could talk without shouting down the music. From the instant he spoke to her, she was his. He knew it. She showed it in the proud humility of her eyes, in every caressing movement of her proudly carried body, and in the way she hung upon his speech. She was not the young girl as he had known her. She was a woman, now, and Martin noted that her wild, defiant beauty had improved, losing none of its wildness, while the defiance and the fire seemed more in control. “A beauty, a perfect beauty,” he murmured admiringly under his breath. And he knew she was his, that all he had to do was to say “Come,” and she would go with him over the world wherever he led.

text: Close to home, he encountered a sample of this odd, jumbled mixture. His sister Marian had been seeing a hardworking young mechanic of German descent who, after mastering his trade, had opened his own bicycle-repair shop. He’d also secured a dealership for a cheap brand of bicycle and was doing well. A short while earlier, Marian had visited Martin in his room to share news of her engagement, and during that call she’d playfully examined his palm and told his fortune. On her next visit, she brought Hermann von Schmidt along. Martin played host and offered congratulations with such effortless, polished ease that it grated on the rough peasant sensibility of his sister’s suitor. The poor impression deepened when Martin read aloud the half-dozen stanzas he’d written to mark Marian’s previous visit. It was a light, delicate piece of society verse he’d titled “The Palmist.” When he finished reading, he was startled to see no pleasure on his sister’s face. Instead, her eyes were fixed anxiously on her fiancé, and following her look, Martin found spread across that worthy’s lopsided features only dark, glowering disapproval. The moment passed, they left early, and Martin put it out of his mind, though briefly he’d been baffled that any woman, even one from the working class, wouldn’t be flattered and thrilled to have poetry written about her.
source_text: A sample of this curious messy mixture he encountered nearer home. His sister Marian had been keeping company with an industrious young mechanic, of German extraction, who, after thoroughly learning the trade, had set up for himself in a bicycle-repair shop. Also, having got the agency for a low-grade make of wheel, he was prosperous. Marian had called on Martin in his room a short time before to announce her engagement, during which visit she had playfully inspected Martin’s palm and told his fortune. On her next visit she brought Hermann von Schmidt along with her. Martin did the honors and congratulated both of them in language so easy and graceful as to affect disagreeably the peasant-mind of his sister’s lover. This bad impression was further heightened by Martin’s reading aloud the half-dozen stanzas of verse with which he had commemorated Marian’s previous visit. It was a bit of society verse, airy and delicate, which he had named “The Palmist.” He was surprised, when he finished reading it, to note no enjoyment in his sister’s face. Instead, her eyes were fixed anxiously upon her betrothed, and Martin, following her gaze, saw spread on that worthy’s asymmetrical features nothing but black and sullen disapproval. The incident passed over, they made an early departure, and Martin forgot all about it, though for the moment he had been puzzled that any woman, even of the working class, should not have been flattered and delighted by having poetry written about her.

text: He dismissed the gesture with a wave of his hand, murmuring that it was nothing, nothing at all, something anyone would have done. She saw that the hand he lifted was marked with fresh, healing scrapes, and a quick look at the other, hanging loosely at his side, revealed the same raw condition. Her sharp, appraising gaze caught a scar on his cheek, another just visible beneath the hair at his temple, and a third that traced downward and vanished under the stiff white collar. She stifled a smile at the red welt where the starched collar chafed his sun-bronzed neck—he was clearly unaccustomed to such formality. Her feminine eye also absorbed the cheap, graceless cut of his clothes, the coat straining into creases across the shoulders, and the rows of wrinkles along the sleeves that betrayed the swell of powerful biceps.
source_text: He waved his hand deprecatingly and muttered that it was nothing at all, what he had done, and that any fellow would have done it. She noticed that the hand he waved was covered with fresh abrasions, in the process of healing, and a glance at the other loose-hanging hand showed it to be in the same condition. Also, with quick, critical eye, she noted a scar on his cheek, another that peeped out from under the hair of the forehead, and a third that ran down and disappeared under the starched collar. She repressed a smile at sight of the red line that marked the chafe of the collar against the bronzed neck. He was evidently unused to stiff collars. Likewise her feminine eye took in the clothes he wore, the cheap and unaesthetic cut, the wrinkling of the coat across the shoulders, and the series of wrinkles in the sleeves that advertised bulging biceps muscles.

text: The end result of all my reflections, my studies, and my passions is this: I am bound for Grub Street. I’ll abandon any thought of masterpieces and turn to hack-work—jokes, squibs, feature pieces, light verse, society rhymes—all the fluff that seems to be in such endless demand. Then there are the newspaper syndicates, the short-story syndicates, and the syndicates that feed the Sunday supplements. I can churn out exactly the stuff they want and pull in the equivalent of a decent salary. There are free-lances, as you know, who make four or five hundred a month. I have no wish to become one of them; but I’ll earn a comfortable living and keep ample time for myself, which no fixed position would ever allow me.
source_text: “But the upshot of it all—of my thinking and reading and loving—is that I am going to move to Grub Street. I shall leave masterpieces alone and do hack-work—jokes, paragraphs, feature articles, humorous verse, and society verse—all the rot for which there seems so much demand. Then there are the newspaper syndicates, and the newspaper short-story syndicates, and the syndicates for the Sunday supplements. I can go ahead and hammer out the stuff they want, and earn the equivalent of a good salary by it. There are free-lances, you know, who earn as much as four or five hundred a month. I don’t care to become as they; but I’ll earn a good living, and have plenty of time to myself, which I wouldn’t have in any position.”

text: Martin arrived for dinner, turning on the charm for the portly wholesale butcher and his even portlier wife—people of consequence, the sort who could prove valuable to an ambitious go-getter like Hermann von Schmidt. Yet it had taken no lesser lure than his celebrated brother-in-law to coax them through the door. Another guest who had bitten at the same hook was the man overseeing the Pacific Coast operations for the Asa Bicycle Company. Von Schmidt was eager to win him over and stay in his good graces, since he held the keys to the Oakland bicycle franchise. So Hermann von Schmidt counted Martin as a prized connection, though deep down he could never quite grasp what the fuss was about. In the quiet hours of the night, with his wife asleep beside him, he had stumbled through Martin’s stories and verses, and concluded the world was full of fools for buying them.
source_text: And to dinner Martin came, making himself agreeable with the fat wholesale butcher and his fatter wife—important folk, they, likely to be of use to a rising young man like Hermann von Schmidt. No less a bait, however, had been required to draw them to his house than his great brother-in-law. Another man at table who had swallowed the same bait was the superintendent of the Pacific Coast agencies for the Asa Bicycle Company. Him Von Schmidt desired to please and propitiate because from him could be obtained the Oakland agency for the bicycle. So Hermann von Schmidt found it a goodly asset to have Martin for a brother-in-law, but in his heart of hearts he couldn’t understand where it all came in. In the silent watches of the night, while his wife slept, he had floundered through Martin’s books and poems, and decided that the world was a fool to buy them.

text: But the reading faltered. The enchantment of transient loveliness enfolding them was too potent. The golden season was fading as it had flourished—a gorgeous, unashamed hedonist—and memories of delight and satisfaction weighed heavily in the atmosphere. It seeped into them, drowsy and listless, sapping the sinews of resolve, veiling the visage of conscience, or of reason, in fog and violet shadow. Martin felt softened and yielding, and now and then subtle waves of heat rippled through him. His head rested close to hers, and when stray ghosts of wind lifted her hair so that it brushed his cheek, the words on the page blurred before his gaze.
source_text: But the reading languished. The spell of passing beauty all about them was too strong. The golden year was dying as it had lived, a beautiful and unrepentant voluptuary, and reminiscent rapture and content freighted heavily the air. It entered into them, dreamy and languorous, weakening the fibres of resolution, suffusing the face of morality, or of judgment, with haze and purple mist. Martin felt tender and melting, and from time to time warm glows passed over him. His head was very near to hers, and when wandering phantoms of breeze stirred her hair so that it touched his face, the printed pages swam before his eyes.

text: “Curse all their rooftops!” cried Aldric, when Corin offered to peddle his verses on his behalf. “Cherish Loveliness for her own sake,” he urged, “and shun the periodicals. Return to your vessels and your tides—that is my charge to you, Corin Lorne. What do you seek in these fevered, crumbling hives of mankind? Each day you squander among them, hawking grace to fill the maw of print, you slit your own veins. What was that line you recited to me lately?—Ah, yes, ‘Man, the briefest of the mayflies.’ Tell me, then, what would you, the briefest of the mayflies, do with renown? Should you grasp it, it would turn venom in your blood. You are too unspoiled, too primal, and too clear-sighted, by my troth, to fatten on such slop. I pray you never barter a single stanza to the journals. Beauty is the sole sovereign worth obeying. Obey her, and let the rabble go hang! Triumph! What in damnation is triumph if it does not blaze already in your Aldington sonnet, which towers above Marlowe’s ‘Spectre,’ in that ‘Devotion-cycle,’ in those ocean-odes?”
source_text: “A plague on all their houses!” was Brissenden’s answer to Martin’s volunteering to market his work for him. “Love Beauty for its own sake,” was his counsel, “and leave the magazines alone. Back to your ships and your sea—that’s my advice to you, Martin Eden. What do you want in these sick and rotten cities of men? You are cutting your throat every day you waste in them trying to prostitute beauty to the needs of magazinedom. What was it you quoted me the other day?—Oh, yes, ‘Man, the latest of the ephemera.’ Well, what do you, the latest of the ephemera, want with fame? If you got it, it would be poison to you. You are too simple, too elemental, and too rational, by my faith, to prosper on such pap. I hope you never do sell a line to the magazines. Beauty is the only master to serve. Serve her and damn the multitude! Success! What in hell’s success if it isn’t right there in your Stevenson sonnet, which outranks Henley’s ‘Apparition,’ in that ‘Love-cycle,’ in those sea-poems?

text: As he moved to bid her good night, she inclined toward him—not with demand or allure, but with a quiet, almost reverent longing. He felt it strike deep within him. A vast forbearance swelled in his chest. He drew her into his embrace and kissed her, sensing on his own mouth a kiss as honest as any ever granted to a man.
source_text: She leaned toward him as he was about to say good night. But she leaned not imperatively, not seductively, but wistfully and humbly. He was touched to the heart. His large tolerance rose up in him. He put his arms around her, and kissed her, and knew that upon his own lips rested as true a kiss as man ever received.

text: _Coleman’s Weekly_ dispatched a sprawling cable to Martin, at a cost of almost three hundred dollars, proposing he write twenty pieces at a thousand dollars each. He would journey across America, all charges covered, and choose any subjects that seized his fancy. The message’s bulk sketched out imaginary themes, meant to illustrate the boundless latitude he would enjoy. The sole constraint was that his travels must stay within the nation’s borders. Martin wired back his refusal and apologies, sending the reply with charges reversed.
source_text: _Coleman’s Weekly_ sent Martin a lengthy telegram, costing nearly three hundred dollars, offering him a thousand dollars an article for twenty articles. He was to travel over the United States, with all expenses paid, and select whatever topics interested him. The body of the telegram was devoted to hypothetical topics in order to show him the freedom of range that was to be his. The only restriction placed upon him was that he must confine himself to the United States. Martin sent his inability to accept and his regrets by wire “collect.”

text: “—Ah, but of course,” Aldridge hurried forward, forestalling her protest, “I realize you prefer to call it broad cultivation. Yet the subject itself hardly signifies when the aim is breadth. Take up French, or take up German, or discard them entirely and master Esperanto—the cultural veneer will be identical. You might even devote yourself to Greek or Latin with the same end in view, though neither will ever serve you practically. They will be cultivation, nonetheless. Why, Ruth once immersed herself in Anglo-Saxon, attained real proficiency—that was two winters past—and all she retains now is ‘Whan that sweet Aprile with his schowers soote’—is that how the line runs?”
source_text: “—Oh, yes,” Olney rushed on, heading off her objection, “I know you call it general culture. But it doesn’t matter what you study if you want general culture. You can study French, or you can study German, or cut them both out and study Esperanto, you’ll get the culture tone just the same. You can study Greek or Latin, too, for the same purpose, though it will never be any use to you. It will be culture, though. Why, Ruth studied Saxon, became clever in it,—that was two years ago,—and all that she remembers of it now is ‘Whan that sweet Aprile with his schowers soote’—isn’t that the way it goes?”

text: “Only one obstacle stands between you and my consent.” Brissenden paused briefly. “It is vast—the grandest work I have ever shaped. I am certain of that. It is my farewell piece. I am fiercely proud of it. I adore it. It surpasses any spirit. It is the vision I chased—the flawless and magnificent creation—when I was an untested youth, filled with tender hopes and unsullied convictions. And now I hold it, in these final moments, and I will not let it be mauled and defiled by a herd of beasts. No, I refuse the wager. It belongs to me. I forged it, and I have given it to you.”
source_text: “There’s just one thing that prevents me from taking you.” Brissenden waited a moment. “The thing is big—the biggest I’ve ever done. I know that. It’s my swan song. I am almighty proud of it. I worship it. It’s better than whiskey. It is what I dreamed of—the great and perfect thing—when I was a simple young man, with sweet illusions and clean ideals. And I’ve got it, now, in my last grasp, and I’ll not have it pawed over and soiled by a lot of swine. No, I won’t take the bet. It’s mine. I made it, and I’ve shared it with you.”

text: The shimmering tableau was shattered and scattered by Arthur, who had spent the entire evening coaxing forth his untamed companion. Martin Eden recalled his resolve. For the first time he stepped fully into himself, at first with conscious intent, but swiftly surrendering to the rapture of shaping life as he had lived it into vivid being before his audience. He had sailed among the crew of the smuggler _Halcyon_ on the night a revenue cutter ran her down. He had watched with hungry eyes, and he knew how to render what he saw. He summoned the heaving ocean into the room, the men and the vessels that rode upon it. He lent them his own sight until they gazed through his eyes at all he had witnessed. With an artist’s instinct he plucked from the immense clutter of particulars, sketching scenes that blazed and smoldered with light and hue, infusing such motion that his hearers were swept beside him on a torrent of raw eloquence, fervor, and force. He startled them at turns with the starkness of the tale and his coarse words, yet loveliness always came hard upon the heels of brutality, and sorrow was leavened by laughter, by glimpses into the crooked and curious workings of seamen’s minds.
source_text: The glimmering vision was rent asunder and dissipated by Arthur, who, all evening, had been trying to draw his wild man out. Martin Eden remembered his decision. For the first time he became himself, consciously and deliberately at first, but soon lost in the joy of creating in making life as he knew it appear before his listeners’ eyes. He had been a member of the crew of the smuggling schooner _Halcyon_ when she was captured by a revenue cutter. He saw with wide eyes, and he could tell what he saw. He brought the pulsing sea before them, and the men and the ships upon the sea. He communicated his power of vision, till they saw with his eyes what he had seen. He selected from the vast mass of detail with an artist’s touch, drawing pictures of life that glowed and burned with light and color, injecting movement so that his listeners surged along with him on the flood of rough eloquence, enthusiasm, and power. At times he shocked them with the vividness of the narrative and his terms of speech, but beauty always followed fast upon the heels of violence, and tragedy was relieved by humor, by interpretations of the strange twists and quirks of sailors’ minds.

text: “Indeed, I’m grateful,” she murmured. “For all his fire, Swinburne falters—he lacks, at bottom, a certain refinement. Whole verses of his ought to remain unopened. The masters, by contrast, pour into every stroke a luminous verity, summoning whatever in us reaches toward the exalted and the good. To cut even one line from their work would diminish the world by exactly that measure.”
source_text: “Yes, thank you,” she said. “Swinburne fails, when all is said, because he is, well, indelicate. There are many of his poems that should never be read. Every line of the really great poets is filled with beautiful truth, and calls to all that is high and noble in the human. Not a line of the great poets can be spared without impoverishing the world by that much.”

text: Martin set the alarm, pulled his chair to the table, and opened Fiske. He couldn’t get through the first paragraph. The words smeared and tangled, and his head kept drooping. He paced the room, striking his skull with his fists, but the dull weight of sleep would not lift. He wedged the book upright and held his eyelids apart with his fingers, yet drifted off with his eyes still open. At last he gave in and, barely aware of his movements, shed his clothes and crawled into bed. He sank into seven hours of dense, brutish sleep, and when the alarm dragged him back, he felt cheated of rest.
source_text: Martin set his alarm, drew up to the table, and opened Fiske. He did not finish the first paragraph. The lines blurred and ran together and his head nodded. He walked up and down, batting his head savagely with his fists, but he could not conquer the numbness of sleep. He propped the book before him, and propped his eyelids with his fingers, and fell asleep with his eyes wide open. Then he surrendered, and, scarcely conscious of what he did, got off his clothes and into bed. He slept seven hours of heavy, animal-like sleep, and awoke by the alarm, feeling that he had not had enough.

text: In the span of a single afternoon during that inaugural week, Martin and his companion Joe dispatched two hundred pale shirts. Joe operated the tiler—a contraption where a heated iron dangled from a steel cord that supplied the necessary weight. With it, he pressed the yoke, cuffs, and collar band, aligning the latter at a sharp angle to the garment, and polished the chest to a gleaming finish. Each completed shirt he tossed onto a rack suspended between himself and Martin, who seized them and set about “backing” them—a duty that involved smoothing every unstarched fold and seam.
source_text: Martin learned to do many things. In the course of the first week, in one afternoon, he and Joe accounted for the two hundred white shirts. Joe ran the tiler, a machine wherein a hot iron was hooked on a steel string which furnished the pressure. By this means he ironed the yoke, wristbands, and neckband, setting the latter at right angles to the shirt, and put the glossy finish on the bosom. As fast as he finished them, he flung the shirts on a rack between him and Martin, who caught them up and “backed” them. This task consisted of ironing all the unstarched portions of the shirts.

text: This much was beyond dispute: the Morses had never wanted him for his own sake or for his writing. So they could hardly want him now for his own sake or for his writing, but only for the renown that clung to him, because he counted among men, and—why pretend otherwise?—because he had a hundred thousand dollars or thereabouts. That was how the middle class weighed a man, and what right had he to imagine anything different? Yet he was proud. He scorned such reckoning. He longed to be prized for himself, or for his writing, which was, in the end, a part of himself. That was how Lizzie prized him. With her, the writing did not even enter. She prized him, the man. That was how Jimmy, the plumber, and all the old crew prized him. They had shown it often enough in the days when he ran beside them; they had shown it that Sunday at Shell Mound Park. His writing could be damned. What they loved, and would fight for, was simply Mart Eden, one of their own and a decent fellow.
source_text: One thing was certain: the Morses had not cared to have him for himself or for his work. Therefore they could not want him now for himself or for his work, but for the fame that was his, because he was somebody amongst men, and—why not?—because he had a hundred thousand dollars or so. That was the way bourgeois society valued a man, and who was he to expect it otherwise? But he was proud. He disdained such valuation. He desired to be valued for himself, or for his work, which, after all, was an expression of himself. That was the way Lizzie valued him. The work, with her, did not even count. She valued him, himself. That was the way Jimmy, the plumber, and all the old gang valued him. That had been proved often enough in the days when he ran with them; it had been proved that Sunday at Shell Mound Park. His work could go hang. What they liked, and were willing to scrap for, was just Mart Eden, one of the bunch and a pretty good guy.

text: Often during those moments, he’d suddenly notice a young tough slouching into the gathering, wearing a boxy coat and a stiff-brimmed Stetson. It came upon him one afternoon at the Gallina Society in Oakland. As he got up from his seat and moved toward the front of the stage, he watched the young hoodlum in the boxy coat and rigid-brim hat stride through the broad doorway at the back of the hall. Five hundred elegantly dressed women turned their heads, drawn by the intensity and fixity of Martin’s stare, trying to spot what he saw. But they found only the vacant central aisle. He watched the young rough shamble down that aisle and wondered if he’d take off the stiff brim, which Martin had never yet glimpsed him without. Straight down the aisle he came, then up onto the stage. Martin felt like weeping over that spectral young self when he considered everything the boy still had to face. The figure swaggered across the platform, right up to Martin, and then faded from the foreground of his mind. The five hundred women applauded gently with gloved hands, trying to hearten the shy, celebrated man who was their guest. And Martin shook the image from his thoughts, smiled, and began his speech.
source_text: And often, at such times, he would abruptly see slouch in among the company a young hoodlum in square-cut coat and under a stiff-rim Stetson hat. It happened to him at the Gallina Society in Oakland one afternoon. As he rose from his chair and stepped forward across the platform, he saw stalk through the wide door at the rear of the great room the young hoodlum with the square-cut coat and stiff-rim hat. Five hundred fashionably gowned women turned their heads, so intent and steadfast was Martin’s gaze, to see what he was seeing. But they saw only the empty centre aisle. He saw the young tough lurching down that aisle and wondered if he would remove the stiff-rim which never yet had he seen him without. Straight down the aisle he came, and up the platform. Martin could have wept over that youthful shade of himself, when he thought of all that lay before him. Across the platform he swaggered, right up to Martin, and into the foreground of Martin’s consciousness disappeared. The five hundred women applauded softly with gloved hands, seeking to encourage the bashful great man who was their guest. And Martin shook the vision from his brain, smiled, and began to speak.

text: Just as his foot touched the pavement’s edge, the thought of Clara surfaced. Clara had always been a stickler for form. On only their second stroll together, she had scolded him sharply for keeping to the inner path, and had decreed with finality that a proper man always took the outer side—when escorting a woman. She had even made a habit of tapping his ankle with her toe whenever they crossed a road, a silent prompt to shift himself to the curb. He puzzled over where she might have gleaned that particular rule of conduct, and whether it had trickled down from some higher circle and was truly correct.
source_text: Just before he reached the sidewalk, he remembered Minnie. Minnie had always been a stickler. She had called him down the second time she walked out with him, because he had gone along on the inside, and she had laid the law down to him that a gentleman always walked on the outside—when he was with a lady. And Minnie had made a practice of kicking his heels, whenever they crossed from one side of the street to the other, to remind him to get over on the outside. He wondered where she had got that item of etiquette, and whether it had filtered down from above and was all right.

text: Then he was standing at the press once more, though now it was the publisher himself who thrust envelopes at him from the far side. Each envelope was a cheque, and Martin snatched at them with a desperate, burning hope, but every one was void. He stood there accepting the empty envelopes through an age of waiting, refusing to release a single one lest it prove to be the prize. Until at length he seized it. With unsteady hands he raised it toward the glow. It read five dollars. “Ha! Ha!” roared the publisher across the ironing board. “Very well, I shall end you,” Martin replied. He stepped into the scullery to retrieve the hatchet, and discovered Joe pressing manuscripts with starch. He pleaded with him to stop, then raised the blade to strike. Yet the hatchet hung motionless in the air, for Martin was abruptly back in the laundry amid a blizzard. No, it was not snow that descended, but cheques of vast sums, the humblest being a thousand dollars. He began to gather and arrange them, bundling them into stacks of one hundred, binding each stack tightly with cord.
source_text: And then he found himself before the mangle, this time receiving the cuffs an editor of a magazine was feeding from the other side. Each cuff was a check, and Martin went over them anxiously, in a fever of expectation, but they were all blanks. He stood there and received the blanks for a million years or so, never letting one go by for fear it might be filled out. At last he found it. With trembling fingers he held it to the light. It was for five dollars. “Ha! Ha!” laughed the editor across the mangle. “Well, then, I shall kill you,” Martin said. He went out into the wash-room to get the axe, and found Joe starching manuscripts. He tried to make him desist, then swung the axe for him. But the weapon remained poised in mid-air, for Martin found himself back in the ironing room in the midst of a snow-storm. No, it was not snow that was falling, but checks of large denomination, the smallest not less than a thousand dollars. He began to collect them and sort them out, in packages of a hundred, tying each package securely with twine.

text: “It is not too late,” she cried out. “Let me show you. Let me prove my love has deepened—that it now outweighs my class and everything I once held sacred. I will scorn all that the bourgeoisie cherishes. I am no longer afraid of living. I will abandon my father and mother, let my name become a mockery among my friends. I will come to you here, this very moment, in free love if you’ll have me, and I shall be proud and joyful at your side. If I once betrayed love, I will now, for love’s sake, betray everything that drove me to that earlier betrayal.”
source_text: “It is not too late,” she cried. “I will show you. I will prove to you that my love has grown, that it is greater to me than my class and all that is dearest to me. All that is dearest to the bourgeoisie I will flout. I am no longer afraid of life. I will leave my father and mother, and let my name become a by-word with my friends. I will come to you here and now, in free love if you will, and I will be proud and glad to be with you. If I have been a traitor to love, I will now, for love’s sake, be a traitor to all that made that earlier treason.”

text: “And what drives Martin to write?” he continued. “It’s because he isn’t swimming in money. Why do you stuff your mind with Saxon and all that culture? Because you don’t have to fight for a living. Your father handles that. He pays for your dresses, your upkeep, everything. What miserable use is our education—yours, mine, Arthur’s, Norman’s? We’re drenched in liberal learning, yet if our fathers went bankrupt today, we’d be scrambling tomorrow to pass teachers’ exams. The finest position you could land, Ruth, would be in a village school or teaching music at some girls’ boarding academy.”
source_text: “And why does Martin want to write?” he went on. “Because he isn’t rolling in wealth. Why do you fill your head with Saxon and general culture? Because you don’t have to make your way in the world. Your father sees to that. He buys your clothes for you, and all the rest. What rotten good is our education, yours and mine and Arthur’s and Norman’s? We’re soaked in general culture, and if our daddies went broke to-day, we’d be falling down to-morrow on teachers’ examinations. The best job you could get, Ruth, would be a country school or music teacher in a girls’ boarding-school.”

text: To crown his misery, “The Crucible”—which he regarded as the finest piece he had ever crafted—slipped from his grasp. In a fit of desperation, rifling wildly through periodicals, he had dispatched it to _The Zephyr_, a San Francisco weekly devoted to fashionable circles. His principal motive for choosing that outlet was its proximity: a mere crossing of the bay from Oakland promised a swift verdict. A fortnight later, his heart surged when the latest issue on the stand displayed his tale in its entirety, adorned with illustrations and given the seat of honor. He strode home with a racing pulse, calculating what sum they might tender for one of his supreme accomplishments. The speed of its acceptance and publication, too, was a balm to his thoughts. That the editor had sent no word of its receipt only deepened the astonishment. After lingering a week, then two, then half a week beyond, need overpowered shyness, and he penned a note to the editor of _The Zephyr_, delicately hinting that perhaps through some oversight of the business manager his modest remuneration had been neglected.
source_text: To cap the situation, “The Pot,” which he looked upon as one of the best things he had written, was lost to him. In despair, casting about frantically among the magazines, he had sent it to _The Billow_, a society weekly in San Francisco. His chief reason for submitting it to that publication was that, having only to travel across the bay from Oakland, a quick decision could be reached. Two weeks later he was overjoyed to see, in the latest number on the news-stand, his story printed in full, illustrated, and in the place of honor. He went home with leaping pulse, wondering how much they would pay him for one of the best things he had done. Also, the celerity with which it had been accepted and published was a pleasant thought to him. That the editor had not informed him of the acceptance made the surprise more complete. After waiting a week, two weeks, and half a week longer, desperation conquered diffidence, and he wrote to the editor of _The Billow_, suggesting that possibly through some negligence of the business manager his little account had been overlooked.

text: He had never volunteered to show her his writing. A fastidious reserve had held him back. Besides, she had been deep in her university studies, and he shrank from stealing any of her time. But once she had earned her degree, she herself asked to see something of what he had been working on. Martin felt a surge of pride and shyness. Here was a judge. She was a bachelor of arts. She had studied literature under accomplished teachers. Perhaps the editors were capable judges too. Yet she would be unlike them. She would not pass him a formulaic rejection slip, nor would she explain that a lack of taste for his work did not necessarily signal a lack of worth in it. She would speak, a warm living presence, in her rapid, luminous fashion, and, most vital of all, she would glimpse the true Martin Eden. In his work she would perceive the shape of his heart and soul, and she would begin to grasp something, some small thing, of the fabric of his dreams and the force of his power.
source_text: He had never offered to show her his work. A fastidious delicacy had prevented him. Besides, she had been studying heavily at the university, and he felt averse to robbing her of her time. But when she had taken her degree, she asked him herself to let her see something of what he had been doing. Martin was elated and diffident. Here was a judge. She was a bachelor of arts. She had studied literature under skilled instructors. Perhaps the editors were capable judges, too. But she would be different from them. She would not hand him a stereotyped rejection slip, nor would she inform him that lack of preference for his work did not necessarily imply lack of merit in his work. She would talk, a warm human being, in her quick, bright way, and, most important of all, she would catch glimpses of the real Martin Eden. In his work she would discern what his heart and soul were like, and she would come to understand something, a little something, of the stuff of his dreams and the strength of his power.

text: Dinner invitations descended upon Martin in a flood, and the greater their number, the deeper his bewilderment grew. He found himself the guest of honor at an Arden Club banquet, surrounded by distinguished men he had known of and read about his entire life; they assured him that upon reading “The Ring of Bells” in the *Transcontinental* and “The Peri and the Pearl” in *The Hornet*, they had instantly marked him for success. Dear God, he thought, I was starving and dressed in tatters then. Why didn’t you offer me a dinner in those days? That was the moment. The work was already done. If you are feasting me now for work accomplished, why did you not feed me when I was desperate? Not a single word in “The Ring of Bells” or “The Peri and the Pearl” has been altered. No, you are not honoring me now for the work I did. You are honoring me because everyone else is doing so and because it has become a mark of prestige to honor me. You are feeding me now because you are herd creatures; because you belong to the crowd; because the single blind, mechanical impulse of the crowd-mind at this moment is to feed me. And where do Martin Eden and the work Martin Eden produced figure in all of this? he asked himself mournfully, before rising to answer a clever and witty toast with clever and witty words of his own.
source_text: Invitations to dinner poured in on Martin; and the more they poured, the more he puzzled. He sat, the guest of honor, at an Arden Club banquet, with men of note whom he had heard about and read about all his life; and they told him how, when they had read “The Ring of Bells” in the _Transcontinental_, and “The Peri and the Pearl” in _The Hornet_, they had immediately picked him for a winner. My God! and I was hungry and in rags, he thought to himself. Why didn’t you give me a dinner then? Then was the time. It was work performed. If you are feeding me now for work performed, why did you not feed me then when I needed it? Not one word in “The Ring of Bells,” nor in “The Peri and the Pearl” has been changed. No; you’re not feeding me now for work performed. You are feeding me because everybody else is feeding me and because it is an honor to feed me. You are feeding me now because you are herd animals; because you are part of the mob; because the one blind, automatic thought in the mob-mind just now is to feed me. And where does Martin Eden and the work Martin Eden performed come in in all this? he asked himself plaintively, then arose to respond cleverly and wittily to a clever and witty toast.

text: “Listen, Mart,” the other man said, his voice low but heating up, “I showed up this morning to see you. Get it? I didn’t come to pick up any damn laundry. I came to catch up, like old friends do, and you push a bundle of laundry at me. Let me tell you something. You can take that laundry and go straight to hell.”
source_text: “Now look here, Mart,” the other said slowly, with kindling anger, “I come here this mornin’ to see you. Savve? I didn’t come here to get no laundry. I come here for a talk for old friends’ sake, and you shove a laundry at me. I tell you what you can do. You can take that laundry an’ go to hell.”

text: During the twenty-five days he toiled over “The Sun’s Disgrace,” he scraped together six dollars and fifty cents from mercenary scribbling. One jest netted him fifty cents; another, peddled to a prestigious comic periodical, yielded a dollar. Afterward, a pair of comic verses brought in two and three dollars apiece. Consequently, with his credit among the shopkeepers drained (though he had coaxed the grocer’s account up to five dollars), his bicycle and suit were reclaimed by the pawnbroker. The typewriter firm renewed its demands for payment, sternly reminding him that the lease stipulated rent must be settled strictly beforehand.
source_text: During the twenty-five days spent on “The Shame of the Sun,” he sold hack-work to the extent of six dollars and fifty cents. A joke had brought in fifty cents, and a second one, sold to a high-grade comic weekly, had fetched a dollar. Then two humorous poems had earned two dollars and three dollars respectively. As a result, having exhausted his credit with the tradesmen (though he had increased his credit with the grocer to five dollars), his wheel and suit of clothes went back to the pawnbroker. The type-writer people were again clamoring for money, insistently pointing out that according to the agreement rent was to be paid strictly in advance.

text: The brief tales came back to him in much the same manner. He pored over them repeatedly, and found them so pleasing that he could not fathom the reason for their return, until one afternoon he stumbled upon a notice in a journal advising that all submissions must be typed. That made it clear. Naturally, editors were too harried to spare the effort and patience for deciphering script. Martin hired a typewriting machine and devoted a full day to conquering its keys. Daily he transcribed his fresh work, and he typed up his older stories the moment they were sent back. He felt a jolt of astonishment when the typewritten pieces, too, began returning. His jaw seemed to set more squarely, his chin jutted with fresh defiance, and he dispatched the manuscripts to unfamiliar editors.
source_text: The short stories were returned in similar fashion. He read them over and over, and liked them so much that he could not puzzle out the cause of their rejection, until, one day, he read in a newspaper that manuscripts should always be typewritten. That explained it. Of course editors were so busy that they could not afford the time and strain of reading handwriting. Martin rented a typewriter and spent a day mastering the machine. Each day he typed what he composed, and he typed his earlier manuscripts as fast as they were returned him. He was surprised when the typed ones began to come back. His jaw seemed to become squarer, his chin more aggressive, and he bundled the manuscripts off to new editors.

text: “I envy how lightly you harvest wisdom,” Martin exclaimed after thirty minutes of silent scrutiny. He had been meticulously dissecting Brissenden’s intellectual machinery. “You’re an unrepentant dogmatist, and therein lies your brilliance. You proclaim as gospel the freshest truths that science can only validate through slow, retrospective proof. You leap straight to right verdicts. You bypass all the drudgery with breathtaking speed. By some supralogical instinct, you race like light itself toward the heart of things.”
source_text: “I wish I could gather knowledge as carelessly,” Martin broke out half an hour later. He had been closely analyzing Brissenden’s mental equipment. “You are a sheer dogmatist, and that’s what makes it so marvellous. You state dogmatically the latest facts which science has been able to establish only by _à posteriori_ reasoning. You jump at correct conclusions. You certainly short-cut with a vengeance. You feel your way with the speed of light, by some hyperrational process, to truth.”

text: “I think they’re lovely, truly lovely,” she said, “but you can’t sell them, can you? You understand what I’m trying to say,” she added, almost pleading. “This writing of yours simply isn’t practical. Something—perhaps the market—keeps you from making a living by it. And please, darling, don’t take this the wrong way. I’m flattered, even proud—I wouldn’t be a real woman if I weren’t—that you write these poems for me. But they don’t bring our marriage any closer. Can’t you see, Martin? Don’t imagine I’m being mercenary. It’s love, the weight of our future, that burdens me. A full year has passed since we discovered we loved each other, and our wedding day is no nearer. Don’t think me immodest for speaking of our wedding so plainly—truly, my whole heart, everything I am, hangs in the balance. If you’re so devoted to writing, why not try for a position on a newspaper? Why not become a reporter—just for a time, at least?”
source_text: “I think they are beautiful, very beautiful,” she said; “but you can’t sell them, can you? You see what I mean,” she said, almost pleaded. “This writing of yours is not practical. Something is the matter—maybe it is with the market—that prevents you from earning a living by it. And please, dear, don’t misunderstand me. I am flattered, and made proud, and all that—I could not be a true woman were it otherwise—that you should write these poems to me. But they do not make our marriage possible. Don’t you see, Martin? Don’t think me mercenary. It is love, the thought of our future, with which I am burdened. A whole year has gone by since we learned we loved each other, and our wedding day is no nearer. Don’t think me immodest in thus talking about our wedding, for really I have my heart, all that I am, at stake. Why don’t you try to get work on a newspaper, if you are so bound up in your writing? Why not become a reporter?—for a while, at least?”

text: Five dollars for five thousand words—a penny per ten words, the going rate for art. The letdown of it, the fraud of it, the disgrace of it, consumed his mind; behind his shut lids, in blazing digits, glowed the $3.85 he owed the grocer. He trembled, feeling a deep ache in his bones. The base of his spine throbbed in particular. His skull pounded, the crown pounded, the rear pounded, the brain inside pounded and seemed to expand, while the pain above his eyebrows became unbearable. And beneath those brows, lodged under his eyelids, sat the pitiless $3.85. He opened his eyes to flee it, but the room’s white glare scorched his eyeballs and drove him to shut them again, where the $3.85 rose up to meet him once more.
source_text: Five dollars for five thousand words, ten words for a cent, the market price for art. The disappointment of it, the lie of it, the infamy of it, were uppermost in his thoughts; and under his closed eyelids, in fiery figures, burned the “$3.85” he owed the grocer. He shivered, and was aware of an aching in his bones. The small of his back ached especially. His head ached, the top of it ached, the back of it ached, the brains inside of it ached and seemed to be swelling, while the ache over his brows was intolerable. And beneath the brows, planted under his lids, was the merciless “$3.85.” He opened his eyes to escape it, but the white light of the room seemed to sear the balls and forced him to close his eyes, when the “$3.85” confronted him again.

text: The book rested closed upon his forefinger, and before he turned he felt a tremor from a fresh impression—not of the girl herself, but of her brother’s phrasing. Beneath that muscular frame he was a knot of trembling sensitivity. At the lightest touch of the outer world upon his awareness, his thoughts, sympathies, and feelings would leap and flicker like dancing flame. He was exceptionally open and reactive, and his high-strung imagination constantly wove connections of similarity and contrast. “Mr. Eden”—that was what had stirred him, he who had been called “Eden,” or “Martin Eden,” or simply “Martin,” his entire life. And “_Mister_!” That’s really something, he remarked inwardly. His mind seemed to shift, in a flash, into a vast dark chamber, and around his consciousness he glimpsed endless scenes from his past: stokeholds and forecastles, camps and shorelines, jails and drinking dens, fever wards and slum alleys, all threaded together by the way he had been addressed in each of those places.
source_text: The book was closed on his forefinger, and before he turned he was thrilling to the first new impression, which was not of the girl, but of her brother’s words. Under that muscled body of his he was a mass of quivering sensibilities. At the slightest impact of the outside world upon his consciousness, his thoughts, sympathies, and emotions leapt and played like lambent flame. He was extraordinarily receptive and responsive, while his imagination, pitched high, was ever at work establishing relations of likeness and difference. “Mr. Eden,” was what he had thrilled to—he who had been called “Eden,” or “Martin Eden,” or just “Martin,” all his life. And “_Mister_!” It was certainly going some, was his internal comment. His mind seemed to turn, on the instant, into a vast camera obscura, and he saw arrayed around his consciousness endless pictures from his life, of stoke-holes and forecastles, camps and beaches, jails and boozing-kens, fever-hospitals and slum streets, wherein the thread of association was the fashion in which he had been addressed in those various situations.

text: They talked. They covered a wide range of subjects, taking turns ordering Scotch and soda—now Brissenden, now Martin. Martin, whose head was unusually strong, was astonished by how much the other could drink, and every so often he paused to be amazed by Brissenden’s talk. It didn’t take him long to realize that Brissenden seemed to know everything, and to conclude that this was the second truly intellectual man he had ever encountered. Yet he observed that Brissenden possessed something Professor Caldwell lacked—namely, fire, that incisive flash of insight, the wild, unrestrained blaze of genius. Living speech poured out of him. His thin lips worked like a machine’s dies, stamping out phrases that cut and stung; then again, pursing tenderly around the half-formed sounds they were shaping, those lips produced soft, velvety utterances, mellow phrases full of warmth and splendor, of aching beauty, echoing with life’s mystery and unfathomability; and at other times those thin lips became a bugle, blaring the crash and chaos of cosmic struggle, phrases that rang as pure as silver, that glowed like star-filled expanses, that captured science’s ultimate conclusions yet said something more—the poet’s truth, the transcendent reality, elusive and beyond the reach of ordinary words, yet somehow conveyed through the subtle, nearly ungraspable overtones of everyday language. Through some miraculous vision, he saw past the furthest boundaries of empirical thought, where no language yet existed to tell of it, and yet, by a golden miracle of speech, investing familiar words with unfamiliar meanings, he delivered to Martin’s mind messages that would be incommunicable to ordinary souls.
source_text: They talked. They talked about many things, and now Brissenden and now Martin took turn in ordering Scotch and soda. Martin, who was extremely strong-headed, marvelled at the other’s capacity for liquor, and ever and anon broke off to marvel at the other’s conversation. He was not long in assuming that Brissenden knew everything, and in deciding that here was the second intellectual man he had met. But he noted that Brissenden had what Professor Caldwell lacked—namely, fire, the flashing insight and perception, the flaming uncontrol of genius. Living language flowed from him. His thin lips, like the dies of a machine, stamped out phrases that cut and stung; or again, pursing caressingly about the inchoate sound they articulated, the thin lips shaped soft and velvety things, mellow phrases of glow and glory, of haunting beauty, reverberant of the mystery and inscrutableness of life; and yet again the thin lips were like a bugle, from which rang the crash and tumult of cosmic strife, phrases that sounded clear as silver, that were luminous as starry spaces, that epitomized the final word of science and yet said something more—the poet’s word, the transcendental truth, elusive and without words which could express, and which none the less found expression in the subtle and all but ungraspable connotations of common words. He, by some wonder of vision, saw beyond the farthest outpost of empiricism, where was no language for narration, and yet, by some golden miracle of speech, investing known words with unknown significances, he conveyed to Martin’s consciousness messages that were incommunicable to ordinary souls.

text: Joy doesn’t come from what you accomplish—it comes from the act itself. You don’t need to tell me. I know it. You know it too. Beauty wounds you. It’s a constant ache inside, an unhealing gash, a blade of fire. Why waste yourself on magazines? Make beauty your purpose. Why would you turn beauty into currency? Besides, you can’t; so there’s no point in me getting worked up about it. You could read magazines for a thousand years and never find a single line worth what Keats offers. Walk away from fame and money, book passage on a ship tomorrow, and return to your sea.
source_text: “It is not in what you succeed in doing that you get your joy, but in the doing of it. You can’t tell me. I know it. You know it. Beauty hurts you. It is an everlasting pain in you, a wound that does not heal, a knife of flame. Why should you palter with magazines? Let beauty be your end. Why should you mint beauty into gold? Anyway, you can’t; so there’s no use in my getting excited over it. You can read the magazines for a thousand years and you won’t find the value of one line of Keats. Leave fame and coin alone, sign away on a ship to-morrow, and go back to your sea.”

text: Sunday was meant for cramming for the high school exam, but the article on pearl diving seduced him, and he surrendered the hours to the white-hot thrill of reshaping the beauty and romance blazing inside him. That morning’s _Examiner_ hadn’t printed his treasure-hunting piece, yet his mood stayed aloft, untouched. Ignoring two calls to the table, he skipped the hefty Sunday dinner Mr. Higginbotham always laid on as a trophy of his worldly success. To Mr. Higginbotham, that meal proclaimed his prosperity, and he sanctified it with trite little sermons on American institutions and the chances they offered any industrious man to climb—his own climb, as he never failed to note, having lifted him from grocery clerk to proprietor of Higginbotham’s Cash Store.
source_text: Sunday he had intended to devote to studying for the high school examination, but the pearl-diving article lured him away, and he spent the day in the white-hot fever of re-creating the beauty and romance that burned in him. The fact that the _Examiner_ of that morning had failed to publish his treasure-hunting article did not dash his spirits. He was at too great a height for that, and having been deaf to a twice-repeated summons, he went without the heavy Sunday dinner with which Mr. Higginbotham invariably graced his table. To Mr. Higginbotham such a dinner was advertisement of his worldly achievement and prosperity, and he honored it by delivering platitudinous sermonettes upon American institutions and the opportunity said institutions gave to any hard-working man to rise—the rise, in his case, which he pointed out unfailingly, being from a grocer’s clerk to the ownership of Higginbotham’s Cash Store.

text: While Martin spoke, he studied Brissenden intently, his gaze traveling from the long, gaunt, aristocratic features and slumped shoulders to the overcoat draped over a nearby chair, its pockets stretched and swollen with the weight of many books. Brissenden's face and his long, fine hands were tanned by the sun—deeply tanned, Martin noted with surprise. The tan unsettled him. It was obvious that Brissenden was no lover of the outdoors. So how had the sun marked him so fiercely? There was something troubling and suggestive in that tan, Martin mused, as his attention shifted back to the face—thin, with prominent cheekbones and deep hollows, and adorned by the most elegantly chiseled aquiline nose he had ever encountered. The eyes themselves were unremarkable in size, neither notably large nor small, and their hue was an indeterminate brown; yet within them smoldered a fire, or more precisely, an expression that was twofold and strangely at odds with itself. Bold, unyielding, even brutally severe, they simultaneously stirred compassion. Martin caught himself feeling sorry for the man without understanding why, though he would soon discover the reason.
source_text: And while Martin told him, he was busy studying Brissenden, ranging from a long, lean, aristocratic face and drooping shoulders to the overcoat on a neighboring chair, its pockets sagged and bulged by the freightage of many books. Brissenden’s face and long, slender hands were browned by the sun—excessively browned, Martin thought. This sunburn bothered Martin. It was patent that Brissenden was no outdoor man. Then how had he been ravaged by the sun? Something morbid and significant attached to that sunburn, was Martin’s thought as he returned to a study of the face, narrow, with high cheek-bones and cavernous hollows, and graced with as delicate and fine an aquiline nose as Martin had ever seen. There was nothing remarkable about the size of the eyes. They were neither large nor small, while their color was a nondescript brown; but in them smouldered a fire, or, rather, lurked an expression dual and strangely contradictory. Defiant, indomitable, even harsh to excess, they at the same time aroused pity. Martin found himself pitying him he knew not why, though he was soon to learn.

text: For an instant, it felt as if his heart had ceased to beat. _Filthy_! He had never imagined such a thing. He hadn't intended it. The entire drawing blazed before him in fiery script, and in that searing light he searched desperately for any trace of filth. Then his pulse resumed. He was innocent.
source_text: For the moment it seemed to him that his heart stood still. _Nasty_! He had never dreamed it. He had not meant it. The whole sketch stood before him in letters of fire, and in such blaze of illumination he sought vainly for nastiness. Then his heart began to beat again. He was not guilty.

text: “It’s no use now,” he murmured. Lizzie’s voice echoed in his mind. “I’m unwell—not in my flesh, no. It’s my spirit, my mind. All sense of worth has drained from me. Nothing matters anymore. Had you come to me like this a few months back, things might have turned out otherwise. But now, it’s simply too far gone.”
source_text: “It is too late,” he said. He remembered Lizzie’s words. “I am a sick man—oh, not my body. It is my soul, my brain. I seem to have lost all values. I care for nothing. If you had been this way a few months ago, it would have been different. It is too late, now.”

text: His rebellious limbs thrashed weakly, twitching in erratic spasms. Yet he had outwitted them, and the stubborn life force that drove their flailing. He had sunk too far. They could never haul him back up. He drifted as if suspended in a drowsy sea of hallucinatory light. Hues and luminous glows wrapped around him, soaked through him, saturated his being. What was that? It looked like a lighthouse, but it pulsed inside his skull—a flickering, brilliant white beam. It blinked faster and faster. A long, rolling thunder of noise swelled, and he felt himself tumbling down an immense, never-ending staircase. And somewhere at its base he plunged into blackness. That much he grasped. He had plunged into blackness. And the moment he grasped it, he grasped nothing at all.
source_text: His wilful hands and feet began to beat and churn about, spasmodically and feebly. But he had fooled them and the will to live that made them beat and churn. He was too deep down. They could never bring him to the surface. He seemed floating languidly in a sea of dreamy vision. Colors and radiances surrounded him and bathed him and pervaded him. What was that? It seemed a lighthouse; but it was inside his brain—a flashing, bright white light. It flashed swifter and swifter. There was a long rumble of sound, and it seemed to him that he was falling down a vast and interminable stairway. And somewhere at the bottom he fell into darkness. That much he knew. He had fallen into darkness. And at the instant he knew, he ceased to know.

text: One afternoon, having fed her children the final scraps the pantry would yield, Ana spent her last copper coins on a jug of sour red. When Tomás stepped into her kitchen to draw water, she beckoned him to the table and poured. He lifted his cup to her enduring wellness, and she answered with a toast to his. She drank next to the fortune of his labors, while he raised his glass to the wish that Elias Voss might appear and settle his laundry debt. Elias Voss was a traveling woodworker who rarely honored his accounts and stood three dollars in arrears with Ana.
source_text: On a day when she had filled her brood with what was left in the house, Maria invested her last fifteen cents in a gallon of cheap wine. Martin, coming into her kitchen to fetch water, was invited to sit down and drink. He drank her very-good health, and in return she drank his. Then she drank to prosperity in his undertakings, and he drank to the hope that James Grant would show up and pay her for his washing. James Grant was a journeymen carpenter who did not always pay his bills and who owed Maria three dollars.

text: It came across as a warning. His tone was resolute, his gaze burned bright, the contours of his features had sharpened into severity. She felt as though the slant of his chin had shifted; its set had turned disturbingly combative. Simultaneously, a surge of raw masculinity appeared to radiate from him and press against her.
source_text: It sounded like a threat. His voice was determined, his eyes were flashing, the lines of his face had grown harsh. And to her it seemed that the angle of his jaw had changed; its pitch had become unpleasantly aggressive. At the same time a wave of intense virility seemed to surge out from him and impinge upon her.

text: More wondrous than his explorations of the mind was the romance that consumed him. The cosmos wasn’t astonishing merely because of its atoms and molecules, driven by the thrust of inescapable forces; it was astonishing because Ruth inhabited it. She was the most extraordinary being he had ever encountered, imagined, or divined.
source_text: Greater than his adventure in the world of thought was his love-adventure. The world itself was not so amazing because of the atoms and molecules that composed it according to the propulsions of irresistible force; what made it amazing was the fact that Ruth lived in it. She was the most amazing thing he had ever known, or dreamed, or guessed.

text: “Look, here’s how it struck me,” he continued. “Back when I was fully myself, not a soul beyond my own circle gave a damn about me. Once my pages were all penned, those who glanced at the drafts showed no feeling for them. Indeed, on account of the things I’d set down, they seemed to hold me in still lower regard. To have written such things felt as though I’d performed deeds that were, to put it mildly, degrading. ‘Find work,’ they all told me.”
source_text: “You see, it appears this way to me,” he went on. “When I was all that I am now, nobody out of my own class seemed to care for me. When my books were all written, no one who had read the manuscripts seemed to care for them. In point of fact, because of the stuff I had written they seemed to care even less for me. In writing the stuff it seemed that I had committed acts that were, to say the least, derogatory. ‘Get a job,’ everybody said.”

text: No sooner had their paths crossed than the Market Street trolley rumbled up and halted to swallow a swarm of late-day buyers. From the pressure of his hand at her elbow as he guided her aboard, Mrs. Delacourt knew he would not climb after her. She pivoted on the running board and gazed back at him. The gauntness of his features pierced her heart anew.
source_text: Scarcely had they met when the Telegraph Avenue car came along and stopped to take on a crowd of afternoon shoppers. Mrs. Higginbotham divined from the grip on her arm as he helped her on, that he was not going to follow her. She turned on the step and looked down upon him. His haggard face smote her to the heart again.

text: Each evening, having reviewed the grammar manual until its rules were fixed in his mind, he opened the lexicon and committed twenty fresh terms to memory. He soon discovered this was no trifling exercise, and while manning the helm or standing watch he ceaselessly rehearsed his swelling catalogue of sounds and meanings, invariably murmuring himself into slumber. Constructions such as “I have never,” “were I to,” and “these matters,” along with numerous variants, he whispered under his breath to school his tongue to the speech that Ruth employed. The syllables “and” and “ing,” with the final consonants struck sharply, he practiced thousands of times; and to his astonishment he perceived that his English was growing purer and more precise than that of the officers themselves and the well-bred speculators in the saloon who had underwritten the voyage.
source_text: After he had been through the grammar repeatedly, he took up the dictionary and added twenty words a day to his vocabulary. He found that this was no light task, and at wheel or lookout he steadily went over and over his lengthening list of pronunciations and definitions, while he invariably memorized himself to sleep. “Never did anything,” “if I were,” and “those things,” were phrases, with many variations, that he repeated under his breath in order to accustom his tongue to the language spoken by Ruth. “And” and “ing,” with the “d” and “g” pronounced emphatically, he went over thousands of times; and to his surprise he noticed that he was beginning to speak cleaner and more correct English than the officers themselves and the gentleman-adventurers in the cabin who had financed the expedition.

text: “Listen to me, Mart,” he said, “there’ll be no children toiling in my laundry—not on your life. And no living soul will work a minute past six in the evening. Mark my words! There’ll be machines enough and hands enough to get it all done in proper working hours, and Mart, so help me, I’ll make you superintendent of the whole outfit—the entire operation, every bit of it. Now here’s the plan. I’ll climb onto the water-wagon and save every penny for two years—save hard—and then—”
source_text: “I tell yeh, Mart, they won’t be no kids workin’ in my laundry—not on yer life. An’ they won’t be no workin’ a livin’ soul after six P.M. You hear me talk! They’ll be machinery enough an’ hands enough to do it all in decent workin’ hours, an’ Mart, s’help me, I’ll make yeh superintendent of the shebang—the whole of it, all of it. Now here’s the scheme. I get on the water-wagon an’ save my money for two years—save an’ then—”

text: For, in truth, he had never discovered his lasting sanctuary. Wherever circumstance cast him, he had adapted—always welcomed, always admired, for he pulled his weight in labor and leisure and never shrank from defending his dignity or earning esteem. Yet he had never truly settled. His adjustment was enough to content those around him but never enough to content his own soul. A quiet disquiet had forever haunted him, a summons from some distant realm echoing perpetually, and he had roamed through existence pursuing it until he stumbled upon literature, beauty, and devotion. And now, surrounded by all this, he stood alone among the companions of his wanderings—the sole one capable of crossing the threshold of the Morse household.
source_text: For, after all, he had never found his permanent abiding place. He had fitted in wherever he found himself, been a favorite always and everywhere by virtue of holding his own at work and at play and by his willingness and ability to fight for his rights and command respect. But he had never taken root. He had fitted in sufficiently to satisfy his fellows but not to satisfy himself. He had been perturbed always by a feeling of unrest, had heard always the call of something from beyond, and had wandered on through life seeking it until he found books and art and love. And here he was, in the midst of all this, the only one of all the comrades he had adventured with who could have made themselves eligible for the inside of the Morse home.

text: While he traded banalities with those simple souls, the towering shelves of the library rose before his mind’s eye, laden with centuries of wisdom. A bitter smile crossed his lips at the absurd contrast, and a wave of doubt swept over him. Yet between his inward reflections and outward civilities, he still managed to observe the crowd streaming from the playhouse. And then he saw Her, bathed in lamplight, flanked by her brother and the unfamiliar bespectacled youth, and his heart faltered. He had long awaited this instant. He had just enough time to register the airy, pale veil that crowned her regal head, the elegant drape of her cloaked form, the poised grace of her step and of the gloved hand lifting her hem; and then she vanished, leaving him gazing at the two cannery girls, at their gaudy attempts at finery, their pitiable striving for neatness and charm, the shoddy fabric, the shoddy bows, and the shoddy rings upon their fingers. A pull at his sleeve roused him, and a voice said—
source_text: And while he exchanged the stupidities of stupid minds with them, before his inner sight towered the book-shelves of the library, filled with the wisdom of the ages. He smiled bitterly at the incongruity of it, and was assailed by doubts. But between inner vision and outward pleasantry he found time to watch the theatre crowd streaming by. And then he saw Her, under the lights, between her brother and the strange young man with glasses, and his heart seemed to stand still. He had waited long for this moment. He had time to note the light, fluffy something that hid her queenly head, the tasteful lines of her wrapped figure, the gracefulness of her carriage and of the hand that caught up her skirts; and then she was gone and he was left staring at the two girls of the cannery, at their tawdry attempts at prettiness of dress, their tragic efforts to be clean and trim, the cheap cloth, the cheap ribbons, and the cheap rings on the fingers. He felt a tug at his arm, and heard a voice saying:-

text: He possessed a scholar’s intellect, and beneath his capacity to absorb knowledge lay the unyielding force of his character and his devotion to Ruth. The grammar book he had brought aboard he studied over and over, until his fresh, untiring mind had made it his own. He caught the slovenly speech of his fellow sailors and took quiet care to reshape their clumsy phrases in his thoughts. To his deep delight, he sensed his ear growing attuned, as if he were acquiring grammatical nerves. A double negative struck him like a sour note, though often, from sheer want of habit, the sour note slipped from his own mouth. His tongue could not unlearn its old ways in a single day.
