source_text: His was the student’s mind, and behind his ability to learn was the indomitability of his nature and his love for Ruth. The grammar he had taken along he went through again and again until his unjaded brain had mastered it. He noticed the bad grammar used by his shipmates, and made a point of mentally correcting and reconstructing their crudities of speech. To his great joy he discovered that his ear was becoming sensitive and that he was developing grammatical nerves. A double negative jarred him like a discord, and often, from lack of practice, it was from his own lips that the jar came. His tongue refused to learn new tricks in a day.

text: “It feels so childish, attending evening classes at my age. Still, I could bear that if I believed it would be worth the effort. But I doubt it will be. I can pick things up faster than any instructor could impart them. It would only squander my hours—” his mind turned to her and the ache to possess her—“and time is the one luxury I cannot afford. Truthfully, I have none to lose.”
source_text: “It seems so babyish for me to be going to night school. But I wouldn’t mind that if I thought it would pay. But I don’t think it will pay. I can do the work quicker than they can teach me. It would be a loss of time—” he thought of her and his desire to have her—“and I can’t afford the time. I haven’t the time to spare, in fact.”

text: “Well,” he admitted, “I felt a chill of fear at first, but I grew accustomed to it. Still, my heart ached for that poor girl, and pity drove out the dread. She was lovely in soul as much as in face, and her mind was only lightly clouded; yet she was condemned to languish there, living like some primitive creature while her body decayed by inches. Leprosy is more dreadful than you could ever conceive.”
source_text: “Well,” he confessed, “I was a bit shivery at first; but I got used to it. I used to feel sorry for that poor girl, though. That made me forget to be afraid. She was such a beauty, in spirit as well as in appearance, and she was only slightly touched; yet she was doomed to lie there, living the life of a primitive savage and rotting slowly away. Leprosy is far more terrible than you can imagine it.”

text: What the hell does Latin matter?" he asked his reflection that night. "I wish the dead would just stay dead. Why should they, long gone, have any hold over me and the beauty inside me? Beauty is living and timeless. Languages rise and fade. They're nothing but the ashes of the dead.
source_text: “What in hell has Latin to do with it?” he demanded before his mirror that night. “I wish dead people would stay dead. Why should I and the beauty in me be ruled by the dead? Beauty is alive and everlasting. Languages come and go. They are the dust of the dead.”

text: That summer proved grueling for Martin. Editors and manuscript readers had scattered on holiday, and magazines that typically sent a verdict within three weeks now held onto his work for three months or longer. The only comfort he found was that the standstill cut his postage costs. Only the predatory journals seemed to stay in business, and to them he sent all his earliest pieces, like “Pearl-diving,” “The Sea as a Career,” “Turtle-catching,” and “The Northeast Trades.” He never earned a cent from these submissions. Indeed, after six months of back-and-forth, he reached a settlement: he got a safety razor in exchange for “Turtle-catching,” while _The Acropolis_, having promised five dollars cash and five yearly subscriptions for “The Northeast Trades,” made good solely on the subscriptions.
source_text: It was a hard summer for Martin. Manuscript readers and editors were away on vacation, and publications that ordinarily returned a decision in three weeks now retained his manuscript for three months or more. The consolation he drew from it was that a saving in postage was effected by the deadlock. Only the robber-publications seemed to remain actively in business, and to them Martin disposed of all his early efforts, such as “Pearl-diving,” “The Sea as a Career,” “Turtle-catching,” and “The Northeast Trades.” For these manuscripts he never received a penny. It is true, after six months’ correspondence, he effected a compromise, whereby he received a safety razor for “Turtle-catching,” and that _The Acropolis_, having agreed to give him five dollars cash and five yearly subscriptions: for “The Northeast Trades,” fulfilled the second part of the agreement.

text: He proved a fine combatant—tenacious, wholehearted—and would gladly have fed the machine for many seasons more; yet he was bleeding out, and the contest would be decided not in seasons but in days. Every week his lodging fee drew him closer to ruin, while the postage on forty scripts drained him nearly as deeply. He had ceased acquiring books, pinched pennies in small ways, and struggled to postpone the certain finish; though thrift was foreign to him, and he hastened the end by a week when he sent his sister Marian five dollars for a gown.
source_text: He was a good fighter, whole-souled and stubborn, and he would have been content to continue feeding the machine for years; but he was bleeding to death, and not years but weeks would determine the fight. Each week his board bill brought him nearer destruction, while the postage on forty manuscripts bled him almost as severely. He no longer bought books, and he economized in petty ways and sought to delay the inevitable end; though he did not know how to economize, and brought the end nearer by a week when he gave his sister Marian five dollars for a dress.

text: Through his reading, he had come to recognize two distinct traditions in fiction. One portrayed man as a god, disregarding his earthbound beginnings; the other cast him as mere dirt, blind to his heaven-sent visions and divine potential. In Martin’s view, both the godly and the earthy traditions failed, each erring through an overly narrow focus and intent. A middle path came closer to the truth—one that offered no flattery to the godly school, yet pushed back against the brute savagery of the earthy school. Martin believed his story “Adventure,” which had bored Ruth so thoroughly, had reached his ideal of truth in fiction, and he had laid out his broader thoughts on the matter in an essay titled “God and Clod.”
source_text: He had discovered, in the course of his reading, two schools of fiction. One treated of man as a god, ignoring his earthly origin; the other treated of man as a clod, ignoring his heaven-sent dreams and divine possibilities. Both the god and the clod schools erred, in Martin’s estimation, and erred through too great singleness of sight and purpose. There was a compromise that approximated the truth, though it flattered not the school of god, while it challenged the brute-savageness of the school of clod. It was his story, “Adventure,” which had dragged with Ruth, that Martin believed had achieved his ideal of the true in fiction; and it was in an essay, “God and Clod,” that he had expressed his views on the whole general subject.

text: Martin looked at her and saw the truth of her words reflected in her overall disheveled look—the unhealthy plumpness, the slumped shoulders, the weary face etched with slack lines, and the way her feet landed with a dull heaviness, utterly drained of spring. It was a distorted echo of the stride that comes from a body both liberated and joyful.
source_text: Martin glanced at her and verified her statement in her general slovenly appearance, in the unhealthy fat, in the drooping shoulders, the tired face with the sagging lines, and in the heavy fall of her feet, without elasticity—a very caricature of the walk that belongs to a free and happy body.

text: Martin had yet to receive the ten dollars promised for “Treasure Hunters” by the Chicago newspaper. He had confirmed its publication while sifting through the archives in the Central Reading-room, but the editor remained silent. His inquiries went unanswered. To be certain his letters had reached their destination, he sent several by registered post. In the end, he could only see it as outright theft—a calculated act of plunder. While he went hungry, his work was stolen from him, the very goods whose sale was his only means of buying bread.
source_text: The ten dollars for which Martin had sold “Treasure Hunters” to the Chicago newspaper did not come to hand. The article had been published, as he had ascertained at the file in the Central Reading-room, but no word could he get from the editor. His letters were ignored. To satisfy himself that they had been received, he registered several of them. It was nothing less than robbery, he concluded—a cold-blooded steal; while he starved, he was pilfered of his merchandise, of his goods, the sale of which was the sole way of getting bread to eat.

text: This was surely passion, she reasoned in the single lucid interval granted to her. If it was not passion, it was something too degrading to name. It could be only passion. She adored the man who encircled her with his arms and whose mouth covered hers. She nestled closer, curling her frame against him with a small, yielding motion. Then, wrenching herself partly free an instant later, she lifted both hands in a swift, triumphant gesture and clasped them around Martin Eden’s bronzed throat. So piercing was the ache of satisfied love and longing that a faint cry escaped her, her fingers loosened, and she sank back into his embrace, nearly unconscious.
source_text: This must be love, she thought, in the one rational moment that was vouchsafed her. If it was not love, it was too shameful. It could be nothing else than love. She loved the man whose arms were around her and whose lips were pressed to hers. She pressed more tightly to him, with a snuggling movement of her body. And a moment later, tearing herself half out of his embrace, suddenly and exultantly she reached up and placed both hands upon Martin Eden’s sunburnt neck. So exquisite was the pang of love and desire fulfilled that she uttered a low moan, relaxed her hands, and lay half-swooning in his arms.

text: A bonita slashed at his pale flesh, and he let out a ringing laugh. The fish had torn a chunk from him, and the sharp pain jolted him back to his purpose. In the labor of swimming, he had lost sight of why he was there. The _Mariposa’s_ lights were fading far behind, and here he was, stroking through the water with a strange assurance, as if he truly meant to strike out for land a thousand miles distant.
source_text: A bonita struck at his white body, and he laughed aloud. It had taken a piece out, and the sting of it reminded him of why he was there. In the work to do he had forgotten the purpose of it. The lights of the _Mariposa_ were growing dim in the distance, and there he was, swimming confidently, as though it were his intention to make for the nearest land a thousand miles or so away.

text: His raised hand came down and then continued its rapid, even motion. The young man writhed and swore and twisted, yet made no move to bite. Brissenden watched with a solemn expression, though at one point he became agitated, seized the whiskey bottle, and begged, “Here, just let me hit him once.”
source_text: His uplifted hand descended, and thereafter rose and fell in a swift and steady rhythm. The cub struggled and cursed and squirmed, but did not offer to bite. Brissenden looked on gravely, though once he grew excited and gripped the whiskey bottle, pleading, “Here, just let me swat him once.”

text: “Man, this feels just like the good old days,” Jimmy said to the crowd that chuckled at him while Martin and the blonde spun off into a waltz. “And I couldn’t care less. I’m just so damn happy to have them back. Look at them waltz, will you? Smooth as silk. Can you fault any girl?”
source_text: “Gee, it’s like old times,” Jimmy explained to the gang that gave him the laugh as Martin and the blonde whirled away in a waltz. “An’ I don’t give a rap. I’m too damned glad to see ’m back. Watch ’m waltz, eh? It’s like silk. Who’d blame any girl?”

text: He rose and leaned through the cabin’s round window, staring into the churning cream of the wake. The _Mariposa_ rode low under her cargo, and if he gripped the sill and dangled, his boots would graze the sea. He could vanish without a sound. Not a soul would catch it. A burst of spindrift slapped his cheek, salt settling on his mouth like a promise, and he savored it. For a moment he considered penning a farewell verse, then dismissed the notion with a dry chuckle. There was no leisure for it. The urge to be away gnawed at him too fiercely.
source_text: He arose and thrust his head out the port-hole, looking down into the milky wash. The _Mariposa_ was deeply loaded, and, hanging by his hands, his feet would be in the water. He could slip in noiselessly. No one would hear. A smother of spray dashed up, wetting his face. It tasted salt on his lips, and the taste was good. He wondered if he ought to write a swan-song, but laughed the thought away. There was no time. He was too impatient to be gone.

text: “What I intended to say was that I’d be devoting myself to my craft.” Her expression dimmed, and he hurried onward. “Don’t mistake my meaning. This time I’m not entering with some shimmering fantasy. It’s to be a plain, unadorned, practical undertaking. It surpasses another voyage at sea, and the income will exceed anything an untrained man could secure in Oakland.”
source_text: “I meant that I was going to work at my writing.” Her face fell, and he went on hastily. “Don’t misjudge me. I am not going in this time with any iridescent ideas. It is to be a cold, prosaic, matter-of-fact business proposition. It is better than going to sea again, and I shall earn more money than any position in Oakland can bring an unskilled man.”

text: So reasoning, Martin began to doubt the worth of his own fame. It was the middle class that purchased his books and poured its money into his purse, and from what little he understood of that class, he could not see how it might truly grasp or value what he had created. The inner beauty and force of his work meant nothing to the hundreds of thousands who celebrated him and bought his volumes. He was merely the craze of the moment, the fortune-seeker who had scaled Parnassus while the deities slumbered. The masses read him and cheered him with the same brute incomprehension with which they had hurled themselves upon Brissenden’s “Ephemera” and rent it apart—a wolf-pack that now fawned upon him rather than baring its fangs. Fawning or biting, it was all mere accident. One truth he held with utter certainty: “Ephemera” was far greater than anything he had ever written. It was far greater than anything he carried within him. It was a poem for the ages. The homage the crowd paid him, then, was a wretched homage indeed, for that very crowd had dragged “Ephemera” through the mud. He sighed deeply, yet with a kind of contentment. He was glad the last manuscript was sold and that he would soon be finished with everything.
source_text: So it was, reasoning thus, that Martin questioned the validity of his popularity. It was the bourgeoisie that bought his books and poured its gold into his money-sack, and from what little he knew of the bourgeoisie it was not clear to him how it could possibly appreciate or comprehend what he had written. His intrinsic beauty and power meant nothing to the hundreds of thousands who were acclaiming him and buying his books. He was the fad of the hour, the adventurer who had stormed Parnassus while the gods nodded. The hundreds of thousands read him and acclaimed him with the same brute non-understanding with which they had flung themselves on Brissenden’s “Ephemera” and torn it to pieces—a wolf-rabble that fawned on him instead of fanging him. Fawn or fang, it was all a matter of chance. One thing he knew with absolute certitude: “Ephemera” was infinitely greater than anything he had done. It was infinitely greater than anything he had in him. It was a poem of centuries. Then the tribute the mob paid him was a sorry tribute indeed, for that same mob had wallowed “Ephemera” into the mire. He sighed heavily and with satisfaction. He was glad the last manuscript was sold and that he would soon be done with it all.

text: “I hope I’m learning how to speak,” he faltered. “There’s so much inside me, pressing to be uttered. But it’s all so vast. I can’t discover the shapes to give it voice. Sometimes I feel as if the whole earth, existence itself, every last thing, had moved into my chest and was crying out for me to be its tongue. I sense—oh, I can’t put it into words—I sense the immensity of it, yet when I try to talk, I chatter like an infant. To turn feeling and sensation into language, written or spoken, so that the one who reads or hears will feel it turn back again into the very same feeling and sensation—that is a noble labor. Look: I press my face into the grass, and the air I draw through my nose sets me trembling with a swarm of ideas and images. It’s a draught of the cosmos I’ve taken in. I know melody and mirth, and triumph and grief, and strife and dying; and I see apparitions that rise in my mind somehow from the smell of the grass, and I long to recount them to you, to everyone. But how can I? My mouth is locked. I’ve just attempted, aloud, to capture for you what the scent of the grass does to me. And I’ve failed. I’ve only gestured clumsily. My words sound like nonsense to my own ears. And still I’m smothered by the urge to tell. Oh!—” he flung up his hands in a hopeless motion—“it can’t be done! It can’t be grasped! It can’t be shared!”
source_text: “I hope I am learning to talk,” he stammered. “There seems to be so much in me I want to say. But it is all so big. I can’t find ways to say what is really in me. Sometimes it seems to me that all the world, all life, everything, had taken up residence inside of me and was clamoring for me to be the spokesman. I feel—oh, I can’t describe it—I feel the bigness of it, but when I speak, I babble like a little child. It is a great task to transmute feeling and sensation into speech, written or spoken, that will, in turn, in him who reads or listens, transmute itself back into the selfsame feeling and sensation. It is a lordly task. See, I bury my face in the grass, and the breath I draw in through my nostrils sets me quivering with a thousand thoughts and fancies. It is a breath of the universe I have breathed. I know song and laughter, and success and pain, and struggle and death; and I see visions that arise in my brain somehow out of the scent of the grass, and I would like to tell them to you, to the world. But how can I? My tongue is tied. I have tried, by the spoken word, just now, to describe to you the effect on me of the scent of the grass. But I have not succeeded. I have no more than hinted in awkward speech. My words seem gibberish to me. And yet I am stifled with desire to tell. Oh!—” he threw up his hands with a despairing gesture—“it is impossible! It is not understandable! It is incommunicable!”

text: The rough crew faltered for a moment, thrown off by the sudden move, then burst from the streetcar in hot pursuit. The quiet, respectable passengers from Oakland, seated sedately inside, barely registered the young man and woman who dashed ahead and settled onto the forward outside bench. To them, the pair had no link to Jimmy, who vaulted onto the running board, shouting toward the driver—
source_text: The hostile gang was temporarily disconcerted by the manoeuvre, then it dashed from the train in pursuit. The staid and sober Oakland folk who sat upon the car scarcely noted the young fellow and the girl who ran for it and found a seat in front on the outside. They did not connect the couple with Jimmy, who sprang on the steps, crying to the motorman:-

text: “That settlement-house dame is nothing but a rote-reciting sociological magpie. I’m convinced, if you sifted her essence among the constellations, like Tomlinson, you’d uncover not a single fresh idea. Regarding the portraitist, she was an utter weariness. She’d pair off nicely with the cashier. And that musical lady! Never mind how agile her hands, how flawless her method, how remarkable her feeling—the truth remains, she understands nothing of music.”
source_text: “That social-settlement woman is no more than a sociological poll-parrot. I swear, if you winnowed her out between the stars, like Tomlinson, there would be found in her not one original thought. As for the portrait-painter, she was a positive bore. She’d make a good wife for the cashier. And the musician woman! I don’t care how nimble her fingers are, how perfect her technique, how wonderful her expression—the fact is, she knows nothing about music.”

text: “Why not try journalism?” she said once he’d stopped speaking. “You’re so passionate about writing, and I have no doubt you’d thrive. You could climb the ranks in the press and build a reputation. There are quite a few prominent foreign correspondents. They earn handsome pay, and the whole globe is their beat. They get dispatched anywhere—deep into Africa, like Stanley, or to sit down with the Pope, or to venture into uncharted Tibet.”
source_text: “Why don’t you become a reporter?” she asked when he had finished. “You love writing so, and I am sure you would succeed. You could rise in journalism and make a name for yourself. There are a number of great special correspondents. Their salaries are large, and their field is the world. They are sent everywhere, to the heart of Africa, like Stanley, or to interview the Pope, or to explore unknown Thibet.”

text: He took the ferry across to San Francisco and headed straight for a hiring hall. “Anything at all—I’ve got no skilled trade,” he said to the clerk, just as another man walked in, turned out with a touch of dandyism, the way certain laborers dress when they’ve an inborn taste for something finer. The clerk gave a dispirited shake of his head.
source_text: He crossed on the ferry to San Francisco and made his way to an employment office. “Any kind of work, no trade,” he told the agent; and was interrupted by a new-comer, dressed rather foppishly, as some workingmen dress who have instincts for finer things. The agent shook his head despondently.

text: Martin had caught echoes of Herbert Spencer’s name drifting through the park on other days, but one afternoon a gaunt apostle of the philosopher materialized—a threadbare wanderer whose grimy coat was fastened tight at the neck to hide the want of a collar. A fierce dispute erupted, wreathed in cigarette smoke and punctuated by dark arcs of tobacco spit, in which the vagrant stoutly defended his ground, even when a workingman of socialist leanings mocked, “There is no god but the Unknowable, and Herbert Spencer is his mouthpiece.” The whole argument baffled Martin, yet as he traveled onward to the library, he bore with him a freshly kindled curiosity about Spencer, and since the wanderer had repeatedly invoked “First Principles,” Martin sought out that very book.
source_text: Martin had heard Herbert Spencer quoted several times in the park, but one afternoon a disciple of Spencer’s appeared, a seedy tramp with a dirty coat buttoned tightly at the throat to conceal the absence of a shirt. Battle royal was waged, amid the smoking of many cigarettes and the expectoration of much tobacco-juice, wherein the tramp successfully held his own, even when a socialist workman sneered, “There is no god but the Unknowable, and Herbert Spencer is his prophet.” Martin was puzzled as to what the discussion was about, but when he rode on to the library he carried with him a new-born interest in Herbert Spencer, and because of the frequency with which the tramp had mentioned “First Principles,” Martin drew out that volume.

text: The week crept by in a slow grind. The hotel brimmed with guests, and a flood of extra “fancy starch” descended upon them. They rose to feats of heroic endurance. Under the glare of electric lights they battled deep into the night, wolfed down their meals, and even snatched a half hour of labor before breakfast. Martin had given up his cold baths. Every instant was a relentless push, push, push, and Joe presided as the domineering guardian of those instants, herding them with care, letting none slip away, tallying them like a miser hoarding coins, working in a fevered trance, a mania of toil, a frenzied machine, ably seconded by that other machine which dimly remembered it had once called itself Martin Eden—a man.
source_text: The week dragged on. The hotel was full, and extra “fancy starch” poured in upon them. They performed prodigies of valor. They fought late each night under the electric lights, bolted their meals, and even got in a half hour’s work before breakfast. Martin no longer took his cold baths. Every moment was drive, drive, drive, and Joe was the masterful shepherd of moments, herding them carefully, never losing one, counting them over like a miser counting gold, working on in a frenzy, toil-mad, a feverish machine, aided ably by that other machine that thought of itself as once having been one Martin Eden, a man.

text: He looked to Clara for comfort, like a voyager who, struck by a sudden dread of foundering, instinctively scans the dark for the gleam of a lifebuoy. That, at least, had survived the wreck—love and Clara. Everything else had crumbled when measured against the canon of his reading. Yet love and Clara endured that reckoning; in them he discovered a vital principle. Love was life’s most sublime gesture. Nature had laboured to shape him, as she laboured over all sound men, expressly for loving. She had devoted ten thousand generations—yes, a hundred thousand, a million generations—to the work, and he stood as her finest achievement. She had forged love as the sovereign force within him, magnified its potency a thousandfold through the gift of fancy, and cast him into the fleeting world to tremble, dissolve, and unite. Beneath the table his fingers found Clara’s, and a tender clasp was silently exchanged. She flicked her gaze toward him for one luminous moment, her eyes melting and ablaze. So were his own in the rapture that surged through him; nor did he perceive how much of that melting radiance in her look had been kindled by the fire she glimpsed in his.
source_text: He glanced at Ruth for reassurance, much in the same manner that a passenger, with sudden panic thought of possible shipwreck, will strive to locate the life preservers. Well, that much had come out of it—love and Ruth. All the rest had failed to stand the test of the books. But Ruth and love had stood the test; for them he found a biological sanction. Love was the most exalted expression of life. Nature had been busy designing him, as she had been busy with all normal men, for the purpose of loving. She had spent ten thousand centuries—ay, a hundred thousand and a million centuries—upon the task, and he was the best she could do. She had made love the strongest thing in him, increased its power a myriad per cent with her gift of imagination, and sent him forth into the ephemera to thrill and melt and mate. His hand sought Ruth’s hand beside him hidden by the table, and a warm pressure was given and received. She looked at him a swift instant, and her eyes were radiant and melting. So were his in the thrill that pervaded him; nor did he realize how much that was radiant and melting in her eyes had been aroused by what she had seen in his.

text: At once his ambition surged, driving him to reach for something like eternal life. He was unworthy even to fetch water for her—this he knew full well; it had taken a miraculous stroke of luck, a preposterous fluke, for him to set eyes on her, to stand beside her, to speak with her that night. It was pure chance. No credit belonged to him. He had no right to such fortune. His temper turned devout. He grew humble and meek, steeped in self-scorn and prostration. In that condition, sinners approach the penitent bench. He stood convicted of sin. Yet just as the meek and lowly at the penitent bench catch luminous visions of their future lordly estate, so he caught like visions of the condition he would attain by possessing her. But this possession was hazy and formless and utterly unlike any possession he had ever known. His ambition soared on delirious wings, and he saw himself scaling the heights beside her, sharing thoughts with her, delighting in beautiful and noble things with her. It was a possession of the soul he dreamed of, refined past all coarseness, a free comradeship of spirit that he could not shape into clear thought. He did not think it. For that matter, he did not think at all. Sensation overthrew reason, and he quivered and throbbed with emotions he had never known, drifting deliciously on a sea of sensibility where feeling itself was exalted and spiritualized and borne beyond the summits of life.
source_text: And promptly urged his ambition to grasp at eternal life. He was not fit to carry water for her—he knew that; it was a miracle of luck and a fantastic stroke that had enabled him to see her and be with her and talk with her that night. It was accidental. There was no merit in it. He did not deserve such fortune. His mood was essentially religious. He was humble and meek, filled with self-disparagement and abasement. In such frame of mind sinners come to the penitent form. He was convicted of sin. But as the meek and lowly at the penitent form catch splendid glimpses of their future lordly existence, so did he catch similar glimpses of the state he would gain to by possessing her. But this possession of her was dim and nebulous and totally different from possession as he had known it. Ambition soared on mad wings, and he saw himself climbing the heights with her, sharing thoughts with her, pleasuring in beautiful and noble things with her. It was a soul-possession he dreamed, refined beyond any grossness, a free comradeship of spirit that he could not put into definite thought. He did not think it. For that matter, he did not think at all. Sensation usurped reason, and he was quivering and palpitant with emotions he had never known, drifting deliciously on a sea of sensibility where feeling itself was exalted and spiritualized and carried beyond the summits of life.

text: “And what’s the upshot of that? It means you can never grasp ultimate reality. When you come into the world, your mind is a blank. Phenomena, the way things appear, are all that your five senses can ever deliver to your consciousness. As for noumena—things as they are in themselves—they aren’t present in your mind at birth, and they have no doorway to enter later.”
source_text: “And what does that mean? It means that you can never know ultimate reality. Your brains are empty when you are born. Appearances, or phenomena, are all the content your minds can receive from your five senses. Then noumena, which are not in your minds when you are born, have no way of getting in—”

text: “I remain the same. I possess no employment. I seek none. What is more, I shall not begin to seek any. And I continue to hold that Herbert Spencer stands as a towering and admirable figure, while Judge Blount is a thorough and irredeemable fool. I shared supper with him not long ago, so my judgment is firsthand.”
source_text: “I am not changed. I haven’t got a job. I’m not looking for a job. Furthermore, I am not going to look for a job. And I still believe that Herbert Spencer is a great and noble man and that Judge Blount is an unmitigated ass. I had dinner with him the other night, so I ought to know.”

text: The letter held no fire. Not a trace of fury colored its lines. Yet from opening to close, a single chord resounded—wounded pride and dashed hopes. She had believed more of him. She had imagined his reckless youth was behind him, that her devotion would prove reason enough for him to build a steady, honorable life. Now her parents had drawn a hard line and insisted the engagement end. She couldn’t deny they had cause. The match could never bring joy. It had been ill-starred from the beginning. Only one lament slipped through the entire letter, and it cut Martin deep. “If only you had found your footing somewhere and tried to become someone,” she wrote. “But it wasn’t meant to be. Your history was simply too unruly and erratic. I know you aren’t at fault. You could only follow your own character and upbringing. So I don’t hold it against you, Martin. Please keep that in mind. It was nothing more than a mistake. As Father and Mother have always argued, we were never suited, and we should both be grateful it came to light before it was too late.” … “There’s no point in trying to see me,” she added near the end. “It would only be a painful encounter for both of us, and for my mother as well. As things stand, I feel I’ve already caused her enough distress and trouble. I’ll need a lifetime to make up for it.”
source_text: It was not a passionate letter. There were no touches of anger in it. But all the way through, from the first sentence to the last, was sounded the note of hurt and disappointment. She had expected better of him. She had thought he had got over his youthful wildness, that her love for him had been sufficiently worth while to enable him to live seriously and decently. And now her father and mother had taken a firm stand and commanded that the engagement be broken. That they were justified in this she could not but admit. Their relation could never be a happy one. It had been unfortunate from the first. But one regret she voiced in the whole letter, and it was a bitter one to Martin. “If only you had settled down to some position and attempted to make something of yourself,” she wrote. “But it was not to be. Your past life had been too wild and irregular. I can understand that you are not to be blamed. You could act only according to your nature and your early training. So I do not blame you, Martin. Please remember that. It was simply a mistake. As father and mother have contended, we were not made for each other, and we should both be happy because it was discovered not too late.” . . “There is no use trying to see me,” she said toward the last. “It would be an unhappy meeting for both of us, as well as for my mother. I feel, as it is, that I have caused her great pain and worry. I shall have to do much living to atone for it.”

text: The half hour he spent with her before dinner left him radiantly content, wholly at peace with existence. Yet once they were seated at the table, the inevitable letdown and the bone-weariness of the long day closed in on him. He felt the ache behind his eyes and the sharp edge of his own temper. He recalled that it was at this very table—where he now sat mocking and often listless—that he had first dined among civilized people in what he had then taken for an atmosphere of lofty culture and grace. For an instant he saw his younger self, a pitiable figure: a self-conscious brute, drenched in nervous sweat, paralyzed by dread, baffled by the bewildering array of silverware, tormented by the looming servant, straining to vault in a single bound to that dizzy social height, and finally resolving to be simply himself, affecting neither a knowledge nor a polish he did not have.
source_text: The half hour he had with her, before they went in to dinner, left him supremely happy and supremely satisfied with life. Nevertheless, at table, the inevitable reaction and exhaustion consequent upon the hard day seized hold of him. He was aware that his eyes were tired and that he was irritable. He remembered it was at this table, at which he now sneered and was so often bored, that he had first eaten with civilized beings in what he had imagined was an atmosphere of high culture and refinement. He caught a glimpse of that pathetic figure of him, so long ago, a self-conscious savage, sprouting sweat at every pore in an agony of apprehension, puzzled by the bewildering minutiae of eating-implements, tortured by the ogre of a servant, striving at a leap to live at such dizzy social altitude, and deciding in the end to be frankly himself, pretending no knowledge and no polish he did not possess.

text: “Let me point out where your reasoning falters—or rather, what thins the fabric of your judgments,” he said. “You’re missing biology. It finds no foothold in your vision of the world. And I mean the true, interpretive biology: the kind that rises from bedrock, from the laboratory bench and the glassware, from quickened inert matter, all the way up to the broadest aesthetic and social conclusions.”
source_text: “I’ll tell you where you are wrong, or, rather, what weakens your judgments,” he said. “You lack biology. It has no place in your scheme of things.—Oh, I mean the real interpretative biology, from the ground up, from the laboratory and the test-tube and the vitalized inorganic right on up to the widest aesthetic and sociological generalizations.”

text: The very strangeness of his perspectives unsettled Ruth. They were not merely unfamiliar and opposed to her own convictions—she sensed within them seeds of truth that might dislodge or reshape her certainties. At fourteen, she might have been remade by them; but she was twenty-four now, conservative in instinct and training, already hardened into the narrow crevice where birth and formation had placed her. True, his peculiar judgments disturbed her as they fell from his lips, yet she attributed them to his novel character and foreign way of life, and they quickly faded from mind. Still, though she condemned them, the force with which he spoke, the glint in his eyes and the intensity of his expression, never failed to stir her and pull her closer. She could not have imagined that this man, arriving from beyond her world, was in those moments blazing further past her world with larger and deeper visions. Her own boundaries were the boundaries of her world; but narrow minds perceive narrowness only in others. And so she believed her own outlook was exceedingly broad, and that wherever his clashed with hers, it revealed his limitations; and she dreamed of guiding him to see as she saw, of stretching his horizon until it merged with her own.
source_text: It was just such uniqueness of points of view that startled Ruth. Not only were they new to her, and contrary to her own beliefs, but she always felt in them germs of truth that threatened to unseat or modify her own convictions. Had she been fourteen instead of twenty-four, she might have been changed by them; but she was twenty-four, conservative by nature and upbringing, and already crystallized into the cranny of life where she had been born and formed. It was true, his bizarre judgments troubled her in the moments they were uttered, but she ascribed them to his novelty of type and strangeness of living, and they were soon forgotten. Nevertheless, while she disapproved of them, the strength of their utterance, and the flashing of eyes and earnestness of face that accompanied them, always thrilled her and drew her toward him. She would never have guessed that this man who had come from beyond her horizon, was, in such moments, flashing on beyond her horizon with wider and deeper concepts. Her own limits were the limits of her horizon; but limited minds can recognize limitations only in others. And so she felt that her outlook was very wide indeed, and that where his conflicted with hers marked his limitations; and she dreamed of helping him to see as she saw, of widening his horizon until it was identified with hers.

text: He caught glimpses of Lizzie now and then, and it was obvious she mourned the success that had elevated him. It deepened the distance between them. Maybe hoping to close that gap, she gave in when he urged her to enroll in evening classes and a commercial college, and to let an extraordinary dressmaker clothe her at scandalous expense. Day by day she transformed noticeably, until Martin began to question whether he was acting wisely, for he understood that all her submission and striving was done for him. She was laboring to become valuable in his sight—the kind of valuable he appeared to prize. Still, he offered her no encouragement, treating her like a sister and seldom seeking her out.
source_text: He saw Lizzie occasionally, and it was patent that she regretted the greatness that had come to him. It widened the space between them. Perhaps it was with the hope of narrowing it that she yielded to his persuasions to go to night school and business college and to have herself gowned by a wonderful dressmaker who charged outrageous prices. She improved visibly from day to day, until Martin wondered if he was doing right, for he knew that all her compliance and endeavor was for his sake. She was trying to make herself of worth in his eyes—of the sort of worth he seemed to value. Yet he gave her no hope, treating her in brotherly fashion and rarely seeing her.

text: I admit it. And if they were failures in the shop, just think of my torment if they had held forth on other topics. You see, I used to believe that here, where all the refinements of learning were within reach—” He broke off for a moment, observing the ghost of his younger self, in a stiff-rimmed hat and square-cut suit, stride through the door and swagger across the room. “As I was saying, I once thought that everyone here was clever and luminous. But now, from the little I’ve glimpsed of them, most seem a crowd of fools, and ninety percent of the rest are dullards. Now, Professor Caldwell—he stands apart. He is a man, through every fibre of his being and every spark of his intellect.”
source_text: “I confess it. And if they were failures on shop, imagine my sufferings if they had discoursed on other subjects. Why, I used to think that up here, where all the advantages of culture were enjoyed—” He paused for a moment, and watched the youthful shade of himself, in stiff-rim and square-cut, enter the door and swagger across the room. “As I was saying, up here I thought all men and women were brilliant and radiant. But now, from what little I’ve seen of them, they strike me as a pack of ninnies, most of them, and ninety percent of the remainder as bores. Now there’s Professor Caldwell—he’s different. He’s a man, every inch of him and every atom of his gray matter.”

text: They lingered as the day’s splendor slowly faded, speaking in the way lovers do—awed by the miracle of love and by the fate that had so oddly thrown them into each other’s path, while holding the unshakable conviction that their passion reached heights no lovers had ever scaled. Time and again, they circled back to recount their earliest glimpses of one another and to futile efforts to dissect exactly what it was they felt and the full measure of its depth.
source_text: They sat on through the passing glory of the day, talking as lovers are prone to talk, marvelling at the wonder of love and at destiny that had flung them so strangely together, and dogmatically believing that they loved to a degree never attained by lovers before. And they returned insistently, again and again, to a rehearsal of their first impressions of each other and to hopeless attempts to analyze just precisely what they felt for each other and how much there was of it.

text: When Norton strayed deep into the mazes of Kant, Kreis observed that every well-behaved little German philosophy, upon its demise, found its way to Oxford. Shortly thereafter, Norton brought up Hamilton’s Law of Parsimony, whose relevance they at once asserted for each of their own intellectual maneuvers. Meanwhile, Martin clasped his knees and delighted in the whole affair. Yet Norton held no allegiance to Spencer, and he too contended for Martin’s philosophical spirit, directing his words as much toward him as toward his two adversaries.
source_text: When Norton wandered into the intricacies of Kant, Kreis reminded him that all good little German philosophies when they died went to Oxford. A little later Norton reminded them of Hamilton’s Law of Parsimony, the application of which they immediately claimed for every reasoning process of theirs. And Martin hugged his knees and exulted in it all. But Norton was no Spencerian, and he, too, strove for Martin’s philosophic soul, talking as much at him as to his two opponents.

text: “I will,” he declared fervently. “And I vow to you, Miss Morse, that I shall prove myself. I have journeyed a long way, I realize that; and the road ahead is vast, but I will traverse it even if I must crawl on my hands and knees.” He lifted a sheaf of manuscript. “Here are the ‘Sea Lyrics.’ When you return home, I shall entrust them to you to peruse at your leisure. And you must promise to tell me exactly what you think of them. What I require, you see, above all else, is honest critique. And please, do be candid with me.”
source_text: “I will,” he said passionately. “And I promise you, Miss Morse, that I will make good. I have come far, I know that; and I have far to go, and I will cover it if I have to do it on my hands and knees.” He held up a bunch of manuscript. “Here are the ‘Sea Lyrics.’ When you get home, I’ll turn them over to you to read at your leisure. And you must be sure to tell me just what you think of them. What I need, you know, above all things, is criticism. And do, please, be frank with me.”

text: He retrieved the axe from a swirling galaxy of banknotes, starched collars, and scattered drafts, steeling himself to strike Joe the moment he descended. But Joe never came down. Near two in the morning, Maria, catching his muffled groans through the flimsy wall, slipped into his room to press heated irons against his skin and lay cool, damp cloths over his throbbing eyes.
source_text: He recovered the axe in the midst of the Milky Way of checks, starched shirts, and manuscripts, and prepared, when he came down, to kill Joe. But he did not come down. Instead, at two in the morning, Maria, having heard his groans through the thin partition, came into his room, to put hot flat-irons against his body and damp cloths upon his aching eyes.

text: But his wife gave a small, firm shake of her head. “That won’t be needed. Ruth tells me he’s shipping out in a matter of days. By the time he returns, she’ll be gone. We’ll send her to stay with Aunt Clara. And anyway, a year out East—new air, new faces, new ways of thinking—is exactly what she requires.”
source_text: But his wife shook her head. “It will not be necessary. Ruth says he is going to sea in a few days. When he comes back, she will not be here. We will send her to Aunt Clara’s. And, besides, a year in the East, with the change in climate, people, ideas, and everything, is just the thing she needs.”

text: He let his arms fall onto the table and pressed his face into them. A raw ache filled his throat, and tears threatened to come. The sensation carried him back to his first brawl at six years old—swinging his fists blindly while tears streaked his cheeks, as a boy two years older battered him until he could barely stand. He could still see the circle of boys, shrieking like savages when he finally collapsed, twisting in waves of sickness, blood pouring from his nose and tears spilling from his swollen eyes.
source_text: He dropped his arms upon the table and buried his face in them. His throat was aching, and he wanted to cry. It reminded him of his first fight, when he was six years old, when he punched away with the tears running down his cheeks while the other boy, two years his elder, had beaten and pounded him into exhaustion. He saw the ring of boys, howling like barbarians as he went down at last, writhing in the throes of nausea, the blood streaming from his nose and the tears from his bruised eyes.

text: “They came by doing what couldn’t be done,” he replied. “Their work was so fierce and brilliant it reduced every opponent to cinders. They came by a path of miracles, beating odds stacked a thousand to one against them. They came as Carlyle’s war-worn titans who refuse to stay crushed. And that’s what I have to do now—I have to pull off the impossible.”
source_text: “They arrived by achieving the impossible,” he answered. “They did such blazing, glorious work as to burn to ashes those that opposed them. They arrived by course of miracle, by winning a thousand-to-one wager against them. They arrived because they were Carlyle’s battle-scarred giants who will not be kept down. And that is what I must do; I must achieve the impossible.”

text: “We’ve got something else in mind for you, Ruth, darling—your father and I—oh, no, no; not some fellow we’ve chosen for you, nothing of that sort. Our hopes don’t stretch beyond you wedding a man of your own standing, a decent and upright gentleman, someone you’ll pick for yourself, once you’re in love with him.”
source_text: “We have other plans for you, Ruth, dear, your father and I—oh, no, no; no man picked out for you, or anything like that. Our plans go no farther than your marrying some man in your own station in life, a good and honorable gentleman, whom you will select yourself, when you love him.”

text: “He’s incapable of holding on to his cash, that much is certain,” Hermann von Schmidt admitted to his wife. “He flew into a rage when I brought up the interest, and he declared to hell with the principal, and if I so much as breathed another word about it, he’d knock my Dutch block clean off. His exact words—my Dutch block. Still, he’s a decent fellow, even if he’s no businessman. He handed me my opportunity, and he’s a decent fellow.”
source_text: “He can’t never keep his money, that’s sure,” Hermann von Schmidt confided to his wife. “He got mad when I spoke of interest, an’ he said damn the principal and if I mentioned it again, he’d punch my Dutch head off. That’s what he said—my Dutch head. But he’s all right, even if he ain’t no business man. He’s given me my chance, an’ he’s all right.”

text: “It’s an extraordinary turn of events,” Singletree, Darnley & Co. wrote to Martin. “A dense philosophical essay selling like a popular novel. You couldn’t have picked a better topic, and every surrounding circumstance has been inexplicably favorable. We hardly need to tell you we’re striking while the iron is hot. More than forty thousand copies have already moved in the U.S. and Canada, and a fresh run of twenty thousand is being printed. We’re scrambling to keep up with the orders. Still, we’ve done our part to fuel that demand. We’ve already poured five thousand dollars into promotion. The book is sure to shatter records.”
source_text: “It is a most marvellous happening,” Singletree, Darnley & Co. wrote Martin, “a critical philosophic essay selling like a novel. You could not have chosen your subject better, and all contributory factors have been unwarrantedly propitious. We need scarcely to assure you that we are making hay while the sun shines. Over forty thousand copies have already been sold in the United States and Canada, and a new edition of twenty thousand is on the presses. We are overworked, trying to supply the demand. Nevertheless we have helped to create that demand. We have already spent five thousand dollars in advertising. The book is bound to be a record-breaker.”

text: Martin set the letter aside and did a quick calculation in his head—fifteen cents multiplied by sixty thousand came out to nine thousand dollars. He put his signature on the fresh contract, wrote “The Smoke of Joy” into the empty field, and sent it back to the publishers together with the twenty short pieces he’d drafted before he cracked the code of the newspaper short. Almost as fast as the U.S. mail could go out and come back, a check for five thousand dollars arrived from Singletree, Darnley & Co.
source_text: Martin laid down the letter and worked a problem in mental arithmetic, finding the product of fifteen cents times sixty thousand to be nine thousand dollars. He signed the new contract, inserting “The Smoke of Joy” in the blank space, and mailed it back to the publishers along with the twenty storiettes he had written in the days before he discovered the formula for the newspaper storiette. And promptly as the United States mail could deliver and return, came Singletree, Darnley & Co.’s check for five thousand dollars.

text: Much of this Martin had already deduced, and the rest he would piece together later. Meanwhile, he labored steadily, allowing himself no diversion beyond his visits to Ruth, and lived with the austerity of a Spartan. For his cramped room he paid two dollars and fifty cents a month to his Portuguese landlady, Maria Silva—a fierce-tongued widow who worked relentlessly, her temper worn thin, somehow managing to raise her swarm of children while drowning her exhaustion and grief at uneven intervals in a gallon of the cheap, sour wine she purchased for fifteen cents at the corner grocery and bar. Though he initially loathed her and her vicious speech, Martin came to respect her as he witnessed the valiant struggle she waged. The tiny house held just four rooms—three, once Martin’s was excluded. One, the parlor, brightened by an ingrain carpet and darkened by a funeral card and a memorial portrait of one of her many lost infants, was reserved strictly for visitors. The blinds stayed drawn, and her barefoot brood never crossed that hallowed threshold except on formal occasions. She cooked, and everyone ate, in the kitchen, where she also washed, starched, and pressed clothes every day but Sunday; the bulk of her income came from doing laundry for more well-to-do neighbors. That left the bedroom, as small as Martin’s own, into which she and her seven little ones packed themselves to sleep. It remained a perpetual wonder to Martin how they managed it, and through the thin wall he heard each night the entire ritual of bedding down—the wails and squabbles, the hushed murmurs, the drowsy, birdlike chirps. Maria also drew income from her two cows, which she milked at dawn and dusk and which sustained themselves furtively on vacant lots and the grass lining the public sidewalks, always escorted by one or more of her tattered boys, whose vigilant duty consisted mainly of scanning for the poundmen.
source_text: Much of this Martin had already reasoned out, and some of it he reasoned out later. In the meantime he worked, taking no recreation except when he went to see Ruth, and living like a Spartan. He paid two dollars and a half a month rent for the small room he got from his Portuguese landlady, Maria Silva, a virago and a widow, hard working and harsher tempered, rearing her large brood of children somehow, and drowning her sorrow and fatigue at irregular intervals in a gallon of the thin, sour wine that she bought from the corner grocery and saloon for fifteen cents. From detesting her and her foul tongue at first, Martin grew to admire her as he observed the brave fight she made. There were but four rooms in the little house—three, when Martin’s was subtracted. One of these, the parlor, gay with an ingrain carpet and dolorous with a funeral card and a death-picture of one of her numerous departed babes, was kept strictly for company. The blinds were always down, and her barefooted tribe was never permitted to enter the sacred precinct save on state occasions. She cooked, and all ate, in the kitchen, where she likewise washed, starched, and ironed clothes on all days of the week except Sunday; for her income came largely from taking in washing from her more prosperous neighbors. Remained the bedroom, small as the one occupied by Martin, into which she and her seven little ones crowded and slept. It was an everlasting miracle to Martin how it was accomplished, and from her side of the thin partition he heard nightly every detail of the going to bed, the squalls and squabbles, the soft chattering, and the sleepy, twittering noises as of birds. Another source of income to Maria were her cows, two of them, which she milked night and morning and which gained a surreptitious livelihood from vacant lots and the grass that grew on either side the public side walks, attended always by one or more of her ragged boys, whose watchful guardianship consisted chiefly in keeping their eyes out for the poundmen.

text: The first man unlocked the door with his key and stepped inside, trailed by a younger companion who fumbled with his cap as he took it off. His garments were coarse and carried the unmistakable tang of the sea, making him look thoroughly ill at ease in the broad, grand hallway that now enclosed him. Unsure how to handle his cap, he was about to shove it into his coat pocket when the older man smoothly relieved him of it. The gesture was so understated and effortless that the uneasy youth felt a surge of gratitude. *He gets it*, he told himself. *He'll steer me through this just fine.*
source_text: The one opened the door with a latch-key and went in, followed by a young fellow who awkwardly removed his cap. He wore rough clothes that smacked of the sea, and he was manifestly out of place in the spacious hall in which he found himself. He did not know what to do with his cap, and was stuffing it into his coat pocket when the other took it from him. The act was done quietly and naturally, and the awkward young fellow appreciated it. “He understands,” was his thought. “He’ll see me through all right.”

text: “I’m gravely ill,” he replied. “Your case is different. You have vitality, much to live for—you must be tethered to life, one way or another. As for me, you wonder why I’m a socialist. I’ll explain. It’s because Socialism is inescapable; because this decayed, senseless order cannot last; because the day of the man on horseback is over. The slaves won’t abide it. They are too many, and like it or not they’ll pull the would-be rider down before he ever swings into the saddle. You can’t escape them, and you’ll be forced to swallow the whole slave-morality. A nasty draught, I grant you. But it’s been brewing, and swallow it you must. You’re antediluvian, in any case, with your Nietzsche notions. The past is dead, and whoever says history repeats itself is a liar. Naturally I don’t care for the mob, but what’s a poor fellow to do? We can’t have the man on horseback, and anything is better than the timid swine who govern now. But let’s move on. I’m loaded to the gunwales already, and if I sit here much longer I’ll be drunk. And you know the doctor says—damn the doctor! I’ll cheat him yet.”
source_text: “I’m very sick,” was the answer. “With you it is different. You have health and much to live for, and you must be handcuffed to life somehow. As for me, you wonder why I am a socialist. I’ll tell you. It is because Socialism is inevitable; because the present rotten and irrational system cannot endure; because the day is past for your man on horseback. The slaves won’t stand for it. They are too many, and willy-nilly they’ll drag down the would-be equestrian before ever he gets astride. You can’t get away from them, and you’ll have to swallow the whole slave-morality. It’s not a nice mess, I’ll allow. But it’s been a-brewing and swallow it you must. You are antediluvian anyway, with your Nietzsche ideas. The past is past, and the man who says history repeats itself is a liar. Of course I don’t like the crowd, but what’s a poor chap to do? We can’t have the man on horseback, and anything is preferable to the timid swine that now rule. But come on, anyway. I’m loaded to the guards now, and if I sit here any longer, I’ll get drunk. And you know the doctor says—damn the doctor! I’ll fool him yet.”

text: He boarded a Shattuck Avenue tram bound for the Berkeley hills. The car brimmed with young men and boys, all of them chanting college anthems and now and then loosing sharp fraternity cries. He watched them with a detached, searching gaze. They were students of the university—her university. They moved in her social sphere, shared her classrooms, might speak with her, might glimpse her any ordinary day if the whim took them. It puzzled him that the whim did not take them, that they had chosen an evening’s carousal instead of her company, her conversation, the chance to gather round her in reverent, devoted attention. His mind drifted. He marked one with thin-slitted eyes and a mouth too slack at the corners. That one, he judged, was mean-hearted. On a ship he would prove a skulker, a complainer, a tale-bearer. He, Martin Eden, stood above that breed. The thought braced him. It seemed to lessen the distance between himself and Her. He fell to measuring his own worth against the students. He became aware of his body’s corded machinery and felt a sure pride that he could master any of them in physical things. Yet their heads held the learning that let them speak her language—the reflection weighed him down. But what, after all, was a brain meant for? he asked with sudden heat. Whatever they had done, he could do. They had been pulling life from printed pages while he had been wrestling life itself. His mind stored just as much knowledge as theirs, only of another grain. How many of them could whip a lanyard knot, or stand a wheel, or keep a lookout watch? His past unrolled before him like a moving panorama of risk and mettle, privation and labor. He recalled his stumbles and narrow escapes while he learned. That much, at least, he had gained. In time they would have to start living life and grinding through the mill as he had ground. So be it. While they were taken up with that, he could be mastering the other side of life from books.
source_text: He caught a Telegraph Avenue car that was going to Berkeley. It was crowded with youths and young men who were singing songs and ever and again barking out college yells. He studied them curiously. They were university boys. They went to the same university that she did, were in her class socially, could know her, could see her every day if they wanted to. He wondered that they did not want to, that they had been out having a good time instead of being with her that evening, talking with her, sitting around her in a worshipful and adoring circle. His thoughts wandered on. He noticed one with narrow-slitted eyes and a loose-lipped mouth. That fellow was vicious, he decided. On shipboard he would be a sneak, a whiner, a tattler. He, Martin Eden, was a better man than that fellow. The thought cheered him. It seemed to draw him nearer to Her. He began comparing himself with the students. He grew conscious of the muscled mechanism of his body and felt confident that he was physically their master. But their heads were filled with knowledge that enabled them to talk her talk,—the thought depressed him. But what was a brain for? he demanded passionately. What they had done, he could do. They had been studying about life from the books while he had been busy living life. His brain was just as full of knowledge as theirs, though it was a different kind of knowledge. How many of them could tie a lanyard knot, or take a wheel or a lookout? His life spread out before him in a series of pictures of danger and daring, hardship and toil. He remembered his failures and scrapes in the process of learning. He was that much to the good, anyway. Later on they would have to begin living life and going through the mill as he had gone. Very well. While they were busy with that, he could be learning the other side of life from the books.

text: His expression shone with the fervor of creation, even as she trembled in the chilly room, still unsettled by the icy touch of his hands when they’d met. She attended carefully as he recited, and though he had caught only what looked like disapproval flickering across her face now and then, when he finished he inquired:—
source_text: His face was bright with the creative glow, though she shivered in the cold room and had been struck by the coldness of his hands at greeting. She listened closely while he read, and though he from time to time had seen only disapprobation in her face, at the close he asked:-

text: Downward he stroked, deeper and deeper, until his limbs grew leaden and barely answered his call. He sensed the abyss around him. A sharp ache pressed against his eardrums, and a low hum filled his skull. His strength was fraying, yet he forced his weary arms and legs to propel him farther still, until at last his will shattered and the breath burst from his chest in a violent, billowing surge. The escaping bubbles darted and skimmed like tiny silver spheres across his cheeks and eyelids as they spiraled toward the surface. Then agony and choking seized him. This suffering is not death, the thought flickered through his spinning mind. Death brought no torment. It was life—the sharp, searing grip of life—this dreadful, smothering sensation; it was the final wound life could inflict upon him.
source_text: Down, down, he swam till his arms and legs grew tired and hardly moved. He knew that he was deep. The pressure on his ear-drums was a pain, and there was a buzzing in his head. His endurance was faltering, but he compelled his arms and legs to drive him deeper until his will snapped and the air drove from his lungs in a great explosive rush. The bubbles rubbed and bounded like tiny balloons against his cheeks and eyes as they took their upward flight. Then came pain and strangulation. This hurt was not death, was the thought that oscillated through his reeling consciousness. Death did not hurt. It was life, the pangs of life, this awful, suffocating feeling; it was the last blow life could deal him.

text: Martin Eden gave a slight nod. He had glimpsed what seemed like boundless expanses of understanding. The vision took on a palpable weight. His extraordinary perceptive gift turned abstractions into solid shapes. Within the crucible of his mind, trigonometry and mathematics, along with all the knowledge they implied, were transformed into a sweeping landscape. The vistas he beheld were filled with green canopies and forest clearings, all gently aglow or pierced by darting gleams of light. Far off, the details softened and blurred beneath a violet mist, yet behind that violet mist, he sensed, lay the enchantment of the undiscovered, the pull of romance. It was like wine to him. Here was adventure, a task for mind and hand, a world to master—and instantly from the depths of his awareness surged the thought, _mastering, to reach her, that lily-white spirit seated beside him_.
source_text: Martin Eden nodded. He had caught a glimpse of the apparently illimitable vistas of knowledge. What he saw took on tangibility. His abnormal power of vision made abstractions take on concrete form. In the alchemy of his brain, trigonometry and mathematics and the whole field of knowledge which they betokened were transmuted into so much landscape. The vistas he saw were vistas of green foliage and forest glades, all softly luminous or shot through with flashing lights. In the distance, detail was veiled and blurred by a purple haze, but behind this purple haze, he knew, was the glamour of the unknown, the lure of romance. It was like wine to him. Here was adventure, something to do with head and hand, a world to conquer—and straightway from the back of his consciousness rushed the thought, _conquering, to win to her, that lily-pale spirit sitting beside him_.

text: That final day proved an agonizing ordeal. After spotting news of his departure in the morning editions, Bernard Higginbotham, Gertrude, and the entire household arrived to bid him farewell, along with Hermann von Schmidt and Marian. Then came practical matters to settle, outstanding accounts to clear, and those relentless journalists to suffer through. He took leave of Lizzie Connolly with a curt word at the night-school doorway and hastened off. Back at the hotel, he discovered Joe, who had been swamped all day at the laundry and couldn't get away sooner. It was the breaking point, but Martin clamped his hands on the chair arms and forced himself through half an hour of conversation.
source_text: The last day was a sore trial. Having read of his sailing in the morning papers, Bernard Higginbotham, Gertrude, and all the family came to say good-by, as did Hermann von Schmidt and Marian. Then there was business to be transacted, bills to be paid, and everlasting reporters to be endured. He said good-by to Lizzie Connolly, abruptly, at the entrance to night school, and hurried away. At the hotel he found Joe, too busy all day with the laundry to have come to him earlier. It was the last straw, but Martin gripped the arms of his chair and talked and listened for half an hour.

text: The only joyful moments in his life were those he shared with Ruth, yet even those were not entirely free of sorrow. A persistent, aching unease tormented him, sharper now than in the time before he had won her love; for although her love was his, actually having her remained as distant as ever. He had pleaded for two years, but time was slipping away, and he had nothing to show for it. Moreover, he could not escape the awareness that she disapproved of his pursuits. She never voiced it outright, but indirectly she made her feelings just as plain and unmistakable as if she had spoken them aloud. It was not bitterness she felt, but a quiet censure; a less gentle woman might have grown resentful where she felt only let down. Her disappointment stemmed from the fact that the man she had set out to shape refused to be shaped. She had found his nature somewhat malleable at first, but then it had grown obstinate, unwilling to be formed into the likeness of her father or Mr. Butler.
source_text: The hours he spent with Ruth were the only happy ones he had, and they were not all happy. He was afflicted always with a gnawing restlessness, more tantalizing than in the old days before he possessed her love; for now that he did possess her love, the possession of her was far away as ever. He had asked for two years; time was flying, and he was achieving nothing. Again, he was always conscious of the fact that she did not approve what he was doing. She did not say so directly. Yet indirectly she let him understand it as clearly and definitely as she could have spoken it. It was not resentment with her, but disapproval; though less sweet-natured women might have resented where she was no more than disappointed. Her disappointment lay in that this man she had taken to mould, refused to be moulded. To a certain extent she had found his clay plastic, then it had developed stubbornness, declining to be shaped in the image of her father or of Mr. Butler.

text: “Breakfast’s keeping warm in the oven,” she said quickly. “Jim should be stirring by now. I had to rise early for the laundry. Go on, now, and leave the house soon. It’s going to be a rough day, with Tom gone and only Bernard left to handle the wagon.”
source_text: “You’ll find breakfast in the oven,” she said hurriedly. “Jim ought to be up now. I had to get up early for the washing. Now get along with you and get out of the house early. It won’t be nice to-day, what of Tom quittin’ an’ nobody but Bernard to drive the wagon.”

text: The laundry was a compact, immaculate establishment, fitted with the latest machines to perform every task a machine could undertake. After a brief orientation, Martin set to sorting the mountainous piles of soiled linen, while Joe fired up the masher and prepared a fresh batch of soft-soap—a brew so acrid with harsh chemicals that he had to wrap his mouth, nose, and eyes in bath-towels until he looked like a mummy. Once the sorting was done, Martin helped with the wringing. They heaved the wet garments into a spinning drum that whirled at several thousand revolutions per minute, centrifugal force slinging the water out of the cloth. From then on, Martin shuttled between the dryer and the wringer, pausing now and then to “shake out” socks and stockings. By afternoon, one man feeding and the other stacking, they were running hosiery through the mangle while the irons heated. Then came hot irons and undergarments until six o’clock, when Joe gave a doubtful shake of his head.
source_text: It was a perfectly appointed, small steam laundry, wherein the most modern machinery did everything that was possible for machinery to do. Martin, after a few instructions, sorted the great heaps of soiled clothes, while Joe started the masher and made up fresh supplies of soft-soap, compounded of biting chemicals that compelled him to swathe his mouth and nostrils and eyes in bath-towels till he resembled a mummy. Finished the sorting, Martin lent a hand in wringing the clothes. This was done by dumping them into a spinning receptacle that went at a rate of a few thousand revolutions a minute, tearing the water from the clothes by centrifugal force. Then Martin began to alternate between the dryer and the wringer, between times “shaking out” socks and stockings. By the afternoon, one feeding and one stacking up, they were running socks and stockings through the mangle while the irons were heating. Then it was hot irons and underclothes till six o’clock, at which time Joe shook his head dubiously.

text: “I’m ill, deeply ill,” he declared, throwing up his hands in despair. “I never realized how ill until this moment. Something has drained away from me. I’ve never feared life, but I never imagined I could grow so weary of it. Life has stuffed me so full that I’m hollow of any longing. If there were space left, I’d reach for you now. You see how sick I’ve become.”
source_text: “I am sick, very sick,” he said with a despairing gesture. “How sick I did not know till now. Something has gone out of me. I have always been unafraid of life, but I never dreamed of being sated with life. Life has so filled me that I am empty of any desire for anything. If there were room, I should want you, now. You see how sick I am.”

text: In the scullery he discovered the other lodger, Hugh, spooning porridge with a listless air, his gaze clouded and remote as if fixed on some inner malady. Hugh served as a tinker’s boy, and his receding jaw, pleasure-loving nature, and a jittery dullness of mind seemed to guarantee he would never gain ground in the scramble for his daily crust.
source_text: In the kitchen he found Jim, the other boarder, eating mush very languidly, with a sick, far-away look in his eyes. Jim was a plumber’s apprentice whose weak chin and hedonistic temperament, coupled with a certain nervous stupidity, promised to take him nowhere in the race for bread and butter.

text: “Not my words.” The judge’s gaze glinted, though he held his composure. “You know, Martin, I’ve listened to your stump speeches. Through some henidical trick—henidical, I should note, is a pet term of mine that baffles everyone—through some henidical trick you convince yourself you champion the competitive order and the thriving of the fittest, while simultaneously throwing your full weight behind every scheme to strip the strong of their strength.”
source_text: “Out of your mouth.” Martin’s eyes were sparkling, but he kept control of himself. “You see, Judge, I’ve heard your campaign speeches. By some henidical process—henidical, by the way is a favorite word of mine which nobody understands—by some henidical process you persuade yourself that you believe in the competitive system and the survival of the strong, and at the same time you indorse with might and main all sorts of measures to shear the strength from the strong.”

text: Martin did not reply. Another round, and he sensed the first stirrings of drunkenness worming through his mind. Yes, this was truly being alive—the initial gasp of existence he’d tasted in three weeks. His old reveries flooded back. Imagination stepped from the shadowed chamber and beckoned him forward, a figure of blazing radiance. The lens of his inner sight shone like polished silver, a shimmering, brilliant overlay of pictures. Wonder and loveliness accompanied him, clasped together, and all strength belonged to him. He tried to share it with Joe, but Joe nursed his own daydreams, foolproof plans to break free from the drudgery of the laundry and one day own a vast steam laundry himself.
source_text: But Martin made no answer. A few more drinks, and in his brain he felt the maggots of intoxication beginning to crawl. Ah, it was living, the first breath of life he had breathed in three weeks. His dreams came back to him. Fancy came out of the darkened room and lured him on, a thing of flaming brightness. His mirror of vision was silver-clear, a flashing, dazzling palimpsest of imagery. Wonder and beauty walked with him, hand in hand, and all power was his. He tried to tell it to Joe, but Joe had visions of his own, infallible schemes whereby he would escape the slavery of laundry-work and become himself the owner of a great steam laundry.

text: He didn’t hesitate to draw on it. He was certain his friend always had ample funds, and he was equally sure, with deep conviction, that his own triumph would let him pay it back. That morning he settled every outstanding account, gave Maria three months’ rent in advance, and reclaimed every item from the pawnshop. Next he purchased Marian’s wedding gift, along with simpler, Christmas-appropriate presents for Ruth and Gertrude. Finally, with the money still left, he shepherded the entire Silva clan into Oakland. He was a winter late in keeping his promise, but keep it he did, for the very last and smallest Silva received a pair of shoes, and Maria did too. There were also horns, dolls, toys of every kind, and armloads of candy and nuts in parcels and bundles that brimmed over in all the Silvas’ arms.
source_text: He did not scruple to use it. He knew his friend had always plenty of money, and he knew also, with profound certitude, that his success would enable him to repay it. In the morning he paid every bill, gave Maria three months’ advance on the room, and redeemed every pledge at the pawnshop. Next he bought Marian’s wedding present, and simpler presents, suitable to Christmas, for Ruth and Gertrude. And finally, on the balance remaining to him, he herded the whole Silva tribe down into Oakland. He was a winter late in redeeming his promise, but redeemed it was, for the last, least Silva got a pair of shoes, as well as Maria herself. Also, there were horns, and dolls, and toys of various sorts, and parcels and bundles of candies and nuts that filled the arms of all the Silvas to overflowing.

text: As though responding, the figure transformed with sudden grace. The rigid collar and sharp cut of the coat dissolved into softer attire; the severity faded from the jaw, the flint from the gaze; and the countenance, now tempered and refined, glowed with an inward light born of devotion to learning and loveliness. The image closely resembled his current form, and as he studied it, he marked the reading-lamp that lit its features and the volume beneath its bent head. He caught the title and saw, *The Philosophy of Beauty*. Then, stepping into the vision, he adjusted the lamp and resumed reading *The Philosophy of Beauty* himself.
source_text: As if in reply, the vision underwent a swift metamorphosis. The stiff-rim and the square-cut vanished, being replaced by milder garments; the toughness went out of the face, the hardness out of the eyes; and, the face, chastened and refined, was irradiated from an inner life of communion with beauty and knowledge. The apparition was very like his present self, and, as he regarded it, he noted the student-lamp by which it was illuminated, and the book over which it pored.
