He glanced at the title and read, “The Science of Æsthetics.” Next, he entered into the apparition, trimmed the student-lamp, and himself went on reading “The Science of Æsthetics.”

text: “It slipped my mind entirely,” he said with a note of humility. “You see, this morning the poundman seized Maria’s two cows and her young calf, and—as it turned out—Maria had no money at all. So I had to get her cows back for her. That’s where the _Transcontinental_ five-pound note went—‘The Ring of Bells’ ended up in the poundman’s pocket.”
source_text: “I forgot all about it,” he said humbly. “You see, this morning the poundman got Maria’s two cows and the baby calf, and—well, it happened that Maria didn’t have any money, and so I had to recover her cows for her. That’s where the _Transcontinental_ fiver went—‘The Ring of Bells’ went into the poundman’s pocket.”

text: The tawdriness and coarseness of the scene sickened him, yet Martin observed with dull indifference that his stomach barely turned. He longed to summon rage, but lacked the strength even to make the attempt. He felt too frozen. His blood had thickened past the point where it could surge into the swift, rising tide of outrage. In the end, what difference did it make? It matched perfectly all the other things Brissenden had scorned in the world of the bourgeoisie.
source_text: The cheapness and vulgarity of it was nauseating, and Martin noted apathetically that he was not nauseated very much. He wished he could get angry, but did not have energy enough to try. He was too numb. His blood was too congealed to accelerate to the swift tidal flow of indignation. After all, what did it matter? It was on a par with all the rest that Brissenden had condemned in bourgeois society.

text: Very well, he had swallowed the hook—those glossy falsehoods in the papers about authors and their fortunes—and squandered two years chasing the lie. But now he would spit the hook out. Never again would he set down a single sentence. He would do what Ruth wished, what the whole world wished—find steady employment. The mere image of labor summoned Joe to mind: Joe, wandering through that vast country of empty hours. A deep sigh of envy escaped Martin. The toll of nineteen-hour days, piled one upon another, pressed down on him heavily. Yet Joe was not entangled in love, bore none of love’s burdens, and so could drift unburdened through the land of empty hours. He, Martin, possessed something to strive for, and strive he would. At dawn he would rise and go hunting for a position. And he would tell Ruth, too, that he had reformed at last and was ready to enter her father’s firm.
source_text: Well, he had taken the bait, the newspaper lies about writers and their pay, and he had wasted two years over it. But he would disgorge the bait now. Not another line would he ever write. He would do what Ruth wanted him to do, what everybody wanted him to do—get a job. The thought of going to work reminded him of Joe—Joe, tramping through the land of nothing-to-do. Martin heaved a great sigh of envy. The reaction of nineteen hours a day for many days was strong upon him. But then, Joe was not in love, had none of the responsibilities of love, and he could afford to loaf through the land of nothing-to-do. He, Martin, had something to work for, and go to work he would. He would start out early next morning to hunt a job. And he would let Ruth know, too, that he had mended his ways and was willing to go into her father’s office.

text: “You were no different from the others, young man,” Martin said with scorn. “Your sense of right and your learning matched theirs exactly. You never thought or acted on your own. Your beliefs, like your attire, came off the rack; your deeds were cut to fit the crowd’s applause. You led your pack only because the rest hailed you as the genuine article. You battled and bossed the gang, not from any love of it—you know you secretly held it in contempt—but because the others clapped you on the back. You beat Cheese-Face because you refused to yield, and you refused to yield partly because you were a mindless brute, and for the rest because you swallowed what everyone around you believed: that a man’s worth was measured by the flesh-ripping savagery he showed in breaking and scarring his fellows’ bodies. Why, you pup, you even stole other men’s girls, not from any desire for them, but because deep in the bones of those who set your moral stride ran the raw instinct of the wild stallion and the bull seal. Well, the years have slipped by—what do you make of it now?”
source_text: “You were like all the rest, young fellow,” Martin sneered. “Your morality and your knowledge were just the same as theirs. You did not think and act for yourself. Your opinions, like your clothes, were ready made; your acts were shaped by popular approval. You were cock of your gang because others acclaimed you the real thing. You fought and ruled the gang, not because you liked to,—you know you really despised it,—but because the other fellows patted you on the shoulder. You licked Cheese-Face because you wouldn’t give in, and you wouldn’t give in partly because you were an abysmal brute and for the rest because you believed what every one about you believed, that the measure of manhood was the carnivorous ferocity displayed in injuring and marring fellow-creatures’ anatomies. Why, you whelp, you even won other fellows’ girls away from them, not because you wanted the girls, but because in the marrow of those about you, those who set your moral pace, was the instinct of the wild stallion and the bull-seal. Well, the years have passed, and what do you think about it now?”

text: The urge to compose kindled within Martin again. Verses and tales blossomed unbidden in his mind, and he jotted outlines for the hour when he might shape them into form. Yet his hand stayed still. This interlude was his brief reprieve; he had vowed to yield it wholly to repose and affection, and in each he flourished. Soon an exuberant energy brimmed over in him, and every day he met Ruth, and at the instant of greeting, she felt anew the familiar jolt of his vigor and vitality.
source_text: The desire to write was stirring in Martin once more. Stories and poems were springing into spontaneous creation in his brain, and he made notes of them against the future time when he would give them expression. But he did not write. This was his little vacation; he had resolved to devote it to rest and love, and in both matters he prospered. He was soon spilling over with vitality, and each day he saw Ruth, at the moment of meeting, she experienced the old shock of his strength and health.

text: Over coffee in his cramped room, Martin scanned the morning paper. Seeing his name splashed across the front page as a headline was a strange new sensation, and he was startled to discover himself branded the most infamous ringleader of Oakland’s socialists. He skimmed the incendiary speech the young reporter had invented for him; initially incensed by the falsification, he finally cast the paper aside with a chuckle.
source_text: Over the coffee, in his little room, Martin read next morning’s paper. It was a novel experience to find himself head-lined, on the first page at that; and he was surprised to learn that he was the most notorious leader of the Oakland socialists. He ran over the violent speech the cub reporter had constructed for him, and, though at first he was angered by the fabrication, in the end he tossed the paper aside with a laugh.

text: “Yes, she’s certainly agile when it comes to the surface mechanics of music, yet the deeper essence of it remains completely beyond her reach. I asked her what music signified for her—you know I’m forever intrigued by that specific question; and she couldn’t say what it signified, only that she worshipped it, that it towered above all other arts, and that it mattered more to her than life itself.”
source_text: “Yes, she’s undoubtedly gymnastic in the externals of music, but the intrinsic spirit of music is unguessed by her. I asked her what music meant to her—you know I’m always curious to know that particular thing; and she did not know what it meant to her, except that she adored it, that it was the greatest of the arts, and that it meant more than life to her.”

text: “I’m not sure what you’ll think of it,” he said with a note of regret. “It’s a peculiar piece. I fear I ventured out of my depth, though my aims were sincere. Don’t get caught up in the minor details. Just try to sense the larger essence within it. It is grand, and it is real, even if I’ve likely failed to render it clear.”
source_text: “I don’t know what you’ll make of this,” he said apologetically. “It’s a funny thing. I’m afraid I got beyond my depth in it, but my intentions were good. Don’t bother about the little features of it. Just see if you catch the feel of the big thing in it. It is big, and it is true, though the chance is large that I have failed to make it intelligible.”

text: The _Transcontinental_ cost a quarter, its sober and elegant cover announcing it as a journal of the highest rank. It was a settled, dignified publication that had appeared without interruption since long before his own birth. Every month, the outside cover bore the pronouncement of one of the world’s eminent authors—a declaration of the magazine’s inspired purpose, penned by a literary luminary whose earliest flashes had first gleamed within those very pages. And this exalted, heaven-touched _Transcontinental_ offered five dollars for five thousand words! The celebrated writer had lately passed away in a distant country—in stark poverty, Martin recalled, which hardly came as a surprise, given the princely sums paid to authors.
source_text: The _Transcontinental_ sold for twenty-five cents, and its dignified and artistic cover proclaimed it as among the first-class magazines. It was a staid, respectable magazine, and it had been published continuously since long before he was born. Why, on the outside cover were printed every month the words of one of the world’s great writers, words proclaiming the inspired mission of the _Transcontinental_ by a star of literature whose first coruscations had appeared inside those self-same covers. And the high and lofty, heaven-inspired _Transcontinental_ paid five dollars for five thousand words! The great writer had recently died in a foreign land—in dire poverty, Martin remembered, which was not to be wondered at, considering the magnificent pay authors receive.

text: “There you err,” he had plunged ahead. “Every circle in society, every coterie—or, rather, nearly every person and set—mimics its superiors. And who, pray, are the foremost superiors? The leisured, the moneyed idlers. They remain ignorant, by and large, of the matters familiar to those who truly accomplish something in the world. To endure talk of such matters would be tedium, so the idlers pronounce those subjects shop and forbid their discussion. Equally they ordain what is not shop and may be spoken of, and those topics are the freshest operas, freshest novels, cards, billiards, cocktails, motorcars, horse shows, trout angling, tuna angling, big-game stalking, yacht cruising, and the like—and observe, these are the very things the idlers know. In sober fact, they constitute the shop-talk of the idlers. And the most amusing stroke is that many clever people, and all the would-be clever people, permit the idlers to foist this upon them. For my part, I demand the best a man possesses, call it shop coarseness or what you will.”
source_text: “There, you mistake,” he had rushed on. “All persons in society, all cliques in society—or, rather, nearly all persons and cliques—ape their betters. Now, who are the best betters? The idlers, the wealthy idlers. They do not know, as a rule, the things known by the persons who are doing something in the world. To listen to conversation about such things would mean to be bored, wherefore the idlers decree that such things are shop and must not be talked about. Likewise they decree the things that are not shop and which may be talked about, and those things are the latest operas, latest novels, cards, billiards, cocktails, automobiles, horse shows, trout fishing, tuna-fishing, big-game shooting, yacht sailing, and so forth—and mark you, these are the things the idlers know. In all truth, they constitute the shop-talk of the idlers. And the funniest part of it is that many of the clever people, and all the would-be clever people, allow the idlers so to impose upon them. As for me, I want the best a man’s got in him, call it shop vulgarity or anything you please.”

text: For a sonnet on Stevenson, he managed to squeeze two dollars from a Boston editor whose magazine had Matthew Arnold aspirations but a penny-dreadful budget. “The Peri and the Pearl,” a nimble, freshly finished skit of two hundred lines, still glowing from his imagination, captured the fancy of a San Francisco editor whose periodical served a powerful railroad’s interests. When the editor wrote offering him travel vouchers as payment, Martin replied to ask whether the fare could be transferred. Told it could not, and with no way to sell it, he requested the poem’s return. It came back with the editor’s regrets, so Martin dispatched it once more to San Francisco, this time to _The Hornet_, a grandiose monthly that had once been lifted into a blazing prominence by the gifted journalist who had launched it. But _The Hornet’s_ brilliance had already faded long before Martin’s birth. The editor pledged him fifteen dollars for the poem, yet once it appeared in print, he seemed to forget the debt. After several letters went unanswered, Martin fired off an indignant note, which drew a reply—from a new editor, who coldly explained that he refused to answer for his predecessor’s blunders and, in any case, thought little of “The Peri and the Pearl.”
source_text: For a sonnet on Stevenson he managed to wring two dollars out of a Boston editor who was running a magazine with a Matthew Arnold taste and a penny-dreadful purse. “The Peri and the Pearl,” a clever skit of a poem of two hundred lines, just finished, white hot from his brain, won the heart of the editor of a San Francisco magazine published in the interest of a great railroad. When the editor wrote, offering him payment in transportation, Martin wrote back to inquire if the transportation was transferable. It was not, and so, being prevented from peddling it, he asked for the return of the poem. Back it came, with the editor’s regrets, and Martin sent it to San Francisco again, this time to _The Hornet_, a pretentious monthly that had been fanned into a constellation of the first magnitude by the brilliant journalist who founded it. But _The Hornet’s_ light had begun to dim long before Martin was born. The editor promised Martin fifteen dollars for the poem, but, when it was published, seemed to forget about it. Several of his letters being ignored, Martin indicted an angry one which drew a reply. It was written by a new editor, who coolly informed Martin that he declined to be held responsible for the old editor’s mistakes, and that he did not think much of “The Peri and the Pearl” anyway.

text: “I’m afraid I didn’t make that clear,” she said with a smile. “A double negative is—how shall I put it—when you say, ‘never helped nobody.’ ‘Never’ is one negative. ‘Nobody’ is another. The rule is that two negatives yield a positive. So ‘never helped nobody’ actually means that, by not helping nobody, they must have helped somebody.”
source_text: “I’m afraid I didn’t explain that,” she smiled. “A double negative is—let me see—well, you say, ‘never helped nobody.’ ‘Never’ is a negative. ‘Nobody’ is another negative. It is a rule that two negatives make a positive. ‘Never helped nobody’ means that, not helping nobody, they must have helped somebody.”

text: Publishers dispatched letters urging him to dictate his own price, and he obliged, though always for labor already delivered. He would not bind himself to any fresh undertaking. The mere notion of once more lowering nib to page sent a fury through his veins. He had watched the multitude rip Carrington limb from limb, and even though that same multitude now lionized him, he could neither shake the horror nor muster a shred of reverence for them. His very renown struck him as a betrayal and a stain upon Carrington. It made him flinch, yet he steeled himself to press onward and swell the purse.
source_text: Editors wrote to him telling him to name his own terms, which he did, but it was always for work performed. He refused resolutely to pledge himself to any new thing. The thought of again setting pen to paper maddened him. He had seen Brissenden torn to pieces by the crowd, and despite the fact that him the crowd acclaimed, he could not get over the shock nor gather any respect for the crowd. His very popularity seemed a disgrace and a treason to Brissenden. It made him wince, but he made up his mind to go on and fill the money-bag.

text: But there was scant time for wonder. All of Martin’s awareness had narrowed to the task. Head and hand worked without pause, a sentient engine, and everything that made him a man was bent on feeding that intelligence. His brain held no space for the cosmos or its grave enigmas. All the wide, airy halls of his mind were shut and sealed tight. The resonant chamber of his soul had shrunk to a cramped room, a pilot house, from which he commanded the muscles of his arm and shoulder, his ten quick fingers, and the darting iron as it swept along its steaming track in wide, fluent arcs—exactly so many arcs and no more, exactly so far with each stroke and not a hair’s breadth farther—racing down endless sleeves, sides, backs, and tails, and flipping the finished shirts, uncrumpled, onto the waiting rack. And even as his hurrying soul let go, it was already grasping for the next shirt. This continued, hour upon hour, while beyond the walls the world lay dazed beneath the high California sun. But inside that superheated room, no one swooned. The cool guests on the verandas required fresh linen.
source_text: But there was little time in which to marvel. All Martin’s consciousness was concentrated in the work. Ceaselessly active, head and hand, an intelligent machine, all that constituted him a man was devoted to furnishing that intelligence. There was no room in his brain for the universe and its mighty problems. All the broad and spacious corridors of his mind were closed and hermetically sealed. The echoing chamber of his soul was a narrow room, a conning tower, whence were directed his arm and shoulder muscles, his ten nimble fingers, and the swift-moving iron along its steaming path in broad, sweeping strokes, just so many strokes and no more, just so far with each stroke and not a fraction of an inch farther, rushing along interminable sleeves, sides, backs, and tails, and tossing the finished shirts, without rumpling, upon the receiving frame. And even as his hurrying soul tossed, it was reaching for another shirt. This went on, hour after hour, while outside all the world swooned under the overhead California sun. But there was no swooning in that superheated room. The cool guests on the verandas needed clean linen.

text: The image dissolved, leaving him staring at the chaos of his grimy room. He tried uselessly to conjure Tahiti once more. He understood that voices rose in song beneath the trees and girls swayed under the moon, yet the vision eluded him. All that met his eyes were the cluttered desk, the bare spot where the typewriter had rested, and the filthy glass of the window. With a moan, he shut his eyes and fell asleep.
source_text: The picture faded, and before his eyes stretched the disorder of his squalid room. He strove in vain to see Tahiti again. He knew there was singing among the trees and that the maidens were dancing in the moonlight, but he could not see them. He could see only the littered writing-table, the empty space where the type-writer had stood, and the unwashed window-pane. He closed his eyes with a groan, and slept.

text: Lucian had burned with the need to see Elara. His soul was built for affection, and he craved a deeper measure of fellow-feeling than most men required. He thirsted for that fellow-feeling—which, to him, signified a keen, perceptive comprehension—and he did not yet grasp that Elara’s warmth arose more from tender sentiment and graceful instinct than from any true insight into the source of his pain. Thus, as Lucian cradled her hand and spoke with eager joy, her fondness moved her to answer his clasp, and her gaze grew bright and dewy at the sight of his frailty and the traces that grief had carved upon his features.
source_text: Martin had been wild to see Ruth. His was essentially a love-nature, and he possessed more than the average man’s need for sympathy. He was starving for sympathy, which, with him, meant intelligent understanding; and he had yet to learn that Ruth’s sympathy was largely sentimental and tactful, and that it proceeded from gentleness of nature rather than from understanding of the objects of her sympathy. So it was while Martin held her hand and gladly talked, that her love for him prompted her to press his hand in return, and that her eyes were moist and luminous at sight of his helplessness and of the marks suffering had stamped upon his face.

text: “There is nothing wrong with you, Mr. Eden,” he declared, “absolutely nothing amiss. You are in the peak of health. Frankly, I find myself envying your constitution. It is magnificent. Observe that chest. There—and in your stomach—resides the key to your extraordinary vitality. Physically, you are one man among a thousand—among ten thousand. Short of mishap, you could well live to a hundred.”
source_text: “There is nothing the matter with you, Mr. Eden,” he said, “positively nothing the matter with you. You are in the pink of condition. Candidly, I envy you your health. It is superb. Look at that chest. There, and in your stomach, lies the secret of your remarkable constitution. Physically, you are a man in a thousand—in ten thousand. Barring accidents, you should live to be a hundred.”

text: At times, he would gently challenge her, planting fleeting uncertainties about the precision of her own musical ideas and categories. Yet her voice he never doubted. It was entirely her own, and he would sit motionless, awed by the celestial flow of her clear, silvery soprano. Invariably, he found himself measuring it against the frail, reedy twittering of underfed, untaught mill girls, and the hoarse, gin-ravaged cries of harbour-town women. She took pleasure in performing for him—in both song and instrument. Indeed, this was the first occasion she had ever possessed a living spirit to shape, and the malleable substance of him was a joy to work; for she believed she was sculpting him, and her motives were kind. Moreover, his company was agreeable. He no longer repulsed her. That initial recoil had truly been a dread of her own hidden nature, and the dread had since fallen dormant. Unknown to herself, she harboured a sense of rightful claim over him. He also acted on her as a restorative. She was labouring intensely at the university, and it seemed to fortify her to step away from the arid texts and feel the brisk sea-wind of his presence sweep across her. Strength! It was strength she craved, and he supplied it in abundant draughts. Simply to enter a room where he waited, or to encounter him at the threshold, was to draw renewed courage for living. And once he had departed, she would go back to her studies with a sharper hunger and a replenished vigour.
source_text: Sometimes he questioned, and induced in her mind temporary doubts as to the correctness of her own definitions and conceptions of music. But her singing he did not question. It was too wholly her, and he sat always amazed at the divine melody of her pure soprano voice. And he could not help but contrast it with the weak pipings and shrill quaverings of factory girls, ill-nourished and untrained, and with the raucous shriekings from gin-cracked throats of the women of the seaport towns. She enjoyed singing and playing to him. In truth, it was the first time she had ever had a human soul to play with, and the plastic clay of him was a delight to mould; for she thought she was moulding it, and her intentions were good. Besides, it was pleasant to be with him. He did not repel her. That first repulsion had been really a fear of her undiscovered self, and the fear had gone to sleep. Though she did not know it, she had a feeling in him of proprietary right. Also, he had a tonic effect upon her. She was studying hard at the university, and it seemed to strengthen her to emerge from the dusty books and have the fresh sea-breeze of his personality blow upon her. Strength! Strength was what she needed, and he gave it to her in generous measure. To come into the same room with him, or to meet him at the door, was to take heart of life. And when he had gone, she would return to her books with a keener zest and fresh store of energy.

text: “And once he’d done with the pressing, he set to washing the fleeces,” she would later recount. “He said to me, ‘Maria, you’re the biggest simpleton. Let me teach you how to scour the wool,’ and he taught me, truly. In ten minutes he’d fashioned the contraption—a single cask, a hub from a cartwheel, two rods, as simple as that.”
source_text: “An’ when he feenish da iron’ he washa da wools,” as she described it afterward. “He say, ‘Maria, you are da greata fool. I showa you how to washa da wools,’ an’ he shows me, too. Ten minutes he maka da machine—one barrel, one wheel-hub, two poles, justa like dat.”

text: Later, seated at the piano, she played for him—and against him—fiercely, as if to underscore the impassable chasm that lay between them. Her music became a cudgel she swung savagely at his head; and though it battered and beat him down, it also roused him. He watched her in wonder. In his mind, as in hers, the chasm widened; yet faster than it widened rose his fierce desire to bridge it. But he was too intricate a mesh of sensibilities to spend an entire evening staring into an abyss, especially with music in the air. He was extraordinarily vulnerable to music. It worked on him like strong spirits, goading him to reckless intensities of emotion—a drug that seized his imagination and sent it soaring through the clouds. It swept away the squalid real, flooded his mind with loveliness, set romance free and lent wings to its feet. He did not grasp the music she played. It was nothing like the saloon piano-thumping and brassy blare he had known. Yet he had glimpsed such music in books, and he accepted her playing largely on trust, waiting patiently at first for lilting, clearly marked rhythms, then puzzled because those rhythms never lasted. Just as he caught their sway and began to rise, his imagination catching the current, they would dissolve into a chaotic scramble of sound that meant nothing to him, dropping his fancy, a dead weight, back to the ground.
source_text: Later, at the piano, she played for him, and at him, aggressively, with the vague intent of emphasizing the impassableness of the gulf that separated them. Her music was a club that she swung brutally upon his head; and though it stunned him and crushed him down, it incited him. He gazed upon her in awe. In his mind, as in her own, the gulf widened; but faster than it widened, towered his ambition to win across it. But he was too complicated a plexus of sensibilities to sit staring at a gulf a whole evening, especially when there was music. He was remarkably susceptible to music. It was like strong drink, firing him to audacities of feeling,—a drug that laid hold of his imagination and went cloud-soaring through the sky. It banished sordid fact, flooded his mind with beauty, loosed romance and to its heels added wings. He did not understand the music she played. It was different from the dance-hall piano-banging and blatant brass bands he had heard. But he had caught hints of such music from the books, and he accepted her playing largely on faith, patiently waiting, at first, for the lilting measures of pronounced and simple rhythm, puzzled because those measures were not long continued. Just as he caught the swing of them and started, his imagination attuned in flight, always they vanished away in a chaotic scramble of sounds that was meaningless to him, and that dropped his imagination, an inert weight, back to earth.

text: “Listen, Lizzie. I can’t even start to express how deeply I care for you. It goes far beyond caring—I hold you in the highest esteem. You’re extraordinary, and your goodness is just as extraordinary. But what good are words, really? Still, there’s something I want to offer. You’ve endured so much hardship; let me smooth the way for you.” (A bright gladness rose in her eyes, then dimmed once more.) “I’m fairly certain I’ll come into some money soon—a great deal of it.”
source_text: “But look here, Lizzie. I can’t begin to tell you how much I like you. I do more than like you. I admire and respect you. You are magnificent, and you are magnificently good. But what’s the use of words? Yet there’s something I’d like to do. You’ve had a hard life; let me make it easy for you.” (A joyous light welled into her eyes, then faded out again.) “I’m pretty sure of getting hold of some money soon—lots of it.”

text: She missed all that was magnificent and powerful in him—or worse, misunderstood it entirely. Here was a man so malleable, so richly adaptable, that he could inhabit any number of narrow compartments life offered, yet she judged him willful and stubborn simply because she could not press him into the only compartment she knew. When his thoughts soared beyond her reach, she dismissed him as erratic. No one else’s mind had ever outstripped hers. She could always keep pace with her father and mother, her brothers, even Olney; so when she could not follow Martin, she assumed the failing was his. It was the age-old tragedy of narrowness presuming to guide the boundless.
source_text: What was great and strong in him, she missed, or, worse yet, misunderstood. This man, whose clay was so plastic that he could live in any number of pigeonholes of human existence, she thought wilful and most obstinate because she could not shape him to live in her pigeonhole, which was the only one she knew. She could not follow the flights of his mind, and when his brain got beyond her, she deemed him erratic. Nobody else’s brain ever got beyond her. She could always follow her father and mother, her brothers and Olney; wherefore, when she could not follow Martin, she believed the fault lay with him. It was the old tragedy of insularity trying to serve as mentor to the universal.

text: He inhabited every instant of his conscious hours, and even in slumber he never truly ceased—his unbridled inner mind rampaged through five hours of respite, weaving the day’s thoughts and events into monstrous and impossible fantasies. In truth, he found no rest at all, and a frailer frame or a less steadfast intellect would have shattered in utter collapse. His late-afternoon visits to Ruth had grown less frequent now, for June drew near, when she would claim her degree and be done with the university. Bachelor of Arts!—the mere thought of her diploma made her seem to fly beyond him faster than he could ever hope to follow.
source_text: He lived every moment of his waking hours, and he lived in his sleep, his subjective mind rioting through his five hours of surcease and combining the thoughts and events of the day into grotesque and impossible marvels. In reality, he never rested, and a weaker body or a less firmly poised brain would have been prostrated in a general break-down. His late afternoon calls on Ruth were rarer now, for June was approaching, when she would take her degree and finish with the university. Bachelor of Arts!—when he thought of her degree, it seemed she fled beyond him faster than he could pursue.

text: Beneath the table, Ruth’s fingers groped for Martin’s in desperate entreaty, yet the heat of his fury would not be quelled. The sham intellect and duplicity of those enthroned in eminence kindled a fire in him. A Justice of the High Court! Only a handful of years past, he had lifted his gaze from the filth and mistaken such radiant figures for divinities.
source_text: Ruth’s hand sought Martin’s beseechingly under the table, but his blood was up. He was inflamed by the intellectual pretence and fraud of those who sat in the high places. A Superior Court Judge! It was only several years before that he had looked up from the mire at such glorious entities and deemed them gods.

text: He wasn’t boasting or trying to impress. All he wanted was to bring her joy, to make her proud, to prove her years of belief in him had been worth it. She placed the book in the front room, resting it on the family Bible. This volume her boarder had created was something hallowed, a token of friendship. It eased the sting of his past as a laundry worker, and even though she couldn’t grasp a single sentence, she understood that every sentence was important. She was a plain, pragmatic, industrious woman, yet she carried an abundant gift of faith.
source_text: He was not bragging, not showing off. His sole motive was to make her happy, to make her proud of him, to justify her long faith in him. She put the book in the front room on top of the family Bible. A sacred thing was this book her lodger had made, a fetich of friendship. It softened the blow of his having been a laundryman, and though she could not understand a line of it, she knew that every line of it was great. She was a simple, practical, hard-working woman, but she possessed faith in large endowment.

text: “And next week we’ll have to go through the whole grind once more,” he said with a mournful note. “What’s the point of any of it, eh? There are moments I wish I were a tramp. They don’t lift a finger and still scrape by. Lord, I’d give anything for a glass of beer, but I just can’t muster the will to walk down to the village and fetch one. You’ll spend the night, and ship your books later by express—unless you’re a damned fool.”
source_text: “An’ next week we got to do it all over again,” he said sadly. “An’ what’s the good of it all, hey? Sometimes I wish I was a hobo. They don’t work, an’ they get their livin’. Gee! I wish I had a glass of beer; but I can’t get up the gumption to go down to the village an’ get it. You’ll stay over, an’ send your books down by express, or else you’re a damn fool.”

text: He recited to her a tale he secretly believed stood among the crown of his creations. He titled it “The Essence of Being,” and the essence that had seeped into his thoughts while composing it now seeped back as he spoke the lines. The original vision possessed a peculiar enchantment, and he had embroidered it with further enchantments of language and detail. All the former blaze and fervor that had fueled the writing flared anew within him, and he was carried and overwhelmed until he could neither perceive nor hear its blemishes. Yet with Ruth it was otherwise. Her disciplined ear caught the excesses and distortions, the novice’s heavy hand, and she marked at once each place where the cadence stumbled and broke. She barely noticed the cadence otherwise, save when it swelled into grandiosity, at which times she was unpleasantly struck by its rawness. Such was her concluding verdict on the story as a whole—raw, though she kept the word from him. Instead, when he finished, she noted the small imperfections and told him the story pleased her.
source_text: He read to her a story, one that he flattered himself was among his very best. He called it “The Wine of Life,” and the wine of it, that had stolen into his brain when he wrote it, stole into his brain now as he read it. There was a certain magic in the original conception, and he had adorned it with more magic of phrase and touch. All the old fire and passion with which he had written it were reborn in him, and he was swayed and swept away so that he was blind and deaf to the faults of it. But it was not so with Ruth. Her trained ear detected the weaknesses and exaggerations, the overemphasis of the tyro, and she was instantly aware each time the sentence-rhythm tripped and faltered. She scarcely noted the rhythm otherwise, except when it became too pompous, at which moments she was disagreeably impressed with its amateurishness. That was her final judgment on the story as a whole—amateurish, though she did not tell him so. Instead, when he had done, she pointed out the minor flaws and said that she liked the story.

text: A fierce enticement swept over her. She had observed, in fleeting yet vivid flashes, the broad and unhurried currents of his temperament, and she believed with certainty that, were she to request he abandon his literary strivings, he would yield without protest. In the breathless moment that passed, the plea quivered at the edge of her tongue. Yet she let it remain unspoken. Her courage faltered just short of the mark; she could not bring herself to risk it. So instead she inclined toward him, closing the distance, and whispered against his embrace:—
source_text: A great temptation assailed her. In an insistent way she had caught glimpses of the large, easy-going side of his nature, and she felt sure, if she asked him to cease attempting to write, that he would grant her wish. In the swift instant that elapsed, the words trembled on her lips. But she did not utter them. She was not quite brave enough; she did not quite dare. Instead, she leaned toward him to meet him, and in his arms murmured:-

text: “You don’t recall how I spoke when we first crossed paths. I’ve picked up a whole different way of speaking since those days. Back then, I sounded just like that girl does. Now I’ve gotten good enough in your tongue to tell you that you don’t grasp the language she’s using. And have you any idea why she holds herself the way she does? I ponder such matters nowadays, though I never used to, and I’m starting to see—quite a bit.”
source_text: “You forget how I talked when you first met me. I have learned a new language since then. Before that time I talked as that girl talks. Now I can manage to make myself understood sufficiently in your language to explain that you do not know that other girl’s language. And do you know why she carries herself the way she does? I think about such things now, though I never used to think about them, and I am beginning to understand—much.”

text: “Hold on until I’ve finished,” Norton cried. “All you can grasp of the dance and clash of energy and substance is what brushes against our senses in some fashion. Observe—I’ll concede, purely for the sake of debate, that matter is real; and what I mean to do next is erase you using your own reasoning. There’s no other path, since you’re both by nature incapable of comprehending a philosophical abstraction.”
source_text: “You wait till I’m done,” Norton shouted. “You can know only that much of the play and interplay of force and matter as impinges in one way or another on our senses. You see, I am willing to admit, for the sake of the argument, that matter exists; and what I am about to do is to efface you by your own argument. I can’t do it any other way, for you are both congenitally unable to understand a philosophic abstraction.

text: They were twin storms of malice, circling each other with grotesque intensity. The minutes dragged, and the two warring factions fell utterly silent. Never before had they seen such depths of savagery, and it left them stunned. The pair of combatants were more beast than man. The first fine gloss of youth and fitness faded, and they began to spar with greater wariness and calculation. Neither had seized the upper hand. “No man’s won it yet,” Martin caught a voice murmuring. Then he lunged behind a feint, right and left, met a vicious counter, and sensed his cheek split clean to the bone. No naked fist could inflict that. He heard low cries of horror at the ruin done, and his own blood drenched him. Yet he betrayed nothing. He grew profoundly watchful, schooled as he was in the base cunning and foul depravity of his breed. He bided his time, feigning a reckless charge, then halted halfway—for he had glimpsed the flash of steel.
source_text: They were twin whirlwinds of hatred, revolving about each other monstrously. The time passed, and the two hostile gangs became very quiet. They had never witnessed such intensity of ferocity, and they were awed by it. The two fighters were greater brutes than they. The first splendid velvet edge of youth and condition wore off, and they fought more cautiously and deliberately. There had been no advantage gained either way. “It’s anybody’s fight,” Martin heard some one saying. Then he followed up a feint, right and left, was fiercely countered, and felt his cheek laid open to the bone. No bare knuckle had done that. He heard mutters of amazement at the ghastly damage wrought, and was drenched with his own blood. But he gave no sign. He became immensely wary, for he was wise with knowledge of the low cunning and foul vileness of his kind. He watched and waited, until he feigned a wild rush, which he stopped midway, for he had seen the glint of metal.

text: “It was almost inevitable,” Mr. Morse concluded. “This sailor was the sole man within her reach. She was bound to stir into awareness sooner or later; and when she did, there he was—the only man available at that instant. Naturally, she fell in love with him at once, or believed she had, which comes to precisely the same thing.”
source_text: “It could hardly have come otherwise,” was Mr. Morse’s judgment. “This sailor-fellow has been the only man she was in touch with. Sooner or later she was going to awaken anyway; and she did awaken, and lo! here was this sailor-fellow, the only accessible man at the moment, and of course she promptly loved him, or thought she did, which amounts to the same thing.”

text: From the very instant I first beheld you, I was utterly lost to love. In all the days that have followed, that wildness has only deepened. Now, my darling, I am at the height of my madness. My mind is so dizzied with bliss that I am nearly unhinged.
source_text: “From the first, the very first, the first moment I laid eye on you. I was mad for love of you then, and in all the time that has passed since then I have only grown the madder. I am maddest, now, dear. I am almost a lunatic, my head is so turned with joy.”

text: “You see, I was correct,” she said. “You possess more knowledge than any of the pupils entering high school, yet you still fail the exams. That’s because your learning is scattered, incomplete. You require the rigour of proper study, the kind only experienced teachers can provide. You need a solid foundation. Professor Hilton is right, and if I were in your place, I’d enrol in night school. A year and a half there might let you close that six-month gap. What’s more, it would free your days for writing, or, should you be unable to earn a living with your pen, you’d have your days free to take up some employment.”
source_text: “You see I was right,” she said. “You know far more than any of the students entering high school, and yet you can’t pass the examinations. It is because what education you have is fragmentary, sketchy. You need the discipline of study, such as only skilled teachers can give you. You must be thoroughly grounded. Professor Hilton is right, and if I were you, I’d go to night school. A year and a half of it might enable you to catch up that additional six months. Besides, that would leave you your days in which to write, or, if you could not make your living by your pen, you would have your days in which to work in some position.”

text: They had eaten supper side by side in San Francisco and were now at the Ferry Building, heading back to Oakland, when a sudden fancy seized him to reveal to Martin the “true filth.” He wheeled and darted along the waterfront, a gaunt silhouette in a billowing greatcoat, Martin struggling to stay near him. At a liquor wholesaler’s he procured two gallon-jugs of aged port, and gripping one in each hand he climbed onto a Mission Street trolley, Martin close behind laden with a cluster of whiskey quarts.
source_text: They had dined together in San Francisco, and were at the Ferry Building, returning to Oakland, when the whim came to him to show Martin the “real dirt.” He turned and fled across the water-front, a meagre shadow in a flapping overcoat, with Martin straining to keep up with him. At a wholesale liquor store he bought two gallon-demijohns of old port, and with one in each hand boarded a Mission Street car, Martin at his heels burdened with several quart-bottles of whiskey.

text: Because they had proposed an honorarium of three hundred and fifty dollars, Martin decided it wasn’t worth cabling. Besides, he still needed Brissenden’s approval. He had been correct, after all. Here at last was an editor who recognized genuine poetry when he encountered it. The price was magnificent, even for the poem of the century. As for Cartwright Bruce, Martin understood that he was the sole critic whose judgment Brissenden truly valued.
source_text: Since the honorarium they had offered was three hundred and fifty dollars, Martin thought it not worth while to telegraph. Then, too, there was Brissenden’s consent to be gained. Well, he had been right, after all. Here was one magazine editor who knew real poetry when he saw it. And the price was splendid, even though it was for the poem of a century. As for Cartwright Bruce, Martin knew that he was the one critic for whose opinions Brissenden had any respect.

text: “You realize no one’s ever truly refuted Berkeley,” he remarked, fixing his gaze on Martin. “Herbert Spencer got closer than anyone, but that’s not saying much. Even his most loyal disciples won’t venture beyond that. I came across a piece by Saleeby recently, and the strongest claim he could muster was that Spencer _almost_ managed to answer Berkeley.”
source_text: “You know Berkeley has never been answered,” he said, looking directly at Martin. “Herbert Spencer came the nearest, which was not very near. Even the stanchest of Spencer’s followers will not go farther. I was reading an essay of Saleeby’s the other day, and the best Saleeby could say was that Herbert Spencer _nearly_ succeeded in answering Berkeley.”

text: Both Maria and Martin had drunk the tart young wine on empty stomachs, and its effect rushed straight to their heads. Though they were profoundly different beings, each was isolated in their own unhappiness, and while that unhappiness went unspoken between them, it was the very thing that connected them. Maria was astonished to discover he had visited the Azores, where she had spent her childhood until the age of eleven. She was even more astonished that he had been to the Hawaiian Islands, the place to which her family had emigrated from the Azores. But her astonishment knew no limits when he revealed he had set foot on Maui, the specific island where she had come of age and married. Kahului, the town where she first encountered her husband—Martin had been there twice! Yes, she recalled the sugar steamers, and he had traveled on them—imagine that, the world was indeed tiny. And Wailuku! He had been there as well! Did he know the head overseer of the plantation? He did, and had even shared a few drinks with the man.
source_text: Both Maria and Martin drank the sour new wine on empty stomachs, and it went swiftly to their heads. Utterly differentiated creatures that they were, they were lonely in their misery, and though the misery was tacitly ignored, it was the bond that drew them together. Maria was amazed to learn that he had been in the Azores, where she had lived until she was eleven. She was doubly amazed that he had been in the Hawaiian Islands, whither she had migrated from the Azores with her people. But her amazement passed all bounds when he told her he had been on Maui, the particular island whereon she had attained womanhood and married. Kahului, where she had first met her husband,—he, Martin, had been there twice! Yes, she remembered the sugar steamers, and he had been on them—well, well, it was a small world. And Wailuku! That place, too! Did he know the head-luna of the plantation? Yes, and had had a couple of drinks with him.

text: Then they threw themselves at each other like young bulls in the full flush of youth, bare-knuckled, brimming with hatred and the lust to wound, to cripple, to annihilate. All the painful gains of humanity’s thousand-year ascent through creation were erased. Only the electric light remained—a lone milestone on the road of the grand human venture. Martin and Cheese-Face were two primitives from the Stone Age, from the crouching-ground and the tree-shelter. They plunged deeper and deeper into the mire, back into the dregs of life’s raw beginnings, grappling blindly and chemically, the way atoms grapple, the way the star-dust of the cosmos grapples, crashing together, rebounding, and crashing again and endlessly again.
source_text: Then they fell upon each other, like young bulls, in all the glory of youth, with naked fists, with hatred, with desire to hurt, to maim, to destroy. All the painful, thousand years’ gains of man in his upward climb through creation were lost. Only the electric light remained, a milestone on the path of the great human adventure. Martin and Cheese-Face were two savages, of the stone age, of the squatting place and the tree refuge. They sank lower and lower into the muddy abyss, back into the dregs of the raw beginnings of life, striving blindly and chemically, as atoms strive, as the star-dust of the heavens strives, colliding, recoiling, and colliding again and eternally again.

text: Mr. Ainsworth encountered Edward in the lobby of the Grand Continental. Edward could never fully decide whether the older man had appeared there by mere chance, absorbed in unrelated business, or whether he had sought him out with the deliberate intention of extending a dinner invitation—though Edward’s suspicions leaned toward the latter. In any event, an invitation to dine was precisely what Mr. Ainsworth offered—Ruth’s own father, the very man who had barred him from the household and severed their betrothal.
source_text: Mr. Morse met Martin in the office of the Hotel Metropole. Whether he had happened there just casually, intent on other affairs, or whether he had come there for the direct purpose of inviting him to dinner, Martin never could quite make up his mind, though he inclined toward the second hypothesis. At any rate, invited to dinner he was by Mr. Morse—Ruth’s father, who had forbidden him the house and broken off the engagement.

text: Today the menu had shifted more than usual. The soups were thinner; pork had vanished from the entrées, appearing only beside Russian turnips among the roasts. A gentle breath of spring ran through every dish. Lamb, which not long ago had frolicked on the greening slopes, was now being dressed in a sauce that recalled its playful leaps. The oyster’s voice, though not yet hushed, was fading in a tender decrescendo. The frying-pan seemed to rest idly behind the kindly shelter of the broiler. The list of pies grew longer; the heavier puddings had disappeared; the sausage, wrapped snugly in its own covering, lingered faintly in a quiet meditation on mortality alongside the buckwheat cakes and the sweet, soon-to-be-sacrificed maple.
source_text: To-day there were more changes on the bill of fare than usual. The soups were lighter; pork was eliminated from the entrées, figuring only with Russian turnips among the roasts. The gracious spirit of spring pervaded the entire menu. Lamb, that lately capered on the greening hillsides, was becoming exploited with the sauce that commemorated its gambols. The song of the oyster, though not silenced, was _dimuendo con amore_. The frying-pan seemed to be held, inactive, behind the beneficent bars of the broiler. The pie list swelled; the richer puddings had vanished; the sausage, with his drapery wrapped about him, barely lingered in a pleasant thanatopsis with the buckwheats and the sweet but doomed maple.

text: “Let’s be clear, Dell,” he said, “about where I stand. Nothing as simple as a trim or a shave or a wash could ever dim how I feel about my girl. But go on and open that package—then you’ll understand why you nearly had me fooled at the start.”
source_text: “Don’t make any mistake, Dell,” he said, “about me. I don’t think there’s anything in the way of a haircut or a shave or a shampoo that could make me like my girl any less. But if you’ll unwrap that package you may see why you had me going a while at first.”

text: Downtown, tucked where the Bowery and First Avenue nearly touch, stands the Lantern Chemist’s shop. The Lantern holds no brief for the notion that a pharmacy should traffic in curios, perfumes, and fountain treats. Ask it for a soporific, and you will not be handed a sugared almond.
source_text: The Blue Light Drug Store is downtown, between the Bowery and First Avenue, where the distance between the two streets is the shortest. The Blue Light does not consider that pharmacy is a thing of bric-a-brac, scent and ice-cream soda. If you ask it for pain-killer it will not give you a bonbon.

text: “Forgive me for intruding upon you,” he began, “but I can see that something weighs heavily on your spirit. To lessen the boldness of my approach, allow me to introduce myself: I am Prince Michael, successor to the throne of the Electorate of Valleluna. As you might infer from my dress, I am traveling incognito. It is a whim of mine to offer help to those I deem deserving. Perhaps the trouble that so plainly afflicts you might be more easily resolved if we were to face it together.”
source_text: “I beg your pardon for addressing you,” he said, “but I perceive that you are disturbed in mind. If it may serve to mitigate the liberty I have taken I will add that I am Prince Michael, heir to the throne of the Electorate of Valleluna. I appear incognito, of course, as you may gather from my appearance. It is a fancy of mine to render aid to others whom I think worthy of it. Perhaps the matter that seems to distress you is one that would more readily yield to our mutual efforts.”

text: Grandma clutches the funny pages with a grip that’s lasted two hours, while baby Tottie wobbles her way through the property listings as best she can. The scene is meant to feel comforting, because it’s best if a few lines of this tale go unread. After all, it brings hard liquor into the picture.
source_text: Grandma is holding to the comic supplement with a two-hours’ grip; and little Tottie, the baby, is rocking along the best she can with the real estate transfers. This view is intended to be reassuring, for it is desirable that a few lines of this story be skipped. For it introduces strong drink.

text: The crowning triumph of Sarah’s long skirmish with circumstance was the compact she struck with Schulenberg’s Home Restaurant. The establishment stood flush against the weathered brick tenement where she rented a narrow hall bedroom. One evening, having dispatched Schulenberg’s forty-cent, five-plate _table d’hôte_ (flung at you as swiftly as the five balls one hurls at the painted target-man’s head), Sarah carried off the menu card. It was inscribed in a hand nearly indecipherable, neither English nor German in spirit, and so perversely ordered that an unwary diner might commence with a toothpick and a custard and conclude with consommé and the name of a Tuesday.
source_text: The most brilliant and crowning feat of Sarah’s battle with the world was the deal she made with Schulenberg’s Home Restaurant. The restaurant was next door to the old red brick in which she hall-roomed. One evening after dining at Schulenberg’s 40-cent, five-course _table d’hôte_ (served as fast as you throw the five baseballs at the coloured gentleman’s head) Sarah took away with her the bill of fare. It was written in an almost unreadable script neither English nor German, and so arranged that if you were not careful you began with a toothpick and rice pudding and ended with soup and the day of the week.

text: “Oh, that powder you handed me!” Chunk said, his grin stretching wider. “Well, here’s how it played out. Last night at supper over at Riddle’s place, I took my seat, glanced at Rosy, and thought, ‘Chunk, if you’re going to win her, do it honestly—no cheap tricks on a lady of her quality.’ So I left that paper you gave me tucked in my pocket. Then my eyes landed on another guest at the table, someone who, I told myself, wasn’t showing the right warmth toward his future son-in-law. So I bided my time and slipped that powder into old man Riddle’s coffee—you follow?”
source_text: “Oh, that stuff you gave me!” said Chunk, broadening his grin; “well, it was this way. I sat down at the supper table last night at Riddle’s, and I looked at Rosy, and I says to myself, ‘Chunk, if you get the girl get her on the square—don’t try any hocus-pocus with a thoroughbred like her.’ And I keeps the paper you give me in my pocket. And then my lamps fall on another party present, who, I says to myself, is failin’ in a proper affection toward his comin’ son-in-law, so I watches my chance and dumps that powder in old man Riddle’s coffee—see?”

text: The Gape-Mobile quivered on the verge of departure. The smiling passengers perched on the upper deck had been settled into their places by a courteous steward. The pavement lay choked with onlookers who had flocked to gawk at onlookers, confirming the ancient rule that every living thing is devoured by some other living thing.
source_text: The Rubberneck Auto was about ready to start. The merry top-riders had been assigned to their seats by the gentlemanly conductor. The sidewalk was blockaded with sightseers who had gathered to stare at sightseers, justifying the natural law that every creature on earth is preyed upon by some other creature.

text: “Awe,” the boy said, with a shrug that rippled through his whole frame, “you know what I’m talkin’ about, lady. It ain’t a turn, it’s the wind. He told me to tell you he’s packed his collars and cuffs in that grip and he’s makin’ a clean break for ’Frisco. After that, he’s headin’ to the Klondike to shoot snowbirds. He says you told him to stop sendin’ pink notes and quit hangin’ around the garden gate, and this is his way of lettin’ you know. He says you tossed him aside like a has-been, never givin’ him a chance to argue the call. He says you swiped him clean, and never told him why.”
source_text: “Awe,” said the boy, with a shrug down the length of him, “yer know what I mean, lady. ’Tain’t a turn, it’s wind. He told me to tell yer he’s got his collars and cuffs in dat grip for a scoot clean out to ’Frisco. Den he’s goin’ to shoot snow-birds in de Klondike. He says yer told him not to send ’round no more pink notes nor come hangin’ over de garden gate, and he takes dis means of puttin’ yer wise. He says yer refereed him out like a has-been, and never give him no chance to kick at de decision. He says yer swiped him, and never said why.”

text: In the vast metropolis, the twin deities Romance and Peril forever wander, searching for ardent suitors. As we drift along the avenues, they steal glances at us and beckon in a dozen shifting masks. Without conscious reason, we abruptly lift our eyes to discover in a pane of glass a countenance that might have stepped from our private album of cherished likenesses; on a hushed lane we overhear a wail of terror and despair leaking from a dark, sealed building; a hansom sets us down not at our accustomed corner but before an unfamiliar portal, which someone, smiling faintly, swings wide and gestures us inside; a scribbled scrap of paper spirals from the high trellises of Fortune to brush our shoes; we exchange with darting strangers looks of sudden loathing, tenderness, and dread amid the streaming multitude; a swift downpour—and our umbrella may be shielding the daughter of the Crescent Moon and near kin to the Celestial Spheres; at every turning kerchiefs tumble, fingers summon, eyes assail, and the forsaken, the solitary, the ecstatic, the enigmatic, the hazardous, ever-shifting filaments of intrigue are pressed into our palms. Yet hardly any of us dare to clutch and trace them. We have grown rigid, the iron rod of propriety thrust along our spines. We march onward; and eventually we pause, at the close of a profoundly colorless existence, to realize that our romance has amounted to a bloodless wedding or two, a satin favor locked in a strongbox, and a lifelong quarrel with a hissing radiator.
source_text: In the big city the twin spirits Romance and Adventure are always abroad seeking worthy wooers. As we roam the streets they slyly peep at us and challenge us in twenty different guises. Without knowing why, we look up suddenly to see in a window a face that seems to belong to our gallery of intimate portraits; in a sleeping thoroughfare we hear a cry of agony and fear coming from an empty and shuttered house; instead of at our familiar curb, a cab-driver deposits us before a strange door, which one, with a smile, opens for us and bids us enter; a slip of paper, written upon, flutters down to our feet from the high lattices of Chance; we exchange glances of instantaneous hate, affection and fear with hurrying strangers in the passing crowds; a sudden douse of rain—and our umbrella may be sheltering the daughter of the Full Moon and first cousin of the Sidereal System; at every corner handkerchiefs drop, fingers beckon, eyes besiege, and the lost, the lonely, the rapturous, the mysterious, the perilous, changing clues of adventure are slipped into our fingers. But few of us are willing to hold and follow them. We are grown stiff with the ramrod of convention down our backs. We pass on; and some day we come, at the end of a very dull life, to reflect that our romance has been a pallid thing of a marriage or two, a satin rosette kept in a safe-deposit drawer, and a lifelong feud with a steam radiator.

text: Ikey Schoenstein served as the night clerk at the Blue Light pharmacy and was a confidant to his patrons. This was the way of the East Side, where the drugstore’s heart hasn’t frozen over. There, as it ought to be, the pharmacist acts as counselor, confessor, guide—a devoted and capable missionary and sage whose learning earns respect, whose mysterious knowledge is revered, and whose remedies are often tipped, unsipped, into the gutter. So Ikey’s horn-rimmed, spectacled nose and his slender, studiously stooped frame were familiar sights around the Blue Light, and both his counsel and his attention were eagerly sought.
source_text: Ikey Schoenstein was the night clerk of the Blue Light and the friend of his customers. Thus it is on the East Side, where the heart of pharmacy is not glacé. There, as it should be, the druggist is a counsellor, a confessor, an adviser, an able and willing missionary and mentor whose learning is respected, whose occult wisdom is venerated and whose medicine is often poured, untasted, into the gutter. Therefore Ikey’s corniform, be-spectacled nose and narrow, knowledge-bowed figure was well known in the vicinity of the Blue Light, and his advice and notice were much desired.

text: If men had any real notion of how women fill the solitary hours, they would never consent to marry. Laura Lean Jibbey novels, brittle shards of peanut candy, a dab of almond cream worked into the neck, dishes left to sit, a half-hour chat with the iceman, leafing through a bundle of old correspondence, a couple of pickles and two bottles of malt extract, a full hour spent spying through a tear in the window shade at the flat across the air-shaft—that is the sum of it. Then, twenty minutes before he is due home from work, she tidies the rooms, pins her hair-rat securely out of sight, and lays out a heap of sewing to sustain a ten-minute deception.
source_text: If men knew how women pass the time when they are alone they’d never marry. Laura Lean Jibbey, peanut brittle, a little almond cream on the neck muscles, dishes unwashed, half an hour’s talk with the iceman, reading a package of old letters, a couple of pickles and two bottles of malt extract, one hour peeking through a hole in the window shade into the flat across the air-shaft—that’s about all there is to it. Twenty minutes before time for him to come home from work she straightens up the house, fixes her rat so it won’t show, and gets out a lot of sewing for a ten-minute bluff.

text: At dusk, near the stroke of six, my lady commanded him to stir himself and perform the atmosphere trick for Sweetling. I’ve kept it hidden till this moment, but that was her name for me. The brindle was dubbed “Chirp.” I maintain I have the advantage over him in any race a hare might run. Yet “Sweetling” remains a tin label rattling behind one’s dignity.
source_text: One evening about 6 o’clock my mistress ordered him to get busy and do the ozone act for Lovey. I have concealed it until now, but that is what she called me. The black-and-tan was called “Tweetness.” I consider that I have the bulge on him as far as you could chase a rabbit. Still “Lovey” is something of a nomenclatural tin can on the tail of one’s self respect.

text: Maggie Toole, with her heavy-lidded gaze, wide lips, and an awkward, southpaw rhythm in the two-step, always attended the Saturday dances alongside Anna McCarty and Anna’s young man. The pair labored shoulder to shoulder at the mill and were inseparable confidantes. For that reason, Anna insisted that Jimmy Burns collect her by way of Maggie’s cottage each weekend, ensuring her friend might join them at the hall.
source_text: Maggie Toole, on account of her dull eyes, broad mouth and left-handed style of footwork in the two-step, went to the dances with Anna McCarty and her “fellow.” Anna and Maggie worked side by side in the factory, and were the greatest chums ever. So Anna always made Jimmy Burns take her by Maggie’s house every Saturday night so that her friend could go to the dance with them.

text: I endured a cur’s existence in those rooms. Nearly the whole day I sprawled in my nook, observing that stout lady murder the hours. Now and then I drowsed and conjured idle fancies of bolting after strays into cellars and snarling at dowagers in lace gloves, the proper work of a hound. Then she’d descend on me with a flood of simpering lapdog nonsense and peck my snout—but what choice had I? A beast can’t gnaw tobacco.
source_text: I led a dog’s life in that flat. ‘Most all day I lay there in my corner watching that fat woman kill time. I slept sometimes and had pipe dreams about being out chasing cats into basements and growling at old ladies with black mittens, as a dog was intended to do. Then she would pounce upon me with a lot of that drivelling poodle palaver and kiss me on the nose—but what could I do? A dog can’t chew cloves.

text: Sarah’s room faced the rear of the house. From her window, the view was once the blank brick face of the box factory on the adjoining street. Yet now that wall had turned to flawless glass, and Sarah found herself gazing along a green lane canopied by cherry and elm, its edges thick with raspberry canes and Cherokee roses.
source_text: Sarah’s room was at the back of the house. Looking out the window she could see the windowless rear brick wall of the box factory on the next street. But the wall was clearest crystal; and Sarah was looking down a grassy lane shaded with cherry trees and elms and bordered with raspberry bushes and Cherokee roses.

text: But the soul of Grimshaw’s was Tessa. Tessa was a server. She embodied, in full, Schlegel’s notion of nature refined by craft. She was given over, chiefly, to service, as Diana was to the discipline of the chase, or Aphrodite to the subtle art of prolonged glances. Cast in marble and set upon a plinth, she might have stood beside her most exalted sisters as “Toast-and-Tea Warming the World.” She was part of Grimshaw’s. One looked for her towering shape to materialize through those dense, greasy veils of griddle smoke as one watches for the white cliffs to emerge from a Channel mist. There, amidst the fog of boiling cabbage and the exhalations of endless “pie and,” the shattering of plates, the ringing of cutlery, the shrieking of quick orders, the wails of the ravenous and the whole hideous din of men devouring, besieged by clouds of those humming winged pests Pharaoh’s legacy bestowed upon us, Tessa charted her majestic course like a tall ship cleaving through a flotilla of clamouring skiffs.
source_text: But the chief thing at Cypher’s was Milly. Milly was a waitress. She was a grand example of Kraft’s theory of the artistic adjustment of nature. She belonged, largely, to waiting, as Minerva did to the art of scrapping, or Venus to the science of serious flirtation. Pedestalled and in bronze she might have stood with the noblest of her heroic sisters as “Liver-and-Bacon Enlivening the World.” She belonged to Cypher’s. You expected to see her colossal figure loom through that reeking blue cloud of smoke from frying fat just as you expect the Palisades to appear through a drifting Hudson River fog. There amid the steam of vegetables and the vapours of acres of “ham and,” the crash of crockery, the clatter of steel, the screaming of “short orders,” the cries of the hungering and all the horrid tumult of feeding man, surrounded by swarms of the buzzing winged beasts bequeathed us by Pharaoh, Milly steered her magnificent way like some great liner cleaving among the canoes of howling savages.

text: Presently we stepped outside, for it was eleven o’clock, and paused a while on the pavement. Then the gentleman announced that he must be heading home, and asked me and Tobin to accompany him. We came to a side street two blocks distant, lined with brick houses that had tall stoops and iron railings. The man halted before one of them and gazed up at the uppermost windows, which he saw were dark.
source_text: By and by we moved out, for ’twas eleven o’clock, and stands a bit upon the sidewalk. And then the man says he must be going home, and invites me and Tobin to walk that way. We arrives on a side street two blocks away where there is a stretch of brick houses with high stoops and iron fences. The man stops at one of them and looks up at the top windows which he finds dark.

text: Dulcie pivoted toward the bureau to retrieve her kerchief, then halted abruptly, pressing her teeth into her lower lip. In the glass she had glimpsed an enchanted realm and herself a royal maiden stirring from centuries of sleep. She had overlooked the one who observed her with mournful, lovely, severe eyes—the sole presence there to sanction or censure her deeds. Erect and lean and towering, his fine, sorrowing features stamped with grieving admonition, Lord Ashworth gazed upon her from his gilded portrait frame atop the bureau with those piercing, unforgettable eyes.
source_text: Dulcie turned to the dresser to get her handkerchief; and then she stopped still, and bit her underlip hard. While looking in her mirror she had seen fairyland and herself, a princess, just awakening from a long slumber. She had forgotten one that was watching her with sad, beautiful, stern eyes—the only one there was to approve or condemn what she did. Straight and slender and tall, with a look of sorrowful reproach on his handsome, melancholy face, General Kitchener fixed his wonderful eyes on her out of his gilt photograph frame on the dresser.

text: One afternoon, Miss Leeson appeared in search of a room. She hauled along a typewriter that seemed designed for a far sturdier woman to tote. She was an exceedingly small young lady, with eyes and hair that had apparently continued to grow long after she herself had ceased, and they forever seemed to be exclaiming, “Good heavens! Why couldn’t you keep pace with us?”
source_text: One day Miss Leeson came hunting for a room. She carried a typewriter made to be lugged around by a much larger lady. She was a very little girl, with eyes and hair that had kept on growing after she had stopped and that always looked as if they were saying: “Goodness me! Why didn’t you keep up with us?”

text: A soft rustling stirred within, and the door eased open. A girl, still in her teens, stood before him, pale and unsteady. Her grip on the handle slackened as she swayed, reaching out blindly. Rudolf caught her and lowered her onto a threadbare couch pressed against the wall. He shut the door and quickly scanned the room, lit by the quivering glow of a gas jet. It was tidy, but the tale it told was one of utter destitution.
source_text: A faint rustle was heard inside, and the door slowly opened. A girl not yet twenty stood there, white-faced and tottering. She loosed the knob and swayed weakly, groping with one hand. Rudolf caught her and laid her on a faded couch that stood against the wall. He closed the door and took a swift glance around the room by the light of a flickering gas jet. Neat, but extreme poverty was the story that he read.

text: It merits a paragraph to note that you can observe this striking ritual any evening in countless New York City cafés. Gallons of beer have been drained while patrons spin explanations for it. Some have jumped to the conclusion that every Southerner in the city rushes to a café at dusk. The popularity of the “rebel” tune in a Northern metropolis is mildly baffling, but not beyond untangling. The Spanish-American War, a string of abundant mint and watermelon harvests, a few long-odds triumphs at the New Orleans racetrack, and the lavish dinners thrown by the Indiana and Kansas transplants who make up the North Carolina Society have turned the South into something of a craze in Manhattan. Your manicurist will murmur that your left index finger puts her so in mind of a gentleman’s back in Richmond, Virginia. Well, of course; but many women have to earn a living now—the war, you understand.
source_text: It is worth a paragraph to say that this remarkable scene can be witnessed every evening in numerous cafés in the City of New York. Tons of brew have been consumed over theories to account for it. Some have conjectured hastily that all Southerners in town hie themselves to cafés at nightfall. This applause of the “rebel” air in a Northern city does puzzle a little; but it is not insolvable. The war with Spain, many years’ generous mint and watermelon crops, a few long-shot winners at the New Orleans race-track, and the brilliant banquets given by the Indiana and Kansas citizens who compose the North Carolina Society have made the South rather a “fad” in Manhattan. Your manicure will lisp softly that your left forefinger reminds her so much of a gentleman’s in Richmond, Va. Oh, certainly; but many a lady has to work now—the war, you know.

text: Then you climbed a single set of steps and examined the rear chamber on the second level, priced at eight dollars. Persuaded by her practiced second-story air that this room merited the twelve dollars Mr. Hollingsworth had unfailingly paid before departing to oversee his cousin’s citrus grove in Florida close to Coral Cove, where Mrs. Pendleton habitually wintered in the two-windowed front suite with its own washroom, you somehow stammered that you required something even more modest.
source_text: Next you ascended one flight of stairs and looked at the second-floor-back at $8. Convinced by her second-floor manner that it was worth the $12 that Mr. Toosenberry always paid for it until he left to take charge of his brother’s orange plantation in Florida near Palm Beach, where Mrs. McIntyre always spent the winters that had the double front room with private bath, you managed to babble that you wanted something still cheaper.

text: “I cannot,” the fellow murmurs, “for the binding would fail to contain you. Not now. The finest I may manage is to savor you alone, since the hour has not come to shatter the confines of the page. You would gleam splendidly in ink. By my solitary self must I drain this chalice of delight. Yet, I thank you, lads; I am deeply obliged.”
source_text: “I will not,” says the man, “for the covers will not hold ye. Not yet. The best I can do is to enjoy ye meself, for the time is not ripe for destroying the limitations of print. Ye would look fantastic in type. All alone by meself must I drink this cup of joy. But, I thank ye, boys; I am truly grateful.”

text: He burst through the green door and raced down the steps. Twenty minutes later, he returned, tapping the door with his foot to signal her to let him in. His arms were wrapped around a collection of items from the market and the eatery. He spread them across the table—loaves and butter, chilled cuts of meat, pastries, tarts, brined vegetables, shellfish, a whole roasted bird, a jug of milk, and a flask of steaming tea.
source_text: He dashed out the green door and down the stairs. In twenty minutes he was back again, kicking at the door with his toe for her to open it. With both arms he hugged an array of wares from the grocery and the restaurant. On the table he laid them—bread and butter, cold meats, cakes, pies, pickles, oysters, a roasted chicken, a bottle of milk and one of red-hot tea.

text: “You’re not thinking straight, Pitcher,” Maxwell snapped. “When would I have ever told you to do that? Miss Leslie has been nothing but excellent the whole year she’s worked here. This job is hers for as long as she wants it. There’s no vacancy here, ma’am. Cancel that request with the agency, Pitcher, and don’t send any more candidates my way.”
source_text: “You are losing your mind, Pitcher,” said Maxwell. “Why should I have given you any such instructions? Miss Leslie has given perfect satisfaction during the year she has been here. The place is hers as long as she chooses to retain it. There’s no place open here, madam. Countermand that order with the agency, Pitcher, and don’t bring any more of ’em in here.”

text: “I found no students,” she admitted softly. “And I couldn’t stand the thought of you abandoning your studies; so I took a position pressing shirts at that vast laundry on Twenty-fourth Street. I flatter myself I invented both General Pinkney and Clementina rather cleverly, wouldn’t you say, Joe? And when one of the laundry girls let a scorching iron fall against my hand this afternoon, I spent the whole walk home conjuring that tale about the Welsh rabbit. You’re not cross with me, are you, Joe? And if I hadn’t secured the work, you might never have sold your drawings to that gentleman from Peoria.”
source_text: “I couldn’t get any pupils,” she confessed. “And I couldn’t bear to have you give up your lessons; and I got a place ironing shirts in that big Twenty-fourth street laundry. And I think I did very well to make up both General Pinkney and Clementina, don’t you, Joe? And when a girl in the laundry set down a hot iron on my hand this afternoon I was all the way home making up that story about the Welsh rabbit. You’re not angry, are you, Joe? And if I hadn’t got the work you mightn’t have sold your sketches to that man from Peoria.”

text: “Absolutely! I only wish Jimmy’s done nearly as well for himself. He was a bit of a slow-and-steady type, decent soul that he was.
