I’ve had to match myself against some of the keenest minds around to build my fortune. In New York, a fellow settles into a rut. It’s the West that sharpens him to a fine point.”
source_text: “You bet! I hope Jimmy has done half as well. He was a kind of plodder, though, good fellow as he was. I’ve had to compete with some of the sharpest wits going to get my pile. A man gets in a groove in New York. It takes the West to put a razor-edge on him.”

text: “Look, Wiggle-and-Skip,” I said, “you’re well aware that no genuine man would be caught dead babysitting a dog out in the open. Every fellow I’ve ever seen tethered to a mutt had the expression of someone itching to take a swing at anyone who so much as glanced his way. Yet your employer strolls in daily, as chipper and self-assured as a novice magician pulling off the egg illusion. What’s his secret? Don’t try to convince me he enjoys it.”
source_text: “See, here, Wiggle-and-Skip,” I says, “you know that it ain’t the nature of a real man to play dry nurse to a dog in public. I never saw one leashed to a bow-wow yet that didn’t look like he’d like to lick every other man that looked at him. But your boss comes in every day as perky and set up as an amateur prestidigitator doing the egg trick. How does he do it? Don’t tell me he likes it.”

text: But in the space of that single minute, a crosstown streetcar halted squarely before the cab. The driver attempted to veer left, only to be blocked by a cumbersome express wagon. Swinging right, he was forced to retreat from a furniture van that had no right being there. He tried reversing, but let the reins fall and let out a dutiful string of curses. He found himself trapped in a snarled knot of carriages and horses.
source_text: But within that minute a crosstown car had stopped directly in front of the cab. The cabman tried to pass to the left, but a heavy express wagon cut him off. He tried the right, and had to back away from a furniture van that had no business to be there. He tried to back out, but dropped his reins and swore dutifully. He was blockaded in a tangled mess of vehicles and horses.

text: “You’re a true gentleman,” Anthony pronounced with finality. “I’ve read about these fashionable lads laying out twenty-four dollars a dozen for bath soap, and crossing the century line for their tailoring. You’ve got just as much coin to squander as any of that set, yet you hold fast to what’s proper and restrained. Myself, I keep to the old Eureka—not only out of fondness, but it’s the most honest soap on the market. The moment you spend more than a dime a cake, you’re purchasing cheap scents and fancy wrappers. Still, fifty cents serves a young fellow of your age, standing, and circumstances handsomely. As I remarked, you’re a gentleman. They claim it requires three generations to fashion one. Nonsense. Money works the trick as smoothly as soap grease. It’s fashioned you into one. By Jove! it’s nearly fashioned me as well. I’ve grown almost as rude and cross and uncivil as these two ancient Knickerbocker gentlemen flanking me, who lie awake at night because I purchased the space between them.”
source_text: “You’re a gentleman,” said Anthony, decidedly. “I’ve heard of these young bloods spending $24 a dozen for soap, and going over the hundred mark for clothes. You’ve got as much money to waste as any of ’em, and yet you stick to what’s decent and moderate. Now I use the old Eureka—not only for sentiment, but it’s the purest soap made. Whenever you pay more than 10 cents a cake for soap you buy bad perfumes and labels. But 50 cents is doing very well for a young man in your generation, position and condition. As I said, you’re a gentleman. They say it takes three generations to make one. They’re off. Money’ll do it as slick as soap grease. It’s made you one. By hokey! it’s almost made one of me. I’m nearly as impolite and disagreeable and ill-mannered as these two old Knickerbocker gents on each side of me that can’t sleep of nights because I bought in between ’em.”

text: In a shop where prices ran low, Cecily paused and purchased a counterfeit lace collar, surrendering her last half-dollar for it. Those coins had been allotted differently: fifteen for an evening meal, ten for the morning’s bread, another ten for the noon repast. A further dime was meant to fatten her meager hoard, and the final nickel had been destined for licorice pastilles—the sort that bulged against the jaw like a swollen tooth, their sweetness lingering nearly as long. The licorice was a wild indulgence, a tiny revel, yet what existence endures without its joys?
source_text: Dulcie stopped in a store where goods were cheap and bought an imitation lace collar with her fifty cents. That money was to have been spent otherwise—fifteen cents for supper, ten cents for breakfast, ten cents for lunch. Another dime was to be added to her small store of savings; and five cents was to be squandered for licorice drops—the kind that made your cheek look like the toothache, and last as long. The licorice was an extravagance—almost a carouse—but what is life without pleasures?

text: The Give and Take Athletic Association was true to its name. Its Orchard Street clubhouse brimmed with muscle-building contraptions. Bolstered by these sinews, the members regularly took on the police and competing social and athletic clubs in exuberant brawls. Between these weightier pursuits, the Saturday night dance with the paper-box factory girls served as both a civilizing touch and a handy cover. For sometimes word got around, and if you were among the chosen who crept up the unlit rear staircase, you might witness a tidy, satisfying little welterweight bout fought to a finish—as crisp as any that ever took place within the ropes.
source_text: The Give and Take Athletic Association lived up to its name. The hall of the association in Orchard street was fitted out with muscle-making inventions. With the fibres thus builded up the members were wont to engage the police and rival social and athletic organisations in joyous combat. Between these more serious occupations the Saturday night hop with the paper-box factory girls came as a refining influence and as an efficient screen. For sometimes the tip went ’round, and if you were among the elect that tiptoed up the dark back stairway you might see as neat and satisfying a little welter-weight affair to a finish as ever happened inside the ropes.

text: The young man looked at the clock once more, his brow furrowing with a deep scowl. As his eyes drifted from the luminous timepiece, they fixed themselves keenly on a four-storey red-brick house among the row of residences across from where he sat. The blinds were lowered, and through them the lights in several rooms glowed faintly.
source_text: The young man glanced at the clock again and frowned darkly. When his gaze strayed from the glowing horologue of time it rested intently upon a four-story red brick house in the row of dwellings opposite to where he sat. The shades were drawn, and the lights in many rooms shone dimly through them.

text: Sarah had forced the shells open with her clumsy tool just enough to taste a morsel of the chilly, damp universe inside. She understood shorthand no better than a stenography graduate freshly unleashed upon the world by a commercial college. Unable to take dictation, she couldn’t join that brilliant constellation of office skill. Instead, she worked as an independent typist, wandering in search of stray copying assignments.
source_text: Sarah had managed to pry apart the shells with her unhandy weapon far enough to nibble a wee bit at the cold and clammy world within. She knew no more shorthand than if she had been a graduate in stenography just let slip upon the world by a business college. So, not being able to stenog, she could not enter that bright galaxy of office talent. She was a free-lance typewriter and canvassed for odd jobs of copying.

text: “She has stood within these walls,” he exclaimed, and he lunged forward to seize some relic from the space, certain he would know even the faintest trace of her presence or possession. That pervasive fragrance of reseda, the perfume she had cherished and claimed as her signature—from where did it arise?
source_text: “She has been in this room,” he cried, and he sprang to wrest from it a token, for he knew he would recognize the smallest thing that had belonged to her or that she had touched. This enveloping scent of mignonette, the odour that she had loved and made her own—whence came it?

text: The glance pierced the explorer like a needle. He sensed within it an unspoken charge of inadequacy. Whatever secret message those inscribed symbols on the cards conveyed, the dark figure had twice singled him from the crowd to receive them—and now appeared to judge him lacking the cleverness and nerve to confront the puzzle.
source_text: The look stung the adventurer. He read in it a silent accusation that he had been found wanting. Whatever the mysterious written words on the cards might mean, the black had selected him twice from the throng for their recipient; and now seemed to have condemned him as deficient in the wit and spirit to engage the enigma.

text: The Blue Light disdains the shortcuts of today’s pharmaceutical industry. It steeps its own opium and strains its own laudanum and paregoric. Even now, pills are compounded behind its towering prescription counter—shaped on a private pill-tile, cut with a spatula, rounded between finger and thumb, coated with calcined magnesia, and dispensed in small circular pasteboard boxes. The shop sits on a corner where flocks of scruffy, rowdy children frolic, marking themselves as future recipients of the cough drops and calming syrups that await them within.
source_text: The Blue Light scorns the labour-saving arts of modern pharmacy. It macerates its opium and percolates its own laudanum and paregoric. To this day pills are made behind its tall prescription desk—pills rolled out on its own pill-tile, divided with a spatula, rolled with the finger and thumb, dusted with calcined magnesia and delivered in little round pasteboard pill-boxes. The store is on a corner about which coveys of ragged-plumed, hilarious children play and become candidates for the cough drops and soothing syrups that wait for them inside.

text: I doubt it will unsettle any of you to come across a piece written by an animal. Mr. Kipling and quite a few others have already shown that creatures can voice their thoughts in perfectly profitable English, and these days no periodical goes to print without an animal tale tucked inside—except those old-fashioned monthlies still recycling engravings of Bryan and the Mont Pélee disaster.
source_text: I don’t suppose it will knock any of you people off your perch to read a contribution from an animal. Mr. Kipling and a good many others have demonstrated the fact that animals can express themselves in remunerative English, and no magazine goes to press nowadays without an animal story in it, except the old-style monthlies that are still running pictures of Bryan and the Mont Pélee horror.

text: Behold the sorcery of Spring! A herald was needed for that vast, frozen metropolis of granite and steel. None could carry the tidings save that small, steadfast field-messenger in his coarse emerald cloak and unassuming manner. A true mercenary of the seasons is the dandelion—the _dent-de-lion_, as the French cooks name him. In bloom, he attends courtship, twined through my lady’s chestnut tresses; unripe and green and unflowered, he plunges into the bubbling kettle and speaks the decree of his queen.
source_text: But what a witch is Spring! Into the great cold city of stone and iron a message had to be sent. There was none to convey it but the little hardy courier of the fields with his rough green coat and modest air. He is a true soldier of fortune, this _dent-de-lion_—this lion’s tooth, as the French chefs call him. Flowered, he will assist at love-making, wreathed in my lady’s nut-brown hair; young and callow and unblossomed, he goes into the boiling pot and delivers the word of his sovereign mistress.

text: Once you made it through Mrs. Parker’s contempt, you’d be escorted to the third floor to see Mr. Skidder’s spacious hall bedroom. The room wasn’t available—he spent his days there writing plays and chain-smoking. Still, every prospective tenant was paraded through to admire the drapery valances. Each time this happened, Mr. Skidder, terrified at the thought of being thrown out, would scrape together a little toward his back rent.
source_text: If you survived Mrs. Parker’s scorn, you were taken to look at Mr. Skidder’s large hall room on the third floor. Mr. Skidder’s room was not vacant. He wrote plays and smoked cigarettes in it all day long. But every room-hunter was made to visit his room to admire the lambrequins. After each visit, Mr. Skidder, from the fright caused by possible eviction, would pay something on his rent.

text: It is no fanciful notion that the driver’s absolute focus and narrowed philosophy spring from the peculiar design of the carriage itself. The master of the reins perches high, a solitary god on an indivisible throne, clutching your destiny between two straps of unreliable hide. Powerless, absurd, boxed in, jiggling like a puppet emperor, you crouch like a mouse in a snare—you, whom footmen bow to on steady ground—and are forced to chirp skyward through a crack in your moving tomb to voice your fragile commands.
source_text: It is not an extravagant theory that the cabby’s singleness of purpose and concentrated view of life are the results of the hansom’s peculiar construction. The cock-of-the-roost sits aloft like Jupiter on an unsharable seat, holding your fate between two thongs of inconstant leather. Helpless, ridiculous, confined, bobbing like a toy mandarin, you sit like a rat in a trap—you, before whom butlers cringe on solid land—and must squeak upward through a slit in your peripatetic sarcophagus to make your feeble wishes known.

text: “Not until tonight,” Joe replied. “And I wouldn’t have known even then, except I brought up some cotton waste and oil from the engine-room this afternoon for a girl on the upper floor—she’d scorched her hand with a flatiron. I’ve been stoking the boiler in that laundry these past two weeks.”
source_text: “I didn’t,” said Joe, “until to-night. And I wouldn’t have then, only I sent up this cotton waste and oil from the engine-room this afternoon for a girl upstairs who had her hand burned with a smoothing-iron. I’ve been firing the engine in that laundry for the last two weeks.”

text: I began life as a yellow puppy of distinguished lineage, only to mature into a nameless yellow mongrel resembling something between an Angora cat and a crate of lemons. Yet my mistress never caught on. She believed those two primordial hounds that Noah herded onto the ark were merely a distant offshoot of my family tree. It required two officers to restrain her from entering me in the Madison Square Garden show for the Siberian bloodhound award.
source_text: From a pedigreed yellow pup I grew up to be an anonymous yellow cur looking like a cross between an Angora cat and a box of lemons. But my mistress never tumbled. She thought that the two primeval pups that Noah chased into the ark were but a collateral branch of my ancestors. It took two policemen to keep her from entering me at the Madison Square Garden for the Siberian bloodhound prize.

text: One afternoon, Mr. Seeders arrived for his meal with the flush of beer already upon him. The restaurant was nearly empty, holding just a couple of other patrons. Once he had polished off his weakfish, he rose, slid an arm around Tildy’s waist, and planted a loud, brazen kiss on her. Then he sauntered out to the street, snapped his fingers toward the laundry, and headed off to lose pennies in the slot machines at the Amusement Arcade.
source_text: One day when Mr. Seeders came in to dinner he had been drinking beer. There were only two or three customers in the restaurant. When Mr. Seeders had finished his weakfish he got up, put his arm around Tildy’s waist, kissed her loudly and impudently, walked out upon the street, snapped his fingers in the direction of the laundry, and hied himself to play pennies in the slot machines at the Amusement Arcade.

text: The third sip I desired from the wellspring of understanding was illumination regarding the figure called A Man About Town. He lingered in my thoughts more indistinctly than any archetype should. We must possess a tangible notion of a thing, even an invented one, before we can truly grasp it. For instance, I carry a mental image of John Doe as crisp as an etched copperplate. His gaze is a watery blue; he sports a tan waistcoat and a glossy black serge jacket. He is forever poised in sunlight, chewing on something, his pocket knife half-closed and flicked open again with his thumb. And should the Man Higher Up ever be discovered, you may rely on my word—he will be a tall, pallid gentleman with blue wristbands peeking beneath his sleeves, seated to have his boots buffed within earshot of a skittle alley, and somewhere near him there will be turquoises.
source_text: The third draught that I craved from the fount of knowledge was enlightenment concerning the character known as A Man About Town. He was more vague in my mind than a type should be. We must have a concrete idea of anything, even if it be an imaginary idea, before we can comprehend it. Now, I have a mental picture of John Doe that is as clear as a steel engraving. His eyes are weak blue; he wears a brown vest and a shiny black serge coat. He stands always in the sunshine chewing something; and he keeps half-shutting his pocket knife and opening it again with his thumb. And, if the Man Higher Up is ever found, take my assurance for it, he will be a large, pale man with blue wristlets showing under his cuffs, and he will be sitting to have his shoes polished within sound of a bowling alley, and there will be somewhere about him turquoises.

text: When her weeping had subsided, Dulcie rose and slipped out of her finest dress, drawing on the faded blue kimono instead. She had no appetite for supper. She murmured a couple of verses from “Sammy.” Then a tiny crimson blemish on the edge of her nose seized her attention completely. Once she had dealt with that, she pulled a chair up to the unsteady table and began telling her fortune with a worn pack of cards.
source_text: When her cry was over Dulcie got up and took off her best dress, and put on her old blue kimono. She wanted no dinner. She sang two verses of “Sammy.” Then she became intensely interested in a little red speck on the side of her nose. And after that was attended to, she drew up a chair to the rickety table, and told her fortune with an old deck of cards.

text: Richard rose from his seat in the cab and surveyed the scene. Before him, a dense torrent of wagons, trucks, cabs, vans, and streetcars crammed the enormous junction where Broadway, Sixth Avenue, and Thirty-Fourth Street collided—like a twenty-six-inch woman squeezing into a twenty-two-inch corset. From every approach, vehicles still raced and clattered headlong toward the intersection, flinging themselves into the tangled mass, locking wheels and mingling their drivers’ curses with the uproar. It seemed as though all of Manhattan’s traffic had gridlocked around them. Even the oldest New Yorker among the thousands lining the sidewalks had never seen a street blockade of such magnitude.
source_text: Richard stood up in the cab and looked around. He saw a congested flood of wagons, trucks, cabs, vans and street cars filling the vast space where Broadway, Sixth Avenue and Thirty-fourth street cross one another as a twenty-six inch maiden fills her twenty-two inch girdle. And still from all the cross streets they were hurrying and rattling toward the converging point at full speed, and hurling themselves into the struggling mass, locking wheels and adding their drivers’ imprecations to the clamour. The entire traffic of Manhattan seemed to have jammed itself around them. The oldest New Yorker among the thousands of spectators that lined the sidewalks had not witnessed a street blockade of the proportions of this one.

text: “Hear me out,” I tell him. “Daniel Tobin’s mind remains as sound as it ever was. He may be slightly unhinged from taking just enough liquor to rattle his senses without quieting them, but he’s merely pursuing the natural course of his own fancies and dilemmas, which I’ll lay out for you.” With that I recount the business of the palm-reading woman and how the arrow of fate now designates him as a vessel of luck. “Now, grasp,” I finish, “where I stand in this uproar. I am the comrade of my comrade Tobin, by my own reckoning. It’s simple to befriend the thriving, for there’s profit in it; it’s no great feat to befriend the destitute, for you swell with thanks and get your likeness taken before a slum dwelling, a coal bucket in one fist and an orphan in the other. But the craft of friendship is truly tested when you stand by a natural simpleton. And that is my present labor,” I declare, “since, to my eye, no destiny is etched on my palm that wasn’t carved there by a pickaxe handle. And though you bear the most twisted nose in all New York, I doubt every soothsayer in trade could coax good fortune out of you. Yet the creases in Danny’s palm pointed straight to you, and I’ll aid him in trying you out till he sees you’re barren of luck.”
source_text: “Listen, man,” says I to him. “Daniel Tobin is as sensible as he ever was. Maybe he is a bit deranged on account of having drink enough to disturb but not enough to settle his wits, but he is no more than following out the legitimate path of his superstitions and predicaments, which I will explain to you.” With that I relates the facts about the palmist lady and how the finger of suspicion points to him as an instrument of good fortune. “Now, understand,” I concludes, “my position in this riot. I am the friend of me friend Tobin, according to me interpretations. ’Tis easy to be a friend to the prosperous, for it pays; ’tis not hard to be a friend to the poor, for ye get puffed up by gratitude and have your picture printed standing in front of a tenement with a scuttle of coal and an orphan in each hand. But it strains the art of friendship to be true friend to a born fool. And that’s what I’m doing,” says I, “for, in my opinion, there’s no fortune to be read from the palm of me hand that wasn’t printed there with the handle of a pick. And, though ye’ve got the crookedest nose in New York City, I misdoubt that all the fortune-tellers doing business could milk good luck from ye. But the lines of Danny’s hand pointed to ye fair, and I’ll assist him to experiment with ye until he’s convinced ye’re dry.”

text: “Him?” replies the black-and-tan. “Why, he takes Nature’s Own Cure. He gets thoroughly sozzled. When we first head out, he’s as timid as the fellow on the steamboat who’d sooner play pedro even when every hand’s a jackpot. After we’ve hit eight saloons, he no longer minds whether the creature tugging at his line is a dog or a catfish. I’ve sacrificed two inches of my tail just dodging those swinging doors.”
source_text: “Him?” says the black-and-tan. “Why, he uses Nature’s Own Remedy. He gets spifflicated. At first when we go out he’s as shy as the man on the steamer who would rather play pedro when they make ’em all jackpots. By the time we’ve been in eight saloons he don’t care whether the thing on the end of his line is a dog or a catfish. I’ve lost two inches of my tail trying to sidestep those swinging doors.”

text: On a bend of Hawthorne Street, gas lamps and artfully arranged goods behind polished glass rendered a storefront striking. Silas seized a loose paving stone and hurled it through the pane. A crowd surged around the curve, a constable at the fore. Silas remained motionless, hands buried in his coat, and grinned at the glint of copper badges.
source_text: At a corner of Sixth Avenue electric lights and cunningly displayed wares behind plate-glass made a shop window conspicuous. Soapy took a cobblestone and dashed it through the glass. People came running around the corner, a policeman in the lead. Soapy stood still, with his hands in his pockets, and smiled at the sight of brass buttons.

text: The young man seated in the chair let these reflections pass through his mind with silent, padded steps, as the room filled with borrowed noises and borrowed odors. From one chamber came a giggling, loose and unrestrained laughter; from others, a scold’s endless harangue, the clatter of dice, a cradle song, and a muffled weeping; overhead, a banjo twanged with energy. Doors slammed somewhere in the distance; elevated trains thundered at intervals; a cat wailed wretchedly on a back fence. And he inhaled the house’s exhalation—a damp essence more than a smell—a chill, stale vapor as from subterranean crypts blended with the pungent emissions of linoleum and decaying, moldy timber.
source_text: The young tenant in the chair allowed these thoughts to file, soft-shod, through his mind, while there drifted into the room furnished sounds and furnished scents. He heard in one room a tittering and incontinent, slack laughter; in others the monologue of a scold, the rattling of dice, a lullaby, and one crying dully; above him a banjo tinkled with spirit. Doors banged somewhere; the elevated trains roared intermittently; a cat yowled miserably upon a back fence. And he breathed the breath of the house—a dank savour rather than a smell—a cold, musty effluvium as from underground vaults mingled with the reeking exhalations of linoleum and mildewed and rotten woodwork.

text: The tempo of commerce surged into something ever more cutthroat and rapid. Out on the trading floor, half a dozen securities in which Maxwell’s clients held heavy stakes were being hammered. Buy and sell orders darted back and forth like swallows slicing the air. Some of his personal positions were at risk, and he drove himself like a high-tension, finely calibrated engine—strung taut, racing at full throttle, precise, never faltering, every word and judgment and motion clicking into place with clockwork certainty. Stocks and bonds, loans and mortgages, margins and securities—this was the realm of finance, and it left no crevice for the human world or the world of nature.
source_text: The rush and pace of business grew fiercer and faster. On the floor they were pounding half a dozen stocks in which Maxwell’s customers were heavy investors. Orders to buy and sell were coming and going as swift as the flight of swallows. Some of his own holdings were imperilled, and the man was working like some high-geared, delicate, strong machine—strung to full tension, going at full speed, accurate, never hesitating, with the proper word and decision and act ready and prompt as clockwork. Stocks and bonds, loans and mortgages, margins and securities—here was a world of finance, and there was no room in it for the human world or the world of nature.

text: The young wanderer had an agreeable look about him, and he carried himself with a quiet, observant grace. During business hours he sold pianos in a downtown shop. He threaded his necktie through a topaz ring rather than securing it with a pin, and he had once sent a letter to a magazine editor confessing that Miss Libbey’s “Junie’s Love Test” was the book that had most shaped his life.
source_text: The young adventurer was of pleasing presence, and moved serenely and watchfully. By daylight he was a salesman in a piano store. He wore his tie drawn through a topaz ring instead of fastened with a stick pin; and once he had written to the editor of a magazine that “Junie’s Love Test” by Miss Libbey, had been the book that had most influenced his life.

text: Capitalise it, my typographic friend—that final word—that word supreme in the revelation of life and passion. The perfume of the blossoms, the plunder of the bee, the first murmur of vernal streams, the prelude of the thrush, the curl of orange rind upon the libation of existence—such is the bride. Sacred is the wife; honoured the mother; exquisite is the summer maiden—but the bride is the sealed bond among the nuptial gifts the heavens deliver when man weds his mortal fate.
source_text: Capitalise it, friend typo—that last word—word of words in the epiphany of life and love. The scent of the flowers, the booty of the bee, the primal drip of spring waters, the overture of the lark, the twist of lemon peel on the cocktail of creation—such is the bride. Holy is the wife; revered the mother; galliptious is the summer girl—but the bride is the certified check among the wedding presents that the gods send in when man is married to mortality.

text: “I stepped into the Home Restaurant adjoining the building tonight,” he remarked. “Let the world hear me say it—I relish a plate of fresh greens when the season turns just so. I scanned that neatly printed menu from top to bottom, hunting for anything of the sort. The moment I passed the cabbage listing, I knocked my chair aside and shouted for the owner. He gave me your address.”
source_text: “I dropped into that Home Restaurant next door this evening,” said he. “I don’t care who knows it; I like a dish of some kind of greens at this time of the year. I ran my eye down that nice typewritten bill of fare looking for something in that line. When I got below cabbage I turned my chair over and hollered for the proprietor. He told me where you lived.”

text: “This is the room,” the housekeeper announced, her voice burred deep in her throat. “A lovely room. Doesn’t come free often. Last summer I had some truly refined guests—no trouble whatsoever, and their payment arrived right on the dot. The water closet’s down the hall. Sprowls and Mooney had it for three months. They performed a vaudeville act. Miss B’retta Sprowls—the name might ring a bell—well, those were just their stage names—right there above the dresser is where the marriage certificate used to hang, all framed. The gas jet’s here, and you can see there’s ample closet space. It’s a room that charms everyone. It never stays empty long.”
source_text: “This is the room,” said the housekeeper, from her furry throat. “It’s a nice room. It ain’t often vacant. I had some most elegant people in it last summer—no trouble at all, and paid in advance to the minute. The water’s at the end of the hall. Sprowls and Mooney kept it three months. They done a vaudeville sketch. Miss B’retta Sprowls—you may have heard of her—Oh, that was just the stage names—right there over the dresser is where the marriage certificate hung, framed. The gas is here, and you see there is plenty of closet room. It’s a room everybody likes. It never stays idle long.”

text: My journalist companion departed, and I drifted farther out into the city. By now, the 3,126 electric bulbs along the Rialto had begun to glow. Crowds streamed past, but none detained my attention. Siren glances fell upon me and left me unmoved. Diners, homeward-bound workers, shop assistants, swindlers, beggars, actors, bandits, magnates, and strangers rushed, ambled, slouched, crept, strutted, and darted by; I registered none of them. I had known them all already; I had deciphered their souls; they had played their parts. I was searching for my Man About Town. He was a distinct figure, and to abandon him would be a misstep—a typographical error—but no! let us press on.
source_text: My friend the reporter left me, and I wandered further afield. By this time the 3126 electric lights on the Rialto were alight. People passed, but they held me not. Paphian eyes rayed upon me, and left me unscathed. Diners, heimgangers, shop-girls, confidence men, panhandlers, actors, highwaymen, millionaires and outlanders hurried, skipped, strolled, sneaked, swaggered and scurried by me; but I took no note of them. I knew them all; I had read their hearts; they had served. I wanted my Man About Town. He was a type, and to drop him would be an error—a typograph—but no! let us continue.

text: “Dearest Lady Marian,” he cried—“labour! Picture yourself attiring for supper each evening, paying a round of visits through the afternoon—while at every crossing a constable lurks, poised to leap into your carriage and haul you to the magistrate should you dare exceed the pace of a milk-horse. We idle folk are the most tireless toilers in the kingdom.”
source_text: “My dear Miss Marian,” he exclaimed—“work! Think of dressing every day for dinner, of making half a dozen calls in an afternoon—with a policeman at every corner ready to jump into your auto and take you to the station, if you get up any greater speed than a donkey cart’s gait. We do-nothings are the hardest workers in the land.”

text: A single word suffices for Porcus. When the children christened him so, a shame unearned settled upon the venerable lineage of hogs. The primer of three-letter terms in the faded azure spelling book opens with the life story of Porcus. He was stout; he possessed the spirit of a weasel, the customs of a moth, and the generosity of a lizard . . . He dressed in costly garments, and was an epicure of emptiness. He could observe a counter-girl and reckon to the minute when she had last swallowed anything more substantial than fondant and weak broth. He loitered through the mercantile quarters, and drifted among the emporiums bearing his bids to supper. Gentlemen who walk hounds along the pavement by a tether regard him with scorn. He stands as a category; I can linger on him no further; my quill is not fashioned for his likeness; I am no joiner.
source_text: Piggy needs but a word. When the girls named him, an undeserving stigma was cast upon the noble family of swine. The words-of-three-letters lesson in the old blue spelling book begins with Piggy’s biography. He was fat; he had the soul of a rat, the habits of a bat, and the magnanimity of a cat . . . He wore expensive clothes; and was a connoisseur in starvation. He could look at a shop-girl and tell you to an hour how long it had been since she had eaten anything more nourishing than marshmallows and tea. He hung about the shopping districts, and prowled around in department stores with his invitations to dinner. Men who escort dogs upon the streets at the end of a string look down upon him. He is a type; I can dwell upon him no longer; my pen is not the kind intended for him; I am no carpenter.

text: In my eyes, the finest part was the domestic rhythm of that small apartment—the eager, flowing conversations once the day’s work was done; the snug suppers and bright, simple breakfasts; the sharing of dreams, each one braided into the other’s or else too slight to matter alone; the steady give-and-take of encouragement; and—forgive my guilelessness—midnight bites of cheese sandwiches and stuffed olives.
source_text: But the best, in my opinion, was the home life in the little flat—the ardent, voluble chats after the day’s study; the cozy dinners and fresh, light breakfasts; the interchange of ambitions—ambitions interwoven each with the other’s or else inconsiderable—the mutual help and inspiration; and—overlook my artlessness—stuffed olives and cheese sandwiches at 11 p.m.

text: Anthony Rockwall—former industrialist and owner of Rockwall’s Eureka Soap—stood at the library window of his Fifth Avenue residence and smiled. Next door, the patrician clubman G. Van Schuylight Suffolk-Jones emerged and approached his chauffeur-driven car, curling a disdainful nostril, as he always did, toward the Italian Renaissance façade of the soap magnate’s mansion.
source_text: Old Anthony Rockwall, retired manufacturer and proprietor of Rockwall’s Eureka Soap, looked out the library window of his Fifth Avenue mansion and grinned. His neighbour to the right—the aristocratic clubman, G. Van Schuylight Suffolk-Jones—came out to his waiting motor-car, wrinkling a contumelious nostril, as usual, at the Italian renaissance sculpture of the soap palace’s front elevation.

text: For years, the welcoming Blackwell’s had been his winter refuge. Just as his more prosperous fellow New Yorkers purchased tickets to Palm Beach and the Riviera each season, Soapy had made his modest preparations for his annual migration to the Island. And now the moment had arrived. The previous night, three Sabbath newspapers tucked beneath his coat, wrapped around his ankles, and spread across his lap had not kept the cold at bay as he slept on his bench near the splashing fountain in the old square. So the Island rose large and opportune in Soapy’s thoughts. He disdained the provisions offered in charity’s name for the city’s needy. In Soapy’s view, the Law was kinder than Philanthropy. There was a vast circuit of institutions, both municipal and charitable, where he might present himself and receive food and shelter suited to a simple life. But to a spirit as proud as Soapy’s, the gifts of charity came with burdens. If not in coin, you paid in spiritual humiliation for every kindness received from the hands of philanthropy. As Caesar had his Brutus, every charitable bed exacted its tribute of a bath, every loaf of bread its cost of a personal and private interrogation. Therefore, it was better to be a guest of the law, which, though governed by rules, did not intrude excessively upon a gentleman’s private affairs.
source_text: For years the hospitable Blackwell’s had been his winter quarters. Just as his more fortunate fellow New Yorkers had bought their tickets to Palm Beach and the Riviera each winter, so Soapy had made his humble arrangements for his annual hegira to the Island. And now the time was come. On the previous night three Sabbath newspapers, distributed beneath his coat, about his ankles and over his lap, had failed to repulse the cold as he slept on his bench near the spurting fountain in the ancient square. So the Island loomed big and timely in Soapy’s mind. He scorned the provisions made in the name of charity for the city’s dependents. In Soapy’s opinion the Law was more benign than Philanthropy. There was an endless round of institutions, municipal and eleemosynary, on which he might set out and receive lodging and food accordant with the simple life. But to one of Soapy’s proud spirit the gifts of charity are encumbered. If not in coin you must pay in humiliation of spirit for every benefit received at the hands of philanthropy. As Caesar had his Brutus, every bed of charity must have its toll of a bath, every loaf of bread its compensation of a private and personal inquisition. Wherefore it is better to be a guest of the law, which though conducted by rules, does not meddle unduly with a gentleman’s private affairs.

text: Bogle’s stands on Eighth Avenue, that bustling thoroughfare of the middle class, the promenade of the ordinary Joneses, Browns, and Robinsons. The dining room holds twelve tables, arranged in two neat rows of six. Each table bears a caster filled with bottles of seasoning and condiments. From the pepper shaker you can coax only a sad, flavorless puff, fine as volcanic ash. From the salt cellar you can hope for nothing at all. A man might draw a bloody stream from a pale turnip, yet all his strength will fail when he tries to force salt from Bogle’s casters. And on every table sits an imitation of that kindly sauce supposedly crafted “from the recipe of a nobleman in India.”
source_text: Bogle’s is situated in that highway of _bourgeoisie_, that boulevard of Brown-Jones-and-Robinson, Eighth Avenue. There are two rows of tables in the room, six in each row. On each table is a caster-stand, containing cruets of condiments and seasons. From the pepper cruet you may shake a cloud of something tasteless and melancholy, like volcanic dust. From the salt cruet you may expect nothing. Though a man should extract a sanguinary stream from the pallid turnip, yet will his prowess be balked when he comes to wrest salt from Bogle’s cruets. Also upon each table stands the counterfeit of that benign sauce made “from the recipe of a nobleman in India.”

text: “By my soul!” cried the newcomer, seizing his companion’s hands in both of his. “It’s Jack, as I live and breathe. I knew I’d meet you here if you were still above ground. Ah, dear, dear!—twenty years does stretch a man’s memory. The old chophouse is vanished, Jack; I lament its passing, for we might have shared another meal within its walls. What fortune has the Orient dealt you, my old friend?”
source_text: “Bless my heart!” exclaimed the new arrival, grasping both the other’s hands with his own. “It’s Bob, sure as fate. I was certain I’d find you here if you were still in existence. Well, well, well!—twenty years is a long time. The old restaurant’s gone, Bob; I wish it had lasted, so we could have had another dinner there. How has the West treated you, old man?”

text: Having resolved to make his way to the Retreat, Soapy began without delay to put his plan into motion. The paths to his goal were plentiful and simple. The most agreeable, he thought, would be to feast sumptuously at a costly establishment, and afterward, upon confessing an empty purse, be delivered calmly and without fuss into the custody of an officer. A compliant judge would handle all that followed.
source_text: Soapy, having decided to go to the Island, at once set about accomplishing his desire. There were many easy ways of doing this. The pleasantest was to dine luxuriously at some expensive restaurant; and then, after declaring insolvency, be handed over quietly and without uproar to a policeman. An accommodating magistrate would do the rest.

text: It took me a fortnight to discover what women stow in their dress suitcases. After that, I started wondering why a mattress is manufactured in two separate sections. At first, people met my earnest question with distrust, as though I were posing a riddle. Eventually, I was told the dual construction was meant to ease the labor of the woman who makes the beds. Foolishly, I pressed further, asking why the halves weren’t made equal in size—and from that moment, I was avoided.
source_text: It took me two weeks to find out what women carry in dress suit cases. And then I began to ask why a mattress is made in two pieces. This serious query was at first received with suspicion because it sounded like a conundrum. I was at last assured that its double form of construction was designed to make lighter the burden of woman, who makes up beds. I was so foolish as to persist, begging to know why, then, they were not made in two equal pieces; whereupon I was shunned.

text: Jim snapped abruptly from his reverie and wrapped Della in his arms. For ten seconds, let us discreetly turn our attention to some trifling object elsewhere. Whether one earns eight dollars weekly or a million annually—what does it matter? A mathematician or a jester would offer you an incorrect reply. The wise men carried precious offerings, yet that particular truth was not one of them. This cryptic remark will be made clear in due course.
source_text: Out of his trance Jim seemed quickly to wake. He enfolded his Della. For ten seconds let us regard with discreet scrutiny some inconsequential object in the other direction. Eight dollars a week or a million a year—what is the difference? A mathematician or a wit would give you the wrong answer. The magi brought valuable gifts, but that was not among them. This dark assertion will be illuminated later on.

text: “Timepieces,” declared the Duke, “are fetters clamped round the ankles of humanity. I have watched you glancing repeatedly at that chronometer. Its visage is a despot’s, its numerals as deceptive as the figures on a gaming slip; its pointers are those of a confidence trickster, who arranges a meeting destined to undo you. Permit me to implore you to cast aside its degrading restraints and to stop governing your life by that unfeeling sentinel of metal and alloy.”
source_text: “Clocks,” said the Prince, “are shackles on the feet of mankind. I have observed you looking persistently at that clock. Its face is that of a tyrant, its numbers are false as those on a lottery ticket; its hands are those of a bunco steerer, who makes an appointment with you to your ruin. Let me entreat you to throw off its humiliating bonds and to cease to order your affairs by that insensate monitor of brass and steel.”

text: “That’s my error,” he says. “Why would I imagine something so splendid and extraordinary would simply appear around the bend? I almost proved myself undeserving. Nearby,” he continues, “there’s a cozy little café, just right for indulging eccentricities. Let’s step in, have a drink, and talk over the elusiveness of absolutes.”
source_text: “’Tis my mistake,” says he. “How could I be expecting anything so fine and wonderful to be turning the corner upon me? I came near being found unworthy. Hard by,” says he, “is a café, snug and suitable for the entertainment of idiosyncrasies. Let us go there and have drink while we discuss the unavailability of the categorical.”

text: “Miss Tildy,” he began, “I’ve come to beg your pardon for my behavior t’other night. Truth is, I’d had more than a skinful, else I’d never have acted so. No decent man would treat a lady in such a fashion with his wits about him. So I trust, Miss Tildy, you’ll take my apology to heart, and rest assured I’d not have done it had I been in my right mind and not deep in my cups.”
source_text: “Miss Tildy,” said he, “I want to apologise for what I done the other evenin’. Tell you the truth, I was pretty well tanked up or I wouldn’t of done it. I wouldn’t do no lady that a-way when I was sober. So I hope, Miss Tildy, you’ll accept my ’pology, and believe that I wouldn’t of done it if I’d known what I was doin’ and hadn’t of been drunk.”

text: Mr. and Mrs. Larrabee set up their home in an apartment. It was a solitary little apartment—rather like the note A flat, situated far down at the bass end of the keyboard. Yet they were content, for they possessed their Art, and they possessed one another. My counsel to any wealthy young gentleman would be this: part with all your goods, bestow them upon the poor—janitor, and earn the right to dwell in an apartment alongside your Art and your Delia.
source_text: Mr. and Mrs. Larrabee began housekeeping in a flat. It was a lonesome flat—something like the A sharp way down at the left-hand end of the keyboard. And they were happy; for they had their Art, and they had each other. And my advice to the rich young man would be—sell all thou hast, and give it to the poor—janitor for the privilege of living in a flat with your Art and your Delia.

text: But presently, Craft faltered. It often will, even when no signalman halts it. All outflow and no intake, as the common tongue phrases it. Funds were wanting to meet the fees of Signor Maestro and Herr Goldbloom. When one adores one’s Craft no labor feels too steep. Thus, Delia resolved she must teach the keyboard to keep the kettle simmering.
source_text: But after a while Art flagged. It sometimes does, even if some switchman doesn’t flag it. Everything going out and nothing coming in, as the vulgarians say. Money was lacking to pay Mr. Magister and Herr Rosenstock their prices. When one loves one’s Art no service seems too hard. So, Delia said she must give music lessons to keep the chafing dish bubbling.

text: Yet on a strangely silent bend of the street, Jasper halted. There stood an ancient chapel, crooked and sprawling, its roof peaked with spires. Behind a pane dyed the color of crushed plums, a gentle radiance flickered, where surely the musician lingered over the manuals, testing his command of the next day’s hymn. For a melody drifted out to Jasper’s hearing, so dulcet that it seized him and pinned him motionless against the twists of the wrought-iron railing.
source_text: But on an unusually quiet corner Soapy came to a standstill. Here was an old church, quaint and rambling and gabled. Through one violet-stained window a soft light glowed, where, no doubt, the organist loitered over the keys, making sure of his mastery of the coming Sabbath anthem. For there drifted out to Soapy’s ears sweet music that caught and held him transfixed against the convolutions of the iron fence.

text: The young man pressed a fifty-cent piece into the boy’s palm. The boy regarded him briefly, his shrewd, shining gaze peering from a grimy, sharp-featured face, then bolted away at a sprint. He neared the woman on the bench with a trace of hesitation, yet entirely at ease. He lifted the edge of the faded tartan cycling cap slouched at the back of his head. The woman studied him evenly, offering neither warmth nor disdain.
source_text: The young man dropped a half-dollar into the boy’s hand. The boy looked at him for a moment with bright, canny eyes out of a dirty, intelligent face, and then set off at a run. He approached the lady on the bench a little doubtfully, but unembarrassed. He touched the brim of the old plaid bicycle cap perched on the back of his head. The lady looked at him coolly, without prejudice or favour.

text: “Not a single one,” E. R. Coglan broke in, breezily. “This lump of matter, a roundish planetary globe squashed a bit at the poles and called Earth—that’s where I live. I’ve run into plenty of folks from this country who can’t see past their own backyard when they’re overseas. I once watched men from Chicago, drifting in a Venetian gondola under the moon, boasting about their drainage canal. I saw a Southerner, when presented to the King of England, inform the monarch without so much as a blink that his maternal great-aunt was connected by marriage to the Perkins family of Charleston. I knew a New Yorker who got snatched for ransom by bandits in Afghanistan. His family wired the cash, and he turned up in Kabul with the agent. ‘Afghanistan?’ the locals asked him through a translator. ‘Well, not too shabby, eh?’ ‘Oh, I don’t know,’ he replied, and launched into a story about a cabbie at Sixth Avenue and Broadway. That mindset isn’t for me. I’m not chained to anything smaller than eight thousand miles across. Just list me as E. Rushmore Coglan, citizen of the earthly sphere.”
source_text: “Nary a spot,” interrupted E. R. Coglan, flippantly. “The terrestrial, globular, planetary hunk of matter, slightly flattened at the poles, and known as the Earth, is my abode. I’ve met a good many object-bound citizens of this country abroad. I’ve seen men from Chicago sit in a gondola in Venice on a moonlight night and brag about their drainage canal. I’ve seen a Southerner on being introduced to the King of England hand that monarch, without batting his eyes, the information that his grand-aunt on his mother’s side was related by marriage to the Perkinses, of Charleston. I knew a New Yorker who was kidnapped for ransom by some Afghanistan bandits. His people sent over the money and he came back to Kabul with the agent. ‘Afghanistan?’ the natives said to him through an interpreter. ‘Well, not so slow, do you think?’ ‘Oh, I don’t know,’ says he, and he begins to tell them about a cab driver at Sixth avenue and Broadway. Those ideas don’t suit me. I’m not tied down to anything that isn’t 8,000 miles in diameter. Just put me down as E. Rushmore Coglan, citizen of the terrestrial sphere.”

text: “Why the long face, you tar-soaked crab? She isn’t showering you with kisses. You don’t have to perch in her lap and endure chatter that would turn a musical comedy’s script into the wisdom of Epictetus by comparison. You should count your blessings you’re not a dog. Stiffen your spine, Benedick, and send those blues packing.”
source_text: “What are you looking so sour about, you oakum trimmed lobster? She don’t kiss you. You don’t have to sit on her lap and listen to talk that would make the book of a musical comedy sound like the maxims of Epictetus. You ought to be thankful you’re not a dog. Brace up, Benedick, and bid the blues begone.”

text: Della stopped weeping and dabbed at her cheeks with a powder puff. She lingered by the window, gazing absently at a gray cat padding along a gray fence in a gray yard. Christmas was tomorrow, and all she had to buy Jim a gift was a dollar and eighty-seven cents. For months she’d scraped together every cent she could, and this was the outcome. Twenty dollars a week doesn’t stretch far. The costs had run higher than she’d figured. They always do. Just one dollar and eighty-seven cents to get Jim a present. Her Jim. She’d passed countless joyful hours dreaming up something lovely for him. Something exquisite and uncommon and genuine—something that came at least a little close to meriting the privilege of belonging to Jim.
source_text: Della finished her cry and attended to her cheeks with the powder rag. She stood by the window and looked out dully at a grey cat walking a grey fence in a grey backyard. Tomorrow would be Christmas Day, and she had only $1.87 with which to buy Jim a present. She had been saving every penny she could for months, with this result. Twenty dollars a week doesn’t go far. Expenses had been greater than she had calculated. They always are. Only $1.87 to buy a present for Jim. Her Jim. Many a happy hour she had spent planning for something nice for him. Something fine and rare and sterling—something just a little bit near to being worthy of the honour of being owned by Jim.

text: You won’t find any pretentious prose in my account, the sort where Berold, the bear, and Serpina, the snake, and Tigram, the tiger, hold lofty conversations in jungle tales. A tawny cur who has passed the bulk of his days in a shabby tenement in Brooklyn, dozing in a nook upon a faded satin petticoat (the very one stained with claret at the Gentlewoman Stevedores’ gala), cannot be counted on to execute any flourishes with the craft of language.
source_text: But you needn’t look for any stuck-up literature in my piece, such as Bearoo, the bear, and Snakoo, the snake, and Tammanoo, the tiger, talk in the jungle books. A yellow dog that’s spent most of his life in a cheap New York flat, sleeping in a corner on an old sateen underskirt (the one she spilled port wine on at the Lady Longshoremen’s banquet), mustn’t be expected to perform any tricks with the art of speech.

text: The following week, the Larrabees took their breakfast at dawn each day. Joe was fired up about a series of early-morning light studies he was painting in Central Park, and Delia sent him out the door by seven—fed, fussed over, flattered, and kissed. Art is a mistress who commands devotion. Most evenings, he didn’t get back until seven o’clock.
source_text: During all of the next week the Larrabees had an early breakfast. Joe was enthusiastic about some morning-effect sketches he was doing in Central Park, and Delia packed him off breakfasted, coddled, praised and kissed at 7 o’clock. Art is an engaging mistress. It was most times 7 o’clock when he returned in the evening.

text: Soapy resolved to play the part of the loathsome, reviled street-corner lothario. His intended target’s polished, graceful bearing, together with the nearness of that dutiful policeman, heartened him: any moment now, he felt sure, he would savor the satisfying official grip on his sleeve that promised a snug winter berth on that trim, secluded little island.
source_text: It was Soapy’s design to assume the role of the despicable and execrated “masher.” The refined and elegant appearance of his victim and the contiguity of the conscientious cop encouraged him to believe that he would soon feel the pleasant official clutch upon his arm that would insure his winter quarters on the right little, tight little isle.

text: The officer’s thoughts couldn’t register Soapy as a suspect. People who break windows don’t hang around to chat with the enforcers of the law. They bolt. The policeman spotted a figure halfway along the street, sprinting after a streetcar. Baton out, he charged into the chase. Soapy, his heart sour with disgust, ambled on, having failed twice.
source_text: The policeman’s mind refused to accept Soapy even as a clue. Men who smash windows do not remain to parley with the law’s minions. They take to their heels. The policeman saw a man half way down the block running to catch a car. With drawn club he joined in the pursuit. Soapy, with disgust in his heart, loafed along, twice unsuccessful.

text: “A leftover from the era of flint and cave,” announced Marcheson, with fervor. “Every soul is kin—the Bengali, the Scot, the Maasai, the folk of Tierra del Fuego, and those who dwell where the Missouri curls. The hour will arrive when this trifling vanity over one’s town or province or region or nation shall be erased, and we shall stand as children of the whole earth, as it was always meant to be.”
source_text: “A relic of the stone age,” declared Coglan, warmly. “We are all brothers—Chinamen, Englishmen, Zulus, Patagonians and the people in the bend of the Kaw River. Some day all this petty pride in one’s city or State or section or country will be wiped out, and we’ll all be citizens of the world, as we ought to be.”

text: “It’s John McCaskey and his wife going at each other once more,” the officer reflected. “Should I head up there and put an end to the racket? No, I won’t. They’re a married couple, and they don’t get many enjoyments. It’ll be over soon enough. Anyway, they’d need to scrounge more plates to keep this up.”
source_text: “’Tis Jawn McCaskey and his missis at it again,” meditated the policeman. “I wonder shall I go up and stop the row. I will not. Married folks they are; and few pleasures they have. ’Twill not last long. Sure, they’ll have to borrow more dishes to keep it up with.”

text: “I believe I know the ones you speak of,” she replied, a soft smile touching her lips. “We encounter them in the same haunts evening after evening. They are the devil’s own sentinels, and if any army’s soldiers were half as loyal, their generals would be fortunate indeed. We move among them, coaxing a handful of pennies from their vice into the service of the Lord.”
source_text: “I think I know whom you mean,” she answered, with a gentle smile. “We see them in the same places night after night. They are the devil’s body guard, and if the soldiers of any army are as faithful as they are, their commanders are well served. We go among them, diverting a few pennies from their wickedness to the Lord’s service.”

text: Then he crossed the room like a hound pursuing a scent, skirting the walls, dropping to hands and knees to examine the corners of the swollen matting, searching the mantel and tables, the drapes and curtains, the tilted cabinet in the corner, for any visible token—yet he could not grasp that she was there beside him, around him, pressed against him, within him, above him, clinging and beckoning, calling him so piercingly through the finer senses that even his coarser ones grew aware of the summons. Once more he answered aloud, “Yes, dear!” and wheeled about, wild-eyed, staring into emptiness, for he still could not discern form and colour and love and reaching arms in the scent of mignonette. Oh, God—where did that scent come from, and since when do scents have a voice to call? So he fumbled on.
source_text: And then he traversed the room like a hound on the scent, skimming the walls, considering the corners of the bulging matting on his hands and knees, rummaging mantel and tables, the curtains and hangings, the drunken cabinet in the corner, for a visible sign, unable to perceive that she was there beside, around, against, within, above him, clinging to him, wooing him, calling him so poignantly through the finer senses that even his grosser ones became cognisant of the call. Once again he answered loudly: “Yes, dear!” and turned, wild-eyed, to gaze on vacancy, for he could not yet discern form and colour and love and outstretched arms in the odour of mignonette. Oh, God! whence that odour, and since when have odours had a voice to call? Thus he groped.

text: We sat around our evening meal of mutton broth and withered apricots when he strode through the doorway like a man chasing a sled pack, and settled himself among the clutter of our board. Armed with the rough liberty of trail life, he stormed our silence and demanded the brotherhood of souls adrift in a greasy cook tent. We took him up as a curiosity, and within moments had nearly perished of devotion, each for the other, like sworn companions.
source_text: We were at our supper of beef stew and dried apples when he trotted in as if on the heels of a dog team, and made one of the mess at our table. With the freedom of the camps he assaulted our ears and claimed the fellowship of men lost in the wilds of a hash house. We embraced him as a specimen, and in three minutes we had all but died for one another as friends.

text: The evening streets brimmed with the surging tide of rush-hour crowds. Broadway’s electric lights blazed, summoning moths from miles, from leagues, from hundreds of leagues out of the surrounding dark to flutter into that scorching academy. Men in immaculate suits, their features etched like the miniature carvings on cherry stones done by retired sailors in seamen’s homes, turned to gaze at Dulcie as she hurried past, oblivious. Manhattan, that night-blooming cereus, was just beginning to unfurl its corpselike-white, heavily scented petals.
source_text: The streets were filled with the rush-hour floods of people. The electric lights of Broadway were glowing—calling moths from miles, from leagues, from hundreds of leagues out of darkness around to come in and attend the singeing school. Men in accurate clothes, with faces like those carved on cherry stones by the old salts in sailors’ homes, turned and stared at Dulcie as she sped, unheeding, past them. Manhattan, the night-blooming cereus, was beginning to unfold its dead-white, heavy-odoured petals.

text: “This is a friend of mine, Mr. Terry O’Sullivan,” Maggie would say as she introduced him. She guided him through the room, making introductions to every Clover Leaf who came through the door. In those moments she was nearly beautiful, her eyes lit with that special glow a girl gets with her first admirer, much like a kitten with its first catch.
source_text: “A friend of mine, Mr. Terry O’Sullivan,” was Maggie’s formula of introduction. She led him around the room, presenting him to each new-arriving Clover Leaf. Almost was she pretty now, with the unique luminosity in her eyes that comes to a girl with her first suitor and a kitten with its first mouse.

text: Three years back, Kraft, Bill Judkins—who wrote verse—and I used to eat at Cypher’s place on Eighth Avenue. I say “used to.” Whenever we had cash, Cypher, as he put it, “took it off of us.” We carried no tab; we simply walked in, ordered, and ate. Sometimes we settled up, sometimes we didn’t. We relied on Cypher’s brooding, pent-up rage. In the lightless depths of his spirit, he was either a nobleman, an idiot, or an artist. He sat behind a worm-gnawed desk buried under stacks of waiters’ checks so ancient I felt certain the bottommost one was for shellfish Hendrik Hudson himself had consumed and paid for. Like Napoleon III and the pop-eyed perch, Cypher could draw a veil across his eyes, clouding the windows of his soul. Once, when we’d walked out without paying, trailing flimsy excuses, I glanced back and saw him quaking with silent laughter behind that veil. Every so often, we cleared our old debts.
source_text: Three years ago Kraft, Bill Judkins (a poet), and I took our meals at Cypher’s, on Eighth Avenue. I say “took.” When we had money, Cypher got it “off of” us, as he expressed it. We had no credit; we went in, called for food and ate it. We paid or we did not pay. We had confidence in Cypher’s sullenness and smouldering ferocity. Deep down in his sunless soul he was either a prince, a fool or an artist. He sat at a worm-eaten desk, covered with files of waiters’ checks so old that I was sure the bottomest one was for clams that Hendrik Hudson had eaten and paid for. Cypher had the power, in common with Napoleon III. and the goggle-eyed perch, of throwing a film over his eyes, rendering opaque the windows of his soul. Once when we left him unpaid, with egregious excuses, I looked back and saw him shaking with inaudible laughter behind his film. Now and then we paid up back scores.

text: “Woman!” Mr. McCaskey exclaimed, flinging his coat and hat onto a chair, “your racket is a slap in the face to my hunger. When you trample courtesy, you’re yanking the cement right out of society’s bedrock. It’s nothing but a gentleman’s sharpness to request that ladies who block the path step aside. Will you haul your ugly mug from that window and tend to the meal?”
source_text: “Woman!” said Mr. McCaskey, dashing his coat and hat upon a chair, “the noise of ye is an insult to me appetite. When ye run down politeness ye take the mortar from between the bricks of the foundations of society. ’Tis no more than exercisin’ the acrimony of a gentleman when ye ask the dissent of ladies blockin’ the way for steppin’ between them. Will ye bring the pig’s face of ye out of the windy and see to the food?”

text: Arthur Pennington was one of the steady, unhurried sort. He threaded deliberately through the clustered riders toward the front stairwell of the tram. His wife came after, yet paused to glance back, catching sight of the fugitive traveler steal out from the shadow of the moving truck and vanish behind an oak at the border of the small green, scarcely a stone’s throw distant.
source_text: James Williams belonged among the level heads. With necessary slowness he picked his way through the passengers down to the steps at the front of the car. His wife followed, but she first turned her eyes and saw the escaped tourist glide from behind the furniture van and slip behind a tree on the edge of the little park, not fifty feet away.

text: Chandler stretched his stroll until the Forties met the broad, shimmering avenue lit like pale gold, for the evening was still fresh, and when one belongs to the fashionable world just one day in seventy, one likes to draw out the delight. Bright, sly, inquisitive, admiring, challenging, enticing eyes turned his way, since his attire and manner marked him as a worshipper of the hour of ease and enjoyment.
source_text: Chandler protracted his walk until the Forties began to intersect the great and glittering primrose way, for the evening was yet young, and when one is of the _beau monde_ only one day in seventy, one loves to protract the pleasure. Eyes bright, sinister, curious, admiring, provocative, alluring were bent upon him, for his garb and air proclaimed him a devotee to the hour of solace and pleasure.

text: Then from the shadows emerged Lorna, balancing a mountain of porcelain on her naked forearm—she rose immense and pale and flushed and terrifying as the cliffs of Dover—bearing a grin like sunrise splitting a canyon. And the prospector cast aside his furs and gold as worthless slag, let his mouth hang agape, and gazed at her. One could nearly glimpse the ruby coronets upon Lorna’s temples and the hand-stitched velvet Venetian gowns he resolved to lavish on her.
source_text: And then Milly loomed up with a thousand dishes on her bare arm—loomed up big and white and pink and awful as Mount Saint Elias—with a smile like day breaking in a gulch. And the Klondiker threw down his pelts and nuggets as dross, and let his jaw fall half-way, and stared at her. You could almost see the diamond tiaras on Milly’s brow and the hand-embroidered silk Paris gowns that he meant to buy for her.

text: Some fifty paces ahead, he halted abruptly and lowered himself onto a bench by the path. The lad set down the valise and regarded him with a curious, knowing gaze. The gentleman drew a kerchief from his pocket and dabbed his forehead. It was a fine kerchief, a fine forehead, and the gentleman was fine to behold. He addressed the boy:
source_text: Some fifty yards further on he suddenly stopped and sat on a bench at one side. The boy dropped the suit-case and stared at him with wondering, shrewd eyes. The young man took out his handkerchief and wiped his brow. It was a good handkerchief, a good brow, and the young man was good to look at. He said to the boy:

text: The seconds that slipped by before the door swung open held the electric pulse of genuine adventure. Who could guess what waited beyond those green panels? Perhaps gamblers bent over their game, sly tricksters setting snares with deft cunning, a beauty enamored of bravery and scheming to draw it near—peril, demise, passion, letdown, mockery—any of these might answer that reckless knock.
source_text: Moments like those that passed before his knock was answered measure the quick breath of true adventure. What might not be behind those green panels! Gamesters at play; cunning rogues baiting their traps with subtle skill; beauty in love with courage, and thus planning to be sought by it; danger, death, love, disappointment, ridicule—any of these might respond to that temerarious rap.

text: “Come now, don’t talk like that,” old Anthony replied, taken aback. “I back cash against cash every chance I get. I’ve combed the encyclopedia clear through to the letter Y hunting for a thing it can’t purchase, and I reckon I’ll need to tackle the appendix come next week. I’ll take money over the whole lot. Name me one item it can’t acquire.”
source_text: “Now, don’t say that,” said old Anthony, shocked. “I bet my money on money every time. I’ve been through the encyclopaedia down to Y looking for something you can’t buy with it; and I expect to have to take up the appendix next week. I’m for money against the field. Tell me something money won’t buy.”

text: The park lay quiet, empty of its usual visitors at this time of year and day. The young woman, who had settled onto a bench along the path, had probably just given in to a spontaneous urge to pause and breathe in the first faint promise of approaching spring.
source_text: It was neither the season nor the hour when the Park had frequenters; and it is likely that the young lady, who was seated on one of the benches at the side of the walk, had merely obeyed a sudden impulse to sit for a while and enjoy a foretaste of coming Spring.

text: But McCaskey was no bargain-basement boarder. Let those thriftless drifters of Bohemia deem the meal complete with coffee, if they wished. Let them commit that blunder. He was shrewder by far. Finger-bowls lay well within the bounds of his sophistication. They were absent from the Pension Murphy, yet their substitute was close by. In victory he launched the enamel washbasin toward the head of his domestic foe. Mrs. McCaskey sidestepped just in time. She snatched a pressing iron, with which, as a kind of bracer, she meant to conclude the culinary skirmish. But a piercing, lamenting cry from below caused both her and Mr. McCaskey to halt in a sort of unwitting truce.
source_text: But Mr. McCaskey was no 50-cent _table d’hôter_. Let cheap Bohemians consider coffee the end, if they would. Let them make that _faux pas_. He was foxier still. Finger-bowls were not beyond the compass of his experience. They were not to be had in the Pension Murphy; but their equivalent was at hand. Triumphantly he sent the granite-ware wash basin at the head of his matrimonial adversary. Mrs. McCaskey dodged in time. She reached for a flatiron, with which, as a sort of cordial, she hoped to bring the gastronomical duel to a close. But a loud, wailing scream downstairs caused both her and Mr. McCaskey to pause in a sort of involuntary armistice.

text: Between the windows of the room hung a tall, narrow mirror. You might have come across such a pier-glass in an eight-dollar apartment. A person who is both lean and nimble can, by catching glimpses of themselves in the swift succession of its vertical panels, piece together a reasonably true picture of their appearance. Della, slight of frame, had perfected this skill.
source_text: There was a pier-glass between the windows of the room. Perhaps you have seen a pier-glass in an $8 flat. A very thin and very agile person may, by observing his reflection in a rapid sequence of longitudinal strips, obtain a fairly accurate conception of his looks. Della, being slender, had mastered the art.

text: How fickle are the heart’s tides, and how peculiar that tenacious affection for existence which grips us even in the depths of wretchedness! I fashioned another sail from a strip of my garments and steered with desperate intent toward the shore. The coast bore a rugged and desolate aspect, yet as I drew closer I could plainly discern the marks of husbandry. I spied craft at anchor near the beach and found myself abruptly restored to the precincts of human fellowship. I followed the sinuous contour of the coastline and saluted a spire that at last emerged from behind a low headland. Being in a condition of profound exhaustion, I determined to make straight for the town, as a haven where I might most readily obtain sustenance. By good fortune I carried coin upon my person. Rounding the headland, I beheld a tidy little township and a sheltered harbour, into which I glided, my spirit leaping with gladness at my unforeseen deliverance.
source_text: How mutable are our feelings, and how strange is that clinging love we have of life even in the excess of misery! I constructed another sail with a part of my dress and eagerly steered my course towards the land. It had a wild and rocky appearance, but as I approached nearer I easily perceived the traces of cultivation. I saw vessels near the shore and found myself suddenly transported back to the neighbourhood of civilised man. I carefully traced the windings of the land and hailed a steeple which I at length saw issuing from behind a small promontory. As I was in a state of extreme debility, I resolved to sail directly towards the town, as a place where I could most easily procure nourishment. Fortunately I had money with me. As I turned the promontory I perceived a small neat town and a good harbour, which I entered, my heart bounding with joy at my unexpected escape.

text: The gentle warmth of the sun and the untainted breeze of morning coaxed me back toward a measure of calm; and as I turned over in my mind all that had unfolded at the dwelling, I found it difficult not to think I had leapt too rashly to my judgments. My conduct had surely been unwise. It was plain that my words had stirred the elder’s sympathy in my favor, and I had been a simpleton to let his children witness my form before they were ready. I should have slowly accustomed the aged De Lacey to my presence, and only by careful steps revealed myself to the rest of the household once their hearts had been steadied for the meeting. Yet I could not believe my missteps beyond mending, and after long reflection I determined to go back to the cottage, find the old man alone, and through earnest speech draw him to my cause.
source_text: “The pleasant sunshine and the pure air of day restored me to some degree of tranquillity; and when I considered what had passed at the cottage, I could not help believing that I had been too hasty in my conclusions. I had certainly acted imprudently. It was apparent that my conversation had interested the father in my behalf, and I was a fool in having exposed my person to the horror of his children. I ought to have familiarised the old De Lacey to me, and by degrees to have discovered myself to the rest of his family, when they should have been prepared for my approach. But I did not believe my errors to be irretrievable, and after much consideration I resolved to return to the cottage, seek the old man, and by my representations win him to my party.

text: “Stay your fury, I beg you—listen to me before you pour your hatred upon my cursed head. Have I not endured enough, that you would heap still more sorrow upon me? Life, even if it is but a store of torment, remains precious to me, and I shall guard it. Recall that you have shaped me stronger than yourself; my stature exceeds yours, my limbs are more agile. Yet I have no wish to rise against you. I am your creation, and I will be gentle and obedient to my rightful lord and master, if only you will fulfil the duty you owe me. Oh, Frankenstein, do not deal fairly with every other soul and spurn me alone—me, who most deserves your fairness, your mercy, even your love. Remember that I am your creature; I should have been your Adam, but instead I am the fallen angel, whom you banish from joy for no crime of my own. Everywhere I behold happiness from which I alone am eternally shut out. I was kind and good; anguish made me a monster. Grant me happiness, and I shall be virtuous once more.”
source_text: “Be calm! I entreat you to hear me before you give vent to your hatred on my devoted head. Have I not suffered enough, that you seek to increase my misery? Life, although it may only be an accumulation of anguish, is dear to me, and I will defend it. Remember, thou hast made me more powerful than thyself; my height is superior to thine, my joints more supple. But I will not be tempted to set myself in opposition to thee. I am thy creature, and I will be even mild and docile to my natural lord and king if thou wilt also perform thy part, the which thou owest me. Oh, Frankenstein, be not equitable to every other and trample upon me alone, to whom thy justice, and even thy clemency and affection, is most due. Remember that I am thy creature; I ought to be thy Adam, but I am rather the fallen angel, whom thou drivest from joy for no misdeed. Everywhere I see bliss, from which I alone am irrevocably excluded. I was benevolent and good; misery made me a fiend. Make me happy, and I shall again be virtuous.”

text: September 9th: the ice began to stir, and from far off came thunderous roars as the frozen islands split and fractured on all sides. We faced the most pressing danger, but since we could do nothing but wait, my thoughts stayed mostly with my wretched guest, whose sickness had so worsened that he could no longer leave his bed. Behind us the ice broke apart and was driven violently northward; a breeze rose out of the west, and by the 11th the southern passage lay completely open. When the sailors saw this, and saw that their way home to their own country seemed assured, they burst into a wild, sustained shout of joy. Frankenstein, who had been dozing, woke and asked what the uproar meant. “They are shouting,” I told him, “because they will soon return to England.”
source_text: September 9th, the ice began to move, and roarings like thunder were heard at a distance as the islands split and cracked in every direction. We were in the most imminent peril, but as we could only remain passive, my chief attention was occupied by my unfortunate guest whose illness increased in such a degree that he was entirely confined to his bed. The ice cracked behind us and was driven with force towards the north; a breeze sprang from the west, and on the 11th the passage towards the south became perfectly free. When the sailors saw this and that their return to their native country was apparently assured, a shout of tumultuous joy broke from them, loud and long-continued. Frankenstein, who was dozing, awoke and asked the cause of the tumult. “They shout,” I said, “because they will soon return to England.”
