text: The shifting fortunes of existence are less mutable than the passions that stir the human soul. For nigh on two years I had laboured without cease, bent wholly upon breathing vitality into a lifeless frame. Rest and vigour I had sacrificed to this end. My yearning had burned with a flame that scorned all moderation; yet now, the work complete, the loveliness of the vision dissolved, and a suffocating loathing and dread flooded my spirit. Unable to bear the sight of the thing I had fashioned, I fled the chamber and paced my bedroom for hours, powerless to quiet my thoughts toward slumber. At last exhaustion overcame the storm within me, and I cast myself upon the bed fully clothed, striving to seize a brief oblivion. But it was futile; I slept, truly, yet was harried by the most savage visions. I fancied I beheld my beloved Clara, radiant with health, strolling the lanes of Heidelberg. Overjoyed and astonished, I clasped her to me, but as I pressed the first kiss to her mouth, her lips grew ashen with the pallor of the grave; her countenance seemed to shift, and I found myself clutching the body of my departed sister; a winding-sheet swathed her shape, and I glimpsed the grave-worms writhing in the folds of the linen. I started awake in terror; an icy sweat glazed my brow, my teeth clattered, and every muscle spasmed; when, by the sickly amber gleam of the moon forcing through the shutter slats, I saw the fiend—the wretched abomination I had conjured into being. He drew aside the bed-curtain; and his eyes, if eyes they could be named, were trained upon me. His mouth gaped, and he mumbled some guttural noise, while a leer creased his cheeks. He may have uttered words, but I heard nothing; one arm extended, as if to seize me, but I wrenched free and plunged down the stairs. I sought sanctuary in the courtyard of the dwelling I occupied, and there I stayed till dawn, striding to and fro in a fever of agitation, straining my ears, starting at and dreading every murmur as though it heralded the approach of that demon-corpse to which I had so wretchedly bestowed life.
source_text: The different accidents of life are not so changeable as the feelings of human nature. I had worked hard for nearly two years, for the sole purpose of infusing life into an inanimate body. For this I had deprived myself of rest and health. I had desired it with an ardour that far exceeded moderation; but now that I had finished, the beauty of the dream vanished, and breathless horror and disgust filled my heart. Unable to endure the aspect of the being I had created, I rushed out of the room and continued a long time traversing my bed-chamber, unable to compose my mind to sleep. At length lassitude succeeded to the tumult I had before endured, and I threw myself on the bed in my clothes, endeavouring to seek a few moments of forgetfulness. But it was in vain; I slept, indeed, but I was disturbed by the wildest dreams. I thought I saw Elizabeth, in the bloom of health, walking in the streets of Ingolstadt. Delighted and surprised, I embraced her, but as I imprinted the first kiss on her lips, they became livid with the hue of death; her features appeared to change, and I thought that I held the corpse of my dead mother in my arms; a shroud enveloped her form, and I saw the grave-worms crawling in the folds of the flannel. I started from my sleep with horror; a cold dew covered my forehead, my teeth chattered, and every limb became convulsed; when, by the dim and yellow light of the moon, as it forced its way through the window shutters, I beheld the wretch—the miserable monster whom I had created. He held up the curtain of the bed; and his eyes, if eyes they may be called, were fixed on me. His jaws opened, and he muttered some inarticulate sounds, while a grin wrinkled his cheeks. He might have spoken, but I did not hear; one hand was stretched out, seemingly to detain me, but I escaped and rushed downstairs. I took refuge in the courtyard belonging to the house which I inhabited, where I remained during the rest of the night, walking up and down in the greatest agitation, listening attentively, catching and fearing each sound as if it were to announce the approach of the demoniacal corpse to which I had so miserably given life.

text: Such were the lessons my father imparted. Yet the memory of that threat stirred within me once more; and you cannot marvel that, given the fiend’s boundless mastery in bloodshed, I almost believed him unconquerable, and that when he uttered the words “_I shall be with you on your wedding-night_,” I felt the doom he promised was inescapable. But death held no terror for me if it meant Elizabeth’s loss would be recompensed, and so, with a serene, even glad expression, I consented to my father’s wish that, should my cousin agree, the wedding would take place in ten days, thereby sealing, as I thought, my fate.
source_text: Such were the lessons of my father. But to me the remembrance of the threat returned; nor can you wonder that, omnipotent as the fiend had yet been in his deeds of blood, I should almost regard him as invincible, and that when he had pronounced the words “_I shall be with you on your wedding-night_,” I should regard the threatened fate as unavoidable. But death was no evil to me if the loss of Elizabeth were balanced with it, and I therefore, with a contented and even cheerful countenance, agreed with my father that if my cousin would consent, the ceremony should take place in ten days, and thus put, as I imagined, the seal to my fate.

text: “I am delighted,” said M. Waldman, “to have found a pupil; and if your diligence matches your talent, I am confident you will thrive. Chemistry is that field of natural philosophy where the greatest advances have been made and still await us; for this reason, I have devoted myself to it above all. Yet I have not ignored the other sciences. A man would be a poor chemist indeed if he confined himself to that single branch of knowledge. If you aspire to be a true man of science, not a mere dabbler in experiments, I would urge you to pursue every area of natural philosophy, mathematics included.”
source_text: “I am happy,” said M. Waldman, “to have gained a disciple; and if your application equals your ability, I have no doubt of your success. Chemistry is that branch of natural philosophy in which the greatest improvements have been and may be made; it is on that account that I have made it my peculiar study; but at the same time, I have not neglected the other branches of science. A man would make but a very sorry chemist if he attended to that department of human knowledge alone. If your wish is to become really a man of science and not merely a petty experimentalist, I should advise you to apply to every branch of natural philosophy, including mathematics.”

text: The road hugged the shoreline, the lake constricting as I neared the town of my birth. The dark flanks of the Jura sharpened into view, and the luminous crown of Mont Blanc rose before me. I broke down and wept like a child. “Beloved mountains! my exquisite lake! is this how you greet your wayward son? Your peaks stand cloudless; the heavens and the water are calm and blue. Do you promise me peace, or do you taunt my despair?”
source_text: The road ran by the side of the lake, which became narrower as I approached my native town. I discovered more distinctly the black sides of Jura, and the bright summit of Mont Blanc. I wept like a child. “Dear mountains! my own beautiful lake! how do you welcome your wanderer? Your summits are clear; the sky and lake are blue and placid. Is this to prognosticate peace, or to mock at my unhappiness?”

text: The Turk permitted this closeness to grow, feeding the hopes of the young lovers, while secretly nursing quite different intentions. He abhorred the thought of his daughter wedding a Christian, yet he dreaded Felix’s anger should his own enthusiasm seem to cool, for he remained at the mercy of the man who had rescued him—a man who could still denounce him to the Italian authorities. He turned over scheme after scheme, seeking to stretch out the deception until it could safely be shed, and to spirit his daughter away with him when the moment came to flee. News arriving from Paris made his designs easier to accomplish.
source_text: “The Turk allowed this intimacy to take place and encouraged the hopes of the youthful lovers, while in his heart he had formed far other plans. He loathed the idea that his daughter should be united to a Christian, but he feared the resentment of Felix if he should appear lukewarm, for he knew that he was still in the power of his deliverer if he should choose to betray him to the Italian state which they inhabited. He revolved a thousand plans by which he should be enabled to prolong the deceit until it might be no longer necessary, and secretly to take his daughter with him when he departed. His plans were facilitated by the news which arrived from Paris.

text: Out of the dead silence of the night, I received a reply: a loud, demonic laugh. It echoed long and heavy in my ears; the mountains flung it back at me, and I felt as though all of hell had encircled me with jeering laughter. In that instant, I surely would have been seized by madness and put an end to my wretched life—had my oath not been heard and had I not been kept alive for retribution. As the laughter faded, a familiar and loathsome voice, seemingly right beside my ear, spoke in a clear whisper: “I am satisfied, miserable wretch! You have chosen to live, and I am satisfied.”
source_text: I was answered through the stillness of night by a loud and fiendish laugh. It rang on my ears long and heavily; the mountains re-echoed it, and I felt as if all hell surrounded me with mockery and laughter. Surely in that moment I should have been possessed by frenzy and have destroyed my miserable existence but that my vow was heard and that I was reserved for vengeance. The laughter died away, when a well-known and abhorred voice, apparently close to my ear, addressed me in an audible whisper, “I am satisfied, miserable wretch! You have determined to live, and I am satisfied.”

text: “‘No, they hail from France. Yet let us speak of something else. I am a wretched, forsaken soul; I gaze about me and find neither kin nor companion on this earth. The kind strangers I approach have never laid eyes on me and scarcely know my story. I am brimming with dread, for should I falter among them, I shall be an exile in this world for all time.’”
source_text: “‘No, they are French. But let us change the subject. I am an unfortunate and deserted creature, I look around and I have no relation or friend upon earth. These amiable people to whom I go have never seen me and know little of me. I am full of fears, for if I fail there, I am an outcast in the world for ever.’

text: Through the desolate stretches of Siberia and the steppe, though he forever slipped beyond my grasp, I pursued his shadow without rest. At times, frightened villagers, trembling at the ghastly specter, whispered news of his passage; at other times, he himself—dreading that utter loss of his trail would plunge me into despair and death—left behind a sign to steer me forward. Snows gathered upon my shoulders, and I beheld the imprint of his colossal stride across the pale expanse. How can you, who stand at life’s threshold, unacquainted with anguish and untouched by torment, fathom what I have endured and still endure? Cold, privation, and weariness were but the gentlest afflictions laid upon me; I was hounded by a demon and bore within me my own everlasting perdition; yet still a benevolent spirit attended my wanderings, and when I grumbled loudest would suddenly deliver me from trials that seemed impassable. Now and again, when nature, vanquished by famine, collapsed in exhaustion, a meal awaited me in the wilderness that renewed and fortified my soul. The food was, admittedly, rough—no better than the country folk consumed—but I cannot doubt it was placed there by the very spirits I had summoned to my aid. Often, when all lay parched, the sky a blank of cloudless heat, and thirst withered my throat, a slender mist would dim the firmament, loose a few reviving drops, and dissolve.
source_text: Amidst the wilds of Tartary and Russia, although he still evaded me, I have ever followed in his track. Sometimes the peasants, scared by this horrid apparition, informed me of his path; sometimes he himself, who feared that if I lost all trace of him I should despair and die, left some mark to guide me. The snows descended on my head, and I saw the print of his huge step on the white plain. To you first entering on life, to whom care is new and agony unknown, how can you understand what I have felt and still feel? Cold, want, and fatigue were the least pains which I was destined to endure; I was cursed by some devil and carried about with me my eternal hell; yet still a spirit of good followed and directed my steps and when I most murmured would suddenly extricate me from seemingly insurmountable difficulties. Sometimes, when nature, overcome by hunger, sank under the exhaustion, a repast was prepared for me in the desert that restored and inspirited me. The fare was, indeed, coarse, such as the peasants of the country ate, but I will not doubt that it was set there by the spirits that I had invoked to aid me. Often, when all was dry, the heavens cloudless, and I was parched by thirst, a slight cloud would bedim the sky, shed the few drops that revived me, and vanish.

text: From that point on, natural philosophy—especially chemistry in its broadest meaning—became almost my entire focus. I eagerly devoured the writings of modern researchers on these topics, works brimming with insight and sharp judgment. I went to lectures and sought out the company of the university’s scientific minds, and even in M. Krempe I discovered a wealth of solid reasoning and genuine knowledge, despite his off-putting face and manner, which made his expertise no less worthwhile. In M. Waldman I found a sincere friend. His kindness never carried a trace of arrogance, and he delivered his teaching with such open, good-humored honesty that any hint of pedantry vanished. He cleared the way for my learning in countless ways, rendering the most obscure investigations plain and easy to grasp. My dedication wavered at first, uncertain; it gathered momentum as I advanced and soon grew so intense and fervent that the stars would often fade into dawn while I still worked in my laboratory.
source_text: From this day natural philosophy, and particularly chemistry, in the most comprehensive sense of the term, became nearly my sole occupation. I read with ardour those works, so full of genius and discrimination, which modern inquirers have written on these subjects. I attended the lectures and cultivated the acquaintance of the men of science of the university, and I found even in M. Krempe a great deal of sound sense and real information, combined, it is true, with a repulsive physiognomy and manners, but not on that account the less valuable. In M. Waldman I found a true friend. His gentleness was never tinged by dogmatism, and his instructions were given with an air of frankness and good nature that banished every idea of pedantry. In a thousand ways he smoothed for me the path of knowledge and made the most abstruse inquiries clear and facile to my apprehension. My application was at first fluctuating and uncertain; it gained strength as I proceeded and soon became so ardent and eager that the stars often disappeared in the light of morning whilst I was yet engaged in my laboratory.

text: I endured a night of pure and undiluted misery. When morning came, I made my way to the court, my mouth and throat dry as dust. I could not bring myself to voice the dreaded question, but the officer recognized me and understood why I was there. The votes had been cast; every single one was black, and Justine stood condemned.
source_text: I passed a night of unmingled wretchedness. In the morning I went to the court; my lips and throat were parched. I dared not ask the fatal question, but I was known, and the officer guessed the cause of my visit. The ballots had been thrown; they were all black, and Justine was condemned.

text: “I have no doubt he lingers close to where I dwell, and should he truly have fled into the Alps, he might be tracked like a chamois and slain like a wild creature. Yet I read your mind—you mistrust my tale and have no wish to visit upon my foe the retribution he so richly deserves.”
source_text: “I do not doubt that he hovers near the spot which I inhabit, and if he has indeed taken refuge in the Alps, he may be hunted like the chamois and destroyed as a beast of prey. But I perceive your thoughts; you do not credit my narrative and do not intend to pursue my enemy with the punishment which is his desert.”

text: “Well enough, and quite content—only a touch anxious that your letters come so rarely. In fact, I’ve half a mind to scold you on their behalf myself. But, my dear Frankenstein,” he went on, halting abruptly and staring straight into my eyes, “I hadn’t noticed until now how dreadfully unwell you seem; so gaunt and colorless; you look as though you’ve kept vigil for nights on end.”
source_text: “Very well, and very happy, only a little uneasy that they hear from you so seldom. By the by, I mean to lecture you a little upon their account myself. But, my dear Frankenstein,” continued he, stopping short and gazing full in my face, “I did not before remark how very ill you appear; so thin and pale; you look as if you had been watching for several nights.”

text: Wandering on, I eventually found myself facing the inn where the mail coaches and carriages customarily halted. There I stopped, uncertain why, and stood for a while with my gaze fastened on a vehicle approaching from the far end of the street. As it came closer, I recognized the Swiss diligence; it drew to a halt precisely where I lingered, and when the door swung open, I saw Henry Clerval, who leaped out the instant he noticed me. “My dear Frankenstein,” he cried, “how overjoyed I am to see you! What a stroke of luck that you should be here just as I step down!”
source_text: Continuing thus, I came at length opposite to the inn at which the various diligences and carriages usually stopped. Here I paused, I knew not why; but I remained some minutes with my eyes fixed on a coach that was coming towards me from the other end of the street. As it drew nearer I observed that it was the Swiss diligence; it stopped just where I was standing, and on the door being opened, I perceived Henry Clerval, who, on seeing me, instantly sprung out. “My dear Frankenstein,” exclaimed he, “how glad I am to see you! How fortunate that you should be here at the very moment of my alighting!”

text: That reply jolted me, yet I quickly steadied myself. I had done no wrong—that could readily be shown—so I walked quietly behind my guide and was taken to one of the finest residences in the city. Exhaustion and hunger nearly made me collapse, but with so many people around, I judged it wise to summon every ounce of vigor, lest any bodily weakness be mistaken for fear or secret guilt. I had no inkling then of the disaster that would, within minutes, crash down upon me and drown in dread and hopelessness any concern for shame or death.
source_text: This answer startled me, but I presently recovered myself. I was innocent; that could easily be proved; accordingly I followed my conductor in silence and was led to one of the best houses in the town. I was ready to sink from fatigue and hunger, but being surrounded by a crowd, I thought it politic to rouse all my strength, that no physical debility might be construed into apprehension or conscious guilt. Little did I then expect the calamity that was in a few moments to overwhelm me and extinguish in horror and despair all fear of ignominy or death.

text: In those hours I cried without restraint, longing for calm to return to my spirit so I could offer them comfort and joy. Yet it was impossible. Guilt smothered every flicker of hope. I had set in motion irreparable suffering and lived in constant dread that the creature I had brought into being would commit some fresh atrocity. A dim intuition told me the story was far from finished and that he would still enact a crime so monstrous it would nearly blot out all memory of what had come before. Fear had endless ground to stand on as long as anyone I cherished remained alive. My loathing for that demon defies description. When his image entered my mind, I ground my teeth, my eyes burned with fury, and I burned to snuff out the existence I had so recklessly ignited. Dwelling on his wickedness and spite, my hatred and thirst for vengeance shattered all restraint. I would have trekked to the loftiest summit of the Andes if, once there, I could have hurled him into the depths below. I yearned to face him again, to pour the full measure of my detestation upon him and exact justice for the deaths of William and Justine.
source_text: At these moments I wept bitterly and wished that peace would revisit my mind only that I might afford them consolation and happiness. But that could not be. Remorse extinguished every hope. I had been the author of unalterable evils, and I lived in daily fear lest the monster whom I had created should perpetrate some new wickedness. I had an obscure feeling that all was not over and that he would still commit some signal crime, which by its enormity should almost efface the recollection of the past. There was always scope for fear so long as anything I loved remained behind. My abhorrence of this fiend cannot be conceived. When I thought of him I gnashed my teeth, my eyes became inflamed, and I ardently wished to extinguish that life which I had so thoughtlessly bestowed. When I reflected on his crimes and malice, my hatred and revenge burst all bounds of moderation. I would have made a pilgrimage to the highest peak of the Andes, could I, when there, have precipitated him to their base. I wished to see him again, that I might wreak the utmost extent of abhorrence on his head and avenge the deaths of William and Justine.

text: London became the place where we now paused; we resolved to stay for a number of months in this astonishing and renowned metropolis. Clerval longed for the company of the brilliant and gifted minds who thrived in that era, yet for me this was a lesser concern. My main focus was on securing the knowledge required to fulfill my pledge, and I promptly made use of the introductory letters I had carried, directed to the most eminent natural philosophers.
source_text: London was our present point of rest; we determined to remain several months in this wonderful and celebrated city. Clerval desired the intercourse of the men of genius and talent who flourished at this time, but this was with me a secondary object; I was principally occupied with the means of obtaining the information necessary for the completion of my promise and quickly availed myself of the letters of introduction that I had brought with me, addressed to the most distinguished natural philosophers.

text: The morning appointed for the Turk’s death drew near, yet on the eve of that grim hour he slipped from his cell, and by dawn lay far beyond the borders of Paris. Felix had secured travel papers bearing the names of his father, his sister, and himself. Long before, he had shared the scheme with the elder man, who lent himself to the ruse by departing his residence on the pretext of a journey and hiding away, together with his daughter, in a shadowed quarter of the city.
source_text: “The day for the execution of the Turk was fixed, but on the night previous to it he quitted his prison and before morning was distant many leagues from Paris. Felix had procured passports in the name of his father, sister, and himself. He had previously communicated his plan to the former, who aided the deceit by quitting his house, under the pretence of a journey and concealed himself, with his daughter, in an obscure part of Paris.

text: The following morning, Justine was executed. Even Elizabeth’s piercing, grief-stricken words could not shake the judges from their fixed belief in the guilt of that blameless, suffering soul. My own fervent, outraged pleas fell on deaf ears. When I met their frigid replies and listened to the cruel, callous logic of those men, the confession I had meant to make withered unspoken on my tongue. I could only declare myself insane, but I could not undo the doom they had laid upon my pitiable victim. She died on the gallows branded a murderer.
source_text: And on the morrow Justine died. Elizabeth’s heart-rending eloquence failed to move the judges from their settled conviction in the criminality of the saintly sufferer. My passionate and indignant appeals were lost upon them. And when I received their cold answers and heard the harsh, unfeeling reasoning of these men, my purposed avowal died away on my lips. Thus I might proclaim myself a madman, but not revoke the sentence passed upon my wretched victim. She perished on the scaffold as a murderess!

text: It’s over now—I’m on my way back to England. Any chance of doing good or winning renown has slipped away; I’ve lost my friend. Still, I’ll try to set down these painful events for you, my dear sister, and as I’m carried homeward across the sea toward you, I won’t give in to despair.
source_text: It is past; I am returning to England. I have lost my hopes of utility and glory; I have lost my friend. But I will endeavour to detail these bitter circumstances to you, my dear sister; and while I am wafted towards England and towards you, I will not despond.

text: Beloved, gentle Elizabeth! I read her letter over and over, and a tender warmth crept into my breast, daring to murmur paradisiacal visions of love and bliss; but the fruit had already been tasted, and the angel’s arm was stretched forth to bar me from all hope. Still, I would gladly perish to secure her happiness. If the fiend carried out his threat, death was unavoidable; yet I paused to wonder whether my marriage might hasten that end. My ruin could come a few months earlier, but if my tormentor guessed that I delayed out of fear of his warnings, he would surely devise other and perhaps still more terrible revenges. He had sworn to be with me on my wedding night, yet he plainly did not consider that oath a restraint upon his violence in the interim, for—as though to prove his thirst for blood was not yet slaked—he had murdered Clerval directly after uttering those menaces. I determined, then, that if my swift union with my cousin would bring happiness to her or to my father, the murderer’s designs against my life should not postpone it by a single hour.
source_text: Sweet and beloved Elizabeth! I read and reread her letter, and some softened feelings stole into my heart and dared to whisper paradisiacal dreams of love and joy; but the apple was already eaten, and the angel’s arm bared to drive me from all hope. Yet I would die to make her happy. If the monster executed his threat, death was inevitable; yet, again, I considered whether my marriage would hasten my fate. My destruction might indeed arrive a few months sooner, but if my torturer should suspect that I postponed it, influenced by his menaces, he would surely find other and perhaps more dreadful means of revenge. He had vowed _to be with me on my wedding-night_, yet he did not consider that threat as binding him to peace in the meantime, for as if to show me that he was not yet satiated with blood, he had murdered Clerval immediately after the enunciation of his threats. I resolved, therefore, that if my immediate union with my cousin would conduce either to hers or my father’s happiness, my adversary’s designs against my life should not retard it a single hour.

text: The days drifted by as calmly as they once had, with only one change: the sorrow that had shadowed my companions’ faces was now replaced by gladness. Safie remained ever cheerful and light-hearted; she and I advanced swiftly in our grasp of speech, so that within two months I could understand nearly everything my guardians said.
source_text: “The days now passed as peaceably as before, with the sole alteration that joy had taken place of sadness in the countenances of my friends. Safie was always gay and happy; she and I improved rapidly in the knowledge of language, so that in two months I began to comprehend most of the words uttered by my protectors.

text: When my father came back from Milan, he discovered in the villa’s entrance hall a child playing beside me—a girl lovelier than any painted cherub, a being whose face seemed to cast its own light and whose every gesture and step was lighter than a mountain chamois. The mystery was quickly resolved. With his consent, my mother had persuaded the girl’s country guardians to entrust their ward to her. They cherished the gentle orphan; her presence had felt like a benediction in their home, yet they believed it would be unjust to confine her to hardship and obscurity when Providence had offered her such strong protectors. After speaking with their village priest, they agreed, and so Elizabeth Lavenza became a member of my parents’ household—more than a sister to me—the lovely and cherished partner of all my pursuits and joys.
source_text: When my father returned from Milan, he found playing with me in the hall of our villa a child fairer than pictured cherub—a creature who seemed to shed radiance from her looks and whose form and motions were lighter than the chamois of the hills. The apparition was soon explained. With his permission my mother prevailed on her rustic guardians to yield their charge to her. They were fond of the sweet orphan. Her presence had seemed a blessing to them, but it would be unfair to her to keep her in poverty and want when Providence afforded her such powerful protection. They consulted their village priest, and the result was that Elizabeth Lavenza became the inmate of my parents’ house—my more than sister—the beautiful and adored companion of all my occupations and my pleasures.

text: Her words brought me a strange sense of peace. In my heart I held an unshakable certainty that Justine, and in truth every soul involved, bore no guilt for this killing. I had no dread that any chain of mere circumstances could ever prove weighty enough to condemn her. My own story was not fit for public telling; its monstrous terror would strike common minds as sheer lunacy. Was there a single person alive, apart from me, the maker, who could credit—without the proof of his own senses—the reality of that living emblem of arrogant and reckless ignorance I had unleashed upon the earth?
source_text: This speech calmed me. I was firmly convinced in my own mind that Justine, and indeed every human being, was guiltless of this murder. I had no fear, therefore, that any circumstantial evidence could be brought forward strong enough to convict her. My tale was not one to announce publicly; its astounding horror would be looked upon as madness by the vulgar. Did any one indeed exist, except I, the creator, who would believe, unless his senses convinced him, in the existence of the living monument of presumption and rash ignorance which I had let loose upon the world?

text: One evening, as the chill of the world sank into my bones, I stumbled upon a flame left smoldering by vagrant travelers, and a surge of bliss overtook me at the sudden heat. In my rapture I plunged my fingers into the glowing coals, only to snatch them back with a sharp wail. How curious, I mused, that a single origin could yield such contrary outcomes! I studied the fire’s substance and was cheered to discover it was formed of timber. Swiftly I gathered fallen limbs, yet they were damp and refused to kindle. This grieved me, and I crouched motionless, observing the blaze’s labor. The moist boughs I had laid beside the warmth gradually dried and burst into flame. I pondered this, and by handling the assorted sticks, I unraveled the secret, then set to work amassing a vast store of wood, so I might desiccate it and secure an ample hearth. When darkness descended and brought drowsiness, I grew terrified that my fire would perish. I shielded it with dry kindling and foliage, heaped wet branches above, and then, casting my mantle wide, I stretched upon the earth and slipped into slumber.
source_text: “One day, when I was oppressed by cold, I found a fire which had been left by some wandering beggars, and was overcome with delight at the warmth I experienced from it. In my joy I thrust my hand into the live embers, but quickly drew it out again with a cry of pain. How strange, I thought, that the same cause should produce such opposite effects! I examined the materials of the fire, and to my joy found it to be composed of wood. I quickly collected some branches, but they were wet and would not burn. I was pained at this and sat still watching the operation of the fire. The wet wood which I had placed near the heat dried and itself became inflamed. I reflected on this, and by touching the various branches, I discovered the cause and busied myself in collecting a great quantity of wood, that I might dry it and have a plentiful supply of fire. When night came on and brought sleep with it, I was in the greatest fear lest my fire should be extinguished. I covered it carefully with dry wood and leaves and placed wet branches upon it; and then, spreading my cloak, I lay on the ground and sank into sleep.

text: In the interval, the household underwent subtle transformations. Safie’s arrival brought a quiet radiance into the lives of those within, and I perceived that a fuller measure of comfort now blessed them. Felix and Agatha gave their hours more freely to leisure and talk, aided in their tasks by hired hands. They were not wealthy, yet they wore their lot with tranquil satisfaction; their spirits rested in calm contentment, while mine grew daily more tempestuous. Each new insight only sharpened the truth that I was a miserable exile. I clung to hope, indeed, but it dissolved whenever I glimpsed my form in a pool’s mirror or my shadow cast by the moon—as fleeting as that fragile reflection and that wavering darkness.
source_text: “Several changes, in the meantime, took place in the cottage. The presence of Safie diffused happiness among its inhabitants, and I also found that a greater degree of plenty reigned there. Felix and Agatha spent more time in amusement and conversation, and were assisted in their labours by servants. They did not appear rich, but they were contented and happy; their feelings were serene and peaceful, while mine became every day more tumultuous. Increase of knowledge only discovered to me more clearly what a wretched outcast I was. I cherished hope, it is true, but it vanished when I beheld my person reflected in water or my shadow in the moonshine, even as that frail image and that inconstant shade.

text: “I don’t understand what you’re saying,” my brother answered, his voice full of astonishment, “but for us, this revelation has sealed our wretchedness. At first, nobody would accept it; and even now, despite every proof, Elizabeth refuses to believe it. After all, who could imagine that Justine Moritz, so gentle and devoted to our entire household, could abruptly turn into someone capable of such a horrifying, such a monstrous deed?”
source_text: “I do not know what you mean,” replied my brother, in accents of wonder, “but to us the discovery we have made completes our misery. No one would believe it at first; and even now Elizabeth will not be convinced, notwithstanding all the evidence. Indeed, who would credit that Justine Moritz, who was so amiable, and fond of all the family, could suddenly become so capable of so frightful, so appalling a crime?”

text: What shall become of your thoughts, Eleanor, in the days to come? Word of my ruin will never reach you, and you shall wait in trembling hope for my homecoming. Seasons will drift by, and despair will brush against you, only to be chased by the sharp sting of expectation. Oh, my dear sister—the slow, sickening collapse of your deepest hopes, when I imagine it, strikes me with a dread sharper than my own end. Yet you have a husband, sweet children; joy may still find you. May Heaven grant it and keep you so.
source_text: And what, Margaret, will be the state of your mind? You will not hear of my destruction, and you will anxiously await my return. Years will pass, and you will have visitings of despair and yet be tortured by hope. Oh! My beloved sister, the sickening failing of your heart-felt expectations is, in prospect, more terrible to me than my own death. But you have a husband and lovely children; you may be happy. Heaven bless you and make you so!

text: I rose from where I sat and pressed onward, though the gale and blackness swelled with each passing moment, and a clap of thunder broke overhead with a shattering roar. It rang back from Salêve, the Juras, and the Savoyard Alps; sharp blades of lightning blinded me, setting the lake ablaze until it gleamed like a single sheet of flame, then plunged everything into an ink-black void while my sight struggled to recover. The tempest, as so often happens in that country, erupted simultaneously across several quarters of the sky. Its fiercest knot brooded due north of the town, above the stretch of water caught between the Belrive headland and the hamlet of Copêt. A second squall lit the Jura with pale, trembling flickers; a third shrouded and fitfully unmasked the Môle, that sharp peak rising eastward from the lake.
source_text: I quitted my seat, and walked on, although the darkness and storm increased every minute, and the thunder burst with a terrific crash over my head. It was echoed from Salêve, the Juras, and the Alps of Savoy; vivid flashes of lightning dazzled my eyes, illuminating the lake, making it appear like a vast sheet of fire; then for an instant every thing seemed of a pitchy darkness, until the eye recovered itself from the preceding flash. The storm, as is often the case in Switzerland, appeared at once in various parts of the heavens. The most violent storm hung exactly north of the town, over the part of the lake which lies between the promontory of Belrive and the village of Copêt. Another storm enlightened Jura with faint flashes; and another darkened and sometimes disclosed the Môle, a peaked mountain to the east of the lake.

text: It was late in September when I once more left my homeland. The voyage had been my own idea, so Elizabeth had consented, but she was deeply troubled by the thought of me enduring sorrow and anguish far from her side. She had thoughtfully arranged for Clerval to accompany me—yet a man overlooks countless small details that a woman’s careful eye would catch. She wished to urge me to come back quickly; a host of warring feelings kept her silent as she gave me a tearful, wordless goodbye.
source_text: It was in the latter end of September that I again quitted my native country. My journey had been my own suggestion, and Elizabeth therefore acquiesced, but she was filled with disquiet at the idea of my suffering, away from her, the inroads of misery and grief. It had been her care which provided me a companion in Clerval—and yet a man is blind to a thousand minute circumstances which call forth a woman’s sedulous attention. She longed to bid me hasten my return; a thousand conflicting emotions rendered her mute as she bade me a tearful, silent farewell.

text: “I vow,” he declared, “upon the moon, and upon the vast canopy of night, and upon the fervent flame that consumes my soul, that should you consent to my plea, you will never lay eyes on me again so long as they endure. Return now to your dwelling and begin your toil; I shall observe its unfolding with indescribable suspense; and doubt not that when you have finished I shall come forth.”
source_text: “I swear,” he cried, “by the sun, and by the blue sky of heaven, and by the fire of love that burns my heart, that if you grant my prayer, while they exist you shall never behold me again. Depart to your home and commence your labours; I shall watch their progress with unutterable anxiety; and fear not but that when you are ready I shall appear.”

text: She halted, tears streaming, then resumed: “It struck me with dread, my gentle mistress, that you might think your Justine—whom your sainted aunt held in such esteem, and whom you cherished—could commit a deed that only the fiend himself might enact. Sweet William! most precious, hallowed boy! I shall soon behold you once more in paradise, where joy awaits us all; and this comforts me, even as I walk toward disgrace and the grave.”
source_text: She paused, weeping, and then continued, “I thought with horror, my sweet lady, that you should believe your Justine, whom your blessed aunt had so highly honoured, and whom you loved, was a creature capable of a crime which none but the devil himself could have perpetrated. Dear William! dearest blessed child! I soon shall see you again in heaven, where we shall all be happy; and that consoles me, going as I am to suffer ignominy and death.”

text: When her song concluded, she handed the instrument to Agatha, who hesitated before accepting it. She performed a modest melody, her voice rising in gentle tones, yet it lacked the haunting beauty of the unknown singer. The elder man seemed transported and murmured something that Agatha tried to interpret for Safie, as if he meant to convey that her playing had brought him the deepest joy.
source_text: “When she had finished, she gave the guitar to Agatha, who at first declined it. She played a simple air, and her voice accompanied it in sweet accents, but unlike the wondrous strain of the stranger. The old man appeared enraptured and said some words which Agatha endeavoured to explain to Safie, and by which he appeared to wish to express that she bestowed on him the greatest delight by her music.

text: Recalling the dawn of my existence is a struggle; those early moments are a blur, tangled and hazy. A flood of sensations overwhelmed me—I saw, felt, heard, and smelled all at once—and it took ages before I could tell one sense from another. Gradually, I remember, a harsher light beat against my nerves, forcing me to close my eyes. Then darkness swept in and unsettled me, but no sooner had I registered it than, by opening my eyes, as I now understand, the light flooded back. I moved and, I think, went downward, but soon my perceptions shifted dramatically. Before, dense and solid forms had enclosed me, resisting my touch and sight; now I discovered I could roam freely, meeting no barriers I couldn’t either climb over or dodge. The light grew increasingly unbearable, and the heat exhausted me as I trudged along, so I looked for a spot offering shelter. That was the woods near Ingolstadt; there I stretched out beside a stream to recover from my weariness, until hunger and thirst began to gnaw at me. This pulled me from my near-trance, and I fed on berries I found dangling from branches or scattered on the earth. I quenched my thirst at the stream, then lay back down and surrendered to sleep.
source_text: “It is with considerable difficulty that I remember the original era of my being; all the events of that period appear confused and indistinct. A strange multiplicity of sensations seized me, and I saw, felt, heard, and smelt at the same time; and it was, indeed, a long time before I learned to distinguish between the operations of my various senses. By degrees, I remember, a stronger light pressed upon my nerves, so that I was obliged to shut my eyes. Darkness then came over me and troubled me, but hardly had I felt this when, by opening my eyes, as I now suppose, the light poured in upon me again. I walked and, I believe, descended, but I presently found a great alteration in my sensations. Before, dark and opaque bodies had surrounded me, impervious to my touch or sight; but I now found that I could wander on at liberty, with no obstacles which I could not either surmount or avoid. The light became more and more oppressive to me, and the heat wearying me as I walked, I sought a place where I could receive shade. This was the forest near Ingolstadt; and here I lay by the side of a brook resting from my fatigue, until I felt tormented by hunger and thirst. This roused me from my nearly dormant state, and I ate some berries which I found hanging on the trees or lying on the ground. I slaked my thirst at the brook, and then lying down, was overcome by sleep.

text: Crime still seemed to me a far-off shadow, while kindness and open-heartedness constantly surrounded me, stirring a wish to step onto that bustling stage where such fine traits were summoned into action and put on show. Yet in tracing the growth of my mind, I cannot pass over an event that took place early in August of that very year.
source_text: “As yet I looked upon crime as a distant evil, benevolence and generosity were ever present before me, inciting within me a desire to become an actor in the busy scene where so many admirable qualities were called forth and displayed. But in giving an account of the progress of my intellect, I must not omit a circumstance which occurred in the beginning of the month of August of the same year.

text: M. Krempe showed no such gentleness; and given my state at the time—a near-unbearable rawness of feeling—his rough, blunt praise stung me far worse than M. Waldman’s kind approval. “Damn the man!” he burst out. “Why, M. Clerval, I tell you he has outstripped every one of us. Yes, stare if you like, but it’s the truth nonetheless. A young man who, just a few years ago, put as much faith in Cornelius Agrippa as in the Scriptures, has now risen to the very top of the university; and if he isn’t soon taken down a peg, we’ll all be left looking like fools.—Ah, yes,” he added, catching the pain on my face, “M. Frankenstein is modest—a fine trait in a young man. Young men should mistrust themselves, you know, M. Clerval: I did myself in my youth; but that habit wears off very quickly.”
source_text: M. Krempe was not equally docile; and in my condition at that time, of almost insupportable sensitiveness, his harsh blunt encomiums gave me even more pain than the benevolent approbation of M. Waldman. “D—n the fellow!” cried he; “why, M. Clerval, I assure you he has outstript us all. Ay, stare if you please; but it is nevertheless true. A youngster who, but a few years ago, believed in Cornelius Agrippa as firmly as in the gospel, has now set himself at the head of the university; and if he is not soon pulled down, we shall all be out of countenance.—Ay, ay,” continued he, observing my face expressive of suffering, “M. Frankenstein is modest; an excellent quality in a young man. Young men should be diffident of themselves, you know, M. Clerval: I was myself when young; but that wears out in a very short time.”

text: We climbed up to my chamber, and the attendant soon brought a morning meal; yet I could not hold myself in check. It was not merely gladness that had seized me; I sensed a prickling along my skin from an overabundance of feeling, and my heart raced at a gallop. I could not stay fixed for even a moment in one spot; I vaulted across the seats, beat my palms together, and burst into ringing laughter. At first Clerval ascribed my strange elation to delight at his coming, but as he studied me more closely, he perceived a fevered glare in my gaze for which he could find no cause, and my piercing, unchecked, hollow mirth both startled and dismayed him.
source_text: We ascended into my room, and the servant presently brought breakfast; but I was unable to contain myself. It was not joy only that possessed me; I felt my flesh tingle with excess of sensitiveness, and my pulse beat rapidly. I was unable to remain for a single instant in the same place; I jumped over the chairs, clapped my hands, and laughed aloud. Clerval at first attributed my unusual spirits to joy on his arrival, but when he observed me more attentively, he saw a wildness in my eyes for which he could not account, and my loud, unrestrained, heartless laughter frightened and astonished him.

text: The moon had slipped from the sky, then returned in a thinner shape, while I still lingered among the trees. By now my senses had sharpened, and each day brought fresh thoughts to my mind. My eyes grew used to the light and learned to see things as they truly were; I could tell an insect from a leaf, and little by little, one leaf from another. I noticed that the sparrow gave only rough, grating cries, while the songs of the blackbird and thrush were sweet and alluring.
source_text: “The moon had disappeared from the night, and again, with a lessened form, showed itself, while I still remained in the forest. My sensations had by this time become distinct, and my mind received every day additional ideas. My eyes became accustomed to the light and to perceive objects in their right forms; I distinguished the insect from the herb, and by degrees, one herb from another. I found that the sparrow uttered none but harsh notes, whilst those of the blackbird and thrush were sweet and enticing.

text: You have undone the labor you first set in motion; what is it you mean to do now? Will you truly break your word? I have borne hardship and suffering—I journeyed with you out of Switzerland, traced the Rhine’s shoreline, passed through its willow-thronged islands and across the crowns of its hills. I have lingered for long months on England’s heaths and in Scotland’s desolate wastes. I have withstood unmeasured exhaustion, cold, and hunger; will you dare shatter my hopes?
source_text: “You have destroyed the work which you began; what is it that you intend? Do you dare to break your promise? I have endured toil and misery; I left Switzerland with you; I crept along the shores of the Rhine, among its willow islands and over the summits of its hills. I have dwelt many months in the heaths of England and among the deserts of Scotland. I have endured incalculable fatigue, and cold, and hunger; do you dare destroy my hopes?”

text: I found yet another way to lighten their toil. I noticed that the young man devoted many hours each day to gathering fuel for the household fire, so in the darkness I would often take up his implements—whose handling I swiftly mastered—and return with enough wood to sustain them for days.
source_text: “I discovered also another means through which I was enabled to assist their labours. I found that the youth spent a great part of each day in collecting wood for the family fire, and during the night I often took his tools, the use of which I quickly discovered, and brought home firing sufficient for the consumption of several days.

text: My days in the hovel fell into a steady rhythm. In the morning I watched the cottagers’ movements, and once they scattered to their separate tasks, I slept; the rest of my waking hours I devoted to observing my companions. After they had gone to bed, if moonlight or stars lit the sky, I slipped into the woods to gather my own food and fuel for the cottage. When I came back, as often as need arose, I cleared the snow from their path and performed the small services I had seen Felix do. Later I learned that these labours, carried out by an unseen hand, filled them with wonder; once or twice I heard them exclaim, “good spirit, wonderful,” though at the time I did not grasp what those words meant.
source_text: “My mode of life in my hovel was uniform. During the morning I attended the motions of the cottagers, and when they were dispersed in various occupations, I slept; the remainder of the day was spent in observing my friends. When they had retired to rest, if there was any moon or the night was star-light, I went into the woods and collected my own food and fuel for the cottage. When I returned, as often as it was necessary, I cleared their path from the snow and performed those offices that I had seen done by Felix. I afterwards found that these labours, performed by an invisible hand, greatly astonished them; and once or twice I heard them, on these occasions, utter the words _good spirit, wonderful_; but I did not then understand the signification of these terms.

text: Many months slipped by before I understood what cast a shadow over this gentle household: it was want, and they endured it with a most harrowing keenness. Their meals were drawn solely from the garden’s yield and the scant milk of a single cow, whose supply dwindled through the cold months when its keepers could barely scrape together fodder to sustain it. I am certain they often felt the sharp teeth of hunger, above all the two younger dwellers, for more than once I saw them set nourishment before the elder while keeping back nothing for their own mouths.
source_text: “A considerable period elapsed before I discovered one of the causes of the uneasiness of this amiable family: it was poverty, and they suffered that evil in a very distressing degree. Their nourishment consisted entirely of the vegetables of their garden and the milk of one cow, which gave very little during the winter, when its masters could scarcely procure food to support it. They often, I believe, suffered the pangs of hunger very poignantly, especially the two younger cottagers, for several times they placed food before the old man when they reserved none for themselves.

text: No joy could surpass what swept through me at the sight of Clerval; his figure conjured up my father, Elizabeth, and every beloved corner of home that memory held sacred. I seized his hand, and in that instant my dread and calamity dissolved; I felt, all at once, and for the first time in countless months, a placid and untroubled gladness. I greeted my companion, then, with the warmest embrace of friendship, and we set off toward my college. Clerval spoke at length of our shared acquaintances and of his own rare luck in gaining leave to journey to Ingolstadt. “You can scarcely conceive,” he remarked, “what a struggle it was to convince my father that the whole of necessary knowledge is not folded within the estimable craft of double-entry ledgers; and, truth be told, I suspect he remained unconvinced to the very end, for his unvarying reply to my tireless pleas echoed that of the Dutch pedagogue in The Vicar of Wakefield: ‘I possess ten thousand florins a year without Greek, I dine contentedly without Greek.’ Yet his tenderness for me at last conquered his scorn for scholarship, and he has granted me permission to embark upon a journey of exploration into the country of learning.”
source_text: Nothing could equal my delight on seeing Clerval; his presence brought back to my thoughts my father, Elizabeth, and all those scenes of home so dear to my recollection. I grasped his hand, and in a moment forgot my horror and misfortune; I felt suddenly, and for the first time during many months, calm and serene joy. I welcomed my friend, therefore, in the most cordial manner, and we walked towards my college. Clerval continued talking for some time about our mutual friends and his own good fortune in being permitted to come to Ingolstadt. “You may easily believe,” said he, “how great was the difficulty to persuade my father that all necessary knowledge was not comprised in the noble art of book-keeping; and, indeed, I believe I left him incredulous to the last, for his constant answer to my unwearied entreaties was the same as that of the Dutch schoolmaster in The Vicar of Wakefield: ‘I have ten thousand florins a year without Greek, I eat heartily without Greek.’ But his affection for me at length overcame his dislike of learning, and he has permitted me to undertake a voyage of discovery to the land of knowledge.”

text: Upon reaching my seventeenth year, my mother and father determined that I ought to enroll at the university in Ingolstadt. Until then, I had studied in the academies of Geneva, yet my father believed that a proper finish to my schooling required exposure to traditions beyond those of my homeland. My leave-taking was accordingly scheduled for a near day, but before that appointed moment could come, the earliest calamity of my existence befell me—a portent, so to speak, of the wretchedness that lay ahead.
source_text: When I had attained the age of seventeen my parents resolved that I should become a student at the university of Ingolstadt. I had hitherto attended the schools of Geneva, but my father thought it necessary for the completion of my education that I should be made acquainted with other customs than those of my native country. My departure was therefore fixed at an early date, but before the day resolved upon could arrive, the first misfortune of my life occurred—an omen, as it were, of my future misery.

text: At the outset, no one believed it; but gradually certain details emerged that have all but compelled us to accept the truth, and her own manner has been so bewildered and disjointed that it has lent the material proofs a gravity which, I am afraid, permits no room for uncertainty. She is to be examined today, however, and then you will learn everything.
source_text: “No one did at first; but several circumstances came out, that have almost forced conviction upon us; and her own behaviour has been so confused, as to add to the evidence of facts a weight that, I fear, leaves no hope for doubt. But she will be tried today, and you will then hear all.”

text: How can I convey the turmoil that seized me at this disaster, or sketch the creature I had laboured to shape with such relentless devotion? His limbs were well-proportioned, and I had chosen each feature for its beauty. Beautiful! Merciful God! The yellow skin barely veiled the pull of muscles and the web of arteries underneath; his hair flowed in glossy black waves; his teeth gleamed like pearls—but these splendours only deepened the ghastly contrast with those watery eyes, nearly matching the pale dun sockets that held them, the withered complexion, and the thin, straight blackness of his lips.
source_text: How can I describe my emotions at this catastrophe, or how delineate the wretch whom with such infinite pains and care I had endeavoured to form? His limbs were in proportion, and I had selected his features as beautiful. Beautiful! Great God! His yellow skin scarcely covered the work of muscles and arteries beneath; his hair was of a lustrous black, and flowing; his teeth of a pearly whiteness; but these luxuriances only formed a more horrid contrast with his watery eyes, that seemed almost of the same colour as the dun-white sockets in which they were set, his shrivelled complexion and straight black lips.

text: Other teachings sank into me with even greater force. I learned of the distinction between the sexes, of the birth and nurturing of children—how a father treasured an infant’s first smiles and the spirited play of a growing child, how a mother’s entire existence and devotion became entwined with that cherished duty, how the young mind unfolded and absorbed understanding, and of brother, sister, and all the manifold ties that link one human soul to another in shared connection.
source_text: “Other lessons were impressed upon me even more deeply. I heard of the difference of sexes, and the birth and growth of children, how the father doted on the smiles of the infant, and the lively sallies of the older child, how all the life and cares of the mother were wrapped up in the precious charge, how the mind of youth expanded and gained knowledge, of brother, sister, and all the various relationships which bind one human being to another in mutual bonds.

text: I stepped into the cabin where the body of my noble, ill-starred friend lay. Bending above him was a shape I struggle to put into words—immense in height, yet strangely twisted and misshapen in its frame. As it leaned over the coffin, tangled hanks of unkempt hair hid its face, but one enormous hand was outstretched, the skin and hue resembling a mummy’s. At the sound of my footsteps, it broke off its cries of sorrow and dread and lunged toward the window. I had never seen a sight so ghastly as that face, so repellent yet terrifying in its ugliness. My eyes closed of their own accord, and I forced myself to remember what duty required of me concerning this murderer. I shouted for him to stop.
source_text: I entered the cabin where lay the remains of my ill-fated and admirable friend. Over him hung a form which I cannot find words to describe—gigantic in stature, yet uncouth and distorted in its proportions. As he hung over the coffin, his face was concealed by long locks of ragged hair; but one vast hand was extended, in colour and apparent texture like that of a mummy. When he heard the sound of my approach, he ceased to utter exclamations of grief and horror and sprung towards the window. Never did I behold a vision so horrible as his face, of such loathsome yet appalling hideousness. I shut my eyes involuntarily and endeavoured to recollect what were my duties with regard to this destroyer. I called on him to stay.

text: Week followed week, and month after month slipped by while I lingered in Geneva, still unable to muster the resolve to resume my labors. I dreaded the wrath of the thwarted demon, yet I could not quell my loathing for the charge laid upon me. To fashion a female companion, I realized, would demand another long immersion in arduous research and painstaking inquiry. I had learned of certain breakthroughs credited to an English scholar, insights vital to my undertaking, and I toyed now and then with seeking my father’s leave to travel to England for that end; but I seized on every excuse for postponement and recoiled from the first motion toward a project whose urgency had begun, in my mind, to soften. A shift had indeed come over me: my health, long failing, was largely renewed, and my spirits, when not shadowed by the recollection of my wretched vow, lifted in equal measure. My father observed this alteration with gladness and bent his thoughts toward dispelling what lingered of my gloom, which still welled up at intervals and, with a ravenous darkness, swallowed the encroaching light. In those hours I fled into utter seclusion. Entire days I drifted on the lake, alone in a skiff, studying the cloud-shapes and heeding the lap of the water, mute and adrift. Yet the bright air and vivid sun rarely failed to settle me into something like calm, and when I came ashore I answered the greetings of my companions with a freer smile and a lighter spirit.
source_text: Day after day, week after week, passed away on my return to Geneva; and I could not collect the courage to recommence my work. I feared the vengeance of the disappointed fiend, yet I was unable to overcome my repugnance to the task which was enjoined me. I found that I could not compose a female without again devoting several months to profound study and laborious disquisition. I had heard of some discoveries having been made by an English philosopher, the knowledge of which was material to my success, and I sometimes thought of obtaining my father’s consent to visit England for this purpose; but I clung to every pretence of delay and shrank from taking the first step in an undertaking whose immediate necessity began to appear less absolute to me. A change indeed had taken place in me; my health, which had hitherto declined, was now much restored; and my spirits, when unchecked by the memory of my unhappy promise, rose proportionably. My father saw this change with pleasure, and he turned his thoughts towards the best method of eradicating the remains of my melancholy, which every now and then would return by fits, and with a devouring blackness overcast the approaching sunshine. At these moments I took refuge in the most perfect solitude. I passed whole days on the lake alone in a little boat, watching the clouds and listening to the rippling of the waves, silent and listless. But the fresh air and bright sun seldom failed to restore me to some degree of composure, and on my return I met the salutations of my friends with a readier smile and a more cheerful heart.

text: I shook with overwhelming emotion as I spoke; my bearing held a kind of wild fervour, and no doubt some trace of that proud, unyielding fire attributed to the martyrs of antiquity. Yet to a Genevan magistrate, whose thoughts were absorbed by concerns far removed from piety and heroic self-sacrifice, this intensity of spirit seemed little more than madness. He tried to calm me as a nurse would a child, and dismissed my story as the ravings of delirium.
source_text: I trembled with excess of agitation as I said this; there was a frenzy in my manner, and something, I doubt not, of that haughty fierceness which the martyrs of old are said to have possessed. But to a Genevan magistrate, whose mind was occupied by far other ideas than those of devotion and heroism, this elevation of mind had much the appearance of madness. He endeavoured to soothe me as a nurse does a child and reverted to my tale as the effects of delirium.

text: So placed, trapped in the most loathsome of tasks, sunk in a loneliness that offered not a single moment’s reprieve from the scene before me, my mood grew fractured; I turned edgy and unsettled. At every instant I dreaded encountering my tormentor. At times I sat staring rigidly at the ground, afraid to lift my gaze lest it meet the very thing I most feared to see. I grew afraid to stray from the presence of other people, lest in solitude he should appear to reclaim his companion.
source_text: Thus situated, employed in the most detestable occupation, immersed in a solitude where nothing could for an instant call my attention from the actual scene in which I was engaged, my spirits became unequal; I grew restless and nervous. Every moment I feared to meet my persecutor. Sometimes I sat with my eyes fixed on the ground, fearing to raise them lest they should encounter the object which I so much dreaded to behold. I feared to wander from the sight of my fellow creatures lest when alone he should come to claim his companion.

text: The day unfolded much like the one before. The young man stayed busy outside, while the girl kept herself occupied with a range of demanding tasks indoors. The elderly man, whose blindness I quickly noticed, filled his free time either playing his instrument or lost in quiet thought. The tenderness and reverence the younger pair showed their aged companion was beyond measure. They attended to his every small need with soft, devoted care, and he repaid them with kindly smiles.
source_text: “This day was passed in the same routine as that which preceded it. The young man was constantly employed out of doors, and the girl in various laborious occupations within. The old man, whom I soon perceived to be blind, employed his leisure hours on his instrument or in contemplation. Nothing could exceed the love and respect which the younger cottagers exhibited towards their venerable companion. They performed towards him every little office of affection and duty with gentleness, and he rewarded them by his benevolent smiles.

text: On the final day of July, a Monday, the vessel found itself all but imprisoned by a vast ring of ice that pressed in from every quarter, granting the hull barely enough open water to breathe. A measure of peril attended our position, made graver still by the dense fog that wrapped us completely about. We therefore hove to, trusting that the skies and the elements would soon shift their mood.
source_text: Last Monday (July 31st) we were nearly surrounded by ice, which closed in the ship on all sides, scarcely leaving her the sea-room in which she floated. Our situation was somewhat dangerous, especially as we were compassed round by a very thick fog. We accordingly lay to, hoping that some change would take place in the atmosphere and weather.

text: Science has been the guiding spirit of my life; so in this account, I want to set down the events that sparked my devotion to it. When I was thirteen, our family took a pleasure trip to the springs near Thonon, but harsh weather forced us to stay a full day shut inside the inn. There I stumbled upon a book by Cornelius Agrippa. I began reading it with indifference, yet the ideas he sought to prove and the astonishing tales he told quickly turned my mood to excitement. A fresh understanding seemed to break over me, and leaping with delight, I rushed to tell my father what I had found. He glanced idly at the title page and remarked, "Ah, Cornelius Agrippa! My dear Victor, don't squander your time on that; it's worthless nonsense."
source_text: Natural philosophy is the genius that has regulated my fate; I desire, therefore, in this narration, to state those facts which led to my predilection for that science. When I was thirteen years of age we all went on a party of pleasure to the baths near Thonon; the inclemency of the weather obliged us to remain a day confined to the inn. In this house I chanced to find a volume of the works of Cornelius Agrippa. I opened it with apathy; the theory which he attempts to demonstrate and the wonderful facts which he relates soon changed this feeling into enthusiasm. A new light seemed to dawn upon my mind, and bounding with joy, I communicated my discovery to my father. My father looked carelessly at the title page of my book and said, “Ah! Cornelius Agrippa! My dear Victor, do not waste your time upon this; it is sad trash.”

text: Do not fear that I will become the source of further sorrow. My purpose is nearly finished. Neither your death nor any other’s is required to complete the pattern of my existence and fulfill what must be fulfilled—only my own. Do not imagine I will hesitate to make this offering. I will leave your ship on the same ice raft that carried me here and travel to the farthest frozen edge of the world; there I will build my own pyre and reduce this wretched body to ashes, so that no trace remains to tempt some profane and prying soul to fashion another being like the one I have been. I will die. I will no longer endure the torments that now devour me, nor be hunted by desires unsatisfied yet never extinguished. The man who summoned me into life is gone, and once I am gone too, the very memory of us both will swiftly fade. I will no longer watch the sun or the stars or feel the wind brush my face. Light, sensation, and awareness will slip away—and in that state I must seek my peace. Years ago, when the visions of this world first broke upon me, when I felt the glad warmth of summer and heard the rustle of leaves and the song of birds, and these were everything to me, I would have wept to die; now death is my sole comfort. Stained by wrongdoing and torn by the sharpest remorse, where can I discover stillness except in dying?
source_text: “Fear not that I shall be the instrument of future mischief. My work is nearly complete. Neither yours nor any man’s death is needed to consummate the series of my being and accomplish that which must be done, but it requires my own. Do not think that I shall be slow to perform this sacrifice. I shall quit your vessel on the ice raft which brought me thither and shall seek the most northern extremity of the globe; I shall collect my funeral pile and consume to ashes this miserable frame, that its remains may afford no light to any curious and unhallowed wretch who would create such another as I have been. I shall die. I shall no longer feel the agonies which now consume me or be the prey of feelings unsatisfied, yet unquenched. He is dead who called me into being; and when I shall be no more, the very remembrance of us both will speedily vanish. I shall no longer see the sun or stars or feel the winds play on my cheeks. Light, feeling, and sense will pass away; and in this condition must I find my happiness. Some years ago, when the images which this world affords first opened upon me, when I felt the cheering warmth of summer and heard the rustling of the leaves and the warbling of the birds, and these were all to me, I should have wept to die; now it is my only consolation. Polluted by crimes and torn by the bitterest remorse, where can I find rest but in death?

text: The letter brought rushing back what I’d managed to bury—the creature’s grim promise: “I will be with you on your wedding-night!” That was my verdict, and on that night the demon would use every trick to annihilate me, to wrench me from the faint prospect of joy that seemed poised to soften my sorrows. He meant to cap his atrocities with my murder. So let it come; a mortal contest was then inevitable, and if he triumphed I would find rest and his tyranny over me would cease. If I defeated him, I would stand a free man. But what kind of freedom? The sort a peasant knows after his family is slaughtered before him, his home torched, his fields ravaged, and he is cast out—shelterless, destitute, solitary, yet free. That would be my liberty, except that in Elizabeth I held a jewel, though it was counterweighted by the remorse and guilt that would hound me to the grave.
source_text: This letter revived in my memory what I had before forgotten, the threat of the fiend—“_I will be with you on your wedding-night!_” Such was my sentence, and on that night would the dæmon employ every art to destroy me and tear me from the glimpse of happiness which promised partly to console my sufferings. On that night he had determined to consummate his crimes by my death. Well, be it so; a deadly struggle would then assuredly take place, in which if he were victorious I should be at peace and his power over me be at an end. If he were vanquished, I should be a free man. Alas! What freedom? Such as the peasant enjoys when his family have been massacred before his eyes, his cottage burnt, his lands laid waste, and he is turned adrift, homeless, penniless, and alone, but free. Such would be my liberty except that in my Elizabeth I possessed a treasure, alas, balanced by those horrors of remorse and guilt which would pursue me until death.

text: After Clerval’s death, I fled back to Switzerland, shattered and desolate. I mourned for Frankenstein, but my mourning curdled into dread; I loathed my own being. Yet when I learned that he—the architect of my life and of its unutterable agonies—presumed to reach for joy, that even as he heaped misery and ruin upon me he pursued his own pleasures in affections and desires from which I was eternally shut out, a helpless envy and a fierce resentment seized me with a ravenous hunger for retribution. I remembered my vow and determined it must be fulfilled. I understood I was fashioning an exquisite torment for myself, but I was the thrall, not the sovereign, of a compulsion I despised and could not defy. But when she perished—no, then I was not wretched. I had stripped away every sentiment, crushed every sorrow, to revel in the fullness of my ruin. Wickedness from that hour became my virtue. Driven to such a pass, I could only shape my spirit to the element I had freely embraced. The consummation of my monstrous scheme grew into a devouring obsession. And now it is finished; here lies my final offering!
source_text: “After the murder of Clerval I returned to Switzerland, heart-broken and overcome. I pitied Frankenstein; my pity amounted to horror; I abhorred myself. But when I discovered that he, the author at once of my existence and of its unspeakable torments, dared to hope for happiness, that while he accumulated wretchedness and despair upon me he sought his own enjoyment in feelings and passions from the indulgence of which I was for ever barred, then impotent envy and bitter indignation filled me with an insatiable thirst for vengeance. I recollected my threat and resolved that it should be accomplished. I knew that I was preparing for myself a deadly torture, but I was the slave, not the master, of an impulse which I detested yet could not disobey. Yet when she died! Nay, then I was not miserable. I had cast off all feeling, subdued all anguish, to riot in the excess of my despair. Evil thenceforth became my good. Urged thus far, I had no choice but to adapt my nature to an element which I had willingly chosen. The completion of my demoniacal design became an insatiable passion. And now it is ended; there is my last victim!”

text: I crossed the span of Pélissier, where the chasm carved by the river yawned before me, and I started to climb the slope that loomed above it. Not long after, I came into the vale of Chamounix. This vale was more awe-inspiring and majestic, yet lacked the loveliness and charm of Servox, which I had lately traversed. Towering, snow-clad peaks formed its nearest walls, but I beheld no further remnants of fortresses or verdant meadows. Vast glaciers crept close to the path; I caught the distant roar of a tumbling avalanche and glimpsed the haze of its descent. Mont Blanc, the sovereign and resplendent Mont Blanc, soared above the encircling _aiguilles_, its immense _dôme_ commanding the entire valley.
source_text: I passed the bridge of Pélissier, where the ravine, which the river forms, opened before me, and I began to ascend the mountain that overhangs it. Soon after, I entered the valley of Chamounix. This valley is more wonderful and sublime, but not so beautiful and picturesque as that of Servox, through which I had just passed. The high and snowy mountains were its immediate boundaries, but I saw no more ruined castles and fertile fields. Immense glaciers approached the road; I heard the rumbling thunder of the falling avalanche and marked the smoke of its passage. Mont Blanc, the supreme and magnificent Mont Blanc, raised itself from the surrounding _aiguilles_, and its tremendous _dôme_ overlooked the valley.

text: “Why do you drag into my mind,” I answered, “events I recoil from even thinking about—events whose wretched source and creator I am? Damn the day, you loathsome fiend, when you first drew breath! Damn the hands that shaped you, even if in damning them I curse myself! You’ve plunged me into a misery words cannot capture. You’ve stripped me of any ability to weigh whether I’m being fair to you. Leave me! Free my eyes from the sight of your vile shape.”
source_text: “Why do you call to my remembrance,” I rejoined, “circumstances of which I shudder to reflect, that I have been the miserable origin and author? Cursed be the day, abhorred devil, in which you first saw light! Cursed (although I curse myself) be the hands that formed you! You have made me wretched beyond expression. You have left me no power to consider whether I am just to you or not. Begone! Relieve me from the sight of your detested form.”

text: You have taken in this uncanny and dreadful account, Margaret; and does not your very blood thicken with terror, as mine does even now? At moments, gripped by a spasm of torment, he could not go on with his narrative; at others, his voice, fractured yet piercing, labored to shape words so steeped in misery. His fine, luminous eyes now blazed with outrage, now sank into downcast grief and were extinguished in bottomless despair.
