Sometimes he mastered his features and his speech and recounted the most appalling scenes in a level voice, erasing every trace of disturbance; then, like a volcano erupting, his face would abruptly twist into an expression of the most savage fury as he screamed curses upon his tormentor.
source_text: You have read this strange and terrific story, Margaret; and do you not feel your blood congeal with horror, like that which even now curdles mine? Sometimes, seized with sudden agony, he could not continue his tale; at others, his voice broken, yet piercing, uttered with difficulty the words so replete with anguish. His fine and lovely eyes were now lighted up with indignation, now subdued to downcast sorrow and quenched in infinite wretchedness. Sometimes he commanded his countenance and tones and related the most horrible incidents with a tranquil voice, suppressing every mark of agitation; then, like a volcano bursting forth, his face would suddenly change to an expression of the wildest rage as he shrieked out imprecations on his persecutor.

text: Driven by a sudden compulsion, I caught the lad mid-stride and pulled him close. The moment his gaze fell upon my figure, he clapped both palms over his eyes and let out a piercing cry; I wrenched his hands away from his countenance and spoke, "Boy, why do you shrink so? I mean you no harm; hear what I have to say."
source_text: “Urged by this impulse, I seized on the boy as he passed and drew him towards me. As soon as he beheld my form, he placed his hands before his eyes and uttered a shrill scream; I drew his hand forcibly from his face and said, ‘Child, what is the meaning of this? I do not intend to hurt you; listen to me.’

text: For days I lingered in the place where those events unfolded, at times yearning for a glimpse of you, at others determined to abandon life and its torments altogether. Eventually I drifted toward these mountains and have roamed their vast hidden depths, devoured by a fierce longing that only you can satisfy. We cannot separate until you vow to grant my demand. I am solitary and wretched; mankind shuns my company; yet a being as misshapen and monstrous as I am would not refuse me. My partner must share my nature and bear the same flaws. This creature you must bring into existence.
source_text: “For some days I haunted the spot where these scenes had taken place, sometimes wishing to see you, sometimes resolved to quit the world and its miseries for ever. At length I wandered towards these mountains, and have ranged through their immense recesses, consumed by a burning passion which you alone can gratify. We may not part until you have promised to comply with my requisition. I am alone and miserable; man will not associate with me; but one as deformed and horrible as myself would not deny herself to me. My companion must be of the same species and have the same defects. This being you must create.”

text: Such were the musings that occupied my mind throughout the opening days of my stay in Ingolstadt, a period devoted largely to learning the layout of the streets and meeting the chief inhabitants of my unfamiliar surroundings. Yet as the second week began, my thoughts returned to the counsel Professor Krempe had offered regarding the lectures. And while I remained unwilling to attend the discourses of that vain little man declaiming from his podium, I recalled his remarks about Professor Waldman, a figure still unknown to me, since he had until then been away from the city.
source_text: Such were my reflections during the first two or three days of my residence at Ingolstadt, which were chiefly spent in becoming acquainted with the localities and the principal residents in my new abode. But as the ensuing week commenced, I thought of the information which M. Krempe had given me concerning the lectures. And although I could not consent to go and hear that little conceited fellow deliver sentences out of a pulpit, I recollected what he had said of M. Waldman, whom I had never seen, as he had hitherto been out of town.

text: Upon hearing this news, I was momentarily overcome by despair. He had slipped from my grasp, and now I would have to begin a ruinous, nearly interminable trek across the mountainous sea-ice, through a cold that few living things could long withstand—a cold that I, born to a warm and kindly climate, could scarcely hope to survive. But the thought of the demon still alive and triumphant brought my fury and thirst for vengeance flooding back, surging like a great tide and drowning every other emotion. After a brief rest, haunted by the hovering spirits of the dead who spurred me toward toil and revenge, I readied myself for the journey.
source_text: On hearing this information I suffered a temporary access of despair. He had escaped me, and I must commence a destructive and almost endless journey across the mountainous ices of the ocean, amidst cold that few of the inhabitants could long endure and which I, the native of a genial and sunny climate, could not hope to survive. Yet at the idea that the fiend should live and be triumphant, my rage and vengeance returned, and like a mighty tide, overwhelmed every other feeling. After a slight repose, during which the spirits of the dead hovered round and instigated me to toil and revenge, I prepared for my journey.

text: But these are idle lamentations; I will surely find no companion on the boundless sea, nor even here in Archangel, among traders and sailors. Yet even in these rough hearts beat sentiments untainted by the baser stuff of human nature. My lieutenant, for one, is a man of extraordinary bravery and initiative; he burns with a wild hunger for renown—or, to put it more precisely, for professional advancement. He is an Englishman, and amid national and vocational biases, unrefined by education, he still harbors some of humanity’s finest qualities. I first met him aboard a whaling ship; discovering he was without work in this city, I readily enlisted him to aid my undertaking.
source_text: Well, these are useless complaints; I shall certainly find no friend on the wide ocean, nor even here in Archangel, among merchants and seamen. Yet some feelings, unallied to the dross of human nature, beat even in these rugged bosoms. My lieutenant, for instance, is a man of wonderful courage and enterprise; he is madly desirous of glory, or rather, to word my phrase more characteristically, of advancement in his profession. He is an Englishman, and in the midst of national and professional prejudices, unsoftened by cultivation, retains some of the noblest endowments of humanity. I first became acquainted with him on board a whale vessel; finding that he was unemployed in this city, I easily engaged him to assist in my enterprise.

text: A grey and sodden morning finally broke, revealing to my weary, sleepless gaze the Ingolstadt chapel, its pale spire and clock marking the sixth hour. The gatekeeper unbarred the courtyard doors—my refuge through that long night—and I stepped out into the lanes, striding swiftly as though to elude the creature I dreaded would appear at every corner. I could not bear to go back to my own lodgings; instead, some force drove me onward, though the rain fell steadily from a sky both dark and pitiless, soaking me through.
source_text: Morning, dismal and wet, at length dawned and discovered to my sleepless and aching eyes the church of Ingolstadt, its white steeple and clock, which indicated the sixth hour. The porter opened the gates of the court, which had that night been my asylum, and I issued into the streets, pacing them with quick steps, as if I sought to avoid the wretch whom I feared every turning of the street would present to my view. I did not dare return to the apartment which I inhabited, but felt impelled to hurry on, although drenched by the rain which poured from a black and comfortless sky.

text: Beaufort had managed to hide himself so effectively that nearly a year passed before my father tracked down where he lived. Elated by the breakthrough, he rushed to the address, which turned out to be a shabby street close to the Reuss. Yet when he stepped inside, he was met only by wretchedness and hopelessness. From the collapse of his affairs, Beaufort had salvaged a meager sum—just enough to keep himself alive for a few months—and during that time he intended to secure some decent position with a trading firm. So the days slipped by in idleness; with nothing to occupy him, his sorrow festered and deepened, until at last it consumed him so thoroughly that after three months he was bedridden, too weak to stir.
source_text: Beaufort had taken effectual measures to conceal himself, and it was ten months before my father discovered his abode. Overjoyed at this discovery, he hastened to the house, which was situated in a mean street near the Reuss. But when he entered, misery and despair alone welcomed him. Beaufort had saved but a very small sum of money from the wreck of his fortunes, but it was sufficient to provide him with sustenance for some months, and in the meantime he hoped to procure some respectable employment in a merchant’s house. The interval was, consequently, spent in inaction; his grief only became more deep and rankling when he had leisure for reflection, and at length it took so fast hold of his mind that at the end of three months he lay on a bed of sickness, incapable of any exertion.

text: “Before long, a pair of rustics wandered past, yet stopped beside the hut and began to talk with sweeping, emphatic gestures; still, I could not grasp their words, for they used the local tongue, quite unlike the speech of my guardians. Not long afterward, though, Felix drew near with a stranger; I felt astonishment, knowing he had not left the dwelling all day, and waited in suspense to glean from their exchange the sense of these strange events.”
source_text: “Presently two countrymen passed by, but pausing near the cottage, they entered into conversation, using violent gesticulations; but I did not understand what they said, as they spoke the language of the country, which differed from that of my protectors. Soon after, however, Felix approached with another man; I was surprised, as I knew that he had not quitted the cottage that morning, and waited anxiously to discover from his discourse the meaning of these unusual appearances.

text: A woman on horseback appeared, a local man serving as her guide. She wore a dark riding habit and a heavy black veil concealed her face. Agatha spoke to her, but the stranger answered only with the name “Felix,” spoken in a sweet tone. Her voice carried a musical quality, yet it differed from any voice I knew among my companions. At the sound of his name, Felix rushed forward, and as the lady caught sight of him, she lifted her veil. I saw a face of astonishing loveliness and grace. Her hair gleamed like polished jet, woven into intricate braids; her eyes, dark and soft, sparkled with life; her features were perfectly balanced, and her skin was remarkably fair, with a delicate rose blush on each cheek.
source_text: “It was a lady on horseback, accompanied by a country-man as a guide. The lady was dressed in a dark suit and covered with a thick black veil. Agatha asked a question, to which the stranger only replied by pronouncing, in a sweet accent, the name of Felix. Her voice was musical but unlike that of either of my friends. On hearing this word, Felix came up hastily to the lady, who, when she saw him, threw up her veil, and I beheld a countenance of angelic beauty and expression. Her hair of a shining raven black, and curiously braided; her eyes were dark, but gentle, although animated; her features of a regular proportion, and her complexion wondrously fair, each cheek tinged with a lovely pink.

text: Night had fully fallen by the time I reached the outskirts of Geneva; the city gates stood barred, forcing me to seek shelter for the night in Secheron, a hamlet half a league distant. The sky above lay calm and clear, yet sleep eluded me, and I felt drawn to visit the place where my dear William had met his end. Unable to enter the town, I had to cross the lake by boat to reach Plainpalais. On that brief passage, I watched lightning trace exquisite, shifting patterns across the summit of Mont Blanc. The storm drew swiftly nearer, and once ashore, I climbed a low rise to watch its advance. The clouds thickened overhead, and soon heavy, scattered raindrops began to fall, their fury mounting fast.
source_text: It was completely dark when I arrived in the environs of Geneva; the gates of the town were already shut; and I was obliged to pass the night at Secheron, a village at the distance of half a league from the city. The sky was serene; and, as I was unable to rest, I resolved to visit the spot where my poor William had been murdered. As I could not pass through the town, I was obliged to cross the lake in a boat to arrive at Plainpalais. During this short voyage I saw the lightning playing on the summit of Mont Blanc in the most beautiful figures. The storm appeared to approach rapidly, and, on landing, I ascended a low hill, that I might observe its progress. It advanced; the heavens were clouded, and I soon felt the rain coming slowly in large drops, but its violence quickly increased.

text: “I understand,” murmured the wretched prisoner, “how damningly this single detail stands against me, yet I possess no means to account for it; and having confessed my absolute bewilderment, I am reduced to guessing at the chances that might have slipped it into my coat. But there, too, I falter. I trust I have no adversary in the world, and surely none would be so merciless as to ruin me without cause. Could the killer have planted it? I can recall no moment he might have seized for such an act; and even if he had, what motive would drive him to steal the gem, only to cast it away so swiftly?”
source_text: “I know,” continued the unhappy victim, “how heavily and fatally this one circumstance weighs against me, but I have no power of explaining it; and when I have expressed my utter ignorance, I am only left to conjecture concerning the probabilities by which it might have been placed in my pocket. But here also I am checked. I believe that I have no enemy on earth, and none surely would have been so wicked as to destroy me wantonly. Did the murderer place it there? I know of no opportunity afforded him for so doing; or, if I had, why should he have stolen the jewel, to part with it again so soon?

text: The opening portion of that testimony stirred no curiosity in me whatever, but the instant the imprint of the hand was spoken of, the memory of my brother’s slaying surged back, and a violent disquiet seized me; my frame shook, a haze gathered before my sight, forcing me to grip the back of a chair to keep from falling. The examining officer watched me with a sharp glance and naturally inferred a dark omen from my bearing.
source_text: The first part of this deposition did not in the least interest me, but when the mark of the fingers was mentioned I remembered the murder of my brother and felt myself extremely agitated; my limbs trembled, and a mist came over my eyes, which obliged me to lean on a chair for support. The magistrate observed me with a keen eye and of course drew an unfavourable augury from my manner.

text: My father was overjoyed to see me cleared of the torments of a criminal accusation, free once more to breathe the open air and allowed to return to my homeland. I could not share his delight, for to me a prison’s walls and a palace’s chambers were equally loathsome. The draught of life had been forever tainted, and though the sun fell on me as it does on the light-hearted and the joyful, I perceived nothing around me but a thick and terrible darkness, broken by no gleam save the glint of two eyes that stared back at me. At times they were Henry’s speaking eyes, fading in death, their dark pupils almost veiled by the lids and the long black lashes that edged them; at other times they were the clouded, watery eyes of the creature, just as I had first beheld them in my room at Ingolstadt.
source_text: My father was enraptured on finding me freed from the vexations of a criminal charge, that I was again allowed to breathe the fresh atmosphere and permitted to return to my native country. I did not participate in these feelings, for to me the walls of a dungeon or a palace were alike hateful. The cup of life was poisoned for ever, and although the sun shone upon me, as upon the happy and gay of heart, I saw around me nothing but a dense and frightful darkness, penetrated by no light but the glimmer of two eyes that glared upon me. Sometimes they were the expressive eyes of Henry, languishing in death, the dark orbs nearly covered by the lids and the long black lashes that fringed them; sometimes it was the watery, clouded eyes of the monster, as I first saw them in my chamber at Ingolstadt.

text: “It’s impossible; and whatever I might tell you would serve no purpose. You care nothing for my vengeance. I admit it’s a flaw, yet I own it as the consuming, singular fire of my spirit. My fury defies words when I consider that the killer I set free to roam the world still breathes. You deny me what is rightfully mine; I have only one course left, and I pledge myself, in life or in death, to annihilate him.”
source_text: “That cannot be; but all that I can say will be of little avail. My revenge is of no moment to you; yet, while I allow it to be a vice, I confess that it is the devouring and only passion of my soul. My rage is unspeakable when I reflect that the murderer, whom I have turned loose upon society, still exists. You refuse my just demand; I have but one resource, and I devote myself, either in my life or death, to his destruction.”

text: Yesterday the stranger told me, "You can readily see, Captain Walton, that I've endured immense and extraordinary suffering. I once resolved to let the memory of these horrors perish with me, but you've persuaded me to change my mind. You chase knowledge and wisdom, just as I once did, and I fervently hope the fulfillment of your desires won't turn into a serpent that bites you, as mine did. I can't say whether recounting my calamities will benefit you; still, when I consider that you're following the same path, courting the very dangers that have made me what I am, I think you might draw a fitting lesson from my story—one that could guide you if you succeed and comfort you if you fail. Ready yourself to hear of events typically considered extraordinary. If we stood among nature's milder landscapes, I might worry about your disbelief, even your mockery; but in these wild and enigmatic expanses, many things will seem plausible that would stir laughter in those ignorant of nature's endlessly varied forces. And I cannot doubt that my narrative carries within its sequence the inner proof of the truth of the events it comprises."
source_text: Yesterday the stranger said to me, “You may easily perceive, Captain Walton, that I have suffered great and unparalleled misfortunes. I had determined at one time that the memory of these evils should die with me, but you have won me to alter my determination. You seek for knowledge and wisdom, as I once did; and I ardently hope that the gratification of your wishes may not be a serpent to sting you, as mine has been. I do not know that the relation of my disasters will be useful to you; yet, when I reflect that you are pursuing the same course, exposing yourself to the same dangers which have rendered me what I am, I imagine that you may deduce an apt moral from my tale, one that may direct you if you succeed in your undertaking and console you in case of failure. Prepare to hear of occurrences which are usually deemed marvellous. Were we among the tamer scenes of nature I might fear to encounter your unbelief, perhaps your ridicule; but many things will appear possible in these wild and mysterious regions which would provoke the laughter of those unacquainted with the ever-varied powers of nature; nor can I doubt but that my tale conveys in its series internal evidence of the truth of the events of which it is composed.”

text: Elizabeth contracted scarlet fever and grew gravely ill, her life hanging in the balance. Throughout her sickness, we pleaded repeatedly with my mother to stay away from her bedside. At first she gave in to our appeals, but the moment she learned that her cherished girl’s life was threatened, her worry overpowered all restraint. She went to nurse her; her vigilant care defeated the virulence of the disease—Elizabeth recovered, yet this reckless devotion proved fatal to the one who saved her. By the third day my mother had fallen ill; her fever brought on the most dreadful signs, and the faces of her doctors foretold the darkest outcome. Even as she lay dying, the courage and gentleness of this finest of women never wavered. She took Elizabeth’s hand and mine and pressed them together. “My children,” she said, “my dearest hope for lasting joy rested on the promise of your marriage. That hope will now be your father’s comfort. Elizabeth, my dear, you must fill my place for my younger ones. Oh, it grieves me to be torn from you; and having known such love and happiness, is it not bitter to leave you all behind? But such thoughts do not become me; I will try to yield willingly to death and hold on to the hope of seeing you again in another life.”
source_text: Elizabeth had caught the scarlet fever; her illness was severe, and she was in the greatest danger. During her illness many arguments had been urged to persuade my mother to refrain from attending upon her. She had at first yielded to our entreaties, but when she heard that the life of her favourite was menaced, she could no longer control her anxiety. She attended her sickbed; her watchful attentions triumphed over the malignity of the distemper—Elizabeth was saved, but the consequences of this imprudence were fatal to her preserver. On the third day my mother sickened; her fever was accompanied by the most alarming symptoms, and the looks of her medical attendants prognosticated the worst event. On her deathbed the fortitude and benignity of this best of women did not desert her. She joined the hands of Elizabeth and myself. “My children,” she said, “my firmest hopes of future happiness were placed on the prospect of your union. This expectation will now be the consolation of your father. Elizabeth, my love, you must supply my place to my younger children. Alas! I regret that I am taken from you; and, happy and beloved as I have been, is it not hard to quit you all? But these are not thoughts befitting me; I will endeavour to resign myself cheerfully to death and will indulge a hope of meeting you in another world.”

text: In that moment, the cottage door swung open and in stepped Felix, Safie, and Agatha. What words could capture the shock and dread upon their faces when they saw me? Agatha collapsed in a swoon, and Safie, unable to tend to her, fled from the room. Felix sprang forward and, with a strength beyond nature, wrenched me from his father’s knees, where I desperately held on. In a fit of rage, he hurled me to the floor and struck me fiercely with a stick. I might have ripped him apart, as a lion tears an antelope, but a deep, sickening heaviness overcame me, and I held back. I saw him ready to strike again when, crushed by agony and despair, I fled the cottage and, amid the chaos, slipped away unseen to my hovel.
source_text: “At that instant the cottage door was opened, and Felix, Safie, and Agatha entered. Who can describe their horror and consternation on beholding me? Agatha fainted, and Safie, unable to attend to her friend, rushed out of the cottage. Felix darted forward, and with supernatural force tore me from his father, to whose knees I clung, in a transport of fury, he dashed me to the ground and struck me violently with a stick. I could have torn him limb from limb, as the lion rends the antelope. But my heart sank within me as with bitter sickness, and I refrained. I saw him on the point of repeating his blow, when, overcome by pain and anguish, I quitted the cottage, and in the general tumult escaped unperceived to my hovel.”

text: Do you find my fervor for this celestial drifter excessive? You would not, had you glimpsed him. Your sensibilities have been shaped by solitude and scholarship, leaving you somewhat exacting in your tastes; yet this very refinement equips you all the better to recognize the singular genius of this remarkable soul. I have often tried to isolate that elusive trait which lifts him so far beyond every other being I have encountered. I suspect it is an instinctive perceptiveness, a swift and unerring clarity of insight, a grasp of the hidden springs of things, unmatched in its lucidity and exactness; and alongside this, a gift for utterance, a voice whose shifting cadences are spirit-taming melody.
source_text: Will you smile at the enthusiasm I express concerning this divine wanderer? You would not if you saw him. You have been tutored and refined by books and retirement from the world, and you are therefore somewhat fastidious; but this only renders you the more fit to appreciate the extraordinary merits of this wonderful man. Sometimes I have endeavoured to discover what quality it is which he possesses that elevates him so immeasurably above any other person I ever knew. I believe it to be an intuitive discernment, a quick but never-failing power of judgment, a penetration into the causes of things, unequalled for clearness and precision; add to this a facility of expression and a voice whose varied intonations are soul-subduing music.

text: At first, this reading bewildered me utterly, but gradually I realized that many of the sounds he produced while reading matched those he used in speech. I guessed, then, that the marks on the page represented words he knew, and I burned with desire to grasp them too; yet how could I, when I didn’t even understand the spoken sounds those marks stood for? Still, I made steady progress in this study, though never enough to follow any real exchange, despite devoting my whole mind to the task. For I clearly saw that, however desperately I wished to reveal myself to the cottagers, I must not try until I had first mastered their language—such knowledge might let them overlook my misshapen form, a fact I had learned from the constant comparisons my eyes forced upon me.
source_text: “This reading had puzzled me extremely at first, but by degrees I discovered that he uttered many of the same sounds when he read as when he talked. I conjectured, therefore, that he found on the paper signs for speech which he understood, and I ardently longed to comprehend these also; but how was that possible when I did not even understand the sounds for which they stood as signs? I improved, however, sensibly in this science, but not sufficiently to follow up any kind of conversation, although I applied my whole mind to the endeavour, for I easily perceived that, although I eagerly longed to discover myself to the cottagers, I ought not to make the attempt until I had first become master of their language, which knowledge might enable me to make them overlook the deformity of my figure, for with this also the contrast perpetually presented to my eyes had made me acquainted.

text: I understood perfectly, then, what my father’s emotions would be, yet I could not wrench my mind away from the task at hand—repulsive as it was in its own right, it had seized my imagination with a grip I could not break. I wanted, in a sense, to postpone everything connected to my affections until the great purpose that consumed every instinct of my being was finished.
source_text: I knew well therefore what would be my father’s feelings, but I could not tear my thoughts from my employment, loathsome in itself, but which had taken an irresistible hold of my imagination. I wished, as it were, to procrastinate all that related to my feelings of affection until the great object, which swallowed up every habit of my nature, should be completed.

text: For weeks I dragged out a wretched existence in the forest, struggling to heal the injury I had sustained. The bullet had buried itself in my shoulder, and I could not tell whether it still lodged there or had gone clean through; in either case, I possessed no instrument to remove it. My pain was deepened by the crushing awareness of the cruelty and thanklessness that had dealt it to me. Each day my prayers ascended, pleading for vengeance—a profound and lethal vengeance, the only recompense fit to answer the wrongs and torments I had borne.
source_text: “For some weeks I led a miserable life in the woods, endeavouring to cure the wound which I had received. The ball had entered my shoulder, and I knew not whether it had remained there or passed through; at any rate I had no means of extracting it. My sufferings were augmented also by the oppressive sense of the injustice and ingratitude of their infliction. My daily vows rose for revenge—a deep and deadly revenge, such as would alone compensate for the outrages and anguish I had endured.

text: Our passage had long since shed the charm of lovely landscapes, yet within a handful of days we reached Rotterdam, and from there took ship for England. I caught my first glimpse of Britain’s pale chalk cliffs on a lucid morning near December’s close. The shores of the Thames unfolded a fresh prospect: level yet rich, with nearly every settlement bearing the imprint of some remembered tale. Tilbury Fort rose before us, calling to mind the Spanish Armada; then came Gravesend, Woolwich, and Greenwich—names I had known even in my own distant land.
source_text: Our journey here lost the interest arising from beautiful scenery, but we arrived in a few days at Rotterdam, whence we proceeded by sea to England. It was on a clear morning, in the latter days of December, that I first saw the white cliffs of Britain. The banks of the Thames presented a new scene; they were flat but fertile, and almost every town was marked by the remembrance of some story. We saw Tilbury Fort and remembered the Spanish Armada, Gravesend, Woolwich, and Greenwich—places which I had heard of even in my country.

text: In truth, I was gravely sick, and nothing but my friend’s limitless and unwavering care could have drawn me back from the brink of death. The shape of the creature I had brought into being hovered perpetually before my sight, and I spoke of him in ceaseless, fevered outbursts. My words must have astonished Henry; at first he took them for the ravings of a troubled mind, but the stubborn way I returned again and again to the same theme convinced him that my illness sprang from some strange and dreadful occurrence.
source_text: But I was in reality very ill, and surely nothing but the unbounded and unremitting attentions of my friend could have restored me to life. The form of the monster on whom I had bestowed existence was for ever before my eyes, and I raved incessantly concerning him. Doubtless my words surprised Henry; he at first believed them to be the wanderings of my disturbed imagination, but the pertinacity with which I continually recurred to the same subject persuaded him that my disorder indeed owed its origin to some uncommon and terrible event.

text: Many moons drifted by in such a fashion. Her father’s health declined steadily; her days were wholly consumed with nursing him; her slender funds dwindled further; and by the tenth month he breathed his last in her embrace, leaving her parentless and penniless. This final sorrow broke her utterly, and she knelt beside Alistair’s bier shedding helpless tears, when my father stepped into the room. He arrived like a guardian angel to the destitute young woman, who entrusted herself to his keeping; and once his friend was laid to rest, he escorted her to Geneva and settled her beneath the shelter of a kinswoman. Two years after that season, Caroline became his bride.
source_text: Several months passed in this manner. Her father grew worse; her time was more entirely occupied in attending him; her means of subsistence decreased; and in the tenth month her father died in her arms, leaving her an orphan and a beggar. This last blow overcame her, and she knelt by Beaufort’s coffin weeping bitterly, when my father entered the chamber. He came like a protecting spirit to the poor girl, who committed herself to his care; and after the interment of his friend he conducted her to Geneva and placed her under the protection of a relation. Two years after this event Caroline became his wife.

text: “Think about it,” his friend said to him, “you’ll have to cover three months’ rent and give up whatever your garden yields. I’ve no desire to gain at your expense, so I ask you to take a few days and reflect before you decide.”
source_text: “‘Do you consider,’ said his companion to him, ‘that you will be obliged to pay three months’ rent and to lose the produce of your garden? I do not wish to take any unfair advantage, and I beg therefore that you will take some days to consider of your determination.’

text: How strangely our souls are fashioned, and by what frail threads we are tethered to fortune or disaster. Looking back, I feel as though this nearly wondrous shift in desire and resolve came as a direct whisper from the guardian spirit of my existence—a final effort by the preserving force to turn aside the tempest that already loomed in the constellations, poised to swallow me. Its triumph was heralded by a rare stillness and lightness of heart that arrived the moment I abandoned my long-accursed studies. So I was schooled to link misery with their pursuit, and joy with their release.
source_text: Thus strangely are our souls constructed, and by such slight ligaments are we bound to prosperity or ruin. When I look back, it seems to me as if this almost miraculous change of inclination and will was the immediate suggestion of the guardian angel of my life—the last effort made by the spirit of preservation to avert the storm that was even then hanging in the stars and ready to envelop me. Her victory was announced by an unusual tranquillity and gladness of soul which followed the relinquishing of my ancient and latterly tormenting studies. It was thus that I was to be taught to associate evil with their prosecution, happiness with their disregard.

text: It is true, though—I am a monster. I have destroyed the innocent and the vulnerable; I have choked sleeping souls whose only fault was never having harmed me or anyone else. I have condemned my maker, that rarest example of all that deserves human love and reverence, to a life of torment; I have hunted him straight into this hopeless end. There he rests, pale and still in death. You despise me, but your disgust is nothing compared to the loathing I feel for myself. I stare at the hands that carried out the crime; I dwell on the mind where the thought first took shape, and I ache for the hour when those hands will vanish from my sight, when that thought will no longer shadow my mind.
source_text: “But it is true that I am a wretch. I have murdered the lovely and the helpless; I have strangled the innocent as they slept and grasped to death his throat who never injured me or any other living thing. I have devoted my creator, the select specimen of all that is worthy of love and admiration among men, to misery; I have pursued him even to that irremediable ruin. There he lies, white and cold in death. You hate me, but your abhorrence cannot equal that with which I regard myself. I look on the hands which executed the deed; I think on the heart in which the imagination of it was conceived and long for the moment when these hands will meet my eyes, when that imagination will haunt my thoughts no more.

text: At that moment, my father came in. His face was etched with sorrow, though he tried to greet me with a brave smile. Once we had shared our somber salutations, he seemed ready to steer the conversation away from our tragedy. But before he could, Ernest burst out, "Dear Lord, Father! Victor claims he knows who killed our poor William."
source_text: At that instant my father entered. I saw unhappiness deeply impressed on his countenance, but he endeavoured to welcome me cheerfully; and, after we had exchanged our mournful greeting, would have introduced some other topic than that of our disaster, had not Ernest exclaimed, “Good God, papa! Victor says that he knows who was the murderer of poor William.”

text: One evening, during my habitual excursion into the nearby woods—where I gathered my own sustenance and carried back kindling for those who sheltered me—I stumbled upon a leather traveling case lying on the earth. It held several garments and a few books. I seized the prize with eager hands and bore it back to my hovel. By good fortune, the volumes were composed in the tongue whose rudiments I had absorbed at the cottage; they included *Paradise Lost*, a copy of *Plutarch’s Lives*, and *The Sorrows of Werther*. To possess such treasures filled me with profound joy. From that moment I ceaselessly pored over these works and exercised my intellect upon their narratives, while my companions went about their daily tasks.
source_text: “One night during my accustomed visit to the neighbouring wood where I collected my own food and brought home firing for my protectors, I found on the ground a leathern portmanteau containing several articles of dress and some books. I eagerly seized the prize and returned with it to my hovel. Fortunately the books were written in the language, the elements of which I had acquired at the cottage; they consisted of _Paradise Lost_, a volume of _Plutarch’s Lives_, and the _Sorrows of Werter_. The possession of these treasures gave me extreme delight; I now continually studied and exercised my mind upon these histories, whilst my friends were employed in their ordinary occupations.

text: While I busied myself with securing the vessel and adjusting the canvas, a small crowd began to gather near where I stood. Their expressions betrayed considerable astonishment at the sight of me, yet rather than stepping forward to help, they exchanged hushed words and motions that, under different circumstances, might have stirred a faint unease within me. In that moment, however, I simply noted that their murmurs were in English, so I spoke to them in that tongue. “Kind sirs,” I asked, “would you be good enough to give me the name of this place and tell me where I find myself?”
source_text: As I was occupied in fixing the boat and arranging the sails, several people crowded towards the spot. They seemed much surprised at my appearance, but instead of offering me any assistance, whispered together with gestures that at any other time might have produced in me a slight sensation of alarm. As it was, I merely remarked that they spoke English, and I therefore addressed them in that language. “My good friends,” said I, “will you be so kind as to tell me the name of this town and inform me where I am?”

text: You, who claim kinship with Victor, appear acquainted with my transgressions and his sorrows. Yet in the account he rendered you, he could not reckon the seasons and ages of torment I passed, consumed by futile rage. For though I shattered his aspirations, I never quenched my own longings. They remained perpetually burning and ravenous; still I yearned for tenderness and communion, and still I was rejected. Was there no wrong in this? Must I alone bear the name of malefactor, when all of mankind trespassed against me? Why do you not revile Felix, who thrust his companion from his threshold with scorn? Why do you not curse the peasant who strove to murder the rescuer of his child? Ah, but these are spotless and righteous souls! I, the wretch and the outcast, am a monstrosity, to be derided, and beaten, and ground into the dust. Even now my veins surge with fire at the memory of this inequity.
source_text: “You, who call Frankenstein your friend, seem to have a knowledge of my crimes and his misfortunes. But in the detail which he gave you of them he could not sum up the hours and months of misery which I endured wasting in impotent passions. For while I destroyed his hopes, I did not satisfy my own desires. They were for ever ardent and craving; still I desired love and fellowship, and I was still spurned. Was there no injustice in this? Am I to be thought the only criminal, when all humankind sinned against me? Why do you not hate Felix, who drove his friend from his door with contumely? Why do you not execrate the rustic who sought to destroy the saviour of his child? Nay, these are virtuous and immaculate beings! I, the miserable and the abandoned, am an abortion, to be spurned at, and kicked, and trampled on. Even now my blood boils at the recollection of this injustice.

text: My hatred didn’t stay bottled up in idle fantasies for long; I started thinking through the surest way to trap him. With that in mind, roughly a month after I got out, I went to a magistrate in town and said I had a charge to bring. I told him I knew who had wrecked my family and demanded he use every power at his disposal to arrest the killer.
source_text: Nor did my hate long confine itself to useless wishes; I began to reflect on the best means of securing him; and for this purpose, about a month after my release, I repaired to a criminal judge in the town and told him that I had an accusation to make, that I knew the destroyer of my family, and that I required him to exert his whole authority for the apprehension of the murderer.

text: You know well, Victor, that our parents have cherished the idea of our marriage since we were small children. From an early age, we heard this and were raised to see it as something destined to happen. As children we were loving playmates, and as we matured, I think we became treasured and trusted companions. Yet just as a brother and sister can feel deep fondness for each other without longing for a closer bond, might that not be true for us as well? Tell me honestly, my dearest Victor. I implore you, in the name of our shared happiness, answer me plainly—is there someone else you love?
source_text: “You well know, Victor, that our union had been the favourite plan of your parents ever since our infancy. We were told this when young, and taught to look forward to it as an event that would certainly take place. We were affectionate playfellows during childhood, and, I believe, dear and valued friends to one another as we grew older. But as brother and sister often entertain a lively affection towards each other without desiring a more intimate union, may not such also be our case? Tell me, dearest Victor. Answer me, I conjure you by our mutual happiness, with simple truth—Do you not love another?

text: The counsel, though sound, was utterly unsuited to my situation. I would have been the first to conceal my sorrow and comfort those around me, had not remorse infused its sharp pang and terror its cold dread into all I felt. As it was, I could only meet my father’s gaze with a look of hopelessness and try to shrink from his sight.
source_text: This advice, although good, was totally inapplicable to my case; I should have been the first to hide my grief and console my friends if remorse had not mingled its bitterness, and terror its alarm, with my other sensations. Now I could only answer my father with a look of despair and endeavour to hide myself from his view.

text: As darkness fell, I left my hiding place and roamed the forest. Free now from the fear of being found, I unleashed my torment in terrible cries. I was like a wild animal that had snapped its snares, smashing whatever blocked my path and racing through the trees with the speed of a stag. Oh, what a wretched night I endured! The icy stars glittered with scorn, and the leafless branches swayed above me; every so often, a bird’s gentle song broke the deep silence. Everything but me lay peaceful or content; I, like the arch-demon, carried a hell inside me, and, feeling utterly alone, I longed to rip the trees from the ground, unleash chaos and wreckage, then sit down and savor the devastation.
source_text: “When night came I quitted my retreat and wandered in the wood; and now, no longer restrained by the fear of discovery, I gave vent to my anguish in fearful howlings. I was like a wild beast that had broken the toils, destroying the objects that obstructed me and ranging through the wood with a stag-like swiftness. Oh! What a miserable night I passed! The cold stars shone in mockery, and the bare trees waved their branches above me; now and then the sweet voice of a bird burst forth amidst the universal stillness. All, save I, were at rest or in enjoyment; I, like the arch-fiend, bore a hell within me, and finding myself unsympathised with, wished to tear up the trees, spread havoc and destruction around me, and then to have sat down and enjoyed the ruin.

text: A full week has slipped by as I have absorbed the most extraordinary story ever spun by mortal fancy. My mind and every pulse of my soul have been consumed by the fascination for my visitor which his narrative and his own lofty, tender bearing have awakened. I long to comfort him, yet dare I urge a being so fathomlessly wretched, so stripped of any hope of solace, to endure? Ah, no! The sole happiness left to him now will arrive when he lays his fractured spirit to rest in stillness and oblivion. Still, he possesses one strange consolation, born of solitude and fevered vision; he trusts that when in sleep he converses with his companions and draws from that fellowship either relief for his sorrows or fuel for his revenge, they are no phantoms of his own invention, but the very souls who visit him from the borders of some distant sphere. This conviction lends his trances a gravity that makes them, to my perception, nearly as majestic and compelling as reality.
source_text: Thus has a week passed away, while I have listened to the strangest tale that ever imagination formed. My thoughts and every feeling of my soul have been drunk up by the interest for my guest which this tale and his own elevated and gentle manners have created. I wish to soothe him, yet can I counsel one so infinitely miserable, so destitute of every hope of consolation, to live? Oh, no! The only joy that he can now know will be when he composes his shattered spirit to peace and death. Yet he enjoys one comfort, the offspring of solitude and delirium; he believes that when in dreams he holds converse with his friends and derives from that communion consolation for his miseries or excitements to his vengeance, that they are not the creations of his fancy, but the beings themselves who visit him from the regions of a remote world. This faith gives a solemnity to his reveries that render them to me almost as imposing and interesting as truth.

text: As the day set for our wedding approached, my spirits inexplicably faltered—whether from sheer timidity or some dark foreboding, I cannot say. Yet I masked this sinking of the heart with a show of cheerfulness that lit up my father’s face with gladness, though it barely slipped past the keener, ever-alert gaze of Elizabeth. She awaited our marriage with a quiet serenity, tinged though it was with a faint unease born of past sorrows, a sense that the happiness now seemingly solid and within reach might soon dissolve into an empty reverie, leaving behind nothing but profound and lasting sorrow.
source_text: As the period fixed for our marriage drew nearer, whether from cowardice or a prophetic feeling, I felt my heart sink within me. But I concealed my feelings by an appearance of hilarity that brought smiles and joy to the countenance of my father, but hardly deceived the ever-watchful and nicer eye of Elizabeth. She looked forward to our union with placid contentment, not unmingled with a little fear, which past misfortunes had impressed, that what now appeared certain and tangible happiness might soon dissipate into an airy dream and leave no trace but deep and everlasting regret.

text: But there is one longing I’ve never been able to fulfill, and its absence now strikes me as a grave affliction: I have no friend, Margaret. When I burn with the thrill of success, no one will share my delight; if disappointment strikes, no one will try to lift me from despondency. I do set my thoughts down on paper, it’s true, but that’s a feeble channel for conveying feeling. I yearn for the companionship of a man who could truly sympathize, whose gaze would answer mine. You may think me fanciful, dear sister, but I keenly feel the lack of a friend. I have no one close at hand—gentle yet brave, with a mind both refined and expansive, whose tastes match my own—to endorse or refine my schemes. How thoroughly such a friend might mend the flaws of your poor brother! I am too impulsive in action and too quick to chafe at obstacles. Yet an even greater misfortune is that I am self-taught: for the first fourteen years of my life, I roamed wild on the heath and read nothing but our Uncle Thomas’s travelogues. At that age I discovered the celebrated poets of our land; but only when it was too late to reap the deepest rewards of that awakening did I grasp the need to learn languages beyond my native tongue. Now I am twenty-eight and in truth more ignorant than many schoolboys of fifteen. It’s true I’ve thought more, and my daydreams are vaster and more splendid, but they lack—as painters say—harmony; and I sorely need a friend with sense enough not to dismiss me as a dreamer, and affection enough to help steady my mind.
source_text: But I have one want which I have never yet been able to satisfy, and the absence of the object of which I now feel as a most severe evil, I have no friend, Margaret: when I am glowing with the enthusiasm of success, there will be none to participate my joy; if I am assailed by disappointment, no one will endeavour to sustain me in dejection. I shall commit my thoughts to paper, it is true; but that is a poor medium for the communication of feeling. I desire the company of a man who could sympathise with me, whose eyes would reply to mine. You may deem me romantic, my dear sister, but I bitterly feel the want of a friend. I have no one near me, gentle yet courageous, possessed of a cultivated as well as of a capacious mind, whose tastes are like my own, to approve or amend my plans. How would such a friend repair the faults of your poor brother! I am too ardent in execution and too impatient of difficulties. But it is a still greater evil to me that I am self-educated: for the first fourteen years of my life I ran wild on a common and read nothing but our Uncle Thomas’ books of voyages. At that age I became acquainted with the celebrated poets of our own country; but it was only when it had ceased to be in my power to derive its most important benefits from such a conviction that I perceived the necessity of becoming acquainted with more languages than that of my native country. Now I am twenty-eight and am in reality more illiterate than many schoolboys of fifteen. It is true that I have thought more and that my daydreams are more extended and magnificent, but they want (as the painters call it) _keeping;_ and I greatly need a friend who would have sense enough not to despise me as romantic, and affection enough for me to endeavour to regulate my mind.

text: About a week after Elizabeth’s letter reached me, we made our way back to Geneva. The dear girl greeted me with tender warmth, though her eyes glistened as she took in my gaunt body and flushed cheeks. I noticed a shift in her as well. She had grown more slender, and much of the radiant liveliness that once enchanted me had faded; yet her quiet kindness and the gentle pity in her gaze suited her all the better as a companion for someone as shattered and wretched as I had become.
source_text: In about a week after the arrival of Elizabeth’s letter we returned to Geneva. The sweet girl welcomed me with warm affection, yet tears were in her eyes as she beheld my emaciated frame and feverish cheeks. I saw a change in her also. She was thinner and had lost much of that heavenly vivacity that had before charmed me; but her gentleness and soft looks of compassion made her a more fit companion for one blasted and miserable as I was.

text: She collapsed and was revived only after great effort. Upon regaining consciousness, she could do nothing but weep and sigh. She confided to me that, earlier that evening, William had begged her to let him wear a precious miniature she kept of your mother. The portrait is now missing, and it was surely the lure that drove the killer to commit the crime. We have not yet found any sign of him, though our efforts to track him down are relentless—but nothing can bring my dear William back.
source_text: “She fainted, and was restored with extreme difficulty. When she again lived, it was only to weep and sigh. She told me, that that same evening William had teased her to let him wear a very valuable miniature that she possessed of your mother. This picture is gone, and was doubtless the temptation which urged the murderer to the deed. We have no trace of him at present, although our exertions to discover him are unremitted; but they will not restore my beloved William!

text: Such were the musings of my bleak and lonely hours; yet when I dwelled upon the cottagers’ goodness—their gentle, generous natures—I convinced myself that once they perceived how deeply I revered their kindness, they would pity me and disregard my monstrous form. Could they really spurn a creature, however hideous, who came begging only for their sympathy and fellowship? I determined, at least, not to surrender to despair, but to prepare myself by every means for the meeting that would seal my destiny. I deferred the attempt some months longer, for the gravity of its outcome filled me with a terror of failing. Moreover, I sensed my understanding sharpening with each day’s experience, and I was loath to begin the venture until a few more months had seasoned my wisdom.
source_text: “These were the reflections of my hours of despondency and solitude; but when I contemplated the virtues of the cottagers, their amiable and benevolent dispositions, I persuaded myself that when they should become acquainted with my admiration of their virtues they would compassionate me and overlook my personal deformity. Could they turn from their door one, however monstrous, who solicited their compassion and friendship? I resolved, at least, not to despair, but in every way to fit myself for an interview with them which would decide my fate. I postponed this attempt for some months longer, for the importance attached to its success inspired me with a dread lest I should fail. Besides, I found that my understanding improved so much with every day’s experience that I was unwilling to commence this undertaking until a few more months should have added to my sagacity.

text: A fresh vitality now stirred within the stranger’s failing body. He grew intensely eager to go on deck and scan the horizon for the sledge he had glimpsed before, but I urged him to stay inside the cabin—his strength was far too diminished to endure the biting air. I assured him that a lookout would be kept, and he would be alerted at once should anything new come into view.
source_text: From this time a new spirit of life animated the decaying frame of the stranger. He manifested the greatest eagerness to be upon deck to watch for the sledge which had before appeared; but I have persuaded him to remain in the cabin, for he is far too weak to sustain the rawness of the atmosphere. I have promised that someone should watch for him and give him instant notice if any new object should appear in sight.

text: We stepped into the shadowed cell and found Celeste at its far edge, seated upon a heap of straw; iron cuffs bound her wrists, and her brow lay heavy upon her knees. At the sight of our entrance she stood, and the moment the guards withdrew and left us three alone, she cast herself at Helena’s feet in a storm of tears. My cousin wept with her.
source_text: We entered the gloomy prison chamber and beheld Justine sitting on some straw at the farther end; her hands were manacled, and her head rested on her knees. She rose on seeing us enter, and when we were left alone with her, she threw herself at the feet of Elizabeth, weeping bitterly. My cousin wept also.

text: “The old masters of this art,” he declared, “pledged the impossible and delivered nothing. The new thinkers promise little; they accept that metals cannot be transformed and that the elixir of life is a fantasy, yet these men, whose hands appear fit only for grubbing in soil and whose eyes are bent over the microscope or crucible, have worked genuine wonders. They pry into nature’s hidden depths and reveal how she labors in her secret places. They climb into the skies; they have mapped the circulation of the blood and uncovered the essence of the air we breathe. They have gained new, nearly boundless powers; they can summon the thunder, imitate the earthquake, and even taunt the unseen world with its own specters.”
source_text: “The ancient teachers of this science,” said he, “promised impossibilities and performed nothing. The modern masters promise very little; they know that metals cannot be transmuted and that the elixir of life is a chimera but these philosophers, whose hands seem only made to dabble in dirt, and their eyes to pore over the microscope or crucible, have indeed performed miracles. They penetrate into the recesses of nature and show how she works in her hiding-places. They ascend into the heavens; they have discovered how the blood circulates, and the nature of the air we breathe. They have acquired new and almost unlimited powers; they can command the thunders of heaven, mimic the earthquake, and even mock the invisible world with its own shadows.”

text: “I understand how little solace an unfamiliar voice can offer to someone crushed beneath the weight of such a peculiar calamity. Yet I trust you shall soon depart this sorrowful place, since surely proof can swiftly be gathered to absolve you of this accusation.”
source_text: “I know that the sympathy of a stranger can be but of little relief to one borne down as you are by so strange a misfortune. But you will, I hope, soon quit this melancholy abode, for doubtless evidence can easily be brought to free you from the criminal charge.”

text: A shudder passed through me, and my spirit faltered, when, glancing upward, I perceived by the moon’s pale gleam the fiend framed in the window. A hideous smile creased his mouth as he watched me, crouched there labouring at the appointed work he had bound me to. Indeed, he had tracked me across my wanderings; he had lurked in woodlands, concealed himself in hollows, or sheltered in barren and lonely moors; and now he had come to measure my advance and demand the keeping of my vow.
source_text: I trembled and my heart failed within me, when, on looking up, I saw by the light of the moon the dæmon at the casement. A ghastly grin wrinkled his lips as he gazed on me, where I sat fulfilling the task which he had allotted to me. Yes, he had followed me in my travels; he had loitered in forests, hid himself in caves, or taken refuge in wide and desert heaths; and he now came to mark my progress and claim the fulfilment of my promise.

text: I shook uncontrollably—I could not bear to dwell on, much less speak of, the horrors of the night before. We walked swiftly, and before long we reached my college. Then a thought struck me that made me shudder: the being I had abandoned in my room might still be there, alive and stirring. I dreaded confronting that abomination, but I feared even more that Henry might catch sight of him. So I begged him to wait a few minutes at the foot of the stairs and rushed upward toward my chamber. My hand was already on the latch before I came to my senses. I halted, and an icy tremor ran through me. I flung the door violently open, the way children do when they expect a ghost to be waiting just beyond the threshold; but nothing was there. I stepped fearfully inside: the room was vacant, and my bedroom was likewise free of its dreadful occupant. I could scarcely believe such a stroke of luck had found me, but once I was certain my adversary had truly vanished, I clapped my hands in joy and raced back down to Clerval.
source_text: I trembled excessively; I could not endure to think of, and far less to allude to, the occurrences of the preceding night. I walked with a quick pace, and we soon arrived at my college. I then reflected, and the thought made me shiver, that the creature whom I had left in my apartment might still be there, alive and walking about. I dreaded to behold this monster, but I feared still more that Henry should see him. Entreating him, therefore, to remain a few minutes at the bottom of the stairs, I darted up towards my own room. My hand was already on the lock of the door before I recollected myself. I then paused, and a cold shivering came over me. I threw the door forcibly open, as children are accustomed to do when they expect a spectre to stand in waiting for them on the other side; but nothing appeared. I stepped fearfully in: the apartment was empty, and my bedroom was also freed from its hideous guest. I could hardly believe that so great a good fortune could have befallen me, but when I became assured that my enemy had indeed fled, I clapped my hands for joy and ran down to Clerval.

text: Our plan was to travel down the Rhine by boat from Strasburgh to Rotterdam, where we could catch a vessel bound for London. Along the way we drifted past numerous islands fringed with willows and caught glimpses of several handsome towns. We paused for a day in Mannheim, and on the fifth morning after leaving Strasburgh, we reached Mainz. Beyond Mainz the Rhine’s character grows far more striking. The current quickens and twists through hills that are modest in height but steep and gracefully shaped. We spotted many ruined castles perched on cliff edges, encircled by dense, forbidding woods that rose high and seemed unreachable. This stretch of the Rhine truly offers a landscape of extraordinary variety. At one moment you face craggy hills, shattered fortresses looming above sheer drops, with the dark Rhine racing below; then, rounding a sudden headland, the view opens onto thriving vineyards, gentle green banks, a winding river, and busy towns.
source_text: We had agreed to descend the Rhine in a boat from Strasburgh to Rotterdam, whence we might take shipping for London. During this voyage we passed many willowy islands and saw several beautiful towns. We stayed a day at Mannheim, and on the fifth from our departure from Strasburgh, arrived at Mainz. The course of the Rhine below Mainz becomes much more picturesque. The river descends rapidly and winds between hills, not high, but steep, and of beautiful forms. We saw many ruined castles standing on the edges of precipices, surrounded by black woods, high and inaccessible. This part of the Rhine, indeed, presents a singularly variegated landscape. In one spot you view rugged hills, ruined castles overlooking tremendous precipices, with the dark Rhine rushing beneath; and on the sudden turn of a promontory, flourishing vineyards with green sloping banks and a meandering river and populous towns occupy the scene.

text: The sleeper shifted; a shudder of dread passed through me. If she truly woke, and glimpsed my face, and damned me, and named the killer? Surely that would be her course, should those clouded eyes open and find me standing there. The notion was lunacy—it roused the demon in my breast: not I, but she, would bear the agony. The death I dealt because I am eternally deprived of all she might have offered me, she must repay. The transgression sprang from her; let the penalty be hers! Schooled by Felix’s teachings and mankind’s bloody statutes, I had now mastered the art of ruin. I leaned above her and tucked the locket deep into a fold of her gown. She stirred once more, and I vanished.
source_text: “The sleeper stirred; a thrill of terror ran through me. Should she indeed awake, and see me, and curse me, and denounce the murderer? Thus would she assuredly act if her darkened eyes opened and she beheld me. The thought was madness; it stirred the fiend within me—not I, but she, shall suffer; the murder I have committed because I am for ever robbed of all that she could give me, she shall atone. The crime had its source in her; be hers the punishment! Thanks to the lessons of Felix and the sanguinary laws of man, I had learned now to work mischief. I bent over her and placed the portrait securely in one of the folds of her dress. She moved again, and I fled.

text: When I looked at the boy, I noticed something shining against his chest. I picked it up—a miniature of an exquisitely beautiful woman. Despite my hatred, it moved and drew me in. For a short while I stared joyfully at her dark eyes, edged with thick lashes, and her graceful mouth; but soon my fury surged back. I recalled that I was permanently cut off from the pleasures such lovely beings could offer, and that the woman whose likeness I studied would, upon seeing me, have replaced that expression of heavenly kindness with one of revulsion and terror.
source_text: “As I fixed my eyes on the child, I saw something glittering on his breast. I took it; it was a portrait of a most lovely woman. In spite of my malignity, it softened and attracted me. For a few moments I gazed with delight on her dark eyes, fringed by deep lashes, and her lovely lips; but presently my rage returned; I remembered that I was for ever deprived of the delights that such beautiful creatures could bestow and that she whose resemblance I contemplated would, in regarding me, have changed that air of divine benignity to one expressive of disgust and affright.

text: I cannot say how long I lay there, but when I came to, the sun had already climbed well into the sky. A fierce wind was blowing, and the swells kept threatening to swamp my little craft. The wind was coming out of the northeast, which meant it must have carried me a great distance from the shore I had set out from. I tried to alter my heading, only to realize at once that any such move would fill the skiff with water in an instant. With no other choice, I could do nothing but run before the gale. I admit that a cold dread crept over me. I had no compass, and my knowledge of this region’s geography was so thin that the sun offered me almost no guidance. I might be swept into the vast Atlantic and endure all the agonies of starvation, or be swallowed by the boundless waters that roared and hammered against my boat. I had already been out for many hours and was suffering the torture of a burning thirst, a foretaste of worse to come. I lifted my eyes to the sky, which was thick with clouds that raced before the wind only to be replaced by others; I looked down at the sea—it was to be my tomb. “Fiend,” I cried, “your work is already done!” I thought of Elizabeth, of my father, of Clerval—all of them left behind, at the mercy of the monster’s bloodthirsty and pitiless rage. This thought pulled me into a trance so hopeless and terrible that even now, with the scene about to close upon me forever, I tremble to recall it.
source_text: I do not know how long I remained in this situation, but when I awoke I found that the sun had already mounted considerably. The wind was high, and the waves continually threatened the safety of my little skiff. I found that the wind was northeast and must have driven me far from the coast from which I had embarked. I endeavoured to change my course but quickly found that if I again made the attempt the boat would be instantly filled with water. Thus situated, my only resource was to drive before the wind. I confess that I felt a few sensations of terror. I had no compass with me and was so slenderly acquainted with the geography of this part of the world that the sun was of little benefit to me. I might be driven into the wide Atlantic and feel all the tortures of starvation or be swallowed up in the immeasurable waters that roared and buffeted around me. I had already been out many hours and felt the torment of a burning thirst, a prelude to my other sufferings. I looked on the heavens, which were covered by clouds that flew before the wind, only to be replaced by others; I looked upon the sea; it was to be my grave. “Fiend,” I exclaimed, “your task is already fulfilled!” I thought of Elizabeth, of my father, and of Clerval—all left behind, on whom the monster might satisfy his sanguinary and merciless passions. This idea plunged me into a reverie so despairing and frightful that even now, when the scene is on the point of closing before me for ever, I shudder to reflect on it.

text: I tried to follow after them, moving no more than a few paces beyond the door, but the world began to spin, my legs stumbled as though I were drunk, and I collapsed at length in complete fatigue. A mist dimmed my sight, and my flesh burned dry with fever’s heat. They bore me inside and laid me upon a bed, while I barely grasped what had occurred; my gaze drifted across the chamber, searching for something I had misplaced.
source_text: I attempted to accompany them and proceeded a short distance from the house, but my head whirled round, my steps were like those of a drunken man, I fell at last in a state of utter exhaustion; a film covered my eyes, and my skin was parched with the heat of fever. In this state I was carried back and placed on a bed, hardly conscious of what had happened; my eyes wandered round the room as if to seek something that I had lost.

text: The sun dipped further down the sky; we crossed the river Drance and watched its course threading the clefts of the upper peaks and the hollows of the lower slopes. Here the Alps press nearer to the lake, and we drew close to the mountain amphitheatre that hems its eastern edge. The spire of Evian gleamed beneath the encircling woods and the tier upon tier of mountains that loomed above it.
source_text: The sun sank lower in the heavens; we passed the river Drance and observed its path through the chasms of the higher and the glens of the lower hills. The Alps here come closer to the lake, and we approached the amphitheatre of mountains which forms its eastern boundary. The spire of Evian shone under the woods that surrounded it and the range of mountain above mountain by which it was overhung.

text: My resolve and endurance were steeled by those mocking taunts; I vowed not to falter in my quest, and beseeching Providence to sustain me, I pressed on with undiminished zeal across boundless wastes, till the sea glimmered afar and marked the final rim of the world. Ah! How alien it was to the azure summers of the south! Sheathed in frost, it could scarcely be told from the shore save by its greater savagery and desolation. The Hellenes shed glad tears when they glimpsed the Aegean from the Asian highlands, and acclaimed with ecstasy the end of their labours. I shed none, but I dropped to my knees and with an overflowing spirit thanked my tutelary power for bringing me unscathed to the spot where I trusted, despite my foe's scorn, to confront and wrestle with him.
source_text: My courage and perseverance were invigorated by these scoffing words; I resolved not to fail in my purpose, and calling on Heaven to support me, I continued with unabated fervour to traverse immense deserts, until the ocean appeared at a distance and formed the utmost boundary of the horizon. Oh! How unlike it was to the blue seasons of the south! Covered with ice, it was only to be distinguished from land by its superior wildness and ruggedness. The Greeks wept for joy when they beheld the Mediterranean from the hills of Asia, and hailed with rapture the boundary of their toils. I did not weep, but I knelt down and with a full heart thanked my guiding spirit for conducting me in safety to the place where I hoped, notwithstanding my adversary’s gibe, to meet and grapple with him.

text: At times I could manage the crushing hopelessness that swallowed me, but at other times the storming emotions of my heart pushed me to find, through physical exertion and a shift of surroundings, some escape from my unbearable feelings. It was during one such episode that I abruptly departed my dwelling, and turning my path toward the nearby Alpine valleys, tried in the grandeur and timelessness of that landscape to lose myself and my fleeting, all-too-human grief. My roaming led me to the valley of Chamounix. I had gone there often in my youth. Six years had gone by since then: _I_ lay in ruins, but nothing had altered in those wild and lasting vistas.
source_text: Sometimes I could cope with the sullen despair that overwhelmed me, but sometimes the whirlwind passions of my soul drove me to seek, by bodily exercise and by change of place, some relief from my intolerable sensations. It was during an access of this kind that I suddenly left my home, and bending my steps towards the near Alpine valleys, sought in the magnificence, the eternity of such scenes, to forget myself and my ephemeral, because human, sorrows. My wanderings were directed towards the valley of Chamounix. I had visited it frequently during my boyhood. Six years had passed since then: _I_ was a wreck, but nought had changed in those savage and enduring scenes.

text: When my senses returned, I was encircled by the folk of the tavern; their faces wore a stunned and breathless dread, yet the terror of those around me seemed but a pale echo, a mere spectre of the anguish that crushed my spirit. I fled from them into the chamber where rested the form of Helena, my beloved, my bride, so lately vibrant, so treasured, so noble. She had been shifted from the attitude in which I had first glimpsed her, and now, as she reclined, her head cradled on her arm and a veil cast across her brow and throat, I could almost have fancied her slumbering. I sprang toward her and clasped her with fervour, but the mortal chill and stillness of her limbs disclosed that what I now gathered in my arms was no longer the Helena whom I had adored and sheltered. The damning imprint of the demon’s grip marked her throat, and no whisper of breath passed her lips.
source_text: When I recovered I found myself surrounded by the people of the inn; their countenances expressed a breathless terror, but the horror of others appeared only as a mockery, a shadow of the feelings that oppressed me. I escaped from them to the room where lay the body of Elizabeth, my love, my wife, so lately living, so dear, so worthy. She had been moved from the posture in which I had first beheld her, and now, as she lay, her head upon her arm and a handkerchief thrown across her face and neck, I might have supposed her asleep. I rushed towards her and embraced her with ardour, but the deadly languor and coldness of the limbs told me that what I now held in my arms had ceased to be the Elizabeth whom I had loved and cherished. The murderous mark of the fiend’s grasp was on her neck, and the breath had ceased to issue from her lips.

text: In solitude, Safie quietly shaped the course she must follow in this crisis. To live in Turkey repelled her; both her faith and her heart recoiled from it. Through certain papers of her father’s that came into her possession, she discovered her lover had been banished and learned the place where he now dwelled. She wavered for a time, but at last her resolve hardened. Gathering a few of her own jewels and a store of money, she left Italy with a servant—a native of Leghorn who knew the common tongue of Turkey—and set out for Germany.
source_text: “When alone, Safie resolved in her own mind the plan of conduct that it would become her to pursue in this emergency. A residence in Turkey was abhorrent to her; her religion and her feelings were alike averse to it. By some papers of her father which fell into her hands she heard of the exile of her lover and learnt the name of the spot where he then resided. She hesitated some time, but at length she formed her determination. Taking with her some jewels that belonged to her and a sum of money, she quitted Italy with an attendant, a native of Leghorn, but who understood the common language of Turkey, and departed for Germany.

text: Upon hearing this testimony, Mr. Kirwin ordered that I be brought into the chamber where the corpse was being prepared for burial, so that my reaction to seeing it might be noted. This notion likely arose from the violent distress I had shown when the manner of the killing was recounted. I was therefore escorted by the magistrate and a small group of others to the inn. I couldn’t suppress a sense of wonder at the bizarre coincidences that had marked that fateful night; yet, aware that I’d been speaking with several residents of the island I lived on around the time the body was discovered, I remained entirely calm about the outcome of the matter.
source_text: Mr. Kirwin, on hearing this evidence, desired that I should be taken into the room where the body lay for interment, that it might be observed what effect the sight of it would produce upon me. This idea was probably suggested by the extreme agitation I had exhibited when the mode of the murder had been described. I was accordingly conducted, by the magistrate and several other persons, to the inn. I could not help being struck by the strange coincidences that had taken place during this eventful night; but, knowing that I had been conversing with several persons in the island I had inhabited about the time that the body had been found, I was perfectly tranquil as to the consequences of the affair.

text: Preparations for the occasion were soon underway; well-wishers arrived with congratulations, and every face beamed with good cheer. I concealed as best I could the gnawing dread within my own heart, and with feigned earnestness I entered into my father’s designs, though they seemed to me only the ornamental drapery of my impending tragedy. Through my father’s efforts, a portion of Elizabeth’s inheritance had been reclaimed for her by the Austrian government. A modest estate on the shores of Como belonged to her. It was settled that, immediately after our marriage, we would journey to Villa Lavenza and spend our first days of happiness beside the lovely lake on whose banks it lay.
source_text: Preparations were made for the event, congratulatory visits were received, and all wore a smiling appearance. I shut up, as well as I could, in my own heart the anxiety that preyed there and entered with seeming earnestness into the plans of my father, although they might only serve as the decorations of my tragedy. Through my father’s exertions a part of the inheritance of Elizabeth had been restored to her by the Austrian government. A small possession on the shores of Como belonged to her. It was agreed that, immediately after our union, we should proceed to Villa Lavenza and spend our first days of happiness beside the beautiful lake near which it stood.

text: A light sleep momentarily freed me from the ache of my thoughts, until it was broken by the arrival of a lovely child, who darted into the secluded nook I had settled in, brimming with the playful energy of early youth.
