As I watched him, a thought struck me—this small being was still untainted, too new to life to have learned revulsion at any misshapen form. If I could but take him and raise him as my companion and friend, I might no longer feel so utterly alone on this crowded earth.
source_text: “At this time a slight sleep relieved me from the pain of reflection, which was disturbed by the approach of a beautiful child, who came running into the recess I had chosen, with all the sportiveness of infancy. Suddenly, as I gazed on him, an idea seized me that this little creature was unprejudiced and had lived too short a time to have imbibed a horror of deformity. If, therefore, I could seize him and educate him as my companion and friend, I should not be so desolate in this peopled earth.

text: A full hour I lingered in that turmoil of thought, until at once it struck me what terror the clash I awaited at any instant must strike into my wife, and I implored her with fervor to withdraw, determined that I would not follow until I had gleaned some clue to the whereabouts of my foe.
source_text: I passed an hour in this state of mind, when suddenly I reflected how fearful the combat which I momentarily expected would be to my wife, and I earnestly entreated her to retire, resolving not to join her until I had obtained some knowledge as to the situation of my enemy.

text: I also recalled the pressing choice before me: either travel to England or begin a protracted exchange of letters with the natural philosophers there, whose knowledge and discoveries were essential to my current work. A correspondence would be slow and deeply unsatisfying. Moreover, I felt an unconquerable repugnance at the thought of carrying out my dreadful labour in my father’s house, surrounded by the daily intimacy of those I cherished. I understood that a thousand terrible mishaps could occur, the smallest of which might reveal a truth that would horrify everyone bound to me. I knew, too, that I would often lose all mastery over myself, all ability to conceal the wrenching emotions that would seize me during the progress of my ghastly occupation. I had to withdraw from everyone I loved while so engaged. Once begun, the work would be finished swiftly, and I could return to my family in tranquillity and joy. My vow fulfilled, the daemon would vanish forever. Or else—so my desperate hope imagined—some accident might intervene to destroy him and end my bondage for all time.
source_text: I remembered also the necessity imposed upon me of either journeying to England or entering into a long correspondence with those philosophers of that country whose knowledge and discoveries were of indispensable use to me in my present undertaking. The latter method of obtaining the desired intelligence was dilatory and unsatisfactory; besides, I had an insurmountable aversion to the idea of engaging myself in my loathsome task in my father’s house while in habits of familiar intercourse with those I loved. I knew that a thousand fearful accidents might occur, the slightest of which would disclose a tale to thrill all connected with me with horror. I was aware also that I should often lose all self-command, all capacity of hiding the harrowing sensations that would possess me during the progress of my unearthly occupation. I must absent myself from all I loved while thus employed. Once commenced, it would quickly be achieved, and I might be restored to my family in peace and happiness. My promise fulfilled, the monster would depart for ever. Or (so my fond fancy imaged) some accident might meanwhile occur to destroy him and put an end to my slavery for ever.

text: Something breaks my focus. What might these noises foretell? The hour is late; the wind moves gently, and the deck watch barely shifts. Once more I hear a sound like a human cry, yet rougher; it rises from the cabin where Frankenstein’s body rests. I have to get up and look. Rest well, my sister.
source_text: I am interrupted. What do these sounds portend? It is midnight; the breeze blows fairly, and the watch on deck scarcely stir. Again there is a sound as of a human voice, but hoarser; it comes from the cabin where the remains of Frankenstein still lie. I must arise and examine. Good night, my sister.

text: The gentle rains and tender warmth of spring transformed the face of the land. Figures who had earlier seemed buried in shadowy hollows now emerged and scattered across the fields, devoting themselves to the various tasks of cultivation. The birds lifted their voices in lighter, gladder strains, and fresh leaves began to unfurl upon the branches. Blessed, blessed earth! A dwelling worthy of gods, where only a brief span before all had been raw, sodden, and sickly. My heart rose with the enchanting beauty of the world around me; the past was erased from my mind, the present lay serene, and the future gleamed with bright shafts of hope and the promise of joy.
source_text: “The pleasant showers and genial warmth of spring greatly altered the aspect of the earth. Men who before this change seemed to have been hid in caves dispersed themselves and were employed in various arts of cultivation. The birds sang in more cheerful notes, and the leaves began to bud forth on the trees. Happy, happy earth! Fit habitation for gods, which, so short a time before, was bleak, damp, and unwholesome. My spirits were elevated by the enchanting appearance of nature; the past was blotted from my memory, the present was tranquil, and the future gilded by bright rays of hope and anticipations of joy.”

text: When I left Geneva, my first task was to seize upon some thread that might lead me along the path of my demonic pursuer. But my intentions were still formless, and I roamed for hours along the city’s edges, unsure which direction to take. As dusk fell, I came to the gates of the graveyard where William, Elizabeth, and my father lay at rest. I passed through and drew near the monument that sheltered their remains. All was still save for the leaves overhead, stirred faintly by the breeze; the darkness was nearly complete, and the sight would have been grave and moving even to a stranger’s eye. The shades of the dead seemed to hover near, casting a presence that was sensed rather than seen upon the mourner’s brow.
source_text: When I quitted Geneva my first labour was to gain some clue by which I might trace the steps of my fiendish enemy. But my plan was unsettled, and I wandered many hours round the confines of the town, uncertain what path I should pursue. As night approached I found myself at the entrance of the cemetery where William, Elizabeth, and my father reposed. I entered it and approached the tomb which marked their graves. Everything was silent except the leaves of the trees, which were gently agitated by the wind; the night was nearly dark, and the scene would have been solemn and affecting even to an uninterested observer. The spirits of the departed seemed to flit around and to cast a shadow, which was felt but not seen, around the head of the mourner.

text: My wretched guest looks upon me with the deepest sympathy. He strives to instill hope in me, speaking as though life were a treasure he cherished. He points out how often the same misfortunes have befallen other explorers who dared this ocean, and against my will, he fills me with bright predictions. Even the crew succumbs to his persuasive charm; when he talks, their despair lifts; he stirs their spirits, and while they listen they imagine these towering ice peaks are mere hillocks that will melt before human determination. Such moods are fleeting; each day of deferred hope plunges them into dread, and I nearly fear a rebellion born of this desperation.
source_text: My unfortunate guest regards me with the tenderest compassion. He endeavours to fill me with hope and talks as if life were a possession which he valued. He reminds me how often the same accidents have happened to other navigators who have attempted this sea, and in spite of myself, he fills me with cheerful auguries. Even the sailors feel the power of his eloquence; when he speaks, they no longer despair; he rouses their energies, and while they hear his voice they believe these vast mountains of ice are mole-hills which will vanish before the resolutions of man. These feelings are transitory; each day of expectation delayed fills them with fear, and I almost dread a mutiny caused by this despair.

text: Had I undertaken this voyage in the years of my contentment and learning, it would have filled me with unutterable delight. But a shadow had fallen across my life, and I sought out these people only for what they might reveal about the matter that consumed me with such dreadful intensity. The presence of others wearied me; in solitude, I could steep my mind in the visions of earth and sky; Henry’s voice brought me comfort, and I could deceive myself into a fleeting calm. Yet bustling, indifferent, cheerful faces plunged me back into despair. I perceived an impassable gulf fixed between me and my fellow creatures, a gulf sealed with the blood of William and Justine, and to dwell upon the memories those names summoned wracked my soul with torment.
source_text: If this journey had taken place during my days of study and happiness, it would have afforded me inexpressible pleasure. But a blight had come over my existence, and I only visited these people for the sake of the information they might give me on the subject in which my interest was so terribly profound. Company was irksome to me; when alone, I could fill my mind with the sights of heaven and earth; the voice of Henry soothed me, and I could thus cheat myself into a transitory peace. But busy, uninteresting, joyous faces brought back despair to my heart. I saw an insurmountable barrier placed between me and my fellow men; this barrier was sealed with the blood of William and Justine, and to reflect on the events connected with those names filled my soul with anguish.

text: “I am,” she began, “the kinswoman of the wretched boy who was slain—his sister, in truth, for I was reared alongside his parents and have dwelt with them from long before his birth. It may be thought unseemly that I should step forward at such a moment, yet when I behold a fellow soul doomed by the faint-heartedness of those who feign friendship, I crave leave to speak, that I may tell what I know of her nature. I have been intimately acquainted with the prisoner. I shared a roof with her, once for a span of five years, and again for nearly two. Throughout that time she seemed to me the gentlest and kindest of beings. She tended Madame Frankenstein, my aunt, through her final decline with the tenderest devotion and vigilance, and later cared for her own mother in a protracted sickness with a grace that drew the wonder of all who witnessed it; afterward she returned to my uncle’s household, where every member cherished her. She was deeply devoted to the child now lost, and behaved toward him as the fondest of mothers. For myself, I do not scruple to declare that, despite every proof arrayed against her, I trust wholly in her blamelessness. She lacked any motive for such a deed; as for the trinket on which the accusation so heavily leans, had she truly longed for it, I would have bestowed it upon her freely—so dearly do I prize and reverence her.”
source_text: “I am,” said she, “the cousin of the unhappy child who was murdered, or rather his sister, for I was educated by and have lived with his parents ever since and even long before his birth. It may therefore be judged indecent in me to come forward on this occasion, but when I see a fellow creature about to perish through the cowardice of her pretended friends, I wish to be allowed to speak, that I may say what I know of her character. I am well acquainted with the accused. I have lived in the same house with her, at one time for five and at another for nearly two years. During all that period she appeared to me the most amiable and benevolent of human creatures. She nursed Madame Frankenstein, my aunt, in her last illness, with the greatest affection and care and afterwards attended her own mother during a tedious illness, in a manner that excited the admiration of all who knew her, after which she again lived in my uncle’s house, where she was beloved by all the family. She was warmly attached to the child who is now dead and acted towards him like a most affectionate mother. For my own part, I do not hesitate to say that, notwithstanding all the evidence produced against her, I believe and rely on her perfect innocence. She had no temptation for such an action; as to the bauble on which the chief proof rests, if she had earnestly desired it, I should have willingly given it to her, so much do I esteem and value her.”

text: Darkness fell swiftly, yet to my utter astonishment, the cottagers possessed a method of extending the day by lighting slender candles, and I rejoiced that the sun’s descent did not end the delight I took in observing my human neighbours. During the evening the young woman and her companion busied themselves with tasks I could not fathom, and the old man once more lifted the device that had poured forth those heavenly melodies which had captivated me at dawn. The moment he ceased, the young man began, not to play, but to produce sounds that were flat and droning, bearing no resemblance to the old man’s melodious strains or the birds’ carolling; I later learned that he was reading aloud, though at that time I understood nothing of the art of words or letters.
source_text: “Night quickly shut in, but to my extreme wonder, I found that the cottagers had a means of prolonging light by the use of tapers, and was delighted to find that the setting of the sun did not put an end to the pleasure I experienced in watching my human neighbours. In the evening the young girl and her companion were employed in various occupations which I did not understand; and the old man again took up the instrument which produced the divine sounds that had enchanted me in the morning. So soon as he had finished, the youth began, not to play, but to utter sounds that were monotonous, and neither resembling the harmony of the old man’s instrument nor the songs of the birds; I since found that he read aloud, but at that time I knew nothing of the science of words or letters.

text: But now, as I drew within arm’s reach of my adversary, my expectations were abruptly quenched, and I lost all sign of him more completely than ever before. A deep swell rose beneath me; the rumble of its advance, as the waters heaved and gathered underfoot, grew each instant more menacing and dreadful. I hurried forward, but to no purpose. The gale awakened; the ocean bellowed; and, like the violent convulsion of an earthquake, it fractured and split with a deafening, ruinous roar. The task was soon complete; within moments a churning sea surged between me and my foe, and I was left adrift upon a crumbling slab of ice that shrank without pause, thus fashioning for me a ghastly end.
source_text: But now, when I appeared almost within grasp of my foe, my hopes were suddenly extinguished, and I lost all trace of him more utterly than I had ever done before. A ground sea was heard; the thunder of its progress, as the waters rolled and swelled beneath me, became every moment more ominous and terrific. I pressed on, but in vain. The wind arose; the sea roared; and, as with the mighty shock of an earthquake, it split and cracked with a tremendous and overwhelming sound. The work was soon finished; in a few minutes a tumultuous sea rolled between me and my enemy, and I was left drifting on a scattered piece of ice that was continually lessening and thus preparing for me a hideous death.

text: The proceedings opened, and once the prosecutor had laid out the accusation, a string of witnesses took the stand. A cluster of peculiar circumstances gathered against her—enough to unnerve anyone lacking the absolute certainty of her innocence that I possessed. She had been absent the entire night of the slaying, and near dawn a costermonger had glimpsed her close to the spot where the slain infant’s corpse was later discovered. The woman demanded what business she had there, but she merely stared queerly and muttered a garbled, incoherent reply. She came home near eight in the morning, and when someone asked where she had spent the night, she answered that she had been searching for the child and pressed urgently for any tidings of him. Upon being shown the remains, she collapsed into fierce hysterics and did not leave her bed for days. Then the miniature was presented—the one the maid had uncovered in her pocket—and when Elizabeth, her voice trembling, confirmed it was the very likeness she had fastened around the boy’s neck an hour before he vanished, a low surge of shock and outrage swept through the courtroom.
source_text: The trial began, and after the advocate against her had stated the charge, several witnesses were called. Several strange facts combined against her, which might have staggered anyone who had not such proof of her innocence as I had. She had been out the whole of the night on which the murder had been committed and towards morning had been perceived by a market-woman not far from the spot where the body of the murdered child had been afterwards found. The woman asked her what she did there, but she looked very strangely and only returned a confused and unintelligible answer. She returned to the house about eight o’clock, and when one inquired where she had passed the night, she replied that she had been looking for the child and demanded earnestly if anything had been heard concerning him. When shown the body, she fell into violent hysterics and kept her bed for several days. The picture was then produced which the servant had found in her pocket; and when Elizabeth, in a faltering voice, proved that it was the same which, an hour before the child had been missed, she had placed round his neck, a murmur of horror and indignation filled the court.

text: “I’m glad to see, my dear son, that you’ve taken up your old pastimes again and appear to be coming back to who you were. Still, you remain unhappy and keep away from our company. For a while I puzzled over what might be causing this, but yesterday a thought came to me, and if it’s true, I urge you to confess it. Holding back on such a matter would not only be pointless, but would bring threefold suffering upon all of us.”
source_text: “I am happy to remark, my dear son, that you have resumed your former pleasures and seem to be returning to yourself. And yet you are still unhappy and still avoid our society. For some time I was lost in conjecture as to the cause of this, but yesterday an idea struck me, and if it is well founded, I conjure you to avow it. Reserve on such a point would be not only useless, but draw down treble misery on us all.”

text: “Victor, my heart, please come. Only you can soothe Elizabeth now. She cries without end, blaming herself without reason for his passing, and each word she speaks cuts me to the soul. Sorrow grips us all—yet should that not move you, my son, to come back and be the balm for our wounds? Your sweet mother! Oh, Victor, I can only murmur, Thank Heaven she was spared the sight of her youngest beloved’s cruel, wretched end.”
source_text: “Come, dearest Victor; you alone can console Elizabeth. She weeps continually, and accuses herself unjustly as the cause of his death; her words pierce my heart. We are all unhappy; but will not that be an additional motive for you, my son, to return and be our comforter? Your dear mother! Alas, Victor! I now say, Thank God she did not live to witness the cruel, miserable death of her youngest darling!

text: For my part, I encountered a break in the action just then, but I didn't mind—it couldn't have come at a more opportune moment. Had it occurred sooner, it might have vexed me, but as things stood, all was well managed and progressing smoothly. The king had lately dropped several reminders that the four-year deferment I'd requested was nearly at its end. He was nudging me to set forth, seek out exploits, and build a reputation substantial enough to merit the honor of crossing swords with Sir Sagramor, who remained abroad on his grail quest, though various rescue parties were searching for him and he might be located any year now. So you understand, I was anticipating this disruption; it caught me off guard not at all.
source_text: Personally, I struck an interruption, now, but I did not mind it, it could not have happened at a better time. Earlier it could have annoyed me, but now everything was in good hands and swimming right along. The king had reminded me several times, of late, that the postponement I had asked for, four years before, had about run out now. It was a hint that I ought to be starting out to seek adventures and get up a reputation of a size to make me worthy of the honor of breaking a lance with Sir Sagramor, who was still out grailing, but was being hunted for by various relief expeditions, and might be found any year, now. So you see I was expecting this interruption; it did not take me by surprise.

text: Sir Gawaine and Sir Uwaine journeyed on until they reached a monastery, where the monks gave them comfortable lodging. In the morning they attended Mass within the abbey, then mounted and rode forward until they entered a vast forest. There, in a valley near a tower, Sir Gawaine caught sight of twelve lovely maidens and two knights armed and seated upon tall horses; the maidens were passing back and forth beside a tree. Then Sir Gawaine noticed a white shield hanging from that tree, and each time the damsels drew near it they spat upon it, and some flung mud onto the shield—
source_text: “Sir Gawaine and Sir Uwaine. And so they came to an abbey of monks, and there were well lodged. So on the morn they heard their masses in the abbey, and so they rode forth till they came to a great forest; then was Sir Gawaine ware in a valley by a turret, of twelve fair damsels, and two knights armed on great horses, and the damsels went to and fro by a tree. And then was Sir Gawaine ware how there hung a white shield on that tree, and ever as the damsels came by it they spit upon it, and some threw mire upon the shield--”

text: Meanwhile, a thought had been lurking in the back of my mind, half-formed: that once word of the disaster I intended reached those superstitious folk, they’d be shaken enough to seek a settlement. So when I caught the sound of approaching footsteps, that notion surfaced again, and I told myself, “This will be the compromise, I’m certain. If it’s fair, fine—I’ll take it. But if not, I’m resolved to hold firm and play my hand for everything it’s worth.”
source_text: Meantime there was one thing which had got pushed into the background of my mind. That was the half-conviction that when the nature of my proposed calamity should be reported to those superstitious people, it would have such an effect that they would want to compromise. So, by and by when I heard footsteps coming, that thought was recalled to me, and I said to myself, “As sure as anything, it's the compromise. Well, if it is good, all right, I will accept; but if it isn't, I mean to stand my ground and play my hand for all it is worth.”

text: And spun into the violet blaze! There they erupted, all together—crimson, cobalt, emerald, amethyst!—four raging volcanoes heaving colossal plumes of glowing fume skyward, unfurling a dazzling rainbow noon across the farthest reaches of that basin. Far off, you could make out the figure on the pillar frozen against the skyline, his seesaw stilled for the first time in two decades. I knew the lads were at the pump by now and set. So I told the abbot:
source_text: --and whirled on the purple glare! There they were, all going at once, red, blue, green, purple!--four furious volcanoes pouring vast clouds of radiant smoke aloft, and spreading a blinding rainbowed noonday to the furthest confines of that valley. In the distance one could see that fellow on the pillar standing rigid against the background of sky, his seesaw stopped for the first time in twenty years. I knew the boys were at the pump now and ready. So I said to the abbot:

text: But the plan collapsed like a house of cards! As soon as I rounded the first bend, I ran smack into one of our servants, skulking about with a guard. I coughed just then, and he shot me a glance that cut straight to the bone. I figured he thought he’d heard that cough somewhere before. I ducked right into a shop and edged along the counter, pretending to examine goods while watching from the corner of my eye. The two men had halted, chatting together and peering in through the doorway. I decided to slip out the rear exit, if one existed, and I asked the shopkeeper if I might step back there to search for the runaway slave, who was rumored to be hiding somewhere out back. I told her I was an officer in plain clothes, and my partner was over by the front door holding one of the killers in custody. Would she be kind enough to go tell him not to wait, but to head straight to the far end of the back alley and be ready to cut the fugitive off when I flushed him out.
source_text: But the scheme fell through like scat! The first corner I turned, I came plump upon one of our slaves, snooping around with a watchman. I coughed at the moment, and he gave me a sudden look that bit right into my marrow. I judge he thought he had heard that cough before. I turned immediately into a shop and worked along down the counter, pricing things and watching out of the corner of my eye. Those people had stopped, and were talking together and looking in at the door. I made up my mind to get out the back way, if there was a back way, and I asked the shopwoman if I could step out there and look for the escaped slave, who was believed to be in hiding back there somewhere, and said I was an officer in disguise, and my pard was yonder at the door with one of the murderers in charge, and would she be good enough to step there and tell him he needn't wait, but had better go at once to the further end of the back alley and be ready to head him off when I rousted him out.

text: Ordinarily, these folk conducted themselves with a refined and ceremonious courtesy; and I observed that, whenever no brawl was stirring, they made excellent and earnest listeners to any speaker’s tale. They were, moreover, a guileless and childlike company: they would spin falsehoods of the most majestic design with a tender, disarming simplicity, and were ever eager to receive another’s fabrication and credit it wholeheartedly. One struggled to connect them with any thought of brutality or horror; yet they traded in chronicles of anguish and slaughter with such an artless delight that I nearly forgot to recoil.
source_text: As a rule, the speech and behavior of these people were gracious and courtly; and I noticed that they were good and serious listeners when anybody was telling anything--I mean in a dog-fightless interval. And plainly, too, they were a childlike and innocent lot; telling lies of the stateliest pattern with a most gentle and winning naivety, and ready and willing to listen to anybody else's lie, and believe it, too. It was hard to associate them with anything cruel or dreadful; and yet they dealt in tales of blood and suffering with a guileless relish that made me almost forget to shudder.

text: Did they yearn to discover what occupied the Exalted Ruler of Inde? Indeed. He revealed to them the Exalted Ruler’s pursuits. Next he recounted the designs of the Sultan of Egypt; then the schemes of the Monarch of the Distant Oceans. And onward he went; with every fresh wonder their awe at his faultlessness swelled further. They imagined he must at last stumble upon some uncertain patch; yet he never faltered, he perpetually knew, and perpetually with flawless exactitude. I perceived that if this continued I would forfeit my dominance, this stranger would seize my disciples, I would be cast aside into oblivion. I must jam a spoke into his turning wheel, and do it instantly, as well. I spoke:
source_text: Would they like to know what the Supreme Lord of Inde was doing? Yes. He told them what the Supreme Lord of Inde was doing. Then he told them what the Sultan of Egypt was at; also what the King of the Remote Seas was about. And so on and so on; and with each new marvel the astonishment at his accuracy rose higher and higher. They thought he must surely strike an uncertain place some time; but no, he never had to hesitate, he always knew, and always with unerring precision. I saw that if this thing went on I should lose my supremacy, this fellow would capture my following, I should be left out in the cold. I must put a cog in his wheel, and do it right away, too. I said:

text: Yes, what a victory it is to understand this! No one can wound him now, no one can slight him again. He dwells in heaven and is at peace; or if not there, he abides in hell and is satisfied—for in that realm he will encounter neither abbot nor bishop. We were children together, boy and girl; we were husband and wife for twenty-five years, and never parted until this day. Consider how long that is to love and endure together. This morning his mind had slipped away, and in his imagining we were boy and girl once more, roaming the joyous meadows; and so, in that blameless, happy talk, he wandered on and farther, still murmuring softly, and stepped into those other fields unknown to us, and was hidden from mortal eyes. And thus there was no farewell, for in his fancy I walked beside him; he did not know but that I went with him, my hand in his—my young, tender hand, not this withered claw. Ah, yes, to go and not know it; to be severed and not know it; how could one slip away more gently than that? It was his recompense for a harsh life endured without complaint.
source_text: “Yes, what triumph it is to know it! None can harm him, none insult him more. He is in heaven now, and happy; or if not there, he bides in hell and is content; for in that place he will find neither abbot nor yet bishop. We were boy and girl together; we were man and wife these five and twenty years, and never separated till this day. Think how long that is to love and suffer together. This morning was he out of his mind, and in his fancy we were boy and girl again and wandering in the happy fields; and so in that innocent glad converse wandered he far and farther, still lightly gossiping, and entered into those other fields we know not of, and was shut away from mortal sight. And so there was no parting, for in his fancy I went with him; he knew not but I went with him, my hand in his--my young soft hand, not this withered claw. Ah, yes, to go, and know it not; to separate and know it not; how could one go peacefuler than that? It was his reward for a cruel life patiently borne.”

text: I had done it again—bungled things thoroughly, this time with a twofold blunder. There were a dozen simple, credible ways to shake off that officer, but no, I had to choose a theatrical one; it’s the fatal flaw in my nature. And then I had banked my whole plan on what the man, being human, would *naturally* do—forgetting that just when you least expect it, a fellow will turn around and do the one thing that isn’t natural at all. The natural course for the officer was to stick right to my heels; he’d come up against a solid oaken door, firmly locked between us, and long before he could smash it down I’d be far off, slipping into a chain of bewildering disguises that would soon wrap me in a costume far safer from meddling British law-dogs than any amount of spotless innocence and virtue. But instead of doing the natural thing, he took me at my word and followed my directions to the letter. And so, just as I came trotting out of that blind alley, brimming with self-congratulation, he rounded the corner and I walked straight into his handcuffs. If only I’d known it was a dead end—still, there’s no excusing a blunder like that; let it pass. Mark it down to profit and loss.
source_text: Well, I had gone and spoiled it again, made another mistake. A double one, in fact. There were plenty of ways to get rid of that officer by some simple and plausible device, but no, I must pick out a picturesque one; it is the crying defect of my character. And then, I had ordered my procedure upon what the officer, being human, would _naturally_ do; whereas when you are least expecting it, a man will now and then go and do the very thing which it's _not_ natural for him to do. The natural thing for the officer to do, in this case, was to follow straight on my heels; he would find a stout oaken door, securely locked, between him and me; before he could break it down, I should be far away and engaged in slipping into a succession of baffling disguises which would soon get me into a sort of raiment which was a surer protection from meddling law-dogs in Britain than any amount of mere innocence and purity of character. But instead of doing the natural thing, the officer took me at my word, and followed my instructions. And so, as I came trotting out of that cul de sac, full of satisfaction with my own cleverness, he turned the corner and I walked right into his handcuffs. If I had known it was a cul de sac--however, there isn't any excusing a blunder like that, let it go. Charge it up to profit and loss.

text: Merlin, that colossal fraud and sorcerer—may hellfire scorch him for the exhaustion he inflicts with his single story! If people did not dread him because he commands the tempests, the lightning bolts, and every fiend lurking in the pit at his whim, they would have torn his guts out long ago just to seize that tale and silence it. He always tells it in the third person, pretending he is too humble to sing his own praises—curses rain down upon him, may sorrow be his portion! Dear friend, please summon me when it is time for evening prayer.
source_text: “Merlin, the mighty liar and magician, perdition singe him for the weariness he worketh with his one tale! But that men fear him for that he hath the storms and the lightnings and all the devils that be in hell at his beck and call, they would have dug his entrails out these many years ago to get at that tale and squelch it. He telleth it always in the third person, making believe he is too modest to glorify himself--maledictions light upon him, misfortune be his dole! Good friend, prithee call me for evensong.”

text: I settled the king into a comfortable spot by the road and gave him a few bites to quiet his hunger. Then I told him I’d fetch some water and wandered off. Part of my aim was to slip out of sight, sit down, and catch a moment’s rest myself. It was my habit never to sit in his presence—even at council meetings, unless the session dragged on for hours. On those rare occasions I used a small, backless stool shaped like an overturned culvert, about as soothing as a toothache. I had no intention of shocking him all at once; I meant to ease him into it gradually. From now on we’d have to sit together in company, or people would remark on it, but it would be poor strategy to flaunt equality with him when there was no need.
source_text: I found a comfortable seat for the king by the roadside, and then gave him a morsel or two to stay his stomach with. Then I said I would find some water for him, and strolled away. Part of my project was to get out of sight and sit down and rest a little myself. It had always been my custom to stand when in his presence; even at the council board, except upon those rare occasions when the sitting was a very long one, extending over hours; then I had a trifling little backless thing which was like a reversed culvert and was as comfortable as the toothache. I didn't want to break him in suddenly, but do it by degrees. We should have to sit together now when in company, or people would notice; but it would not be good politics for me to be playing equality with him when there was no necessity for it.

text: Through my sorrow and anguish, I managed to stammer a few words—enough to suggest I would show mercy to the sun. In return, the boy’s eyes shone with such profound and tender thankfulness that I couldn’t bring myself to confess that his kind, foolish act had undone me and condemned me to my end.
source_text: I choked out some words through my grief and misery; as much as to say I would spare the sun; for which the lad's eyes paid me back with such deep and loving gratitude that I had not the heart to tell him his good-hearted foolishness had ruined me and sent me to my death.

text: But in that place there was ever a scarcity of water. Once, the holy abbot prayed, and in answer a mighty spring of crystalline water broke forth miraculously in the barren wild. Then were the wayward monks tempted by the Fiend, and they pressed their abbot without rest, with pleadings and entreaties, that he should build a bath; and when he was worn down and could no longer withstand them, he said, Have your desire, then, and granted what they sought. Now behold what it is to abandon the ways of purity which He loves, and to dally with things worldly and profane. Those monks went into the bath and came out washed white as snow; and see, in that instant His sign appeared, a miraculous chastisement! for His affronted waters ceased their flowing and vanished utterly away.
source_text: “But always there was lack of water there. Whereas, upon a time, the holy abbot prayed, and for answer a great stream of clear water burst forth by miracle in a desert place. Now were the fickle monks tempted of the Fiend, and they wrought with their abbot unceasingly by beggings and beseechings that he would construct a bath; and when he was become aweary and might not resist more, he said have ye your will, then, and granted that they asked. Now mark thou what 'tis to forsake the ways of purity the which He loveth, and wanton with such as be worldly and an offense. These monks did enter into the bath and come thence washed as white as snow; and lo, in that moment His sign appeared, in miraculous rebuke! for His insulted waters ceased to flow, and utterly vanished away.”

text: I longed to halt the entire affair and loose the bondsmen on the spot, yet such a course was impossible. I dared not meddle overmuch and earn a reputation for trampling the laws of the land and the liberties of its people underfoot. Should I survive and thrive, I meant to be the ruin of bondage—on that I was fixed—but I would strive to arrange matters so that when I struck the fatal blow, it would fall by the nation’s own decree.
source_text: I wanted to stop the whole thing and set the slaves free, but that would not do. I must not interfere too much and get myself a name for riding over the country's laws and the citizen's rights roughshod. If I lived and prospered I would be the death of slavery, that I was resolved upon; but I would try to fix it so that when I became its executioner it should be by command of the nation.

text: Up to the appointed day, the whole of Britain spoke of nothing but this coming trial. Every other subject dwindled into irrelevance and faded from men’s minds. It was not because a tournament was, in itself, a grand affair; it was not because Sir Sagramor had discovered the Holy Grail—he had not, but had instead failed; nor was it because the second-highest personage in the realm stood as one of the combatants. No, all those features were ordinary enough. Yet there was ample cause for the extraordinary fascination this impending fight aroused. It sprang from the nation’s knowledge that this was to be no duel between mere men, as it were, but a contest between two mighty magicians—a duel not of sinew but of intellect, not of human prowess but of supernatural art and cunning; a decisive struggle for mastery between the age’s two supreme enchanters. Everyone understood that the most prodigious feats of the most celebrated knights could not compare with such a spectacle; they would be mere child’s play beside this mysterious and dreadful battle of gods. Indeed, the whole world knew it was, in truth, to be a duel between Merlin and myself, a testing of his magical powers against my own. It was common knowledge that Merlin had labored whole days and nights together, steeping Sir Sagramor’s arms and armor in supernal powers of attack and defense, and that he had obtained for him, from the spirits of the air, a fleecy veil that would render its wearer invisible to his opponent while remaining visible to all others. Against Sir Sagramor, so armed and shielded, a thousand knights could achieve nothing; no known enchantments could prevail against him. These facts were certain; of them there was no doubt, nor any reason for doubt. Only one question remained: might there exist still other enchantments, unknown to Merlin, that could render Sir Sagramor’s veil transparent to my sight and make his enchanted mail vulnerable to my weapons? That was the single thing to be decided in the lists. Until that hour, the world must hang in suspense.
source_text: Up to the day set, there was no talk in all Britain of anything but this combat. All other topics sank into insignificance and passed out of men's thoughts and interest. It was not because a tournament was a great matter, it was not because Sir Sagramor had found the Holy Grail, for he had not, but had failed; it was not because the second (official) personage in the kingdom was one of the duellists; no, all these features were commonplace. Yet there was abundant reason for the extraordinary interest which this coming fight was creating. It was born of the fact that all the nation knew that this was not to be a duel between mere men, so to speak, but a duel between two mighty magicians; a duel not of muscle but of mind, not of human skill but of superhuman art and craft; a final struggle for supremacy between the two master enchanters of the age. It was realized that the most prodigious achievements of the most renowned knights could not be worthy of comparison with a spectacle like this; they could be but child's play, contrasted with this mysterious and awful battle of the gods. Yes, all the world knew it was going to be in reality a duel between Merlin and me, a measuring of his magic powers against mine. It was known that Merlin had been busy whole days and nights together, imbuing Sir Sagramor's arms and armor with supernal powers of offense and defense, and that he had procured for him from the spirits of the air a fleecy veil which would render the wearer invisible to his antagonist while still visible to other men. Against Sir Sagramor, so weaponed and protected, a thousand knights could accomplish nothing; against him no known enchantments could prevail. These facts were sure; regarding them there was no doubt, no reason for doubt. There was but one question: might there be still other enchantments, _unknown_ to Merlin, which could render Sir Sagramor's veil transparent to me, and make his enchanted mail vulnerable to my weapons? This was the one thing to be decided in the lists. Until then the world must remain in suspense.

text: My sway in the Valley of Holiness had grown immense. It struck me as worth channeling toward some practical gain. The notion arrived the following morning, sparked by the sight of one of my knights—a man in the soap trade—riding into camp. If history was accurate, the monks here, two hundred years past, had been worldly enough to crave a wash. Perhaps a residue of that impious inclination still lingered. So I probed a Brother.
source_text: My influence in the Valley of Holiness was something prodigious now. It seemed worth while to try to turn it to some valuable account. The thought came to me the next morning, and was suggested by my seeing one of my knights who was in the soap line come riding in. According to history, the monks of this place two centuries before had been worldly minded enough to want to wash. It might be that there was a leaven of this unrighteousness still remaining. So I sounded a Brother:

text: Sir Kay now got to his feet and started stoking the engine of his tall tales with me as kindling. It was high time I took things seriously—and I did. He recounted how he had come upon me in some distant country of savages, all dressed in the same absurd costume I wore—a costume wrought by magic, meant to shield its wearer from all harm by mortal hands. But he had broken the spell’s power through prayer, slain my thirteen knights in a battle lasting three hours, and captured me, granting me my life only so that such an outlandish oddity as myself could be displayed to the awe and acclaim of the king and his court. Throughout, he referred to me in the smoothest tones as “this stupendous giant,” “this dreadful sky-scraping monster,” and “this fanged and clawed man-eating ogre,” and everyone swallowed all this rubbish with the most artless credulity, never once cracking a smile or appearing to register any gap between these inflated figures and my actual self. He claimed that while trying to flee him I vaulted into the crown of a tree two hundred cubits high in a single leap, but he knocked me loose with a boulder the size of a cow, which “all-to brast” most of my bones, and then bound me by oath to present myself at Arthur’s court for judgment. He finished by sentencing me to die at noon on the twenty-first, and was so untroubled by the matter that he paused to yawn before naming the day.
source_text: Now Sir Kay arose and began to fire up on his history-mill with me for fuel. It was time for me to feel serious, and I did. Sir Kay told how he had encountered me in a far land of barbarians, who all wore the same ridiculous garb that I did--a garb that was a work of enchantment, and intended to make the wearer secure from hurt by human hands. However he had nullified the force of the enchantment by prayer, and had killed my thirteen knights in a three hours' battle, and taken me prisoner, sparing my life in order that so strange a curiosity as I was might be exhibited to the wonder and admiration of the king and the court. He spoke of me all the time, in the blandest way, as “this prodigious giant,” and “this horrible sky-towering monster,” and “this tusked and taloned man-devouring ogre”, and everybody took in all this bosh in the naivest way, and never smiled or seemed to notice that there was any discrepancy between these watered statistics and me. He said that in trying to escape from him I sprang into the top of a tree two hundred cubits high at a single bound, but he dislodged me with a stone the size of a cow, which “all-to brast” the most of my bones, and then swore me to appear at Arthur's court for sentence. He ended by condemning me to die at noon on the 21st; and was so little concerned about it that he stopped to yawn before he named the date.

text: He traced an imagined circle across the stone rooftop and set a pinch of powder aflame inside it. A slender plume of fragrant smoke curled upward, and at the sight everyone shrank back, crossing themselves with visible unease. Then he started murmuring and carving gestures through the air with his hands. Slowly, deliberately, he worked himself into a kind of fever, until he was flailing his arms about like the sails of a windmill. By now the storm was nearly upon us; gusts whipped the torch flames and sent shadows lurching across the walls, the first fat drops of rain began to fall, the world beyond us had turned pitch-black, and the lightning flickered in nervous bursts. My rod, of course, would be charging itself at this very moment. Everything was pressing close. So I said:
source_text: He drew an imaginary circle on the stones of the roof, and burnt a pinch of powder in it, which sent up a small cloud of aromatic smoke, whereat everybody fell back and began to cross themselves and get uncomfortable. Then he began to mutter and make passes in the air with his hands. He worked himself up slowly and gradually into a sort of frenzy, and got to thrashing around with his arms like the sails of a windmill. By this time the storm had about reached us; the gusts of wind were flaring the torches and making the shadows swash about, the first heavy drops of rain were falling, the world abroad was black as pitch, the lightning began to wink fitfully. Of course, my rod would be loading itself now. In fact, things were imminent. So I said:

text: Is it credible? The sovereign and his entire Circle of Champions were beside themselves with delight at this absurd invitation to peril. Each lord of the fellowship sprang at the prospect, pleading for it; yet to their mortification and dismay the monarch bestowed the quest upon me, who had never sought it in the slightest.
source_text: Would you believe it? The king and the whole Round Table were in raptures over this preposterous opportunity for adventure. Every knight of the Table jumped for the chance, and begged for it; but to their vexation and chagrin the king conferred it upon me, who had not asked for it at all.

text: True—it had slipped my mind entirely, so deeply was I absorbed in mapping out a vast campaign against Gaul"—by now he was on his feet, though a farm might have risen faster if there'd been any sort of surge in the property market—"and just then a notion came drifting crosswise through this grand vision, one that—
source_text: “True--I had forgot it, so lost was I in planning of a huge war with Gaul”--he was up by this time, but a farm could have got up quicker, if there was any kind of a boom in real estate--“and right-so a thought came randoming overthwart this majestic dream the which--”

text: Ah, it was truly magnificent—a genuine masterpiece. I can't recall ever constructing a scene more skillfully, or squeezing such delightfully dramatic results from the ingredients at hand. The blacksmith—well, the man was utterly crushed. Good heavens! I wouldn't have wanted to experience what he was going through, not for all the riches on earth. There he'd been, puffing up and boasting about his lavish meat-feast held twice each year, his fresh meat twice a month, his salt meat twice a week, and his white bread every Sunday without fail—all to feed a household of three; the whole annual expense amounting to no more than 69.2.6 (sixty-nine cents, two mills, and six milrays). Then, out of nowhere, a stranger appears who casually throws down nearly four dollars on a single extravagant meal—and what's more, behaves as though handling such trifling sums exhausted him. Yes, Dowley was thoroughly deflated, shriveled and crumpled; he resembled a bladder-balloon that's been trampled by a cow.
source_text: Ah, well, it was immense; yes, it was a daisy. I don't know that I ever put a situation together better, or got happier spectacular effects out of the materials available. The blacksmith--well, he was simply mashed. Land! I wouldn't have felt what that man was feeling, for anything in the world. Here he had been blowing and bragging about his grand meat-feast twice a year, and his fresh meat twice a month, and his salt meat twice a week, and his white bread every Sunday the year round--all for a family of three; the entire cost for the year not above 69.2.6 (sixty-nine cents, two mills and six milrays), and all of a sudden here comes along a man who slashes out nearly four dollars on a single blow-out; and not only that, but acts as if it made him tired to handle such small sums. Yes, Dowley was a good deal wilted, and shrunk-up and collapsed; he had the aspect of a bladder-balloon that's been stepped on by a cow.

text: We stole off an hour before first light, and by the time the sun stood high we'd covered eight or ten miles, deep in a thinly peopled countryside. My pack sat weighty on my back, stuffed with food—supplies meant to wean the king gently onto the rougher local diet without harm.
source_text: We slipped away an hour before dawn, and by broad sun-up had made eight or ten miles, and were in the midst of a sparsely settled country. I had a pretty heavy knapsack; it was laden with provisions--provisions for the king to taper down on, till he could take to the coarse fare of the country without damage.

text: I took Clarence partly into my trust, and we set to work in secret. I explained that this was a kind of wonder needing a small amount of setup, and that speaking of these arrangements to anyone would mean instant death. That kept him securely silent. In hiding, we produced several bushels of excellent blasting powder, and I oversaw my metalworkers as they built a lightning rod and some cables. The ancient stone tower was enormously solid—and somewhat decayed, too, since it was Roman and stood four centuries old. It was also striking, in a rough way, and wrapped in ivy from bottom to top, like a coat of chain mail. It rose on a solitary hill, clearly visible from the castle, about half a mile distant.
source_text: I took Clarence into my confidence, to a certain degree, and we went to work privately. I told him that this was a sort of miracle that required a trifle of preparation, and that it would be sudden death to ever talk about these preparations to anybody. That made his mouth safe enough. Clandestinely we made a few bushels of first-rate blasting powder, and I superintended my armorers while they constructed a lightning-rod and some wires. This old stone tower was very massive--and rather ruinous, too, for it was Roman, and four hundred years old. Yes, and handsome, after a rude fashion, and clothed with ivy from base to summit, as with a shirt of scale mail. It stood on a lonely eminence, in good view from the castle, and about half a mile away.

text: But the crowd soon reached the same judgment on its own; accordingly, they halted the attack and fell to discussing fresh schemes. Arms they lacked, yet stones lay everywhere, and stones might serve. We offered no complaint. A stone might chance to find us now and then, but the odds were slight; we lay well screened by branches and leaves, hidden from any decent vantage for a throw. If they would only squander thirty minutes in pelting us, dusk would arrive to aid us. We felt deeply content. We could smile; very nearly laugh.
source_text: However, the mob soon came to that conclusion themselves; wherefore they called off the assault and began to debate other plans. They had no weapons, but there were plenty of stones, and stones might answer. We had no objections. A stone might possibly penetrate to us once in a while, but it wasn't very likely; we were well protected by boughs and foliage, and were not visible from any good aiming point. If they would but waste half an hour in stone-throwing, the dark would come to our help. We were feeling very well satisfied. We could smile; almost laugh.

text: By now I’d had my fill of that grim spot and longed to get away, but I couldn’t, because something kept nagging at my mind—my conscience wouldn’t quit jabbing me and refused to let it go. If I could redesign human beings, I’d leave out the conscience entirely. It’s one of the most irritating fixtures a person carries around; and though it admittedly accomplishes a fair amount of good, it hardly repays the trouble in the long term—you’d be far better off with less virtue and more ease. Still, that’s only my view, and I’m just one man; others with less mileage might see it otherwise, and they’re entitled to their opinion. I’ll only say this: I’ve watched my conscience for many years, and I know it causes me more vexation and nuisance than anything else I was born with. I imagine that at first I treasured it, the way we treasure whatever belongs to us—but what a foolish notion that was. Look at it from another angle and you see how ridiculous it is: if I had an anvil inside me, would I treasure that? Of course not. Yet when you really consider it, there’s no practical difference between a conscience and an anvil—I mean in terms of comfort. I’ve observed it a thousand times. And you could dissolve an anvil with acids once you couldn’t bear it any longer; but there’s no method for dissolving a conscience—at least not so it stays dissolved; none that I’ve ever discovered, anyway.
source_text: I had had enough of this grisly place by this time, and wanted to leave, but I couldn't, because I had something on my mind that my conscience kept prodding me about, and wouldn't let me forget. If I had the remaking of man, he wouldn't have any conscience. It is one of the most disagreeable things connected with a person; and although it certainly does a great deal of good, it cannot be said to pay, in the long run; it would be much better to have less good and more comfort. Still, this is only my opinion, and I am only one man; others, with less experience, may think differently. They have a right to their view. I only stand to this: I have noticed my conscience for many years, and I know it is more trouble and bother to me than anything else I started with. I suppose that in the beginning I prized it, because we prize anything that is ours; and yet how foolish it was to think so. If we look at it in another way, we see how absurd it is: if I had an anvil in me would I prize it? Of course not. And yet when you come to think, there is no real difference between a conscience and an anvil--I mean for comfort. I have noticed it a thousand times. And you could dissolve an anvil with acids, when you couldn't stand it any longer; but there isn't any way that you can work off a conscience--at least so it will stay worked off; not that I know of, anyway.

text: I am a son of this nation. I first drew breath and grew to manhood in Hartford, Connecticut—or, to be precise, just across the water, out in the countryside. So I count myself a Yankee through and through—a pragmatist; yes, and almost wholly empty of sentiment, I'd say—or poetry, to call it by its name. My father worked the forge, my uncle tended horses, and I followed both trades in the beginning. Later I entered the great armory and mastered my true craft; learned every inch of it; learned to build whatever was needed: rifles, sidearms, field guns, steam vessels, engines, every kind of mechanism that spares a man his sweat. Indeed, I could fashion anything a fellow might ask for—anything under the sun, no matter its nature; and if no brisk modern method existed for the making, I could devise one—and accomplish it as smoothly as slipping off a stone. I rose to chief superintendent; held two thousand workers under my charge.
source_text: I am an American. I was born and reared in Hartford, in the State of Connecticut--anyway, just over the river, in the country. So I am a Yankee of the Yankees--and practical; yes, and nearly barren of sentiment, I suppose--or poetry, in other words. My father was a blacksmith, my uncle was a horse doctor, and I was both, along at first. Then I went over to the great arms factory and learned my real trade; learned all there was to it; learned to make everything: guns, revolvers, cannon, boilers, engines, all sorts of labor-saving machinery. Why, I could make anything a body wanted--anything in the world, it didn't make any difference what; and if there wasn't any quick new-fangled way to make a thing, I could invent one--and do it as easy as rolling off a log. I became head superintendent; had a couple of thousand men under me.

text: One fact appeared beyond dispute: the current ran so fiercely that it slew before a man could utter a sound. Soon we caught a dull, heavy noise, and in the next instant we understood it. A surprise assault was rolling toward us! I murmured to Clarence to rouse the army and instruct it to wait silently in the cave for further commands. He returned quickly, and we stood beside the inner fence and watched the mute lightning perform its dreadful labor on that teeming multitude. Little detail was visible; yet one could see a dark mass piling up beyond the second fence. That growing mound was dead men! Our camp was ringed by a solid wall of the dead—a rampart, a breastwork, of bodies, you might say. One horrifying feature of this was the absence of human voices; no cheers rose, no battle cries; bent on surprise, these men advanced as soundlessly as possible; and always when the foremost rank drew close enough to their target that it would have been fitting to begin raising a shout, they inevitably hit the deadly line and collapsed without a sound.
source_text: One thing seemed to be sufficiently demonstrated: our current was so tremendous that it killed before the victim could cry out. Pretty soon we detected a muffled and heavy sound, and next moment we guessed what it was. It was a surprise in force coming! I whispered Clarence to go and wake the army, and notify it to wait in silence in the cave for further orders. He was soon back, and we stood by the inner fence and watched the silent lightning do its awful work upon that swarming host. One could make out but little of detail; but he could note that a black mass was piling itself up beyond the second fence. That swelling bulk was dead men! Our camp was enclosed with a solid wall of the dead--a bulwark, a breastwork, of corpses, you may say. One terrible thing about this thing was the absence of human voices; there were no cheers, no war cries; being intent upon a surprise, these men moved as noiselessly as they could; and always when the front rank was near enough to their goal to make it proper for them to begin to get a shout ready, of course they struck the fatal line and went down without testifying.

text: The missionary knight introduced himself as La Cote Male Taile and explained that the castle belonged to Morgan le Fay, King Arthur’s sister and the wife of King Uriens, who ruled a territory roughly the size of the District of Columbia—so small you could stand at its center and hurl stones into the neighboring kingdom. Kings and kingdoms were packed across Britain as densely as they had been in tiny Palestine during Joshua’s era, when folk slept with their knees drawn up because stretching out required a passport.
source_text: This missionary knight's name was La Cote Male Taile, and he said that this castle was the abode of Morgan le Fay, sister of King Arthur, and wife of King Uriens, monarch of a realm about as big as the District of Columbia--you could stand in the middle of it and throw bricks into the next kingdom. “Kings” and “Kingdoms” were as thick in Britain as they had been in little Palestine in Joshua's time, when people had to sleep with their knees pulled up because they couldn't stretch out without a passport.

text: “As for that, we’ll find out soon enough. If I stretch him on the rack till he dies and he still admits nothing, it may well prove he truly had nothing to admit—surely you’ll agree that’s the truth of it? In that case, I won’t be damned for the sake of a man who died without confession but had nothing to confess—so, I’ll be in the clear.”
source_text: “As to that, we shall see, anon. An I rack him to death and he confess not, it will peradventure show that he had indeed naught to confess--ye will grant that that is sooth? Then shall I not be damned for an unconfessed man that had naught to confess --wherefore, I shall be safe.”

text: Crack! One rider tumbled from his saddle. Crack! a second followed. Crack—crack, and I had dropped two more. The contest was balanced on a knife's edge, and I was fully aware of it. If I fired my eleventh round without breaking their resolve, the twelfth man would surely be the death of me. So you can imagine the rush of relief when my ninth shot brought down its target and I caught that subtle tremor in the group that signals the onset of panic. A single wasted second now could forfeit my final opportunity. But I didn't waste it. I leveled both pistols and took aim—the stalled assembly held firm for just about one solid, square instant, then shattered and scattered in flight.
source_text: Bang! One saddle empty. Bang! another one. Bang--bang, and I bagged two. Well, it was nip and tuck with us, and I knew it. If I spent the eleventh shot without convincing these people, the twelfth man would kill me, sure. And so I never did feel so happy as I did when my ninth downed its man and I detected the wavering in the crowd which is premonitory of panic. An instant lost now could knock out my last chance. But I didn't lose it. I raised both revolvers and pointed them--the halted host stood their ground just about one good square moment, then broke and fled.

text: That age was marked by an obstinate irrationality. There was no point in reasoning with her. Reason stands powerless against hardened conditioning, just as the sea makes no impression on a cliff. And her conditioning was shared by all. Even the most brilliant mind in the country would have failed to recognize the flaw in her stance.
source_text: It was the stubborn unreasoning of the time. It was useless to argue with her. Arguments have no chance against petrified training; they wear it as little as the waves wear a cliff. And her training was everybody's. The brightest intellect in the land would not have been able to see that her position was defective.

text: King Uther had rushed the muster of the troops far faster than I had foreseen. I had never dreamed he would press forward with the affair while I was abroad; thus I had drafted no method for sifting the worth of commanders. I had merely observed that it would be prudent to subject each aspirant to a rigorous and probing trial; and in secret I intended to compile a catalogue of soldierly requisites that none but my academy men could satisfy. That task should have been finished ere my departure, for the monarch was so enamoured of the notion of a permanent soldiery that he could not brook delay, but must set to work forthwith, and fashion as sound a test of merit as his own wits could devise.
source_text: King Arthur had hurried up the army business altogether beyond my calculations. I had not supposed he would move in the matter while I was away; and so I had not mapped out a scheme for determining the merits of officers; I had only remarked that it would be wise to submit every candidate to a sharp and searching examination; and privately I meant to put together a list of military qualifications that nobody could answer to but my West Pointers. That ought to have been attended to before I left; for the king was so taken with the idea of a standing army that he couldn't wait but must get about it at once, and get up as good a scheme of examination as he could invent out of his own head.

text: As the hour crept toward four in the afternoon, we found ourselves just beyond the walls of London. The day was cool, pleasant, magnificent, with a blazing sun—the sort of day that stirs a hunger for life, not death. The crowd stretched vast and endless; yet among that multitude, we fifteen wretches had not a single friend. However you turned it over in your mind, the thought stung with a peculiar grief. There we perched upon our high scaffold, the target of all that throng’s scorn and derision. We had been turned into a festive spectacle. A kind of raised gallery had been erected for the nobles and gentlefolk, and they had turned out in full strength, their ladies beside them. We knew many of their faces well.
source_text: Nearing four in the afternoon. The scene was just outside the walls of London. A cool, comfortable, superb day, with a brilliant sun; the kind of day to make one want to live, not die. The multitude was prodigious and far-reaching; and yet we fifteen poor devils hadn't a friend in it. There was something painful in that thought, look at it how you might. There we sat, on our tall scaffold, the butt of the hate and mockery of all those enemies. We were being made a holiday spectacle. They had built a sort of grand stand for the nobility and gentry, and these were there in full force, with their ladies. We recognized a good many of them.

text: Yet it proved a letdown. They settled on the earth side by side, peering into one another’s faces with a faint, bewildered, almost brute inquisitiveness for a time; then they lost all awareness of each other, let their gazes fall, and you could tell they had slipped back into that distant realm of visions and phantoms entirely unknown to us.
source_text: But it was a disappointment. They sat together on the ground and looked dimly wondering into each other's faces a while, with a sort of weak animal curiosity; then forgot each other's presence, and dropped their eyes, and you saw that they were away again and wandering in some far land of dreams and shadows that we know nothing about.

text: “That won’t work, Father—combining approaches. It wouldn’t be proper professional conduct either. Two in the same line can’t undercut one another. We’d end up slashing prices anyway; that’s where it would finally lead. Merlin holds the contract, and no other magician can lay a hand on it until he lets it go.”
source_text: “It will not answer to mix methods, Father; neither would it be professional courtesy. Two of a trade must not underbid each other. We might as well cut rates and be done with it; it would arrive at that in the end. Merlin has the contract; no other magician can touch it till he throws it up.”

text: There it surfaced once more. He could perceive just a single aspect of the matter. He had been shaped that way from birth, schooled that way, his bloodstream saturated with inherited callousness passed down through a lineage of hearts, each having contributed its portion to corrupting the current. To confine such men without evidence and let their families go hungry was no injury at all, for they were nothing but peasants, bound to their master's whims and desires, however monstrous those might be; yet for those same men to free themselves from wrongful imprisonment was a scandal and a provocation, something no scrupulous person mindful of his obligations to his hallowed caste could ever condone.
source_text: There it was again. He could see only one side of it. He was born so, educated so, his veins were full of ancestral blood that was rotten with this sort of unconscious brutality, brought down by inheritance from a long procession of hearts that had each done its share toward poisoning the stream. To imprison these men without proof, and starve their kindred, was no harm, for they were merely peasants and subject to the will and pleasure of their lord, no matter what fearful form it might take; but for these men to break out of unjust captivity was insult and outrage, and a thing not to be countenanced by any conscientious person who knew his duty to his sacred caste.

text: We resolved to venture along the path between the inner barriers. We chose to move erect, for ease of motion; we reasoned that if spotted, we would be mistaken for allies rather than foes, and besides, we would remain beyond the sweep of blades, and these fellows appeared to carry no lances with them. It was, I must say, a strange journey. On every side fallen men lay scattered beyond the second paling—not starkly exposed, yet still discernible; and we tallied fifteen of those sorrowful figures—slain soldiers upright with their fingers clutching the topmost strand.
source_text: We concluded to make a tour between the inner fences. We elected to walk upright, for convenience's sake; we argued that if discerned, we should be taken for friends rather than enemies, and in any case we should be out of reach of swords, and these gentry did not seem to have any spears along. Well, it was a curious trip. Everywhere dead men were lying outside the second fence--not plainly visible, but still visible; and we counted fifteen of those pathetic statues--dead knights standing with their hands on the upper wire.

text: I told myself, as I wound up my lariat and slung it over the saddle-horn, sitting there intoxicated with triumph, “The victory is absolute—no one else will dare challenge me—the age of knight-errantry is finished.” But then imagine my shock—and the shock of the whole crowd—when that strange bugle sounded, signaling a new challenger about to enter the field! Something uncanny was afoot; I could make no sense of it. A moment later, I saw Merlin slipping quietly away from my side; and then I realized my lasso had vanished! The cunning old master of tricks had surely filched it and hidden it beneath his robes.
source_text: Said I to myself, as I coiled my lariat and hung it on my saddle-horn, and sat there drunk with glory, “The victory is perfect--no other will venture against me--knight-errantry is dead.” Now imagine my astonishment--and everybody else's, too--to hear the peculiar bugle-call which announces that another competitor is about to enter the lists! There was a mystery here; I couldn't account for this thing. Next, I noticed Merlin gliding away from me; and then I noticed that my lasso was gone! The old sleight-of-hand expert had stolen it, sure, and slipped it under his robe.

text: The sovereign had no choice but to capitulate. A horn rang out its command, and we wheeled away to take our appointed places. There we halted, separated by a hundred yards, confronting one another, fixed and still as equestrian carvings. And thus we lingered, in a breathless quiet, for what felt like a full minute, all eyes fixed, not a soul shifting. It seemed as though the monarch could not muster the resolve to launch the contest. Yet finally his arm rose, the horn’s bright summons followed, Sir Valerius’s long sword carved a glinting arc through the air, and the sight of his charge was magnificent. I kept my seat. Closer he surged. I remained unmoving. The onlookers grew so inflamed that they cried out to me:
source_text: The king had to yield. The bugle made proclamation, and we turned apart and rode to our stations. There we stood, a hundred yards apart, facing each other, rigid and motionless, like horsed statues. And so we remained, in a soundless hush, as much as a full minute, everybody gazing, nobody stirring. It seemed as if the king could not take heart to give the signal. But at last he lifted his hand, the clear note of the bugle followed, Sir Sagramor's long blade described a flashing curve in the air, and it was superb to see him come. I sat still. On he came. I did not move. People got so excited that they shouted to me:

text: The night dragged on, but morning finally crept in. I laid out the whole truth plainly before the court. I explained that I was a bondman, owned by the mighty Earl Grip, who had reached the Tabard inn in the hamlet across the river just after nightfall and been forced to stay there, struck down by a sudden and violent illness. My orders were to race into town and fetch the finest doctor; I was hurrying as fast as I could; the darkness was thick, and I collided with this ordinary fellow here, who grabbed me by the neck and started beating me, even after I told him my mission and begged him, for the sake of my lord the great earl's life-threatening danger—
source_text: It was a long night, but the morning got around at last. I made a full and frank explanation to the court. I said I was a slave, the property of the great Earl Grip, who had arrived just after dark at the Tabard inn in the village on the other side of the water, and had stopped there over night, by compulsion, he being taken deadly sick with a strange and sudden disorder. I had been ordered to cross to the city in all haste and bring the best physician; I was doing my best; naturally I was running with all my might; the night was dark, I ran against this common person here, who seized me by the throat and began to pummel me, although I told him my errand, and implored him, for the sake of the great earl my master's mortal peril--

text: Here was the sorrowful close of a lovely honeymoon, barely three months begun. Poor young souls! For all those weeks they had tasted every worldly pleasure to the full. The garments and ornaments they wore were as elegant and delicate as the strictest limits of the sumptuary code permitted to folk of their station; and dressed in those fine things, she weeping against his shoulder while he strove to soothe her with hopeful phrases tuned to the key of hopelessness, they passed from the judge’s bench into a world without shelter, without a bed, without bread. Indeed, the very paupers along the hedgerows were not so destitute as they.
source_text: Here was a tragic end to a beautiful honeymoon not yet three months old. Poor young creatures! They had lived these three months lapped to the lips in worldly comforts. These clothes and trinkets they were wearing were as fine and dainty as the shrewdest stretch of the sumptuary laws allowed to people of their degree; and in these pretty clothes, she crying on his shoulder, and he trying to comfort her with hopeful words set to the music of despair, they went from the judgment seat out into the world homeless, bedless, breadless; why, the very beggars by the roadsides were not so poor as they.

text: “We’re just peaceable outsiders, sir,” I told him, “minding our own business on the road. We hail from a distant land and don’t know this place. We meant no mischief—yet without your bold intervention and shield, these folk would have slain us. As you’ve guessed, sir, we’re not lunatics, nor are we fierce or murderous.”
source_text: “We are but peaceful strangers, sir,” I said, “and traveling upon our own concerns. We are from a far country, and unacquainted here. We have purposed no harm; and yet but for your brave interference and protection these people would have killed us. As you have divined, sir, we are not mad; neither are we violent or bloodthirsty.”

text: We wandered now with a lazy, unhurried gait, conversing as we went. We had to fill roughly the stretch of time required to reach the little village of Abblasoure, set the law on the trail of those killers, and return. And all the while I had a secondary fascination that had never dimmed, never lost its freshness since I first entered Arthur’s realm: the conduct—born of fine and precise gradations of rank—that chance passersby showed one another. Toward the tonsured monk who trudged along with his hood thrown back and sweat streaming down his plump cheeks, the charcoal-burner was deeply reverent; to the gentleman he was groveling; with the small farmer and the free craftsman he was warm and chatty; but when a slave passed with a properly downcast face, the fellow’s nose went straight into the air—he could not even see him. There are moments, truly, when one feels like hanging the entire human race and putting an end to the farce.
source_text: We strolled along in a sufficiently indolent fashion now, and talked. We must dispose of about the amount of time it ought to take to go to the little hamlet of Abblasoure and put justice on the track of those murderers and get back home again. And meantime I had an auxiliary interest which had never paled yet, never lost its novelty for me since I had been in Arthur's kingdom: the behavior--born of nice and exact subdivisions of caste--of chance passers-by toward each other.
