Toward the shaven monk who trudged along with his cowl tilted back and the sweat washing down his fat jowls, the coal-burner was deeply reverent; to the gentleman he was abject; with the small farmer and the free mechanic he was cordial and gossipy; and when a slave passed by with a countenance respectfully lowered, this chap's nose was in the air--he couldn't even see him. Well, there are times when one would like to hang the whole human race and finish the farce.

text: “It bore no spell in former days,” she murmured, half lost in reverie, as though speaking to her own soul. “And what a strange and fearsome wonder this is—that to one beholder it appears bewitched and cloaked in a mean and shameful guise; while to the other’s sight it remains unaltered, steadfast and proud, encircled by its waters and flying its pennons from the towers against the high, blue sky. And Heaven preserve us, how it wounds the spirit to look once more upon these gentle prisoners, with grief etched deeper in their tender faces! We have lingered too long, and the fault is ours.”
source_text: “It was not enchanted aforetime,” she said in a musing fashion, as if to herself. “And how strange is this marvel, and how awful --that to the one perception it is enchanted and dight in a base and shameful aspect; yet to the perception of the other it is not enchanted, hath suffered no change, but stands firm and stately still, girt with its moat and waving its banners in the blue air from its towers. And God shield us, how it pricks the heart to see again these gracious captives, and the sorrow deepened in their sweet faces! We have tarried along, and are to blame.”

text: “Hold on! There, you see, it’s perfectly plain; this time you’ll grasp it. Say your wife needs 42 days to pay for her dress, at 2 mills per day—seven full weeks of labor; but mine earns hers in forty days—two days shy of seven weeks. Your wife has a dress, and her entire seven weeks’ pay is spent; mine has a dress, and two days’ wages remaining, to put toward something else. There—now you’ve got it!”
source_text: “Wait! Now, you see, the thing is very simple; this time you'll understand it. For instance, it takes your woman 42 days to earn her gown, at 2 mills a day--7 weeks' work; but ours earns hers in forty days--two days _short_ of 7 weeks. Your woman has a gown, and her whole seven weeks wages are gone; ours has a gown, and two days' wages left, to buy something else with. There--_now_ you understand it!”

text: About two hundred yards distant, upon the level ground, we constructed an enclosure of slender timbers, rising some four feet, and laid planks across it to form a stage. This we draped with sumptuous tapestries borrowed for the purpose, and crowned it with the abbot’s own throne. When one means to perform a miracle before an unlettered people, every detail that tells must be attended to; the properties must strike the public gaze, and the chief guest must be seated in comfort; only then can you give yourself free rein and work your effects for all they are worth. I understand such things, for I understand human nature. You cannot invest a miracle with too much ceremony. It costs exertion, labour, and sometimes coin; but it repays itself in the end. We ran the wires down to the chapel wall, then carried them underground to the platform, and concealed the batteries there. Around the stage we set a rope barrier a hundred feet square, to hold back the common crowd, and with that the preparations were complete. My design was to open the doors at half past ten, the performance to begin precisely at twenty-five minutes past eleven. I wished I might charge admission, but naturally that would not serve. I directed my lads to be in the chapel by ten, before any spectators gathered, ready to work the pumps at the appointed moment and make the fur fly. Then we went home to supper.
source_text: About two hundred yards off, in the flat, we built a pen of scantlings, about four feet high, and laid planks on it, and so made a platform. We covered it with swell tapestries borrowed for the occasion, and topped it off with the abbot's own throne. When you are going to do a miracle for an ignorant race, you want to get in every detail that will count; you want to make all the properties impressive to the public eye; you want to make matters comfortable for your head guest; then you can turn yourself loose and play your effects for all they are worth. I know the value of these things, for I know human nature. You can't throw too much style into a miracle. It costs trouble, and work, and sometimes money; but it pays in the end. Well, we brought the wires to the ground at the chapel, and then brought them under the ground to the platform, and hid the batteries there. We put a rope fence a hundred feet square around the platform to keep off the common multitude, and that finished the work. My idea was, doors open at 10:30, performance to begin at 11:25 sharp. I wished I could charge admission, but of course that wouldn't answer. I instructed my boys to be in the chapel as early as 10, before anybody was around, and be ready to man the pumps at the proper time, and make the fur fly. Then we went home to supper.

text: “I feared the low-drooping limb might put a notion in their heads,” I murmured, “yet the letdown troubles me little. Onward, my lord—let us spend the hour wisely. We have slipped round their flank. Dusk will soon be upon us. Should we ford the brook, gain a fair lead, and borrow two nags from some field for a short spell, we’ll be well out of harm’s way.”
source_text: “I was afraid that the overhanging branch would suggest something to them,” said I, “but I don't mind the disappointment. Come, my liege, it were well that we make good use of our time. We've flanked them. Dark is coming on, presently. If we can cross the stream and get a good start, and borrow a couple of horses from somebody's pasture to use for a few hours, we shall be safe enough.”

text: “And six wooden cups, along with six wooden plates and two made of pewter, for our eating and drinking,” the mason declared with weight. “I speak this knowing the Lord above hears me, and we don’t linger in this world forever, but will be called to account on the final day for every word spoken in the flesh, whether it be lies or truth.”
source_text: “And six wooden goblets, and six platters of wood and two of pewter to eat and drink from withal,” said the mason, impressively. “And I say it as knowing God is my judge, and we tarry not here alway, but must answer at the last day for the things said in the body, be they false or be they sooth.”

text: Indeed, his employer was a gentleman of substance and good standing, who twice annually laid out a splendid spread of roasted joints, accompanied by loaves of the purest wheat flour—bread as pale as cream—and altogether kept a table that might be called lordly. In due course, the trade and the daughter both passed into Dowley’s hands.
source_text: Yes, and his master was a fine man, and prosperous, and always had a great feast of meat twice in the year, and with it white bread, true wheaten bread; in fact, lived like a lord, so to speak. And in time Dowley succeeded to the business and married the daughter.

text: At last, however, I slipped off my final shackle and stood liberated again. I drew a deep, grateful breath and stretched my hand toward the monarch’s chains. But I was too slow! The overseer entered, bearing a lamp in one fist and his stout cudgel in the other. I pressed myself deep into the heap of slumbering bodies, hoping to hide my bare ankles; and I watched him keenly, ready to leap upon him the instant he leaned above me.
source_text: But finally I did get my last iron off, and was a free man once more. I took a good breath of relief, and reached for the king's irons. Too late! in comes the master, with a light in one hand and his heavy walking-staff in the other. I snuggled close among the wallow of snorers, to conceal as nearly as possible that I was naked of irons; and I kept a sharp lookout and prepared to spring for my man the moment he should bend over me.

text: Just then, she swayed, overcome with exhaustion, and the whip came slicing down, peeling a strip of skin from her bare shoulder. The sting hit me as sharply as if I’d taken the blow myself. The overseer stopped the line and dismounted. He raged and cursed at the girl, shouting that her idleness had caused trouble enough, and since this was his final opportunity, he’d square the debt right now. She sank to her knees, lifted her hands, and began pleading, weeping, and beseeching in a frenzy of fear, but he paid no mind. He tore the child from her, then ordered the enslaved men chained ahead and behind her to force her to the ground, pin her down, and bare her body; then he swung the whip like a madman until her back was raw, while she screamed and writhed in pitiful desperation. One of the men restraining her averted his face, and for that scrap of compassion he was cursed and beaten.
source_text: She reeled just then, giddy with fatigue, and down came the lash and flicked a flake of skin from her naked shoulder. It stung me as if I had been hit instead. The master halted the file and jumped from his horse. He stormed and swore at this girl, and said she had made annoyance enough with her laziness, and as this was the last chance he should have, he would settle the account now. She dropped on her knees and put up her hands and began to beg, and cry, and implore, in a passion of terror, but the master gave no attention. He snatched the child from her, and then made the men-slaves who were chained before and behind her throw her on the ground and hold her there and expose her body; and then he laid on with his lash like a madman till her back was flayed, she shrieking and struggling the while piteously. One of the men who was holding her turned away his face, and for this humanity he was reviled and flogged.

text: After his whispered amen, they slipped the hemp loop over the maiden’s throat, struggling fiercely to seat the knot beneath her jawbone, for she was consuming the infant without pause—raining frantic kisses upon it, clutching it to her cheeks and her bosom, soaking its wrappings with weeping, all the while half-sobbing, half-wailing, and the babe cooing and chortling and drumming its tiny heels in glee at what it mistook for a merry game. Even the executioner could not endure the sight and averted his face. When the preparations were complete, the friar gently pried and drew and wrested the little one from the mother’s grasp and retreated swiftly beyond her reach; but she locked her fingers, lunged toward him with a piercing cry—only the cord, and the bailiff’s grip, jerked her back. Then she sank to her knees, flung out her arms, and wept aloud:
source_text: After his prayer they put the noose around the young girl's neck, and they had great trouble to adjust the knot under her ear, because she was devouring the baby all the time, wildly kissing it, and snatching it to her face and her breast, and drenching it with tears, and half moaning, half shrieking all the while, and the baby crowing, and laughing, and kicking its feet with delight over what it took for romp and play. Even the hangman couldn't stand it, but turned away. When all was ready the priest gently pulled and tugged and forced the child out of the mother's arms, and stepped quickly out of her reach; but she clasped her hands, and made a wild spring toward him, with a shriek; but the rope--and the under-sheriff--held her short. Then she went on her knees and stretched out her hands and cried:

text: Indeed, I formed a number of acquaintances in the little village, and one thing that particularly pleased me was discovering our new money already circulating—plenty of milrays, mills, cents, quite a few nickels, and some silver, all passing freely among the craftsmen and ordinary folk. Yes, and even a little gold—though that was at the bank, or rather, the goldsmith’s shop. I stepped in there while Marco, son of Marco, was wrangling with a shopkeeper over a quarter-pound of salt, and I asked for change for a twenty-dollar gold piece. They gave it to me—that is, after they had bitten the coin, rung it on the counter, tested it with acid, and inquired where I had got it, who I was, where I came from, where I was headed, when I expected to arrive, and perhaps a couple of hundred other questions. When they finally ran aground, I sailed right on and freely supplied them with a great deal of information: I told them I owned a dog named Watch, that my first wife had been a Free Will Baptist, her grandfather a Prohibitionist, and that I once knew a fellow with two thumbs on each hand and a wart inside his upper lip who died in the hope of a glorious resurrection—and so on, and so on, and so on, until even that famished village interrogator began to look satisfied, though also a little put out. Still, he had to respect a man of my financial standing, so he gave me no cheek; but I noticed he took it out on his underlings, which was entirely natural. Yes, they changed my twenty, though I gathered it strained the bank a bit—only to be expected, for it was like walking into some shabby village shop in the nineteenth century and suddenly asking the proprietor to break a two-thousand-dollar bill. He might manage it, perhaps, but all the while he would wonder how a small farmer came to be carrying so much cash in his pocket—which was likely this goldsmith’s thought as well, for he followed me to the door and stood gazing after me with reverent admiration.
source_text: Yes, I made various acquaintances in the hamlet and a thing that gratified me a good deal was to find our new coins in circulation --lots of milrays, lots of mills, lots of cents, a good many nickels, and some silver; all this among the artisans and commonalty generally; yes, and even some gold--but that was at the bank, that is to say, the goldsmith's. I dropped in there while Marco, the son of Marco, was haggling with a shopkeeper over a quarter of a pound of salt, and asked for change for a twenty-dollar gold piece. They furnished it--that is, after they had chewed the piece, and rung it on the counter, and tried acid on it, and asked me where I got it, and who I was, and where I was from, and where I was going to, and when I expected to get there, and perhaps a couple of hundred more questions; and when they got aground, I went right on and furnished them a lot of information voluntarily; told them I owned a dog, and his name was Watch, and my first wife was a Free Will Baptist, and her grandfather was a Prohibitionist, and I used to know a man who had two thumbs on each hand and a wart on the inside of his upper lip, and died in the hope of a glorious resurrection, and so on, and so on, and so on, till even that hungry village questioner began to look satisfied, and also a shade put out; but he had to respect a man of my financial strength, and so he didn't give me any lip, but I noticed he took it out of his underlings, which was a perfectly natural thing to do. Yes, they changed my twenty, but I judged it strained the bank a little, which was a thing to be expected, for it was the same as walking into a paltry village store in the nineteenth century and requiring the boss of it to change a two thousand-dollar bill for you all of a sudden. He could do it, maybe; but at the same time he would wonder how a small farmer happened to be carrying so much money around in his pocket; which was probably this goldsmith's thought, too; for he followed me to the door and stood there gazing after me with reverent admiration.

text: The funeral arrangements for the late Sir Dalliance, the Duke of Cornwall's son—slain last Tuesday in a clash with the Giant of the Knotted Bludgeon along the edge of the Plain of Enchantment—were managed by the unfailingly genial and capable Mumble, a prince among morticians. There is no one in whose hands the final solemn rites are more gratifying to entrust. Give him a try.
source_text: The bdsiness end of the funeral of the late Sir Dalliance the duke's son of Cornwall, killed in an encounter with the Giant of the Knotted Bludgeon last Tuesday on the borders of the Plain of Enchantment was in the hands of the ever affable and efficient Mumble, prince of un3ertakers, then whom there exists none by whom it were a more satisfying pleasure to have the last sad offices performed. Give him a trial.

text: Consider this: among these forty-seven prisoners, five had names, crimes, and dates of confinement that no one could now recall. One woman and four men—all stooped, creased, and mentally extinguished elders. They themselves had long since forgotten these facts; at best they held only dim conjectures, nothing precise and nothing they ever recounted the same way twice. The line of priests whose duty it was to pray with the captives daily, to remind them that God had placed them there for some wise end or other, and to instruct them that patience, meekness, and submission to tyranny were what He delighted to see in those of lower station, had inherited stories about these wretched human remnants, but nothing more. Those stories reached back only a little way, for they touched solely on the length of the imprisonment, not on the names of the crimes. And even with the aid of these tales, the only thing that could be established was that none of the five had glimpsed daylight for thirty-five years: how much longer this deprivation had lasted was beyond guessing. The king and queen knew nothing of these pitiful souls, save that they were heirlooms, assets passed down, along with the crown, from the previous establishment. No record of their past had been handed down with their bodies, and so the inheriting proprietors had deemed them worthless and felt no curiosity about them. I remarked to the queen:
source_text: Consider it: among these forty-seven captives there were five whose names, offenses, and dates of incarceration were no longer known! One woman and four men--all bent, and wrinkled, and mind-extinguished patriarchs. They themselves had long ago forgotten these details; at any rate they had mere vague theories about them, nothing definite and nothing that they repeated twice in the same way. The succession of priests whose office it had been to pray daily with the captives and remind them that God had put them there, for some wise purpose or other, and teach them that patience, humbleness, and submission to oppression was what He loved to see in parties of a subordinate rank, had traditions about these poor old human ruins, but nothing more. These traditions went but little way, for they concerned the length of the incarceration only, and not the names of the offenses. And even by the help of tradition the only thing that could be proven was that none of the five had seen daylight for thirty-five years: how much longer this privation has lasted was not guessable. The king and the queen knew nothing about these poor creatures, except that they were heirlooms, assets inherited, along with the throne, from the former firm. Nothing of their history had been transmitted with their persons, and so the inheriting owners had considered them of no value, and had felt no interest in them. I said to the queen:

text: At that moment, the king rejoined us, thoroughly revived by his sleep and in high spirits. By now, anything could set me on edge, so unsettled was I—our lives hung in the balance. It troubled me deeply to catch a self-satisfied gleam in his eye, as though he had been fortifying himself for some sort of performance. Damn it all, why did he have to pick a time like this?
source_text: The king joined us, about this time, mightily refreshed by his nap, and feeling good. Anything could make me nervous now, I was so uneasy--for our lives were in danger; and so it worried me to detect a complacent something in the king's eye which seemed to indicate that he had been loading himself up for a performance of some kind or other; confound it, why must he go and choose such a time as this?

text: By night we labored, secreting the powder within the tower—prying stones loose from the inner face and burying the charges inside the walls, which measured fifteen feet thick at their foundation. We placed a peck at a time, scattering it across a dozen locations. Those charges could have shattered the Tower of London. When the thirteenth night arrived, we raised our lightning-rod, bedded it in one of the powder caches, and strung wires from it to the remaining caches. Ever since my proclamation, everyone had avoided that spot, but on the morning of the fourteenth I thought it prudent to send heralds warning the people to keep well away—a full quarter of a mile distant. Then, by royal order, I added that at some hour within the next twenty-four I would perform the miracle, but would first give brief notice: by flags flown from the castle towers if by day, by torch-baskets set in the same places if by night.
source_text: Working by night, we stowed the powder in the tower--dug stones out, on the inside, and buried the powder in the walls themselves, which were fifteen feet thick at the base. We put in a peck at a time, in a dozen places. We could have blown up the Tower of London with these charges. When the thirteenth night was come we put up our lightning-rod, bedded it in one of the batches of powder, and ran wires from it to the other batches. Everybody had shunned that locality from the day of my proclamation, but on the morning of the fourteenth I thought best to warn the people, through the heralds, to keep clear away--a quarter of a mile away. Then added, by command, that at some time during the twenty-four hours I would consummate the miracle, but would first give a brief notice; by flags on the castle towers if in the daytime, by torch-baskets in the same places if at night.

text: I knew it would happen. The moment arrived. They were compelled to speak. It was painful to watch those poor boys, so ashen, so exhausted, so distressed. At first, their chosen speaker could scarcely summon voice or language, but soon he found both. Here is what he said—delivered in the crisp modern English my schools had instilled in him:
source_text: I was right. The time came. They HAD to speak. Poor lads, it was pitiful to see, they were so pale, so worn, so troubled. At first their spokesman could hardly find voice or words; but he presently got both. This is what he said--and he put it in the neat modern English taught him in my schools:

text: The parcel will set him back 32 cents. He has to toil 32 working days to scrape that together—five weeks and two days. Send him our way to labor 32 days at half the pay; he can acquire all those goods for just under 14 1/2 cents; they’ll cost him just under 29 days of labor, and he’ll still have about half a week’s pay left over. Stretch that across a year; he’d pocket nearly a week’s wages every two months, while your fellow saves nothing; that means banking five or six weeks’ wages annually, your man not a penny. Now I trust you see that ‘high wages’ and ‘low wages’ are empty terms until you determine which one actually purchases the most!
source_text: “The lot will cost him 32 cents. It takes him 32 working days to earn the money--5 weeks and 2 days. Let him come to us and work 32 days at _half_ the wages; he can buy all those things for a shade under 14 1/2 cents; they will cost him a shade under 29 days' work, and he will have about half a week's wages over. Carry it through the year; he would save nearly a week's wages every two months, _your_ man nothing; thus saving five or six weeks' wages in a year, your man not a cent. _Now_ I reckon you understand that 'high wages' and 'low wages' are phrases that don't mean anything in the world until you find out which of them will _buy_ the most!”

text: So, being a practical-minded Connecticut fellow, I promptly pushed the entire puzzle out of my head until its designated moment arrived, freeing me to focus completely on the here and now, staying sharp and poised to squeeze every drop of advantage from whatever came my way. My rule is: tackle one thing at a time—and play that hand for all it’s worth, even if you’re holding nothing but a low pair and a jack. I settled on two resolutions: if this was still the nineteenth century and I was trapped among madmen with no escape, I’d soon run that asylum or demand a damn good explanation; and if, instead, it truly was the sixth century, fine—I wouldn’t ask for an easier deal: I’d take command of the entire realm within three months, since I figured I’d have a head start on the kingdom’s most learned man by something like thirteen hundred years or more. I’m not one to dawdle once my mind’s fixed and there’s work to be done, so I turned to the page and spoke.
source_text: Wherefore, being a practical Connecticut man, I now shoved this whole problem clear out of my mind till its appointed day and hour should come, in order that I might turn all my attention to the circumstances of the present moment, and be alert and ready to make the most out of them that could be made. One thing at a time, is my motto--and just play that thing for all it is worth, even if it's only two pair and a jack. I made up my mind to two things: if it was still the nineteenth century and I was among lunatics and couldn't get away, I would presently boss that asylum or know the reason why; and if, on the other hand, it was really the sixth century, all right, I didn't want any softer thing: I would boss the whole country inside of three months; for I judged I would have the start of the best-educated man in the kingdom by a matter of thirteen hundred years and upward. I'm not a man to waste time after my mind's made up and there's work on hand; so I said to the page:

text: I couldn’t wake him, so I suggested we carry him to her and find out—to the bride who had once been, in his eyes, the loveliest being on earth, roses, pearls, and morning dew turned living for his sake; a marvel, nature’s finest creation: with eyes beyond compare, a voice beyond compare, and a dewy, supple youthful elegance and beauty that, as he believed, belonged only to the phantoms of dreams. The glimpse of her would send his sluggish blood racing; the glimpse of her—
source_text: I could not rouse the man; so I said we would take him to her, and see--to the bride who was the fairest thing in the earth to him, once--roses, pearls, and dew made flesh, for him; a wonder-work, the master-work of nature: with eyes like no other eyes, and voice like no other voice, and a freshness, and lithe young grace, and beauty, that belonged properly to the creatures of dreams--as he thought--and to no other. The sight of her would set his stagnant blood leaping; the sight of her--

text: Morgan le Fay moved with her usual melodic grace, a woman of wonder. Her gaze alone was a force—when she cast it in reproach upon the servants, they withered and trembled like frightened souls caught beneath lightning breaking from a cloud. I could have learned that habit myself. It was no different with poor old Brer Uriens; he lived perpetually on the frayed edge of dread. She had only to turn in his direction, and he would flinch.
source_text: Morgan le Fay rippled along as musically as ever. Marvelous woman. And what a glance she had: when it fell in reproof upon those servants, they shrunk and quailed as timid people do when the lightning flashes out of a cloud. I could have got the habit myself. It was the same with that poor old Brer Uriens; he was always on the ragged edge of apprehension; she could not even turn toward him but he winced.

text: “Ah, good sir, it would be grievously difficult to say, for they are so numerous and lie so tangled one atop another, and being shaped all alike and stained with the selfsame hue, a man cannot tell one league from its neighbor, nor reckon their number unless they be separated—and well you know that would be God’s own labor, beyond the reach of mortal hands; for you will observe—”
source_text: “Ah, fair sir, it were woundily hard to tell, they are so many, and do so lap the one upon the other, and being made all in the same image and tincted with the same color, one may not know the one league from its fellow, nor how to count them except they be taken apart, and ye wit well it were God's work to do that, being not within man's capacity; for ye will note--”

text: I delivered my instructions directly to the Academy’s superintendent. In addition, I requested that he fetch me a sheaf of writing paper, an ink pen, and a couple of boxes of strike-anywhere matches. The lack of such small comforts had begun to wear on me. Now I could indulge in them, since I had no immediate need to don armor and could finally reach into my pockets.
source_text: I gave my order to the superintendent of the Academy personally. I also asked him to bring me some paper and a fountain pen and a box or so of safety matches. I was getting tired of doing without these conveniences. I could have them now, as I wasn't going to wear armor any more at present, and therefore could get at my pockets.

text: I wasn't pretending now. I fully intended my words; I had the ability to carry out my threat. The meaning behind that dare couldn't be mistaken. Even the slowest among the knights understood it was a clear demand to act or fall silent. Prudently, they chose silence. Over the following three years, they caused me no trouble to speak of.
source_text: I was not bluffing this time. I meant what I said; I could do what I promised. There wasn't any way to misunderstand the language of that challenge. Even the dullest of the chivalry perceived that this was a plain case of “put up, or shut up.” They were wise and did the latter. In all the next three years they gave me no trouble worth mentioning.

text: Damn him, he exhausted me with proofs that in any proper auction he would have commanded twenty-five dollars, no question—a claim so absurd, so thick with vanity, it made me wince; I wouldn’t have paid that sum for myself. Yet it was delicate footing for dispute. Truly, I had to abandon logic altogether and take up diplomacy. I was forced to smother my scruples and impudently agree that he should have brought twenty-five dollars; while knowing perfectly well that in all recorded time, the globe had never produced a monarch worth half that amount, and for the next thirteen hundred years would not yield one worth a quarter of it. Yes, he wore me down. If he launched into the harvests; or the late rains; or the state of the government; or hounds, or housecats, or ethics, or divinity—it made no difference—I groaned inwardly, for I foresaw the end; he would somehow extract from it an excuse for that wearisome seven-dollar transaction. Wherever we paused and a throng gathered, he would cast me a glance that said as clearly as speech: “were the sale to happen anew, among this sort of people, you would witness another outcome.” Well, when he first went for seven dollars, it privately amused me; but by the time he finished his fretting and stewing, I longed that he had drawn a hundred. The matter never got leave to rest, for each day, in some hamlet or crossroads, prospective buyers inspected us, and, as often as not, their remark upon the king ran something like:
source_text: Confound him, he wearied me with arguments to show that in anything like a fair market he would have fetched twenty-five dollars, sure--a thing which was plainly nonsense, and full of the baldest conceit; I wasn't worth it myself. But it was tender ground for me to argue on. In fact, I had to simply shirk argument and do the diplomatic instead. I had to throw conscience aside, and brazenly concede that he ought to have brought twenty-five dollars; whereas I was quite well aware that in all the ages, the world had never seen a king that was worth half the money, and during the next thirteen centuries wouldn't see one that was worth the fourth of it. Yes, he tired me. If he began to talk about the crops; or about the recent weather; or about the condition of politics; or about dogs, or cats, or morals, or theology--no matter what --I sighed, for I knew what was coming; he was going to get out of it a palliation of that tiresome seven-dollar sale. Wherever we halted where there was a crowd, he would give me a look which said plainly: “if that thing could be tried over again now, with this kind of folk, you would see a different result.” Well, when he was first sold, it secretly tickled me to see him go for seven dollars; but before he was done with his sweating and worrying I wished he had fetched a hundred. The thing never got a chance to die, for every day, at one place or another, possible purchasers looked us over, and, as often as any other way, their comment on the king was something like this:

text: Fires held a keen interest for me, since I was building up a solid insurance enterprise while also breaking horses and constructing steam-powered fire engines, all with the eventual aim of a salaried fire brigade. The clergy fought my fire and life insurance alike, calling it a brazen scheme to thwart God’s decrees; and when I observed that policies did nothing to thwart those decrees—merely softened their harsh effects if you were fortunate enough to hold one—they shot back that this was wagering against Providence, and just as sinful. In this way they managed to hobble those ventures somewhat, but I more than recovered my ground through the accident line. A knight, as a rule, is a lumbering oaf and sometimes a downright fool, easily swayed by the smooth talk of a superstition-peddler, yet even he could occasionally grasp a thing’s practical value; and so, lately, you could scarcely sweep up after a tournament without turning up one of my accident vouchers tucked inside every helmet.
source_text: Fires interested me considerably, because I was getting a good deal of an insurance business started, and was also training some horses and building some steam fire-engines, with an eye to a paid fire department by and by. The priests opposed both my fire and life insurance, on the ground that it was an insolent attempt to hinder the decrees of God; and if you pointed out that they did not hinder the decrees in the least, but only modified the hard consequences of them if you took out policies and had luck, they retorted that that was gambling against the decrees of God, and was just as bad. So they managed to damage those industries more or less, but I got even on my accident business. As a rule, a knight is a lummux, and some times even a labrick, and hence open to pretty poor arguments when they come glibly from a superstition-monger, but even _he_ could see the practical side of a thing once in a while; and so of late you couldn't clean up a tournament and pile the result without finding one of my accident-tickets in every helmet.

text: The early birds trickled in one after another, appearing in our vicinity roughly every five minutes over the span of half an hour. They carried no offensive weapons beyond their swords, which they typically held at the ready, extended forward to probe for the wires. Now and then a blue spark flickered from a knight so distant he remained invisible to us, but we understood all too well what it meant: the poor soul had touched a charged wire with his blade and been electrocuted. Brief stretches of grim silence fell, broken with pitiable regularity by the clatter of an armored body collapsing. This went on without pause, deeply unnerving in the darkness and isolation.
source_text: These early birds came scattering along after each other, about one every five minutes in our vicinity, during half an hour. They brought no armor of offense but their swords; as a rule, they carried the sword ready in the hand, and put it forward and found the wires with it. We would now and then see a blue spark when the knight that caused it was so far away as to be invisible to us; but we knew what had happened, all the same; poor fellow, he had touched a charged wire with his sword and been electrocuted. We had brief intervals of grim stillness, interrupted with piteous regularity by the clash made by the falling of an iron-clad; and this sort of thing was going on, right along, and was very creepy there in the dark and lonesomeness.

text: The thirteen Gatling guns unleashed a torrent of death upon the doomed ten thousand. The soldiers faltered, held their position for a heartbeat against the withering storm of bullets, then shattered, turned, and rushed toward the ditch like husks swept before a tempest. A full quarter of their force never made it to the crest of the towering embankment; the remaining three-fourths reached it and tumbled over—into death by water.
source_text: The thirteen gatlings began to vomit death into the fated ten thousand. They halted, they stood their ground a moment against that withering deluge of fire, then they broke, faced about and swept toward the ditch like chaff before a gale. A full fourth part of their force never reached the top of the lofty embankment; the three-fourths reached it and plunged over--to death by drowning.

text: As I dragged out the final syllables of that utterance, I triggered one of my galvanic circuits and the entire shadowed multitude sprang into ghastly blue illumination! The spectacle was colossal. Cries rang out, women crumpled and scattered on all sides, orphans dropped in clusters. The abbot and his monks sketched rapid crosses and their lips trembled with frantic orisons. Merlin kept his composure, but amazement sank into his very bones; nothing in his experience had ever approached such a display. Now was the moment to heap on the wonders. I raised my arms and moaned this word—as if in torment:
source_text: Just as I was moaning out the closing hunks of that word, I touched off one of my electric connections and all that murky world of people stood revealed in a hideous blue glare! It was immense --that effect! Lots of people shrieked, women curled up and quit in every direction, foundlings collapsed by platoons. The abbot and the monks crossed themselves nimbly and their lips fluttered with agitated prayers. Merlin held his grip, but he was astonished clear down to his corns; he had never seen anything to begin with that, before. Now was the time to pile in the effects. I lifted my hands and groaned out this word--as it were in agony:

text: “Well, you understand we can’t afford to linger over this. Can’t you grasp that we could scatter these souls across the globe faster than it’ll take you to justify why we can’t? We’ve no time for debate—we must move. You need to watch yourself; don’t let your mind run ahead of you like that, not at a moment such as this. Now, to the task at hand—and swiftly. Who will escort the nobility home?”
source_text: “Well, you know we haven't got time for this sort of thing. Don't you see, we could distribute these people around the earth in less time than it is going to take you to explain that we can't. We mustn't talk now, we must act. You want to be careful; you mustn't let your mill get the start of you that way, at a time like this. To business now--and sharp's the word. Who is to take the aristocracy home?”

text: Right away, my lads and I set to work. The old bath's remains still lay in the monastery cellar, not a single stone displaced. All these generations they had been left untouched, shunned with a kind of holy dread, as things under a curse. Within two days we had finished the job and filled it—a roomy basin of clean, limpid water fit for swimming. It was fresh running water, too, flowing in and out through the age-old pipes. The old abbot kept his promise and went down first to test it. He descended dark-skinned and trembling, while the whole black community above fretted and murmured, heavy with foreboding; but he came back up white and radiant, and the venture was won—another victory notched.
source_text: I and my boys were at work, straight off. The ruins of the old bath were there yet in the basement of the monastery, not a stone missing. They had been left just so, all these lifetimes, and avoided with a pious fear, as things accursed. In two days we had it all done and the water in--a spacious pool of clear pure water that a body could swim in. It was running water, too. It came in, and went out, through the ancient pipes. The old abbot kept his word, and was the first to try it. He went down black and shaky, leaving the whole black community above troubled and worried and full of bodings; but he came back white and joyful, and the game was made! another triumph scored.

text: Who was behind this atrocity? The finger pointed toward a lowly household in the district, one that had recently endured the baron’s singular brutality; from that seed, the blame crept outward to their kin and close acquaintances. Mere conjecture sufficed. The baron’s uniformed servants launched a swift crusade against these folk, and the wider populace eagerly enlisted. The woman’s husband had marched with the rabble and had not reappeared until near daybreak. Now he had slipped away to learn the final reckoning. While our words still hung in the air, he returned from his errand. His tidings were sickening. Eighteen souls hanged or slaughtered, and two freeholders and thirteen captives consumed in the blaze.
source_text: Who had done this? Suspicion fell upon a humble family in the neighborhood who had been lately treated with peculiar harshness by the baron; and from these people the suspicion easily extended itself to their relatives and familiars. A suspicion was enough; my lord's liveried retainers proclaimed an instant crusade against these people, and were promptly joined by the community in general. The woman's husband had been active with the mob, and had not returned home until nearly dawn. He was gone now to find out what the general result had been. While we were still talking he came back from his quest. His report was revolting enough. Eighteen persons hanged or butchered, and two yeomen and thirteen prisoners lost in the fire.

text: “Your heart is kind, and your counsel is not without wisdom. Yet it would be disgrace for a sovereign to cower, and disgrace for a girded warrior to stay his blade when souls cry out for aid. Enough. I shall not depart. It is you who must take leave. The anathema of the Church rests not on my head, but it bars you from this ground, and she will strike with a stern rod should whisper of your trespass reach her ear.”
source_text: “Ye mean well, and ye speak not unwisely. But it were shame that a king should know fear, and shame that belted knight should withhold his hand where be such as need succor. Peace, I will not go. It is you who must go. The Church's ban is not upon me, but it forbiddeth you to be here, and she will deal with you with a heavy hand an word come to her of your trespass.”

text: He stripped the skin from their living bodies, and they had it coming—at any rate, the entire kingdom celebrated. Among those flayed were Sir Agravaine and Sir Mordred, the king’s own nephews. That closed the first act. Act two, scene one: a chamber in Carlisle castle, where the court had withdrawn for a few days’ sport. Those in the room were the whole clan of the king’s nephews. Mordred and Agravaine urge that the unsuspecting Arthur be made to see what Guenever and Sir Launcelot are about. Sir Gawaine, Sir Gareth, and Sir Gaheris refuse to take part. A quarrel breaks out, voices rising; in the middle of it the king walks in. Mordred and Agravaine unleash their ruinous accusation. _Frozen moment_. By the king’s order a snare is set for Launcelot, and Sir Launcelot steps straight into it. He made things decidedly unpleasant for the hidden onlookers—namely, Mordred, Agravaine, and twelve lesser knights, since he slaughtered every last one of them except Mordred; but naturally that could not set things right between Launcelot and the king, and it didn’t.
source_text: “He skinned them alive, and they deserved it--anyway, the whole kingdom rejoiced. Well, among the flayed were Sir Agravaine and Sir Mordred, nephews to the king. End of the first act. Act second, scene first, an apartment in Carlisle castle, where the court had gone for a few days' hunting. Persons present, the whole tribe of the king's nephews. Mordred and Agravaine propose to call the guileless Arthur's attention to Guenever and Sir Launcelot. Sir Gawaine, Sir Gareth, and Sir Gaheris will have nothing to do with it. A dispute ensues, with loud talk; in the midst of it enter the king. Mordred and Agravaine spring their devastating tale upon him. _Tableau_. A trap is laid for Launcelot, by the king's command, and Sir Launcelot walks into it. He made it sufficiently uncomfortable for the ambushed witnesses--to wit, Mordred, Agravaine, and twelve knights of lesser rank, for he killed every one of them but Mordred; but of course that couldn't straighten matters between Launcelot and the king, and didn't.”

text: A young girl soon appeared, a slender thing of perhaps ten, with a cascade of blonde hair tumbling over her shoulders. She wore a crown of scarlet poppies around her head. It was as charming a getup as I’d ever laid eyes on, scant though it was. She ambled by lazily, her mind untroubled, its calm mirrored in her guileless face. The circus performer took no notice of her; he didn’t even appear to register her presence. And she—she was no more taken aback by his outlandish costume than if she encountered such sights daily. She passed him as casually as she might pass a pair of grazing cattle; but when she chanced to spot me, _that_ altered everything! Her hands flew up, and she froze in place; her jaw fell, her eyes stretched wide and fearful, a perfect image of stunned curiosity laced with dread. There she remained, staring in a kind of dazed captivation, until we rounded a bend in the trees and vanished from her sight. That she should be shocked by me rather than by the other fellow completely baffled me; I couldn’t make sense of it at all. And that she should view me as a spectacle while utterly blind to her own qualifications in that department was another perplexing matter—and a show of generosity, too, that was astonishing in one so young. This gave me plenty to ponder. I drifted onward as if in a trance.
source_text: Presently a fair slip of a girl, about ten years old, with a cataract of golden hair streaming down over her shoulders, came along. Around her head she wore a hoop of flame-red poppies. It was as sweet an outfit as ever I saw, what there was of it. She walked indolently along, with a mind at rest, its peace reflected in her innocent face. The circus man paid no attention to her; didn't even seem to see her. And she--she was no more startled at his fantastic make-up than if she was used to his like every day of her life. She was going by as indifferently as she might have gone by a couple of cows; but when she happened to notice me, _then_ there was a change! Up went her hands, and she was turned to stone; her mouth dropped open, her eyes stared wide and timorously, she was the picture of astonished curiosity touched with fear. And there she stood gazing, in a sort of stupefied fascination, till we turned a corner of the wood and were lost to her view. That she should be startled at me instead of at the other man, was too many for me; I couldn't make head or tail of it. And that she should seem to consider me a spectacle, and totally overlook her own merits in that respect, was another puzzling thing, and a display of magnanimity, too, that was surprising in one so young. There was food for thought here. I moved along as one in a dream.

text: “That’s just one more falsehood. Half of them are off enjoying their diversions, the queen among them, and the other half aren’t asleep—they’re in the saddle. So perhaps you might now enlarge a little, and tell us where the king and queen, and all those riding with them at this very moment, are bound?”
source_text: “That is merely another lie. Half of them are about their amusements, the queen and the other half are not sleeping, they ride. Now perhaps you can spread yourself a little, and tell us where the king and queen and all that are this moment riding with them are going?”

text: Muffled footsteps drew near, and we pressed ourselves flat where we lay. A second knight came dimly into view, moving with extreme caution, groping his way forward. He was close enough now that we could watch him reach out, touch the top wire, then duck beneath it and step carefully over the lower one. He approached the first knight—and gave a small start when he recognized him. He paused briefly, likely puzzled that the other man hadn’t stirred; then he murmured, “Why dost thou linger dreaming, good Sir Mar—” and placed his hand on the dead man’s shoulder. A faint, soft groan escaped him, and he collapsed lifeless. Slain by a dead man, you understand—slain by a dead friend, really. There was something dreadful in it.
source_text: We heard muffled sounds approaching, and we sank down to the ground where we were. We made out another knight vaguely; he was coming very stealthily, and feeling his way. He was near enough now for us to see him put out a hand, find an upper wire, then bend and step under it and over the lower one. Now he arrived at the first knight--and started slightly when he discovered him. He stood a moment--no doubt wondering why the other one didn't move on; then he said, in a low voice, “Why dreamest thou here, good Sir Mar--” then he laid his hand on the corpse's shoulder--and just uttered a little soft moan and sunk down dead. Killed by a dead man, you see--killed by a dead friend, in fact. There was something awful about it.

text: I was swiftly growing accustomed to my new state and surroundings. For a while, I’d rise at dawn, grinning at what I took for a “vision,” and strain to hear the factory horn from Colt’s; but that phase faded little by little, until at length I could fully grasp that I dwelt truly in the sixth century, in King Arthur’s realm, and not in some madhouse. Once that settled in, I felt as native to that age as I could to any other; and if it came to choosing, I’d not have swapped it for the twentieth. Consider the prospects here for a fellow of learning, wit, nerve, and ambition to plunge in and rise alongside the land itself. The noblest arena ever conceived; and mine alone; no rival; not a soul who wasn’t an infant next to me in knowledge and gifts; whereas, what would I count for in the twentieth century? A factory foreman, more or less; and I could haul a net down any street and land a hundred men better than me.
source_text: I was fast getting adjusted to my situation and circumstances. For a time, I used to wake up, mornings, and smile at my “dream,” and listen for the Colt's factory whistle; but that sort of thing played itself out, gradually, and at last I was fully able to realize that I was actually living in the sixth century, and in Arthur's court, not a lunatic asylum. After that, I was just as much at home in that century as I could have been in any other; and as for preference, I wouldn't have traded it for the twentieth. Look at the opportunities here for a man of knowledge, brains, pluck, and enterprise to sail in and grow up with the country. The grandest field that ever was; and all my own; not a competitor; not a man who wasn't a baby to me in acquirements and capacities; whereas, what would I amount to in the twentieth century? I should be foreman of a factory, that is about all; and could drag a seine down street any day and catch a hundred better men than myself.

text: The travelers were mortal creatures. Had they been otherwise, their response would not have been the same. They had endured a harsh and wearisome passage, and now, with the end nearly in sight, upon discovering that the very object of their quest had vanished from the world, they did not behave as oxen or ravens or blind serpents might have done—retreat and seek some gain elsewhere—no, fervent as they had once been to behold the enchanted spring, they were now fortyfold more fervent to gaze upon the spot where it once had flowed. The human heart defies all reckoning.
source_text: The pilgrims were human beings. Otherwise they would have acted differently. They had come a long and difficult journey, and now when the journey was nearly finished, and they learned that the main thing they had come for had ceased to exist, they didn't do as horses or cats or angle-worms would probably have done--turn back and get at something profitable--no, anxious as they had before been to see the miraculous fountain, they were as much as forty times as anxious now to see the place where it had used to be. There is no accounting for human beings.

text: Was I proud? Absolutely. There stood Arthur, ruler of Britain; there stood Guenever; and beyond them, whole clusters of minor kings and petty chieftains. In the tented field nearby waited celebrated knights from distant realms, along with the most elite company chivalry could boast—the Knights of the Round Table, the brightest lights in all Christendom. And greatest of all, the very sun around which their glittering world revolved was right there leveling his lance, the focus of forty thousand worshiping eyes. And here I was, all alone, lying in wait for him. Through my thoughts drifted the sweet face of a certain telephone operator from West Hartford, and I longed for her to see me now. Then, in an instant, the Invincible came crashing down with a whirlwind’s fury—the elegant assembly surged to its feet and leaned forward—the decisive coils whistled through the air, and before you could blink I was dragging Sir Launcelot across the field on his back, blowing kisses to the wild flurry of waving handkerchiefs and the thunderous roar of applause that met me!
source_text: A proud moment for me? I should think so. Yonder was Arthur, King of Britain; yonder was Guenever; yes, and whole tribes of little provincial kings and kinglets; and in the tented camp yonder, renowned knights from many lands; and likewise the selectest body known to chivalry, the Knights of the Table Round, the most illustrious in Christendom; and biggest fact of all, the very sun of their shining system was yonder couching his lance, the focal point of forty thousand adoring eyes; and all by myself, here was I laying for him. Across my mind flitted the dear image of a certain hello-girl of West Hartford, and I wished she could see me now. In that moment, down came the Invincible, with the rush of a whirlwind--the courtly world rose to its feet and bent forward --the fateful coils went circling through the air, and before you could wink I was towing Sir Launcelot across the field on his back, and kissing my hand to the storm of waving kerchiefs and the thunder-crash of applause that greeted me!

text: In the quiet and the gloom, understanding began to deepen beyond mere awareness. The bare awareness of a truth is colorless; but when you begin to _feel_ that truth, it flushes with hue. It is the gulf between being told of a blade driven through a man’s heart, and watching the act unfold. In the quiet and the gloom, the awareness that I stood in mortal peril gathered richer and richer significance with each passing moment; a thing called comprehension stole inch by inch along my nerves and chilled me to the marrow.
source_text: In the stillness and the darkness, realization soon began to supplement knowledge. The mere knowledge of a fact is pale; but when you come to _realize_ your fact, it takes on color. It is all the difference between hearing of a man being stabbed to the heart, and seeing it done. In the stillness and the darkness, the knowledge that I was in deadly danger took to itself deeper and deeper meaning all the time; a something which was realization crept inch by inch through my veins and turned me cold.

text: Back then I had no notion I was selecting a treasure, but a treasure is precisely what I found. Before a year had passed I had turned into her devoted admirer, and what we shared was the most tender and flawless union ever known. Folk often praise the lovely bonds that form between two of the same sex. Yet set the finest example of that kind beside the bond of husband and wife, where their noblest desires and loftiest dreams run in perfect accord—what comparison can stand? There is no ground for measuring one against the other; the former is of the earth, the latter belongs to heaven.
source_text: Now I didn't know I was drawing a prize, yet that was what I did draw. Within the twelvemonth I became her worshiper; and ours was the dearest and perfectest comradeship that ever was. People talk about beautiful friendships between two persons of the same sex. What is the best of that sort, as compared with the friendship of man and wife, where the best impulses and highest ideals of both are the same? There is no place for comparison between the two friendships; the one is earthly, the other divine.

text: He conceded that I belonged to him, a prize taken by his lance. The reasoning was on his side—and so was most of the leverage—so I figured it was wiser to play along. We struck a bargain: I’d accompany him, and he’d do me no harm. I climbed down, and we set off, me trudging beside his mount. We traveled at an easy pace, threading through clearings and crossing streams I had no recollection of ever seeing—which baffled me and set me to wondering—yet no circus or trace of a circus appeared. So I let go of the circus notion and decided he’d escaped from an asylum. But we never reached an asylum—so I was at a dead end, you might say. I asked how far we were from Hartford. He claimed he’d never heard of the town; I figured that for a falsehood, but let it pass. After an hour, we glimpsed a distant town nestled in a valley beside a meandering river; and on a hill beyond it, an immense gray stronghold, bristling with towers and turrets—the first I’d ever laid eyes on outside a book.
source_text: He allowed that I was his property, the captive of his spear. There was argument on his side--and the bulk of the advantage --so I judged it best to humor him. We fixed up an agreement whereby I was to go with him and he was not to hurt me. I came down, and we started away, I walking by the side of his horse. We marched comfortably along, through glades and over brooks which I could not remember to have seen before--which puzzled me and made me wonder--and yet we did not come to any circus or sign of a circus. So I gave up the idea of a circus, and concluded he was from an asylum. But we never came to an asylum--so I was up a stump, as you may say. I asked him how far we were from Hartford. He said he had never heard of the place; which I took to be a lie, but allowed it to go at that. At the end of an hour we saw a far-away town sleeping in a valley by a winding river; and beyond it on a hill, a vast gray fortress, with towers and turrets, the first I had ever seen out of a picture.

text: “May every kind spirit—if any still exist—bless you for those words. God, I’d give anything for a mouthful of water! But no, stop, forget I spoke, and go—flee this place. For here dwells something that even the man who mocks the Church must dread: this sickness that is killing us. Leave us, brave and kindly stranger, and carry with you whatever pure and heartfelt blessing such cursed souls as we can offer.”
source_text: “Now all good spirits--if there be any such--bless thee for that word. Would God I had a sup of water!--but hold, hold, forget I said it, and fly; for there is that here that even he that feareth not the Church must fear: this disease whereof we die. Leave us, thou brave, good stranger, and take with thee such whole and sincere blessing as them that be accursed can give.”

text: I soon abandoned the search, for the darkness fell like a curtain of pitch, but I kept my hearing sharpened for the faintest hint of danger, reckoning I had only to be patient, and my vigil would be rewarded. Yet the wait stretched on and on. Eventually I caught what might be called blurred fragments of noise—a muffled, metallic clinking. I straightened my ears then and stilled my breath, for this was precisely the token I had anticipated. The noise swelled and drew nearer—from the direction of the north. Before long, I perceived it at my own elevation—the crest of the facing ridge, a hundred feet distant or more. Then I fancied a line of dark specks emerging along that ridge—human crowns? I could not be sure; it might be nothing whatsoever; one cannot trust the eyes when the mind’s focus falters. But the riddle was soon resolved. I caught that metallic clatter descending into the vast trench. It quickened rapidly, it rippled along the whole line, and it plainly delivered me this truth: a body of armed men was settling into the ditch. Indeed, these folk were preparing a modest ambuscade for us. We might look for amusement around daybreak, perhaps sooner.
source_text: I presently gave up looking, the night shut down so black, but I kept my ears strained to catch the least suspicious sound, for I judged I had only to wait, and I shouldn't be disappointed. However, I had to wait a long time. At last I caught what you may call indistinct glimpses of sound--dulled metallic sound. I pricked up my ears, then, and held my breath, for this was the sort of thing I had been waiting for. This sound thickened, and approached--from toward the north. Presently, I heard it at my own level--the ridge-top of the opposite embankment, a hundred feet or more away. Then I seemed to see a row of black dots appear along that ridge--human heads? I couldn't tell; it mightn't be anything at all; you can't depend on your eyes when your imagination is out of focus. However, the question was soon settled. I heard that metallic noise descending into the great ditch. It augmented fast, it spread all along, and it unmistakably furnished me this fact: an armed host was taking up its quarters in the ditch. Yes, these people were arranging a little surprise party for us. We could expect entertainment about dawn, possibly earlier.

text: “The moment is here, Father. I will soon speak the awful name and bid the enchantment break. You must steady yourself, and grasp hold of something.” Then I cried out to the crowd: “Watch now—within a minute the spell will shatter, or no human power can ever shatter it. If it shatters, none can doubt it, for you will see the holy water surge from the chapel door!”
source_text: “The time is come, Father. I am about to pronounce the dread name and command the spell to dissolve. You want to brace up, and take hold of something.” Then I shouted to the people: “Behold, in another minute the spell will be broken, or no mortal can break it. If it break, all will know it, for you will see the sacred water gush from the chapel door!”

text: “That won’t do, Alisande. Sir Marhaus, the king’s son of Ireland, speaks just like everyone else; you must furnish him with a brogue, or at the very least a signature oath. That way, the moment he opens his mouth, a listener would know him without needing his name. It’s a standard trick among the great writers. You ought to have him say, ‘In this land, be jabers, no knight has ever come since its christening without meeting strange adventures, be jabers.’ There—you hear how much finer that rings.”
source_text: “This is not good form, Alisande. Sir Marhaus the king's son of Ireland talks like all the rest; you ought to give him a brogue, or at least a characteristic expletive; by this means one would recognize him as soon as he spoke, without his ever being named. It is a common literary device with the great authors. You should make him say, 'In this country, be jabers, came never knight since it was christened, but he found strange adventures, be jabers.' You see how much better that sounds.”

text: Ah, what a strange land it was, brimming with marvels. And its inhabitants! They were the most innocent and artless and credulous of creatures—sheep, pure and simple. It wrung the heart of anyone raised in a clean, unfettered climate to hear their meek and earnest gushings of devotion to their sovereign and clergy and lords; as though they possessed any greater cause to cherish and revere crown and altar and title than a beast of burden has to cherish the whip, or a cur has to revere the boot that spurns him! Gracious heavens, any species of monarchy, however softened, any species of peerage, however trimmed, is in its essence a mockery; yet if you draw your first breath and take your nurture inside such a scheme, you may never perceive it unaided, and you’ll scarce credit it when another soul lays it bare. A man could blush for his own lineage reflecting on the foam that has eternally squatted upon its seats of power without a wisp of justice or logic, and the tenth-rate folk who have eternally posed as its patricians—a parade of kings and lords who, on the whole, would have reaped only want and namelessness had they been cast, like their superiors, upon their own merits.
source_text: Well, it was a curious country, and full of interest. And the people! They were the quaintest and simplest and trustingest race; why, they were nothing but rabbits. It was pitiful for a person born in a wholesome free atmosphere to listen to their humble and hearty outpourings of loyalty toward their king and Church and nobility; as if they had any more occasion to love and honor king and Church and noble than a slave has to love and honor the lash, or a dog has to love and honor the stranger that kicks him! Why, dear me, _any_ kind of royalty, howsoever modified, _any_ kind of aristocracy, howsoever pruned, is rightly an insult; but if you are born and brought up under that sort of arrangement you probably never find it out for yourself, and don't believe it when somebody else tells you. It is enough to make a body ashamed of his race to think of the sort of froth that has always occupied its thrones without shadow of right or reason, and the seventh-rate people that have always figured as its aristocracies--a company of monarchs and nobles who, as a rule, would have achieved only poverty and obscurity if left, like their betters, to their own exertions.

text: “My lads, your spirits are true, you’ve conceived a noble idea, and you’ve carried out a noble deed. You are sons of England, you shall ever be sons of England, and you will leave that title unstained. Trouble yourselves no more, set your thoughts at rest. Think on this: when all England advances upon us, who stands at the forefront? Who, by the simplest laws of battle, will take the lead? Tell me.”
source_text: “My boys, your hearts are in the right place, you have thought the worthy thought, you have done the worthy thing. You are English boys, you will remain English boys, and you will keep that name unsmirched. Give yourselves no further concern, let your minds be at peace. Consider this: while all England is marching against us, who is in the van? Who, by the commonest rules of war, will march in the front? Answer me.”

text: My envoys were drilled to decipher the gilded letters upon their surcoats—that flashy ornamentation was a clever stroke; I might have draped the monarch himself in a placard just to relish the primitive magnificence—they were to read these inscriptions aloud and then instruct the assembled nobles on the nature of soap; and should the nobles shrink from it, persuade them to test it upon a hound. The envoy’s subsequent task was to gather the household and demonstrate it upon his own person; he was to flinch at no trial, no matter how reckless, that might persuade the aristocracy of its safety; if any lingering suspicion persisted, he must seize a hermit—the forests abounded with them; they styled themselves holy men, and the world took them at their word. They were unutterably sacred, performed wonders, and all folk trembled before them. Should a hermit endure a scrubbing and still fail to sway a baron, abandon the fellow, leave him to his fate.
source_text: My missionaries were taught to spell out the gilt signs on their tabards--the showy gilding was a neat idea, I could have got the king to wear a bulletin-board for the sake of that barbaric splendor--they were to spell out these signs and then explain to the lords and ladies what soap was; and if the lords and ladies were afraid of it, get them to try it on a dog. The missionary's next move was to get the family together and try it on himself; he was to stop at no experiment, however desperate, that could convince the nobility that soap was harmless; if any final doubt remained, he must catch a hermit--the woods were full of them; saints they called themselves, and saints they were believed to be. They were unspeakably holy, and worked miracles, and everybody stood in awe of them. If a hermit could survive a wash, and that failed to convince a duke, give him up, let him alone.

text: By ten in the morning on the sixteenth, the fairgrounds stretched wide but held no empty patch beyond the tournament barriers. The colossal grandstand blazed with banners, pennants, and sumptuous hangings, crammed with acres of minor vassal kings, their retinues, and England's nobility; our own royal crew occupied the center, every figure a dazzling burst of brilliant silks and velvets—frankly, the only thing I could compare it to was a collision between a Mississippi sunset and the northern lights. At one end of the lists sprawled an enormous encampment of flagged and vividly colored tents, each doorway guarded by a rigid sentinel with a polished shield displayed beside him for challenges—another striking spectacle. The truth was, every knight with a shred of pride or class loyalty had turned out, since my disdain for their brotherhood was hardly a secret, and this was their opportunity. Should I best Sir Sagramor in combat, others would gain the right to summon me for as long as I chose to answer.
source_text: Vast as the show-grounds were, there were no vacant spaces in them outside of the lists, at ten o'clock on the morning of the 16th. The mammoth grand-stand was clothed in flags, streamers, and rich tapestries, and packed with several acres of small-fry tributary kings, their suites, and the British aristocracy; with our own royal gang in the chief place, and each and every individual a flashing prism of gaudy silks and velvets--well, I never saw anything to begin with it but a fight between an Upper Mississippi sunset and the aurora borealis. The huge camp of beflagged and gay-colored tents at one end of the lists, with a stiff-standing sentinel at every door and a shining shield hanging by him for challenge, was another fine sight. You see, every knight was there who had any ambition or any caste feeling; for my feeling toward their order was not much of a secret, and so here was their chance. If I won my fight with Sir Sagramor, others would have the right to call me out as long as I might be willing to respond.

text: Before long we came upon a mishap. A ragged troop of half-dressed children burst from the forest, terrified and wailing. The oldest of the lot could not have been above twelve or fourteen. They begged for aid, but their frenzy was such that we could not discern the cause of their panic. Still, we rushed in among the trees, the youngsters darting ahead, and the mischief soon lay bare: they had strung up a small boy with a cord of bark, and he was writhing and flailing, on the verge of suffocation. We cut him loose and revived him. It was another glimpse of mankind’s nature; the adoring little ones mimicking their grown-ups; they had been staging a lynching, and had carried it to a success that threatened to be far graver than they had intended.
source_text: Presently we struck an incident. A small mob of half-naked boys and girls came tearing out of the woods, scared and shrieking. The eldest among them were not more than twelve or fourteen years old. They implored help, but they were so beside themselves that we couldn't make out what the matter was. However, we plunged into the wood, they skurrying in the lead, and the trouble was quickly revealed: they had hanged a little fellow with a bark rope, and he was kicking and struggling, in the process of choking to death. We rescued him, and fetched him around. It was some more human nature; the admiring little folk imitating their elders; they were playing mob, and had achieved a success which promised to be a good deal more serious than they had bargained for.

text: Every pilgrim among us watched and remarked on the deftness with which the whip was wielded. Their sensibilities had been so calloused by a lifetime of daily, unthinking exposure to slavery that nothing else in the scene seemed to them worthy of note. Such was slavery’s power to petrify what might be called the higher chambers of human sympathy; for these were, at heart, kindly souls, and they would never have stood by while a man treated a horse in such a fashion.
source_text: All our pilgrims looked on and commented--on the expert way in which the whip was handled. They were too much hardened by lifelong everyday familiarity with slavery to notice that there was anything else in the exhibition that invited comment. This was what slavery could do, in the way of ossifying what one may call the superior lobe of human feeling; for these pilgrims were kind-hearted people, and they would not have allowed that man to treat a horse like that.

text: The group halted, two or three hundred yards distant, and this unsettled me. My confidence crumbled, and dread crept in; I knew I was doomed. But Elara’s face shone; she was poised to speak with fervor—but I cut her short, and confessed my sorcery had failed, somehow, and she must leap up, with all speed, and we must flee for our lives. No, she refused. She declared my spell had crippled those warriors; they were not advancing, because they could not; wait, they would soon tumble from their mounts, and we would claim their steeds and armor. I could not abuse such guileless faith, so I admitted it was a blunder; that when my pyrotechnics slew at all, they slew at once; no, the men would not perish, something was amiss with my device, I could not say what; but we must hasten and escape, for those foes would assail us again, in a moment. Elara laughed, and answered:
source_text: But these people stopped, two or three hundred yards away, and this troubled me. My satisfaction collapsed, and fear came; I judged I was a lost man. But Sandy was radiant; and was going to be eloquent--but I stopped her, and told her my magic had miscarried, somehow or other, and she must mount, with all despatch, and we must ride for life. No, she wouldn't. She said that my enchantment had disabled those knights; they were not riding on, because they couldn't; wait, they would drop out of their saddles presently, and we would get their horses and harness. I could not deceive such trusting simplicity, so I said it was a mistake; that when my fireworks killed at all, they killed instantly; no, the men would not die, there was something wrong about my apparatus, I couldn't tell what; but we must hurry and get away, for those people would attack us again, in a minute. Sandy laughed, and said:

text: That, it seems to me, could very well be true," said the abbot, seizing his chance to ease tensions and ward off calamity, "since it is hardly plausible that such a remarkable gift would be bestowed to disclose the affairs of lesser souls than those born close to the pinnacles of power. Our King Arthur—
source_text: “That, it meseemeth, might well be,” said the abbot, who saw his opportunity to smooth things and avert disaster, “for it were not likely that so wonderful a gift as this would be conferred for the revelation of the concerns of lesser beings than such as be born near to the summits of greatness. Our Arthur the king--”

text: “Hark! I charge the foul shade that clings to this sacred spring to spew forth into the heavens every last ember of its hellish flame, and at once unmake its curse and fly to the abyss, chained there for a millennium. By its own dire title I compel it—XZTRRMMALPPHH!”
source_text: “Lo, I command the fell spirit that possesses the holy fountain to now disgorge into the skies all the infernal fires that still remain in him, and straightway dissolve his spell and flee hence to the pit, there to lie bound a thousand years. By his own dread name I command it--BGWJJILLIGKKK!”

text: Before my departure, there was a task I needed to face, though it was an unpleasant one, and I dreaded beginning it. It nagged at me all morning. I could have brought it up with the old king, but what good would that have done? He was nothing more than a spent volcano—once fiery in his prime, but now long cooled, a dignified mound of ash. He was gentle and kind enough for my purposes, no doubt, but utterly useless. This so-called king was a cipher; the queen alone held real power. And she was a Vesuvius. She might, as a kindness, consent to warm a few sparrows for you, but in that very moment she could just as easily erupt and bury a city. Still, I reminded myself that, as often as not, when you brace for the worst, what comes is not so terrible after all.
source_text: There was something I wanted to do before leaving, but it was a disagreeable matter, and I hated to go at it. Well, it bothered me all the morning. I could have mentioned it to the old king, but what would be the use?--he was but an extinct volcano; he had been active in his time, but his fire was out, this good while, he was only a stately ash-pile now; gentle enough, and kindly enough for my purpose, without doubt, but not usable. He was nothing, this so-called king: the queen was the only power there. And she was a Vesuvius. As a favor, she might consent to warm a flock of sparrows for you, but then she might take that very opportunity to turn herself loose and bury a city. However, I reflected that as often as any other way, when you are expecting the worst, you get something that is not so bad, after all.

text: It was true, at least in recent memory—there was proof of it, and more reliable proof than a monk’s word. Only twenty or thirty feet of the chain bore the marks of wear and handling; the rest was untouched, furred with rust. What had occurred the last time the well failed? Undoubtedly, some shrewd fellow had come along, patched the leak, and then emerged to inform the abbot that through holy divination he had learned the sinful bath must be destroyed for the water to return. Now the leak had sprung again, and these children would have prayed, marched in procession, and rung their bells beseeching heaven’s aid until they were all withered and scattered to the wind—and not one innocent among them would ever have thought to lower a fishing line or climb down to discover the true cause. Old habits of thought are among the hardest things in the world to shed. They pass down like physical features and traits; and in those times, for a man to conceive an idea his forebears had not held would have cast doubt on his very legitimacy. I said to the monk:
source_text: It was true--as to recent times at least--for there was witness to it, and better witness than a monk; only about twenty or thirty feet of the chain showed wear and use, the rest of it was unworn and rusty. What had happened when the well gave out that other time?
