Without doubt some practical person had come along and mended the leak, and then had come up and told the abbot he had discovered by divination that if the sinful bath were destroyed the well would flow again. The leak had befallen again now, and these children would have prayed, and processioned, and tolled their bells for heavenly succor till they all dried up and blew away, and no innocent of them all would ever have thought to drop a fish-line into the well or go down in it and find out what was really the matter. Old habit of mind is one of the toughest things to get away from in the world. It transmits itself like physical form and feature; and for a man, in those days, to have had an idea that his ancestors hadn't had, would have brought him under suspicion of being illegitimate. I said to the monk:

text: Here stood these freeholders gathered at dawn to labour upon their lord bishop’s highway three days apiece—without wage; every householder, every householder’s son, three days apiece, without wage, and an extra day or two thrown in for their hirelings. It was as though one were perusing a chronicle of France and her people before that ever-memorable and hallowed Revolution, which scoured a millennium of such iniquity clean in a single great salt tide of slaughter—one: a settling of that ancient account at the rate of half a blood-drop for every hogshead of it that had been wrung by creeping agonies from that nation across the long-drawn horror of ten centuries of injury and disgrace and suffering whose match could be found nowhere save in the pit. There were two “Reigns of Terror,” if we would only recollect and weigh the matter; the one dealt murder in furious heat, the other in passionless cold calculation; the one endured bare months, the other had endured a thousand years; the one dealt death to ten thousand souls, the other to a hundred millions; yet all our trembling is reserved for the “atrocities” of the lesser Terror, the fleeting Terror, so to call it; while what is the dread of quick dying by the blade, set beside lifelong dying of starvation, freezing, scorn, brutality, and despair? What is quick dying by a thunderbolt beside dying by slow coals at the stake? A municipal graveyard might hold the coffins packed by that short Terror we have all been so sedulously schooled to shudder at and bewail; but the whole of France could scarce contain the coffins packed by that elder and genuine Terror—that unutterably harsh and grievous Terror which none of us has been taught to behold in its immensity or to compassionate as it merits.
source_text: And here were these freemen assembled in the early morning to work on their lord the bishop's road three days each--gratis; every head of a family, and every son of a family, three days each, gratis, and a day or so added for their servants. Why, it was like reading about France and the French, before the ever memorable and blessed Revolution, which swept a thousand years of such villany away in one swift tidal-wave of blood--one: a settlement of that hoary debt in the proportion of half a drop of blood for each hogshead of it that had been pressed by slow tortures out of that people in the weary stretch of ten centuries of wrong and shame and misery the like of which was not to be mated but in hell. There were two “Reigns of Terror,” if we would but remember it and consider it; the one wrought murder in hot passion, the other in heartless cold blood; the one lasted mere months, the other had lasted a thousand years; the one inflicted death upon ten thousand persons, the other upon a hundred millions; but our shudders are all for the “horrors” of the minor Terror, the momentary Terror, so to speak; whereas, what is the horror of swift death by the axe, compared with lifelong death from hunger, cold, insult, cruelty, and heart-break? What is swift death by lightning compared with death by slow fire at the stake? A city cemetery could contain the coffins filled by that brief Terror which we have all been so diligently taught to shiver at and mourn over; but all France could hardly contain the coffins filled by that older and real Terror --that unspeakably bitter and awful Terror which none of us has been taught to see in its vastness or pity as it deserves.

text: Behold, it was the darkening sun. The notion struck me at the very moment of need—how some great explorer, perhaps Cortez or another of that ilk, had once wielded an eclipse like a hidden ace against a band of primitives. And here lay my opportunity. I could stage the trick myself, right then, and it would be no mere theft of history, for I would beat those fellows to the deed by near a thousand years.
source_text: You see, it was the eclipse. It came into my mind in the nick of time, how Columbus, or Cortez, or one of those people, played an eclipse as a saving trump once, on some savages, and I saw my chance. I could play it myself, now, and it wouldn't be any plagiarism, either, because I should get it in nearly a thousand years ahead of those parties.

text: “Well, I didn't suppose you had, but I wanted to ask just to be certain—that's how I was brought up. Now don't take offense if I point out that, since we're strangers to you, we need to proceed with some caution. You might be perfectly fine, and we certainly hope so, but assuming so without proof isn't sensible. _You_ see that. I have to put a few questions to you; just answer honestly and openly, and don't worry. Where do you make your home, when you're there?”
source_text: “Well, I didn't expect you had, but I thought I would ask, to make sure; it's the way I've been raised. Now you mustn't take it unkindly if I remind you that as we don't know you, we must go a little slow. You may be all right, of course, and we'll hope that you are; but to take it for granted isn't business. _You_ understand that. I'm obliged to ask you a few questions; just answer up fair and square, and don't be afraid. Where do you live, when you are at home?”

text: I hurried with the message to our lord the king, and at once he summoned me before him. He was terrified to his very core, and was ready to decree your immediate release, to have you dressed in splendid garments and housed as befits a man of your stature; but then Merlin arrived and ruined everything. He convinced the king that you are insane and don’t understand your own words, insisting that your warning is mere foolishness and empty boasting. They argued at length, but finally Merlin, sneering, said, “Why hasn’t he _named_ this grand disaster of his? Surely it’s because he can’t.” This jab so abruptly silenced the king that he could find no reply to counter the point; and so, unwilling and deeply reluctant to treat you with disrespect, he nevertheless begs you to appreciate his difficult position, given how matters stand, and to name the disaster—if you have indeed determined its nature and the time of its arrival. Oh, I implore you, don’t delay; to hesitate at such a moment would be to multiply the dangers already surrounding you. Oh, be sensible—name the disaster!
source_text: “I hasted the message to our liege the king, and straightway he had me to his presence. He was frighted even to the marrow, and was minded to give order for your instant enlargement, and that you be clothed in fine raiment and lodged as befitted one so great; but then came Merlin and spoiled all; for he persuaded the king that you are mad, and know not whereof you speak; and said your threat is but foolishness and idle vaporing. They disputed long, but in the end, Merlin, scoffing, said, 'Wherefore hath he not _named_ his brave calamity? Verily it is because he cannot.' This thrust did in a most sudden sort close the king's mouth, and he could offer naught to turn the argument; and so, reluctant, and full loth to do you the discourtesy, he yet prayeth you to consider his perplexed case, as noting how the matter stands, and name the calamity--if so be you have determined the nature of it and the time of its coming. Oh, prithee delay not; to delay at such a time were to double and treble the perils that already compass thee about. Oh, be thou wise--name the calamity!”

text: “Of a hotel? That’s what you’d call a hostel. A man capable of such a marvel could run a hostel. I am capable of this marvel; I will perform this marvel; still, I won’t hide from you that it’s a marvel that will push occult powers to their absolute limit.”
source_text: “Of hotel? It's what you call hostel. The man that can do this miracle can keep hostel. I can do this miracle; I shall do this miracle; yet I do not try to conceal from you that it is a miracle to tax the occult powers to the last strain.”

text: Earlier, I had seized a wooden bowl and hurried past the king toward the brook, only ten yards distant. By the time I returned and stepped inside, the king was already there, unfastening the shutter over the window-hole to admit fresh air and daylight. A vile odor saturated the room. I raised the bowl to the woman's lips, and just as she clutched it with desperate, claw-like hands, the shutter swung wide, spilling harsh light across her face. Smallpox.
source_text: But before this I had picked up a wooden bowl and was rushing past the king on my way to the brook. It was ten yards away. When I got back and entered, the king was within, and was opening the shutter that closed the window-hole, to let in air and light. The place was full of a foul stench. I put the bowl to the woman's lips, and as she gripped it with her eager talons the shutter came open and a strong light flooded her face. Smallpox!

text: The king might have lived out his years in cheerful ignorance had it not been for one of your contemporary contrivances—the stock ticker. By the time you departed, three miles of the London, Canterbury and Dover line were primed for the tracks, and equally primed and ready for speculation on the exchange. It was a reckless venture, and no one was fooled. The shares were practically being given away. So what does Sir Launcelot do but—
source_text: “Well, the king might have gone on, still happy and unsuspecting, to the end of his days, but for one of your modern improvements --the stock-board. When you left, three miles of the London, Canterbury and Dover were ready for the rails, and also ready and ripe for manipulation in the stock-market. It was wildcat, and everybody knew it. The stock was for sale at a give-away. What does Sir Launcelot do, but--”

text: I released forty-seven prisoners from those vile, cramped cells, keeping only one man locked up. He was a nobleman who had slain another noble—some distant relative of the queen’s. The other lord had waylaid him with murderous intent, but this man had prevailed and slit his enemy’s throat. Yet that wasn’t why I left him imprisoned; it was because he had spitefully ruined the sole public well in one of his own miserable villages. The queen was determined to hang him for killing her kinsman, but I forbade it: slaying a would-be murderer was no offense. However, I told her I’d permit the hanging for the well’s destruction; so she settled for that, reckoning it was better than nothing at all.
source_text: I set forty-seven prisoners loose out of those awful rat-holes, and left only one in captivity. He was a lord, and had killed another lord, a sort of kinsman of the queen. That other lord had ambushed him to assassinate him, but this fellow had got the best of him and cut his throat. However, it was not for that that I left him jailed, but for maliciously destroying the only public well in one of his wretched villages. The queen was bound to hang him for killing her kinsman, but I would not allow it: it was no crime to kill an assassin. But I said I was willing to let her hang him for destroying the well; so she concluded to put up with that, as it was better than nothing.

text: We had to herd those pigs back ten miles, and no fine ladies ever matched them for whimsical stubbornness. They refused to keep to any lane or track, bursting through the undergrowth on every side and spilling away in all directions across boulders, slopes, and the most broken ground they could discover. And you could not strike them or speak to them harshly; Sandy could not stand to see them handled in a manner unfitting their station. Even the most exasperating old sow had to be addressed as my Lady and your Highness, just like the rest. Chasing after pigs in full armor is a vexing and cumbersome business. There was one little countess, with an iron ring in her snout and a nearly bald back, who was sheer perversity incarnate. She led me on an hour-long chase over every kind of terrain, and then we ended up exactly where we began, without a yard of genuine headway. At last I caught her by the tail and hauled her along shrieking. When I caught up with Sandy, she was appalled and declared it the height of indelicacy to drag a countess by her train.
source_text: We had to drive those hogs home--ten miles; and no ladies were ever more fickle-minded or contrary. They would stay in no road, no path; they broke out through the brush on all sides, and flowed away in all directions, over rocks, and hills, and the roughest places they could find. And they must not be struck, or roughly accosted; Sandy could not bear to see them treated in ways unbecoming their rank. The troublesomest old sow of the lot had to be called my Lady, and your Highness, like the rest. It is annoying and difficult to scour around after hogs, in armor. There was one small countess, with an iron ring in her snout and hardly any hair on her back, that was the devil for perversity. She gave me a race of an hour, over all sorts of country, and then we were right where we had started from, having made not a rod of real progress. I seized her at last by the tail, and brought her along squealing. When I overtook Sandy she was horrified, and said it was in the last degree indelicate to drag a countess by her train.

text: We were constructing multiple railway lines, and our route from Camelot to London was already complete and running. I was clever enough to make every position tied to passenger service a role of high prestige and honor. My aim was to draw in the knights and nobles, put them to good use, and keep them out of trouble. The scheme succeeded brilliantly—the scramble for these posts was fierce. The conductor of the 4:33 express was a duke; not a single passenger conductor on the line ranked below an earl. They were fine men, all of them, but they had two flaws I couldn’t fix and so had to overlook: they refused to take off their armor, and they would “knock down” fare—by which I mean steal from the company.
source_text: We were building several lines of railway, and our line from Camelot to London was already finished and in operation. I was shrewd enough to make all offices connected with the passenger service places of high and distinguished honor. My idea was to attract the chivalry and nobility, and make them useful and keep them out of mischief. The plan worked very well, the competition for the places was hot. The conductor of the 4.33 express was a duke; there wasn't a passenger conductor on the line below the degree of earl. They were good men, every one, but they had two defects which I couldn't cure, and so had to wink at: they wouldn't lay aside their armor, and they would “knock down” fare --I mean rob the company.

text: I nearly wished my notion about the well’s failure hadn’t been right, for I’d spun another idea with a couple of flashy touches that would have seemed miraculous. I recalled how in America, ages later, when an oil well stopped pumping, they’d fire a dynamite charge to break it open. If I came upon this well bone-dry with no obvious cause, I could dazzle the locals splendidly by having some thoroughly expendable soul drop a dynamite bomb down it. I’d meant to volunteer Merlin. But clearly, there was no need for the bomb. You can’t always arrange things to your liking. A fellow shouldn’t let a setback sink his spirits, anyhow; he ought to resolve to settle the score. That’s precisely what I did. I told myself, there’s no rush, I can bide my time; that bomb will still prove its worth. And so it did.
source_text: I almost regretted that my theory about the well's trouble was correct, because I had another one that had a showy point or two about it for a miracle. I remembered that in America, many centuries later, when an oil well ceased to flow, they used to blast it out with a dynamite torpedo. If I should find this well dry and no explanation of it, I could astonish these people most nobly by having a person of no especial value drop a dynamite bomb into it. It was my idea to appoint Merlin. However, it was plain that there was no occasion for the bomb. One cannot have everything the way he would like it. A man has no business to be depressed by a disappointment, anyway; he ought to make up his mind to get even. That is what I did. I said to myself, I am in no hurry, I can wait; that bomb will come good yet. And it did, too.

text: My appetite for literature was so keen that I longed to devour the entire newspaper in a single sitting, but I managed only a few mouthfuls before I had to set it aside. The monks clustered around me, pelting me with urgent inquiries: What is this strange object? What purpose does it serve? Is it a kerchief—a saddle cloth—a fragment of a shirt? What fabric is this? See how slender and delicate it is, how crisp and rustling. Will it hold up, do you imagine, and won’t the rain spoil it? Are those marks upon it some kind of script, or mere decoration? They half guessed it was writing, because the few among them who could read Latin and had a faint acquaintance with Greek recognized scattered letters, yet they could wrest no coherent meaning from the whole. I framed my explanation as plainly as I could:
source_text: I was hungry enough for literature to want to take down the whole paper at this one meal, but I got only a few bites, and then had to postpone, because the monks around me besieged me so with eager questions: What is this curious thing? What is it for? Is it a handkerchief?--saddle blanket?--part of a shirt? What is it made of? How thin it is, and how dainty and frail; and how it rattles. Will it wear, do you think, and won't the rain injure it? Is it writing that appears on it, or is it only ornamentation? They suspected it was writing, because those among them who knew how to read Latin and had a smattering of Greek, recognized some of the letters, but they could make nothing out of the result as a whole. I put my information in the simplest form I could:

text: We raced along at a brisk pace, and before long the noises faded behind us, softening into a distant hum. We came upon a creek and plunged straight into it. In the muted glow of the forest, we hurried downstream for perhaps three hundred yards, until we spotted an oak with a thick limb jutting out above the water. We scrambled onto that limb and started inching our way along it toward the trunk; now the sounds began to reach us more clearly—the crowd had picked up our scent. For a time, the noises drew closer quite rapidly. Then, for another stretch, they didn’t. The hounds must have discovered the spot where we’d stepped into the creek and were now casting back and forth along the banks, trying to recover the trail.
source_text: We tore along at a good gait, and soon left the sounds far behind and modified to a murmur. We struck a stream and darted into it. We waded swiftly down it, in the dim forest light, for as much as three hundred yards, and then came across an oak with a great bough sticking out over the water. We climbed up on this bough, and began to work our way along it to the body of the tree; now we began to hear those sounds more plainly; so the mob had struck our trail. For a while the sounds approached pretty fast. And then for another while they didn't. No doubt the dogs had found the place where we had entered the stream, and were now waltzing up and down the shores trying to pick up the trail again.

text: The leave-taking—ah, truly, that was a trial. While I smothered the little one in parting embraces, it suddenly perked up and chirped out its entire stock of words!—the first utterance in over a fortnight, and we were made giddy with delight. Those precious mangled syllables of infancy!—Lord, no symphony can rival them; and what a sorrow it is when they fade and harden into proper speech, for one knows they will never grace his lonely ear again. How splendid, then, to bear that sweet remembrance with me as I went.
source_text: The parting--ah, yes, that was hard. As I was devouring the child with last kisses, it brisked up and jabbered out its vocabulary! --the first time in more than two weeks, and it made fools of us for joy. The darling mispronunciations of childhood!--dear me, there's no music that can touch it; and how one grieves when it wastes away and dissolves into correctness, knowing it will never visit his bereaved ear again. Well, how good it was to be able to carry that gracious memory away with me!

text: That journey was far from tedious for me. I filled the hours quite pleasantly. I struck up a number of acquaintances, and being a newcomer gave me license to inquire about whatever I pleased. As a politician, I was naturally drawn to the question of wages. Throughout the afternoon I gathered whatever insights I could on that topic. Someone with limited experience, who doesn't reflect deeply, tends to judge a country's wealth or poverty solely by the nominal level of its wages: high wages signal prosperity, low ones the reverse. That's a mistake. The crucial point isn't the amount you receive, but how much that amount will actually purchase; that alone reveals whether your pay is genuinely high or merely high on paper. I recalled how things stood during our great nineteenth-century civil war. In the North, a carpenter earned three dollars a day in gold; in the South, he earned fifty—paid in Confederate notes so devalued you could scoop up a bushel for a dollar. In the North, a pair of overalls cost three dollars—a single day's labor; in the South, the same pair cost seventy-five—eating up two days' wages. Everything else scaled accordingly. So in reality, wages in the North were double those in the South, because the Northern dollar commanded that much greater buying power.
source_text: It was not a dull excursion for me. I managed to put in the time very well. I made various acquaintanceships, and in my quality of stranger was able to ask as many questions as I wanted to. A thing which naturally interested me, as a statesman, was the matter of wages. I picked up what I could under that head during the afternoon. A man who hasn't had much experience, and doesn't think, is apt to measure a nation's prosperity or lack of prosperity by the mere size of the prevailing wages; if the wages be high, the nation is prosperous; if low, it isn't. Which is an error. It isn't what sum you get, it's how much you can buy with it, that's the important thing; and it's that that tells whether your wages are high in fact or only high in name. I could remember how it was in the time of our great civil war in the nineteenth century. In the North a carpenter got three dollars a day, gold valuation; in the South he got fifty--payable in Confederate shinplasters worth a dollar a bushel. In the North a suit of overalls cost three dollars--a day's wages; in the South it cost seventy-five --which was two days' wages. Other things were in proportion. Consequently, wages were twice as high in the North as they were in the South, because the one wage had that much more purchasing power than the other had.

text: Sylvaine received the afflicted in their turn. She assessed each supplicant; those unfit were turned aside, while the promising were sent onward to the sovereign. A cleric intoned the formula, “They shall place their hands upon the infirm, and health shall return.” The queen then traced the sores with her fingertips as the chant proceeded; at last, the sufferer was released, receiving a copper coin—the queen fastening it about his throat with her own hands—and was discharged. Could one believe such a rite would heal? It did, beyond question. Any rite will heal if the patient’s credence is fervent enough. Near Astolat stood a shrine where the Lady had once revealed herself to a goose-girl—the maid attested it herself—and a chapel was raised on that very ground, adorned with an image of the marvel, a depiction so ghastly you would deem it perilous for any ailing body to draw near; yet, on the contrary, multitudes of the halt and the diseased journeyed there each year to kneel before it and departed restored; even the hale could gaze on it and survive. Naturally, when these tales reached me I dismissed them; but once I traveled there and witnessed with my own eyes, I had to yield. I beheld the healings myself, and they were true healings, not dubious ones. I watched cripples I had known for years in Camelot hobbling on supports arrive, pray before that image, set aside their props, and stride away without a trace of lameness. Heaps of abandoned crutches lay there as their testament.
source_text: Marinel took the patients as they came. He examined the candidate; if he couldn't qualify he was warned off; if he could he was passed along to the king. A priest pronounced the words, “They shall lay their hands on the sick, and they shall recover.” Then the king stroked the ulcers, while the reading continued; finally, the patient graduated and got his nickel--the king hanging it around his neck himself--and was dismissed. Would you think that that would cure? It certainly did. Any mummery will cure if the patient's faith is strong in it. Up by Astolat there was a chapel where the Virgin had once appeared to a girl who used to herd geese around there--the girl said so herself--and they built the chapel upon that spot and hung a picture in it representing the occurrence--a picture which you would think it dangerous for a sick person to approach; whereas, on the contrary, thousands of the lame and the sick came and prayed before it every year and went away whole and sound; and even the well could look upon it and live. Of course, when I was told these things I did not believe them; but when I went there and saw them I had to succumb. I saw the cures effected myself; and they were real cures and not questionable. I saw cripples whom I had seen around Camelot for years on crutches, arrive and pray before that picture, and put down their crutches and walk off without a limp. There were piles of crutches there which had been left by such people as a testimony.

text: A glance at our advertisements reveals that the public is in for a remarkable tournament spectacle. The performers’ reputations guarantee fine entertainment. The ticket window opens at midday on the 13th; entry costs three cents, with reserved spots at five; all earnings will support the hospital fund. The royal couple and the entire Court will attend. Aside from these guests, along with journalists and ministers, the complimentary list has been completely cut off. Patrons are cautioned not to purchase tickets from scalpers; such passes will be refused at the entrance. Everyone knows and admires The Boss, everyone knows and admires Sir Sag.; so let’s give these fellows a rousing farewell. Keep in mind, the revenue goes to a vast and unconditional charity, one whose generous mercy extends a helping hand, pulsing with the warmth of a compassionate heart, to all in need—without regard to race, faith, circumstance, or skin color. It remains the only charity yet founded on earth that places no political or religious valve on its pity, but declares: Here the waters flow, let EVERYONE come and drink! Turn out in force, everyone! Bring your doughnuts and gumdrops and enjoy yourselves. Pies will be sold on the premises, with stones provided to break them; plus circus-style lemonade—three drops of lime essence to a barrel of water.
source_text: It will be observed, by a gl7nce at our advertising columns, that the commu- nity is to be favored with a treat of un- usual interest in the tournament line. The n ames of the artists are warrant of good enterTemment. The box-office will be open at noon of the 13th; ad- mission 3 cents, reserved seatsh 5; pro- ceeds to go to the hospital fund The royal pair and all the Court will be pres- ent. With these exceptions, and the press and the clergy, the free list is strict- ly susPended. Parties are hereby warn- ed against buying tickets of speculators; they will not be good at the door. Everybody knows and likes The Boss, everybody knows and likes Sir Sag.; come, let us give the lads a good send- off. ReMember, the proceeds go to a great and free charity, and one whose broad begevolence stretches out its help- ing hand, warm with the blood of a lov- ing heart, to all that suffer, regardless of race, creed, condition or color—the only charity yet established in the earth which has no politico-religious stop- cock on its compassion, but says Here flows the stream, let ALL come and drink! Turn out, all hands! fetch along your dou3hnuts and your gum-drops and have a good time. Pie for sale on the grounds, and rocks to crack it with; and ciRcus-lemonade—three drops of lime juice to a barrel of water.

text: I was nursing a private sting of failure. An unmerited failure, but did that matter? It didn’t lessen the sting one bit. And the sheer absurdity of the setting! The foremost mind of the era, the most accomplished figure, the most deeply read man on the globe, the most towering uncrowned intellect to stride through the skies of any political age in five hundred years, brought low in dispute, to all appearances, by an unlettered village blacksmith! I could feel the pity radiating from the others—pity that set my face burning until I half believed I could smell my own side-whiskers singeing. Trade places with me; taste that same smallness I tasted, that identical scalding shame—wouldn’t _you_ have swung low to settle the score? Of course you would; it is the bare grain of human nature. And that is precisely what I did. I am not mounting a defense; I am merely noting that I was furious, and _anyone_ would have acted likewise.
source_text: Well, I was smarting under a sense of defeat. Undeserved defeat, but what of that? That didn't soften the smart any. And to think of the circumstances! the first statesman of the age, the capablest man, the best-informed man in the entire world, the loftiest uncrowned head that had moved through the clouds of any political firmament for centuries, sitting here apparently defeated in argument by an ignorant country blacksmith! And I could see that those others were sorry for me--which made me blush till I could smell my whiskers scorching. Put yourself in my place; feel as mean as I did, as ashamed as I felt--wouldn't _you_ have struck below the belt to get even? Yes, you would; it is simply human nature. Well, that is what I did. I am not trying to justify it; I'm only saying that I was mad, and _anybody_ would have done it.

text: These supposed free men sharing their meal and conversation with me were steeped in as much meek devotion to their monarch, Church, and aristocracy as their fiercest critic could wish. There was a sadly absurd quality to it. I put it to them: could they imagine any people in history, each holding a free ballot, willingly choosing to let a single lineage and its heirs rule over them forever, whether brilliant or fools, shutting out all other families—including the voter's own? And choosing, too, to elevate a select hundred families to staggering heights of status, draping them in outrageous, inheritable honors and privileges while barring the rest of the nation's families—including his own.
source_text: These poor ostensible freemen who were sharing their breakfast and their talk with me, were as full of humble reverence for their king and Church and nobility as their worst enemy could desire. There was something pitifully ludicrous about it. I asked them if they supposed a nation of people ever existed, who, with a free vote in every man's hand, would elect that a single family and its descendants should reign over it forever, whether gifted or boobies, to the exclusion of all other families--including the voter's; and would also elect that a certain hundred families should be raised to dizzy summits of rank, and clothed on with offensive transmissible glories and privileges to the exclusion of the rest of the nation's families--_including his own_.

text: Marco’s delight flared up brilliantly—but only for an instant; then he became pensive, and after that, sorrowful. When he overheard me inform Dowley that I intended to bring Dickon, the head mason, and Smug, the head wheelwright, along as well, the coal grime on his cheeks seemed to pale to a ghostly white, and his composure faltered. But I understood exactly what troubled him: it was the cost. He envisioned utter ruin ahead; he believed his financial end was fast approaching. Still, as we set out to summon the others, I remarked—
source_text: Marco's joy was exuberant--but only for a moment; then he grew thoughtful, then sad; and when he heard me tell Dowley I should have Dickon, the boss mason, and Smug, the boss wheelwright, out there, too, the coal-dust on his face turned to chalk, and he lost his grip. But I knew what was the matter with him; it was the expense. He saw ruin before him; he judged that his financial days were numbered. However, on our way to invite the others, I said:

text: “Now then, I’ll instruct you in what to say.” I halted, and for a full minute loomed over that trembling boy in a dreadful silence; then, in a tone that was low, deliberate, and freighted with judgment, I began, climbing by carefully heightened steps to my towering finale, which I delivered with as much majestic grandeur as I have ever summoned in my life: “Return and tell your king that at that appointed moment I will smother the entire world in the dead blackness of midnight; I will extinguish the sun, and it shall never shine again; the fruits of the earth will rot for want of light and warmth, and all the peoples of the earth shall starve and perish, down to the very last soul!”
source_text: “Now then, I will tell you what to say.” I paused, and stood over that cowering lad a whole minute in awful silence; then, in a voice deep, measured, charged with doom, I began, and rose by dramatically graded stages to my colossal climax, which I delivered in as sublime and noble a way as ever I did such a thing in my life: “Go back and tell the king that at that hour I will smother the whole world in the dead blackness of midnight; I will blot out the sun, and he shall never shine again; the fruits of the earth shall rot for lack of light and warmth, and the peoples of the earth shall famish and die, to the last man!”

text: When I beheld the Board, I was torn between tears and laughter. At its head sat the official whom later ages would call Norroy King-at-Arms. The other two members were bureau chiefs from his own department, and naturally all three were priests—any office that required literacy belonged to the clergy.
source_text: When I saw the Board, I did not know whether to cry or to laugh. The head of it was the officer known to later centuries as Norroy King-at-Arms! The two other members were chiefs of bureaus in his department; and all three were priests, of course; all officials who had to know how to read and write were priests.

text: LET ALL MEN TAKE HEED. Seeing that the sovereign is departed without issue, it falls to me to wield the governing charge laid upon me, till such time as a new polity shall be framed and given breath. The crown is extinguished, it abides no more. Hence, every strand of civil rule has flowed back to its first wellspring, the common body of the realm. With the kingship perished likewise its sundry dependencies; therefore there abides no peerage, no order set apart, no church by law established; all persons are rendered wholly equal, they stand upon a single plane, and conscience is unbound. _A Commonwealth is herewith declared_, being the native condition of a people when other dominion has fallen away. It behooves the folk of Britain to assemble without delay, and by their suffrage choose deputies and commit into their trust the governance.
source_text: “BE IT KNOWN UNTO ALL. Whereas the king having died and left no heir, it becomes my duty to continue the executive authority vested in me, until a government shall have been created and set in motion. The monarchy has lapsed, it no longer exists. By consequence, all political power has reverted to its original source, the people of the nation. With the monarchy, its several adjuncts died also; wherefore there is no longer a nobility, no longer a privileged class, no longer an Established Church; all men are become exactly equal; they are upon one common level, and religion is free. _A Republic is hereby proclaimed_, as being the natural estate of a nation when other authority has ceased. It is the duty of the British people to meet together immediately, and by their votes elect representatives and deliver into their hands the government.”

text: Damn him, he swore it was the _twenty-second_! A chill crept over me at the sound of his words. I pleaded with him to be certain, to reconsider, but he stood firm—he insisted it was the 22nd. That scatterbrained lad had muddled it all once more! The hour was correct for the darkening of the sun; I had confirmed it myself, at the outset, from the sundial standing close at hand. Indeed, I stood within King Arthur’s court, and I resolved to wring whatever advantage I could from the situation.
source_text: Hang him, he said it was the _twenty-first_! It made me turn cold to hear him. I begged him not to make any mistake about it; but he was sure; he knew it was the 21st. So, that feather-headed boy had botched things again! The time of the day was right for the eclipse; I had seen that for myself, in the beginning, by the dial that was near by. Yes, I was in King Arthur's court, and I might as well make the most out of it I could.

text: “Indeed, I see nothing odd in it. All posts of honor and profit belong by natural right to those of noble lineage, so these army dignities are their inheritance and would remain so with or without this regulation. The rule merely draws a boundary. Its object is to bar blood too newly risen, which would bring those offices into scorn, and men of high descent would turn away in disdain and refuse them. I would be at fault if I allowed such a calamity. _You_ may allow it if you choose, since you hold the authority by delegation, but for the king to do so would be a most incomprehensible madness.”
source_text: “Why, truly I see naught about it that is strange. All places of honor and of profit do belong, by natural right, to them that be of noble blood, and so these dignities in the army are their property and would be so without this or any rule. The rule is but to mark a limit. Its purpose is to keep out too recent blood, which would bring into contempt these offices, and men of lofty lineage would turn their backs and scorn to take them. I were to blame an I permitted this calamity. _You_ can permit it an you are minded so to do, for you have the delegated authority, but that the king should do it were a most strange madness and not comprehensible to any.”

text: I drew up beside a heavyset man with a plump, cheerful face, hoping to charm him and glean a few more scraps of information. But I’d barely introduced myself when he started steering the conversation, with clumsy eagerness and in that age-old fashion, toward the same tired story—the one Sir Dinadan had told me back when I got crosswise with Sir Sagramor and he challenged me over it. I begged off and fell back to the rear of the column, heavy-hearted, ready to leave behind this weary existence, this valley of sorrows, this fleeting span of fractured rest, of squalls and darkness, of grinding effort and unvarying failure. Yet I recoiled from the prospect, recalling how long eternity stretches, and how many have journeyed there who already know that tale.
source_text: I closed up alongside of a burly fellow with a fat good-humored face, purposing to make myself agreeable and pick up some further crumbs of fact; but I had hardly more than scraped acquaintance with him when he began eagerly and awkwardly to lead up, in the immemorial way, to that same old anecdote--the one Sir Dinadan told me, what time I got into trouble with Sir Sagramor and was challenged of him on account of it. I excused myself and dropped to the rear of the procession, sad at heart, willing to go hence from this troubled life, this vale of tears, this brief day of broken rest, of cloud and storm, of weary struggle and monotonous defeat; and yet shrinking from the change, as remembering how long eternity is, and how many have wended thither who know that anecdote.

text: “It’s exactly as I’ve said. I didn’t uncover these matters straightaway, but in the end I did piece them together. Did you dispatch word to me, through the ship’s captain, that once he came back to you with provisions, you intended to depart from Cadiz—”
source_text: “It is just as I tell you. I did not find out these things at once, but I found them out finally. Did you send me verbal information, by the commander of the ship, to the effect that upon his return to you, with supplies, you were going to leave Cadiz--”

text: The talk at the Round Table consisted mostly of monologues—long narrative accounts of the adventures in which these captives had been taken, their companions and champions slain, and their horses and armor stripped away. As far as I could discern, these deadly encounters were not raids meant to avenge some wrong, nor to resolve ancient grudges or sudden quarrels; no, they were, as a rule, simply duels between strangers—duels between men who had never so much as been introduced, and between whom no conceivable offense existed. Many a time I had watched two boys, unknown to each other, meet by chance and declare in the same breath, “I can lick you,” and set to fighting on the instant; but until now I had always supposed such behavior belonged to children alone, and was a hallmark of childhood. Yet here were these great lumbering fellows clinging to it and glorying in it straight into full manhood and beyond. Still, there was something deeply engaging about these large, simple-hearted creatures, something attractive and lovable. There did not seem to be, so to speak, enough brains in the entire nursery to bait a fishhook; but after a while you hardly minded, for you soon realized that intellect was not required in a society like that—indeed, it would have disfigured it, hampered it, spoiled its perfect balance, perhaps even made its existence impossible.
source_text: Mainly the Round Table talk was monologues--narrative accounts of the adventures in which these prisoners were captured and their friends and backers killed and stripped of their steeds and armor. As a general thing--as far as I could make out--these murderous adventures were not forays undertaken to avenge injuries, nor to settle old disputes or sudden fallings out; no, as a rule they were simply duels between strangers--duels between people who had never even been introduced to each other, and between whom existed no cause of offense whatever. Many a time I had seen a couple of boys, strangers, meet by chance, and say simultaneously, “I can lick you,” and go at it on the spot; but I had always imagined until now that that sort of thing belonged to children only, and was a sign and mark of childhood; but here were these big boobies sticking to it and taking pride in it clear up into full age and beyond. Yet there was something very engaging about these great simple-hearted creatures, something attractive and lovable. There did not seem to be brains enough in the entire nursery, so to speak, to bait a fish-hook with; but you didn't seem to mind that, after a little, because you soon saw that brains were not needed in a society like that, and indeed would have marred it, hindered it, spoiled its symmetry--perhaps rendered its existence impossible.

text: “Oh, it’s fine, it’s fine, just give us a break; forget the directions, to hell with the directions—I apologize, I sincerely apologize, I’m not myself today; don’t mind me when I ramble, it’s an old habit, a nasty old habit, tough to shake when your digestion’s all wrecked from eating food that was grown ages and ages before you were born; good heavens, a man can’t keep his system running right on thirteen-hundred-year-old spring chickens. But come on—let that go; tell me—do you happen to have a map of that area with you? Now a decent map—”
source_text: “Oh, that's all right, that's all right, give us a rest; never mind about the direction, _hang_ the direction--I beg pardon, I beg a thousand pardons, I am not well to-day; pay no attention when I soliloquize, it is an old habit, an old, bad habit, and hard to get rid of when one's digestion is all disordered with eating food that was raised forever and ever before he was born; good land! a man can't keep his functions regular on spring chickens thirteen hundred years old. But come--never mind about that; let's--have you got such a thing as a map of that region about you? Now a good map--”

text: How sweet that voice rang! The man who spoke was every inch the patrician: his clothes, striking and rich; his bearing, one of authority; his face, stern and weathered by excess. The crowd shrank away meekly, like a pack of beaten hounds. He studied us with a cold, exacting eye, then snapped at the rustics:
source_text: How good it sounded! The owner of the voice bore all the marks of a gentleman: picturesque and costly raiment, the aspect of command, a hard countenance, with complexion and features marred by dissipation. The mob fell humbly back, like so many spaniels. The gentleman inspected us critically, then said sharply to the peasants:

text: Had I known in advance what that venture would turn into, I’d have refused outright—if someone wants to earn a living parading a monarch as a peasant, they’re welcome to the scheme; I can put together a traveling show and make it pay longer. Even so, for those first three days I kept him out of any cottage or roofed shelter. During his early training, if he stood any chance of passing, it would be on the open road and in modest roadside taverns, so we stuck strictly to those. He gave it his all, certainly—but what did that matter? As far as I could tell, he never got one whit better.
source_text: If I could have foreseen what the thing was going to be like, I should have said, No, if anybody wants to make his living exhibiting a king as a peasant, let him take the layout; I can do better with a menagerie, and last longer. And yet, during the first three days I never allowed him to enter a hut or other dwelling. If he could pass muster anywhere during his early novitiate it would be in small inns and on the road; so to these places we confined ourselves. Yes, he certainly did the best he could, but what of that? He didn't improve a bit that I could see.

text: The crowd scattered in a heartbeat, the riders charging after them, lashing out with their whips and trampling without mercy those foolish enough to remain on the path instead of fleeing into the undergrowth. The cries and pleas gradually faded into the distance, and before long the horsemen began to drift back. Meanwhile, the gentleman had been pressing us with more detailed questions, but he extracted no further information from us. We expressed abundant gratitude for the aid he was providing, yet disclosed nothing beyond the fact that we were solitary strangers from a distant land. Once the entire escort had returned, the gentleman addressed one of his attendants:
source_text: The mob vanished in an instant; and after them plunged the horsemen, laying about them with their whips and pitilessly riding down such as were witless enough to keep the road instead of taking to the bush. The shrieks and supplications presently died away in the distance, and soon the horsemen began to straggle back. Meantime the gentleman had been questioning us more closely, but had dug no particulars out of us. We were lavish of recognition of the service he was doing us, but we revealed nothing more than that we were friendless strangers from a far country. When the escort were all returned, the gentleman said to one of his servants:

text: “And so, Sir Knight,” said Sir Marhaus, “I have felt plainly that you are a surpassingly good knight, and a man of such marvellous might as I have rarely encountered, for as long as it endures. Yet our quarrel is no great matter, and it would be a pity to do you harm, for I perceive you are grown passing feeble.” “Ah,” said Sir Gawaine, “gentle knight, you speak the very words I would have spoken.” With that they removed their helms and each kissed the other, and there they swore together to love one another as brethren.
source_text: --“and so, Sir Knight, said Sir Marhaus, I have well felt that ye are a passing good knight, and a marvelous man of might as ever I felt any, while it lasteth, and our quarrels are not great, and therefore it were a pity to do you hurt, for I feel you are passing feeble. Ah, said Sir Gawaine, gentle knight, ye say the word that I should say. And therewith they took off their helms and either kissed other, and there they swore together either to love other as brethren--”

text: With those words and more, he casually took up his spear and departed. Mid-afternoon, we ourselves encountered one of those very patriarchs on the outskirts of a wretched village. He was basking in the affection of kin and companions he had not seen for fifty years; around him, stroking him, were also descendants of his own blood whom he had never laid eyes on until now. Yet to him these were all strangers—his memory had vanished, his mind lay stagnant. It seemed unbelievable that a man could endure half a century locked in a dark burrow like a rat, but here stood his aged wife and a few old comrades to bear witness. They could recall him in the bloom and vigor of early manhood, when he had kissed his child, placed it in its mother’s arms, and vanished into that long oblivion. The folk at the castle could not say, within half a generation, how long the man had been immured for his unrecorded, forgotten crime; but this old wife knew. And so did her elderly daughter, who stood among her married sons and daughters, striving to grasp a father who had been all her life a name, a notion, a shapeless phantom, a story—and was now suddenly made solid flesh and blood and set before her eyes.
source_text: And with these words and others, he lightly took his spear and gat him thence. In the middle of the afternoon we came upon one of those very patriarchs ourselves, in the edge of a poor village. He was basking in the love of relatives and friends whom he had not seen for fifty years; and about him and caressing him were also descendants of his own body whom he had never seen at all till now; but to him these were all strangers, his memory was gone, his mind was stagnant. It seemed incredible that a man could outlast half a century shut up in a dark hole like a rat, but here were his old wife and some old comrades to testify to it. They could remember him as he was in the freshness and strength of his young manhood, when he kissed his child and delivered it to its mother's hands and went away into that long oblivion. The people at the castle could not tell within half a generation the length of time the man had been shut up there for his unrecorded and forgotten offense; but this old wife knew; and so did her old child, who stood there among her married sons and daughters trying to realize a father who had been to her a name, a thought, a formless image, a tradition, all her life, and now was suddenly concreted into actual flesh and blood and set before her face.

text: I left Sandy on her knees, her face ashen but her spirit unbroken and full of resolve, and made my way down to the pigsty, where I struck a bargain with the swineherds. I earned their thanks by purchasing every hog outright for a flat sum of sixteen pennies—a price a little above the going rate. My timing was fortunate; the Church, the lord of the manor, and the other tax collectors would have arrived the next day and seized nearly all the livestock, leaving the swineherds with few pigs and Sandy bereft of princesses. Now, however, the taxes could be settled in coin, with something left over besides. One of the men had ten children, and he told me how the previous year, when a priest came and claimed the plumpest of his ten pigs as a tithe, his wife had flown at him and, offering up one of their children, had cried:
source_text: I left Sandy kneeling there, corpse-faced but plucky and hopeful, and rode down to the pigsty, and struck up a trade with the swine-herds. I won their gratitude by buying out all the hogs at the lump sum of sixteen pennies, which was rather above latest quotations. I was just in time; for the Church, the lord of the manor, and the rest of the tax-gatherers would have been along next day and swept off pretty much all the stock, leaving the swine-herds very short of hogs and Sandy out of princesses. But now the tax people could be paid in cash, and there would be a stake left besides. One of the men had ten children; and he said that last year when a priest came and of his ten pigs took the fattest one for tithes, the wife burst out upon him, and offered him a child and said:

text: When the melody faded, the cleric stationed behind the sovereign board intoned an ornate, protracted blessing in a semblance of Latin. Then the regiment of servers surged from their stations—they bolted, scurried, swooped, bore and delivered—and the colossal feasting commenced; not a syllable anywhere, only rapt devotion to the task. The ranks of jaws parted and clamped in immense synchrony, and the noise of it resembled the muted drone of engines buried deep underground.
source_text: After this music, the priest who stood behind the royal table said a noble long grace in ostensible Latin. Then the battalion of waiters broke away from their posts, and darted, rushed, flew, fetched and carried, and the mighty feeding began; no words anywhere, but absorbing attention to business. The rows of chops opened and shut in vast unison, and the sound of it was like to the muffled burr of subterranean machinery.

text: One morning, while I was out on a long walk to build up my strength for the journey ahead, I scaled the ridge that rimmed the valley’s northern edge. There, in the face of a low cliff, I stumbled upon a man-made opening. I knew the spot at once—it was a hermitage that had often been pointed out to me from afar, famed as the lair of a hermit celebrated for his squalor and severe self-denial. I’d heard he had recently accepted a post in the Great Sahara, where lions and sandflies made the solitary life both especially alluring and arduous, and had already departed for Africa to claim it. So I decided to peer inside and see whether the atmosphere of the den lived up to its legend.
source_text: One morning I was out on a long walk to get up muscle for my trip, and had climbed the ridge which bordered the northern extremity of the valley, when I came upon an artificial opening in the face of a low precipice, and recognized it by its location as a hermitage which had often been pointed out to me from a distance as the den of a hermit of high renown for dirt and austerity. I knew he had lately been offered a situation in the Great Sahara, where lions and sandflies made the hermit-life peculiarly attractive and difficult, and had gone to Africa to take possession, so I thought I would look in and see how the atmosphere of this den agreed with its reputation.

text: “I understand, I understand—it’s all one and the same. It’s astonishing. On the one hand, a man exists in a living death for four generations—embalmed in stupidity and indolence—and that entitles him to rule over the living, to hold their happiness and misery in his powerless grasp; on the other, a man has lain in the grave among decay and maggots for four generations, and that entitles him to a post in the heavenly host. Does his majesty endorse this peculiar statute?”
source_text: “I see, I see--it is the same thing. It is wonderful. In the one case a man lies dead-alive four generations--mummified in ignorance and sloth--and that qualifies him to command live people, and take their weal and woe into his impotent hands; and in the other case, a man lies bedded with death and worms four generations, and that qualifies him for office in the celestial camp. Does the king's grace approve of this strange law?”

text: And so it came to pass, after many seasons had turned, that the kindly abbot knelt in quiet submission and had the bath dismantled. Then, in that very instant, His wrath was soothed, and the waters burst forth once more in lavish abundance, and from that day to this they have never slackened their bountiful flow.
source_text: “And so upon a time, after year and day, the good abbot made humble surrender and destroyed the bath. And behold, His anger was in that moment appeased, and the waters gushed richly forth again, and even unto this day they have not ceased to flow in that generous measure.”

text: “It’s wasteful—squanders energy for no reason. You don’t want any ground link except the one through the negative terminal. Every wire’s far end must loop back into the cave and be secured separately, with absolutely no connection to the earth. Now watch how efficient it is. A cavalry charge slams into the barrier; you’re expending no power, you’re spending no money, because there’s only a single ground link until those horses hit the wire; the instant they make contact, they complete a circuit with the negative terminal through the ground and fall dead. Do you see?—you’re using no energy until the moment it’s required; your lightning sits there, ready, like a bullet in a chamber; but it doesn’t cost you a penny until you trigger it. Oh yes, the lone ground connection—”
source_text: “It's too expensive--uses up force for nothing. You don't want any ground-connection except the one through the negative brush. The other end of every wire must be brought back into the cave and fastened independently, and _without_ any ground-connection. Now, then, observe the economy of it. A cavalry charge hurls itself against the fence; you are using no power, you are spending no money, for there is only one ground-connection till those horses come against the wire; the moment they touch it they form a connection with the negative brush _through the ground_, and drop dead. Don't you see?--you are using no energy until it is needed; your lightning is there, and ready, like the load in a gun; but it isn't costing you a cent till you touch it off. Oh, yes, the single ground-connection--”

text: It was an odd circumstance; yet I have not set it down for that reason, but for something that struck me as odder still. Namely, that this grim affair drew from those crushed souls no surge of fury against their tormentors. They had endured inheritance and subjection to brutality and wrong so long that only a mercy could have jolted them. Indeed, here was a strange unveiling of the abyss into which this folk had been plunged by bondage. Their whole spirit was leveled to a flat, unvarying plain of forbearance, surrender, mute unquestioning assent to whatever might come upon them in this world. Their very fancy was extinguished. When that can be said of a man, he has touched the floor, I figure; there is no deeper pit for him.
source_text: It was a curious situation; yet it is not on that account that I have made room for it here, but on account of a thing which seemed to me still more curious. To wit, that this dreadful matter brought from these downtrodden people no outburst of rage against these oppressors. They had been heritors and subjects of cruelty and outrage so long that nothing could have startled them but a kindness. Yes, here was a curious revelation, indeed, of the depth to which this people had been sunk in slavery. Their entire being was reduced to a monotonous dead level of patience, resignation, dumb uncomplaining acceptance of whatever might befall them in this life. Their very imagination was dead. When you can say that of a man, he has struck bottom, I reckon; there is no lower deep for him.

text: We came to a village inn just as dusk was falling, and when I rose the next morning and gazed out, I saw a knight riding through the golden blaze of dawn. I knew him at once for one of my own—Sir Ozana le Cure Hardy. His trade was gentlemen’s outfitting, and his particular missionary charge was the promotion of plug hats. He was sheathed from head to foot in steel, clad in the finest armor of the age—up to the point where his helmet should have sat; but he wore no helmet. Instead, a glossy stovepipe hat crowned his head, and a more ludicrous figure you could hardly hope to behold. This was another of my quiet stratagems to kill off knighthood by rendering it grotesque and absurd. Sir Ozana’s saddle was festooned with leather hat-boxes, and whenever he vanquished a knight-errant he made him swear fealty to me, fitted him with a plug hat, and forced him to wear it. I dressed quickly and hurried down to greet Sir Ozana and hear his tidings.
source_text: We put up at the inn in a village just at nightfall, and when I rose next morning and looked abroad, I was ware where a knight came riding in the golden glory of the new day, and recognized him for knight of mine--Sir Ozana le Cure Hardy. He was in the gentlemen's furnishing line, and his missionarying specialty was plug hats. He was clothed all in steel, in the beautifulest armor of the time--up to where his helmet ought to have been; but he hadn't any helmet, he wore a shiny stove-pipe hat, and was ridiculous a spectacle as one might want to see. It was another of my surreptitious schemes for extinguishing knighthood by making it grotesque and absurd. Sir Ozana's saddle was hung about with leather hat boxes, and every time he overcame a wandering knight he swore him into my service and fitted him with a plug and made him wear it. I dressed and ran down to welcome Sir Ozana and get his news.

text: Oh, for heaven's sake, can't you grasp something so straightforward? Here, let me paint you a picture. We spend four cents on a woman's plain work dress, while you shell out eight dollars and forty cents—more than double, by a hair. What do you pay a farm woman for her labor?
source_text: “Oh, great Scott, isn't it possible to get such a simple thing through your head? Now look here--let me illustrate. We pay four cents for a woman's stuff gown, you pay 8.4.0, which is four mills more than _double_. What do you allow a laboring woman who works on a farm?”

text: Once we had settled ourselves comfortably within the tree, veiled by its dense leaves, the king felt content, but I remained uncertain. I thought we might inch along a limb and cross into the neighboring tree, and I deemed the attempt worthwhile. We ventured it and succeeded, though at the joining point the king lost his footing and nearly missed his grip. We found a secure perch and ample concealment among the branches, and then there was nothing left for us but to attend to the sounds of the chase.
source_text: When we were snugly lodged in the tree and curtained with foliage, the king was satisfied, but I was doubtful. I believed we could crawl along a branch and get into the next tree, and I judged it worth while to try. We tried it, and made a success of it, though the king slipped, at the junction, and came near failing to connect. We got comfortable lodgment and satisfactory concealment among the foliage, and then we had nothing to do but listen to the hunt.

text: A bondsman was hooded; the executioner loosed his noose. Beneath us stretched the level highway, we massed along its nearer edge, the tiered crowd keening on the farther verge—a fine open road, held empty by the guards—what a sight it would be to watch my five hundred riders come thundering along it! Yet no, it lay beyond all likelihood. I traced its dwindling line far into the haze—not a single horseman upon it, nor any token of one.
source_text: Then a slave was blindfolded; the hangman unslung his rope. There lay the smooth road below us, we upon one side of it, the banked multitude wailing its other side--a good clear road, and kept free by the police--how good it would be to see my five hundred horsemen come tearing down it! But no, it was out of the possibilities. I followed its receding thread out into the distance--not a horseman on it, or sign of one.

text: Truly, in the all-knowing and unfathomable design of God, who moves in shadowed ways to bring forth His wonders, I have never encountered a question like this one for tangling the mind and clogging the channels of reason. So I implore you, let the dog and the onions and these people with their strange, godless names find their own paths out of their pitiful and astonishing troubles without my aid, for their burden is heavy enough already—and if I tried to help, I would only wound their cause further, and perhaps not even survive to witness the ruin left behind.
source_text: “Verily, in the all-wise and unknowable providence of God, who moveth in mysterious ways his wonders to perform, have I never heard the fellow to this question for confusion of the mind and congestion of the ducts of thought. Wherefore I beseech you let the dog and the onions and these people of the strange and godless names work out their several salvations from their piteous and wonderful difficulties without help of mine, for indeed their trouble is sufficient as it is, whereas an I tried to help I should but damage their cause the more and yet mayhap not live myself to see the desolation wrought.”

text: For a fortnight and a half we kept vigil beside the little bed, so wholly absorbed in our anxious watch that the world beyond that chamber ceased to exist. Then came our recompense: the axis of all creation rounded the bend and started to recover. Gratitude? The word falls short. There is no word that can compass it. You understand this yourself, if ever you have stood guard over your own child through the Vale of Shadows and beheld it return to life and banish darkness from the globe with a single, world-kindling smile you could eclipse with your palm.
source_text: Well, during two weeks and a half we watched by the crib, and in our deep solicitude we were unconscious of any world outside of that sick-room. Then our reward came: the center of the universe turned the corner and began to mend. Grateful? It isn't the term. There _isn't_ any term for it. You know that yourself, if you've watched your child through the Valley of the Shadow and seen it come back to life and sweep night out of the earth with one all-illuminating smile that you could cover with your hand.

text: Dawn broke soon, pouring its unhindered brilliance across the earth, and we beheld an immense multitude advancing upon us with the measured surge and even crest of an ocean swell. Closer and closer it drew, and ever more awesomely magnificent its appearance grew; truly, the whole of England seemed assembled before us. Presently we discerned the countless pennons tossing, and then the sunlight smote that metal tide and kindled it into a glittering blaze. Indeed, it was a splendid spectacle; I had never witnessed its equal.
source_text: The sun rose presently and sent its unobstructed splendors over the land, and we saw a prodigious host moving slowly toward us, with the steady drift and aligned front of a wave of the sea. Nearer and nearer it came, and more and more sublimely imposing became its aspect; yes, all England was there, apparently. Soon we could see the innumerable banners fluttering, and then the sun struck the sea of armor and set it all aflash. Yes, it was a fine sight; I hadn't ever seen anything to beat it.

text: No one disputes that. If the law holds any meaning, what belongs to the lord is his, and what is mine is his as well. Our farm was held by lease, so it was likewise his to dispose of as he pleased. A short while back, three of those trees were discovered chopped down. Our three grown sons ran in terror to report the offense. Well, now they lie in his lordship's dungeon, where he declares they shall stay and rot until they confess. They have nothing to confess, being blameless, so there they will remain until they die. You know that perfectly well, I suspect. Consider what this did to us: a man, a woman, and two children left to harvest a crop planted by far stronger hands, and to guard it day and night from pigeons and prowling beasts that are sacred and must not be harmed by anyone of our kind. When my lord's crop was nearly ready for reaping, so was ours; when his bell summoned us to his fields to harvest his crop for no wages, he would not permit me and my two girls to stand in for our three imprisoned sons, but only for two of them; thus, for the missing one we were fined each day. All this while our own crop was wasting away from neglect; and so both the priest and his lordship fined us because their shares were suffering damage. In the end the fines devoured our harvest—and they seized it all; they took everything and forced us to reap it for them, without pay or food, while we starved. Then the worst arrived when I, driven mad by hunger and the loss of my boys, and the grief of seeing my husband and my little daughters in rags, misery, and hopelessness, let out a deep blasphemy—oh, a thousand of them!—against the Church and the Church's practices. It was ten days ago. I had fallen ill with this sickness, and it was to the priest I spoke the words, for he had come to scold me for lacking proper humility under God's chastening hand. He reported my offense to his superiors; I remained defiant; whereupon, upon my head and upon all heads dear to me, fell the curse of Rome.
source_text: “None denieth that, indeed; an the law mean anything, what is the lord's is his, and what is mine is his also. Our farm was ours by lease, therefore 'twas likewise his, to do with it as he would. Some little time ago, three of those trees were found hewn down. Our three grown sons ran frightened to report the crime. Well, in his lordship's dungeon there they lie, who saith there shall they lie and rot till they confess. They have naught to confess, being innocent, wherefore there will they remain until they die. Ye know that right well, I ween. Think how this left us; a man, a woman and two children, to gather a crop that was planted by so much greater force, yes, and protect it night and day from pigeons and prowling animals that be sacred and must not be hurt by any of our sort. When my lord's crop was nearly ready for the harvest, so also was ours; when his bell rang to call us to his fields to harvest his crop for nothing, he would not allow that I and my two girls should count for our three captive sons, but for only two of them; so, for the lacking one were we daily fined. All this time our own crop was perishing through neglect; and so both the priest and his lordship fined us because their shares of it were suffering through damage. In the end the fines ate up our crop--and they took it all; they took it all and made us harvest it for them, without pay or food, and we starving. Then the worst came when I, being out of my mind with hunger and loss of my boys, and grief to see my husband and my little maids in rags and misery and despair, uttered a deep blasphemy--oh! a thousand of them! --against the Church and the Church's ways. It was ten days ago. I had fallen sick with this disease, and it was to the priest I said the words, for he was come to chide me for lack of due humility under the chastening hand of God. He carried my trespass to his betters; I was stubborn; wherefore, presently upon my head and upon all heads that were dear to me, fell the curse of Rome.

text: A suspicion took hold of me—that the well’s lining had failed somewhere deep below, that stones near its base had crumbled away and laid bare cracks through which the water drained. I gauged the chain: ninety-eight feet. Summoning two brothers, I bolted the door, seized a taper, and had them lower me down in the pail. Once the full length of chain was let out, the flame bore out my fear: a broad stretch of the wall had collapsed, revealing a sizable rift.
source_text: I had an idea that the well had sprung a leak; that some of the wall stones near the bottom had fallen and exposed fissures that allowed the water to escape. I measured the chain--98 feet. Then I called in a couple of monks, locked the door, took a candle, and made them lower me in the bucket. When the chain was all paid out, the candle confirmed my suspicion; a considerable section of the wall was gone, exposing a good big fissure.

text: I didn’t move a muscle until that roaring phantom closed to within fifteen steps of me; then I whipped a cavalry revolver from my holster, there came a burst of flame and a deafening report, and the gun was back in its sheath before anyone could grasp what had occurred.
source_text: I never budged so much as an inch till that thundering apparition had got within fifteen paces of me; then I snatched a dragoon revolver out of my holster, there was a flash and a roar, and the revolver was back in the holster before anybody could tell what had happened.

text: Exercising my sovereign prerogative, I consigned Merlin to the very dungeon that had once held me. Then I proclaimed far and wide, by clarion and crier, that for a fortnight I should be absorbed in matters of governance; thereafter, I might snatch a brief hour to shatter Merlin’s stone spire with celestial flame. Meanwhile, let any who gave ear to slanders against me take heed. Moreover, this single marvel would I work for now, and none other; should it prove insufficient and any dare mutter, those malcontents I would transform to draught horses and set them to labor. A hush descended.
source_text: By my authority as executive I threw Merlin into prison--the same cell I had occupied myself. Then I gave public notice by herald and trumpet that I should be busy with affairs of state for a fortnight, but about the end of that time I would take a moment's leisure and blow up Merlin's stone tower by fires from heaven; in the meantime, whoso listened to evil reports about me, let him beware. Furthermore, I would perform but this one miracle at this time, and no more; if it failed to satisfy and any murmured, I would turn the murmurers into horses, and make them useful. Quiet ensued.

text: I returned at once, speaking to no one. When I told Sandy the dreadful tidings, we found ourselves utterly unable to account for it. Had some invasion struck? An earthquake? A plague? Had the entire kingdom been blotted from the earth? Speculation was useless. I had to leave immediately. I requisitioned the king’s navy—a vessel no larger than a steam launch—and made ready without delay.
source_text: I went swiftly back, saying not a word to anybody. I told Sandy this ghastly news. We could imagine no explanation that would begin to explain. Had there been an invasion? an earthquake? a pestilence? Had the nation been swept out of existence? But guessing was profitless. I must go--at once. I borrowed the king's navy--a “ship” no bigger than a steam launch--and was soon ready.

text: Thus she began once more to murmur and croon above the maiden, tenderly brushing her brow and tresses, pressing kisses upon her, and whispering fond epithets; yet hardly a flicker of awareness remained within those dulling eyes. I beheld moisture gather in the monarch’s gaze and course silently along his cheeks. The lady observed this as well, and spoke:
source_text: And so she fell to murmuring and cooing over the girl again, and softly stroking her face and hair, and kissing her and calling her by endearing names; but there was scarcely sign of response now in the glazing eyes. I saw tears well from the king's eyes, and trickle down his face. The woman noticed them, too, and said:

text: The harsh laws and customs mentioned in this story are drawn from history, and the events that bring them to life are likewise rooted in the past. There is no claim that these practices held sway in sixth-century England; rather, the suggestion is simply that since they endured in English society and other cultures in much later eras, it is no slander against that earlier century to imagine they were in force then as well. One may reasonably conclude that wherever such a law or custom was absent in that distant age, its absence was amply supplied by something even more severe.
source_text: The ungentle laws and customs touched upon in this tale are historical, and the episodes which are used to illustrate them are also historical. It is not pretended that these laws and customs existed in England in the sixth century; no, it is only pretended that inasmuch as they existed in the English and other civilizations of far later times, it is safe to consider that it is no libel upon the sixth century to suppose them to have been in practice in that day also. One is quite justified in inferring that whatever one of these laws or customs was lacking in that remote time, its place was competently filled by a worse one.

text: Whenever my missionaries bested a knight-errant on the highway, they would bathe him, and once he recovered, they made him swear to acquire a bulletin-board and spend the rest of his life spreading soap and civilization. As a result, the number of laborers in the field gradually grew, and the reform steadily expanded. My soap factory soon felt the pressure. Initially, I employed only two workers; but before I departed home, I was already running fifteen hands, operating day and night. The atmospheric effects became so pronounced that the king staggered about, faint and gasping, declaring he doubted he could endure it much longer. Sir Launcelot grew so troubled that he did little besides pace the rooftop and curse, even though I warned him it was worse up there than anywhere else—but he insisted he needed plenty of air. He constantly grumbled that a palace was no fit site for a soap factory, and said if any man tried to start one in his own house, he’d be damned if he wouldn’t throttle him. There were ladies present as well, but these folk hardly cared a whit for that; they would swear in front of children if the wind blew their way while the factory was in operation.
source_text: Whenever my missionaries overcame a knight errant on the road they washed him, and when he got well they swore him to go and get a bulletin-board and disseminate soap and civilization the rest of his days. As a consequence the workers in the field were increasing by degrees, and the reform was steadily spreading. My soap factory felt the strain early.
