At first I had only two hands; but before I had left home I was already employing fifteen, and running night and day; and the atmospheric result was getting so pronounced that the king went sort of fainting and gasping around and said he did not believe he could stand it much longer, and Sir Launcelot got so that he did hardly anything but walk up and down the roof and swear, although I told him it was worse up there than anywhere else, but he said he wanted plenty of air; and he was always complaining that a palace was no place for a soap factory anyway, and said if a man was to start one in his house he would be damned if he wouldn't strangle him. There were ladies present, too, but much these people ever cared for that; they would swear before children, if the wind was their way when the factory was going.

text: “Indeed. The hosts were encamped not far from Salisbury. Gawaine—his skull now rests at Dover keep, for he perished in that clash—Gawaine visited Arthur in a vision, or rather his shade did, and counselled him to hold back from battle for a moon, whatever the postponement might forfeit. Yet strife was sparked by a mischance. Arthur had commanded that if a blade were lifted during the parley over the proposed accord with Mordred, the horns should blast and the charge begin! for he placed no trust in Mordred. Mordred had issued a matching decree to his own forces. Presently, an asp stung a soldier’s ankle; the man, forgetting the injunction entirely, struck a cut at the serpent with his steel. Ere thirty heartbeats had passed, those two immense armies collided with a roar! They slaughtered through the long light. Then the sovereign—but we have embarked on a new matter since your departure—our journal has.”
source_text: “Yes. The two armies lay near Salisbury. Gawaine--Gawaine's head is at Dover Castle, he fell in the fight there--Gawaine appeared to Arthur in a dream, at least his ghost did, and warned him to refrain from conflict for a month, let the delay cost what it might. But battle was precipitated by an accident. Arthur had given order that if a sword was raised during the consultation over the proposed treaty with Mordred, sound the trumpet and fall on! for he had no confidence in Mordred. Mordred had given a similar order to _his_ people. Well, by and by an adder bit a knight's heel; the knight forgot all about the order, and made a slash at the adder with his sword. Inside of half a minute those two prodigious hosts came together with a crash! They butchered away all day. Then the king--however, we have started something fresh since you left--our paper has.”

text: With this pilfered item, I’ll spring these latches and shed these shackles before the night is through. The moment he arrives around half-past nine to check on us for the evening, we’ll grab him, silence him, beat him down, and at first light we’ll stride out of this settlement, masters of this slave caravan.
source_text: “With this thing which I have stolen, I will unlock these locks and cast off these chains to-night. When he comes about nine-thirty to inspect us for the night, we will seize him, gag him, batter him, and early in the morning we will march out of this town, proprietors of this caravan of slaves.”

text: You told us you'd keep to this road, and that's the way we're headed too. So my master, Earl Grip, has ordered that you keep the horses and ride on, and that a few of us go with you for twenty miles to a decent town called Cambenet, once you're clear of danger.
source_text: “Ye have said ye should continue upon this road, which is our direction likewise; wherefore my lord, the earl Grip, hath given commandment that ye retain the horses and ride, and that certain of us ride with ye a twenty mile to a fair town that hight Cambenet, whenso ye shall be out of peril.”

text: After a while, we made our way to behold one of the truly towering figures. He was a colossal luminary, his renown spreading through every corner of the Christian world; aristocrats and illustrious souls traveled from the farthest reaches of the earth to honor him. His position lay at the heart of the valley’s broadest expanse, and even that vast area was barely enough to contain the multitudes who gathered.
source_text: By and by we went to see one of the supremely great ones. He was a mighty celebrity; his fame had penetrated all Christendom; the noble and the renowned journeyed from the remotest lands on the globe to pay him reverence. His stand was in the center of the widest part of the valley; and it took all that space to hold his crowds.

text: “Let me reflect... I can’t quite lay hold of the solution. Truthfully, nothing will mend this but rehearsal. Here is a fitting spot: tangled roots and rocky soil to shatter your measured stride, a stretch of land safe from disturbance—just one field and a single cottage visible, and those so distant no eye could reach us. We’d best step a little aside from the track and devote the entire day to schooling you, sire.”
source_text: “Let me think... I can't seem to quite get at it. In fact, there isn't anything that can right the matter but practice. This is a good place for it: roots and stony ground to break up your stately gait, a region not liable to interruption, only one field and one hut in sight, and they so far away that nobody could see us from there. It will be well to move a little off the road and put in the whole day drilling you, sire.”

text: He was true to his promise. He gave all he had, though I have witnessed finer. Should you ever have observed a restless, thoughtless, daring little boy marching steadfastly from one folly straight into the next from dawn till dusk, with a fretful mother forever trailing behind him, snatching him back by a breath from tumbling into a well or snapping his spine with every fresh venture, then you have seen the sovereign and myself.
source_text: He kept his word. He did the best he could, but I've seen better. If you have ever seen an active, heedless, enterprising child going diligently out of one mischief and into another all day long, and an anxious mother at its heels all the while, and just saving it by a hair from drowning itself or breaking its neck with each new experiment, you've seen the king and me.

text: The priest managed admirably, all things considered. He packed in every particular, which is the secret of a good local notice: you see, in his younger days he had kept the books for the burial side of his church, and in that line the profit lies in the particulars—the more particulars, the more profit: pallbearers, hired mourners, tapers, orisons—nothing is overlooked; and if the grieving family scrimps on prayers, you simply mark up the candles with a split pencil, and the account comes out handsomely. He also had a fine instinct for slipping in a flattering word now and then about a lord who was likely to bring custom—no, I mean a lord who carried weight; and he possessed a tidy talent for embellishment, having once kept the gate for a devout hermit who lived in a pigsty and performed wonders.
source_text: Well, the priest did very well, considering. He got in all the details, and that is a good thing in a local item: you see, he had kept books for the undertaker-department of his church when he was younger, and there, you know, the money's in the details; the more details, the more swag: bearers, mutes, candles, prayers --everything counts; and if the bereaved don't buy prayers enough you mark up your candles with a forked pencil, and your bill shows up all right. And he had a good knack at getting in the complimentary thing here and there about a knight that was likely to advertise--no, I mean a knight that had influence; and he also had a neat gift of exaggeration, for in his time he had kept door for a pious hermit who lived in a sty and worked miracles.

text: “Ah, I missed thy meaning. That shall I relate anon.” Then she murmured to herself, low and dreamily, tasting the syllables as if they were honeyed: “Where bide they--where bide they--where do--where do they bide; aye, just so; where do they bide. Verily the saying hath a lovely and delicate music, and is most sweetly turned. I shall whisper it again and again in my leisure, that perchance I may make it mine own. Where do they bide. Even thus! already it slips lightly from my lips, and since--”
source_text: “Ah, I understood thee not. That will I tell eftsoons.” Then she said musingly, and softly, turning the words daintily over her tongue: “Hang they out--hang they out--where hang--where do they hang out; eh, right so; where do they hang out. Of a truth the phrase hath a fair and winsome grace, and is prettily worded withal. I will repeat it anon and anon in mine idlesse, whereby I may peradventure learn it. Where do they hang out. Even so! already it falleth trippingly from my tongue, and forasmuch as--”

text: The label "intellectual labor" is a misnomer; it is a delight, an indulgence, and carries its own supreme compensation. The most underpaid architect, engineer, general, writer, sculptor, painter, speaker, lawyer, lawmaker, performer, minister, vocalist is essentially in paradise while engaged in his craft; and as for the musician, bow in hand, seated within a vast orchestra while surging currents of sublime sound cascade over him—well, yes, he is working, if you insist on calling it that, but heavens, the term is a mockery all the same. The principle of work appears profoundly unjust—yet there it stands, immutable: the greater the pleasure a worker derives from his toil, the greater his monetary reward will be as well. And this is precisely the same rule governing those blatant frauds, hereditary aristocracy and monarchy.
source_text: Intellectual “work” is misnamed; it is a pleasure, a dissipation, and is its own highest reward. The poorest paid architect, engineer, general, author, sculptor, painter, lecturer, advocate, legislator, actor, preacher, singer is constructively in heaven when he is at work; and as for the musician with the fiddle-bow in his hand who sits in the midst of a great orchestra with the ebbing and flowing tides of divine sound washing over him--why, certainly, he is at work, if you wish to call it that, but lord, it's a sarcasm just the same. The law of work does seem utterly unfair--but there it is, and nothing can change it: the higher the pay in enjoyment the worker gets out of it, the higher shall be his pay in cash, also. And it's also the very law of those transparent swindles, transmissible nobility and kingship.

text: It was even odds that Clarence had secured that privilege for me—and supplied the wording as well. When that fearsome spearhead came within a yard and a half of my chest, I casually swerved my horse aside, and the hulking knight charged past, hitting nothing. I drew a wave of applause that time. We wheeled, steadied ourselves, and thundered toward each other again. Another miss for the knight, a burst of cheers for me. The same sequence played out once more, stirring such a gale of acclaim that Sir Sagramor lost his composure and abruptly shifted tactics, devoting himself to running me down. Truth be told, he stood no chance at that; it was a game of chase, with every edge in my favor; I veered out of his path effortlessly whenever I pleased, and once I gave him a slap on the back as I slipped past to his rear. Eventually I seized control of the pursuit myself, and from then on, whatever twisting or turning he attempted, he could never get behind me again; at the end of every move he found himself facing me. So he abandoned that approach and withdrew to his end of the field. By now his temper had wholly evaporated, and he forgot himself and hurled an insult at me that settled mine. I freed my lasso from my saddle horn and gripped its coils in my right hand. You should have seen him charge this time!—it was a purposeful ride, no doubt; his stride showed murder in his eye. I sat my horse relaxed, swinging the wide loop of my lasso in broad circles above my head; the instant he started forward, I spurred toward him; when the gap between us shrank to forty feet, I sent the rope's serpentine coils slicing through the air, then veered sharply aside, spun around, and brought my well-schooled horse to a stop with all four legs planted for a jolt. In the next heartbeat the rope snapped tight and wrenched Sir Sagramor clean out of his saddle! Good heavens, what a stir that caused!
source_text: It was an even bet that Clarence had procured that favor for me --and furnished the language, too. When that formidable lance-point was within a yard and a half of my breast I twitched my horse aside without an effort, and the big knight swept by, scoring a blank. I got plenty of applause that time. We turned, braced up, and down we came again. Another blank for the knight, a roar of applause for me. This same thing was repeated once more; and it fetched such a whirlwind of applause that Sir Sagramor lost his temper, and at once changed his tactics and set himself the task of chasing me down. Why, he hadn't any show in the world at that; it was a game of tag, with all the advantage on my side; I whirled out of his path with ease whenever I chose, and once I slapped him on the back as I went to the rear. Finally I took the chase into my own hands; and after that, turn, or twist, or do what he would, he was never able to get behind me again; he found himself always in front at the end of his maneuver. So he gave up that business and retired to his end of the lists. His temper was clear gone now, and he forgot himself and flung an insult at me which disposed of mine. I slipped my lasso from the horn of my saddle, and grasped the coil in my right hand. This time you should have seen him come!--it was a business trip, sure; by his gait there was blood in his eye. I was sitting my horse at ease, and swinging the great loop of my lasso in wide circles about my head; the moment he was under way, I started for him; when the space between us had narrowed to forty feet, I sent the snaky spirals of the rope a-cleaving through the air, then darted aside and faced about and brought my trained animal to a halt with all his feet braced under him for a surge. The next moment the rope sprang taut and yanked Sir Sagramor out of the saddle! Great Scott, but there was a sensation!

text: The query was baffling. She had no idea why she hadn't—the thought had simply never entered her mind. And so here she stood, unwittingly foretelling the actual fate of future inmates of the Castle d'If. It was clear to me now that, given her upbringing, those inherited captives were just possessions—nothing beyond that. After all, when we inherit something, it doesn't occur to us to discard it, even if we attach no value to it.
source_text: The question was a puzzler. She didn't know _why_ she hadn't, the thing had never come up in her mind. So here she was, forecasting the veritable history of future prisoners of the Castle d'If, without knowing it. It seemed plain to me now, that with her training, those inherited prisoners were merely property--nothing more, nothing less. Well, when we inherit property, it does not occur to us to throw it away, even when we do not value it.

text: He never made the depth of his old feeling for Mary plain to her. Even as he courted Edith, it remained obscure to himself. He was shaping a fresh emotional story, and both wittingly and unwittingly he pushed aside a swarm of memories that tried to break into the new chapter, memories that would have struck a false note. Without ever consciously planning it, he built between himself and Edith a quiet understanding where talk of Mary shrank to almost nothing, and where Hugh was simply taken in rather than accounted for. He managed to convince himself that she grasped all manner of wordless confidences; he conjured wonders of utterly foreign silent communion.
source_text: Of course he did not make his former passion for Mary at all clear to her. Indeed, while he was winning Edith it was by no means clear to himself. He was making a new emotional drama, and consciously and subconsciously he dismissed a hundred reminiscences that sought to invade the new experience, and which would have been out of key with it. And without any deliberate intention to that effect he created an atmosphere between himself and Edith in which any discussion of Mary was reduced to a minimum, and in which Hugh was accepted rather than explained. He contrived to believe that she understood all sorts of unsayable things; he invented miracles of quite uncongenial mute mutuality....

text: Beyond these customary and comprehensible figures, there sat a wholly enigmatic young woman in blue linen, poised beside Mr. Britling with a quiet smile, while on Mr. Direck’s right a darker-haired girl of a similar cast struck him at once as even lovelier and—though he could not immediately identify her—strangely known. A large, unconnected lady of middle years, dressed in black with a gold chain and a prominent nose, occupied the space between Teddy and the tutor; a tall, keen-faced man of middle age might have been a chance visitor; an impeccably attired Indian gentleman, faultless to the edge of his soft brown collar and cuffs, gave way above to an exuberant surge of sculpted bronze features and a profusion of curling hair; and lastly, bolt upright in a perambulator, a baby of twelve months or fewer gestured with cheerful vigor at the company. It was this infant that most disordered Mr. Direck’s methodical mind. The puzzle of its parentage rendered his exchange with Mrs. Britling nearly as fragmented and wandering as her own. It could hardly be hers. The girl beside him, or the one beside Mr. Britling, or the lady in black might each be married—but where, then, was the husband? Surely no one would trundle a foundling into a luncheon.
source_text: But in addition to these normal and understandable presences, there was an entirely mysterious pretty young woman in blue linen who sat and smiled next to Mr. Britling, and there was a rather kindred-looking girl with darker hair on the right of Mr. Direck who impressed him at the very outset as being still prettier, and--he didn't quite place her at first--somehow familiar to him; there was a large irrelevant middle-aged lady in black with a gold chain and a large nose, between Teddy and the tutor; there was a tall middle-aged man with an intelligent face, who might be a casual guest; there was an Indian young gentleman faultlessly dressed up to his brown soft linen collar and cuffs, and thereafter an uncontrolled outbreak of fine bronze modelling and abundant fuzzy hair; and there was a very erect and attentive baby of a year or less, sitting up in a perambulator and gesticulating cheerfully to everybody. This baby it was that most troubled the orderly mind of Mr. Direck. The research for its paternity made his conversation with Mrs. Britling almost as disconnected and absent-minded as her conversation with him. It almost certainly wasn't Mrs. Britling's. The girl next to him or the girl next to Mr. Britling or the lady in black might any of them be married, but if so where was the spouse? It seemed improbable that they would wheel out a foundling to lunch....

text: He's a captive," she said. "Doesn't that suffice? Why needle me with those words? A broken captive. Isn't that contemptible cunning enough for God to inflict on Teddy—our own Teddy? Right up to the final instant he won't be gone. Not till the war ends. Not till six months beyond the war....
source_text: "He is a prisoner," she said. "Isn't that enough? Why do you jab at me by saying that? A wounded prisoner. Isn't that enough despicable trickery for God even to play on Teddy--our Teddy? To the very last moment he shall not be dead. Until the war is over. Until six months after the war....

text: We will despise you with an enduring contempt; our loathing shall never be abandoned—loathing across the oceans and loathing over the earth, loathing from the mind and loathing from the fist, loathing from the forge and loathing from the throne, loathing of seventy million souls, swallowed hard; we cherish as one, we detest as one, we have a single enemy, and only one—ENGLAND!
source_text: "_You_ will we hate with a lasting hate; We will never forgo our hate-- Hate by water and hate by land, Hate of the head and hate of the hand, Hate of the hammer and hate of the crown, Hate of seventy millions, choking down; We love as one, we hate as one, We have _one_ foe, and one alone-- ENGLAND!"

text: There is no question that Mr. Britling possessed a certain distinction. At the outset of this story, the man who stands at its center was already a figure of note. His name appeared in the _Who's Who_ of two continents. Over the preceding few years, he had swiftly emerged as an author recognized and embraced by the more discerning American readership, and even known among a small, selective audience in Britain. To those who first discovered him across the Atlantic, he had presented himself as a serious essayist, one who explored aesthetics, Eastern philosophy, national identity, poets, and the visual arts. Years before, he had traveled through America as a Kahn fellow—one of those gifted writers and thinkers funded by the Parisian philanthropist Auguste Kahn, who sends such promising individuals around the globe in comfort and esteem to cultivate an international outlook. Earlier still, he had served as a critic of art and literature and composed reflective op-ed pieces for the London _Times_. His career had begun with a fellowship at Pembroke and a prize-winning poem. After returning from his global journey, he settled back into his contemplative yet distinctive niche at _The Times_ and began producing books on national relations and social psychology, which quickly propelled him into the limelight.
source_text: For there can be no denying that Mr. Britling was, in a sense, distinguished. The hero and subject of this novel was at its very beginning a distinguished man. He was in the _Who's Who_ of two continents. In the last few years he had grown with some rapidity into a writer recognised and welcomed by the more cultivated sections of the American public, and even known to a select circle of British readers. To his American discoverers he had first appeared as an essayist, a serious essayist who wrote about aesthetics and Oriental thought and national character and poets and painting. He had come through America some years ago as one of those Kahn scholars, those promising writers and intelligent men endowed by Auguste Kahn of Paris, who go about the world nowadays in comfort and consideration as the travelling guests of that original philanthropist--to acquire the international spirit. Previously he had been a critic of art and literature and a writer of thoughtful third leaders in the London _Times_. He had begun with a Pembroke fellowship and a prize poem. He had returned from his world tour to his reflective yet original corner of _The Times_ and to the production of books about national relationships and social psychology, that had brought him rapidly into prominence.

text: He remembered when he first spotted Alaric at the crossroads station, and how he had chuckled at the sheer extravagance of his Teutonic display. The gleaming, close-shaven blond head topped by a pale gold student cap had bobbed and weaved through the crowd on the platform, clearly seeking directions. His face had flushed a deep rose from wrestling with an unfamiliar language. The youth had worn a white flannel suit set off by a single violet stripe; his boots were of that greenish-yellow leather which only a German scholar would dare call “smart”; his rucksack hung from his shoulders, and the treasured violin in its case was cradled with great tenderness in one hand—this same now-silent violin. The other hand gripped a walking stick with a carved head and a sharp ferrule. He had been impossibly, almost fabulously German. “Herr Alaric!” Mr. Fielding had called, and instantly the heels had snapped together for a bow, a deep bow from the waist, a bow that a distracted elderly woman laden with market vegetables had thoroughly upset. From that moment on, amid our casual English manners, Herr Alaric had preserved his bow—and always it had been in the process of being upset.
source_text: He recalled his first sight of Heinrich at the junction, and how he had laughed at the sight of his excessive Teutonism. The close-cropped shining fair head surmounted by a yellowish-white corps cap had appeared dodging about among the people upon the platform, and manifestly asking questions. The face had been very pink with the effort of an unaccustomed tongue. The young man had been clad in a suit of white flannel refined by a purple line; his boots were of that greenish yellow leather that only a German student could esteem "chic"; his rucksack was upon his back, and the precious fiddle in its case was carried very carefully in one hand; this same dead fiddle. The other hand held a stick with a carved knob and a pointed end. He had been too German for belief. "Herr Heinrich!" Mr. Britling had said, and straightway the heels had clashed together for a bow, a bow from the waist, a bow that a heedless old lady much burthened with garden produce had greatly disarranged. From first to last amidst our off-hand English ways Herr Heinrich had kept his bow--and always it had been getting disarranged.

text: Under cover of darkness, a few of our soldiers ventured out to mend the barbed-wire barriers, and one crept close to the enemy lines to eavesdrop. But my duties for the day were finished, and I was expected to rest inside the dugout. I was much too wound up to sleep. Every nerve in my body was jangling, and my thoughts were like countless splinters hurtling wildly through my head.
source_text: "In the night some of our men went out to repair the wire entanglements, and one man crawled halfway to the enemy trenches to listen. But I had done my bit for the day, and I was supposed to sleep in the dug-out. I was far too excited to sleep. All my nerves were jumping about, and my mind was like a lot of flying fragments flying about very fast....

text: She won't.... Years ago, I was convinced a European war was unavoidable," Mr. Britling told Mr. Direck, "but it's been looming for so long that I've finally stopped believing in it. The Great Powers bicker and bluster. They're much too careful and refined to actually start shooting. If war were truly coming, it would have broken out two years ago when the Balkan League descended on Turkey. Or when Bulgaria struck at Serbia....
source_text: "She won't.... Some years ago I used to believe in the inevitable European war," Mr. Britling explained to Mr. Direck, "but it's been threatened so long that at last I've lost all belief in it. The Powers wrangle and threaten. They're far too cautious and civilised to let the guns go off. If there was going to be a war it would have happened two years ago when the Balkan League fell upon Turkey. Or when Bulgaria attacked Serbia...."

text: Mr. Direck noticed a tall, slouching figure with flowing hair and long, lanky legs draped in even longer white flannel trousers, whose face struck him as oddly recognizable. The man was speaking to Manning with warm, close familiarity, and it came to Mr. Direck in a flash: he knew those features from Manning's weekly journal, *The Sectarian*, where a savage caricaturist spiced a caustic commentary. It was Raeburn—Raeburn the subtle, Raeburn the ultimate distillation of the party machine.... Well, that was how the English did things. "Come on for the pickup!" shouted the youngest Britling, grabbing Mr. Direck's elbow. It turned out that Mr. Britling and the guest who had stayed the night—Mr. Direck never caught his name—were choosing sides.
source_text: Mr. Direck became aware of a tall, drooping man with long hair and long digressive legs in still longer white flannel trousers, and a face that was somehow familiar. He was talking with affectionate intimacy to Manning, and suddenly Mr. Direck remembered that it was in Manning's weekly paper, _The Sectarian_, in which a bitter caricaturist enlivened a biting text, that he had become familiar with the features of Manning's companion. It was Raeburn, Raeburn the insidious, Raeburn the completest product of the party system.... Well, that was the English way. "Come for the pick up!" cried the youngest Britling, seizing upon Mr. Direck's elbow. It appeared that Mr. Britling and the overnight dinner guest--Mr. Direck never learnt his name--were picking up.

text: They used to play skat regularly with Herr Heinrich, who had taught them the rules with painstaking clarity. But Herr Heinrich was gone now—and, in any case, German card games had fallen from favor. The two philosophers confessed that they had long found skat elaborate yet lacking in real depth, and that its main pleasure had lain in Herr Heinrich’s flushed seriousness, his failure to perceive that they fully yet silently grasped its guileless tactics, and his unfailing inability to execute the brilliant moves his mind kept conjuring.
source_text: It had been their custom to play skat with Herr Heinrich, who had shown them the game very explicitly and thoroughly. But there was no longer any Herr Heinrich--and somehow German games were already out of fashion. The two philosophers admitted that they had already considered skat to be complicated without subtlety, and that its chief delight for them had been the pink earnestness of Herr Heinrich, his inability to grasp their complete but tacit comprehension of its innocent strategy, and his invariable ill-success to bring off the coups that flashed before his imagination.

text: Mr. Britling resumed their exchange by sending a brief note of regret about the edge of the small snapdragon plot, which drew a response of remarkably poignant grace. He answered with unforced warmth, full of promises and affirmations. Yet before his second letter reached her, her feelings had shifted. She concluded that if his remorse had been genuine and heartfelt, he would not have merely penned a message but would have appeared before her in a fever of dramatic passion, imploring pardon at her feet. So she composed a second letter along those lines, which crossed paths with his, and, carried away by her flair for writing, she broadened her argument into a sweeping critique of his chronic neglect of her, renounced any hope of future happiness with him, resolved to finish things permanently, and, after a suitable pause of solemn regret, to call Oliver to claim the prize of his steadfast devotion and kindness. By now the tensions in Europe had frayed Mr. Britling's composure, and he responded with a letter meant to soothe but that slipped into serious complaints about her "irrationality." In the meantime, his second, tenderly persuasive letter had arrived; it stirred her profoundly, and having vented the feelings that had been restlessly troubling her, she answered with a letter of gentle affection. From then on their correspondence took on a split character, veering between spells of anger and tenderness; her third letter was loving, and his fourth replied in kind, but in the gap she had received his third and fired back with sharp bitterness, to which his fifth was a counterblow, just failing to intersect with her magnanimous and final fifth. She answered his fifth on a Saturday evening—that fateful Saturday, the first of August, 1914—with a telegram. Oliver was away in Holland, taking a much-needed respite for his emotions, and she wired Mr. Britling: "Have summoned Oliver, he will join me, no need to reply to this."
source_text: It was Mr. Britling who reopened the correspondence by writing a little apology for the corner of the small snapdragon bed, and this evoked an admirably touching reply. He replied quite naturally with assurances and declarations. But before she got his second letter her mood had changed. She decided that if he had really and truly been lovingly sorry, instead of just writing a note to her he would have rushed over to her in a wild, dramatic state of mind, and begged forgiveness on his knees. She wrote therefore a second letter to this effect, crossing his second one, and, her literary gift getting the better of her, she expanded her thesis into a general denunciation of his habitual off-handedness with her, to an abandonment of all hope of ever being happy with him, to a decision to end the matter once for all, and after a decent interval of dignified regrets to summon Oliver to the reward of his patience and goodness. The European situation was now at a pitch to get upon Mr. Britling's nerves, and he replied with a letter intended to be conciliatory, but which degenerated into earnest reproaches for her "unreasonableness." Meanwhile she had received his second and tenderly eloquent letter; it moved her deeply, and having now cleared her mind of much that had kept it simmering uncomfortably, she replied with a sweetly loving epistle. From this point their correspondence had a kind of double quality, being intermittently angry and loving; her third letter was tender, and it was tenderly answered in his fourth; but in the interim she had received his third and answered it with considerable acerbity, to which his fifth was a retort, just missing her generous and conclusive fifth. She replied to his fifth on a Saturday evening--it was that eventful Saturday, Saturday the First of August, 1914--by a telegram. Oliver was abroad in Holland, engaged in a much-needed emotional rest, and she wired to Mr. Britling: "Have wired for Oliver, he will come to me, do not trouble to answer this."

text: He arrived at a square, red-brick house of some age, handsome in its plain Georgian symmetry, with a wide lawn, towering blue cedars, and a drive that led straight to the front door before curving away with Mr. Britling and the motor into hidden regions behind. The heart of the house was a spacious, airy hall lined with oak, heated in winter only by a single great hearth, and full of doors that he understood gave onto the square, distinct rooms so typical of England. On the landing outside his bedroom, bookshelves and cases of stuffed birds offered a quiet welcome. Coming downstairs, he found the hall occupied by a small, bright, bristling boy in a white flannel shirt, knickerbockers, and bare legs and feet, standing before the empty fireplace in a pose Mr. Direck instantly recognized as Mr. Britling’s own. “Lunch is in the garden,” the young Britling announced, “and I’ve been sent to bring you. And, I say—is it true? Are you American?”
source_text: It was a square-looking old red-brick house he had come to, very handsome in a simple Georgian fashion, with a broad lawn before it and great blue cedar trees, and a drive that came frankly up to the front door and then went off with Mr. Britling and the car round to unknown regions at the back. The centre of the house was a big airy hall, oak-panelled, warmed in winter only by one large fireplace and abounding in doors which he knew opened into the square separate rooms that England favours. Bookshelves and stuffed birds comforted the landing outside his bedroom. He descended to find the hall occupied by a small bright bristling boy in white flannel shirt and knickerbockers and bare legs and feet. He stood before the vacant open fireplace in an attitude that Mr. Direck knew instantly was also Mr. Britling's. "Lunch is in the garden," the Britling scion proclaimed, "and I've got to fetch you. And, I say! is it true? Are you American?"

text: Writing is about capturing life, not truly living it. But right now I'm struck by the sensation that we're living with extraordinary intensity. It feels as though the very texture of existence is transforming. Such moments do come. There are moments when the soul of life shifts entirely. The ancient world understood this far better than we do. They drew lines between ordinary days and holy days, between celebrations and periods of fasting and sacred observance. That's precisely what's unfolding now. The rules of everyday life must be suspended. Just a few days ago—oh, barely three days past—rivalry seemed fair, it seemed right and acceptable to scramble for the best provisions and guard what was yours. But that no longer fits. War creates a Sabbath, and we close the shops. The banks are closed, and the world still carries the feeling of an endless Sunday....
source_text: "Writing is recording, not living. But now I feel suddenly that we are living intensely. It is as if the whole quality of life was changing. There are such times. There are times when the spirit of life changes altogether. The old world knew that better than we do. It made a distinction between weekdays and Sabbaths, and between feasts and fasts and days of devotion. That is just what has happened now. Week-day rules must be put aside. Before--oh! three days ago, competition was fair, it was fair and tolerable to get the best food one could and hold on to one's own. But that isn't right now. War makes a Sabbath, and we shut the shops. The banks are shut, and the world still feels as though Sunday was keeping on...."

text: Everywhere the emblems of the Allies fluttered—draped in shop windows, pinned above doorways, fixed to the bonnets of motorcars, worn upon lapels—and the hoardings and glass fronts were thick with recruiting posters. “Your King and Country Need You” ran the dominant refrain, and the placards still cried for “A Hundred Thousand Men,” though Lord Kitchener’s summons had already swelled to half a million. Nearly every taxicab carried a call for volunteers. The great windows of the Norddeutscher Lloyd offices in Cockspur Street stood shuttered and plastered over with layer upon layer of urgent recruiting appeals.
source_text: Everywhere there were the flags of the Allies; in shop windows, over doors, on the bonnets of automobiles, on people's breasts, and there was a great quantity of recruiting posters on the hoardings and in windows: "Your King and Country Need You" was the chief text, and they still called for "A Hundred Thousand Men" although the demand of Lord Kitchener had risen to half a million. There were also placards calling for men on nearly all the taxicabs. The big windows of the offices of the Norddeutscher Lloyd in Cockspur Street were boarded up, and plastered thickly with recruiting appeals.

text: She had written as though his quiet withdrawal were a trifle, a mere oversight unworthy of remark. Evidently she had not summoned the steadfast and besotted Oliver as she had vowed; at least, his name did not stain the page. Instead, she chided Mr. Britling for abandoning her, crying out for his nearness, his tender and fortifying phrases. The war, she confessed, had frightened her. She was a frail creature, and the whole affair was too monstrous, and there was not a single being left to clasp her hand—no one who grasped in the faintest measure what she endured. (But why had Oliver’s hand not found hers?) She was a child forsaken in the blackness. It was utterly wicked how truths were hidden. The rumours one caught, “_often from lips beyond reproach,_” were enough to crush and horrify anyone. Vessel after vessel had been swallowed by German torpedoes, a fact veiled from us for no sane cause, and Prince Louis of Battenberg had been unmasked as a traitor and cast into the Tower. Haldane, too, was a traitor. Our forces in France had been “all but _bartered away_” by the French. Nearly every French commander took German coin. The censors and the journals smothered it all, yet what purpose did smothering serve? It was madness not to confide in the people! Still, it was all too hideous for a pitiful little spirit whose sole wish was to dwell in peace. Why would he not hasten to her and wrap her in the shelter of his arms? By day her thoughts would not settle; by night sleep fled.
source_text: She had written ignoring his silence and absence, or rather treating it as if it were an incident of no particular importance. Apparently she had not called upon the patient and devoted Oliver as she had threatened; at any rate, there were no signs of Oliver in her communication. But she reproached Mr. Britling for deserting her, and she clamoured for his presence and for kind and strengthening words. She was, she said, scared by this war. She was only a little thing, and it was all too dreadful, and there was not a soul in the world to hold her hand, at least no one who understood in the slightest degree how she felt. (But why was not Oliver holding her hand?) She was like a child left alone in the dark. It was perfectly horrible the way that people were being kept in the dark. The stories one heard, "_often from quite trustworthy sources_," were enough to depress and terrify any one. Battleship after battleship had been sunk by German torpedoes, a thing kept secret from us for no earthly reason, and Prince Louis of Battenberg had been discovered to be a spy and had been sent to the Tower. Haldane too was a spy. Our army in France had been "practically _sold_" by the French. Almost all the French generals were in German pay. The censorship and the press were keeping all this back, but what good was it to keep it back? It was folly not to trust people! But it was all too dreadful for a poor little soul whose only desire was to live happily. Why didn't he come along to her and make her feel she had protecting arms round her? She couldn't think in the daytime: she couldn't sleep at night....

text: Instead of shattering beneath the weight of it, he was freed by a quirk of his sanguine nature. His thoughts flipped, all at once, to the four chances in five. It was like a dislocated joint snapping back into its socket. Just as sudden. He realized he had adjusted to the idea of Hugh in mortal peril. It had settled into a fixed fact, an ordinary thing. He could endure it now and carry on with his life.
source_text: Then instead of breaking under that pressure, he was released by the trick of the sanguine temperament. His mind turned over, abruptly, to the four chances out of five. It was like a dislocated joint slipping back into place. It was as sudden as that. He found he had adapted himself to the prospect of Hugh in mortal danger. It had become a fact established, a usual thing. He could bear with it and go about his affairs.

text: She delighted in tormenting him with Oliver. She delighted in tormenting him altogether. In truth, this far-from-unusual romance was charged with a fierce antagonism. She appeared to resent the hold Mr. Britling had over her, and the feelings and joys she experienced in his company. She seemed driven by an instinctual need to make him pay a steep price for her—in time, in passion, in dignity. The thought that he might possess her lightly and contentedly was unbearable to her. That would mean he was getting her too cheaply. She measured his offerings by the trouble they exacted from him, and was resolved that the course of true love would not, if she had any say, run smooth. Mr. Britling, by contrast, belonged to the school of courteous and cheerful lovers. He considered quarrelling and contradiction to be scandalous, and believed that courtship should be a genial, cozy affair conducted in a spirit of high amusement and deep mutual regard. This lightness offended the lady’s pride. She drew unflattering parallels with Oliver. If Oliver lacked allure, he certainly did not lack intensity. He seemed to crave sacrifice almost more than fulfillment. Oliver was a model of exemplary wretchedness; he would weep profusely and often. She could make him weep whenever she chose. By offering hope and then snatching it away, if no other method came to hand. Why did Mr. Britling never weep? She wept.
source_text: She liked to vex him with Oliver. She liked to vex him generally. Indeed in this by no means abnormal love affair, there was a very strong antagonism. She seemed to resent the attraction Mr. Britling had for her and the emotions and pleasure she had with him. She seemed under the sway of an instinctive desire to make him play heavily for her, in time, in emotion, in self-respect. It was intolerable to her that he could take her easily and happily. That would be taking her cheaply. She valued his gifts by the bother they cost him, and was determined that the path of true love should not, if she could help it, run smooth. Mr. Britling on the other hand was of the school of polite and happy lovers. He thought it outrageous to dispute and contradict, and he thought that making love was a cheerful, comfortable thing to be done in a state of high good humour and intense mutual appreciation. This levity offended the lady's pride. She drew unfavourable contrasts with Oliver. If Oliver lacked charm he certainly did not lack emotion. He desired sacrifice, it seemed, almost more than satisfactions. Oliver was a person of the most exemplary miserableness; he would weep copiously and frequently. She could always make him weep when she wanted to do so. By holding out hopes and then dashing them if by no other expedient. Why did Mr. Britling never weep? She wept.

text: An Indian fellow!" She cast a quick look along the table, as if anticipating a figure draped in saffron and scarlet. "Ah, that is one of Mr. Lawrence Carmine's protégés!" she went on, her tone growing more hushed and intimate, much as if she were remarking upon the crystal decanter set before him. "He's a renowned scholar of Eastern letters, a member of some society that arranges comforts for Indian scholars in the city, and he invites them to visit.
source_text: "Coloured gentleman!" She gave a swift glance down the table as though she expected to see something purple with yellow spots. "Oh, that is one of Mr. Lawrence Carmine's young men!" she explained even more confidentially and with an air of discussing the silver bowl of roses before him. "He's a great authority on Indian literature, he belongs to a society for making things pleasant for Indian students in London, and he has them down."

text: The English, cut off by their island geography, had for countless generations remained political novices. Their greatest flaw, and equally their greatest strength, was a relaxed, unbothered disposition. They moved through life as if it were a comedy, refusing in the whole fabric of their existence to acknowledge that life holds tragedy. Not even the Americans had been so secluded. The Americans had contended with their native tribes, their enslaved people, their Civil War. Until the Great War arrived, the Channel had felt as vast as the Atlantic in warding off any fundamental threat. Even Ireland lay at a distance—a four-hour voyage away. And so the English had cultivated to the utmost the merits and defects of security and ease; they harbored a distrust of science and theatrical gestures; they saw no point in precision or fervor; they shunned going “to extremes.” In the end, everything would sort itself out. Yet they understood what it meant to be swept into conflicts by zealous minorities and the cunning of events, and they stood ready to sympathize with any other nation that had met that destiny. All their instincts led them to wage war with good humor and comfort, to dispute with a government rather than a populace. It required Mr. Britling at least two months of combat to grasp that the Germans were fighting in a spirit entirely different.
source_text: The English because of their insularity had been political amateurs for endless generations. It was their supreme vice, it was their supreme virtue, to be easy-going. They had lived in an atmosphere of comedy, and denied in the whole tenor of their lives that life is tragic. Not even the Americans had been more isolated. The Americans had had their Indians, their negroes, their War of Secession. Until the Great War the Channel was as broad as the Atlantic for holding off every vital challenge. Even Ireland was away--a four-hour crossing. And so the English had developed to the fullest extent the virtues and vices of safety and comfort; they had a hatred of science and dramatic behaviour; they could see no reason for exactness or intensity; they disliked proceeding "to extremes." Ultimately everything would turn out all right. But they knew what it is to be carried into conflicts by energetic minorities and the trick of circumstances, and they were ready to understand the case of any other country which has suffered that fate. All their habits inclined them to fight good-temperedly and comfortably, to quarrel with a government and not with a people. It took Mr. Britling at least a couple of months of warfare to understand that the Germans were fighting in an altogether different spirit.

text: It's rather odd," Mr. Direck remarked, "that here in England I'm much more inclined to reach for something fortifying, and I've entirely lost that craving for ice water one always has back home. I put it down to the damper atmosphere. You're less parched, but also less invigorated. Thirst doesn't hound you anymore, yet you feel the want of a little pick-me-up. Thank you, that will do.
source_text: "It is a very curious thing," said Mr. Direck, "that in England I find myself more disposed to take stimulants and that I no longer have the need for iced water that one feels at home. I ascribe it to a greater humidity in the air. One is less dried and one is less braced. One is no longer pursued by a thirst, but one needs something to buck one up a little. Thank you. That is enough."

text: A two-headed rabid hound," Aunt Wilshire declared. "He and his eagles!... A man of his sort should never have been permitted to wage a war.... Not even a minor one.... If he'd been confined when I first warned them, none of this would have come to pass. But naturally, I count for nothing.... My words were deemed unworthy of notice.
source_text: "A double-headed mad dog," said Aunt Wilshire. "Him and his eagles!... A man like that ought never to have been allowed to make a war.... Not even a little war.... If he had been put under restraint when I said so, none of these things would have happened. But, of course I am nobody.... It was not considered worth attending to."

text: Of course I shan't claim there haven't been others," Mr. Aldridge abruptly continued. "There have been. One in particular. But I can promise you I've never known such height and depth, nor anything approaching this kind of Still Clear Certainty.... And now I'm simply telling you these matters, Miss Vane, I'm not sure if it will matter to you to hear that you are really and wholly the first love I ever had, and also the last. I've had sent across--it came only yesterday--this small photograph of a miniature likeness of one of my forebear's kin--a Vane, exactly as you are. It's here....
source_text: "Of course I won't pretend there haven't been others," Mr. Direck suddenly resumed. "There have. One particularly. But I can assure you I've never felt the depth and height or anything like the sort of Quiet Clear Conviction.... And now I'm just telling you these things, Miss Corner, I don't know whether it will interest you if I tell you that you're really and truly the very first love I ever had as well as my last. I've had sent over--I got it only yesterday--this lil' photograph of a miniature portrait of one of my ancestor's relations--a Corner just as you are. It's here...."

text: Perhaps three thousand years from now," Mr. Britling said, "men will still carry this same sorrow… And yet—and yet… No. Right now, I have no spring left in me. It isn't in me to give way to despair, but circumstances are bearing down on me. I don't bounce back the way I once did. I still tell myself that, however long and hard the road, the spirit of hope, the creative impulse, the generosity and gallantry in the human heart, must triumph in the end. But I say it the way one murmurs a threadbare prayer. The light has gone from the sky for me. Sometimes I wonder if it will ever return. Let younger men take courage and carry the world forward. If I could die now for something true—instead of just having to drag on in this world of futile striving—I would be glad to die now, Carmine…
source_text: "Three thousand years ahead perhaps," said Mr. Britling, "there will still be men with the same sadness.... And yet--and yet.... No. Just now I have no elasticity. It is not in my nature to despair, but things are pressing me down. I don't recover as I used to recover. I tell myself still that though the way is long and hard the spirit of hope, the spirit of creation, the generosities and gallantries in the heart of man, must end in victory. But I say that over as one repeats a worn-out prayer. The light is out of the sky for me. Sometimes I doubt if it will ever come back. Let younger men take heart and go on with the world. If I could die for the right thing now--instead of just having to live on in this world of ineffective struggle--I would be glad to die now, Carmine...."

text: He went back to his desk, turning the lemon slice in his tea. He would set down the straightforward common sense of the Irish predicament. He would lay it all out so clearly that this bickering nonsense would be forced to stop. The tone should be severe, laced with an ironic bluntness. No insults, no rancor—only a profound fervor for reason.
source_text: He returned to his desk stirring the lemon in his glass of tea. He would write the plain common sense of this Irish situation. He would put things so plainly that this squabbling folly would _have_ to cease. It should be done austerely, with a sort of ironical directness. There should be no abuse, no bitterness, only a deep passion of sanity.

text: She had spoken her piece. She had delivered her final testament. Her eyes fell shut. He was astonished to see that this strange, withered being had, in an instant, turned lovely—possessing that pale, luminous beauty one occasionally glimpses in the very old. She seemed transfigured, the way a master painter can ennoble the face of age. He felt compelled to press his lips to her brow.
source_text: She had said what she wanted to say. She had made her testament. She closed her eyes. He was amazed to find this grotesque old creature had suddenly become beautiful, in that silvery vein of beauty one sometimes finds in very old men. She was exalted as great artists will sometimes exalt the portraits of the aged. He was moved to kiss her forehead.

text: On an October afternoon, Arthur appeared in a uniform that hung awkwardly from his frame, his features already thickened and his nose burned raw by the autumn glare. He declared the existence was harsh, yet it left him feeling strangely vigorous; perhaps men were designed to labour till they collapsed in fatigue, to go ten or twelve hours without food and then devour their rations like beasts. He was developing a fondness for cheap cigarillos and a fierce carbonated drink called Behemoths. The prospect of advancement unnerved him; he could not imagine adopting the tone of command over others that a lance-corporal required. He still attempted to study fragments of chemistry and crystallography, but it wouldn't "mesh with the life." In the thin free hours of a trainee, it was pleasanter to sprawl idle and scribble clumsy rhymes and sketch grotesques of the fellows in his section. When his people asked what he desired, he requested a vast provision hamper like the one he had known at school, only "_far_ larger," and a great canister of flea powder. It must be potent enough to destroy ticks....
source_text: Hugh came along one day in October in an ill-fitting uniform, looking already coarser in fibre and with a nose scorched red by the autumnal sun. He said the life was rough, but it made him feel extraordinarily well; perhaps man was made to toil until he dropped asleep from exhaustion, to fast for ten or twelve hours and then eat like a wolf. He was acquiring a taste for Woodbine cigarettes, and a heady variety of mineral waters called Monsters. He feared promotion; he felt he could never take the high line with other human beings demanded of a corporal. He was still trying to read a little chemistry and crystallography, but it didn't "go with the life." In the scanty leisure of a recruit in training it was more agreeable to lie about and write doggerel verses and draw caricatures of the men in one's platoon. Invited to choose what he liked by his family, he demanded a large tuckbox such as he used to have at school, only "_much_ larger," and a big tin of insect powder. It must be able to kill ticks....

text: At the start, I couldn't accept that this conflict would mirror all the others," he said. "I never imagined it. I believed we'd finally learned something. It felt like the beginning of a great reckoning. I expected humanity's basic reason to erupt like a furious fire, sweeping away the outdated nonsense of empires, flags, and militarism the moment it threatened our wellbeing. So many things I now see as absurd, I assumed would unfold—naturally. I thought America would speak out against the violation of Belgium; that it couldn't stomach the crushing of a sister republic—if only out of respect for Lafayette. Well—it seems America mainly worries about us making cotton contraband. I believed the Balkan states could manage sensible compromise, a shared commitment to their collective liberty. Now I watch three German princelings bartering peasants, with no one in their territories daring to object. I viewed this war, as many Frenchmen did, as something that could rightfully inspire a magnificent wave of outrage... It was nothing but a fantasy, the fantasy of a sheltered, comfortable man who'd never faced life's sharp edges. Everywhere cunning, everywhere petty rivalries and resentments, suspicions, deceits, cowardice, weak resolve, stunted visions, swarm over the vast and fundamental questions... It's a war now like any other of the rabble-driven, scattered-purpose disasters that have broken empires and ravaged the earth; a war without meaning, a war that has lost its spirit, reduced to mindless slaughter and ruin, a spectacle in colossal and tragic forms of our species' foolishness and futility..."
source_text: "When it began I did not believe that this war could be like other wars," he said. "I did not dream it. I thought that we had grown wiser at last. It seemed to me like the dawn of a great clearing up. I thought the common sense of mankind would break out like a flame, an indignant flame, and consume all this obsolete foolery of empires and banners and militarism directly it made its attack upon human happiness. A score of things that I see now were preposterous, I thought must happen--naturally. I thought America would declare herself against the Belgian outrage; that she would not tolerate the smashing of the great sister republic--if only for the memory of Lafayette. Well--I gather America is chiefly concerned about our making cotton contraband. I thought the Balkan States were capable of a reasonable give and take; of a common care for their common freedom. I see now three German royalties trading in peasants, and no men in their lands to gainsay them. I saw this war, as so many Frenchmen have seen it, as something that might legitimately command a splendid enthusiasm of indignation.... It was all a dream, the dream of a prosperous comfortable man who had never come to the cutting edge of life. Everywhere cunning, everywhere small feuds and hatreds, distrusts, dishonesties, timidities, feebleness of purpose, dwarfish imaginations, swarm over the great and simple issues.... It is a war now like any other of the mobbing, many-aimed cataclysms that have shattered empires and devastated the world; it is a war without point, a war that has lost its soul, it has become mere incoherent fighting and destruction, a demonstration in vast and tragic forms of the stupidity and ineffectiveness of our species...."

text: My dear Parent, all of this is mere venting. I have been starved for no reason, worked to utter exhaustion, and driven to the edge of fury. We covered twenty-five miles cross-country in fifty-seven hours, and went without food for some eighteen of them. I have been at my Captain’s side while he billeted us here in Cheasingholt. Oh, he is an absolute MUFF! Dear God! God in Heaven! what a MUFF! He is terrified of printed matter, yet he masters himself with heroic resolve. He prides himself on having no “sense of locality, confound it!” Prides himself! He trotted about this village—which is rather scattered—at a brisk little jog, and refused to use the one-inch map I happened to have. He gauged each room’s capacity by eye alone and would not let me measure, not even with the God-given strides I inherited. “We’ll put five fellahs hea!” he announced. “What d’you want to measure the room for? We haven’t come to lay down carpets.” Then, having assigned men by _coup d’oeil_ so that half the village was miserably crammed, he discovered the other half stood empty and had to start all over again. “If you measured the floor space first, sir,” I said, “and drew up a list of the houses—” “That isn’t the way I’m going to do it,” he replied, fixing me with a pitiless eye.
source_text: "All of this, my dear Parent, is just a blow off. I have been needlessly starved, and fagged to death and exasperated. We have moved five-and-twenty miles across country--in fifty-seven hours. And without food for about eighteen hours. I have been with my Captain, who has been billeting us here in Cheasingholt. Oh, he is a MUFF! Oh God! oh God of Heaven! what a MUFF! He is afraid of printed matter, but he controls himself heroically. He prides himself upon having no 'sense of locality, confound it!' Prides himself! He went about this village, which is a little dispersed, at a slight trot, and wouldn't avail himself of the one-inch map I happened to have. He judged the capacity of each room with his eye and wouldn't let me measure, even with God's own paces. Not with the legs I inherit. 'We'll put five fellahs hea!' he said. 'What d'you want to measure the room for? We haven't come to lay down carpets.' Then, having assigned men by _coup d'oeil_, so as to congest half the village miserably, he found the other half unoccupied and had to begin all over again. 'If you measured the floor space first, sir,' I said, 'and made a list of the houses--' 'That isn't the way I'm going to do it,' he said, fixing me with a pitiless eye....

text: The old way of life was built on structure. That’s why Germany remains the most archaic of European nations—a throwback to tribal worship, a kind of atavism. To organise, to discipline, to shape character or impose authority, is to act as if you’ve arrived at a final truth in your overall philosophy. It presupposes a fixed destination. Heinrich has his fixed destination, his professorship in philology or something like it, slotted into the Germanic machinery. And that, in turn, has its fixed destination in German national self-assertion. Over here, we hold no such certainties. We know we haven’t reached finality, so we stay open, apologetic, receptive, instead of willful. You see, all organisation, because it implies finality, is death. We sense that. The Germans don’t. Whatever you organise, you kill. Organised morals, organised religion, organised thought—these are dead morals, dead religion, dead thought. And yet some organisation is necessary. Organisation is like slaughtering cattle. If you don’t kill some, the herd simply goes to waste. But you mustn’t kill them all, or you destroy the herd itself. The unkilled cattle are the herd, the ongoing life; the unorganised side of existence is the real life. The essence of life is adventure, not achievement. What isn’t adventure isn’t life. What can be governed can be mechanised. But priests and schoolmasters and bureaucrats seize hold of life and try to turn it entirely into rules, entirely into etiquette and regulation and correctness. And parents, and parental love, push toward the same end. It’s all very well to experiment on one’s own, but when you watch those dear creatures of your own, so young and untried and so ready for every kind of splendid folly, stepping along the narrow plank, descending into dark jungles—ah, then you long to swaddle them in laws and foresight and fence them round with “Verboten” signs on every conceivable side.
source_text: "The old system of life was organisation. That is where Germany is still the most ancient of European states. It's a reversion to a tribal cult. It's atavistic.... To organise or discipline, or mould characters or press authority, is to assume that you have reached finality in your general philosophy. It implies an assured end. Heinrich has his assured end, his philological professorship or thereabouts as a part of the Germanic machine. And that too has its assured end in German national assertion. Here, we have none of those convictions. We know we haven't finality, and so we are open and apologetic and receptive, rather than wilful.... You see all organisation, with its implication of finality, is death. We feel that. The Germans don't. What you organise you kill. Organised morals or organised religion or organised thought are dead morals and dead religion and dead thought. Yet some organisation you must have. Organisation is like killing cattle. If you do not kill some the herd is just waste. But you musn't kill all or you kill the herd. The unkilled cattle are the herd, the continuation; the unorganised side of life is the real life. The reality of life is adventure, not performance. What isn't adventure isn't life. What can be ruled about can be machined. But priests and schoolmasters and bureaucrats get hold of life and try to make it _all_ rules, _all_ etiquette and regulation and correctitude.... And parents and the love of parents make for the same thing. It is all very well to experiment for oneself, but when one sees these dear things of one's own, so young and inexperienced and so capable of every sort of gallant foolishness, walking along the narrow plank, going down into dark jungles, ah! then it makes one want to wrap them in laws and foresight and fence them about with 'Verboten' boards in all the conceivable aspects...."

text: In England, one must understand, Mr. Carroway ordinarily employed a polished and deliberate English, yet he now struggled fiercely to suppress a twanging, adenoidal drawl and the temptation to sprinkle his speech with arid colloquialisms and poker-table figures at every turn. When addressed, he yearned to answer "Yep" or "Sure," locutions he would as soon have uttered back home as brandish a hunting knife. But he was possessed by a theatrical instinct. He wished to stand forth, visibly and audibly, as America observing. He longed to embody precisely what he imagined an Englishman would anticipate of him. In any case, his suit had been cut by an emphatically American tailor in New York, and on the strength of that, a cabman had courteously but unwaveringly mistaken the shillings on his meter for dollars—a mishap that greatly fortified, in Mr. Carroway's own estimation, his emergence as something almost archetypally distinct against the English backdrop.... So much so, in fact, that the cabman received the dollars.
source_text: In America, it must be explained, Mr. Direck spoke a very good and careful English indeed, but he now found the utmost difficulty in controlling his impulse to use a high-pitched nasal drone and indulge in dry "Americanisms" and poker metaphors upon all occasions. When people asked him questions he wanted to say "Yep" or "Sure," words he would no more have used in America than he could have used a bowie knife. But he had a sense of rôle. He wanted to be visibly and audibly America eye-witnessing. He wanted to be just exactly what he supposed an Englishman would expect him to be. At any rate, his clothes had been made by a strongly American New York tailor, and upon the strength of them a taxi-man had assumed politely but firmly that the shillings on his taximeter were dollars, an incident that helped greatly to sustain the effect of Mr. Direck, in Mr. Direck's mind, as something standing out with an almost representative clearness against the English scene.... So much so that the taxi-man got the dollars....

text: Lady Frensham did not catch that question. She pressed on with her grand subject. Lloyd George, once fit only for the noose, had now risen to stand as the sole minister worthy of her idol. He had captured her admiration with his denunciation of the working class. He alone dared to speak the truth—to tell them they drank, to tell them they dodged and idled, to tell them outright that if this nation met defeat, the fault would lie with _them_!
source_text: Lady Frensham did not hear that question. She pursued her glorious theme. Lloyd George, who had once been worthy only of the gallows, was now the sole minister fit to put beside her hero. He had won her heart by his condemnation of the working man. He was the one man who was not afraid to speak out, to tell them they drank, to tell them they shirked and loafed, to tell them plainly that if defeat came to this country the blame would fall upon _them_!

text: Thus he would have remained seated, until at last he stretched out upon coarse coverlets and endured, alone, a vast unease; lay still and now and then, perhaps, recalled Matching’s Easy and mused what Hugh and Teddy might be about. Then a fever took him, and the world turned garish and grotesque and monstrous in his sight. Until a day came when a boundless frailty seized him, and his suffering dimmed, and all his recollections and fancies dimmed—and dimmer still….
source_text: So he must have sat, and presently he lay on some rough bedding and suffered, untended, in infinite discomfort; lay motionless and thought at times, it may be, of Matching's Easy and wondered what Hugh and Teddy were doing. Then he became fevered, and the world grew bright-coloured and fantastic and ugly for him. Until one day an infinite weakness laid hold of him, and his pain grew faint and all his thoughts and memories grew faint--and still fainter....

text: Mr. Van der Pant also stirred in Mr. Britling a strange, stubborn optimism about the war and the imminence of Germany’s defeat. He would assure his visitor that he would be back in Antwerp before May, and that the Germans would be pushed across the Rhine by July. He knew full well that his grasp of military realities was nonexistent, yet the moment he slipped into the language of the Entente Cordiale—the Anglo-French alliance, that is—he couldn’t stop himself. Something in the dynamic between them forced him to play the part of the staunch, almost absurdly protective Englishman. At moments he felt like a bankrupt knowingly delaying the moment his ruin would be exposed. But what Mr. Britling was really trying to express, across the barrier of an unfamiliar tongue and a foreign sensibility, was that England’s honour could never be cleansed until Belgium was restored and its suffering repaid.
source_text: And another curious effect that Mr. Van der Pant had upon Mr. Britling was to produce an obstinate confidence about the war and the nearness of the German collapse. He would promise Mr. Van der Pant that he should be back in Antwerp before May; that the Germans would be over the Rhine by July. He knew perfectly well that his ignorance of all the military conditions was unqualified, but still he could not restrain himself from this kind of thing so soon as he began to speak Entente Cordiale--Anglo-French, that is to say. Something in his relationship to Mr. Van der Pant obliged him to be acutely and absurdly the protecting British.... At times he felt like a conscious bankrupt talking off the hour of disclosure. But indeed all that Mr. Britling was trying to say against the difficulties of a strange language and an alien temperament, was that the honour of England would never be cleared until Belgium was restored and avenged....

text: Not every German fled. A handful stayed with their machine guns until they were run through with bayonets. Both trenches had been battered to pieces, and in the forward one lay scattered clusters of dead. We set to immediately, heaving sandbags from the trench’s old front over to its rear wall. Our artillery had never let up the whole while; now it was hammering the third-line trenches. And almost at once German shells began falling among us. Naturally, they had the range down to the inch. There was no time to feel or reflect; you just threw every ounce of yourself into reversing the trench.
source_text: "Not all the Germans ran. Three or four stuck to their machine-guns until they got bayoneted. Both the trenches were frightfully smashed about, and in the first one there were little knots and groups of dead. We got to work at once shying the sandbags over from the old front of the trench to the parados. Our guns had never stopped all the time; they were now plastering the third line trenches. And almost at once the German shells began dropping into us. Of course they had the range to an inch. One didn't have any time to feel and think; one just set oneself with all one's energy to turn the trench over....

text: Perhaps that’s the only way such wars will ever truly stop. By women resolving to eliminate the sort of men who start them. Hunting them down. Through a campaign of tracking and killing that stretches on for years and years beyond the war’s official end.... Murder is such a small, tender penalty for the offense of war.... It would be scarcely more than a quiet rebuke for everything that’s occurred. Drifting down like snow. One death after another. Flake by flake. This prince. That statesman. The count whose editorials roar so fiercely for conflict.... That’s what I mean to do. If Teddy really is gone.... We women were perfectly willing a year or so ago to starve and die for the Vote, and that was a trifling matter next to this undertaking.... Don’t you grasp what I’m saying? It’s so straightforward and logical, Cissie. Whenever a man sits weighing whether to launch a war, he’ll also weigh the thought of women, women with knives, with bombs; of a retribution that never wearies or pauses; of dedicated, patient women prepared to set out on a pilgrimage that ends only with his death.... I wouldn’t wound these war makers. No. Despite the poison gas. Despite trench foot and the men blinded and the wounded who’ve lain for days, slowly dying in the mud. Women should not wound. But I would kill. Like exterminating dangerous vermin. It would continue year after year. Balkan kings, German princes, chancellors, they would have plotted for so much—and come to nothing but a rasp in the throat.... And if soon other kings and emperors started to strut about and inspect armies, they too would go.
source_text: "That perhaps is the only way in which wars of this kind will ever be brought to an end. By women insisting on killing the kind of people who make them. Rooting them out. By a campaign of pursuit and assassination that will go on for years and years after the war itself is over.... Murder is such a little gentle punishment for the crime of war.... It would be hardly more than a reproach for what has happened. Falling like snow. Death after death. Flake by flake. This prince. That statesman. The count who writes so fiercely for war.... That is what I am going to do. If Teddy is really dead.... We women were ready enough a year or so ago to starve and die for the Vote, and that was quite a little thing in comparison with this business.... Don't you see what I mean? It's so plain and sensible, Cissie. Whenever a man sits and thinks whether he will make a war or not, then he will think too of women, women with daggers, bombs; of a vengeance that will never tire nor rest; of consecrated patient women ready to start out upon a pilgrimage that will only end with his death.... I wouldn't hurt these war makers. No. In spite of the poison gas. In spite of trench feet and the men who have been made blind and the wounded who have lain for days, dying slowly in the wet. Women ought not to hurt. But I would kill. Like killing dangerous vermin. It would go on year by year. Balkan kings, German princes, chancellors, they would have schemed for so much--and come to just a rattle in the throat.... And if presently other kings and emperors began to prance about and review armies, they too would go....

text: The grief of the hopeful spirit strikes fast and hard, then fades; it rarely draws the world’s attention. The world has no reason to take it into account. It drives no one to suicide and spawns few misdeeds. It rushes through, lashing its target as it passes. Yet it is agony all the same. In these states, Mr. Britling might not have tasted the bleak, hopeless emptiness of the melancholic or the blazing curses of the wrathful, but he perceived a landscape thick with calamity and blunder, with venomous barbs and snares and bogs and irreparable follies. An almost unbearable shame at being Mr. Britling would hound him—finding a hundred reasons to condemn itself.
source_text: The sorrows of the sanguine temperament are brief but furious; the world knows little of them. The world has no need to reckon with them. They cause no suicides and few crimes. They hurry past, smiting at their victim as they go. None the less they are misery. Mr. Britling in these moods did not perhaps experience the grey and hopeless desolations of the melancholic nor the red damnation of the choleric, but he saw a world that bristled with misfortune and error, with poisonous thorns and traps and swampy places and incurable blunderings. An almost insupportable remorse for being Mr. Britling would pursue him--justifying itself upon a hundred counts....

text: After calling out repeatedly, it grew clear that he had caught the notice of two obliging yet unhurried farmhands. They rose gradually, at first just watchful crowns, from the fields. With their help the motor was guided back onto the lane. Mr. Direck lent his strength stoutly, and observed with a cool perceptiveness the deference shown to Mr. Britling and the coins that passed into the men’s palms. They tugged their caps, they addressed Mr. Britling as “Sir.” They studied the machine from a polite distance but with goodwill. “She ain’t ’armed, not a scratch she ain’t, not truly,” one offered cheerfully. And in truth, save for a slight buckling of the wing and the loosening of a headlamp’s wiring, the vehicle was unharmed. Mr. Britling settled back behind the wheel; Mr. Direck, solemn and wordless, climbed in beside him. They set off with the customary shudder, as if something had jabbed the car unexpectedly and indecently from the rear. And from that moment Mr. Britling, steering with scrupulous caution, reached home without further trouble, apart from grazing the metal rim of his running board against the gatepost of his most pleasant garden.
source_text: After shouting again, several times, it became manifest that he had attracted the attention of two willing but deliberate labouring men. They emerged slowly, first as attentive heads, from the landscape. With their assistance the car was restored to the road again. Mr. Direck assisted manfully, and noted the respect that was given to Mr. Britling and the shillings that fell to the men, with an intelligent detachment.
