They touched their hats, they called Mr. Britling "Sir." They examined the car distantly but kindly. "Ain't 'urt 'e, not a bit 'e ain't, not really," said one encouragingly. And indeed except for a slight crumpling of the mud-guard and the detachment of the wire of one of the headlights the automobile was uninjured. Mr. Britling resumed his seat; Mr. Direck gravely and in silence got up beside him. They started with the usual convulsion, as though something had pricked the vehicle unexpectedly and shamefully behind. And from this point Mr. Britling, driving with meticulous care, got home without further mishap, excepting only that he scraped off some of the metal edge of his footboard against the gate-post of his very agreeable garden.

text: I grant that we grapple in chaos, led by wretched commanders, squandering lives and time without measure; that the dragging on of this war owes itself to our lack of discipline and to the deceits and stratagems our half-formed nature breeds. When the conflict began, I believed I would witness militarism toppled within a year.
source_text: _But that we do struggle confusedly, with pitiful leaders and infinite waste and endless delay; that it is to our indisciplines and to the dishonesties and tricks our incompleteness provokes, that the prolongation of this war is to be ascribed, I readily admit. At the outbreak of this war I had hoped to see militarism felled within a year...._

text: Mr. Britling had altogether stopped writing about the war. Every later attempt lay discarded, half-made. He could not conceive of his words reaching anyone, swaying opinion, stirring the least result. His pen moved now only in fits and starts. The pieces he had once sent to _The Times_ had dwindled to nothing. His mind churned endlessly with the war, with mortality and eternity, with religious questions that had seemed impossibly distant in the tranquil years; yet none of his reflections crystallized enough for composition. The steady constellations of his intellect were smothered beneath the squalls of agitation that each morning’s paper revived and each day’s unfolding events fed. And even as his professional earnings withered in the face of his confusion and paralysis, the private income drawn from his and his wife’s holdings grew precarious. She had placed two thousand pounds in the Constantinople loan, seven hundred in Ottoman railway debentures; he had committed comparable sums to two Hungarian bonds and one Bulgarian, to a linoleum works at Rouen and a Swiss hotel venture. All ceased to pay, and the returns on their remaining investments shrank. There appeared no boundary to the possible contraction of capital and revenue. The income tax had vaulted to gigantic heights, the price of nearly everything had climbed, and the snarl of daily existence was now tightened by the imperative of thrift and sacrifice. He concluded that Gladys, the motorcar they had christened in jest, was an extravagance, and let her go for two hundred pounds. His gardener departed for better wages at a miller’s, and finding a replacement proved so vexing that the grounds fell into a shabby, disheartening neglect. He was forced to abandon his frequent journeys to London. Statesminster had to be postponed for the boys; for the present, at least, they would attend Brinsmead as day pupils. At every turn he collided with this uncongenial reckoning of means. For years he had moved lightly, lived with a measure of indulgence. It was maddeningly bitter to have one’s large sorrows for country and son and creed crossed and tangled by these petty vexations of the extra shilling and the inopportune account.
source_text: Mr. Britling had ceased to write about the war at all. All his later writings about it had been abandoned unfinished. He could not imagine them counting, affecting any one, producing any effect. Indeed he was writing now very intermittently. His contributions to _The Times_ had fallen away. He was perpetually thinking now about the war, about life and death, about the religious problems that had seemed so remote in the days of the peace; but none of his thinking would become clear and definite enough for writing. All the clear stars of his mind were hidden by the stormy clouds of excitement that the daily newspaper perpetually renewed and by the daily developments of life. And just as his professional income shrank before his mental confusion and impotence, the private income that came from his and his wife's investments became uncertain. She had had two thousand pounds in the Constantinople loan, seven hundred in debentures of the Ottoman railway; he had held similar sums in two Hungarian and one Bulgarian loan, in a linoleum factory at Rouen and in a Swiss Hotel company. All these stopped payments, and the dividends from their other investments shrank. There seemed no limit set to the possibilities of shrinkage of capital and income. Income tax had leapt to colossal dimensions, the cost of most things had risen, and the tangle of life was now increased by the need for retrenchments and economies. He decided that Gladys, the facetiously named automobile, was a luxury, and sold her for a couple of hundred pounds. He lost his gardener, who had gone to higher priced work with a miller, and he had great trouble to replace him, so that the garden became disagreeably unkempt and unsatisfactory. He had to give up his frequent trips to London. He was obliged to defer Statesminster for the boys. For a time at any rate they must go as day boys to Brinsmead. At every point he met this uncongenial consideration of ways and means. For years now he had gone easy, lived with a certain self-indulgence. It was extraordinarily vexatious to have one's greater troubles for one's country and one's son and one's faith crossed and complicated by these little troubles of the extra sixpence and the untimely bill.

text: The missive’s contents seeped into the amber pool of lamplight; it demanded a reply, and the ramifications of that reply coursed through Mr. Aldridge’s mind, banishing any detached literary work. He pictured the elderly couple far off in Westphalia—he suspected, though he could not be certain, that Friedrich had been their sole child—and the genial, bespectacled form that had now dwindled into a shattered, rotting remnant in a captive’s meagre trench.
source_text: The substance of this letter invaded the orange circle of the lamp; it would have to be answered, and the potentialities of the answer were running through Mr. Britling's brain to the exclusion of any impersonal composition. He thought of the old parents away there in Pomerania--he believed but he was not quite sure, that Heinrich had been an only son--and of the pleasant spectacled figure that had now become a broken and decaying thing in a prisoner's shallow grave....

text: Now and then it was only young Heinrich who held back Mr. Britling from the conviction that Germany and all its folk were at heart a wicked, hypocritical race of plunderers. The boy turned into a kind of spokesman for his nation in the quiet court of Mr. Britling’s thoughts. (Perched upon his shoulder rested a squirrel, ridiculously spoiled.) His fresh, pink, earnest face, carefully setting his spectacles, murmuring “If you would,” would step in and plead for a stay of verdict.
source_text: At times young Heinrich alone stood between Mr. Britling and the belief that Germany and the whole German race was essentially wicked, essentially a canting robber nation. Young Heinrich became a sort of advocate for his people before the tribunal of Mr. Britling's mind. (And on his shoulder sat an absurdly pampered squirrel.) s fresh, pink, sedulous face, very earnest, adjusting his glasses, saying "Please," intervened and insisted upon an arrest of judgment....

text: While Mr. Britling groped for his duty in the tedium of a special constable’s round, Mrs. Britling flung herself into a flurry of classes, determined to earn her place in the Red Cross. Then, early in October, came the great German thrust toward Antwerp and the sea—a drive plainly aimed at Calais—and before it fled a tide of Flemish refugees. All ranks poured out of Antwerp into Holland and England, and a vast emptying of Flanders and the Pas de Calais followed. This human flood washed into the eastern and southern counties, especially London, where hastily cobbled relief bodies funnelled it out to local committees. Each committee took its quota, found and furnished empty houses for the destitute, and guided those with money into decent lodgings. The Matching’s Easy committee discovered it had beds for sixty but only a mixed bag of thirty souls to mind—part of the cargo of a rakish little steamer from Ostend. Among them were two Flemish peasant families; the rest were mostly middle-class fugitives from Antwerp. They were conveyed from the station to the Tithe barn at Claverings and there apportioned, under Lady Homartyn’s eye and her agent’s, to households ready to receive them. A kind of rivalry sprang up among the would-be hosts; everyone relished the chance to “do something” and was eager to show the Belgians what England felt for their plucky little land. Mr. Britling was proud to usher a Mr. Van der Pant—a trim, bearded man in a black frock-coat, black bowler, and knitted muffler, burdened with a large rucksack and a conspicuously foreign bicycle—into the comforts of Dower House. Mr. Van der Pant had slipped out of Antwerp at the final moment, had caught a nasty cold, and, it seemed, mislaid his wife and family along the way; he had a great deal to tell Mr. Britling, and in his eagerness did not immediately notice that while Mr. Britling knew French respectably, he did not know it fast.
source_text: While Mr. Britling was trying to find his duty in the routine of a special constable, Mrs. Britling set to work with great energy to attend various classes and qualify herself for Red Cross work. And early in October came the great drive of the Germans towards Antwerp and the sea, the great drive that was apparently designed to reach Calais, and which swept before it multitudes of Flemish refugees. There was an exodus of all classes from Antwerp into Holland and England, and then a huge process of depopulation in Flanders and the Pas de Calais. This flood came to the eastern and southern parts of England and particularly to London, and there hastily improvised organisations distributed it to a number of local committees, each of which took a share of the refugees, hired and furnished unoccupied houses for the use of the penniless, and assisted those who had means into comfortable quarters. The Matching's Easy committee found itself with accommodation for sixty people, and with a miscellaneous bag of thirty individuals entrusted to its care, who had been part of the load of a little pirate steam-boat from Ostend. There were two Flemish peasant families, and the rest were more or less middle-class refugees from Antwerp. They were brought from the station to the Tithe barn at Claverings, and there distributed, under the personal supervision of Lady Homartyn and her agent, among those who were prepared for their entertainment. There was something like competition among the would-be hosts; everybody was glad of the chance of "doing something," and anxious to show these Belgians what England thought of their plucky little country. Mr. Britling was proud to lead off a Mr. Van der Pant, a neat little bearded man in a black tail-coat, a black bowler hat, and a knitted muffler, with a large rucksack and a conspicuously foreign-looking bicycle, to the hospitalities of Dower House. Mr. Van der Pant had escaped from Antwerp at the eleventh hour, he had caught a severe cold and, it would seem, lost his wife and family in the process; he had much to tell Mr. Britling, and in his zeal to tell it he did not at once discover that though Mr. Britling knew French quite well he did not know it very rapidly.

text: Letty stood inside the cottage, straining to catch and make sense of matters far too tangled for her feeling-flooded thoughts. Mr. Direck was attempting to clarify something about a telegram that had arrived late, just after she’d left. In truth, Mr. Direck was attempting to clarify a great deal. But what could any explanation truly signify when Teddy was right there, with only an unfamiliar beard and a bandaged arm standing between him and her? At first, she clung to a foolish certainty that these two oddities would soon be stripped away too, so that Teddy would revert entirely to the Teddy he had always been.
source_text: Letty was in the cottage struggling to hear and understand things too complicated for her emotion-crowded mind. There was something that Mr. Direck was trying to explain about a delayed telegram that had come soon after she had gone out. There was much indeed that Mr. Direck was trying to explain. What did any explanation really matter when you had Teddy, with nothing but a strange beard and a bandaged arm between him and yourself? She had an absurd persuasion at first that those two strangenesses would also presently be set aside, so that Teddy would become just exactly what Teddy had always been.

text: All the time Mr. Britling had been lost in these mental massacres, pouring out the grief and fury that had pooled inside him, his reason had hovered detached above the turmoil—like the sun poised beyond a storm, like a calm nurse watching over a child's wild tantrums with patient stillness. And through it all, that reason had been holding its ground, quietly and unwaveringly, without outcry or declaration, insisting that the men who had brought about this moment were not fiends, no more fiends than Mr. Britling himself was a fiend, but flawed human beings of the same kind as he—stubborn, obtuse, tangled in the same nets of circumstance. "Rage against them, kill them in your heart if you must," reason seemed to say, "but see clearly. This was not the work of demons or fools, but of foolish impulses conspiring together, of clever people yielding weakly, of a crime that belonged to no single person but sprang inevitably from the fecklessness, the blind drives, the muddled thinking of all humanity."
source_text: All the while that Mr. Britling had been indulging in these imaginative slaughterings and spending the tears and hate that had gathered in his heart, his reason had been sitting apart and above the storm, like the sun waiting above thunder, like a wise nurse watching and patient above the wild passions of a child. And all the time his reason had been maintaining silently and firmly, without shouting, without speech, that the men who had made this hour were indeed not devils, were no more devils than Mr. Britling was a devil, but sinful men of like nature with himself, hard, stupid, caught in the same web of circumstance. "Kill them in your passion if you will," said reason, "but understand. This thing was done neither by devils nor fools, but by a conspiracy of foolish motives, by the weak acquiescences of the clever, by a crime that was no man's crime but the natural necessary outcome of the ineffectiveness, the blind motives and muddleheadedness of all mankind."

text: I am not angry, nor am I sunken in despair. I am simply aching with grief over the close of something I had hoped to witness—always—for the whole of my life. I cannot speak for how it stands between most fathers and sons, but I revered Hugh. I discovered rare and delicate qualities in him that I suspect went unseen by others. He was reserved. He could appear ungainly. Yet he possessed an extraordinary refinement. He was a being of the most subtle and instantaneous sensitivities. These are not the tender illusions of a father. It was the truth. Even when he was just days old, he would flinch at any unfamiliar noise. From the very start, he was alive like an Æolian harp. And his hair at birth—he had so much of it—was like the soft down on a bird’s breast. I recall it now with piercing clarity, and how I used to love stroking my hand over it. It was silk, pure spun silk. Before he turned two, he could speak in full sentences. He possessed the most finely tuned ear. He adored long words. And then," he said, his voice thickening with tears, "all that beautiful, intricate architecture—that mind, that new life as nimble as running water, as springy as tempered steel—it is shattered.
source_text: "I'm not angry. I'm not depressed. I'm just bitterly hurt by the ending of something I had hoped to watch--always--all my life," he said. "I don't know how it is between most fathers and sons, but I admired Hugh. I found exquisite things in him. I doubt if other people saw them. He was quiet. He seemed clumsy. But he had an extraordinary fineness. He was a creature of the most delicate and rapid responses.... These aren't my fond delusions. It was so.... You know, when he was only a few days old, he would start suddenly at any strange sound. He was alive like an Æolian harp from the very beginning.... And his hair when he was born--he had a lot of hair--was like the down on the breast of a bird. I remember that now very vividly--and how I used to like to pass my hand over it. It was silk, spun silk. Before he was two he could talk--whole sentences. He had the subtlest ear. He loved long words.... And then," he said with tears in his voice, "all this beautiful fine structure, this brain, this fresh life as nimble as water--as elastic as a steel spring, it is destroyed....

text: She sustained a modest legacy by lodging with kin. In Mr. Britling’s early recollections, she emerged as a slight woman of thirty, possessed of a nose that small boys were cautioned never to remark upon. Yet she herself would remark upon it, directing his gaze to its striking similarity to that of the illustrious Duke of Wellington. “He was, I understand,” Cousin Wilshire informed the rapt boy, “a close companion of your great-grandmother’s. In any case, they moved in the same circles. Ever since, this nose has graced our line. He would have been the last to obscure it, but customs shift with the age. ‘Print,’ he declared, ‘and face the consequences.’”
source_text: She eked out a small inheritance by staying with relatives. Mr. Britling's earlier memories presented her as a slender young woman of thirty, with a nose upon which small boys were forbidden to comment. Yet she commented upon it herself, and called his attention to its marked resemblance to that of the great Duke of Wellington. "He was, I am told," said Cousin Wilshire to the attentive youth, "a great friend of your great-grandmother's. At any rate, they were contemporaries. Since then this nose has been in the family. He would have been the last to draw a veil over it, but other times, other manners. 'Publish,' he said, 'and be damned.'"

text: Deep within Mr. Britling’s mind lay a presence that hadn’t always been there—a warm, reassuring undercurrent that made existence and his broader reflections on it smoother and more agreeable than they might have been. It was the awareness of a carefully ordered portfolio, a prudently diversified spread of holdings across government bonds, municipal securities, and railway debentures, totaling altogether a little more than twenty-five thousand pounds—his and Mrs. Britling’s, a shared nest egg. This was, in a manner of speaking, his financial gut. It supported him, kept him functioning and at ease. When everything was sound, he barely sensed it; he simply enjoyed a mild sensation of overall contentment. If some security here or there slipped slightly out of place, he felt a faint unease. Now he was conscious of serious disturbances. It was as though he’d realized he had unwittingly been eating toadstools and couldn’t quite tell if they were the deadliest kind. But perhaps one can push a comparison too far...
source_text: Tucked away in the back of Mr. Britling's consciousness was something that had not always been there, something warm and comforting that made life and his general thoughts about life much easier and pleasanter than they would otherwise have been, the sense of a neatly arranged investment list, a shrewdly and geographically distributed system of holdings in national loans, municipal investments, railway debentures, that had amounted altogether to rather over five-and-twenty thousand pounds; his and Mrs. Britling's, a joint accumulation. This was, so to speak, his economic viscera. It sustained him, and kept him going and comfortable. When all was well he did not feel its existence; he had merely a pleasant sense of general well-being. When here or there a security got a little disarranged he felt a vague discomfort. Now he became aware of grave disorders. It was as if he discovered he had been accidentally eating toadstools, and didn't quite know whether they weren't a highly poisonous sort. But an analogy may be carried too far....

text: The trouble became plain when he tried to shift into high gear—a fumble that killed the engine altogether—and it grew shockingly clearer moments later when Mr. Britling nearly sideswiped a baker’s cart at a corner. “I hit the accelerator,” he explained afterward, “instead of the brake. Happens when you’re new. I missed him by less than a foot.” That measurement was charitable. From then on, Mr. Direck was too worried about diverting his host’s attention to press his conversational gambits. A watchful silence settled over both men, broken only when Mr. Britling let out a sudden cry and the gears gave a tortured howl. “Damn!” shouted Mr. Britling, and “What the _devil_?”
source_text: The thing became evident when he struggled to get into the high gear--an attempt that stopped the engine, and it was even more startlingly so when Mr. Britling narrowly missed a collision with a baker's cart at a corner. "I pressed the accelerator," he explained afterwards, "instead of the brake. One does at first. I missed him by less than a foot." The estimate was a generous one. And after that Mr. Direck became too anxious not to distract his host's thoughts to persist with his conversational openings. An attentive silence came upon both gentlemen that was broken presently by a sudden outcry from Mr. Britling and a great noise of tormented gears. "Damn!" cried Mr. Britling, and "How the _devil_?"

text: She pivoted away as if they’d slipped her mind entirely. For a heartbeat she was motionless, her feet planted like blocks of wood, not stepping with her customary lightness. Then she moved in slow, broad, hesitant strides. She passed outside—like a creature wounded beyond saving yet still upright—into the golden autumn light. The door gaped open behind her.
source_text: She turned her back as though she had forgotten them. She stood for a moment as though her feet were wooden, not putting her feet as she usually put her feet. She took slow, wide, unsure steps. She went out--like something that is mortally injured and still walks--into the autumnal sunshine. She left the door wide open behind her.

text: But before Mr. Direck could get his bearings in this fresh turn of events, Cecily had already slipped past the right honourable gentleman with the same baffling smoothness she’d shown when she swept by Mr. Direck, and was now descending upon the motley Landwehr troops who made up the rearguard of Mr. Direck’s own side.
source_text: But before Mr. Direck could adjust his mind to this new phase of affairs, Cecily had passed the right honourable gentleman with the same mysterious ease with which she had flashed by Mr. Direck, and was bearing down upon the miscellaneous Landwehr which formed the "backs" of Mr. Direck's side.

text: Raeburn thought it over and then replied. "Insofar as it's foolish, yes. A great deal of fault will be assigned; a settling of scores is inevitable, and I think we all have a natural tendency to hunt for someone to carry the burden of our collective failures. The Conservative newspapers are fairly corrupt, and there's a powerful streak of pure personal malice—take the attacks on Churchill, for instance. Probably driven by envy. Our 'old families' seem to have grown coarse-spirited without anyone noticing—over the span of a generation or so. They bicker and dodge responsibility and point fingers just like the worst kind of servants—and far too much still rests in their control. Matters are being botched, there's no denying it—not catastrophically, but still quite gravely. And the government—it was fallible before the war, and we haven't exactly introduced any saints since. There's confusion. There's distrust. You can never tell which newspaper office Lloyd George will be connected to next. He's sincere and devoted to his country and tireless, but he's terrified of old biddies and class scheming. He doesn't know where to find his support. He has all the fear of a Labour member of a knife in the back. There's also a failure of courage when it comes to removing high-ranking officers—who have connections."
source_text: Raeburn, after reflection, decided to answer. "As far as it is stupid, yes. There's a lot of blame coming; there's bound to be a day of reckoning, and I suppose we've all got an instinctive disposition to find a scapegoat for our common sins. The Tory press is pretty rotten, and there's a strong element of mere personal spite--in the Churchill attacks for example. Personal jealousy probably. Our 'old families' seem to have got vulgar-spirited imperceptibly--in a generation or so. They quarrel and shirk and lay blame exactly as bad servants do--and things are still far too much in their hands. Things are getting muffed, there can be no doubt about that--not fatally, but still rather seriously. And the government--it was human before the war, and we've added no archangels. There's muddle. There's mutual suspicion. You never know what newspaper office Lloyd George won't be in touch with next. He's honest and patriotic and energetic, but he's mortally afraid of old women and class intrigues. He doesn't know where to get his backing. He's got all a labour member's terror of the dagger at his back. There's a lack of nerve, too, in getting rid of prominent officers--who have friends."

text: “Good God!” he burst out, “if I had struck a child! I might have struck a child!” The notion blazed up with the thought, tried to flee, and was seized. It was typical of Mr. Britling’s midnight fancies to give the child a sharp particularity—a rather plain, slight figure with reddish hair, wide staring eyes, its ribcage crushed in vivid, appalling detail, pinned against the wall, tangled among bricks, only to be drawn out, oh! _hideously_.
source_text: "Good God!" he cried, "if I had hit a child! I might have hit a child!" The hypothesis flashed into being with the thought, tried to escape and was caught. It was characteristic of Mr. Britling's nocturnal imagination that he should individualise this child quite sharply as rather plain and slender, with reddish hair, staring eyes, and its ribs crushed in a vivid and dreadful manner, pinned against the wall, mixed up with some bricks, only to be extracted, oh! _horribly_.

text: Consider the aviators he’d encountered a handful of times at Eastchurch and Salisbury—Frenchmen and Englishmen who handled their machines with a skill no others in the world could match. Such gifts are bred in the bone, a matter of blood and national character. Let the German keep clutching his gasbags. “We’ll best them in the skies,” he murmured. “We’ll best them on the water. We must best them on the water. If we can muster enough men and enough guns, we’ll best them on the land as well… Still—they’ve been readying themselves for years…”
source_text: And what other aviators in the world could fly as the Frenchmen and Englishmen he had met once or twice at Eastchurch and Salisbury could fly? These are things of race and national quality. Let the German cling to his gasbags. "We shall beat them in the air," he whispered. "We shall beat them on the seas. Surely we shall beat them on the seas. If we have men enough and guns enough we shall beat them on land.... Yet--For years they have been preparing...."

text: Weeks had drifted by. November had settled into its heart, and Mr. Britling, swathed snugly in a heavy dressing-gown and pyjamas of dense llama wool, sat at his nocturnal writing table, returning time and again to a composition of absurdly lofty scope, for it bore the heading "On the Wiser Ordering of Human Affairs."
source_text: It was some weeks later. It was now the middle of November, and Mr. Britling, very warmly wrapped in his thick dressing-gown and his thick llama wool pyjamas, was sitting at his night desk, and working ever and again at an essay, an essay of preposterous ambitions, for the title of it was "The Better Government of the World."

text: This is the path you and I must walk, Letty. What other road remains open to us? ... I shall set down no other thought, I shall harbor no other purpose now save that of order and shelter. So that every one of these beloved lost—not a single soul among them—will have drawn the great dawn of tranquility and man’s true commencement a stride nearer, and these savage forces that make grown men sob like frightened children, that shatter radiant spirits into ruin and slay youth in the very instant it stretches forth its unblemished hands to embrace life—these savageries, these horrors of chaos, shall be wiped from the face of the earth forever.
source_text: "That is what you and I must do, Letty. What else is there left for us to do?... I will write of nothing else, I will think of nothing else now but of safety and order. So that all these dear dead--not one of them but will have brought the great days of peace and man's real beginning nearer, and these cruel things that make men whimper like children, that break down bright lives into despair and kill youth at the very moment when it puts out its clean hands to take hold of life--these cruelties, these abominations of confusion, shall cease from the earth forever."

text: She was bewildered when two whole days passed without an answer. The silence stretched because, at that very moment, strange upheavals were coursing through England’s telegraph lines, and her note—entrusted to a boy scout pedaling through the lanes—did not reach Mr. Britling’s doorstep until Monday afternoon. He was away at Claverings, deep in talk about the invasion of Belgium that was dragging Britain irrevocably toward war, and he only broke open the small russet envelope around half-past six. He neglected to note the exact date and hour, yet he saw at once that it was, in its essence, a summons. He was meant, he understood, to hasten over with his restored Gladys and settle this wretched quarrel once and for all in a single vivid, impassioned scene. But his thoughts were so crowded with the war that the errand felt drained of colour, an unwelcome chore. Still, he felt tethered by the shadowy code of illicit love to do his part. He put off leaving until after supper—there was no cause to dread a moonlit drive if he took it slowly—because Hugh burst in with Cissie, clamouring for a game of hockey. Hockey promised a tingling release, a fleet-footed escape from the vast calamity of a war he had always sworn could never come, and he welcomed the interruption with fierce relief.
source_text: She was astonished to get no reply for two days. She got no reply for two days because remarkable things were happening to the telegraph wires of England just then, and her message, in the hands of a boy scout on a bicycle, reached Mr. Britling's house only on Monday afternoon. He was then at Claverings discussing the invasion of Belgium that made Britain's participation in the war inevitable, and he did not open the little red-brown envelope until about half-past six. He failed to mark the date and hours upon it, but he perceived that it was essentially a challenge. He was expected, he saw, to go over at once with his renovated Gladys and end this unfortunate clash forever in one striking and passionate scene. His mind was now so full of the war that he found this the most colourless and unattractive of obligations. But he felt bound by the mysterious code of honour of the illicit love affair to play his part. He postponed his departure until after supper--there was no reason why he should be afraid of motoring by moonlight if he went carefully--because Hugh came in with Cissie demanding a game of hockey. Hockey offered a nervous refreshment, a scampering forgetfulness of the tremendous disaster of this war he had always believed impossible, that nothing else could do, and he was very glad indeed of the irruption....

text: All manner of rumors had reached Mr. Britling concerning the fleets of England, France, and Germany; there had been open quarrels among authorities, and a great deal of hushed conjecture behind closed doors. Britain possessed the weightier hulls and the larger ordnance, yet it was far from certain that she led in science and ingenuity. Were they staking everything on Dreadnoughts as we were, or did they hold hidden stratagems and startling innovations in reserve? Tonight, perhaps, the towering warships were already steaming toward battle.
source_text: All sorts of talk had come to Mr. Britling's ears about the navies of England and France and Germany; there had been public disputes of experts, much whispering and discussion in private. We had the heavier vessels, the bigger guns, but it was not certain that we had the preeminence in science and invention. Were they relying as we were relying on Dreadnoughts, or had they their secrets and surprises for us? To-night, perhaps, the great ships were steaming to conflict....

text: Alistair stopped. “That was the Bedford Mansions affair. I doubt you recall it or followed the accounts back then. He had taken a life… The details aren’t important now, but what I’m getting at is the atmosphere. The feeling of a warm, lamp-lit, settled room full of ordinary, inoffensive people—and then the door swings wide, the constable steps in, and a sharp chill sweeps through. _Murder!_ A young woman who clearly knew the household whispered to me what was unfolding. It felt exactly like a hidden hatch springing open beneath your feet, plunging into some abyss you’d always sensed was there but had never truly credited.”
source_text: Hugh paused. "It was the Bedford Mansions mystery. I don't suppose you remember about it or read about it at the time. He'd killed a man.... It doesn't matter about the particulars anyhow, but what I mean is the effect. The effect of a comfortable well-lit orderly room and the sense of harmless people--and then the door opening and the policeman and the cold draught flowing in. _Murder!_ A girl who seemed to know the people well explained to me in whispers what was happening. It was like the opening of a trap-door going down into some pit you have always known was there, but never really believed in."

text: Mr. Direck settled back in his chair, savoring with quiet contentment the single after-lunch cigar he permitted himself. If he himself felt somewhat tongue-tied, at least the fountain chattered on with lively grace. He followed the conversation in a loose, drifting way—it would have been impossible to track it carefully through all its crevices and bends—while his gaze roamed the garden and returned again and again to the swift tennis-players darting beyond the lawn. Everything was bright and easeful and complete; it was varied and charming without a trace of opulence—one of those modest secrets America had yet to grasp. It didn’t seem made or purchased or costly at all; it seemed, rather, to have come about by happy accident.
source_text: Mr. Direck sat comfortably, and smoked with quiet appreciation the one after-lunch cigar he allowed himself. At any rate, if he himself felt rather word-bound, the fountain was nimble and entertaining. He listened in a general sort of way to the talk, it was quite impossible to follow it thoughtfully throughout all its chinks and turnings, while his eyes wandered about the garden and went ever and again to the flitting tennis-players beyond the green. It was all very gay and comfortable and complete; it was various and delightful without being in the least _opulent_; that was one of the little secrets America had to learn. It didn't look as though it had been made or bought or cost anything, it looked as though it had happened rather luckily....

text: Mr. Carmine foresaw a protracted, grinding conflict; Mr. Britling, by contrast, imagined Russian troops marching into Berlin by May. He even feared they might arrive before the year was out. In his view, the Germans would exhaust themselves hammering against the French and Belgian fronts, never gaining the freedom to wheel eastward against Russia. He felt certain the strength and resolve of both France and Britain had been sorely underestimated. “The Russians, all the while,” he declared, “will keep advancing—slowly, steadily, inexorably…”
source_text: Mr. Carmine thought it would be a long struggle; Mr. Britling thought that the Russians would be in Berlin by the next May. He was afraid they might get there before the end of the year. He thought that the Germans would beat out their strength upon the French and Belgian lines, and never be free to turn upon the Russian at all. He was sure they had underrated the strength and energy of the French and of ourselves. "The Russians meanwhile," he said, "will come on, slowly, steadily, inevitably...."

text: So the great war broke upon Europe and reached a solitary soul in Matching's Easy, just as it reached innumerable thoughtful men in innumerable comfortable households that had barely noticed its approach throughout the long years of its pitiless forging. The known backdrop of existence was swept away, and War revealed itself naked. "I am the Fact," War declared, "and I straddle the road of life. I am the menace of death and obliteration that has ever trodden alongside life from its first breath. There can be no other matter and no further meaning in human existence till you have settled with me."
source_text: In this fashion it was that the great war began in Europe and came to one man in Matching's Easy, as it came to countless intelligent men in countless pleasant homes that had scarcely heeded its coming through all the years of its relentless preparation. The familiar scenery of life was drawn aside, and War stood unveiled. "I am the Fact," said War, "and I stand astride the path of life. I am the threat of death and extinction that has always walked beside life, since life began. There can be nothing else and nothing more in human life until you have reckoned with me."

text: Abruptly the world reshaped itself. I started sobbing. Like an infant. I went on pressing the canteen against his mouth ages after I knew he'd gone. I couldn't bear him to slip out of the whole affair! Heaven knows why I couldn't. I longed for my precious little London gutter-rat to return. Oh! most desperately I longed for him back.
source_text: "And suddenly it was all different. I began to cry. Like a baby. I kept on with the water-bottle at his teeth long after I was convinced he was dead. I didn't want him to be aut of it! God knows how I didn't. I wanted my dear little Cockney cad back. Oh! most frightfully I wanted him back.

text: The Britling household, still mourning their departed Belgian guests, greeted the twenty-five new arrivals with open arms. Their presence restored a sense of purpose. For three days Mrs. Britling—whose kitchen machinery had, predictably, malfunctioned—fed the soldiers so lavishly that their tales of the billet became nearly unbearable to the rest of their battery. The official allowance was ninepence per man, but Mr. Britling, unwilling to profit from his nation’s need, provided not only generous heat and light but endless cigarettes, cards, games, illustrated papers, a cocoa supper featuring modest treats like sprats and jam roly-poly, and countless smaller comforts. The men, who had marched in hungry under two quietly seasoned corporals, repaid Mrs. Britling’s kindness with a string of earnest promises, many of which they honored. They kept silent after half-past ten—barring the occasional sing-song that erupted with sudden fervor—rose and slipped out at five or six without a whisper, and proved almost excessively eager to help with washing-up and tidying.
source_text: The whole Britling family, who were lamenting the loss of their Belgians, welcomed the coming of the twenty-five with great enthusiasm. It made them feel that they were doing something useful once more. For three days Mrs. Britling had to feed her new lodgers--the kitchen motors had as usual gone astray--and she did so in a style that made their boastings about their billet almost insufferable to the rest of their battery. The billeting allowance at that time was ninepence a head, and Mr. Britling, ashamed of making a profit out of his country, supplied not only generous firing and lighting, but unlimited cigarettes, cards and games, illustrated newspapers, a cocoa supper with such little surprises as sprats and jam roly-poly, and a number of more incidental comforts. The men arrived fasting under the command of two very sage middle-aged corporals, and responded to Mrs. Britling's hospitalities by a number of good resolutions, many of which they kept. They never made noises after half-past ten, or at least only now and then when a singsong broke out with unusual violence; they got up and went out at five or six in the morning without a sound; they were almost inconveniently helpful with washing-up and tidying round.

text: This account traces the dawning and deepening awareness of the Great War as it unfolded for a small circle of people in Essex, and above all as it took shape within one individual mind. At the outset, the war presented itself to them all as a grand spectacle—a distant, dramatic, international affair, a performance played out in headlines, something that belonged to a historic turning point rather than to private life. Only gradually did the conflict and its repercussions seep into the everyday fabric of English existence. If one were to render this story in drawings or tableaux, the focal figure would be Mr. Britling: sometimes seated at his desk day or night, drafting first his pamphlet “And Now War Ends” and later other writings; sometimes strolling through his garden or across Claverings park, or journeying back and forth to London, where in his club he watches the ticker or in his hallway scans the newspaper—while all the time ideas and impressions keep gathering, swelling, proliferating more and more richly in his mind, sorting themselves, sparking off one another, crystallising into generalisations and settled convictions.
source_text: This story is essentially the history of the opening and of the realisation of the Great War as it happened to one small group of people in Essex, and more particularly as it happened to one human brain. It came at first to all these people in a spectacular manner, as a thing happening dramatically and internationally, as a show, as something in the newspapers, something in the character of an historical epoch rather than a personal experience; only by slow degrees did it and its consequences invade the common texture of English life. If this story could be represented by sketches or pictures the central figure would be Mr. Britling, now sitting at his desk by day or by night and writing first at his tract "And Now War Ends" and then at other things, now walking about his garden or in Claverings park or going to and fro in London, in his club reading the ticker or in his hall reading the newspaper, with ideas and impressions continually clustering, expanding, developing more and more abundantly in his mind, arranging themselves, reacting upon one another, building themselves into generalisations and conclusions....

text: Consider this: if he truly is gone, then Cruelty rules the world, and I am owed a debt for his death... Someone must settle that debt... I will bide my time for six months after the war, my love, and then I shall travel to Germany and learn its streets and corners. And I will kill a German. Not an ordinary German, but one of the guilty breed. An offering. It should be, for example, fairly simple to slay one of the Crown Prince's little ones, or a child of the Bavarian line. I shall choose German children. I will lay them upon Teddy's altar. It cannot be so hard to find those who are plainly accountable—the architects of the poison gas, say—and end them, or to strike at those they cherish. Or those they need... Women can manage such tasks far more easily than men...
source_text: "You see, if he _is_ dead, then Cruelty is the Law, and some one must pay me for his death.... Some one must pay me.... I shall wait for six months after the war, dear, and then I shall go off to Germany and learn my way about there. And I will murder some German. Not just a common German, but a German who belongs to the guilty kind. A sacrifice. It ought, for instance, to be comparatively easy to kill some of the children of the Crown Prince or some of the Bavarian princes. I shall prefer German children. I shall sacrifice them to Teddy. It ought not to be difficult to find people who can be made directly responsible, the people who invented the poison gas, for instance, and kill them, or to kill people who are dear to them. Or necessary to them.... Women can do that so much more easily than men....

text: A peculiar distinction exists," he remarked. "It eludes precise description, yet broadly I might frame it thus: a company like this, were it assembled in America, would be etched with sharper contours, its skeleton laid bare, every feature rendered more pronounced. And to seize upon a single instance: in America, amid such a circle, there would inevitably circulate a handful of jests, as it were—running quips and ongoing mockeries, persisting from day to day and week to week. There would be witticisms at the expense of your prose and your sway, and pleasantries aimed at Miss Corner's esoteric studies. You understand, in America we accord far greater weight to individual character. Here, I perceive, people are scarcely addressed regarding their private natures at all, and many appear neither conscious of nor troubled by whatever private natures they possess.
source_text: "There's a curious sort of difference," he was saying. "It is difficult to define, but on the whole I might express it by saying that such a gathering as this if it was in America would be drawn with harder lines, would show its bones more and have everything more emphatic. And just to take one illustrative point: in America in such a gathering as this there would be bound to be several jokes going on as it were, running jokes and running criticisms, from day to day and from week to week.... There would be jokes about your writing and your influence and jokes about Miss Corner's advanced reading.... You see, in America we pay much more attention to personal character. Here people, I notice, are not talked to about their personal characters at all, and many of them do not seem to be aware and do not seem to mind what personal characters they have....

text: The sentry had been posted at a railway cutting, and a locomotive had struck him down as he darted across the tracks behind a passing freight. Thus did the Sarajevo blast claim its first life on English soil. Lieutenant Ashworth discovered the corpse. He came upon it under a veiled and fitful moon, nearly stumbling headlong over the remains, and the shock that seized him was profoundly sickening. He had been obliged to haul the body—hideously torn apart—clear of the iron road, the nearly severed neck lolling and twisting grotesquely in the shifting half-light, and only later did he notice that his cuffs were drenched in gore. He had not perceived it in the moment, and when the realisation struck him he had retched. The whole affair had seemed to him more abominable and hellish than any fever-dream, yet he had managed to act with enough grim competence to steady his men. Since that night, not a single hour of unbroken slumber had visited him.
source_text: The man had been guarding a tunnel, and he had been knocked down by one train when crossing the line behind another. So it was that the bomb of Sarajevo killed its first victim in Essex. Captain Carmine had found the body. He had found the body in a cloudy moonlight; he had almost fallen over it; and his sensations and emotions had been eminently disagreeable. He had had to drag the body--it was very dreadfully mangled--off the permanent way, the damaged, almost severed head had twisted about very horribly in the uncertain light, and afterwards he had found his sleeves saturated with blood. He had not noted this at the time, and when he had discovered it he had been sick. He had thought the whole thing more horrible and hateful than any nightmare, but he had succeeded in behaving with a sufficient practicality to set an example to his men. Since this had happened he had not had an hour of dreamless sleep.

text: Mr. Van der Pant showed no fury toward the Germans. Yet he plainly saw them as creatures marked for death. He did not condemn their actions; he simply recounted them. They were a dark misfortune that had befallen Belgium and the world. They must be wiped out. He left Mr. Britling with the vivid sense that in every cellar across Brussels and Antwerp, blades were being honed for the moment of inevitable withdrawal, a determination to erase the occupier so profound it lay beyond revenge… And the man was utterly unbroken. Mr. Britling noticed the label on his usual dinner wine with a touch of unease. "Would you mind," he asked, "drinking a German wine? This is a Berncasteler from the Moselle." Mr. Van der Pant paused. "But it is a fine wine," he said. "After the peace, it will be Belgian… Yes, if we are to be shielded in the future from a war like this, our borders must reach all the way to the Rhine."
source_text: Mr. Van der Pant did not seem to be angry with the Germans. But he manifestly regarded them as people to be killed. He denounced nothing that they had done; he related. They were just an evil accident that had happened to Belgium and mankind. They had to be destroyed. He gave Mr. Britling an extraordinary persuasion that knives were being sharpened in every cellar in Brussels and Antwerp against the day of inevitable retreat, of a resolution to exterminate the invader that was far too deep to be vindictive.... And the man was most amazingly unconquered. Mr. Britling perceived the label on his habitual dinner wine with a slight embarrassment. "Do you care," he asked, "to drink a German wine? This is Berncasteler from the Moselle." Mr. Van der Pant reflected. "But it is a good wine," he said. "After the peace it will be Belgian.... Yes, if we are to be safe in the future from such a war as this, we must have our boundaries right up to the Rhine."

text: The papers had come late that morning, and he’d been reading them, going back over them, and brooding in fits and starts until his family finally made him join the celebrations. They told him that if nothing else, he absolutely had to see Aunt Wilshire’s uncanny talent at the coconut shy—she could outdo anyone. You couldn’t miss a spectacle like that. The headlines shouted: “The Great Powers Clash; Germany Invades France; Russia Invades Germany; 100,000 Germans Enter Luxemburg; Will England Stay Out? Fifty-Million Loan to Be Floated.” And Germany hadn’t just broken the Treaty of London—she’d also commandeered a British vessel in the Kiel Canal.… The merry-go-rounds spun busily, piping their windy tunes, and the shooting gallery kept up its steady pop and clatter as bottles shattered, while the voices of young men and women calling out to the crowd to try their luck rang crisp and bright. Teddy, Letty, Cissie, and Hugh were getting alarmingly good at the dart game and were determined to assemble an entire tea set for the Teddy cottage from their prizes. Near the entrance to the fenced-off section of the park set aside for the fair stood a score of motorcars and a scattering of traps and gigs; the little Britling boys had met some children from the Claverings House nursery and were showing off their poise and prowess on the merry-go-round ostriches. And less than four hundred miles away, along a front stretching from Nancy to Liège, more than a million and a quarter grey-uniformed soldiers—the largest and most superbly outfitted army the world had ever witnessed—were surging westward to capture Paris, seize and cripple France, take the Channel ports, land in England, and crown the German Empire ruler of the globe. Their gear was a wonder of planning and scientific precision, from the motorised kitchens rumbling behind them to the telescopic sights on the marksmen’s rifles, the countless infantry machine guns, the stockpiles of entrenching tools, the arrangements already laid in the occupied territory.…
source_text: The morning papers had arrived late, and he had been reading them and re-reading them and musing over them intermittently until his family had insisted upon his coming out to the festivities. They said that if for no other reason he must come to witness Aunt Wilshire's extraordinary skill at the cocoanut shy. She could beat everybody. Well, one must not miss a thing like that. The headlines proclaimed, "The Great Powers at War; France Invaded by Germany; Germany invaded by Russia; 100,000 Germans march into Luxemburg; Can England Abstain? Fifty Million Loan to be Issued." And Germany had not only violated the Treaty of London but she had seized a British ship in the Kiel Canal.... The roundabouts were very busy and windily melodious, and the shooting gallery kept popping and jingling as people shot and broke bottles, and the voices of the young men and women inviting the crowd to try their luck at this and that rang loud and clear. Teddy and Letty and Cissie and Hugh were developing a quite disconcerting skill at the dart-throwing, and were bent upon compiling a complete tea-set for the Teddy cottage out of their winnings. There was a score of automobiles and a number of traps and gigs about the entrance to the portion of the park that had been railed off for the festival, the small Britling boys had met some nursery visitors from Claverings House and were busy displaying skill and calm upon the roundabout ostriches, and less than four hundred miles away with a front that reached from Nancy to Liège more than a million and a quarter of grey-clad men, the greatest and best-equipped host the world had ever seen, were pouring westward to take Paris, grip and paralyse France, seize the Channel ports, invade England, and make the German Empire the master-state of the earth. Their equipment was a marvel of foresight and scientific organisation, from the motor kitchens that rumbled in their wake to the telescopic sights of the sharp-shooters, the innumerable machine-guns of the infantry, the supply of entrenching material, the preparations already made in the invaded country....

text: Had someone prophesied that he would shortly be dazed for several minutes, slashed across the forehead and cheeks, suffer a dislocated wrist-bone, and emerge from the ordeal oddly content and buoyant, he would have scoffed at the forecast. Yet there he was, stretched rigidly upon his back, his injured wrist strapped tightly against his ribs, beaming into the blackness with a radiance keener than the smile he had bestowed upon the Essex countryside two mornings earlier. Pain, in truth, stings or vexes, but by itself it does not render a soundly built man wretched. The dread of agony, the certitude of harm may breed despair; the actual event summons our defiance. No one seeks a fractured bone or a fragile joint, yet scarcely anyone sinks into gloom upon acquiring one. A man can be far more dejected by smoking a hundred cigarettes over three days or by forfeiting one per cent of his fortune.
source_text: If any one had told him he was going to be stunned for five or six minutes, cut about the brow and face and have a bone in his wrist put out, and that as a consequence he would find himself pleased and exhilarated, he would have treated the prophecy with ridicule; but here he was lying stiffly on his back with his wrist bandaged to his side and smiling into the darkness even more brightly than he had smiled at the Essex landscape two days before. The fact is pain hurts or irritates, but in itself it does not make a healthily constituted man miserable. The expectation of pain, the certainty of injury may make one hopeless enough, the reality rouses our resistance. Nobody wants a broken bone or a delicate wrist, but very few people are very much depressed by getting one. People can be much more depressed by smoking a hundred cigarettes in three days or losing one per cent. of their capital.

text: After Mr. Direck left, international affairs surged forward with startling speed—almost as if his measured American tempo had been holding events in check. Before his postcard from Rotterdam reached the Dower House, Austria had delivered an ultimatum to Serbia, and before Cecily had opened the letter he sent from Cologne—penned in that oddly unformed script the stenographer and typewriter are making a native American trait—Russia was mobilizing, and the immense curtain of a European war had risen, revealing the previous week’s concerns as mere threadwork on its hem. This unveiled reality pressed so hard that even the shootings in Dublin after the Howth gun-running shrank to insignificance. Britling’s mind ceased its restless drifting and fixed itself with gathering intensity on the racing storm-clouds that materialized from nowhere to blacken his whole sky. He watched, astonished and unwilling to believe, this contradiction of every confident confession he had ever made about German sanity and pacifism; he watched with all that was detached in his nature, while his personal life narrowed into a deeper, tighter channel as his intellect withdrew from it.
source_text: After the departure of Mr. Direck things international began to move forward with great rapidity. It was exactly as if his American deliberation had hitherto kept things waiting. Before his postcard from Rotterdam reached the Dower House Austria had sent an ultimatum to Serbia, and before Cecily had got the letter he wrote her from Cologne, a letter in that curiously unformed handwriting the stenographer and the typewriter are making an American characteristic, Russia was mobilising, and the vast prospect of a European war had opened like the rolling up of a curtain on which the interests of the former week had been but a trivial embroidery. So insistent was this reality that revealed itself that even the shooting of the Dublin people after the gun-running of Howth was dwarfed to unimportance. The mind of Mr. Britling came round from its restless wanderings to a more and more intent contemplation of the hurrying storm-clouds that swept out of nothingness to blacken all his sky. He watched it, he watched amazed and incredulous, he watched this contradiction of all his reiterated confessions of faith in German sanity and pacifism, he watched it with all that was impersonal in his being, and meanwhile his personal life ran in a continually deeper and narrower channel as his intelligence was withdrawn from it.

text: She devoured memoirs from the 1700s, and shared a few with him. Her cast of mind ran entirely along the lines of Rousseau’s and Voltaire’s friendships, and with a light, nimble charm she guided him down the enticing path of a meeting of minds. She first visited Matching’s Easy, where she was warm, lively, keenly engaged, and a striking foil to Mrs. Britling; afterward they rendezvoused in London and, with a spirited feeling of escapade, spent a day together at Richmond; later he brought some work to her home and lingered there.
source_text: She was a great reader of eighteenth century memoirs, and some she conveyed to him. Her mental quality was all in the vein of the friendships of Rousseau and Voltaire, and pleasantly and trippingly she led him along the primrose path of an intellectual liaison. She came first to Matching's Easy, where she was sweet and bright and vividly interested and a great contrast to Mrs. Britling, and then he and she met in London, and went off together with a fine sense of adventure for a day at Richmond, and then he took some work with him to her house and stayed there....

text: The grounds of Claverings—above all the vast rock garden, the ornamental pool, and the long perennial borders—were strangely thronged with unfamiliar guests and timid young pairs. Mr. Britling was obliged to seek direction at the manor, and under the steward’s guidance discovered Lady Homartyn secluded in the brick-walled Dutch garden beyond the dairy. She had just distributed the floral prizes and was reclining in a canvas chair while an unmarried cousin oversaw the tea table. Mrs. Britling had already withdrawn from the earlier festivities and was seated beside the tea service. Lady Meade and a small party had driven over from Hartleytree to lend support, and Manning had arrived bearing his staggering corroboration of all that the morning gazettes had intimated.
source_text: The Claverings gardens, and particularly the great rockery, the lily pond, and the herbaceous borders, were unusually populous with unaccustomed visitors and shy young couples. Mr. Britling had to go to the house for instructions, and guided by the under-butler found Lady Homartyn hiding away in the walled Dutch garden behind the dairy. She had been giving away the prizes of the flower-show, and she was resting in a deck chair while a spinster relation presided over the tea. Mrs. Britling had fled the outer festival earlier, and was sitting by the tea-things. Lady Meade and two or three visitors had motored out from Hartleytree to assist, and Manning had come in with his tremendous confirmation of all that the morning papers had foreshadowed.

text: He refused to accept the hints of Brussels’ collapse and the German advance into Dinant. The first genuine blow to his overblown hopes for an Allied triumph arrived when Mr. Direck unexpectedly turned up at Matching’s Easy, visibly shocked and distressed. He sent a wire from the Strand office: “On my way to explain the situation,” and showed up right after his message.
source_text: He resisted the intimations of the fall of Brussels and the appearance of the Germans at Dinant. The first real check to his excessive anticipations of victory for the Allies came with the sudden reappearance of Mr. Direck in a state of astonishment and dismay at Matching's Easy. He wired from the Strand office, "Coming to tell you about things," and arrived on the heels of his telegram.

text: Monday fell upon the First Monday in August, a bank holiday hallowed by immemorial rite to the Matching's Easy Flower Show within the precincts of Claverings Park. That day would sear itself into Mr. Britling’s recollection with a stark brilliance, akin to the glare that sometimes flares along the hem of a gathering storm. There stood pavilions housing the displays, and a marquee devoted to "Popular Refreshments"; a resplendent gold-and-yellow steam carousel whirled with motorcars and chargers, while another, sheathed in green and silver, bore ostriches and lions that rose and fell in wondrous undulation—each machine possessed a steam-driven organ. Cocoanut shies abounded, together with an array of artful shooting, dart-hurling, and quoit-tossing booths, every one proffering a dazzling hoard of crockery, timepieces, gilt ornaments, and similar trophies. A race of gas-balloons was held, each bearing a postcard that entreated the discoverer to record where it alighted; for a shilling one might purchase a balloon and stand to gain sundry imposing yet awkward prizes should it travel far enough—fish carvers, a silver-mounted walking-stick, a bog-oak cabinet for gramophone records, and objects of that ilk. And through a private gate, for the sum of sixpence, one could enter the gardens of Claverings, that sixpence to be matched by Lady Homartyn and consecrated the following winter to the Matching's Easy coal fund. Mr. Britling traversed the whole fête with his sons, at length bestowing upon each a shilling and his paternal goodwill, then tendered his sixpence at the garden gate and bent his steps, as he had pledged, toward tea with Lady Homartyn.
source_text: Monday was a public holiday, the First Monday in August, and the day consecrated by long-established custom to the Matching's Easy Flower Show in Claverings Park. The day was to live in Mr. Britling's memory with a harsh brightness like the brightness of that sunshine one sees at times at the edge of a thunderstorm. There were tents with the exhibits, and a tent for "Popular Refreshments," there was a gorgeous gold and yellow steam roundabout with motor-cars and horses, and another in green and silver with wonderfully undulating ostriches and lions, and each had an organ that went by steam; there were cocoanut shies and many ingenious prize-giving shooting and dart-throwing and ring-throwing stalls, each displaying a marvellous array of crockery, clocks, metal ornaments, and suchlike rewards. There was a race of gas balloons, each with a postcard attached to it begging the finder to say where it descended, and you could get a balloon for a shilling and have a chance of winning various impressive and embarrassing prizes if your balloon went far enough--fish carvers, a silver-handled walking-stick, a bog-oak gramophone-record cabinet, and things like that. And by a special gate one could go for sixpence into the Claverings gardens, and the sixpence would be doubled by Lady Homartyn and devoted next winter to the Matching's Easy coal club. And Mr. Britling went through all the shows with his boys, and finally left them with a shilling each and his blessing and paid his sixpence for the gardens and made his way as he had promised, to have tea with Lady Homartyn.

text: Mr. Direck had consumed a considerable volume of that printed assertiveness which belonged to Mr. Britling; he deemed it both amusing and provocative matter, and it was with unfeigned eagerness that he had journeyed to meet the originator in the flesh. Throughout his Atlantic crossing and in the days that followed, he had staged their encounter in shifting tones, yet always upon the assumption that Mr. Britling would prove a tall, subdued, contemplative figure, a man disposed to arrange himself in attentive tiers like a civic assembly and listen. Accordingly, Mr. Direck had fashioned a store of genial and engaging preludes, and now he sensed that the hour had arrived for one of those various plain but resonant declarations. What none of these anticipations had allowed for, however, was either the unscripted energies of Mr. Britling or the stationmaster of Matching’s Easy. Unmindful of any conversational obligation hanging between Mr. Direck and Mr. Britling, this functionary now assumed custody of Mr. Direck’s valise, and, falling into step beside the pair as they moved toward the platform gate, continued what was plainly a suspended disquisition on sweet peas, originally directed at Mr. Britling.
source_text: Mr. Direck had read a very great deal of all this expressed opiniativeness of Mr. Britling: he found it entertaining and stimulating stuff, and it was with genuine enthusiasm that he had come over to encounter the man himself. On his way across the Atlantic and during the intervening days, he had rehearsed this meeting in varying keys, but always on the supposition that Mr. Britling was a large, quiet, thoughtful sort of man, a man who would, as it were, sit in attentive rows like a public meeting and listen. So Mr. Direck had prepared quite a number of pleasant and attractive openings, and now he felt was the moment for some one of these various simple, memorable utterances. But in none of these forecasts had he reckoned with either the spontaneous activities of Mr. Britling or with the station-master of Matching's Easy. Oblivious of any conversational necessities between Mr. Direck and Mr. Britling, this official now took charge of Mr. Direck's grip-sack, and, falling into line with the two gentlemen as they walked towards the exit gate, resumed what was evidently an interrupted discourse upon sweet peas, originally addressed to Mr. Britling.

text: None of what they claim about the war," she said, "holds the least importance. It all comes down to a ruined carpet and nothing else on earth—a madman and a ruined carpet. If anyone had exercised a shred of common sense, this war would never have begun. The facts were entirely clear. He was a frail child, hard to raise and prone to screaming tantrums. As a result, he was never thwarted, permitted to do whatever he pleased. No one but his grandmother held any sway over him. And she stopped him from destroying that carpet as thoroughly as he wanted. The story is widely known. It happened at Windsor—when he was eight. From that moment on, he had only one obsession: war with England....
source_text: "None of these things they are saying about the war," she said, "really matter in the slightest degree. It is all about a spoilt carpet and nothing else in the world--a madman and a spoilt carpet. If people had paid the slightest attention to common sense none of this war would have happened. The thing was perfectly well known. He was a delicate child, difficult to rear and given to screaming fits. Consequently he was never crossed, allowed to do everything. Nobody but his grandmother had the slightest influence with him. And she prevented him spoiling this carpet as completely as he wished to do. The story is perfectly well known. It was at Windsor--at the age of eight. After that he had but one thought: war with England....

text: Politically regarded, the British Empire is an ungainly patchwork of haphazard occurrences, no more a cause for pride than the contour of a chipped stone or the silhouette of a turnip. To the ordinary English multitude, India and Egypt and the whole apparatus of that dominion signify less than a cipher; our commerce is a riddle they cannot read, our imperial profits a store they never touch. Britain has been four democracies tangled in the mesh of an immense yet careless imperialism; the common man here drifts in political bewilderment from birth to burial. Yet there exists a great people here, just as a great people dwells in Russia, a people with its own soul and temper, a people of indomitable generosity and a strange genius, still groping toward purpose and utterance. We have set out upon that same bold experiment which France and America and Switzerland and China pursue, the experiment of democracy. It is the newest mode of human fellowship, and we remain only half roused to its demands and its prerequisites. For it is vain to pretend that the miniature city democracies of antiquity were akin to the vast ventures in practical republicanism that humanity attempts today. This era of the democratic republics whose dawn is breaking is a fresh era. It has not yet endured a century, not a meagre hundred years…. All new things are frail things; a rodent can slay a human infant with ease; the grander the destiny, the feebler the immediate self-defence may prove. And to my mind your finished and flawless imperialism, governed by Germans for Germans, is in its reach and vision a more obsolete and narrower and baser thing than these sprawling, emergent giant democracies of the West that struggle so chaotically against it….
source_text: _Politically the British Empire is a clumsy collection of strange accidents. It is a thing as little to be proud of as the outline of a flint or the shape of a potato. For the mass of English people India and Egypt and all that side of our system mean less than nothing; our trade is something they do not understand, our imperial wealth something they do not share. Britain has been a group of four democracies caught in the net of a vast yet casual imperialism; the common man here is in a state of political perplexity from the cradle to the grave. None the less there is a great people here even as there is a great people in Russia, a people with a soul and character of its own, a people of unconquerable kindliness and with a peculiar genius, which still struggle towards will and expression. We have been beginning that same great experiment that France and America and Switzerland and China are making, the experiment of democracy. It is the newest form of human association, and we are still but half awake to its needs and necessary conditions. For it is idle to pretend that the little city democracies of ancient times were comparable to the great essays in practical republicanism that mankind is making to-day. This age of the democratic republics that dawn is a new age. It has not yet lasted for a century, not for a paltry hundred years.... All new things are weak things; a rat can kill a man-child with ease; the greater the destiny, the weaker the immediate self-protection may be. And to me it seems that your complete and perfect imperialism, ruled by Germans for Germans, is in its scope and outlook a more antiquated and smaller and less noble thing than these sprawling emergent giant democracies of the West that struggle so confusedly against it...._

text: Dearest Lorna," he penned. "I journeyed here this morning intending to call on you—then reconsidered. I shall depart at once to discover what has become of Arthur. One way or another, I will resolve that matter. I'll dash about and see it through, even if it means venturing to the Flanders line. And once that is put to rest, I have another notion stirring within me—one that will erase this trifling quarrel that has swelled so large between us over impartiality. And I cherish you deeply, Lorna.
source_text: "Dear Cissie," he wrote. "I came down to-day to see you--and thought better of it. I'm going right off to find out about Teddy. Somehow I'll get that settled. I'll fly around and do that somehow if I have to go up to the German front to do it. And when I've got that settled I've got something else in my mind--well, it will wipe out all this little trouble that's got so big between us about neutrality. And I love you dearly, Cissie."

text: Mr. Aldridge had endured that torrent of observations with a tolerant half-smile and a deliberate inclination of his chin. “Your remarks,” he offered, “stand in rather sharp opposition to the way matters proceed back home. That acquaintance I mentioned earlier, the fellow tied to a motor works out in Cleveland—”
source_text: Mr. Direck had followed this cascade of remarks with a patient smile and a slowly nodding head. "What you say," he said, "forms a very marked contrast indeed with the sort of thing that goes on in America. This friend of mine I was speaking of, the one who is connected with an automobile factory in Toledo--"

text: When Mr. Direck and Mr. Britling got back to the Dower House, the visitor was entrusted to Mrs. Britling, while Mr. Britling disappeared, only to surface again at supper—since the Britlings took their evening meal as supper rather than dinner. By the time Mr. Britling surfaced once more, every sign of his irritation with the frivolities of British politics and the governing class had completely dissolved, and his mind had drifted far from whatever might be unfolding in Germany or India.
source_text: When Mr. Direck and Mr. Britling returned to the Dower House the guest was handed over to Mrs. Britling and Mr. Britling vanished, to reappear at supper time, for the Britlings had a supper in the evening instead of dinner. When Mr. Britling did reappear every trace of his vexation with the levities of British politics and the British ruling class had vanished altogether, and he was no longer thinking of all that might be happening in Germany or India....

text: Five minutes earlier, Aunt Wilshire had been settled in the parlour of the lodging-house, absorbed in a grand and solemn game of "Patience"—the Emperor’s Patience ("Napoleon, my love!—none of that Potsdam impostor") that demanded hours to complete. Five minutes later she was transformed into a figure of primal horror and anguish, her body torn with bleeding gashes and crushed bones, flailing blindly through the dark amid a chaos of splintered debris. And already the German pilots were humming back out to sea, swollen with self-regard, no doubt delighted—like lads who have hurled a stone through a window—speeding home toward commendations and prizes, toward iron crosses and the fervent embraces of adoring Frauen and Fräuleins....
source_text: Five minutes before, Aunt Wilshire had been sitting in the boarding-house drawing-room playing a great stern "Patience," the Emperor Patience ("Napoleon, my dear!--not that Potsdam creature") that took hours to do. Five minutes later she was a thing of elemental terror and agony, bleeding wounds and shattered bones, plunging about in the darkness amidst a heap of wreckage. And already the German airmen were buzzing away to sea again, proud of themselves, pleased no doubt--like boys who have thrown a stone through a window, beating their way back to thanks and rewards, to iron crosses and the proud embraces of delighted Fraus and Fräuleins....

text: At that moment, Mr. Direck felt prompted to share a story. "Let me give you an example that bears on your point about systematic organization, Mr. Britling. I recall a small episode involving a friend of mine in Toledo, where a large factory is being built with the aim of cornering the whole American and European market for cars in the thousand-dollar range—"
source_text: At this point Mr. Direck was moved by an anecdote. "It will help to illustrate what you are saying, Mr. Britling, about systematic organisation if I tell you a little incident that happened to a friend of mine in Toledo, where they are setting up a big plant with a view to capturing the entire American and European market in the class of the thousand-dollar car--"

text: The hours he spent in their company only hardened his sense that they hadn’t the faintest grasp of what any of it meant. Fortune had so steadily smiled on them that even Teddy’s departure for the front struck them as a kind of diversion. It was simply an undertaking he had embarked upon—a grave, gravely entertaining, and highly honourable undertaking. It meant he got to put on those curious garments and be acknowledged with salutes on the pavement…. They picked over every conceivable detail of his soldiering future with the relish of children cataloguing the virtues of a fresh pastime. They were hurtling Teddy through his milestones at a breakneck rhythm. Before very long, he suspected, they would see him granted the opening for a commission.
source_text: The afternoon with them deepened his conviction that they really didn't in the slightest degree understand. Life had been so good to them hitherto, that even the idea of Teddy's going off to the war seemed a sort of fun to them. It was just a thing he was doing, a serious, seriously amusing, and very creditable thing. It involved his dressing up in these unusual clothes, and receiving salutes in the street....
