They discussed every possible aspect of his military outlook with the zest of children, who recount the merits of a new game. They were putting Teddy through his stages at a tremendous pace. In quite a little time he thought he would be given the chance of a commission.

text: By this point, it ought to have been evident that Mr. Direck possessed a straightforwardness and candor exceeding even the European norm, and his designs upon the young woman were as uncomplicated, sincere, and thoroughly honorable as such designs could possibly be. Among all peoples, it is perhaps the American notion of courtship that most insists the woman should set the pace; the gentleman’s role is to be sheltering, conciliatory, obliging, and shrewd, while the lady’s part is to remain elusive yet charming until he wins her, installs her in grand style, furnishes her with a generous allowance, and returns to his own affairs. Operating on these premises, Mr. Direck set about his pursuit. Yet he soon perceived that Cecily did not share his assumptions. She grew flustered when he fetched her modest offerings of sweets and blossoms from London—the Britling lads were far more grateful—she refused to let him arrange lavish little outings for her, and she objected to flattery, threatening to absent herself whenever he attempted it. Nor was she satisfied by his sweeping pronouncements on existence; instead, she posed startlingly forthright queries about his work and his pursuits. His primary occupation was serving as the comfortably endowed heir of a proficient attorney, and his pursuits, examined under her scrutiny, struck him as trivial and somewhat dilettantish—shortcomings he had never before regarded as defects. Consequently, he found himself leaning instead on ambitions and the prospect, given the right encouragement, of a more vigorously useful life ahead.
source_text: Mr. Direck, it should be clear by this time, was a man of more than European simplicity and directness, and his intentions towards the young lady were as simple and direct and altogether honest as such intentions can be. It is the American conception of gallantry more than any other people's, to let the lady call the tune in these affairs; the man's place is to be protective, propitiatory, accommodating and clever, and the lady's to be difficult but delightful until he catches her and houses her splendidly and gives her a surprising lot of pocket-money, and goes about his business; and upon these assumptions Mr. Direck went to work. But quite early it was manifest to him that Cecily did not recognise his assumptions. She was embarrassed when he got down one or two little presents of chocolates and flowers for her from London--the Britling boys were much more appreciative--she wouldn't let him contrive costly little expeditions for her, and she protested against compliments and declared she would stay away when he paid them. And she was not contented by his general sentiments about life, but asked the most direct questions about his occupation and his activities. His chief occupation was being the well provided heir of a capable lawyer, and his activities in the light of her inquiries struck him as being light and a trifle amateurish, qualities he had never felt as any drawback about them before. So that he had to rely rather upon aspirations and the possibility, under proper inspiration, of a more actively serviceable life in future.

text: The squirrel, Mr. Direck learned, had been acquired by Mrs. Britling for the children several weeks before; they had named it "Nimble," worshipped it briefly, and then let it slip from their attention, until Herr Heinrich assumed its care. It had nestled into a hollow of his capacious heart left vacant by the reserved affections of the Britling household. He nearly forsook his philological studies altogether to devote himself to the cosseting and veneration of this restless, agile little soul. He bore it away to his private quarters, where it roamed unfettered and trespassed upon him and his belongings with utter impunity. For a squirrel, it was a remarkably fearless and untamed creature, yet Herr Heinrich had fixed his heart and his immense, enduring resolve upon cultivating a bond of sentiment. He trusted that in time Nimble would consent to be caressed, that he would teach Nimble to love and comprehend him, and that his would remain the sole hand Nimble would ever tolerate upon its fur. Meanwhile, even in its wild state, Nimble was a marvel to observe. One could gaze at it endlessly. Its forepaws resembled hands—slender, elongated, like a musician’s hands. "He would make a musician, if only he could spread his fingers apart, for when I play my violin he attends. He is rapt."
source_text: The squirrel, Mr. Direck gathered, had been bought by Mrs. Britling for the boys some month or so ago; it had been christened "Bill" and adored and then neglected, until Herr Heinrich took it over. It had filled a place in his ample heart that the none too demonstrative affection of the Britling household had left empty. He abandoned his pursuit of philology almost entirely for the cherishing and adoration of this busy, nimble little creature. He carried it off to his own room, where it ran loose and took the greatest liberties with him and his apartment. It was an extraordinarily bold and savage little beast even for a squirrel, but Herr Heinrich had set his heart and his very large and patient will upon the establishment of sentimental relations. He believed that ultimately Bill would let himself be stroked, that he would make Bill love him and understand him, and that his would be the only hand that Bill would ever suffer to touch him. In the meanwhile even the untamed Bill was wonderful to watch. One could watch him forever. His front paws were like hands, like a musician's hands, very long and narrow. "He would be a musician if he could only make his fingers go apart, because when I play my violin he listens. He is attentive."

text: Mr. Britling paused in thought. His immediate fear was that at any moment word might arrive that Hugh had been wounded and was unaccounted for. He then turned his efforts toward reassuring Cissie, explaining that the omission of the word "seriously" implied Teddy’s injury was likely manageable, and that being listed as "missing" might simply mean the War Office couldn’t yet provide the precise details she craved. But Cissie only replied that "Letty was in an awful state," and after Mr. Britling gave her a few typing tasks, he walked down to the cottage to offer Letty the same softening reflections. He discovered her even paler than her sister, gripped by a cold fury toward the War Office. She plainly believed the institution should have safeguarded Teddy more carefully. There was a strange sense about her that something was being deliberately withheld. It was also evident she was inclined to see Mr. Britling as someone whose sympathies lay too readily with the authorities.
source_text: Mr. Britling thought. His first thought was that now news might come at any time that Hugh was wounded and missing. Then he set himself to persuade Cissie that the absence of "seriously" meant that Teddy was only quite bearably wounded, and that if he was also "missing" it might be difficult for the War Office to ascertain at once just exactly what she wanted to know. But Cissie said merely that "Letty was in an awful state," and after Mr. Britling had given her a few instructions for his typing, he went down to the cottage to repeat these mitigatory considerations to Letty. He found her much whiter than her sister, and in a state of cold indignation with the War Office. It was clear she thought that organisation ought to have taken better care of Teddy. She had a curious effect of feeling that something was being kept back from her. It was manifest too that she was disposed to regard Mr. Britling as biased in favour of the authorities.

text: But Herr Heinrich understood the truth. “No,” he insisted. “It’s the war. It has arrived. I heard it discussed often in Germany. Yet I never thought it was bound to happen. Ach! It spares nobody. As long as Esperanto is ignored, all of this is inevitable.”
source_text: But Herr Heinrich knew better. "No," he said. "It is the war. It has come. I have heard it talked about in Germany many times. But I have never believed that it was obliged to come. Ach! It considers no one. So long as Esperanto is disregarded, all these things must be."

text: He tried to envision Heinrich’s parents. They must be, he thought, gentle and cultivated souls. It was plain that the boy had come to him from a home that was orderly and tender. Yet he imagined them—for reasons he couldn’t name—as far older than himself. Perhaps young Heinrich had once mentioned they were old; he couldn’t recall. And a strange impulse stirred in him to write them words of comfort, as though their loss were somehow more deserving of pity than his own. He doubted they possessed the solace of his hopeful nature, doubted they could lean as easily as he could on his faith, doubted that in Pomerania there existed the same consoling prospect of an essay on the Better Government of the World. The thought was not sharply formed, but that was what lay at the back of his mind. He continued writing.
source_text: He tried to picture these Heinrich parents. He supposed they were kindly, civilised people. It was manifest the youngster had come to him from a well-ordered and gentle-spirited home. But he imagined them--he could not tell why--as people much older than himself. Perhaps young Heinrich had on some occasion said they were old people--he could not remember. And he had a curious impulse too to write to them in phrases of consolation; as if their loss was more pitiable than his own. He doubted whether they had the consolation of his sanguine temperament, whether they could resort as readily as he could to his faith, whether in Pomerania there was the same consoling possibility of an essay on the Better Government of the World. He did not think this very clearly, but that was what was at the back of his mind. He went on writing.

text: Not one syllable of this nonsense did he credit. It was a fierce, unreasoning devotion to his homeland that drove him to utter such sentiments.... Time and again he had landed himself in trouble for penning and insinuating the very same reproaches that Mr. Van der Pant now voiced so crudely.... Yet he would not suffer an outsider’s hand in cutting open his own mother.
source_text: He did not believe a word of this stuff. His deep irrational love for England made him say these things.... For years he had been getting himself into hot water because he had been writing and hinting just such criticisms as Mr. Van der Pant expressed so bluntly.... But he wasn't going to accept foreign help in dissecting his mother....

text: The fortress of Namur was lost. The litany of towns now drifted ever south and west. Suddenly, at Mons, the British army—or some fragment of it—emerged into view. For eight and thirty hours it had battled, throwing back vastly larger enemy hosts. This brought comfort, but only briefly, for within a day the phrase “the Cambrai–Le Cateau line” forced Mr. Britling to grasp that the triumphant British had been hurled back twenty-five miles.
source_text: Namur fell. The place names continued to shift southward and westward. The British army or a part of it came to light abruptly at Mons. It had been fighting for thirty-eight hours and defeating enormously superior forces of the enemy. That was reassuring until a day or so later "the Cambray--Le Cateau line" made Mr. Britling realise that the victorious British had recoiled five and twenty miles....

text: He drifted along, murmuring to himself. He took deep solace in plotting elaborate revenge upon innumerable Germans. He pictured swift armored planes diving onto the drifting airship, hurling it spinning toward the ground while its crew shrieked. He imagined a broken Zeppelin lurching down into the meadows behind the Dower House, and saw himself rushing out with a spade to strike the Germans dead. "Quarter indeed! Kamerad! Take _that_, you filthy murderer!"
source_text: He wandered, muttering. He found great comfort in scheming vindictive destruction for countless Germans. He dreamt of swift armoured aeroplanes swooping down upon the flying airship, and sending it reeling earthward, the men screaming. He imagined a shattered Zeppelin staggering earthward in the fields behind the Dower House, and how he would himself run out with a spade and smite the Germans down. "Quarter indeed! Kamerad! Take _that_, you foul murderer!"

text: Things might come right again," he conceded. "But then again, nothing might. This whole existence of ours, which has felt so steady and dependable—it isn't dependable at all. I've always believed we were planted here, anchored for good. What if events suddenly sweep us away from it? That's a prospect we may need to reckon with. This morning I feel as though two vast doors have swung open in our lives, like the doors that lead into an arena, doors opening onto a void—through which anything could enter. Even death. Imagine if we suddenly glimpsed one of those huge Zeppelins overhead, or caught the rumble of artillery far off toward the shore. And if a messenger came cycling up, telling us to abandon everything and head inland....
source_text: "Everything may recover," he admitted. "But also nothing may recover. All this life of ours which has seemed so settled and secure--isn't secure. I have felt that we were fixed here and rooted--for all our lives. Suppose presently things sweep us out of it? It's a possibility we may have to face. I feel this morning as if two enormous gates had opened in our lives, like the gates that give upon an arena, gates giving on a darkness--through which anything might come. Even death. Suppose suddenly we were to see one of those great Zeppelins in the air, or hear the thunder of guns away towards the coast. And if a messenger came upon a bicycle telling us to leave everything and go inland...."

text: Save for the questionable case of Miss Mamie Nelson, Mr. Direck generally enjoyed good luck. Agreeable opportunities tended to find him. Consider his role as the paid secretary of that association of reflective Massachusetts businessmen already mentioned. Its aim was to connect itself swiftly with the finest thinking of the era.
source_text: Except in the doubtful instance of Miss Mamie Nelson, Mr. Direck's habit was good fortune. Pleasant things came to him. Such was his position as the salaried secretary of this society of thoughtful Massachusetts business men to which allusion has been made. Its purpose was to bring itself expeditiously into touch with the best thought of the age.

text: His words seemed to drift out from some inner engine of contemplation, as though released from a quietly humming mental workshop. Until now, Mr. Direck had taken this quiet, watchful young man—hands buried in his pockets, shoulders faintly stooped—for someone shy and youthfully ineffectual. Yet it was plain that his mind was very much at work.
source_text: His remarks had the effect of floating off from some busy mill of thought within him. Hitherto Mr. Direck had been inclined to think this silent observant youth, with his hands in his pockets and his shoulders a little humped, as probably shy and adolescently ineffective. But the head was manifestly quite busy....

text: The trouble he had been reluctant to lay before his father concerned that most solemn stake of youth: his Purpose in Life. It had nothing to do with the amorous turmoil that had so troubled his father’s mind. Whatever might be stirring beneath the surface of Hugh’s amiable, reflective manner in that regard had yet to break through and declare itself. What did nag at him was a deep division in his own intellectual makeup. Two opposing passions tugged at him—one, which many readers may find arid, but which for Hugh shone and mesmerised, was crystallography and molecular physics; the other was caricature. Both gifts sprang, no doubt, from the same uncommon sensitivity to form. As a schoolboy he had indulged both with equal delight, but now he was reaching the age of specialisation and found himself swinging restlessly between science and art. After a stretch of scientific work he would hit a phase of exhaustion and see nothing in the world but absurdities and a lark to be rendered with comic flair; after a run of humorous sketches his mind would veer back to light and crystals and films, like a penitent Magdalen returning to a church. He had turned down Cambridge after public school and gone instead to University College, London, to study under the great and rousing Professor Cardinal; but just then Cardinal was arranging his own move to Cambridge, and Hugh had barely begun his London work when Cardinal was replaced by the plodding, earnest, dispiriting Pelkingham, under whose touch crystals grew stodgy as puddings, bubble films turned to cotton sheets, transparency drained from the world, and X-rays shrank and expired. Hugh promptly degenerated into a mocking idler who longed to abandon science for art.
source_text: Now the trouble that he had been hesitating to bring before his father was concerned with that very grave interest of the young, his Object in Life. It had nothing to do with those erotic disturbances that had distressed his father's imagination. Whatever was going on below the surface of Hugh's smiling or thoughtful presence in that respect had still to come to the surface and find expression. But he was bothered very much by divergent strands in his own intellectual composition. Two sets of interests pulled at him, one--it will seem a dry interest to many readers, but for Hugh it glittered and fascinated--was crystallography and molecular physics; the other was caricature. Both aptitudes sprang no doubt from the same exceptional sensitiveness to form. As a schoolboy he exercised both very happily, but now he was getting to the age of specialisation, and he was fluctuating very much between science and art. After a spell of scientific study he would come upon a fatigue period and find nothing in life but absurdities and a lark that one could represent very amusingly; after a bout of funny drawings his mind went back to his light and crystals and films like a Magdalen repenting in a church. After his public school he had refused Cambridge and gone to University College, London, to work under the great and inspiring Professor Cardinal; simultaneously Cardinal had been arranging to go to Cambridge, and Hugh had scarcely embarked upon his London work when Cardinal was succeeded by the dull, conscientious and depressing Pelkingham, at whose touch crystals became as puddings, bubble films like cotton sheets, transparency vanished from the world, and X rays dwarfed and died. And Hugh degenerated immediately into a scoffing trifler who wished to give up science for art.

text: The room had become unbearable. He had to escape it. At the window, he faced the restless churring of nightjars, the far-off bellowing of stags, the black weave of trees and deeper blackness, and a sky so clear and distant it was crowded with stars. The stars appeared watchful. They trembled without moving, as though they were the eyes of sentinels. He would go out to them.
source_text: This room was unendurable. He must go out. He turned to the window, and outside was a troublesome noise of night-jars and a distant roaring of stags, black trees, blacknesses, the sky clear and remote with a great company of stars.... The stars seemed attentive. They stirred and yet were still. It was as if they were the eyes of watchers. He would go out to them....

text: But the very next day, this angelic foe of quarrels erupted in the most dreadful fashion over Edith, and he was forced to tell her more bluntly than before that he would not endure such behavior. He would not have Edith mocked. He would not have Edith made to appear low. And from that arose great strife between them, with weeping and mention of Oliver.
source_text: And yet the very next day this angel enemy of controversies broke out in the most abominable way about Edith, and he had to tell her more plainly than he had done hitherto, that he could not tolerate that sort of thing. He wouldn't have Edith guyed. He wouldn't have Edith made to seem base. And at that there was much trouble between them, and tears and talk of Oliver....

text: The tale of the Windsor Castle carpet lingered in her thoughts, though now it was tangled with fragments from the day’s newspaper debates over the war’s management. She was still pondering the dynastic dimensions of the conflict. And spies. Something about the King’s more German aunts weighed on her mind.
source_text: The Windsor Castle carpet story was still running through her mind, but mixed up with it now were scraps of the current newspaper controversies about the conduct of the war. And she was still thinking of the dynastic aspects of the war. And of spies. She had something upon her mind about the King's more German aunts.

text: The message from Commander Ashford softened his temper. He replied with brevity yet warmth. As a man of honour would. “How could you question our navy or our troops?” formed the core of his note. He overlooked entirely every hint of a journey to the Whitmores that her correspondence had implied. He feigned that it had held no such suggestion at all.... And with that, she slipped from his thoughts once more beneath the weight of more pressing concerns....
source_text: The despatch from General French put him into a kindlier frame of mind. He wrote instead briefly but affectionately. As a gentleman should. "How could you doubt our fleet or our army?" was the gist of his letter. He ignored completely every suggestion of a visit to Pyecrafts that her letter had conveyed. He pretended that it had contained nothing of the sort.... And with that she passed out of his mind again under the stress of more commanding interests....

text: My oldest boy is just shy of seventeen," he said. "He's eager to enlist, and I'd be troubled if he weren't. Naturally, he'll join some cadet unit—he's already had a taste of that at school. Or they'll bring him into the Territorials. But before he turns nineteen, the whole business will be finished, one way or the other. I fear, poor lad, he'll feel cheated....
source_text: "My eldest boy is barely seventeen," he said. "He's keen to go, and I'd be sorry if he wasn't. He'll get into some cadet corps of course--he's already done something of that kind at school. Or they'll take him into the Territorials. But before he's nineteen everything will be over, one way or another. I'm afraid, poor chap, he'll feel sold...."

text: A twelvemonth past, Mr. Britling had taken his midday meal aboard a dreadnought, peering into its tangled apparatus. At the time, he had fancied that nowhere on earth could one discover finer human material than those composed, bronze-faced sailors and commanders he encountered.... And now, too, our modest force must be mustering in the darkness—the small army that had been tempered and remade upon the veldt, which he privately trusted was, man for man, more daring, more resourceful, and more proficient than any other fighting body in existence. Had another English voice uttered such a claim, he would have scoffed or bristled; yet in his secret self he cherished that fond conviction....
source_text: Only a year ago Mr. Britling had been lunching on a battleship and looking over its intricate machinery. It had seemed to him then that there could be no better human stuff in the world than the quiet, sunburnt, disciplined men and officers he had met.... And our little army, too, must be gathering to-night, the little army that had been chastened and reborn in South Africa, that he was convinced was individually more gallant and self-reliant and capable than any other army in the world. He would have sneered or protested if he had heard another Englishman say that, but in his heart he held the dear belief....

text:
Section 4

It was not until they had withdrawn to the quiet of the barn court, beyond the reach of Mrs. Britling and the children, that Captain Carmine could account for his weary manner and drained expression. He was in the grip of a severe nervous collapse. Scarcely had he assumed his post when one of his soldiers had died—and died in a way that had seared itself into his thoughts.
source_text: Section 4 It was only when they sat together in the barn court out of the way of Mrs. Britling and the children that Captain Carmine was able to explain his listless bearing and jaded appearance. He was suffering from a bad nervous shock. He had hardly taken over his command before one of his men had been killed--and killed in a manner that had left a scar upon his mind.

text: The letter arrived at a wretched moment. Its drivel about Prince Louis of Battenberg, Lord Haldane, and torpedoed battleships irritated him beyond measure. He possessed just enough credulity toward such stories to find their persistence maddening. The prospect of decamping to Pyecrafts to spend his days soothing a frightened little creature consumed by these terrors held no appeal whatever. He had already gathered sufficient disquieting gossip at Claverings to leave him deeply unsettled. He had been questioning whether, after all, his "Examination of War" was truly any less pointless than "And Now War Ends"; his thoughts were crowded with a sense of half-finished assertions and flimsy reasoning. He was, in fact, in a state of acute intellectual distress. Furthermore, he was remarkably out of charity with Mrs. Harrowdean. Never in the entire chronicle of Mr. Britling's affections had a passion collapsed so abruptly and totally. He was left unable to believe he had ever cared for her at all. Most likely he never had. Most likely the whole affair had been a sustained self-deception from beginning to end. The "dear little thing" posture, he reflected, was all very well as a flirtatious pastime, but these were grave times, and a woman of her wit ought to do something better than wallow in panic and cultivate an artful helplessness. A thoroughly needless and wearisome helplessness. He very nearly resolved to write her exactly that.
source_text: It was an ill-timed letter. The nonsense about Prince Louis of Battenberg and Lord Haldane and the torpedoed battleships annoyed him extravagantly. He had just sufficient disposition to believe such tales as to find their importunity exasperating. The idea of going over to Pyecrafts to spend his days in comforting a timid little dear obsessed by such fears, attracted him not at all. He had already heard enough adverse rumours at Claverings to make him thoroughly uncomfortable. He had been doubting whether after all his "Examination of War" was really much less of a futility than "And Now War Ends"; his mind was full of a sense of incomplete statements and unsubstantial arguments. He was indeed in a state of extreme intellectual worry. He was moreover extraordinarily out of love with Mrs. Harrowdean. Never had any affection in the whole history of Mr. Britling's heart collapsed so swiftly and completely. He was left incredulous of ever having cared for her at all. Probably he hadn't. Probably the whole business had been deliberate illusion from first to last. The "dear little thing" business, he felt, was all very well as a game of petting, but times were serious now, and a woman of her intelligence should do something better than wallow in fears and elaborate a winsome feebleness. A very unnecessary and tiresome feebleness. He came almost to the pitch of writing that to her.

text: Scarcely more than three weeks had passed since the arrival of Hugh’s final note when Mr. Direck returned to Matching’s Easy. He had been away in the Low Countries—a journey undertaken as much to escape Cissie’s recriminations as to pursue any real investigation. He had planned to push onward into Flanders, where he had lately been engaged in worthy relief efforts beneath Mr. Hoover’s direction, but the turmoil of his own heart had arrested him and drawn him home.
source_text: It was just a day or so over three weeks after this last letter from Hugh that Mr. Direck reappeared at Matching's Easy. He had had a trip to Holland--a trip that was as much a flight from Cissie's reproaches as a mission of inquiry. He had intended to go on into Belgium, where he had already been doing useful relief work under Mr. Hoover, but the confusion of his own feelings had checked him and brought him back.

text: Could this be a variant of the very affliction that had so twisted and stained and marred his father’s hidden dignity? And how could Mr. Britling, disfigured by careless missteps, burdened by tangled secrets and evasions, guide this earnest young soul through his bewilderments? He envisioned the shapes those bewilderments might take. Graceless shapes. Ugly shapes. Shapes that only the mind in darkness would have ventured to conjure.
source_text: Was it some form of the same trouble that had so tangled and tainted and scarred the private pride of his father? And how was it possible for Mr. Britling, disfigured by heedless misadventures, embarrassed by complications and concealments, to help this honest youngster out of his perplexities? He imagined possible forms of these perplexities. Graceless forms. Ugly forms. Such forms as only the nocturnal imagination would have dared present....

text: Mr. Britling would settle into his armchair, gazing into the flames, as a tangle of memories about Germany surfaced—visions of a charming countryside, of warm-hearted folk. He had passed many a merry vacation there. As lately as 1911, the entire Britling household had journeyed up the Rhine from Rotterdam, touring a succession of grand cities before lingering for a sunlit, carefree month at Neunkirchen in the Odenwald.
source_text: Mr. Britling would sit in his armchair and stare at his fire, and recall conflicting memories of Germany--of a pleasant land, of friendly people. He had spent many a jolly holiday there. So recently as 1911 all the Britling family had gone up the Rhine from Rotterdam, had visited a string of great cities and stayed for a cheerful month of sunshine at Neunkirchen in the Odenwald.

text: Lately, sleep had become a torment. By daylight, existence was bearable, but at night—unless he fended it off with work—the war’s losses and brutalities would leer at him, unendurable. Sometimes he was pursued by endless columns of refugees; other times he pictured the dead, rigid and contorted in a thousand hideous poses. Then he would be swamped by visions of the catastrophic economic and social collapse that might be coming. At still other moments, he brooded on wounds and the mutilations of flesh and spirit that injury inflicted. And occasionally he would dwell on the victory of wickedness. Stupid, triumphant figures strutted through a world that stupidity had laid waste, swaggering, smiling with a smug sense of their own swollen importance, their disdainful hatred of all that was measured, moderate, and gentle now hardened into scornful contempt. And trampled into the dirt they walked on lay Hugh’s body, facedown. At the back of the boy’s head, fringed with hair stiffened by blood—hair that had once been “soft as a bird’s down”—was a large red cavity. That cavity was always pitilessly sharp. They stepped on him—unthinking. They ground the scattered fragments of his fine mind into the mud.
source_text: Latterly he had had much sleepless misery. In the day life was tolerable, but in the night--unless he defended himself by working, the losses and cruelties of the war came and grimaced at him, insufferably. Now he would be haunted by long processions of refugees, now he would think of the dead lying stiff and twisted in a thousand dreadful attitudes. Then again he would be overwhelmed with anticipations of the frightful economic and social dissolution that might lie ahead.... At other times he thought of wounds and the deformities of body and spirit produced by injuries. And sometimes he would think of the triumph of evil. Stupid and triumphant persons went about a world that stupidity had desolated, with swaggering gestures, with a smiling consciousness of enhanced importance, with their scornful hatred of all measured and temperate and kindly things turned now to scornful contempt. And mingling with the soil they walked on lay the dead body of Hugh, face downward. At the back of the boy's head, rimmed by blood-stiffened hair--the hair that had once been "as soft as the down of a bird"--was a big red hole. That hole was always pitilessly distinct. They stepped on him--heedlessly. They heeled the scattered stuff of his exquisite brain into the clay....

text: Mr. Britling perused the letter, and his mind drifted once more to Teddy’s vanishing before circling back to Hugh. The boy was already at the forward edge of the fighting, and he had to brace himself for whatever might come. In some way, Mr. Britling had not anticipated Hugh would be thrust into the line of fire so swiftly, though he would have been hard-pressed to explain why it should have been otherwise. Yet he realized he must start learning the discipline of composure from scratch.
source_text: Mr. Britling read this through, and then his thoughts went back to Teddy's disappearance and then returned to Hugh. The youngster was right in the front now, and one had to steel oneself to the possibilities of the case. Somehow Mr. Britling had not expected to find Hugh so speedily in the firing line, though he would have been puzzled to find a reason why this should not have happened. But he found he had to begin the lesson of stoicism all over again.

text: It was a full mile and a quarter from the post office to the Dower House, and he always made a point of tipping any telegraph messenger who arrived at his door with tuppence. Now he felt a pressing need to be rid of the girl, who lingered before him in expectation, one hand resting on her red bicycle. A sick, taut sensation had taken hold of him; he was certain that unless he kept his composure rigidly cool and dry, he would break into a wail. He searched his pocket for coins—there were a few coppers and a shilling. He drew them all out in a single handful and gazed at them.
source_text: It was a mile and a quarter from the post office to the Dower House, and it was always his custom to give telegraph messengers who came to his house twopence, and he wanted very much to get rid of the telegraph girl, who stood expectantly before him holding her red bicycle. He felt now very sick and strained; he had a conviction that if he did not by an effort maintain his bearing cool and dry he would howl aloud. He felt in his pocket for money; there were some coppers and a shilling. He pulled it all out together and stared at it.

text: Should you believe that these two lads have died, not united in some grand shared purpose but each at the other's hand amid a clash of empires and frontiers and commerce and oppressive dominions, then I think you cannot help but share my conviction that this conflict stands as the most sorrowful and appalling calamity ever to befall humanity.
source_text: _If you think that these two boys have both perished, not in some noble common cause but one against the other in a struggle of dynasties and boundaries and trade routes and tyrannous ascendancies, then it seems to me that you must feel as I feel that this war is the most tragic and dreadful thing that has ever happened to mankind._

text: Additionally, he enjoyed watching her scan the morning newspaper. She approached it as if she considered the entire planet an irredeemable idiot, utterly impervious to counsel. And as if she grasped, with a touch of amusement, how the journalists attempted to conceal the true state of affairs from her.
source_text: Moreover, he liked to see her casting her eye over the morning paper. She did it with a manner as though she thought the terrestrial globe a great fool, and quite beyond the reach of advice. And as though she understood and was rather amused at the way in which the newspaper people tried to keep back the real facts of the case from her.

text: While he roamed the rural lanes beneath the vast dome of the Sussex heavens, or rested his back against weathered stiles, or dozed half-aware on field gates, or hid from lashing storms beneath hayricks and barn eaves, his mind sank into long reflection, burrowing far beneath the initial face of the war. He no longer weighed the justice or injustice of this single quarrel but instead the buried compulsions of human nature that bred war itself; he devised no more clever accords and diplomatic frameworks among powers, but confronted the darker mysteries of innate cruelty and the possible transformations of the human spirit. And the rain lashed him and brambles clawed him, and the sodden, treacherous pastures sucked at his straying boots, and the secret world of hedgerows and culverts whispered and shrieked through the black hours, chasing and fleeing, devouring or falling slain.
source_text: As he prowled the countryside under the great hemisphere of Essex sky, or leant against fences or sat drowsily upon gates or sheltered from wind and rain under ricks or sheds, he had much time for meditation, and his thoughts went down and down below his first surface impressions of the war. He thought no longer of the rights and wrongs of this particular conflict but of the underlying forces in mankind that made war possible; he planned no more ingenious treaties and conventions between the nations, and instead he faced the deeper riddles of essential evil and of conceivable changes in the heart of man. And the rain assailed him and thorns tore him, and the soaked soft meadows bogged and betrayed his wandering feet, and the little underworld of the hedges and ditches hissed and squealed in the darkness and pursued and fled, and devoured or were slain.

text: The sole conceivable regime for Illyria," he declared, his gaze fixed unwaveringly on the valley below the slope, "is a federation of free communes modeled on the Helvetic system. I perceive no alternative that does not affront the Almighty. Our debts to Dalmatia or to Rome are of no consequence whatsoever. We must place this earth upon a new foundation. We must finally establish the world—upon fairness and rationality.
source_text: "The only possible government in Albania," he said, looking steadfastly before him down the hill-side, "is a group of republican cantons after the Swiss pattern. I can see no other solution that is not offensive to God. It does not matter in the least what we owe to Serbia or what we owe to Italy. We have got to set this world on a different footing. We have got to set up the world at last--on justice and reason."

text: Mr. Direck kept close to Cissie and the young man she danced with. Their movements flowed as one; a quiet ease and mutual fondness showed in every step. Given how constantly they were in each other’s company, it was only to be expected that such a pair would grow tender.... And yet, she had those few years on him.
source_text: Mr. Direck hung on to Cissie and her partner. They danced very well together; they seemed to like and understand each other. It was natural of course for two young people like that, thrown very much together, to develop an affection for one another.... Still, she was older by three or four years.

text: After their midday meal, Mr. Britling grew more sanguine at the wheel. The highway running from Market Saffron toward Blandish, where one veers for Matching's Easy, follows the old London and Norwich route—an ancient Roman track, forthright and guileless in its long reaches. The crossroads announce themselves half a mile distant, and the clipped hedgerows afford no hiding place for sudden mishaps. Such a road lifts every driver's spirits, coaxing a bolder, swifter hand, and Mr. Britling in particular took courage from it, easing Gladys little by little from the almost stifling restraint that had defined the morning. "Nothing can go amiss on a road like this," he declared.
source_text: After lunch Mr. Britling became more cheerful about his driving. The road from Market Saffron to Blandish, whence one turns off to Matching's Easy, is the London and Norwich high road; it is an old Roman Stane Street and very straightforward and honest in its stretches. You can see the cross roads half a mile away, and the low hedges give you no chance of a surprise. Everybody is cheered by such a road, and everybody drives more confidently and quickly, and Mr. Britling particularly was heartened by it and gradually let out Gladys from the almost excessive restriction that had hitherto marked the day. "On a road like this nothing can happen," said Mr. Britling.

text: Mr. Britling remained heated and discomposed as he and his companion made their way back to the Dower House. He could disparage England himself without restraint, yet he recoiled at the notion that she might truly lack flawlessness; even her shortcomings he preferred to conceive as a more refined expression of strength and sagacity. And Lady Frensham had pierced those tender illusions with her intonations and her motions. He resembled a suitor who teasingly calls his beloved a silly minx, only to be jolted when circumstance and outsiders take him at his word.
source_text: Mr. Britling was still flushed and ruffled as he and his guest returned towards the Dower House. He criticised England himself unmercifully, but he hated to think that in any respect she fell short of perfection; even her defects he liked to imagine were just a subtler kind of power and wisdom. And Lady Frensham had stuck her voice and her gestures through all these amiable illusions. He was like a lover who calls his lady a foolish rogue, and is startled to find that facts and strangers do literally agree with him.

text: Well, they're both brimming with notions. They're deeply schooled in the way that they truly reason on their own. Almost with a kind of ferocity. Teddy is the same. If he suspects he hasn't arrived at a thought independently, he retreats and rethinks it from another angle. The sister is a teacher with ambitions to earn her B.A. at London University. In the meantime, she bears the cost of being a woman.
source_text: "Well, they're both idea'd. They're highly educated in the sense that they do really think for themselves. Almost fiercely. So does Teddy. If he thinks he hasn't thought anything he thinks for himself, he goes off and thinks it different. The sister is a teacher who wants to take the B.A. degree in London University. Meanwhile she pays the penalty of her sex."

text: He sensed an urgent need to look upon the ocean. He decided to drive past the Rodwell hamlets, then climb toward the summit of Eastonbury Hill. There he arrived and gazed through a cleft in the lower hills at a wedge of moonlit sea, shimmering yet motionless. He pulled his car to the verge and remained seated for a great while, watching the water and lost in thought. At one moment, he thought he saw three tiny forms, like stubby dark needles, gliding in single file across the liquid silver.
source_text: He felt that he must see the sea. He would follow the road beyond the Rodwell villages, and then turn up to the crest of Eastonbury Hill. And thither he went and saw in the gap of the low hills beyond a V-shaped level of moonlit water that glittered and yet lay still. He stopped his car by the roadside, and sat for a long time looking at this and musing. And once it seemed to him three little shapes like short black needles passed in line ahead across the molten silver.

text: For over a thousand years, war had been absent from the everyday texture of English life. The national mindset, shaped across fifty generations, instinctively resisted its emotional truth. The English were the pampered offspring of peace. Not in three centuries had they been fully at war, and for more than eight hundred years they had never battled for survival against a foreign enemy. Spain and France had each posed threats, but neither had ever managed to cross the sea. True, England had endured its own civil strife and waged wars and seized territories across every corner of the world, building a vast empire—but that, as Mr. Britling once explained to Mr. Direck, was merely "an excursion." She had simply dispatched younger sons and excess population, emigrants and expeditionary troops. Her own ground had never suffered a successful foreign incursion; her homeland, the great mass of her households, her ordinary existence, had carried on untouched by such events. Nineteen out of twenty people, the middle class and most of the working class, knew no more about the empire than they did about the Argentine Republic or the Italian Renaissance. It lay beyond their concern. A war that summons every man and endangers every life in the country, a war of the entire national organism, was something wholly outside English experience and the reach of the British imagination. It remained unbelievable, still beyond the horizon of Mr. Britling's thinking, all through the staggering advance and abrupt halt of the German offensive in the west that launched the great war. During those two months he was, in effect, an increasingly thrilled spectator at a spectacle, a spectacle like a baseball game, a spectator with a wager riding on the outcome, rather than a genuinely engaged citizen of a nation truly at war.
source_text: War had not been a reality of the daily life of England for more than a thousand years. The mental habit of the nation for fifty generations was against its emotional recognition. The English were the spoilt children of peace. They had never been wholly at war for three hundred years, and for over eight hundred years they had not fought for life against a foreign power. Spain and France had threatened in turn, but never even crossed the seas. It is true that England had had her civil dissensions and had made wars and conquests in every part of the globe and established an immense empire, but that last, as Mr. Britling had told Mr. Direck, was "an excursion." She had just sent out younger sons and surplus people, emigrants and expeditionary forces. Her own soil had never seen any successful foreign invasion; her homeland, the bulk of her households, her general life, had gone on untouched by these things. Nineteen people out of twenty, the middle class and most of the lower class, knew no more of the empire than they did of the Argentine Republic or the Italian Renaissance. It did not concern them. War that calls upon every man and threatens every life in the land, war of the whole national being, was a thing altogether outside English experience and the scope of the British imagination. It was still incredible, it was still outside the range of Mr. Britling's thoughts all through the tremendous onrush and check of the German attack in the west that opened the great war. Through those two months he was, as it were, a more and more excited spectator at a show, a show like a baseball match, a spectator with money on the event, rather than a really participating citizen of a nation thoroughly at war....

text: The automobile itself was born from the liaison. It had been Mrs. Harrowdean's fancy, she envisioned mostly delightful outings to secluded country taverns, places steeped in a quiet sense of shared secrets, but it suited Mr. Britling's private frustration with the absurd difficulty of the train link between Matching's Easy and her stop at Pyecrafts, which meant a trip into Liverpool Street and an endless wait at a connecting station. And now the motor lay wrecked—precisely when he had grown deft enough to drive it to Pyecrafts without embarrassment, and by Tuesday or Wednesday at the very latest he would be forced to travel the old route on the London train....
source_text: The car itself was an outcome of the affair. It was Mrs. Harrowdean's idea, she thought chiefly of pleasant expeditions to friendly inns in remote parts of the country, inns with a flavour of tacit complicity, but it fell in very pleasantly with Mr. Britling's private resentment at the extraordinary inconvenience of the railway communications between Matching's Easy and her station at Pyecrafts, which involved a journey to Liverpool Street and a long wait at a junction. And now the car was smashed up--just when he had acquired skill enough to take it over to Pyecrafts without shame, and on Tuesday or Wednesday at latest he would have to depart in the old way by the London train....

text: Absence is a strange state. It lacks the finality of tragedy—or the ache of pity. Nor does it offer the half-comfort of a word like "captured." It simply sets the mind wandering, endlessly wandering. I can't locate a soul who knows where the 14th Essex have got to. Units shift around here with such stealth that, for all I can tell, we might stumble into them in the next dugout the next time we move forward. Still, there is a chance for Teddy. It's worth pushing back against Letty as hard as you can. And yet, at the same time, the odds are stacked against him. That, plainly and coldly, is how the situation sits in my thoughts. My mind keeps returning to Letty. I'm grateful Cissie is beside her, and I'm grateful she has the child. Keep her occupied. She loved him terribly. I've witnessed all manner of moments between them, and I understand that.... I'll try to write to her soon, and I'll search for something hopeful to say.
source_text: "Missing's a queer thing. It isn't tragic--or pitiful. Or partly reassuring like 'prisoner.' It just sends one speculating and speculating. I can't find any one who knows where the 14th Essex are. Things move about here so mysteriously that for all I know we may find them in the next trench next time we go up. But there _is_ a chance for Teddy. It's worth while bucking Letty all you can. And at the same time there's odds against him. There plainly and unfeelingly is how things stand in my mind. I think chiefly of Letty. I'm glad Cissie is with her, and I'm glad she's got the boy. Keep her busy. She was frightfully fond of him. I've seen all sorts of things between them, and I know that.... I'll try and write to her soon, and I'll find something hopeful to tell her.

text: All of Germany is gripped by that mad rage," his companion remarked. "Here we wander, dazed and half-disbelieving this war, still half-disposed to smile, yet that is the German temper.... There's something calculated in it. They believe it grants them power. They _long_ to froth at the lips. They strain to froth all the more. They glorify themselves in print. Have you come across the 'Anthem of Loathing'?
source_text: "The whole of Germany is in that blind fury," said his friend. "While we are going about astonished and rather incredulous about this war, and still rather inclined to laugh, that's the state of mind of Germany.... There's a sort of deliberation in it. They think it gives them strength. They _want_ to foam at the mouth. They do their utmost to foam more. They write themselves up. Have you heard of the 'Hymn of Hate'?"

text: Edith spoke of a man named Arthur, a figure entirely unknown to Mr. Britling, who seeped into the fabric of their lives by quiet, almost imperceptible degrees. He held a post in the civil service, somewhere in Whitehall; she described him as profoundly unremarkable, devoid of all the wit and fascination Mr. Britling possessed, yet steadfast, gentle, and utterly devoted. And notably junior in years to Mr. Britling. He sought nothing more than to adore her. He laid before her the promise of decent matrimony. And when the heart within her grew too full to bear, she could sob without restraint upon his tolerant embrace. In time, that tolerant embrace of Arthur's became the rival Mr. Britling found most impossible to endure.
source_text: Oliver was a person Mr. Britling had never seen. He grew into the scheme of things by insensible gradations. He was a government official in London; he was, she said, extraordinarily dull, he was lacking altogether in Mr. Britling's charm and interest, but he was faithful and tender and true. And considerably younger than Mr. Britling. He asked nothing but to love. He offered honourable marriage. And when one's heart was swelling unendurably one could weep in safety on his patient shoulder. This patient shoulder of Oliver's ultimately became Mr. Britling's most exasperating rival.

text: Mr. Direck felt slightly put out at having his tale about Robinson cut short—once he started something, he preferred to see it through—yet he found Mr. Britling’s home unmistakably British, entirely un-American, and somewhat hard to get a handle on. It possessed an elusive quality he couldn’t immediately name. Set against anything he had previously encountered in his life, it struck him as being—he finally landed on the word—sketchy. For example, he wasn’t introduced to anyone apart from his hostess, and she was merely gestured at with a casual wave of Mr. Britling’s hand. “That’s Edith,” he announced, then promptly went back to his car to park it. Mrs. Britling was a tall woman with freckled skin, attractive light-brown hair, and distracted brown eyes. She greeted him with a handshake, after which a splendid English parlourmaid—she, at least, lived up to the stereotype—took his bag and led him to his room. “Lunch, sir,” she said, “is outside,” then shut the door and left him alone with that information and a towel-draped can of hot water.
source_text: Mr. Direck, though he was a little incommoded by the suppression of his story about Robinson--for when he had begun a thing he liked to finish it--found Mr. Britling's household at once thoroughly British, quite un-American and a little difficult to follow. It had a quality that at first he could not define at all. Compared with anything he had ever seen in his life before it struck him as being--he found the word at last--sketchy. For instance, he was introduced to nobody except his hostess, and she was indicated to him by a mere wave of Mr. Britling's hand. "That's Edith," he said, and returned at once to his car to put it away. Mrs. Britling was a tall, freckled woman with pretty bright brown hair and preoccupied brown eyes. She welcomed him with a handshake, and then a wonderful English parlourmaid--she at least was according to expectations--took his grip-sack and guided him to his room. "Lunch, sir," she said, "is outside," and closed the door and left him to that and a towel-covered can of hot water.

text: Mr. Britling had never been more aware of his shortcomings as a writer—feeble, foolish, poorly read, and rash—yet never had he been more unshakably certain that a divine spirit moved within him, appointing him to help shape a new way of life on earth. The part he might play could be the humblest imaginable against the scale of the work, but for him it must now become his deepest calling. And it caused him an almost unbearable sorrow that his offerings, for all his longing, remained so meager in substance, so feebly conceived. He perpetually felt himself hovering at the edge of some radiant, eloquent declaration of his purpose; perpetually his writing fell short, a flimsy betrayal of his heart’s urgency; perpetually he found his efforts frail and fruitless. In this case, at the start, he had glimpsed with luminous clarity a message of fellowship, of pardon, of a shared summons. To whom could such tidings be more fittingly directed than to those grieving parents, and from whom could they come more tellingly than from himself? And now he surveyed what he had made of that message. To his weary mind it read as a pitifully weary piece of work. It held no radiance, no profundity. It read like the speech of a debating club.
source_text: Never had it been so plain to Mr. Britling that he was a weak, silly, ill-informed and hasty-minded writer, and never had he felt so invincible a conviction that the Spirit of God was in him, and that it fell to him to take some part in the establishment of a new order of living upon the earth; it might be the most trivial part by the scale of the task, but for him it was to be now his supreme concern. And it was an almost intolerable grief to him that his services should be, for all his desire, so poor in quality, so weak in conception. Always he seemed to be on the verge of some illuminating and beautiful statement of his cause; always he was finding his writing inadequate, a thin treachery to the impulse of his heart, always he was finding his effort weak and ineffective. In this instance, at the outset he seemed to see with a golden clearness the message of brotherhood, or forgiveness, of a common call. To whom could such a message be better addressed than to those sorrowing parents; from whom could it come with a better effect than from himself? And now he read what he had made of this message. It seemed to his jaded mind a pitifully jaded effort. It had no light, it had no depth. It was like the disquisition of a debating society.

text: He walked so deep in thought that before long he had lost the way to Pyecrafts—if, indeed, he had ever truly been on it at all. He came instead upon a highway stretching across a level plain, and soon realized, by the faint but discernible outline of the Great Bear glimmering in the blue above, that he was heading straight north. Well, in due course he would turn south and west; there was time enough for that. Just now he wanted to feel, to think. How could he best serve England in the immense conflict for which the hushed beauty and emptiness of this night seemed to be waiting? But in truth he was not thinking at all—only feeling, feeling a sense of wonder such as he had not known since youth had slipped away from him. This war might bring an end to nearly everything in the world as he had understood it; the thought worked its way slowly through the moonlight into his awareness, until it took hold and dominated his mind.
source_text: He went so thoughtfully that presently he missed the road to Pyecrafts--if ever he had been on the road to Pyecrafts at all--altogether. He found himself upon a highway running across a flattish plain, and presently discovered by the sight of the Great Bear, faint but traceable in the blue overhead, that he was going due north. Well, presently he would turn south and west; that in good time; now he wanted to feel; he wanted to think. How could he best help England in the vast struggle for which the empty silence and beauty of this night seemed to be waiting? But indeed he was not thinking at all, but feeling, feeling wonder, as he had never felt it since his youth had passed from him. This war might end nearly everything in the world as he had known the world; that idea struggled slowly through the moonlight into consciousness, and won its way to dominance in his mind.

text: I want you to know that, amid all the strain and fervour of this war, we here in Matching’s Easy have not forgotten your son, who was our friend. He belonged among us, held our affection, and still has friends in this place who remain loyal to his memory. We found him upright and good company, and we feel a portion of your grief as our own. I have gathered for you a few snapshots I happen to have, in which you will see him in sunlight, and which may help you to picture a little more clearly the life he led here. One in particular I have marked. Our family is dining out of doors, and you will notice beside your son a young man, perhaps a year younger, touching glasses with him. I have placed a cross above his head. He is my eldest boy, he was very precious to me, and he too has been killed in this war. You see them there, smiling so warmly at one another.
source_text: _I want to tell you that all the stress and passion of this war has not made us here in Matching's Easy forget our friend your son. He was one of us, he had our affection, he had friends here who are still his friends. We found him honourable and companionable, and we share something of your loss. I have got together for you a few snapshots I chance to possess in which you will see him in the sunshine, and which will enable you perhaps to picture a little more definitely than you would otherwise do the life he led here. There is one particularly that I have marked. Our family is lunching out-of-doors, and you will see that next to your son is a youngster, a year or so his junior, who is touching glasses with him. I have put a cross over his head. He is my eldest son, he was very dear to me, and he too has been, killed in this war. They are, you see, smiling very pleasantly at each other._

text: The sinking of the _Lusitania_ came fast upon the _Falaba_ atrocity, where German seamen were said to have mocked the drowning as they struggled in the sea. A swell of genuine fury then swept across the Empire. At last, hatred was breeding hatred. Fierce riots erupted in Britain and in South Africa. Pitiful little German barbers and pastry-cooks and the like ran for their lives, paying the price for the Kaiser’s and Herr Ballin’s fleeting gratification. Dozens of German dwellings in England were gutted and plundered; many Germans suffered brutal handling. War is war. Close behind the _Lusitania_ uproar arrived the Bryce Report, with its unsparing procession of witnesses, its details of innumerable deeds of cruelty, arrogant unreason, and foulness in Belgium and occupied France. There came too the choking agony of "gas," the deployment of liquid fire, and a fresh sharpening of the ferocity of the front-line struggle. For a while it seemed as if the taking of captives along the western line might end altogether. Stories of torment and dismemberment, stories of the sort that spring from nowhere and out of nothing, and poison the mind toward the most merciless vengeance, drifted up and down the opposing trenches.
source_text: The _Falaba_ crime, in which the German sailors were reported to have jeered at the drowning victims in the water, was followed by the sinking of the _Lusitania_. At that a wave of real anger swept through the Empire. Hate was begetting hate at last. There were violent riots in Great Britain and in South Africa. Wretched little German hairdressers and bakers and so forth fled for their lives, to pay for the momentary satisfaction of the Kaiser and Herr Ballin. Scores of German homes in England were wrecked and looted; hundreds of Germans maltreated. War is war. Hard upon the _Lusitania_ storm came the publication of the Bryce Report, with its relentless array of witnesses, its particulars of countless acts of cruelty and arrogant unreason and uncleanness in Belgium and the occupied territory of France. Came also the gasping torture of "gas," the use of flame jets, and a new exacerbation of the savagery of the actual fighting. For a time it seemed as though the taking of prisoners along the western front would cease. Tales of torture and mutilation, tales of the kind that arise nowhere and out of nothing, and poison men's minds to the most pitiless retaliations, drifted along the opposing fronts....

text: And then Mrs. Britling picked up their talk once more. She confessed she knew so little of American life that she wasn’t even sure if they grew strawberries over there. In any case, here they had reached the peak of the season, and a particularly fine one at that. The roses were at their height as well. It was among the most cherished little vanities of the English to believe their apples, their roses, and their strawberries were unrivaled anywhere on earth.
source_text: And then Mrs. Britling resumed her conversation with him. She was so ignorant, she said, of things American, that she did not even know if they had strawberries there. At any rate, here they were at the crest of the season, and in a very good year. And in the rose season too. It was one of the dearest vanities of English people to think their apples and their roses and their strawberries the best in the world.

text: What deepened his unease over Mrs. Harrowdean was a fresh attitude she had lately adopted toward Mrs. Britling. She had begun with the hasty conviction that Mr. Britling cared little for his wife, only to discover that, in truth, he was in certain respects deeply protective of her. This she took as a scandalous betrayal. Rather than relish the contradiction, she nursed a sense of infidelity. For days she smouldered in bewildered bitterness, then startled her lover with a string of pronouncements—hostile, withering—upon the mistress of the Dower House.
source_text: What exacerbated his feeling about Mrs. Harrowdean was a new line she had recently taken with regard to Mrs. Britling. From her first rash assumption that Mr. Britling was indifferent to his wife, she had come to realise that on the contrary he was in some ways extremely tender about his wife. This struck her as an outrageous disloyalty. Instead of appreciating a paradox she resented an infidelity. She smouldered with perplexed resentment for some days, and then astonished her lover by a series of dissertations of a hostile and devastating nature upon the lady of the Dower House.

text: The words he had spoken that afternoon at Claverings drifted back into his mind. They were, he felt certain, the very words that now demanded utterance. This war might be conceived as the most exalted of conflicts, humanity’s ultimate stand against the tyranny of nations and the lust for conquest; or it might be nothing more than a blind clash of peoples, sheer ruin and calamity. Its vast meanings, he sensed, must not be squandered in trivial quarrels over the war’s lesser questions. But were those vast meanings being articulated with sufficient clarity? Were they reaching the great body of liberal and peace-minded thinkers? He let the car slow, step by step, as these thoughts pressed in, until at last he halted altogether… “Certain things must be said plainly,” he murmured. “Certain things—the soul of England… The deep, too-long-silent yearning for gentleness and justice… Now is the hour to speak. It must be delivered as direct as her gunnery now, as true as the course of her ships.”
source_text: The phrases he had used that afternoon at Claverings came back presently into his head. They were, he felt assured, the phrases that had to be said now. This war could be seen as the noblest of wars, as the crowning struggle of mankind against national dominance and national aggression; or else it was a mere struggle of nationalities and pure destruction and catastrophe. Its enormous significances, he felt, must not be lost in any petty bickering about the minor issues of the conflict. But were these enormous significances being stated clearly enough? Were they being understood by the mass of liberal and pacific thinkers? He drove more and more slowly as these questions crowded upon his attention until at last he came to a stop altogether.... "Certain things must be said clearly," he whispered. "Certain things--The meaning of England.... The deep and long-unspoken desire for kindliness and fairness.... Now is the time for speaking. It must be put as straight now as her gun-fire, as honestly as the steering of her ships."

text: He became entangled in a fierce dispute with his own prose. In sheer frustration at his inability to articulate, at his faltering command over those unruly words and phrases—each dragging behind it a train of private echoes and insinuations that thwarted his intent—he forgot entirely about his Pomeranian parents. He reread the offending sentence.
source_text: He fell into an extraordinary quarrel with his style. He forgot about those Pomeranian parents altogether in his exasperation at his own inexpressiveness, at his incomplete control of these rebel words and phrases that came trailing each its own associations and suggestions to hamper his purpose with it. He read over the offending sentence.

text: Mr. Direck, following a habit that had quietly overtaken him all evening, searched the room for Miss Cissie Corner and could not see her. Then, suddenly, there she was—coming down the staircase with the enigmatic baby cradled in her arms. She lifted a finger to her lips in caution, then looked down at the sleeping child. She seemed a silvery Madonna. And Mr. Direck recalled that the mystery of that baby was still unresolved.
source_text: Then Mr. Direck, in accordance with a habit that had been growing on him throughout the evening, looked around for Miss Cissie Corner and failed to find her. And then behold she was descending the staircase with the mysterious baby in her arms. She held up a warning finger, and then glanced at her sleeping burthen. She looked like a silvery Madonna. And Mr. Direck remembered that he was still in doubt about that baby....

text: Mr. Direck was presented as an American traveler, pleased to encounter a classic English country home, and Lady Homartyn, with practiced ease, enumerated the merits of her Tudor specimen. Unfamiliar with the aristocracy, Mr. Direck found himself uncertain whether to address a baroness as "My Lady" or "Your Ladyship," so he prudently avoided any direct form until Mr. Britling supplied a cue. Mr. Britling soon referred to her as "Lady Homartyn." She guided Mr. Direck to a seat beside a woman whose name he missed, but who had been closely involved with the British Embassy in Washington, then consigned Mr. Britling to the Rt. Hon. George Philbert, eager to debate certain passages in the latest essay collection. The Washington lady’s talk was informed yet undemanding, allowing Mr. Direck to spare some notice for the overall impression of the gathering.
source_text: Mr. Direck was introduced as a travelling American gratified to see a typical English country house, and Lady Homartyn in an habituated way ran over the points of her Tudor specimen. Mr. Direck was not accustomed to titled people, and was suddenly in doubt whether you called a baroness "My Lady" or "Your Ladyship," so he wisely avoided any form of address until he had a lead from Mr. Britling. Mr. Britling presently called her "Lady Homartyn." She took Mr. Direck and sat him down beside a lady whose name he didn't catch, but who had had a lot to do with the British Embassy at Washington, and then she handed Mr. Britling over to the Rt. Honble. George Philbert, who was anxious to discuss certain points in the latest book of essays. The conversation of the lady from Washington was intelligent but not exacting, and Mr. Direck was able to give a certain amount of attention to the general effect of the scene.

text: They settled into their seats, and Mr. Direck began to lay out his argument. He fumbled with the task, self-conscious and awkward, for he had been brooding over it with painful intensity, and he dreaded appearing ponderous or overly philosophical—anything other than natural and unguarded in Cecily’s eyes. Even once he had presented his case, he sensed that it came across as trite and underwhelming. And yet, when he had first worked through it in his mind, it had struck him as deeply meaningful and vividly alive.
source_text: They sat down, and Mr. Direck opened his case. He was shy and clumsy about opening it, because he had been thinking dreadfully hard about it, and he hated to seem heavy or profound or anything but artless and spontaneous to Cecily. And he felt even when he did open his case that the effect of it was platitudinous and disappointing. Yet when he had thought it out it had seemed very profound and altogether living.

text: The image Mr. Direck formed of Mr. Britling was that of a man buoyed by unflagging contentment. Yet there were moments when fate exacted from Mr. Britling a heavy toll for his prevailing lightness of spirit, delivering it all at once in dark, concentrated grief. On certain nights—particularly after stretches of heightened fervor and mental strain—the bill would come due, and Mr. Britling would lie sprawled and moaning beneath a tempestuous heaven of misery, a restless, ravenous misery that lashed him like whips.
source_text: The impression Mr. Britling had made upon Mr. Direck was one of indefatigable happiness. But there were times when Mr. Britling was called upon to pay for his general cheerful activity in lump sums of bitter sorrow. There were nights--and especially after seasons of exceptional excitement and nervous activity--when the reckoning would be presented and Mr. Britling would welter prostrate and groaning under a stormy sky of unhappiness--active insatiable unhappiness--a beating with rods.

text: You can’t even start to grasp in England the scale of what you’re facing. You’ve no notion what it’s like to live in a land where everyone—women, the old, the settled middle-aged—treats war with the same gravity as commerce. They feel not a shred of remorse. I can’t speak for Germany before the war, since I’d barely stepped off my train when it broke out; but the Germany of now is a single vast military encampment. The place is swarming with troops. And each soldier carries his uniform, his boots, his weapons, and his gear.
source_text: "You don't begin to realise in England what you are up against. You have no idea what it means to be in a country where everybody, the women, the elderly people, the steady middle-aged men, are taking war as seriously as business. They haven't the slightest compunction. I don't know what Germany was like before the war, I had hardly gotten out of my train before the war began; but Germany to-day is one big armed camp. It's all crawling with soldiers. And every soldier has his uniform and his boots and his arms and his kit.

text: Dr. Alden's sprained ankle mended far more swiftly than he wished. From the outset he insisted that such an injury could easily be managed with a cane, that he was perfectly fit to resume his travels and fend for himself in any lodging, that he lingered at Thornfield Manor solely because he delighted in basking in Lady Marwood's hospitality and Lord Marwood's conversation. Yet in truth he basked as fully as possible in the vivacity and warmth of Miss Eleanor Vane, and for well over half this interval Lord Marwood was entirely absent from the estate.
source_text: Mr. Direck's broken wrist healed sooner than he desired. From the first he had protested that it was the sort of thing that one can carry about in a sling, that he was quite capable of travelling about and taking care of himself in hotels, that he was only staying on at Matching's Easy because he just loved to stay on and wallow in Mrs. Britling's kindness and Mr. Britling's company. While as a matter of fact he wallowed as much as he could in the freshness and friendliness of Miss Cecily Corner, and for more than a third of this period Mr. Britling was away from home altogether.

text: The ingredients then pass to a cook—an ordinary infantryman transformed into one through a brief ritual. Someone says, “You’re the cook now,” and he tries his best to live up to it. Typically he starts by roasting or baking, guessing when the meat is ready, and later chops up whatever remains of the joints to make a stew for the following day. A stew is simply hacked meat boiled in a large pot, with a thick layer of grease on top. Once you’ve eaten your fill, you feel like sitting quietly. The men usually eat in a sizeable tent or barn. We have a barn. It isn’t a clean barn, and to heighten the picnic atmosphere there aren’t enough plates, knives, or forks. (Believe me, no one in the army can count past eight or ten.) The corporals, after their morning duties, must do the carving. When they’ve finished, they tell me they feel as though they’ve already had their dinner. They sit around looking drained, then drift off to the village pub. (I’ll likely be made a corporal before long.) On these islands, before the war started, women outnumbered men by roughly a million. (See the Fabian Society pamphlets, so fashionable now among the young.) Not one of those women has been entrusted by the government with the demanding work of cooking and serving food to our troops. No man from the ordinary ranks ever prepares a meal until he joins the army.… Any food left after the stew, or otherwise spoiled by the cook, gets discarded. We throw out buckets of it. _We bury meat._
source_text: "Then the raw material is handed over to a cook. He is a common soldier who has been made into a cook by a simple ceremony. He is told, 'You are a cook.' He does his best to be. Usually he roasts or bakes to begin with, guessing when the joint is done, afterwards he hacks up what is left of his joints and makes a stew for next day. A stew is hacked meat boiled up in a big pot. It has much fat floating on the top. After you have eaten your fill you want to sit about quiet. The men are fed usually in a large tent or barn. We have a barn. It is not a clean barn, and just to make it more like a picnic there are insufficient plates, knives and forks. (I tell you, no army people can count beyond eight or ten.) The corporals after their morning's work have to carve. When they have done carving they tell me they feel they have had enough dinner. They sit about looking pale, and wander off afterwards to the village pub. (I shall probably become a corporal soon.) In these islands before the war began there was a surplus of women over men of about a million. (See the publications of the Fabian Society, now so popular among the young.) None of these women have been trusted by the government with the difficult task of cooking and giving out food to our soldiers. No man of the ordinary soldier class ever cooks anything until he is a soldier.... All food left over after the stew or otherwise rendered uneatable by the cook is thrown away. We throw away pail-loads. _We bury meat_....

text: A single overwhelming obsession was evident in all of them. The German cartoonist appeared incapable of depicting his foes unless they were clad in absurdly snug breeches or entirely naked; nor could he portray them without plunging a blade or bayonet, dripping crimson, into the most unseemly regions of their bodies. This was the recurring theme of the war as the German satirists rendered it. "But," Mr. Britling remarked, "such images cannot possibly reflect the prevailing mentality across Germany."
source_text: One incredible craving was manifest in every one of them. The German caricaturist seemed unable to represent his enemies except in extremely tight trousers or in none; he was equally unable to represent them without thrusting a sword or bayonet, spluttering blood, into the more indelicate parts of their persons. This was the _leit-motif_ of the war as the German humorists presented it. "But," said Mr. Britling, "these things can't represent anything like the general state of mind in Germany."

text: Mr. Britling found himself adrift in a spell of liberated fancy. Such liberation was among the earliest visitations of the war upon cultivated intellects. Realities that had once appeared immovably fixed now visibly melted; what had seemed granite quickened with breath. Every frontier, every regime, revealed itself as a makeshift arrangement. He spoke of his World Congress assembling year upon year, until it shed the husk of conjecture and ripened into mere sober foresight; he spoke of the "palpable necessity" of a Supreme Tribunal for all nations.
