He glimpsed that vision seated at the Hague, but Mr. Carmine inclined rather toward Delhi or Samarkand or Alexandria or Nankin. "Let us shake off the mirage of Europe, in any case," said Mr. Carmine....
source_text: Mr. Britling was in a phase of imaginative release. Such a release was one of the first effects of the war upon many educated minds. Things that had seemed solid forever were visibly in flux; things that had seemed stone were alive. Every boundary, every government, was seen for the provisional thing it was. He talked of his World Congress meeting year by year, until it ceased to be a speculation and became a mere intelligent anticipation; he talked of the "manifest necessity" of a Supreme Court for the world. He beheld that vision at the Hague, but Mr. Carmine preferred Delhi or Samarkand or Alexandria or Nankin. "Let us get away from the delusion of Europe anyhow," said Mr. Carmine....

text: Things have grown far more animated here than before. Our artillery has started speaking. They discharge in bursts lasting an hour or so, three or four times daily, and just when they appear to be ceasing they resume once more. Two days past the Germans abruptly found the measure of our trenches and commenced hammering us with high explosive.... Well, it wears on a man. You can never quite tell when the next crash will arrive, and that keeps your nerves strung tight; it seems to clench your muscles and exhaust you. We have done nothing but keep our heads down all day, and I feel as drained as though I had tramped twenty miles. Then 'crack,' one lands close, and there comes a glare and everything scatters. It is a furious kind of chaos. One fell far too near for comfort; as close as the old oil jars stand from the barn court door. It flung me headlong and showered me with gravel. When I rose there was twenty yards of trench battered into a mere crater, and men sprawled about, some of them moaning and one buried to his shoulders. We had to set to and dig them out as best we could....
source_text: "Things are much livelier here than they were. Our guns are getting to work. They are firing in spells of an hour or so, three or four times a day, and just when they seem to be leaving off they begin again. The Germans suddenly got the range of our trenches the day before yesterday, and begun to pound us with high explosive.... Well, it's trying. You never seem quite to know when the next bang is coming, and that keeps your nerves hung up; it seems to tighten your muscles and tire you. We've done nothing but lie low all day, and I feel as weary as if I had marched twenty miles. Then 'whop,' one's near you, and there is a flash and everything flies. It's a mad sort of smash-about. One came much too close to be pleasant; as near as the old oil jars are from the barn court door. It bowled me clean over and sent a lot of gravel over me. When I got up there was twenty yards of trench smashed into a mere hole, and men lying about, and some of them groaning and one three-quarters buried. We had to turn to and get them out as well as we could....

text: Oh, it makes me sick of mankind to see so many soldiers and so few assassins in the world… after all we have witnessed. If men did their duty with the blade, there would be no such creature as a War Lord. Not one. The Kaiser and his sons and his sons’ sons would know nothing but terror for the rest of their days. Fear would only stop its hunt when the coffin sank into the earth. Fear on the water, fear on the land, for the ship he boarded, the train he rode in; fear while he slept, for the death lurking in his dreams; fear when he woke, for the death hiding in every shadow; fear in every crowd, fear whenever he was alone. Fear would track him through the woods, crouch in the turn of the stair; it would taint every mouthful so strangely that he would long to spit it out.
source_text: "Oh, it makes me despise humanity to see how many soldiers and how few assassins there are in the world.... After the things we have seen. If people did their duty by the dagger there wouldn't be such a thing as a War Lord in the world. Not one.... The Kaiser and his sons and his sons' sons would know nothing but fear now for all their lives. Fear would only cease to pursue as the coffin went down into the grave. Fear by sea, fear by land, for the vessel he sailed in, the train he travelled in, fear when he slept for the death in his dreams, fear when he waked for the death in every shadow; fear in every crowd, fear whenever he was alone. Fear would stalk him through the trees, hide in the corner of the staircase; make all his food taste perplexingly, so that he would want to spit it out...."

text: The vivid novelty of this official role did more than any other remedy to quiet Mr. Direck’s hunger for the Robinson story, and when his talk with Mr. Britling soon picked up again, he veered without pause toward the astonishing unearthing of a kinsman so long vanished and wholly unimagined. “I guess it’s a typically American impulse,” he said with a note of apology, “but I half considered pushing on, come Monday, to Market Saffron—the old Hinkinson country—and spending a day or two poking among the gravestones in the churchyard.”
source_text: The bright interest of this consulship helped Mr. Direck more than anything to get the better of his Robinson-anecdote crave, and when presently he found his dialogue with Mr. Britling resumed, he turned at once to this remarkable discovery of his long lost and indeed hitherto unsuspected relative. "It's an American sort of thing to do, I suppose," he said apologetically, "but I almost thought of going on, on Monday, to Market Saffron, which was the locality of the Hinkinsons, and just looking about at the tombstones in the churchyard for a day or so."

text: She had a gift for irritating Mr. Britling's father, a talent she also wielded, though less sharply, against the son. But the elder Mr. Britling never mastered the trick of ignoring her. Her approach—if one can call a personality's unforced style an approach—was an unshakably superior grasp of things, a quiet, barely hidden conviction that every remark uttered in her presence was mistaken and mostly ridiculous, delivered with a poise that was steady, self-possessed, and measured. She might have come into the world with it; it is documented that by age ten she was deemed a remarkably difficult child. She might have come into the world wearing the look of someone who thought the physician a fool and felt she could handle all of this far better herself. Mr. Britling had encountered her only in her full maturity. As a youngster, he had savored her whispered disclosures—about others and the widespread disregard of her counsel. He matured into a fondness for her—most people grew rather fond of her—and came to invest a certain weight in her elusive approval. She could be generous at times, and she was often preposterous.
source_text: She had a knack of exasperating Mr. Britling's father, a knack which to a less marked degree she also possessed in relation to the son. But Mr. Britling senior never acquired the art of disregarding her. Her method--if one may call the natural expression of a personality a method--was an invincibly superior knowledge, a firm and ill-concealed belief that all statements made in her hearing were wrong and most of them absurd, and a manner calm, assured, restrained. She may have been born with it; it is on record that at the age of ten she was pronounced a singularly trying child. She may have been born with the air of thinking the doctor a muff and knowing how to manage all this business better. Mr. Britling had known her only in her ripeness. As a boy, he had enjoyed her confidences--about other people and the general neglect of her advice. He grew up rather to like her--most people rather liked her--and to attach a certain importance to her unattainable approval. She was sometimes kind, she was frequently absurd....

text: As Mr. Britling sat at his desk that morning, contemplating the immense horizons of possibility that war was unveiling, the disaster increasingly took on a redemptive character. "I suppose the world can only remake itself through upheavals like these," I remarked. And for much of that afternoon he was inclined to hope that the vast military apparatus would not break apart too swiftly. "We need the nations to feel how much they require each other," he said. "Too short a conflict might just lead to a scramble for spoils. The Englishman must discover his reliance on the Irishman, the Russian must be shown the worth of learning and the fellowship of the Pole.... Europe will now have to turn toward Asia, and acknowledge that Indians and Chinamen are also 'white.'... But these insights demand time and strain if they are to be truly absorbed...."
source_text: As Mr. Britling had sat at his desk that morning and surveyed the stupendous vistas of possibility that war was opening, the catastrophe had taken on a more and more beneficial quality. "I suppose that it is only through such crises as these that the world can reconstruct itself," I said. And, on the whole that afternoon he was disposed to hope that the great military machine would not smash itself too easily. "We want the nations to feel the need of one another," he said. "Too brief a campaign might lead to a squabble for plunder. The Englishman has to learn his dependence on the Irishman, the Russian has to be taught the value of education and the friendship of the Pole.... Europe will now have to look to Asia, and recognise that Indians and Chinamem are also 'white.'... But these lessons require time and stresses if they are to be learnt properly...."

text: Once the initial shock of the food crisis and a fleeting monetary dread had passed, the immense, sluggish routine of daily English life reasserted its hold. When people approached the banks for the freshly issued paper currency, the banks—almost apologetically—offered gold instead. The new notes were in woefully short supply, while gold remained abundant. Following that first sensation of an all-encompassing disaster, an eerie calm settled, as though nothing had occurred at all.
source_text: After the jolt of the food panic and a brief, financial scare, the vast inertia of everyday life in England asserted itself. When the public went to the banks for the new paper money, the banks tendered gold--apologetically. The supply of the new notes was very insufficient, and there was plenty of gold. After the first impression that a universal catastrophe had happened there was an effect as if nothing had happened.

text: Herr Heinrich used Mr. Britling as his prime example. Were Mr. Britling a German, he would undoubtedly hold some kind of official designation, a clear rank, a sphere of duty. In this country, he wasn’t even addressed as Herr Doktor. He spoke freely. No one gave him awards; no one rebuked him. When Herr Heinrich inquired about his standing—whether he ranked above or below Mr. Bernard Shaw, or Mr. Arnold White, or Mr. Garvin, or any other commentator—he simply made light of it. No one here, it seemed, possessed a formal title or occupied a fixed place in the hierarchy. Take Mr. Lawrence Carmine: he studied Eastern affairs, was attached to some London institution that received Indian students, and was a Geheimrath—
source_text: Herr Heinrich made Mr. Britling his instance. If Mr. Britling were a German he would certainly have some sort of title, a definite position, responsibility. Here he was not even called Herr Doktor. He said what he liked. Nobody rewarded him; nobody reprimanded him. When Herr Heinrich asked him of his position, whether he was above or below Mr. Bernard Shaw or Mr. Arnold White or Mr. Garvin or any other publicist, he made jokes. Nobody here seemed to have a title and nobody seemed to have a definite place. There was Mr. Lawrence Carmine; he was a student of Oriental questions; he had to do with some public institution in London that welcomed Indian students; he was a Geheimrath--

text: When I level these accusations at Germany, I feel little urge to claim any moral high ground for Britain. There has been scant glory in this war for Germany, Britain, or Russia; we three happen to be the largest belligerents, but the true radiance belongs to unyielding France. It is France, Belgium, and Serbia who stand out as the heroic nations. They have fought on the defensive, surpassing all hopes, for beloved homeland and liberty. For them, this war has been a struggle of clear, unambiguous stakes, to which they have risen with complete nobility. Both Englishmen and Germans may well envy them that clarity. I appeal to you, as an honest man tempered by the brutal teachings of this war, to join me in my fervent wish to see France, Belgium, and Serbia emerge from all this carnage and conflict restored, expanded to the full reach of their national identity, justified and safe. Of Russia I will not speak here; let me turn at once to tell you about my own country, noting only that between England and Russia there are endless parallels. We share similar complexities, kindred predicaments. We have, for instance, an imported royal house, a spirit-crushing State Church that stifles and taints the education of our governing class, a populace estranged from a secretive government, and the same inherited disdain for science. We have our Irelands and our Polands. Even our kings bear a curious resemblance…
source_text: _When I bring these charges against Germany I have little disposition to claim any righteousness for Britain. There has been small splendour in this war for either Germany or Britain or Russia; we three have chanced to be the biggest of the combatants, but the glory lies with invincible France. It is France and Belgium and Serbia who shine as the heroic lands. They have fought defensively and beyond all expectation, for dear land and freedom. This war for them has been a war of simple, definite issues, to which they have risen with an entire nobility. Englishman and German alike may well envy them that simplicity. I look to you, as an honest man schooled by the fierce lessons of this war, to meet me in my passionate desire to see France, Belgium and Serbia emerge restored from all this blood and struggle, enlarged to the limits of their nationality, vindicated and secure. Russia I will not write about here; let me go on at once to tell you about my own country; remarking only that between England and Russia there are endless parallelisms. We have similar complexities, kindred difficulties. We have for instance an imported dynasty, we have a soul-destroying State Church which cramps and poisons the education of our ruling class, we have a people out of touch with a secretive government, and the same traditional contempt for science. We have our Irelands and Polands. Even our kings bear a curious likeness...._

text: There, to his misfortune, Mr. Direck spotted an issue of that guileless American journal _The New Republic_, resting beside a few copies of _The Fatherland_, a German-leaning magazine that in those days pressed itself upon British authors with relentless resolve. Mr. Direck observed that _The New Republic_ represented a worthy endeavor from "_la Jeunesse Américaine_." Mr. Britling surveyed the worthy endeavor with a weary, indifferent gaze.
source_text: There Mr. Direck was unfortunate enough to notice a copy of that innocent American publication _The New Republic_, lying close to two or three numbers of _The Fatherland_, a pro-German periodical which at that time inflicted itself upon English writers with the utmost determination. Mr. Direck remarked that _The New Republic_ was an interesting effort on the part of "_la Jeunesse Américaine_." Mr. Britling regarded the interesting effort with a jaded, unloving eye.

text: Deep down," he continued with bitterness, "I imagine we believed the French would shoulder the brutal fighting on land while we merely held the seas. If we thought at all. We're so measured, so tangled in caveats and scruples... so unhurried. And now France has fallen. We must fight for her across the wreckage of Paris—because you and I, Manning, never understood the magnitude of this, because we traded in abstractions when we should have been training and toiling. Because we clung to 'business as usual' and all those comfortable phrases while Western civilisation convulsed in its final throes. If this becomes another 1871, but vaster, aimed not just at France but at all Europe, if Prussian brutality is to trample civilisation, if France is crushed and Belgium slaughtered, then life forfeits its meaning. Beside such a crisis, no other cause, no other concern, holds weight. Yet what are we doing to shape the outcome—you and I? How can it end in anything but German victory if millions like us simply stand aside...
source_text: "At the back of our minds," he went on bitterly, "I suppose we thought the French would do the heavy work on land--while we stood by at sea. So far as we thought at all. We're so temperate-minded; we're so full of qualifications and discretions.... And so leisurely.... Well, France is down. We've got to fight for France now over the ruins of Paris. Because you and I, Manning, didn't grasp the scale of it, because we indulged in generalisations when we ought to have been drilling and working. Because we've been doing 'business as usual' and all the rest of that sort of thing, while Western civilisation has been in its death agony. If this is to be another '71, on a larger scale and against not merely France but all Europe, if Prussianism is to walk rough-shod over civilisation, if France is to be crushed and Belgium murdered, then life is not worth having. Compared with such an issue as that no other issue, no other interest matters. Yet what are we doing to decide it--you and I? How can it end in anything but a German triumph if you and I, by the million, stand by...."

text: High upon the folded hills, cradled in forest, the hamlet clings, and at its heart stand the tavern and the lime tree and—Konrad Voss. Or at least Konrad Voss once stood there. Whether he remains now, only the shifting wind of time could say; should he live to see a century, no kindly English step will ever again come trudging down the path of the Blaue Breiecke or the Weisse Streiche to taste his welcome; between them flow torrents of blood, and a thousand bitter recollections....
source_text: The little village perches high among the hills and woods, and at its very centre is the inn and the linden tree and--Adam Meyer. Or at least Adam Meyer _was_ there. Whether he is there now, only the spirit of change can tell; if he live to be a hundred no friendly English will ever again come tramping along by the track of the Blaue Breiecke or the Weisse Streiche to enjoy his hospitality; there are rivers of blood between, and a thousand memories of hate....

text: Mr. Britling’s wife assisted in stowing a valise, then rode beside him to the platform so she might steer the motor back to the Dower House; the gardener’s lad, who once managed such trifling errands, had lately departed to take unskilled work at a munitions shed near Chelmsford. Seated in the dawdling cross-country train that bore him toward the Filmington junction, Mr. Britling could not properly grasp what had befallen the elderly woman. He nursed the foolish notion that it was just like her to thrust herself into matters this way. She had ever been so hardy and unyielding a figure that, until he beheld her, he could not picture her truly and grievously wounded.
source_text: Mrs. Britling helped him pack a bag, and came with him to the station in order to drive the car back to the Dower House; for the gardener's boy who had hitherto attended to these small duties had now gone off as an unskilled labourer to some munition works at Chelmsford. Mr. Britling sat in the slow train that carried him across country to the junction for Filmington, and failed altogether to realise what had happened to the old lady. He had an absurd feeling that it was characteristic of her to intervene in affairs in this manner. She had always been so tough and unbent an old lady that until he saw her he could not imagine her as being really seriously and pitifully hurt....

text: And with Hugh safely tucked into a back chamber of his thoughts—still a boy, shouldering a boy’s burden, nowhere near a man—Mr. Britling could let his fancy gallop unchecked toward visions of a nation in arms. From the notion of universal commitment to the conflict he glided without friction into the image of all Britain standing arrayed in sober battle order. Across the chasms of stubborn fact. He himself stood ready to declare, and therefore he sensed that the great body of the British must stand ready to declare to the government: “We place ourselves at your command. This is no diplomats’ quarrel, no War Office campaign; it is the people’s war entire. We are all prepared to set aside our daily trades and pledge our goods and our persons. Whims and private pursuits belong to peacetime. Take us and deploy us however you see fit. Take everything we have.” When he conjured the government thus, he forgot the governing set he actually knew. The baggy-trousered Raeburn, the neat, ever-watchful Philbert, Lady Frensham in full cry, the stern and absurd Carson, sodden Bandershoot and cunning Taper, slippery Asquith, the silver-tongued but hollow George, the immovable Grey—all vanished from his mind; every one of those representative masters of how affairs are managed in Great Britain dissolved in the radiance of his imaginative leap; he forgot the sodden debates, the flailing press, the feints, the cabals, the covert deals of the country weekend, the “public-school honour” of adult men, the pervasive petty dishonesty of thought; he thought only of a purified, ideal government that truly governed. He thought hazily of something behind and above them, England, the tutelary spirit of the land; something of composed authority and fixed resolve. He pictured this spectral ruler miraculously furnished with charts and tallies against this crowning emergency, which for so many years had been the most glaring likelihood confronting the nation. His mind vaulted ahead to the conception of a great people reluctantly swinging its immense strength into the waging of a just defensive war, and supplied the plain corollaries of design and reckoning. He believed that somewhere “up there” there must be minds that could count and had counted every item we might require for such a contest, and planners who had schemed and measured down to workable and governable particulars....
source_text: And having thrust Hugh safely into the background of his mind as--juvenile, doing a juvenile share, no sort of man yet--Mr. Britling could give a free rein to his generous imaginations of a national uprising. From the idea of a universal participation in the struggle he passed by an easy transition to an anticipation of all Britain armed and gravely embattled. Across gulfs of obstinate reality. He himself was prepared to say, and accordingly he felt that the great mass of the British must be prepared to say to the government: "Here we are at your disposal. This is not a diplomatists' war nor a War Office war; this is a war of the whole people. We are all willing and ready to lay aside our usual occupations and offer our property and ourselves. Whim and individual action are for peace times. Take us and use us as you think fit. Take all we possess." When he thought of the government in this way, he forgot the governing class he knew. The slack-trousered Raeburn, the prim, attentive Philbert, Lady Frensham at the top of her voice, stern, preposterous Carson, boozy Bandershoot and artful Taper, wily Asquith, the eloquent yet unsubstantial George, and the immobile Grey, vanished out of his mind; all those representative exponents of the way things are done in Great Britain faded in the glow of his imaginative effort; he forgot the dreary debates, the floundering newspapers, the "bluffs," the intrigues, the sly bargains of the week-end party, the "schoolboy honour" of grown men, the universal weak dishonesty in thinking; he thought simply of a simplified and ideal government that governed. He thought vaguely of something behind and beyond them, England, the ruling genius of the land; something with a dignified assurance and a stable will. He imagined this shadowy ruler miraculously provided with schemes and statistics against this supreme occasion which had for so many years been the most conspicuous probability before the country. His mind leaping forwards to the conception of a great nation reluctantly turning its vast resources to the prosecution of a righteous defensive war, filled in the obvious corollaries of plan and calculation. He thought that somewhere "up there" there must be people who could count and who had counted everything that we might need for such a struggle, and organisers who had schemed and estimated down to practicable and manageable details....

text: It was a natural progression, then, when it gradually became clear that behind imperialism and Junkerdom stood not a reluctant, liberal Germany but an apparently unified and fervent nation, to imagine the Germans as uniquely wicked—racially more covetous, haughty, and belligerent than the rest of humanity. On this assumption many English people made themselves at home. They decided the Germans possessed their own peculiar demon—and had to be dealt with as such. This marked the second phase in the nation’s growing alarm, distinguished by the earliest spy scares, the first condemnations of naturalised foreigners, and some anti-German disturbances among the mixed immigrant communities of the East End. Most of the bakers in London’s East End were German, and for several months after the war began they carried on their business undisturbed. Now many of those shops lay in ruins.... It was only in October that the British gave these first indications of sensing they were fighting not just political Germany, but the Germans themselves.
source_text: It was a natural next stage to this, when after all it became manifest that instead of there being a liberal and reluctant Germany at the back of imperialism and Junkerdom, there was apparently one solid and enthusiastic people, to suppose that the Germans were in some distinctive way evil, that they were racially more envious, arrogant, and aggressive than the rest of mankind. Upon that supposition a great number of English people settled. They concluded that the Germans had a peculiar devil of their own--and had to be treated accordingly. That was the second stage in the process of national apprehension, and it was marked by the first beginnings of a spy hunt, by the first denunciation of naturalised aliens, and by some anti-German rioting among the mixed alien population in the East End. Most of the bakers in the East End of London were Germans, and for some months after the war began they went on with their trade unmolested. Now many of these shops were wrecked.... It was only in October that the British gave these first signs of a sense that they were fighting not merely political Germany but the Germans.

text: But now the casual, free-flowing nature of hockey as played at the Dower House revealed itself. Mr. Direck had last glimpsed the tall young Indian gentleman, brimming with energy and bent on havoc, stationed far off on the opposite right flank. Yet now, ignoring the game's more conventional tactics, this young man had decided, without a moment's hesitation and no matter the consequences, to strike the ball with force, and he was hurtling forward like some Asian typhoon at tremendous speed, cutting through the protests of Mr. Britling and the entire formation of his team. Mr. Direck registered his approach only an instant before the crash. There was a kind of impact from which Mr. Direck recovered with the sensation that one cheek had been permanently compressed, though he remained bravely determined to hit the relatively sluggish ball. He and the reeling but steadfast Indian clashed sticks once more. And Mr. Direck came out on top. A lifetime of practice could not have delivered a finer pass to the captain.
source_text: But now the easy-fitting discipline of the Dower House style of hockey became apparent. Mr. Direck had last observed the tall young Indian gentleman, full of vitality and anxious for destruction, far away in the distance on the opposing right wing. But now, regardless of the more formal methods of the game, this young man had resolved, without further delay and at any cost, to hit the ball hard, and he was travelling like some Asiatic typhoon with an extreme velocity across the remonstrances of Mr. Britling and the general order of his side. Mr. Direck became aware of him just before his impact. There was a sort of collision from which Mr. Direck emerged with a feeling that one side of his face was permanently flattened, but still gallantly resolved to hit the comparatively lethargic ball. He and the staggered but resolute Indian clashed sticks again. And Mr. Direck had the best of it. Years of experience couldn't have produced a better pass to the captain....

text: Cissie had read her sister’s state of mind accurately. The brooding fantasy of vengeance had not yet rooted itself deeply enough in Letty’s thoughts to withstand the final proof of Teddy’s death. Stepping out into the sunlit world, she was now nearly certain that he was gone, and she understood with quiet clarity that her vision of a dramatic, fearsome retribution had slipped away. She knew, in all honesty, that she was incapable of any such act.
source_text: Cissie's estimate of her sister's psychology had been just. The reverie of revenge had not yet taken a grip upon Letty's mind sufficiently strong to meet the challenge of this conclusive evidence of Teddy's death. She walked out into a world of sunshine now almost completely convinced that Teddy was dead, and she knew quite well that her dream of some dramatic and terrible vindication had gone from her. She knew that in truth she could do nothing of that sort....

text: He had almost given up hope of uncovering what he was searching for. At last, it surfaced—a creased and battered telegram. He flung it onto the table in front of her, shoved his chair back with an awkward scrape, and hurried from the room. She caught the sound of a stifled sob. She did not have the courage to lift her eyes to his face again.
source_text: It seemed he would never find what he sought. It came at last, a crumpled telegram. He threw it down before her, and then thrust his chair back clumsily and went hastily out of the room. She heard him sob. She had not dared to look at his face again.

text: At the start, the cuffs had hung somewhat slack and rolled above his shoes, but as the match wore on, pleat upon pleat of bunched wool accumulated around his calves. Whenever a pause in play allowed, Mr. Raeburn would take the chance to fold back another half-foot of this gathering. Naturally Mr. Direck assumed such a strategy would finish badly. He had yet to learn that Mr. Raeburn’s flannel trousers were like a spring, forever unspooling and never running dry....
source_text: To begin with they had been rather loose over the feet and turned up, and as the game progressed, fold after fold of concertina-ed flannel gathered about his ankles. Every now and then Mr. Raeburn would seize the opportunity of some respite from the game to turn up a fresh six inches or so of this accumulation. Naturally Mr. Direck expected this policy to end unhappily. He did not know that the flannel trousers of Mr. Raeburn were like a river, that they could come down forever and still remain inexhaustible....

text: The contest around the Britling net abandoned any pretense of sport and transformed into a curious hybrid of brawl and garden party. From the thick of it, Mr. Britling's keeper bellowed, "I've lost the ball! _Get your boots up!_" The packed scrum swayed and staggered toward the uprights. "My leg!" yelped Mr. Manning. "Not on your _life!_"
source_text: The struggle about the Britling goal ceased to be a game and became something between a fight and a social gathering. Mr. Britling's goal-keeper could be heard shouting, "I can't see the ball! _Lift your feet!_" The crowded conflict lurched towards the goal posts. "My shin!" cried Mr. Manning. "No, you _don't!_"

text: Then it came back to him—she bore the same expression as that faded ambrotype of his great-aunt’s niece, the one he’d cherished with a boy’s silent devotion. It was the mouth, the way it curved. Yes! Yes!... He’d half worshipped that likeness once.... A strange impulse stirred in him to confess it to her now....
source_text: Then he remembered that she was like that old daguerreotype of a cousin of his grandmother's that he had fallen in love with when he was a boy. It was her smile. Of course! Of course!... And he'd sort of adored that portrait.... He felt a curious disposition to tell her as much....

text: This is the most justified conflict ever fought," she declared. "Were I an American, I'd feel ashamed to remain on the sidelines at a moment like this. I wish I were a man right now, so I could take action for all the decency and civilization the Germans have trampled. I can't fathom how any man can sit back, content to stay out of it, and simply watch Belgium being annihilated. It's like standing by while a murder unfolds. It's like watching a hound tear a kitten apart....
source_text: "This is the rightest war in history," she said. "If I was an American I should be sorry to be one now and to have to stand out of it. I wish I was a man now so that I could do something for all the decency and civilisation the Germans have outraged. I can't understand how any man can be content to keep out of this, and watch Belgium being destroyed. It is like looking on at a murder. It is like watching a dog killing a kitten...."

text: Her deep-seated urge to divide the nation, to level spiteful charges at entire groups, regions, and officials, was now in full swing. She strained to draw Mr. Britling into an argument, hoping to cast some shadow over his patriotism when set against her own clamorous and rigid notions of “loyalty.”
source_text: Her incurable propensity to split the country, to make mischievous accusations against classes and districts and public servants, was having full play. She did her best to provoke Mr. Britling into a dispute, and throw some sort of imputation upon his patriotism as distinguished from her own noisy and intolerant conceptions of "loyalty."

text: That evening’s supper at the Dower House marked a definite new stage in the Great War’s encroachment upon the settled rhythms and comforts of Matching’s Easy. From its very outbreak the conflict had monopolized every conversation, sweeping Herr Heinrich back to Germany, Teddy to London, and Hugh to Colchester; it had clasped a distinctive armlet about Mr. Britling’s sleeve and dispatched him into the dark, bestowed various certificates upon Mrs. Britling, and stemmed the easy flow of Mr. Lawrence Carmine’s calls and chatter. Yet until now it had forged no immediate bond between the life of the village and the stark realities of rifle-fire, shrapnel, and cold steel at the front. Tonight, however, the Dower House was producing marvellous feats of Anglo-French, and a vivid visitor was recounting—sometimes with piercing clarity, sometimes through a fog of mangled phrases—how men had been torn asunder before his eyes, how scraps of human bodies lay scattered in the roadways; there was a puzzled flurry over the term _omoplate d'une femme_, until one of the young people fetched the lexicon and discovered it meant a woman’s shoulder-blade; of lakes of blood—everywhere—and of desperate flight through the blackness.
source_text: The dinner that night at the Dower House marked a distinct fresh step in the approach of the Great War to the old habits and securities of Matching's Easy. The war had indeed filled every one's mind to the exclusion of all other topics since its very beginning; it had carried off Herr Heinrich to Germany, Teddy to London, and Hugh to Colchester, it had put a special brassard round Mr. Britling's arm and carried him out into the night, given Mrs. Britling several certificates, and interrupted the frequent visits and gossip of Mr. Lawrence Carmine; but so far it had not established a direct contact between the life of Matching's Easy and the grim business of shot, shell, and bayonet at the front. But now here was the Dower House accomplishing wonderful idioms in Anglo-French, and an animated guest telling them--sometimes one understood clearly and sometimes the meaning was clouded--of men blown to pieces under his eyes, of fragments of human beings lying about in the streets; there was trouble over the expression _omoplate d'une femme_, until one of the youngsters got the dictionary and found out it was the shoulder-blade of a woman; of pools of blood--everywhere--and of flight in the darkness.

text: It leaves me—speechless with fury," Mr. Britling said at last, circling back to his chief vexation. "They refuse to entertain any middle ground. It's a pure appetite for conflict.... Those people over there believe nothing can truly go wrong. They're like children in a nursery, staging a make-believe revolt. Untouched and careless. Until death drops at their feet, they'll never grasp that they're toying with loaded weapons....
source_text: "It makes me--expressionless with anger," said Mr. Britling after a pause, reverting to his main annoyance. "They won't consider any compromise. It's sheer love of quarrelling.... Those people there think that nothing can possibly happen. They are like children in a nursery playing at rebellion. Unscathed and heedless. Until there is death at their feet they will never realise they are playing with loaded guns...."

text: From every shade of such dread, Mr. Britling’s pool of lamplight was his only sanctuary. His labor could summon mirages, like the phantasms of opium, of a realm governed by order and fairness. In the thick darkness of global ruin, he clung to the blueprint of a bolder venture—heedless of whether any backers would ever appear.
source_text: From all such moods of horror Mr. Britling's circle of lamplight was his sole refuge. His work could conjure up visions, like opium visions, of a world of order and justice. Amidst the gloom of world bankruptcy he stuck to the prospectus of a braver enterprise--reckless of his chances of subscribers....

text: He caught himself reflecting on young Heinrich much as he did on his own boy, though with a shade less anguish—both of them, he felt, were bright futures pointlessly cut short. His thoughts brushed aside the reality that Heinrich was a foe, that in the cold arithmetic of a grinding war his death evened the scales for Hugh’s. He cut directly to the core truth: they had been brave and gentle souls, and the same force had taken them both.
source_text: He found himself thinking of young Heinrich in the very manner, if with a lesser intensity, in which he thought about his own son, as of hopes senselessly destroyed. His mind took no note of the fact that Heinrich was an enemy, that by the reckoning of a "war of attrition" his death was balance and compensation for the death of Hugh. He went straight to the root fact that they had been gallant and kindly beings, and that the same thing had killed them both....

text: For an hour she had sat with Mr. Britling, and her thoughts now circled entirely around this altered, unadorned man who spoke of the Almighty as casually as he might describe a sparrow he had glimpsed or an oak beneath which he had once rested. Entwined with this impression of Mr. Britling was the peculiar notion of a God who was Himself a bounded being, able to draw as near as Teddy had, murmuring affection in the dark. She entertained the absurd conviction that God strove much as Mr. Britling did, and that Mr. Britling, with only some nameless smallness of vision, loved after the fashion of God. She cherished him for his charts and his reveries and the stark simplicity of his words to her. It was curious how the taut memory of the dead Teddy had now slipped entirely from her consciousness. She was seized by a sensation of closure and commencement, as if a leaf had been turned in her existence and all things were remade. For years she had met religion with nothing but disdain, ever since her ripening mind had cast it off as a system of unpardonable prohibitions and hollow shows, a discordant invention where no discords had existed save those it manufactured. She had been a contented unbeliever. She had frolicked in the sunlight, a child of nature wholly trusting in the fundamental benevolence of the world she inhabited. She had barred every thought of suffering or ugliness. Until the gory fist of conflict had shattered all her certainties. Teddy, the companion, was finished, the amorous sport concluded forever; the buoyant, unthinking embrace of life as mere life; and where Teddy had stood now rose the grief of existence, the compassion of existence, and this arrival of God from absolute distance into a graspable bond with her own being.
source_text: She had been talking to Mr. Britling for an hour, and her mind was full of the thought of this changed and simplified man, who talked of God as he might have done of a bird he had seen or of a tree he had sheltered under. And all mixed up with this thought of Mr. Britling was this strange idea of God who was also a limited person, who could come as close as Teddy, whispering love in the darkness. She had a ridiculous feeling that God really struggled like Mr. Britling, and that with only some indefinable inferiority of outlook Mr. Britling loved like God. She loved him for his maps and his dreams and the bareness of his talk to her. It was strange how the straining thought of the dead Teddy had passed now out of her mind. She was possessed by a sense of ending and beginning, as though a page had turned over in her life and everything was new. She had never given religion any thought but contemptuous thought for some years, since indeed her growing intelligence had dismissed it as a scheme of inexcusable restraints and empty pretences, a thing of discords where there were no discords except of its making. She had been a happy Atheist. She had played in the sunshine, a natural creature with the completest confidence in the essential goodness of the world in which she found herself. She had refused all thought of painful and disagreeable things. Until the bloody paw of war had wiped out all her assurance. Teddy, the playmate, was over, the love game was ended for ever; the fresh happy acceptance of life as life; and in the place of Teddy was the sorrow of life, the pity of life, and this coming of God out of utter remoteness into a conceivable relation to her own existence.

text: He saw his part in the sweeping national revolt as a very small one. He was a writer, a marginal note on the real world; he possessed no gift for leading others, his function was to watch rather than to organize, and he pictured himself merely as a tiny figure shedding his private self to slot into a vast apparatus—shouldering a rifle in some trench, standing sentry at a bridge, feeding a cartridge belt, perhaps with nothing more than an armband to mark his role—until the enormous work was finished. That Sunday night brimmed with visions of structure, of the countryside rising squarely to its duty, of highways cleared and supplies drawn together, of every small personal concern pushed aside. And woven through this, Mr. Britling could still—he was not yet too old—conjure those fantasies of individual service, of sudden crises met with speed and courage, of striking gallantry and rare honors, the kind of daydreams that drift through the mind of every imaginative volunteer.
source_text: His conception of his own share in the great national uprising was a very modest one. He was a writer, a footnote to reality; he had no trick of command over men, his rôle was observation rather than organisation, and he saw himself only as an insignificant individual dropping from his individuality into his place in a great machine, taking a rifle in a trench, guarding a bridge, filling a cartridge--just with a brassard or something like that on--until the great task was done. Sunday night was full of imaginations of order, of the countryside standing up to its task, of roads cleared and resources marshalled, of the petty interests of the private life altogether set aside. And mingling with that it was still possible for Mr. Britling, he was still young enough, to produce such dreams of personal service, of sudden emergencies swiftly and bravely met, of conspicuous daring and exceptional rewards, such dreams as hover in the brains of every imaginative recruit....

text: As the second hour of these lamentations drew to a close, Mr. Britling’s spirit rose up in rebellion. He raised his voice against her, filling it with wounded sorrow, and declared that he had come to Pyecrafts with nothing in his heart but tenderness and goodwill, that he had only waited for Gladys to be ready before setting out, that he had brought along the draft of "The Silent Places" to refine and complete, that for days upon days he had yearned to do this in the ambience of the beloved old summer-house with its far-off prospect of the cherished sea, and that now all of it was beyond reach, that Mrs. Harrowdean had placed it beyond reach, and that she was swiftly rendering everything beyond reach.
source_text: Towards the end of the second hour of such complaints the spirit of Mr. Britling rose in revolt. He lifted up his voice against her, he charged his voice with indignant sorrow and declared that he had come over to Pyecrafts with no thought in his mind but sweet and loving thoughts, that he had but waited for Gladys to be ready before he came, that he had brought over the manuscript of "The Silent Places" with him to polish and finish up, that "for days and days" he had been longing to do this in the atmosphere of the dear old summer-house with its distant view of the dear old sea, and that now all that was impossible, that Mrs. Harrowdean had made it impossible and that indeed she was rapidly making everything impossible....

text: After the Bank Holiday, shops lifted their shutters with a hesitant air that soon hardened into confidence; citizens resumed their daily rounds, and for the bulk of English people the war remained, through those early weeks, an agitation of thought and nerve rather than a bodily and immediate truth. There was a sharp hunger for tidings, yet for days together there were scarcely any tidings to be had. The newspapers strained to meet the vast moment. With the _Daily Express_ in the vanguard, the halfpenny sheets took up a novel and more ringing species of title—the streamer, a stripe of heavy type stretched the full breadth of the page, proclaiming triumphs or unsettling events. This they printed day after day, whether there was a major engagement to report or merely the sinking of a fishing smack, and the popular temper swiftly tuned itself to the new key.
source_text: Shops re-opened after the Bank Holiday, in a tentative spirit that speedily became assurance; people went about their business again, and the war, so far as the mass of British folk were concerned, was for some weeks a fever of the mind and intelligence rather than a physical and personal actuality. There was a keen demand for news, and for a time there was very little news. The press did its best to cope with this immense occasion. Led by the _Daily Express_, all the halfpenny newspapers adopted a new and more resonant sort of headline, the streamer, a band of emphatic type that ran clean across the page and announced victories or disconcerting happenings. They did this every day, whether there was a great battle or the loss of a trawler to announce, and the public mind speedily adapted itself to the new pitch.

text: His mustache is all waxed and pointed!" burst out Aunt Wilshire, having stewed on it for a while. "They claim he's entirely lost the elbow in his left arm—holds it rigid, like a marionette—and yet he means to overrun Europe.... As long as his grandmother was alive, there was somebody to rein him in. He was terrified of her. He loathed her, but he never dared cross her. Even his uncle could check him somewhat. Now, there's nobody left to hold him back.
source_text: "With his moustaches turned up!" exclaimed Aunt Wilshire after an interval of accumulation.... "They say he has completely lost the use of the joint in his left arm, he carries it stiff like a Punch and Judy--and he wants to conquer Europe.... While his grandmother lived there was some one to keep him in order. He stood in Awe of her. He hated her, but he did not dare defy her. Even his uncle had some influence. Now, nothing restrains him.

text: Then, after only the briefest pause, those tireless players resumed. Mr. Direck quietly excused himself and came back dressed in a white silk shirt, tennis trousers, and a belt. Now he and Cecily were partners; the familiar Cecily-Teddy pairing had been dissolved, and it appeared he was to step into the formidable Teddy’s role on the left wing beside her.
source_text: And then with the very slightest of delays these insatiable people picked up again. Mr. Direck slipped away and returned in a white silk shirt, tennis trousers and a belt. This time he and Cecily were on the same side, the Cecily-Teddy combination was broken, and he it seemed was to take the place of the redoubtable Teddy on the left wing with her.

text: For the first time, Mr. Britling felt he truly grasped the raw horror of war—the thick, mindless cruelty of it, stark and immediate. It was as though he had never really seen it before, as if everything up to now had been mere stories, images, performances he’d always known were false. But that this dear, ridiculous old woman, this piece of home, this creature of familiar quirks and familiar annoyances, should be blown apart, left suffering like a crushed mouse beneath a car—that brought the whole monstrous reality into a sharp, throbbing focus. Every single soul among the millions who had been ripped and broken and tormented in this vast agony was, to anyone who truly knew them or had been close, somehow lovable, somehow absurd, somehow deserving of tenderness and regard. Poor Aunt Wilshire was only the sample shoved before his eyes of all that shattered multitude, whose greenish lips had sweated in pain, whose splintered bones had jutted through torn, bleeding flesh… The loathsome face of the German Crown Prince thrust itself into the center of Mr. Britling’s vision. The young man stood there in his smart uniform, smirking beneath his long nose, carrying himself with a swagger, delighted by his colossal sway over so many lives…
source_text: For the first time it seemed to Mr. Britling he really saw the immediate horror of war, the dense cruel stupidity of the business, plain and close. It was as if he had never perceived anything of the sort before, as if he had been dealing with stories, pictures, shows and representations that he knew to be shams. But that this dear, absurd old creature, this thing of home, this being of familiar humours and familiar irritations, should be torn to pieces, left in torment like a smashed mouse over which an automobile has passed, brought the whole business to a raw and quivering focus. Not a soul among all those who had been rent and torn and tortured in this agony of millions, but was to any one who understood and had been near to it, in some way lovable, in some way laughable, in some way worthy of respect and care. Poor Aunt Wilshire was but the sample thrust in his face of all this mangled multitude, whose green-white lips had sweated in anguish, whose broken bones had thrust raggedly through red dripping flesh.... The detested features of the German Crown Prince jerked into the centre of Mr. Britling's picture. The young man stood in his dapper uniform and grinned under his long nose, carrying himself jauntily, proud of his extreme importance to so many lives....

text: In that final hour, he had drawn unexpectedly close to her. She was surprised by a swell of unfamiliar tenderness toward him. Until now, she had never bothered to examine her attachments to anyone but Teddy. Yet suddenly she felt herself unfolding toward the whole world in a spirit of goodwill. This fresh notion of a benevolent God—one who faced his own trials, who could be imagined as akin to Mr. Britling, akin even to Teddy—had seized her imagination. He was present in the amber light of autumn; He was in the small bird that had seemed so bold and companionable. Wherever kindness lived, wherever gentleness dwelt, there was God. And countless old phrases she had read or heard without attention, phrases that had lain like dry bones in her memory, now took on flesh and quickened into life. This God—if this truly was God—then it was not foolish to say that God was love, that He was a friend and a companion. With Him, it might be possible to confront a world where she and Teddy would never again walk side by side or plan any future happiness. After all, she had been deeply happy; she had known a wondrous happiness. In her brief years, she had received far more joy, far more love, than most people gathered in a lifetime. And so, in the swing of her feelings, Letty, who had set out with her mind crowded by murder and vengeance, returned through the sunset pondering pity, pondering the thousand kindnesses and tendernesses of Teddy—which were, perhaps, only a glimpse of the boundless kindnesses and tendernesses of God. What claim did she have to a pale and bitter sorrow, self-absorbed and spiteful, while old Britling could still envision an age of mercy on the earth and a red-gold sunlight, warm as Teddy’s smile, lay over all the world?
source_text: In the last hour he had come very near to her. She found herself full of an unwonted affection for him. She had never troubled her head about her relations with any one except Teddy before. Now suddenly she seemed to be opening out to all the world for kindness. This new idea of a friendly God, who had a struggle of his own, who could be thought of as kindred to Mr. Britling, as kindred to Teddy--had gripped her imagination. He was behind the autumnal sunshine; he was in the little bird that had seemed so confident and friendly. Whatever was kind, whatever was tender; there was God. And a thousand old phrases she had read and heard and given little heed to, that had lain like dry bones in her memory, suddenly were clothed in flesh and became alive. This God--if this was God--then indeed it was not nonsense to say that God was love, that he was a friend and companion.... With him it might be possible to face a world in which Teddy and she would never walk side by side again nor plan any more happiness for ever. After all she had been very happy; she had had wonderful happiness. She had had far more happiness, far more love, in her short years or so than most people had in their whole lives. And so in the reaction of her emotions, Letty, who had gone out with her head full of murder and revenge, came back through the sunset thinking of pity, of the thousand kindnesses and tendernesses of Teddy that were, after all, perhaps only an intimation of the limitless kindnesses and tendernesses of God.... What right had she to a white and bitter grief, self-centred and vindictive, while old Britling could still plan an age of mercy in the earth and a red-gold sunlight that was warm as a smile from Teddy lay on all the world....

text: At the trunk-line interchange, Mr. Direck discovered he must ask the conductor to halt the train at Matching's Easy; it was a request-only stop; the whole affair grew more charming by the minute; and when he snatched his valise and alighted, there stood a single elderly Essex stationmaster—who was also porter, signalman, and everything else—clutching a red pennant and chatting with Mr. Britling about the sweet-pea borders that adorned the platform. And there was the very Mr. Britling who constituted the sole purpose and supreme hope of Mr. Direck's European tour, and he looked nothing like the photographs Mr. Direck had studied yet was unmistakably Mr. Britling nonetheless, since no other soul occupied the platform, and he was stepping forward with a welcoming hand.
source_text: Mr. Direck had learnt at the main-line junction that he had to tell the guard to stop the train for Matching's Easy; it only stopped "by request"; the thing was getting better and better; and when Mr. Direck seized his grip and got out of the train there was just one little old Essex station-master and porter and signalman and everything, holding a red flag in his hand and talking to Mr. Britling about the cultivation of the sweet peas which glorified the station. And there was the Mr. Britling who was the only item of business and the greatest expectation in Mr. Direck's European journey, and he was quite unlike the portraits Mr. Direck had seen and quite unmistakably Mr. Britling all the same, since there was nobody else upon the platform, and he was advancing with a gesture of welcome.

text: Now, it appeared, was the moment for Mr. Direck to deliver the remarks he had been rehearsing. Yet an unforeseen snag would thwart that plan. Mr. Direck realized almost instantly that Mr. Britling was likely operating a motorcar for the first, second, or at the very most third time in his entire existence.
source_text: And now it seemed was the time for Mr. Direck to make his meditated speeches. But an unexpected complication was to defeat this intention. Mr. Direck perceived almost at once that Mr. Britling was probably driving an automobile for the first or second or at the extremest the third time in his life.

text: For a long while he had vowed to make this journey, yet being by nature more buoyant than brisk—though he steadfastly regarded himself as a wellspring of lucid American vigor—he had permitted a host of distractions, above all the waverings of Miss Cora Ellsworth, to detain him. But now the waverings of Miss Cora Ellsworth were resolved, and Mr. Alden had traveled to England precisely to prove to himself and to the world that life held other fascinations for him beyond Cora.
source_text: He had promised himself this visit for many years, but being of a sunny rather than energetic temperament--though he firmly believed himself to be a reservoir of clear-sighted American energy--he had allowed all sorts of things, and more particularly the uncertainties of Miss Mamie Nelson, to keep him back. But now there were no more uncertainties about Miss Mamie Nelson, and Mr. Direck had come over to England just to convince himself and everybody else that there were other interests in life for him than Mamie....

text: That night, Britain declared war on Germany. To almost every Englishman, this was entirely expected; indeed, one of history’s most astonishing facts is that the Germans were taken aback by it. When Mr. Britling, as a representative Englishman, had insisted there would never be war between their two nations, he had always meant it was unthinkable to him that Germany would ever invade Belgium or France. Had Germany been willing to fight only a defensive war along her western border and leave Belgium untouched, there would scarcely have been a pro-war faction in Britain. But the assault on Belgium, the lunge westward, ignited the whole country into unanimous war. It resolved a question that had been fiercely debated right up to the moment hostilities began. Until the very end, the English had clung to the belief that in Germany, just as in England, ordinary people were amiable, peaceable, and disengaged. That had been England’s error. Germany was in truth exactly what it had been declaring itself to be for forty years: a nation built for war. With a sigh—and a long-dormant shiver of excitement—England stirred herself to fight. Even now, she roused herself slowly. It was going to be a colossal affair, but just how colossal no one in England had yet begun to conceive.
source_text: That night brought the British declaration of war against Germany. To nearly every Englishman that came as a matter of course, and it is one of the most wonderful facts in history that the Germans were surprised by it. When Mr. Britling, as a sample Englishman, had said that there would never be war between Germany and England, he had always meant that it was inconceivable to him that Germany should ever attack Belgium or France. If Germany had been content to fight a merely defensive war upon her western frontier and let Belgium alone, there would scarcely have been such a thing as a war party in Great Britain. But the attack upon Belgium, the westward thrust, made the whole nation flame unanimously into war. It settled a question that was in open debate up to the very outbreak of the conflict. Up to the last the English had cherished the idea that in Germany, just as in England, the mass of people were kindly, pacific, and detached. That had been the English mistake. Germany was really and truly what Germany had been professing to be for forty years, a War State. With a sigh--and a long-forgotten thrill--England roused herself to fight. Even now she still roused herself sluggishly. It was going to be an immense thing, but just how immense it was going to be no one in England had yet imagined.

text: The organ _motif_ surged forth and seized absolute command. Every secondary melody was either swallowed or subsumed. Mr. Aldridge slipped from the part of a hapless motorist into that of a stripped spirit adrift in the void, grappling with monumental riddles. Such vast unease, perhaps, is the final cost of godlessness. Had Huxley spoken true—was all mankind, like Mr. Aldridge himself, a flighty, intermittent presence, engaged in a doomed and tragic sport, musing too shallowly, darting too swiftly, before an unforgiving foe?
source_text: The organ _motif_ battled its way to complete predominance. The lesser themes were drowned or absorbed. Mr. Britling returned from the rôle of an incompetent automobilist to the rôle of a soul naked in space and time wrestling with giant questions. These cosmic solicitudes, it may be, are the last penalty of irreligion. Was Huxley right, and was all humanity, even as Mr. Britling, a careless, fitful thing, playing a tragically hopeless game, thinking too slightly, moving too quickly, against a relentless antagonist?

text: I'd cancel his _Daily Mail_," Raeburn said. "I'd keep _The Times_, but I'd drop the _Daily Mail_ for its placards alone. It exaggerates Northcliffe. It turns him into the screeching and howling of subordinates. The simple truth is that Northcliffe is terrified of German efficiency—and in wartime, when a man is terrified, whether he's honest or not, you put a bag over his head or hold a pistol to it to steady him.... What's the use of all this noise about changing the government? We haven't got a change of government. It's like telling a beggar to get a change of clothes. Our men, all our public men, are second-rate, with the instincts of lawyers. There's nothing commanding in their minds. How can you expect the system to produce anything different? But they're doing the best they can, and there's no way of putting anyone else in now, and that's how it stands.
source_text: "I'd stop his _Daily Mail_," said Raeburn. "I'd leave _The Times_, but I'd stop the _Daily Mail_ on the score of its placards alone. It overdoes Northcliffe. It translates him into the shrieks and yells of underlings. The plain fact is that Northcliffe is scared out of his wits by German efficiency--and in war time when a man is scared out of his wits, whether he is honest or not, you put his head in a bag or hold a pistol to it to calm him.... What is the good of all this clamouring for a change of government? We haven't a change of government. It's like telling a tramp to get a change of linen. Our men, all our public men, are second-rate men, with the habits of advocates. There is nothing masterful in their minds. How can you expect the system to produce anything else? But they are doing as well as they can, and there is no way of putting in any one else now, and there you are."

text: By night, London wore an unfamiliar strangeness. The authorities, fearing an air raid, were trying to dampen the brighter lights along the main streets. Shopkeepers had to draw their blinds, and many of the tall streetlamps stood dark. Mr. Britling considered these measures overly fussy and needless, likely to cause traffic accidents. Yet they lent the city a Rembrandtesque atmosphere, transforming it into a play of brown shadows and slanting shafts of light. At first, plenty of people resisted, and here and there a restaurant or a draper’s window still flared defiantly through the gloom. A few rebellious automobiles also roamed with blazing headlamps. But the police were uncommonly resolute.
source_text: By night there was a new strangeness about London. The authorities were trying to suppress the more brilliant illumination of the chief thoroughfares, on account of the possibility of an air raid. Shopkeepers were being compelled to pull down their blinds, and many of the big standard lights were unlit. Mr. Britling thought these precautions were very fussy and unnecessary, and likely to lead to accidents amidst the traffic. But it gave a Rembrandtesque quality to the London scene, turned it into mysterious arrangements of brown shadows and cones and bars of light. At first many people were recalcitrant, and here and there a restaurant or a draper's window still blazed out and broke the gloom. There were also a number of insubordinate automobiles with big head-lights. But the police were being unusually firm....

text: For a while, a small detail snagged his drowsy thoughts. As he sped along, dark, soundless birds kept springing up in alarm from the road's dust ahead, then glided silently past the twin beams of his headlights. What kind of birds were they? Could they be nightjars? Or were they various species stealing the stillness of the night for a dust-bath in the sand? This stray thread of curiosity wove through the fabric of his mind before fading out.
source_text: For a time a little thing caught hold of his dreaming mind. Continually as he ran on, black, silent birds rose startled out of the dust of the road before him, and fluttered noiselessly beyond his double wedge of light. What sort of bird could they be? Were they night-jars? Were they different kinds of birds snatching at the quiet of the night for a dust bath in the sand? This little independent thread of inquiry ran through the texture of his mind and died away....

text: “That’s not how they’ll manage it, Father! The same blunders committed in this lush campaign will be repeated out there in the barren. They’ll lag behind schedule; they’ll forfeit their cannons just as they now forfeit our hearths. I’m a silent rifleman; I must obey my orders; yet, I’m starting to grasp the folly of the Somme.”
source_text: "That isn't the way they are going to do it, Daddy! The sort of thing that is done over here in the green army will be done over there in the dry. They won't be in time; they'll lose their guns where now they lose our kitchens. I'm a mute soldier; I've got to do what I'm told; still, I begin to understand the Battle of Neuve Chapelle.

text: For a rug. A court rug. One I could not command. I said what I thought. He understood I was aware. I hid nothing. So they pursued me. Through long seasons he had nursed his vengeance. At last it is his. Yet at such a price! And men name him Sovereign. Sovereign!
source_text: "All this on account of a carpet. A palace carpet. Over which I had no control. I spoke my mind. He knew I knew of it. I never concealed it. So I was hunted. For years he had meditated revenge. Now he has it. But at what a cost! And they call him Emperor. Emperor!

text: He had a half-shaped notion of cutting directly in front of the motorcyclist, and then they were plunging down the short grassy incline between the road and the wall, heading straight for the wall, still moving fast. The motorcyclist struck something and disappeared from the equation. The wall appeared to surge toward them and then—crumble. A massive jolt followed. Mr. Direck grabbed for his companion the emergency brake, but only managed to brush it before his head struck the frame of the glass windscreen, and a curtain dropped over everything.
source_text: He had an imperfectly formed idea of getting across right in front of the motor cyclist, and then they were going down the brief grassy slope between the road and the wall, straight at the wall, and still at a good speed. The motor cyclist smacked against something and vanished from the problem. The wall seemed to rush up at them and then--collapse. There was a tremendous concussion. Mr. Direck gripped at his friend the emergency brake, but had only time to touch it before his head hit against the frame of the glass wind-screen, and a curtain fell upon everything....

text: He faltered, the words catching in his throat. His elbows rested on his knees, and he buried his face in his hands, trembling before falling into stillness. His hair was disheveled. The clipped edge of his moustache and a small fold of flesh visible beside his hand made him seem doubly pitiable. His heavy atlas, with papers jutting from its pages, lay forgotten at his side. He remained like that for a long while, and neither he nor Letty stirred or spoke. Yet they shared the same shadow. They drew deep solace from each other’s presence—a comfort they had not known since their sorrows first descended.
source_text: He choked and stopped speaking. His elbows were on his knees, and he put his face between his hands and shuddered and became still. His hair was troubled. The end of his stumpy moustache and a little roll of flesh stood out at the side of his hand, and made him somehow twice as pitiful. His big atlas, from which papers projected, seemed forgotten by his side. So he sat for a long time, and neither he nor Letty moved or spoke. But they were in the same shadow. They found great comfort in one another. They had not been so comforted before since their losses came upon them.

text: The firing line itself? It was much the same—a ditch fronted by a sandbag parapet—but behind it opened a series of dug-outs, strange deep hollows shored up with sleepers scavenged from a railway track nearby. Dug-outs differ widely. Many recalled the little cubby-house we built last summer at the orchard’s edge, except here the walls were thick enough to withstand a high-explosive shell. The finest dug-out in our company’s sector was rather a smart affair, fitted with woodwork and a door salvaged from a ruined house twenty or thirty yards to the rear. It even had a stove, a chimney, and pans for holding water. It was the best dug-out for miles around. That house had a well, and a dedicated trench ran back to it; all day long there was a constant traffic of men fetching water. A pump had once stood above the well, but a shell had shattered it.
source_text: "And the firing line itself? Just the same sort of ditch with a parapet of sandbags, but with dug-outs, queer big holes helped out with sleepers from a nearby railway track, opening into it from behind. Dug-outs vary a good deal. Many are rather like the cubby-house we made at the end of the orchard last summer; only the walls are thick enough to stand a high explosive shell. The best dug-out in our company's bit of front was quite a dressy affair with some woodwork and a door got from the ruins of a house twenty or thirty yards behind us. It had a stove in it too, and a chimbley, and pans to keep water in. It was the best dug-out for miles. This house had a well, and there was a special trench ran back to that, and all day long there was a coming and going for water. There had once been a pump over the well, but a shell had smashed that....

text: Other glimpses of Heinrich drifted through Mr. Britling's daydream. Heinrich on the hockey field, darting with tremendous speed and scant control, bewildered and outmaneuvered by Letty, eluded by Hugh, plunging recklessly forward and scrambling backward, then collapsing flat on the turf with a groan of exhaustion.... Or Heinrich, solemn and flushed, squinting through his spectacles at his Skat hand.... Or Heinrich rowing across the wide lake, or Heinrich swimming, or Heinrich concealing himself with elaborate cunning among the garden shrubs from the other boys, following some private theory of invisibility, or Heinrich in contorted poses, creeping after the deer in Claverings Park. For a season he had cherished the ambition to steal near enough to a deer to _lay a hand upon it_.... Or Heinrich compiling. He had a mania for cataloguing and indexing books, scores, any stray items that could be sorted. His chief delight lay in designing systems of thumb-notches for dictionary pages, so that one could flip directly to the required entry. He had acquired and trimmed the margins of three dictionaries, each one an improvement on its predecessor; he had nursed grand visions of patents and fortunes springing from the idea.... And his chamber had been a wellspring of peculiar noises; his quest for melody upon the violin. He had imagined that upon coming to Matching's Easy he would join "some string quartette." But Matching's Easy yielded no string quartette. He was forced to resort to the pianola, and attempt to perform duets with it. Only the pianola insisted on playing the entire duet alone, and under the fingers of a young Britling was prone to exhibit a whimsical capriciousness; abrupt shifts between furious velocity and profound lethargy....
source_text: Other memories of Heinrich flitted across Mr. Britling's reverie. Heinrich at hockey, running with extreme swiftness and little skill, tricked and baffled by Letty, dodged by Hugh, going headlong forward and headlong back, and then with a cry flinging himself flat on the ground exhausted.... Or again Heinrich very grave and very pink, peering through his glasses at his cards at Skat.... Or Heinrich in the boats upon the great pond, or Heinrich swimming, or Heinrich hiding very, very artfully from the boys about the garden on a theory of his own, or Heinrich in strange postures, stalking the deer in Claverings Park. For a time he had had a great ambition to creep quite close to a deer and _touch_ it.... Or Heinrich indexing. He had a passion for listing and indexing books, music, any loose classifiable thing. His favourite amusement was devising schemes for the indentation of dictionary leaves, so that one could turn instantly to the needed word. He had bought and cut the edges of three dictionaries; each in succession improved upon the other; he had had great hopes of patents and wealth arising therefrom.... And his room had been a source of strange sounds; his search for music upon the violin. He had hoped when he came to Matching's Easy to join "some string quartette." But Matching's Easy produced no string quartette. He had to fall back upon the pianola, and try to play duets with that. Only the pianola did all the duet itself, and in the hands of a small Britling was apt to betray a facetious moodiness; sudden alternations between extreme haste and extreme lassitude....

text: Another source of bright amusement in the trenches, a prank of truly exquisite mischief, involves the business of fuses. We have two types: a slow fuse that burns at about a yard per minute, used for hand grenades and similar devices, and a quick fuse that tears along at a hundred yards per second—meant for detonating mines and such. The quick variety is clearly marked with a vivid red thread to set it apart. As you’re aware, when the opposing lines lie close, both we and the enemy have a habit of cheering each other up by lobbing homemade but effective tin-can grenades. If one lands in a British trench, somebody snatches it up at once and hurls it back. Turning the German’s own weapon against him strikes the British soldier as especially satisfying. When a grenade drops into a German trench, everyone scatters. (At least, that’s what our fellows report; though I suppose both sides have their exceptions.) If the bomb goes off, it goes off. If it doesn’t, Hans and Fritz soon creep back to investigate. Perhaps the fuse failed to catch—thrown by a shaky hand—or it smouldered out. Then Hans or Fritz fits a fresh fuse and returns it with tender attention. Turning the Briton’s own weapon against him strikes the German soldier as especially satisfying… But here military brilliance enters the scene. Some inspired soul on our side got hold of a length of mine fuse—the fast kind, probably through theft—and devoted a painstaking day to stripping out the red thread and making it resemble its sluggish cousin. Then short pieces were fixed to tin bombs and lobbed—unlit, of course—into the German trenches. A long, delighted silence followed. I can picture our lads struggling to keep straight faces. Hans and Fritz, we gathered, were creeping back, inspecting the unlit fuse, touching a flame to it, so they could return it to its sender in the usual way…
source_text: "Another thing that appeals to the sunny humour of the trenches as a really delicious practical joke is the trick of the fuses. We have two kinds of fuse, a slow-burning fuse such as is used for hand-grenades and such-like things, a sort of yard-a-minute fuse, and a rapid fuse that goes a hundred yards a second--for firing mines and so on. The latter is carefully distinguished from the former by a conspicuous red thread. Also, as you know, it is the habit of the enemy and ourselves when the trenches are near enough, to enliven each other by the casting of homely but effective hand-grenades made out of tins. When a grenade drops in a British trench somebody seizes it instantly and throws it back. To hoist the German with his own petard is particularly sweet to the British mind. When a grenade drops into a German trench everybody runs. (At least that is what I am told happens by the men from our trenches; though possibly each side has its exceptions.) If the bomb explodes, it explodes. If it doesn't, Hans and Fritz presently come creeping back to see what has happened. Sometimes the fuse hasn't caught properly, it has been thrown by a nervous man; or it hasn't burnt properly. Then Hans or Fritz puts in a new fuse and sends it back with loving care. To hoist the Briton with his own petard is particularly sweet to the German mind.... But here it is that military genius comes in. Some gifted spirit on our side procured (probably by larceny) a length of mine fuse, the rapid sort, and spent a laborious day removing the red thread and making it into the likeness of its slow brother. Then bits of it were attached to tin-bombs and shied--unlit of course--into the German trenches. A long but happy pause followed. I can see the chaps holding themselves in. Hans and Fritz were understood to be creeping back, to be examining the unlit fuse, to be applying a light thereunto, in order to restore it to its maker after their custom....
