text: The shift from scaling emotional heights to fervent intimacy came so swiftly and impulsively that each stage erased the one before it, and Mr. Britling found himself, with a dim bewilderment, no longer a towering summit for dainty pilgrims but an eager lover chasing the happiness of one of the most capricious, tangled, and captivating of feminine souls. This was far from his notion of how men and women should relate, but Mrs. Harrowdean had a knack for rousing his chivalry. She kept him bustling after her; she never asked but commanded gifts and treats and surprises; she even stirred a certain jealousy in him. His work began to fray under constant breaks. Still, they shared radiant, diverting hours that could soften his mutinous thoughts with the dread of irreplaceable loss. "One must love, and all things in life are imperfect," was how Mr. Britling framed his surrender. And she gripped him, too, with a kind of easy pathos. She was small; the slightest prick could sting her, and then her blue eyes would glisten with tears.
source_text: The transitions from emotional mountaineering to ardent intimacies were so rapid and impulsive that each phase obliterated its predecessor, and it was only with a vague perplexity that Mr. Britling found himself transferred from the rôle of a mountainous objective for pretty little pilgrims to that of a sedulous lover in pursuit of the happiness of one of the most uncertain, intricate, and entrancing of feminine personalities. This was not at all his idea of the proper relations between men and women, but Mrs. Harrowdean had a way of challenging his gallantry. She made him run about for her; she did not demand but she commanded presents and treats and surprises; she even developed a certain jealousy in him. His work began to suffer from interruptions. Yet they had glowing and entertaining moments together that could temper his rebellious thoughts with the threat of irreparable loss. "One must love, and all things in life are imperfect," was how Mr. Britling expressed his reasons for submission. And she had a hold upon him too in a certain facile pitifulness. She was little; she could be stung sometimes by the slightest touch and then her blue eyes would be bright with tears.

text: Mr. Lawrence Carmine, who had stopped by unannounced, found himself abruptly recalling the mournful old song “The Mistletoe Bough,” and began to intone its frayed, lingering verses with somber gravity. The ballad relates how a lovely young woman concealed herself in an oaken chest during a Christmas game of hide-and-seek, only to be discovered years later as a withered relic. The tale seized Herr Heinrich’s fancy with extraordinary force. “Come,” he said, “let us now inspect every trunk, every closet, every drawer. And mark each one as we proceed….”
source_text: Mr. Lawrence Carmine, who had dropped in, was suddenly reminded of that lugubrious old ballad, "The Mistletoe Bough," and recited large worn fragments of it impressively. It tells of how a beautiful girl hid away in a chest during a Christmas game of hide-and-seek, and how she was found, a dried vestige, years afterwards. It took a very powerful hold upon Herr Heinrich's imagination. "Let us now," he said, "make an examination of every box and cupboard and drawer. Marking each as we go...."

text: Our own British laureate supplied the apologia for a thousand arrogant ventures in the Orient! "Cling to the tangible and the true," ran the silent sermon of torpedo and shell and U-boat. What claim, in truth, had we English to lack a cannon or a warplane capable of swatting that airship from the heavens with shameful finality? Had we not assumed the burden of Empire? Did we not stand at the vanguard of mighty peoples? Did we possess any genuine warrant to cry out when our imperial swagger was mocked? "Here, if anywhere," murmured Mr. Britling's intellect, "lies one of the chains of reasoning that summoned that invisible malice from the darkness high above the rooftops of Filmington-on-Sea. Here, in a fashion, is the root of this slaughter. Savage it is and vile, assuredly, but is it purely savage? Does it not, in its way, possess a kind of brutish justice?—is it not a brutish retort to a sloth no less brutish?"
source_text: The very justification our English poet has found for a thousand overbearing actions in the East! "Forget not order and the real," that was the underlying message of bomb and gas and submarine. After all, what right had we English _not_ to have a gun or an aeroplane fit to bring down that Zeppelin ignominiously and conclusively? Had we not undertaken Empire? Were we not the leaders of great nations? Had we indeed much right to complain if our imperial pose was flouted? "There, at least," said Mr. Britling's reason, "is one of the lines of thought that brought that unseen cruelty out of the night high over the houses of Filmington-on-Sea. That, in a sense, is the cause of this killing. Cruel it is and abominable, yes, but is it altogether cruel? Hasn't it, after all, a sort of stupid rightness?--isn't it a stupid reaction to an indolence at least equally stupid?"

text: Mr. Direck became the attentive eavesdropper to an authentic weekend colloquy on politics in an English country manor. This, if nothing else, resembled the England he had gleaned from Mrs. Humphry Ward’s fiction, though not with perfect fidelity. Perhaps the discrepancy arose because her narratives chiefly portrayed the England of the eighteen-nineties, and the years since had stripped away a measure of its grandeur. Yet here, undeniably, were titled persons and men of political weight, and their discourse circled round the “land.”
source_text: Mr. Direck found himself the interested auditor of a real English country-house week-end political conversation. This at any rate was like the England of which Mrs. Humphry Ward's novels had informed him, but yet not exactly like it. Perhaps that was due to the fact that for the most part these novels dealt with the England of the 'nineties, and things had lost a little in dignity since those days. But at any rate here were political figures and titled people, and they were talking about the "country."...

text: Well," he murmured, returning to her side after shutting the gate with deliberate care. "What I truly wished for was a chance to mention something that might matter to you—if by chance it does matter to you.... I expect I should state it as plainly as I can.... In plain truth.... I'm utterly and completely in love with you.... I felt I wanted you to know....
source_text: "Well," he said as he rejoined her after very carefully closing the gate. "What I really wanted was an opportunity of just mentioning something that happens to be of interest to you--if it does happen to interest you.... I suppose I'd better put the thing as simply as possible.... Practically.... I'm just right over the head and all in love with you.... I thought I'd like to tell you...."

text: Hugh spent the whole of August at home. He had abandoned his London lodgings along with his painter’s aspirations, and his father was quietly arranging for him to follow Cardinal to Cambridge. In the meantime, Hugh resumed his scientific studies exactly where he had left off. He grudgingly devoted a couple of afternoon hours to the puzzles of Little-go Greek, and otherwise occupied himself with mathematics, mathematical physics, or experiments in a small upstairs room carved from the barn’s loft. Only in the final days of August did it begin to occur to him—or to Mr. Britling—that the war might demand more than a spectator’s distant sympathy. Until now, contemporary history had unfolded without his personal involvement. He saw no reason why it should not continue to do so with the same detachment. The last general election—the sole occasion on which a reasonable Englishman’s life touches the public sphere—had taken place four years earlier, when he was thirteen.
source_text: Hugh was at home all through August. He had thrown up his rooms in London with his artistic ambitions, and his father was making all the necessary arrangements for him to follow Cardinal to Cambridge. Meanwhile Hugh was taking up his scientific work where he had laid it down. He gave a reluctant couple of hours in the afternoon to the mysteries of Little-go Greek, and for the rest of his time he was either working at mathematics and mathematical physics or experimenting in a little upstairs room that had been carved out of the general space of the barn. It was only at the very end of August that it dawned upon him or Mr. Britling that the war might have more than a spectacular and sympathetic appeal for him. Hitherto contemporary history had happened without his personal intervention. He did not see why it should not continue to happen with the same detachment. The last elections--and a general election is really the only point at which the life of the reasonable Englishman becomes in any way public--had happened four years ago, when he was thirteen.

text: Countless souls that morning, already claimed by Fate for slaughter or disfigurement, gaped at the tidings and grinned at the thrill of the banner-prints, never once imagining that any of this havoc would draw within a hundred leagues of their own hearths. What was a foreign war to Mallow's End—to all the Mallow's Ends drowsing and thriving that compose this island? The final cottage ever set ablaze by a foe inside a hundred leagues of Mallow's End was torched by Norse raiders somewhat more than a millennium ago.... And the sole surviving relic of those very Norsemen on English soil consisted of certain leathery scraps of toughened flesh wedged beneath the nail-heads in the door of St. Clement Danes in London....
source_text: Countless men that day whom Fate had marked for death and wounds stared open-mouthed at the news, and smiled with the excitement of the headlines, not dreaming that any of these things would come within three hundred miles of them. What was war to Matching's Easy--to all the Matching's Easies great and small that make up England? The last home that was ever burnt by an enemy within a hundred miles of Matching's Easy was burnt by the Danes rather more than a thousand years ago.... And the last trace of those particular Danes in England were certain horny scraps of indurated skin under the heads of the nails in the door of St. Clement Danes in London....

text: For a while he remained silent, offering only a distracted ear as Mr. Direck endeavoured to sketch the temper of New England regarding the Irish Question, along with the tangled predicaments confronting any American statesman on that score. When Mr. Britling resumed speaking, his words scarcely touched upon Mr. Direck’s remarks.
source_text: For a time he said no more; and listened perfunctorily while Mr. Direck tried to indicate the feeling in New England towards the Irish Question and the many difficult propositions an American politician has to face in that respect. And when Mr. Britling took up the thread of speech again it had little or no relation to Mr. Direck's observations.
